srt.q: SRS Winston-Lutz tolerance?
srt.a: ≤ 1 mm
c: ≤ 1 mm
≤ 2 mm
≤ 3 mm
srt.e: SRS demands sub-mm precision. Winston-Lutz verifies radiation and mechanical isocentre coincidence.

srt.q: Small-field dosimetry protocol?
srt.a: TRS-483
c: TG-51
TRS-398
TRS-483
srt.e: IAEA TRS-483 provides the framework for small and non-standard field dosimetry.

srt.q: SBRT motion management threshold?
srt.a: > 5 mm
c: > 2 mm
> 5 mm
> 10 mm
> 15 mm
srt.e: If tumour motion exceeds 5 mm on 4D-CT, active management is required.

srt.q: FFF beam profile shape?
srt.a: Peaked (non-flat)
c: Flat for accurate dose delivery
Peaked (non-flat)
Concave for quality assurance purposes
Saddle-shaped
srt.e: No flattening filter → peaked intensity, sharper penumbra, lower out-of-field dose.

srt.q: SRS planning grid size?
srt.a: ≤ 2 mm
c: ≤ 1 mm
≤ 2 mm
≤ 5 mm
srt.e: 1 mm ideal for SRS; 2 mm acceptable for SBRT.

srt.q: Ideal conformity index?
srt.e: CI = prescription isodose volume / target volume. 1.0 = perfect conformity.

srt.q: SBRT fractionation (SASQART)?
srt.a: ≥ 5 Gy, ≤ 5 fx
c: ≥ 2 Gy, ≤ 30 fx
≥ 5 Gy, ≤ 5 fx
≥ 8 Gy, ≤ 3 fx
≥ 10 Gy, 1 fx
srt.e: SASQART 2025: ≥ 5 Gy per fraction in ≤ 5 fractions.

srt.q: E2E test is?
srt.a: CT → plan → deliver → measure
c: Output check during the planning stage
CT → plan → deliver → measure
MLC calibration in modern radiation oncology
Beam scan per standard operating procedures
srt.e: Full chain test with anthropomorphic phantom. Gold standard for SRT commissioning.

srt.q: Gradient index measures?
srt.a: Dose falloff steepness
c: Target coverage
Dose falloff steepness
OAR sparing
Output constancy
srt.e: GI = V50%/Vprescription. Lower = steeper falloff = better.

srt.q: TG-101 covers?
srt.a: SBRT/SRS guidance
c: Linac QA
IMRT commissioning
SBRT/SRS guidance
Shielding
srt.e: Comprehensive SBRT guidance: tolerances, QA, clinical protocols.

srt.q: TRS-483 small-field condition #1?
srt.a: Loss of lateral CPE
c: Loss of lateral CPE
Detector saturation
Beam hardening
Penumbra widening
srt.e: When field < lateral electron range, charged particle equilibrium is lost.

srt.q: Recommended SRS detector?
srt.a: microDiamond
c: Farmer chamber
microDiamond
Parallel plate
Pencil chamber
srt.e: PTW 60019: ~0.004 mm³ volume — ideal for small-field OFs per TRS-483.

srt.q: Paddick CI penalises?
srt.a: Over- and under-coverage
c: Only underdose
Only overdose
Over- and under-coverage
Conformity only
srt.e: PCI = (TV_PIV)²/(TV × PIV). Penalises both miss and excess.

srt.q: Frame-based vs frameless SRS?
srt.a: Frameless is current standard
c: Frame-based is standard
Frameless is current standard
No difference
Frameless is experimental
srt.e: Frameless + IGRT is standard. SASQART: sole laser positioning no longer acceptable.

srt.q: SBRT isocenter tolerance?
srt.a: ≤ 2 mm
c: ≤ 1 mm
≤ 2 mm
≤ 5 mm
srt.e: Slightly larger than SRS (≤ 1 mm) due to bigger PTV margins.

srt.q: Why not Farmer chamber for 1×1 cm² field?
srt.a: Volume averaging
c: Too sensitive
Volume averaging
Not waterproof
Polarity issues
srt.e: reads too high. 0.6 cc volume averages over the steep gradient, giving falsely high readings.

srt.q: TRS-483 output correction factor symbol?
srt.a: k_Qclin,Qmsr
c: k_Q for quality assurance purposes
k_TP according to clinical protocols
k_Qclin,Qmsr
k_s for accurate dose delivery
srt.e: Corrects detector reading in small clinical field relative to the machine-specific reference field.

srt.q: SRS single-fraction intracranial dose typically?
c: 5-8 Gy
srt.e: Single-fraction SRS for brain mets typically 15-24 Gy depending on target size.

srt.q: CALC: PCI if TV=2cc, PIV=3cc, TV_PIV=1.8cc?
CALC: GI if V50%=12cc and V100%=4cc?
srt.e: GI = V50%/V100% = 12/4 = 3.0. Lower is better.

srt.q: CALC: CI if 95% isodose volume=5cc, PTV=4cc?
srt.e: CI = PIV/TV = 5/4 = 1.25. Slightly over-irradiation.

srt.q: 4D-CT primarily assesses?
srt.a: Tumour respiratory motion
c: Image noise in the radiation therapy context
Tumour respiratory motion
Contrast uptake for radiation dose management
Bone density for radiation dose management
srt.e: Bins CT acquisition by breathing phase to visualise motion extent for ITV definition.

srt.q: ITV stands for?
srt.a: Internal Target Volume
c: Irradiated Target Volume
Internal Target Volume
Integrated Treatment Volume
Isodose Target Volume
srt.e: Encompasses CTV throughout all phases of respiratory motion. Used for SBRT lung.

srt.q: E2E dosimetric accuracy tolerance for SRS?
srt.e: Combined uncertainty from planning, delivery, and measurement. 5% is accepted standard.

srt.q: Cone-based SRS: what defines field size?
srt.a: Fixed circular collimator
c: MLC leaves as specified by guidelines
Fixed circular collimator
Jaws only as specified by guidelines
Electron applicator
srt.e: Tertiary cones provide sharp, fixed-diameter circular fields for small brain targets.

srt.q: Which MLC is preferred for intracranial SRS?
c: Millennium 120 (5 mm)
SX2 dual-layer
Agility (5 mm)
srt.e: 2.5 mm central leaf width provides superior conformity for small brain mets.

srt.q: SBRT lung: typical PTV margin?
srt.a: 3-5 mm
c: 0-1 mm
3-5 mm
20 mm
srt.e: Accounts for setup uncertainty and residual motion after ITV definition.

srt.q: SRS brain: typical PTV margin?
srt.a: 0-2 mm
c: 0-2 mm
5-7 mm
10 mm
15 mm
srt.e: Very tight margins due to rigid immobilisation and high-precision IGRT.

srt.q: CALC: Farmer reads OF=0.72 for 1×1cm², microDiamond reads 0.64. Which is correct?
srt.a: 0.64 (microDiamond)
c: 0.72 (Farmer)
0.64 (microDiamond)
Average: 0.68
Neither — use film
srt.e: Farmer volume-averages in small fields → falsely high. Trust small-volume detector per TRS-483.

srt.q: Non-coplanar arcs are essential for?
srt.a: Intracranial SRS conformity
c: Breast tangents for accurate dose delivery
Intracranial SRS conformity
Prostate VMAT as part of the clinical workflow
Pelvis IMRT for the treatment procedure
srt.e: Multiple non-coplanar arcs create steep dose gradients around brain targets. Requires C-arm linac.

srt.q: Lung SBRT preferred algorithm?
srt.a: AcurosXB
c: AAA
AcurosXB
Pencil beam
Superposition
srt.e: Solves LBTE — accurate in heterogeneous lung tissue where AAA overestimates by 5-15%.

srt.q: Spine SBRT critical OAR tolerance?
srt.a: Cord Dmax < 14 Gy (1 fx)
c: Cord Dmax < 5 Gy
Cord Dmax < 14 Gy (1 fx)
Cord Dmax < 30 Gy
Cord Dmean < 20 Gy
srt.e: Spinal cord is the dose-limiting structure. Steep gradients needed to spare cord while treating vertebral body.

srt.q: SBRT liver: typical dose?
c: 5 × 4 Gy = 20 Gy
srt.e: Common regimen: 45 Gy in 3 fractions. Motion management essential (gating/tracking).

srt.q: ExacTrac is?
srt.a: kV X-ray stereoscopic positioning system
c: MV portal system — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
kV X-ray stereoscopic positioning system
Ultrasound tracker — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Surface guidance system
srt.e: Two floor-mounted kV X-ray tubes provide real-time stereoscopic verification for SRS positioning.

srt.q: Surface-guided RT (SGRT) uses?
srt.a: Optical surface tracking cameras
c: Implanted fiducials
Optical surface tracking cameras
Electromagnetic transponders
Ultrasound probes
srt.e: AlignRT/Catalyst: camera-based surface monitoring for real-time intrafraction tracking.

srt.q: Typical SRS treatment time (single met)?
srt.a: 3-10 minutes
c: 30-60 seconds
3-10 minutes
30-45 minutes
2-3 hours
srt.e: FFF beams + VMAT arcs enable rapid delivery. Multiple mets take longer.

srt.q: CALC: Beam ON time for 2000 MU at 2400 MU/min?
srt.a: 50 seconds
c: 50 seconds
83 seconds
120 seconds
200 seconds
srt.e: Time = 2000/2400 = 0.833 min = 50 seconds.

srt.q: Flattening filter free: what is removed?
srt.a: Conical metal filter in beam path
c: Ionisation chamber
Conical metal filter in beam path
Primary collimator
Target — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
srt.e: The flattening filter (typically steel/copper) that creates uniform profile is physically removed.

srt.q: What does the abbreviation SRS stand for in radiation therapy?
srt.a: Stereotactic Radiosurgery
c: Stereotactic Radiosurgery
Standard Radiation System
Sequential Radiation Strategy
Systematic Radiotherapy Session
srt.e: SRS stands for Stereotactic Radiosurgery, a technique that delivers a high dose of radiation to a small, precisely defined target, typically in the brain, in a single or few fractions.

srt.q: What does SBRT stand for?
srt.a: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy
c: Standard Beam Radiation Treatment
Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy
Sequential Body Radiotherapy Technique
Systematic Bilateral Radiation Therapy
srt.e: SBRT stands for Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy. It uses the same principles as SRS but is applied to targets outside the brain, such as lung, liver, or spine tumours.

srt.q: What is a key characteristic of stereotactic treatments compared to conventional radiotherapy?
srt.a: They deliver high doses to very small, precisely targeted volumes
c: They always use proton beams according to clinical protocols
They deliver high doses to very small, precisely targeted volumes
They treat the entire body at once in the treatment process
They use only low-energy X-rays for quality assurance purposes
srt.e: Stereotactic treatments are characterised by the delivery of high radiation doses to small, well-defined target volumes with very steep dose gradients, requiring high precision in patient positioning and beam delivery.

srt.q: In stereotactic treatments, why is precise patient immobilisation important?
srt.a: To ensure the radiation is accurately delivered to the target
c: To make the patient more comfortable
To reduce the cost of treatment in radiation therapy practice
To ensure the radiation is accurately delivered to the target
To increase the treatment time in radiation therapy practice
srt.e: Because stereotactic treatments deliver high doses to very small volumes with steep dose fall-off, even small positioning errors can cause the target to be missed or surrounding healthy tissue to receive excessive dose. Precise immobilisation is therefore essential.

srt.q: What type of field sizes are typically used in stereotactic radiosurgery?
srt.a: Small fields, often less than 4 cm
c: Very large fields covering the whole body
Small fields, often less than 4 cm
Medium fields of 20 to 30 cm
Only rectangular fields of exactly 10 cm
srt.e: SRS uses small radiation fields, often less than 4 cm in diameter, to conform the high-dose region tightly to the small target volume. This is why accurate small-field dosimetry is critical for these treatments.

srt.q: CALC: A 0.5 cm brain metastasis is treated with SRS via a 5 mm cone. The microDiamond detector measures an output factor of 0.620 relative to the 10×10 cm² msr field. TRS-483 lists k_Qclin,Qmsr = 0.976 for this detector/field. What is the corrected output factor?
srt.e: Corrected OF = measured OF × k_Qclin,Qmsr = 0.620 × 0.976 = 0.6055 ≈ 0.605. TRS-483 correction factors account for detector volume-averaging and density perturbation effects that cause even the microDiamond to slightly over-respond in very small fields. Omitting this correction would overdose the patient by approximately 2.5%.

srt.q: CALC: A lung SBRT plan prescribes 50 Gy/5 fx. The 50% isodose volume is 64 cm³ and the prescription isodose volume (PIV) is 25 cm³. What is the Paddick Gradient Index (GI)?
srt.e: Gradient Index = V_50% / PIV = 64 / 25 = 2.56. GI quantifies dose falloff steepness outside the target. Lower GI means steeper gradient and better normal tissue sparing. Values below 3.0 are generally considered acceptable for SRS/SBRT plans.

srt.q: CALC: A single-isocenter multi-target (SIMT) SRS plan treats 4 brain mets. The farthest target is 7 cm off-axis. If gantry sag introduces 0.2° rotational error, what is the approximate positional displacement at that off-axis target?
srt.e: Displacement = radius × angle(rad) = 70 mm × (0.2 × π/180) = 70 × 0.00349 = 0.244 mm ≈ 0.24 mm. For SIMT SRS, rotational errors scale linearly with off-axis distance, making distant targets most vulnerable. This is why recent guidance recommends per-target PTV margin analysis and sub-degree rotational accuracy for SIMT treatments.

srt.q: CALC: For a 1×1 cm² field, a diode measures OF = 0.710 (TRS-483 k = 0.952) and a microDiamond measures OF = 0.680 (k = 0.994). What are the corrected output factors and which detector is preferred?
srt.a: Both give ~0.676; microDiamond preferred (smaller correction)
c: 0.710 from diode — no correction needed
Both give ~0.676; microDiamond preferred (smaller correction)
0.680 from microDiamond — no correction needed
Results disagree — use Monte Carlo
srt.e: Diode corrected: 0.710 × 0.952 = 0.676. microDiamond corrected: 0.680 × 0.994 = 0.676. Both agree after TRS-483 corrections, providing high confidence. The microDiamond is preferred because its correction factor is closer to unity (0.994 vs 0.952), meaning it has less intrinsic perturbation and lower correction uncertainty.

srt.q: CALC: A spine SBRT plan delivers 24 Gy/2 fx. The spinal cord Dmax is 12 Gy in 2 fx. Using α/β = 2 Gy for cord, what is the cord BED and EQD2?
srt.a: BED = 48 Gy₂, EQD2 = 24 Gy
c: BED = 24 Gy₂, EQD2 = 12 Gy
BED = 48 Gy₂, EQD2 = 24 Gy
BED = 36 Gy₂, EQD2 = 18 Gy
BED = 60 Gy₂, EQD2 = 30 Gy
srt.e: Dose per fraction to cord = 12/2 = 6 Gy. BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 2 × 6 × (1 + 6/2) = 12 × 4 = 48 Gy₂. EQD2 = BED/(1 + 2/(α/β)) = 48/(1 + 2/2) = 48/2 = 24 Gy. This is just under the commonly cited spinal cord EQD2 tolerance of approximately 25 Gy for partial volume, illustrating the tight margins in spine SBRT planning.

srt.q: In TRS-483, what is the purpose of the field output correction factor k_Qclin,Qmsr?
srt.a: Corrects for detector volume-averaging and fluence perturbation in small fields
c: Converts absorbed dose from water to tissue equivalent materials
Corrects for detector volume-averaging and fluence perturbation in small fields
Accounts for beam quality differences between Co-60 and linac beams
Adjusts output for temperature and pressure variations during measurement
srt.e: TRS-483 introduces k_Qclin,Qmsr to correct detector-specific perturbations in small fields where lateral charged particle equilibrium is lost. Detector volume averaging and fluence perturbation cause incorrect readings without this correction.

srt.q: SBRT lung treatment: what margin strategy accounts for respiratory motion assessed by 4D-CT?
srt.a: ITV from all phases of 4D-CT with reduced PTV margin (3-5 mm)
c: Uniform 2 cm CTV-to-PTV margin without motion assessment
ITV from all phases of 4D-CT with reduced PTV margin (3-5 mm)
No margin needed if gating is available on the linac
MIP reconstruction only with standard 1 cm PTV margin
srt.e: 4D-CT generates an ITV encompassing tumour motion across all respiratory phases. Because motion is already incorporated in the ITV, the PTV margin can be reduced to 3-5 mm to account for setup uncertainty only.

srt.q: Why is the Winston-Lutz test performed at multiple gantry, collimator, and couch angles?
srt.a: To detect mechanical isocentre deviations contributed independently by each axis of rotation
c: To verify the MLC leaf speed is consistent at all angles
To ensure the beam flatness is uniform regardless of gantry angle
To detect mechanical isocentre deviations contributed independently by each axis of rotation
To calibrate the on-board imaging system at multiple positions
srt.e: Each rotating axis (gantry, collimator, couch) has its own mechanical flex and sag. Testing at multiple angle combinations isolates each axis contribution to isocentre walk-out, ensuring total coincidence meets SRS tolerance (≤1 mm).

srt.q: CALC: SRS single-fraction plan delivers 20 Gy to a metastasis. Using the LQ model with alpha/beta = 10 Gy, what is the BED?
srt.a: 60 Gy
c: 40 Gy
60 Gy
80 Gy
100 Gy
srt.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(alpha/beta)) = 1 × 20 × (1 + 20/10) = 20 × 3 = 60 Gy₁₀. Single fraction delivers high BED due to the large dose per fraction.

srt.q: In cranial SRS using a cone-based system, why is a Monte Carlo dose algorithm preferred over pencil beam convolution?
srt.a: Monte Carlo accurately models lateral electron disequilibrium and tissue heterogeneity effects in small fields
c: Monte Carlo is faster to compute, reducing planning time significantly
Pencil beam cannot model any field size below 10 cm accurately
Monte Carlo accurately models lateral electron disequilibrium and tissue heterogeneity effects in small fields
Pencil beam overestimates dose uniformly by exactly 5% in all SRS fields
srt.e: In SRS cone fields (typically 4-30 mm), lateral charged particle equilibrium is lost. Pencil beam algorithms assume equilibrium and fail to model electron transport accurately at tissue interfaces. Monte Carlo simulates individual particle histories, correctly predicting dose in heterogeneous small-field geometries.

srt.q: CALC: A stereotactic cone has a field size of 7.5 mm. The TRS-483 output factor measured with a microdiamond detector is 0.825, and k_Qclin,Qmsr for this detector at this field size is 0.988. What is the corrected output factor?
srt.e: Corrected OF = measured OF × k_Qclin,Qmsr = 0.825 × 0.988 = 0.8151 ≈ 0.815. The correction factor accounts for the detector over-response in this small field.

srt.q: TRS-483 defines a small field primarily when which condition is met?
srt.a: Loss of lateral charged particle equilibrium
c: Field size is below 10 × 10 cm²
Loss of lateral charged particle equilibrium
The beam energy exceeds 10 MV
The source-to-surface distance exceeds 100 cm
srt.e: TRS-483 identifies three hallmarks of small fields: loss of lateral charged particle equilibrium, partial occlusion of the primary source, and detector size approaching the field size. Loss of LCPE is the fundamental physical condition.

srt.q: What is the machine-specific reference field for CyberKnife in TRS-483?
srt.a: The largest available fixed cone (60 mm)
c: A 10 × 10 cm² jaw-defined field
A 5 × 5 cm² MLC-shaped field
The largest available fixed cone (60 mm)
A 3 × 3 cm² IRIS collimator field
srt.e: CyberKnife cannot produce a conventional 10 × 10 cm² field. TRS-483 defines the machine-specific reference (msr) field as the largest fixed cone (60 mm diameter) for such non-standard machines.

srt.q: Which detector has the smallest perturbation correction in sub-centimetre fields?
srt.a: Plastic scintillator detector
c: Farmer-type ionisation chamber
Plastic scintillator detector
Semiflex 3D ionisation chamber
Parallel-plate ionisation chamber
srt.e: Plastic scintillator detectors have a very small sensitive volume and are nearly water-equivalent (Zeff close to water). This results in minimal perturbation and volume averaging corrections in small fields.

srt.q: CALC: A 6 MV SRS plan delivers 21 Gy in 1 fraction. What is the BED for tumour (alpha/beta = 10 Gy)?
srt.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 1 × 21 × (1 + 21/10) = 21 × 3.1 = 65.1 Gy. This high BED reflects the ablative radiobiological effect of single-fraction SRS treatments.

srt.q: How many cobalt-60 sources does the GammaKnife Perfexion/Icon contain?
srt.a: 192 sources
c: 60 sources
120 sources
192 sources
256 sources
srt.e: The GammaKnife Perfexion and Icon models use 192 cobalt-60 sources arranged in a hemispheric configuration. Sectors can be moved to produce 4 mm, 8 mm, and 16 mm shot sizes.

srt.q: CALC: SBRT liver delivers 50 Gy in 5 fractions. What is the EQD2 for tumour (alpha/beta = 10 Gy)?
srt.e: d = 50/5 = 10 Gy. EQD2 = D × (d + α/β)/(2 + α/β) = 50 × (10 + 10)/(2 + 10) = 50 × 20/12 = 83.3 Gy. The high dose per fraction substantially increases the equivalent dose.

srt.q: What is the primary advantage of cone-based collimation over MLC for SRS?
srt.a: Sharper penumbra due to closer proximity to isocentre
c: Ability to treat irregularly shaped targets easily
Sharper penumbra due to closer proximity to isocentre
Faster treatment delivery due to higher dose rate
Elimination of the need for Winston-Lutz testing
srt.e: Cones are positioned closer to the isocentre than MLCs, producing a sharper dose penumbra. This geometric advantage makes them preferred for very small, spherical targets in SRS.

srt.q: What is the recommended Winston-Lutz testing frequency per TG-142?
srt.a: Daily before SRS treatments
c: Monthly during routine QA
Weekly as part of safety checks
Daily before SRS treatments
Annually during machine commissioning
srt.e: TG-142 recommends daily Winston-Lutz testing on days SRS/SRT treatments are performed. This verifies that the radiation and mechanical isocentres coincide within tolerance.

srt.q: CALC: A spine SBRT plan delivers 24 Gy in 3 fractions to the PTV. What is the BED for the spinal cord (alpha/beta = 2 Gy) if cord Dmax is 18 Gy in 3 fractions?
srt.e: d(cord) = 18/3 = 6 Gy per fraction. BED = 18 × (1 + 6/2) = 18 × 4 = 72.0 Gy₂. Spinal cord has a low alpha/beta, so hypofractionation dramatically increases BED for late-responding tissues.

srt.q: In TRS-483, what does the symbol k_Qclin,Qmsr represent?
srt.a: The detector-specific field output correction factor
c: The beam quality correction for reference dosimetry
The detector-specific field output correction factor
The temperature and pressure correction factor
The ion recombination correction for small fields
srt.e: k_Qclin,Qmsr is the field output correction factor that accounts for detector-specific perturbations in the clinical small field relative to the machine-specific reference field. It corrects for volume averaging and density effects.

srt.q: Why is a microDiamond detector preferred over a Pinpoint chamber for fields below 1 cm?
srt.a: Its sensitive volume is much smaller, reducing volume averaging
c: It has a higher sensitivity to low-energy scattered photons
Its sensitive volume is much smaller, reducing volume averaging
It requires no correction factors in any field size
It can measure dose rate independently of field size
srt.e: The PTW microDiamond has an active volume of approximately 0.004 mm³ compared to ~0.015 cm³ for a Pinpoint chamber. This dramatically reduces volume averaging in sub-centimetre fields.

srt.q: What immobilisation device is standard for frame-based intracranial SRS?
srt.a: Invasive stereotactic head frame pinned to the skull
c: Invasive stereotactic head frame pinned to the skull
Thermoplastic mask with bite block fixation
Vacuum cushion with optical surface monitoring
Reinforced thermoplastic shell with S-frame
srt.e: Frame-based SRS uses an invasive metal frame (e.g., Leksell frame) pinned to the skull under local anaesthesia. It provides sub-millimetre immobilisation accuracy and defines the stereotactic coordinate system.

srt.q: CALC: A diode measures an output factor of 0.710 for a 1 × 1 cm² field. The TRS-483 correction factor k is 0.996. What is the corrected output factor?
srt.e: Corrected OF = measured OF × k = 0.710 × 0.996 = 0.707. Diodes slightly over-respond in small fields due to their high-Z material, so the correction factor is slightly less than 1.

srt.q: TG-101 recommends what minimum number of non-coplanar beam angles for intracranial SRS?
srt.a: At least 4-5 non-coplanar arcs or beam angles
c: A single coplanar arc is always sufficient
At least 2-3 coplanar opposed beam angles
At least 4-5 non-coplanar arcs or beam angles
A minimum of 10 equally-spaced coplanar beams
srt.e: TG-101 recommends multiple non-coplanar arcs or beams (typically 4-5 or more) for intracranial SRS. Non-coplanarity spreads the low-dose bath and improves conformality and gradient index.

srt.q: What is the typical prescription isodose line for GammaKnife treatments?
srt.a: 50% isodose line
c: 50% isodose line
80% isodose line
90% isodose line
95% isodose line
srt.e: GammaKnife typically prescribes to the 50% isodose line. This means the maximum dose within the target is twice the prescription dose, which produces a very steep dose gradient at the target periphery.

srt.q: CALC: A lung SBRT plan delivers 54 Gy in 3 fractions. What is the EQD2 for tumour (alpha/beta = 10 Gy)?
srt.e: d = 54/3 = 18 Gy. EQD2 = D × (d + α/β)/(2 + α/β) = 54 × (18 + 10)/(2 + 10) = 54 × 28/12 = 126.0 Gy. This very high EQD2 explains the excellent local control rates seen in lung SBRT.

srt.q: Which CyberKnife tracking system compensates for respiratory motion in real time?
srt.a: Synchrony respiratory tracking system
c: ExacTrac Dynamic X-ray system
Synchrony respiratory tracking system
Calypso electromagnetic transponder system
BrainLab surface-guided monitoring system
srt.e: The CyberKnife Synchrony system uses external LED markers on the chest correlated with internal fiducial positions from orthogonal X-rays. The robotic arm adjusts beam delivery in real time to follow tumour motion.

srt.q: What is the principal concern when using unshielded silicon diodes for small field dosimetry?
srt.a: Over-response due to high-Z silicon in low-energy scatter
c: Under-response due to low sensitivity in small volumes
Over-response due to high-Z silicon in low-energy scatter
Temperature dependence exceeding 2% per degree Celsius
Directional dependence only in coplanar beam geometry
srt.e: Unshielded diodes over-respond in small fields because their silicon sensitive volume (Z=14) has enhanced photoelectric absorption of low-energy scattered photons. TRS-483 provides correction factors to account for this.

srt.q: CALC: BED for 48 Gy in 4 fractions to a chest wall (alpha/beta = 3 Gy)?
srt.e: d = 48/4 = 12 Gy. BED = 48 × (1 + 12/3) = 48 × 5 = 240.0 Gy₃. This very high BED for late-responding tissue explains the risk of chest wall toxicity in lung SBRT.

srt.q: CALC: An Farmer chamber reads an output factor of 0.780 for a 2 × 2 cm² field. The microDiamond reads 0.725. The expected true OF from Monte Carlo is 0.720. What is the Farmer volume averaging error?
srt.e: Volume averaging error = (0.780 - 0.720)/0.720 × 100 = 0.060/0.720 × 100 = 8.3%. The Farmer chamber's large collecting volume (0.6 cm³) averages over a significant dose gradient in this field.

srt.q: What is the purpose of the stereotactic body frame in SBRT?
srt.a: Reproducible immobilisation with external coordinate reference
c: Reducing the dose rate during treatment delivery
Reproducible immobilisation with external coordinate reference
Eliminating the need for image-guided verification
Increasing the beam penetration in soft tissue targets
srt.e: The stereotactic body frame (e.g., Elekta body frame) provides rigid immobilisation and an external fiducial coordinate system. It enables accurate target localisation and reproducible setup between imaging and treatment.

srt.q: In Monte Carlo dose calculation, what makes it more accurate than analytical algorithms in small fields?
srt.a: Explicit modelling of electron transport and scatter conditions
c: Use of a fixed tissue density for all calculation voxels
Explicit modelling of electron transport and scatter conditions
Faster computation time allowing finer grid resolution
Application of broad-beam convolution kernels at all depths
srt.e: Monte Carlo explicitly simulates individual particle interactions and transport, accurately modelling the lack of lateral electronic equilibrium and complex scatter conditions in small fields. Analytical algorithms rely on broad-beam kernels that break down in these conditions.

srt.q: CALC: A single-isocentre multi-target SRS plan treats 3 mets. The total MU is 8500 and the beam-on time is 5.3 minutes at 1600 MU/min. What percentage of total MU is delivered?
srt.e: MU delivered = 1600 × 5.3 = 8480 MU. Percentage = 8480/8500 × 100 = 99.76% ≈ 99.8%. This question tests the relationship between dose rate, time, and total MU delivery.

srt.q: What motion management technique uses fiducial markers and kV imaging to track tumour position during SBRT?
srt.a: Real-time fluoroscopic tumour tracking
c: Abdominal compression with a belt device
Voluntary deep inspiration breath hold technique
Real-time fluoroscopic tumour tracking
Slow CT scanning with phase-averaged images
srt.e: Fluoroscopic tumour tracking (e.g., CyberKnife Synchrony, Vero) uses implanted fiducial markers visualised with kV X-ray imaging to track and compensate for tumour motion during beam delivery.

srt.q: What is the typical leaf width of an HD-MLC used for intracranial SRS on Varian systems?
srt.a: 2.5 mm at isocentre for central leaves
c: 1.0 mm at isocentre for all leaves
2.5 mm at isocentre for central leaves
5.0 mm at isocentre for central leaves
10.0 mm at isocentre for central leaves
srt.e: The Varian HD-MLC (High-Definition MLC) has 2.5 mm leaf width at isocentre for the central 32 leaf pairs, and 5.0 mm for the outer 28 pairs. This resolution is important for conforming to small intracranial targets.

srt.q: CALC: EQD2 for spinal cord receiving 14 Gy in 1 fraction (alpha/beta = 2 Gy)?
srt.e: EQD2 = D × (d + α/β)/(2 + α/β) = 14 × (14 + 2)/(2 + 2) = 14 × 16/4 = 56.0 Gy. The high single-fraction dose results in a very large EQD2 for late-responding spinal cord tissue.

srt.q: What is the primary advantage of flattening filter free (FFF) beams for SBRT?
srt.a: Higher dose rate enabling shorter treatment times
c: More uniform beam profile across the field
Higher dose rate enabling shorter treatment times
Lower surface dose compared to flattened beams
Elimination of need for output factor corrections
srt.e: FFF beams can achieve dose rates of 1400-2400 MU/min compared to 400-600 MU/min for flattened beams. The shorter treatment time reduces intrafraction motion, which is critical for SBRT accuracy.

srt.q: What is the TG-101 recommended overall spatial accuracy for linac-based SBRT?
srt.a: ≤ 1 mm end-to-end spatial accuracy
c: ≤ 0.5 mm end-to-end spatial accuracy
≤ 1 mm end-to-end spatial accuracy
≤ 2 mm end-to-end spatial accuracy
≤ 3 mm end-to-end spatial accuracy
srt.e: TG-101 recommends that the overall end-to-end spatial accuracy for SBRT should be within 1 mm. This is verified through end-to-end phantom testing encompassing imaging, planning, and delivery.

srt.q: CALC: A 5 mm cone produces an output factor of 0.650 measured with film. The microDiamond measures 0.658 with k = 0.990. What is the microDiamond corrected OF?
srt.e: Corrected OF = measured OF × k = 0.658 × 0.990 = 0.6514 ≈ 0.651. Film is often used as a reference for very small fields because it has excellent spatial resolution and minimal volume averaging.

srt.q: In SBRT lung treatment, what imaging method is used to define the internal target volume (ITV)?
srt.a: 4D-CT scan capturing respiratory phases
c: Single helical CT with breath hold
4D-CT scan capturing respiratory phases
MRI with diffusion-weighted sequences
PET/CT with respiratory gating disabled
srt.e: 4D-CT acquires images across all respiratory phases, allowing visualisation of tumour motion throughout the breathing cycle. The ITV is created by contouring the tumour on each phase and combining the volumes.

srt.q: Why is source occlusion a concern in small field dosimetry?
srt.a: The collimator partially blocks the finite-size source from the detector's view
c: The collimator partially blocks the finite-size source from the detector's view
The radiation source physically moves when the field size changes
Multiple scattering events dominate the central axis dose profile
The monitor chamber cannot accurately measure low dose rates
srt.e: In small fields, the collimator edges can partially obscure the extended radiation source (focal spot) as seen from the measurement point. This reduces the fluence at the detector and alters the output factor compared to large fields.

srt.q: CALC: For a 3-fraction SBRT plan, the prescription is 30 Gy. The normal liver (alpha/beta = 2 Gy) receives a mean dose of 15 Gy. What is the liver mean BED?
srt.e: d(liver) = 15/3 = 5 Gy per fraction. BED = 15 × (1 + 5/2) = 15 × 3.5 = 52.5 Gy₂. The low alpha/beta of liver parenchyma means hypofractionation significantly increases BED for normal tissue.

srt.q: What is the main limitation of Gafchromic film for small field output factor measurements?
srt.a: Sensitivity dependence on scanning orientation and post-irradiation time
c: Film sensitive layer is too thick for sub-cm field measurements
Film cannot be submerged in water for phantom-based measurements
Sensitivity dependence on scanning orientation and post-irradiation time
Film saturates at doses below typical SRS prescription dose levels
srt.e: Gafchromic film requires careful handling: consistent scanning orientation (landscape vs portrait affects optical density), controlled post-irradiation development time (24-48 hours), and proper calibration. These factors introduce uncertainty if not controlled.

srt.q: The CyberKnife uses what type of radiation delivery system?
srt.a: Compact linac mounted on a 6-axis robotic arm
c: Co-60 sources in a hemispherical arrangement
Compact linac mounted on a 6-axis robotic arm
Standard C-arm linac with cone collimation
Helical tomotherapy ring-based delivery system
srt.e: The CyberKnife uses a compact 6 MV X-band linear accelerator mounted on a 6-axis robotic manipulator (Kuka robot). This provides non-isocentric, non-coplanar beam delivery from hundreds of possible positions.

srt.q: CALC: A GammaKnife plan uses a 16 mm shot with an output factor of 0.998 relative to the 16 mm reference. The 4 mm shot has OF = 0.815. What is the relative dose rate reduction when switching from 16 mm to 4 mm?
srt.e: Reduction = (0.998 - 0.815)/0.998 × 100 = 0.183/0.998 × 100 = 18.3%. The smaller collimator setting reduces the dose rate due to greater collimation of the cobalt-60 beam.

srt.q: What is the typical PTV margin for frameless intracranial SRS with mask immobilisation?
srt.a: 1-2 mm
c: 0 mm (no margin added)
1-2 mm
3-5 mm
srt.e: Frameless mask-based SRS typically uses 1-2 mm PTV margins to account for residual setup uncertainty. Frame-based SRS may use 0-1 mm margins due to the superior immobilisation accuracy of the invasive frame.

srt.q: In TRS-483, what is the recommended reference detector for cross-calibration in small fields?
srt.a: A detector with well-characterised correction factors from the protocol
c: Only Farmer-type chambers may serve as the reference standard
Any detector can be used without correction factor considerations
A detector with well-characterised correction factors from the protocol
Only film dosimetry is acceptable as the primary reference method
srt.e: TRS-483 recommends using a detector whose field output correction factors are well characterised and tabulated in the protocol. Multiple detectors should be used with independent correction factors to verify consistency.

srt.q: CALC: A patient receives 60 Gy in 5 fractions to a lung tumour. What is the BED for tumour (alpha/beta = 10 Gy)?
srt.e: d = 60/5 = 12 Gy. BED = 60 × (1 + 12/10) = 60 × 2.2 = 132.0 Gy₁₀. Lung SBRT protocols typically achieve BED₁₀ > 100 Gy for optimal tumour control.

srt.q: What is abdominal compression used for in SBRT treatments?
srt.a: Reducing diaphragmatic excursion and tumour motion amplitude
c: Increasing the dose rate at the target isocentre
Improving image contrast for CBCT verification scans
Reducing diaphragmatic excursion and tumour motion amplitude
Replacing the need for 4D-CT simulation imaging
srt.e: Abdominal compression applies gentle pressure to the anterior abdomen, limiting diaphragm excursion and reducing cranio-caudal tumour motion. This decreases the ITV size and allows tighter PTV margins in lung and liver SBRT.

srt.q: CALC: A PTW Pinpoint chamber (volume 0.016 cm³) measures OF = 0.745 in a 1.5 × 1.5 cm² field. The TRS-483 k factor is 0.993. What is the corrected OF?
srt.e: Corrected OF = 0.745 × 0.993 = 0.7398 ≈ 0.740. The Pinpoint chamber has a small enough volume for moderate small fields, but still requires a correction factor as tabulated in TRS-483.

srt.q: What is the Paddick conformity index formula?
srt.a: PCI = (TV_PIV)² / (TV × PIV)
c: PCI = PIV / TV
PCI = (TV_PIV)² / (TV × PIV)
PCI = TV / (TV + PIV)
PCI = TV_PIV / (PIV - TV)
srt.e: The Paddick conformity index accounts for both target coverage and dose spillage: PCI = (TV_PIV)² / (TV × PIV), where TV_PIV is the target volume within the prescription isodose. A perfect score is 1.0.

srt.q: What is the typical dose prescription for single-fraction lung SBRT for peripheral lesions?
srt.a: 30-34 Gy in 1 fraction
c: 12-15 Gy in 1 fraction
20-22 Gy in 1 fraction
30-34 Gy in 1 fraction
45-50 Gy in 1 fraction
srt.e: Single-fraction lung SBRT for small peripheral lesions typically prescribes 30-34 Gy (e.g., RTOG 0915). Central lesions near the proximal bronchial tree use multi-fraction regimens due to toxicity risk.

srt.q: CALC: A spine SBRT plan delivers 16 Gy in 1 fraction. The EQD2 for the tumour (alpha/beta = 10 Gy) is?
srt.e: EQD2 = D × (d + α/β)/(2 + α/β) = 16 × (16 + 10)/(2 + 10) = 16 × 26/12 = 34.7 Gy. Despite the high physical dose, the EQD2 is moderate because the tumour alpha/beta ratio is relatively high.

srt.q: What is the Winston-Lutz test specifically designed to verify?
srt.a: Coincidence of radiation and mechanical isocentres
c: Accuracy of the multileaf collimator leaf positions
Coincidence of radiation and mechanical isocentres
Flatness and symmetry of the radiation beam profile
Accuracy of the on-board imaging system calibration
srt.e: The Winston-Lutz test uses a radio-opaque ball at the mechanical isocentre and acquires portal images at various gantry, collimator, and couch angles. The offset between ball shadow and radiation field centre reveals isocentre coincidence errors.

srt.q: CALC: A patient with a brain metastasis receives 18 Gy in 1 fraction. What is the EQD2 for late-responding normal brain (alpha/beta = 2 Gy)?
srt.e: EQD2 = 18 × (18 + 2)/(2 + 2) = 18 × 20/4 = 18 × 5 = 90.0 Gy₂. This very high EQD2 for normal brain emphasises the importance of steep dose gradients to protect critical structures.

srt.q: What is the recommended TG-101 dose grid resolution for SBRT planning?
srt.a: ≤ 2 mm calculation grid spacing
c: ≤ 1 mm calculation grid spacing
≤ 2 mm calculation grid spacing
≤ 3 mm calculation grid spacing
≤ 5 mm calculation grid spacing
srt.e: TG-101 recommends a dose calculation grid of ≤ 2 mm for SBRT planning. This fine resolution is needed to accurately model the steep dose gradients characteristic of stereotactic treatments.

srt.q: Which beam model is most accurate for heterogeneous tissue dose calculation in lung SBRT?
srt.a: Monte Carlo or AcurosXB (linear Boltzmann transport)
c: Pencil beam convolution with 1D density correction
Analytical anisotropic algorithm with 3D superposition
Monte Carlo or AcurosXB (linear Boltzmann transport)
Clarkson sector integration with scatter corrections
srt.e: Monte Carlo and AcurosXB solve particle transport equations explicitly, accurately handling the lack of electronic equilibrium at tissue-lung interfaces. Simpler algorithms like pencil beam significantly overestimate dose in low-density regions.

srt.q: CALC: A Farmer chamber (0.6 cm³) reads 1.025 Gy and a microDiamond reads 0.962 Gy at the same point in a 1.5 × 1.5 cm² field. What is the percentage difference?
srt.e: Percentage difference = (1.025 - 0.962)/0.962 × 100 = 0.063/0.962 × 100 = 6.55% ≈ 6.5%. The Farmer over-reads due to volume averaging across the steep dose gradient in this small field.

srt.q: What is the recommended detector for end-to-end (E2E) dosimetric verification of a stereotactic plan according to TG-101?
srt.a: Radiochromic film in an anthropomorphic phantom
c: Farmer ionisation chamber in a slab phantom
Radiochromic film in an anthropomorphic phantom
Silicon diode array in a cylindrical phantom
Parallel-plate chamber in a scanning phantom
srt.e: TG-101 recommends end-to-end tests using radiochromic film inserted into a head or body anthropomorphic phantom to simultaneously verify spatial accuracy and dose delivery under realistic clinical conditions.

srt.q: CALC: A brain SRS plan delivers 24 Gy in 1 fraction to a 2.5 cm metastasis. Using the LQ model with alpha/beta = 10 Gy, what is the BED for the tumour?
srt.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(alpha/beta)) = 1 x 24 x (1 + 24/10) = 24 x 3.4 = 81.6 Gy. Single high-dose fractions produce high BED values, which is the radiobiological rationale for SRS efficacy.

srt.q: In single-isocentre multi-target (SIMT) SRS, what is the primary concern for off-axis targets located far from the isocentre?
srt.a: Rotational errors cause large translational shifts at distant targets
c: The MLC cannot physically reach targets beyond 5 cm from axis
Rotational errors cause large translational shifts at distant targets
Off-axis targets always receive higher dose than prescribed amount
The beam energy spectrum is too soft at peripheral target sites
srt.e: In SIMT plans, a small rotational error (e.g. 0.5 degrees) translates into a significant positional error at targets far from the isocentre (arc length = radius x angle in radians). At 7 cm off-axis, 1 degree rotation causes approximately 1.2 mm displacement.

srt.q: What is the typical mechanical isocentre accuracy requirement for a linac used for SRS treatments?
srt.a: Less than or equal to 1.0 mm diameter sphere
c: Less than or equal to 0.2 mm diameter sphere
Less than or equal to 1.0 mm diameter sphere
Less than or equal to 2.0 mm diameter sphere
Less than or equal to 3.0 mm diameter sphere
srt.e: For linac-based SRS, TG-142 and TG-101 recommend the mechanical isocentre (as measured by the Winston-Lutz test at all gantry and couch angles) be contained within a sphere of 1.0 mm diameter or less.

srt.q: CALC: A liver SBRT plan delivers 45 Gy in 3 fractions. The normal liver (alpha/beta = 2.5 Gy) receives a mean dose of 18 Gy in 3 fractions. What is the mean liver BED?
srt.e: Mean liver dose per fraction = 18/3 = 6 Gy. BED = 3 x 6 x (1 + 6/2.5) = 18 x (1 + 2.4) = 18 x 3.4 = 61.2 Gy. Liver tolerance must be carefully managed in SBRT to avoid radiation-induced liver disease.

srt.q: What is the main advantage of the GammaKnife Icon system over the Perfexion for brain SRS?
srt.a: Integrated cone-beam CT enables frameless mask-based treatments
c: It uses higher-activity cobalt-60 sources for faster delivery
Integrated cone-beam CT enables frameless mask-based treatments
It employs dynamic MLC shaping for non-spherical dose patterns
It supports body stereotactic treatments below the neck region
srt.e: The GammaKnife Icon introduced onboard CBCT and real-time infrared patient monitoring, enabling frameless thermoplastic mask-based SRS as an alternative to the invasive stereotactic frame.

srt.q: In TRS-483, what is the approximate equivalent square field size below which detector-specific output correction factors must be applied?
srt.a: Approximately 4 cm equivalent square
c: Approximately 2 cm equivalent square
Approximately 4 cm equivalent square
Approximately 6 cm equivalent square
Approximately 8 cm equivalent square
srt.e: TRS-483 defines small fields as those where lateral charged particle equilibrium is lost, source occlusion occurs, or detector perturbation is significant. Below approximately 4 x 4 cm equivalent square, detector-specific correction factors are needed.

srt.q: What is the primary clinical rationale for using hypofractionated SBRT over conventional fractionation for early-stage peripheral lung cancer?
srt.a: Higher BED overcomes radioresistance and improves local control rates
c: It eliminates the need for pre-treatment imaging verification
Higher BED overcomes radioresistance and improves local control rates
It avoids any dose delivery to normal lung tissue altogether
Conventional fractionation is contraindicated for all lung tumours
srt.e: SBRT delivers very high BED (typically >100 Gy for tumour), which achieves local control rates exceeding 90% for peripheral early-stage NSCLC, comparable to surgical outcomes, while keeping treatment to 3-5 fractions.

srt.q: CALC: A stereotactic diode measures an output factor of 0.695 for a 0.5 x 0.5 cm field. The TRS-483 correction factor for this detector and field size is 0.975. What is the corrected output factor?
srt.e: Corrected OF = measured OF x k = 0.695 x 0.975 = 0.6776, rounded to 0.678. The correction factor accounts for detector-specific perturbation effects that become significant in very small fields.

srt.q: In CyberKnife Synchrony respiratory tracking, what determines the correlation model between external markers and internal tumour position?
srt.a: Periodic orthogonal kV images combined with LED chest markers
c: A pre-treatment breath-hold CT scan acquired at two phases only
Periodic orthogonal kV images combined with LED chest markers
Continuous MRI-based soft tissue tracking during beam delivery
Electromagnetic transponder signals paired with surface monitoring
srt.e: CyberKnife Synchrony builds a correlation model by periodically acquiring orthogonal kV images (to locate internal fiducials) and continuously tracking external LED markers on the chest. The model is updated throughout treatment.

srt.q: CALC: A linac-based SRS plan for a brain metastasis uses a 6 MV FFF beam at 1400 MU/min dose rate. The plan requires 3200 MU total. What is the beam-on time?
srt.a: Approximately 2.3 minutes
c: Approximately 1.4 minutes
Approximately 2.3 minutes
Approximately 3.8 minutes
Approximately 5.1 minutes
srt.e: Beam-on time = total MU / dose rate = 3200 / 1400 = 2.286 minutes, approximately 2.3 minutes. FFF beams with high dose rates significantly reduce treatment time for stereotactic procedures.

srt.q: What is the primary purpose of the ExacTrac Dynamic system in stereotactic radiotherapy?
srt.a: Surface monitoring combined with X-ray verification for real-time positioning
c: Automated treatment planning optimisation during beam delivery sessions
Continuous MRI-guided adaptive re-planning between treatment fractions
Surface monitoring combined with X-ray verification for real-time positioning
Fiducial-free electromagnetic transponder tracking of deep-seated targets
srt.e: ExacTrac Dynamic combines non-ionising surface monitoring (thermal or optical) with stereoscopic X-ray imaging to provide continuous patient positioning verification and intra-fraction motion detection during SRS/SBRT.

srt.q: What is the key difference between respiratory gating and respiratory tracking for SBRT motion management?
srt.a: Gating treats only during a breathing phase window whereas tracking moves the beam continuously
c: Gating uses electromagnetic transponders while tracking uses optical cameras only
Gating eliminates all respiratory motion while tracking reduces it by fifty percent
Gating treats only during a breathing phase window whereas tracking moves the beam continuously
Gating requires general anaesthesia while tracking requires only conscious sedation
srt.e: Respiratory gating delivers beam only during a selected phase (e.g. end-expiration), turning beam off during other phases. Tracking (e.g. CyberKnife Synchrony, MLC tracking) continuously adjusts the beam to follow tumour motion.

srt.q: CALC: An SBRT plan delivers 50 Gy in 5 fractions to a peripheral lung tumour. The chest wall receives a maximum of 40 Gy in 5 fractions. What is the chest wall BED (alpha/beta = 3 Gy)?
srt.e: Chest wall dose per fraction = 40/5 = 8 Gy. BED = 5 x 8 x (1 + 8/3) = 40 x (1 + 2.667) = 40 x 3.667 = 146.7 Gy. Chest wall toxicity (pain, rib fracture) is a recognised side effect of lung SBRT.

srt.q: What type of immobilisation is typically used for spinal SBRT treatments?
srt.a: Vacuum cushion body mould with thermoplastic overlay and knee support
c: Invasive stereotactic head frame bolted to the outer skull table
Vacuum cushion body mould with thermoplastic overlay and knee support
Standard wingboard with arms-up positioning and no external mould
Full-body plaster cast with fiducial markers embedded in the shell
srt.e: Spine SBRT requires rigid body immobilisation to achieve sub-millimetre setup accuracy. A vacuum cushion combined with thermoplastic mask or overlay ensures reproducible positioning, typically verified with daily CBCT.

srt.q: What is the primary concern when prescribing high single-fraction doses to lesions near the proximal bronchial tree in lung SBRT?
srt.a: Risk of fatal haemorrhage or bronchial stenosis from proximity to central airways
c: Excessive treatment time making the fraction impractical to deliver safely
Risk of fatal haemorrhage or bronchial stenosis from proximity to central airways
Inadequate tumour coverage because tissue heterogeneity prevents dose buildup
Patient discomfort from prolonged breath-hold requirements during beam delivery
srt.e: Ultra-central and central lung lesions near the proximal bronchial tree are at high risk of serious toxicity (bronchial stenosis, haemorrhage, fistula). Risk-adapted fractionation (e.g. 50 Gy/5 fx or 60 Gy/8 fx) is used instead of single-fraction SBRT.

srt.q: In SBRT planning, what does the R50% conformity metric evaluate?
srt.a: The ratio of 50% prescription isodose volume to PTV volume
c: The ratio of 50% prescription isodose volume to PTV volume
The percentage of PTV receiving at least 50% of prescription
The ratio of maximum point dose to the 50th percentile dose
The fraction of normal tissue within the 50% isodose surface
srt.e: R50% = V50%/V_PTV. This metric, used in RTOG protocols, evaluates dose falloff beyond the target. Lower R50% indicates steeper dose gradients and better sparing of surrounding normal tissue in SBRT plans.

srt.q: CALC: A GammaKnife plan uses three shots of different sizes. Shot 1 (16 mm) delivers 60%, Shot 2 (8 mm) delivers 25%, and Shot 3 (4 mm) delivers 15%. If the total prescription is 20 Gy, what dose does the 8 mm shot contribute?
srt.a: 5.0 Gy
c: 3.0 Gy
5.0 Gy
8.0 Gy
srt.e: Dose from 8 mm shot = 25% x 20 Gy = 0.25 x 20 = 5.0 Gy. GammaKnife plans combine multiple shots of various collimator sizes to conform the dose distribution to irregular target shapes.

srt.q: What is the primary advantage of the PTW microDiamond detector over unshielded silicon diodes for small field dosimetry?
srt.a: Near water-equivalent atomic number minimises energy-dependent perturbation
c: Much larger sensitive volume provides improved signal-to-noise ratio
Near water-equivalent atomic number minimises energy-dependent perturbation
Active temperature compensation eliminates the need for correction factors
Wireless readout system reduces cable-related positioning uncertainties
srt.e: The PTW microDiamond (synthetic diamond, Z=6) is near water-equivalent, resulting in minimal energy and dose-rate dependence. Unshielded silicon diodes (Z=14) over-respond to low-energy scatter, requiring larger correction factors in small fields.

srt.q: What is the typical CBCT imaging frequency protocol for a 5-fraction lung SBRT course?
srt.a: Daily CBCT before every fraction with online correction applied
c: Single CBCT at the first fraction only with no further imaging
CBCT on fractions one and three with offline review of results
Daily CBCT before every fraction with online correction applied
Weekly CBCT with portal imaging used on non-CBCT treatment days
srt.e: Due to the high dose per fraction and tight margins used in SBRT, daily CBCT with online correction before every fraction is standard practice. This ensures sub-centimetre targeting accuracy for each high-dose delivery.

srt.q: CALC: A single-isocentre multi-target SRS plan treats 3 brain metastases. Target C is 8 cm off-axis. If a 0.5-degree rotational setup error occurs, what is the approximate positional error at Target C?
srt.e: Positional error = radius x angle(rad) = 8 cm x (0.5 x pi/180) = 8 x 0.00873 = 0.0698 cm = 0.70 mm. This demonstrates why rotational accuracy is critical for distant off-axis targets in SIMT plans.

srt.q: What is the typical dose tolerance for the spinal cord in single-fraction spine SBRT (point maximum)?
srt.a: 14 Gy maximum point dose to the spinal cord
c: 8 Gy maximum point dose to the spinal cord
14 Gy maximum point dose to the spinal cord
20 Gy maximum point dose to the spinal cord
24 Gy maximum point dose to the spinal cord
srt.e: For single-fraction spine SBRT, the commonly accepted spinal cord Dmax constraint is approximately 14 Gy (per TG-101 and clinical trials like RTOG 0631). This corresponds to an EQD2 of approximately 56 Gy for alpha/beta = 2 Gy.

srt.q: Why is the Acuros XB algorithm considered superior to AAA for lung SBRT dose calculations?
srt.a: It solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation for more accurate heterogeneity corrections
c: It runs significantly faster allowing real-time adaptive re-planning during delivery
It solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation for more accurate heterogeneity corrections
It does not require CT calibration curves for converting Hounsfield units to density
It automatically accounts for respiratory motion without needing a 4D-CT acquisition
srt.e: Acuros XB solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation (LBTE), providing more accurate dose calculations in heterogeneous media (lung/bone interfaces) compared to AAA's convolution/superposition approach, which tends to overestimate dose in low-density lung tissue.

srt.q: CALC: A microDiamond detector measures an output factor of 0.580 in a 0.5 x 0.5 cm field of a 6 MV FFF beam. The TRS-483 correction factor k is 1.004. What is the corrected output factor?
srt.e: Corrected OF = measured OF x k = 0.580 x 1.004 = 0.5823, rounded to 0.582. The microDiamond has k factors very close to unity even in very small fields, confirming its suitability as a reference small-field detector.

srt.q: In SBRT for hepatocellular carcinoma, what additional imaging consideration is important compared to lung SBRT?
srt.a: Multiphase contrast-enhanced CT or MRI fusion to delineate tumour within liver parenchyma
c: Bone window CT reconstruction to assess vertebral body invasion near the hepatic lesion
Multiphase contrast-enhanced CT or MRI fusion to delineate tumour within liver parenchyma
4D-CT with ten respiratory phases to measure diaphragmatic motion amplitude exclusively
Non-contrast CT alone is always sufficient because liver tumours have high inherent contrast
srt.e: Liver tumours often have poor soft-tissue contrast on standard CT. Multiphase contrast-enhanced CT (arterial, portal venous phases) or MRI fusion is essential for accurate GTV delineation in liver SBRT, particularly for HCC.

srt.q: What is the typical prescription isodose line used for VMAT-based intracranial SRS on a linac?
srt.a: 80% isodose line to create a hotspot within the target volume
c: 95% isodose line to ensure homogeneous dose across the target
80% isodose line to create a hotspot within the target volume
50% isodose line matching the GammaKnife convention exactly
100% isodose line normalised to the maximum dose point only
srt.e: Linac-based SRS commonly prescribes to the 80% isodose line (range 70-90%), allowing a central hotspot within the target. This produces steeper dose gradients at the target periphery, improving conformity and sparing adjacent normal tissue.

srt.q: CALC: A patient is treated with spine SBRT delivering 27 Gy in 3 fractions. What is the EQD2 for the tumour (alpha/beta = 10 Gy)?
srt.e: Dose per fraction = 27/3 = 9 Gy. EQD2 = nd x (d + alpha/beta)/(2 + alpha/beta) = 27 x (9 + 10)/(2 + 10) = 27 x 19/12 = 42.75 Gy. Spine SBRT achieves high local control with adequate tumour BED.

srt.q: What imaging technique provides real-time soft tissue visualisation during SBRT delivery without ionising radiation?
srt.a: MR-guided radiotherapy using an integrated MR-Linac system
c: Kilovoltage cone-beam CT acquired at regular intervals during arcs
MR-guided radiotherapy using an integrated MR-Linac system
Megavoltage portal imaging with enhanced contrast algorithms
Diagnostic ultrasound probe positioned against patient skin
srt.e: MR-Linac systems (e.g. Elekta Unity, ViewRay MRIdian) provide real-time MRI during beam delivery, enabling continuous soft tissue visualisation without additional ionising radiation. This is particularly valuable for liver and pancreatic SBRT.

srt.q: In TRS-483, what is the machine-specific reference (msr) field size for a standard C-arm linac with conventional jaws?
srt.a: 10 x 10 cm field defined by the secondary collimator jaws
c: 5 x 5 cm field defined by the multi-leaf collimator leaves
10 x 10 cm field defined by the secondary collimator jaws
3 x 3 cm field defined by a circular stereotactic cone
20 x 20 cm field defined by both primary and secondary jaws
srt.e: For standard C-arm linacs with jaws, the machine-specific reference field in TRS-483 is the conventional 10 x 10 cm defined by the jaws, which is also the reference condition in TRS-398 and TG-51.

srt.q: CALC: A DIBH lung SBRT plan reduces the mean lung dose from 8.5 Gy to 5.2 Gy. What is the percentage reduction in mean lung dose?
srt.e: Percentage reduction = (8.5 - 5.2)/8.5 x 100 = 3.3/8.5 x 100 = 38.82%, approximately 38.8%. DIBH increases lung volume, distributing dose over more tissue and reducing the mean dose to normal lung.

srt.q: What is the primary limitation of using an ionisation chamber for output factor measurements in fields smaller than 1.5 cm?
srt.a: Volume averaging across the non-uniform field artificially increases the measured output
c: The chamber becomes saturated due to very high dose rates from FFF beams
Volume averaging across the non-uniform field artificially increases the measured output
Ion recombination losses cause the reading to drop by more than twenty percent
The polarity effect reverses completely in fields below 1.5 cm equivalent square
srt.e: When the chamber sensitive volume is comparable to the field size, the detector averages dose across regions of steep gradient. Since dose falls off at the field edges, the average over the chamber volume is higher than the central-axis dose, artificially increasing the measured output factor.

srt.q: CALC: A lung SBRT plan prescribes 48 Gy in 4 fractions. What is the BED for late-responding brachial plexus (alpha/beta = 3 Gy) receiving the full prescription dose?
srt.a: 240 Gy
c: 112 Gy
160 Gy
240 Gy
288 Gy
srt.e: Dose per fraction = 48/4 = 12 Gy. BED = nd(1 + d/(alpha/beta)) = 4 x 12 x (1 + 12/3) = 48 x (1 + 4) = 48 x 5 = 240 Gy. This very high BED for late-responding tissue highlights the importance of strict OAR constraints in SBRT.

srt.q: What is the purpose of pre-treatment patient-specific QA measurement for an SRS VMAT plan?
srt.a: To verify the deliverability and dosimetric accuracy of the complex MLC sequence
c: To replace the need for daily Winston-Lutz and imaging verification checks
To verify the deliverability and dosimetric accuracy of the complex MLC sequence
To calibrate the linac output specifically for each individual patient plan
To confirm the prescription dose is radiobiologically appropriate for tumour
srt.e: PSQA for SRS/SBRT VMAT plans verifies that the TPS-calculated dose matches the delivered dose by measuring the plan on a phantom. This catches errors in MLC modulation, dose rate, and gantry motion before treating the patient.

srt.q: In the context of small field dosimetry, what does the term source occlusion refer to?
srt.a: Part of the extended radiation source is blocked by the field-defining collimator
c: The primary collimator shadow falls directly on the central axis detector
Part of the extended radiation source is blocked by the field-defining collimator
Scattered photons from the flattening filter obscure the primary beam signal
The detector cable attenuates the beam creating a shadowed measurement region
srt.e: Source occlusion occurs when the collimator-defined field size becomes comparable to the projected focal spot size. Parts of the extended source are geometrically blocked, reducing the output factor below what simple geometric models predict.

srt.q: CALC: An SRS plan uses the Paddick conformity index. If TV = 3.5 cm3, PIV = 4.2 cm3, and TV_PIV = 3.3 cm3, what is the Paddick CI?
srt.e: Paddick CI = (TV_PIV)^2 / (TV x PIV) = (3.3)^2 / (3.5 x 4.2) = 10.89 / 14.7 = 0.7408, rounded to 0.74. A Paddick CI of 1.0 indicates perfect conformity; values below 0.8 suggest room for plan improvement.

srt.q: What is the recommended approach when the TRS-483 correction factor table does not include a specific detector-field size combination?
srt.a: Use two or more detector types with independent correction factors and compare results
c: Extrapolate the correction factor linearly from the nearest available table entry
Assume a correction factor of unity since the table omission indicates negligible error
Use two or more detector types with independent correction factors and compare results
Switch to Monte Carlo simulation as the sole method for determining output factor
srt.e: TRS-483 recommends using multiple detector types with independent correction factors when a specific k-factor is not tabulated. Agreement between corrected results from different detectors provides confidence in the measured output factor.

srt.q: CALC: A fractionated SRS plan delivers 25 Gy in 5 fractions to the optic chiasm. What is the EQD2 for the optic chiasm (alpha/beta = 2 Gy)?
srt.e: Dose per fraction = 25/5 = 5 Gy. EQD2 = nd x (d + alpha/beta)/(2 + alpha/beta) = 25 x (5 + 2)/(2 + 2) = 25 x 7/4 = 25 x 1.75 = 43.75 Gy. The optic chiasm tolerance is typically 54 Gy EQD2, so this plan is within tolerance.

srt.q: According to TRS-483, what is the recommended reference field size for small field output factor measurements in a CyberKnife unit?
srt.a: The machine-specific reference field (msr) defined by the largest available collimator
c: A standard 10 x 10 cm² field using external jaws and MLC
The machine-specific reference field (msr) defined by the largest available collimator
A 5 x 5 cm² field which is the universal small field reference
A 3 x 3 cm² field as recommended for all cone-based systems
srt.e: TRS-483 introduces the concept of a machine-specific reference field (f_msr) for machines that cannot establish the conventional 10 x 10 cm² reference field. For CyberKnife, this is the largest fixed cone (typically 60 mm). Output factors are then daisy-chained from msr to smaller fields.

srt.q: During a Winston-Lutz test for a Gamma Knife Icon, what is the typical isocentre accuracy tolerance?
srt.a: Less than or equal to 0.5 mm total displacement
c: Less than or equal to 0.5 mm total displacement
Less than or equal to 1.5 mm total displacement
Less than or equal to 2.0 mm total displacement
Less than or equal to 0.1 mm total displacement
srt.e: Gamma Knife systems are held to very tight mechanical accuracy requirements. The Winston-Lutz test verifies coincidence of the radiation and mechanical isocentres. A tolerance of ≤ 0.5 mm is consistent with SRS-level accuracy requirements per TG-142 and manufacturer specifications.

srt.q: CALC: A stereotactic cone with a 12.5 mm diameter has an output factor of 0.673 relative to the 10 x 10 cm² field. If the prescription is 18 Gy in a single fraction, what MU is required assuming tissue-maximum ratio of 0.800 and calibration of 1 cGy/MU at d_max for the 10 x 10 cm² field?
srt.e: MU = Dose / (Calibration x OF x TMR) = 1800 cGy / (1.0 x 0.673 x 0.800) = 1800 / 0.5384 = 3342 MU. The high MU count is typical for small cone-based SRS due to the low output factor and field size.

srt.q: What is the primary advantage of flattening filter free (FFF) beams in stereotactic radiosurgery?
srt.a: Significantly higher dose rate enabling shorter treatment times for high-dose fractions
c: Improved beam flatness across the entire field which reduces hot spots
Significantly higher dose rate enabling shorter treatment times for high-dose fractions
Better beam penetration at depth due to the harder photon energy spectrum
Elimination of the need for output factor measurements during commissioning
srt.e: FFF beams can deliver dose rates up to 2400 MU/min (6 MV FFF) compared to ~600 MU/min for flattened beams. This reduces intra-fraction motion effects in SRS/SBRT by shortening treatment delivery time. The beam profile is peaked centrally, not flat.

srt.q: In TRS-483, which detector is recommended as the primary choice for measuring output factors in fields smaller than 1 cm?
srt.a: A microDiamond detector or equivalent small-volume solid-state detector
c: A Farmer-type ionisation chamber with a 0.6 cc sensitive volume
A microDiamond detector or equivalent small-volume solid-state detector
A standard parallel-plate ionisation chamber with guard ring design
A radiochromic film calibrated against a large-volume ion chamber
srt.e: TRS-483 recommends detectors with very small sensitive volumes for small field measurements to minimise volume averaging. The PTW microDiamond (0.004 mm³ active volume) is a preferred choice. Radiochromic film is acceptable but is not the primary recommendation. Farmer chambers severely underestimate output in small fields.

srt.q: CALC: A TRS-483 small field output factor measurement yields a reading ratio of 0.582 for a 0.5 x 0.5 cm² field using a microDiamond detector. The correction factor k is 0.997. What is the corrected output factor?
srt.e: The corrected output factor = reading ratio x k = 0.582 x 0.997 = 0.580254 ≈ 0.580. TRS-483 provides detector-specific correction factors (k) to account for any residual volume averaging or density perturbation effects. The microDiamond has corrections very close to unity for most field sizes.

srt.q: What is the typical GTV-to-PTV margin applied for frameless single-fraction intracranial SRS when using mask-based immobilisation with CBCT verification?
srt.a: 1 to 2 mm uniform expansion
c: 0 mm, the GTV is treated directly without any margin
1 to 2 mm uniform expansion
5 mm uniform expansion as per standard fractionated treatments
3 to 5 mm asymmetric expansion weighted toward the superior direction
srt.e: Frameless SRS with thermoplastic mask immobilisation and image guidance typically uses 1-2 mm GTV-to-PTV margins. This accounts for residual setup uncertainty and intra-fraction motion. Frame-based SRS may use 0-1 mm margins. South African centres using Brainlab or Varian HyperArc commonly apply 1-2 mm margins for mask-based SRS.

srt.q: In a single-isocentre multi-met SRS plan, what geometric effect increases dosimetric error for targets far from the isocentre?
srt.a: Rotational errors cause translational displacement that increases linearly with distance from the isocentre
c: Beam divergence causes systematic underdosage at all peripheral targets equally
Rotational errors cause translational displacement that increases linearly with distance from the isocentre
Couch sag produces a uniform shift that affects all targets by the same magnitude
MLC leaf positioning errors are amplified by the inverse square law at distance
srt.e: For single-isocentre multi-met SRS, a small rotational error of 1° translates to approximately 1.7 mm displacement per 10 cm distance from isocentre. Targets far from isocentre are therefore more susceptible to geographic miss, requiring careful attention to setup accuracy and potentially larger margins.

srt.q: What is the primary purpose of HyperArc in Varian TrueBeam-based SRS treatments?
srt.a: Automated non-coplanar arc geometry optimisation for cranial SRS with standardised setup
c: Real-time tumour tracking using implanted fiducial markers during beam delivery
Automated non-coplanar arc geometry optimisation for cranial SRS with standardised setup
Dose rate modulation that adapts to respiratory motion during lung SBRT
Automatic MLC leaf sequencing that replaces the need for inverse planning
srt.e: HyperArc is a Varian workflow for frameless cranial SRS that automates non-coplanar VMAT arc arrangement, provides standardised isocentric setup, and enables efficient single-isocentre multi-met treatments. Several SA centres including private oncology units have adopted HyperArc for brain metastases SRS.

srt.q: CALC: A spine SBRT plan delivers 24 Gy in 3 fractions. What is the BED₁₀ for the tumour (alpha/beta = 10 Gy)?
srt.e: Dose per fraction d = 24/3 = 8 Gy. BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 24(1 + 8/10) = 24 × 1.8 = 43.2 Gy₁₀. This is a common spine SBRT fractionation, with the spinal cord typically limited to a max point dose of ~18-21 Gy over 3 fractions.

srt.q: Which immobilisation device is most appropriate for lung SBRT at a South African radiation oncology centre?
srt.a: A vacuum cushion body mould with abdominal compression or active breathing control
c: A standard thermoplastic head and shoulder shell without additional devices
A vacuum cushion body mould with abdominal compression or active breathing control
A simple foam wedge under the knees with arms at the patient's sides
A rigid stereotactic body frame with invasive skeletal fixation screws
srt.e: Lung SBRT requires robust immobilisation that also addresses respiratory motion. Vacuum cushion body moulds (e.g., BlueBAG) combined with abdominal compression or ABC are widely used in SA. Invasive fixation is not used for body SBRT. Motion management is mandated by SASQP and international protocols.

srt.q: What is the recommended frequency of the Winston-Lutz test for a linac dedicated to SRS treatments according to TG-142?
srt.a: Daily before SRS treatments
c: Weekly as part of routine linac QA
Daily before SRS treatments
Monthly during comprehensive machine QA
Annually during the full linac recommissioning
srt.e: TG-142 recommends daily verification of the coincidence of radiation and mechanical isocentres for SRS-capable linacs. The Winston-Lutz test (or equivalent) should be performed on each SRS treatment day with a tolerance of ≤ 1 mm (≤ 0.75 mm radius).

srt.q: CALC: An SRS plan uses a 6 MV FFF beam at 1400 MU/min dose rate. The total plan requires 4200 MU across all arcs. What is the minimum beam-on time?
srt.a: 3.0 minutes
c: 1.5 minutes
3.0 minutes
6.0 minutes
7.0 minutes
srt.e: Beam-on time = Total MU / Dose rate = 4200 / 1400 = 3.0 minutes. In practice, total treatment time is longer due to gantry rotation, imaging, and couch movements between arcs. FFF beams significantly reduce beam-on time compared to flattened beams.

srt.q: In VMAT-based SRS, what is the significance of the maximum MLC leaf speed constraint during optimisation?
srt.a: It limits the achievable dose rate modulation per gantry angle, potentially affecting plan conformity
c: It determines the maximum prescription dose that can be safely delivered
It limits the achievable dose rate modulation per gantry angle, potentially affecting plan conformity
It controls the total number of monitor units required for the treatment plan
It sets the minimum tumour size that can be treated with VMAT-based SRS
srt.e: MLC leaf speed (typically max 2.5 cm/s for Varian) limits how rapidly fluence can vary during arc delivery. If the optimizer requires faster modulation than the leaves can achieve, the gantry must slow down or dose rate must decrease, affecting delivery efficiency and potentially plan conformity for complex targets.

srt.q: What volume averaging artefact occurs when measuring output factors for a 5 mm cone using a 0.125 cc ionisation chamber?
srt.a: The chamber significantly underestimates the output factor due to partial volume irradiation
c: The chamber overestimates the output factor due to the stem effect enhancement
The chamber significantly underestimates the output factor due to partial volume irradiation
The chamber reading is unaffected because the cone is larger than the detector
The chamber produces erratic readings that cannot be corrected mathematically
srt.e: When the detector sensitive volume is comparable to or larger than the radiation field, the detector averages dose over regions of high and low (or zero) dose, leading to underestimation of the central axis output. TRS-483 recommends detectors with dimensions much smaller than the field size to minimise this effect.

srt.q: CALC: A patient undergoing frameless SRS has a residual setup error of 0.8 mm (translational) and a rotational error of 0.5°. If the target is 6 cm from the isocentre, what is the approximate total positional uncertainty at the target?
srt.a: 1.3 mm
c: 0.8 mm
1.0 mm
1.3 mm
1.8 mm
srt.e: Rotational displacement at distance r = r × sin(θ) ≈ r × θ (in radians for small angles). 0.5° = 0.00873 rad. Displacement = 60 mm × 0.00873 = 0.524 mm. Total ≈ √(0.8² + 0.524²) = √(0.64 + 0.275) = √0.915 ≈ 0.96 mm, but adding linearly for worst case: 0.8 + 0.52 ≈ 1.3 mm. This highlights why single-iso multi-met plans require careful margin consideration.

srt.q: For adaptive radiotherapy in lung SBRT, what is the primary indication for re-planning during a treatment course?
srt.a: Significant tumour shrinkage or anatomical change causing dosimetric deviation from the original plan
c: A change in the treatment machine assignment from one linac to another
Significant tumour shrinkage or anatomical change causing dosimetric deviation from the original plan
The patient developing mild skin erythema at the beam entry point
An increase in the daily setup time beyond the scheduled appointment slot
srt.e: Adaptive SBRT re-planning is triggered when anatomical changes (tumour regression, atelectasis resolution, pleural effusion changes) cause the delivered dose to deviate significantly from the planned dose. CBCT-based assessment at each fraction helps identify these changes. Some SA centres are beginning to implement adaptive workflows for lung SBRT.

srt.q: What is the typical prescription isodose line used for Gamma Knife radiosurgery?
srt.a: 50% isodose line
c: 50% isodose line
80% isodose line
95% isodose line
100% isodose line
srt.e: Gamma Knife plans conventionally prescribe to the 50% isodose line, meaning the maximum dose within the target is approximately twice the prescription dose. This produces a steep dose gradient at the target boundary, which is desirable for SRS. Linac-based SRS typically prescribes to 60-80% isodose.

srt.q: In spine SBRT, what is the typical maximum point dose constraint for the spinal cord (PRV) in a 3-fraction regimen?
srt.a: 18 to 21.9 Gy over 3 fractions
c: 8 to 10 Gy over 3 fractions
18 to 21.9 Gy over 3 fractions
30 to 33 Gy over 3 fractions
45 to 50 Gy over 3 fractions
srt.e: For spine SBRT in 3 fractions, the spinal cord PRV maximum point dose is typically constrained to 18-21.9 Gy (AAPM TG-101, Timmerman constraints). This corresponds to an EQD2 of approximately 40-53 Gy₃, respecting the ~50 Gy₃ myelopathy threshold. SA guidelines follow these international constraints.

srt.q: What is the key dosimetric advantage of non-coplanar arcs over coplanar arcs in cranial SRS?
srt.a: Improved dose conformity and faster dose fall-off by distributing entrance dose over a larger solid angle
c: Higher maximum dose rate delivery due to reduced gantry travel requirements
Improved dose conformity and faster dose fall-off by distributing entrance dose over a larger solid angle
Elimination of the need for heterogeneity corrections in the treatment planning system
Reduced total monitor units which decreases the peripheral dose to the patient
srt.e: Non-coplanar arcs spread beam entrance over a larger solid angle around the target, reducing dose to surrounding normal brain while maintaining target coverage. This improves the conformity index and gradient index. The trade-off is longer treatment times due to couch rotations between arcs.

srt.q: CALC: A lung SBRT plan prescribes 54 Gy in 3 fractions to the PTV periphery (covering 95% of PTV). If the internal target volume (ITV) receives a mean dose of 62 Gy, what is the ratio of the mean ITV dose to the prescription dose?
srt.e: Ratio = Mean ITV dose / Prescription dose = 62 / 54 = 1.148 ≈ 1.15. In lung SBRT, it is common for the ITV to receive a higher mean dose than the PTV prescription due to the dose gradient across the PTV. This heterogeneity is generally accepted and is a feature of SBRT planning.

srt.q: What is the typical maximum leaf width used in dedicated SRS micro-multileaf collimators?
srt.a: 2.5 mm
c: 2.5 mm
5.0 mm
1.0 mm
srt.e: Dedicated SRS micro-MLC systems typically use leaf widths of 2.5 mm or smaller at isocenter to achieve the sharp dose gradients required for stereotactic treatments of small targets.

srt.q: What is the primary purpose of the Winston-Lutz test in stereotactic radiosurgery?
srt.a: To verify radiation isocenter coincidence with mechanical isocenter
c: To verify radiation isocenter coincidence with mechanical isocenter
To measure the absolute dose output of the treatment beam
To confirm the accuracy of the multileaf collimator positions
To validate the homogeneity of the flattening filter profile
srt.e: The Winston-Lutz test uses a small radiopaque ball placed at the mechanical isocenter and irradiated from multiple gantry and couch angles to verify that the radiation field center coincides with the mechanical isocenter.

srt.q: What tolerance is generally recommended for the Winston-Lutz test in SRS programs?
srt.a: Less than or equal to 1.0 mm
c: Less than or equal to 1.0 mm
Less than or equal to 2.0 mm
Less than or equal to 0.1 mm
Less than or equal to 3.0 mm
srt.e: For SRS programs, the Winston-Lutz test should demonstrate that the radiation isocenter and mechanical isocenter coincide within 1.0 mm or better, with many institutions aiming for sub-millimeter accuracy.

srt.q: What is the key advantage of flattening-filter-free (FFF) beams in stereotactic treatments?
srt.a: Higher dose rate enabling shorter treatment times
c: Higher dose rate enabling shorter treatment times
More uniform dose profiles across the field
Reduced neutron contamination at all energies
Improved beam penetration in deep-seated tumors
srt.e: FFF beams remove the flattening filter, resulting in significantly higher dose rates (up to 2400 MU/min for 6 MV FFF) which reduces treatment time. This is particularly beneficial for SRS/SBRT where high doses per fraction are delivered.

srt.q: In CyberKnife treatments, what type of robotic system is used to deliver radiation?
srt.a: A compact linear accelerator mounted on a robotic arm
c: A compact linear accelerator mounted on a robotic arm
A rotating gantry with a fixed patient position
A cobalt-60 source array with collimator helmets
A proton beam delivery system with scanning nozzle
srt.e: The CyberKnife system uses a compact 6 MV linear accelerator mounted on a six-degree-of-freedom robotic arm (Kuka robot), allowing non-isocentric and non-coplanar beam delivery with real-time image guidance.

srt.q: What is the typical prescription isodose line used for Gamma Knife radiosurgery treatments?
srt.a: 50% isodose line
c: 50% isodose line
80% isodose line
95% isodose line
65% isodose line
srt.e: Gamma Knife plans typically prescribe to the 50% isodose line, which provides a steep dose gradient at the target periphery. This results in a hot spot of approximately 200% of the prescription dose at the center of the target.

srt.q: According to RTOG SRS guidelines, what is the maximum recommended dose to the optic chiasm for single-fraction SRS?
srt.a: 8 Gy
c: 8 Gy
12 Gy
15 Gy
10 Gy
srt.e: The optic chiasm and optic nerves are critical dose-limiting structures in SRS with a recommended maximum point dose of 8 Gy in a single fraction to avoid radiation-induced optic neuropathy.

srt.q: What is the primary purpose of an end-to-end (E2E) test in a stereotactic radiosurgery program?
srt.a: To verify the entire treatment chain from imaging to delivery
c: To verify the entire treatment chain from imaging to delivery
To calibrate the linear accelerator output to a standard
To test the emergency shutoff systems of the machine
To measure the beam energy spectrum at the isocenter
