srt.e: An E2E test evaluates the complete SRS workflow including imaging, target localization, treatment planning, patient setup, and dose delivery using an anthropomorphic phantom, ensuring all components work together accurately.

srt.q: In single-isocenter multi-target (SIMT) SRS, what is the primary concern for targets far from isocenter?
srt.a: Increased positional error due to rotational uncertainties
c: Increased positional error due to rotational uncertainties
Decreased monitor units required for distant targets
Improved dose conformity at peripheral target locations
Reduced imaging artifact from the stereotactic frame
srt.e: In SIMT SRS, small rotational setup errors are amplified for targets located far from the isocenter. A small rotation translates to a larger translational displacement at greater distances, potentially causing geographic miss of distant lesions.

srt.q: What is HyperArc in the context of stereotactic radiosurgery?
srt.a: An automated non-coplanar VMAT planning solution for brain SRS
c: An automated non-coplanar VMAT planning solution for brain SRS
A frameless immobilization system using vacuum-formed masks
A real-time MRI-guided adaptive treatment delivery system
A proton therapy technique for intracranial stereotactic use
srt.e: HyperArc is a Varian technology that automates non-coplanar VMAT treatment planning and delivery for brain SRS. It uses predefined couch angles and optimized arc geometries to improve plan quality and streamline the treatment workflow.

srt.q: What is the typical cone size range available for cone-based SRS treatments on a linear accelerator?
srt.a: 4 mm to 17.5 mm diameter
c: 4 mm to 17.5 mm diameter
1 mm to 5 mm diameter
20 mm to 40 mm diameter
10 mm to 60 mm diameter
srt.e: Cone-based SRS systems typically offer circular collimators ranging from 4 mm to 17.5 mm in diameter. These cones produce small, well-defined circular fields suitable for treating small spherical intracranial targets.

srt.q: Which detector is recommended by TG-155 for commissioning small SRS cone fields below 10 mm?
srt.a: Stereotactic diode or microdiamond detector
c: Stereotactic diode or microdiamond detector
Standard Farmer-type ionization chamber (0.6 cc)
Large-volume parallel plate ionization chamber
Radiographic film with optical density scanner
srt.e: For small SRS fields below 10 mm, volume averaging in large chambers leads to significant measurement errors. Small-volume detectors like stereotactic diodes or synthetic microdiamond detectors are recommended due to their high spatial resolution and minimal volume averaging.

srt.q: What is the RTOG conformity index (CI) formula used for evaluating SRS plans?
srt.a: Prescription isodose volume divided by target volume
c: Prescription isodose volume divided by target volume
Target volume divided by prescription isodose volume
Maximum dose divided by prescription dose in the plan
Volume of 50% isodose divided by target volume
srt.e: The RTOG conformity index is defined as CI = PIV/TV, where PIV is the prescription isodose volume and TV is the target volume. An ideal CI equals 1.0, indicating perfect conformity between the prescription isodose and the target.

srt.q: In frameless SRS, what immobilization device is most commonly used for intracranial treatments?
srt.a: Thermoplastic mask with bite block or headrest
c: Thermoplastic mask with bite block or headrest
Invasive stereotactic head frame with skull pins
Vacuum cushion body mold with arm shuttle device
Halo ring with plaster cast cranial fixation system
srt.e: Frameless SRS uses non-invasive thermoplastic masks combined with a bite block or custom headrest to immobilize the patient. Image guidance with CBCT or ExacTrac compensates for the slightly reduced immobilization rigidity compared to invasive frames.

srt.q: What is the typical dose rate achievable with 6 MV FFF beams used in SRS?
srt.a: Up to 1400 MU/min
c: Up to 1400 MU/min
Up to 600 MU/min
Up to 400 MU/min
Up to 3000 MU/min
srt.e: With the flattening filter removed, 6 MV FFF beams on modern linacs such as the Varian TrueBeam can achieve dose rates up to approximately 1400 MU/min, compared to the typical 600 MU/min maximum with the flattening filter in place.

srt.q: What is the gradient index (GI) used to evaluate in SRS treatment plans?
srt.a: The rate of dose falloff beyond the target boundary
c: The rate of dose falloff beyond the target boundary
The ratio of maximum dose to the prescription dose
The uniformity of dose distribution within the target
The number of monitor units needed per unit dose
srt.e: The gradient index is defined as the ratio of the volume receiving half the prescription dose to the prescription isodose volume (V50%Rx/VRx). A lower GI indicates a steeper dose falloff, which is desirable for sparing normal tissue adjacent to the target.

srt.q: Which RTOG protocol established the dose-volume guidelines for single-fraction lung SBRT?
srt.a: RTOG 0236
c: RTOG 0236
RTOG 0915
RTOG 9005
RTOG 0631
srt.e: RTOG 0236 was a landmark phase II trial that established 54 Gy in 3 fractions (18 Gy per fraction) as the standard regimen for peripheral early-stage non-small cell lung cancer treated with SBRT, along with associated dose-volume constraints.

srt.q: What is the purpose of the stereotactic localizer box used with invasive head frames?
srt.a: To establish a coordinate system for target localization on imaging
c: To establish a coordinate system for target localization on imaging
To provide additional radiation shielding during CT scanning
To immobilize the patient jaw during treatment delivery time
To measure the absorbed dose at the treatment isocenter
srt.e: The stereotactic localizer box attaches to the head frame and contains fiducial rods or plates visible on CT/MRI. These fiducials define a stereotactic coordinate system that allows precise mathematical transformation between image space and treatment space.

srt.q: In SBRT for liver tumors, what is the most common motion management strategy?
srt.a: Abdominal compression to reduce respiratory motion amplitude
c: Abdominal compression to reduce respiratory motion amplitude
General anesthesia with ventilator-controlled breath hold
Electromagnetic transponder tracking with beam gating only
Voluntary deep inspiration breath hold with visual feedback
srt.e: Abdominal compression devices (e.g., compression plate or belt) are widely used in liver SBRT to reduce the amplitude of respiratory-induced tumor motion, typically limiting diaphragmatic excursion to less than 5 mm.

srt.q: What is the recommended minimum number of gantry/couch angle combinations for a Winston-Lutz test?
srt.a: At least 8 combinations of gantry and couch angles
c: At least 8 combinations of gantry and couch angles
Only 2 orthogonal gantry angles are sufficient
At least 20 combinations with collimator rotation
A single gantry angle with four couch rotations
srt.e: A comprehensive Winston-Lutz test should include multiple gantry angles (0, 90, 180, 270) combined with multiple couch angles (0, 45, 90, 270 or 315) to characterize the radiation isocenter sphere under different geometric conditions.

srt.q: What volume averaging correction factor is essential when measuring output factors for small SRS fields?
srt.a: The field output correction factor from TG-155/IAEA TRS-483
c: The field output correction factor from TG-155/IAEA TRS-483
The ion recombination correction factor from TG-51 protocol
The temperature and pressure correction for chamber volume
The polarity correction factor for the ionization chamber
srt.e: TRS-483 and TG-155 define field output correction factors that account for detector-specific perturbations in small fields, including volume averaging, density effects, and fluence perturbation. These corrections are essential for accurate small field dosimetry.

srt.q: What margin is typically used for the PTV expansion in frameless intracranial SRS?
srt.a: 1 to 2 mm
c: 1 to 2 mm
5 to 7 mm
3 to 5 mm
0 mm (no margin)
srt.e: Frameless SRS typically uses 1-2 mm PTV margins to account for residual setup uncertainty after image-guided correction. This is slightly larger than frame-based SRS (0-1 mm) due to the reduced immobilization rigidity of thermoplastic masks.

srt.q: In Gamma Knife Perfexion, how are different collimator sizes achieved?
srt.a: By moving source sectors over different sized collimator openings
c: By moving source sectors over different sized collimator openings
By physically exchanging collimator helmets before each shot
By adjusting the source-to-focus distance with a motor drive
By inserting different tungsten collimator cones at isocenter
srt.e: The Perfexion model uses eight movable source sectors that slide over a fixed tungsten collimator array containing 4 mm, 8 mm, and 16 mm openings. This eliminates the need to manually change collimator helmets as in older Gamma Knife models.

srt.q: What is the maximum recommended dose heterogeneity within the PTV for linac-based SRS per RTOG guidelines?
srt.a: Maximum dose should not exceed 200% of prescription dose
c: Maximum dose should not exceed 200% of prescription dose
Maximum dose should not exceed 110% of prescription dose
Maximum dose should not exceed 150% of prescription dose
Maximum dose should not exceed 120% of prescription dose
srt.e: RTOG 9005 established that for SRS, the maximum dose within the target should ideally not exceed 200% of the prescription dose, though considerable heterogeneity is acceptable in SRS compared to conventional fractionated radiotherapy.

srt.q: What imaging modality is most commonly fused with CT for target delineation in brain SRS for metastases?
srt.a: Gadolinium-enhanced T1-weighted MRI
c: Gadolinium-enhanced T1-weighted MRI
FDG-PET with CT attenuation correction
Non-contrast T2-weighted MRI sequences
Contrast-enhanced cone beam CT imaging
srt.e: Gadolinium-enhanced T1-weighted MRI provides excellent contrast for visualizing brain metastases. It is routinely fused with the planning CT to improve target delineation accuracy in SRS treatment planning.

srt.q: What is the source-to-focus distance in the Gamma Knife Perfexion unit?
srt.a: Approximately 40 cm
c: Approximately 40 cm
Approximately 100 cm
Approximately 80 cm
Approximately 25 cm
srt.e: The Gamma Knife Perfexion has a source-to-focus distance of approximately 40 cm, which is shorter than a conventional linac SAD of 100 cm. This shorter distance contributes to the steep dose gradients characteristic of Gamma Knife treatments.

srt.q: In SBRT for spine tumors, what is the primary dose-limiting structure?
srt.a: The spinal cord or cauda equina
c: The spinal cord or cauda equina
The vertebral body bone marrow
The adjacent lung parenchyma tissue
The paravertebral skeletal muscle
srt.e: The spinal cord (or cauda equina in lumbar cases) is the primary dose-limiting structure in spine SBRT. Typical constraints limit the maximum point dose to 14 Gy in a single fraction or 24 Gy in 3 fractions to avoid radiation myelopathy.

srt.q: What is the role of the 4D-CT scan in SBRT treatment planning for lung tumors?
srt.a: To capture tumor motion throughout the respiratory cycle
c: To capture tumor motion throughout the respiratory cycle
To improve the contrast resolution of tumor boundaries
To reduce CT artifacts from cardiac motion interference
To generate a diagnostic-quality image for tumor staging
srt.e: 4D-CT acquires images at multiple phases of the respiratory cycle, creating a motion-inclusive dataset. This allows generation of an internal target volume (ITV) that encompasses the full range of tumor motion, ensuring adequate coverage during SBRT delivery.

srt.q: What is the primary purpose of the Winston-Lutz test in stereotactic radiosurgery quality assurance?
srt.a: To verify the coincidence of the radiation isocenter with the mechanical isocenter of the system
c: To measure the absolute dose output of the linac at the stereotactic treatment distance
To verify the coincidence of the radiation isocenter with the mechanical isocenter of the system
To assess the MLC leaf positioning accuracy during dynamic conformal arc delivery
To evaluate the image quality of the onboard cone-beam CT used for SRS localization
srt.e: The Winston-Lutz test uses a small radio-opaque ball bearing at the mechanical isocenter and images it against the radiation field at multiple gantry and couch angles to quantify isocenter coincidence.

srt.q: According to TRS-483, what is the recommended minimum detector for measuring output factors of small stereotactic fields?
srt.a: An unshielded diode, microdiamond, or other small-volume detector with appropriate correction factors
c: A standard Farmer-type ionization chamber with 0.6 cm³ active volume and no corrections
An unshielded diode, microdiamond, or other small-volume detector with appropriate correction factors
A parallel plate ionization chamber oriented perpendicular to the beam central axis direction
A large-area ionization chamber providing averaged readings across the entire field aperture
srt.e: TRS-483 recommends detectors with small active volumes (microdiamond, unshielded diodes, microionization chambers) for small field output factors, along with field-size and detector-specific correction factors.

srt.q: What is the TG-142 recommended tolerance for Winston-Lutz test results on a dedicated SRS/SBRT linac?
srt.a: ±1 mm maximum deviation of the radiation center from the ball bearing center
c: ±0.5 mm maximum deviation of the radiation center from the ball bearing center
±1 mm maximum deviation of the radiation center from the ball bearing center
±2 mm maximum deviation of the radiation center from the ball bearing center
±3 mm maximum deviation of the radiation center from the ball bearing center
srt.e: TG-142 specifies ±1 mm for the Winston-Lutz test on SRS/SBRT units, reflecting the tighter geometric accuracy requirements for stereotactic treatments with small margins.

srt.q: In small field dosimetry, what causes the output factor to decrease rapidly below a critical field size?
srt.a: Loss of lateral charged particle equilibrium and partial source occlusion by the collimator
c: Increased phantom scatter factor due to reduced field size approaching detector dimensions
Loss of lateral charged particle equilibrium and partial source occlusion by the collimator
Enhanced buildup region effects from electron contamination in very narrow beam profiles
Greater air gap attenuation between the collimating system and phantom measurement surface
srt.e: Below a critical field size, the lateral range of secondary electrons exceeds the field radius, causing loss of charged particle equilibrium. Additionally, the finite source size is partially blocked by small collimator openings.

srt.q: What is the typical prescription isodose line used for Gamma Knife radiosurgery treatment planning?
srt.a: The 50% isodose line normalized to the maximum dose within the target volume
c: The 80% isodose line normalized to the maximum dose within the target volume
The 50% isodose line normalized to the maximum dose within the target volume
The 95% isodose line normalized to the maximum dose within the target volume
The 100% isodose line normalized to the maximum dose within the target volume
srt.e: Gamma Knife plans typically prescribe to the 50% isodose line, which provides a steep dose gradient at the target periphery. This is different from linac-based SRS which typically prescribes to 80% or higher.

srt.q: In CyberKnife treatments, what is the primary function of the Synchrony respiratory tracking system?
srt.a: Real-time correlation of external marker motion with internal tumor position for beam tracking
c: Gating the beam on and off based on a fixed respiratory phase window during delivery
Real-time correlation of external marker motion with internal tumor position for beam tracking
Compressing the patient's abdomen to reduce respiratory motion below a set threshold
Post-treatment verification of tumor position using a time-averaged volumetric CT dataset
srt.e: Synchrony creates a correlation model between external LED markers and internal fiducial positions, enabling the robotic arm to continuously adjust beam pointing and track tumor motion in real-time.

srt.q: According to TRS-483, what is the machine-specific reference field (fmsr) for a CyberKnife unit with fixed cones?
srt.a: The largest available fixed cone, typically the 60 mm diameter cone
c: A 10 × 10 cm² field defined by the standard jaw collimation system
The smallest available fixed cone to minimize volume averaging effects
The largest available fixed cone, typically the 60 mm diameter cone
A 5 × 5 cm² field defined by the Iris variable aperture collimator
srt.e: TRS-483 defines the fmsr for CyberKnife as the largest available fixed cone (typically 60 mm), since CyberKnife cannot produce a conventional 10 × 10 cm² reference field.

srt.q: What is the typical single-fraction dose range for SRS treatment of brain metastases?
srt.a: 15 to 24 Gy in a single fraction depending on the tumor size
c: 5 to 8 Gy in a single fraction regardless of tumor size or location
15 to 24 Gy in a single fraction depending on the tumor size
30 to 40 Gy in a single fraction for all intracranial targets
50 to 60 Gy in a single fraction for targets smaller than one cm
srt.e: SRS doses for brain metastases typically range from 15-24 Gy in a single fraction, with RTOG 90-05 establishing maximum tolerated doses of 24, 18, and 15 Gy for tumors ≤20, 21-30, and 31-40 mm respectively.

srt.q: What is the recommended end-to-end test for commissioning a new SRS program at a clinical facility?
srt.a: Anthropomorphic phantom irradiation with film and point dose measurement compared to the treatment plan
c: Output factor measurement for each cone size using a standard Farmer chamber in water
Anthropomorphic phantom irradiation with film and point dose measurement compared to the treatment plan
Verification of gantry angle accuracy using a digital inclinometer at cardinal angles only
MLC log file analysis of a delivered clinical plan without any physical dose measurement
srt.e: End-to-end testing with an anthropomorphic phantom validates the entire SRS workflow from imaging through planning to delivery, with film and point dose measurements providing comprehensive dosimetric verification.

srt.q: In SBRT for lung tumors, what is the primary challenge that distinguishes dosimetry from conventional treatments?
srt.a: Tissue heterogeneity effects on dose calculation accuracy in and around low-density lung tissue
c: The need for higher photon beam energies to penetrate the chest wall and reach the tumor
Tissue heterogeneity effects on dose calculation accuracy in and around low-density lung tissue
The requirement for larger field sizes to accommodate respiratory motion of the target volume
Increased scatter contribution from the ribs requiring additional monitor units per fraction
srt.e: Lung SBRT dosimetry is challenged by the large density heterogeneity between tumor and surrounding lung tissue, requiring advanced dose calculation algorithms (Monte Carlo, AXB) for accurate dose prediction.

srt.q: What volume averaging effect must be considered when measuring beam profiles of small SRS fields?
srt.a: The finite detector size causes apparent broadening of the penumbra and underestimation of the central axis dose
c: Large detectors overestimate the central axis dose due to increased sensitivity in narrow beams
The finite detector size causes apparent broadening of the penumbra and underestimation of the central axis dose
Small detectors create artificial peaks in the profile center due to over-response effects noted
Volume averaging only affects depth dose measurements and not lateral beam profile shapes
srt.e: When the detector volume is comparable to the field size, averaging across the dose gradient causes the measured profile to appear broader and the central axis dose to appear lower than the true values.

srt.q: For SBRT spine treatments, what is the typical dose fractionation scheme and the critical dose constraint?
srt.a: 16-24 Gy in 1 fraction or 24-30 Gy in 3 fractions with spinal cord Dmax limited per protocol
c: 60 Gy in 30 fractions with spinal cord limited to 45 Gy maximum point dose
16-24 Gy in 1 fraction or 24-30 Gy in 3 fractions with spinal cord Dmax limited per protocol
70 Gy in 35 fractions with spinal cord limited to a mean dose below 20 Gy
10 Gy in 5 fractions with the spinal cord excluded from the treatment field entirely
srt.e: Spine SBRT uses hypofractionated regimens (e.g., 16-24 Gy/1 fx or 24-30 Gy/3 fx) with strict spinal cord maximum dose constraints typically around 14 Gy in 1 fraction or 21.9 Gy in 3 fractions.

qa.q: Daily output tolerance?
qa.e: TG-142 daily check. Exceeding requires investigation before treating.

qa.q: Monthly output tolerance?
qa.e: More rigorous than daily — calibrated chamber in water-equivalent phantom.

qa.q: Annual calibration accuracy?
qa.e: TRS-398 absolute calibration. Monthly and daily checks anchor to this.

qa.q: Linac QA TG?
c: TG-51
qa.e: Replaced TG-40. Covers daily/monthly/annual tolerances for modern linacs.

qa.q: IMRT commissioning TG?
qa.e: Benchmark plans and confidence limits for IMRT commissioning.

qa.q: Beam data commissioning TG?
c: TG-51
qa.e: Covers scanning systems, detectors, and measurement protocols.

qa.q: Standard IMRT gamma criteria?
qa.e: TG-218: ≥ 95% universal tolerance, ≥ 90% action limit.

qa.q: Picket fence checks?
qa.a: MLC leaf positioning
c: Output as specified by guidelines
MLC leaf positioning
Gantry angle for radiation dose management
kV quality as specified by guidelines
qa.e: Equally-spaced MLC strips reveal systematic position errors.

qa.q: TG-218 covers?
qa.a: IMRT verification QA
c: Commissioning
IMRT verification QA
Electron dosimetry
Brachytherapy
qa.e: Patient-specific IMRT/VMAT measurement-based verification tolerances.

qa.q: Monthly MLC position tolerance?
qa.a: ± 1 mm
c: ± 1 mm
± 2 mm
± 3 mm
qa.e: Checked with picket fence and log files.

qa.q: TG-275 covers?
qa.a: Physics plan review
c: Linac QA for optimal treatment outcomes
Physics plan review
SBRT in radiation therapy practice
Shielding as part of the clinical workflow
qa.e: Effective physics plan and chart review strategies.

qa.q: Varian MPC checks?
qa.a: Output, geometry, beam symmetry
c: MLC only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Output, geometry, beam symmetry
Imaging only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Couch only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
qa.e: Machine Performance Check: automated daily QA.

qa.q: Daily laser tolerance (SRS)?
qa.a: ± 1 mm
c: ± 1 mm
± 2 mm
± 3 mm
qa.e: Tighter than conventional (± 2 mm).

qa.q: SBRT gamma criteria (TG-218)?
qa.a: 2%/2mm or tighter
c: 5%/5mm in the treatment process
3%/3mm per standard operating procedures
2%/2mm or tighter
1%/1mm in modern radiation oncology
qa.e: Higher dose per fraction demands tighter verification than standard IMRT.

qa.q: Star shot test measures?
qa.a: Radiation isocenter size
c: Output during the planning stage
Radiation isocenter size
Field flatness in the healthcare environment
Beam energy in the radiation therapy context
qa.e: Multiple collimated beams at different gantry angles — intersection circle = radiation isocenter.

qa.q: Monthly flatness tolerance (TG-142)?
qa.e: Beam flatness within central 80% of field.

qa.q: Monthly symmetry tolerance (TG-142)?
qa.e: Left-right and gun-target symmetry within ± 2%.

qa.q: EPID stands for?
qa.a: Electronic Portal Imaging Device
c: Electronic Portal Imaging Device
Enhanced Photon Intensity Detector
External Patient Identification Device
Electron Positioning and Imaging Display
qa.e: Flat-panel detector used for portal imaging and IMRT QA.

qa.q: CBCT geometric accuracy tolerance?
qa.a: ≤ 2 mm
c: ≤ 1 mm
≤ 2 mm
≤ 5 mm
qa.e: Measured with known phantom geometry. Tighter (≤1mm) for SRS.

qa.q: Couch position accuracy tolerance?
qa.e: 6-DOF couch position and rotation accuracy for SRS/SBRT.

qa.q: What does a light-radiation field coincidence test check?
qa.a: Light field matches radiation field edges
c: Output accuracy — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Light field matches radiation field edges
MLC calibration — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Gantry angle accuracy
qa.e: Light should match radiation within ± 2 mm. Checked monthly.

qa.q: Jaw position accuracy (monthly)?
qa.a: ± 2 mm
c: ± 1 mm
± 2 mm
± 5 mm
qa.e: Verified against digital readout and radiation field.

qa.q: What is portal dosimetry?
qa.a: EPID-based transit or non-transit dose measurement
c: CT-based dose calc — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
EPID-based transit or non-transit dose measurement
Film-based verification — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
In-vivo TLD measurement — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
qa.e: Uses EPID to measure delivered fluence and compare to planned. Fast IMRT QA.

qa.q: ArcCHECK is?
qa.a: Cylindrical diode array for VMAT QA
c: Film phantom — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Cylindrical diode array for VMAT QA
Water tank scanner
MLC calibration tool
qa.e: 1386 diodes on a cylindrical surface — rotational geometry ideal for VMAT verification.

qa.q: CALC: Daily output reads 103.2 cGy for 100 MU. Within tolerance?
qa.a: 103.2 cGy exceeds ±2% tolerance — adjust output before treating patients today
c: 103.2 cGy is within ±3% of 100 cGy — passes daily tolerance, document and proceed
103.2 cGy exceeds ±2% tolerance — adjust output before treating patients today
103.2 cGy is exactly at tolerance limit — recheck with second measurement to confirm
103.2 cGy requires immediate service call — do not treat any patients until resolved
qa.e: (103.2-100)/100 = 3.2%. Exceeds ±3% tolerance. Machine out of service until investigated.

qa.q: Gantry angle accuracy tolerance?
qa.e: TG-142 monthly. Digital readout vs spirit level or inclinometer.

qa.q: Collimator angle accuracy tolerance?
qa.e: Verified monthly. Affects MLC-defined field orientation.

qa.q: What is a baseline shift in QA?
qa.a: Progressive drift from calibration value
c: Random fluctuation — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Progressive drift from calibration value
Sudden failure — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Seasonal variation — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
qa.e: Trending output over time reveals systematic drift requiring recalibration.

qa.q: TG-142 replaced which earlier TG?
qa.a: TG-40
c: TG-21
TG-40
TG-51
TG-25
qa.e: TG-40 (1994) was outdated — didn't cover IMRT, IGRT, or modern MLC systems.

qa.q: SRS daily QA must include?
qa.a: Winston-Lutz + output + lasers
c: Output only during the planning stage
Lasers only in the treatment process
Winston-Lutz + output + lasers
Monthly checks daily
qa.e: The trinity of SRS daily QA: mechanical accuracy, dosimetric accuracy, and alignment.

qa.q: CALC: Flatness if Dmax=104, Dmin=96 in central 80%?
qa.e: Flatness = (Dmax-Dmin)/(Dmax+Dmin) × 100 = 8/200 × 100 = 4.0%.

qa.q: CALC: Symmetry if left=52.1%, right=47.9% at symmetric points?
qa.e: Symmetry = (Dmax-Dmin)/Davg × 100 = (52.1-47.9)/50 × 100 = 4.2 %. Exceeds ± 2%.

qa.q: Log file analysis checks?
qa.a: Actual vs planned MLC positions during delivery
c: Patient dose — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Actual vs planned MLC positions during delivery
Beam energy — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Couch weight — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
qa.e: Dynalog/trajectory log files record real-time MLC positions. Deviations indicate delivery errors.

qa.q: Independent MU/dose check purpose?
qa.a: Verify TPS calculation independently
c: Replace TPS — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Verify TPS calculation independently
Check MLC positions
Measure beam energy
qa.e: Second-check calculation using different algorithm/software. Catches gross TPS errors.

qa.q: What QA phantom is standard for CT number accuracy?
qa.a: Catphan
c: CIRS
Catphan
Lucy
StereoPHAN
qa.e: Catphan has known-density inserts for HU accuracy, uniformity, resolution, and noise.

qa.q: Annual mechanical isocenter tolerance?
qa.a: ≤ 2 mm diameter
c: ≤ 0.5 mm as specified by guidelines
≤ 1 mm during routine clinical operations
≤ 2 mm diameter
≤ 5 mm for therapeutic applications
qa.e: Combined gantry + collimator + couch rotation isocenter (star shot). ≤ 1 mm for SRS.

qa.q: TG-100: what paradigm shift does it introduce to radiotherapy QA?
qa.a: Risk-based QA using Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) instead of purely prescriptive checklists
c: More frequent daily checks, following current regulatory and professional standards
Risk-based QA using Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) instead of purely prescriptive checklists
Eliminate all QA — the standard approach used in most clinical departments worldwide
Only equipment-based QA — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
qa.e: TG-100 (2016): shift from 'check everything at fixed frequency' to 'identify highest-risk failure modes and allocate QA resources proportionally.' Uses FMEA: identify failure modes → score Occurrence × Severity × Detectability = Risk Priority Number (RPN). Focus QA on high-RPN items.

qa.q: CALC: FMEA scoring: Occurrence=4, Severity=8, Detectability=6. Risk Priority Number (RPN)?
qa.e: RPN = O × S × D = 4 × 8 × 6 = 192. Each scored 1-10. Max RPN = 1000. High RPN items (e.g., >125) get priority QA attention. Note: same RPN can have very different risk profiles (high S vs high O), so also consider individual scores.

qa.q: Fault tree analysis (FTA) in TG-100 context: what does it determine?
qa.a: The probability that a top-level adverse event occurs by mapping all contributing failure pathways
c: Only equipment failure rate — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
The probability that a top-level adverse event occurs by mapping all contributing failure pathways
Patient satisfaction — based on established physics principles and clinical evidence
Only software errors — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
qa.e: FTA: starts with undesired event (e.g., 'wrong dose delivered') and traces backward through AND/OR gates to root causes. Complements FMEA (bottom-up). Together they form the TG-100 risk assessment framework.

qa.q: Process map in TG-100: what is it and why is it the first step?
qa.a: A flowchart of every step from referral to treatment completion
c: A building floor plan — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
A flowchart of every step from referral to treatment completion
A dose distribution map — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
A QA schedule — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
qa.e: identifies where failures can occur. Process map: referral → consult → sim → contouring → planning → plan check → treatment → follow-up. Each step has sub-steps. FMEA is then applied to each step. Without the map, failure modes are missed. Every department's map is different.

qa.q: MapCHECK (Sun Nuclear): what is it and what does it measure?
qa.a: 2D diode array for patient-specific IMRT/VMAT QA
c: A CT scanner QA tool — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
2D diode array for patient-specific IMRT/VMAT QA
A survey meter — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
An MLC calibration tool — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
qa.e: measures planar dose distribution. MapCHECK: 1527 SunPoint diodes on a 2D plane. Placed perpendicular to beam (or in phantom). Measures composite dose distribution. Compared to TPS-predicted planar dose using gamma analysis. Alternative to film for PSQA.

qa.q: ArcCHECK vs MapCHECK: key geometric difference?
qa.a: ArcCHECK is cylindrical (captures dose from all gantry angles)
c: Same geometry — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
ArcCHECK is cylindrical (captures dose from all gantry angles)
ArcCHECK is smaller — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
MapCHECK is 3D — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
qa.e: MapCHECK is planar (single plane measurement). ArcCHECK: 1386 diodes on cylindrical surface → captures entrance and exit dose for full arc rotation. Better geometric representation of VMAT delivery. MapCHECK: faster setup, good for fixed-gantry IMRT, SBRT composite shots.

qa.q: Octavius 4D (PTW): how does it reconstruct 3D dose from 2D measurements?
qa.a: Rotating phantom with 2D ion chamber array
c: Multiple 3D detectors — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
CT reconstruction — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Rotating phantom with 2D ion chamber array
Only measures point dose
qa.e: samples dose plane at each gantry angle, reconstructs 3D volumetric dose. Octavius: 2D 729/1500 array inside rotating phantom (inclinometer tracks gantry angle). Each gantry angle → one 2D dose plane. Software reconstructs 3D dose distribution. Enables DVH-based gamma analysis (clinically more meaningful than planar).

qa.q: CALC: Gamma analysis: point has measured dose 198 cGy, calculated 205 cGy at same location. Distance-to-agreement = 1.8 mm. Using 3%/3mm criteria, does this point pass?
qa.a: Yes — dose difference 3.4% fails dose criterion but DTA 1.8 mm passes distance criterion → gamma <1
c: Fails both dose difference and distance-to-agreement criteria — gamma definitely >1
Yes — dose difference 3.4% fails dose criterion but DTA 1.8 mm passes distance criterion → gamma <1
Cannot determine without knowing the full 2D dose distribution around the point
Passes the dose criterion marginally but fails the distance-to-agreement criterion
qa.e: Dose diff: |205-198|/205 = 3.4% > 3% (fails dose). DTA: 1.8 mm < 3 mm (passes distance). Gamma = min of combined metric over nearby points → γ = √((3.4/3)² + (1.8/3)²)... but actually gamma searches for closest agreement, and since DTA alone passes, γ<1. Point passes.

qa.q: Global vs local gamma normalisation: which is more stringent and when is each used?
qa.a: Local is more stringent (normalises to each point's dose); global normalises to max/prescription dose. TG-218 recommends global
c: Global is more stringent — normalises to maximum dose, making low-dose regions easier to pass
Local is more stringent (normalises to each point's dose); global normalises to max/prescription dose. TG-218 recommends global
No practical difference between local and global — results are always within 1% of each other
Neither is recommended by TG-218 — absolute dose comparison should replace gamma analysis entirely
qa.e: Local: %DD at each point relative to that point's dose → very strict in low-dose regions (small absolute difference = large %). Global: relative to max or Rx dose → consistent threshold. TG-218: global normalisation with dose threshold (e.g., 10%) to exclude low-dose noise.

qa.q: Varian TrueBeam Machine Performance Check (MPC): what does it automate?
qa.a: Daily checks of beam output, MLC positions, jaw positions, gantry/collimator angles, and imaging isocentre in ~3 minutes
c: Only output constancy check using a single ion chamber measurement at isocentre
Daily checks of beam output, MLC positions, jaw positions, gantry/collimator angles, and imaging isocentre in ~3 minutes
Monthly comprehensive QA including water tank scanning and full beam data verification
Annual calibration protocol replacing the need for TRS-398 physicist measurements
qa.e: MPC: automated phantom (IsoCal) attached to gantry → rapid sequence of tests. Results auto-compared to baseline. Physicist reviews dashboard. Does NOT replace annual calibration or monthly QA but greatly streamlines daily checks.

qa.q: Elekta iCom/machine log file QA: what parameters are recorded?
qa.a: MLC positions, gantry angle, dose rate, jaw positions, and cumulative MU at each control point
c: Only total MU — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
MLC positions, gantry angle, dose rate, jaw positions, and cumulative MU at each control point
Only gantry angle — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Nothing — Elekta doesn't log, as recommended by current international guidelines
qa.e: Elekta iCom logs: ~40 ms sampling. Varian: DynaLog (older) or TrajectoryLog (TrueBeam, 20 ms). Both record actual vs planned parameters. Analysis software (e.g., FractionCheck, Mobius, PerFraction) reconstructs delivered dose from logs.

qa.q: Winston-Lutz test: what does it specifically verify?
qa.a: Coincidence of radiation isocentre with mechanical isocentre — essential for SRS/SBRT
c: Only gantry accuracy — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Coincidence of radiation isocentre with mechanical isocentre — essential for SRS/SBRT
MLC leaf speed — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Output constancy — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
qa.e: BB phantom at mechanical isocentre. Acquire images at multiple gantry/collimator/couch angles. Measure BB-to-radiation-field-centre offset at each. Maximum offset = isocentre accuracy. SRS tolerance: ≤1 mm (TG-142); many centres aim ≤0.5 mm.

qa.q: CALC: Winston-Lutz: BB offsets measured at 8 gantry angles. Maximum offset = 0.8 mm. For SRS with 1 mm tolerance, result?
qa.a: Pass — 0.8 mm < 1.0 mm tolerance
c: Fail — any measurable offset means isocentre accuracy is insufficient for SRS treatment
Pass — 0.8 mm < 1.0 mm tolerance
Fail — exceeds the 0.5 mm tolerance required by AAPM TG-142 for all SRS treatments
Cannot determine from this data — need to also measure at multiple couch angles
qa.e: TG-142 SRS: ≤1 mm radiation-mechanical isocentre coincidence. 0.8 mm passes. However: if using frameless SRS with tight margins (e.g., 1 mm PTV), may want ≤0.5 mm. Clinical context determines whether 0.8 mm is acceptable.

qa.q: CBCT image quality QA (TG-179): what parameters must be tested?
qa.a: HU accuracy, spatial resolution, contrast-to-noise ratio, uniformity, and geometric accuracy
c: Only HU accuracy — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
HU accuracy, spatial resolution, contrast-to-noise ratio, uniformity, and geometric accuracy
Only geometric accuracy — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
No QA needed for CBCT — international standards have removed this requirement from clinical practice
qa.e: TG-179: CBCT quality inferior to diagnostic CT. Monthly: HU constancy (not accuracy — CBCT HU unreliable for dose calc), spatial resolution, CNR, uniformity. Geometric accuracy critical for IGRT alignment. Use CATPHAN or vendor phantom.

qa.q: kV imaging dose from daily CBCT: typical dose per scan and QA consideration?
qa.a: ~10-30 mGy per pelvis CBCT
c: Zero dose
~10-30 mGy per pelvis CBCT
~1 mGy total
Same dose as treatment beam
qa.e: cumulative dose over 30 fractions is ~0.3-0.9 Gy, should be considered in OAR dose. CBCT dose depends on protocol (head ~2-3 mGy, pelvis ~20-30 mGy per scan). Over 30 fractions: pelvis = ~600-900 mGy cumulative to organs not in treatment field. TG-75 recommends tracking. Low-dose CBCT protocols reduce this.

qa.q: HDR Ir-192 source QA: what must be verified before first clinical use of a new source?
qa.a: Source strength (well chamber measurement), source position accuracy (autoradiograph or camera), and transit dose
c: Only source strength — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Source strength (well chamber measurement), source position accuracy (autoradiograph or camera), and transit dose
Only dwell position — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
No QA needed — vendor certificate is sufficient — established commissioning data remains valid throughout the equipment lifetime
qa.e: New source: (1) Measure air kerma strength with calibrated well chamber → compare to vendor certificate (±3%). (2) Verify dwell positions with autoradiograph/camera in catheter → ±1 mm accuracy. (3) Characterise transit dose contribution. (4) Update TPS source data.

qa.q: Well chamber (re-entrant ionisation chamber) for brachytherapy: what does it measure?
qa.a: Source air kerma strength (reference air kerma rate) — calibrated against primary standard for specific isotope
c: Patient surface dose rate for discharge clearance and radiation protection decisions
Source air kerma strength (reference air kerma rate) — calibrated against primary standard for specific isotope
Ambient room dose rate during source transfer to verify shielding adequacy
Cumulative patient dose integrated over the entire brachytherapy treatment duration
qa.e: Well chamber: source inserted into central well → uniform 4π geometry → high reproducibility. Must be calibrated for specific isotope (Ir-192, Co-60, Cs-137) with ADCL/SSDL traceable certificate. Electrometer + well chamber = source strength measurement system.

qa.q: CALC: HDR source measured strength = 38,500 U. Vendor certificate = 40,000 U. Percentage difference?
qa.a: 3.75% — at the limit of ±5% tolerance; investigate
c: 1.5% — well within tolerance, no further investigation warranted
3.75% — at the limit of ±5% tolerance; investigate
5.0% — exactly at tolerance boundary, mandatory investigation required
10% — far outside tolerance, source or measurement system failure likely
qa.e: (40000-38500)/40000 × 100 = 3.75%. AAPM TG-56: ±5% acceptable, but >3% should trigger investigation (check decay correction, chamber calibration date, electrometer reading). Some centres use ±3% as action level.

qa.q: Afterloader QA: daily checks before treatment include?
qa.a: Source position verification, timer accuracy, door interlock, emergency retraction, and radiation monitor function
c: Only source strength — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Source position verification, timer accuracy, door interlock, emergency retraction, and radiation monitor function
Only room survey — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
No daily QA needed — established commissioning data remains valid throughout the equipment lifetime
qa.e: Daily: (1) Source position check (ruler/camera). (2) Timer accuracy (±1%). (3) Door interlock test. (4) Emergency retraction — manual and automatic. (5) Area monitors functional. (6) Survey meter available. (7) Emergency procedures posted. Safety-critical items checked before ANY patient treated.

qa.q: CT simulator QA: what must be verified that differs from diagnostic CT QA?
qa.a: Flat couch top geometric accuracy, external laser alignment to CT slice plane, and HU-to-density table stability
c: Nothing different from diagnostic CT, following current regulatory and professional standards
Flat couch top geometric accuracy, external laser alignment to CT slice plane, and HU-to-density table stability
Only kV accuracy — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Only gantry tilt — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
qa.e: CT sim specific: (1) Flat couch (not curved diagnostic couch) — geometric distortion check. (2) External lasers must align to CT coordinate system for patient marking (≤1 mm). (3) HU-density table verified for dose calculation accuracy. (4) Large bore (80 cm) geometric accuracy at periphery.

qa.q: CALC: CT sim laser alignment: measured offset between laser crosshair and CT isocentre is 1.8 mm. Tolerance is 2 mm. Action?
qa.a: Passes tolerance but close to limit — schedule adjustment soon and monitor trend
c: No action needed — 1.8 mm is well within the 2 mm tolerance with comfortable margin
Passes tolerance but close to limit — schedule adjustment soon and monitor trend
Immediately stop scanning — 1.8 mm is clinically unacceptable for all patient types
Recalibrate CT gantry and replace laser system before any further clinical scans
qa.e: 1.8 mm < 2 mm: technically passes. But trending toward limit suggests drift. Good QA practice: adjust at ~1.5 mm to prevent crossing tolerance. TG-66: CT sim QA tolerances. Laser alignment affects all patient setup marks.

qa.q: AI-based virtual PSQA (e.g., ArcherQA): how does it work?
qa.a: ML model predicts gamma pass rate from plan complexity metrics
c: Replaces all QA permanently
ML model predicts gamma pass rate from plan complexity metrics
Only checks patient ID — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Uses AI to operate measurement equipment
qa.e: low-risk plans skip measurement, high-risk flagged for physical QA. Trained on thousands of measured plans: features include MU, aperture shapes, modulation complexity, small-field fraction. Predicts pass rate with >95% accuracy. Reduces measurement workload ~40-60%. Must be validated locally. Physicist reviews all predictions.

qa.q: Log-file-based dose reconstruction for VMAT QA: advantage over phantom measurement?
qa.a: Captures actual delivered fluence in patient geometry
c: Faster only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Captures actual delivered fluence in patient geometry
More accurate detector — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
No advantage — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
qa.e: detects errors that phantom measurements might miss. Log files (DynaLog/TrajectoryLog/iCom): actual MLC positions, dose rate, gantry angle per control point. Software (Mobius3D, PerFraction): reconstructs dose on patient CT using actual delivery data. Catches: interplay effects, anatomy-specific errors. Complements (not replaces) pre-treatment QA.

qa.q: EPID-based transit dosimetry for in-vivo VMAT QA: what does it detect during treatment?
qa.a: Setup errors, anatomy changes, wrong plan/patient, and dose delivery deviations — in real time
c: Only MLC errors — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Setup errors, anatomy changes, wrong plan/patient, and dose delivery deviations — in real time
Only output errors — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Nothing during treatment — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
qa.e: Exit dose measured by EPID during each fraction. Back-projected to reconstruct dose in patient. Compared to planned dose. Detects: weight loss, tumour regression, effusion, wrong patient/plan. Alerts physicist same day. PerFraction, EPID dosimetry gaining clinical adoption.

qa.q: TG-51 annual calibration: what environmental conditions must be recorded?
qa.a: Temperature, pressure, and humidity
c: Only temperature — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Only pressure — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Temperature, pressure, and humidity
No environmental data needed
qa.e: k_TP = (273.15+T)/(273.15+22) × 101.325/P. Temperature and pressure directly affect ion chamber response (air density). Humidity: typically between 20-80% RH where correction is negligible for most chambers, but must be verified.

qa.q: CALC: Annual calibration: raw reading = 25.67 nC, k_TP = 1.023, k_s = 1.003, k_pol = 0.999, k_elec = 1.000. Corrected reading?
Redundancy check for annual calibration: what independent verification is required?
qa.a: Second chamber + electrometer system (different serial numbers), or external audit
c: No redundancy needed — modern equipment self-monitoring eliminates the need for external verification
Same chamber measured twice — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
Second chamber + electrometer system (different serial numbers), or external audit
Only check TPS agrees — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
qa.e: If primary chamber fails undetected, patients receive wrong dose for a year. Redundancy: independent system (different chamber + electrometer) should agree within ±0.5%. External audit: IAEA postal TLD or NPL alanine audit provides fully independent check.

qa.q: CALC: Two chambers measure output: Chamber A reads 1.003 cGy/MU, Chamber B reads 0.996 cGy/MU. Difference?
qa.a: 0.7% — acceptable redundancy agreement (within ±0.5-1.0% tolerance between chambers)
c: 0.07% — excellent agreement, well within tolerance for redundancy
0.7% — acceptable redundancy agreement (within ±0.5-1.0% tolerance between chambers)
3.5% — exceeds tolerance, investigate chamber calibration and setup differences
7.0% — serious discrepancy, one system likely faulty, do not treat until resolved
qa.e: (1.003-0.996)/1.000 × 100 = 0.7%. Most protocols: agreement within ±1% between independent systems is acceptable. If >1%: investigate chamber calibrations, setup differences, or electrometer issues.

qa.q: TBI (Total Body Irradiation) QA: unique considerations?
qa.a: Extended SSD (~400 cm), whole-body dose uniformity verification, in-vivo dosimetry at multiple anatomical sites
c: Same as standard QA — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
Extended SSD (~400 cm), whole-body dose uniformity verification, in-vivo dosimetry at multiple anatomical sites
Only output check — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
No QA needed — current protocols and vendor specifications provide adequate safety margins
qa.e: TBI: extended distance → output factors differ from standard, beam uniformity across large field critical. Compensators/bolus may be used. In-vivo diodes at head, neck, umbilicus, knees, ankles verify ±10% uniformity. Lung shielding dose verified separately.

qa.q: TSET (Total Skin Electron Therapy) QA: critical measurement?
qa.a: Dose uniformity over body surface using multiple diode/TLD measurements, including shadowed regions
c: Only central axis output — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Dose uniformity over body surface using multiple diode/TLD measurements, including shadowed regions
Only PDD — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Only energy check — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
qa.e: TSET: 6 dual-field positions (Stanford technique). Dose uniformity ±10% over body surface. TLDs/diodes placed at 10+ body locations including axillae, perineum, soles. Shadowed areas (scalp, perineum) may need boost fields. PDD and output at treatment distance verified.

qa.q: Respiratory gating QA: what must be verified?
qa.a: Gating window accuracy, residual motion within gate, beam-on latency, and dose delivery accuracy with gating enabled
c: Only respiratory trace quality — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Gating window accuracy, residual motion within gate, beam-on latency, and dose delivery accuracy with gating enabled
Only patient compliance — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
No QA for gating — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
qa.e: Gating QA: (1) Phase/amplitude gating accuracy with moving phantom. (2) Beam-on/off latency (typically <100 ms). (3) Dose per MU same with gating as without. (4) Duty cycle measurement. (5) Interplay effect for VMAT. Use motion phantom (QUASAR, CIRS) with known amplitude/frequency.

qa.q: FFF (Flattening Filter Free) beam QA: what differs from flattened beam QA?
qa.a: Higher dose rate → verify ion chamber collection efficiency, profile shape has conical peak (not flat), and output factor field-size dependence differs
c: Same profile shape and dose rate as flattened beams — no additional QA considerations needed
Higher dose rate → verify ion chamber collection efficiency, profile shape has conical peak (not flat), and output factor field-size dependence differs
Only requires checking output constancy — profile and symmetry checks not applicable for FFF
Lower dose rate than flattened beams but sharper penumbra requiring more frequent MLC checks
qa.e: FFF: dose rate up to 2400 MU/min (6FFF). Ion chamber saturation (k_s) must be checked. Profiles: peaked (not flat) — symmetry/flatness definitions different. Output factors: steeper field-size dependence. Both Varian (TrueBeam) and Elekta (Versa HD) offer FFF.

qa.q: TG-218: recommended universal tolerance for IMRT/VMAT gamma analysis?
qa.a: 3%/2mm global, ≥95% pass rate
c: 3%/3mm, ≥90% according to clinical protocols
3%/2mm global, ≥95% pass rate
5%/3mm, ≥90% for accurate dose delivery
2%/1mm, ≥98% during the planning stage
qa.e: TG-218 (2018): standardised IMRT QA. Global normalisation, 3%/2mm, 95% pass rate, 10% low-dose threshold. Action limit: <90% = investigate. SBRT: tighter criteria (2%/1mm or 2%/2mm) often used. Absolute dose comparison preferred over relative.

qa.q: Phantom selection for PSQA: homogeneous slab vs anthropomorphic phantom?
qa.a: Homogeneous slab: routine QA (faster, reproducible). Anthropomorphic: commissioning/end-to-end tests
c: Always use anthropomorphic, following current regulatory and professional standards
Always use homogeneous — based on established physics principles and clinical evidence
Homogeneous slab: routine QA (faster, reproducible). Anthropomorphic: commissioning/end-to-end tests
Phantom choice doesn't matter, verified through standard quality assurance protocols
qa.e: Routine PSQA: homogeneous (water-equivalent) phantom = reproducible geometry, well-characterised dose. Commissioning/end-to-end: anthropomorphic phantom with heterogeneities tests full algorithm accuracy. IROC Houston phantom: gold standard for clinical trial credentialing.

qa.q: New linac commissioning: minimum beam data set per AAPM TG-106?
qa.a: PDDs, profiles (inline/crossline/diagonal) at multiple depths and field sizes, output factors, wedge data, electron cones, MLC characteristics
c: Only one PDD curve — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Only output at 10×10 — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
PDDs, profiles (inline/crossline/diagonal) at multiple depths and field sizes, output factors, wedge data, electron cones, MLC characteristics
Vendor data is sufficient, following current regulatory and professional standards
qa.e: TG-106: comprehensive scanning data. Photons: PDDs (5×5 to 40×40), profiles at d_max/5/10/20/30 cm, OFs, wedge. Electrons: PDDs per cone/energy, profiles, cone factors. MLC: transmission, DLG, leaf speed, interdigitation. Data quality = plan quality.

qa.q: CALC: Commissioning water tank scanning: step size 1 mm in buildup region for 6MV PDD. If d_max ≈ 15 mm, approximately how many measurement points in buildup?
qa.a: ~15 points
c: ~5 points
~15 points
~50 points
~150 points
qa.e: 1 mm steps from surface to 15 mm = 15 measurement points (plus surface). Fine resolution in buildup is critical: d_max position, surface dose, and buildup shape all affect TPS beam model. Beyond buildup: 2-5 mm steps adequate. TG-106 recommends ≤1 mm in buildup.

qa.q: What does QA stand for in the context of medical physics?
qa.a: Quality Assurance
c: Quantitative Analysis
Quality Assurance
Quick Assessment
Qualified Approval
qa.e: QA stands for Quality Assurance. It encompasses all planned and systematic actions necessary to provide adequate confidence that a radiotherapy process will produce the intended clinical outcome safely.

qa.q: What is the primary purpose of performing quality assurance in radiation therapy?
qa.a: To ensure treatments are delivered safely and accurately
c: To increase the speed of treatment delivery
To ensure treatments are delivered safely and accurately
To reduce the number of staff needed
To eliminate the need for treatment planning
qa.e: The primary purpose of QA is to ensure that radiation treatments are delivered safely and accurately as prescribed. QA procedures verify that equipment is functioning correctly and that doses delivered match what was planned.

qa.q: How often are daily QA checks typically performed on a linear accelerator?
qa.a: Every day before treating patients
c: Once a year for the treatment procedure
Once a month according to clinical protocols
Every day before treating patients
Only when a problem is noticed
qa.e: Daily QA checks are performed every treatment day before patients are treated. These quick checks verify basic machine parameters such as radiation output, laser alignment, and safety interlocks to ensure the machine is safe to use that day.

qa.q: What is a tolerance level in the context of radiotherapy QA?
qa.a: The maximum acceptable deviation from the expected value
c: The radiation dose a patient can tolerate
The maximum acceptable deviation from the expected value
The number of treatments a machine can deliver per day
The amount of shielding required in a treatment room
qa.e: A tolerance level defines the maximum acceptable deviation of a measured value from its expected or baseline value. If a measurement falls outside the tolerance, corrective action must be taken before treating patients.

qa.q: Which of the following is an example of a basic daily QA check on a linac?
qa.a: Verifying the radiation output is within tolerance
c: Replacing the X-ray target
Verifying the radiation output is within tolerance
Performing a full calibration of the machine
Redesigning the treatment room shielding
qa.e: Verifying that the radiation output (dose rate) is within an acceptable tolerance of the baseline value is a fundamental daily QA check. This ensures the machine is delivering the correct amount of radiation before patients are treated.

qa.q: CALC: An FMEA analysis for SBRT identifies a failure mode with occurrence (O) = 4, severity (S) = 9, and detectability (D) = 7. A proposed QA check reduces O to 2 and D to 3. What are the RPN values before and after, and is the mitigation effective?
qa.a: RPN 252 → 54; effective mitigation
c: RPN 252 → 54; effective mitigation
RPN 180 → 36; effective mitigation
RPN 252 → 54; insufficient — need RPN < 50
RPN 280 → 60; marginal improvement
qa.e: RPN = O × S × D. Before: 4 × 9 × 7 = 252. After: 2 × 9 × 3 = 54. The 79% reduction (252 to 54) demonstrates effective mitigation. In TG-100 FMEA methodology, high-severity failure modes (S >= 8) receive priority even if RPN drops, because severity itself cannot be reduced — only occurrence and detectability can be improved through process controls.

qa.q: CALC: A TG-218 gamma analysis uses 3%/2mm criteria with a 10% dose threshold. The plan has 1200 points above threshold: 1140 pass, 60 fail. The failing points cluster at the PTV-lung interface. What is the pass rate, and what is the most likely cause?
qa.a: 95.0%; lateral disequilibrium at tissue interface
c: 95.0%; lateral disequilibrium at tissue interface
95.0%; MLC calibration error
97.5%; acceptable random noise
93.3%; systematic dose calculation error
qa.e: Pass rate = 1140/1200 = 0.950 = 95.0%. The clustering of failures at the PTV-lung interface suggests lateral electronic disequilibrium effects that the TPS algorithm may not model accurately. AAA is known to overestimate dose at lung-tissue boundaries. This pattern is distinct from MLC errors (which produce scattered failures) or systematic errors (which produce diffuse failures).

qa.q: During monthly QA, the flatness of a 6 MV beam at dmax is measured as 103.8% using the IEC definition (max/CAX × 100%). The TG-142 monthly tolerance for flatness is ±2% from baseline. If the baseline flatness was 101.5%, is this within tolerance?
qa.a: No — 2.3% deviation exceeds the ±2% tolerance
c: Yes — 103.8% is normal for a 6 MV beam
No — 2.3% deviation exceeds the ±2% tolerance
Yes — flatness only matters at 10 cm depth
Cannot determine without symmetry data
qa.e: Deviation from baseline = 103.8% - 101.5% = 2.3%, which exceeds the TG-142 monthly tolerance of ±2% change from baseline. This requires investigation — common causes include flattening filter misalignment, steering coil drift, or bending magnet current change. TG-142 specifies flatness constancy (change from baseline), not absolute flatness limits.

qa.q: CALC: A Winston-Lutz test acquires images at 8 gantry/collimator/couch combinations. The measured BB-to-radiation-center offsets (mm) are: 0.3, 0.4, 0.2, 0.5, 0.3, 0.6, 0.4, 0.3. What is the maximum deviation, and does it meet the TG-142 SRS tolerance?
qa.a: 0.6 mm max — passes the ≤1.0 mm SRS tolerance
c: 0.6 mm max — passes the ≤1.0 mm SRS tolerance
0.6 mm max — fails the ≤0.5 mm tolerance
0.375 mm mean — passes the ≤1.0 mm tolerance
0.6 mm max — requires recalibration
qa.e: The maximum single offset is 0.6 mm. For SRS, TG-142 specifies the Winston-Lutz maximum deviation should be ≤1.0 mm. At 0.6 mm, this passes. The mean (0.375 mm) is informative but the maximum governs acceptance. Some institutions use a tighter action level of 0.75 mm for SRS.

qa.q: CALC: A monthly output QA measurement gives an ion chamber reading of 25.20 nC. The baseline reading under identical conditions (same T, P, or both corrected to reference) was 25.50 nC. What is the percentage output deviation, and does it meet TG-142 monthly tolerance?
qa.a: -1.2%; within ±2% monthly tolerance
c: +1.2%; within tolerance
-1.2%; within ±2% monthly tolerance
-3.0%; exceeds monthly tolerance
-0.6%; within tolerance
qa.e: Output deviation = (current - baseline)/baseline × 100% = (25.20 - 25.50)/25.50 × 100% = -0.30/25.50 × 100% = -1.18% ≈ -1.2%. TG-142 monthly output tolerance is ±2%, so -1.2% is within tolerance but warrants trending. If successive months show a consistent downward drift, target/flattening filter degradation or monitor chamber response change should be investigated before the deviation reaches the action level.

qa.q: TG-218 recommends which gamma analysis passing rate threshold for IMRT/VMAT patient-specific QA?
qa.a: 95% at 3%/2mm with universal tolerance limit and 90% action limit
c: 90% at 3%/3mm with no specific action limit defined
95% at 3%/2mm with universal tolerance limit and 90% action limit
100% at 2%/2mm as universal standard for all plans
85% at 5%/3mm for screening only, no action limit needed
qa.e: TG-218 established statistically derived tolerance and action limits. The recommended criteria are 3%/2mm with 95% tolerance limit (investigate if below) and 90% action limit (do not treat if below), using global dose normalisation.

qa.q: In linac QA, what does the starshot test evaluate?
qa.a: Radiation isocentre size by analysing the intersection of beams at multiple gantry angles
c: Output constancy across multiple energy modes for radiation dose management
MLC leaf position accuracy for individual leaves during the planning stage
Radiation isocentre size by analysing the intersection of beams at multiple gantry angles
EPID image quality and spatial resolution performance
qa.e: A starshot test exposes a film or EPID with narrow strip beams at multiple gantry angles. The minimum circle encompassing all beam intersections defines the radiation isocentre size. TG-142 tolerance for non-SRS: ≤2 mm diameter.

qa.q: Why must gamma analysis use global normalisation rather than local normalisation for routine IMRT QA?
qa.a: Local normalisation inflates pass/fail sensitivity in low-dose regions, producing artificially high failure rates
c: Global normalisation is faster to compute on clinical QA systems
Local normalisation inflates pass/fail sensitivity in low-dose regions, producing artificially high failure rates
There is no difference between local and global normalisation results
Local normalisation only works for 3D dose distributions, not planar
qa.e: Local normalisation divides dose difference by the local calculated dose. In low-dose regions, even small absolute differences become large percentages, causing high failure rates that do not correlate with clinical significance. TG-218 recommends global normalisation to maximum dose.

qa.q: CALC: Monthly output measurement gives readings of 1.005, 1.003, 1.007, 1.002, 1.004 cGy/MU over five sessions. The baseline is 1.000 cGy/MU. TG-142 monthly output tolerance is ±2%. Does this pass, and what is the mean deviation?
qa.a: Passes; mean deviation is +0.42%
c: Fails; mean deviation exceeds +2% tolerance
Passes; mean deviation is +0.42%
Passes; mean deviation is +0.21%
Fails; individual readings exceed tolerance requiring recalibration

qa.q: TG-142: what test specifically evaluates the coincidence of radiation and mechanical isocentre, and what is the SRS tolerance?
qa.a: Winston-Lutz test with ≤1 mm tolerance for SRS
c: Starshot test with ≤2 mm tolerance for all treatments
Winston-Lutz test with ≤1 mm tolerance for SRS
Picket fence test with ≤0.5 mm tolerance for SRS
Light-radiation field coincidence with ≤1 mm tolerance
qa.e: TG-142 specifies the Winston-Lutz test for radiation-mechanical isocentre coincidence. For SRS, the tolerance is ≤1 mm (combining all gantry, collimator, and couch angle contributions). For non-SRS, ≤2 mm is acceptable.

qa.q: CALC: An IMRT plan has 250 measurement points in gamma analysis (3%/2mm, global). 237 points pass. What is the passing rate, and does it meet the TG-218 action limit?
qa.a: 94.8% — above the 90% action limit but below the 95% tolerance limit, requiring investigation
c: 94.8% — above the 90% action limit but below the 95% tolerance limit, requiring investigation
94.8% — plan passes all criteria and can proceed to treatment
95.2% — passes both tolerance and action limits
94.8% — below action limit, plan must not be treated
qa.e: Passing rate = 237/250 × 100 = 94.8%. TG-218: action limit is 90% (do not treat below this), tolerance limit is 95% (investigate below this). At 94.8%, the plan is above the action limit but below the tolerance limit, so investigation is required but treatment is not prohibited.

qa.q: TG-142 monthly tolerance for photon beam flatness constancy?
qa.a: Within 1% of baseline value
c: Within 1% of baseline value
Within 2% of baseline value
Within 3% of baseline value
Within 5% of baseline value
qa.e: TG-142 recommends monthly flatness constancy within 1% of baseline. Changes beyond this suggest beam steering or flattening filter issues requiring investigation.

qa.q: What is the primary purpose of the Winston-Lutz test?
qa.a: Verify radiation isocentre coincidence with mechanical isocentre
c: Verify radiation isocentre coincidence with mechanical isocentre
Measure absolute dose output at the machine calibration point
Evaluate multi-leaf collimator positional accuracy under load
Confirm the uniformity of dose across the radiation field size
qa.e: The Winston-Lutz test uses a small ball bearing at isocentre imaged with a small radiation field at multiple gantry/collimator/couch angles to quantify the radiation-mechanical isocentre offset.

qa.q: CALC: A daily output check gives 102.5 cGy when the baseline reading was 100.0 cGy. What is the percentage deviation?
qa.a: +2.5% above the established baseline
c: +1.5% above the established baseline
+2.5% above the established baseline
+3.5% above the established baseline
+5.0% above the established baseline
qa.e: Deviation = (102.5 - 100.0)/100.0 x 100% = +2.5%. This is within the TG-142 daily tolerance of +/-3% but warrants monitoring for trending.

qa.q: Which device is commonly used for patient-specific VMAT QA with a cylindrical detector array?
qa.a: ArcCHECK (Sun Nuclear) with 1386 diodes in a helical arrangement
c: MapCHECK 2 (Sun Nuclear) with 1527 diodes in a planar layout
ArcCHECK (Sun Nuclear) with 1386 diodes in a helical arrangement
Delta4 Phantom+ (ScandiDos) with two orthogonal planar arrays
MatriXX (IBA Dosimetry) with 1020 ion chambers in a flat grid
qa.e: ArcCHECK is a cylindrical PMMA phantom with 1386 diodes arranged helically, measuring dose in a near-full 360-degree geometry, making it ideal for VMAT verification.

qa.q: What is the typical gamma analysis pass rate criterion used for patient-specific IMRT QA?
qa.a: 3%/3mm with a pass rate threshold of 95% or higher
c: 1%/1mm with a pass rate threshold of 99% or higher
2%/2mm with a pass rate threshold of 98% or higher
3%/3mm with a pass rate threshold of 95% or higher
5%/5mm with a pass rate threshold of 90% or higher
qa.e: The 3%/3mm gamma criterion with at least 95% pass rate is the most commonly applied clinical standard for IMRT/VMAT patient-specific QA, per TG-218 recommendations.

qa.q: What does the picket fence test evaluate on a linear accelerator?
qa.a: MLC leaf position accuracy using equally spaced narrow strips
c: MLC leaf position accuracy using equally spaced narrow strips
Gantry rotation speed uniformity using continuous arc delivery
Beam flatness constancy using a single large open field size
Collimator jaw alignment accuracy using asymmetric field edges
qa.e: The picket fence test delivers narrow MLC-defined strips at known positions. Deviations in strip width or position indicate individual leaf position errors, typically analysed with EPID or film.

qa.q: TG-142 tolerance for daily laser localisation accuracy?
qa.a: +/-2 mm from the defined room isocentre
c: +/-1 mm from the defined room isocentre
+/-2 mm from the defined room isocentre
+/-3 mm from the defined room isocentre
+/-5 mm from the defined room isocentre
qa.e: TG-142 specifies +/-2 mm for daily laser alignment for non-SRS/SBRT treatments. For SRS/SBRT, tighter tolerances of +/-1 mm are recommended.

qa.q: CALC: An ion chamber reads 25.3 nC in a constancy check. The baseline reading was 24.8 nC. What is the percentage output change?
qa.a: +2.0% increase from the baseline value
c: +1.0% increase from the baseline value
+2.0% increase from the baseline value
+3.0% increase from the baseline value
+4.0% increase from the baseline value
qa.e: Change = (25.3 - 24.8)/24.8 x 100% = 0.5/24.8 x 100% = 2.0%. This is within the +/-3% daily tolerance but should be tracked over time for trends.

qa.q: What is the purpose of the star shot test on a linear accelerator?
qa.a: Determine radiation isocentre size by exposing strips at multiple angles
c: Determine radiation isocentre size by exposing strips at multiple angles
Verify beam flatness across several field sizes at different gantry stops
Evaluate patient couch positioning accuracy at multiple table rotations
Measure beam energy constancy by comparing depth dose at several angles
qa.e: Star shots use narrow beam strips exposed at multiple gantry or collimator angles on film/EPID. The intersection pattern reveals the radiation isocentre diameter, ideally less than 1 mm for SRS.

qa.q: What does EPID stand for in the context of linear accelerator QA?
qa.a: Electronic Portal Imaging Device used for treatment verification
c: Electronic Portal Imaging Device used for treatment verification
External Photon Intensity Detector used for dose calibration
Electrostatic Planar Ionisation Detector used for beam scanning
Energised Pixel Irradiation Display used for field visualisation
qa.e: EPIDs are amorphous silicon flat panel detectors mounted on the linac, originally for patient positioning verification but now widely used for dosimetric QA including transit dosimetry and pre-treatment plan verification.

qa.q: What is the recommended frequency for comprehensive annual linac output calibration per TG-142?
qa.a: Full absolute calibration annually following TG-51 or TRS-398 protocol
c: Full absolute calibration annually following TG-51 or TRS-398 protocol
Monthly constancy check using a thimble chamber and solid phantom
Daily dose check with a diode array device before first treatment
Weekly output verification using thermoluminescent dosimeter capsules
qa.e: TG-142 requires annual absolute dose calibration per TG-51 (AAPM) or TRS-398 (IAEA). In South Africa, TRS-398 is the standard protocol. Monthly checks are constancy checks, not full calibrations.

qa.q: What type of detector is used in the Delta4 phantom for patient-specific QA?
qa.a: p-type silicon diodes arranged in two orthogonal detector planes
c: Ionisation chambers arranged in a single horizontal planar array
p-type silicon diodes arranged in two orthogonal detector planes
Diamond detectors arranged in a helical pattern inside cylinder
MOSFET detectors arranged in three perpendicular measurement axes
qa.e: The Delta4 Phantom+ contains 1069 p-type silicon diodes in two orthogonal planes, allowing real-time 3D dose reconstruction and comparison with the treatment planning system.

qa.q: In South Africa, which organisation conducts external dosimetry audit programmes for radiotherapy centres?
qa.a: IAEA/WHO TLD postal dose audit programme coordinated via SSDL network
c: IAEA/WHO TLD postal dose audit programme coordinated via SSDL network
SAHPRA through its annual mandatory on-site dosimetry inspection visits
South African Bureau of Standards calibration laboratory audit directly
HPCSA through its continuing professional development audit framework
qa.e: The IAEA/WHO TLD postal dose audit programme sends TLD capsules to participating centres for irradiation under reference conditions. Results are compared to stated dose. SA centres participate through the IAEA SSDL network.

qa.q: What is the TG-142 tolerance for monthly MLC leaf position accuracy?
qa.a: +/-1 mm from the programmed leaf position
c: +/-1 mm from the programmed leaf position
+/-2 mm from the programmed leaf position
+/-3 mm from the programmed leaf position
+/-5 mm from the programmed leaf position
qa.e: TG-142 recommends +/-1 mm MLC leaf position accuracy checked monthly. This is critical for IMRT/VMAT delivery where small leaf position errors can significantly affect dose distributions.

qa.q: CALC: The measured dose is 0.987 Gy per 100 MU during annual calibration. What adjustment factor restores output to 1.000 Gy/100 MU?
qa.a: Increase output by a factor of 1.013, approximately 1.3%
c: Increase output by a factor of 1.007, approximately 0.7%
Increase output by a factor of 1.013, approximately 1.3%
Decrease output by a factor of 0.987, approximately 1.3%
Increase output by a factor of 1.020, approximately 2.0%
qa.e: Adjustment = 1.000/0.987 = 1.0132, i.e., increase output by approximately 1.3%. This is typically achieved by adjusting the linac gun current or bending magnet current to increase dose per MU.

qa.q: What is the primary purpose of water tank commissioning measurements for a new linear accelerator?
qa.a: Acquire beam data including PDDs, profiles, and output factors for TPS modelling
c: Acquire beam data including PDDs, profiles, and output factors for TPS modelling
Verify that the manufacturer warranty specifications have been fully satisfied
Calibrate the internal linac monitor chambers to the national dosimetry standard
Determine the maximum dose rate capability of each available photon beam energy
qa.e: Commissioning water tank scanning provides PDDs, cross-plane and in-plane profiles, and output factors for all energies and field sizes. This beam data library is essential for accurate TPS dose calculation modelling.

qa.q: What is an IROC Houston phantom used for in radiation therapy?
qa.a: End-to-end credentialing audit for clinical trial participation eligibility
c: End-to-end credentialing audit for clinical trial participation eligibility
Daily machine output verification replacing the standard morning checks
Annual electron beam energy calibration using an independent reference
Patient-specific quality assurance for individual treatment plan approval
qa.e: IROC Houston (formerly RPC) provides anthropomorphic phantoms for end-to-end testing. Centres irradiate the phantom following their clinical workflow, and IROC analyses the results to credential centres for NCI-funded clinical trials.

qa.q: What is the typical leakage radiation tolerance for a linear accelerator treatment head?
qa.a: Less than 0.1% of the useful beam maximum dose rate at 1 metre
c: Less than 0.1% of the useful beam maximum dose rate at 1 metre
Less than 1.0% of the useful beam maximum dose rate at 1 metre
Less than 0.5% of the useful beam maximum dose rate at 1 metre
Less than 0.01% of useful beam maximum dose rate at 1 metre
qa.e: IEC 60601-2-1 specifies head leakage must not exceed 0.1% of the useful beam dose rate averaged over 100 cm2 at 1 m from the source, and 0.2% at any point. This is verified during acceptance testing.

qa.q: CALC: A Farmer chamber has N_D,w = 5.42 x 10^7 Gy/C. If corrected charge is 18.5 nC, what is the absorbed dose to water?
qa.a: Approximately 1.003 Gy at the measurement reference point
c: Approximately 0.753 Gy at the measurement reference point
Approximately 1.003 Gy at the measurement reference point
Approximately 1.253 Gy at the measurement reference point
Approximately 1.503 Gy at the measurement reference point
qa.e: D_w = M_corr x N_D,w = 18.5 x 10^-9 C x 5.42 x 10^7 Gy/C = 18.5 x 5.42 x 10^-2 = 100.27 x 10^-2 = 1.003 Gy. This follows TRS-398 absorbed dose to water formalism.

qa.q: What frequency does TG-142 recommend for optical distance indicator (ODI) verification?
qa.a: Monthly verification with a tolerance of +/-2 mm accuracy
c: Daily verification with a tolerance of +/-1 mm accuracy
Monthly verification with a tolerance of +/-2 mm accuracy
Weekly verification with a tolerance of +/-3 mm accuracy
Annual verification with a tolerance of +/-1 mm accuracy
qa.e: TG-142 specifies monthly ODI checks with +/-2 mm tolerance. The optical distance indicator must agree with the actual SSD to ensure correct patient setup and dose delivery.

qa.q: What is the purpose of a monthly output constancy check using a chamber in solid water?
qa.a: Monitor short-term output stability relative to the last calibration baseline
c: Monitor short-term output stability relative to the last calibration baseline
Provide an absolute dose measurement traceable to the national standard
Replace the annual TRS-398 calibration with a faster verification method
Measure the beam energy spectrum to detect any shifts in beam quality
qa.e: Monthly constancy checks track output relative to baseline. They do not replace annual absolute calibration but provide early warning of output drifts, typically using the same chamber and setup each time.

qa.q: CALC: A TLD in a postal audit reports 1.96 Gy. The stated dose was 2.00 Gy. What is the percentage deviation?
qa.a: -2.0% below the stated reference dose value
c: -1.0% below the stated reference dose value
-2.0% below the stated reference dose value
-3.0% below the stated reference dose value
-4.0% below the stated reference dose value
qa.e: Deviation = (1.96 - 2.00)/2.00 x 100% = -2.0%. The IAEA/WHO TLD audit acceptance criterion is typically +/-5%, so this result passes. Most well-calibrated centres achieve within +/-3%.

qa.q: What is the MapCHECK device primarily designed to measure in QA?
qa.a: Planar dose distributions using a 2D diode array for IMRT verification
c: Planar dose distributions using a 2D diode array for IMRT verification
Volumetric dose reconstruction using 3D gel dosimetry analysis tools
Beam energy spectra using a multi-element scintillation detector system
Patient surface dose using an array of thin film radiation dosimeters
qa.e: MapCHECK (Sun Nuclear) is a 2D diode array with 1527 detectors arranged in a planar configuration. It measures dose distributions for comparison with TPS-calculated planar doses using gamma analysis.

qa.q: TG-142 annual photon beam energy constancy tolerance measured via PDD or TMR?
qa.a: Within +/-1% of baseline PDD value at the reference depth
c: Within +/-1% of baseline PDD value at the reference depth
Within +/-2% of baseline PDD value at the reference depth
Within +/-3% of baseline PDD value at the reference depth
Within +/-5% of baseline PDD value at the reference depth
