qa.e: TG-142 specifies +/-1% for annual photon energy constancy check, typically verified by comparing PDD(10) or %dd(10)x with baseline values. Energy shifts affect depth dose and may indicate accelerator waveguide problems.

qa.q: What information does the beam quality specifier %dd(10)x provide?
qa.a: Photon beam energy indicator based on dose ratio at 10 cm depth in water
c: Photon beam energy indicator based on dose ratio at 10 cm depth in water
Electron contamination level measured as surface dose relative to maximum
Beam flatness parameter defined by dose variation across the field width
Monitor chamber linearity coefficient derived from multiple dose readings
qa.e: %dd(10)x is the photon component of percentage depth dose at 10 cm depth in a 10x10 cm2 field. It is used in TG-51 to select beam quality conversion factors and is measured using a lead foil to remove electron contamination.

qa.q: What is the recommended Winston-Lutz tolerance for SRS treatments per TG-142?
qa.a: Radiation-mechanical isocentre offset must be 1.0 mm or less overall
c: Radiation-mechanical isocentre offset must be 0.5 mm or less overall
Radiation-mechanical isocentre offset must be 1.0 mm or less overall
Radiation-mechanical isocentre offset must be 2.0 mm or less overall
Radiation-mechanical isocentre offset must be 3.0 mm or less overall
qa.e: TG-142 recommends 1 mm or less for SRS Winston-Lutz tests. Many SRS programmes aim for 0.75 mm or better. The test is performed at multiple gantry, collimator, and couch angles to characterise the isocentre sphere.

qa.q: CALC: Monthly output readings are 100.2, 100.5, 99.8, 100.1, and 100.4 cGy. What is the mean output?
qa.a: 100.2 cGy as the average of the five readings taken
c: 100.0 cGy as the average of the five readings taken
100.2 cGy as the average of the five readings taken
100.4 cGy as the average of the five readings taken
100.6 cGy as the average of the five readings taken
qa.e: Mean = (100.2 + 100.5 + 99.8 + 100.1 + 100.4)/5 = 501.0/5 = 100.2 cGy. Multiple readings reduce random measurement uncertainty and provide a more reliable constancy result.

qa.q: What ion chamber type does TRS-398 recommend for reference dosimetry in photon beams?
qa.a: Cylindrical Farmer-type chamber with a volume of approximately 0.6 cm3
c: Cylindrical Farmer-type chamber with a volume of approximately 0.6 cm3
Parallel-plate Markus chamber with a volume of approximately 0.05 cm3
Micro-ionisation chamber with a sensitive volume of under 0.01 cm3
Large-volume spherical chamber with a volume of approximately 30 cm3
qa.e: TRS-398 recommends Farmer-type cylindrical chambers (0.6 cm3) for reference photon and high-energy electron dosimetry. Their stable response, well-characterized perturbation factors, and robust construction make them the standard.

qa.q: What does OSLD stand for in dosimetry audit programmes?
qa.a: Optically Stimulated Luminescence Dosimeter for dose verification
c: Optically Stimulated Luminescence Dosimeter for dose verification
Organic Semiconductor Luminescent Diode for beam quality checks
Optical Surface Localisation Device for patient positioning setup
Output Signal Level Detector for electrometer quality assurance
qa.e: OSLDs (typically Al2O3:C) are stimulated with light after irradiation to produce luminescence proportional to dose. They offer advantages over TLDs including re-readability and simpler readout, increasingly used in postal audits.

qa.q: CALC: A linac delivers 400 MU/min. A plan requires 250 MU. What is the beam-on time?
qa.a: 37.5 seconds of continuous beam-on delivery time
c: 25.0 seconds of continuous beam-on delivery time
37.5 seconds of continuous beam-on delivery time
45.0 seconds of continuous beam-on delivery time
62.5 seconds of continuous beam-on delivery time
qa.e: Time = 250 MU / (400 MU/min) = 0.625 min = 37.5 seconds. Actual treatment time is longer due to gantry rotation, MLC motion, and beam hold-off periods in VMAT delivery.

qa.q: What is the purpose of an electrometer linearity check?
qa.a: Verify that electrometer response is proportional to charge across its range
c: Verify that electrometer response is proportional to charge across its range
Calibrate the electrometer voltage output against the national standard
Determine the electrometer leakage current contribution to measurements
Confirm the electrometer can withstand high radiation fields undamaged
qa.e: Electrometer linearity ensures accurate charge measurement across the clinical range. Non-linearity would cause systematic dose measurement errors. Linearity is typically checked by varying MU or using a charge injection source.

qa.q: TG-142 recommends which frequency for MLC picket fence or garden fence tests?
qa.a: Monthly verification of MLC leaf position accuracy is recommended
c: Daily verification of MLC leaf position accuracy is recommended
Monthly verification of MLC leaf position accuracy is recommended
Quarterly verification of MLC leaf position is recommended
Annual verification of MLC leaf position accuracy is recommended
qa.e: TG-142 recommends monthly MLC leaf position tests (picket fence). For IMRT/VMAT linacs, more frequent checks (weekly) may be warranted depending on clinical workload and plan complexity.

qa.q: CALC: Ion chamber with k_pol = 1.003, k_s = 1.002 measures 20.0 nC raw. What is the corrected charge?
qa.a: 20.10 nC after applying both correction factors to reading
c: 19.90 nC after applying both correction factors to reading
20.00 nC after applying both correction factors to reading
20.10 nC after applying both correction factors to reading
20.20 nC after applying both correction factors to reading
qa.e: M_corr = M_raw x k_pol x k_s = 20.0 x 1.003 x 1.002 = 20.0 x 1.005006 = 20.10 nC. These correction factors account for charge collection inefficiency and polarity-dependent response.

qa.q: What is the primary advantage of EPID-based dosimetry over ion chamber array devices?
qa.a: Much higher spatial resolution enabling detection of small delivery errors
c: Much higher spatial resolution enabling detection of small delivery errors
No calibration required since the EPID is factory-calibrated permanently
Direct measurement of absolute dose without any correction factors needed
Ability to function without any connection to the treatment control system
qa.e: EPIDs offer approximately 0.4 mm pixel resolution compared to 5-10 mm detector spacing in chamber/diode arrays. This high resolution improves sensitivity to MLC positioning errors, particularly for small IMRT segments.

qa.q: What does the ion recombination correction factor k_s account for?
qa.a: Incomplete charge collection due to ion recombination in the chamber cavity
c: Incomplete charge collection due to ion recombination in the chamber cavity
Temperature dependence of the gas density inside the ionisation chamber
Energy-dependent response variation of the chamber across photon spectrum
Stem and cable irradiation effects contributing to spurious signal in readout
qa.e: k_s corrects for ions that recombine before being collected. It depends on dose rate and collecting voltage. The two-voltage technique is used to determine k_s: measure at full and half voltage, then apply the TRS-398 formula.

qa.q: CALC: Water tank scan shows PDD(10) = 67.2% for 10x10 cm2. Using %dd(10)x = PDD(10) x 0.97, what is %dd(10)x?
qa.a: Approximately 65.2% for beam quality factor selection in TG-51
c: Approximately 61.5% for beam quality factor selection in TG-51
Approximately 63.3% for beam quality factor selection in TG-51
Approximately 65.2% for beam quality factor selection in TG-51
Approximately 69.2% for beam quality factor selection in TG-51
qa.e: %dd(10)x = 67.2 x 0.97 = 65.18 = approximately 65.2%. This approximate relationship removes electron contamination contribution. The precise method uses a 1 mm lead foil at 50 cm depth.

qa.q: What is the TG-142 daily tolerance for radiation and light field coincidence?
qa.a: Light and radiation field edges must agree within +/-2 mm
c: Light and radiation field edges must agree within +/-1 mm
Light and radiation field edges must agree within +/-2 mm
Light and radiation field edges must agree within +/-3 mm
Light and radiation field edges must agree within +/-5 mm
qa.e: TG-142 specifies +/-2 mm for daily radiation/light field coincidence for non-IMRT/non-SRS treatments. The light field is used for patient setup, so it must accurately represent the radiation field.

qa.q: What is the two-voltage technique used for in ion chamber dosimetry?
qa.a: Determination of the ion recombination correction factor k_s value
c: Determination of the ion recombination correction factor k_s value
Measurement of the polarity correction factor k_pol for collection
Verification of the electrometer linearity across operating range
Calibration of the chamber sensitivity against a reference source
qa.e: The two-voltage technique measures charge at full (V1) and reduced (V2, typically V1/2) collecting voltage. k_s is calculated from the ratio of readings using formulas in TRS-398, accounting for initial and general recombination.

qa.q: CALC: Annual TG-51 calibration yields 1.005 cGy/MU at d_ref. Clinical requirement is 1.000 cGy/MU. What adjustment?
qa.a: Decrease output by 0.5% to match clinical specification
c: Decrease output by 0.5% to match clinical specification
Increase output by 0.5% to match clinical specification
Decrease output by 1.0% to match clinical specification
Increase output by 1.0% to match clinical specification
qa.e: The output is 0.5% high (1.005 vs 1.000). Reduce by (1.005 - 1.000)/1.000 x 100% = 0.5%. Adjustment is made via the linac service mode, typically modifying the target current or pulse forming network.

qa.q: What is the significance of the k_Q factor in TRS-398 absorbed dose determination?
qa.a: Converts the Co-60 chamber calibration factor to the user beam quality
c: Converts the Co-60 chamber calibration factor to the user beam quality
Corrects for air temperature and pressure at time of the measurement
Accounts for the polarity effect of chamber at different bias voltages
Compensates for ion recombination losses at clinical dose rate values
qa.e: k_Q is the beam quality correction factor that converts N_D,w,Q0 (calibrated at Co-60) to the user beam quality Q. It depends on chamber type and beam quality index, and is tabulated in TRS-398.

qa.q: Which test verifies MLC leaf speed adequacy for VMAT delivery?
qa.a: Dynamic MLC delivery tests at maximum planned leaf speed with dose check
c: Dynamic MLC delivery tests at maximum planned leaf speed with dose check
Static picket fence test with all leaves positioned at fixed locations
Gantry rotation speed test with MLC leaves fully retracted from field
Collimator jaw calibration check with leaves parked behind backup jaws
qa.e: VMAT requires leaves to move at high speeds. Dynamic tests deliver dose while leaves travel at maximum planned velocities, verifying dose accuracy under realistic VMAT conditions. Dose errors indicate leaf speed limitations.

qa.q: CALC: A MapCHECK shows 920 out of 960 points pass 3%/3mm gamma. What is the pass rate?
qa.a: 95.8% of evaluated points pass the gamma criteria
c: 93.2% of evaluated points pass the gamma criteria
95.8% of evaluated points pass the gamma criteria
97.4% of evaluated points pass the gamma criteria
98.6% of evaluated points pass the gamma criteria
qa.e: Pass rate = (920/960) x 100% = 95.83 = approximately 95.8%. This exceeds the common 95% threshold for 3%/3mm and would typically be considered clinically acceptable per TG-218 guidelines.

qa.q: What is the purpose of performing a couch walk-out test during annual linac QA?
qa.a: Verify couch travels accurately in vertical, lateral, and longitudinal axes
c: Verify couch travels accurately in vertical, lateral, and longitudinal axes
Confirm that the couch can support maximum rated patient weight safely
Check that couch rotation angle matches the digital readout precisely
Ensure the couch surface attenuation is within acceptable dose limits
qa.e: Couch walk-out tests measure positional accuracy over the full range of travel. A dial indicator or ruler tracks actual vs displayed position. TG-142 specifies +/-2 mm for non-SRS and +/-1 mm for SRS applications.

qa.q: What is TG-142 recommended frequency for backup dose monitor channel verification?
qa.a: Annual verification of the secondary backup dose monitor system
c: Daily verification of the secondary backup dose monitor system
Monthly verification of the secondary backup dose monitor
Annual verification of the secondary backup dose monitor system
Only at acceptance testing of the backup dose monitor system
qa.e: TG-142 recommends annual verification of the backup monitor chamber. The dual monitor chamber system is a critical safety interlock: if the primary fails, the backup terminates the beam. Constancy between channels is checked annually.

qa.q: CALC: Cross-plane profile shows D_max = 104.2% and D_min = 96.1% in the flattened region. What is the flatness?
qa.a: Flatness of 8.1% calculated as maximum minus minimum range
c: Flatness of 4.2% calculated as maximum minus minimum range
Flatness of 6.3% calculated as maximum minus minimum range
Flatness of 8.1% calculated as maximum minus minimum range
Flatness of 10.5% calculated as maximum minus minimum range
qa.e: Flatness = D_max - D_min = 104.2 - 96.1 = 8.1%. Using the IEC formula: (D_max - D_min)/(D_max + D_min) x 200 = 8.1/200.3 x 200 = 8.09%. Both approaches give approximately 8.1%.

qa.q: In South Africa, what is the HPCSA role regarding medical physicists performing QA?
qa.a: Registers medical physicists and defines scope of practice for clinical work
c: Registers medical physicists and defines scope of practice for clinical work
Directly approves each individual QA protocol before clinical implementation
Manufactures and distributes calibrated dosimetry equipment to all centres
Performs mandatory quarterly on-site inspections of all QA test results
qa.e: The Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA) registers clinical medical physicists and defines their scope of practice, ensuring qualified professionals perform QA. SAHPRA handles equipment licensing separately.

qa.q: CALC: Electron output calibration measures 0.978 Gy per 100 MU at d_ref. Tolerance is +/-2%. Is this within tolerance?
qa.a: No, deviation is -2.2% which just exceeds the +/-2% tolerance limit
c: Yes, the output is within tolerance at only -1.8% deviation from unity
No, deviation is -2.2% which just exceeds the +/-2% tolerance limit
Yes, the output is within tolerance at only -2.0% deviation exactly
No, deviation is -3.2% which far exceeds the +/-2% tolerance limit
qa.e: Deviation = (0.978 - 1.000)/1.000 x 100% = -2.2%. This exceeds the +/-2% tolerance. Output adjustment is required before clinical use. The beam current or scattering foil position may need service adjustment.

qa.q: According to TG-142, what is the tolerance for photon beam output constancy during daily morning checkout?
qa.a: Within ± 3% of the baseline value
c: Within ± 1% of the baseline value
Within ± 2% of the baseline value
Within ± 3% of the baseline value
Within ± 5% of the baseline value
qa.e: TG-142 specifies a ± 3% tolerance for daily photon output constancy checks. This is a functional check; the more stringent ± 1% tolerance applies to monthly calibration verification. Daily checks use a calibrated ion chamber or diode in a reproducible geometry.

qa.q: CALC: A gamma analysis of an IMRT QA plan shows 285 out of 300 measured points pass the 3%/2mm criteria. What is the gamma pass rate?
qa.e: Gamma pass rate = (passing points / total points) × 100% = (285 / 300) × 100% = 95.0%. Per TG-218, the recommended action limit for IMRT QA is a gamma pass rate ≥ 90% at 3%/2mm, so this plan passes. The tolerance limit is 95%.

qa.q: What is the TG-142 monthly tolerance for MLC leaf position accuracy?
qa.a: ± 1 mm from the expected position
c: ± 0.5 mm from the expected position
± 1 mm from the expected position
± 2 mm from the expected position
± 3 mm from the expected position
qa.e: TG-142 recommends a monthly MLC leaf position accuracy check with a tolerance of ± 1 mm. This is verified using a picket fence or garden fence test pattern, typically analysed from an EPID image. For IMRT/VMAT delivery, leaf positioning accuracy is critical for plan fidelity.

qa.q: In EPID-based dosimetry for IMRT QA, what is the primary advantage over traditional phantom-based measurement?
qa.a: It enables transit dosimetry with in-vivo dose verification during actual patient treatment
c: It eliminates the need for any baseline calibration of the measurement system
It enables transit dosimetry with in-vivo dose verification during actual patient treatment
It measures dose in water-equivalent conditions without any corrections needed
It provides better spatial resolution than all other detector systems available
qa.e: EPID dosimetry can be used for pre-treatment verification (like phantom QA) and for transit dosimetry during patient treatment, providing true in-vivo dose verification. This is a significant advantage as it verifies the actual delivered dose including patient-specific anatomy effects.

qa.q: CALC: A linac monthly output check measures 1.014 Gy/100 MU. The baseline output is 1.000 Gy/100 MU. What is the percentage deviation, and does it meet the TG-142 monthly tolerance?
qa.a: +1.4%, which is within the ± 2% monthly tolerance
c: +1.4%, which is within the ± 2% monthly tolerance
+1.4%, which exceeds the ± 1% monthly tolerance requiring adjustment
+0.14%, which is well within all tolerance levels
+14%, which grossly exceeds all acceptable tolerance levels
qa.e: Deviation = (1.014 - 1.000)/1.000 × 100% = +1.4%. TG-142 monthly output constancy tolerance is ± 2%, so +1.4% is within tolerance. However, the trend should be monitored, and if it continues to drift, output calibration adjustment may be warranted at the next scheduled full calibration.

qa.q: What jaw calibration tolerance does TG-142 recommend for monthly symmetric jaw position checks?
qa.a: ± 2 mm for each jaw position
c: ± 0.5 mm for each jaw position
± 1 mm for each jaw position
± 2 mm for each jaw position
± 3 mm for each jaw position
qa.e: TG-142 specifies ± 2 mm monthly tolerance for jaw position accuracy. This is verified by comparing light field size to radiation field size or using the digital jaw readout against a known standard. More stringent tolerances may apply for IMRT-capable machines.

qa.q: According to TG-218, what are the recommended tolerance and action limits for IMRT QA gamma analysis at 3%/2mm criteria?
qa.a: Tolerance limit of 95% and action limit of 90% pass rate
c: Tolerance limit of 98% and action limit of 95% pass rate
Tolerance limit of 95% and action limit of 90% pass rate
Tolerance limit of 90% and action limit of 85% pass rate
Tolerance limit of 100% and action limit of 95% pass rate
qa.e: TG-218 recommends universal tolerance and action limits for IMRT QA: at 3%/2mm with local normalisation, the tolerance limit (investigation threshold) is 95% and the action limit (requiring plan modification or investigation) is 90%. These are based on statistical analysis of multi-institutional data.

qa.q: What is the TG-142 tolerance for laser alignment accuracy as checked during daily QA?
qa.a: ± 2 mm from the defined isocentre
c: ± 0.5 mm from the defined isocentre
± 1 mm from the defined isocentre
± 2 mm from the defined isocentre
± 3 mm from the defined isocentre
qa.e: TG-142 specifies daily laser alignment accuracy within ± 2 mm of the defined isocentre position. Lasers are checked against room marks or a dedicated laser QA phantom. For SRS machines, a tighter ± 1 mm tolerance applies. Monthly laser checks also have ± 1 mm tolerance.

qa.q: CALC: A beam symmetry measurement shows readings of 82.3 cGy and 78.1 cGy at symmetric points on either side of the central axis. What is the symmetry value?
qa.e: Symmetry = (D_max - D_min) / (D_max + D_min) × 200% or using the common formula: |(D_max - D_min)/(D_max + D_min)| × 200 = |(82.3-78.1)/(82.3+78.1)| × 200 = |4.2/160.4| × 200 = 0.02618 × 200 = 5.24% ≈ 5.2%. TG-142 monthly tolerance for symmetry is ± 3%, so this exceeds tolerance and requires investigation.

qa.q: What parameter does the beam flatness measurement assess in a radiation field profile?
qa.a: The maximum variation in dose across the flattened region (80% of field width) of the beam profile
c: The dose rate at the central axis compared to the dose rate at the field edge
The maximum variation in dose across the flattened region (80% of field width) of the beam profile
The penumbra width measured from 80% to 20% dose levels at the field border
The ratio of surface dose to maximum dose along the central axis depth profile
qa.e: Flatness is defined as (D_max - D_min)/(D_max + D_min) × 100% within the flattened region, typically the central 80% of the field width at a reference depth. TG-142 monthly tolerance for flatness is ± 2%. This metric is only applicable to flattened beams, not FFF beams.

qa.q: What is the TG-142 tolerance for couch lateral and longitudinal position accuracy?
qa.a: ± 2 mm for both lateral and longitudinal positions
c: ± 1 mm for both lateral and longitudinal positions
± 2 mm for both lateral and longitudinal positions
± 3 mm for both lateral and longitudinal positions
± 5 mm for both lateral and longitudinal positions
qa.e: TG-142 specifies ± 2 mm tolerance for treatment couch position accuracy in lateral, longitudinal, and vertical directions during monthly checks. Couch walkout (position change during rotation) should also be verified. For SRS, tighter tolerances of ± 1 mm apply.

qa.q: CALC: During annual TG-51 calibration, a physicist measures M_raw = 25.67 nC, P_TP = 1.023, P_pol = 0.998, P_ion = 1.003, P_elec = 1.000, and N_D,w = 5.432 cGy/nC. What is the absorbed dose to water per 100 MU?
What is the recommended frequency for kV imaging QA (geometric accuracy and image quality) according to TG-142?
qa.a: Monthly for geometric accuracy and annually for comprehensive image quality
c: Daily for both geometric accuracy and comprehensive image quality
Monthly for geometric accuracy and annually for comprehensive image quality
Quarterly for geometric accuracy and annually for comprehensive image quality
Only annually for both geometric accuracy and comprehensive image quality
qa.e: TG-142 recommends monthly checks for kV imaging geometric accuracy (scaling, isocentre coincidence with ± 2 mm tolerance) and annual comprehensive image quality evaluation (spatial resolution, contrast, noise, uniformity). Daily checks include basic functionality verification.

qa.q: For CBCT QA, what is the TG-142 tolerance for the coincidence of the imaging isocentre and the treatment isocentre?
qa.a: ± 2 mm for non-SRS and ± 1 mm for SRS applications
c: ± 1 mm for all clinical applications regardless of technique
± 2 mm for non-SRS and ± 1 mm for SRS applications
± 3 mm for all clinical applications as a universal standard
± 5 mm for non-SRS and ± 2 mm for SRS applications
qa.e: TG-142 specifies that the CBCT imaging isocentre must coincide with the radiation/treatment isocentre within ± 2 mm for conventional treatments and ± 1 mm for SRS/SBRT. This is verified monthly using dedicated imaging QA phantoms (e.g., Catphan or equivalent).

qa.q: CALC: An MLC picket fence test measured on EPID shows leaf bank A has a maximum deviation of 0.7 mm and leaf bank B has a maximum deviation of 1.2 mm. Are both banks within TG-142 monthly tolerance?
qa.a: Bank A passes (≤ 1 mm) but Bank B fails (> 1 mm tolerance)
c: Both banks pass as the tolerance is ± 2 mm for monthly checks
Bank A passes (≤ 1 mm) but Bank B fails (> 1 mm tolerance)
Both banks fail as the tolerance is ± 0.5 mm for MLC checks
Bank B passes but Bank A fails due to different tolerances per bank
qa.e: TG-142 monthly MLC leaf position tolerance is ± 1 mm. Bank A at 0.7 mm maximum deviation passes. Bank B at 1.2 mm exceeds the ± 1 mm tolerance and requires investigation — the affected leaf or leaves may need mechanical adjustment or recalibration.

qa.q: What is the purpose of the daily morning checkout procedure for a medical linear accelerator?
qa.a: To verify basic safety interlocks, beam output constancy, and mechanical parameters before patient treatments
c: To perform a complete recalibration of all beam energies to TG-51 standards
To verify basic safety interlocks, beam output constancy, and mechanical parameters before patient treatments
To validate all IMRT patient plans scheduled for the day against measured data
To conduct a full mechanical and dosimetric annual inspection in abbreviated form
qa.e: The daily morning checkout is a functional safety verification performed before the first patient. It includes safety interlock checks (door, beam-on indicators, emergency stops), output constancy (± 3%), laser alignment (± 2 mm), and basic mechanical checks. This is standard practice at all SA radiotherapy centres.

qa.q: In gamma analysis, what does a gamma index value of exactly 1.0 at a measurement point signify?
qa.a: The point is exactly on the boundary of the acceptance criteria ellipsoid
c: The measured and calculated dose are identical at that point with no error
The point is exactly on the boundary of the acceptance criteria ellipsoid
The point fails the gamma criteria and requires plan modification
The dose difference is exactly 1% at that measurement location
qa.e: The gamma index combines dose difference and distance-to-agreement into a single metric. A gamma value ≤ 1.0 means the point passes (within the acceptance ellipsoid). A value of exactly 1.0 means the point is precisely on the boundary of the acceptance criteria. Values > 1.0 indicate failure.

qa.q: CALC: A monthly beam energy check measures %dd(10) = 67.8% for a 6 MV photon beam. The baseline value is 66.5%. What is the deviation and is it within TG-142 monthly tolerance?
qa.a: +1.3%, which exceeds the ± 1% monthly tolerance
c: +1.3%, which is within the ± 2% monthly tolerance
+1.3%, which exceeds the ± 1% monthly tolerance
+0.13%, which is well within all tolerance levels
+1.3%, which is within the ± 1.5% monthly tolerance
qa.e: Deviation = 67.8% - 66.5% = 1.3 percentage points. TG-142 monthly tolerance for photon beam energy (%dd(10) or equivalent) is ± 1%. A 1.3% deviation exceeds this tolerance and requires investigation — possible causes include target degradation, bending magnet current drift, or energy switch issues.

qa.q: What annual QA test is specifically required by TG-142 to verify the accuracy of the treatment planning system beam model?
qa.a: Comparison of measured and calculated dose distributions for a set of standard test fields
c: A full re-commissioning of all beam data in the water scanning tank
Comparison of measured and calculated dose distributions for a set of standard test fields
Measurement of absolute dose output at a single reference point only
Review of the software version number and vendor release documentation
qa.e: TG-142 annual QA includes verification that the TPS beam model remains accurate by comparing calculated doses against measurements for standard field configurations. This includes open fields, wedged fields, off-axis points, and heterogeneous geometries. Full re-commissioning is not required annually unless significant beam changes occur.

qa.q: What is the TG-142 annual tolerance for radiation-mechanical isocentre coincidence (star shot or equivalent)?
qa.a: ≤ 2 mm diameter for non-SRS linacs
c: ≤ 0.5 mm diameter for all linacs
≤ 1 mm diameter for all linacs
≤ 2 mm diameter for non-SRS linacs
≤ 5 mm diameter for non-SRS linacs
qa.e: TG-142 specifies the radiation-mechanical isocentre coincidence (isocentre walkout) tolerance as ≤ 2 mm diameter for non-SRS linacs and ≤ 1 mm diameter for SRS-dedicated linacs. This is tested annually using star shot films or electronic portal imaging at multiple gantry and collimator angles.

qa.q: CALC: A physicist performs monthly output constancy using a farmer chamber. Three readings are: 24.56 nC, 24.61 nC, 24.58 nC. The baseline corrected reading is 24.30 nC. Assuming identical environmental conditions, what is the output deviation?
qa.e: Mean reading = (24.56 + 24.61 + 24.58)/3 = 73.75/3 = 24.583 nC. Deviation = (24.583 - 24.30)/24.30 × 100% = 0.283/24.30 × 100% = 1.165% ≈ +1.2%. This is within the TG-142 monthly tolerance of ± 2% but should be trended to detect systematic drift.

qa.q: What is the primary purpose of TG-142 in the context of South African medical physics practice?
qa.a: It provides comprehensive QA guidelines and tolerances for medical linear accelerators used in radiation therapy
c: It establishes the legal framework for radiation safety licensing in South Africa
It provides comprehensive QA guidelines and tolerances for medical linear accelerators used in radiation therapy
It defines the scope of practice and professional registration requirements for medical physicists
It specifies the dose limits for occupationally exposed workers at radiotherapy centres
qa.e: TG-142 (AAPM Task Group 142) provides quality assurance guidelines for medical accelerators, defining test frequencies and tolerances for mechanical, dosimetric, safety, and imaging parameters. While not a South African regulation itself, SASQP and SA radiotherapy centres widely adopt TG-142 as the standard of practice for linac QA.

qa.q: According to TG-142, what is the tolerance for daily photon beam output constancy checks?
qa.a: 3% from baseline
c: 3% from baseline
1% from baseline
5% from baseline
2% from baseline
qa.e: TG-142 recommends that daily photon beam output constancy measured with a dosimetry device should be within 3% of the baseline value. If the output deviates beyond this tolerance, the machine should not be used until investigated.

qa.q: What is the recommended frequency for TG-51-based annual calibration of photon and electron beams?
qa.a: Annually with monthly constancy checks
c: Annually with monthly constancy checks
Every two years with quarterly constancy
Semi-annually with no constancy checks needed
Only at commissioning and after major repairs
qa.e: TG-51 absolute dose calibration is performed annually for each beam energy, while monthly output constancy checks using a calibrated ion chamber verify that output remains within tolerance between annual calibrations.

qa.q: In gamma analysis for IMRT QA, what does the 3%/3mm criteria represent?
qa.a: 3% dose difference and 3 mm distance-to-agreement thresholds
c: 3% dose difference and 3 mm distance-to-agreement thresholds
3% volume receiving at least 3 mm of beam penumbra
3 Gy absolute dose tolerance and 3 mm positional error
3% statistical uncertainty with 3 mm spatial resolution
qa.e: The 3%/3mm gamma criteria combine a dose difference threshold of 3% (typically normalized to maximum dose or prescription dose) with a distance-to-agreement (DTA) threshold of 3 mm. A point passes if it satisfies either criterion.

qa.q: What is the generally accepted minimum passing rate for IMRT QA using 3%/3mm gamma criteria?
qa.a: 90% or greater
c: 90% or greater
95% or greater
80% or greater
85% or greater
qa.e: A gamma passing rate of 90% or greater using 3%/3mm criteria (with a 10% low-dose threshold) is widely considered the minimum acceptable standard for IMRT QA, though many institutions aim for 95% or higher.

qa.q: What is the ArcCHECK device used for in radiation therapy quality assurance?
qa.a: Patient-specific IMRT/VMAT plan verification using a diode array
c: Patient-specific IMRT/VMAT plan verification using a diode array
Daily output constancy measurement using an ion chamber
Annual beam energy verification using a scanning system
Machine mechanical alignment check using optical indicators
qa.e: The ArcCHECK (Sun Nuclear) is a cylindrical diode array detector containing 1386 diodes arranged in a helical pattern. It measures the delivered dose distribution for patient-specific IMRT/VMAT QA and compares it to the planned distribution.

qa.q: What does TG-51 use as the beam quality specifier for photon beams?
qa.a: Percent depth dose at 10 cm depth (%dd(10)x)
c: Percent depth dose at 10 cm depth (%dd(10)x)
The tissue-phantom ratio at 20/10 cm depths
The beam half-value layer measured in copper
The peak-to-surface dose ratio in the buildup
qa.e: TG-51 uses %dd(10)x, the photon component of percent depth dose at 10 cm depth in water for a 10x10 cm2 field at SSD=100 cm, as the beam quality specifier for selecting the appropriate kQ factor for photon beam calibration.

qa.q: What correction does the TG-51 addendum primarily address that was not fully detailed in the original protocol?
qa.a: Measurement of the beam quality specifier %dd(10)x for high-energy beams
c: Measurement of the beam quality specifier %dd(10)x for high-energy beams
Temperature and pressure correction for ion chamber readings
Polarity correction factor measurement for electron beams
Electrometer linearity verification across all dose ranges
qa.e: The TG-51 addendum provides detailed guidance on accurately measuring %dd(10)x, particularly for high-energy beams where electron contamination affects the measurement, and recommends using a lead foil to remove the electron contamination component.

qa.q: What is the purpose of the ion recombination correction factor (Pion) in TG-51 calibration?
qa.a: To correct for incomplete collection of charge in the ion chamber
c: To correct for incomplete collection of charge in the ion chamber
To account for ion chamber stem and cable leakage current
To adjust for variations in chamber wall material composition
To compensate for energy dependence of the chamber response
qa.e: Pion corrects for the loss of ionization signal due to recombination of ions before they are collected at the electrodes. It is measured using the two-voltage technique, comparing readings at full and reduced collecting voltages.

qa.q: In TG-142, what is the recommended tolerance for monthly MLC leaf position accuracy?
qa.a: 1 mm from expected position
c: 1 mm from expected position
2 mm from expected position
0.5 mm from expected position
3 mm from expected position
qa.e: TG-142 recommends that MLC leaf position accuracy be verified monthly with a tolerance of 1 mm. This is typically assessed using a picket fence test or similar pattern that reveals systematic leaf positioning errors.

qa.q: What is the MapCHECK device primarily used for in radiation therapy?
qa.a: Planar dose verification for IMRT patient-specific QA
c: Planar dose verification for IMRT patient-specific QA
In-vivo skin dose measurement during treatment delivery
Beam scanning for commissioning water tank measurements
CT number to density calibration curve verification
qa.e: MapCHECK (Sun Nuclear) is a 2D diode array detector placed in a phantom that measures planar dose distributions for patient-specific IMRT QA. The measured dose map is compared to the treatment planning system calculation using gamma analysis.

qa.q: What is the primary purpose of performing an independent MU calculation (second check) for treatment plans?
qa.a: To verify the TPS dose calculation using an independent algorithm
c: To verify the TPS dose calculation using an independent algorithm
To replace the need for patient-specific IMRT QA measurements
To determine the optimal beam arrangement for the treatment
To generate a backup plan in case of treatment machine failure
qa.e: Independent MU calculations use a separate algorithm (hand calculation or commercial software) to verify the monitor units computed by the TPS, serving as a check against systematic errors in the primary dose calculation.

qa.q: According to TG-142, what is the tolerance for daily laser alignment verification?
qa.a: 2 mm from isocenter
c: 2 mm from isocenter
1 mm from isocenter
3 mm from isocenter
5 mm from isocenter
qa.e: TG-142 specifies that daily verification of room lasers (sagittal, lateral, and ceiling) should demonstrate agreement within 2 mm of the mechanical isocenter. For SRS/SBRT machines, a tighter tolerance of 1 mm may be applied.

qa.q: What parameter does the picket fence test evaluate for the multileaf collimator?
qa.a: Leaf positioning accuracy and leaf gap consistency
c: Leaf positioning accuracy and leaf gap consistency
Maximum leaf travel speed during dynamic treatments
Leaf transmission through the tungsten leaf material
Interlock response time when a leaf malfunctions
qa.e: The picket fence test delivers a series of narrow strip fields at known MLC positions. Analysis of the resulting pattern reveals leaf positioning errors, gap width variations, and systematic offsets in individual leaf pairs.

qa.q: What is the recommended frequency for beam flatness and symmetry checks according to TG-142?
qa.a: Monthly with annual comprehensive profiles
c: Monthly with annual comprehensive profiles
Daily before each treatment session begins
Only during initial beam commissioning process
Quarterly with no annual requirement needed
qa.e: TG-142 recommends monthly verification of beam flatness and symmetry using a profiler or array device, with annual comprehensive scanning of beam profiles in a water tank to fully characterize the beam.

qa.q: What is the Delta4 phantom used for in radiation therapy quality assurance?
qa.a: 3D dose verification for IMRT and VMAT plan QA
c: 3D dose verification for IMRT and VMAT plan QA
Mechanical isocenter verification using ball bearings
Water tank scanning for beam commissioning measurements
Patient alignment verification using embedded fiducials
qa.e: The Delta4 (ScandiDos) phantom contains two orthogonal planes of diode detectors that measure the dose distribution during delivery. Software reconstructs a 3D dose distribution for comparison with the TPS plan using gamma analysis.

qa.q: In FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) for radiation therapy, what does the Risk Priority Number (RPN) represent?
qa.a: Product of severity, occurrence probability, and detectability rankings
c: Product of severity, occurrence probability, and detectability rankings
The total number of potential failure modes identified in process
The probability that a critical error reaches the patient
The financial cost associated with each identified failure mode
qa.e: The RPN is calculated as RPN = Severity x Occurrence x Detectability, where each factor is ranked on a scale (typically 1-10). Higher RPNs indicate higher risk process steps that should be prioritized for quality improvement.

qa.q: What is the purpose of the polarity correction factor (Ppol) in ion chamber dosimetry?
qa.a: To correct for differences in chamber reading with positive vs negative voltage
c: To correct for differences in chamber reading with positive vs negative voltage
To account for the directional dependence of the chamber response
To compensate for charge leakage through the chamber stem cables
To adjust for the chamber wall material's atomic number effect
qa.e: Ppol corrects for the asymmetric charge collection that can occur when the polarity of the collecting voltage is reversed. It is calculated as the average of readings at positive and negative polarity divided by the reading at the standard polarity.

qa.q: What does the electrometer calibration factor (Pelec) account for in absolute dose measurements?
qa.a: The accuracy of the charge measurement by the electrometer device
c: The accuracy of the charge measurement by the electrometer device
The energy response variation of the ionization chamber used
The temperature stability of the electrometer internal circuits
The cable length attenuation between chamber and electrometer
qa.e: Pelec is the electrometer calibration coefficient that corrects for any inaccuracy in the electrometer's charge or current measurement. When the chamber and electrometer are calibrated together, Pelec is unity; otherwise it must be determined separately.

qa.q: In gamma analysis, what does a gamma value less than 1 at a measurement point indicate?
qa.a: The point passes both dose difference and DTA criteria
c: The point passes both dose difference and DTA criteria
The point fails the dose difference criterion but passes DTA
The measured dose exceeds the planned dose at that point
The point is located outside the defined region of interest
qa.e: A gamma value less than 1 indicates that the measurement point agrees with the calculation within the specified dose difference and distance-to-agreement criteria. Points with gamma greater than 1 fail the combined criteria.

qa.q: What type of dosimeter is most commonly used for in-vivo entrance dose measurements?
qa.a: Semiconductor diode detectors placed on the patient skin
c: Semiconductor diode detectors placed on the patient skin
Thimble ion chambers taped to the patient treatment area
Optically stimulated luminescence dosimeters inside the body
Radiochromic film sheets placed under the patient on couch
qa.e: Semiconductor diode detectors are the most common in-vivo dosimeters due to their small size, real-time readout, high sensitivity, and ease of placement on the patient's skin surface for entrance dose verification.

qa.q: What is the primary advantage of using global normalization over local normalization in gamma analysis?
qa.a: It avoids artificially high failure rates in low-dose regions
c: It avoids artificially high failure rates in low-dose regions
It provides higher sensitivity to errors in high-dose areas
It eliminates the need for a distance-to-agreement criterion
It requires fewer measurement points for a valid analysis
qa.e: Global normalization references the dose difference criterion to a single value (e.g., maximum dose or prescription dose), while local normalization references each point's planned dose. Local normalization can cause high failure rates in low-dose regions where small absolute differences represent large percentages.

qa.q: What does TG-142 recommend for the daily check of radiation/mechanical isocenter coincidence for SRS machines?
qa.a: 1 mm tolerance using a Winston-Lutz or equivalent test
c: 1 mm tolerance using a Winston-Lutz or equivalent test
3 mm tolerance using visual alignment with room lasers
2 mm tolerance using portal imaging with a phantom only
No daily check is required for radiation isocenter position
qa.e: For machines used in SRS, TG-142 recommends daily verification of radiation-mechanical isocenter coincidence with a tolerance of 1 mm, typically using a Winston-Lutz test or equivalent procedure.

qa.q: What is the recommended action level for an incident learning system when a near-miss event is identified?
qa.a: Document, investigate root cause, and implement corrective actions
c: Document, investigate root cause, and implement corrective actions
Report only to the regulatory authority within 24 hours
Suspend all treatments until a full external audit is done
No action is required since the error did not reach patient
qa.e: Near-miss events should be documented, investigated for root causes, and corrective actions should be implemented to prevent recurrence. Incident learning systems aim to create a culture of safety where near-misses are valuable learning opportunities.

qa.q: What is the two-voltage technique used to measure in ion chamber dosimetry?
qa.a: The ion recombination correction factor Pion
c: The ion recombination correction factor Pion
The polarity correction factor for the ion chamber
The beam quality conversion factor for photon beams
The chamber stem leakage current at zero radiation
qa.e: The two-voltage technique measures Pion by taking chamber readings at two different collecting voltages (typically full voltage and half voltage). The ratio of readings is used in a formula to calculate the recombination correction.

qa.q: What is the TG-142 recommended tolerance for annual photon beam energy verification (%dd(10)x)?
qa.a: 1% from baseline
c: 1% from baseline
2% from baseline
3% from baseline
0.5% from baseline
qa.e: TG-142 recommends that the annual measurement of photon beam quality (%dd(10)x or PDD at 10 cm) should be within 1% of the commissioning baseline value, ensuring the beam energy has not drifted significantly.

qa.q: In FMEA for radiation therapy, which process step typically has the highest risk priority number?
qa.a: Treatment plan data transfer to the delivery system
c: Treatment plan data transfer to the delivery system
Initial patient registration in the hospital database
Fabrication of custom shielding blocks for electron beams
Scheduling the patient for their first treatment session
qa.e: Plan data transfer is frequently identified as a high-RPN step because errors in parameter transfer (e.g., wrong plan, wrong beam data) have high severity, may occur with moderate frequency, and can be difficult to detect before treatment delivery.

qa.q: What device is commonly used for daily photon beam output constancy checks?
qa.a: A solid-state detector or ion chamber in a daily QA phantom
c: A solid-state detector or ion chamber in a daily QA phantom
A full water tank scanning system with beam profiles
A portal imaging device with no additional phantom needed
A thermoluminescent dosimeter read out weekly in batch
qa.e: Daily output constancy is typically verified using devices such as the Sun Nuclear Daily QA3 or Standard Imaging QCkV, which contain solid-state detectors or small ion chambers in a fixed phantom geometry for quick and reproducible measurements.

qa.q: What low-dose threshold is commonly applied during gamma analysis to exclude noise from evaluation?
qa.a: 10% of the maximum dose
c: 10% of the maximum dose
50% of the maximum dose
5% of the maximum dose
25% of the maximum dose
qa.e: A low-dose threshold of 10% of the maximum (or prescription) dose is commonly used to exclude low-dose regions from gamma analysis, where measurement noise and small absolute differences can produce misleadingly high gamma values.

qa.q: What is the recommended tolerance for couch position readout accuracy according to TG-142?
qa.a: 2 mm / 1 degree
c: 2 mm / 1 degree
5 mm / 2 degrees
1 mm / 0.5 degree
3 mm / 1.5 degrees
qa.e: TG-142 recommends that couch position indicators (vertical, longitudinal, lateral) be accurate within 2 mm, and rotational indicators within 1 degree, verified on a monthly basis.

qa.q: What is the purpose of performing a constancy check on the ion chamber and electrometer system between annual calibrations?
qa.a: To verify the dosimetry system response has not changed significantly
c: To verify the dosimetry system response has not changed significantly
To replace the need for annual ADCL recalibration of equipment
To measure the absolute dose in Gray at reference conditions
To determine the beam quality conversion factor for new beams
qa.e: Constancy checks using a check source (e.g., Sr-90) or cross-comparison with another chamber verify that the ion chamber and electrometer sensitivity have remained stable, ensuring reliable dose measurements between ADCL calibration cycles.

qa.q: According to TG-142, what is the daily tolerance for photon beam output constancy using a standard dosimetry check device?
qa.a: ±3% from the baseline established during monthly calibration checks
c: ±1% from the baseline established during monthly calibration checks
±2% from the baseline established during monthly calibration checks
±3% from the baseline established during monthly calibration checks
±5% from the baseline established during monthly calibration checks
qa.e: TG-142 specifies ±3% tolerance for daily photon output constancy checks, providing a practical threshold for detecting significant output changes while accounting for measurement variability.

qa.q: What is the TG-142 monthly tolerance for photon beam flatness constancy for a conventional linac?
qa.a: ±1% from the baseline value measured during annual commissioning
c: ±0.5% from the baseline value measured during annual commissioning
±1% from the baseline value measured during annual commissioning
±2% from the baseline value measured during annual commissioning
±3% from the baseline value measured during annual commissioning
qa.e: TG-142 specifies ±1% monthly tolerance for photon flatness constancy, ensuring the beam profile remains consistent with the commissioned data used in treatment planning.

qa.q: In gamma analysis for IMRT QA, what does the distance-to-agreement (DTA) criterion evaluate?
qa.a: The minimum distance from a measurement point to the nearest point with the same dose in the calculation
c: The percentage difference between measured and calculated dose at corresponding spatial positions
The minimum distance from a measurement point to the nearest point with the same dose in the calculation
The angular deviation between the measured and calculated dose gradient vector orientations
The spatial offset between planned and delivered isocenter positions for each treatment beam
qa.e: DTA finds the closest point in the calculated distribution that matches the measured dose value, which is particularly useful in high dose gradient regions where small spatial shifts cause large dose differences.

qa.q: What is the recommended tolerance for MLC leaf position accuracy according to TG-142 for IMRT treatments?
qa.a: ±1 mm from the intended leaf position for IMRT delivery mode
c: ±0.5 mm from the intended leaf position for IMRT delivery mode
±1 mm from the intended leaf position for IMRT delivery mode
±2 mm from the intended leaf position for IMRT delivery mode
±3 mm from the intended leaf position for IMRT delivery mode
qa.e: TG-142 specifies ±1 mm MLC leaf position accuracy for IMRT, as leaf positioning errors directly impact the modulated fluence pattern and delivered dose distribution.

qa.q: According to TG-142, what is the annual tolerance for radiation-light field coincidence?
qa.a: ±2 mm or 1% on each side, whichever is greater, on each field edge
c: ±1 mm or 0.5% on each side, whichever is greater, on each field edge
±2 mm or 1% on each side, whichever is greater, on each field edge
±3 mm or 2% on each side, whichever is greater, on each field edge
±5 mm or 3% on each side, whichever is greater, on each field edge
qa.e: TG-142 requires radiation-light field coincidence within ±2 mm or 1% of the field size on each side, ensuring accurate field placement based on the light field during patient setup.

qa.q: What is the purpose of the TG-142 recommended monthly mechanical check of gantry angle indicators?
qa.a: To verify that the digital and mechanical angle readouts agree within ±1 degree tolerance
c: To verify that the digital and mechanical angle readouts agree within ±1 degree tolerance
To confirm that the gantry rotation speed is constant throughout the full arc range
To test the automatic gantry collision avoidance system response at all positions
To calibrate the gantry angle encoder for sub-degree accuracy in arc therapy modes
qa.e: Monthly gantry angle verification ensures the angle indicators remain accurate within ±1 degree, which is important for correct beam direction during patient treatment delivery.

qa.q: Which detector is most commonly used for patient-specific IMRT QA measurements in clinical practice?
qa.a: Two-dimensional diode or ionization chamber array for planar dose verification
c: Single Farmer-type ionization chamber for absolute point dose measurements only
Two-dimensional diode or ionization chamber array for planar dose verification
Thermoluminescent dosimeter chips placed at multiple points within a phantom
Radiochromic film calibrated with an absolute dose curve for each measurement
qa.e: 2D arrays (diode or ion chamber based) are the most widely used IMRT QA tools, providing efficient planar dose measurements for gamma analysis comparison with calculated distributions.

qa.q: According to TG-142, what is the tolerance for coincidence of radiation and mechanical isocenter (star shot test)?
qa.a: ±2 mm diameter for non-SRS/SBRT conventional treatments
c: ±0.5 mm diameter for non-SRS/SBRT conventional treatments
±1 mm diameter for non-SRS/SBRT conventional treatments
±2 mm diameter for non-SRS/SBRT conventional treatments
±3 mm diameter for non-SRS/SBRT conventional treatments
qa.e: TG-142 specifies ±2 mm for the radiation-mechanical isocenter coincidence in conventional mode, tightening to ±1 mm for SRS/SBRT machines.

qa.q: What is the TG-218 recommended universal action limit for IMRT QA gamma passing rate using 3%/2mm criteria?
qa.a: 90% passing rate as the action limit requiring investigation and potential replanning
c: 85% passing rate as the action limit requiring investigation and potential replanning
90% passing rate as the action limit requiring investigation and potential replanning
92% passing rate as the action limit requiring investigation and potential replanning
95% passing rate as the action limit requiring investigation and potential replanning
qa.e: TG-218 defines 90% as the universal action limit for 3%/2mm gamma analysis. Results below this require investigation, while the 95% level serves as the tolerance limit.

qa.q: For linac kV imaging QA, what does the TG-142 monthly test of kV image quality assess?
qa.a: Spatial resolution, contrast, noise, and uniformity using a standard image quality phantom
c: Only the geometric accuracy of the kV imaging arm relative to the treatment isocenter
Spatial resolution, contrast, noise, and uniformity using a standard image quality phantom
Exclusively the signal-to-noise ratio using a uniform water-equivalent slab phantom
Only the kV tube output and half-value layer using an ionization chamber measurement
qa.e: Monthly kV imaging QA evaluates multiple image quality parameters including spatial resolution, contrast, noise, and uniformity to ensure consistent image quality for patient setup verification.

qa.q: What is the primary purpose of performing an independent monitor unit calculation (second check) for each treatment plan?
qa.a: To detect systematic errors in the treatment planning system dose calculation for the specific plan
c: To replace the need for patient-specific quality assurance measurement verification
To verify that the treatment machine output matches the annual calibration certificate
To detect systematic errors in the treatment planning system dose calculation for the specific plan
To confirm that the physician-prescribed dose matches the institutional treatment protocol
qa.e: Independent MU calculations provide a secondary verification of the TPS dose calculation, using a separate algorithm or method to detect potential systematic calculation errors before treatment.

qa.q: According to TG-142, what is the daily tolerance for laser localization system alignment to the mechanical isocenter?
qa.a: ±2 mm from the established mechanical isocenter position
c: ±0.5 mm from the established mechanical isocenter position
±1 mm from the established mechanical isocenter position
±2 mm from the established mechanical isocenter position
±3 mm from the established mechanical isocenter position
qa.e: TG-142 specifies ±2 mm daily tolerance for laser alignment, ensuring that patient setup using room lasers positions the treatment site within acceptable proximity to the mechanical isocenter.

qa.q: What specific test does TG-142 recommend for monthly verification of the optical distance indicator (ODI)?
qa.a: ODI accuracy check at multiple SSDs within ±2 mm of the actual distance measured mechanically
c: ODI accuracy check at a single SSD of 100 cm only within ±1 mm of mechanical measurement
ODI accuracy check at multiple SSDs within ±2 mm of the actual distance measured mechanically
ODI functionality test confirming only that the digital readout displays a numerical value
ODI calibration requiring adjustment of the projector mirror until exact agreement is achieved
qa.e: TG-142 requires monthly ODI checks at several distances (e.g., 90, 100, 110 cm) to verify accuracy within ±2 mm, ensuring reliable SSD settings across the clinical range.

equip.q: TrueBeam 10 FFF dose rate?
equip.a: 2400 MU/min
c: 800 MU/min
1400 MU/min
2400 MU/min
600 MU/min
equip.e: Highest clinical dose rate — fast SRS/SBRT delivery.

equip.q: Halcyon electrons?
equip.a: No — 6 FFF only
c: 6 MeV only
No — 6 FFF only
Optional
equip.e: Only 6 FFF photons. No electron beams.

equip.q: Halcyon beam model?
equip.a: Pre-configured (golden)
c: User-configured
Pre-configured (golden)
No model needed
Monte Carlo
equip.e: Pre-validated beam data. Limited user tuning.

equip.q: Millennium 120 central leaf width?
equip.a: 5 mm
c: 2.5 mm
5 mm
7.5 mm
10 mm
equip.e: Central 20 pairs: 5 mm. Outer 20 pairs: 10 mm.

equip.q: HD120 central leaf width?
equip.a: 2.5 mm
c: 2.5 mm
5 mm
1 mm
3 mm

Harmony Pro adds FFF beams and SBRT capability

1.5T MRI with 70 cm bore, integrated 7 MV FFF linac
3T MRI with 60 cm bore, integrated 6 MV flattened linac for diagnostic imaging
1.5T open-bore MRI with separate linac room connected by patient shuttle
equip.e: Unity: diagnostic-quality 1.5T MRI (Philips Marlin) + 7MV FFF linac mounted on ring gantry. 70 cm bore limits patient size. Cryostat split to allow beam path. Electron return effect must be accounted for in dose calculation. Online adaptive RT capable.

equip.q: Elekta MOSAIQ vs Varian ARIA: what are they?
equip.a: Oncology information systems (OIS)
c: Treatment planning systems
Oncology information systems (OIS)
Dose calculation engines
QA measurement systems
equip.e: record-and-verify, scheduling, documentation for the entire RT workflow. MOSAIQ (Elekta): interfaces with Elekta and non-Elekta equipment. ARIA (Varian): tightly integrated with Varian ecosystem. Both: patient demographics, prescription, plan parameters verified at treatment console before beam-on. Critical safety system. DICOM-RT communication.

equip.q: Elekta Flexitron afterloader: what type of brachytherapy does it deliver?
equip.a: HDR and PDR brachytherapy using Ir-192 or Co-60 sources with up to 40 channels
c: Only HDR with Ir-192 — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
HDR and PDR brachytherapy using Ir-192 or Co-60 sources with up to 40 channels
Only LDR — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Only electron beam — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
equip.e: Flexitron: 40-channel afterloader. HDR Ir-192 (most common), HDR Co-60 (longer life, fewer source exchanges), or PDR mode. Source step size: 1 mm. Oncentra TPS integration. Replaces older MicroSelectron. Elekta dominates brachytherapy market.

equip.q: Varian Ethos: what distinguishes it from TrueBeam?
equip.a: Integrated AI-driven online adaptive RT workflow — CBCT → auto-contour → reoptimise → treat in ~15 minutes
c: Only faster gantry rotation speed compared to TrueBeam — same planning workflow
Integrated AI-driven online adaptive RT workflow — CBCT → auto-contour → reoptimise → treat in ~15 minutes
Only different MLC leaf design — Ethos uses micro-MLC with 1.25 mm leaf width
Dedicated electron-only delivery system optimised for total skin electron therapy
equip.e: Ethos: closed-bore gantry (like Halcyon) + CBCT + AI autocontouring + adaptive planning engine. Entire adapt-replan-treat workflow in one session. AI proposes adapted plan, therapist/physician reviews and approves. Designed for bladder, cervix, H&N where anatomy changes daily.

equip.q: Varian HD120 MLC: leaf configuration?
equip.a: Central 8 cm: 2.5 mm leaves (32 pairs); outer: 5 mm leaves (28 pairs) — total 120 leaves
c: All 120 leaves with uniform 5 mm width at isocentre — maximum field 40×40 cm²
Central 8 cm: 2.5 mm leaves (32 pairs); outer: 5 mm leaves (28 pairs) — total 120 leaves
All 120 leaves with uniform 2.5 mm width at isocentre — maximum field 22×22 cm²
Central 4 cm: 1.25 mm leaves (16 pairs); outer: 5 mm leaves (44 pairs) — total 120 leaves
equip.e: HD120: designed for SRS/SBRT. 2.5 mm central leaves provide high-resolution shaping for small intracranial targets. Max field: 40×22 cm². Standard on TrueBeam STx. Millennium 120: all 5 mm central/10 mm outer, max 40×40 cm².

equip.q: CALC: Varian TrueBeam 6FFF dose rate is 1400 MU/min. SRS plan delivers 4200 MU in a single arc. Minimum beam-on time?
equip.a: 3 minutes
c: 1 minute
3 minutes
6 minutes
10 minutes
equip.e: 4200 MU / 1400 MU/min = 3.0 minutes beam-on time. Actual delivery slightly longer due to gantry speed limits and dose rate modulation. FFF enables fast SRS delivery, reducing intrafraction motion impact.

equip.q: Flattening filter purpose in a conventional linac?
equip.a: Produces uniform (flat) dose profile across the field by preferentially attenuating the central peaked beam
c: Increases beam energy — the standard approach used in most clinical departments worldwide
Produces uniform (flat) dose profile across the field by preferentially attenuating the central peaked beam
Collimates the beam — the standard approach used in most clinical departments worldwide
Accelerates electrons — the standard approach used in most clinical departments worldwide
equip.e: Bremsstrahlung from target: forward-peaked. Conical metal filter (thicker centrally): attenuates centre more than edges → flat profile. Also hardens beam (increases mean energy) and generates scatter. FFF beams remove this → peaked profile, softer spectrum, higher dose rate.

equip.q: CALC: Klystron peak power = 5 MW, duty cycle = 0.1%. Average power?
equip.a: 5 kW
c: 5 kW
50 kW
5 MW
equip.e: Average power = peak × duty cycle = 5 × 10⁶ × 0.001 = 5000 W = 5 kW. Pulsed operation: high peak power in short bursts (~5 μs pulses at ~200 Hz). Low average power manageable for cooling.

equip.q: Ion chamber monitor system in linac head: how many chambers and why?
equip.a: Two independent sealed chambers
c: One chamber only
Two independent sealed chambers
Three chambers — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
No monitor chambers
equip.e: one monitors dose (MU), the other monitors dose rate, symmetry, and flatness. Dual redundancy: if primary chamber fails, secondary terminates beam. Segmented into quadrants → real-time symmetry monitoring (detects steering errors). Sealed (no T/P correction needed). Calibrated to deliver 1 cGy/MU at reference conditions.

equip.q: Cyclotron vs synchrotron for proton therapy: key operational difference?
equip.a: Cyclotron: fixed extraction energy, energy selection via degrader. Synchrotron: variable extraction energy, no degrader needed
c: Both use identical operating principles — choice is purely based on manufacturer preference
Cyclotron: fixed extraction energy, energy selection via degrader. Synchrotron: variable extraction energy, no degrader needed
Cyclotron: variable extraction energy with no degrader needed. Synchrotron: fixed energy with degrader
Both extract protons at variable energies — cyclotron is compact and synchrotron is larger
equip.e: Cyclotron (IBA, Varian): compact, continuous beam, ~230 MeV fixed → energy degrader + energy selection system for clinical energies. Synchrotron (Hitachi, ProNova): ramps B-field → extracts at desired energy directly. Both support pencil beam scanning.

equip.q: Pencil beam scanning (PBS) in proton therapy: how is the dose delivered?
equip.a: Thin proton beam steered by magnets to paint dose spot-by-spot across each energy layer (depth)
c: Uniform broad beam with aperture, based on established clinical physics practice
Thin proton beam steered by magnets to paint dose spot-by-spot across each energy layer (depth)
Only passive scattering — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Fixed beam with patient movement, based on established clinical physics practice
equip.e: PBS: scanning magnets deflect ~3-10 mm pencil beam across field. Energy changed layer-by-layer (distal to proximal). Each spot deposited at specific position + depth. No patient-specific hardware (apertures, compensators) needed. Enables IMPT (intensity-modulated proton therapy).

equip.q: Range shifter in proton therapy: when is it needed?
equip.a: For superficial targets where the minimum machine energy still overshoots
c: Always required — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
For superficial targets where the minimum machine energy still overshoots
Only for deep targets — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
To increase range — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
equip.e: degrades beam to reach shallow depths. Minimum proton energy ~70 MeV → range ~4 cm water. Targets shallower than this need a range shifter (plastic slab) in nozzle to degrade energy. Introduces scatter → larger spot size → reduced lateral penumbra. Air gap between shifter and patient should be minimised.

equip.q: CALC: Proton beam range in water ≈ E^1.77 / 470 (cm), where E is in MeV. Range of 200 MeV protons?
c: ~15 cm
~35 cm
~50 cm
equip.e: R = 200^1.77 / 470. 200^1.77: ln(200)=5.298, ×1.77=9.378, e^9.378 = 11,831. R = 11831/470 ≈ 25.2 cm. Sufficient for most deep-seated tumours. Clinical range: 4-30 cm water equivalent for typical 70-230 MeV systems.

equip.q: Co-60 teletherapy unit: photon energies emitted?
equip.a: Two gamma rays: 1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV (average ~1.25 MeV)
c: Single gamma ray at 6 MeV produced by electron acceleration and bremsstrahlung conversion
Two gamma rays: 1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV (average ~1.25 MeV)
Continuous X-ray spectrum from 0 to 1.25 MeV produced by bremsstrahlung in target
Single gamma ray at 0.662 MeV from isomeric transition to ground state
equip.e: Co-60 β-decays to Ni-60 excited state → emits two γ-rays. T½ = 5.27 years → source replacement every ~5 years. Penumbra larger than linac (source diameter ~2 cm). Still widely used in LMICs including SA. No electrons, no FFF, no IMRT capability.

equip.q: CALC: Co-60 source activity was 10,000 Ci at installation. Activity after 5.27 years (one half-life)?
equip.e: One half-life: A = A₀/2 = 10000/2 = 5000 Ci. Treatment time doubles after one half-life to deliver same dose. At ~3-4 half-lives (~16-21 years), treatment times become impractically long → source replacement typically every 5-7 years.

equip.q: Penumbra difference: Co-60 vs linac 6MV?
equip.a: Co-60 penumbra ~10-12 mm vs linac ~5-6 mm due to large Co-60 source diameter (~2 cm)
c: Same penumbra — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
Co-60 penumbra ~10-12 mm vs linac ~5-6 mm due to large Co-60 source diameter (~2 cm)
Linac penumbra is larger — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Penumbra depends only on field size, based on established clinical physics practice
equip.e: Geometric penumbra ∝ source size × (SSD - SDD)/SDD. Co-60 source: ~2 cm diameter → large geometric penumbra. Linac focal spot: ~2-3 mm → sharp penumbra. Co-60 disadvantage for small fields. Advantage: no complex accelerator to maintain.

equip.q: X-ray tube: rotating anode advantage over stationary anode?
equip.a: Heat distributed over larger target area
c: Better image quality only
Heat distributed over larger target area
Smaller focal spot only
No advantage — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
equip.e: higher tube loading (mA × time) for clinical imaging. Rotating anode: tungsten disc spins ~3000-10000 RPM. Electron beam hits different part of track each moment → heat spread over ring rather than point. Enables high mA techniques (CT, angiography). Stationary anode: only dental, portable.

equip.q: Flat panel detector (FPD) in digital radiography: two types?
equip.a: Indirect (scintillator + a-Si photodiode) and direct (a-Se photoconductor) conversion
c: Only indirect — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Only direct — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Indirect (scintillator + a-Si photodiode) and direct (a-Se photoconductor) conversion
CCD-based only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
equip.e: Indirect FPD: X-ray → CsI scintillator → visible light → a-Si photodiode → charge. Direct FPD: X-ray → a-Se → charge directly. Direct: sharper (no light scatter) but lower quantum efficiency. Indirect (CsI): widely used in general radiography and CBCT.

equip.q: CT detector type in modern multi-slice scanners?
equip.a: Solid-state scintillator (GOS or garnet-based) coupled to photodiodes
c: Xenon gas detectors — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Film cassettes — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Solid-state scintillator (GOS or garnet-based) coupled to photodiodes
Image intensifier — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
equip.e: Modern CT: Gd₂O₂S (GOS) or garnet scintillators → photodiode arrays. Replaced xenon gas (lower efficiency). 64-320 detector rows. Photon-counting CT (Siemens NAEOTOM Alpha): CdTe/CdZnTe direct conversion detectors — next generation.

equip.q: HDR vs LDR brachytherapy: dose rate definitions per ICRU?
equip.a: HDR: >12 Gy/hr. LDR: 0.4–2 Gy/hr. PDR: pulsed (HDR-like pulses mimicking LDR radiobiology)
c: HDR: >2 Gy/hr, LDR: <0.4 Gy/hr — with no intermediate dose rate category defined
HDR: >12 Gy/hr. LDR: 0.4–2 Gy/hr. PDR: pulsed (HDR-like pulses mimicking LDR radiobiology)
No standardised definitions exist — each institution sets their own dose rate thresholds
HDR: >100 Gy/hr exclusively, LDR: <1 Gy/hr — with MDR covering all rates between
equip.e: ICRU 38: LDR 0.4-2 Gy/hr (e.g., Cs-137 tubes, I-125 seeds). MDR 2-12 Gy/hr. HDR >12 Gy/hr (Ir-192 afterloader). PDR: ~1 Gy pulse every 1-4 hours → radiobiological effect similar to LDR. HDR most common for cervix, prostate, breast.

equip.q: Ir-192 source for HDR: physical characteristics?
equip.a: T½ = 73.8 days, average γ energy ~0.37 MeV, source diameter ~0.9 mm, active length ~3.5 mm
c: T½ = 5.27 years, average γ energy ~1.25 MeV, source diameter ~2 cm, active length ~2 cm
T½ = 73.8 days, average γ energy ~0.37 MeV, source diameter ~0.9 mm, active length ~3.5 mm
T½ = 30 years, average γ energy ~0.662 MeV, source diameter ~3 mm, active length ~5 mm
T½ = 8 days, average γ energy ~0.364 MeV, source diameter ~1.5 mm, active length ~4 mm
equip.e: Ir-192: complex decay scheme, ~20 γ-ray energies (weighted average ~370 keV). High specific activity → small source size (fits through catheters/applicators). Short T½ → source exchange every ~3-4 months. HVL ~3 mm Pb.

equip.q: I-125 permanent seed implant: clinical use and physical characteristics?
equip.a: Low-energy (27-35 keV), T½=59.4 days
c: High energy, used for cervix
Low-energy (27-35 keV), T½=59.4 days
Medium energy, used for lung
High dose rate, temporary implant
equip.e: used for permanent prostate LDR brachytherapy. I-125: low energy characteristic X-rays + γ. Permanent implant: seeds stay in prostate forever. Dose delivered over weeks as activity decays. Typical: 80-120 seeds, ~0.3-0.5 mCi each. Total prescribed dose ~145 Gy. Minimal shielding needed due to low energy.

equip.q: ExacTrac (BrainLab): components and clinical use?
equip.a: Floor-mounted kV X-ray pairs + infrared camera tracking
c: Only optical cameras — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Floor-mounted kV X-ray pairs + infrared camera tracking
MRI-based system — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Ultrasound guidance — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
equip.e: used for frameless SRS patient positioning. ExacTrac: (1) Two floor-mounted kV tubes + ceiling detectors → stereoscopic X-ray images compared to DRRs → calculate 6DOF correction. (2) IR camera tracks surface markers for initial setup and intrafraction monitoring. Sub-millimetre accuracy. Used with Varian and Elekta linacs.

equip.q: AlignRT / SGRT (Surface Guided RT): how does it work?
equip.a: 3D optical surface cameras capture patient surface in real-time
c: kV X-ray imaging — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
3D optical surface cameras capture patient surface in real-time
MRI during treatment — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Ultrasound probe on patient
equip.e: compare to reference surface from planning CT. SGRT (AlignRT, Catalyst, IDENTIFY): structured light projected → 3D surface captured at ~5 Hz. Compared to reference (CT body surface). Real-time 6DOF offsets displayed. Zero radiation dose for positioning. Enables: tattoo-free setup, DIBH monitoring, intrafraction SRS monitoring.

equip.q: ViewRay MRIdian: how does it differ from Elekta Unity?
equip.a: 0.35T MRI (vs Unity's 1.5T)
c: Same magnetic field
0.35T MRI (vs Unity's 1.5T)
No MRI capability
Higher magnetic field
equip.e: lower field causes less electron return effect and smaller susceptibility artefacts near metal. MRIdian: 0.35T split-bore MRI + 6MV FFF linac. Lower B-field: smaller ERE dose perturbation, less susceptibility artefact (better for patients with implants). Tradeoff: lower image quality than Unity's 1.5T. Both enable real-time gating and online adaptive RT.

equip.q: FLASH therapy delivery systems: current platforms capable of ultra-high dose rates?
equip.a: Modified electron linacs, proton cyclotrons (transmission mode), and specialised FLASH-specific accelerators
c: Standard clinical linacs — the standard approach used in most clinical departments worldwide
Modified electron linacs, proton cyclotrons (transmission mode), and specialised FLASH-specific accelerators
Only Co-60 units — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Only synchrotrons — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
equip.e: FLASH (>40 Gy/s): electron FLASH relatively easy (modified clinical linac or dedicated e.g., IntraOp Mobetron). Proton FLASH: high beam current from cyclotron, transmission mode (shoot-through). Photon FLASH: extremely difficult (target heat). PHASER concept: many X-ray sources simultaneously. All experimental/early clinical.

equip.q: Gamma Knife Icon: what is the frame vs frameless option?
equip.a: Icon adds CBCT + infrared tracking
c: Icon requires frame only
Icon adds CBCT + infrared tracking
Icon eliminates all immobilisation
Icon uses MRI guidance
equip.e: enables frameless mask-based SRS with intrafraction motion monitoring. Classic GK: invasive frame (Leksell frame). Icon: frame OR thermoplastic mask. CBCT verifies position. IR tracking via nose marker monitors intrafraction motion → beam holds if motion exceeds threshold. Enables fractionated GK (2-5 fx) and reduces patient discomfort.

equip.q: CyberKnife M6/S7: Synchrony system does what?
equip.a: Correlates external chest markers with internal fiducial/tumour position to track respiratory motion in real-time
c: Only tracks external markers — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Correlates external chest markers with internal fiducial/tumour position to track respiratory motion in real-time
Only uses breath-hold — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
No motion management — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
equip.e: Synchrony: LED markers on chest track breathing (external). Periodic kV X-ray pairs image internal fiducials. Correlation model built → robotic arm adjusts beam in real-time to follow tumour. No gating needed → continuous treatment. Used for lung, liver, pancreas SBRT.

equip.q: Proton therapy gantry: why is it so large?
equip.a: 230 MeV protons have magnetic rigidity ~100× greater than MeV electrons
c: Design choice only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
230 MeV protons have magnetic rigidity ~100× greater than MeV electrons
Contains the accelerator — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Houses patient imaging — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
equip.e: requires massive bending magnets (gantry diameter ~10 m, weight ~100+ tonnes). Magnetic rigidity Bρ = p/q. 230 MeV proton: Bρ ≈ 2.4 Tm vs ~0.02 Tm for 6 MeV electrons. Gantry bending magnets must be ~2-3T over large aperture → extremely heavy. Superconducting gantry designs (Varian) reduce size/weight. Fixed beam rooms avoid gantry but limit angles.

equip.q: CALC: Linac produces 6MV X-rays. Electrons are accelerated to ~6 MeV. What fraction of electron energy is converted to X-rays in the tungsten target?
equip.a: ~1-2% (rest is heat)
c: ~1-2% (rest is heat)
~25% for accurate dose delivery
~50% in modern radiation oncology
~90% within the clinical setting
equip.e: Bremsstrahlung efficiency ≈ 9 × 10⁻¹⁰ × Z × E (MeV). For tungsten (Z=74) at 6 MeV: ≈ 9e-10 × 74 × 6e6 ≈ 0.04 ≈ 4%. But this is total radiation yield; usable forward-directed fraction is ~1-2%. 98% of energy becomes heat → massive target cooling required.

equip.q: What does the term 'linac' stand for in radiation therapy?
equip.a: Linear Accelerator
c: Linear Accelerator
Light Amplification Controller
Longitudinal Analysis Computer
Laser Intensity Calibrator
equip.e: Linac is short for Linear Accelerator. It is the most common type of machine used in external beam radiation therapy. It accelerates electrons in a straight line using microwave energy and produces high-energy X-rays or electron beams for treatment.

equip.q: What does MLC stand for in the context of a linear accelerator?
equip.a: Multi-Leaf Collimator
c: Maximum Limit Controller
Multi-Leaf Collimator
Mechanical Load Compensator
Modulated Light Chamber
equip.e: MLC stands for Multi-Leaf Collimator. It consists of many individually movable tungsten leaves that can shape the radiation beam to conform to the outline of the tumour, sparing surrounding healthy tissue.

equip.q: What is the primary function of a linear accelerator in cancer treatment?
equip.a: To produce high-energy radiation beams to destroy tumour cells
c: To perform surgical removal of tumours
To produce high-energy radiation beams to destroy tumour cells
To take diagnostic X-ray images only
To administer chemotherapy drugs
equip.e: The primary function of a linear accelerator is to generate high-energy X-ray or electron beams that are directed at the tumour to damage the DNA of cancer cells, ultimately destroying them.

equip.q: What is the gantry of a linear accelerator?
equip.a: The rotating arm that directs the radiation beam around the patient
c: The treatment couch where the patient lies
The computer that controls the treatment plan
The rotating arm that directs the radiation beam around the patient
The room where the machine is housed
equip.e: The gantry is the large rotating structure of the linac that houses the treatment head. It can rotate 360 degrees around the patient, allowing radiation to be delivered from multiple angles.

equip.q: What is the treatment couch (or table) used for on a linear accelerator?
equip.a: To support and position the patient during treatment
c: To store radiation sources throughout the treatment course
To measure the radiation dose per standard operating procedures
To support and position the patient during treatment
To generate the radiation beam for accurate dose delivery
equip.e: The treatment couch supports the patient and allows precise positioning so that the radiation beam is directed accurately at the target. It can move in multiple directions to align the patient with the treatment plan.

equip.q: CALC: A TrueBeam linac klystron delivers 5.5 MW peak RF power with a pulse repetition frequency of 360 Hz and a pulse width of 4 μs. What is the RF duty cycle and average power?
equip.a: Duty cycle = 0.144%, average power = 7.9 kW
c: Duty cycle = 0.144%, average power = 7.9 kW
Duty cycle = 1.44%, average power = 79 kW
Duty cycle = 14.4%, average power = 792 kW
Duty cycle = 0.036%, average power = 2.0 kW
