equip.e: Duty cycle = PRF × pulse width = 360 Hz × 4 × 10⁻⁶ s = 1.44 × 10⁻³ = 0.144%. Average power = peak power × duty cycle = 5.5 MW × 0.00144 = 7.92 kW. Medical linac RF systems have very low duty cycles (<1%), producing high peak power but low average power. This is why the accelerating waveguide does not require the extreme cooling that continuous-wave accelerators need.

equip.q: CALC: A Varian Millennium 120 MLC has a maximum leaf speed of 2.5 cm/s. During a VMAT arc, the gantry rotates at 4.8°/s. What is the maximum MLC travel distance achievable over a 15° gantry arc segment?
equip.a: 7.8 cm
c: 3.1 cm
5.2 cm
7.8 cm
equip.e: Time for 15° arc = 15° / 4.8°/s = 3.125 s. Maximum leaf travel = 2.5 cm/s × 3.125 s = 7.8125 cm ≈ 7.8 cm. This calculation is critical in VMAT optimization — if the optimizer demands leaf travel exceeding this limit, the delivery system must either slow the gantry (reducing dose rate) or compromise the aperture shape, potentially degrading plan quality.

equip.q: A 6 MV linac uses a standing-wave accelerating structure with a bend magnet. After maintenance, the beam energy is suspected to have shifted. Which measurement would most sensitively detect a small energy change?
equip.a: PDD₂₀/PDD₁₀ ratio
c: Output at dmax
PDD₂₀/PDD₁₀ ratio
Cross-plane profile flatness
Surface dose measurement
equip.e: The PDD₂₀/PDD₁₀ ratio (also called D₂₀/D₁₀) is the most sensitive indicator of photon beam energy. This ratio is also the TRS-398 beam quality specifier for high-energy photons. A change of 0.01 in D₂₀/D₁₀ corresponds to roughly 0.3-0.5 MeV in mean energy. Output at dmax is relatively insensitive to small energy changes. Profile flatness detects steering issues but not energy. Surface dose changes with energy but is difficult to measure precisely.

equip.q: CALC: The Halcyon dual-layer (SX2) MLC has two banks with individual leaf transmission of 6% each, offset by half a leaf width. What is the effective combined transmission through both layers?
equip.e: Combined transmission = T₁ × T₂ = 0.06 × 0.06 = 0.0036 = 0.36%. The dual-layer design multiplies the attenuation rather than adding it, dramatically reducing MLC leakage. The half-leaf-width offset ensures interleaf gaps in one layer are covered by leaf bodies in the other, eliminating direct interleaf leakage paths. This is why Halcyon achieves approximately 0.4% effective transmission compared to approximately 1.4% for the single-layer Millennium MLC.

equip.q: CALC: An Elekta Agility MLC has a maximum leaf speed of 3.5 cm/s. During a step-and-shoot IMRT delivery, the slowest leaf in a transition must travel 2.0 cm (this leaf limits the transition). What is the minimum segment transition time?
equip.e: Since all leaves move simultaneously, the transition time is governed by the leaf with the maximum travel distance. Time = distance / speed = 2.0 cm / 3.5 cm/s = 0.571 s ≈ 0.57 s. In practice, acceleration and deceleration phases add overhead, making actual transition times somewhat longer. For highly modulated plans with many segments, cumulative transition times become a significant fraction of total treatment time.

equip.q: What is the primary advantage of a flattening filter-free (FFF) beam in linac design?
equip.a: Higher dose rate delivery with reduced head scatter, beneficial for SBRT
c: Improved beam flatness across the entire field
Higher dose rate delivery with reduced head scatter, beneficial for SBRT
Elimination of the need for MLC beam shaping
Reduced photon energy spectrum compared to flattened beams
equip.e: Removing the flattening filter increases dose rate by 2-4× (e.g., TrueBeam 6FFF: 1400 MU/min vs 6X: 600 MU/min), reduces head scatter and out-of-field dose, and shortens treatment time. The peaked profile is acceptable for IMRT/VMAT where modulation creates uniformity.

equip.q: In an Elekta linac, what is the function of the interlock system when the beam stopper is not in position?
equip.a: Prevents beam-on to protect against unshielded primary beam transmission through the couch
c: Alerts staff but allows beam delivery at reduced dose rate
Automatically moves the beam stopper into position and continues treatment
Prevents beam-on to protect against unshielded primary beam transmission through the couch
Switches to electron mode where the beam stopper is not required
equip.e: Elekta linacs use a retractable beam stopper (counterweight) behind the patient to attenuate the primary beam. If not deployed, the interlock prevents irradiation to avoid excessive dose to the floor/areas below the vault.

equip.q: What distinguishes the Varian TrueBeam from the Clinac series in terms of beam generation control?
equip.a: TrueBeam uses a digitally controlled beam pulse system with interlocked gantry-dose-rate servo
c: TrueBeam uses a betatron instead of a standing wave linac
TrueBeam uses a digitally controlled beam pulse system with interlocked gantry-dose-rate servo
TrueBeam has no flatness filter option and only delivers FFF beams
Clinac series has higher maximum dose rate than TrueBeam
equip.e: TrueBeam uses a digital control system that synchronises beam generation at the pulse level with gantry speed and MLC positions, enabling precise dose-rate modulation for VMAT. Clinac uses analogue servo systems. TrueBeam also supports Developer Mode for research.

equip.q: Varian Halcyon uses a dual-layer MLC. What is the purpose of the proximal (upper) layer?
equip.a: Acts as a dynamic jaw replacement, reducing transmitted and scattered radiation between leaves
c: Provides finer spatial resolution by staggering leaf positions with the distal layer
Acts as a dynamic jaw replacement, reducing transmitted and scattered radiation between leaves
Serves as a backup in case the distal layer malfunctions during treatment
Filters low-energy photons to harden the beam spectrum
equip.e: Halcyon has no conventional jaws. The proximal MLC layer acts as a jaw substitute, reducing interleaf leakage and tongue-and-groove effect from the distal layer. The two layers offset leaf-gap transmission, achieving <0.5% leakage.

equip.q: MR-linac systems must address the electron return effect (ERE). In what clinical scenario is ERE most dosimetrically significant?
equip.a: At air-tissue interfaces such as lung tumours or bowel gas boundaries
c: At the beam entry point on the skin surface in all treatment sites
At air-tissue interfaces such as lung tumours or bowel gas boundaries
Within homogeneous soft tissue targets like prostate or liver
Only in the bore of the MRI when no patient is present
equip.e: The Lorentz force from the magnetic field curves secondary electrons. At air-tissue interfaces, electrons leaving tissue into air are returned to the tissue surface, creating hot spots. This is most significant at lung-tissue and bowel gas interfaces. Monaco MC algorithm models ERE for Unity/MRIdian planning.

equip.q: An Elekta Agility MLC has 160 leaves with 5 mm leaf width at isocentre. What is the maximum field size and leaf speed?
equip.a: 40 × 40 cm field with maximum leaf speed of 3.5 cm/s
c: 40 × 40 cm field with maximum leaf speed of 3.5 cm/s
26 × 40 cm field with maximum leaf speed of 2.5 cm/s
40 × 40 cm field with maximum leaf speed of 1.5 cm/s
20 × 40 cm field with maximum leaf speed of 5.0 cm/s
equip.e: Agility: 160 leaves (80 pairs), 5 mm width at isocentre, covering 40 × 40 cm. Leaf speed up to 3.5 cm/s enables fast VMAT delivery. Interdigitation is allowed. Compare to Varian HD-MLC: 2.5 mm central leaves but slower.

equip.q: What is the typical frequency of a magnetron used in a medical linear accelerator?
equip.a: Approximately 3 GHz (S-band microwave frequency)
c: Approximately 3 GHz (S-band microwave frequency)
Approximately 10 GHz (X-band microwave frequency)
Approximately 300 MHz (UHF radio frequency range)
Approximately 30 GHz (Ka-band microwave frequency)
equip.e: Most medical linacs operate at S-band (~3 GHz, 2856 MHz specifically). X-band (~10 GHz) is used in some compact linacs like CyberKnife. The magnetron generates microwaves at this frequency to accelerate electrons in the waveguide.

equip.q: In a Varian Millennium 120 MLC, how many leaf pairs are in the central region and what is the projected leaf width at isocentre?
equip.a: 40 central leaf pairs projecting 5 mm width at isocentre
c: 40 central leaf pairs projecting 5 mm width at isocentre
60 central leaf pairs projecting 5 mm width at isocentre
40 central leaf pairs projecting 2.5 mm width at isocentre
20 central leaf pairs projecting 10 mm width at isocentre
equip.e: The Millennium 120 MLC has 60 leaf pairs total: 40 central pairs with 5 mm projected width and 20 outer pairs (10 each side) with 10 mm projected width, giving a maximum field of 40 cm x 40 cm.

equip.q: What is the key advantage of a klystron over a magnetron as a microwave power source in a linac?
equip.a: Klystrons are amplifiers providing more stable and higher power output
c: Klystrons are amplifiers providing more stable and higher power output
Klystrons are oscillators that require no external microwave input signal
Klystrons are significantly smaller and cheaper than magnetrons overall
Klystrons produce microwaves across a very wide frequency bandwidth
equip.e: A klystron is a microwave amplifier (requires an input signal), producing stable, high-power output suitable for high-energy linacs (>10 MV). Magnetrons are oscillators (self-generating) that are simpler and cheaper but less stable, typically used for lower-energy machines.

equip.q: CALC: A Co-60 source has an initial activity of 12,000 Ci. What is the approximate activity after 10.54 years? (Co-60 half-life = 5.27 years)
equip.a: 3,000 Ci after two half-lives of radioactive decay
c: 3,000 Ci after two half-lives of radioactive decay
6,000 Ci after one half-life of radioactive decay
1,500 Ci after three half-lives of radioactive decay
4,000 Ci representing one-third of original activity
equip.e: 10.54 years = 2 half-lives of Co-60 (2 x 5.27 years). After 2 half-lives: A = 12,000 x (1/2)^2 = 12,000 x 0.25 = 3,000 Ci. This is why Co-60 sources in SA facilities must be replaced roughly every 5 years to maintain adequate dose rates.

equip.q: What material is typically used for the X-ray target in a medical linac producing megavoltage photon beams?
equip.a: Tungsten, due to its high atomic number and high melting point
c: Tungsten, due to its high atomic number and high melting point
Copper, due to its excellent thermal conductivity properties
Lead, due to its very high atomic number and high density
Molybdenum, due to its moderate Z and heat resistance
equip.e: Tungsten (Z=74) is the standard target material for MV X-ray production. Its high Z maximises bremsstrahlung yield, and its very high melting point (3422 degrees C) allows it to withstand the intense electron bombardment. The target is typically a thin tungsten disc backed by copper for heat dissipation.

equip.q: In a flattening-filter-free (FFF) beam, what is the characteristic dose profile shape compared to a flattened beam?
equip.a: Forward-peaked conical profile, highest intensity on central axis
c: Forward-peaked conical profile, highest intensity on central axis
Perfectly uniform flat profile across the entire field width
Concave profile with lowest dose intensity on the central axis
Bimodal profile with two symmetric off-axis intensity peaks
equip.e: Without the flattening filter, the bremsstrahlung X-ray beam retains its natural forward-peaked distribution, highest at the central axis and decreasing toward the edges. FFF beams allow much higher dose rates (up to 2400 MU/min for 10 FFF on TrueBeam) and have softer beam quality off-axis.

equip.q: CALC: A linear accelerator waveguide is 1.5 metres long and operates at 3 GHz. If the phase velocity equals the speed of light, how many wavelengths fit in the waveguide?
equip.a: 15 wavelengths fit within the 1.5-metre long waveguide structure
c: 15 wavelengths fit within the 1.5-metre long waveguide structure
10 wavelengths fit within the 1.5-metre long waveguide structure
30 wavelengths fit within the 1.5-metre long waveguide structure
45 wavelengths fit within the 1.5-metre long waveguide structure
equip.e: Wavelength = c/f = (3 x 10^8 m/s) / (3 x 10^9 Hz) = 0.1 m = 10 cm. Number of wavelengths = 1.5 m / 0.1 m = 15. In practice, the waveguide has irises that slow the phase velocity below c so electrons can ride the microwave wave.

equip.q: What detector technology is used in a Varian aS1200 electronic portal imaging device (EPID)?
equip.a: Amorphous silicon flat-panel detector with copper build-up plate
c: Amorphous silicon flat-panel detector with copper build-up plate
Amorphous selenium direct conversion flat-panel detector unit
Cadmium zinc telluride photon-counting semiconductor detector
Liquid-filled ionisation chamber matrix array detector system
equip.e: The Varian aS1200 EPID uses an indirect-detection amorphous silicon (a-Si) flat panel: MV X-rays interact in a copper plate and gadolinium oxysulphide scintillator, producing light detected by the a-Si photodiode array. Resolution is 1280x1280 pixels with 0.336 mm pitch.

equip.q: What is the Elekta Agility MLC maximum leaf speed, and why is this clinically significant?
equip.a: Up to 6.5 cm/s, enabling efficient VMAT delivery with rapid modulation
c: Up to 6.5 cm/s, enabling efficient VMAT delivery with rapid modulation
Up to 2.5 cm/s, enabling standard conformal treatment field shaping
Up to 12.0 cm/s, enabling ultra-fast FLASH-rate beam delivery only
Up to 0.8 cm/s, enabling only slow step-and-shoot IMRT treatments
equip.e: The Elekta Agility MLC has a maximum leaf speed of 6.5 cm/s, significantly faster than older MLCs. This high speed reduces beam-on time during VMAT delivery and allows greater modulation complexity, improving plan quality for techniques like volumetric modulated arc therapy.

equip.q: In the Gamma Knife Perfexion, how many Co-60 sources are arranged and how are different collimator sizes achieved?
equip.a: 192 sources in 8 sectors; collimation by sliding sector movement over fixed rings
c: 192 sources in 8 sectors; collimation by sliding sector movement over fixed rings
201 sources in a single ring; collimation by exchanging external helmet inserts
120 sources in 4 sectors; collimation by rotating tungsten collimator wheels
256 sources in 16 sectors; collimation by individual source shutter mechanisms
equip.e: The Perfexion has 192 Co-60 sources arranged in 8 movable sectors of 24 sources each. Each sector can be positioned over one of three collimator sizes (4, 8, or 16 mm) or blocked, eliminating the need for manual helmet changes used in older Gamma Knife models.

equip.q: What type of accelerator does the CyberKnife robotic radiosurgery system use?
equip.a: Compact X-band (10 GHz) linear accelerator mounted on a robotic arm
c: Compact X-band (10 GHz) linear accelerator mounted on a robotic arm
Standard S-band (3 GHz) linear accelerator mounted on a C-arm gantry
Multiple cobalt-60 sources arranged in a fixed hemispherical geometry
Compact cyclotron producing proton beams mounted on a robotic gantry
equip.e: CyberKnife uses a compact 6 MV X-band (approximately 10 GHz) linac mounted on a KUKA robotic arm with 6 degrees of freedom. The higher microwave frequency allows a shorter waveguide and lighter linac, suitable for robotic mounting. It treats with multiple non-coplanar non-isocentric beams.

equip.q: CALC: An HDR Ir-192 source has an air kerma strength of 40,000 U. Using the dose rate constant for Ir-192 of 1.108 cGy/(h*U) at 1 cm, what is the dose rate at 1 cm?
equip.a: Approximately 44,320 cGy/h at 1 cm distance from the source centre
c: Approximately 44,320 cGy/h at 1 cm distance from the source centre
Approximately 4,432 cGy/h at 1 cm distance from the source centre
Approximately 443,200 cGy/h at 1 cm distance from the source centre
Approximately 88,640 cGy/h at 1 cm distance from the source centre
equip.e: Dose rate = dose rate constant x S_K / r^2 = 1.108 x 40,000 / 1^2 = 44,320 cGy/h. This extremely high dose rate is why HDR treatments take only minutes. The dose rate constant accounts for the conversion from air kerma to dose in water.

equip.q: What imaging system does the Varian TrueBeam use for kilovoltage cone-beam CT (kV CBCT)?
equip.a: On-board imager (OBI) with kV source and flat-panel detector on retractable arms
c: On-board imager (OBI) with kV source and flat-panel detector on retractable arms
Ring-mounted diagnostic CT scanner integrated within the treatment gantry bore
Megavoltage portal imaging detector used in tomographic reconstruction mode
Ceiling-mounted stereoscopic kV X-ray tubes with floor-embedded detectors
equip.e: TrueBeam's OBI system consists of a kV X-ray tube and amorphous silicon flat-panel detector mounted on retractable arms perpendicular to the MV treatment beam. Rotating the gantry acquires projections for CBCT reconstruction, providing volumetric soft-tissue imaging for patient positioning.

equip.q: How does a TomoTherapy Hi-Art system deliver radiation differently from a conventional C-arm linac?
equip.a: Helical delivery: fan beam rotates continuously while couch translates through bore
c: Helical delivery: fan beam rotates continuously while couch translates through bore
Static delivery: multiple fixed beams from a ring of linac sources fire sequentially
Arc delivery: wide cone beam rotates in single arcs with stationary patient couch
Robotic delivery: linac on robotic arm moves freely around the stationary patient
equip.e: TomoTherapy delivers IMRT helically: a 6 MV fan beam (modulated by binary MLCs) continuously rotates while the couch translates through the ring gantry, similar to a helical CT scan. This allows treatment of long targets and built-in MVCT imaging for daily image guidance.

equip.q: What is the typical source-to-axis distance (SAD) for a Co-60 teletherapy unit such as the Theratron 780?
equip.a: 80 cm SAD, which is shorter than the 100 cm SAD of most linacs
c: 80 cm SAD, which is shorter than the 100 cm SAD of most linacs
100 cm SAD, which is identical to the standard linac treatment distance
60 cm SAD, which provides a very compact treatment unit configuration
120 cm SAD, which provides improved depth dose for cobalt treatments
equip.e: Most Co-60 units (Theratron 780, Equinox) operate at 80 cm SAD, compared to the 100 cm standard for linacs. This shorter SAD contributes to larger geometric penumbra. Many SA treatment centres still use Co-60 units, and planning must account for this different SAD.

equip.q: CALC: A Co-60 unit has a source diameter of 2 cm and source-to-collimator distance of 45 cm. What is the geometric penumbra at 80 cm from the source?
equip.a: Approximately 1.56 cm penumbra due to the large extended cobalt source
c: Approximately 1.56 cm penumbra due to the large extended cobalt source
Approximately 0.56 cm penumbra due to the large extended cobalt source
Approximately 3.56 cm penumbra due to the large extended cobalt source
Approximately 0.16 cm penumbra due to the large extended cobalt source
equip.e: Geometric penumbra P = source_diameter x (SDD - SCD) / SCD = 2 x (80 - 45) / 45 = 2 x 35/45 = 70/45 = 1.56 cm. The large source size of Co-60 (1.5-2 cm) is why Co-60 beams have significantly larger penumbra than linac beams, where the focal spot is ~2 mm.

equip.q: What are the two gamma-ray energies emitted by a Co-60 source used in teletherapy?
equip.a: 1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV, giving an average energy of 1.25 MeV
c: 1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV, giving an average energy of 1.25 MeV
0.66 MeV and 1.17 MeV, giving an average energy of 0.92 MeV
1.33 MeV and 1.77 MeV, giving an average energy of 1.55 MeV
0.51 MeV and 1.25 MeV, giving an average energy of 0.88 MeV
equip.e: Co-60 decays by beta emission to Ni-60, which de-excites by emitting two gamma rays: 1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV in cascade. The average energy of 1.25 MeV determines the beam penetration characteristics, roughly equivalent to a 4 MV linac beam.

equip.q: In HDR brachytherapy, what is the typical activity range of an Ir-192 source at the time of installation?
equip.a: Approximately 5 to 10 Ci (185 to 370 GBq) when newly installed
c: Approximately 5 to 10 Ci (185 to 370 GBq) when newly installed
Approximately 50 to 100 Ci (1850 to 3700 GBq) at installation
Approximately 0.5 to 1.0 Ci (18.5 to 37 GBq) at installation
Approximately 500 to 1000 Ci (18.5 to 37 TBq) at installation
equip.e: HDR Ir-192 sources typically have an activity of 5-10 Ci (185-370 GBq) at installation. Due to the 73.8-day half-life, sources are replaced every 3-4 months. In SA, SAHPRA regulates the import and disposal of these sealed sources, and facilities must maintain proper source records.

equip.q: What is the primary advantage of the Elekta Unity MR-linac over conventional linac-based IGRT?
equip.a: Real-time MRI soft-tissue imaging during beam delivery for adaptive therapy
c: Real-time MRI soft-tissue imaging during beam delivery for adaptive therapy
Higher photon beam energy output allowing deeper tumour dose penetration
Faster gantry rotation speed enabling sub-second volumetric arc delivery
Integrated PET imaging capability for functional tumour targeting daily
equip.e: The Elekta Unity combines a 1.5T MRI scanner with a 7 MV linac, enabling real-time soft-tissue visualisation during treatment. This allows online adaptive replanning and intrafraction motion monitoring without ionising radiation imaging dose, particularly beneficial for abdominal and pelvic targets.

equip.q: CALC: A standing-wave linac has 30 accelerating cavities, each providing 200 keV energy gain. What is the final electron kinetic energy?
equip.a: 6 MeV total kinetic energy from 30 cavities at 200 keV each
c: 6 MeV total kinetic energy from 30 cavities at 200 keV each
3 MeV total kinetic energy from 30 cavities at 100 keV each
12 MeV total kinetic energy from 30 cavities at 400 keV each
60 MeV total kinetic energy from 30 cavities at 2 MeV each
equip.e: Total energy = number of cavities x energy gain per cavity = 30 x 200 keV = 6000 keV = 6 MeV. In a standing-wave guide, side-coupled cavities allow a more compact design. Note that in practice, the electron gun provides initial injection energy of ~20-50 keV as well.

equip.q: What is the purpose of the electron gun in a medical linear accelerator?
equip.a: Injects electrons into the waveguide by thermionic emission from heated cathode
c: Injects electrons into the waveguide by thermionic emission from heated cathode
Generates the microwave power needed to accelerate electrons in the waveguide
Steers the accelerated electron beam onto the tungsten target for X-ray production
Monitors the output dose rate and flatness of the clinical treatment beam
equip.e: The electron gun contains a heated cathode (typically barium-impregnated tungsten) that emits electrons by thermionic emission. These electrons are injected into the accelerating waveguide at approximately 20-50 keV. Pulsed grids control injection timing synchronised with the microwave pulses.

equip.q: In a Varian Halcyon, what replaces the conventional jaws-plus-MLC collimation system?
equip.a: Dual-layer stacked and staggered MLC system without independent jaws
c: Dual-layer stacked and staggered MLC system without independent jaws
Single-layer high-definition MLC with 2.5 mm leaves at isocentre
Conventional upper and lower jaws with single-layer MLC underneath
Circular collimator system with variable aperture iris mechanism
equip.e: The Halcyon uses a unique dual-layer (proximal and distal) staggered MLC design that eliminates the need for backup jaws. The two leaf banks are offset to reduce interleaf transmission. This simplified design enables the compact bore-style gantry architecture.

equip.q: CALC: An Ir-192 HDR source has a half-life of 73.8 days. After how many days will the source activity fall to 25% of its initial value?
equip.a: 147.6 days, which corresponds to exactly two half-lives of Ir-192
c: 147.6 days, which corresponds to exactly two half-lives of Ir-192
73.8 days, which corresponds to exactly one half-life of Ir-192
221.4 days, which corresponds to three full half-lives of Ir-192
110.7 days, which corresponds to exactly 1.5 half-lives of Ir-192
equip.e: 25% = (1/2)^2 = after 2 half-lives. Time = 2 x 73.8 = 147.6 days. In SA HDR brachytherapy departments, sources are typically replaced every 3-4 months (~90-120 days) before activity drops too low for practical treatment times.

equip.q: What is the function of the primary collimator in a medical linac treatment head?
equip.a: Defines the maximum circular field size and absorbs off-axis primary radiation
c: Defines the maximum circular field size and absorbs off-axis primary radiation
Provides fine beam modulation by moving individual tungsten leaf elements
Flattens the beam intensity profile to achieve uniform dose across the field
Monitors the dose output by measuring ionisation in dual sealed chambers
equip.e: The primary collimator is a thick tungsten block with a conical opening located closest to the target. It defines the maximum available beam size (typically slightly larger than the 40x40 cm field) and absorbs primary radiation outside this cone, serving as the first stage of beam collimation.

equip.q: How does the Varian RapidArc (VMAT) technique differ from conventional step-and-shoot IMRT delivery?
equip.a: Continuous gantry rotation with simultaneous MLC motion and variable dose rate
c: Continuous gantry rotation with simultaneous MLC motion and variable dose rate
Multiple static gantry angles with MLC segments delivered sequentially at each
Helical fan beam delivery through ring gantry with binary collimator leaves
Robotic non-coplanar beams delivered from hundreds of fixed positions in space
equip.e: VMAT (RapidArc) delivers dose during continuous gantry rotation, simultaneously varying MLC leaf positions, gantry speed, and dose rate. This provides highly conformal dose distributions with significantly shorter treatment times compared to step-and-shoot IMRT, which uses discrete static beam angles.

equip.q: What type of MLC does the TomoTherapy system use, and how does it differ from a conventional MLC?
equip.a: Binary pneumatic MLC with 64 leaves that are either fully open or fully closed
c: Binary pneumatic MLC with 64 leaves that are either fully open or fully closed
Conventional sliding-leaf MLC with 120 pairs of continuously moving leaves
Rotating tungsten collimator wheel with variable circular aperture openings
Micro-MLC with 160 leaf pairs providing 1.25 mm resolution at isocentre
equip.e: TomoTherapy uses 64 interdigitated binary leaves driven by compressed air that can rapidly switch between open and closed positions. Unlike conventional MLCs that slide to arbitrary positions, each leaf is either fully open or fully closed, and modulation is achieved by varying the open time of each leaf.

equip.q: CALC: A linac produces 6 MV X-rays with a dose rate of 600 MU/min at isocentre. How long does it take to deliver 200 MU?
equip.a: 20 seconds to deliver the required 200 monitor units at this rate
c: 20 seconds to deliver the required 200 monitor units at this rate
33 seconds to deliver the required 200 monitor units at this rate
60 seconds to deliver the required 200 monitor units at this rate
12 seconds to deliver the required 200 monitor units at this rate
equip.e: Time = MU / dose rate = 200 MU / 600 MU/min = 0.333 min = 20 seconds. In clinical practice, actual beam-on times may be slightly longer due to dose rate ramp-up/down and machine communication overhead.

equip.q: What distinguishes LDR (low dose rate) brachytherapy from HDR (high dose rate) brachytherapy according to ICRU definitions?
equip.a: LDR delivers 0.4-2 Gy/h; HDR delivers greater than 12 Gy/h at prescription
c: LDR delivers 0.4-2 Gy/h; HDR delivers greater than 12 Gy/h at prescription
LDR delivers 2-12 Gy/h; HDR delivers greater than 50 Gy/h at prescription
LDR delivers less than 0.1 Gy/h; HDR delivers 0.4-2 Gy/h at prescription
LDR delivers 12-50 Gy/h; HDR delivers greater than 100 Gy/h at prescription
equip.e: Per ICRU, LDR is 0.4-2 Gy/h, MDR (medium dose rate) is 2-12 Gy/h, and HDR is >12 Gy/h. In SA, HDR brachytherapy with Ir-192 is widely used for cervical cancer treatment, particularly important given the high cervical cancer burden in the country.

equip.q: What is the purpose of the carousel or filter wheel assembly in a modern dual-energy linac?
equip.a: Holds and positions different flattening filters and scattering foils per energy
c: Holds and positions different flattening filters and scattering foils per energy
Rotates the bending magnet assembly to select different beam energy pathways
Switches between different target materials optimised for each beam energy
Adjusts the microwave power coupling to the waveguide for each beam energy
equip.e: The carousel (or sliding filter assembly) in a multi-energy linac holds different components that can be moved into the beam path: flattening filters of different thickness for each photon energy, electron scattering foils for different electron energies, and sometimes the FFF position (empty slot).

equip.q: In proton therapy, what is the Bragg peak and why is it clinically advantageous?
equip.a: Sharp dose maximum at end of proton range, sparing tissue beyond the target
c: Sharp dose maximum at end of proton range, sparing tissue beyond the target
Gradual dose build-up region near the surface, similar to photon beam entry
Uniform dose plateau throughout the entire beam path from entry to exit
Exponential dose decrease with depth, identical to megavoltage photon beams
equip.e: Protons deposit most of their energy at the end of their range, creating the Bragg peak. Beyond this point, dose drops to near zero. This characteristic allows excellent sparing of normal tissue distal to the target. A spread-out Bragg peak (SOBP) covers the full tumour volume.

equip.q: CALC: A proton beam must treat a target at 15 cm depth in water. Using Range (cm) = E^1.77 / 470 (E in MeV), what initial proton energy is needed?
equip.a: Approximately 155 MeV initial proton energy for 15 cm water range
c: Approximately 155 MeV initial proton energy for 15 cm water range
Approximately 100 MeV initial proton energy for 15 cm water range
Approximately 230 MeV initial proton energy for 15 cm water range
Approximately 200 MeV initial proton energy for 15 cm water range
equip.e: Range = E^1.77 / 470. So 15 = E^1.77 / 470, giving E^1.77 = 7050. Taking ln: ln(E) = ln(7050)/1.77 = 8.861/1.77 = 5.006, so E = e^5.006 = 149-160 MeV, approximately 155 MeV. Proton therapy facilities typically accelerate protons up to 230 MeV to reach deep-seated tumours.

equip.q: What electron scattering foil configuration is used in medical linacs for electron beam therapy?
equip.a: Dual scattering foil system: thin high-Z primary foil then shaped secondary foil
c: Dual scattering foil system: thin high-Z primary foil then shaped secondary foil
Single thick tungsten foil that both scatters and flattens the electron beam
Triple scattering foil stack with alternating high-Z and low-Z material layers
No scattering foils are needed as MLC leaves shape the electron field directly
equip.e: Most linacs use a dual-foil system for electron beams: the first (primary) thin high-Z foil (e.g., lead or copper) broadly scatters the narrow pencil beam, and the second (secondary) thicker foil with a Gaussian-shaped profile flattens the dose distribution across the field.

equip.q: What is the Equinox Co-60 unit, and what makes it relevant to radiation therapy in developing countries?
equip.a: Modern digital Co-60 unit designed to provide affordable IMRT-capable treatment
c: Modern digital Co-60 unit designed to provide affordable IMRT-capable treatment
High-energy linac replacement designed to eliminate need for Co-60 worldwide
Portable diagnostic cobalt imaging unit for rural screening programmes in SA
Research cobalt irradiator designed for radiobiology experiments exclusively
equip.e: The Equinox (by Best Theratronics/Hyundai) is a modern Co-60 unit with MLC, digital imaging, and IMRT capability. It provides advanced treatment techniques at lower cost than linacs, making it valuable for SA and other developing nations where maintenance infrastructure and reliable power may be limited.

equip.q: What is the function of the monitor ionisation chamber system in a medical linac treatment head?
equip.a: Measures beam output in real-time using sealed dual-chamber system for dose control
c: Measures beam output in real-time using sealed dual-chamber system for dose control
Generates the treatment beam by ionising air molecules within the collimator head
Shapes the radiation field by selectively attenuating portions of the beam profile
Provides megavoltage imaging capability by detecting transmitted radiation below
equip.e: Two sealed, flat ionisation chambers in the linac head continuously monitor the beam output (dose rate), flatness, and symmetry. The primary chamber controls beam termination when the set MU is reached. The secondary (backup) chamber independently monitors and terminates if the primary fails.

equip.q: CALC: A Varian TrueBeam delivers 10 FFF at 2400 MU/min. An SRS plan requires 8000 MU. What is the approximate beam-on time?
equip.a: 3 minutes and 20 seconds of total beam-on time for this delivery
c: 3 minutes and 20 seconds of total beam-on time for this delivery
5 minutes and 30 seconds of total beam-on time for this delivery
1 minute and 40 seconds of total beam-on time for this delivery
6 minutes and 40 seconds of total beam-on time for this delivery
equip.e: Time = MU / dose rate = 8000 / 2400 = 3.33 minutes = 3 minutes and 20 seconds. The high dose rate of 10 FFF (2400 MU/min) enables rapid delivery of high-dose SRS fractions, reducing intrafraction motion effects and improving patient throughput.

equip.q: In electron beam therapy, what is the purpose of the electron applicator (cone or cerrobend cutout)?
equip.a: Defines field size close to patient surface and reduces scatter contamination
c: Defines field size close to patient surface and reduces scatter contamination
Accelerates the electrons to the required clinical treatment beam energy
Converts the electron beam into a photon beam for deeper tissue treatment
Monitors the electron beam dose rate output during clinical beam delivery
equip.e: Electron applicators (cones) are attached to the linac head and extend close to the patient surface. They define the treatment field and reduce dose outside the field from scattered electrons. Custom cerrobend or lead cutout inserts placed in the applicator further shape the field to the target.

equip.q: What is the electron contamination issue in photon beams, and how do beam modifiers contribute?
equip.a: High-Z components in beam path generate secondary electrons increasing skin dose
c: High-Z components in beam path generate secondary electrons increasing skin dose
Electrons in the waveguide contaminate the photon spectrum reducing beam energy
Scattered photons contaminate the electron beam reducing therapeutic depth range
Neutron contamination from the beam modifiers activates treatment room walls
equip.e: Photon beam interactions in the flattening filter, jaws, MLC, and other head components produce secondary electrons that increase surface/skin dose. This is more pronounced for higher energies and with accessories like wedges or trays. FFF beams have less electron contamination due to the removed filter.

equip.q: CALC: A physical wedge attenuates the beam from 100% at the thin end to 30% at the thick end across a 20 cm field. What is the approximate wedge angle?
equip.a: Approximately 60 degrees based on the isodose line tilt at reference depth
c: Approximately 60 degrees based on the isodose line tilt at reference depth
Approximately 30 degrees based on the isodose line tilt at reference depth
Approximately 45 degrees based on the isodose line tilt at reference depth
Approximately 15 degrees based on the isodose line tilt at reference depth
equip.e: The wedge angle is defined as the angle of the isodose tilt from perpendicular at a reference depth (usually 10 cm). With ~70% dose gradient across the field (100% to 30%), the isodose lines tilt approximately 60 degrees from the beam axis. Standard physical wedge angles are 15, 30, 45, and 60 degrees.

equip.q: What is the Synchrony system on the CyberKnife, and what clinical problem does it address?
equip.a: Real-time respiratory motion tracking correlating external markers with internal fiducials
c: Real-time respiratory motion tracking correlating external markers with internal fiducials
Automatic patient setup verification system using surface-guided body contour matching
Collision avoidance system preventing robotic arm contact with patient or treatment couch
Treatment plan optimisation algorithm that adapts the dose calculation during delivery
equip.e: Synchrony tracks respiratory motion by correlating external LED markers on the chest with internal fiducial markers or tumour position from orthogonal kV X-rays. The robotic arm adjusts beam pointing in real-time to follow the tumour, enabling accurate treatment of lung and liver lesions.

equip.q: What are the main components of the Gamma Knife Icon that enable frameless SRS treatment?
equip.a: Integrated CBCT, infrared motion tracking, and thermoplastic mask immobilisation
c: Integrated CBCT, infrared motion tracking, and thermoplastic mask immobilisation
Built-in MRI scanner, robotic patient positioner, and invasive stereotactic frame
Megavoltage portal imaging, optical surface scanner, and bite-block head fixation
Ultrasound guidance system, electromagnetic tracking, and vacuum cushion system
equip.e: The Gamma Knife Icon added CBCT for image-guided positioning, a high-definition motion management (HDMM) infrared tracking system that monitors patient motion during treatment, and compatibility with thermoplastic masks. This enables frameless, fractionated treatments in addition to traditional frame-based single-fraction SRS.

equip.q: What advantage does pencil beam scanning (PBS) offer over passive scattering in proton therapy?
equip.a: Enables intensity-modulated proton therapy (IMPT) with superior dose conformality
c: Enables intensity-modulated proton therapy (IMPT) with superior dose conformality
Produces a much broader Bragg peak that eliminates need for range modulation
Requires significantly less room shielding because it generates fewer neutrons
Delivers dose at much higher rates enabling ultra-fast treatment plan completion
equip.e: Pencil beam scanning steers a narrow proton beam magnetically to paint the dose layer by layer (spot scanning). This enables IMPT, where spots of varying intensity create highly conformal dose distributions without patient-specific hardware (apertures, compensators) needed in passive scattering.

equip.q: CALC: An electron beam has a nominal energy of 12 MeV. Using the approximation R50 (cm) = E/2.33, what is the depth of the 50% dose (R50)?
equip.a: Approximately 5.2 cm depth for the 50% isodose of a 12 MeV beam
c: Approximately 5.2 cm depth for the 50% isodose of a 12 MeV beam
Approximately 3.0 cm depth for the 50% isodose of a 12 MeV beam
Approximately 8.0 cm depth for the 50% isodose of a 12 MeV beam
Approximately 6.5 cm depth for the 50% isodose of a 12 MeV beam
equip.e: R50 = E(MeV) / 2.33 = 12 / 2.33 = 5.15 cm, approximately 5.2 cm. Other useful rules: therapeutic range R80 = E/3 = 4 cm, practical range Rp = E/2 = 6 cm. These approximations are essential for quick clinical electron beam energy selection in treatment planning.

equip.q: What is the role of SAHPRA in regulating radiation-emitting medical equipment in South Africa?
equip.a: Registers and regulates all medical devices including linacs and Co-60 units in SA
c: Registers and regulates all medical devices including linacs and Co-60 units in SA
Only regulates pharmaceutical drugs and has no jurisdiction over medical devices
Exclusively handles radioactive source import and export but not device approval
Only provides voluntary quality guidelines without any legal enforcement power
equip.e: The South African Health Products Regulatory Authority (SAHPRA) regulates medical devices including radiation therapy equipment under the Medicines and Related Substances Act. All linacs, Co-60 units, brachytherapy afterloaders, and imaging devices must be registered with SAHPRA before clinical use in SA.

equip.q: What is the typical MV energy used in a TomoTherapy Hi-Art system and why was this energy chosen?
equip.a: 6 MV nominal energy, balancing treatment penetration and MVCT imaging quality
c: 6 MV nominal energy, balancing treatment penetration and MVCT imaging quality
10 MV nominal energy, chosen to maximise penetration for deep-seated tumours
15 MV nominal energy, chosen to treat all tumour sites including very deep ones
18 MV nominal energy, providing the highest possible dose rate for treatments
equip.e: TomoTherapy uses a 6 MV beam because it provides adequate penetration for most clinical sites while also being suitable for MVCT imaging (at reduced energy ~3.5 MV). The single-energy design simplifies the compact ring-gantry architecture.

equip.q: CALC: A linac has dual monitor chambers. Chamber 1 reads 100.0 MU and Chamber 2 reads 100.3 MU for the same exposure. What is the percentage difference?
equip.a: 0.3% difference between the two independent monitor chamber readings
c: 0.3% difference between the two independent monitor chamber readings
3.0% difference between the two independent monitor chamber readings
0.03% difference between the two independent monitor chamber readings
1.0% difference between the two independent monitor chamber readings
equip.e: Percentage difference = (100.3 - 100.0) / 100.0 x 100% = 0.3%. Typical tolerance for agreement between primary and secondary monitor chambers is 2% (IEC) or tighter per institutional protocol. This dual-chamber redundancy is a critical safety feature in all medical linacs.

equip.q: What is the ViewRay MRIdian system, and how does its magnetic field strength compare to the Elekta Unity?
equip.a: MR-guided linac system using 0.35T MRI, lower than Unity's 1.5T magnet field
c: MR-guided linac system using 0.35T MRI, lower than Unity's 1.5T magnet field
MR-guided linac system using 1.5T MRI, identical to Unity's magnetic field
MR-guided linac system using 3.0T MRI, higher than Unity's 1.5T magnet field
CT-guided linac system using no MRI at all, relying only on CBCT for guidance
equip.e: The ViewRay MRIdian uses a 0.35T MRI (superconducting magnet) integrated with a 6 MV FFF linac. The lower field strength reduces the electron return effect (ERE) at tissue-air interfaces compared to Unity's 1.5T but provides lower soft-tissue contrast. Both enable real-time adaptive radiotherapy.

equip.q: CALC: A photon beam passes through a Perspex tray with a transmission factor of 0.96. What percentage of dose is attenuated by the tray?
equip.a: 4% of the beam dose is attenuated by the Perspex beam modifier tray
c: 4% of the beam dose is attenuated by the Perspex beam modifier tray
96% of the beam dose is attenuated by the Perspex beam modifier tray
0.4% of the beam dose is attenuated by the Perspex modifier tray
40% of the beam dose is attenuated by the Perspex beam modifier tray
equip.e: Attenuation = 1 - transmission factor = 1 - 0.96 = 0.04 = 4%. All beam modifiers (trays, wedges, blocks) attenuate the beam, and their transmission factors must be measured during commissioning and applied in dose calculations. Typical Perspex tray factors range from 0.95 to 0.97.

equip.q: CALC: A cyclotron for proton therapy operates at magnetic field 1.5 T. Given proton mass 1.67e-27 kg and charge 1.6e-19 C, what is the cyclotron frequency?
equip.a: Approximately 22.9 MHz, which is the proton cyclotron resonance frequency
c: Approximately 22.9 MHz, which is the proton cyclotron resonance frequency
Approximately 2.29 MHz, which is the proton cyclotron resonance frequency
Approximately 229 MHz, which is the proton cyclotron resonance frequency
Approximately 0.229 MHz, the proton cyclotron resonance frequency value
equip.e: Cyclotron frequency f = qB/(2*pi*m) = (1.6e-19 x 1.5)/(2*pi x 1.67e-27) = 2.4e-19 / 1.049e-26 = 2.29e7 Hz = 22.9 MHz. This non-relativistic frequency is constant and independent of radius. At therapeutic energies (~230 MeV), relativistic effects require frequency modulation (synchrocyclotron).

equip.q: The flattening filter in a linear accelerator serves to:
equip.a: Create a uniform dose profile across the treatment field
c: Increase the beam energy by filtering low-energy photons
Create a uniform dose profile across the treatment field
Reduce neutron contamination in the beam output
Collimate the beam to the desired field size
equip.e: The conical flattening filter attenuates the forward-peaked bremsstrahlung beam more centrally, producing a flat dose profile. FFF beams omit this for higher dose rates.

equip.q: What is the function of the bending magnet in a linear accelerator?
equip.a: To deflect the electron beam toward the target or scattering foil
c: To accelerate electrons to treatment energy
To deflect the electron beam toward the target or scattering foil
To focus the photon beam onto the patient
To filter out unwanted photon energies
equip.e: The bending magnet (typically 90° or 270°) redirects the horizontally accelerated electron beam downward toward the target (photon mode) or scattering foil (electron mode).

equip.q: MLC leaf interdigitation refers to:
equip.a: Adjacent leaves extending past each other, possible in some MLC designs
c: Leaves moving in opposite directions simultaneously
Adjacent leaves extending past each other, possible in some MLC designs
The interlocking mechanism that prevents leaf collision
Alternating leaf widths in the MLC bank design
equip.e: Interdigitation allows complex field shapes where adjacent leaves overlap. Not all MLCs support this (e.g., Varian Millennium does not; some newer designs do).

equip.q: The waveguide in a linear accelerator:
equip.a: Is the structure where electrons are accelerated by microwave fields
c: Transmits the photon beam to the patient
Is the structure where electrons are accelerated by microwave fields
Cools the target after bremsstrahlung production
Shapes the radiation field to match the tumour
equip.e: The accelerating waveguide contains a series of cavities where microwave power from the magnetron/klystron accelerates electrons to MeV energies.

equip.q: What is the purpose of the ion chamber in the linac head?
equip.a: To monitor beam output in real-time for accurate dose delivery
c: To generate the therapeutic radiation beam
To monitor beam output in real-time for accurate dose delivery
To flatten the beam profile across the field
To filter out electron contamination from photon beams
equip.e: The monitor chamber (dual-channel transmission ionisation chamber) measures dose rate and flatness/symmetry in real-time. It controls beam-off when preset MUs are reached.

equip.q: CBCT (cone-beam CT) on a linac is primarily used for:
equip.a: Volumetric image guidance for patient setup verification before treatment
c: Treatment planning dose calculation with high accuracy
Volumetric image guidance for patient setup verification before treatment
Diagnostic quality imaging to replace CT simulation
Measuring delivered dose during the treatment fraction
equip.e: CBCT acquires 3D images at the treatment position using the linac kV (or MV) imaging system. Used for soft tissue matching and setup corrections prior to treatment delivery.

equip.q: The klystron differs from the magnetron in a linac in that:
equip.a: The klystron amplifies microwaves while the magnetron generates them
c: The klystron generates electrons while the magnetron accelerates them
The klystron amplifies microwaves while the magnetron generates them
The klystron is used only in low-energy machines below 6 MV
The klystron is smaller and cheaper than the magnetron
equip.e: Magnetrons generate microwaves directly (self-oscillating). Klystrons amplify a low-power microwave signal from a separate oscillator. Klystrons are used in higher-energy linacs (≥10 MV).

equip.q: The carousel or turret in Elekta linacs contains:
equip.a: Multiple scattering foils and applicator inserts for different electron energies
c: Different flattening filters for various photon energies
Multiple scattering foils and applicator inserts for different electron energies
Backup ion chambers for redundant dose monitoring
Various target materials for different treatment modalities
equip.e: Elekta machines use a carousel with scattering foils matched to each electron energy. The correct foil is rotated into position when selecting an electron beam.

equip.q: Portal imaging using the electronic portal imaging device (EPID) provides:
equip.a: MV radiographic images for treatment field verification
c: Diagnostic quality images for tumour detection
MV radiographic images for treatment field verification
Real-time 3D volumetric imaging of the patient
Dose measurement replacing ion chambers completely
equip.e: EPIDs use flat-panel amorphous silicon detectors to acquire MV images. Used for setup verification by comparing with DRRs, and increasingly for transit/exit dosimetry.

equip.q: The electron gun in a linac produces electrons by:
equip.a: Thermionic emission from a heated cathode filament
c: Photoelectric effect from laser illumination
Thermionic emission from a heated cathode filament
Beta decay of a radioactive source
Pair production in a high-Z target material
equip.e: The electron gun heats a tungsten or barium-oxide cathode to ~1000°C, causing thermionic emission of electrons. These electrons are then injected into the accelerating waveguide.

equip.q: FFF (flattening filter free) beams offer the advantage of:
equip.a: Higher dose rates (up to 4× conventional) with reduced head scatter
c: Better beam flatness across large field sizes
Higher dose rates (up to 4× conventional) with reduced head scatter
Lower surface dose compared to flattened beams
Elimination of the need for MLC collimation
equip.e: FFF beams remove the flattening filter, giving a peaked profile but 2-4× higher dose rate. Reduced head scatter improves out-of-field dose. Ideal for SBRT and SRS treatments.

equip.q: The primary collimator in a linac treatment head:
equip.a: Defines the maximum circular field size from the bremsstrahlung target
c: Creates the final rectangular treatment field shape
Defines the maximum circular field size from the bremsstrahlung target
Monitors beam output and symmetry in real-time
Filters electron contamination from the photon beam
equip.e: The primary collimator is a fixed tungsten block with a conical opening that limits the maximum beam angle emerging from the target, reducing leakage radiation.

equip.q: Gantry sag in a linear accelerator refers to:
equip.a: Mechanical deflection of the gantry under gravity at different rotation angles
c: The decrease in beam output with increasing gantry angle
Mechanical deflection of the gantry under gravity at different rotation angles
Electrical drift in the control system over time
The change in beam quality with gantry rotation
equip.e: The heavy gantry head deflects slightly under gravity, varying with angle. This affects isocentre accuracy. TG-142 tolerance for gantry rotation isocentre is ≤ 1 mm diameter.

equip.q: The backup monitor chamber in a linac serves to:
equip.a: Terminate the beam if the primary monitor chamber fails
c: Provide more accurate dose monitoring than the primary
Terminate the beam if the primary monitor chamber fails
Measure beam energy during treatment delivery
Monitor room radiation levels during treatment
equip.e: Dual redundant chambers ensure safety: if the primary channel fails to terminate the beam at the set MU, the backup (secondary) channel independently stops irradiation.

equip.q: The ExacTrac system provides:
equip.a: X-ray based real-time patient positioning using stereoscopic kV imaging
c: MRI-guided treatment on a conventional linac
X-ray based real-time patient positioning using stereoscopic kV imaging
Ultrasound-guided soft tissue tracking during treatment
Optical surface monitoring using infrared cameras only
equip.e: ExacTrac (Brainlab) uses floor-mounted kV X-ray tubes and ceiling detectors for stereoscopic imaging. Provides sub-millimetre positioning for SRS/SBRT with 6DOF corrections.

equip.q: What is the typical isocentre accuracy specification for a modern linac?
equip.a: ≤ 1 mm sphere diameter for all mechanical axes
c: ≤ 0.1 mm sphere diameter for all axes
≤ 1 mm sphere diameter for all mechanical axes
≤ 5 mm sphere diameter for all axes
≤ 10 mm sphere diameter for all axes
equip.e: TG-142 specifies isocentre accuracy (walk-out) of ≤ 1 mm diameter for non-SRS linacs. SRS-capable machines require ≤ 0.75 mm. This includes gantry, collimator, and couch rotation.

equip.q: The optical distance indicator (ODI) on a linac is used to:
equip.a: Verify source-to-surface distance using a projected light scale
c: Measure the beam energy at the patient surface
Verify source-to-surface distance using a projected light scale
Align the laser crosshairs with room lasers
Display the current monitor unit setting during treatment
equip.e: The ODI projects a light scale from the gantry head onto the patient surface, showing the SSD. TG-142 tolerance for ODI accuracy is ± 2 mm.

equip.q: Interlock systems on a linac include:
equip.a: Door, beam energy, collimator position, and couch interlocks that prevent unsafe operation
c: Only the door interlock to prevent room entry during beam
Door, beam energy, collimator position, and couch interlocks that prevent unsafe operation
Software-only protections in the treatment management system
Audio alarms that warn but do not stop the beam
equip.e: Multiple hardware interlocks ensure safety: door (beam off if open), collimator (prevents excessive field size), gantry/couch position, dose rate, and beam energy verification.

equip.q: The virtual wedge (Siemens) creates a wedge profile by:
equip.a: Varying the dose rate while one jaw sweeps across the field
c: Inserting a physical metal wedge into the beam path
Varying the dose rate while one jaw sweeps across the field
Using the MLC leaves to create a step pattern
Rotating the collimator during beam delivery
equip.e: Virtual wedge (Siemens) combines jaw motion with dose rate modulation to create wedge-shaped profiles. Similar in concept to Varian enhanced dynamic wedge (EDW) which moves a jaw at constant dose rate.

equip.q: CALC: A linac isocentre test using a star shot pattern shows a maximum spoke deviation of 0.8 mm from the centre. Is this acceptable per TG-142 for non-SRS use?
equip.a: Yes, it is within the ≤ 1 mm tolerance for non-SRS linacs
c: No, the tolerance is ≤ 0.5 mm for all clinical linacs
Yes, it is within the ≤ 1 mm tolerance for non-SRS linacs
No, the tolerance is ≤ 0.3 mm for non-SRS linacs
Yes, any result up to 5 mm is clinically acceptable
equip.e: TG-142 non-SRS tolerance for isocentre (radiation and mechanical) is ≤ 1 mm diameter. 0.8 mm is within this limit. For SRS, the tolerance tightens to ≤ 0.75 mm.

equip.q: What is the fundamental difference between a magnetron and a klystron in a medical linear accelerator?
equip.a: A magnetron generates microwave power directly while a klystron amplifies an externally supplied microwave signal
c: A magnetron operates at higher frequencies than a klystron for clinical beams
A magnetron generates microwave power directly while a klystron amplifies an externally supplied microwave signal
A klystron is used only for electron beams while a magnetron serves photon beams
A klystron generates microwave power directly while a magnetron only amplifies it
equip.e: The magnetron is an oscillator that generates microwave power (typically 2-5 MW peak) and is used in lower-energy linacs (e.g., 6 MV only machines). The klystron is an amplifier requiring a low-power RF input signal and can produce higher peak power (5-7 MW), making it suitable for dual-energy and high-energy linacs.

equip.q: In a modern C-arm linac, what is the purpose of the bending magnet system?
equip.a: To redirect the horizontally accelerated electron beam downward toward the treatment head components
c: To focus the microwave power from the magnetron into the accelerating waveguide
To redirect the horizontally accelerated electron beam downward toward the treatment head components
To separate photons from electrons after the beam strikes the tungsten target
To modulate the beam energy during VMAT delivery by varying the magnetic field
equip.e: The accelerating waveguide is typically oriented horizontally in the gantry. The bending magnet (270° achromatic or 90° slalom design) redirects electrons downward toward the target or scattering foil. The 270° system also provides energy selection, focusing electrons of the correct energy onto the target while dispersing off-energy electrons.

equip.q: CALC: A linac waveguide operates at 2856 MHz (S-band). What is the wavelength of the accelerating microwave field?
equip.e: Wavelength = c / f = (3 × 10⁸ m/s) / (2856 × 10⁶ Hz) = 3 × 10⁸ / 2.856 × 10⁹ = 0.1050 m = 10.5 cm. Medical linacs typically use S-band (around 3 GHz) microwaves. The waveguide cavity dimensions are related to this wavelength.

equip.q: What material is used as the photon-producing target in the treatment head of a high-energy medical linac?
equip.a: Tungsten (or tungsten-copper composite) due to its high atomic number and melting point
c: Lead due to its effective radiation shielding properties and availability
Tungsten (or tungsten-copper composite) due to its high atomic number and melting point
Aluminium due to its lightweight nature and ease of manufacturing
Gold due to its highest atomic number among stable elements
equip.e: Tungsten (Z=74) is the preferred target material because of its high atomic number (efficient bremsstrahlung production), very high melting point (3422°C), and good thermal conductivity when alloyed with copper. The target is typically a thin tungsten disc brazed onto a copper heat sink.

equip.q: What is the typical leaf width at isocentre for the central leaves of a Varian Millennium 120-leaf MLC?
equip.a: 5 mm for the central 40 leaf pairs and 10 mm for the outer 20 leaf pairs
c: 2.5 mm uniform width for all 120 leaves across the full field
5 mm for the central 40 leaf pairs and 10 mm for the outer 20 leaf pairs
10 mm uniform width for all 60 leaf pairs across the full field
3 mm for the central 20 leaf pairs and 5 mm for the outer 40 leaf pairs
equip.e: The Varian Millennium 120 MLC has 60 leaf pairs: 40 central pairs with 5 mm width at isocentre and 20 outer pairs (10 each side) with 10 mm width. This provides fine resolution in the central treatment region while maintaining reasonable maximum field size. The HD120 MLC uses 2.5 mm central leaves for SRS.

equip.q: What distinguishes the Elekta Versa HD from a standard Elekta linac in terms of clinical capability?
equip.a: It combines high-dose-rate FFF beams with the Agility MLC for both conventional and stereotactic treatments
c: It uses a completely different accelerating structure based on X-band microwave technology
It combines high-dose-rate FFF beams with the Agility MLC for both conventional and stereotactic treatments
It replaces the standard gantry with a robotic arm for non-isocentric delivery
It incorporates an integrated MRI scanner for real-time adaptive treatment guidance
equip.e: The Elekta Versa HD is a versatile linac offering both flattened and FFF beam modes with dose rates up to 2400 MU/min (FFF). Combined with the Agility MLC (5 mm leaves, 160 leaves), it supports conventional RT through to SRS/SBRT. Several SA centres operate Versa HD units for comprehensive radiotherapy services.

equip.q: CALC: A Varian TrueBeam delivers 6 MV FFF at 1400 MU/min. A standard 6 MV flattened beam delivers at 600 MU/min. What factor faster is the FFF dose rate compared to the flattened beam?
equip.a: 2.33 times faster
c: 1.50 times faster
2.33 times faster
3.00 times faster
4.00 times faster
equip.e: Factor = 1400/600 = 2.333 ≈ 2.33. The higher dose rate of FFF beams is a major clinical advantage for SRS/SBRT, reducing treatment time and thereby reducing the impact of intra-fraction patient motion. The TrueBeam can deliver up to 2400 MU/min in 10 MV FFF mode.

equip.q: What is the function of the monitor chamber (transmission ionisation chamber) in a linac treatment head?
equip.a: To continuously measure the beam output and terminate irradiation when the set monitor units are reached
c: To shape the radiation field into the required treatment aperture configuration
To continuously measure the beam output and terminate irradiation when the set monitor units are reached
To filter out low-energy photons and harden the beam for clinical treatment use
To detect the presence of the patient in the beam path for safety purposes
equip.e: The monitor chamber is a dual-channel sealed transmission ionisation chamber positioned after the target/flattening filter. It integrates the charge produced by the beam, proportional to dose. When the preset MU is reached, the beam is terminated. The dual channels provide redundancy — if primary and secondary disagree, an interlock triggers.

equip.q: Which safety interlock would prevent beam delivery if the treatment room door is opened during irradiation?
equip.a: The door interlock, which immediately terminates the beam and prevents re-initiation until the door is closed
c: The dose rate interlock, which monitors the beam output level continuously
The door interlock, which immediately terminates the beam and prevents re-initiation until the door is closed
The gantry position interlock, which verifies correct gantry angle before delivery
The MLC interlock, which checks leaf positions against the planned sequence
equip.e: The door interlock is a critical safety system that terminates the beam instantly if the treatment room door is opened during irradiation. This protects staff and public from radiation exposure. In SA, door interlocks are mandatory under the National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) requirements and must be tested daily.

equip.q: What is the collimation hierarchy (from broadest to finest) in a modern dual-energy linac treatment head?
equip.a: Primary collimator, then upper and lower jaws, then MLC leaves
c: MLC leaves, then jaws, then primary collimator from broadest to finest
Primary collimator, then upper and lower jaws, then MLC leaves
Flattening filter, then primary collimator, then MLC leaves only
Upper jaws, then lower jaws, then primary collimator, then MLC leaves
equip.e: The primary collimator (fixed tungsten cone) defines the maximum field size. The jaws (upper Y-jaws and lower X-jaws) further collimate the beam into a rectangular field. The MLC provides the finest field shaping, conforming the beam to the target shape. Each level refines the field progressively.

equip.q: CALC: A linac gantry rotates at a maximum speed of 6 degrees per second during VMAT delivery. How long does a full 360° arc take at maximum speed?
equip.a: 60 seconds
c: 30 seconds
45 seconds
60 seconds
90 seconds
equip.e: Time = Total rotation / Speed = 360° / 6°/s = 60 s. In clinical VMAT delivery, the gantry often rotates slower than maximum to allow sufficient MU delivery per control point, so actual arc times are typically longer. Variable gantry speed is a key feature of VMAT.

equip.q: How does FFF mode differ from flattened mode in terms of the beam profile shape at the patient plane?
equip.a: FFF beams have a peaked (forward-peaked) central axis profile that decreases toward the field edges
c: FFF beams have a perfectly uniform flat profile identical to flattened beams
FFF beams have a peaked (forward-peaked) central axis profile that decreases toward the field edges
FFF beams have a concave profile with higher dose at the edges than at the centre
FFF beams have a random irregular profile that varies unpredictably with field size
equip.e: Without the flattening filter to attenuate the central high-intensity region, FFF beams exhibit a forward-peaked (conical) profile with the highest intensity on the central axis, dropping off toward the edges. The TPS accounts for this non-uniform profile during treatment planning. This is acceptable for IMRT/VMAT where fluence modulation compensates.

equip.q: What imaging modalities are typically available on a modern Varian TrueBeam system for image-guided radiotherapy?
equip.a: kV planar imaging, kV CBCT, MV planar imaging, and MV CBCT capabilities
c: Only MV portal imaging using the treatment beam and EPID detector
Only kV planar imaging from a single fixed tube mounted on the ceiling
kV planar imaging, kV CBCT, MV planar imaging, and MV CBCT capabilities
Integrated diagnostic CT scanner and ultrasound system mounted on the gantry
equip.e: The TrueBeam features an on-board kV imaging system (X-ray tube and flat-panel detector) for 2D kV radiographs and kV CBCT, plus MV imaging via the EPID for portal images and MV CBCT. This multi-modality IGRT capability supports diverse clinical applications from standard setup verification to SRS-level image guidance.

equip.q: What is the function of the robotic couch (6 degrees of freedom) in modern radiotherapy?
equip.a: It enables translational and rotational corrections to precisely align the patient to the planned treatment position
c: It replaces the need for any immobilisation devices by holding the patient mechanically
It enables translational and rotational corrections to precisely align the patient to the planned treatment position
It moves the patient continuously during treatment to distribute dose evenly across normal tissue
It measures the patient weight to calculate the correct dose for each treatment fraction
equip.e: A 6 DOF robotic couch (e.g., Varian PerfectPitch, Elekta HexaPOD) provides 3 translational (lateral, longitudinal, vertical) and 3 rotational (pitch, roll, yaw) corrections. After image guidance comparison, the couch applies corrections to match the patient position to the planning CT position with sub-millimetre precision.

equip.q: CALC: An accelerating waveguide has 30 coupled cavities and each cavity provides an energy gain of 0.4 MeV to the electron beam. What is the final electron kinetic energy assuming the gun injection energy is 50 keV?
equip.e: Total energy gain from cavities = 30 × 0.4 MeV = 12.0 MeV. Adding the injection energy: 12.0 + 0.05 MeV = 12.05 MeV. The electron gun typically operates at 25-50 kV. This final energy determines the photon beam spectrum after target interaction and defines the nominal beam energy (e.g., approximately a 6 MV photon beam for ~6 MeV electrons).

equip.q: In the Elekta Agility MLC, what is the maximum leaf speed and how does it benefit VMAT delivery?
equip.a: Up to 3.5 cm/s, enabling rapid field shape changes during dynamic arc delivery
c: Up to 0.5 cm/s, sufficient for static field delivery only without arcs
Up to 1.5 cm/s, adequate for step-and-shoot IMRT but limiting for VMAT
Up to 3.5 cm/s, enabling rapid field shape changes during dynamic arc delivery
Up to 10 cm/s, eliminating all leaf travel time constraints during delivery
equip.e: The Elekta Agility MLC leaves can travel at up to 3.5 cm/s, which is faster than the Varian Millennium MLC (2.5 cm/s). This higher leaf speed allows more complex fluence modulation during VMAT delivery without requiring the gantry to slow down, improving treatment efficiency and plan quality.

equip.q: What happens to the electron beam in a linac when it strikes the target to produce a clinical photon beam?
equip.a: Electrons undergo bremsstrahlung interactions with target nuclei, producing a spectrum of photon energies
c: Electrons are reflected back through the waveguide to produce coherent X-ray radiation
Electrons undergo bremsstrahlung interactions with target nuclei, producing a spectrum of photon energies
Electrons are absorbed completely and converted to monoenergetic photons at the beam energy
Electrons pass through the target unchanged but cause nuclear fission releasing photons
equip.e: When high-energy electrons strike the high-Z tungsten target, they undergo bremsstrahlung (braking radiation) interactions with the nuclear electric fields. This produces a continuous spectrum of photon energies from zero up to the maximum electron kinetic energy. Characteristic X-rays also contribute. The resulting beam is polyenergetic, with the maximum energy defining the nominal MV designation.

equip.q: CALC: A Varian HD120 MLC has 32 central leaf pairs of 2.5 mm width and 28 outer leaf pairs of 5.0 mm width at isocentre. What is the maximum field width that this MLC can define?
equip.e: Central region width = 32 × 2.5 mm = 80 mm = 8.0 cm. Outer region width = 28 × 5.0 mm = 140 mm = 14.0 cm. Total MLC-defined field width = 8.0 + 14.0 = 22.0 cm. The maximum field length (perpendicular to leaf travel) is 40 cm, defined by the jaws. The HD120 is specifically designed for SRS with fine central resolution.

equip.q: What is the primary purpose of the electron gun in a medical linear accelerator?
equip.a: To produce and inject electrons into the accelerating waveguide at the correct phase of the microwave cycle
c: To generate the microwave power that accelerates particles through the waveguide
To produce and inject electrons into the accelerating waveguide at the correct phase of the microwave cycle
To bend the electron beam through 270 degrees toward the treatment head target
To monitor the beam current and provide feedback to the klystron power supply
equip.e: The electron gun uses a heated cathode (thermionic emission) to produce free electrons, which are then injected into the accelerating waveguide. The gun is pulsed synchronously with the microwave power to ensure electrons enter the waveguide at the correct phase for acceleration. Typical injection energies are 25-50 keV.

equip.q: What is the primary difference between a magnetron and a klystron in a linear accelerator?
equip.a: A magnetron generates microwaves while a klystron amplifies them
c: A magnetron generates microwaves while a klystron amplifies them
A magnetron operates at higher frequency than a klystron does
A klystron generates microwaves while a magnetron amplifies them
A magnetron is used for electrons while klystron is for photons
equip.e: A magnetron is a microwave oscillator that generates radiofrequency power, while a klystron is a microwave amplifier that boosts a low-power RF signal. Klystrons offer better frequency stability and are used in higher-energy linacs.

equip.q: What is the purpose of the bending magnet in a linear accelerator?
equip.a: To redirect the electron beam toward the target or scattering foil
c: To redirect the electron beam toward the target or scattering foil
To generate the radiofrequency power for electron acceleration
To collimate the treatment beam to the desired field size
To flatten the photon beam profile across the treatment field
equip.e: In linacs where the accelerating waveguide is horizontal, a bending magnet (typically 270 degrees for achromatic and energy-selective properties) redirects the electron beam downward toward the target (photon mode) or scattering foil (electron mode).

equip.q: Why is a 270-degree bending magnet preferred over a 90-degree bending magnet in medical linacs?
equip.a: It provides energy selection and achromatic beam transport
c: It provides energy selection and achromatic beam transport
It requires less electrical power to operate the magnet
It occupies less physical space inside the gantry head
It produces a higher dose rate at the treatment isocenter
equip.e: A 270-degree achromatic bending magnet focuses electrons of the correct energy to a single point while dispersing off-energy electrons onto a beam stop. This acts as an energy filter, producing a more monoenergetic beam with a smaller focal spot.

equip.q: What material is the photon target made of in a medical linear accelerator?
equip.a: High-Z material such as tungsten or tungsten-copper alloy
c: High-Z material such as tungsten or tungsten-copper alloy
Low-Z material such as aluminum or beryllium window
Lead with a thin gold foil backing for heat dissipation
Stainless steel matching the waveguide wall composition
equip.e: The target is made of a high atomic number (high-Z) material, typically tungsten or a tungsten-copper alloy. High-Z materials maximize bremsstrahlung x-ray production efficiency, and copper provides thermal conductivity for heat dissipation.

equip.q: What is the purpose of the flattening filter in a conventional medical linear accelerator?
equip.a: To produce a uniform dose profile across the radiation field
c: To produce a uniform dose profile across the radiation field
To remove low-energy photon contamination from the beam
To reduce the electron contamination in the photon beam
To collimate the beam to the desired treatment field shape
equip.e: The bremsstrahlung beam is forward-peaked, with higher intensity on the central axis. The flattening filter (typically made of steel or copper) is thicker at the center to differentially attenuate the beam and produce a flat profile at a reference depth.

equip.q: What is the typical leaf width at isocenter for standard MLCs on modern medical linacs?
equip.a: 5 mm for the central leaves
c: 5 mm for the central leaves
2.5 mm for all leaf pairs
10 mm for the central leaves
1 mm for the central leaves
equip.e: Modern linacs (e.g., Varian Millennium 120 MLC) typically have 5 mm leaf width at isocenter for the central 20 cm of the field, with 10 mm leaves at the periphery. The Varian HD120 MLC offers 2.5 mm central leaves for SRS.

equip.q: What is the tongue-and-groove effect in multileaf collimator design?
equip.a: Underdosage between adjacent leaves due to interlocking leaf edges
c: Underdosage between adjacent leaves due to interlocking leaf edges
Overdosage at leaf tips caused by transmission through leaf ends
Increased penumbra width from rounded leaf tip design features
Dose enhancement from scatter between opposing leaf bank edges
equip.e: MLC leaves have interlocking tongue-and-groove edges to reduce interleaf leakage. However, when adjacent leaves have different open/closed positions across multiple segments, the overlapping projections of the tongue-and-groove create lines of underdosage.

equip.q: What is the function of the EPID (Electronic Portal Imaging Device) on a linear accelerator?
equip.a: To acquire megavoltage images for patient position verification
c: To acquire megavoltage images for patient position verification
To measure daily beam output for quality assurance checks
To generate kilovoltage images for treatment plan creation
To monitor the dose rate during patient treatment delivery
equip.e: The EPID, typically an amorphous silicon flat-panel detector, acquires megavoltage (MV) images using the treatment beam. It is used for patient positioning verification, but increasingly also for transit dosimetry and machine QA applications.

equip.q: What is the typical kV imaging system mounted on a modern linear accelerator called?
equip.a: On-board imager (OBI)
c: On-board imager (OBI)
Electronic portal imaging device (EPID)
Megavoltage computed tomography (MVCT) unit
Beam's eye view imaging system (BEV unit)
equip.e: The on-board imager (OBI) consists of a kV x-ray source and amorphous silicon detector mounted orthogonal to the treatment beam. It provides kV planar images and cone-beam CT for high-contrast patient positioning verification.

equip.q: What type of waveguide is used in lower-energy medical linacs (4-6 MV)?
equip.a: Standing wave accelerating waveguide
c: Standing wave accelerating waveguide
Traveling wave accelerating waveguide
Circular waveguide with TM010 excitation
Rectangular waveguide with TE10 propagation
equip.e: Lower-energy linacs (4-6 MV) typically use standing wave waveguides, which are shorter and more compact. Traveling wave guides are used in higher-energy machines where longer acceleration paths are needed to achieve higher electron energies.

equip.q: What does a 6 degrees-of-freedom (6DoF) treatment couch allow that a standard couch does not?
equip.a: Rotational corrections in pitch, roll, and yaw axes
c: Rotational corrections in pitch, roll, and yaw axes
Faster lateral movement speed during patient positioning
Higher weight capacity for bariatric patient treatments
Automated collision detection with the gantry structure
equip.e: A 6DoF couch provides three translational corrections (lateral, longitudinal, vertical) plus three rotational corrections (pitch, roll, yaw). Standard couches only provide translational corrections, unable to correct for rotational patient setup errors.

equip.q: What is the purpose of the primary collimator in a linear accelerator treatment head?
equip.a: To define the maximum circular field size from the target
c: To define the maximum circular field size from the target
To flatten the beam profile across the treatment field
To shape the beam to conform to the tumor outline shape
To monitor the beam output in real-time during delivery
equip.e: The primary collimator is a fixed tungsten block with a conical opening located immediately after the target. It defines the maximum available circular field size and absorbs radiation outside this cone to reduce leakage.

equip.q: What is MLC interdigitation and when is it NOT allowed?
equip.a: Opposing leaves passing each other; not allowed in some MLC designs
c: Opposing leaves passing each other; not allowed in some MLC designs
Adjacent leaves overlapping vertically; not allowed in electron mode
Leaf pairs closing beyond the central axis; not allowed in IMRT
Leaves moving during beam-on time; not allowed in step-and-shoot
equip.e: Interdigitation refers to leaves from opposite banks passing each other (one bank's leaf extending past the opposing bank's leaf in the same row). Some MLC designs prohibit this due to mechanical constraints, while others allow it for improved field shaping.

equip.q: What type of respiratory gating system uses external markers placed on the patient's chest?
equip.a: The Varian RPM (Real-time Position Management) system
c: The Varian RPM (Real-time Position Management) system
The Calypso electromagnetic transponder tracking system
The Accuray Synchrony system with internal fiducial markers
The Siemens Anzai belt pressure-based gating system only
equip.e: The Varian RPM system uses an infrared camera to track a reflective marker block placed on the patient's anterior chest surface. The respiratory waveform is derived from marker motion to gate beam delivery at specific phases of the breathing cycle.

equip.q: What are the two main types of accelerating waveguide structures used in medical linacs?
equip.a: Standing wave and traveling wave structures
c: Standing wave and traveling wave structures
Continuous wave and pulsed wave structures
Circular waveguide and rectangular waveguide types
Side-coupled and end-coupled cavity structures
equip.e: Medical linacs use either standing wave (shorter, used in lower-energy machines with side-coupled cavities) or traveling wave (longer, used in higher-energy machines with a terminating load) accelerating waveguide structures.

equip.q: What is the function of the monitor ion chambers in the linear accelerator treatment head?
equip.a: To measure beam output and terminate irradiation at preset MU
c: To measure beam output and terminate irradiation at preset MU
To shape the radiation beam to match the target volume shape
