To filter out low-energy photons from the treatment beam
To detect patient motion during the treatment delivery time
equip.e: Two independent sealed transmission ion chambers in the linac head continuously monitor the beam output (dose rate), flatness, and symmetry. They terminate irradiation when the preset monitor units are delivered and serve as redundant safety systems.

equip.q: What is the typical source-to-axis distance (SAD) for modern medical linear accelerators?
equip.a: 100 cm
c: 100 cm
80 cm
120 cm
150 cm
equip.e: Modern medical linacs are designed with a source-to-axis distance (SAD) of 100 cm, meaning the distance from the x-ray target to the machine isocenter is 100 cm. This is a standard used across most manufacturers.

equip.q: What type of interlock prevents the gantry from colliding with the patient couch?
equip.a: Anti-collision interlock system
c: Anti-collision interlock system
Radiation beam output monitoring interlock
Door interlock safety switch at the entrance
Beam energy selection verification interlock
equip.e: Anti-collision interlocks use proximity sensors, physical bumper bars, or predictive software to detect potential collisions between the gantry, couch, and patient. They halt gantry or couch motion before contact occurs to prevent injury.

equip.q: What happens to the electron beam in a medical linac when photon mode is switched to electron mode?
equip.a: The target and flattening filter are retracted and scattering foils are inserted
c: The target and flattening filter are retracted and scattering foils are inserted
The bending magnet field strength is reversed to change beam direction
The microwave power source is switched from klystron to magnetron
The accelerating waveguide frequency is changed to produce electrons
equip.e: When switching from photon to electron mode, the target and flattening filter are moved out of the beam path and replaced with scattering foils (dual-foil system) that spread the narrow electron pencil beam into a clinically useful broad beam.

equip.q: In a medical linear accelerator, what structure accelerates electrons to therapeutic energies?
equip.a: A copper waveguide structure powered by microwave radiofrequency fields
c: A betatron magnetic induction coil surrounding a toroidal vacuum chamber
A copper waveguide structure powered by microwave radiofrequency fields
A Van de Graaff electrostatic belt-charged high-voltage terminal system
A cyclotron with superconducting magnets producing a spiral beam path
equip.e: Medical linacs use standing or traveling wave accelerating structures (copper waveguides) powered by magnetron or klystron microwave sources operating at approximately 3 GHz.

equip.q: What is the primary function of the flattening filter in a conventional linac photon beam?
equip.a: To produce a uniform dose profile across the radiation treatment field
c: To remove electron contamination from the photon beam at the target
To produce a uniform dose profile across the radiation treatment field
To harden the beam by filtering out low-energy scattered photons
To collimate the beam to the prescribed treatment field size accurately
equip.e: The conical flattening filter preferentially attenuates the forward-peaked bremsstrahlung beam on the central axis, creating a more uniform lateral dose profile across the field.

equip.q: What is a key dosimetric advantage of flattening-filter-free (FFF) beams over conventional flattened beams?
equip.a: Significantly higher dose rates enabling faster treatment delivery times
c: Improved lateral dose profile uniformity across all clinical field sizes
Significantly higher dose rates enabling faster treatment delivery times
Reduced neutron contamination for beams above 10 MV photon energy
Sharper penumbra at all depths compared to conventional flattened beams
equip.e: Removing the flattening filter eliminates a major source of beam attenuation, allowing dose rates 2-4 times higher than conventional beams, which benefits SRS/SBRT treatments.

equip.q: What does a multileaf collimator (MLC) primarily provide in modern radiotherapy delivery?
equip.a: Dynamic or static field shaping to conform the beam to the target volume
c: Dynamic or static field shaping to conform the beam to the target volume
Beam energy modulation by varying the thickness of tungsten in the beam path
Patient position verification through integrated kilovoltage imaging capability
Dose rate control by modulating the electron gun current during treatment
equip.e: MLCs consist of individually motorized tungsten leaves that move in and out of the beam to create irregular field shapes matching the target projection at each gantry angle.

equip.q: What is the typical leaf width at isocenter for MLCs used in stereotactic radiosurgery applications?
equip.a: 2.5 mm to 5 mm for high-definition stereotactic MLC models
c: 0.5 mm to 1.0 mm for high-definition stereotactic MLC models
2.5 mm to 5 mm for high-definition stereotactic MLC models
7.5 mm to 10 mm for high-definition stereotactic MLC models
12.5 mm to 15 mm for high-definition stereotactic MLC models
equip.e: High-definition MLCs like the Varian HD120 have 2.5 mm central leaves, while the BrainLab micro-MLC uses 3 mm leaves, providing fine resolution for small SRS targets.

equip.q: In Varian TrueBeam systems, what technology enables beam-on imaging during treatment delivery?
equip.a: Triggered imaging synchronized with the linac pulse repetition frequency
c: Continuous kV fluoroscopy running independently of treatment beam pulses
Triggered imaging synchronized with the linac pulse repetition frequency
A separate dedicated imaging linac mounted orthogonal to the treatment head
Cerenkov emission cameras detecting light produced by the treatment beam
equip.e: TrueBeam uses triggered imaging where kV or MV images are acquired between treatment beam pulses, enabling intrafraction monitoring without interrupting dose delivery.

equip.q: What is the function of the bending magnet in a medical linear accelerator?
equip.a: To redirect the accelerated electron beam toward the patient treatment axis
c: To focus the microwave power from the magnetron into the waveguide structure
To redirect the accelerated electron beam toward the patient treatment axis
To separate electrons by energy for dual-energy photon beam production
To steer the beam laterally for scanning delivery without moving the gantry
equip.e: Most linacs accelerate electrons horizontally, so a bending magnet (typically 270 degrees) redirects the beam downward toward the isocenter along the treatment head axis.

eclipse.q: AcurosXB solves?
eclipse.a: LBTE
c: Schrödinger
LBTE
Monte Carlo
Pencil beam
eclipse.e: Linear Boltzmann Transport Equation for photon/electron transport.

eclipse.q: AcurosXB dose reporting modes?
eclipse.a: D_m and D_w
c: D_m only
D_w only
D_m and D_w
Relative only
eclipse.e: D_m physically correct; D_w for comparison with older algorithms.

eclipse.q: D_m vs D_w: biggest difference in?
c: Water as part of the clinical workflow
Lung during routine clinical operations
Air during the planning stage
eclipse.e: Different stopping power ratios in bone.

eclipse.q: Eclipse default grid size?
eclipse.a: 2.5 mm
c: 1 mm
2 mm
2.5 mm
5 mm
eclipse.e: Reduce to ≤2mm for SBRT, ≤1mm for SRS.

eclipse.q: DLG measurement method?
eclipse.a: Sweeping gap
c: Picket fence
Sweeping gap
Star shot
Output ratio
eclipse.e: Sliding windows with known gaps. Extrapolate to zero gap.

eclipse.q: NTO in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: Normal Tissue Objective
c: New Treatment Optimiser
Normal Tissue Objective
Nominal Target Outline
Non-Target OAR
eclipse.e: Reduces dose to all non-target tissue automatically.

eclipse.q: Van Herk margin formula?
c: M = 2Σ + σ
M = Σ + 2σ
eclipse.e: 95% dose to CTV for 90% of patients.

eclipse.q: Jaw tracking in VMAT does?
eclipse.a: Reduces scatter/leakage
c: Speeds delivery
Reduces scatter/leakage
Improves imaging
Locks jaws
eclipse.e: Jaws follow MLC dynamically. Enable for SBRT.

eclipse.q: HI formula?
c: D50% / Dprescribed
Dmax / Dmin
eclipse.e: Lower = more uniform.

eclipse.q: VMAT vs IMRT delivery?
eclipse.a: VMAT is faster
c: IMRT is faster
VMAT is faster
Same speed
Depends on energy
eclipse.e: 2-3 min vs 10-15 for step-and-shoot.

eclipse.q: CT calibration curve maps?
eclipse.a: HU to electron density
c: HU to dose in clinical practice settings
HU to electron density
Dose to MU as part of standard practice
MU to HU for quality assurance purposes
eclipse.e: Wrong curve = systematic dose errors in every plan.

eclipse.q: Wrong CT curve causes?
eclipse.a: Systematic dose errors
c: Nothing
Systematic dose errors
Image artefacts only
MLC errors
eclipse.e: Most critical in lung and bone.

eclipse.q: Plan modulation factor?
eclipse.a: Total MU / prescribed cGy
c: Dose rate / MU
Total MU / prescribed cGy
Arcs × segments
Field size ratio
eclipse.e: High (>3-4×) = complex delivery, harder QA.

eclipse.q: CALC: HI if D2%=52Gy, D98%=47Gy, D50%=50Gy?
CALC: CI if V_prescription=25cc, V_PTV=20cc?
eclipse.e: CI = 25/20 = 1.25. Slight over-irradiation beyond PTV.

eclipse.q: V20 for lungs means?
eclipse.a: % lung volume receiving ≥ 20 Gy
c: 20% of lung volume
% lung volume receiving ≥ 20 Gy
20 Gy isodose volume
Volume at 20 cm depth
eclipse.e: Lung toxicity predictor. V20 < 30-35% reduces pneumonitis risk.

eclipse.q: Eclipse: what is the AAA algorithm type?
eclipse.a: Pencil beam convolution/superposition
c: Monte Carlo — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Pencil beam convolution/superposition
Finite element — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Ray tracing — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
eclipse.e: Separable primary and scatter dose kernels convolved over the 3D dose grid.

eclipse.q: Eclipse: MCO stands for?
eclipse.a: Multi-Criteria Optimisation
c: Monte Carlo Optimisation
Multi-Criteria Optimisation
Maximum Conformity Objective
MLC Control Optimiser
eclipse.e: Generates Pareto-optimal plan set. Navigate trade-offs in real time.

eclipse.q: CALC: Van Herk margin if Σ=3mm, σ=2mm?
eclipse.a: 8.9 mm
c: 5.0 mm
7.5 mm
8.9 mm

eclipse.q: CALC: Modulation factor if 800 MU prescribed for 200 cGy?
eclipse.e: MF = 800/200 = 4.0. High modulation — plan complexity may need review.

eclipse.q: What does the Eclipse beam model need for electrons?
eclipse.a: PDDs, profiles, and absolute output per applicator/energy
c: Only profiles — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
PDDs, profiles, and absolute output per applicator/energy
MLC data only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
CT calibration curve — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
eclipse.e: Each applicator/energy/insert combination needs characterisation.

eclipse.q: Field-in-field technique is used for?
eclipse.a: Breast tangent dose homogeneity
c: SRS brain — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Breast tangent dose homogeneity
Prostate VMAT — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Electron treatments
eclipse.e: Forward-planned subfields with MLC blocking hot spots to improve uniformity.

eclipse.q: What is SIB in VMAT?
eclipse.a: Simultaneous Integrated Boost
c: Single Isocenter Beam
Simultaneous Integrated Boost
Standard Intensity Beam
Sequential Irradiation Block
eclipse.e: Different dose levels to different targets in the same fraction. Efficient and conformal.

eclipse.q: Eclipse: PRV stands for?
eclipse.a: Planning organ at Risk Volume
c: Primary Radiation Volume
Planning organ at Risk Volume
Prescribed Reference Volume
Posterior Radiation Volume
eclipse.e: OAR + margin for uncertainty = PRV. Used for dose constraints in optimisation.

eclipse.q: CALC: D95 = 48 Gy, prescription = 50 Gy. What % coverage?
eclipse.e: 48/50 × 100 = 96%. Adequate for most protocols (target ≥ 95%).

eclipse.q: What is the dose painting concept?
eclipse.a: Variable dose prescription based on biological imaging
c: Uniform dose everywhere — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Variable dose prescription based on biological imaging
Painting on patient skin — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Colour dose display — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
eclipse.e: PET-guided boost to metabolically active sub-volumes within the tumour.

eclipse.q: Eclipse body structure must?
eclipse.a: Encompass all other structures
c: Be hollow as part of the clinical workflow
Encompass all other structures
Be exactly 5mm thick
Only cover PTV as specified by guidelines
eclipse.e: External body contour defines the calculation boundary. Everything must be inside.

eclipse.q: Collimator angle rotation in VMAT optimises?
eclipse.a: Leaf travel direction relative to target shape
c: Dose rate — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Leaf travel direction relative to target shape
Gantry speed — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Couch position — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
eclipse.e: Rotating collimator improves MLC conformity for irregular target shapes.

eclipse.q: What is inverse planning?
eclipse.a: Define objectives
c: Manually place beams → calculate dose
Define objectives
Copy a previous plan
Auto-contour first
eclipse.e: optimiser finds beam parameters. Specify dose goals and constraints; the algorithm iteratively optimises fluence/MLC to achieve them.

eclipse.q: Forward planning is used for?
eclipse.a: Conventional 3D-CRT and field-in-field
c: VMAT only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Conventional 3D-CRT and field-in-field
SRS only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Adaptive RT only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
eclipse.e: Planner manually selects beams, weights, and wedges. TPS calculates resulting dose.

eclipse.q: CALC: If V20(lung)=28% and limit is <35%, is this acceptable?
eclipse.a: Yes — within tolerance
c: No — too high
Yes — within tolerance
Borderline
Need more data
eclipse.e: 28% < 35%. Acceptable but still monitor for pneumonitis risk factors.

eclipse.q: Monaco TPS dose calculation engine?
eclipse.a: XVMC (X-ray Voxel Monte Carlo)
c: Pencil beam convolution
XVMC (X-ray Voxel Monte Carlo)
Collapsed cone
Analytical Anisotropic Algorithm
eclipse.e: a full Monte Carlo algorithm. Monaco uses XVMC: simulates individual photon/electron histories through voxelised patient geometry. Gold-standard accuracy at interfaces (lung, bone, air cavities). Reports dose as Dm (dose-to-medium) by default.

eclipse.q: Monaco statistical uncertainty parameter: what does '1% per plan' mean?
eclipse.a: Average Monte Carlo statistical uncertainty across all voxels above a dose threshold is ≤1%
c: 1% systematic error — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Average Monte Carlo statistical uncertainty across all voxels above a dose threshold is ≤1%
1% of max dose — based on established physics principles and clinical evidence
1% random patient setup error, based on established clinical physics practice
eclipse.e: MC dose is stochastic — more histories = less noise. The uncertainty parameter sets the convergence target. 1% is typical for clinical plans. Lower (0.5%) used for SRS but takes ~4× longer to compute.

eclipse.q: CALC: Monaco MC uncertainty set at 2%. Prescription is 200 cGy. What is the dose uncertainty range (1 SD)?
c: ±0.4 cGy as specified by guidelines
±2 cGy for radiation dose management
±20 cGy in the radiation therapy context
eclipse.e: 2% of 200 cGy = 4 cGy. Any voxel's calculated dose could vary by ±4 cGy (1σ) due to MC statistics alone. For SRS (single fraction high dose), tighter uncertainty (≤1%) is essential.

eclipse.q: Monaco Segment Shape Optimisation (SSO) vs Direct Machine Parameter Optimisation (DMPO)?
eclipse.a: SSO: optimises fluence then segments. DMPO: optimises apertures and weights directly — more efficient MLC delivery
c: Same algorithm, different name — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
SSO: optimises fluence then segments. DMPO: optimises apertures and weights directly — more efficient MLC delivery
SSO is for electrons only, following current regulatory and professional standards
DMPO only works for 3DCRT, following current regulatory and professional standards
eclipse.e: SSO: two-step (fluence optimisation → leaf sequencing). DMPO: directly optimises deliverable segments, accounting for MLC constraints from the start → fewer MU, less leakage, faster delivery. DMPO preferred for most clinical VMAT/IMRT.

eclipse.q: Monaco biological cost functions: what do they optimise?
eclipse.a: EUD-based (Equivalent Uniform Dose)
c: Only dose-volume metrics
EUD-based (Equivalent Uniform Dose)
Only physical dose
MU minimisation only
eclipse.e: directly incorporates radiobiological parameters. Monaco supports biological cost functions using generalised EUD (Niemierko model). Serial organs: high 'a' parameter (max dose driven). Parallel organs: lower 'a' (mean dose driven). Target: negative 'a' (cold spots penalised). Alternative to pure DVH-based optimisation.

eclipse.q: Monaco commissioning: what beam data is NOT required that Eclipse AAA needs?
eclipse.a: Golden beam data profiles
c: PDD curves
Output factors
Golden beam data profiles
Absolute dose calibration
eclipse.e: Monaco models the linac head geometry and computes fluence from source parameters. Monaco models linac components (target, flattening filter, jaws, MLC) as virtual source model. PDD and profiles are used for fitting source parameters, but the MC engine then independently calculates dose. Eclipse AAA uses measured profiles more directly in kernel fitting.

eclipse.q: Monaco dose-to-medium (Dm) vs dose-to-water (Dw): which is the default and why does it matter?
eclipse.a: Dm is default — MC naturally scores dose in the medium; Dw requires a conversion and is ~2-4% higher in bone
c: Dw is default in Monaco — MC naturally scores dose relative to water stopping powers
No clinically significant difference — Dm and Dw agree within 0.1% in all tissues
Dm is default — MC naturally scores dose in the medium; Dw requires a conversion and is ~2-4% higher in bone
Only affects air cavities where both Dm and Dw are unreliable regardless of algorithm
eclipse.e: MC transports particles through actual medium → naturally yields Dm. Converting to Dw uses stopping power ratios. In bone: Dw ≈ 1.02-1.04 × Dm. In soft tissue: negligible difference. Clinician must know which is reported.

eclipse.q: RayStation TPS: dose calculation algorithms available for photons?
eclipse.a: Collapsed Cone (CC), Monte Carlo, and Pencil Beam
c: Only Monte Carlo — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Only Collapsed Cone — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Collapsed Cone (CC), Monte Carlo, and Pencil Beam
Only AAA — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
eclipse.e: user selects per plan. RayStation offers multiple algorithms: CC (fast, good accuracy), MC (highest accuracy, slower), PB (fastest, least accurate). Physicist validates all commissioned algorithms and advises when MC is needed (lung, small fields, heterogeneities).

eclipse.q: RayStation Multi-Criteria Optimisation (MCO): what does it provide?
eclipse.a: A Pareto-optimal surface — planner interactively explores trade-offs between target coverage and OAR sparing in real time
c: A single automatically generated optimal plan with no user interaction required afterward
A Pareto-optimal surface — planner interactively explores trade-offs between target coverage and OAR sparing in real time
Only a static DVH comparison table showing metrics from three pre-generated plan options
An automatic plan selection algorithm that chooses the best plan without clinician input
eclipse.e: MCO generates multiple Pareto-optimal plans. Interactive navigation: slider moves along trade-off surface (e.g., more cord sparing ↔ less target homogeneity). Clinician explores options without re-optimising. Powerful for complex cases.

eclipse.q: RayStation scripting language?
eclipse.a: Python — full API access for automated planning, QA, and research
c: C++ only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
MATLAB — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Python — full API access for automated planning, QA, and research
No scripting available — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
eclipse.e: RayStation's Python scripting API: automate plan creation, template-based planning, batch processing, DVH extraction, research protocols. Eclipse uses ESAPI (C#). Monaco has limited scripting. RayStation's Python API is considered the most accessible.

eclipse.q: RayStation proton dose calculation: which algorithm is recommended for pencil beam scanning (PBS)?
eclipse.a: Monte Carlo — accurately models nuclear interactions, range straggling, and lateral scatter in heterogeneous media
c: Pencil beam analytical — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
Monte Carlo — accurately models nuclear interactions, range straggling, and lateral scatter in heterogeneous media
Collapsed cone — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
Superposition/convolution, following current regulatory and professional standards
eclipse.e: Proton MC in RayStation: models nuclear interactions (neutron production, fragmentation for carbon), range uncertainties in heterogeneous tissue. PB analytical can miss >5% at tissue interfaces. MC is standard of care for PBS proton planning.

eclipse.q: RayStation robust optimisation for proton plans accounts for?
eclipse.a: Range uncertainty (±3.5% typically) and setup uncertainty simultaneously during optimisation
c: Only setup errors — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Only range errors — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Range uncertainty (±3.5% typically) and setup uncertainty simultaneously during optimisation
No robustness features — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
eclipse.e: Proton plans sensitive to range errors (density changes) and setup shifts. Robust optimisation evaluates worst-case scenarios (shifted/scaled CTs) and optimises the plan to meet goals across all scenarios. Standard for proton planning.

eclipse.q: Pinnacle TPS dose calculation algorithm?
eclipse.a: Collapsed Cone Convolution/Superposition (CCCS)
c: Monte Carlo — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
AAA — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Collapsed Cone Convolution/Superposition (CCCS)
Pencil beam only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
eclipse.e: Pinnacle CCCS: models primary and scatter dose using energy deposition kernels collapsed along ray lines. Good accuracy in heterogeneous media (better than PB, slightly less than MC at extreme interfaces). Widely used historically.

eclipse.q: Pinnacle vs Eclipse: how do they handle heterogeneity corrections differently?
eclipse.a: Pinnacle CCCS uses ray-line kernel scaling
c: Same method
Pinnacle CCCS uses ray-line kernel scaling
Pinnacle ignores heterogeneities
Eclipse ignores heterogeneities
eclipse.e: Eclipse AAA uses lateral kernel scaling with separate modelling of primary, scatter, and electron contamination. CCCS: collapses 3D kernel along rays — efficient but can underestimate lateral scatter in lung. AAA: separates kernels into components, better lateral modelling but still approximate. Both less accurate than MC in extreme cases.

eclipse.q: Pinnacle SmartArc is?
eclipse.a: Pinnacle's VMAT optimisation
c: A patient positioning system
Pinnacle's VMAT optimisation
An imaging protocol
A QA tool
eclipse.e: directly optimises apertures, gantry speed, and dose rate simultaneously. SmartArc: single or dual arc VMAT. Optimises MLC apertures at control points while varying gantry speed and dose rate. Compatible with Elekta and Varian linacs. Replaced older step-and-shoot IMRT approaches.

eclipse.q: Eclipse AcurosXB vs AAA: when does the difference exceed 2%?
eclipse.a: Lung/air interfaces, small fields in heterogeneous media, and low-density tissue
c: Never exceeds 2% in any clinical scenario — both algorithms are equivalent for all treatments
Only in high-density bone structures where AcurosXB underpredicts by 3-5% vs AAA
Lung/air interfaces, small fields in heterogeneous media, and low-density tissue
Only at field edges beyond the 50% isodose line in homogeneous water-equivalent phantoms
eclipse.e: AAA underestimates dose at lung-tissue interfaces (can be 3-5% in some SBRT scenarios). AcurosXB: deterministic LBTE solver — handles interfaces better. Critical for lung SBRT, where target is surrounded by low-density tissue.

eclipse.q: Eclipse ESAPI scripting: what language and what can it access?
eclipse.a: C# — access to patient data, plan parameters, DVHs, structures, and dose distributions
c: Python only — based on established physics principles and clinical evidence
C# — access to patient data, plan parameters, DVHs, structures, and dose distributions
No scripting in Eclipse — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
Java — the standard approach used in most clinical departments worldwide
eclipse.e: ESAPI (Eclipse Scripting API): C# .NET framework. Read/write access for research, automated plan checks, DVH extraction, report generation. More restrictive than RayStation Python but powerful for institutional protocol enforcement.

eclipse.q: Eclipse Photon Optimizer (PO) vs older DVO: key improvement?
eclipse.a: PO uses intermediate dose calculation during optimisation
c: No improvement — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
PO uses intermediate dose calculation during optimisation
PO is faster only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
PO uses Monte Carlo — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
eclipse.e: more accurate final dose vs what was optimised. Older DVO: optimised fluence then calculated final dose → discrepancy between optimised and calculated dose. PO: periodic intermediate dose recalculation during optimisation → final dose closely matches optimised dose. Reduces need for re-optimisation.

eclipse.q: Dose calculation grid resolution: what is the trade-off?
eclipse.a: Finer grid → more accurate dose (especially near interfaces and small structures) but longer calculation time
c: Grid size doesn't matter — the standard approach used in most clinical departments worldwide
Finer grid → more accurate dose (especially near interfaces and small structures) but longer calculation time
Coarser grid is always better, following current regulatory and professional standards
Only affects display — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
eclipse.e: Typical: 2.5 mm for standard RT, 1-1.5 mm for SRS/SBRT/small targets. 1 mm grid has ~15× more voxels than 2.5 mm → much longer calc. Under-sampling small structures (optic nerve, cochlea) with coarse grid → inaccurate DVH.

eclipse.q: CALC: Grid resolution 2.5 mm. Structure is 4 mm diameter sphere. How many voxels sample the structure diameter?
eclipse.a: ~1.6 voxels — severely under-sampled
c: ~1.6 voxels — severely under-sampled
5 voxels — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
10 voxels — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
25 voxels — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
eclipse.e: 4 mm / 2.5 mm = 1.6 voxels across diameter. DVH will be highly inaccurate. Rule: structure should span ≥5 voxels in each dimension for reliable DVH. Need ≤0.8 mm grid for this 4 mm structure.

eclipse.q: CT-to-density calibration curve (HU-ρe table): how often should it be verified?
eclipse.a: At CT scanner commissioning, after tube replacement, and annually
c: Only at TPS commissioning once — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
At CT scanner commissioning, after tube replacement, and annually
Never — it's permanent — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
Daily — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
eclipse.e: using phantom with known density inserts. HU values depend on kVp, filtration, reconstruction. Changes after tube replacement or major service. Incorrect curve → wrong density → wrong dose. CATPHAN or CIRS phantom with known electron density inserts used for verification.

eclipse.q: Deformable image registration (DIR) in TPS: primary clinical applications?
eclipse.a: Dose accumulation across fractions, contour propagation for adaptive RT, and multi-modality registration
c: Only rigid alignment — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Dose accumulation across fractions, contour propagation for adaptive RT, and multi-modality registration
Only image subtraction — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Not used in TPS — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
eclipse.e: DIR: non-rigid mapping between image sets. (1) Propagate contours from planning CT to re-sim CT (saves re-contouring time). (2) Map and accumulate dose across fractions (adaptive RT). (3) Register MRI/PET to CT. QA of DIR accuracy is critical — deformation vector field (DVF) must be validated.

eclipse.q: DIR validation: how does a physicist verify deformable registration quality?
eclipse.a: Landmark comparison, known deformation phantoms, Jacobian determinant analysis (no folding), and contour propagation agreement
c: Visual inspection of overlaid images by an experienced physicist is sufficient for clinical use
Landmark comparison, known deformation phantoms, Jacobian determinant analysis (no folding), and contour propagation agreement
No QA is needed — modern DIR algorithms are validated by the vendor before clinical release
Only Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) metric is required, comparing propagated to manual contours
eclipse.e: Jacobian <0 = topology violation (folding). Landmark TRE (Target Registration Error) on identifiable points. AAPM TG-132: recommends multi-metric approach. Commercial phantoms (ImSimQA, DIRART) with known deformations. Critical for dose accumulation accuracy.

eclipse.q: PTV margin recipe (ICRU 83): what two components are combined?
eclipse.a: CTV-to-PTV margin accounts for both systematic (Σ) and random (σ) setup errors
c: Only systematic errors — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Only random errors — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
CTV-to-PTV margin accounts for both systematic (Σ) and random (σ) setup errors
Only organ motion — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
eclipse.e: Van Herk: M = 2.5Σ + 0.7σ ensures 90% of patients get ≥95% CTV dose. Σ (systematic) dominates because it shifts dose distribution consistently. σ (random) blurs dose — less impact. Internal margin (IM) for organ motion added to get ITV where applicable.

eclipse.q: Beam model commissioning (any TPS): minimum data set per IAEA TRS-430 / TG-106?
eclipse.a: PDDs, profiles at multiple depths and field sizes, output factors, wedge factors, MLC data
c: Only central axis PDD — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Only output factors — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
PDDs, profiles at multiple depths and field sizes, output factors, wedge factors, MLC data
Only one field size needed — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
eclipse.e: AAPM TG-106/IAEA TRS-430: PDDs (5×5 to 40×40), profiles (multiple depths), Sc/Sp factors, MLC transmission, DLG, penumbra. Eclipse: also needs diagonal profiles for AAA. Monaco: source model parameters. All vendors need absolute calibration point.

eclipse.q: Independent dose/MU verification: why is it required for every patient plan?
eclipse.a: Catches TPS errors, data entry mistakes, and algorithm limitations
c: Not required — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
Only for complex plans — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Catches TPS errors, data entry mistakes, and algorithm limitations
Only a legal requirement with no safety value
eclipse.e: second check before treatment. Independent calc: different algorithm or system (RadCalc, MUcheck, hand calc) verifies TPS output. Catches: wrong energy, wrong SSD, CT curve error, optimiser artifacts. AAPM TG-114: ±3-5% agreement expected for 3DCRT; VMAT needs measurement-based QA.

eclipse.q: CALC: Independent MU check: TPS says 250 MU for 200 cGy at isocentre. Independent calc gives 243 MU. Percentage difference?
eclipse.a: 2.8% — within tolerance
c: 0.28% during the planning stage
2.8% — within tolerance
7.0% during routine clinical operations
28% for therapeutic applications
eclipse.e: (250-243)/250 × 100 = 2.8%. TG-114 tolerance for 3DCRT: ±3-5%. This passes. If >5%: investigate TPS setup, algorithm differences, or data entry error.

eclipse.q: Bolus in TPS: how should it be contoured?
eclipse.a: As a separate structure with HU override to water-equivalent density, placed directly on skin surface with no air gap
c: No need to contour bolus — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
As part of the body contour, following current regulatory and professional standards
As a separate structure with HU override to water-equivalent density, placed directly on skin surface with no air gap
Just edit the CT image — no additional physics considerations or verification steps are needed
eclipse.e: Critical: air gap between bolus and skin on CT (always present due to imperfect contact) must be either filled by extending bolus structure or separately overridden. Even 2-3 mm air gap reduces surface dose significantly. Verify with measurement.

eclipse.q: Structure set QA before planning: what should the physicist verify?
eclipse.a: No overlapping/conflicting structures, correct density overrides, body contour integrity, and target/OAR naming per institutional protocol
c: Nothing — contours are the physician's responsibility, following current regulatory and professional standards
Only target volume — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
No overlapping/conflicting structures, correct density overrides, body contour integrity, and target/OAR naming per institutional protocol
Only check after planning — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
eclipse.e: Structure errors propagate to plan: overlaps cause dose calc errors, missing body contour causes algorithm crash, wrong density override (e.g., couch not overridden) → wrong dose. TG-263 naming convention ensures consistency across multi-vendor systems.

eclipse.q: TG-263 structure naming convention purpose?
eclipse.a: Standardised nomenclature for structures across all TPS vendors to enable data sharing, analytics, and automation
c: Only for Eclipse — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Standardised nomenclature for structures across all TPS vendors to enable data sharing, analytics, and automation
Optional cosmetic preference, following current regulatory and professional standards
Only for clinical trials — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
eclipse.e: TG-263: e.g., 'SpinalCord' not 'cord', 'SC', 'Spinal_Cord'. Enables: cross-institutional plan comparison, registry data, AI training data, automated plan checks. Essential in multi-vendor environments. Increasingly adopted internationally.

eclipse.q: Adaptive radiotherapy in TPS: online vs offline approaches?
eclipse.a: Online: reoptimise during treatment fraction (MR-linac, Ethos). Offline: replan between fractions based on new imaging
c: Same workflow — both involve replanning between fractions based on new imaging data
Online: reoptimise during treatment fraction (MR-linac, Ethos). Offline: replan between fractions based on new imaging
Only online adaptive exists in current clinical practice — offline is a historical approach
Only offline adaptive is clinically validated — online remains purely experimental research
eclipse.e: Online adaptive: daily re-imaging → auto-contour → reoptimise → new plan within minutes (Elekta Unity, ViewRay, Varian Ethos). Offline: re-sim CT after several fractions → manual replan → implement next day. Online more responsive but requires fast QA workflows.

eclipse.q: Electron Monte Carlo in TPS: why is MC preferred over pencil beam for electrons?
eclipse.a: Electrons scatter widely — pencil beam overestimates dose uniformity in heterogeneous tissue, especially near bone/air
c: No significant difference — pencil beam and MC agree within 1% for all electron treatments
Electrons scatter widely — pencil beam overestimates dose uniformity in heterogeneous tissue, especially near bone/air
MC is primarily faster than pencil beam for electrons, with identical accuracy in all geometries
Pencil beam is more accurate for electrons because MC cannot model electron scatter properly
eclipse.e: Electron PB: assumes small-angle scatter → fails at bone edges, air cavities, oblique surfaces. MC: tracks each electron → accurate at all interfaces. Eclipse eMC, Monaco XVMC, RayStation MC all support electron MC. Critical for chest wall, ear, nose treatments.

eclipse.q: VMAT optimisation: what is a control point?
eclipse.a: A gantry angle at which MLC position, jaw position, dose rate, and gantry speed are defined — interpolated between points
c: A patient setup mark — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
A QA measurement point — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
A gantry angle at which MLC position, jaw position, dose rate, and gantry speed are defined — interpolated between points
A dose constraint — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
eclipse.e: Typical VMAT: 2-4° spacing → 90-180 control points per arc. At each: MLC positions, jaw settings, cumulative MU defined. Linac interpolates between control points during delivery. More control points = better modulation but longer optimisation.

eclipse.q: Plan sum in TPS: when is rigid summation acceptable vs when is deformable dose accumulation needed?
eclipse.a: Rigid: same anatomy/position (e.g., sequential boost). Deformable: anatomy changed (e.g., re-plan, different treatment course, organ motion)
c: Always use rigid summation regardless of anatomy changes — simpler and more reliable
Always use deformable registration for every plan sum — rigid summation is never appropriate
Rigid: same anatomy/position (e.g., sequential boost). Deformable: anatomy changed (e.g., re-plan, different treatment course, organ motion)
Never sum plans from different treatment courses — each course must be evaluated independently
eclipse.e: Rigid sum: assumes perfect geometric correspondence — valid only if same CT, same setup. If patient re-simulated: anatomy shifted → rigid sum places dose in wrong anatomical location → deformable registration needed. Uncertainty in DIR propagates to summed dose.

eclipse.q: CALC: VMAT plan: 2 arcs, 356° each, 2° control point spacing. Total control points?
eclipse.a: ~358 control points
c: ~90 in the radiation therapy context
~178 throughout the treatment course
~358 control points
~720 in modern radiation oncology
eclipse.e: Per arc: 356°/2° + 1 = 179 control points. Two arcs: 2 × 179 = 358. Each control point stores MLC positions, cumulative MU, gantry angle. More control points allow finer modulation.

eclipse.q: Couch modelling in TPS: why is it essential for VMAT?
eclipse.a: Posterior beams traverse the couch
c: Cosmetic only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Posterior beams traverse the couch
Only for electrons
Only for prone patients
eclipse.e: unmodelled couch causes ~2-5% dose error for posterior fields. Treatment couch: carbon fibre rails + foam core. Attenuates ~2-5% depending on angle and couch structure. All modern TPS include vendor-specific couch models. Must be validated: measure transmission, model in TPS, verify agreement.

eclipse.q: Auto-planning (e.g., Pinnacle Auto-Planning, Eclipse RapidPlan, RayStation ML): what is the physicist's role?
eclipse.a: Validate the model/template, verify each auto-generated plan meets clinical goals, and maintain the knowledge base
c: No role — fully autonomous — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
Only press the button — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Validate the model/template, verify each auto-generated plan meets clinical goals, and maintain the knowledge base
Only for simple cases — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
eclipse.e: Auto-planning tools predict achievable DVH from patient anatomy using prior plan database. Physicist: (1) curates training plans, (2) validates model predictions vs clinical expectations, (3) reviews each plan for outliers, (4) maintains model as practice evolves.

eclipse.q: Plan complexity metrics: modulation complexity score (MCS), total MU, and small aperture percentage predict?
eclipse.a: Deliverability and QA gamma pass rate
c: Only treatment time
Deliverability and QA gamma pass rate
Only patient dose — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Nothing clinically useful
eclipse.e: highly complex plans are more likely to fail PSQA. High complexity: many small irregular segments, high MU, frequent MLC motion. Correlates with lower gamma pass rates, higher sensitivity to MLC errors, longer delivery time. Used in AI-based virtual QA to flag plans needing measurement.

eclipse.q: TPS commissioning end-to-end test: what does it verify?
eclipse.a: Entire chain from CT scan
c: Only dose calculation
Only CT calibration
Entire chain from CT scan
Only MLC accuracy
eclipse.e: contouring → planning → dose calculation → delivery → measurement agrees within tolerance. Anthropomorphic phantom (e.g., CIRS, StereoPHAN): CT scan → contour → plan → calculate → deliver to phantom → measure (film, TLD, ion chamber). Verifies complete workflow. Required at commissioning and after major TPS/linac upgrades. TG-53, TG-119 protocols.

eclipse.q: What does TPS stand for in radiation therapy?
eclipse.a: Treatment Planning System
c: Treatment Planning System
Thermal Processing Software
Total Patient Scanning
Therapeutic Procedure Standard
eclipse.e: TPS stands for Treatment Planning System. It is specialised computer software used by medical physicists and dosimetrists to design radiation treatment plans that deliver the prescribed dose to the tumour while minimising dose to healthy tissues.

eclipse.q: What medical images are most commonly used as the basis for creating a radiation treatment plan?
eclipse.a: CT (Computed Tomography) scans
c: Standard photographs of the patient
Ultrasound images only
CT (Computed Tomography) scans
Blood test results
eclipse.e: CT scans are the primary imaging modality for treatment planning because they provide detailed anatomical information and electron density data needed for accurate dose calculations. MRI or PET scans may also be fused with the CT for additional information.

eclipse.q: What is a treatment plan in radiation therapy?
eclipse.a: A detailed design of how radiation will be delivered to treat the patient's tumour
c: A schedule of the patient's clinic appointments in the treatment process
A detailed design of how radiation will be delivered to treat the patient's tumour
A list of medications the patient must take during the planning stage
A report of the patient's blood counts within the treatment system
eclipse.e: A treatment plan is a comprehensive design that specifies beam arrangements, dose distributions, and delivery parameters to ensure the prescribed radiation dose is delivered accurately to the tumour while keeping doses to surrounding healthy tissues as low as possible.

eclipse.q: What is a dose-volume histogram (DVH) used for in treatment planning?
eclipse.a: To graphically summarise the radiation dose received by structures of interest
c: To measure the patient's blood pressure during treatment
To graphically summarise the radiation dose received by structures of interest
To display the patient's treatment schedule
To calculate the cost of the treatment
eclipse.e: A DVH is a graphical tool that plots the volume of a structure (tumour or organ) against the dose it receives. It helps planners and physicians evaluate whether the plan meets the prescribed dose goals and organ-at-risk constraints.

eclipse.q: In treatment planning, what are organs at risk (OARs)?
eclipse.a: Healthy organs near the tumour that must be protected from excessive radiation
c: Organs that have already been surgically removed
The tumour and all its extensions in radiation therapy practice
Healthy organs near the tumour that must be protected from excessive radiation
Organs that are not visible on CT scans based on protocol requirements
eclipse.e: Organs at risk (OARs) are normal, healthy tissues and organs located near the target volume that are sensitive to radiation. Treatment plans are designed to limit the dose to these structures to avoid serious side effects.

eclipse.q: CALC: In Eclipse, AcurosXB calculates lung SBRT PTV D95 = 48 Gy/4 fx. Recalculation with AAA gives D95 = 52 Gy. The actual delivered dose is closer to which value, and why?
eclipse.a: 48 Gy (AcurosXB) — solves LBTE accurately in low density
c: 52 Gy (AAA) — more clinical experience
50 Gy — average both algorithms
48 Gy (AcurosXB) — solves LBTE accurately in low density
Neither — Monte Carlo required for lung
eclipse.e: AcurosXB solves the Linear Boltzmann Transport Equation, accurately modeling photon and electron transport in heterogeneous media including low-density lung. AAA uses a superposition-convolution approach that overestimates dose in low-density regions because it does not properly account for loss of lateral electronic equilibrium. The 4 Gy (8%) discrepancy is typical for lung SBRT. Monte Carlo validation studies confirm AcurosXB accuracy to within 1-2% in lung.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse VMAT optimization for a head-and-neck plan, the optimizer converges to a solution with MU significantly higher than expected. The plan has acceptable DVH metrics but high modulation complexity. What is the most concerning clinical consequence?
eclipse.a: Increased MLC transmission dose and delivery sensitivity to errors
c: Longer treatment time only
Increased MLC transmission dose and delivery sensitivity to errors
Higher skin dose from electron contamination
Reduced target coverage due to tongue-and-groove effect
eclipse.e: High-MU, highly modulated plans increase the proportion of dose delivered through MLC transmission and leakage (approximately 1.4% per MU for Millennium MLC). This raises the whole-body low-dose bath and makes delivery more sensitive to MLC positioning errors, since small leaf offsets cause proportionally larger dose errors in narrow apertures. Plan complexity metrics and MU constraints during optimization should be evaluated.

eclipse.q: An Eclipse plan uses AAA with a 2.5 mm calculation grid for a 2 cm brain SRS target. Recalculation with 1.0 mm grid shows D95 decreases by 3.2%. What does this indicate and what grid size should be used?
eclipse.a: 2.5 mm grid is too coarse — use ≤1.5 mm for SRS targets <3 cm
c: 2.5 mm is adequate — 3.2% is within uncertainty
2.5 mm grid is too coarse — use ≤1.5 mm for SRS targets <3 cm
Always use 1.0 mm — it is universally more accurate
Grid size does not affect dose accuracy, only display resolution
eclipse.e: A 3.2% change between grid sizes indicates the coarser grid inadequately samples the dose gradient within and around the small target. TG-101 recommends grids of 2 mm or less for SBRT, and ideally 1.0-1.5 mm for SRS targets smaller than 3 cm. The dose difference arises because coarse voxels average across steep dose gradients, artificially inflating coverage metrics. A 1.0 mm grid is not always necessary for larger targets and significantly increases calculation time.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, a prostate VMAT plan optimized with PO (Photon Optimizer) shows excellent target coverage but D2cm³ to rectum is 2 Gy above the constraint. Adding a rectum optimization objective does not improve it further. What advanced technique is most likely to resolve this?
eclipse.a: Add a tuning ring structure with dose falloff objective
c: Increase number of arcs from 2 to 4
Add a tuning ring structure with dose falloff objective
Switch from VMAT to step-and-shoot IMRT
Reduce PTV margin to 3 mm
eclipse.e: A tuning ring (annular structure surrounding the PTV with a dose falloff objective) provides the optimizer with an explicit geometric handle to steepen the dose gradient between PTV and rectum. This is more effective than simply adding rectum objectives because it controls the intermediate dose region. Adding arcs increases degrees of freedom but does not specifically address the gradient direction. Reducing margins is a clinical decision, not an optimization technique.

eclipse.q: CALC: An Eclipse AcurosXB calculation reports dose-to-medium (Dm) for a bone structure with mean dose 45.2 Gy. The Dm-to-Dw conversion factor at this beam energy is approximately 1.04 for cortical bone. What is the dose-to-water value?
eclipse.e: Dw = Dm × conversion factor = 45.2 × 1.04 = 47.008 ≈ 47.0 Gy. AcurosXB natively calculates dose-to-medium, which can differ from dose-to-water by 1-5% in bone and dense tissues due to differences in mass energy-absorption coefficients. Eclipse offers a Dm-to-Dw conversion option. For soft tissues the difference is less than 1%, but for bone or dental hardware, the choice significantly affects reported DVH metrics.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is the fundamental difference between AAA and AcurosXB dose calculation algorithms?
eclipse.a: AAA uses convolution/superposition of pre-calculated kernels while AcurosXB solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation
c: AAA is Monte Carlo-based while AcurosXB uses pencil beam convolution
AAA uses convolution/superposition of pre-calculated kernels while AcurosXB solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation
Both algorithms are identical but AcurosXB uses a finer calculation grid
AAA solves the transport equation while AcurosXB uses kernel superposition
eclipse.e: AAA (Analytical Anisotropic Algorithm) superimposes pre-computed dose kernels scaled by density. AcurosXB directly solves the LBTE, explicitly modelling photon and electron transport in heterogeneous media, providing superior accuracy at interfaces (lung, bone, air).

eclipse.q: What does the Normal Tissue Objective (NTO) function in Eclipse VMAT optimisation control?
eclipse.a: Limits dose fall-off gradient outside the PTV to reduce integral body dose
c: Forces the optimiser to achieve exact DVH constraints on named OARs
Limits dose fall-off gradient outside the PTV to reduce integral body dose
Controls the maximum MU per arc for delivery efficiency
Sets the calculation grid resolution for the optimisation phase
eclipse.e: The NTO function in Eclipse defines how rapidly dose should fall off outside the PTV. It controls dose conformity and reduces hot spots in normal tissue. Parameters include start dose, end dose, fall-off, and priority. Essential for VMAT plan quality.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse plan evaluation, what does the Conformity Index (CI) = reference isodose volume / PTV volume indicate when CI > 1?
eclipse.a: The reference isodose extends beyond the PTV, irradiating some normal tissue
c: The plan is perfectly conformal to the target volume
The PTV is underdosed and coverage is inadequate
The reference isodose extends beyond the PTV, irradiating some normal tissue
The beam energy is too high causing excessive penumbra
eclipse.e: CI = V_RI/V_PTV. CI = 1 is ideal (perfect conformity). CI > 1 means the prescription isodose volume exceeds the PTV, indicating normal tissue receiving prescription dose. CI < 1 means incomplete PTV coverage. ICRU 83 defines CI; values of 1.0-1.2 are generally acceptable.

eclipse.q: CALC: Eclipse AcurosXB reports dose-to-medium for a lung tumour (density 0.3 g/cm³). If dose-to-medium is 200 cGy, estimate the dose-to-water at the same point using the ratio of mass energy absorption coefficients (μen/ρ)water/(μen/ρ)lung ≈ 1.05.
eclipse.a: 210 cGy
c: 190 cGy
200 cGy
210 cGy
220 cGy
eclipse.e: Dose-to-water = Dose-to-medium × (μen/ρ)water/(μen/ρ)medium = 200 × 1.05 = 210 cGy. AcurosXB can report either dose-to-medium or dose-to-water. The difference is significant in non-water-like tissues (bone, lung).

eclipse.q: In Eclipse VMAT multi-criteria optimisation (MCO), what does navigating the Pareto surface allow the planner to do?
eclipse.a: Explore trade-offs between competing objectives in real-time without re-optimising
c: Automatically select the globally optimal plan without planner input
Explore trade-offs between competing objectives in real-time without re-optimising
Bypass dose calculation and directly generate deliverable MLC sequences
Compare plans from different algorithm engines simultaneously
eclipse.e: MCO generates a set of Pareto-optimal plans. The planner interactively adjusts sliders representing different objectives (target coverage vs OAR sparing). Movement along the Pareto surface reveals trade-offs: improving one objective necessarily worsens another. No re-optimisation needed.

eclipse.q: When commissioning AcurosXB in Eclipse, why is the dose calculation grid resolution critical for small-field SBRT plans?
eclipse.a: Coarse grids (>2.5 mm) cause dose averaging that underestimates steep gradients and hot/cold spots in small targets
c: Grid resolution has no effect on dose accuracy for any field size in the healthcare environment
Coarse grids (>2.5 mm) cause dose averaging that underestimates steep gradients and hot/cold spots in small targets
Finer grids always increase dose to the target by exactly 3% in the treatment process
Grid resolution only matters for photon beams above 10 MV for radiation dose management
eclipse.e: For SBRT targets (often 1-5 cm), a 2.5 mm or coarser grid has too few voxels across the target to resolve dose gradients. TG-101 recommends ≤2 mm grid for SBRT. AcurosXB with 1-1.5 mm grid better resolves penumbra and heterogeneity effects.

eclipse.q: What is the primary difference between AAA and AcurosXB dose calculation algorithms in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: AAA uses convolution-superposition while AcurosXB solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation
c: AAA uses Monte Carlo sampling while AcurosXB uses pencil beam convolution methods
AAA uses convolution-superposition while AcurosXB solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation
AAA uses deterministic transport while AcurosXB applies superposition-convolution kernels
AAA uses ray-tracing correction while AcurosXB uses collapsed cone approximation methods
eclipse.e: AAA (Analytical Anisotropic Algorithm) is a convolution-superposition method using pre-calculated Monte Carlo kernels, while AcurosXB directly solves the LBTE for more accurate heterogeneity handling.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what does the Conformity Index (CI) measure in plan evaluation?
eclipse.a: The ratio of the prescription isodose volume to the planning target volume
c: The ratio of the prescription isodose volume to the planning target volume
The ratio of the maximum point dose to the mean dose within the target
The ratio of the monitor units delivered to the expected calculated value
The ratio of the organ-at-risk mean dose to the target prescribed dose
eclipse.e: CI = V(prescription isodose)/V(PTV). A CI of 1.0 indicates perfect conformity. Values >1 indicate the prescription isodose extends beyond the PTV. ICRU 83 recommends reporting CI for all IMRT plans.

eclipse.q: CALC: Given D2% = 48.5 Gy, D98% = 44.2 Gy, and D50% = 46.8 Gy for a PTV, what is the Homogeneity Index (HI)?
eclipse.e: HI = (D2% - D98%)/D50% = (48.5 - 44.2)/46.8 = 4.3/46.8 = 0.092. Lower HI values indicate better dose homogeneity. Typical VMAT plans achieve HI between 0.05 and 0.15.

eclipse.q: What effect does reducing the dose calculation grid size from 5 mm to 2.5 mm have in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: Improves spatial resolution of dose distribution but significantly increases computation time
c: Reduces calculation accuracy near heterogeneities but decreases the total computation time
Improves spatial resolution of dose distribution but significantly increases computation time
Has minimal impact on plan quality but substantially reduces the file storage size
Increases dose to organs at risk but improves overall target coverage significantly
eclipse.e: Smaller grid sizes improve dose resolution, especially near tissue interfaces and small structures. Going from 5 mm to 2.5 mm increases calculation points by ~8x (2^3), significantly increasing computation time. A 2.5 mm grid is recommended for SBRT/SRS.

eclipse.q: In VMAT optimization in Eclipse, what is the purpose of the Normal Tissue Objective (NTO)?
eclipse.a: Controls dose falloff outside the target to reduce dose to surrounding normal tissues globally
c: Forces the optimizer to increase target coverage by adjusting leaf sequencing patterns
Controls dose falloff outside the target to reduce dose to surrounding normal tissues globally
Sets maximum dose constraints for individually contoured organs at risk structures
Adjusts the monitor unit efficiency by modifying the gantry rotation speed profile
eclipse.e: NTO is a global optimization objective in Eclipse that manages dose conformity and falloff around the PTV. It penalises dose outside the target proportional to distance, helping create conformal dose distributions without needing explicit constraints on every tissue.

eclipse.q: Which structure type in Eclipse allows dose-volume histogram analysis but does not influence optimization?
eclipse.a: An evaluation structure that is designated as a non-optimizable volume
c: An avoidance structure explicitly assigned as a high-priority hard constraint
A target structure that is given the lowest possible optimization priority
An evaluation structure that is designated as a non-optimizable volume
A support structure used only for image registration and fusion purposes
eclipse.e: Evaluation structures (or 'evaluation-only' types) in Eclipse can be used to review DVH metrics and assess dose but are excluded from the optimization process. This is useful for reviewing dose to composite structures.

eclipse.q: CALC: A 4-field box plan has individual beam weights of 35%, 25%, 20%, and 20%. If the total plan delivers 180 cGy, what dose does the anterior beam (35%) contribute?
eclipse.a: 63 cGy
c: 45 cGy
63 cGy
72 cGy
54 cGy
eclipse.e: Anterior beam dose = 180 x 0.35 = 63 cGy. Beam weighting is commonly used in 3D-CRT to optimise dose distribution. Unequal weighting shifts the high-dose region towards the more heavily weighted beams.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is the purpose of the PRV (Planning organ-at-Risk Volume)?
eclipse.a: Provides a margin around the OAR to account for geometric uncertainties in organ position
c: Defines the biological effective dose limit that applies to each organ at risk
Provides a margin around the OAR to account for geometric uncertainties in organ position
Creates a cropped structure to exclude target overlap regions from dose analysis
Establishes the minimum acceptable dose coverage for organs near the target volume
eclipse.e: As defined in ICRU 83, the PRV adds a margin to OARs similar to how PTV margins are added to the CTV. This accounts for organ motion and setup uncertainty. Constraints are typically applied to the PRV rather than the OAR alone.

eclipse.q: How does AcurosXB handle tissue heterogeneities differently compared to pencil beam algorithms?
eclipse.a: AcurosXB explicitly models lateral electron transport in heterogeneous media using transport equations
c: AcurosXB applies a simple density scaling correction factor to water-based dose measurements
AcurosXB ignores tissue composition and calculates dose based purely on electron density
AcurosXB explicitly models lateral electron transport in heterogeneous media using transport equations
AcurosXB uses empirical lookup tables derived from phantom measurements in heterogeneous slabs
eclipse.e: AcurosXB solves the LBTE which explicitly accounts for lateral electron scatter in heterogeneous media. Pencil beam algorithms use 1D density scaling which fails at tissue-air and tissue-bone interfaces, particularly in lung and head-and-neck cases.

eclipse.q: What is the recommended dose calculation grid size for stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) plans in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: 1.0 to 2.5 mm grid size for accurate small-field dose modelling
c: 1.0 to 2.5 mm grid size for accurate small-field dose modelling
5.0 to 7.5 mm grid size to improve calculation speed efficiency
3.0 to 4.0 mm grid size matching the standard CT slice thickness
8.0 to 10.0 mm grid size optimised for large treatment field sizes
eclipse.e: SBRT involves small fields and steep dose gradients requiring fine calculation grids (1-2.5 mm). Larger grids can average out dose variations leading to inaccurate DVH metrics and underestimation of maximum doses in small targets.

eclipse.q: CALC: The Gradient Index (GI) is defined as V50%/V100%. If V50% = 180 cc and V100% = 45 cc, what is the GI?
eclipse.e: GI = V50%/V100% = 180/45 = 4.0. The Gradient Index quantifies dose falloff around the target. Lower GI values indicate steeper gradients and better sparing of surrounding tissue. Typical SRS plans achieve GI of 2.5-4.0.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse VMAT optimization, what does the term 'intermediate dose' spillage refer to?
eclipse.a: Moderate dose regions (50-80% isodose) extending into normal tissue between or around targets
c: Moderate dose regions (50-80% isodose) extending into normal tissue between or around targets
High dose hotspots exceeding 110% of prescription dose located within the target volume
Low dose bath below 20% of prescription spread uniformly across the entire patient body
Dose deposited in the buildup region between the skin surface and the depth of maximum
eclipse.e: Intermediate dose spillage is the spread of 50-80% isodose levels into normal tissue. It is especially problematic in multi-target SRS/SBRT plans where dose bridges can form between targets. The NTO and ring structures help control this.

eclipse.q: What is the primary advantage of using RapidPlan (knowledge-based planning) in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: Uses statistical models from prior plans to predict achievable DVH objectives for new patients
c: Eliminates the need for physician-defined dose constraints on all organs at risk
Uses statistical models from prior plans to predict achievable DVH objectives for new patients
Guarantees the optimal plan quality by applying machine learning to beam arrangements
Replaces manual contouring of target volumes using atlas-based segmentation algorithms
eclipse.e: RapidPlan uses a library of high-quality prior plans to build DVH estimation models. For each new patient, it predicts achievable OAR DVHs based on geometric relationships, providing patient-specific optimization objectives that reduce planning time and variability.

eclipse.q: CALC: A plan normalization is set so that 95% of PTV receives the prescribed dose. If the prescription is 60 Gy in 30 fractions and D95% is currently at 57 Gy, by what percentage must the plan be scaled?
eclipse.a: 5.3% increase
c: 3.8% increase
5.3% increase
7.1% increase
4.5% increase
eclipse.e: Scale factor = 60/57 = 1.0526, meaning a 5.3% increase is needed. Plan normalization adjusts the overall dose distribution uniformly. One must verify that this scaling does not push OAR doses beyond tolerance after renormalization.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is the difference between 'dose to medium' and 'dose to water' reporting modes in AcurosXB?
eclipse.a: Dose to medium uses actual tissue stopping powers while dose to water converts back to water equivalence
c: Dose to medium ignores electron density while dose to water uses CT Hounsfield unit conversions
Dose to medium uses actual tissue stopping powers while dose to water converts back to water equivalence
Dose to medium applies to photon beams only while dose to water applies to all particle types
Dose to medium is used for plan optimization while dose to water is restricted to final reporting
eclipse.e: AcurosXB can report dose to the actual medium (Dm) or convert to dose to water (Dw) using stopping power ratios. For most soft tissues the difference is <1%, but in bone it can reach 3-5%. South African protocols should specify which reporting mode is used.

eclipse.q: Which beam arrangement is typically most suitable for a prostate VMAT plan in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: Single or dual full-arc arrangement with collimator rotation to improve MLC modulation
c: Four-field box arrangement using anterior, posterior, and bilateral orthogonal beams
Single or dual full-arc arrangement with collimator rotation to improve MLC modulation
Seven equally-spaced fixed gantry beams optimised using step-and-shoot IMRT delivery
Opposed lateral beams with superior and inferior oblique boost fields added sequentially
eclipse.e: Prostate VMAT typically uses 1-2 full arcs with non-zero collimator angles (e.g., 30 and 330 degrees) to improve MLC modulation and reduce tongue-and-groove effect. This provides superior OAR sparing compared to 3D-CRT box techniques.

eclipse.q: What is the purpose of using a ring structure (avoidance ring) around the PTV during IMRT/VMAT optimization?
eclipse.a: Acts as a dose shaping structure to control dose conformity and gradient near the target boundary
c: Serves as a biological surrogate to estimate the normal tissue complication probability value
Acts as a dose shaping structure to control dose conformity and gradient near the target boundary
Functions as a verification structure for validating the accuracy of dose calculation grids
Provides an automatic expansion margin to account for patient setup positioning uncertainty
eclipse.e: Ring structures (typically 1-3 cm annular regions around the PTV) are assigned optimization objectives to force rapid dose falloff. This controls conformity and reduces intermediate dose spillage without impacting OAR-specific constraints.

eclipse.q: CALC: In a step-and-shoot IMRT plan with 7 beams, each beam averages 6 segments with 15 MU per segment. What is the approximate total MU for the plan?
eclipse.a: 630 MU total
c: 420 MU total
525 MU total
630 MU total
840 MU total
eclipse.e: Total MU = 7 beams x 6 segments x 15 MU = 630 MU. Step-and-shoot IMRT typically delivers 2-5 times more MU than a comparable 3D-CRT plan. Higher MU increases peripheral dose from leakage radiation.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what does the DVH metric V20Gy represent for the lungs in a thoracic treatment plan?
eclipse.a: The percentage of total lung volume receiving 20 Gy or more during treatment
c: The percentage of total lung volume receiving 20 Gy or more during treatment
The volume in cubic centimetres of lung tissue receiving exactly 20 Gy dose
The maximum dose in Gray delivered to twenty percent of the total lung volume
The average dose in Gray calculated over the superior twenty percent of the lung
eclipse.e: V20Gy is a critical DVH metric for lung toxicity prediction. QUANTEC guidelines recommend V20Gy < 30-35% for conventional fractionation to keep pneumonitis risk acceptable. South African oncology centres routinely use this constraint.

eclipse.q: What is the Multi-Resolution Dose Calculation (MRDC) approach used in Eclipse optimization?
eclipse.a: Uses a coarser grid during initial optimization iterations then refines to the final grid resolution
c: Applies different calculation algorithms simultaneously and averages results for final dose
Uses a coarser grid during initial optimization iterations then refines to the final grid resolution
Calculates dose separately for each energy component and then sums the individual results
Runs parallel dose calculations on multiple processor cores using identical grid resolutions
eclipse.e: MRDC speeds up optimization by starting with a coarse calculation grid (e.g., 5 mm) during early iterations when the solution is far from optimal, then switching to the final resolution (e.g., 2.5 mm) for refinement. This reduces computation time significantly.

eclipse.q: CALC: A treatment plan shows Dmax = 65 Gy and Dprescription = 60 Gy. What is the maximum dose as a percentage of the prescription?
eclipse.a: 108.3% of prescription
c: 104.2% of prescription
106.7% of prescription
108.3% of prescription
112.5% of prescription
eclipse.e: Percentage = (65/60) x 100 = 108.3%. ICRU 83 recommends reporting hot spots and suggests keeping Dmax below 107% for conventional 3D-CRT, though IMRT/VMAT plans may have higher hotspots within the target.

eclipse.q: How does the Photon Optimizer (PO) in Eclipse handle multi-criteria optimization (MCO)?
eclipse.a: Generates a set of Pareto-optimal plans allowing planners to navigate trade-offs interactively
c: Generates a set of Pareto-optimal plans allowing planners to navigate trade-offs interactively
Applies a single weighted sum of all objectives to produce one deterministic optimal solution
Uses random sampling of objective weights to explore the solution space stochastically
Requires sequential manual adjustment of each constraint until a feasible plan is found
eclipse.e: MCO in Eclipse generates multiple Pareto-optimal plans representing different trade-off solutions. Planners can interactively adjust sliders to navigate between plans, exploring how improving one OAR affects others. This improves plan quality and planning efficiency.

eclipse.q: What is the recommended action when a DVH constraint for the spinal cord is marginally exceeded in an IMRT plan?
eclipse.a: Increase the cord constraint priority, add a PRV margin, and re-optimise the plan accordingly
c: Accept the plan as clinically adequate since IMRT constraints are only advisory guidelines
Increase the cord constraint priority, add a PRV margin, and re-optimise the plan accordingly
Remove the cord from the optimization entirely and rely on beam arrangement for sparing
Reduce the prescription dose uniformly until the spinal cord constraint is finally satisfied
eclipse.e: Serial organs like the spinal cord have strict dose limits. If the constraint is marginally exceeded, increasing priority, using a PRV (typically 3-5 mm), and re-optimising is preferred. Simply reducing prescription dose compromises target coverage unnecessarily.

eclipse.q: CALC: A lung SBRT plan has a PTV volume of 25 cc. The volume enclosed by the prescription isodose (50 Gy) is 32 cc. What is the Conformity Index?
eclipse.e: CI = V(prescription isodose)/V(PTV) = 32/25 = 1.28. A CI between 1.0 and 1.5 is generally acceptable for SBRT. Values closer to 1.0 indicate better conformity. RTOG protocols specify CI limits for different target sizes.

eclipse.q: What is the primary purpose of the CT-to-density calibration curve (HU-to-density table) in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: Converts Hounsfield Unit values to electron or mass density for accurate dose calculation
c: Converts Hounsfield Unit values to electron or mass density for accurate dose calculation
Corrects CT image artefacts caused by metallic implants within the scanned patient volume
Maps image pixel values to anatomical structures for automatic contouring segmentation
Calibrates the CT scanner output to ensure consistent image quality across all protocols
eclipse.e: The HU-to-density curve is fundamental to dose calculation. Each CT voxel's HU value is converted to relative electron density (for AAA) or mass density (for AcurosXB). This curve must be validated for each CT scanner used in treatment planning.

eclipse.q: How does Monaco TPS differ from Eclipse in its primary dose calculation approach?
eclipse.a: Monaco uses Monte Carlo simulation as its primary dose engine while Eclipse uses deterministic methods
c: Monaco uses Monte Carlo simulation as its primary dose engine while Eclipse uses deterministic methods
Monaco uses finite element analysis for dose computation while Eclipse uses analytical convolution
Monaco calculates dose only in water equivalent paths while Eclipse accounts for heterogeneities
Monaco applies ray-tracing with empirical corrections while Eclipse uses kernel superposition
eclipse.e: Monaco (Elekta) uses a GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo dose engine (XVMC) which statistically simulates particle transport. Eclipse uses deterministic algorithms (AAA, AcurosXB). Both are widely used in South African radiotherapy departments.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is the significance of the jaw tracking feature during VMAT delivery?
eclipse.a: Dynamically adjusts jaw positions during arc delivery to reduce MLC leakage and peripheral dose
c: Dynamically adjusts jaw positions during arc delivery to reduce MLC leakage and peripheral dose
Locks the jaw positions at fixed intervals to improve mechanical stability during rotation
Automatically centres the jaw aperture on the isocentre for each control point position
Synchronises the jaw movement with gantry speed to maintain constant dose rate delivery
eclipse.e: Jaw tracking in VMAT moves the jaws to closely follow the MLC aperture outline at each control point. This reduces radiation transmitted through closed MLC leaves, lowering peripheral dose by 10-30%. This is particularly important for paediatric patients.

eclipse.q: CALC: A plan sum combines a primary plan of 46 Gy in 23 fractions with a boost plan of 14 Gy in 7 fractions. What is the total prescribed dose?
eclipse.a: 60 Gy in 30 fractions
c: 54 Gy in 27 fractions
60 Gy in 30 fractions
64 Gy in 32 fractions
56 Gy in 28 fractions
eclipse.e: Total dose = 46 + 14 = 60 Gy in 23 + 7 = 30 fractions. Plan summation in Eclipse adds dose distributions voxel-by-voxel. For accurate summation, both plans must use the same CT dataset or deformable registration must be applied.

eclipse.q: What is the primary limitation of using the Pencil Beam Convolution algorithm for lung treatment planning?
eclipse.a: Significantly overestimates dose in low-density lung tissue due to inadequate lateral scatter modelling
c: Significantly overestimates dose in low-density lung tissue due to inadequate lateral scatter modelling
Requires excessively long computation times compared to Monte Carlo simulation approaches
Cannot handle fields smaller than 5 cm due to inherent source model geometric limitations
Produces systematic underdosing of all target volumes regardless of the anatomical location
eclipse.e: Pencil beam algorithms use 1D density correction and do not properly account for lateral electron disequilibrium in lung tissue. This leads to dose overestimation in lung, particularly for small fields. AAA and AcurosXB are preferred for thoracic planning.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what does the 'dose volume' option in plan normalization allow the planner to specify?
eclipse.a: Normalises so that a specific percentage of a target volume receives the prescribed dose level
c: Sets the absolute maximum dose anywhere in the plan to equal the prescription dose exactly
Normalises so that a specific percentage of a target volume receives the prescribed dose level
Adjusts the mean dose across all structures to match the intended prescription dose value
Scales each individual beam independently until target coverage criteria are fully achieved
eclipse.e: Dose-volume normalization (e.g., 95% of PTV receives 100% of prescription) is the most common method. This aligns with ICRU 83 reporting standards. Other options include normalization to isocentre, Dmax, or mean dose.

eclipse.q: CALC: An IMRT plan has a mean heart dose of 8 Gy. The clinical goal is V25Gy < 10%. Current V25Gy is 12%. By what factor must the high-dose heart volume be reduced?
eclipse.a: Reduce by a factor of 1.2 from the current 12% to the 10% target
c: Reduce by a factor of 1.5 from the current 12% to meet the limit
Reduce by a factor of 1.2 from the current 12% to the 10% target
Reduce by a factor of 2.0 from the current 12% to achieve goal
Reduce by a factor of 1.8 from the current 12% to satisfy it
eclipse.e: Factor = 12%/10% = 1.2. The V25Gy must decrease by 20% relative to its current value. Strategies include adjusting beam angles to avoid the heart, increasing heart constraint priority, or using cardiac-sparing techniques.

eclipse.q: What is the main advantage of Pinnacle TPS's collapsed cone convolution algorithm over pencil beam methods?
eclipse.a: Better models lateral photon and electron scatter in heterogeneous tissue using polyenergetic kernels
c: Better models lateral photon and electron scatter in heterogeneous tissue using polyenergetic kernels
Provides faster calculation times by simplifying the transport equations to single energy groups
Eliminates the need for CT-based density information by using standardised tissue compositions
Uses pre-calculated water phantom measurements instead of computational kernel generation
eclipse.e: Pinnacle's collapsed cone convolution/superposition algorithm transports energy along discrete cone axes, properly accounting for tissue heterogeneities in 3D. It better handles lateral scatter than pencil beam methods, particularly at tissue-air interfaces.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is the purpose of body structure extension for patients whose anatomy extends beyond the CT scan FOV?
eclipse.a: Assigns appropriate density to unscanned tissue regions to prevent dose calculation errors at periphery
c: Increases the CT image resolution beyond the scanner's physical detector limitations automatically
Assigns appropriate density to unscanned tissue regions to prevent dose calculation errors at periphery
Corrects beam hardening artefacts that occur near the boundary of the CT imaging field of view
Extends the available contouring workspace without affecting the dose calculation accuracy
eclipse.e: When patient anatomy extends beyond the CT FOV (common in obese patients), Eclipse assigns water density to the extended body contour. Without this correction, beams passing through unscanned tissue will have incorrect attenuation calculations.

eclipse.q: CALC: A VMAT prostate plan has isocentre dose of 78 Gy normalised to 100%. If renormalised to 98% at isocentre, what is the new effective isocentre dose?
eclipse.a: 79.6 Gy at isocentre
c: 76.4 Gy at isocentre
78.0 Gy at isocentre
79.6 Gy at isocentre
81.2 Gy at isocentre
eclipse.e: If the isocentre now reads 98% and must correspond to 78 Gy, the scale factor is 100/98 = 1.0204. New isocentre dose = 78 x 1.0204 = 79.6 Gy. Renormalization uniformly scales all doses in the plan.

eclipse.q: What does the term 'convergence error' mean during IMRT/VMAT optimization in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: The optimizer has not found a stable minimum and the objective function is still changing significantly
c: The dose calculation grid is too coarse to accurately represent the treatment field boundaries
The optimizer has not found a stable minimum and the objective function is still changing significantly
The plan cannot be delivered because the MLC leaf speed exceeds the mechanical maximum limit
The gantry rotation arc has been set to an angle range incompatible with the treatment couch
