eclipse.e: Convergence errors indicate the optimization did not reach a stable solution. This may occur with conflicting objectives, insufficient iterations, or overly aggressive constraints. Increasing iterations or relaxing conflicting objectives can help resolve this.

eclipse.q: CALC: A TPS reports mean parotid dose of 28 Gy. QUANTEC recommends mean parotid < 26 Gy. By what percentage is the constraint exceeded?
eclipse.a: Exceeded by approximately 7.7% above the recommended constraint
c: Exceeded by approximately 5.2% above the recommended constraint
Exceeded by approximately 7.7% above the recommended constraint
Exceeded by approximately 9.4% above the recommended constraint
Exceeded by approximately 11.1% above the recommended value
eclipse.e: Percentage exceeded = (28 - 26)/26 x 100 = 2/26 x 100 = 7.7%. Keeping mean parotid dose below 26 Gy significantly reduces the risk of long-term xerostomia. Re-optimization with higher parotid priority may be needed.

eclipse.q: What is the purpose of using a bolus override structure in Eclipse for electron beam planning?
eclipse.a: Assigns tissue-equivalent density to a virtual surface layer to shift the dose buildup region
c: Assigns tissue-equivalent density to a virtual surface layer to shift the dose buildup region
Increases the prescribed electron beam energy to compensate for air gaps during treatment
Removes the buildup effect entirely so that maximum dose occurs at the patient skin surface
Converts the electron beam calculation to a photon beam model for superficial dose planning
eclipse.e: Bolus override structures in Eclipse assign water-equivalent density (typically 1.0 g/cc) to a surface layer, shifting the dose buildup upstream. This ensures adequate surface dose coverage for superficial targets. Physical bolus is then placed during treatment.

eclipse.q: How does Eclipse handle dose calculation for patients with metallic hip prostheses?
eclipse.a: Uses CT density override on the prosthesis structure with appropriate high-density material assignment
c: Automatically detects and removes metallic artefacts from CT dataset before dose calculation
Ignores the prosthesis entirely and assigns water-equivalent density to all structures default
Uses CT density override on the prosthesis structure with appropriate high-density material assignment
Requires a separate CT scan without the prosthesis to be fused with the original planning scan
eclipse.e: CT artefacts from metallic prostheses corrupt HU values. The planner manually contours the prosthesis and assigns an appropriate density override (e.g., titanium ~4.5 g/cc, steel ~7.8 g/cc). This improves dose calculation accuracy through and near the implant.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is the clinical significance of the D0.03cc metric for serial organs at risk?
eclipse.a: Represents the near-maximum dose to a small absolute volume, avoiding single-voxel point dose artefacts
c: Represents the near-maximum dose to a small absolute volume, avoiding single-voxel point dose artefacts
Calculates the average dose across the entire organ volume for comprehensive toxicity prediction
Measures the minimum dose received by the three most inferior slices of the contoured structure
Estimates the biological equivalent dose accounting for the fractionation scheme being employed
eclipse.e: D0.03cc is the minimum dose to the hottest 0.03 cc of a structure. For serial OARs like the spinal cord and brainstem, this is more clinically relevant than Dmax (point dose) which can be affected by single-voxel calculation noise. ICRU 83 recommends its use.

eclipse.q: CALC: A 6 MV VMAT plan calculates 450 MU. Independent MU verification gives 440 MU. What is the percentage difference?
eclipse.a: 2.3% difference between the planned and verified values
c: 1.5% difference between the planned and verified values
2.3% difference between the planned and verified values
3.1% difference between the planned and verified values
4.0% difference between the planned and verified values
eclipse.e: Percentage difference = (450 - 440)/440 x 100 = 10/440 x 100 = 2.3%. Most institutions accept MU verification within 3-5% for IMRT/VMAT. Values exceeding this threshold require investigation before treatment delivery.

eclipse.q: What is the purpose of the optimization convergence plot in Eclipse VMAT planning?
eclipse.a: Displays the objective function value versus iteration number to monitor optimization progress
c: Shows the dose-volume histogram evolution for each structure throughout the treatment course
Displays the objective function value versus iteration number to monitor optimization progress
Plots the MLC leaf positions as a function of gantry angle for each control point
Graphs the relationship between dose rate and gantry speed during planned arc delivery
eclipse.e: The convergence plot tracks the total objective function value (sum of all weighted objectives) across optimization iterations. A well-converged plan shows the curve flattening. If still decreasing steeply, more iterations may improve the plan.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is the effect of collimator angle rotation on VMAT plan quality for elongated targets?
eclipse.a: Aligns MLC leaf travel direction obliquely to the target axis for improved modulation capability
c: Has no measurable impact on plan quality and is only used for collision avoidance purposes
Reduces the total treatment delivery time by decreasing the required number of monitor units
Aligns MLC leaf travel direction obliquely to the target axis for improved modulation capability
Increases the effective field size by rotating the jaw aperture relative to the target volume
eclipse.e: For elongated targets (e.g., head-and-neck), a non-zero collimator angle (typically 10-30 degrees) ensures MLC leaves traverse the target obliquely rather than parallel to its long axis. This provides finer modulation and reduces tongue-and-groove effect.

eclipse.q: CALC: A left breast plan has mean heart dose of 3.2 Gy. If DIBH reduces heart dose by 60%, what is the estimated mean heart dose with DIBH?
eclipse.a: 1.28 Gy mean heart dose
c: 0.96 Gy mean heart dose
1.28 Gy mean heart dose
1.60 Gy mean heart dose
1.92 Gy mean heart dose
eclipse.e: DIBH dose = 3.2 x (1 - 0.60) = 3.2 x 0.40 = 1.28 Gy. DIBH is standard practice for left-sided breast radiotherapy in South Africa where equipment allows it, significantly reducing cardiac dose and long-term cardiac morbidity risk.

eclipse.q: What is the primary advantage of adaptive planning in Eclipse using structure-guided re-optimization?
eclipse.a: Accounts for anatomical changes during treatment by re-optimising on updated imaging data sets
c: Eliminates the need for daily image guidance by predicting patient anatomy from baseline scans
Accounts for anatomical changes during treatment by re-optimising on updated imaging data sets
Automates the entire treatment planning process without requiring any dosimetrist intervention
Reduces the total treatment course duration by escalating the dose per fraction adaptively
eclipse.e: Adaptive planning uses cone-beam CT or CT-on-rails to capture anatomical changes (tumour shrinkage, weight loss, organ motion). Eclipse can re-optimise the plan based on new anatomy, maintaining target coverage and OAR sparing throughout treatment.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is the difference between a hard constraint and an optimization objective in the VMAT optimizer?
eclipse.a: Hard constraints are strict limits the optimizer must satisfy while objectives are goals it tries to achieve
c: Hard constraints apply only to target volumes while objectives are reserved for organs at risk
Hard constraints are strict limits the optimizer must satisfy while objectives are goals it tries to achieve
Hard constraints use absolute dose values while objectives can only use relative dose percentages
Hard constraints are applied after optimization is complete while objectives guide the process
eclipse.e: Hard constraints define inviolable limits (e.g., Dmax spinal cord < 45 Gy). The optimizer must satisfy these first, then minimises objectives (weighted goals). Over-constraining with hard constraints can lead to infeasible solutions or poor plan quality.

eclipse.q: CALC: A cervical VMAT plan uses two arcs: Arc 1 = 280 MU and Arc 2 = 320 MU. At 600 MU/min dose rate, what is the minimum beam-on time?
eclipse.a: 60 seconds beam-on time
c: 48 seconds beam-on time
60 seconds beam-on time
75 seconds beam-on time
90 seconds beam-on time
eclipse.e: Total MU = 280 + 320 = 600 MU. Beam-on time = 600 MU / 600 MU/min = 1.0 minute = 60 seconds. Actual delivery time is longer due to gantry rotation speed, MLC leaf travel, and dose rate modulation during VMAT.

eclipse.q: What is the role of the aperture shape controller (ASC) during VMAT optimization in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: Regulates MLC segment shapes to balance plan quality against deliverability and machine constraints
c: Determines the initial jaw positions for each control point based on the target projection size
Regulates MLC segment shapes to balance plan quality against deliverability and machine constraints
Controls the gantry rotation speed profile to ensure uniform dose delivery across each arc
Adjusts the virtual wedge angle at each control point to modulate dose across the target
eclipse.e: The ASC parameter in Eclipse controls the smoothness and regularity of MLC apertures during VMAT optimization. Higher ASC values produce simpler, more deliverable apertures at the expense of plan quality. Lower values allow more complex modulation.

eclipse.q: What kernel-based superposition method does the AAA algorithm use for dose calculation in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: Pencil beam convolution with multiple lateral photon scatter kernels
c: Single-source point kernel convolution in water equivalent medium
Pencil beam convolution with multiple lateral photon scatter kernels
Full Monte Carlo photon transport with forced interactions enabled
Collapsed cone convolution with single polyenergetic energy kernel
eclipse.e: The Anisotropic Analytical Algorithm (AAA) uses pencil beam convolution/superposition with separate modelling of primary photons, extra-focal photons, and electron contamination, applying multiple lateral scatter kernels to account for tissue heterogeneities.

eclipse.q: How does AcurosXB differ from AAA in handling heterogeneous media such as lung tissue?
eclipse.a: AcurosXB solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation directly in patient media
c: AcurosXB uses water-equivalent path length scaling for all heterogeneities
AcurosXB applies a single density correction factor to homogeneous dose kernels
AcurosXB solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation directly in patient media
AcurosXB performs a full stochastic Monte Carlo simulation on the patient CT
eclipse.e: AcurosXB is a deterministic solver of the Linear Boltzmann Transport Equation (LBTE). Unlike AAA, which scales dose kernels calculated in water, AcurosXB explicitly accounts for material composition and density, providing more accurate dose in heterogeneous regions like lung and bone.

eclipse.q: What is the primary advantage of Monte Carlo dose calculation over convolution/superposition methods?
eclipse.a: More accurate dose in regions of electronic disequilibrium and complex heterogeneities
c: Significantly faster calculation times compared to pencil beam algorithms
More accurate dose in regions of electronic disequilibrium and complex heterogeneities
Elimination of the need for beam model commissioning or measured data input
Automatic optimisation of treatment plan parameters during dose calculation
eclipse.e: Monte Carlo methods simulate individual particle interactions stochastically, making them the gold standard for accuracy in complex geometries, small fields, and regions of charged particle disequilibrium such as lung-tissue interfaces.

eclipse.q: CALC: If a dose calculation grid of 2.5 mm is used instead of 5.0 mm, by approximately what factor does the number of calculation voxels increase?
eclipse.a: 8 times
c: 2 times
4 times
8 times
16 times
eclipse.e: Halving the grid spacing in all three dimensions doubles the number of voxels in each direction: 2 x 2 x 2 = 8. Finer grids improve spatial resolution but significantly increase computation time and memory requirements.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse VMAT optimisation, what does the Progressive Resolution Optimizer (PRO) do?
eclipse.a: Begins with coarse gantry angle sampling and progressively refines angular resolution
c: Optimises each beam angle independently then combines the final MLC sequences
Begins with coarse gantry angle sampling and progressively refines angular resolution
Uses a fixed set of control points and only modifies MLC leaf positions at each
Applies a genetic algorithm that randomly mutates MLC aperture shapes each iteration
eclipse.e: The PRO algorithm in Eclipse starts optimisation with a small number of control points (coarse angular sampling) and progressively increases the number of control points, refining the solution iteratively to produce deliverable VMAT plans efficiently.

eclipse.q: Which DVH metric is most commonly used to evaluate spinal cord dose in a treatment plan?
eclipse.a: D0.03 cc (maximum dose to 0.03 cc volume)
c: D50% (median dose to the entire cord volume)
D0.03 cc (maximum dose to 0.03 cc volume)
V20 Gy (volume receiving at least 20 Gy)
Mean dose averaged over the full cord contour
eclipse.e: For serial organs like the spinal cord, the near-maximum dose (D0.03 cc or D0.1 cc) is the critical metric because even a small volume receiving excessive dose can cause myelopathy. This is recommended by ICRU 83 and local SA protocols.

eclipse.q: What Conformity Index (CI) value indicates ideal dose conformity to the target volume?
eclipse.e: A Conformity Index of 1.0 means the prescription isodose volume exactly matches the target volume. CI > 1 indicates the treated volume is larger than the target (over-irradiation of normal tissue), while CI < 1 indicates incomplete target coverage.

eclipse.q: When using AcurosXB in Eclipse, which dose reporting mode reports the absorbed dose without scaling to water response?
eclipse.a: Dose-to-medium (Dm)
c: Dose-to-water (Dw) with density override applied
Dose-to-medium (Dm)
Water-equivalent dose with Bragg-Gray correction factor
Absolute monitor unit dose normalised to calibration conditions
eclipse.e: AcurosXB can report dose in two modes: dose-to-medium (Dm), which is the actual absorbed dose in the material composition, and dose-to-water (Dw), which converts Dm using stopping power ratios. Dm is the native output of the LBTE solver.

eclipse.q: CALC: A treatment plan delivers 200 MU with a field size of 10x10 cm at 100 cm SSD. If the output factor for 10x10 cm is 1.000 and the calibration is 1 cGy/MU at dmax, what is the dose at dmax?
eclipse.a: 200 cGy
c: 100 cGy
150 cGy
200 cGy
250 cGy
eclipse.e: Dose = MU x Output Factor x cGy/MU = 200 x 1.000 x 1.0 = 200 cGy. Under calibration conditions (10x10 cm, 100 cm SSD, dmax), the output factor is unity and each MU delivers 1 cGy.

eclipse.q: In IMRT optimisation, what is the role of the objective function?
eclipse.a: It quantifies the difference between the current dose distribution and the desired goals
c: It defines the physical constraints of the MLC leaf travel speed limits
It quantifies the difference between the current dose distribution and the desired goals
It determines the optimal gantry angles for beam placement automatically
It calculates the final monitor units required for each treatment field
eclipse.e: The objective function (or cost function) is a mathematical expression that assigns a penalty score based on how far the current dose distribution deviates from planning goals. The optimiser iteratively adjusts fluence maps to minimise this function.

eclipse.q: What effect does reducing the dose calculation grid size have on plan accuracy near tissue-air interfaces?
eclipse.a: Improved accuracy due to better spatial sampling of dose gradients
c: No effect since the algorithm handles interfaces analytically regardless of grid
Decreased accuracy because smaller voxels amplify statistical noise in the dose
Improved accuracy due to better spatial sampling of dose gradients
Improved speed because fewer ray-tracing calculations are required per voxel
eclipse.e: Smaller calculation grid sizes better resolve steep dose gradients at tissue-air interfaces. A 1-2.5 mm grid is recommended for SRS/SBRT and regions near air cavities to capture the build-up/build-down effects accurately.

eclipse.q: During beam model commissioning in Eclipse, which measured data set is essential for configuring the MLC leaf transmission?
eclipse.a: Transmission factor measured through closed MLC leaves at multiple field sizes
c: Central axis percentage depth dose curves for open field configurations only
Output factors measured with an ion chamber for all standard field sizes
Transmission factor measured through closed MLC leaves at multiple field sizes
Wedge profiles measured at several depths for each available wedge angle
eclipse.e: MLC leaf transmission is a key beam model parameter. It is measured by comparing the dose through fully closed MLC leaves to the open field dose. This directly affects IMRT/VMAT dose calculation accuracy, especially for organs receiving transmitted dose.

eclipse.q: CALC: An independent MU calculation yields 245 MU for a field, while Eclipse calculates 250 MU. What is the percentage difference?
eclipse.e: Percentage difference = |250 - 245| / 250 x 100 = 5/250 x 100 = 2.0%. Most institutions accept an MU verification agreement within 3-5% for standard fields (SA regulations align with IAEA recommendations for independent checks).

eclipse.q: What is the purpose of the leaf sequencing step in IMRT treatment planning?
eclipse.a: Converting the optimised fluence map into deliverable MLC leaf positions and MU segments
c: Calculating the ideal fluence pattern before any physical delivery constraints apply
Converting the optimised fluence map into deliverable MLC leaf positions and MU segments
Determining the optimal number of beams and gantry angles for the treatment plan
Verifying that the delivered dose matches the planned dose using gamma analysis
eclipse.e: After inverse optimisation produces ideal fluence maps, leaf sequencing converts these into a series of deliverable MLC apertures (segments) with corresponding MU weights. This step introduces some dose degradation compared to the optimal fluence.

eclipse.q: When a plan is normalised such that 95% of the PTV receives the prescription dose, which parameter is being set?
eclipse.a: D95% = prescription dose
c: Dmax = prescription dose at the isocentre point
D95% = prescription dose
Dmean = prescription dose averaged over PTV volume
D100% = prescription dose covering the entire PTV
eclipse.e: Plan normalisation to D95% means that the dose to 95% of the PTV volume equals the prescribed dose. This is the most common normalisation convention per ICRU 83 and is standard practice in SA radiation oncology departments.

eclipse.q: What is the reference point dose in Eclipse, and where is it typically placed?
eclipse.a: The dose calculated at the ICRU reference point, usually placed at or near the isocentre
c: The maximum dose point found anywhere within the patient CT volume data set
The dose calculated at the ICRU reference point, usually placed at or near the isocentre
The minimum dose recorded at the surface of the patient skin contour structure
The mean dose calculated across all organs at risk within the treatment volume
eclipse.e: The ICRU reference point (Report 83) should be in a region of uniform dose, typically at or near the isocentre. Eclipse uses this point for dose prescription and reporting, and it should be within the PTV where dose gradients are minimal.

eclipse.q: In a field-in-field (FIF) technique, what is the primary purpose of adding subfields?
eclipse.a: To reduce hot spots and improve dose homogeneity within the target volume
c: To increase the overall dose rate and reduce total treatment delivery time
To reduce hot spots and improve dose homogeneity within the target volume
To add additional dose to cold spots near the field edges at organ boundaries
To compensate for patient motion during beam-on time using gated delivery
eclipse.e: Field-in-field (forward-planned IMRT) uses smaller subfields within the main field to shield high-dose regions. By weighting these subfields appropriately, hot spots are reduced, improving the Homogeneity Index. This technique is commonly used for breast treatments in SA.

eclipse.q: When modelling bolus in Eclipse, which parameter must be correctly specified to ensure accurate surface dose calculation?
eclipse.a: The bolus material HU override and physical thickness relative to the skin surface
c: The gantry angle at which the bolus is placed on the patient skin surface
The bolus material HU override and physical thickness relative to the skin surface
The distance from the bolus to the isocentre measured along the beam central axis
The field size reduction factor caused by bolus attenuation in the beam path
eclipse.e: Bolus must be modelled with correct HU (typically water-equivalent, HU = 0) and accurate thickness. If bolus is not contoured or assigned correctly in Eclipse, the TPS will underestimate skin dose. An air gap between bolus and skin must also be minimised clinically.

eclipse.q: CALC: If the gradient dose fall-off index (GI) is defined as V50%/V100%, and V50% = 40 cc while V100% = 10 cc, what is the GI?
eclipse.e: Gradient Index = V50%/V100% = 40/10 = 4.0. A lower GI indicates a steeper dose fall-off outside the target, which is desirable for sparing normal tissue, particularly important in SRS/SBRT planning.

eclipse.q: What is the recommended dose calculation grid size for stereotactic treatment planning in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: 1.0 to 1.5 mm
c: 1.0 to 1.5 mm
2.5 to 3.0 mm
4.0 to 5.0 mm
6.0 to 8.0 mm
eclipse.e: For SRS/SBRT, a fine calculation grid of 1.0-1.5 mm is recommended to accurately resolve the steep dose gradients and small target volumes. Coarser grids may underestimate dose heterogeneity and compromise plan evaluation accuracy.

eclipse.q: CALC: A VMAT plan has 177 control points over a 360-degree arc. What is the approximate gantry angle spacing between control points?
eclipse.a: Approximately 2 degrees
c: Approximately 1 degree
Approximately 2 degrees
Approximately 4 degrees
Approximately 5 degrees
eclipse.e: Gantry spacing = 360 / (177 - 1) = 360 / 176 ≈ 2.05 degrees. Eclipse VMAT plans typically use 2-degree spacing (177 control points for a full arc), providing a good balance between plan quality and calculation efficiency.

eclipse.q: What does the Photon Optimizer (PO) in Eclipse use as its primary optimisation variable for VMAT plans?
eclipse.a: MLC leaf positions and dose rate (MU weight) at each control point
c: Jaw positions and physical wedge angles at each static beam orientation
MLC leaf positions and dose rate (MU weight) at each control point
Gantry rotation speed and collimator angle at each treatment beam angle
Fluence intensity values on a 2D grid perpendicular to each beam direction
eclipse.e: The Photon Optimizer (PO) in Eclipse directly optimises MLC aperture shapes and MU weights at each control point during VMAT optimisation. This is a direct machine parameter optimisation approach, unlike fluence-based optimisation used in step-and-shoot IMRT.

eclipse.q: What does AAA stand for in Eclipse TPS?
eclipse.a: Analytical Anisotropic Algorithm
c: Analytical Anisotropic Algorithm
Advanced Attenuation Algorithm
Automatic Adaptive Algorithm
Anisotropic Aperture Algorithm
eclipse.e: AAA uses a pencil-beam convolution/superposition approach with tissue heterogeneity modeled via 1D density scaling.

eclipse.q: AcurosXB advantage over AAA in heterogeneous media?
eclipse.a: More accurate at tissue interfaces
c: Faster calculation speed overall
More accurate at tissue interfaces
Simpler commissioning process
Better for homogeneous phantoms
eclipse.e: AcurosXB solves the LBTE explicitly, handling lateral scatter at interfaces more accurately than AAA's 1D scaling.

eclipse.q: Reducing dose calculation grid size from 2.5 mm to 1 mm primarily affects what?
eclipse.a: Accuracy in high-gradient regions
c: Overall mean dose to target
Accuracy in high-gradient regions
Monitor unit calculation only
Beam energy determination
eclipse.e: Finer grids better resolve steep dose gradients near field edges and heterogeneity boundaries, at the cost of computation time.

eclipse.q: Recommended Eclipse grid size for SRS/SBRT plans?
eclipse.a: 1.0 to 1.5 mm
c: 1.0 to 1.5 mm
2.0 to 2.5 mm
3.0 to 5.0 mm
0.1 to 0.5 mm
eclipse.e: Small targets and steep gradients in SRS/SBRT require fine grids (1.0-1.5 mm) for adequate dose accuracy.

eclipse.q: What is the Normal Tissue Objective (NTO) in Eclipse optimization?
eclipse.a: Controls dose fall-off outside the target
c: Controls dose fall-off outside the target
Limits maximum dose inside the target
Sets minimum dose to critical organs
Defines beam entry angle constraints
eclipse.e: NTO creates a dose fall-off gradient outside the PTV to spare surrounding normal tissue without explicit OAR constraints.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse IMRT optimization, what does increasing an objective priority value do?
eclipse.a: Gives that objective more weight
c: Gives that objective more weight
Reduces its importance in cost
Removes it from optimization
Locks the constraint value
eclipse.e: Higher priority values increase the penalty weight for that objective in the cost function, making the optimizer work harder to satisfy it.

eclipse.q: What type of optimization constraint cannot be violated in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: Hard constraint (upper/lower limit)
c: Soft objective with high priority
Hard constraint (upper/lower limit)
NTO with automatic setting
Line objective on DVH curve
eclipse.e: Hard constraints impose absolute limits that the optimizer must satisfy, unlike objectives which are goals balanced by priority.

eclipse.q: Eclipse field geometry: what defines the isocenter location?
eclipse.a: User-placed reference point in 3D
c: Center of the CT image always
User-placed reference point in 3D
Geometric center of the MLC field
Midpoint of the couch surface
eclipse.e: The isocenter is placed by the planner, typically at or near the center of the target volume.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what happens when the dose grid extends beyond the CT image boundary?
eclipse.a: Voxels outside are assigned to air density
c: Calculation fails with an error
Voxels outside are assigned to air density
Voxels outside use water density
Grid is automatically cropped to CT
eclipse.e: Eclipse assigns air (or a default density) to dose grid voxels that fall outside the body/CT contour boundary.

eclipse.q: How does AAA handle tissue heterogeneity corrections?
eclipse.a: 1D radiological path-length scaling
c: Full 3D Monte Carlo transport
1D radiological path-length scaling
No heterogeneity correction applied
2D convolution kernel approach
eclipse.e: AAA scales pencil-beam kernels using equivalent path length along the fan line, a 1D density correction approach.

eclipse.q: What does the Conformity Index (CI) measure in plan evaluation?
eclipse.a: Ratio of prescription isodose volume to target volume
c: Ratio of prescription isodose volume to target volume
Maximum dose divided by the mean dose
Ratio of minimum to maximum target dose
Volume of normal tissue receiving zero dose
eclipse.e: CI = V_RI / V_TV. A value of 1.0 indicates perfect conformity of the prescription isodose to the target.

eclipse.q: What does DVH stand for in Eclipse plan evaluation?
eclipse.a: Dose Volume Histogram
c: Dose Volume Histogram
Differential Voxel Heatmap
Digital Verification Hardcopy
Dose Variance Homogeneity
eclipse.e: DVH graphically represents the dose distribution within a structure, showing what volume receives at least a given dose.

eclipse.q: Cumulative DVH: what does the y-axis represent?
eclipse.a: Percentage of volume receiving at least that dose
c: Percentage of volume receiving at least that dose
Absolute dose at each voxel in the plan
Number of beams contributing to that point
Monitor units delivered per fraction
eclipse.e: In a cumulative DVH, each point shows the fractional volume of the structure receiving dose equal to or greater than the x-axis value.

eclipse.q: What scripting language does Eclipse ESAPI primarily use?
eclipse.a: C# (.NET framework)
c: Python with NumPy libraries
C# (.NET framework)
MATLAB scripting language
Java with Eclipse plugin
eclipse.e: Eclipse Scripting API (ESAPI) is built on the .NET framework and uses C# as its primary programming language.

eclipse.q: ESAPI can be run in which two modes in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: Single-patient and stand-alone application
c: Single-patient and stand-alone application
Batch mode and real-time mode only
Read-only and administrator mode only
Planning mode and verification mode only
eclipse.e: ESAPI scripts run as plug-in scripts (single patient context) or as stand-alone executables that can iterate over multiple patients.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is a Plan Sum used for?
eclipse.a: Combining dose distributions from multiple plans
c: Combining dose distributions from multiple plans
Averaging MU values across treatment fields
Summing beam-on times for scheduling
Merging CT datasets from two scanners
eclipse.e: Plan Sum adds dose distributions from separate plans on the same or registered image sets for composite dose evaluation.

eclipse.q: Which Boolean structure operation creates a new structure from overlapping regions of two structures?
eclipse.a: Intersection (AND)
c: Union (OR) of both structures
Intersection (AND)
Subtraction (A minus B)
Exclusive OR (XOR) operation
eclipse.e: Intersection keeps only the overlapping volume common to both input structures.

eclipse.q: Eclipse margin tool: isotropic vs. asymmetric margin difference?
eclipse.a: Isotropic applies equal expansion in all directions
c: Isotropic applies equal expansion in all directions
Asymmetric only works in the axial plane
Isotropic is faster but less accurate
Asymmetric cannot exceed 5 mm per axis
eclipse.e: Isotropic margins expand uniformly; asymmetric margins allow different expansions in each of the six cardinal directions.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what does plan normalization to a point mean?
eclipse.a: 100% is assigned to the dose at that point
c: 100% is assigned to the dose at that point
All voxels are set to the same dose
Maximum dose is forced to that location
MU calculation is bypassed entirely
eclipse.e: Point normalization defines the dose at the selected point as 100%, scaling all other dose values proportionally.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse beam model commissioning, what is the dosimetric leaf gap (DLG)?
eclipse.a: Effective MLC leaf transmission gap width
c: Physical leaf thickness in mm
Effective MLC leaf transmission gap width
Distance between opposing jaw faces
Gap between adjacent leaf pairs
eclipse.e: DLG accounts for the rounded leaf-end transmission, representing the effective gap through which radiation leaks when leaves are closed.

eclipse.q: What beam model parameter describes radiation leaking through closed MLC leaves?
eclipse.a: MLC transmission factor
c: Output factor for open fields
MLC transmission factor
Dosimetric leaf gap value
Penumbra broadening kernel
eclipse.e: MLC transmission factor (typically 1-2%) quantifies the fraction of radiation transmitted through the tungsten leaves.

eclipse.q: AcurosXB reporting option 'dose-to-medium' vs 'dose-to-water': which is more accurate in bone?
eclipse.a: Dose-to-medium
c: Dose-to-water always
Dose-to-medium
Both are identical in bone
Neither applies to bone tissue
eclipse.e: Dose-to-medium reflects the actual absorbed dose in the material. Dose-to-water applies a conversion assuming water-like response.

eclipse.q: Which Eclipse tool allows comparison of two plans on the same CT using dose difference maps?
eclipse.a: Plan comparison workspace
c: Plan comparison workspace
Beam configuration module
Structure template editor
Treatment delivery dashboard
eclipse.e: The plan comparison workspace displays dose difference, DVH comparison, and gamma maps between two plans on the same image set.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what is the purpose of the 'Ring' structure around a PTV?
eclipse.a: To control dose fall-off gradient
c: To define the treatment couch area
To control dose fall-off gradient
To mark the external body contour
To identify the beam entry region
eclipse.e: A ring (donut-shaped structure) around the PTV is used with optimization objectives to create a steep dose gradient outside the target.

eclipse.q: Eclipse VMAT optimization: what does the 'MU objective' control?
eclipse.a: Total plan monitor unit efficiency
c: Total plan monitor unit efficiency
Maximum dose rate of the linac
Number of control points per arc
Collimator rotation speed limits
eclipse.e: The MU objective penalizes excessive MU, promoting more efficient plans with less modulation and lower scatter dose.

eclipse.q: How many control points does a typical single-arc VMAT plan have in Eclipse?
eclipse.a: 177 (every 2 degrees over 360)
c: 36 (every 10 degrees over 360)
177 (every 2 degrees over 360)
360 (every 1 degree over 360)
72 (every 5 degrees over 360)
eclipse.e: Eclipse VMAT uses control points every ~2 degrees of gantry rotation, giving approximately 177 control points for a full arc.

eclipse.q: Which AcurosXB material library element is used for metallic implants?
eclipse.a: Titanium or steel alloy assignment
c: Titanium or steel alloy assignment
Water with adjusted density value
Air cavity with high HU override
Bone with density multiplication
eclipse.e: AcurosXB uses a material library including metals like titanium and steel for accurate dose calculation near implants.

eclipse.q: Eclipse photon beam model: what is the primary measured data needed for commissioning?
eclipse.a: PDDs, profiles, and output factors
c: PDDs, profiles, and output factors
CT images of the linac head only
Patient treatment plan archives
MLC log files from prior treatments
eclipse.e: Beam model commissioning requires measured percent depth doses, lateral profiles at multiple depths, and output factors for various field sizes.

eclipse.q: In Eclipse, what does the 'crop structure from body' operation do?
eclipse.a: Removes parts of a structure outside the body contour
c: Removes parts of a structure outside the body contour
Deletes the body contour from the plan
Expands the structure to match body size
Crops the CT image to the body boundary
eclipse.e: This operation intersects the selected structure with the external body contour, removing any portion extending beyond the patient surface.

eclipse.q: Eclipse calculation grid: what interpolation method is used between grid points?
eclipse.a: Trilinear interpolation
c: Nearest neighbor sampling only
Trilinear interpolation
Bicubic spline fitting method
No interpolation is performed

eclipse.q: What is the primary difference between dose-to-medium and dose-to-water reporting modes in Acuros XB?

eclipse.q: In Eclipse VMAT optimization, what does the multi-resolution dose calculation approach accomplish?

Only works for prostate — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Not clinically usable yet — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
dosimetry.e: tumour contouring still requires physician review. DL autocontour (Limbus, MVision, Manteia): OARs in H&N, thorax, pelvis within ~1-2 mm of expert. GTV contouring: improving but still requires oncologist verification. Saves 30-60 min per patient. Reduces inter-observer variability.

dosimetry.q: EPID-based in-vivo dosimetry uses AI for?
dosimetry.a: Real-time comparison of delivered vs planned dose distribution during treatment
c: Only portal imaging — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Real-time comparison of delivered vs planned dose distribution during treatment
Post-treatment analysis only, based on established clinical physics practice
Replacing ion chamber calibration, based on established clinical physics practice
dosimetry.e: EPID transit dosimetry: AI reconstructs 3D dose in patient from exit EPID signal. Compares to TPS prediction in real time. Detects: wrong patient, wrong plan, anatomy changes, setup errors. Commercial: Adaptivo, PerFraction, iViewDose.

dosimetry.q: Monte Carlo dose calculation in TPS: advantage over AcurosXB?
dosimetry.a: Gold standard accuracy in all geometries including extreme heterogeneities and very small fields
c: Faster computation — based on established physics principles and clinical evidence
Gold standard accuracy in all geometries including extreme heterogeneities and very small fields
Simpler commissioning — sufficient for all standard clinical treatment scenarios
Lower memory requirement — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
dosimetry.e: MC simulates individual particle histories → inherently accurate at interfaces, small fields, lung/bone/air boundaries. AcurosXB is deterministic LBTE solver — very good but can have limitations in extreme scenarios. MC now fast enough for clinical TPS (Monaco, Eclipse MC).

dosimetry.q: 3D-printed patient-specific bolus advantage?
dosimetry.a: Conforms perfectly to surface anatomy
c: Cheaper than standard bolus
Conforms perfectly to surface anatomy
Increases treatment time
Only for electron beams
dosimetry.e: uniform dose build-up, eliminates air gaps. CT surface → 3D model → print in tissue-equivalent material (PLA, TPU). Eliminates air gaps between sheet bolus and irregular surfaces (nose, ear, scalp). Uniform dose build-up. Also used for custom electron cutouts and compensators.

dosimetry.q: Surface-guided radiation therapy (SGRT) dosimetric role?
dosimetry.a: Verifies patient position in real-time without imaging dose
c: Measures absorbed dose — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Verifies patient position in real-time without imaging dose
Replaces TPS — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Only for SRS — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
dosimetry.e: enables gating and breath-hold monitoring. SGRT (AlignRT, Catalyst, IDENTIFY): optical cameras track 3D surface in real-time. No radiation dose for positioning. Enables: DIBH verification for left breast, intrafraction motion monitoring for SRS, setup without tattoos. Dosimetric impact: better reproducibility → smaller margins → less OAR dose.

dosimetry.q: TRS-483: machine-specific reference field (msr) is needed when?
dosimetry.a: When the conventional 10×10 cm² reference field cannot be established
c: For all linacs — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
When the conventional 10×10 cm² reference field cannot be established
Only for electron beams — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Never — always use 10×10 — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
dosimetry.e: Non-standard machines: CyberKnife (max ~6 cm cone), TomoTherapy (max 5×40), GammaKnife. TRS-483 defines intermediate calibration step: calibrate at msr, then apply output correction factors to clinical small fields.

dosimetry.q: TRS-483: three conditions that define a small field?
dosimetry.a: Loss of lateral CPE, partial source occlusion by collimator, detector size comparable to field size
c: Only detector size matters — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Loss of lateral CPE, partial source occlusion by collimator, detector size comparable to field size
Only source occlusion — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Only CPE loss — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
dosimetry.e: All three occur together for small fields: (1) LCPE loss: field < lateral electron range. (2) Source occlusion: collimator partially blocks focal spot. (3) Detector perturbation: detector volume comparable to field → volume averaging. Each causes output factor to differ from large-field behavior.

dosimetry.q: CALC: TRS-483 field output factor: Ω(f_clin) = M(f_clin)/M(f_msr) × k(f_clin,f_msr). If ratio of readings = 0.82 and k = 0.97, corrected OF?
dosimetry.e: Ω = 0.82 × 0.97 = 0.7954 ≈ 0.795. The k-factor corrects for detector-specific perturbation in the small clinical field relative to the reference.

dosimetry.q: EBT3 Gafchromic film advantage over EBT2?
dosimetry.a: Symmetric polyester layer structure
c: Higher sensitivity
Symmetric polyester layer structure
No scanner needed — current protocols and vendor specifications provide adequate safety margins
Energy independent
dosimetry.e: eliminates face-up/face-down orientation dependence. EBT3: symmetric matte polyester layers (125 μm each side) with active layer in centre. Eliminates scanning orientation issue. Also reduces Newton ring artefacts on flatbed scanners. Standard for IMRT/VMAT QA and small-field dosimetry.

dosimetry.q: Film dosimetry: critical handling precaution?
dosimetry.a: Mark orientation (landscape) and scan in consistent position on flatbed scanner
c: Store in fridge — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Mark orientation (landscape) and scan in consistent position on flatbed scanner
Use any scanner setting — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Handle with bare hands — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
dosimetry.e: Gafchromic film is polarisation-sensitive — response varies with orientation relative to scanner CCD array. Must always scan in same direction (landscape convention). Wait 24+ hours post-irradiation for polymerisation to stabilise. Use RGB calibration curve.

dosimetry.q: CALC: Film calibration: OD (optical density) at 400 cGy = 0.85, at 0 cGy = 0.15. Net OD?
dosimetry.e: Net OD = OD_irradiated - OD_background = 0.85 - 0.15 = 0.70. Calibration curve plots net OD vs known dose to convert future film readings to dose.

dosimetry.q: In-vivo dosimetry using diodes: placement for entrance dose verification?
dosimetry.a: On patient skin surface at beam entrance, correcting for SSD and field size
c: Inside patient — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
On patient skin surface at beam entrance, correcting for SSD and field size
On exit surface only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
On the couch — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
dosimetry.e: Entrance diode: measures entrance dose per fraction. Calibration factors account for SSD, field size, wedge, temperature. Action level: typically ±5% from expected. Real-time check of treatment delivery. AAPM TG-62 / IAEA guidelines.

dosimetry.q: Alanine dosimetry is used for?
dosimetry.a: Postal audit dosimetry
c: Daily linac QA
Postal audit dosimetry
Patient in-vivo measurements
Small field dosimetry
dosimetry.e: stable, dose-rate independent, traceable to primary standards. Alanine pellets: irradiated → free radicals measured by EPR spectroscopy. Extremely stable signal (years). Energy/dose-rate independent. Used by IAEA, NPL, NRC for postal TLD audits. Gold standard for external audit of linac calibration.

dosimetry.q: CALC: Electron beam: R50 measured at 4.8 cm in water. Mean energy at surface E₀ ≈ 2.33 × R50?
c: ~6 MeV
~9 MeV
~15 MeV
dosimetry.e: E₀ = 2.33 × 4.8 = 11.18 ≈ 11.2 MeV. Used for selecting beam quality specifier for TRS-398 electron dosimetry. R50 in cm water is the primary beam quality index for electrons.

dosimetry.q: Farmer-type ion chamber: typical sensitive volume and wall material?
dosimetry.a: ~0.6 cm³ active volume, graphite or C-552 air-equivalent plastic wall
c: ~0.01 cm³, steel wall — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
~0.6 cm³ active volume, graphite or C-552 air-equivalent plastic wall
~6 cm³, lead wall — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
~60 cm³, aluminium wall — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
dosimetry.e: Farmer chamber (NE 2571, PTW 30013, Exradin A12): workhorse for photon/electron calibration. 0.6 cm³: good sensitivity, acceptable volume averaging for ≥4×4 cm² fields. Wall: near air/water equivalent to minimise perturbation corrections.

dosimetry.q: Plane-parallel (parallel plate) ion chamber: when is it required over Farmer chamber?
dosimetry.a: Electron beams ≤10 MeV (R50 ≤ 4 cm), surface dose measurements, and buildup region measurements
c: Never required — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
Electron beams ≤10 MeV (R50 ≤ 4 cm), surface dose measurements, and buildup region measurements
Only for photon beams — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Only for proton beams — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
dosimetry.e: PP chamber (Roos, Markus, Advanced Markus, Exradin A10): thin entrance window (~1 mm), minimal perturbation in steep dose gradients. TRS-398: PP chamber required for electrons with R50 < 4 cm. Also preferred for surface/buildup (no over-response from Farmer stem effect).

dosimetry.q: CALC: Uncertainty budget for TRS-398 calibration. Type A (statistical): 0.3%. Type B (systematic — chamber cal, positioning, k factors): 1.0%. Combined standard uncertainty?
dosimetry.e: Combined: u_c = √(0.3² + 1.0²) = √(0.09 + 1.00) = √1.09 = 1.044%. Expanded uncertainty (k=2, 95% CI): ~2.1%. TRS-398 estimates overall absorbed dose uncertainty ~1.5% (k=1) for photon beams.

dosimetry.q: Water-equivalence of solid phantoms: when is solid phantom acceptable for reference dosimetry?
dosimetry.a: TRS-398 requires water for reference calibration
c: Solid phantoms always acceptable
TRS-398 requires water for reference calibration
Only water allowed for any measurement
Solid preferred over water
dosimetry.e: solid phantoms only acceptable for relative measurements and routine constancy checks. Water: reference medium (density, scatter, stopping power well-defined). Solid phantoms (Solid Water, RW3, PMMA, Plastic Water): convenient but require depth and reading conversion factors. TRS-398 mandates water for absolute calibration. Solids acceptable for daily/monthly output constancy.

dosimetry.q: What is the SI unit of absorbed radiation dose?
dosimetry.a: Gray (Gy)
c: Sievert (Sv)
Becquerel (Bq)
Gray (Gy)
Coulomb (C)
dosimetry.e: The Gray (Gy) is the SI unit of absorbed dose. One Gray is defined as the absorption of one joule of radiation energy per kilogram of matter (1 Gy = 1 J/kg).

dosimetry.q: What does absorbed dose measure in radiation therapy?
dosimetry.a: The energy deposited by radiation per unit mass of tissue
c: The number of radiation beams used
The energy deposited by radiation per unit mass of tissue
The speed at which radiation travels
The number of cancer cells destroyed
dosimetry.e: Absorbed dose quantifies the amount of energy deposited by ionising radiation per unit mass of the absorbing material. It is a fundamental quantity in radiation therapy as it directly relates to the biological effect on tissue.

dosimetry.q: What instrument is most commonly used to measure radiation dose in a clinical setting?
dosimetry.a: An ionisation chamber
c: A thermometer
An ionisation chamber
A stethoscope
A Geiger counter
dosimetry.e: An ionisation chamber is the gold standard detector for measuring radiation dose in clinical settings. It works by collecting the electrical charge produced when radiation ionises the air or gas inside the chamber, which can then be related to the absorbed dose.

dosimetry.q: What does dosimetry refer to in medical physics?
dosimetry.a: The measurement and calculation of radiation dose
c: The study of drug dosages
The measurement and calculation of radiation dose
The process of diagnosing cancer
The manufacturing of radiation equipment
dosimetry.e: Dosimetry is the science of measuring and calculating the absorbed dose delivered by ionising radiation. Accurate dosimetry is essential in radiation therapy to ensure the correct dose is delivered to the tumour and that healthy tissue exposure is minimised.

dosimetry.q: How many centigray (cGy) are in one Gray (Gy)?
dosimetry.a: 100 cGy
c: 10 cGy
100 cGy
1000 cGy
1 cGy
dosimetry.e: One Gray equals 100 centigray, just as one metre equals 100 centimetres. The prefix 'centi' means one hundredth. In clinical practice, doses are often expressed in cGy for convenience (e.g., a 2 Gy fraction equals 200 cGy).

dosimetry.q: CALC: For a 12 MeV electron beam calibration using TRS-398, the half-value depth R50 = 4.8 cm. What is the reference depth d_ref?
dosimetry.e: TRS-398 formula for electrons: d_ref = 0.6 × R50 - 0.1 (in cm, for R50 in cm, valid for R50 <= 10 cm). d_ref = 0.6 × 4.8 - 0.1 = 2.88 - 0.1 = 2.78 cm. The reference depth is chosen to avoid the steep dose gradient (bremsstrahlung tail) region while being close to the dose maximum. At d_ref, the beam quality correction factor k_Q has reduced uncertainty compared to measurements at other depths.

dosimetry.q: CALC: A parallel-plate chamber measures surface dose reading of 38.2 nC and dmax reading of 145.6 nC in a 6 MV, 10×10 cm² beam. What is the percentage surface dose?
dosimetry.e: Surface dose = (surface reading / dmax reading) × 100% = (38.2 / 145.6) × 100% = 26.2%. For 6 MV, typical surface dose is 15-30% depending on field size and SSD. The parallel-plate chamber is ideal for surface dose measurements because its effective point of measurement is the inside surface of the entrance window, providing a well-defined shallow measurement depth without the shift corrections required by thimble chambers.

dosimetry.q: In TRS-398 absolute dose determination, which single parameter contributes the largest uncertainty component?
dosimetry.a: The beam quality correction factor k_Q (~1.0% at k=1)
c: Chamber positioning at reference depth (~0.3%)
The beam quality correction factor k_Q (~1.0% at k=1)
Electrometer calibration (~0.1%)
Temperature and pressure correction (~0.1%)
dosimetry.e: The beam quality correction factor k_Q carries the largest single uncertainty component (approximately 1.0% standard uncertainty) in TRS-398 dosimetry. It accounts for differences between the Co-60 calibration beam quality and the user beam quality, incorporating stopping power ratios and chamber perturbation factors. Chamber positioning contributes approximately 0.3%, and electrometer and T/P corrections each contribute around 0.1%. The combined standard uncertainty in D_w is approximately 1.5%.

dosimetry.q: CALC: Ion recombination is measured using the TRS-398 two-voltage technique for a pulsed linac beam. At V₁ = 300 V: M₁ = 25.40 nC. At V₂ = 150 V: M₂ = 25.10 nC. The TRS-398 polynomial coefficients for pulsed beams at V₁/V₂ = 2.0 are: a₀ = 2.337, a₁ = -3.636, a₂ = 2.299. What is k_s?
dosimetry.e: M₁/M₂ = 25.40/25.10 = 1.01195. k_s = a₀ + a₁(M₁/M₂) + a₂(M₁/M₂)² = 2.337 + (-3.636)(1.01195) + 2.299(1.01195)² = 2.337 - 3.6795 + 2.3540 = 1.0115 ≈ 1.012. This small correction (approximately 1.2%) indicates acceptable ion recombination at this dose rate. Values of k_s exceeding 1.05 suggest the operating voltage is too low or the dose-per-pulse is too high for the chamber.

dosimetry.q: In TRS-398, the beam quality correction factor kQ accounts for what when using a calibrated ionisation chamber?
dosimetry.a: Differences in chamber response between the calibration beam quality (Co-60) and the user's clinical beam quality
c: Temperature and pressure variations between calibration lab and clinical measurements
Differences in chamber response between the calibration beam quality (Co-60) and the user's clinical beam quality
Ion recombination losses at high dose rates in pulsed linac beams
Polarity effects caused by cable orientation during measurement
dosimetry.e: kQ converts the Co-60 calibration coefficient (ND,w,Q0) to the user's beam quality Q. It accounts for changes in W/e, perturbation factors, and stopping-power ratios between Q0 and Q. kQ is tabulated in TRS-398 based on beam quality index (TPR20,10 for photons, R50 for electrons).

dosimetry.q: Why is a parallel plate chamber preferred over a cylindrical (Farmer) chamber for electron beam dosimetry below 10 MeV?
dosimetry.a: Parallel plate chambers eliminate the fluence perturbation correction that is significant in low-energy electron beams
c: Parallel plate chambers have a larger sensitive volume for better signal
Cylindrical chambers cannot physically fit in an electron beam
Parallel plate chambers eliminate the fluence perturbation correction that is significant in low-energy electron beams
Parallel plate chambers are more sensitive to ion recombination effects
dosimetry.e: In low-energy electron beams, the in-scattering fluence perturbation (pfl) for cylindrical chambers becomes large and uncertain. Parallel plate chambers have a thin front window and guard ring geometry that eliminates this perturbation effect (pfl ≈ 1). TRS-398 recommends PP chambers for R50 < 4 g/cm² (≈ below 10 MeV).

dosimetry.q: CALC: TRS-398 photon calibration. ND,w = 5.432 cGy/nC, kQ = 0.991, corrected reading M = 24.56 nC. What is the absorbed dose to water at reference depth?

CALC: Ion recombination correction using the two-voltage technique (TRS-398). Readings: M1 = 25.30 nC at V1 = 300 V, M2 = 25.05 nC at V2 = 150 V. Voltage ratio V1/V2 = 2. Using ks = (a0 + a1(M1/M2) + a2(M1/M2)²) with a0=0.25, a1=0.50, a2=0.25 for continuous beams, what is ks?
When performing output factor measurements for IMRT fields using an ionisation chamber, why is a small-volume chamber (e.g., 0.015 cc) sometimes preferred over a Farmer chamber (0.6 cc)?
dosimetry.a: The small volume minimises dose-averaging across steep dose gradients present in modulated fields
c: Small chambers have a higher signal-to-noise ratio than Farmer chambers
Small chambers do not require any correction factors for beam quality
The small volume minimises dose-averaging across steep dose gradients present in modulated fields
Farmer chambers cannot be used in photon beams above 6 MV
dosimetry.e: IMRT fields have steep dose gradients from MLC modulation. A 0.6 cc Farmer chamber averages dose over its large volume (~6 mm inner diameter × 23 mm length), blurring gradients. A 0.015 cc chamber (e.g., Exradin A16) reduces volume averaging but has lower signal, requiring careful leakage and cable assessment.

dosimetry.q: CALC: Farmer chamber reads 22.80 nC at standard conditions. Temperature = 22.5°C, pressure = 101.0 kPa. Reference conditions: T0 = 20°C, P0 = 101.325 kPa. Calculate the temperature-pressure correction factor kTP.
According to TRS-398, what is the reference depth for absorbed dose determination in high-energy photon beams?
dosimetry.a: 10 cm depth in water at 100 cm SSD
c: 10 cm depth in water at 100 cm SSD
5 cm depth in water at 100 cm SSD
Maximum dose depth at 80 cm SSD setup
20 cm depth in water at 100 cm SSD
dosimetry.e: TRS-398 specifies a reference depth of 10 cm in water with 100 cm SSD and a 10x10 cm² field size for high-energy photon beam dosimetry, replacing the older dmax reference used in TRS-277.

dosimetry.q: In the Spencer-Attix cavity theory, what is the primary improvement over Bragg-Gray cavity theory?
dosimetry.a: It accounts for delta-ray production and applies an energy cutoff in the electron spectrum
c: It accounts for delta-ray production and applies an energy cutoff in the electron spectrum
It eliminates the need for any stopping power ratio correction factor in calculations
It allows direct dose measurement without requiring any calibration coefficient at all
It replaces water equivalence with tissue equivalence for all detector materials used
dosimetry.e: Spencer-Attix cavity theory improves upon Bragg-Gray by introducing an energy cutoff (delta) below which electrons are considered to deposit energy locally, properly accounting for delta-ray production and transport within the cavity.

dosimetry.q: CALC: A farmer-type ionisation chamber has an ND,w calibration coefficient of 48.5 mGy/nC. If the corrected charge reading is 24.6 nC and the beam quality correction factor kQ is 0.993, what is the absorbed dose to water?
dosimetry.a: 1184.3 mGy which equals approximately 1.184 Gy
c: 1184.3 mGy which equals approximately 1.184 Gy
1193.3 mGy which equals approximately 1.193 Gy
1210.8 mGy which equals approximately 1.211 Gy
1162.5 mGy which equals approximately 1.163 Gy
dosimetry.e: Using TRS-398: Dw = MQ × ND,w × kQ = 24.6 × 48.5 × 0.993 = 1184.3 mGy ≈ 1.184 Gy. The beam quality correction factor kQ accounts for the difference between the calibration beam quality and the user beam quality.

dosimetry.q: What is the main advantage of using EBT3 Gafchromic film over older EBT2 film in clinical dosimetry?
dosimetry.a: Symmetric layer construction that eliminates orientation dependence during scanning
c: Symmetric layer construction that eliminates orientation dependence during scanning
Significantly higher sensitivity allowing measurement of very low dose values
Complete energy independence across all therapeutic photon beam energies
Built-in calibration markers printed directly on each film by manufacturer
dosimetry.e: EBT3 film features a symmetric polyester laminate structure with the active layer sandwiched between two identical polyester substrates, eliminating the face-up vs face-down scanning orientation dependence that was a source of uncertainty in EBT2 film dosimetry.

dosimetry.q: In TRS-483 small field dosimetry, what defines a small field condition?
dosimetry.a: When at least one of three physical conditions related to source occlusion, LCPE loss, or detector size is met
c: When at least one of three physical conditions related to source occlusion, LCPE loss, or detector size is met
When the field size is smaller than exactly 4 cm x 4 cm regardless of the beam energy being used
When the penumbra region covers more than half of the total irradiated treatment field area
When the detector active volume is larger than five millimetres in any single spatial dimension
dosimetry.e: TRS-483 defines small fields by three conditions: (1) loss of lateral charged particle equilibrium (LCPE), (2) partial occlusion of the primary photon source by the collimators, and (3) the size of the detector is similar to or larger than the beam dimensions. When any one condition is met, small field dosimetry corrections apply.

dosimetry.q: What is the typical sensitivity of lithium fluoride TLD-100 chips used in clinical dosimetry applications?
dosimetry.a: Approximately 1 mGy minimum detectable dose with around ±3% reproducibility at therapy levels
c: Approximately 1 mGy minimum detectable dose with around ±3% reproducibility at therapy levels
Approximately 0.001 mGy minimum detectable dose with ±0.1% reproducibility at all levels
Approximately 50 mGy minimum detectable dose with around ±15% reproducibility at therapy
Approximately 10 mGy minimum detectable dose with around ±10% reproducibility at therapy
dosimetry.e: LiF:Mg,Ti (TLD-100) has a minimum detectable dose of approximately 1 mGy and reproducibility of about ±3% when used with proper annealing protocols in clinical therapeutic dose ranges. This makes them suitable for in-vivo dosimetry and dose verification in radiotherapy.

dosimetry.q: CALC: An electron beam has a measured R50 (depth of 50% dose) of 7.2 cm in water. Using the TRS-398 relationship E0 = 2.33 × R50, what is the mean energy at the phantom surface?
dosimetry.a: 16.78 MeV at the phantom surface entry point
c: 16.78 MeV at the phantom surface entry point
14.40 MeV at the phantom surface entry point
19.56 MeV at the phantom surface entry point
12.24 MeV at the phantom surface entry point
dosimetry.e: Using the TRS-398 approximation: E0 = 2.33 × R50 = 2.33 × 7.2 = 16.776 ≈ 16.78 MeV. This relationship provides the mean electron energy at the phantom surface and is used to determine the appropriate reference depth for electron beam dosimetry.

dosimetry.q: What physical quantity does a graphite calorimeter directly measure in absorbed dose determination?
dosimetry.a: Temperature rise in the absorbing graphite medium due to radiation energy deposition
c: Temperature rise in the absorbing graphite medium due to radiation energy deposition
Electrical charge liberated in the graphite medium per unit mass of absorber material
Optical density change in graphite medium caused by radiation induced lattice defects
Chemical yield of oxidised species produced in the graphite medium by the radiation
dosimetry.e: Calorimetry is the most fundamental method of absorbed dose measurement. A graphite calorimeter directly measures the temperature rise caused by radiation energy absorption. The absorbed dose is calculated from D = c × ΔT, where c is the specific heat capacity and ΔT is the temperature rise, typically only a few mK for a 1 Gy dose.

dosimetry.q: In the context of percentage depth dose (PDD), what is the primary reason PDD increases with increasing field size for photon beams?
dosimetry.a: Increased phantom scatter contribution from the larger irradiated volume of tissue
c: Increased phantom scatter contribution from the larger irradiated volume of tissue
Greater primary beam intensity transmitted through the flattening filter edges
Reduced inverse square law effect due to the larger geometric beam coverage area
Decreased attenuation coefficient of water at larger field size configurations
dosimetry.e: As field size increases, more volume of tissue is irradiated, increasing the amount of Compton scatter reaching the central axis point at depth. This additional phantom scatter increases the PDD, particularly at deeper points where scatter contribution is proportionally more significant.

dosimetry.q: What is the Bragg-Gray cavity theory requirement regarding the size of the air cavity in an ionisation chamber?
dosimetry.a: The cavity must be small enough not to perturb the charged particle fluence in the medium
c: The cavity must be small enough not to perturb the charged particle fluence in the medium
The cavity must be large enough to completely stop all charged particles passing through
The cavity volume must precisely match the atomic number of the surrounding phantom medium
The cavity must contain a medium with identical stopping power to the surrounding material
dosimetry.e: Bragg-Gray cavity theory requires that the cavity is small compared to the range of charged particles, so that the fluence of charged particles is not disturbed by its presence. Additionally, the dose deposited in the cavity must come entirely from charged particles crossing it (no photon interactions within the cavity).

dosimetry.q: CALC: The output of a linac is measured as 1.005 cGy/MU at dmax for a 10x10 cm² field. If the total scatter factor Scp for a 15x15 cm² field is 1.045, what is the expected output for the 15x15 cm² field?
dosimetry.a: 1.050 cGy/MU at the depth of maximum dose
c: 1.050 cGy/MU at the depth of maximum dose
1.095 cGy/MU at the depth of maximum dose
0.962 cGy/MU at the depth of maximum dose
1.150 cGy/MU at the depth of maximum dose
dosimetry.e: Output for 15x15 = reference output × Scp = 1.005 × 1.045 = 1.050 cGy/MU. The total scatter factor Scp accounts for both collimator scatter (Sc) and phantom scatter (Sp) and relates the output at any field size to the reference 10x10 cm² field output.

dosimetry.q: Which detector is recommended as the preferred detector in TRS-483 for small field output factor measurements?
dosimetry.a: An unshielded stereotactic diode or microDiamond detector with appropriate correction factors applied
c: An unshielded stereotactic diode or microDiamond detector with appropriate correction factors applied
A standard Farmer-type ionisation chamber with 0.6 cm³ active volume and no corrections needed
A large area parallel plate ionisation chamber with guard ring but no correction factors required
A radiochromic film stack of at least five films analysed without any field size corrections at all
dosimetry.e: TRS-483 recommends detectors with small sensitive volumes for small field output factor measurements, particularly unshielded diodes and synthetic microDiamond detectors. These are used with field-specific output correction factors to account for volume averaging and other perturbation effects.

dosimetry.q: What is the G-value in Fricke (ferrous sulphate) dosimetry and what does it represent?
dosimetry.a: The radiation chemical yield representing the number of Fe³⁺ ions produced per 100 eV absorbed
c: The radiation chemical yield representing the number of Fe³⁺ ions produced per 100 eV absorbed
The geometric correction factor applied to account for container wall attenuation effects
The gain factor of the spectrophotometer used to measure optical density of the solution
The gravitational settling coefficient for ferric ion precipitation in the dosimetry solution
dosimetry.e: The G-value in Fricke dosimetry represents the radiation chemical yield, defined as the number of ferric (Fe³⁺) ions produced per 100 eV of absorbed energy. For the standard Fricke solution, the G-value is approximately 15.5 ions per 100 eV for Co-60 gamma rays, and it shows slight LET dependence.

dosimetry.q: In electron beam dosimetry according to TRS-398, at what depth should the reference dose measurement be performed?
dosimetry.a: At the reference depth zref equal to 0.6R50 minus 0.1 cm in water
c: At the reference depth zref equal to 0.6R50 minus 0.1 cm in water
At the depth of maximum dose regardless of beam energy value
At a fixed depth of exactly 5 cm for all electron beam energies
At the practical range Rp for maximum electron beam penetration
dosimetry.e: TRS-398 specifies the reference depth for electron beams as zref = 0.6R50 - 0.1 (in cm), where R50 is the half-value depth in water. This depth is chosen because it is located in a region where the depth-dose gradient is not too steep and the mean energy is well characterised.

dosimetry.q: What is the primary purpose of the beam quality correction factor kQ in TRS-398 photon dosimetry?
dosimetry.a: To correct the chamber calibration from the cobalt-60 reference quality to the actual user beam quality
c: To correct the chamber calibration from the cobalt-60 reference quality to the actual user beam quality
To account for the daily temperature and pressure variations in the treatment room environment
To convert the absorbed dose from water to tissue equivalent material for treatment planning
To correct for the recombination of ions that occurs at very high instantaneous dose rate
dosimetry.e: The kQ factor converts the absorbed dose to water calibration coefficient ND,w obtained at the reference beam quality (typically Co-60) to the user's beam quality Q. It accounts for changes in the chamber's response due to differences in stopping power ratios, perturbation factors, and other beam quality dependent parameters.

dosimetry.q: CALC: A parallel plate ionisation chamber measures charge in an electron beam. The temperature is 24°C and pressure is 101.0 kPa. What is the temperature-pressure correction factor kTP using reference conditions of 20°C and 101.325 kPa?
dosimetry.a: 1.017 based on the standard reference conditions
c: 1.017 based on the standard reference conditions
0.987 based on the standard reference conditions
1.043 based on non-standard reference conditions
0.957 based on the standard reference conditions

dosimetry.q: What is the main advantage of using a diamond detector for relative dosimetry measurements in radiotherapy?
dosimetry.a: Near tissue equivalence with atomic number Z=6 and minimal energy dependence across beam qualities
c: Near tissue equivalence with atomic number Z=6 and minimal energy dependence across beam qualities
Complete independence from dose rate effects requiring no correction factors whatsoever
Extremely large sensitive volume that provides high signal to noise in all field sizes
Built-in real-time temperature compensation eliminating need for environmental corrections
dosimetry.e: Diamond has an atomic number of 6, close to the effective atomic number of water/tissue (~7.4), making it nearly tissue equivalent. This results in minimal energy dependence across different photon and electron beam energies. Modern synthetic single crystal diamond detectors also offer excellent spatial resolution due to their small sensitive volume.

dosimetry.q: In photon beam dosimetry, what is the tissue-phantom ratio (TPR) and why is it preferred over PDD in isocentric setups?
dosimetry.a: TPR is the dose ratio at depth to reference depth at constant SAD, removing inverse square dependence
c: TPR is the dose ratio at depth to reference depth at constant SAD, removing inverse square dependence
TPR is the absolute dose value at depth normalised to the surface dose measured in air at isocentre
TPR is the ratio of tissue dose to phantom dose measured simultaneously at the same depth value
TPR is the entrance dose divided by exit dose at a fixed source to surface distance beam setup
dosimetry.e: The tissue-phantom ratio (TPR) is defined as the ratio of dose at a given depth to dose at a reference depth, with both measurements at the same source-to-detector distance (SAD). Unlike PDD, TPR is independent of the source-to-surface distance, making it ideal for isocentric treatment setups commonly used in modern radiotherapy.

dosimetry.q: What characteristic of MOSFET dosimeters makes them particularly useful for in-vivo surface dose measurements?
dosimetry.a: Very thin sensitive volume of approximately 1 micrometre providing excellent spatial resolution
c: Very thin sensitive volume of approximately 1 micrometre providing excellent spatial resolution
Complete reusability with unlimited lifetime and no accumulated dose degradation ever
Perfect tissue equivalence across all photon energies without any calibration needed
Real-time dose rate readout without any physical connection to external equipment
dosimetry.e: MOSFETs have an extremely thin sensitive silicon dioxide layer (~1 µm), allowing measurement at very shallow depths. This makes them ideal for surface and skin dose measurements. However, they have limited lifetime due to accumulated threshold voltage shifts, and their sensitivity decreases with accumulated dose.

dosimetry.q: What is the dose buildup region in a megavoltage photon beam, and what physical process is primarily responsible?
dosimetry.a: The region from surface to dmax where dose increases due to forward-scattered secondary electron buildup
c: The region from surface to dmax where dose increases due to forward-scattered secondary electron buildup
The region beyond dmax where dose gradually decreases primarily due to beam hardening effects
The region near the beam edge where dose increases from collimator scattered photon contamination
The region at the surface only where dose increases due to backscattered photons from deep depths
dosimetry.e: The buildup region extends from the surface to the depth of maximum dose (dmax). It occurs because high-energy photons produce secondary electrons predominantly in the forward direction. As beam energy increases, electrons travel further before depositing dose, shifting dmax deeper. This effect provides clinical skin sparing in megavoltage therapy.

dosimetry.q: According to TRS-398, which beam quality specifier is used for high-energy photon beams?
dosimetry.a: TPR20,10 which is the tissue-phantom ratio at depths of 20 and 10 cm in water
c: TPR20,10 which is the tissue-phantom ratio at depths of 20 and 10 cm in water
PDD at 10 cm depth normalised to maximum dose for a 10x10 cm² field size
The mean photon energy in MeV calculated from the linear attenuation coefficient
The half-value layer measured in millimetres of aluminium at standard geometry
dosimetry.e: TRS-398 uses TPR20,10 as the beam quality specifier for high-energy photon beams. This parameter is defined as the ratio of absorbed doses at depths of 20 and 10 cm in water, for a 10x10 cm² field at an SCD of 100 cm. It is nearly independent of electron contamination and provides robust beam quality specification.

dosimetry.q: What is the Bragg peak and in which type of radiation therapy beam is it a dominant dosimetric feature?
dosimetry.a: A sharp dose maximum near end of range occurring in proton and heavy ion therapy beams
c: A sharp dose maximum near end of range occurring in proton and heavy ion therapy beams
A gradual dose increase at the beam entrance seen in megavoltage photon therapy beams
A dose reduction at depth characteristic of orthovoltage X-ray therapy beam treatments
A broad flat dose region in the middle depth of clinical electron therapy beam profiles
dosimetry.e: The Bragg peak is a pronounced peak in the depth-dose distribution near the end of the particle range, characteristic of proton and heavy charged particle beams. It occurs because the stopping power increases as the particle slows down (proportional to 1/v²), depositing most energy just before stopping.

dosimetry.q: CALC: An ionisation chamber raw reading is 25.2 nC. The ion recombination correction ks is 1.004, polarity correction kpol is 0.998, electrometer calibration kelec is 1.002, and kTP is 1.010. What is the fully corrected reading?
dosimetry.a: 25.56 nC after applying all four correction factors to the raw reading
c: 25.56 nC after applying all four correction factors to the raw reading
25.20 nC with no net change since correction factors cancel each other
24.85 nC after applying all four correction factors to the raw reading
26.12 nC after applying all four correction factors to the raw reading
dosimetry.e: MQ = Mraw × kTP × ks × kpol × kelec = 25.2 × 1.010 × 1.004 × 0.998 × 1.002 = 25.2 × 1.01405 = 25.55 ≈ 25.56 nC. Each correction factor addresses a specific influence quantity: temperature-pressure, ion recombination, polarity effect, and electrometer calibration respectively.

dosimetry.q: What is the primary difference between absolute dosimetry and relative dosimetry in clinical medical physics?
dosimetry.a: Absolute dosimetry determines dose in Gy under reference conditions while relative dosimetry measures dose ratios
c: Absolute dosimetry determines dose in Gy under reference conditions while relative dosimetry measures dose ratios
Absolute dosimetry uses only calorimeters while relative dosimetry uses only ionisation chambers as detectors
Absolute dosimetry is performed annually while relative dosimetry is performed during each patient treatment
Absolute dosimetry requires no calibration factors while relative dosimetry needs multiple calibrations applied
dosimetry.e: Absolute dosimetry determines the absorbed dose in Gray under reference conditions, typically using a calibrated ionisation chamber traceable to a primary standards laboratory. Relative dosimetry measures dose distributions normalised to a reference point, using detectors like diodes, film, or small volume chambers for beam profiles, PDDs, and output factors.

dosimetry.q: In South African clinical practice, which primary standards dosimetry laboratory provides ionisation chamber calibrations?
dosimetry.a: The National Metrology Institute of South Africa (NMISA) located in Pretoria
c: The National Metrology Institute of South Africa (NMISA) located in Pretoria
The South African Bureau of Standards (SABS) medical lab in Cape Town
The Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) in Johannesburg
The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) laboratory based in Vienna
dosimetry.e: NMISA (National Metrology Institute of South Africa) in Pretoria maintains the primary and secondary dosimetry standards for South Africa. They provide absorbed dose to water and air kerma calibrations for ionisation chambers used in radiotherapy, ensuring traceability to international primary standards through BIPM comparisons.

dosimetry.q: What is the main limitation of using a Farmer-type 0.6 cm³ ionisation chamber for small field dosimetry measurements?
dosimetry.a: Volume averaging effect where the large cavity averages dose over a region with steep dose gradients
c: Volume averaging effect where the large cavity averages dose over a region with steep dose gradients
Excessive sensitivity to radiation causing complete signal saturation at clinical dose rates used
Complete inability to detect any ionisation signal in photon beams smaller than 5 cm x 5 cm
Permanent radiation damage to the chamber that occurs only when used in small radiation fields
dosimetry.e: The 0.6 cm³ active volume of a Farmer chamber is too large for small fields, causing significant volume averaging where the measured signal is an average over a region with steep dose gradients. This leads to underestimation of the output factor. TRS-483 recommends detectors with much smaller volumes for field sizes below approximately 4x4 cm².

dosimetry.q: CALC: The collimator scatter factor Sc for a 6x6 cm² field is 0.970 and the phantom scatter factor Sp is 0.985. What is the total scatter factor Scp for this field size?
dosimetry.a: 0.955 obtained by multiplying collimator and phantom scatter factors together
c: 0.955 obtained by multiplying collimator and phantom scatter factors together
1.955 obtained by adding the collimator and phantom scatter factors together
0.985 obtained by using only the phantom scatter factor for the total scatter
0.978 obtained by taking the average of the two individual scatter factors
dosimetry.e: The total scatter factor Scp = Sc × Sp = 0.970 × 0.985 = 0.955. Sc accounts for scatter from the collimation system (head scatter) and is measured in air using a buildup cap. Sp accounts for scatter within the phantom and depends on field size at the measurement depth. Their product gives the total scatter factor.

dosimetry.q: Why is the polarity correction factor kpol measured and applied in ionisation chamber dosimetry?
dosimetry.a: To correct for the difference in collected charge magnitude when the chamber voltage polarity is reversed
c: To correct for the difference in collected charge magnitude when the chamber voltage polarity is reversed
To account for the magnetic field effects on the electron trajectories inside the ionisation chamber
To compensate for the directional angular dependence of the chamber response during beam measurements
To correct for charge leakage through the insulating stem between the chamber guard and the electrode
dosimetry.e: The polarity effect causes a difference in the magnitude of collected charge depending on the sign of the collecting voltage applied to the chamber. It arises from extra-cameral currents, Compton electron contributions, and stem irradiation effects. kpol = (|M+| + |M-|)/(2M), where M+ and M- are readings at positive and negative polarity.

dosimetry.q: What physical property makes lithium fluoride (LiF) a suitable TLD material for clinical dosimetry in radiotherapy?
dosimetry.a: Its effective atomic number of 8.2 is close to soft tissue providing near tissue equivalence
c: Its effective atomic number of 8.2 is close to soft tissue providing near tissue equivalence
Its extremely high atomic number provides maximum sensitivity to low energy photon beams
Its complete transparency to visible light allows direct optical density dose measurement
Its strong ferromagnetic properties enable contactless readout using magnetic resonance
dosimetry.e: LiF has an effective atomic number of approximately 8.2, which is close to that of soft tissue (Zeff ≈ 7.4).
