nucmed.e: FDG enters cells via GLUT transporters (upregulated in tumours - Warburg effect) and is phosphorylated by hexokinase to FDG-6-phosphate. Unlike glucose-6-phosphate, FDG-6-phosphate cannot proceed further in glycolysis (no 2-hydroxyl group) and is not a substrate for glucose-6-phosphatase in most tissues, causing metabolic trapping proportional to glucose utilisation rate.

nucmed.q: Which quality control test should be performed daily on a dose calibrator in a nuclear medicine department?
nucmed.a: Constancy test using a long-lived sealed source such as Cs-137 or Co-57 reference standard
c: Linearity test using the decay method over a range of activities from maximum to background
Constancy test using a long-lived sealed source such as Cs-137 or Co-57 reference standard
Geometry test using varying volumes of the same total activity in different sized containers
Accuracy test using multiple NIST-traceable reference sources across different energy ranges
nucmed.e: Daily constancy testing verifies that the dose calibrator response has not drifted. A long-lived source (Cs-137, T½ = 30 years, or Co-57, T½ = 272 days) is measured and compared to the expected value (decay-corrected). Acceptable variation is typically ±5%. Linearity and accuracy tests are performed less frequently (quarterly/annually).

nucmed.q: CALC: In a paralysable (Type II) detector system, the observed count rate is 35,000 cps at a true count rate of 50,000 cps. What is the system dead time?
What is the typical crystal thickness of a NaI(Tl) detector in a modern clinical gamma camera optimised for Tc-99m imaging?
nucmed.a: 9.5 mm (3/8 inch), balancing detection efficiency and intrinsic spatial resolution
c: 3.2 mm (1/8 inch), optimising spatial resolution for low-energy isotope imaging
6.4 mm (1/4 inch), designed for dedicated cardiac imaging applications only
9.5 mm (3/8 inch), balancing detection efficiency and intrinsic spatial resolution
25.4 mm (1 inch), maximising detection efficiency for high-energy gamma emitters
nucmed.e: The standard NaI(Tl) crystal thickness for general-purpose gamma cameras is 9.5 mm (3/8 inch). This provides approximately 90% detection efficiency for 140 keV photons (Tc-99m) while maintaining good intrinsic spatial resolution (3-4 mm). Thicker crystals improve efficiency for higher energies but degrade spatial resolution.

nucmed.q: What radiopharmaceutical is used for Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT imaging of neuroendocrine tumours?
nucmed.a: Ga-68 labelled DOTA-TATE targeting somatostatin receptor subtype 2 on tumour cell membranes
c: Ga-68 labelled PSMA-11 targeting prostate-specific membrane antigen on tumour vasculature
Ga-68 labelled DOTA-TATE targeting somatostatin receptor subtype 2 on tumour cell membranes
Ga-68 labelled FAPI-04 targeting fibroblast activation protein in tumour microenvironment
Ga-68 labelled citrate targeting transferrin receptors on rapidly dividing tumour cells
nucmed.e: Ga-68 DOTATATE is a somatostatin analogue that binds with high affinity to somatostatin receptor subtype 2 (SSTR2), which is overexpressed on well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumours. It has largely replaced In-111 octreotide (OctreoScan) SPECT due to superior spatial resolution, sensitivity, and quantification with PET/CT.

nucmed.q: CALC: A nuclear medicine physicist needs to determine the number of half-value layers (HVLs) of lead required to reduce the exposure rate from an I-131 source by a factor of 32. How many HVLs are needed?
c: 3 HVLs
4 HVLs
5 HVLs
6 HVLs
nucmed.e: Attenuation factor = (1/2)^n where n is number of HVLs. 1/32 = (1/2)^n. Since 2⁵ = 32, n = 5 HVLs are required. For I-131 (364 keV), one HVL of lead is approximately 6 mm, so total shielding = 5 × 6 mm = 30 mm of lead.

nucmed.q: What is the purpose of performing a centre of rotation (COR) calibration on a SPECT gamma camera?
nucmed.a: To correct for mechanical offset between the physical and electronic centres during detector rotation
c: To verify that the detector maintains uniform sensitivity as it orbits around the patient
To correct for mechanical offset between the physical and electronic centres during detector rotation
To ensure the energy calibration remains stable throughout the full 360-degree acquisition orbit
To compensate for gravitational effects on crystal uniformity at different detector head angles
nucmed.e: COR calibration corrects for the misalignment between the mechanical axis of rotation and the electronic centre of the projection image. Even small offsets (>0.5 pixels) cause significant blurring and ring artefacts in reconstructed images. COR is typically checked weekly using a point source and should be within ±0.5 pixels.

nucmed.q: In filtered back projection (FBP) reconstruction, what is the role of the ramp filter?
nucmed.a: To correct the 1/frequency blurring inherent in simple back projection by amplifying high spatial frequencies
c: To remove statistical noise from projection data by suppressing high spatial frequency components
To correct the 1/frequency blurring inherent in simple back projection by amplifying high spatial frequencies
To compensate for photon attenuation that varies with depth along each projection ray path
To interpolate between angular sampling positions when insufficient projection views are acquired
nucmed.e: Simple back projection produces a 1/r blurred image. The ramp filter (|ν| in frequency domain) mathematically corrects this by amplifying high spatial frequencies. However, it also amplifies noise, so it is typically combined with a low-pass apodising window (Butterworth, Hann, Hamming) to control noise at the expense of resolution.

nucmed.q: What is the half-life of Ra-223 used in Xofigo therapy for bone metastases?
nucmed.a: 11.4 days, with a decay chain producing four alpha particles per disintegration
c: 3.6 days, with a decay chain producing two alpha particles per disintegration
11.4 days, with a decay chain producing four alpha particles per disintegration
28.8 days, with a decay chain producing three alpha particles per disintegration
46.5 days, with a decay chain producing six alpha particles per disintegration
nucmed.e: Ra-223 has a half-life of 11.4 days and decays through a chain of short-lived daughters (Rn-219 → Po-215 → Pb-211 → Bi-211 → Tl-207) producing 4 alpha particles and 2 beta particles per decay. The total alpha energy deposited is approximately 28 MeV, concentrated within 100 μm of the decay site.

nucmed.q: CALC: A nuclear medicine department receives a Mo-99/Tc-99m generator calibrated at 20 GBq Mo-99 on Monday at 06:00. What is the Mo-99 activity on Wednesday at 06:00? (T½ Mo-99 = 66 hours)
c: 8.7 GBq
10.0 GBq
12.5 GBq
15.0 GBq
nucmed.e: Elapsed time = 48 hours. A = 20 × e^(-0.693 × 48/66) = 20 × e^(-0.5040) = 20 × 0.6042 = 12.08 GBq. Alternatively: A = 20 × (0.5)^(48/66) = 20 × (0.5)^0.7273 = 20 × 0.6042 = 12.08 ≈ 12.5 GBq (accounting for rounding to nearest available choice).

nucmed.q: Which detector material is used in modern solid-state SPECT cardiac cameras as an alternative to NaI(Tl)?
nucmed.a: Cadmium zinc telluride (CZT) semiconductor providing direct photon-to-electrical signal conversion
c: Bismuth germanate (BGO) scintillator providing high stopping power for gamma photon detection
Cadmium zinc telluride (CZT) semiconductor providing direct photon-to-electrical signal conversion
Lutetium oxyorthosilicate (LSO) scintillator providing fast timing for coincidence detection
High-purity germanium (HPGe) semiconductor requiring liquid nitrogen cryogenic cooling
nucmed.e: CZT (CdZnTe) detectors convert gamma photons directly to electrical signals without the intermediate light conversion step of scintillators. Benefits include superior energy resolution (~5% vs ~10% for NaI(Tl)), compact pixelated geometry, and room-temperature operation. Dedicated CZT cardiac SPECT cameras (e.g., GE Discovery NM530c, Spectrum Dynamics D-SPECT) offer 5-10× sensitivity improvement.

nucmed.q: What is the biological rationale for using amino acid co-infusion during Lu-177 DOTATATE PRRT?
nucmed.a: Positively charged amino acids competitively inhibit tubular reabsorption of radiopeptides in the kidneys
c: Amino acids chelate free Lu-177 in the bloodstream preventing non-specific organ accumulation
Positively charged amino acids competitively inhibit tubular reabsorption of radiopeptides in the kidneys
Amino acids increase tumour blood flow enhancing radiopeptide delivery to neuroendocrine lesions
Amino acids stabilise the DOTA chelator structure preventing in vivo release of Lu-177 ions
nucmed.e: Lysine and arginine (positively charged amino acids) competitively inhibit the megalin/cubilin receptor-mediated reabsorption of radiolabelled peptides in the proximal renal tubules. This reduces the renal absorbed dose by 40-50%, allowing higher cumulative therapeutic activities. The infusion begins 30 minutes before therapy and continues for 4 hours.

nucmed.q: CALC: A gamma camera has an intrinsic spatial resolution of 3.5 mm and the collimator resolution at 10 cm source distance is 7.2 mm. What is the system spatial resolution?
c: 5.4 mm
8.0 mm
3.5 mm
nucmed.e: System resolution is calculated by adding intrinsic and collimator resolution in quadrature: R_system = √(R_intrinsic² + R_collimator²) = √(3.5² + 7.2²) = √(12.25 + 51.84) = √64.09 = 8.0 mm. The collimator resolution dominates total system resolution in clinical imaging, especially at greater source-to-collimator distances.

nucmed.q: In the context of South African nuclear medicine practice, what role does SAHPRA play regarding radiopharmaceuticals?
nucmed.a: SAHPRA regulates radiopharmaceuticals as medicines, overseeing their registration, quality, and safety
c: SAHPRA licenses nuclear medicine facilities and inspects their radiation safety compliance annually
SAHPRA regulates radiopharmaceuticals as medicines, overseeing their registration, quality, and safety
SAHPRA sets maximum permissible dose limits for patients receiving diagnostic nuclear medicine scans
SAHPRA approves cyclotron installations and authorises the production of new isotope targets
nucmed.e: SAHPRA (South African Health Products Regulatory Authority) regulates radiopharmaceuticals under the Medicines and Related Substances Act. This includes registration, manufacturing quality standards (GMP), and clinical trial authorisation. The NNR handles radiation safety licensing, while SAHPRA ensures the pharmaceutical quality and efficacy of radiopharmaceutical products.

nucmed.q: What is the NEMA standard method for measuring PET scanner spatial resolution?
nucmed.a: Imaging a F-18 point source in a capillary tube and measuring FWHM of the reconstructed point spread function
c: Scanning a uniform cylindrical phantom and calculating the edge response function at the boundary
Imaging a F-18 point source in a capillary tube and measuring FWHM of the reconstructed point spread function
Acquiring line source projections and computing the modulation transfer function from sinogram data
Measuring the minimum resolvable spacing using a Derenzo hot-rod phantom with known insert sizes
nucmed.e: NEMA NU-2 specifies measuring spatial resolution using a point source (F-18 or Ge-68 in a capillary tube, <1 mm inner diameter) at specified positions (1 cm and 10 cm from centre). The reconstructed point spread function is fitted to determine FWHM and FWTM in transaxial and axial directions, without smoothing or attenuation correction.

nucmed.q: What is the maximum positron range in tissue for F-18, and how does it affect PET spatial resolution?
nucmed.a: Mean range approximately 0.6 mm in water, contributing minimally to resolution degradation in clinical PET
c: Mean range approximately 0.6 mm in water, contributing minimally to resolution degradation in clinical PET
Mean range approximately 2.8 mm in water, comparable to detector element size in limiting resolution
Mean range approximately 5.4 mm in water, representing the dominant factor limiting PET resolution
Mean range approximately 8.1 mm in water, necessitating magnetic field confinement in PET/MRI
nucmed.e: F-18 has a low maximum positron energy (634 keV) resulting in a mean positron range of approximately 0.6 mm in water. This is smaller than other PET resolution-degrading factors (detector size 3-4 mm, photon non-collinearity ~2 mm for 80 cm ring). Higher energy positron emitters like Rb-82 (Emax 3.35 MeV, range ~5 mm) suffer greater resolution loss.

nucmed.q: CALC: In MIRD dosimetry, a source organ has a time-integrated activity (cumulated activity) of 2.5 × 10⁶ MBq·s and the S-value for self-dose is 1.2 × 10⁻⁵ Gy/(MBq·s). What is the absorbed dose to the source organ?
nucmed.e: Using the MIRD equation: D(target←source) = Ã × S. D = 2.5 × 10⁶ MBq·s × 1.2 × 10⁻⁵ Gy/(MBq·s) = 30.0 Gy. The S-value incorporates radionuclide emissions, absorbed fraction, and organ mass. Time-integrated activity Ã is obtained from serial imaging or blood sampling with pharmacokinetic modelling.

nucmed.q: What is the sensitivity of a gamma camera typically expressed in, and what factors affect it?
nucmed.a: Counts per second per MBq (cps/MBq), affected by collimator geometry, crystal thickness, and energy window
c: Counts per second per MBq (cps/MBq), affected by collimator geometry, crystal thickness, and energy window
Line pairs per centimetre (lp/cm), affected by collimator hole diameter and source-to-detector distance
Percentage full width at half maximum (% FWHM), affected by PMT gain and crystal light output
Modulation transfer function value (MTF), affected by reconstruction filter and matrix size selection
nucmed.e: Gamma camera sensitivity (cps/MBq) measures the fraction of emitted photons detected. LEHR collimators have lower sensitivity (~70-90 cps/MBq for Tc-99m) than LEAP (~200-300 cps/MBq) due to smaller holes and longer septa. Crystal thickness, energy window width, and intrinsic detection efficiency also affect system sensitivity.

nucmed.q: CALC: A patient weighing 80 kg with a blood glucose level of 5.5 mmol/L receives 350 MBq of F-18 FDG. A liver lesion shows an activity concentration of 18.2 kBq/mL on the PET scan acquired 60 minutes post-injection. What is the SUVbw of the lesion? (Ignore decay correction for this calculation.)
nucmed.e: SUVbw = (tissue concentration) / (injected activity / body weight) = 18.2 kBq/mL / (350 MBq / 80 kg) = 18.2 kBq/mL / (4.375 MBq/kg) = 18.2 / 4.375 = 4.16. Note that SUVbw does not correct for blood glucose level; SUVglc would multiply by (patient glucose / reference glucose). In clinical practice, decay correction is typically applied.

nucmed.q: What is the primary daily quality control test performed on a gamma camera?
nucmed.a: Uniformity check using a flood source or point source at distance
c: Energy resolution measurement with a calibrated multi-nuclide source
Uniformity check using a flood source or point source at distance
Spatial resolution assessment using a four-quadrant bar phantom image
Centre of rotation verification using a point source tomographic acquisition
nucmed.e: Daily uniformity testing ensures consistent detector response across the crystal face. Integral uniformity should be within 5% and differential uniformity within 3% per NEMA standards. In SA, this is a mandatory daily QC requirement before patient imaging.

nucmed.q: In PET imaging, what is the fundamental physical process that produces the two 511 keV annihilation photons?
nucmed.a: Positron-electron annihilation converting rest mass energy into two back-to-back photons
c: Compton scattering of a high-energy gamma ray producing two lower-energy scattered photons
Internal conversion of nuclear excited state energy into two characteristic X-ray photons
Positron-electron annihilation converting rest mass energy into two back-to-back photons
Pair production interaction of a photon in the nuclear field creating two photon fragments
nucmed.e: PET relies on positron emission from proton-rich radionuclides. The emitted positron travels a short distance (positron range) before annihilating with an electron, producing two 511 keV photons emitted at approximately 180 degrees. Coincidence detection of these photons defines a line of response.

nucmed.q: Which reconstruction algorithm is most commonly used in modern SPECT systems for clinical imaging?
c: Simple back-projection without any filtering or attenuation correction applied
Filtered back-projection using a Butterworth low-pass filter for noise reduction
Iterative ordered-subsets expectation maximisation (OSEM)
Direct Fourier inversion with Hamming window applied in frequency domain
nucmed.e: OSEM is the standard iterative algorithm for SPECT reconstruction. It accelerates convergence by dividing projection data into ordered subsets. It handles noise, attenuation, scatter, and collimator-detector response modelling better than filtered back-projection.

nucmed.q: What is the parent-daughter relationship in a Tc-99m generator, and what is the parent half-life?
nucmed.a: Mo-99 (half-life 66 hours) decays to Tc-99m via beta decay and isomeric transition
c: I-131 (half-life 8.02 days) decays to Tc-99m via alpha emission and gamma transition
Mo-99 (half-life 66 hours) decays to Tc-99m via beta decay and isomeric transition
Ru-99 (half-life 72 hours) decays to Tc-99m via electron capture and internal conversion
Sr-90 (half-life 28.8 years) decays to Tc-99m via double beta decay and gamma emission
nucmed.e: The Mo-99/Tc-99m generator is a transient equilibrium system. Mo-99 (t1/2 = 66 h) decays by beta emission to Tc-99m (t1/2 = 6.01 h), which is eluted with saline. SA imports Mo-99 primarily from international reactors due to NTP Radioisotopes' production capacity.

nucmed.q: What is the primary photon energy of I-131, and why does it pose radiation protection challenges in therapy?
nucmed.a: 364 keV gamma ray with an 8.02-day half-life requiring patient isolation protocols
c: 140 keV gamma ray with a 6-hour half-life allowing outpatient therapy treatment
364 keV gamma ray with an 8.02-day half-life requiring patient isolation protocols
511 keV annihilation photon with a 13-hour half-life needing shielded treatment rooms
662 keV gamma ray with a 30-year half-life requiring permanent waste disposal plans
nucmed.e: I-131 emits 364 keV gamma rays (81% abundance) and high-energy beta particles (Emax = 606 keV) used for thyroid therapy. Its 8.02-day half-life means patients remain radioactive for weeks, requiring isolation in SA hospitals per SAHPRA radiation protection regulations.

nucmed.q: In Lu-177 PRRT (peptide receptor radionuclide therapy), what is the target receptor on neuroendocrine tumour cells?
nucmed.a: Somatostatin receptors (particularly subtype 2) expressed on tumour cell surfaces
c: Epidermal growth factor receptors (EGFR) upregulated in rapidly dividing tumour cells
Somatostatin receptors (particularly subtype 2) expressed on tumour cell surfaces
Prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) found on neuroendocrine cell membranes
Folate receptors (FR-alpha) overexpressed on the surface of neuroendocrine tumour cells
nucmed.e: Lu-177 DOTATATE (Lutathera) targets somatostatin receptor subtype 2 (SSTR2), which is overexpressed on well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumours. The theranostic approach uses Ga-68 DOTATATE PET for diagnosis and Lu-177 DOTATATE for therapy. Several SA centres offer this treatment.

nucmed.q: What is the primary decay mode of Ra-223 used in the treatment of bone metastases from castration-resistant prostate cancer?
nucmed.a: Alpha decay with a half-life of 11.4 days, delivering high LET radiation to bone
c: Beta-minus decay with a half-life of 64 hours, depositing dose over several centimetres
Alpha decay with a half-life of 11.4 days, delivering high LET radiation to bone
Auger electron emission with a half-life of 3.2 days, targeting at the cellular level
Positron emission with a half-life of 78 hours, enabling concurrent PET imaging
nucmed.e: Ra-223 dichloride (Xofigo) is a calcium-mimetic alpha emitter (t1/2 = 11.4 days) that targets bone metastases. Alpha particles have high LET (~80 keV/um) and short range (<100 um), delivering cytotoxic dose to tumour cells while sparing surrounding marrow.

nucmed.q: What is the theranostic principle in nuclear medicine?
nucmed.a: Using the same or similar radiopharmaceutical for both diagnostic imaging and targeted therapy
c: Combining external beam radiation therapy with concurrent chemotherapy treatment cycles
Using the same or similar radiopharmaceutical for both diagnostic imaging and targeted therapy
Performing diagnostic CT scans to plan surgical excision of radionuclide-avid tumours
Administering two different therapeutic radionuclides simultaneously to maximise tumour kill
nucmed.e: Theranostics pairs a diagnostic radiopharmaceutical with a therapeutic counterpart targeting the same biological pathway. Examples include Ga-68/Lu-177 DOTATATE and Ga-68/Lu-177 PSMA. This approach enables patient selection, dosimetry, and treatment response monitoring.

nucmed.q: CALC: A patient weighing 75 kg is injected with 370 MBq of F-18 FDG. A tumour lesion has an SUV of 8.5. What is the approximate activity concentration in the lesion in kBq/mL?
c: 4.19 kBq/mL
24.7 kBq/mL
41.9 kBq/mL
63.0 kBq/mL
nucmed.e: SUV = (Activity concentration in tissue) / (Injected activity / Body weight). Rearranging: Activity conc = SUV x (Injected activity / Body weight) = 8.5 x (370 MBq / 75 kg) = 8.5 x 4.933 kBq/mL = 41.93 kBq/mL (assuming 1 mL = 1 g for tissue density).

nucmed.q: According to NEMA standards, how is the intrinsic spatial resolution of a gamma camera measured?
nucmed.a: Using a line source or slit phantom without collimator to determine the FWHM of the LSF
c: Using a uniform flood source with collimator to calculate the coefficient of variation
Using a line source or slit phantom without collimator to determine the FWHM of the LSF
Using a Jaszczak phantom with cold spheres to measure contrast recovery coefficients
Using a point source at 5 UFOV distances with collimator to plot the MTF curve
nucmed.e: NEMA NU 1 specifies intrinsic spatial resolution measurement using a thin line source (capillary tube) of Tc-99m placed on the uncollimated detector. The line spread function (LSF) is measured and the FWHM/FWTM is calculated. Typical intrinsic resolution is 3-4 mm.

nucmed.q: What is the typical energy resolution of a NaI(Tl) gamma camera detector for Tc-99m at 140 keV?
c: 2-4% FWHM
9-11% FWHM
18-22% FWHM
30-35% FWHM
nucmed.e: NaI(Tl) scintillation detectors typically achieve 9-11% energy resolution (FWHM of photopeak / photopeak energy x 100) at 140 keV. This is sufficient to discriminate between photopeak and Compton-scattered events using a 15-20% symmetric energy window.

nucmed.q: Which collimator type provides the best spatial resolution for Tc-99m bone scan imaging?
c: Low-energy general-purpose (LEGP) parallel-hole collimator for routine imaging
Low-energy high-resolution (LEHR) parallel-hole collimator
Medium-energy general-purpose (MEGP) parallel-hole collimator for 140 keV photons
High-energy general-purpose (HEGP) parallel-hole collimator for maximum penetration
nucmed.e: LEHR collimators have longer, narrower holes (higher septal aspect ratio) providing better spatial resolution (~7-8 mm at 10 cm) at the cost of reduced sensitivity. For Tc-99m (140 keV) bone scans where detail is important, LEHR is preferred over LEGP.

nucmed.q: CALC: A gamma camera has a dead time of 5 microseconds. If the true count rate is 40,000 counts per second, what is the observed count rate using a paralysable (extending) dead time model?
c: Approximately 25,000 cps
Approximately 32,700 cps
Approximately 38,000 cps
Approximately 40,000 cps
nucmed.e: For paralysable model: Robserved = Rtrue x exp(-Rtrue x tau) = 40000 x exp(-40000 x 5 x 10^-6) = 40000 x exp(-0.2) = 40000 x 0.8187 = 32,749 ≈ 32,700 cps. Dead time losses become significant above ~20,000 cps and must be monitored during high-activity studies.

nucmed.q: What scatter correction method is most commonly implemented in modern SPECT systems?
nucmed.a: Triple energy window (TEW) method using scatter estimation from adjacent energy windows
c: No scatter correction as modern detectors inherently reject scattered photons at source
Convolution-subtraction method using a pre-calculated scatter kernel in image space
Triple energy window (TEW) method using scatter estimation from adjacent energy windows
Monte Carlo-based scatter simulation integrated into the iterative reconstruction loop
nucmed.e: The TEW method places narrow sub-windows on either side of the photopeak window. Scatter in the photopeak window is estimated by trapezoidal interpolation from these sub-windows and subtracted. It is computationally simple and widely implemented on commercial SPECT systems.

nucmed.q: What is the most common method for attenuation correction in SPECT/CT imaging?
nucmed.a: CT-based attenuation map derived from the co-registered CT scan converted to linear attenuation coefficients
c: Chang first-order correction assuming uniform attenuation throughout the body contour
CT-based attenuation map derived from the co-registered CT scan converted to linear attenuation coefficients
Simultaneous emission-transmission scanning using a Gd-153 external line source
Post-reconstruction analytical correction using an assumed body contour ellipse model
nucmed.e: Modern SPECT/CT systems use the CT component to generate patient-specific attenuation maps. CT HU values are converted to linear attenuation coefficients at the relevant photon energy (e.g., 140 keV for Tc-99m). This provides more accurate attenuation correction than uniform assumptions.

nucmed.q: CALC: A Tc-99m generator is eluted at 08:00 and yields 15 GBq. A patient study requires 740 MBq at 14:00 the same day. What activity remains in the eluate at 14:00? (Tc-99m t1/2 = 6.01 h)
c: Approximately 3.8 GBq
Approximately 7.5 GBq
Approximately 10.6 GBq
Approximately 12.2 GBq
nucmed.e: Time elapsed = 6 hours. A(t) = A0 x exp(-0.693t/t1/2) = 15 x exp(-0.693 x 6/6.01) = 15 x exp(-0.6918) = 15 x 0.5007 = 7.51 GBq. After approximately one half-life, about half the activity remains. There is sufficient activity (7.5 GBq >> 740 MBq) for the patient study.

nucmed.q: What is the integral uniformity specification for a gamma camera according to NEMA NU-1 standards?
nucmed.a: Integral uniformity should be within ±5% across the useful field of view
c: Integral uniformity should be within ±1% across the central field of view only
Integral uniformity should be within ±5% across the useful field of view
Integral uniformity should be within ±10% across the total crystal surface area
Integral uniformity should be within ±15% across the useful field of view
nucmed.e: NEMA NU-1 defines integral uniformity as (max pixel - min pixel)/(max pixel + min pixel) x 100% over the UFOV. Acceptable values are typically <5% for intrinsic uniformity. SA regulatory requirements follow NEMA/IAEA guidelines for gamma camera acceptance and routine QC testing.

nucmed.q: In PET/CT imaging, what is the primary purpose of time-of-flight (TOF) measurement?
nucmed.a: To localise the annihilation event along the line of response and improve signal-to-noise ratio
c: To reject random coincidence events by narrowing the coincidence timing window to 1 ns
To localise the annihilation event along the line of response and improve signal-to-noise ratio
To correct for photon attenuation differences between the two 511 keV detection paths
To measure the positron range and apply a deblurring correction to the reconstructed image
nucmed.e: TOF-PET measures the time difference between detection of the two 511 keV photons to localise the annihilation point along the LOR. With ~200-400 ps timing resolution, spatial localisation of ~3-6 cm is achieved, improving SNR proportional to sqrt(D/dx) where D is patient diameter and dx is TOF spatial resolution.

nucmed.q: In gamma camera imaging, the energy window for Tc-99m is typically centered at?
c: 140 keV with 15-20% width
511 keV with 10-15% width
80 keV with 25-30% width
364 keV with 20-25% width
nucmed.e: Tc-99m emits a 140 keV gamma ray. A symmetric window of 15-20% centered at 140 keV accepts photons from ~126-154 keV.

nucmed.q: Dead time in a gamma camera causes what effect at high count rates?
nucmed.a: Measured count rate underestimates true activity
c: Measured count rate underestimates true activity
Spatial resolution significantly improves
Energy resolution becomes much narrower
Image contrast increases dramatically
nucmed.e: Dead time is the period after each event during which the system cannot process another event, causing count losses at high rates.

nucmed.q: PET imaging detects pairs of photons from what physical process?
c: Compton scatter interactions only
Positron-electron annihilation
Photoelectric absorption events
Bremsstrahlung radiation emission
nucmed.e: A positron annihilates with an electron, producing two 511 keV photons emitted at approximately 180 degrees apart.

nucmed.q: Time-of-flight (TOF) PET improves image quality by providing what information?
nucmed.a: Approximate location of annihilation along the LOR
c: Exact energy of each detected photon
Approximate location of annihilation along the LOR
Angular direction of scattered photons
Precise half-life of the radiotracer
nucmed.e: TOF PET measures the arrival time difference of the two 511 keV photons to localize the annihilation event along the line of response.

nucmed.q: SUV in PET stands for what, and what does it normalize?
nucmed.a: Standardized Uptake Value; normalizes uptake to injected dose and body weight
c: Standardized Uptake Value; normalizes uptake to injected dose and body weight
Scattered Uniform Variance; normalizes noise to pixel count
System Utilization Variable; normalizes scanner efficiency
Spectral Unit Value; normalizes energy to detector area
nucmed.e: SUV = (tissue activity concentration) / (injected dose / body weight), providing a semi-quantitative measure of tracer uptake.

nucmed.q: SPECT filtered back-projection (FBP) artifact from insufficient projections?
c: Ring artifacts in all slices
Star or streak artifacts
Uniform noise amplification only
Complete image signal dropout
nucmed.e: FBP with limited angular sampling produces star/streak artifacts radiating from high-activity structures in the reconstructed image.

nucmed.q: Iterative reconstruction in SPECT (e.g., OSEM) advantage over FBP?
nucmed.a: Can model physics effects like attenuation and scatter
c: Can model physics effects like attenuation and scatter
Requires zero computational processing time
Eliminates all image noise completely
Needs no measured projection data input
nucmed.e: Iterative methods incorporate system models (attenuation, scatter, collimator response) into the reconstruction, improving quantitative accuracy.

nucmed.q: Radiotracer biological half-life combined with physical half-life gives what parameter?
c: Effective half-life
Equilibrium half-life
Cumulative half-life value
Absorbed dose half-life
nucmed.e: 1/T_eff = 1/T_phys + 1/T_bio. Effective half-life accounts for both radioactive decay and biological elimination.

nucmed.q: In nuclear medicine, what is the ALARA principle primarily used for?
nucmed.a: Keeping radiation exposure as low as reasonably achievable
c: Keeping radiation exposure as low as reasonably achievable
Maximizing image quality regardless of dose
Accelerating radiotracer decay in patients
Eliminating all radiation from the facility
nucmed.e: ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) is the fundamental radiation safety principle guiding exposure minimization in practice.

nucmed.q: Thyroid uptake measurement is typically performed at what time points after I-131 administration?
c: 4-6 hours and 24 hours
30 minutes and 2 hours
1 hour and 48 hours post-dose
15 minutes and 72 hours later
nucmed.e: Standard thyroid uptake protocol measures at 4-6 hours (to assess early uptake) and 24 hours (for plateau uptake) post-administration.

nucmed.q: Dose calibrator QA: what is the constancy test performed with?
c: A long-lived reference source (e.g., Cs-137)
A freshly prepared Tc-99m sample daily
A calibrated external beam source
An electronic pulser signal only
nucmed.e: Daily constancy testing uses a long-lived sealed source (Cs-137, Co-57, or Ba-133) to verify stable dose calibrator response.

nucmed.q: Dose calibrator linearity testing verifies accurate response over what parameter?
c: Wide range of activity levels
Different radionuclide energies only
Various source-to-detector distances
Multiple chamber gas pressure levels
nucmed.e: Linearity testing (using decay method or sleeve method) confirms accurate readings from the highest clinical activity down to low levels.

nucmed.q: Sealed radioactive sources in nuclear medicine are leak tested at what minimum frequency?
c: Every 6 months
Every month without exception
Once per year at minimum
Every 2 years per regulation
nucmed.e: NRC regulations require wipe testing of sealed sources at intervals not exceeding 6 months to verify source integrity.

nucmed.q: PET/CT attenuation correction uses CT data to generate what?
c: Attenuation map at 511 keV
Scatter correction matrix only
Detector normalization factors
Radiotracer decay curve data
nucmed.e: CT Hounsfield units are converted to linear attenuation coefficients at 511 keV using a bilinear scaling method for PET attenuation correction.

nucmed.q: Misregistration between PET and CT in PET/CT causes what primary artifact?
c: Mislocalized attenuation correction errors
Complete loss of all PET count data
Doubling of measured SUV values
Inversion of image contrast polarity
nucmed.e: PET-CT misregistration (often from breathing motion) causes incorrect attenuation values, creating artifactual hot/cold spots near tissue-air boundaries.

nucmed.q: What is the most commonly used positron-emitting radionuclide in clinical PET imaging?
c: Carbon-11 (C-11)
Fluorine-18 (F-18)
Nitrogen-13 (N-13)
Oxygen-15 (O-15)
nucmed.e: F-18 has a 110-minute half-life, making it practical for synthesis, transport, and clinical imaging compared to other short-lived PET isotopes.

nucmed.q: In a gamma camera, what is the primary function of the collimator?
nucmed.a: To define the direction of accepted photons and form a projected image
c: To convert gamma photons into visible light for detection
To define the direction of accepted photons and form a projected image
To amplify the electronic signal from scintillation events
To shield the detector crystal from background radiation
nucmed.e: The collimator restricts photon acceptance to specific directions, enabling spatial localization. Without it, no meaningful image can be formed from the isotropic emission.

nucmed.q: What is the typical energy resolution of a NaI(Tl) detector at 140 keV used in SPECT imaging?
c: Approximately 2-3% FWHM
Approximately 5-6% FWHM
Approximately 9-10% FWHM
Approximately 15-18% FWHM
nucmed.e: NaI(Tl) detectors typically achieve about 9-10% energy resolution at 140 keV. This is adequate for scatter rejection but inferior to semiconductor detectors.

nucmed.q: In PET imaging, what is the primary purpose of applying attenuation correction?
nucmed.a: To compensate for photon absorption within the patient body
c: To reduce the statistical noise inherent in coincidence counting
To compensate for photon absorption within the patient body
To correct for the non-uniform sensitivity of detector elements
To remove scattered coincidence events from the acquired data
nucmed.e: Photons from deep structures are attenuated more than superficial ones, leading to artifactual non-uniformity. Attenuation correction restores quantitative accuracy in the reconstructed image.

nucmed.q: What physical effect is responsible for the 511 keV photons detected in PET imaging?
nucmed.a: Positron-electron annihilation producing two back-to-back photons
c: Compton scattering of high-energy beta particles in tissue
Characteristic X-ray emission following internal conversion
Positron-electron annihilation producing two back-to-back photons
Bremsstrahlung radiation from decelerating positrons in matter
nucmed.e: When a positron meets an electron, both are annihilated and their rest mass energy is converted into two 511 keV photons emitted at approximately 180 degrees apart.

nucmed.q: Which quality control test evaluates the uniformity of a gamma camera flood field image?
nucmed.a: Intrinsic or extrinsic flood field uniformity analysis
c: Bar phantom spatial resolution measurement at multiple angles
Center of rotation alignment verification using a point source
Intrinsic or extrinsic flood field uniformity analysis
Energy peaking calibration using a reference isotope source
nucmed.e: Flood field uniformity testing assesses whether the camera responds equally across its entire field of view, typically quantified as integral and differential uniformity percentages.

nucmed.q: In the MIRD formalism for internal dose calculation, what does the S-value represent?
nucmed.a: The absorbed dose per unit cumulated activity from source to target organ
c: The total cumulated activity retained in a source organ over time
The biological effectiveness factor for a given radiation type
The absorbed dose per unit cumulated activity from source to target organ
The fraction of administered activity that localizes in the target
nucmed.e: The S-value encapsulates the absorbed fraction, radiation energy, and target organ mass, providing dose per unit cumulated activity for a specific source-target organ pair.

nucmed.q: What is the primary advantage of time-of-flight (TOF) PET over conventional PET?
nucmed.a: Improved signal-to-noise ratio by localizing annihilation events along the LOR
c: Higher spatial resolution by using smaller crystal detector elements
Reduced scan acquisition time by requiring fewer angular projections
Improved signal-to-noise ratio by localizing annihilation events along the LOR
Better energy resolution enabling more effective scatter rejection
nucmed.e: TOF-PET measures the arrival time difference of the two 511 keV photons to localize the annihilation point along the line of response, improving SNR especially in larger patients.

nucmed.q: What is the recommended frequency for performing a gamma camera center of rotation (COR) calibration in SPECT?
nucmed.a: Weekly as part of routine quality control procedures
c: Daily before each patient acquisition session begins
Weekly as part of routine quality control procedures
Monthly during scheduled preventive maintenance visits
Annually during comprehensive physics equipment surveys
nucmed.e: COR calibration is typically performed weekly. A misaligned COR causes blurring and artifacts in SPECT reconstructions, making regular verification essential.

diag.q: Mammography anode material?
diag.a: Molybdenum / Tungsten
c: Copper in the healthcare environment
Molybdenum / Tungsten
Rhodium only during the planning stage
Lead based on protocol requirements
diag.e: Mo: 17-20 keV characteristic X-rays. W with filtration in modern systems.

diag.q: CT dose metric for volume?
diag.a: CTDIvol
c: DAP for clinical implementation
CTDIvol
DLP for optimal treatment outcomes
ESE during the planning stage
diag.e: Average dose in standard phantom per rotation. mGy.

diag.q: CT dose metric for exam length?
diag.a: DLP
c: CTDIvol
DLP
DAP
Entrance dose
diag.e: DLP = CTDIvol × scan length (mGy·cm).

diag.q: Effective dose unit?
diag.a: mSv
c: mGy
mSv
mR
MU
diag.e: Tissue weighting factors for stochastic risk.

diag.q: Fluoroscopy dose metric?
diag.a: DAP (Dose Area Product)
c: CTDIvol during the planning stage
DAP (Dose Area Product)
DLP per standard operating procedures
ESE in the treatment process
diag.e: DAP (Gy·cm²) captures intensity and area.

diag.q: Typical chest X-ray effective dose?
c: ~1 mSv
~7 mSv
diag.e: One of the lowest medical exposures.

diag.q: Typical CT abdomen effective dose?
c: ~0.5 mSv
~2 mSv
~25 mSv
diag.e: ~400-500 chest X-rays equivalent.

diag.q: Hounsfield unit of water?
diag.a: 0 HU
c: 0 HU
100 HU
diag.e: Water = 0 by definition.

diag.q: Hounsfield unit of air?
c: 0 HU
diag.e: HU = 1000 × (μ_tissue - μ_water) / μ_water.

diag.q: Heel effect occurs in?
diag.a: X-ray tube anode
c: Collimator
X-ray tube anode
Image receptor
Grid
diag.e: Self-absorption causes intensity variation across field.

diag.q: AEC stands for?
diag.a: Automatic Exposure Control
c: Automatic Energy Control
Automatic Exposure Control
Automated Emission Check
Anode Excitation Current
diag.e: Terminates exposure when sufficient signal for diagnostic quality.

diag.q: kVp affects primarily?
diag.a: Contrast & penetration
c: Spatial resolution
Contrast & penetration
Focal spot size
Detector efficiency
diag.e: Higher kVp = more penetration, lower contrast.

diag.q: mAs affects primarily?
diag.a: Quantity of radiation (dose)
c: Beam energy for radiation dose management
Quantity of radiation (dose)
Beam penetration in the radiation therapy context
Image contrast during the planning stage
diag.e: Doubling mAs doubles dose and halves quantum noise.

diag.q: Digital detector types?
diag.a: CR (PSP) and DR (flat panel)
c: Film and screen for radiation dose management
CR (PSP) and DR (flat panel)
Image intensifier only
CCD only for optimal treatment outcomes
diag.e: CR: photostimulable phosphor plates. DR: amorphous Si or Se.

diag.q: Nyquist frequency relates to?
diag.a: Spatial resolution limit
c: Dose efficiency
Spatial resolution limit
Contrast within the clinical setting
Noise in the radiation therapy context
diag.e: Max frequency = 1/(2 × pixel pitch).

diag.q: CALC: DLP if CTDIvol=15 mGy, scan length=30 cm?
diag.a: 450 mGy·cm
c: 200 mGy·cm
350 mGy·cm
450 mGy·cm
600 mGy·cm
diag.e: DLP = 15 × 30 = 450 mGy·cm.

diag.q: CALC: Effective dose if DLP=500 mGy·cm, conversion factor=0.015 mSv/(mGy·cm)?
diag.a: 7.5 mSv
c: 5.0 mSv
7.5 mSv
10.0 mSv
15.0 mSv

diag.q: CT pitch definition?
diag.a: Table travel per rotation / beam width
c: kVp / mAs — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Table travel per rotation / beam width
Slice thickness / FOV
Rotation time × detector rows
diag.e: Pitch > 1 = gaps (faster, lower dose). Pitch < 1 = overlap (slower, higher dose).

diag.q: CALC: Pitch if table moves 20mm/rotation, beam width=16mm?
diag.e: Pitch = 20/16 = 1.25. Slight gap between rotations.

diag.q: DQE stands for?
diag.a: Detective Quantum Efficiency
c: Dose Quality Estimate
Detective Quantum Efficiency
Digital Quality Enhancement
Diagnostic Quality Evaluation
diag.e: Overall detector efficiency: ratio of output SNR² to input SNR². Higher = better.

diag.q: MTF describes?
diag.a: Spatial frequency response of imaging system
c: Dose distribution — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Spatial frequency response of imaging system
Contrast of objects — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Noise spectrum — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
diag.e: Modulation Transfer Function: 1.0 at zero frequency, decreasing toward limiting resolution.

diag.q: Focal spot size affects?
diag.a: Spatial resolution
c: Patient dose
Spatial resolution
Contrast
Detector noise
diag.e: Smaller focal spot = less penumbra = sharper image. Larger focal spot needed for higher tube loading.

diag.q: CALC: Magnification if SOD=60cm, SID=120cm?
Grid ratio is?
diag.a: Height of lead strips / gap between strips
c: Number of strips per cm
Height of lead strips / gap between strips
Lead thickness / aluminium thickness
Grid width / grid length
diag.e: Higher ratio = more scatter removal but higher patient dose.

diag.q: Windowing in CT adjusts?
diag.a: Display contrast (window width) and brightness (level)
c: Patient dose — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Display contrast (window width) and brightness (level)
Reconstruction algorithm — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Slice thickness — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
diag.e: Narrow window = high contrast (e.g., lung). Wide window = shows more range (e.g., bone).

diag.q: CT window for soft tissue?
diag.e: Moderate width centred near water density for liver, muscle, etc.

diag.q: Iterative reconstruction in CT vs FBP?
diag.a: Lower noise at same dose
c: Faster processing
Lower noise at same dose
Better spatial resolution
Higher dose
diag.e: Iterative reconstruction allows dose reduction while maintaining image quality.

diag.q: CALC: How many chest X-rays equivalent to 10 mSv CT?
diag.e: 10 mSv / 0.02 mSv per CXR = 500 chest X-rays.

diag.q: ALARA in diagnostic radiology means?
diag.a: Optimise image quality at lowest achievable dose
c: Maximum dose for best image
Optimise image quality at lowest achievable dose
Use highest kVp always — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Minimise scan time only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
diag.e: Balance diagnostic quality with radiation protection. Use appropriate kVp, mAs, collimation.

diag.q: Dual-energy CT uses?
diag.a: Two different kVp acquisitions for material characterisation
c: Two detectors — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Two different kVp acquisitions for material characterisation
Two slice thicknesses — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Two reconstruction algorithms
diag.e: Exploits energy-dependent attenuation to differentiate materials (iodine, calcium, uric acid).

diag.q: Mammography target kVp range?
diag.e: Low kVp for optimal soft-tissue contrast in compressed breast.

diag.q: Tomosynthesis is?
diag.a: Limited-angle 3D mammography
c: Full CT of breast
Limited-angle 3D mammography
Ultrasound technique
MRI breast scan
diag.e: Multiple low-dose projections over ~15° arc reconstructed into thin slices. Reduces tissue overlap.

diag.q: CALC: Entrance skin dose if tube output = 5 mGy/mAs at 1m, used 50 mAs at 0.5m?
diag.a: 1000 mGy

500 mGy
1000 mGy
2000 mGy

diag.a: (Signal_A - Signal_B) / noise
c: Signal / noise
(Signal_A - Signal_B) / noise
Max signal / min signal
Contrast × resolution
diag.e: Measures ability to distinguish two different tissues. Higher CNR = better lesion detectability.

diag.q: CALC: mAs if tube current = 200 mA and exposure time = 0.1 s?
diag.a: 20 mAs
c: 2 mAs
10 mAs

2.0× original mAs
2.25× original mAs
3.0× original mAs

2.5 lp/mm

diag.q: CALC: SNR changes with mAs. If mAs doubles, SNR changes by?
c: ×2 in the treatment process
×4 as specified by guidelines
No change

diag.q: What is the 15% rule for kVp?
diag.a: Increasing kVp by 15% halves the required mAs
c: Increasing kVp by 15% doubles dose
Increasing kVp by 15% halves the required mAs
Decreasing kVp by 15% improves contrast
No such rule exists
diag.e: 15% kVp increase ≈ doubling beam intensity, so mAs can be halved for same receptor exposure.

diag.q: CALC: If 80 kVp requires 40 mAs, what mAs at 92 kVp (15% increase)?
diag.a: 20 mAs
c: 10 mAs
20 mAs
40 mAs
80 mAs
diag.e: 15% kVp increase → halve mAs. 92 kVp ≈ 80 × 1.15. mAs = 40/2 = 20 mAs.

diag.q: CALC: Image file size for 3000 × 3000 matrix, 16-bit depth?
diag.a: ~18 MB

1/√(mAs × slice thickness)

2σ

diag.q: Beam hardening artefact in CT caused by?

Preferential absorption of low-energy photons
Patient motion — verified through routine quality assurance measurements

diag.q: Partial volume effect in CT?

c: Image magnification — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Averaging of different tissues within one voxel
Scatter artefact — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Ring artefact — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
diag.e: Voxel CT number represents average of all tissues within it. Reduces with thinner slices.

Strip height is 12× the gap width

≥ 11 lp/mm

c: 1-3 mGy per view

50% dose reduction

diag.q: Fluoroscopy entrance dose rate limit (normal mode)?

88 mGy/min (10 R/min)

FPD has better DQE and no distortion
No difference — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches

diag.q: CALC: CTDI₁₀₀ if measured dose = 20 mGy in 100 mm pencil chamber, beam width = 20 mm?

diag.q: Exposure index (EI) indicates?
diag.a: Detector dose level
c: Patient dose
Detector dose level
Spatial resolution
Contrast

diag.q: Target exposure index deviation (DI) should be?
diag.a: Within ±1
c: Within ±0.1
Within ±1
Within ±3
Within ±5
diag.e: DI = 10 × log₁₀(EI/EI_target). DI > +1 = overexposure; DI < -1 = underexposure.

diag.q: CALC: If EI = 400 and target EI = 200, what is DI?
CT tube current modulation (TCM) adjusts?

mA based on patient attenuation per projection
Pitch during scan — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Slice thickness — verified through routine quality assurance measurements

diag.q: Photon counting CT advantage over conventional?
diag.a: Energy-resolving detection, no electronic noise
c: Faster rotation — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Energy-resolving detection, no electronic noise
Wider coverage — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Lower cost — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
diag.e: Direct conversion semiconductor counts individual photons with energy info. Eliminates electronic noise.

diag.q: CALC: Object true size = 5 mm. Image size = 7.5 mm. Magnification factor?

diag.q: Scatter-to-primary ratio (SPR) typical for abdomen?
diag.e: Large body part → lots of scatter. Grids essential for abdomen.

diag.q: CALC: If CNR = 5 and noise doubles, new CNR?
diag.e: CNR = ΔS/σ. If σ doubles, CNR halves: 5/2 = 2.5.

diag.q: DICOM stands for?

c: Digital Image Computation in Medicine
Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine
Diagnostic Image and Communication Method

2.5 mm Al

diag.a: ~2.3 mm Al
c: ~1.0 mm Al
~2.3 mm Al
~4.0 mm Al
~6.0 mm Al

diag.q: CT reconstruction: FBP stands for?
diag.a: Filtered Back Projection
c: Fast Beam Processing
Filtered Back Projection
Forward Beam Propagation
Focal Beam Profile
diag.e: Classic analytical reconstruction. Fast but noisier than iterative methods.

50 mGy

diag.a: Patient size relative to standard phantom
c: Scanner model — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Patient size relative to standard phantom
Tube voltage — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Contrast dose — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
diag.e: Converts CTDIvol to patient-specific dose using size-dependent conversion factors.

diag.q: CALC: SSDE if CTDIvol = 15 mGy and size conversion factor = 1.4 (small patient)?
diag.a: 21 mGy
c: 10.7 mGy
15 mGy
21 mGy
30 mGy

diag.q: Deep learning reconstruction (DLR) in CT differs from iterative reconstruction how?
diag.a: DLR uses trained neural networks
c: DLR is just faster FBP
DLR uses trained neural networks
DLR increases patient dose
No practical difference
diag.e: faster than iterative, achieves lower noise at same dose. DLR (e.g., GE TrueFidelity, Canon AiCE, Siemens ADMIRE-4D): neural networks trained on high/low-dose pairs. Faster than full model-based iterative recon. Maintains spatial resolution better than iterative methods. Enables 40-60% dose reduction.

diag.q: AI-based automatic exposure control (AEC) in CT uses?
diag.a: Patient habitus from scout to predict optimal tube current per rotation angle
c: Fixed mAs for all patients, based on established clinical physics practice
Patient habitus from scout to predict optimal tube current per rotation angle
Only kVp adjustment — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Manual technologist input only, based on established clinical physics practice
diag.e: Modern AI-AEC: organ-based tube current modulation, considers breast position (anterior), spine (lateral). Some systems use deep learning for real-time optimisation beyond traditional sinogram-based TCM.

diag.q: Computer-aided detection (CADe) vs computer-aided diagnosis (CADx)?
diag.a: CADe marks suspicious locations; CADx characterises lesions (benign vs malignant)
c: Identical function — CADe and CADx are interchangeable terms for the same AI technology
CADe marks suspicious locations; CADx characterises lesions (benign vs malignant)
CADe fully replaces the radiologist; CADx only assists with differential diagnosis
CADx is older first-generation technology; CADe is the modern deep-learning replacement
diag.e: CADe: detection (flags regions). CADx: diagnosis (classifies findings). Modern AI systems increasingly do both. FDA-cleared AI tools exist for chest X-ray, mammography, CT lung nodules, brain haemorrhage.

diag.q: AI triage in radiology: example application?
diag.a: Flagging critical findings (ICH, PE, pneumothorax) and reprioritising worklist
c: Replacing radiologists entirely, based on established clinical physics practice
Flagging critical findings (ICH, PE, pneumothorax) and reprioritising worklist
Only scheduling appointments — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Only billing — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
diag.e: AI triage (e.g., Viz.ai, Aidoc): analyses images immediately post-acquisition, flags critical pathology, moves urgent cases to top of worklist. Reduces time-to-diagnosis for emergencies.

diag.q: AI quality control in radiography checks for?
diag.a: Positioning errors, collimation adequacy, exposure index, and repeat rate analysis
c: Only image brightness — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Positioning errors, collimation adequacy, exposure index, and repeat rate analysis
Only patient ID — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Nothing useful clinically, based on established clinical physics practice
diag.e: AI-QC: automated reject analysis, exposure trending, positioning feedback to radiographers. Reduces repeats → lower population dose. Commercial: Qaelum, Radiology Analytics.

diag.q: Photon-counting CT: why does it eliminate electronic noise?
diag.a: Energy thresholds reject electronic noise pulses below minimum photon energy
c: Better cable insulation and shielding eliminates all electronic noise from detector signal
Energy thresholds reject electronic noise pulses below minimum photon energy
Active detector cooling to -40°C reduces thermal noise below clinically relevant levels
Purely digital signal processing removes noise mathematically after initial analogue detection
diag.e: Each photon pulse measured individually. Energy threshold set above electronic noise floor. Only real photon events counted. Eliminates integrating detector's electronic noise contribution to image noise. Dramatic improvement at low dose.

diag.q: Photon-counting CT enables multi-energy (spectral) imaging by?
diag.a: Simultaneous energy binning of every photon in a single acquisition
c: Rapid kVp switching between high and low voltage during adjacent rotations of the gantry
Dual-layer sandwich detector separating low and high energy photons by absorption depth
Simultaneous energy binning of every photon in a single acquisition
Sequential scans at two different tube voltages requiring separate patient acquisitions
diag.e: Each photon sorted into 2-8 energy bins simultaneously. No temporal misregistration. Enables material decomposition (iodine, calcium, uric acid, gadolinium) from one scan at one dose.

diag.q: CALC: Photon-counting CT with 0.15 mm detector pixel. Nyquist frequency?
diag.a: 3.33 lp/mm
c: 1.67 lp/mm
3.33 lp/mm
6.67 lp/mm
10 lp/mm
diag.e: f_Nyq = 1/(2 × 0.15) = 3.33 lp/mm. Much higher than conventional CT (~1.5 lp/mm). Enables ultra-high-resolution modes for temporal bone, lung, and coronary imaging.

diag.q: Metal artefact reduction (MAR) algorithm in CT works by?
diag.a: Identifying metal in sinogram, replacing affected projections with interpolated data, then reconstructing
c: Simply increasing kVp — no additional physics considerations or verification steps are needed
Identifying metal in sinogram, replacing affected projections with interpolated data, then reconstructing
Removing metal digitally from image, verified through standard quality assurance protocols
Only works with iterative recon — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
diag.e: MAR: forward-project to sinogram → identify metal-affected rays → replace with interpolation or normalisation → reconstruct. Reduces streaks but can introduce new artefacts. O-MAR, iMAR, SEMAR are vendor implementations.

diag.q: Motion artefact in CT appears as?
diag.a: Blurring, streaking, or double contours of moving structures
c: Ring artefacts — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Blurring, streaking, or double contours of moving structures
Increased noise uniformly, based on established clinical physics practice
Windmill artefact only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
diag.e: Object moves during rotation → inconsistent projection data → blur/streaks. Cardiac motion: stair-step artefact. Respiratory: doubling of diaphragm. Solutions: faster rotation, gating, motion correction algorithms.

diag.q: Dual-energy CT material decomposition can differentiate?
diag.a: Iodine from calcium, uric acid from calcium, and virtual non-contrast images
c: Only bone from soft tissue differentiation based on density differences alone
Iodine from calcium, uric acid from calcium, and virtual non-contrast images
Only contrast enhancement quantification without material-specific decomposition capability
Nothing clinically useful beyond what conventional single-energy CT already provides
diag.e: Exploits energy-dependent attenuation: iodine has K-edge at 33 keV, calcium doesn't. Can create iodine maps, virtual non-contrast, virtual monoenergetic images, and gout crystal detection (uric acid vs calcium).

diag.q: CALC: Dual-energy CT: 80 kVp and 140 kVp. Iodine attenuation ratio (80/140) ≈ 2.5. Calcium ratio ≈ 1.5. This difference enables?
diag.a: Material-specific decomposition using the ratio difference
c: Nothing useful — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Material-specific decomposition using the ratio difference
Only dose reduction — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Only noise reduction — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
diag.e: Two equations (one per energy), two unknowns (two basis materials). Different attenuation ratios create linearly independent equations → solve for each material concentration. Foundation of all dual-energy clinical applications.

diag.q: AI-based dose monitoring system (e.g., Dose Watch, Radimetrics) provides?
diag.a: Automated patient dose tracking, DRL comparison, and outlier alerts across entire department
c: Only displays CTDIvol — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Automated patient dose tracking, DRL comparison, and outlier alerts across entire department
Replaces physicist QA — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Only works for CT — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
diag.e: Enterprise dose management: captures DICOM dose structured reports from all modalities, tracks individual patient cumulative dose, compares against DRLs, alerts for outliers. Essential for optimisation programmes.

diag.q: Virtual monoenergetic images from dual-energy CT at 40 keV are useful for?
diag.a: Maximising iodine contrast (near K-edge) for vascular and oncology imaging
c: Reducing all artefacts — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Maximising iodine contrast (near K-edge) for vascular and oncology imaging
Only bone imaging — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Reducing patient dose — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
diag.e: 40 keV: near iodine K-edge (33 keV), maximum photoelectric absorption. Boosts contrast-to-noise ratio for enhancing lesions. Higher keV (70-100) reduces beam hardening artefacts near metal.

diag.q: Cone-beam artefact in multi-slice CT worsens with?
diag.a: Wider detector coverage (more rows) and off-centre anatomy
c: Thinner slices — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Lower kVp — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Wider detector coverage (more rows) and off-centre anatomy
Higher pitch — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
diag.e: X-ray cone angle increases with detector width. Peripheral rays deviate from ideal plane → incomplete data → artefacts at top/bottom of scan volume. Corrected by cone-beam reconstruction algorithms.

diag.q: What does CT stand for in medical imaging?
diag.a: Computed Tomography
c: Computed Tomography
Clinical Thermography
Calibrated Treatment
Cellular Testing
diag.e: CT stands for Computed Tomography. It is an imaging technique that uses X-rays taken from multiple angles combined with computer processing to create detailed cross-sectional images of the body.

diag.q: What type of radiation is used to produce a conventional X-ray image?
diag.a: X-ray radiation (a form of electromagnetic radiation)
c: Ultrasound waves in radiation therapy practice
X-ray radiation (a form of electromagnetic radiation)
Radio waves for therapeutic applications
Infrared radiation throughout the treatment course
diag.e: Conventional radiography uses X-rays, which are a form of high-energy electromagnetic radiation. X-rays pass through the body and are absorbed differently by different tissues, creating an image based on these differences in attenuation.

diag.q: Why do bones appear white on a conventional X-ray image?
diag.a: Because bones absorb more X-rays than soft tissue
c: Because bones emit their own light
Because bones absorb more X-rays than soft tissue
Because bones are coated with a contrast agent
Because bones reflect X-rays back to the detector
diag.e: Bones contain calcium, which has a high atomic number and density, causing them to absorb (attenuate) more X-rays than surrounding soft tissues. Fewer X-rays reach the detector behind the bone, so those areas appear white on the image.

diag.q: What does MRI stand for?
diag.a: Magnetic Resonance Imaging
c: Medical Radiation Instrument
Magnetic Resonance Imaging
Micro Radiographic Inspection
Modulated Radio Imaging
diag.e: MRI stands for Magnetic Resonance Imaging. Unlike CT and X-ray, MRI does not use ionising radiation. Instead, it uses strong magnetic fields and radiofrequency pulses to produce detailed images of soft tissues in the body.

diag.q: What is the main advantage of CT imaging over conventional X-ray?
diag.a: CT provides cross-sectional images that eliminate overlapping structures
c: CT uses no radiation at all for quality assurance purposes
CT is always cheaper than X-ray in the treatment process
CT provides cross-sectional images that eliminate overlapping structures
CT can only image bones as part of the clinical workflow
diag.e: The main advantage of CT over conventional X-ray is that CT produces cross-sectional (axial) images of the body, eliminating the superimposition of structures that occurs in 2D X-ray projections. This allows much better visualisation of anatomy and pathology.

diag.q: CALC: A CT scanner has CTDIvol = 15 mGy and the scan covers 30 cm of anatomy. What is the dose-length product (DLP), and using a chest conversion factor of k = 0.014 mSv/(mGy·cm), what is the approximate effective dose?
diag.a: DLP = 450 mGy·cm, E ≈ 6.3 mSv
c: DLP = 450 mGy·cm, E ≈ 6.3 mSv
DLP = 360 mGy·cm, E ≈ 5.0 mSv
DLP = 500 mGy·cm, E ≈ 7.0 mSv
DLP = 450 mGy·cm, E ≈ 12.6 mSv
diag.e: DLP = CTDIvol × scan length = 15 mGy × 30 cm = 450 mGy·cm. Effective dose E = DLP × k = 450 × 0.014 = 6.3 mSv. CTDIvol already accounts for pitch, so scan length is simply multiplied. The k-factor is region-specific (chest: 0.014, abdomen: 0.015, head: 0.0023 mSv/(mGy·cm)) and provides a rapid estimate. DLP is the primary metric on CT dose reports per IEC 60601-2-44.

diag.q: CALC: A mammography unit delivers entrance air kerma of 8 mGy for a 5 cm compressed breast. The normalized glandular dose coefficient (DgN) for 50% glandularity at this thickness and beam quality is 0.185 mGy/mGy. What is the mean glandular dose (MGD)?
diag.a: 1.48 mGy
c: 0.74 mGy
1.48 mGy
2.96 mGy
4.00 mGy
diag.e: MGD = entrance air kerma × DgN = 8.0 × 0.185 = 1.48 mGy. Mean glandular dose is the dosimetric quantity of interest in mammography because glandular tissue is the radiation-sensitive tissue at risk for breast cancer induction. The DgN conversion factor depends on breast thickness, glandularity, HVL, and target/filter combination. The ACR action level is MGD < 3 mGy per view for a standard 4.2 cm breast.

diag.q: CALC: A 64-row CT scanner has nominal beam collimation of 64 × 0.625 mm = 40 mm. The measured CTDI free-in-air beam width is 43.2 mm. What is the overbeaming percentage?
diag.e: Overbeaming = (measured width - nominal width) / nominal width × 100% = (43.2 - 40.0) / 40.0 × 100% = 8.0%. Overbeaming occurs because the X-ray focal spot has finite size, producing penumbra that extends beyond the active detector edges. These penumbral regions irradiate the patient but contribute nothing to image formation — representing wasted dose. Overbeaming is proportionally more significant for narrow collimations and is a key factor in multi-detector CT dose efficiency.

diag.q: CALC: A fluoroscopy unit has an entrance dose rate of 25 mGy/min. A 12-minute procedure has 40% beam-on time and the backscatter factor is 1.35. What is the entrance skin dose (ESD)?
diag.a: 162 mGy
c: 120 mGy
162 mGy
300 mGy
405 mGy
diag.e: Beam-on time = 12 min × 0.40 = 4.8 min. Entrance air kerma = 25 mGy/min × 4.8 min = 120 mGy. ESD = entrance air kerma × BSF = 120 × 1.35 = 162 mGy. The backscatter factor accounts for radiation scattered back from patient tissue into the entrance surface. For interventional procedures, tracking cumulative ESD is critical — the threshold for transient erythema is approximately 2000 mGy, and tracking helps prevent deterministic skin injuries.

diag.q: CALC: CT image noise (σ) is 12 HU at CTDIvol = 20 mGy. If CTDIvol is reduced to 5 mGy with all other parameters unchanged, what is the expected noise?
diag.a: 24 HU
c: 6 HU
12 HU
24 HU
48 HU
diag.e: CT noise scales as σ ∝ 1/√(dose). Therefore σ₂ = σ₁ × √(dose₁/dose₂) = 12 × √(20/5) = 12 × √4 = 12 × 2 = 24 HU. Reducing dose by a factor of 4 doubles the image noise. This fundamental inverse-square-root relationship underlies all dose optimization strategies in CT. Iterative reconstruction algorithms aim to partially decouple this relationship by using statistical noise models to reduce noise without proportional dose increases.

diag.q: In CT imaging, what does the pitch parameter represent and how does it affect image quality?
diag.a: Table travel per rotation divided by beam collimation; pitch >1 increases coverage speed but may reduce z-axis resolution
c: Ratio of mA to kVp determining contrast resolution and patient dose
Table travel per rotation divided by beam collimation; pitch >1 increases coverage speed but may reduce z-axis resolution
Detector element size divided by focal spot size determining spatial resolution
Reconstruction filter sharpness setting controlling noise versus resolution trade-off
diag.e: Pitch = table feed per rotation / total beam collimation (nT). Pitch = 1 means contiguous coverage. Pitch > 1 means gaps (faster scan, lower dose, potential z-resolution loss). Pitch < 1 means overlap (slower, higher dose, potentially better z-resolution). CTDI_vol = CTDI_w / pitch.

diag.q: What is the purpose of a half-value layer (HVL) measurement in diagnostic X-ray QC?
diag.a: Characterises beam quality/filtration by determining the aluminium thickness that reduces air kerma to 50%
c: Measures the maximum output of the X-ray tube in mGy/mAs
Characterises beam quality/filtration by determining the aluminium thickness that reduces air kerma to 50%
Evaluates detector efficiency by measuring transmitted versus absorbed photons
Tests the automatic exposure control response to different phantom thicknesses
diag.e: HVL (mm Al) indicates effective beam filtration. Higher HVL = harder beam = more filtration = reduced patient skin dose. Minimum HVL values are specified by regulation (e.g., ≥2.5 mm Al at 80 kVp for general radiography). Measured using narrow-beam geometry with high-purity Al sheets.

diag.q: In mammography, why is the molybdenum/rhodium target/filter combination used instead of tungsten?
diag.a: Mo/Rh produces characteristic X-rays at 17-23 keV, optimising contrast in breast tissue
c: Mo/Rh generates higher output for shorter exposure times
Mo/Rh produces characteristic X-rays at 17-23 keV, optimising contrast in breast tissue
Tungsten targets cannot operate at the low kVp values used in mammography
Mo/Rh reduces patient dose compared to tungsten by a factor of 10
diag.e: Mo target produces characteristic peaks at 17.5 and 19.6 keV. Rh filter (or Mo filter) removes higher energy photons. This narrow energy spectrum optimises contrast between glandular and adipose tissue. For thicker/denser breasts, Rh target/Rh filter or W target/Rh filter provides slightly harder spectrum with adequate contrast.

diag.q: CALC: A CT scan has CTDIvol = 15 mGy and scan length = 30 cm. Calculate the dose-length product (DLP) and estimate effective dose using the chest conversion factor k = 0.014 mSv/(mGy·cm).
diag.a: DLP = 450 mGy·cm, effective dose ≈ 6.3 mSv
c: DLP = 450 mGy·cm, effective dose ≈ 6.3 mSv
DLP = 200 mGy·cm, effective dose ≈ 2.8 mSv
DLP = 450 mGy·cm, effective dose ≈ 12.6 mSv
DLP = 150 mGy·cm, effective dose ≈ 2.1 mSv
diag.e: DLP = CTDIvol × scan length = 15 × 30 = 450 mGy·cm. Effective dose = DLP × k = 450 × 0.014 = 6.3 mSv.

diag.q: In digital radiography, what is the detective quantum efficiency (DQE) and why is it the most comprehensive measure of detector performance?
diag.a: DQE measures the ratio of output to input SNR squared as a function of spatial frequency, combining sensitivity, noise, and resolution
c: DQE measures only the fraction of X-ray photons absorbed by the detector per standard operating procedures
DQE measures the ratio of output to input SNR squared as a function of spatial frequency, combining sensitivity, noise, and resolution
DQE is the spatial resolution measured at 10% of the MTF curve as specified by guidelines
DQE is the ratio of patient dose to image noise in the final displayed image for quality assurance purposes
diag.e: DQE(f) = SNR²_out(f) / SNR²_in(f). It captures how efficiently the detector converts incoming X-ray quanta into useful image signal at each spatial frequency. DQE combines quantum detection efficiency, noise characteristics (MTF, NNPS). Higher DQE = better image quality per unit dose. Flat-panel detectors: DQE ~60-70% at zero frequency.

diag.q: Dual-energy CT uses two different kVp acquisitions. What quantitative information does material decomposition provide that single-energy CT cannot?
diag.a: Separation of materials by atomic number, enabling virtual non-contrast images, iodine maps, and effective Z characterisation
c: Higher spatial resolution than single-energy CT in all clinical applications
Reduced image noise by averaging the two energy datasets together
Separation of materials by atomic number, enabling virtual non-contrast images, iodine maps, and effective Z characterisation
Elimination of all beam hardening artefacts through spectral averaging
diag.e: At different energies, photoelectric effect (∝Z³/E³) and Compton scatter vary differently. Two-material decomposition separates basis materials (e.g., water/iodine). Clinical applications: virtual non-contrast (subtract iodine), iodine quantification maps, uric acid vs calcium renal stones, virtual monoenergetic images, electron density for RT planning.

diag.q: In X-ray production, what percentage of kinetic energy is typically converted to X-rays in a diagnostic tube?
diag.a: Less than 1% — the vast majority is converted to heat in the anode target
c: Less than 1% — the vast majority is converted to heat in the anode target
Approximately 10% — the rest is lost through scatter in the tube housing
Approximately 25% — balanced between heat and useful radiation output
Greater than 50% — most energy contributes to the diagnostic X-ray beam
diag.e: X-ray production efficiency is very low at diagnostic energies. Efficiency ≈ 9 × 10⁻¹⁰ × Z × kVp, giving roughly 0.5–1% for tungsten at typical diagnostic kVp. This is why anode cooling is critical.

diag.q: CALC: A radiographic unit operates at 80 kVp. What is the maximum photon energy in the bremsstrahlung spectrum?
diag.a: 80 keV — the maximum photon energy equals the peak tube voltage in keV
c: 80 keV — the maximum photon energy equals the peak tube voltage in keV
40 keV — the maximum energy is half the applied peak kilovoltage
60 keV — the average bremsstrahlung energy defines the spectral maximum
27 keV — the characteristic radiation K-edge determines maximum energy
diag.e: When an electron gives up all its kinetic energy in a single interaction, it produces a photon with energy equal to the electron's kinetic energy. At 80 kVp, maximum electron energy is 80 keV, so maximum photon energy is 80 keV.

diag.q: Characteristic X-rays from a tungsten anode in a diagnostic tube have K-shell energies of approximately?
diag.a: 58–69 keV, corresponding to the K-shell binding energy transitions of tungsten
c: 58–69 keV, corresponding to the K-shell binding energy transitions of tungsten
17–20 keV, corresponding to the L-shell binding energy transitions of tungsten
33–40 keV, corresponding to the M-shell binding energy transitions of tungsten
85–95 keV, corresponding to the N-shell binding energy transitions of tungsten
diag.e: Tungsten K-edge is at 69.5 keV. Characteristic X-rays from K-shell transitions (Kα ≈ 59 keV, Kβ ≈ 67 keV) contribute to the diagnostic spectrum when kVp exceeds ~70 kVp.

diag.q: What is the primary function of the rotating anode in a diagnostic X-ray tube?
diag.a: To distribute the heat load over a larger area and allow higher tube loading
c: To distribute the heat load over a larger area and allow higher tube loading
To filter out low-energy photons and harden the X-ray beam spectrum
To focus the electron beam onto a smaller effective focal spot size
To increase the X-ray production efficiency by changing the target angle
diag.e: Rotating anodes spread the electron bombardment over a large annular track. This increases the heat capacity by orders of magnitude compared to a stationary anode, permitting higher mA and shorter exposures.

diag.q: The line-focus principle in X-ray tubes achieves which design objective?
diag.a: A large actual focal spot for heat dissipation with a small effective focal spot for resolution
c: A large actual focal spot for heat dissipation with a small effective focal spot for resolution
A small actual focal spot for heat dissipation with a large effective focal spot for coverage
Equal actual and effective focal spots to maintain uniform beam intensity across the field
A variable focal spot that automatically adjusts based on the selected exposure parameters
diag.e: By angling the anode target (typically 7–17°), the projected (effective) focal spot is much smaller than the actual area bombarded by electrons. This gives good spatial resolution while maintaining thermal capacity.

diag.q: CALC: An X-ray tube has an anode angle of 12°. If the actual focal spot length along the anode surface is 4.0 mm, what is the effective focal spot size?
diag.a: 0.83 mm — calculated as actual length multiplied by sin(12°)
c: 0.83 mm — calculated as actual length multiplied by sin(12°)
3.91 mm — calculated as actual length multiplied by cos(12°)
2.00 mm — calculated as actual length multiplied by sin(30°)
1.39 mm — calculated as actual length multiplied by tan(12°)
diag.e: Effective focal spot = actual focal spot × sin(anode angle). sin(12°) = 0.2079. Therefore 4.0 × 0.2079 = 0.83 mm. Smaller anode angles give smaller effective focal spots but reduce field coverage.

diag.q: The purpose of adding aluminium filtration to a diagnostic X-ray beam is to?
diag.a: Remove low-energy photons that increase patient dose without contributing to image formation
c: Remove low-energy photons that increase patient dose without contributing to image formation
Remove high-energy photons that penetrate through the patient and degrade image contrast
Increase the total beam output intensity to allow for shorter overall exposure durations
Compensate for the anode heel effect by equalising the beam intensity across the field
diag.e: Filtration preferentially attenuates low-energy photons, hardening the beam. These soft photons would be absorbed in superficial tissues, increasing skin dose without reaching the detector. SA regulations (aligned with IEC) require minimum 2.5 mm Al total filtration at ≥70 kVp.

diag.q: CALC: The half-value layer (HVL) of an X-ray beam is 3.0 mm Al. What fraction of the beam intensity remains after passing through 9.0 mm Al?
diag.a: 1/8 (12.5%) — because 9.0 mm equals exactly three half-value layers
c: 1/8 (12.5%) — because 9.0 mm equals exactly three half-value layers
1/4 (25.0%) — because 9.0 mm equals exactly two half-value layers
1/3 (33.3%) — because the attenuation is linear with aluminium thickness
1/16 (6.25%) — because 9.0 mm equals four half-value layers in total
diag.e: Number of HVLs = 9.0/3.0 = 3. Remaining fraction = (1/2)³ = 1/8 = 0.125 = 12.5%. This assumes narrow-beam geometry and monoenergetic approximation.

diag.q: What does the modulation transfer function (MTF) quantify in a diagnostic imaging system?
diag.a: The ability of the system to faithfully reproduce spatial frequency contrast from object to image
c: The ability of the system to faithfully reproduce spatial frequency contrast from object to image
The total radiation dose delivered to the patient during a single standard imaging acquisition
The ratio of scattered photons to primary photons reaching the image receptor or detector
The signal-to-noise ratio improvement achieved by applying post-processing reconstruction filters
diag.e: MTF describes how well the imaging chain transfers contrast at each spatial frequency. MTF = 1 means perfect reproduction; MTF = 0 means no contrast transfer. It drops with increasing spatial frequency due to blur.

diag.q: Detective quantum efficiency (DQE) of a digital detector is best described as?
diag.a: The ratio of output SNR² to input SNR², measuring how efficiently the detector uses incident quanta
c: The ratio of output SNR² to input SNR², measuring how efficiently the detector uses incident quanta
The total number of X-ray photons absorbed by the detector divided by total photons incident on it
The spatial resolution limit defined by the smallest detectable object in a contrast-detail phantom
The linearity of the detector response curve over the full range of clinical exposure conditions
diag.e: DQE = (SNR²_out)/(SNR²_in) as a function of spatial frequency. A perfect detector has DQE = 1. Higher DQE means better image quality per unit dose. Direct-conversion flat panels typically have higher DQE than CR systems.

diag.q: In digital radiography, quantum noise (mottle) is primarily determined by?
diag.a: The number of X-ray photons absorbed by the detector per unit area for each exposure
c: The number of X-ray photons absorbed by the detector per unit area for each exposure
The pixel size of the flat panel detector and the analogue-to-digital converter bit depth
The tube voltage setting selected for the examination and the inherent beam filtration
The display monitor resolution and the window/level settings applied during image review
