diag.e: Quantum noise follows Poisson statistics: σ = √N. Fewer absorbed photons → more relative noise → grainier images. This is the dominant noise source in properly functioning digital systems at clinical dose levels.

diag.q: CALC: A PA chest radiograph delivers a dose-area product (DAP) of 0.15 Gy·cm². If the field area at the patient surface is 900 cm², what is the approximate entrance surface air kerma?
diag.a: 0.167 mGy — calculated by dividing DAP by the irradiated field area at the surface
c: 0.167 mGy — calculated by dividing DAP by the irradiated field area at the surface
0.015 mGy — calculated by dividing DAP by ten times the irradiated field area value
1.350 mGy — calculated by multiplying DAP by the total irradiated field area at surface
0.067 mGy — calculated by dividing DAP by the square root of the irradiated field area
diag.e: DAP = dose × area. Dose = DAP/area = 0.15 Gy·cm² / 900 cm² = 1.67 × 10⁻⁴ Gy = 0.167 mGy. This is approximate as DAP is measured at the tube and backscatter is not included.

diag.q: CALC: A CT scan has a CTDIvol of 15 mGy and a scan length of 30 cm. What is the dose-length product (DLP)?
diag.a: 450 mGy·cm — calculated as CTDIvol multiplied by the total scan length
c: 450 mGy·cm — calculated as CTDIvol multiplied by the total scan length
0.50 mGy·cm — calculated as CTDIvol divided by the total scan length
225 mGy·cm — calculated as CTDIvol multiplied by half the scan length
900 mGy·cm — calculated as CTDIvol multiplied by double the scan length
diag.e: DLP = CTDIvol × scan length = 15 mGy × 30 cm = 450 mGy·cm. DLP accounts for both the dose intensity and the length of anatomy irradiated. It is used with conversion factors to estimate effective dose.

diag.q: CALC: A CT abdomen-pelvis gives a DLP of 600 mGy·cm. Using the ICRP conversion factor of 0.015 mSv/(mGy·cm) for the abdomen, what is the approximate effective dose?
diag.a: 9.0 mSv — calculated by multiplying DLP by the region-specific conversion factor
c: 9.0 mSv — calculated by multiplying DLP by the region-specific conversion factor
4.5 mSv — calculated by multiplying DLP by half the conversion factor value
40.0 mSv — calculated by dividing DLP by the region-specific conversion factor
0.025 mSv — calculated by dividing the conversion factor by the total DLP value
diag.e: Effective dose ≈ DLP × k, where k is the region-specific conversion factor. E = 600 × 0.015 = 9.0 mSv. This method (from ICRP Publication 103/EUR 16262) provides a quick estimate for comparing protocols.

diag.q: In CT, the Hounsfield unit (HU) is defined relative to which two reference materials?
diag.a: Water (0 HU) and air (−1000 HU) as the calibration reference points
c: Water (0 HU) and air (−1000 HU) as the calibration reference points
Bone (+1000 HU) and fat (−100 HU) as the calibration reference points
Muscle (+40 HU) and blood (+55 HU) as the calibration reference points
Iodine (+300 HU) and calcium (+400 HU) as calibration reference points
diag.e: HU = 1000 × (μ_tissue − μ_water)/μ_water. By definition, water = 0 HU and air = −1000 HU. CT number accuracy is verified during QA by scanning water and air phantoms.

diag.q: In helical (spiral) CT, pitch is defined as?
diag.a: Table travel per rotation divided by the total collimated beam width at isocentre
c: Table travel per rotation divided by the total collimated beam width at isocentre
Rotation time multiplied by the number of active detector rows in the scanner
Reconstructed slice thickness divided by the detector element width at isocentre
Tube current multiplied by rotation time divided by the collimated beam width
diag.e: Pitch = table feed per rotation / total beam collimation (N × T). Pitch = 1 means contiguous coverage. Pitch > 1 means gaps (faster scan, potentially lower dose). Pitch < 1 means overlap (higher dose, improved z-resolution).

diag.q: CALC: A helical CT scanner has 64 × 0.625 mm detector configuration. If the table moves 60 mm per rotation, what is the pitch?
diag.a: 1.5 — calculated as 60 mm table travel divided by 40 mm total beam collimation
c: 1.5 — calculated as 60 mm table travel divided by 40 mm total beam collimation
0.67 — calculated as 40 mm total beam collimation divided by 60 mm table travel
0.94 — calculated as 60 mm table travel divided by 64 detector row elements
96.0 — calculated as 60 mm table travel multiplied by the number of detectors
diag.e: Total beam collimation = 64 × 0.625 = 40 mm. Pitch = table travel/collimation = 60/40 = 1.5. This means there are gaps in coverage per rotation, but helical interpolation fills them in during reconstruction.

diag.q: Filtered back projection (FBP) in CT reconstruction requires a filter (kernel) primarily to?
diag.a: Correct the 1/r blurring inherent in simple back projection and recover spatial resolution
c: Correct the 1/r blurring inherent in simple back projection and recover spatial resolution
Remove all patient motion artefacts that occur during the helical scanning acquisition
Convert raw attenuation data from linear scale to the logarithmic Hounsfield unit scale
Compensate for beam hardening caused by polychromatic X-ray spectra in the patient
diag.e: Simple back projection produces star-pattern blurring (1/r PSF). The ramp filter in frequency space corrects this by amplifying high frequencies. Different kernels (soft, standard, sharp) balance noise and resolution for clinical needs.

diag.q: Iterative reconstruction algorithms in CT, compared to filtered back projection, primarily offer?
diag.a: Reduced image noise at the same dose, or equivalent noise quality at a reduced dose level
c: Reduced image noise at the same dose, or equivalent noise quality at a reduced dose level
Higher spatial resolution independent of the detector element size or focal spot geometry
Elimination of all beam hardening and metal artefacts without need for calibration scans
Faster image reconstruction times due to simpler mathematical computations per iteration
diag.e: Iterative reconstruction models the acquisition process and noise statistics, reducing noise while preserving spatial resolution. This enables 20–60% dose reduction. Modern scanners combine model-based and statistical iterative techniques.

diag.q: In fluoroscopy, the primary method to reduce patient dose during prolonged procedures is?
diag.a: Pulsed fluoroscopy at the lowest acceptable pulse rate combined with tight collimation
c: Pulsed fluoroscopy at the lowest acceptable pulse rate combined with tight collimation
Increasing the source-to-skin distance while removing all added filtration from the beam
Using continuous fluoroscopy at maximum frame rate for the shortest possible beam-on time
Removing the anti-scatter grid to reduce exposure factors required for adequate imaging
diag.e: Pulsed fluoro (e.g., 7.5 or 3.75 fps instead of 30 fps continuous) dramatically reduces beam-on time. Combined with collimation, added filtration, and last-image-hold, dose reductions of 50–80% are achievable.

diag.q: In mammography, the average glandular dose (AGD) is the preferred dose metric because it?
diag.a: Represents the dose to the radiation-sensitive glandular tissue where breast cancer originates
c: Represents the dose to the radiation-sensitive glandular tissue where breast cancer originates
Is directly measurable with a standard ionisation chamber placed on the breast support table
Accounts for scatter radiation reaching the image receptor beneath the compressed breast
Provides the total entrance skin dose including backscatter from the compression paddle
diag.e: AGD estimates the mean dose to fibroglandular tissue, the tissue at risk for radiation-induced cancer. It is calculated from entrance air kerma using conversion factors that depend on breast thickness, composition, and beam quality (HVL).

diag.q: Which anode/filter combination is commonly used in modern mammography for imaging dense breasts?
diag.a: Tungsten anode with rhodium or silver filtration to produce a higher-energy optimised spectrum
c: Tungsten anode with rhodium or silver filtration to produce a higher-energy optimised spectrum
Copper anode with aluminium filtration to produce the lowest possible mean photon energy
Molybdenum anode with no added filtration to maximise the total X-ray output intensity
Lead anode with tin filtration to produce only characteristic radiation at fixed energy
diag.e: Modern mammography units use W/Rh or W/Ag for dense breasts. The tungsten spectrum with K-edge filtration provides better penetration while maintaining contrast. Mo/Mo is now mainly used for thin, fatty breasts on older units.

diag.q: In computed radiography (CR), the image receptor uses which technology?
diag.a: A photostimulable phosphor (PSP) imaging plate that stores the latent image as trapped electrons
c: A photostimulable phosphor (PSP) imaging plate that stores the latent image as trapped electrons
A thin-film transistor (TFT) array with amorphous selenium for direct charge conversion
A charge-coupled device (CCD) optically coupled to a scintillator with fibre optic taper
A complementary metal-oxide semiconductor (CMOS) sensor with caesium iodide scintillator
diag.e: CR uses BaFBr:Eu²⁺ phosphor plates. X-ray exposure traps electrons in metastable energy states. During readout, a scanning laser beam stimulates emission of blue/UV light proportional to absorbed dose. The plate is then erased for reuse.

diag.q: Direct-conversion flat panel detectors in digital radiography use which material as the photoconductor?
diag.a: Amorphous selenium (a-Se), which converts X-ray photons directly into electrical charge
c: Amorphous selenium (a-Se), which converts X-ray photons directly into electrical charge
Caesium iodide (CsI), which converts X-ray photons into visible light then electrons
Gadolinium oxysulphide (GOS), which converts X-rays to light via phosphorescence
Silicon dioxide (SiO₂), which converts X-ray photons into thermal energy for detection
diag.e: In direct-conversion DR, a-Se absorbs X-rays and generates electron-hole pairs under an applied electric field. Charge is collected by the TFT array. No light spread occurs, giving better spatial resolution than indirect systems.

diag.q: Indirect-conversion flat panel detectors typically use which scintillator material?
diag.a: Structured caesium iodide (CsI:Tl) columnar crystals that channel light to the photodiode array
c: Structured caesium iodide (CsI:Tl) columnar crystals that channel light to the photodiode array
Amorphous selenium (a-Se) in a layered configuration with an applied high-voltage bias field
Barium fluorobromide (BaFBr:Eu) photostimulable phosphor plates read by a scanning laser
Polycrystalline cadmium telluride (CdTe) elements with individual charge readout channels
diag.e: CsI:Tl needle-like crystals act as light guides, reducing lateral light spread and improving spatial resolution. Light is detected by an amorphous silicon (a-Si) photodiode array coupled to TFT switches.

diag.q: What is the exposure index (EI) in digital radiography primarily used to indicate?
diag.a: The amount of radiation reaching the detector, serving as a measure of detector dose level
c: The amount of radiation reaching the detector, serving as a measure of detector dose level
The spatial resolution capability of the imaging system in line pairs per millimetre
The total effective dose delivered to the patient for the specific radiographic examination
The signal-to-noise ratio achieved in the final displayed image after all post-processing
diag.e: EI (IEC 62494-1) indicates detector exposure level. It helps identify under- or over-exposure. The deviation index (DI) compares actual EI to the target: DI = 10 × log₁₀(EI/EI_target). DI within ±1 is ideal; ±3 requires action.

diag.q: Automatic exposure control (AEC) in radiography terminates the exposure when?
diag.a: The radiation detector behind the patient accumulates a preset signal level for optimal density
c: The radiation detector behind the patient accumulates a preset signal level for optimal density
The X-ray tube current reaches a maximum predetermined milliampere-second value setting
The patient surface dose measured by the entrance dosimeter exceeds the regulatory limit
The timer circuit reaches the preset maximum exposure time regardless of detector signal
diag.e: AEC ionisation chambers (or solid-state detectors) are positioned behind the patient. When accumulated signal reaches the preset level, exposure terminates. This ensures consistent image receptor dose regardless of patient size, within the backup time limit.

diag.q: An anti-scatter grid in radiography improves image quality by?
diag.a: Absorbing scattered photons before they reach the detector, thereby improving image contrast
c: Absorbing scattered photons before they reach the detector, thereby improving image contrast
Increasing the total number of photons reaching the detector to reduce quantum noise levels
Filtering low-energy primary photons from the beam before they enter the patient's anatomy
Reducing the geometric unsharpness caused by the finite size of the X-ray tube focal spot
diag.e: Grids consist of alternating lead strips and radiolucent interspaces. Scattered photons travelling at angles are absorbed by lead strips, while primary photons pass through. Grid ratio (height/interspace width) determines scatter rejection efficiency.

diag.q: CALC: An anti-scatter grid has a grid ratio of 12:1. If the interspace width is 0.3 mm, what is the height of the lead strips?
diag.a: 3.6 mm — calculated as grid ratio multiplied by the interspace width dimension
c: 3.6 mm — calculated as grid ratio multiplied by the interspace width dimension
0.025 mm — calculated as interspace width divided by the grid ratio dimension
4.0 mm — calculated as grid ratio plus interspace width then divided by three
1.2 mm — calculated as grid ratio divided by ten then multiplied by interspace
diag.e: Grid ratio = strip height / interspace width. Therefore height = ratio × interspace = 12 × 0.3 mm = 3.6 mm. Higher grid ratios provide better scatter cleanup but require increased exposure factors.

diag.q: CALC: Using a grid with a Bucky factor of 4, how must the mAs be adjusted compared to a non-grid technique?
diag.a: Multiply the mAs by 4 to compensate for the photons absorbed by the grid structure
c: Multiply the mAs by 4 to compensate for the photons absorbed by the grid structure
Divide the mAs by 4 because the grid increases the effective detector sensitivity level
Multiply the mAs by 2 because the Bucky factor halves the detector exposure needed
Keep the mAs the same because AEC automatically compensates for grid attenuation
diag.e: The Bucky factor is the ratio of incident radiation with the grid to that without. A factor of 4 means only 1/4 of photons reach the detector, so mAs must increase by 4× to maintain the same detector dose (assuming manual technique).

diag.q: Iodinated contrast agents used in CT and angiography enhance image contrast primarily through?
diag.a: Increased photoelectric absorption due to the high atomic number (Z=53) of iodine atoms
c: Increased photoelectric absorption due to the high atomic number (Z=53) of iodine atoms
Increased Compton scattering proportional to the electron density of the contrast solution
Production of characteristic X-rays within the patient that add signal to the image data
Reduced beam hardening effects that improve uniformity of the CT image reconstruction
diag.e: Iodine (Z=53) has a K-edge at 33.2 keV, near diagnostic energies. Photoelectric absorption ∝ Z³/E³, so iodine dramatically increases attenuation. This produces high contrast between enhanced vessels/tissues and surrounding anatomy.

diag.q: Dual-energy CT acquires data at two different kVp settings primarily to enable?
diag.a: Material decomposition and characterisation based on energy-dependent attenuation differences
c: Material decomposition and characterisation based on energy-dependent attenuation differences
Doubling the spatial resolution by combining two datasets acquired at different tube voltages
Eliminating all image noise through destructive interference between the two energy datasets
Reducing total patient radiation dose by averaging the two acquisitions into one final image
diag.e: Different materials have different energy-dependent attenuation curves. By acquiring at two energies (e.g., 80 and 140 kVp), materials like iodine, calcium, uric acid, and water can be distinguished and quantified. Applications include virtual non-contrast, gout crystal detection, and kidney stone characterisation.

diag.q: In the context of South African radiation regulations, which body is responsible for authorising the possession and use of diagnostic X-ray equipment?
diag.a: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) under the National Nuclear Regulator Act (Act 47 of 1999)
c: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) under the National Nuclear Regulator Act (Act 47 of 1999)
The South African Health Products Regulatory Authority (SAHPRA) under the Medicines Act only
The Department of Mineral Resources and Energy (DMRE) under the Mining Charter regulations
The Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA) under the Health Professions Act
diag.e: The NNR regulates all radiation sources in South Africa, including diagnostic X-ray equipment. Facilities must hold a nuclear authorisation (certificate of registration) to possess and operate X-ray units. SAHPRA handles medicines/devices but not radiation licensing.

diag.q: According to South African NNR requirements, diagnostic X-ray rooms must have shielding designed to limit public dose to?
diag.a: 1 mSv per year for members of the public in adjacent areas outside the controlled zone
c: 1 mSv per year for members of the public in adjacent areas outside the controlled zone
20 mSv per year for members of the public in adjacent areas outside controlled zones
5 mSv per year for members of the public who occasionally enter controlled radiation areas
50 mSv per year for members of the public in unrestricted areas surrounding the facility
diag.e: ICRP and NNR dose limits: 1 mSv/year for public, 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years for occupationally exposed workers. Shielding calculations use workload, use factor, and occupancy factor to ensure compliance.

diag.q: CALC: A diagnostic X-ray room requires shielding. If the primary beam workload is 400 mA·min/week at 100 kVp, the distance to the occupied area is 4 m, the use factor is 1/4, and the occupancy factor is 1, what is the product of workload × use factor × occupancy factor / distance²?
diag.a: 6.25 mA·min/(week·m²) — calculated as 400 × 0.25 × 1 divided by 16
c: 6.25 mA·min/(week·m²) — calculated as 400 × 0.25 × 1 divided by 16
25.0 mA·min/(week·m²) — calculated as 400 × 0.25 × 1 divided by 4
100.0 mA·min/(week·m²) — calculated as 400 × 1 × 0.25 times the distance
1.56 mA·min/(week·m²) — calculated as 400 × 0.25 × 0.25 divided by 16
diag.e: W × U × T / d² = 400 × 0.25 × 1 / 4² = 100/16 = 6.25 mA·min/(week·m²). This parameter is used with transmission data tables to determine the required barrier thickness for the specific kVp and shielding material.

diag.q: What is the primary advantage of using a copper (Cu) filter instead of additional aluminium in fluoroscopy?
diag.a: Copper removes more low-energy photons per mm thickness, reducing skin dose with minimal added filtration
c: Copper removes more low-energy photons per mm thickness, reducing skin dose with minimal added filtration
Copper preferentially absorbs high-energy photons, softening the beam for improved image contrast
Copper produces characteristic X-rays at 8 keV that contribute usefully to the fluoroscopic image
Copper has lower cost and is more widely available than aluminium for diagnostic beam filtration
diag.e: Cu (Z=29) is a more efficient filter than Al (Z=13) per unit thickness. Typically 0.1–0.3 mm Cu is added for fluoroscopy, significantly hardening the beam and reducing skin dose by 30–60% with minimal impact on image quality at clinical kVp.

diag.q: The anode heel effect in an X-ray tube results in?
diag.a: Higher beam intensity on the cathode side and lower intensity on the anode side of the field
c: Higher beam intensity on the cathode side and lower intensity on the anode side of the field
Higher beam intensity on the anode side and lower intensity on the cathode side of the field
Uniform beam intensity across the entire field with increased penumbra at the field edges
Reduced beam intensity at the central axis with higher intensity at both peripheral edges
diag.e: X-rays produced deeper in the anode travel through more target material on the anode side, causing self-absorption. This creates up to 30% intensity variation. Thick body parts should be positioned on the cathode side for more uniform exposure.

diag.q: CALC: The inverse square law states that intensity varies as 1/d². If the dose rate at 100 cm from an X-ray tube is 2.0 mGy/min, what is the dose rate at 200 cm?
diag.a: 0.5 mGy/min — the dose rate decreases by a factor of four when distance doubles
c: 0.5 mGy/min — the dose rate decreases by a factor of four when distance doubles
1.0 mGy/min — the dose rate decreases by a factor of two when distance doubles
4.0 mGy/min — the dose rate increases by a factor of two when the distance doubles
0.25 mGy/min — dose rate decreases by a factor of eight when the distance doubles
diag.e: By the inverse square law: I₂ = I₁ × (d₁/d₂)² = 2.0 × (100/200)² = 2.0 × 0.25 = 0.5 mGy/min. Doubling the distance reduces intensity by a factor of 4. This principle is fundamental for radiation protection.

diag.q: Beam hardening artefact in CT appears as?
diag.a: Cupping artefact in uniform phantoms and dark bands between dense structures like bones
c: Cupping artefact in uniform phantoms and dark bands between dense structures like bones
Bright streaks radiating outward from low-density air pockets within the body anatomy
Ring artefacts centred on the isocentre of rotation of the CT gantry scanner system
Uniform increase in noise across the entire reconstructed image without spatial pattern
diag.e: Polychromatic X-rays lose low-energy photons preferentially through thick or dense objects. The beam becomes harder (higher mean energy), causing underestimation of attenuation. This produces cupping in uniform objects and dark bands between dense structures (e.g., posterior fossa).

diag.q: Ring artefacts in CT images are most commonly caused by?
diag.a: A miscalibrated or defective single detector element in the detector array ring
c: A miscalibrated or defective single detector element in the detector array ring
Patient motion during the acquisition causing inconsistent projection data values
Incorrect selection of the reconstruction kernel for the clinical body region scanned
Beam hardening from dense metallic implants located within the scanned field of view
diag.e: A faulty detector element gives consistently wrong readings at one angular position. During reconstruction, this traces out a ring centred on the isocentre. Detector calibration (air scans) should be performed regularly as part of QA to prevent this.

diag.q: CT tube current modulation (automatic exposure control) adjusts the mA based on?
diag.a: Patient attenuation measured from the scout/topogram to equalise noise across anatomy
c: Patient attenuation measured from the scout/topogram to equalise noise across anatomy
The operator-selected image quality level independent of the patient's body habitus
Fixed lookup tables based only on the anatomical region without patient-specific data
Real-time detector dose measurements that maintain a constant detector exposure level
diag.e: TCM uses scout scan data to determine patient attenuation in each projection angle and z-position. mA is increased for high-attenuation projections (lateral through pelvis) and decreased for low-attenuation projections (AP through lungs), reducing dose 20–40% while maintaining image noise.

diag.q: The Nyquist frequency of a digital detector with pixel pitch of 0.2 mm is?
diag.a: 2.5 line pairs per mm — calculated as 1 divided by twice the pixel pitch
c: 2.5 line pairs per mm — calculated as 1 divided by twice the pixel pitch
5.0 line pairs per mm — calculated as 1 divided by the pixel pitch alone
1.0 line pairs per mm — calculated as the pixel pitch multiplied by five
0.4 line pairs per mm — calculated as twice the pixel pitch dimension
diag.e: Nyquist frequency = 1/(2 × pixel pitch) = 1/(2 × 0.2) = 2.5 lp/mm. This is the maximum spatial frequency that can be sampled without aliasing. Frequencies above this are undersampled and can produce aliasing artefacts.

diag.q: CALC: A digital radiography system has a 43 cm × 43 cm active area with a pixel matrix of 3000 × 3000. What is the pixel pitch?
diag.a: 0.143 mm — calculated by dividing the active detector dimension by the matrix size
c: 0.143 mm — calculated by dividing the active detector dimension by the matrix size
0.287 mm — calculated by dividing the active detector dimension by half the matrix
0.072 mm — calculated by dividing the active dimension by double the matrix size
0.430 mm — calculated by dividing the active detector dimension by one thousand
diag.e: Pixel pitch = detector dimension / number of pixels = 430 mm / 3000 = 0.143 mm. This determines the maximum achievable spatial resolution (Nyquist = 1/(2 × 0.143) ≈ 3.5 lp/mm).

diag.q: In mammography quality assurance, the contrast-detail phantom test evaluates?
diag.a: The system's ability to display small, low-contrast objects simulating microcalcifications and masses
c: The system's ability to display small, low-contrast objects simulating microcalcifications and masses
The uniformity of radiation dose distribution across the entire imaging field of the receptor
The accuracy of the automatic exposure control system calibration for varying breast thickness
The linearity of the detector signal response over the full range of exposure levels used
diag.e: Contrast-detail phantoms (e.g., CDMAM) contain gold discs of varying diameter and thickness. Scoring visibility tests the combined effect of contrast, noise, and resolution — directly relevant to detecting subtle breast lesions.

diag.q: CALC: A CT scanner has a CTDI₁₀₀ (centre) of 10 mGy and CTDI₁₀₀ (periphery) of 20 mGy. What is the weighted CTDI (CTDIw)?
diag.a: 16.7 mGy — calculated as (1/3 × centre) + (2/3 × periphery) CTDI values
c: 16.7 mGy — calculated as (1/3 × centre) + (2/3 × periphery) CTDI values
15.0 mGy — calculated as the simple arithmetic average of centre and periphery
30.0 mGy — calculated as the direct sum of the centre and periphery CTDI values
10.0 mGy — calculated using only the centre CTDI value for the weighted result
diag.e: CTDIw = (1/3 × CTDI₁₀₀,centre) + (2/3 × CTDI₁₀₀,periphery) = (1/3 × 10) + (2/3 × 20) = 3.33 + 13.33 = 16.67 mGy. This weighting accounts for the dose distribution across the phantom cross-section.

diag.q: The purpose of the magnification mode in fluoroscopy is to?
diag.a: Improve spatial resolution of a smaller field by using a reduced input field size on the image intensifier or flat panel
c: Improve spatial resolution of a smaller field by using a reduced input field size on the image intensifier or flat panel
Reduce patient radiation dose by decreasing the area irradiated while maintaining tube output
Increase the field of view coverage to visualise a larger anatomical area in a single frame
Decrease image noise by increasing the number of photons detected per unit area of detector
diag.e: Magnification mode uses a smaller portion of the detector, improving resolution but increasing dose rate because the AEC compensates for reduced detector area. Patient dose increases 2–4× in mag mode. This trade-off must be clinically justified.

diag.q: In diagnostic radiology, the ALARA principle requires practitioners to?
diag.a: Keep radiation doses as low as reasonably achievable while maintaining adequate diagnostic image quality
c: Keep radiation doses as low as reasonably achievable while maintaining adequate diagnostic image quality
Eliminate all radiation exposure regardless of the impact on image quality or diagnostic accuracy
Use the maximum allowable dose for every examination to ensure the highest possible image quality
Apply identical exposure parameters to all patients regardless of body habitus or clinical need
diag.e: ALARA (ICRP principle of optimisation) balances dose reduction with diagnostic adequacy. Strategies include proper collimation, technique selection, shielding, digital system optimisation, and clinical audit of dose indicators against diagnostic reference levels (DRLs).

diag.q: Diagnostic reference levels (DRLs) in South Africa are established to?
diag.a: Provide benchmark dose values for common examinations to identify facilities needing dose optimisation
c: Provide benchmark dose values for common examinations to identify facilities needing dose optimisation
Set absolute maximum dose limits that must never be exceeded for any individual patient exposure
Define minimum dose levels required to guarantee adequate image quality for diagnostic purposes
Replace the need for individual patient dose monitoring and quality assurance dose measurements
diag.e: DRLs (typically 75th percentile of facility dose distributions) are not dose limits. They trigger investigation when consistently exceeded. SA DRLs are being developed through national dose surveys coordinated by universities and the NNR.

diag.q: CALC: A QA test measures the CT number of water as +5 HU. The typical acceptance criterion for CT number accuracy of water is?
diag.a: 0 ± 4 HU — meaning +5 HU is outside tolerance and requires corrective calibration action
c: 0 ± 4 HU — meaning +5 HU is outside tolerance and requires corrective calibration action
0 ± 20 HU — meaning +5 HU is well within normal tolerance and requires no action
0 ± 1 HU — meaning any deviation from zero requires immediate scanner service call
0 ± 50 HU — meaning CT number accuracy has very wide acceptable tolerance limits
diag.e: Water CT number should be 0 ± 4 HU (ACR/AAPM guideline). At +5 HU, the scanner fails this criterion and requires recalibration (typically an air/water calibration scan). This is checked monthly or after tube replacement as part of routine QA.

diag.q: Which component of an X-ray tube is responsible for focusing the electron beam onto a small area of the anode?
diag.a: The focusing cup, a negatively charged structure surrounding the cathode filament
c: The focusing cup, a negatively charged structure surrounding the cathode filament
The glass envelope, which maintains vacuum and shapes the electron trajectory
The rotor assembly, which spins the anode to distribute the thermal load evenly
The tube housing, which contains lead lining to absorb off-focus radiation
diag.e: The focusing cup (or Wehnelt cylinder) is a negatively charged metallic structure around the cathode filament. Its negative potential repels electrons inward, converging them onto a narrow focal spot on the anode target. This is critical for spatial resolution.

diag.q: CALC: A CT examination has a CTDIvol of 15 mGy and a scan length of 30 cm. What is the dose-length product (DLP)?
diag.a: 450 mGy·cm — calculated as CTDIvol multiplied by the scan length in centimetres
c: 450 mGy·cm — calculated as CTDIvol multiplied by the scan length in centimetres
45 mGy·cm — calculated as CTDIvol multiplied by the scan length in millimetres
500 mGy·cm — calculated by adding CTDIvol and scan length then multiplying by ten
225 mGy·cm — calculated as CTDIvol multiplied by half the scan length in centimetres
diag.e: DLP = CTDIvol × scan length = 15 mGy × 30 cm = 450 mGy·cm. DLP is the primary metric used for estimating patient dose from a complete CT examination and is used with conversion coefficients to estimate effective dose.

diag.q: What is the primary advantage of direct-conversion flat-panel detectors (DR) over computed radiography (CR) systems?
diag.a: Higher detective quantum efficiency due to elimination of the light-spreading step in signal conversion
c: Higher detective quantum efficiency due to elimination of the light-spreading step in signal conversion
Greater dynamic range allowing imaging at significantly lower and higher exposure levels overall
Ability to reuse imaging plates multiple times without any degradation in image quality
Lower initial cost making them more suitable for resource-limited settings in South Africa
diag.e: Direct-conversion DR detectors (e.g., amorphous selenium) convert X-ray photons directly to electrical charge without an intermediate light stage. This avoids lateral spread of light photons that occurs in CR phosphor plates and indirect DR, yielding superior DQE and spatial resolution.

diag.q: In mammography, the typical anode/filter combination used for imaging dense breast tissue is?
diag.a: Tungsten anode with rhodium filter to provide higher-energy X-ray spectra for penetration
c: Tungsten anode with rhodium filter to provide higher-energy X-ray spectra for penetration
Molybdenum anode with molybdenum filter to maximise characteristic radiation at 17–20 keV
Copper anode with aluminium filter to generate a broad polychromatic X-ray spectrum
Rhodium anode with molybdenum filter to reduce the mean energy of the X-ray beam
diag.e: For dense breasts, a harder beam is needed for adequate penetration. Modern digital mammography units use W/Rh or W/Ag combinations. The tungsten target produces a broader, more penetrating spectrum, and the rhodium filter removes low-energy photons that would only increase dose without contributing to image formation.

diag.q: The modulation transfer function (MTF) of an imaging system describes?
diag.a: The ability of the system to faithfully transfer spatial frequency information from object to image
c: The ability of the system to faithfully transfer spatial frequency information from object to image
The total amount of quantum noise present in the image as a function of radiation exposure
The ratio of output signal to input signal at a single fixed spatial frequency only
The relationship between patient dose and the resulting image contrast for a given object
diag.e: MTF quantifies spatial resolution by measuring how well the system reproduces contrast at each spatial frequency. An MTF of 1.0 at a given frequency means perfect reproduction; as frequency increases, MTF typically drops. It is derived from the Fourier transform of the line spread function.

diag.q: CALC: An X-ray beam has a half-value layer of 3.0 mm Al. What percentage of the original beam intensity remains after passing through 9.0 mm Al?
diag.a: 12.5% — the beam passes through three half-value layers, reducing intensity by half each time
c: 12.5% — the beam passes through three half-value layers, reducing intensity by half each time
25.0% — the beam passes through two half-value layers with a linear correction factor
6.25% — the beam passes through four half-value layers reducing to one-sixteenth
33.3% — the beam intensity is reduced proportionally to the ratio of thickness to HVL
diag.e: Number of HVLs = 9.0 / 3.0 = 3. Remaining intensity = (1/2)^3 = 1/8 = 0.125 = 12.5%. Each HVL reduces the beam intensity by exactly half, assuming narrow-beam geometry with monoenergetic or first-HVL conditions.

diag.q: In fluoroscopy, the purpose of automatic brightness control (ABC) is to?
diag.a: Maintain consistent image brightness by adjusting kVp, mA, and pulse width in response to patient attenuation
c: Maintain consistent image brightness by adjusting kVp, mA, and pulse width in response to patient attenuation
Reduce patient dose by automatically collimating the X-ray beam to the region of clinical interest
Improve spatial resolution by dynamically adjusting the focal spot size during the examination
Eliminate scattered radiation by automatically activating the anti-scatter grid at optimal ratios
diag.e: ABC (also called automatic dose rate control) uses a feedback signal from the image receptor to adjust technique factors — primarily kVp and mA — ensuring adequate image brightness regardless of changes in patient thickness or anatomy during the procedure.

diag.q: The detective quantum efficiency (DQE) of a digital detector is best described as?
diag.a: The ratio of output signal-to-noise ratio squared to input signal-to-noise ratio squared at each spatial frequency
c: The ratio of output signal-to-noise ratio squared to input signal-to-noise ratio squared at each spatial frequency
The total number of X-ray photons absorbed by the detector divided by the number of incident photons
The maximum spatial frequency that can be resolved before the signal falls below the noise floor
The percentage of incident X-ray energy that is converted to electronic signal in the detector
diag.e: DQE = (SNRout²/SNRin²) and is a function of spatial frequency. It is the most comprehensive single metric of detector performance, combining effects of quantum efficiency, noise characteristics (NPS), and spatial resolution (MTF). DQE(f) = MTF(f)² / (NPS(f) × q × gain²).

diag.q: What is the primary purpose of adding copper filtration to a diagnostic X-ray tube?
diag.a: To remove low-energy photons that contribute to patient skin dose without improving image quality
c: To remove low-energy photons that contribute to patient skin dose without improving image quality
To increase the total X-ray output intensity by preferentially transmitting high-energy photons
To reduce the effective focal spot size by collimating the beam at the tube port level
To compensate for the heel effect by providing uniform attenuation across the X-ray field
diag.e: Additional copper filtration (typically 0.1–0.3 mm Cu) hardens the beam by preferentially absorbing low-energy photons. These soft photons are absorbed by superficial tissues, increasing patient skin dose without reaching the detector. This is especially important in fluoroscopy and CT.

diag.q: In paediatric radiography, the most effective strategy for dose reduction compared to adult protocols is?
diag.a: Reducing kVp and mAs appropriately for the child's body habitus and removing the anti-scatter grid
c: Reducing kVp and mAs appropriately for the child's body habitus and removing the anti-scatter grid
Increasing kVp to maximum while maintaining adult mAs values to improve beam penetration
Using the same adult technique factors but applying a shorter exposure time for the examination
Applying a higher grid ratio to compensate for the increased proportion of scattered radiation
diag.e: Children have smaller body habitus, generating less scatter; thus grids can often be removed (especially under 6 years), saving 2–3× in dose. Additionally, technique factors (kVp and mAs) must be scaled down. South African guidelines from the Department of Health emphasise size-specific protocols for paediatric imaging.

diag.q: The noise power spectrum (NPS) of a digital imaging system characterises?
diag.a: The spatial frequency distribution of noise variance within the image
c: The spatial frequency distribution of noise variance within the image
The maximum detective quantum efficiency achievable at zero spatial frequency
The modulation transfer function degradation caused by electronic noise sources
The total integrated dose required to achieve a signal-to-noise ratio of unity
diag.e: NPS (or Wiener spectrum) describes how noise power is distributed across spatial frequencies. It is obtained from the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation of noise. A flat NPS indicates white noise; digital systems often show frequency-dependent noise due to detector structure and processing.

diag.q: In cone-beam CT (CBCT) used in image-guided radiotherapy, the primary image quality limitation compared to conventional CT is?
diag.a: Increased scatter contamination due to the large cone angle producing cupping and shading artefacts
c: Increased scatter contamination due to the large cone angle producing cupping and shading artefacts
Superior spatial resolution that paradoxically reduces soft-tissue contrast in all clinical cases
Inability to acquire volumetric data requiring multiple sequential axial scans for coverage
Limited detector size restricting the maximum field of view to less than ten centimetres
diag.e: CBCT uses a wide cone beam illuminating a large volume simultaneously, generating significantly more scatter than fan-beam CT. This scatter degrades contrast-to-noise ratio and produces cupping artefacts. Scatter rejection (grids, software correction) and calibration protocols are essential for acceptable image quality.

diag.q: Dual-energy CT acquires images at two different kVp settings primarily to?
diag.a: Exploit the energy-dependent attenuation differences between materials for improved tissue characterisation
c: Exploit the energy-dependent attenuation differences between materials for improved tissue characterisation
Double the spatial resolution by combining high-frequency data from both energy acquisitions
Reduce patient dose by averaging the two acquisitions to cancel out quantum noise completely
Eliminate beam-hardening artefacts by using only the higher-energy dataset for final reconstruction
diag.e: Different materials (e.g., iodine, calcium, uric acid, soft tissue) have distinct energy-dependent attenuation profiles, especially near K-edges. By acquiring at two energies (e.g., 80 and 140 kVp), dual-energy CT enables material decomposition, virtual non-contrast images, and improved tissue characterisation.

diag.q: CALC: An AEC system is calibrated to deliver a detector air kerma of 5 μGy. If a patient's abdomen attenuates 98% of the incident beam, and the tube output at the set kVp is 50 μGy/mAs at 100 cm, what approximate mAs will the AEC select (assuming SID of 100 cm)?
diag.a: 5.0 mAs — the AEC increases mAs to compensate for the high attenuation and deliver the target kerma
c: 5.0 mAs — the AEC increases mAs to compensate for the high attenuation and deliver the target kerma
0.1 mAs — the AEC uses minimal mAs since the detector only needs a very small air kerma value
2.5 mAs — the AEC selects half the standard mAs due to the partial attenuation of the beam
50 mAs — the AEC multiplies tube output by the attenuation factor to determine the exposure
diag.e: Transmitted intensity per mAs = 50 × 0.02 = 1.0 μGy/mAs. To achieve 5 μGy at the detector: mAs = 5 / 1.0 = 5.0 mAs. The AEC sensor detects the accumulated signal and terminates exposure once the target detector dose is reached.

diag.q: The purpose of an anti-scatter grid in diagnostic radiography is to?
diag.a: Selectively absorb scattered photons before they reach the detector while transmitting primary radiation
c: Selectively absorb scattered photons before they reach the detector while transmitting primary radiation
Increase the total number of photons reaching the detector to improve the signal-to-noise ratio
Reduce the effective energy of the X-ray beam to improve subject contrast in all examinations
Filter out characteristic radiation from the anode that would degrade image sharpness overall
diag.e: Scatter degrades image contrast. Grids consist of alternating radiopaque (lead) and radiolucent (interspace material) strips aligned with the primary beam. Scattered photons, arriving at oblique angles, are preferentially absorbed by the lead strips, improving contrast at the cost of increased patient dose.

diag.q: Iterative reconstruction algorithms in CT provide improved image quality primarily by?
diag.a: Using statistical modelling and repeated forward-backward projections to reduce noise while preserving spatial resolution
c: Using statistical modelling and repeated forward-backward projections to reduce noise while preserving spatial resolution
Applying a single mathematical back-projection filter that enhances edges and suppresses ring artefacts
Increasing the number of detector elements used during each rotation to improve sampling density
Replacing the fan-beam geometry with a parallel-beam approximation that simplifies reconstruction mathematics
diag.e: Iterative reconstruction (IR) algorithms model the physics of photon statistics, system geometry, and noise. Through repeated cycles of forward projection, comparison with measured data, and correction, IR reduces noise more effectively than filtered back-projection, enabling dose reduction of 30–60% for equivalent image quality.

diag.q: CALC: The effective dose from a CT abdomen with DLP of 600 mGy·cm using a conversion coefficient of 0.015 mSv/(mGy·cm) is?
diag.a: 9.0 mSv — calculated by multiplying the DLP by the region-specific conversion coefficient
c: 9.0 mSv — calculated by multiplying the DLP by the region-specific conversion coefficient
0.9 mSv — calculated by dividing the DLP by the conversion coefficient and adjusting units
90 mSv — calculated by multiplying the DLP by ten times the standard conversion coefficient
6.0 mSv — calculated by multiplying the DLP by a generic whole-body conversion coefficient
diag.e: Effective dose = DLP × k = 600 mGy·cm × 0.015 mSv/(mGy·cm) = 9.0 mSv. The k-factor is region-specific (ICRP Publication 103 values) and provides a rough estimate of stochastic risk. For abdomen/pelvis, k ≈ 0.015 is commonly used.

diag.q: In an X-ray tube, thermionic emission occurs at what component?
diag.a: Cathode filament
c: Rotating anode disc
Cathode filament
Glass envelope wall
Added filtration layer
diag.e: Heating the cathode filament to ~2200 C causes thermionic emission of electrons, which are then accelerated toward the anode.

diag.q: The anode heel effect causes what variation across the X-ray field?
diag.a: Lower intensity on the anode side of the field
c: Lower intensity on the anode side of the field
Higher intensity on the anode side always
Uniform intensity across the entire field
Increased scatter only at field center
diag.e: X-rays produced deeper in the anode target are attenuated more before exiting, reducing intensity on the anode side.

diag.q: Added filtration in a diagnostic X-ray tube primarily removes what?
diag.a: Low-energy photons that increase patient dose
c: Low-energy photons that increase patient dose
High-energy photons above 100 keV
Scatter radiation from the patient
Characteristic X-ray line emissions
diag.e: Filtration (typically aluminum) preferentially absorbs low-energy photons that would be absorbed in the patient without contributing to imaging.

diag.q: Dose-Area Product (DAP) in fluoroscopy is measured in what units?
diag.a: Gy cm2
c: Gy cm2
mSv per hour only
Hounsfield units (HU)
Line pairs per mm
diag.e: DAP (or KAP) is the product of air kerma and beam cross-sectional area, measured in Gy cm2 and remains constant with distance.

diag.q: Reference air kerma in interventional fluoroscopy is measured at what reference point?
diag.a: Interventional reference point (15 cm from isocenter toward source)
c: Interventional reference point (15 cm from isocenter toward source)
At the image receptor entrance surface
On the patient skin surface directly
At one meter from the X-ray tube focus
diag.e: The IEC interventional reference point is located 15 cm from the isocenter toward the focal spot, approximating the patient skin entrance.

diag.q: Modern mammography systems commonly use which target/filter combination?
diag.a: Tungsten target with rhodium filter
c: Tungsten target with rhodium filter
Copper target with aluminum filter
Gold target with lead filtration
Molybdenum target with copper filter
diag.e: Modern digital mammography often uses W/Rh or W/Ag combinations, offering a harder spectrum with better penetration and lower dose.

diag.q: Average glandular dose (AGD) in mammography is determined using what factors?
diag.a: Entrance air kerma, HVL, and conversion factors for breast composition
c: Entrance air kerma, HVL, and conversion factors for breast composition
Total mAs and tube voltage setting only
Image pixel values and display brightness
Patient BMI and compression thickness ratio
diag.e: AGD = K x g x c x s, using entrance air kerma (K), HVL-dependent factor (g), breast composition (c), and X-ray spectrum (s) factors.

diag.q: CT pitch is defined as?
diag.a: Table travel per rotation divided by beam collimation width
c: Table travel per rotation divided by beam collimation width
Number of detector rows times slice thickness
Rotation time divided by total scan length
Tube current multiplied by scan duration
diag.e: Pitch = table movement per gantry rotation / total collimated beam width. Pitch > 1 indicates gaps; pitch < 1 indicates overlap.

diag.q: CTDI100 is measured using what length ionization chamber?
diag.a: 100 mm pencil ionization chamber
c: 100 mm pencil ionization chamber
30 mm thimble ionization chamber
200 mm parallel plate chamber
10 mm micro ion chamber device
diag.e: CTDI100 integrates the dose profile over a 100 mm length using a 100 mm long pencil chamber in a standard CTDI phantom.

diag.q: Dose Length Product (DLP) is calculated from?
diag.a: CTDIvol multiplied by scan length
c: CTDIvol multiplied by scan length
CTDI100 divided by pitch factor
Peak skin dose times field area
Patient weight times tube voltage
diag.e: DLP = CTDIvol x scan length (cm), providing a measure of total radiation dose for the entire CT scan series.

diag.q: Computed Radiography (CR) uses what as the image receptor?
diag.a: Photostimulable phosphor imaging plate
c: Photostimulable phosphor imaging plate
Amorphous selenium flat panel layer
Charge-coupled device (CCD) sensor
Conventional silver halide film base
diag.e: CR uses BaFBr:Eu phosphor plates that store latent images as trapped electrons, released by laser stimulation during readout.

diag.q: Direct conversion DR flat panel detectors use what photoconductor material?
diag.a: Amorphous selenium (a-Se)
c: Amorphous selenium (a-Se)
Cesium iodide scintillator
Gadolinium oxysulfide screen
Silicon dioxide wafer substrate
diag.e: Direct DR uses amorphous selenium to convert X-rays directly to electrical charge, collected by a TFT array, avoiding light spread.

diag.q: Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) describes what image quality property?
diag.a: Spatial resolution as a function of spatial frequency
c: Spatial resolution as a function of spatial frequency
Noise power at each pixel location only
Total dose delivered to the detector
Contrast between bone and soft tissue
diag.e: MTF quantifies how well the imaging system preserves contrast at increasing spatial frequencies, dropping from 1.0 to 0 at the resolution limit.

diag.q: Noise Power Spectrum (NPS) characterizes what aspect of image quality?
diag.a: Noise variance as a function of spatial frequency
c: Noise variance as a function of spatial frequency
Signal intensity versus exposure time
Maximum detectable contrast level only
Geometric distortion across field of view
diag.e: NPS (or Wiener spectrum) decomposes noise into its frequency components, revealing whether noise is dominated by fine or coarse structures.

diag.q: Detective Quantum Efficiency (DQE) combines what two image quality parameters?
diag.a: MTF and NPS relative to input SNR
c: MTF and NPS relative to input SNR
Pixel size and total detector area only
Tube voltage and filtration thickness
Spatial resolution and scan time only
diag.e: DQE = (SNRout2 / SNRin2) and incorporates MTF (signal transfer) and NPS (noise) to describe overall detector dose efficiency.

diag.q: Diagnostic Reference Levels (DRLs) are set at what percentile of the dose distribution from surveys?
diag.a: 75th percentile of the surveyed dose distribution
c: 75th percentile of the surveyed dose distribution
50th percentile (median) of all facilities
95th percentile as absolute maximum limit
25th percentile as the minimum standard
diag.e: DRLs are typically set at the 75th percentile of facility dose surveys, identifying practices with unusually high doses for investigation.

diag.q: In an X-ray tube, what process is primarily responsible for producing the continuous spectrum?
diag.a: Bremsstrahlung radiation from electron deceleration near target nuclei
c: Compton scattering of electrons interacting with outer shell target electrons
Characteristic radiation from electron transitions between atomic energy shells
Bremsstrahlung radiation from electron deceleration near target nuclei
Pair production events occurring at high electron kinetic energies in the target
diag.e: When electrons decelerate in the Coulomb field of target nuclei, they emit bremsstrahlung photons with a continuous energy spectrum up to the maximum electron kinetic energy.

diag.q: What image quality metric describes the ability to distinguish small low-contrast objects in radiography?
diag.a: Contrast-to-noise ratio (CNR) which relates signal difference to image noise
c: Modulation transfer function (MTF) describing spatial resolution at each frequency
Contrast-to-noise ratio (CNR) which relates signal difference to image noise
Detective quantum efficiency (DQE) measuring overall detector dose efficiency
Noise power spectrum (NPS) characterizing the spatial frequency of image noise
diag.e: CNR measures the difference in signal between an object and background relative to image noise, directly determining visibility of low-contrast structures.

diag.q: What dose metric is used to estimate patient skin dose in fluoroscopy-guided interventional procedures?
diag.a: Reference air kerma (cumulative dose) at the interventional reference point
c: CT dose index (CTDI) measured in standard cylindrical acrylic phantoms
Reference air kerma (cumulative dose) at the interventional reference point
Dose-length product (DLP) calculated from scan length and dose index values
Mean glandular dose (MGD) determined from entrance exposure and conversion factors
diag.e: The interventional reference point is located 15 cm from isocenter toward the X-ray tube, and cumulative air kerma at this point estimates potential skin dose for deterministic effects.

diag.q: What is the primary advantage of digital radiography (DR) detectors over computed radiography (CR)?
diag.a: Higher detective quantum efficiency and faster image acquisition workflow
c: Lower equipment purchase and maintenance cost over the detector lifetime
Higher detective quantum efficiency and faster image acquisition workflow
Wider dynamic range enabling imaging across a broader exposure range
Greater portability allowing easier positioning for bedside examinations
diag.e: DR flat-panel detectors have higher DQE than CR phosphor plates and provide immediate image display without the separate plate reading step required by CR systems.

diag.q: What is the purpose of automatic exposure control (AEC) in diagnostic X-ray systems?
diag.a: To terminate the exposure when sufficient signal is detected at the image receptor
c: To automatically select the optimal kVp based on patient body part thickness
To terminate the exposure when sufficient signal is detected at the image receptor
To adjust the focal spot size based on the required spatial resolution level
To control the X-ray tube warm-up sequence before each exposure is initiated
diag.e: AEC sensors behind or within the image receptor measure transmitted radiation and terminate the exposure when adequate signal is reached, ensuring consistent image quality.

diag.q: What effect does increasing tube voltage (kVp) have on the X-ray beam characteristics?
diag.a: Higher maximum photon energy, increased beam intensity, and greater penetration
c: Lower mean photon energy with increased total number of emitted X-ray photons
Higher maximum photon energy, increased beam intensity, and greater penetration
Narrower beam spectrum with reduced bremsstrahlung and more characteristic peaks
Decreased patient dose due to higher efficiency of X-ray photon production overall
diag.e: Increasing kVp raises the maximum and mean photon energies, increases beam output approximately as the square of kVp, and produces a more penetrating beam.

radiobio.q: Linear-quadratic model formula?
radiobio.a: S = e^(−αD − βD²)
c: S = e^(−αD)
S = e^(−αD − βD²)
S = αD + βD²
radiobio.e: α: single-hit kills. β: two-hit kills. Foundation of fractionation theory.

radiobio.q: α/β for late-responding tissue?
radiobio.a: ~3 Gy
c: ~1 Gy
~3 Gy
~10 Gy
~20 Gy
radiobio.e: Spinal cord, lung fibrosis. Sensitive to fraction size — basis for 2 Gy fractionation.

radiobio.q: α/β for early-responding tissue?
radiobio.a: ~10 Gy
c: ~1 Gy
~3 Gy
~10 Gy
~20 Gy
radiobio.e: Most tumours, skin, mucosa. Less sensitive to fraction size changes.

radiobio.q: 4 R's of radiobiology?
radiobio.a: Repair, Redistribution, Repopulation, Reoxygenation
c: Repair, Redistribution, Repopulation, Reoxygenation
Repair, Rejection, Regrowth, Recovery
Resistance, Repair, Redistribution, Rejection
Reoxygenation, Recovery, Resistance, Repair
radiobio.e: Biological rationale for fractionation.

radiobio.q: OER stands for?
radiobio.a: Oxygen Enhancement Ratio
c: Optical Emission Ratio
Oxygen Enhancement Ratio
Output Energy Ratio
Organ Equivalence Ratio
radiobio.e: Dose ratio for same effect: hypoxic vs oxygenated.

radiobio.q: OER for photons?
c: 1.0 in the treatment process
1.5 within the clinical setting
5.0 within the clinical setting
radiobio.e: Hypoxic cells need 2.5-3× more dose.

radiobio.q: RBE stands for?
radiobio.a: Relative Biological Effectiveness
c: Radiation Beam Energy
Relative Biological Effectiveness
Relative Beam Efficiency
Radiobiological Effect

radiobio.q: Radiomics in radiotherapy uses?

AI-based treatment delivery, based on established clinical physics practice
Automated contouring only, based on established clinical physics practice

c: Only genetic sequencing — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Imaging features with genomic data to predict radiosensitivity and outcomes
Radiation dose with gene therapy

radiobio.q: AI-driven adaptive fractionation would allow?

c: Only standard fractionation — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Modifying dose per fraction based on real-time tumour response biomarkers
Eliminating fractionation entirely

radiobio.q: Normal tissue complication probability (NTCP) models using machine learning improve on Lyman-Kutcher-Burman by?
radiobio.a: Incorporating patient-specific factors (genetics, comorbidities, DVH shape) beyond simple dose-volume metrics
c: Using simpler math — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
Incorporating patient-specific factors (genetics, comorbidities, DVH shape) beyond simple dose-volume metrics
Ignoring dose-volume data, following current regulatory and professional standards
Only using imaging — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
radiobio.e: Traditional NTCP: Dmax or Dmean + volume parameter. ML models: include age, chemotherapy, smoking, genetic variants, full 3D dose distribution → more accurate toxicity prediction. Enables truly personalised treatment planning.

radiobio.q: FLASH radiotherapy dose rate threshold?
radiobio.a: > 40 Gy/s (ultra-high dose rate)
c: > 1 Gy/s (conventional high-dose-rate brachytherapy threshold)
> 10 Gy/s (moderately elevated dose rate seen in some electron beams)
> 40 Gy/s (ultra-high dose rate)
> 1000 Gy/s (only achievable in synchrotron microbeam research facilities)
radiobio.e: FLASH effect observed above ~40 Gy/s in preclinical models. Proposed mechanism: transient O₂ depletion in normal tissue (faster than re-oxygenation) while tumour hypoxia is already present. First human patient treated 2019 (skin).

radiobio.q: FLASH sparing mechanism most likely involves?
radiobio.a: Transient oxygen depletion in normal tissue reducing indirect DNA damage
c: Direct DNA damage protection through enhanced base excision repair pathways
Transient oxygen depletion in normal tissue reducing indirect DNA damage
Rapid heat dissipation preventing thermal damage to surrounding normal tissue
Selective immune suppression in normal tissue reducing inflammatory radiation response
radiobio.e: Ultra-high dose rate consumes local O₂ faster than diffusion replaces it. Normal tissue (well-oxygenated): O₂ depleted → reduced radical fixation → less damage. Tumour (already hypoxic): no additional protection. Differential sparing.

radiobio.q: Current FLASH delivery technology challenge?
radiobio.a: Achieving therapeutic dose rates at depth with adequate field size and dose uniformity
c: Producing high enough energy, based on established clinical physics practice
Achieving therapeutic dose rates at depth with adequate field size and dose uniformity
Patient immobilisation — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Treatment planning only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
radiobio.e: Electron FLASH: easy to achieve dose rate but limited depth. Proton FLASH: promising (transmission beams) but requires high beam current. Photon FLASH: extremely difficult due to target heat load. Dosimetry at ultra-high dose rates also challenging (ion chamber saturation).

radiobio.q: Immunotherapy combined with RT: radiobiological rationale?
radiobio.a: RT causes immunogenic cell death → antigen release → synergises with checkpoint inhibitors
c: RT completely suppresses all immune function making combination therapy counterproductive
RT causes immunogenic cell death → antigen release → synergises with checkpoint inhibitors
Immunotherapy fully replaces RT for all solid tumours — combination is unnecessary
No documented biological interaction exists between ionising radiation and immune checkpoint drugs
radiobio.e: RT induces: (1) calreticulin exposure, (2) HMGB1 release, (3) ATP release → dendritic cell activation → T-cell priming. Combined with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors → enhanced systemic anti-tumour immunity. Basis of abscopal effect enhancement.

radiobio.q: Abscopal effect: why is it more commonly observed with concurrent immunotherapy?
radiobio.a: Checkpoint inhibitors prevent T-cell exhaustion, amplifying RT-induced immune response at distant sites
c: Checkpoint inhibitors directly increase radiation dose deposition at distant tumour sites
Checkpoint inhibitors prevent T-cell exhaustion, amplifying RT-induced immune response at distant sites
Immunotherapy causes direct cytotoxic tumour kill at distance independent of immune activation
Random statistical coincidence — no causal relationship between immunotherapy and abscopal effect
radiobio.e: RT alone: tumour antigens released but immune checkpoints (PD-L1, CTLA-4) suppress response. Adding checkpoint inhibitors: unleashes RT-primed T-cells → systemic effect. Clinical trials (KEYNOTE, PACIFIC) confirm synergy.

radiobio.q: Optimal RT dose/fractionation for immune activation?
radiobio.a: Moderate hypofractionation (6-12 Gy × 3-5 fx) may optimise immune priming
c: 2 Gy × 30 fx is always best, based on established clinical physics practice
Single fraction 20+ Gy — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Moderate hypofractionation (6-12 Gy × 3-5 fx) may optimise immune priming
No dose dependence — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
radiobio.e: Preclinical data suggests: too low dose = insufficient antigen release. Too high single dose = activates Trex1 exonuclease → degrades cytosolic DNA → suppresses cGAS-STING immune pathway. 8-12 Gy per fraction appears optimal. Active research area.

radiobio.q: Bystander effect signalling pathways include?
radiobio.a: Gap junctions, soluble factors (ROS, cytokines), and extracellular vesicles
c: Only direct cell contact — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Gap junctions, soluble factors (ROS, cytokines), and extracellular vesicles
Only photon scatter — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Electromagnetic radiation from cells
radiobio.e: Three pathways: (1) gap junctions between adjacent cells. (2) Secreted factors: ROS, NO, TGF-β, TNF-α into medium. (3) Exosomes/microvesicles carrying damaged DNA/RNA. Can cause DNA damage, apoptosis, and genomic instability in unirradiated cells.

radiobio.q: DNA damage response (DDR) inhibitors combined with RT target?
radiobio.a: PARP, ATR, ATM, DNA-PKcs
c: Only PARP for the treatment procedure
PARP, ATR, ATM, DNA-PKcs
Protein synthesis
Cell membrane for therapeutic applications
radiobio.e: blocking DNA repair pathways to radiosensitise tumours. DDR inhibitors block tumour DNA repair: PARP (SSB repair), ATR/ATM (checkpoint signalling), DNA-PKcs (NHEJ). Tumours with existing DDR defects (BRCA-mutant) are especially sensitive. Synthetic lethality principle. Multiple clinical trials ongoing.

radiobio.q: Hyperthermia combined with RT: mechanism of radiosensitisation?
radiobio.a: Inhibits DNA repair (especially homologous recombination), increases blood flow → reoxygenation
c: Only kills cells by heat — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Inhibits DNA repair (especially homologous recombination), increases blood flow → reoxygenation
Increases fraction size — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
No proven mechanism — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
radiobio.e: 41-43°C for 1 hour: (1) denatures DNA repair proteins (BRCA2, RAD51). (2) Increases tumour perfusion → reoxygenation → enhanced indirect action. (3) Immune stimulation. TER ≈ 1.2-1.5. Evidence base: cervix, breast, H&N, sarcoma.

radiobio.q: Genomic-adjusted radiation dose (GARD) is?
radiobio.a: A patient-specific metric that predicts radiation sensitivity based on gene expression profile
c: Standard BED calculation — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
A patient-specific metric that predicts radiation sensitivity based on gene expression profile
A new treatment planning algorithm, based on established clinical physics practice
A quality metric for IMRT, verified through standard quality assurance protocols
radiobio.e: GARD = nd(α + βd × gene expression signature). Derived from radiosensitivity index (RSI) based on 10-gene signature. Patients with high GARD have better outcomes. May guide dose escalation/de-escalation. Moving toward clinical validation.

radiobio.q: MR-guided adaptive radiotherapy (MRgART) radiobiological advantage?
radiobio.a: Daily anatomy visualisation enables margin reduction
c: Better beam energy — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Daily anatomy visualisation enables margin reduction
MRI treats the tumour — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Only for brain tumours — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
radiobio.e: dose escalation to tumour or OAR sparing. MR-linac (Unity, MRIdian): daily MRI → see tumour and OAR positions → reoptimise plan online. Enables: (1) smaller PTV margins. (2) Isotoxic dose escalation (higher BED to tumour at same NTCP). (3) Adaptive fractionation based on tumour regression. Paradigm shift.

radiobio.q: What is radiobiology?
radiobio.a: The study of the effects of ionising radiation on living organisms
c: The study of radio wave communications
The study of the effects of ionising radiation on living organisms
The study of how to build radiation machines
The study of non-ionising light therapy
radiobio.e: Radiobiology is the branch of science that studies the biological effects of ionising radiation on living cells, tissues, and organisms. Understanding radiobiology is essential for optimising radiation therapy and minimising harm to normal tissues.

radiobio.q: What is the most critical target for radiation damage within a cell?
radiobio.a: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid)
c: Cell membrane as part of standard practice
Mitochondria in the treatment process
DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid)
Ribosomes for clinical implementation
radiobio.e: DNA is considered the most critical target for radiation damage in cells. Damage to DNA, particularly double-strand breaks, can lead to cell death, mutations, or loss of the cell's ability to reproduce. This is the primary mechanism by which radiation kills cancer cells.

radiobio.q: What are the two main types of DNA damage caused by ionising radiation?
radiobio.a: Single-strand breaks and double-strand breaks
c: Compression and expansion for the treatment procedure
Heating and cooling within the treatment system
Single-strand breaks and double-strand breaks
Oxidation and reduction only
radiobio.e: Ionising radiation can cause single-strand breaks (SSBs) and double-strand breaks (DSBs) in the DNA molecule. Double-strand breaks are considered the most lethal form of DNA damage because they are more difficult for the cell to repair correctly.

radiobio.q: What does fractionation mean in radiation therapy?
radiobio.a: Dividing the total radiation dose into multiple smaller doses given over several sessions
c: Giving the entire dose in one single treatment as specified by guidelines
Dividing the total radiation dose into multiple smaller doses given over several sessions
Using only a fraction of the prescribed dose according to clinical protocols
Treating only a fraction of the tumour during the planning stage
radiobio.e: Fractionation is the practice of delivering the total prescribed radiation dose in multiple smaller fractions (typically daily over several weeks). This allows normal tissues to repair between fractions while still effectively killing tumour cells.

radiobio.q: What is a cell survival curve used to represent in radiobiology?
radiobio.a: The fraction of cells that survive as a function of radiation dose
c: The number of patients who survive cancer treatment
The fraction of cells that survive as a function of radiation dose
The lifespan of a radioactive isotope
The time taken for a tumour to grow
radiobio.e: A cell survival curve is a graph that plots the fraction (or percentage) of cells that retain their reproductive ability as a function of the radiation dose received. It is a fundamental tool in radiobiology for understanding how different cell types respond to radiation.

radiobio.q: CALC: A conventional prostate plan delivers 78 Gy/39 fx. An alternative delivers 60 Gy/20 fx. Using α/β = 1.5 Gy for prostate tumour, compare the BED values.
radiobio.a: Conventional BED = 182 Gy₁.₅, Hypo BED = 180 Gy₁.₅ — approximately equivalent
c: Conventional BED = 182 Gy₁.₅, Hypo BED = 180 Gy₁.₅ — approximately equivalent
Conventional BED = 120 Gy₁.₅, Hypo BED = 140 Gy₁.₅ — hypo is hotter
Conventional BED = 182 Gy₁.₅, Hypo BED = 120 Gy₁.₅ — conventional much higher
Both BED = 150 Gy₁.₅ — identical
radiobio.e: Conventional: d = 2 Gy/fx. BED = 78 × (1 + 2/1.5) = 78 × 2.333 = 182.0 Gy₁.₅. Hypofractionated: d = 3 Gy/fx. BED = 60 × (1 + 3/1.5) = 60 × 3.0 = 180.0 Gy₁.₅. The BEDs are nearly identical (182 vs 180), confirming biological equivalence for tumour. This is why moderate hypofractionation is effective for prostate cancer — the low α/β ratio means larger fractions produce equivalent tumour BED with fewer treatments. Late normal tissue effects (α/β ≈ 3 Gy) must be separately evaluated.

radiobio.q: CALC: A head-and-neck plan delivers 70 Gy/35 fx over 7 weeks. Treatment is interrupted for 1 week. Tumour repopulation rate Dprolif = 0.6 Gy/day (active after day 28). What BED is lost to the 7-day gap?
radiobio.a: 4.2 Gy₁₀
c: 0 Gy₁₀ — gaps have no radiobiological effect
4.2 Gy₁₀
14 Gy₁₀
2.1 Gy₁₀
radiobio.e: BED loss from treatment gap = Dprolif × gap days = 0.6 Gy/day × 7 days = 4.2 Gy₁₀. The original plan BED = 70(1 + 2/10) = 84 Gy₁₀. A 4.2 Gy₁₀ loss represents a 5% reduction in effective tumour BED. Clinical data show approximately 1.4% loss of local control per day of treatment prolongation during the accelerated repopulation phase. Compensation strategies include adding extra fractions or using twice-daily treatments to restore overall treatment time.

radiobio.q: CALC: For lung SBRT of 54 Gy/3 fx, calculate the tumour BED (α/β = 10 Gy) and the late-tissue BED (α/β = 3 Gy).
radiobio.a: Tumour BED = 151.2 Gy₁₀, Late-tissue BED = 378 Gy₃
c: Tumour BED = 151.2 Gy₁₀, Late-tissue BED = 378 Gy₃
Tumour BED = 86.4 Gy₁₀, Late-tissue BED = 86.4 Gy₃
Tumour BED = 378 Gy₁₀, Late-tissue BED = 151.2 Gy₃
Tumour BED = 108 Gy₁₀, Late-tissue BED = 162 Gy₃
radiobio.e: Dose per fraction d = 54/3 = 18 Gy. Tumour BED = 54(1 + 18/10) = 54 × 2.8 = 151.2 Gy₁₀. Late-tissue BED = 54(1 + 18/3) = 54 × 7.0 = 378 Gy₃. The late-tissue BED is extremely high, yet SBRT is clinically tolerable because only a small volume receives the full dose (steep dose gradients), serial organ architecture tolerates focal high dose, and the volume effect is the dominant factor enabling SBRT safety. The LQ model may also overestimate cell kill at 18 Gy/fx.

radiobio.q: CALC: A head-and-neck tumour has a clonogen doubling time Td = 3 days during accelerated repopulation. If a 7-day unplanned treatment gap occurs, how many extra doublings occur and by what factor does the surviving clonogen population increase?
radiobio.a: 2.33 doublings; ~5-fold increase
c: 1.0 doubling; 2-fold increase
2.33 doublings; ~5-fold increase
7.0 doublings; 128-fold increase
3.5 doublings; ~11-fold increase
radiobio.e: Number of extra doublings = gap / Td = 7 / 3 = 2.33. Factor increase = 2^2.33 = 2² × 2^0.33 = 4 × 1.257 = 5.03, approximately 5-fold. This dramatic increase in surviving clonogens during treatment gaps explains why head-and-neck radiotherapy prolongation is associated with approximately 1.4% loss in local control per day of extension. Every effort must be made to maintain the planned overall treatment time.

radiobio.q: In the linear-quadratic model, what does a high alpha/beta ratio (e.g., 10 Gy) indicate about a tissue's fractionation sensitivity?
radiobio.a: The tissue is relatively insensitive to fractionation changes, with cell kill dominated by the linear (alpha) component
c: The tissue benefits greatly from hypofractionation with very large doses per fraction
The tissue is relatively insensitive to fractionation changes, with cell kill dominated by the linear (alpha) component
The tissue has a large capacity for sublethal damage repair between fractions
The tissue shows increasing cell kill with decreasing dose per fraction
radiobio.e: High α/β means the α (linear, single-hit) term dominates. Changing fraction size has less impact on BED. Typical of rapidly proliferating tumours (α/β ≈ 10 Gy). Low α/β tissues (late-responding, α/β ≈ 3 Gy) are more sensitive to fraction size changes, favouring conventional fractionation to spare them.

radiobio.q: What is the oxygen enhancement ratio (OER) and why does it plateau at oxygen tensions above ~30 mmHg?
radiobio.a: OER is the ratio of dose in hypoxia to dose in normoxia for the same effect; above 30 mmHg, oxygen-mediated radical fixation is saturated
c: OER measures the ratio of oxygen consumption to production in irradiated tissue
OER is the ratio of dose in hypoxia to dose in normoxia for the same effect; above 30 mmHg, oxygen-mediated radical fixation is saturated
OER only applies to high-LET radiation where oxygen has maximal radiosensitising effect
OER is the fraction of cells that are hypoxic in a tumour volume
radiobio.e: OER = D_hypoxic/D_normoxic for same biological effect. For low-LET radiation, OER ≈ 2.5-3.0. Oxygen 'fixes' free radical damage, making it permanent. Above ~30 mmHg pO2, all available radical sites are fixed, so more oxygen provides no additional sensitisation. For high-LET, OER approaches 1.0 because direct DNA damage dominates.

radiobio.q: What are the four Rs of radiobiology and which R primarily justifies fractionated radiotherapy?
radiobio.a: Repair, Redistribution, Reoxygenation, Repopulation — Repair of sublethal damage in normal tissues is the primary justification
c: Repair, Redistribution, Reoxygenation, Repopulation — Repopulation of tumour cells justifies fractionation
Repair, Redistribution, Reoxygenation, Repopulation — Repair of sublethal damage in normal tissues is the primary justification
Resistance, Recovery, Reoxygenation, Redistribution — Resistance of tumour cells justifies fractionation
Repair, Redistribution, Reoxygenation, Repopulation — Redistribution of cells into sensitive phases is the primary justification
radiobio.e: The 4 Rs: (1) Repair: normal tissues repair sublethal damage more efficiently than tumours between fractions. (2) Redistribution: cells reassort into radiosensitive cell cycle phases. (3) Reoxygenation: hypoxic tumour cells gain oxygen access as tumour shrinks. (4) Repopulation: cells proliferate between fractions (disadvantageous for tumour control if treatment is prolonged).

radiobio.q: CALC: A tumour is treated with 30 fractions of 2 Gy (α/β = 10 Gy). An alternative hypofractionated schedule uses 5 fractions. What dose per fraction gives the same tumour BED?
radiobio.a: 8.0 Gy per fraction
c: 5.0 Gy per fraction
6.0 Gy per fraction
7.2 Gy per fraction
8.0 Gy per fraction

radiobio.q: The bystander effect in radiobiology refers to what phenomenon, and what is its clinical significance?
radiobio.a: Unirradiated cells near irradiated cells show biological damage via intercellular signalling, potentially affecting low-dose risk models
c: Radiation damage is confined exclusively to the directly irradiated cell volume
Unirradiated cells near irradiated cells show biological damage via intercellular signalling, potentially affecting low-dose risk models
Bystander cells always show a protective adaptive response after radiation exposure
The effect only occurs with high-LET radiation such as alpha particles
radiobio.e: Bystander effect: irradiated cells release signals (ROS, cytokines, exosomes) that damage neighbouring unirradiated cells, causing DNA damage, mutations, and apoptosis. Demonstrated with both high and low LET radiation. Implications: (1) Low-dose risk may be underestimated by target theory. (2) Abscopal effect in immunotherapy may involve bystander mechanisms.

radiobio.q: CALC: Compare BED for late effects (α/β = 3 Gy) between 60 Gy in 30 fractions and 40.05 Gy in 15 fractions. Which schedule has higher late-tissue BED?
radiobio.a: 60 Gy/30 fx has higher late BED (100 Gy₃) compared to 40.05 Gy/15 fx (75.7 Gy₃)
c: Both schedules have identical late-tissue BED of 100 Gy₃
60 Gy/30 fx has higher late BED (100 Gy₃) compared to 40.05 Gy/15 fx (75.7 Gy₃)
40.05 Gy/15 fx has higher late BED because larger dose per fraction always increases late effects
Cannot be determined without knowing tumour α/β ratio
radiobio.e: 60 Gy/30 fx: BED₃ = 60(1+2/3) = 60×1.667 = 100 Gy₃. 40.05 Gy/15 fx: d=2.67 Gy, BED₃ = 40.05(1+2.67/3) = 40.05×1.89 = 75.7 Gy₃. Conventional (60/30) has HIGHER late BED despite lower dose per fraction because total dose is much higher. The hypofractionated schedule is approximately iso-effective for tumour (BED₁₀ both ≈72) but spares late tissues.

radiobio.q: In the linear-quadratic model, which component of cell kill dominates at low doses per fraction?
radiobio.a: The alpha (αD) component representing single-hit lethal damage
c: The alpha (αD) component representing single-hit lethal damage
The beta (βD²) component representing accumulated sublethal hits
The gamma component representing indirect radiation cell damage
The delta component representing oxygen-dependent lethal damage
radiobio.e: At low doses, the linear (αD) term dominates cell kill because it represents single-track lethal events. The quadratic (βD²) term becomes significant at higher doses where two sublethal lesions interact.

radiobio.q: CALC: Using the LQ model, what is the surviving fraction after 2 Gy if α = 0.3 Gy⁻¹ and β = 0.03 Gy⁻²?
radiobio.a: 0.49 (approximately 49% survival)
c: 0.49 (approximately 49% survival)
0.37 (approximately 37% survival)
0.74 (approximately 74% survival)
0.25 (approximately 25% survival)
