radiobio.e: SF = exp(−αD − βD²) = exp(−0.3×2 − 0.03×4) = exp(−0.6 − 0.12) = exp(−0.72) ≈ 0.49. The α/β ratio here is 10 Gy, typical of acute-responding tumour tissue.

radiobio.q: What is the primary biological advantage of fractionated radiotherapy over a single large dose?
radiobio.a: It exploits differential repair capacity between tumour and normal tissue
c: It exploits differential repair capacity between tumour and normal tissue
It increases the total physical dose that can be delivered to the tumour
It eliminates all hypoxic cells through direct ionisation during treatment
It prevents any repopulation of tumour cells between treatment fractions
radiobio.e: Fractionation allows normal tissues (with efficient sublethal damage repair) to recover between fractions while tumour cells, with less efficient repair, accumulate lethal damage. This widens the therapeutic ratio.

radiobio.q: Which of the following best describes the α/β ratio for late-responding normal tissues?
radiobio.a: Typically 2–4 Gy, indicating high sensitivity to fraction size changes
c: Typically 2–4 Gy, indicating high sensitivity to fraction size changes
Typically 8–12 Gy, indicating low sensitivity to fraction size changes
Typically 15–20 Gy, indicating minimal repair of sublethal damage
Typically 0.1–0.5 Gy, indicating extreme resistance to all radiation
radiobio.e: Late-responding tissues (e.g. spinal cord, lung) have low α/β ratios (2–4 Gy), making them more sensitive to changes in dose per fraction. This is why hypofractionation requires careful planning to spare late-responding structures.

radiobio.q: CALC: Calculate the BED for a regimen of 60 Gy in 30 fractions for a tissue with α/β = 3 Gy.
radiobio.a: 100.0 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
c: 100.0 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
72.0 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
120.0 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
84.0 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
radiobio.e: d = 60/30 = 2 Gy/fraction. BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 60 × (1 + 2/3) = 60 × 1.667 = 100.0 Gy₃. Late-responding tissues experience significant biological effect from standard fractionation.

radiobio.q: DNA double-strand breaks (DSBs) are considered the most critical lesion for radiation-induced cell death because they:
radiobio.a: Are difficult to repair accurately and can lead to chromosomal aberrations
c: Are difficult to repair accurately and can lead to chromosomal aberrations
Are always repaired quickly through base excision repair enzyme pathways
Only occur from direct ionisation and never from indirect radical action
Are exclusively caused by high-LET radiation and not by photon beams
radiobio.e: DSBs involve breakage of both DNA strands in close proximity. They are harder to repair than SSBs because there is no intact complementary strand to use as a template, leading to misrepair, chromosomal aberrations, and cell death.

radiobio.q: Approximately how many DNA double-strand breaks (DSBs) are produced per gray of low-LET radiation per cell?
radiobio.a: Approximately 35–40 DSBs per Gy per cell on average
c: Approximately 35–40 DSBs per Gy per cell on average
Approximately 1–5 DSBs per Gy per cell on average
Approximately 500–1000 DSBs per Gy per cell on average
Approximately 150–200 DSBs per Gy per cell on average
radiobio.e: Low-LET radiation produces approximately 35–40 DSBs per Gy per cell, alongside roughly 1000 SSBs per Gy. While SSBs are efficiently repaired, unrepaired or misrepaired DSBs are the primary cause of radiation-induced cell death.

radiobio.q: Which DNA repair pathway is the primary mechanism for repairing radiation-induced double-strand breaks in mammalian cells?
radiobio.a: Non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) throughout the cell cycle
c: Non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) throughout the cell cycle
Nucleotide excision repair (NER) active only during G1 phase
Mismatch repair (MMR) primarily active during DNA replication
Base excision repair (BER) active predominantly in G2 phase
radiobio.e: NHEJ is the dominant DSB repair pathway in mammalian cells, active throughout all cell cycle phases. It directly ligates broken ends but is error-prone. Homologous recombination is more accurate but limited to S/G2 phases when a sister chromatid template is available.

radiobio.q: Which of the 4 Rs of radiobiology explains why tumour cells in resistant phases at the first fraction may become sensitive by subsequent fractions?
radiobio.a: Redistribution of cells into more radiosensitive cell cycle phases
c: Redistribution of cells into more radiosensitive cell cycle phases
Reoxygenation of hypoxic tumour cells between radiation fractions
Repopulation of surviving cells to maintain the tumour cell number
Repair of sublethal radiation damage in both tumour and normal cells
radiobio.e: Redistribution (reassortment) refers to the progression of surviving cells through the cell cycle between fractions. Cells in resistant phases (late S) move into sensitive phases (G2/M), increasing the effectiveness of subsequent fractions.

radiobio.q: Reoxygenation between radiation fractions benefits tumour control because:
radiobio.a: Previously hypoxic tumour cells gain oxygen and become more radiosensitive
c: Previously hypoxic tumour cells gain oxygen and become more radiosensitive
Normal tissue cells lose oxygen and become selectively more radioresistant
Oxygen directly causes additional DNA strand breaks in all irradiated cells
Reoxygenation eliminates the need for fraction sizes above 1.8 Gy per day
radiobio.e: As well-oxygenated tumour cells are killed by radiation, the surviving hypoxic cells gain better access to oxygen from nearby blood vessels. These reoxygenated cells become radiosensitive, improving tumour kill in subsequent fractions.

radiobio.q: Accelerated repopulation in head and neck tumours typically begins approximately how many weeks after the start of radiotherapy?
radiobio.a: Around 3–4 weeks after the commencement of treatment
c: Around 3–4 weeks after the commencement of treatment
Around 1–2 days after the commencement of treatment
Around 8–10 weeks after the commencement of treatment
Around 6–7 weeks after the commencement of treatment
radiobio.e: Accelerated repopulation begins around 3–4 weeks (the 'kick-off time' or Tk) in head and neck cancers. This rapid clonogen proliferation can offset radiation cell kill, which is why treatment gaps should be minimised and overall treatment time kept short.

radiobio.q: The oxygen enhancement ratio (OER) is defined as the ratio of:
radiobio.a: Dose in hypoxic conditions to dose in oxygenated conditions for the same biological effect
c: Dose in hypoxic conditions to dose in oxygenated conditions for the same biological effect
Dose in oxygenated conditions to dose in hypoxic conditions for the same biological effect
Surviving fraction under hypoxia to surviving fraction under normoxia at equal dose
Tumour control probability under oxygen to normal tissue complication probability ratio
radiobio.e: OER = dose under hypoxia / dose under aerobic conditions for the same biological effect. For low-LET radiation, OER is approximately 2.5–3.0, meaning hypoxic cells require 2.5–3× more dose to achieve the same level of cell kill.

radiobio.q: CALC: If the dose required to achieve 50% cell kill under hypoxic conditions is 7.5 Gy and under aerobic conditions is 2.5 Gy, what is the OER?
radiobio.a: 3.0 (hypoxic dose divided by aerobic dose)
c: 3.0 (hypoxic dose divided by aerobic dose)
5.0 (the absolute dose difference value)
0.33 (aerobic dose divided by hypoxic dose)
1.5 (square root of the dose ratio value)
radiobio.e: OER = dose (hypoxic) / dose (aerobic) = 7.5 / 2.5 = 3.0. This is consistent with the typical OER of 2.5–3.0 for low-LET radiation such as megavoltage photon beams.

radiobio.q: How does the OER change as the linear energy transfer (LET) of radiation increases?
radiobio.a: The OER decreases toward 1.0 as LET increases above 100 keV/μm
c: The OER decreases toward 1.0 as LET increases above 100 keV/μm
The OER increases proportionally with LET up to 200 keV/μm values
The OER remains constant at 2.5–3.0 regardless of the LET value
The OER first increases then decreases but never falls below 2.0
radiobio.e: High-LET radiation (e.g. alpha particles, heavy ions) produces dense ionisation that causes direct, irreparable DNA damage regardless of oxygen status. Thus OER approaches 1.0 at high LET values (~100–200 keV/μm), negating the oxygen effect.

radiobio.q: Relative biological effectiveness (RBE) is defined as the ratio of:
radiobio.a: Reference radiation dose to test radiation dose for the same biological endpoint
c: Reference radiation dose to test radiation dose for the same biological endpoint
Test radiation dose to reference radiation dose for the same biological endpoint
Absorbed dose in tissue to absorbed dose in water for identical beam conditions
Physical dose in gray to biologically effective dose for the same tissue type
radiobio.e: RBE = D(reference) / D(test) for the same biological effect. The reference radiation is typically 250 kVp X-rays or ⁶⁰Co gamma rays. An RBE > 1 means the test radiation is more effective per unit dose than the reference.

radiobio.q: CALC: If 2 Gy of neutron radiation produces the same biological effect as 6 Gy of 250 kVp X-rays, what is the RBE of the neutrons?
radiobio.a: RBE = 3.0 for this neutron beam
c: RBE = 3.0 for this neutron beam
RBE = 0.33 for this neutron beam
RBE = 4.0 for this neutron beam
RBE = 8.0 for this neutron beam
radiobio.e: RBE = D(reference) / D(test) = 6 Gy / 2 Gy = 3.0. This means the neutrons are 3 times more biologically effective than 250 kVp X-rays per unit of absorbed dose for this particular endpoint.

radiobio.q: At what approximate LET value does the RBE reach its maximum before decreasing due to overkill?
radiobio.a: Approximately 100 keV/μm, which corresponds to optimal ionisation density
c: Approximately 100 keV/μm, which corresponds to optimal ionisation density
Approximately 0.2 keV/μm, which corresponds to sparse ionisation patterns
Approximately 500 keV/μm, which corresponds to ultra-dense energy deposition
Approximately 10 keV/μm, which corresponds to moderate ionisation density
radiobio.e: RBE peaks at ~100 keV/μm where the average separation between ionisation events matches the ~2 nm diameter of the DNA double helix, maximising DSB production. Beyond this LET, overkill wastes energy on already-lethally-damaged cells, reducing efficiency.

radiobio.q: The therapeutic ratio (or therapeutic index) in radiotherapy is improved when:
radiobio.a: Tumour control probability increases while normal tissue complication probability decreases
c: Tumour control probability increases while normal tissue complication probability decreases
Both tumour control probability and normal tissue complication probability increase equally
Normal tissue complication probability increases faster than tumour control probability
Tumour control probability decreases while normal tissue complication probability stays fixed
radiobio.e: The therapeutic ratio is the separation between TCP and NTCP curves. It improves when we can shift the TCP curve to lower doses (more effective tumour kill) or shift the NTCP curve to higher doses (better normal tissue tolerance), widening the gap.

radiobio.q: CALC: A treatment delivers 70 Gy in 35 fractions. What is the EQD2 for a tissue with α/β = 10 Gy?
radiobio.a: 70.0 Gy EQD2 (equivalent to the original prescription)
c: 70.0 Gy EQD2 (equivalent to the original prescription)
84.0 Gy EQD2 (higher than the original prescription)
58.3 Gy EQD2 (lower than the original prescription)
93.3 Gy EQD2 (significantly above the prescription)
radiobio.e: d = 70/35 = 2 Gy/fraction. EQD2 = D × (d + α/β)/(2 + α/β) = 70 × (2+10)/(2+10) = 70 × 12/12 = 70.0 Gy. When the dose per fraction equals 2 Gy, EQD2 equals the total physical dose, as expected.

radiobio.q: CALC: What is the EQD2 for 45 Gy in 3 fractions (SBRT) for a tumour with α/β = 10 Gy?
radiobio.a: 93.75 Gy EQD2 for the tumour tissue
c: 93.75 Gy EQD2 for the tumour tissue
67.50 Gy EQD2 for the tumour tissue
112.5 Gy EQD2 for the tumour tissue
150.0 Gy EQD2 for the tumour tissue
radiobio.e: d = 45/3 = 15 Gy/fraction. EQD2 = D × (d + α/β)/(2 + α/β) = 45 × (15+10)/(2+10) = 45 × 25/12 = 93.75 Gy. SBRT delivers very high equivalent doses due to the large dose per fraction.

radiobio.q: The tumour control probability (TCP) curve is typically described as:
radiobio.a: A sigmoid (S-shaped) curve that increases steeply over a narrow dose range
c: A sigmoid (S-shaped) curve that increases steeply over a narrow dose range
A linear curve that increases proportionally with increasing radiation dose
An exponential decay curve that decreases rapidly at higher dose values
A flat horizontal line indicating constant probability regardless of dose
radiobio.e: TCP follows a sigmoid curve: at low doses TCP is near zero, rises steeply through a transition region, and approaches 100% at high doses. The steepness depends on tumour heterogeneity and the slope parameter gamma-50.

radiobio.q: In the context of NTCP modelling, the Lyman-Kutcher-Burman model uses which key parameters?
radiobio.a: TD50 (tolerance dose for 50% complication), n (volume effect), and m (slope parameter)
c: TD50 (tolerance dose for 50% complication), n (volume effect), and m (slope parameter)
Alpha, beta, and gamma coefficients from the linear-quadratic cell survival model
OER, RBE, and LET values derived from the specific radiation beam quality used
TCP, BED, and EQD2 parameters calculated from the prescribed fractionation scheme
radiobio.e: The LKB model is the most widely used NTCP model. TD50 is the dose causing 50% complication rate for uniform whole-organ irradiation, n describes the volume effect (serial vs parallel organ), and m describes the steepness of the dose-response curve.

radiobio.q: CALC: Calculate the BED for 20 Gy in a single fraction for a tumour with α/β = 10 Gy.
radiobio.a: 60 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
c: 60 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
40 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
80 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
30 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
radiobio.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 1 × 20 × (1 + 20/10) = 20 × 3 = 60 Gy₁₀. Single-fraction radiosurgery delivers very high BED due to the large dose per fraction, which is why it is effective for small, well-defined tumour targets.

radiobio.q: CALC: A patient receives 50 Gy in 25 fractions. What is the BED for late-responding tissue with α/β = 2 Gy?
radiobio.a: 100 Gy₂ biologically effective dose
c: 100 Gy₂ biologically effective dose
60 Gy₂ biologically effective dose
75 Gy₂ biologically effective dose
120 Gy₂ biologically effective dose
radiobio.e: d = 50/25 = 2 Gy/fraction. BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 50 × (1 + 2/2) = 50 × 2 = 100 Gy₂. Despite the moderate physical dose, the BED for late-responding tissue is high due to the low α/β ratio.

radiobio.q: Which cell cycle phase is generally considered the most radiosensitive?
radiobio.a: G2 and mitosis (M phase) are the most radiosensitive phases
c: G2 and mitosis (M phase) are the most radiosensitive phases
Late S phase is the most radiosensitive phase of the cell cycle
G0 resting phase is the most radiosensitive phase of the cycle
Early G1 phase is the most radiosensitive phase of the cell cycle
radiobio.e: Cells in G2/M are most radiosensitive because chromosomes are condensed and there is less time for DNA repair before division. Late S phase is most radioresistant due to active homologous recombination repair with the sister chromatid template.

radiobio.q: Tumour hypoxia reduces the effectiveness of radiation primarily because:
radiobio.a: Oxygen is needed to fix (make permanent) the free radical damage to DNA molecules
c: Oxygen is needed to fix (make permanent) the free radical damage to DNA molecules
Hypoxic cells are in G0 phase and therefore cannot undergo mitotic cell death
Low oxygen directly reduces the number of photon interactions in tumour tissue
Hypoxic tumours have much higher α/β ratios making them inherently resistant
radiobio.e: The oxygen fixation hypothesis states that O₂ reacts with radiation-induced free radicals on DNA, making the damage permanent and irreparable. Without oxygen, chemical restitution by sulfhydryl compounds (–SH) can reverse the radical damage before it becomes fixed.

radiobio.q: Nimorazole, used as a radiosensitizer in Danish head and neck cancer trials, works by:
radiobio.a: Mimicking oxygen to fix DNA free radical damage in hypoxic tumour cells
c: Mimicking oxygen to fix DNA free radical damage in hypoxic tumour cells
Directly causing additional double-strand breaks in oxygenated tumour cells
Blocking DNA repair enzymes in both tumour and normal tissue cells equally
Increasing blood flow to deliver more physical dose to the tumour volume
radiobio.e: Nimorazole is a hypoxic cell radiosensitizer (nitroimidazole compound) that acts as an oxygen mimetic. It selectively sensitises hypoxic tumour cells by fixing radiation-induced free radical damage, similar to the oxygen fixation mechanism, improving tumour control.

radiobio.q: Amifostine (WR-2721) selectively protects normal tissues over tumour cells because:
radiobio.a: Normal tissues have higher alkaline phosphatase activity to convert the prodrug
c: Normal tissues have higher alkaline phosphatase activity to convert the prodrug
Tumour cells actively transport amifostine into the nucleus for DNA integration
Amifostine binds exclusively to tumour cell membranes preventing its uptake
The drug requires high LET radiation to become activated in normal tissue only
radiobio.e: Amifostine is a prodrug activated by alkaline phosphatase, which is more concentrated in normal tissues due to their better blood supply and higher pH. The active metabolite (WR-1065) scavenges free radicals, providing selective radioprotection to normal tissues.

radiobio.q: Which of the following correctly describes the bystander effect in radiobiology?
radiobio.a: Non-irradiated cells near irradiated cells exhibit biological damage via intercellular signalling
c: Non-irradiated cells near irradiated cells exhibit biological damage via intercellular signalling
Irradiated cells become resistant to subsequent radiation exposures through adaptive response
Cells exposed to high-LET radiation show reduced sensitivity compared to low-LET exposure
Tumour cells distant from the radiation field shrink due to systemic immune system activation
radiobio.e: The bystander effect describes how cells not directly hit by radiation can exhibit damage (DNA breaks, mutations, apoptosis) through signals received from irradiated neighbouring cells via gap junctions or secreted soluble factors in the medium.

radiobio.q: The abscopal effect in radiation therapy refers to:
radiobio.a: Regression of distant non-irradiated tumour metastases following local irradiation
c: Regression of distant non-irradiated tumour metastases following local irradiation
Enhanced radiosensitivity of tumour cells immediately adjacent to the beam edge
Increased normal tissue toxicity in organs far from the primary treatment field
Reduction in tumour hypoxia caused by systemic administration of oxygen carriers
radiobio.e: The abscopal effect (from Latin 'ab scopus' meaning 'away from the target') involves tumour regression at sites distant from the irradiated volume. It is mediated by the immune system and has gained renewed interest with the combination of radiotherapy and immunotherapy.

radiobio.q: Linear energy transfer (LET) is defined as:
radiobio.a: The average energy deposited per unit path length by a charged particle in medium
c: The average energy deposited per unit path length by a charged particle in medium
The total kinetic energy released per unit mass in the irradiated tissue volume
The ratio of absorbed dose in tissue to absorbed dose in free air for a beam
The energy fluence of a radiation beam measured at a specified depth in water
radiobio.e: LET is expressed in keV/μm and describes how densely ionising a radiation type is. Low-LET radiation (photons, electrons, ~0.2–2 keV/μm) produces sparse ionisation, while high-LET radiation (alpha particles, heavy ions, ~100–200 keV/μm) produces dense ionisation tracks.

radiobio.q: Why do high-LET radiations such as carbon ions have potential advantages for treating radioresistant hypoxic tumours?
radiobio.a: High-LET radiation has a low OER so tumour hypoxia has minimal impact on cell kill
c: High-LET radiation has a low OER so tumour hypoxia has minimal impact on cell kill
High-LET radiation has a higher OER allowing greater dose to be safely delivered
High-LET particles bypass DNA completely and destroy cells via protein denaturation
High-LET beams produce only single-strand breaks which are always lethal to cells
radiobio.e: High-LET radiation produces dense ionisation tracks causing complex, clustered DNA damage (multiple DSBs in close proximity) that is lethal regardless of oxygen status. The OER drops to ~1.0, effectively eliminating hypoxia as a source of radioresistance.

radiobio.q: CALC: If a BED of 72 Gy₁₀ is required for tumour control and we use 2.4 Gy per fraction with α/β = 10 Gy, how many fractions are needed?
radiobio.a: 24 fractions to achieve the required BED
c: 24 fractions to achieve the required BED
30 fractions to achieve the required BED
20 fractions to achieve the required BED
18 fractions to achieve the required BED
radiobio.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)). 72 = n × 2.4 × (1 + 2.4/10) = n × 2.4 × 1.24 = n × 2.976. Therefore n = 72/2.976 = 24.19 ≈ 24 fractions. Total physical dose would be 24 × 2.4 = 57.6 Gy.

radiobio.q: In South African radiotherapy centres, the most common fractionation schedule for radical treatment of cervical cancer is:
radiobio.a: 45–50 Gy external beam in 25 fractions combined with brachytherapy boost
c: 45–50 Gy external beam in 25 fractions combined with brachytherapy boost
70 Gy external beam only in 35 fractions without any brachytherapy component
30 Gy in 10 fractions as a palliative schedule without concurrent chemotherapy
20 Gy in a single fraction using stereotactic radiosurgery technique exclusively
radiobio.e: In South Africa, cervical cancer (a high-burden disease) is typically treated with 45–50 Gy EBRT in 25 fractions with concurrent cisplatin, followed by HDR brachytherapy boost. This follows international guidelines adapted for the SA context where cervical cancer is the leading cancer in women.

radiobio.q: The shoulder region of a cell survival curve for low-LET radiation reflects:
radiobio.a: Accumulation and repair of sublethal damage at low doses before lethal damage dominates
c: Accumulation and repair of sublethal damage at low doses before lethal damage dominates
Increased radiosensitivity of cells in the mitotic phase at very low dose exposures
Direct DNA damage that is immediately lethal to all cells regardless of repair capacity
Oxygen-dependent enhancement of radiation damage specific to the initial dose range
radiobio.e: The shoulder on a survival curve indicates the cell's capacity to accumulate and repair sublethal damage. At low doses, repair is effective and survival is higher than predicted by a purely exponential model. This shoulder is described by the β component in the LQ model.

radiobio.q: CALC: Two treatment schedules deliver the same BED₁₀. Schedule A is 60 Gy in 30 fractions. What total dose in 5 fractions gives the same BED₁₀?
radiobio.a: 40 Gy in 5 fractions (8 Gy per fraction)
c: 40 Gy in 5 fractions (8 Gy per fraction)
50 Gy in 5 fractions (10 Gy per fraction)
30 Gy in 5 fractions (6 Gy per fraction)
35 Gy in 5 fractions (7 Gy per fraction)
radiobio.e: Schedule A: BED = 60 × (1 + 2/10) = 72 Gy₁₀. For Schedule B: BED = D × (1 + d/10) where d = D/5. So 72 = D(1 + D/50). Solving: D = 40 Gy, d = 8 Gy. Check: 40 × (1 + 8/10) = 40 × 1.8 = 72 Gy₁₀. The higher dose per fraction delivers the same tumour BED with fewer fractions.

radiobio.q: Hyperfractionation in radiotherapy involves:
radiobio.a: Using smaller dose per fraction (1.1–1.2 Gy) given two or more times daily
c: Using smaller dose per fraction (1.1–1.2 Gy) given two or more times daily
Using larger dose per fraction (3–5 Gy) given once daily over fewer days
Delivering the same total dose in half the overall treatment time at 2 Gy
Giving a single large fraction of 15–20 Gy to the entire tumour volume
radiobio.e: Hyperfractionation uses smaller-than-conventional fractions (1.1–1.2 Gy) multiple times daily to increase the total dose while keeping late effects acceptable. This exploits the greater fractionation sensitivity (low α/β) of late-responding tissues compared to tumours.

radiobio.q: Accelerated fractionation aims to counter tumour repopulation by:
radiobio.a: Reducing the overall treatment time while maintaining similar total dose levels
c: Reducing the overall treatment time while maintaining similar total dose levels
Increasing the total treatment dose delivered over an extended time period
Using smaller fractions that specifically target cells in the G0 resting phase
Administering radioprotectors before each fraction to speed normal tissue repair
radiobio.e: Accelerated fractionation shortens overall treatment time (e.g. CHART: 54 Gy in 36 fractions over 12 days) to outpace tumour repopulation. This is particularly beneficial for rapidly proliferating tumours like head and neck squamous cell carcinomas.

radiobio.q: The concept of 'potentially lethal damage repair' (PLDR) refers to:
radiobio.a: Damage that can be either repaired or become lethal depending on post-irradiation conditions
c: Damage that can be either repaired or become lethal depending on post-irradiation conditions
DNA damage that is always lethal regardless of the cellular repair mechanisms available
Sublethal damage that requires two independent radiation events to become lethal damage
Damage exclusively caused by high-LET radiation that cannot be modified by any means
radiobio.e: Potentially lethal damage (PLD) is damage that is lethal under normal circumstances but can be repaired if cells are held in non-growth conditions (e.g. plateau phase) after irradiation. PLDR is clinically relevant as slowly growing tumours may have more time for PLDR.

radiobio.q: CALC: A palliative schedule of 30 Gy in 10 fractions has what BED for tumour (α/β = 10 Gy)?
radiobio.a: 39.0 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
c: 39.0 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
30.0 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
48.0 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
54.0 Gy₁₀ biologically effective dose
radiobio.e: d = 30/10 = 3 Gy/fraction. BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 30 × (1 + 3/10) = 30 × 1.3 = 39.0 Gy₁₀. This lower BED compared to radical schedules explains why palliative regimens have lower tumour control rates but are appropriate for symptom relief.

radiobio.q: Which of the following statements about the initial slope (α) of the cell survival curve is correct?
radiobio.a: A larger α value indicates greater intrinsic radiosensitivity to single-hit killing events
c: A larger α value indicates greater intrinsic radiosensitivity to single-hit killing events
A larger α value indicates greater radioresistance and enhanced sublethal damage repair
The α value is only relevant for high-LET radiation and is zero for photon beams
The α value describes the curvature of the survival curve at high dose per fraction
radiobio.e: The α parameter represents the linear component of cell kill from single ionisation events causing lethal damage. Tumours with high α values (e.g. lymphomas, α ≈ 0.5 Gy⁻¹) are intrinsically radiosensitive, while tumours with low α values (e.g. melanomas) are more radioresistant.

radiobio.q: In the context of radiobiology, what does the term 'clonogenic cell' specifically mean?
radiobio.a: A cell that retains unlimited capacity to proliferate and form a colony of progeny
c: A cell that retains unlimited capacity to proliferate and form a colony of progeny
A cell that has been lethally damaged and will die before the next cell division
Any differentiated cell in the tumour regardless of its reproductive capability
A cell that has undergone apoptosis and is being cleared by the immune system
radiobio.e: A clonogenic cell can undergo unlimited proliferation and produce a macroscopic colony (>50 cells). In radiobiology, tumour control requires sterilisation of all clonogenic cells, as even a single surviving clonogen can repopulate the tumour.

radiobio.q: CALC: What is the EQD2 for a brachytherapy dose of 7 Gy × 4 fractions for late-responding tissue (α/β = 3 Gy)?
radiobio.a: 56.0 Gy EQD2 for late-responding tissue
c: 56.0 Gy EQD2 for late-responding tissue
28.0 Gy EQD2 for late-responding tissue
42.0 Gy EQD2 for late-responding tissue
70.0 Gy EQD2 for late-responding tissue
radiobio.e: D = 28 Gy, d = 7 Gy. EQD2 = D × (d + α/β)/(2 + α/β) = 28 × (7+3)/(2+3) = 28 × 10/5 = 28 × 2 = 56.0 Gy. The high dose per fraction in brachytherapy significantly increases the biological dose for late-responding tissues.

radiobio.q: The Withers' 4 Rs of radiobiology exclude which of the following?
radiobio.a: Radiosensitivity (intrinsic), which was added later as the 5th R by Steel
c: Radiosensitivity (intrinsic), which was added later as the 5th R by Steel
Repair of sublethal damage occurring between successive radiation fractions
Redistribution of surviving cells through the phases of the cell cycle
Reoxygenation of hypoxic tumour cells between treatment fractions given
radiobio.e: Withers' original 4 Rs are Repair, Redistribution, Repopulation, and Reoxygenation. Steel later added intrinsic Radiosensitivity as the 5th R, recognising that different tumour types have inherently different sensitivities to radiation independent of the other four factors.

radiobio.q: Mitotic catastrophe as a mode of radiation-induced cell death is characterised by:
radiobio.a: Cell death occurring during or after an aberrant mitotic division with abnormal chromosomes
c: Cell death occurring during or after an aberrant mitotic division with abnormal chromosomes
Programmed cell death triggered by caspase activation with organised cell shrinkage
Cell swelling and membrane rupture leading to inflammatory response in normal tissues
Gradual cellular senescence with permanent growth arrest but continued cell viability
radiobio.e: Mitotic catastrophe is the most common mode of radiation-induced death in solid tumour cells. Cells with unrepaired or misrepaired DNA attempt division but fail due to chromosomal aberrations (dicentrics, rings, fragments), resulting in non-viable daughter cells.

radiobio.q: CALC: If a tumour contains 10⁹ clonogenic cells and each 2 Gy fraction reduces survival by a factor of 0.5, approximately how many 2 Gy fractions are needed for tumour control?
radiobio.a: Approximately 30 fractions (since 2³⁰ ≈ 10⁹ clonogenic cells to sterilise)
c: Approximately 30 fractions (since 2³⁰ ≈ 10⁹ clonogenic cells to sterilise)
Approximately 10 fractions (since 2¹⁰ ≈ 10⁹ clonogenic cells to sterilise)
Approximately 50 fractions (since 2⁵⁰ ≈ 10⁹ clonogenic cells to sterilise)
Approximately 20 fractions (since 2²⁰ ≈ 10⁹ clonogenic cells to sterilise)
radiobio.e: For tumour control, we need to reduce 10⁹ cells to ~1 cell. Each fraction kills half, so after n fractions survival = (0.5)ⁿ × 10⁹ ≤ 1. This gives 0.5ⁿ ≤ 10⁻⁹, so n × log(2) ≥ 9, thus n ≥ 9/0.301 ≈ 30 fractions.

radiobio.q: Which statement about the dose-rate effect in radiobiology is correct?
radiobio.a: Lowering dose rate reduces biological effectiveness due to increased sublethal damage repair
c: Lowering dose rate reduces biological effectiveness due to increased sublethal damage repair
Lowering dose rate increases biological effectiveness by concentrating ionisation density
Dose rate has no effect on biological response for any type of radiation quality used
Higher dose rates allow more sublethal damage repair during the extended exposure time
radiobio.e: At lower dose rates, cells have time to repair sublethal damage during irradiation, reducing biological effectiveness. This is why LDR brachytherapy (0.4–2 Gy/hr) has different biological effects compared to HDR brachytherapy (>12 Gy/hr) and is the basis of the dose-rate effect.

radiobio.q: CALC: A treatment of 8 Gy × 1 fraction is given. What is the BED for late-responding tissue (α/β = 3 Gy)?
radiobio.a: 29.3 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
c: 29.3 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
16.0 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
40.0 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
21.3 Gy₃ biologically effective dose
radiobio.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 1 × 8 × (1 + 8/3) = 8 × (1 + 2.667) = 8 × 3.667 = 29.3 Gy₃. The high dose per fraction significantly impacts late-responding tissues, which is why single-fraction treatments must be carefully planned to avoid critical structures.

radiobio.q: The Elkind recovery experiment demonstrated that cells can repair sublethal damage by showing:
radiobio.a: Increased survival when a dose is split into two fractions separated by several hours
c: Increased survival when a dose is split into two fractions separated by several hours
Decreased survival when irradiation is delivered at a very low continuous dose rate
No change in cell survival regardless of time interval between two dose fractions
Complete cell death when any dose above 1 Gy is administered in a single exposure
radiobio.e: Elkind and Sutton (1960) showed that splitting a dose into two fractions with a time interval of 2–6 hours allowed sublethal damage repair, increasing cell survival (shoulder reappears). This provided the biological basis for fractionated radiotherapy.

radiobio.q: In South African radiotherapy practice, the use of hypofractionated schedules (e.g. 40 Gy in 15 fractions for breast cancer) is particularly important because:
radiobio.a: It reduces treatment time on overburdened linear accelerators while maintaining outcomes
c: It reduces treatment time on overburdened linear accelerators while maintaining outcomes
It eliminates the need for any quality assurance procedures on treatment machines
It allows higher total physical doses to be delivered to the tumour target volume
It increases the biological dose to late-responding tissues to improve local control
radiobio.e: South Africa has limited radiotherapy resources relative to its cancer burden. Hypofractionated breast schedules (e.g. 40 Gy/15# or 42.5 Gy/16#) based on UK START trials reduce machine time per patient, increasing access. These schedules exploit the low α/β ratio (~4 Gy) of breast cancer, giving equivalent tumour control with fewer fractions.

radiobio.q: In the linear-quadratic model, the α/β ratio represents?
radiobio.a: The dose at which the linear and quadratic components of cell killing contribute equally
c: The dose at which the linear and quadratic components of cell killing contribute equally
The maximum dose rate beyond which cell killing becomes independent of fractionation
The ratio of oxygen enhancement to relative biological effectiveness for a given radiation
The minimum dose required to sterilise a tumour with a given number of clonogenic cells
radiobio.e: In the LQ model, surviving fraction = exp(−αd − βd²). Setting αd = βd² gives d = α/β. At this dose, the linear (single-hit, αd) and quadratic (two-hit, βd²) contributions to cell kill are equal. Tissues with high α/β (~10 Gy) are early-responding; low α/β (~3 Gy) are late-responding.

radiobio.q: CALC: A tumour with α/β = 10 Gy receives 60 Gy in 30 fractions. What is the biologically effective dose (BED)?
radiobio.a: 72.0 Gy — calculated using the formula BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) with d = 2 Gy per fraction
c: 72.0 Gy — calculated using the formula BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) with d = 2 Gy per fraction
60.0 Gy — the BED equals the physical dose when α/β equals ten for tumour tissue
84.0 Gy — calculated using a modified formula that accounts for tumour repopulation effects
120 Gy — calculated by multiplying the total dose by the number of fractions and dividing by α/β
radiobio.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 30 × 2 × (1 + 2/10) = 60 × 1.2 = 72.0 Gy. BED accounts for the biological effect of the fractionation scheme. For tumours (α/β = 10 Gy), the quadratic component adds 20% to the physical dose at 2 Gy/fraction.

radiobio.q: CALC: What is the EQD2 for a hypofractionated regimen of 40 Gy in 15 fractions for a tissue with α/β = 3 Gy?
radiobio.a: 45.3 Gy — calculated using EQD2 = D × (d + α/β) / (2 + α/β) with d = 2.67 Gy
c: 45.3 Gy — calculated using EQD2 = D × (d + α/β) / (2 + α/β) with d = 2.67 Gy
40.0 Gy — EQD2 equals the physical dose when the fraction size is close to 2 Gy
37.3 Gy — calculated using the inverse of the standard BED formula for late effects
52.0 Gy — calculated by multiplying the total dose by the ratio of fraction sizes
radiobio.e: Dose per fraction d = 40/15 = 2.667 Gy. EQD2 = D × (d + α/β)/(2 + α/β) = 40 × (2.667 + 3)/(2 + 3) = 40 × 5.667/5 = 40 × 1.133 = 45.3 Gy. EQD2 converts any fractionation scheme to an equivalent dose in 2-Gy fractions, enabling comparison of different regimens.

radiobio.q: The oxygen enhancement ratio (OER) is defined as?
radiobio.a: The ratio of radiation dose required under hypoxic conditions to that under oxygenated conditions for the same biological effect
c: The ratio of radiation dose required under hypoxic conditions to that under oxygenated conditions for the same biological effect
The percentage increase in cell killing when oxygen is present during radiation exposure
The ratio of oxygen partial pressure in tumour tissue to that in normal surrounding tissue
The fraction of tumour cells that are hypoxic at the time of radiation treatment delivery
radiobio.e: OER = dose under hypoxia / dose under oxic conditions for the same biological endpoint. For low-LET radiation (photons), OER is typically 2.5–3.0. For high-LET radiation (heavy ions), OER approaches 1.0, making high-LET radiation effective against hypoxic tumours.

radiobio.q: Which of the four Rs of radiobiology most directly explains the improved therapeutic ratio with fractionation for late-responding normal tissues?
radiobio.a: Repair of sublethal damage, which is more efficient in late-responding tissues with low α/β ratios
c: Repair of sublethal damage, which is more efficient in late-responding tissues with low α/β ratios
Redistribution of cells into radiosensitive phases of the cell cycle between fractions
Re-oxygenation of previously hypoxic tumour cells increasing their radiosensitivity
Repopulation of normal tissue cells between fractions compensating for radiation damage
radiobio.e: Late-responding tissues (low α/β ≈ 3 Gy) have a large quadratic (β) component, meaning sublethal damage repair between fractions significantly reduces the biological effect. Fractionation preferentially spares these tissues compared to tumours (high α/β ≈ 10 Gy), improving the therapeutic ratio.

radiobio.q: The relative biological effectiveness (RBE) of a radiation type depends on all of the following EXCEPT?
radiobio.a: The half-life of the radioactive source producing the radiation beam
c: The half-life of the radioactive source producing the radiation beam
The linear energy transfer of the radiation being compared to the reference
The biological endpoint chosen for comparison such as cell survival level
The dose level at which the comparison between the two radiations is made
radiobio.e: RBE = dose of reference radiation / dose of test radiation for the same biological effect. It depends on LET, dose/fraction, biological endpoint, and tissue type. The physical half-life of the source is a decay property and does not influence the biological effectiveness of the emitted radiation.

radiobio.q: CALC: A single fraction of 8 Gy is delivered to a tumour with α/β = 10 Gy. What is the BED?
radiobio.a: 14.4 Gy — calculated as 8 × (1 + 8/10) using the LQ model for a single fraction
c: 14.4 Gy — calculated as 8 × (1 + 8/10) using the LQ model for a single fraction
8.0 Gy — the BED equals physical dose for single-fraction treatments regardless of α/β
16.0 Gy — calculated by doubling the physical dose for the high-dose single fraction
80.0 Gy — calculated by multiplying the dose by the α/β ratio for tumour tissue
radiobio.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 1 × 8 × (1 + 8/10) = 8 × 1.8 = 14.4 Gy. For single fractions, n = 1 and d equals the total dose. The BED is substantially higher per physical Gy for large fractions due to the quadratic component.

radiobio.q: Cell survival curves for mammalian cells exposed to X-rays typically show?
radiobio.a: An initial shoulder region at low doses followed by a steeper exponential decline at higher doses
c: An initial shoulder region at low doses followed by a steeper exponential decline at higher doses
A purely exponential straight line on a log-linear plot from zero dose to maximum dose
A threshold dose below which no cell killing occurs followed by complete sterilisation above it
An initial steep decline at low doses that plateaus at higher doses due to saturation effects
radiobio.e: The shoulder reflects accumulation and repair of sublethal damage at low doses (quadratic component). At higher doses, the survival curve becomes steeper as the linear component dominates. This shape is well-described by the LQ model: SF = exp(−αd − βd²).

radiobio.q: The therapeutic ratio in radiotherapy is defined as?
radiobio.a: The ratio of tumour control probability to normal tissue complication probability at a given dose
c: The ratio of tumour control probability to normal tissue complication probability at a given dose
The maximum dose a tumour can receive without causing any damage to surrounding tissues
The ratio of the prescribed dose to the minimum dose required for tumour sterilisation
The percentage of patients who achieve complete response divided by those with grade 3 toxicity
radiobio.e: The therapeutic ratio (or therapeutic index) compares the probability of tumour control (TCP) to normal tissue complications (NTCP). A favourable therapeutic ratio means there is a wide dose window where TCP is high but NTCP remains low. Fractionation, conformal techniques, and radioprotectors aim to improve this ratio.

radiobio.q: Hypofractionation is radiobiologically advantageous for tumours with?
radiobio.a: Low α/β ratios, because larger fraction sizes produce proportionally greater biological effect on the tumour than on surrounding late-responding tissues
c: Low α/β ratios, because larger fraction sizes produce proportionally greater biological effect on the tumour than on surrounding late-responding tissues
High α/β ratios, because cells with rapid turnover are always more sensitive to large fractions
Equal α/β ratios to surrounding normal tissues so that fractionation effects cancel out entirely
Any α/β ratio, because the reduced overall treatment time always improves tumour control probability
radiobio.e: When a tumour has a low α/β ratio (e.g., prostate cancer, α/β ≈ 1.5 Gy) and surrounding normal tissues also have low α/β, hypofractionation can exploit the difference. With larger fractions, the BED to the tumour increases more than the BED to normal tissues, particularly when the tumour α/β is lower than normal tissue α/β. This is the rationale for hypofractionated prostate radiotherapy.

radiobio.q: Re-oxygenation during fractionated radiotherapy benefits tumour control because?
radiobio.a: Previously hypoxic tumour cells gain access to oxygen between fractions, making them more radiosensitive
c: Previously hypoxic tumour cells gain access to oxygen between fractions, making them more radiosensitive
Increased oxygen levels directly cause DNA double-strand breaks independent of radiation exposure
Normal tissue oxygen consumption decreases during treatment allowing more oxygen to reach the tumour
The radiation itself generates reactive oxygen species that persist between fractions for days
radiobio.e: Tumours contain hypoxic cells (OER ≈ 2.5–3.0) that are relatively radioresistant. After a radiation fraction kills oxygenated cells, tumour shrinkage and vascular changes allow oxygen to reach previously hypoxic cells, sensitising them to subsequent fractions. This is one of the key advantages of fractionation.

radiobio.q: The bystander effect in radiobiology refers to?
radiobio.a: Biological damage observed in unirradiated cells that received signals from neighbouring irradiated cells
c: Biological damage observed in unirradiated cells that received signals from neighbouring irradiated cells
Enhanced radiosensitivity of cells when they are in direct physical contact during irradiation
Increased radioresistance that develops in cells repeatedly exposed to low doses of radiation
The protective effect that irradiated cells provide to adjacent cells through antioxidant release
radiobio.e: The bystander effect describes a phenomenon where cells not directly hit by radiation exhibit biological responses (DNA damage, apoptosis, mutations) due to signals received from irradiated neighbours via gap junctions or soluble mediators. This has implications for low-dose risk assessment and targeted radionuclide therapy.

radiobio.q: CALC: Two fractionation schemes are compared for a late-responding tissue (α/β = 3 Gy): Scheme A is 50 Gy in 25 fractions and Scheme B is 30 Gy in 10 fractions. Which has the higher BED for this tissue?
radiobio.a: Scheme A with BED of 83.3 Gy, exceeding Scheme B's BED of 60.0 Gy for late-responding tissue
c: Scheme A with BED of 83.3 Gy, exceeding Scheme B's BED of 60.0 Gy for late-responding tissue
Scheme B with BED of 90.0 Gy, exceeding Scheme A's BED of 66.7 Gy for late-responding tissue
Both schemes have identical BED values of 70.0 Gy when calculated for late-responding tissues
Scheme B with BED of 60.0 Gy, compared to Scheme A with BED of 50.0 Gy for this tissue
radiobio.e: Scheme A: BED = 50 × (1 + 2/3) = 50 × 1.667 = 83.3 Gy. Scheme B: BED = 30 × (1 + 3/3) = 30 × 2.0 = 60.0 Gy. Scheme A delivers a higher BED to late-responding tissue despite the lower dose per fraction, because the total physical dose is substantially higher.

radiobio.q: Linear energy transfer (LET) is defined as?
radiobio.a: The average energy deposited per unit path length of the ionising particle traversing the medium
c: The average energy deposited per unit path length of the ionising particle traversing the medium
The total kinetic energy of the particle divided by its range in water equivalent thickness
The ratio of absorbed dose to the number of ion pairs produced along the radiation track
The energy transferred from the radiation beam to the patient per unit area of the field
radiobio.e: LET (keV/μm) describes the density of energy deposition along a particle track. Low-LET radiation (photons, electrons: ~0.2–2 keV/μm) produces sparse ionisation; high-LET radiation (alpha particles, heavy ions: 50–200 keV/μm) produces dense ionisation with more complex, irreparable DNA damage.

radiobio.q: The abscopal effect in radiation therapy describes?
radiobio.a: Tumour regression at distant, unirradiated sites following localised radiation treatment, mediated by the immune system
c: Tumour regression at distant, unirradiated sites following localised radiation treatment, mediated by the immune system
Increased radiosensitivity of the primary tumour after systemic administration of chemotherapy agents
Accelerated repopulation of tumour cells occurring during the later weeks of a fractionated course
Enhanced normal tissue toxicity at sites remote from the treated volume due to circulating cytokines
radiobio.e: The abscopal effect (from Latin 'ab scopus' = away from the target) involves immune-mediated regression of metastases outside the radiation field. Radiation releases tumour antigens and danger signals that activate anti-tumour immunity. This effect is being exploited with immunotherapy-radiation combinations.

radiobio.q: In TCP/NTCP modelling, the sigmoidal shape of the dose-response curve is steeper when?
radiobio.a: The population of cells or patients is more homogeneous in radiosensitivity and other relevant parameters
c: The population of cells or patients is more homogeneous in radiosensitivity and other relevant parameters
The radiation is delivered in fewer fractions with higher dose per fraction overall
The tumour volume is larger requiring more clonogenic cells to be sterilised for control
The α/β ratio of the tissue approaches zero indicating purely quadratic cell killing kinetics
radiobio.e: A steep dose-response curve (high gamma-50 value) indicates that small changes in dose produce large changes in TCP or NTCP. This occurs when inter-patient variability in radiosensitivity, tumour burden, and other factors is small. Heterogeneity flattens the population dose-response curve.

radiobio.q: The half-time for sublethal damage repair in most mammalian cells is approximately?
radiobio.a: 0.5 to 2 hours, which is why a minimum of 6 hours between fractions is recommended
c: 0.5 to 2 hours, which is why a minimum of 6 hours between fractions is recommended
10 to 24 hours, which is why once-daily fractionation ensures complete repair
Less than 5 minutes, which means repair occurs almost instantaneously after each fraction
4 to 8 hours, which is identical for both tumour cells and all normal tissue types
radiobio.e: Sublethal damage repair follows exponential kinetics with a half-time of approximately 0.5–2 hours for most tissues (mono-exponential model). To allow near-complete repair (>95%), a minimum interval of 6 hours between fractions is recommended, particularly important in hyperfractionated and twice-daily regimens. Some late-responding tissues (e.g., spinal cord) may have a slower repair component.

radiobio.q: What does the beta component of the LQ model represent?
radiobio.a: Lethal damage from two independent hits
c: Lethal damage from a single ionisation event
Lethal damage from two independent hits
Sublethal damage that is always repaired
Damage only from high-LET radiation
radiobio.e: Beta represents cell kill from the accumulation of two sublethal lesions interacting to form a lethal lesion. This component is dose-rate and fractionation dependent.

radiobio.q: CALC: A tumour has alpha/beta = 10 Gy. What is the BED for 60 Gy in 30 fractions?
radiobio.a: 72 Gy
c: 60 Gy
72 Gy
80 Gy
66 Gy

radiobio.q: Which phase of the cell cycle is most radiosensitive?
radiobio.a: Late G2 and M phase
c: Early S phase
Late G2 and M phase
G0 resting phase
Late S phase
radiobio.e: Cells in mitosis have condensed chromatin and reduced repair capacity, making them most vulnerable to radiation damage.

radiobio.q: The oxygen enhancement ratio (OER) for low-LET radiation is approximately:
radiobio.e: For sparsely ionising radiation (photons, electrons), OER is about 2.5-3.0. It decreases toward 1.0 as LET increases.

radiobio.q: What is the primary mechanism of DNA damage leading to cell death from ionising radiation?
radiobio.a: Double-strand breaks in DNA
c: Single-strand breaks in DNA
Double-strand breaks in DNA
Base excision from nucleotides
Cross-linking of histone proteins
radiobio.e: DSBs are the most lethal lesion. Single-strand breaks are rapidly repaired using the complementary strand as a template, but DSBs are much harder to repair correctly.

radiobio.q: CALC: A late-responding tissue has alpha/beta = 3 Gy. What is BED for 50 Gy in 25 fractions?
radiobio.e: BED = 50 x (1 + 2/3) = 50 x 1.667 = 83.3 Gy. Late tissues have low alpha/beta so BED is higher per physical dose.

radiobio.q: What does the shoulder on a cell survival curve indicate?
radiobio.a: Accumulation and repair of sublethal damage
c: Accumulation and repair of sublethal damage
Immediate single-hit lethal cell killing
Oxygen-mediated damage enhancement
Presence of resistant hypoxic cells
radiobio.e: The shoulder reflects the capacity to accumulate and repair sublethal damage at low doses. It is characterised by the Dq and n parameters in the multi-target model.

radiobio.q: RBE increases with LET up to a maximum around:
radiobio.a: 100 keV/micrometer
c: 10 keV/micrometer
50 keV/micrometer
100 keV/micrometer
500 keV/micrometer
radiobio.e: RBE peaks near 100 keV/um where the average separation between ionising events matches the DNA double helix diameter (~2 nm). Beyond this, overkill reduces efficiency.

radiobio.q: Which of the 4 Rs of radiobiology most benefits tumour control with fractionation?
radiobio.a: Reoxygenation of hypoxic tumour cells
c: Repair of sublethal damage in tumours
Reoxygenation of hypoxic tumour cells
Repopulation of tumour stem cells
Redistribution into resistant phases
radiobio.e: Reoxygenation between fractions makes previously hypoxic (radioresistant) cells oxic and more radiosensitive, improving tumour kill with each fraction.

radiobio.q: Hyperfractionation aims to exploit which radiobiological advantage?
radiobio.a: Reduced late effects via smaller dose per fraction
c: Shorter overall treatment time for patients
Reduced late effects via smaller dose per fraction
Increased tumour repopulation between doses
Higher physical dose to the gross tumour
radiobio.e: Using smaller fractions (e.g. 1.1-1.2 Gy BID) preferentially spares late-responding tissues (low alpha/beta) while maintaining tumour control.

radiobio.q: CALC: If a single fraction of 2 Gy gives survival fraction 0.5, what is the approximate alpha value assuming pure alpha kill (beta = 0)?
radiobio.e: S = e^(-alphaD), so 0.5 = e^(-alpha x 2). ln(0.5) = -0.693 = -2alpha. alpha = 0.693/2 = 0.35 Gy⁻¹.

radiobio.q: The dose at which the linear and quadratic components of cell kill are equal is:
radiobio.a: The alpha/beta ratio
c: The mean lethal dose D0
The alpha/beta ratio
The quasi-threshold dose Dq
The extrapolation number n
radiobio.e: At dose D = alpha/beta, alphaD = betaD², so the contributions from single-hit and two-hit mechanisms are equal.

radiobio.q: What is the approximate half-time for sublethal damage repair in mammalian cells?
radiobio.a: 0.5 to 2 hours
c: 5 to 10 minutes
0.5 to 2 hours
4 to 8 hours
12 to 24 hours
radiobio.e: Most sublethal damage repair occurs with a half-time of about 1 hour. This is why fractions given BID require at least 6 hours between treatments.

radiobio.q: Accelerated repopulation in head and neck tumours typically begins around:
radiobio.a: 3 to 4 weeks after treatment starts
c: Immediately at treatment start
1 to 2 weeks after treatment starts
3 to 4 weeks after treatment starts
6 to 8 weeks after treatment starts
radiobio.e: After a lag period of about 3-4 weeks, surviving clonogens repopulate rapidly. Treatment gaps beyond this period significantly reduce local control.

radiobio.q: Which statement about the bystander effect is correct?
radiobio.a: Unirradiated cells near hit cells show damage responses
c: Only directly hit cells exhibit radiation effects
Unirradiated cells near hit cells show damage responses
It only occurs at doses above 10 Gy total
It is exclusive to high-LET particle beams
radiobio.e: The bystander effect describes radiation-induced responses in cells not directly traversed by radiation, mediated by gap junctions and secreted signals.

radiobio.q: CALC: An SBRT plan delivers 54 Gy in 3 fractions to a tumour (alpha/beta = 10 Gy). What is the EQD2?
radiobio.e: BED = 54(1 + 18/10) = 54 x 2.8 = 151.2 Gy. EQD2 = BED/(1 + 2/10) = 151.2/1.2 = 126 Gy. Alternatively BED itself is 151.2 Gy₁₀. Note: the question asks for BED (EQD2 = 126 Gy). BED = 151.2 Gy.

radiobio.q: In the linear-quadratic model, what does the alpha/beta ratio represent biologically?
radiobio.a: The dose at which linear and quadratic cell killing contributions are equal
c: The dose at which linear and quadratic cell killing contributions are equal
The maximum tolerable dose for a single fraction treatment delivery
The ratio of repairable to irrepairable DNA damage in irradiated cells
The oxygen enhancement ratio for a specific tissue type and endpoint
radiobio.e: At a dose equal to alpha/beta, the cell kill from the linear (alpha*D) component equals that from the quadratic (beta*D²) component of the LQ model.

radiobio.q: What is the standard alpha/beta ratio typically assumed for late-responding normal tissues?
radiobio.a: Approximately 3 Gy for most late-responding tissue endpoints
c: Approximately 1 Gy for most late-responding tissue endpoints
Approximately 3 Gy for most late-responding tissue endpoints
Approximately 10 Gy for most late-responding tissue endpoints
Approximately 20 Gy for most late-responding tissue endpoints
radiobio.e: Late-responding tissues have low alpha/beta ratios (around 3 Gy), meaning they are more sensitive to changes in fraction size compared to early-responding tissues.

radiobio.q: What does the Biologically Effective Dose (BED) formula account for in fractionated radiotherapy?
radiobio.a: The total biological effect including fraction size and tissue repair capacity
c: Only the physical dose delivered to the target over all treatment fractions
The total biological effect including fraction size and tissue repair capacity
The geometric miss probability due to patient setup error each fraction
The dose rate effect for continuous low-dose-rate brachytherapy treatments
radiobio.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(alpha/beta)) incorporates both total dose and fraction size sensitivity, allowing comparison of different fractionation schemes for a given tissue.

radiobio.q: What is the oxygen enhancement ratio (OER) for sparsely ionizing radiation such as photons?
radiobio.a: Approximately 2.5 to 3.0 for most mammalian cell lines studied
c: Approximately 1.0 to 1.2 for most mammalian cell lines studied
Approximately 1.5 to 1.8 for most mammalian cell lines studied
Approximately 2.5 to 3.0 for most mammalian cell lines studied
Approximately 4.5 to 5.5 for most mammalian cell lines studied
radiobio.e: The OER of about 2.5-3.0 means hypoxic cells require 2.5-3 times more dose to achieve the same level of cell killing as well-oxygenated cells with low-LET radiation.

radiobio.q: In the TCP/NTCP framework, what does a steep dose-response curve indicate clinically?
radiobio.a: A small dose change produces a large change in tumor control or complication probability
c: The tissue responds uniformly regardless of the delivered dose level
A small dose change produces a large change in tumor control or complication probability
The treatment has a very wide therapeutic window for safe dose delivery
The biological response is dominated by stochastic rather than deterministic effects
radiobio.e: A steep curve means the transition from low to high control (or complication) probability occurs over a narrow dose range, making precise dose delivery critically important.

radiobio.q: What is the EQD2 formula used to calculate in clinical radiotherapy practice?
radiobio.a: The equivalent dose in 2-Gy fractions producing the same biological effect
c: The equivalent dose in 2-Gy fractions producing the same biological effect
The effective quadratic dose coefficient for cell survival curve fitting
The equivalent dose to 2 cm depth for superficial treatment prescriptions
The estimated quality-adjusted dose for combined modality treatment plans
radiobio.e: EQD2 converts any fractionation scheme to the biologically equivalent total dose if delivered in standard 2-Gy fractions, enabling comparison across different treatment protocols.

radiobio.q: Which of the four Rs of radiobiology explains why fractionation spares late-responding normal tissues more than tumors?
radiobio.a: Repair of sublethal damage between fractions favors tissues with low alpha/beta
c: Reoxygenation of hypoxic tumor cells between successive treatment fractions
Redistribution of cells into more radiosensitive phases of the cell cycle
Repair of sublethal damage between fractions favors tissues with low alpha/beta
Repopulation of surviving cells during the overall treatment time period
radiobio.e: Late-responding tissues (low alpha/beta) benefit more from fractionation because they repair more sublethal damage between fractions compared to tumors with higher alpha/beta ratios.

radprot.q: ALARA stands for?
radprot.a: As Low As Reasonably Achievable
c: As Low As Readily Available
As Low As Reasonably Achievable
All Levels Are Routinely Assessed
Acute Limits And Radiation Assessments
radprot.e: Overarching protection principle.

radprot.q: Three principles of protection?
radprot.a: Time, Distance, Shielding
c: Time, Distance, Shielding
kVp, mAs, Filtration
Dose, Volume, Fractionation
Energy, Fluence, Exposure
radprot.e: Minimise time, maximise distance, interpose shielding.

radprot.q: Inverse square: double distance →
radprot.a: ¼ dose
c: ½ dose
¼ dose
⅛ dose
Same dose
radprot.e: Dose ∝ 1/r². Most powerful protection tool.

radprot.q: Primary barrier material?
radprot.a: Concrete or lead

Concrete or lead
Glass during routine clinical operations
Aluminium only
radprot.e: High-Z for MV beam attenuation.

radprot.q: HVL stands for?

c: High-Voltage Limit
Half-Value Layer
Hazard Verification Level
Heavy Voltage Load

radprot.q: TVL stands for?
radprot.a: Tenth-Value Layer
c: Total Value Layer
Tenth-Value Layer
Transmission Verification Level

radprot.q: TVL reduces beam to?
radprot.a: 10% (factor of 10)

10% (factor of 10)
1% during routine clinical operations
0.1% for optimal treatment outcomes
radprot.e: 1 TVL = ×10 reduction. 2 TVLs = ×100.

radprot.q: Shielding design report?
radprot.a: NCRP 151

NCRP 116
NCRP 147
NCRP 151
radprot.e: Megavoltage RT structural shielding.

radprot.q: Workload (W) unit?

Gy/week at 1 m
MU/hour for therapeutic applications
R/min during patient treatment sessions
radprot.e: Total dose output at 1 m per week.

radprot.q: Occupancy factor (T) for office?

1/16 as specified by guidelines

radprot.q: Neutron contamination starts at?
radprot.a: > 10 MV photons

> 6 MV
> 10 MV photons

radprot.a: Ion chamber survey meter

Ion chamber survey meter

Access control + monitoring

radprot.q: Primary barrier vs secondary barrier?
radprot.a: Primary: in direct beam. Secondary: scatter/leakage only
c: Same thing — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
Primary: in direct beam. Secondary: scatter/leakage only
Primary: thinner — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Secondary: in direct beam
radprot.e: Primary barriers must attenuate the full useful beam. Secondary only needs to handle scatter and head leakage.

radprot.q: Head leakage limit for linac?
radprot.a: 0.1% of useful beam at 1 m

0.1% of useful beam at 1 m
1% throughout the treatment course

radprot.q: Scatter fraction at 1 m from patient (90°) for 6 MV?

~0.1-0.3% of primary

radprot.a: Diagnostic radiology facilities

Megavoltage therapy installations only
Nuclear medicine departments only

Safety device that prevents beam-ON when unsafe
Patient positioning tool

~1-2 mSv per treatment Gy at isocentre

Lead only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements

radprot.q: Radiation survey frequency for therapy vault?

After installation, annually, and after any modifications

radprot.q: CALC: If a sealed source has activity 2 GBq with Γ = 0.13 mSv·m²/(GBq·hr), dose rate at 30 cm?

radprot.q: Transport index (TI) on a radioactive package indicates?

Maximum dose rate in mSv/hr at 1 m from package surface

Number of sources — verified through routine quality assurance measurements

radprot.e: TI = dose rate at 1m in mSv/hr × 100 = 0.01 × 100 = 1.0.

radprot.a: 500 mSv (voluntary, informed consent)

100 mSv — verified through routine quality assurance measurements

500 mSv (voluntary, informed consent)

radprot.a: 5-10 cm borated polyethylene

5-10 cm borated polyethylene
20 cm concrete in radiation therapy practice
radprot.e: BPE: hydrogen moderates neutrons, boron captures thermals. Most effective neutron shielding per thickness.

radprot.q: CALC: Triple distance from source: dose factor?

radprot.q: CALC: Workload for linac treating 30 patients/day, average 3 Gy/pt at isocentre?

150 Gy/wk for radiation dose management
450 Gy/wk at isocentre
900 Gy/wk for the treatment procedure

radprot.q: CALC: IMRT increases linac MU by factor of ~3 vs 3D-CRT. How does this affect leakage shielding?
radprot.a: Secondary barrier workload for leakage increases ~3×

radprot.e: IMRT: same dose to patient but ~3× more MU → 3× more head leakage. Primary workload unchanged.

radprot.q: CALC: Photoneutron dose at 1m from 18 MV target ≈ 0.15% of photon dose. If 500 Gy/wk photon workload, neutron dose?

radprot.q: What is the controlled-uncontrolled boundary criterion?

radprot.q: CALC: If barrier reduces dose from 2 Sv/wk to 0.05 mSv/wk, what is the transmission factor?

148 cm

radprot.q: AI-based radiation dose tracking systems benefit hospitals by?
radprot.a: Automated cumulative dose monitoring, DRL benchmarking, and real-time alerts for high-dose procedures
c: Replacing RPO entirely — based on established physics principles and clinical evidence
Automated cumulative dose monitoring, DRL benchmarking, and real-time alerts for high-dose procedures
Only tracking staff dose — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Only for nuclear medicine — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
radprot.e: Enterprise dose management (DoseWatch, Radimetrics, DoseMap): captures DICOM dose data from all scanners, tracks per-patient cumulative dose, benchmarks against DRLs, flags outliers. Enables evidence-based optimisation programme.

radprot.q: Machine learning for shielding design could?
radprot.a: Rapidly optimise barrier thickness considering multiple room geometries and workload scenarios simultaneously
c: Replace all shielding standards, following current regulatory and professional standards
Rapidly optimise barrier thickness considering multiple room geometries and workload scenarios simultaneously
Eliminate need for shielding, following current regulatory and professional standards
Only calculate TVLs — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
radprot.e: ML models trained on Monte Carlo simulations can rapidly predict dose distributions through complex geometries. Enables parametric design exploration (what-if scenarios) faster than manual NCRP 151 calculations. Still requires physicist verification.

radprot.q: Automated survey meter data logging and trending improves radiation safety by?
radprot.a: Digital records, trend analysis for early warning, elimination of transcription errors
c: Only saves paper — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Digital records, trend analysis for early warning, elimination of transcription errors
Replaces survey meter calibration, based on established clinical physics practice
Only useful for audits — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
radprot.e: Modern survey meters with Bluetooth/WiFi: auto-log readings with GPS coordinates and timestamps. Software trends readings over time → detect gradual shielding degradation or source strength changes before they become safety issues.

radprot.q: Electronic personal dosimeter (EPD) advantage over passive TLD/OSL?
radprot.a: Real-time dose display, audible alarms, and instant readout — no processing delay
c: Higher absolute measurement accuracy compared to TLD/OSL passive dosimeter systems
Real-time dose display, audible alarms, and instant readout — no processing delay
Significantly lower cost per dosimeter compared to passive monitoring alternatives
No calibration needed — factory settings remain accurate throughout the device lifetime
radprot.e: EPDs: immediate feedback allows workers to modify behaviour during procedure. Set dose rate and cumulative dose alarms. Data downloaded to software for trending. Complement (not replace) legal passive dosimeters in many jurisdictions.

radprot.q: Active personal dosimeter for interventional radiology: where worn?
radprot.a: Outside lead apron at collar level
c: Under lead apron at waist
Outside lead apron at collar level
On finger only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
On shoe — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
radprot.e: Outside apron: reflects dose to unshielded head/neck/eyes. Collar-level badge best estimates eye lens and thyroid dose. If two badges worn: one outside collar (unshielded dose) + one under apron at waist (shielded body dose). Effective dose calculated from both.

radprot.q: CALC: Dose at 0.5 m from point source if 100 μSv/hr at 2 m?
radprot.a: 1600 μSv/hr
c: 400 μSv/hr
800 μSv/hr
1600 μSv/hr
3200 μSv/hr
radprot.e: ISL: 100 × (2/0.5)² = 100 × 16 = 1600 μSv/hr. Inverse square works powerfully in reverse — getting closer increases dose dramatically.

radprot.q: CALC: Source reads 200 μSv/hr at 1 m. At what distance is the dose rate 5 μSv/hr?
Skyshine calculation: when is it the dominant pathway?
radprot.a: When roof shielding is thinner than walls and occupied areas exist adjacent to the facility
c: Always dominant — based on established physics principles and clinical evidence
When roof shielding is thinner than walls and occupied areas exist adjacent to the facility
Only at night — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Only for neutrons — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
radprot.e: Radiation exits through thin roof → scatters from air molecules back down to ground level outside walls. Can exceed direct wall transmission. Must check especially for linac vaults with inadequate roof shielding or rooftop equipment areas.

radprot.q: CALC: Skyshine dose estimation (simplified): H_sky ≈ B × W × Ω/(2π) at distance d, where Ω is solid angle of roof opening. Why does skyshine decrease slowly with distance?
radprot.a: Air-scattered radiation spreads over large area
c: Follows 1/d² like direct radiation
Air-scattered radiation spreads over large area
Increases with distance
Constant at all distances
radprot.e: falls off roughly as 1/d rather than 1/d². Skyshine is a broad scatter source (entire air column above roof). Not a point source → doesn't obey simple ISL. Empirical models show ~1/d^(1-1.5) falloff. Can be significant at 30-50 m from facility.

radprot.q: OSL (Optically Stimulated Luminescence) dosimeter material?
radprot.a: Al₂O₃:C (aluminium oxide doped with carbon)
c: LiF:Mg,Ti — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Al₂O₃:C (aluminium oxide doped with carbon)
CaSO₄:Dy — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
BaFBr:Eu — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
radprot.e: Landauer Luxel/nanoDot: Al₂O₃:C stimulated by green LED light. Advantage over TLD: can be re-read multiple times without signal loss. Energy range 5 keV to 40 MeV. Sensitivity ~10 μSv.

radprot.q: TLD material commonly used for personal monitoring?
radprot.a: LiF:Mg,Ti (TLD-100)
c: NaI(Tl) for radiation dose management
LiF:Mg,Ti (TLD-100)
CdZnTe for the treatment procedure
BaF₂ in clinical practice settings
radprot.e: LiF: near tissue-equivalent (Z_eff ≈ 8.2 vs tissue 7.4). Heated to ~300°C to read. Single-use per read cycle. Also LiF:Mg,Cu,P (TLD-100H) for higher sensitivity.

radprot.q: Eye lens dosimeter (Hp(3)) requirement after ICRP 118?
radprot.a: Dedicated eye lens dosimeter for workers likely to exceed 15 mSv/yr to lens
c: Not required anywhere — existing whole-body dosimeters adequately estimate eye lens dose
Dedicated eye lens dosimeter for workers likely to exceed 15 mSv/yr to lens
Only required for nuclear medicine workers handling unsealed therapeutic radioisotopes
Same measurement as whole-body badge — no separate dosimeter needed for eye lens monitoring
radprot.e: ICRP 118: lens dose limit reduced to 20 mSv/yr (from 150). Interventional cardiology, IR staff most at risk. Hp(3) dosimeter worn at eye level outside lead glasses. Now required in EU and increasingly in SA.

radprot.q: Radiation protection for MR-linac: unique challenge?
radprot.a: Must shield MV radiation AND maintain magnetic field homogeneity
c: Standard concrete vault only
Must shield MV radiation AND maintain magnetic field homogeneity
No shielding needed due to MRI — established commissioning data remains valid throughout the equipment lifetime
Only RF shielding — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
radprot.e: ferromagnetic shielding materials distort B₀. MR-linac: (1) MV photon shielding (concrete). (2) RF shielding (Faraday cage). (3) No ferromagnetic materials near magnet → special non-magnetic rebar. (4) 5 Gauss fringe field line defines controlled area. Multi-physics shielding challenge.

radprot.q: Diagnostic reference level (DRL) is?
radprot.a: Investigation level — not a dose limit, but a trigger to review practice if routinely exceeded
c: Maximum permitted patient dose that must never be exceeded under any clinical circumstance
Investigation level — not a dose limit, but a trigger to review practice if routinely exceeded
Minimum dose required to achieve acceptable diagnostic image quality for each examination type
Only applies to CT examinations — other modalities have no established reference levels
radprot.e: DRLs: 75th percentile of dose distribution across facilities for standard-sized patients. If local median exceeds DRL → investigate and optimise. Not a limit: some patients legitimately need higher dose. Tool for continuous improvement.

radprot.q: What does ALARA stand for in radiation protection?
radprot.a: As Low As Reasonably Achievable
c: As Low As Reasonably Achievable
Always Limit All Radiation Access
Approved Levels of Acceptable Radiation Activity
Annual Limit for Absorbed Radiation Amount
radprot.e: ALARA stands for As Low As Reasonably Achievable. It is a fundamental principle of radiation protection stating that radiation exposure should be kept as low as reasonably achievable, taking into account social and economic factors.

radprot.q: What are the three basic principles of radiation protection?
radprot.a: Time, distance, and shielding
c: Speed, accuracy, and precision
Time, distance, and shielding
Dose, volume, and energy
Planning, delivery, and verification
radprot.e: The three cardinal principles of radiation protection are: minimise Time of exposure, maximise Distance from the radiation source, and use appropriate Shielding. Together, these principles help reduce radiation dose to workers and the public.

radprot.q: How does increasing distance from a radiation source affect the dose received?
radprot.a: The dose decreases as distance increases
c: The dose increases as distance increases
The dose stays the same regardless of distance
The dose decreases as distance increases
Distance only affects dose from alpha particles
radprot.e: Radiation dose decreases with increasing distance from the source, following the inverse square law. If you double the distance from a point source, the dose rate drops to one quarter. This is why maintaining distance is an effective protection strategy.

radprot.q: What is the purpose of shielding in radiation protection?
radprot.a: To absorb or attenuate radiation and reduce exposure to people
c: To make radiation beams more powerful
To change the colour of radiation in the radiation therapy context
To absorb or attenuate radiation and reduce exposure to people
To speed up radioactive decay according to clinical protocols
radprot.e: Shielding involves placing absorbing materials (such as lead, concrete, or steel) between the radiation source and people. The shielding material absorbs or attenuates the radiation, thereby reducing the dose received by individuals on the other side.

radprot.q: Which material is most commonly used for shielding against X-rays and gamma rays?
radprot.a: Lead
c: Wood
Plastic
Lead
Aluminium foil
radprot.e: Lead is the most commonly used shielding material for X-rays and gamma rays due to its high atomic number (Z=82) and high density. These properties make it very effective at attenuating photon radiation. Lead aprons, lead glass, and lead-lined walls are all common in medical facilities.

radprot.q: CALC: A secondary scatter barrier for a 6 MV linac vault requires transmission factor B = 2.5 × 10⁻³. The TVL for scatter at this energy in concrete is 15 cm. What barrier thickness is needed?
radprot.a: 39 cm
c: 15 cm
26 cm
39 cm
52 cm
radprot.e: Number of TVLs = -log₁₀(B) = -log₁₀(2.5 × 10⁻³) = -(log₁₀(2.5) + log₁₀(10⁻³)) = -(0.398 - 3) = 2.602 TVLs. Thickness = n × TVL = 2.602 × 15 cm = 39.03 cm ≈ 39 cm. For secondary barriers (scatter and leakage), a single TVL value is typically used since the beam spectrum is already degraded. The scattered radiation at 6 MV has much lower energy than the primary beam, hence the smaller TVL (15 cm) compared to the primary barrier TVL (34 cm for 6 MV).

radprot.q: CALC: A nuclear medicine storage safe uses 3 mm of lead to shield Tc-99m (140 keV, HVL in Pb = 0.3 mm). What is the transmission factor?
radprot.a: 9.77 × 10⁻⁴ (approximately 0.1%)
c: 9.77 × 10⁻⁴ (approximately 0.1%)
3.1 × 10⁻² (approximately 3%)
0.125 (approximately 12.5%)
3.1 × 10⁻⁵ (approximately 0.003%)
radprot.e: Number of HVLs = thickness / HVL = 3.0 mm / 0.3 mm = 10 HVLs. Transmission = (0.5)^10 = 1/1024 = 9.77 × 10⁻⁴ ≈ 0.1%. This means 99.9% of 140 keV photons are attenuated. Lead is extremely effective for Tc-99m shielding because 140 keV is just above the lead K-edge (88 keV), maximizing photoelectric absorption. Dose rate at 1 m from 20 GBq unshielded Tc-99m is approximately 156 μSv/hr; with 3 mm Pb this drops to approximately 0.15 μSv/hr.

radprot.q: CALC: A radiation worker's monthly deep dose readings (mSv) are: Jan 1.2, Feb 1.5, Mar 2.1, Apr 1.8, May 3.2. The annual occupational limit is 20 mSv. What is the projected annual dose at this rate?
radprot.a: 23.5 mSv — exceeds annual limit
c: 9.8 mSv — well within limit
23.5 mSv — exceeds annual limit
19.6 mSv — just within limit
47.0 mSv — grossly exceeds limit
radprot.e: 5-month total = 1.2 + 1.5 + 2.1 + 1.8 + 3.2 = 9.8 mSv. Monthly average = 9.8/5 = 1.96 mSv. Projected annual = 1.96 × 12 = 23.5 mSv, exceeding the ICRP 103 occupational limit of 20 mSv/year (averaged over defined 5-year periods, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv). The May spike of 3.2 mSv warrants immediate investigation for an incident or procedural breakdown. The RPO must review work practices and implement ALARA measures.

radprot.q: CALC: A linac vault maze has 3 legs. The scattered dose rate entering the maze is 8 μSv/hr. Each maze leg provides a factor-of-10 attenuation. What is the dose rate at the outer maze entrance?
radprot.a: 0.008 μSv/hr
c: 0.8 μSv/hr
0.08 μSv/hr
0.008 μSv/hr
2.4 μSv/hr
radprot.e: Total maze attenuation = 0.1 × 0.1 × 0.1 = 10⁻³. Dose rate at outer entrance = 8 μSv/hr × 10⁻³ = 0.008 μSv/hr = 8 nSv/hr. This is well below the design limit for uncontrolled areas (typically 0.5 μSv/hr from 0.02 mSv/week at 40 hours). Each maze leg provides approximately one tenth-value layer through geometric attenuation of multiply-scattered radiation. A well-designed 3-leg maze often eliminates the need for a heavy shielded door.

radprot.q: CALC: A declared pregnant radiation worker has badge readings of 0.15, 0.12, and 0.18 mSv over the first 3 months. The fetal dose limit is 1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy. At this rate over 6 remaining months, will the limit be met?
radprot.a: Projected 0.90 mSv — within the 1 mSv limit but needs close monitoring
c: Projected 0.45 mSv — well within limit for clinical implementation
Projected 0.90 mSv — within the 1 mSv limit but needs close monitoring
Projected 1.35 mSv — will exceed limit for clinical implementation
Projected 2.70 mSv — far exceeds limit in the healthcare environment
radprot.e: Average monthly dose = (0.15 + 0.12 + 0.18) / 3 = 0.15 mSv/month. Projected dose for 6 remaining months = 0.15 × 6 = 0.90 mSv. This is within the 1 mSv pregnancy limit but leaves only 10% margin.
