The badge reading (Hp(10)) overestimates the actual fetal dose (located deep within the abdomen), but close monthly monitoring is mandatory. ALARA principles apply — the limit is not a dose target. Any month exceeding 0.2 mSv should trigger a work practice review with the RPO.

radprot.q: What is the concept of effective dose (E) in radiation protection and how is it calculated?
radprot.a: Sum of tissue-weighted equivalent doses across all organs, using ICRP tissue weighting factors to reflect stochastic risk
c: The absorbed dose measured directly by a personal dosimeter worn on the chest
Sum of tissue-weighted equivalent doses across all organs, using ICRP tissue weighting factors to reflect stochastic risk
The maximum dose received by any single organ during radiation exposure
The dose equivalent calculated using only the radiation weighting factor
radprot.e: E = Σ wT × HT, where HT = Σ wR × DT,R. Tissue weighting factors (wT) from ICRP 103 reflect relative radiation sensitivity for stochastic effects (cancer, hereditary). E provides a single number for risk comparison across different exposure scenarios. Highest wT: gonads (0.08), bone marrow (0.12), breast (0.12).

radprot.q: In shielding design for a radiotherapy vault, what is the difference between primary and secondary barriers?
radprot.a: Primary barriers attenuate the direct useful beam; secondary barriers attenuate leakage and scattered radiation only
c: Primary barriers are always thicker than secondary barriers regardless of workload
Primary barriers attenuate the direct useful beam; secondary barriers attenuate leakage and scattered radiation only
Primary barriers are made of lead while secondary barriers use concrete exclusively
There is no distinction; all vault walls must meet the same shielding requirement
radprot.e: Primary barriers: in the direct beam path, must attenuate the full useful beam. Typically 1.5-2.5 m concrete for 6-18 MV. Secondary barriers: only see leakage (≤0.1% of primary) and patient/room scatter. Can be thinner (~1 m). NCRP 151 provides calculation methodology. Use factor (U) = fraction of time beam is directed at that wall.

radprot.q: What is the purpose of an area radiation survey after linac installation, and what instrument is appropriate?
radprot.a: Verifies that dose rates outside the vault meet design goals; a calibrated pressurised ion chamber survey meter is used
c: Confirms the linac output matches the manufacturer specification; a Farmer chamber is used
Verifies that dose rates outside the vault meet design goals; a calibrated pressurised ion chamber survey meter is used
Checks for radioactive contamination on vault surfaces; a pancake GM detector is used
Measures the neutron activation of the vault walls; a BF3 detector is used
radprot.e: Post-installation survey measures dose rate at vault boundaries, doors, ceiling, and accessible areas during beam-on conditions at maximum output. A calibrated survey meter (e.g., pressurised ion chamber like Fluke 451P) measures ambient dose equivalent. Results compared to design goals (e.g., 0.02 mSv/week for public areas). Required before clinical use.

radprot.q: CALC: A linac operates at 600 MU/min maximum output. Weekly workload = 500 Gy at isocentre. The primary barrier must reduce dose to 0.1 mSv/week (public area). Dose rate at 4 m without shielding is 150 mSv/week. How many TVLs of shielding are required?
radprot.a: Approximately 3.2 TVLs
c: Approximately 1.5 TVLs
Approximately 2.0 TVLs
Approximately 3.2 TVLs
Approximately 4.5 TVLs
radprot.e: Transmission factor B = P/(dose rate unshielded) = 0.1/150 = 6.67 × 10⁻⁴. Number of TVLs = -log₁₀(B) = -log₁₀(6.67 × 10⁻⁴) = -(−3.176) = 3.18 ≈ 3.2 TVLs.

radprot.q: ICRP 103 dose limits: what is the annual effective dose limit for occupationally exposed workers, and how does the 5-year averaging provision work?
radprot.a: 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years with no single year exceeding 50 mSv
c: 50 mSv/year with no averaging provision over multiple years
20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years with no single year exceeding 50 mSv
100 mSv/year with annual monitoring only and no cumulative limit
5 mSv/year with no provision for exceeding this in any year
radprot.e: ICRP 103 occupational limit: 20 mSv/year averaged over defined 5-year periods, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv. This allows flexibility for higher-dose years while maintaining long-term safety. Total over 5 years: ≤100 mSv. SA regulations align with these ICRP recommendations.

radprot.q: For a high-energy linac (≥10 MV), what additional shielding consideration is required compared to a 6 MV unit?
radprot.a: Photoneutron production requires dedicated neutron shielding (e.g., polyethylene or borated materials) in the vault door and maze
c: No additional considerations; concrete shielding is sufficient for all energies
Only the primary barrier thickness increases; secondary barriers remain unchanged
Photoneutron production requires dedicated neutron shielding (e.g., polyethylene or borated materials) in the vault door and maze
High-energy beams require only lead shielding instead of concrete
radprot.e: Above ~8 MV, photonuclear reactions (γ,n) produce neutrons, mainly from high-Z materials in the linac head (W target, Pb). Neutrons are not effectively attenuated by concrete alone at the maze entrance. BPE (borated polyethylene) or other hydrogenous materials are added to the vault door. NCRP 151 requires neutron dose evaluation for ≥10 MV.

radprot.q: According to NNR regulations in South Africa, what is the annual occupational effective dose limit for radiation workers?
radprot.a: 20 mSv averaged over 5 years, max 50 mSv in any single year
c: 20 mSv averaged over 5 years, max 50 mSv in any single year
50 mSv per year with no averaging period or annual ceiling
10 mSv averaged over 3 years, max 30 mSv in any single year
100 mSv averaged over 5 years, max 50 mSv in single year
radprot.e: The NNR adopts the ICRP 60/103 limit: 100 mSv over 5 consecutive years (20 mSv/yr average), not exceeding 50 mSv in any single year.

radprot.q: What is the annual effective dose limit for members of the public under South African NNR regulations?
radprot.a: 1 mSv per year under normal planned exposure situations
c: 5 mSv per year under normal planned exposure situations
1 mSv per year under normal planned exposure situations
2 mSv per year under normal planned exposure situations
0.5 mSv per year under planned exposure situations
radprot.e: The public dose limit is 1 mSv/yr effective dose, consistent with ICRP recommendations adopted by the NNR.

radprot.q: What is the equivalent dose limit to the lens of the eye for occupationally exposed workers per ICRP 118?
radprot.a: 20 mSv/yr averaged over 5 years, max 50 mSv in any year
c: 20 mSv/yr averaged over 5 years, max 50 mSv in any year
150 mSv per year with no multi-year averaging requirement
50 mSv/yr averaged over 5 years, max 100 mSv any year
100 mSv per year with no multi-year averaging requirement
radprot.e: ICRP 118 reduced the eye lens limit from 150 mSv/yr to 20 mSv/yr averaged over defined periods of 5 years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv.

radprot.q: Once a worker declares pregnancy, what is the recommended equivalent dose limit to the embryo/fetus for the remainder of the pregnancy?
radprot.a: 1 mSv to the surface of the abdomen for the remainder of pregnancy
c: 1 mSv to the surface of the abdomen for the remainder of pregnancy
5 mSv to the surface of the abdomen for the remainder of pregnancy
0.1 mSv to the surface of abdomen for the remainder of pregnancy
2 mSv to the surface of the abdomen for the remainder of pregnancy
radprot.e: ICRP recommends that the dose to the embryo/fetus should not exceed about 1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy after declaration.

radprot.q: CALC: A point source produces 400 microsieverts/hr at 1 m. What is the dose rate at 3 m from the source?
radprot.a: 44.4 microsieverts/hr at 3 m from the point source
c: 44.4 microsieverts/hr at 3 m from the point source
133.3 microsieverts/hr at 3 m from the point source
22.2 microsieverts/hr at 3 m from the point source
66.7 microsieverts/hr at 3 m from the point source
radprot.e: Inverse square law: 400 × (1/3)² = 400/9 = 44.4 µSv/hr.

radprot.q: CALC: The TVL for 6 MV photons in concrete is 34 cm. How many TVLs are needed to reduce the dose rate by a factor of 10,000?
radprot.a: 4 TVLs are required to achieve a reduction factor of 10,000
c: 4 TVLs are required to achieve a reduction factor of 10,000
3 TVLs are required to achieve a reduction factor of 10,000
5 TVLs are required to achieve a reduction factor of 10,000
6 TVLs are required to achieve a reduction factor of 10,000
radprot.e: Each TVL reduces by factor 10. 10^4 = 10,000, so n = log10(10,000) = 4 TVLs needed.

radprot.q: CALC: Concrete TVL1 = 37 cm and TVLe = 33 cm for 6 MV. What total concrete thickness provides 5 TVLs of attenuation?
radprot.a: 169 cm of concrete for 5 TVLs of total beam attenuation
c: 169 cm of concrete for 5 TVLs of total beam attenuation
165 cm of concrete for 5 TVLs of total beam attenuation
185 cm of concrete for 5 TVLs of total beam attenuation
145 cm of concrete for 5 TVLs of total beam attenuation
radprot.e: Thickness = TVL1 + (n-1) x TVLe = 37 + 4 x 33 = 37 + 132 = 169 cm. The first TVL is larger due to beam hardening effects.

radprot.q: In NCRP Report 147, what does the shielding design goal 'P' represent in diagnostic imaging facility design?
radprot.a: The maximum permissible air kerma in the occupied area expressed per week
c: The maximum permissible air kerma in the occupied area expressed per week
The total patient throughput or workload of the imaging room per calendar week
The primary beam attenuation factor of the selected barrier shielding material
The peak instantaneous dose rate measured at one metre from the x-ray source
radprot.e: NCRP 147 uses P as the shielding design goal, typically 0.1 mGy/week for controlled areas and 0.02 mGy/week for uncontrolled areas.

radprot.q: In NCRP Report 151 for megavoltage therapy room shielding, what two types of radiation must secondary barriers attenuate?
radprot.a: Patient scatter radiation and linac head leakage radiation combined
c: Patient scatter radiation and linac head leakage radiation combined
Primary beam radiation and secondary electron contamination combined
Neutron radiation and induced activation gamma radiation combined
Skyshine radiation and groundshine reflected radiation combined
radprot.e: Secondary barriers attenuate scatter from the patient/walls and leakage through the linac head. These are calculated separately then combined using the two-source rule.

radprot.q: What occupancy factor (T) does NCRP 151 assign to an unattended parking area adjacent to a therapy vault?
radprot.a: T = 1/40 for unattended parking and vehicular drop-off areas
c: T = 1/40 for unattended parking and vehicular drop-off areas
T = 1/20 for unattended parking and vehicular drop-off areas
T = 1/5 for unattended parking and vehicular drop-off areas
T = 1/8 for unattended parking and vehicular drop-off areas
radprot.e: NCRP 151 Table B.2 assigns T = 1/40 for areas like outdoor unattended parking, reflecting very low expected occupancy.

radprot.q: CALC: A linac treats 30 patients/day, average 300 MU/patient, 5 days/week. What is the weekly workload W in Gy at isocentre?
radprot.a: 450 Gy/week at the isocentre calibration reference point
c: 450 Gy/week at the isocentre calibration reference point
45 Gy/week at the isocentre calibration reference point
225 Gy/week at the isocentre calibration reference point
900 Gy/week at the isocentre calibration reference point
radprot.e: W = 30 patients x 300 MU/patient x 5 days = 45,000 MU/week. At isocentre 1 MU = 1 cGy, so W = 45,000 cGy = 450 Gy/week.

radprot.q: What is the use factor (U) assigned to the floor primary barrier in a conventional linac vault with 360-degree gantry rotation?
radprot.a: U = 0.25, as roughly one quarter of treatments direct beam downward
c: U = 0.25, as roughly one quarter of treatments direct beam downward
U = 1.0, because the beam can potentially be directed at floor always
U = 0.5, as roughly one half of all treatments direct beam downward
U = 0.1, because the floor is rarely directly irradiated in practice
radprot.e: NCRP 151 assigns U = 0.25 for floor and each lateral wall as a reasonable distribution of gantry angles across four cardinal directions.

radprot.q: A Geiger-Mueller (GM) counter is best suited for which radiation protection measurement task?
radprot.a: Detecting contamination and confirming presence or absence of radiation
c: Detecting contamination and confirming presence or absence of radiation
Measuring precise absorbed dose values for personnel monitoring records
Calibrating therapy-level radiation beams in the megavoltage energy range
Determining the exact photon energy spectrum of a clinical radiation beam
radprot.e: GM counters are highly sensitive and ideal for contamination surveys and detecting low-level radiation, but they saturate at high dose rates and cannot discriminate energy.

radprot.q: Which survey instrument is most appropriate for measuring dose rates in a controlled area around a linac bunker?
radprot.a: A calibrated pressurised ionisation chamber survey meter for readings
c: A calibrated pressurised ionisation chamber survey meter for readings
A thin-window Geiger-Mueller tube detector for contamination screening
A NaI(Tl) scintillation spectrometer for energy-resolved measurements
A neutron rem-ball detector designed for photon area dose monitoring
radprot.e: Pressurised ion chambers provide accurate dose rate measurements over a wide range with relatively flat energy response, making them ideal for area surveys around linac rooms.

radprot.q: What material is used in optically stimulated luminescence (OSL) personal dosimeters?
radprot.a: Aluminium oxide doped with carbon (Al2O3:C) as the detector element
c: Aluminium oxide doped with carbon (Al2O3:C) as the detector element
Lithium fluoride doped with magnesium and titanium as the detector
Calcium fluoride doped with dysprosium (CaF2:Dy) as the detector
Silver-activated metaphosphate glass rod used as the detector element
radprot.e: OSL dosimeters use Al2O3:C which stores energy in traps and releases light proportional to dose when stimulated with a laser. They can be re-read multiple times.

radprot.q: What is a key advantage of OSL dosimeters over TLD badges for personal monitoring in radiation protection?
radprot.a: OSL badges can be re-read multiple times without losing the stored signal
c: OSL badges can be re-read multiple times without losing the stored signal
OSL badges have far superior sensitivity to neutron radiation than TLDs
OSL badges do not require any calibration or quality assurance at all
OSL badges are completely insensitive to any environmental temperature
radprot.e: OSL readout uses only a fraction of the stored signal, allowing re-analysis. TLD heating during readout empties all traps, making re-reading impossible.

radprot.q: In South African regulations, a controlled area is defined as an area where workers might receive doses exceeding what fraction of the occupational limit?
radprot.a: 3/10 of the annual occupational dose limit, which equals about 6 mSv
c: 3/10 of the annual occupational dose limit, which equals about 6 mSv
1/10 of the annual occupational dose limit, which equals about 2 mSv
1/20 of the annual occupational dose limit, which equals about 1 mSv
1/2 of the annual occupational dose limit, which equals about 10 mSv
radprot.e: A controlled area is where workers might receive more than 6 mSv/yr (3/10 of 20 mSv). A supervised area applies where exposure could exceed 1 mSv but is unlikely to exceed 6 mSv.

radprot.q: What defines a supervised area in the ICRP area classification system for radiation protection?
radprot.a: An area where exposure may exceed 1 mSv/yr but unlikely to exceed 6 mSv
c: An area where exposure may exceed 1 mSv/yr but unlikely to exceed 6 mSv
An area where exposure may exceed 6 mSv/yr but unlikely to exceed 20 mSv
An area where no radiation worker may enter without a respirator in use
An area where only classified radiation workers may enter with dosimetry
radprot.e: Supervised areas require monitoring but not the full controls of a controlled area. Workers in supervised areas need not be classified as radiation workers.

radprot.q: CALC: A radioactive source has a half-life of 74 days. What is its decay constant in units of per day?
radprot.a: 0.00937 per day, calculated as ln(2) divided by the half-life
c: 0.00937 per day, calculated as ln(2) divided by the half-life
0.01351 per day, calculated as 1.0 divided by the half-life
0.00469 per day, calculated as ln(2) divided by twice half-life
0.01874 per day, calculated as 2 x ln(2) divided by half-life
radprot.e: Decay constant = ln(2)/T_half = 0.693/74 = 0.00936, which rounds to 0.00937 per day.

radprot.q: CALC: An Ir-192 source (air kerma rate constant 0.111 uGy.m2/(MBq.hr)) has activity 370 GBq. What is the dose rate at 1 m?
radprot.a: 41.1 mGy/hr at 1 metre distance from this high-activity source
c: 41.1 mGy/hr at 1 metre distance from this high-activity source
4.11 mGy/hr at 1 metre distance from this high-activity source
411 mGy/hr at 1 metre distance from this high-activity source
0.411 mGy/hr at 1 m distance from this high-activity source
radprot.e: Dose rate = Gamma x A / d^2 = 0.111 uGy.m2/(MBq.hr) x 370,000 MBq / 1^2 = 41,070 uGy/hr = 41.1 mGy/hr.

radprot.q: CALC: A barrier must reduce dose rate from 200 mSv/hr to 0.02 mSv/hr. If HVL = 2.5 cm lead, what thickness is needed?
radprot.a: Approximately 33.2 cm of lead to achieve the required attenuation
c: Approximately 33.2 cm of lead to achieve the required attenuation
Approximately 25.0 cm of lead to achieve the required attenuation
Approximately 16.6 cm of lead to achieve the required attenuation
Approximately 41.5 cm of lead to achieve the required attenuation
radprot.e: Transmission = 0.02/200 = 10^-4. Number of HVLs = log2(10^4) = 4 x 3.322 = 13.29. Thickness = 13.29 x 2.5 = 33.2 cm.

radprot.q: What type of radiation monitoring device uses a polyethylene moderator sphere surrounding a thermal neutron detector?
radprot.a: A neutron rem meter (rem ball or Bonner sphere) for dose equivalent
c: A neutron rem meter (rem ball or Bonner sphere) for dose equivalent
A pressurised argon ionisation chamber for photon dose rate measurement
A silicon diode detector array for real-time electron beam verification
A lithium-drifted germanium spectrometer for gamma spectral analysis
radprot.e: Rem balls use polyethylene to thermalise fast neutrons, which are then detected by a BF3 or He-3 proportional counter. The moderator shape provides an approximately dose-equivalent response.

radprot.q: In South Africa, which body is the national nuclear regulator responsible for radiation protection oversight?
radprot.a: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) established under the NNR Act
c: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) established under the NNR Act
The South African Health Products Regulatory Authority (SAHPRA)
The Department of Mineral Resources and Energy (DMRE) solely
The Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA) alone
radprot.e: The NNR, established by the National Nuclear Regulator Act (No. 47 of 1999), regulates nuclear and radiation safety in South Africa. SAHPRA regulates medical devices, while HPCSA registers practitioners.

radprot.q: Under South African NNR regulations, a nuclear authorisation is required before a facility may perform what activities?
radprot.a: Possess, use, store, transport, or dispose of radioactive material
c: Possess, use, store, transport, or dispose of radioactive material
Only manufacture new radioactive sources and sealed source assemblies
Only import radioactive sources from international suppliers abroad
Only decommission facilities that previously housed sealed sources
radprot.e: The NNR Act requires nuclear authorisation for any activity involving radioactive materials including possession, use, storage, transport, import, export, and disposal.

radprot.q: What is the purpose of a wipe test (smear test) performed in nuclear medicine and radiation laboratories?
radprot.a: To detect removable radioactive contamination on surfaces and equipment
c: To detect removable radioactive contamination on surfaces and equipment
To measure total fixed and removable activity embedded in shielding
To calibrate the energy response of portable survey meters before use
To verify output consistency of sealed therapeutic radiation source units
radprot.e: Wipe tests involve wiping a defined area (typically 100 cm2) with filter paper and counting it in a well counter or liquid scintillation counter to quantify removable contamination.

radprot.q: According to IAEA transport regulations adopted in SA, what is the maximum surface dose rate for a Type A package?
radprot.a: 2 mSv/hr on the external surface of the transport package
c: 2 mSv/hr on the external surface of the transport package
0.1 mSv/hr on external surface of the transport package
10 mSv/hr on external surface of the transport package
0.5 mSv/hr on external surface of the transport package
radprot.e: IAEA SSR-6 specifies a maximum surface dose rate of 2 mSv/hr for packages transported under routine conditions, and 0.1 mSv/hr at 1 m from the surface.

radprot.q: What does the Transport Index (TI) of a radioactive package quantify for regulatory purposes?
radprot.a: The maximum radiation level in mSv/hr at 1 m from the package, times 100
c: The maximum radiation level in mSv/hr at 1 m from the package, times 100
The total activity of radioactive contents expressed in multiples of A2 value
The mass of shielding material used in the package construction in kilograms
The number of packages that may be stacked together during storage transit
radprot.e: TI = maximum dose rate at 1 m from the package surface in mSv/hr x 100. It determines vehicle placarding, storage spacing, and transport category (I-White, II-Yellow, III-Yellow).

radprot.q: During a radiation emergency involving a lost or orphan radioactive source, what is the first priority action?
radprot.a: Secure the area and prevent access to limit potential radiation exposure
c: Secure the area and prevent access to limit potential radiation exposure
Immediately attempt to retrieve the source manually to reduce exposure
Contact the media to issue a public warning before notifying authorities
Evacuate the entire city or town regardless of the source activity level
radprot.e: The first priority is to establish a safety perimeter and prevent access. The NNR and emergency services should be notified immediately. Source retrieval should only be done by trained personnel.

radprot.q: In a stuck-source emergency during HDR brachytherapy, after the afterloader fails to retract, what is the next step?
radprot.a: Use the manual emergency hand crank to retract the source into safe
c: Use the manual emergency hand crank to retract the source into safe
Leave the patient in room and wait for the physicist to arrive later
Cut the guide tube near the patient without any preparation or tools
Switch off all room power including radiation area monitoring system
radprot.e: Emergency sequence: (1) attempt retraction via afterloader, (2) use manual hand crank, (3) if both fail, use emergency kit to cut tube and place source in emergency container, then remove patient.

radprot.q: CALC: Shielding design goal P = 0.02 mSv/wk for uncontrolled area. Dose rate behind barrier = 0.5 mSv/wk, occupancy T = 1/4. Is the barrier adequate?
radprot.a: No, because 0.5 x 0.25 = 0.125 mSv/wk exceeds the 0.02 mSv/wk goal
c: No, because 0.5 x 0.25 = 0.125 mSv/wk exceeds the 0.02 mSv/wk goal
Yes, because 0.5 mSv/wk is below the 1.0 mSv annual public dose limit
Yes, because the occupancy factor reduces the dose to acceptable levels
No, because any measurable dose behind a barrier violates all regulations
radprot.e: Effective dose = 0.5 x T = 0.5 x 0.25 = 0.125 mSv/wk, which exceeds P = 0.02 mSv/wk. The barrier needs additional shielding material.

radprot.q: CALC: Calculate primary barrier transmission B given: W=450 Gy/wk, U=0.25, T=1, d=4 m, P=0.1 mSv/wk.
radprot.a: B = 1.42 x 10^-5 requiring significant primary barrier thickness
c: B = 1.42 x 10^-5 requiring significant primary barrier thickness
B = 5.69 x 10^-5 requiring moderate primary barrier for shielding
B = 1.42 x 10^-4 requiring relatively thin primary barrier only
B = 3.56 x 10^-6 requiring extremely thick primary barrier walls

radprot.q: What is the maximum linac head leakage specification as a percentage of the useful beam output at isocentre?
radprot.a: 0.1% of the useful beam measured at 1 m from the target in any direction
c: 0.1% of the useful beam measured at 1 m from the target in any direction
1.0% of the useful beam measured at 1 m from the target in any direction
0.01% of useful beam measured at 1 m from the target in any direction
0.5% of the useful beam measured at 1 m from the target in all direction
radprot.e: IEC 60601-2-1 specifies that leakage radiation must not exceed 0.1% (1/1000) of the useful beam dose rate at 1 m from the target, averaged over 100 cm2.

radprot.q: Skyshine becomes the dominant radiation pathway from a linac vault when which condition exists?
radprot.a: When the roof is thinner than the walls and occupied areas are nearby
c: When the roof is thinner than the walls and occupied areas are nearby
When the primary barriers are excessively thick and the maze is short
When the linac energy is below 6 MV and only electrons are being used
When the vault is located underground with metres of earth shielding
radprot.e: Skyshine occurs when radiation escapes through the (often thinner) roof, scatters in the atmosphere, and irradiates areas adjacent to the vault. It is significant when roof shielding is less than wall shielding.

radprot.q: CALC: A room receives 2.5 uSv per treatment. With 40 patients/day, 5 days/week, 50 weeks/year, T=1. What is the annual dose?
radprot.a: 25 mSv per year, which exceeds the occupational annual dose limit
c: 25 mSv per year, which exceeds the occupational annual dose limit
2.5 mSv per year, which is just above the public annual dose limit
12.5 mSv per year, which moderately exceeds the dose limit value
50 mSv per year, reaching the single-year occupational ceiling max
radprot.e: Annual dose = 2.5 uSv x 40 x 5 x 50 x 1 = 25,000 uSv = 25 mSv. This greatly exceeds both public (1 mSv) and occupational (20 mSv avg) limits.

radprot.q: The NaI(Tl) scintillation detector is commonly used for which specific radiation protection application?
radprot.a: Gamma spectroscopy and identification of unknown radioactive contaminants
c: Gamma spectroscopy and identification of unknown radioactive contaminants
Direct measurement of neutron fluence around high-energy accelerator rooms
Calibration of megavoltage photon beams for external beam therapy treatment
Measurement of beta particle surface contamination on protective clothing
radprot.e: NaI(Tl) detectors provide energy discrimination, making them useful for identifying radionuclides by their characteristic gamma-ray energies in environmental and contamination surveys.

radprot.q: What is the two-source rule used for in megavoltage radiation therapy shielding calculations?
radprot.a: Combining dose from two independent sources at the same barrier location
c: Combining dose from two independent sources at the same barrier location
Calculating total activity of two different radionuclides mixed together
Determining the distance where two sources produce equal dose rate value
Estimating required storage spacing between two transport packages safely
radprot.e: Two-source rule: if dose contributions are within a factor of ~10, add them. If one dominates by more than 10x, use the larger and add one HVL to the barrier. Used for combining scatter and leakage at secondary barriers.

radprot.q: CALC: A technologist spends 2 hr/day at 0.8 m from a 740 MBq Tc-99m patient (Gamma = 0.0208 mSv.m2/GBq.hr). Estimate daily dose ignoring decay.
radprot.a: Approximately 0.048 mSv for this daily nuclear medicine exposure
c: Approximately 0.048 mSv for this daily nuclear medicine exposure
Approximately 0.48 mSv for this daily nuclear medicine patient dose
Approximately 0.0048 mSv for daily nuclear medicine exposure dose
Approximately 0.0024 mSv for this daily nuclear medicine exposure

radprot.q: CALC: A Co-60 source has initial activity 300 TBq. After 3 half-lives (T1/2 = 5.27 years), what is the remaining activity?
radprot.a: 37.5 TBq remaining after three half-lives of radioactive decay
c: 37.5 TBq remaining after three half-lives of radioactive decay
75.0 TBq remaining after three half-lives of radioactive decay
150 TBq remaining after three complete half-lives of the decay
18.75 TBq remaining after three half-lives of radioactive decay
radprot.e: After n half-lives, activity = A0 / 2^n = 300 / 2^3 = 300/8 = 37.5 TBq.

radprot.q: Under SAHPRA regulations, which Act governs the control of radiation-emitting devices as hazardous substances?
radprot.a: Hazardous Substances Act (Act 15 of 1973)
c: Medicines and Related Substances Act (Act 101 of 1965)
Hazardous Substances Act (Act 15 of 1973)
Nuclear Energy Act (Act 46 of 1999)
Occupational Health and Safety Act (Act 85 of 1993)
radprot.e: SAHPRA derives its radiation control mandate from the Hazardous Substances Act, which classifies radiation-emitting devices into Groups II, III, and IV.

radprot.q: SAHPRA classifies radiation-emitting devices into hazardous substance groups. Which group covers diagnostic X-ray equipment?
radprot.a: Group II
c: Group I
Group II
Group III
Group IV
radprot.e: Group II = electronic radiation sources (diagnostic X-ray), Group III = therapeutic radiation equipment, Group IV = radioactive materials.

radprot.q: Under SAHPRA, Group III hazardous substances specifically cover which type of radiation equipment?
radprot.a: Therapeutic radiation equipment (linacs, Co-60 units)
c: Diagnostic X-ray machines for quality assurance purposes
Therapeutic radiation equipment (linacs, Co-60 units)
Radioactive sealed sources for accurate dose delivery
Industrial radiography cameras for therapeutic applications
radprot.e: Group III covers equipment used for radiotherapy including linear accelerators and cobalt-60 teletherapy units.

radprot.q: What type of licence must a facility obtain from SAHPRA before operating any radiation-emitting device?
radprot.a: Licence to Use
c: Licence to Use
Manufacturing Licence
Import Permit
Research Authorization
radprot.e: A SAHPRA Licence to Use is required for each facility operating radiation equipment, specifying the equipment type and responsible person.

radprot.q: SAHPRA requires each licensed radiation facility to designate a person responsible for radiation safety. What is this role called?
radprot.a: Responsible Person
c: Radiation Safety Officer
Responsible Person
Medical Physics Expert
Radiation Protection Adviser
radprot.e: The Responsible Person is designated on the SAHPRA licence and bears legal accountability for radiation safety at the facility.

radprot.q: What document must be submitted to SAHPRA before constructing a new radiotherapy bunker?
radprot.a: Shielding design report with calculations
c: Equipment purchase order
Shielding design report with calculations
Staff training certificates
Patient consent forms
radprot.e: SAHPRA requires a shielding design report demonstrating that the proposed bunker meets dose limits at all boundaries.

radprot.q: How often must SAHPRA-licensed high-risk radiation therapy facilities undergo regulatory inspection?
radprot.a: Annually
c: Every 6 months
Annually
Every 2 years
Every 5 years
radprot.e: High-risk facilities including radiotherapy centres are subject to annual SAHPRA compliance inspections.

radprot.q: SAHPRA requires radiation workers to undergo which type of health programme?
radprot.a: Medical surveillance programme
c: Annual fitness test
Medical surveillance programme
Quarterly blood tests
Monthly vision screening
radprot.e: Medical surveillance includes baseline and periodic health assessments, with special attention to haematological parameters and lens of the eye.

radprot.q: What is the SAHPRA Radiation Control Certificate (RCC) number?
radprot.a: A unique identifier assigned to each licensed radiation facility
c: A unique identifier assigned to each licensed radiation facility
A serial number for radiation equipment
A personal dosimeter tracking code for radiation dose management
A waste disposal reference number according to clinical protocols
radprot.e: The RCC number identifies the licensed facility and is referenced in all SAHPRA correspondence and inspection reports.

radprot.q: When a SAHPRA-licensed facility wishes to dispose of or transfer radiation equipment, what approval is required?
radprot.a: Decommissioning approval from SAHPRA
c: No approval needed in the radiation therapy context
Decommissioning approval from SAHPRA
Only NNR approval during the planning stage
Municipal waste permit
radprot.e: SAHPRA must approve decommissioning to ensure proper disposal, source accountability, and updated licence records.

radprot.q: SAHPRA requires a transport permit for moving radioactive sources between facilities. Which international standards does SA transport regulation align with?
radprot.a: IAEA Transport Regulations (SSR-6)
c: ISO 9001 standards
IAEA Transport Regulations (SSR-6)
WHO guidelines on radiation
ICRU Report 91
radprot.e: SA transport regulations for radioactive materials follow IAEA Safety Standards Series SSR-6.

radprot.q: What enforcement action can SAHPRA take if a facility operates radiation equipment without a valid licence?
radprot.a: Suspension of operations and potential criminal prosecution
c: Written warning only for radiation dose management
Suspension of operations and potential criminal prosecution
Reduced fine with no further action
Mandatory retraining of staff during patient treatment sessions
radprot.e: Operating without a SAHPRA licence is a criminal offence under the Hazardous Substances Act, carrying penalties including suspension, fines, and prosecution.

radprot.q: What body preceded SAHPRA in regulating radiation-emitting devices in South Africa?
radprot.a: Directorate of Radiation Control under the Department of Health
c: NNR in the healthcare environment for clinical implementation
Directorate of Radiation Control under the Department of Health
MCC throughout the treatment course within the treatment system
SABS during the planning stage for accurate dose delivery
radprot.e: Before SAHPRA was established in 2018, radiation control of devices fell under the Directorate of Radiation Control within the DoH.

radprot.q: Under SAHPRA regulations, what is the maximum period of validity for a Licence to Use radiation equipment before renewal is required?
radprot.a: 3 years
c: 1 year
2 years
3 years
5 years
radprot.e: SAHPRA licences to use radiation equipment must be renewed every 3 years, requiring updated documentation and compliance verification.

radprot.q: SAHPRA requires adverse events involving radiation devices to be reported within what timeframe for serious incidents?
radprot.a: 48 hours
c: 24 hours
48 hours
7 days
30 days
radprot.e: Serious adverse events must be reported to SAHPRA within 48 hours; less serious events within 10 working days.

radprot.q: In South Africa, which TWO regulatory bodies share oversight of radiation safety — one for devices and one for nuclear materials?
radprot.a: SAHPRA and NNR
c: SAHPRA and NNR
HPCSA and DOH
SABS and NMISA
NECSA and CANSA
radprot.e: SAHPRA regulates radiation-emitting devices (under HSA), while NNR regulates nuclear installations and materials (under NNR Act).

radprot.q: What is the annual effective dose limit for occupationally exposed workers according to ICRP 103?
radprot.a: 20 mSv averaged over 5 years, not exceeding 50 mSv in any single year
c: 50 mSv per year with no averaging in radiation therapy practice
20 mSv averaged over 5 years, not exceeding 50 mSv in any single year
10 mSv per year strict limit during patient treatment sessions
100 mSv per year for emergency workers only
radprot.e: ICRP 103 recommends 20 mSv/yr averaged over defined 5-year periods, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv.

radprot.q: Following ICRP Publication 118, the annual equivalent dose limit to the lens of the eye was reduced to:
radprot.a: 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years
c: 150 mSv/year during the planning stage
20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years
50 mSv/year in radiation therapy practice
500 mSv/year according to clinical protocols
radprot.e: ICRP 118 reduced the eye lens limit from 150 mSv/yr to 20 mSv/yr (averaged over 5 years, max 50 in any year) based on evidence of radiation-induced cataracts.

radprot.q: What is the ICRP-recommended annual equivalent dose limit for the skin (averaged over 1 cm²)?
radprot.a: 500 mSv
c: 150 mSv
250 mSv
500 mSv
1000 mSv
radprot.e: The skin and extremities limit is 500 mSv/year, reflecting the lower radiosensitivity of skin tissue.

radprot.q: What personal dose equivalent quantity is measured by a whole-body dosimeter worn on the trunk?
c: Hp(3)
radprot.e: Hp(10) is the personal dose equivalent at 10 mm depth, used as an estimate of effective dose for whole-body monitoring.

radprot.q: The operational quantity Hp(0.07) is used to estimate dose to which tissue?
radprot.a: Skin (shallow dose)
c: Lens of the eye
Skin (shallow dose)
Deep organs
Bone marrow
radprot.e: Hp(0.07) at 0.07 mm depth estimates skin dose and is measured by extremity or skin dosimeters.

radprot.q: Hp(3) is the operational quantity introduced for monitoring dose to which organ?
radprot.a: Lens of the eye
c: Thyroid
Lens of the eye
Gonads
Red bone marrow
radprot.e: Hp(3) at 3 mm depth was introduced following ICRP 118's reduced eye lens dose limit.

radprot.q: What is the radiation weighting factor (wR) for photons and electrons?
radprot.e: Photons and electrons have wR = 1. Protons have wR = 2, alpha particles wR = 20, and neutrons vary with energy.

radprot.q: What is the radiation weighting factor (wR) for alpha particles?
radprot.e: Alpha particles have wR = 20 due to their high LET and dense ionisation track causing severe biological damage.

radprot.q: According to ICRP 103, which organ has the highest tissue weighting factor (wT)?
radprot.a: Breast, bone marrow, colon, lung, and stomach (each wT = 0.12)
c: Gonads (wT = 0.20 in the radiation therapy context)
Breast, bone marrow, colon, lung, and stomach (each wT = 0.12)
Thyroid (wT = 0.05 for quality assurance purposes)
Skin (wT = 0.01 for therapeutic applications)
radprot.e: ICRP 103 assigns wT = 0.12 to breast, red bone marrow, colon, lung, and stomach. Gonads were reduced from 0.20 to 0.08.

radprot.q: In ICRP 103, the tissue weighting factor for gonads was changed from the ICRP 60 value. What was the change?
radprot.a: Reduced from 0.20 to 0.08
c: Increased from 0.20 to 0.25
Reduced from 0.20 to 0.08
Unchanged at 0.20
Reduced from 0.20 to 0.05
radprot.e: Based on updated epidemiological data showing lower heritable risk than previously estimated.

radprot.q: Internal contamination monitoring: what is the primary method for detecting gamma-emitting radionuclides deposited in the body?
radprot.a: Whole-body counting (in vivo counting)
c: Urine bioassay for clinical implementation
Whole-body counting (in vivo counting)
Blood sampling as part of the clinical workflow
Chest X-ray for clinical implementation
radprot.e: Whole-body counters use NaI or HPGe detectors in shielded rooms to directly measure gamma emissions from internal contamination.

radprot.q: Urine bioassay is the primary monitoring method for internal contamination with which type of radionuclide?
radprot.a: Pure beta emitters (e.g., H-3, C-14)
c: High-energy gamma emitters
Pure beta emitters (e.g., H-3, C-14)
Alpha emitters only
Neutron-activated materials
radprot.e: Pure beta emitters cannot be detected by external counting, so excretion analysis (urine/faeces) is required.

radprot.q: What is the committed effective dose (E50) concept in internal dosimetry?
radprot.a: Total effective dose from an intake, integrated over 50 years for adults
c: Maximum dose in the first year after intake
Total effective dose from an intake, integrated over 50 years for adults
Dose to the most irradiated organ only for clinical implementation
External plus internal dose combined within the treatment system
radprot.e: E50 accounts for the total dose delivered as the radionuclide is metabolised and excreted over 50 years (70 years for children).

radprot.q: What is the Annual Limit on Intake (ALI) for a radionuclide?
radprot.a: The intake that would result in a committed effective dose of 20 mSv
c: The maximum activity that can be stored in a lab
The intake that would result in a committed effective dose of 20 mSv
The activity causing acute radiation syndrome
The daily excretion limit for contaminated workers
radprot.e: ALI corresponds to the occupational dose limit, ensuring that any single radionuclide intake does not exceed 20 mSv committed effective dose.

radprot.q: What is the Derived Air Concentration (DAC) used for in radiation protection?
radprot.a: Setting airborne contamination limits in workplaces — breathing DAC for 2000 hr/yr gives 20 mSv
c: Measuring radon in homes for quality assurance purposes during the planning stage
Setting airborne contamination limits in workplaces — breathing DAC for 2000 hr/yr gives 20 mSv
Calibrating air ionisation chambers in the healthcare environment
Determining ventilation rates in CT rooms as part of the clinical workflow
radprot.e: DAC = ALI / (breathing rate × 2000 hours). Working at DAC concentration for a full year delivers the occupational dose limit.

radprot.q: Potassium iodide (KI) tablets are distributed during nuclear emergencies to protect which organ?
radprot.a: Thyroid gland
c: Liver
Thyroid gland
Kidneys
Bone marrow
radprot.e: KI saturates the thyroid with stable iodine, blocking uptake of radioactive I-131 released during nuclear accidents.

radprot.q: In a radiation emergency, what is the recommended reference level for sheltering of the public?
radprot.a: 10 mSv projected dose in the first 2 days
c: 1 mSv per year for radiation dose management
10 mSv projected dose in the first 2 days
100 mSv lifetime dose in radiation therapy practice
50 mSv in the first hour
radprot.e: IAEA GSR Part 7 recommends sheltering when projected dose in 2 days may exceed 10 mSv, and evacuation above 50-100 mSv.

radprot.q: For life-saving emergency actions, ICRP recommends that informed volunteers should not exceed what effective dose?
radprot.a: No dose restriction, but efforts should keep below ~500 mSv (or ~1 Sv to prevent acute effects)
c: 20 mSv in the radiation therapy context for quality assurance purposes
100 mSv for radiation dose management in radiation therapy practice
No dose restriction, but efforts should keep below ~500 mSv (or ~1 Sv to prevent acute effects)
250 mSv as specified by guidelines for accurate dose delivery
radprot.e: For life-saving, no formal limit applies, but ICRP suggests volunteers be informed and dose kept below levels causing deterministic effects.

radprot.q: What is the decontamination factor (DF) in radiation protection?
radprot.a: Ratio of contamination before to after decontamination
c: Ratio of contamination before to after decontamination
Percentage of radioactive decay per hour
Shielding transmission factor
Dose reduction from distance increase
radprot.e: DF = initial contamination / residual contamination. A DF of 10 means 90% of contamination was removed.

radprot.q: In nuclear medicine spill management, what is the correct order of actions?
radprot.a: Contain, notify, decontaminate, survey
c: Evacuate, call fire department, seal room
Contain, notify, decontaminate, survey
Decontaminate first, then notify
Ignore if activity is below 1 MBq
radprot.e: Contain the spill (absorbent pads), notify the RSO, decontaminate from outside inward, then survey to confirm clearance.

radprot.q: When performing a contamination wipe test, what is the standard area sampled per wipe?
radprot.a: 100 cm²
c: 10 cm²
100 cm²
1000 cm²
1 m²
radprot.e: Standard wipe tests sample a 100 cm² area to allow comparison against regulatory surface contamination limits.

radprot.q: What is the difference between removable (loose) and fixed contamination?
radprot.a: Removable can be transferred by contact; fixed is bound to the surface
c: There is no difference in radiation therapy practice
Removable can be transferred by contact; fixed is bound to the surface
Fixed contamination is always more hazardous
Removable means airborne only for accurate dose delivery
radprot.e: Wipe tests detect removable contamination. Fixed contamination contributes to external dose but cannot spread by contact.

radprot.q: What is the purpose of biokinetic models in internal dosimetry?
radprot.a: To predict the distribution, retention, and excretion of radionuclides in the body over time
c: To calculate external shielding requirements as specified by guidelines
To predict the distribution, retention, and excretion of radionuclides in the body over time
To determine the energy spectrum of X-ray tubes in the radiation therapy context
To model patient positioning errors for quality assurance purposes
radprot.e: Biokinetic models (e.g., ICRP Publication 130) describe how inhaled or ingested radionuclides move through body compartments.

radprot.q: What is the primary purpose of environmental radiation monitoring around a nuclear facility?
radprot.a: To verify that public dose limits are not exceeded and detect abnormal releases
c: To calibrate personal dosimeters during the planning stage
To verify that public dose limits are not exceeded and detect abnormal releases
To measure cosmic radiation levels within the treatment system
To test emergency sirens throughout the treatment course
radprot.e: Environmental monitoring includes air samplers, soil/water sampling, and TLD stations around the facility perimeter.

radprot.q: Radon-222 is the largest natural source of radiation exposure to the general public. Where does it primarily accumulate?
radprot.a: Indoor air in poorly ventilated buildings, especially basements
c: Outdoor air at high altitudes as specified by guidelines
Indoor air in poorly ventilated buildings, especially basements
Drinking water only within the treatment system
Food products in clinical practice settings
radprot.e: Radon seeps from soil into buildings. The WHO recommends action levels of 100-300 Bq/m³ for indoor radon.

radprot.q: What fraction of the average annual background radiation dose (~2.4 mSv) comes from medical exposures globally?
radprot.a: Approximately 20% (0.6 mSv), but much higher in developed countries
c: Less than 1% in the radiation therapy context
Approximately 20% (0.6 mSv), but much higher in developed countries
Exactly 50% for therapeutic applications
Over 80% for the treatment procedure for quality assurance purposes
radprot.e: Medical exposure averages ~0.6 mSv globally but exceeds 3 mSv/year in the US due to CT scanning prevalence.

radprot.q: The linear no-threshold (LNT) model in radiation protection assumes what relationship between dose and cancer risk?
radprot.a: Cancer risk is directly proportional to dose with no safe threshold
c: There is a safe threshold below which no cancer occurs
Cancer risk is directly proportional to dose with no safe threshold
Risk increases exponentially with dose
Risk is only significant above 100 mSv
radprot.e: LNT is the basis for ALARA and dose limits, though it remains debated at low doses. It provides a conservative framework for protection.

radprot.q: What is the nominal risk coefficient for fatal cancer from whole-body radiation exposure according to ICRP 103?
radprot.a: Approximately 5% per Sv for the whole population
c: 0.5% per Sv for the treatment procedure
Approximately 5% per Sv for the whole population
10% per Sv in radiation therapy practice
50% per Sv for radiation dose management
radprot.e: ICRP 103 estimates 5.5% per Sv detriment-adjusted risk for the whole population, often rounded to 5% for fatal cancer risk.

radprot.q: In South Africa, the Vaalputs facility in the Northern Cape is used for what radiation protection purpose?
radprot.a: Disposal of low and intermediate-level radioactive waste
c: Storage of spent nuclear fuel for radiation dose management
Disposal of low and intermediate-level radioactive waste
Calibration of survey instruments
Training of radiation workers for quality assurance purposes
radprot.e: Vaalputs is SA's national radioactive waste disposal facility, operated by NECSA, accepting LILW from medical, industrial, and research sources.

radprot.q: What is the delay-and-decay method of radioactive waste management?
radprot.a: Storing short-lived waste until activity decays to background, then disposing as normal waste
c: Immediately burying all radioactive waste throughout the treatment course
Storing short-lived waste until activity decays to background, then disposing as normal waste
Diluting waste in large water volumes per standard operating procedures
Incinerating all contaminated materials throughout the treatment course
radprot.e: Used for short-lived isotopes (e.g., Tc-99m, F-18). Waste is stored for ~10 half-lives until activity is indistinguishable from background.

radprot.q: In radiation protection, what does the concept of 'dose constraint' mean?
radprot.a: A prospective upper bound on individual dose from a planned source, set below the dose limit
c: The same as the dose limit in the radiation therapy context
A prospective upper bound on individual dose from a planned source, set below the dose limit
The minimum detectable dose for clinical implementation
The dose causing deterministic effects in radiation therapy practice
radprot.e: Dose constraints ensure that no single source uses up the full dose limit, leaving margin for other exposures. They are a tool for optimisation.

radprot.q: What is the purpose of a Radiation Protection Programme (RPP) at a licensed facility?
radprot.a: A documented system describing how the facility ensures compliance with dose limits and ALARA
c: A financial budget for purchasing shielding for the treatment procedure
A documented system describing how the facility ensures compliance with dose limits and ALARA
An emergency evacuation route map based on protocol requirements
A list of all patients treated in clinical practice settings
radprot.e: The RPP covers responsibilities, procedures, monitoring, training, emergency plans, and record-keeping for all radiation activities.

radprot.q: What training frequency does SAHPRA require for radiation workers at licensed facilities?
radprot.a: Initial training before starting work, plus regular refresher training (typically annual)
c: Training only at initial employment during the planning stage
Initial training before starting work, plus regular refresher training (typically annual)
Training every 5 years for accurate dose delivery according to clinical protocols
No formal training requirement exists for the treatment procedure
radprot.e: SAHPRA licence conditions require documented initial and ongoing radiation safety training for all workers.

radprot.q: According to South African regulations (DoH), the annual occupational effective dose limit for radiation workers is?
radprot.a: 20 mSv per year averaged over five consecutive years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv
c: 20 mSv per year averaged over five consecutive years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv
50 mSv per year with no averaging period and no additional constraints on any year
10 mSv per year for all radiation workers regardless of the category of work performed
100 mSv per year for Category A workers and 20 mSv per year for Category B workers
radprot.e: South Africa follows ICRP 103 recommendations adopted into national regulations. The dose limit for occupational exposure is 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years (100 mSv in 5 years), with no single year exceeding 50 mSv. This applies to classified (Category A) radiation workers.

radprot.q: CALC: A shielding barrier must reduce the dose rate from 100 mSv/week to below 0.1 mSv/week. If the tenth-value layer (TVL) for the barrier material is 15 cm, what minimum barrier thickness is required?
radprot.a: 45 cm — requiring three TVLs to achieve the needed reduction factor of 1000
c: 45 cm — requiring three TVLs to achieve the needed reduction factor of 1000
30 cm — requiring two TVLs to achieve a reduction factor of one hundred
15 cm — requiring one TVL since the dose rate only needs to decrease tenfold
60 cm — requiring four TVLs to achieve a reduction factor of ten thousand
radprot.e: Required attenuation factor = 100/0.1 = 1000 = 10³. Number of TVLs = log₁₀(1000) = 3. Barrier thickness = 3 × 15 cm = 45 cm. Each TVL reduces the dose rate by a factor of 10.

radprot.q: The ALARA principle in radiation protection requires that?
radprot.a: All radiation exposures be kept as low as reasonably achievable, considering economic and societal factors
c: All radiation exposures be kept as low as reasonably achievable, considering economic and societal factors
Radiation doses must never exceed fifty percent of the maximum permissible dose limits
All facilities must reduce exposures to zero regardless of cost or operational implications
Only the highest-risk procedures need dose optimisation while routine exposures are exempted
radprot.e: ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) is a fundamental optimisation principle (ICRP Principle 2). It mandates that doses be minimised considering economic, societal, and operational factors — not just below dose limits. In South Africa, the National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) enforces ALARA compliance.

radprot.q: Which type of survey meter is most appropriate for measuring ambient dose equivalent rate from a high-energy linear accelerator room?
radprot.a: A pressurised ionisation chamber survey meter calibrated for the appropriate energy range
c: A pressurised ionisation chamber survey meter calibrated for the appropriate energy range
A thin-window Geiger-Müller counter designed for detecting low-energy beta contamination
A sodium iodide scintillation detector optimised for gamma spectroscopy measurements
A neutron rem meter with polyethylene moderator for thermal neutron detection only
radprot.e: Pressurised ionisation chambers (e.g., Fluke 451) provide accurate dose rate measurements over a wide energy range relevant to linac leakage and scatter. They have good energy response for photons from diagnostic to therapy energies and are the standard instrument for radiation protection surveys around treatment rooms.

radprot.q: CALC: The half-value layer of lead for Co-60 gamma rays is approximately 12 mm. What thickness of lead is needed to reduce the exposure rate to 1/32 of the original value?
radprot.a: 60 mm — since 1/32 equals five half-value layers and each HVL is 12 mm of lead
c: 60 mm — since 1/32 equals five half-value layers and each HVL is 12 mm of lead
48 mm — since 1/32 equals four half-value layers and each HVL is 12 mm of lead
36 mm — since 1/32 equals three half-value layers and each HVL is 12 mm of lead
72 mm — since 1/32 equals six half-value layers and each HVL is 12 mm of lead
radprot.e: 1/32 = (1/2)⁵, so 5 HVLs are needed. Thickness = 5 × 12 mm = 60 mm. Note: 2⁵ = 32. The relationship between HVL and TVL is: TVL = HVL × log₂(10) ≈ 3.32 × HVL.

radprot.q: Personal dosimetry for radiation workers in South Africa most commonly uses?
radprot.a: Optically stimulated luminescence (OSL) dosimeters provided by an approved dosimetry service
c: Optically stimulated luminescence (OSL) dosimeters provided by an approved dosimetry service
Real-time electronic personal dosimeters as the sole legal record of occupational exposure
Film badge dosimeters developed and read on-site by the radiation protection officer
Direct-reading quartz fibre electroscopes that provide immediate dose readings only
radprot.e: In South Africa, OSL dosimeters (e.g., InLight/Landauer or similar) are widely used for legal record personal dosimetry. They offer excellent sensitivity, reanalysis capability, and dose range. The dosimetry service must be approved by the Department of Health/NNR. TLD badges are also used at some institutions.

radprot.q: In the design of a radiotherapy treatment room, the primary barrier must be sufficient to attenuate?
radprot.a: The useful beam directed at the barrier, which carries the highest intensity of radiation
c: The useful beam directed at the barrier, which carries the highest intensity of radiation
Only the leakage radiation from the treatment head housing at all possible gantry angles
The scattered radiation from the patient and room surfaces at maximum field sizes
The neutron contamination generated by the beam interaction with the treatment head
radprot.e: Primary barriers intercept the direct useful beam and must provide the greatest shielding. Their thickness depends on workload (W), use factor (U), occupancy (T), distance, and the required dose limit. Secondary barriers handle leakage and scatter, which are orders of magnitude lower in intensity.

radprot.q: The annual effective dose limit for members of the public from licensed practices in South Africa is?
radprot.a: 1 mSv per year, consistent with ICRP recommendations adopted into national legislation
c: 1 mSv per year, consistent with ICRP recommendations adopted into national legislation
5 mSv per year with provisions for higher limits in special approved circumstances
0.1 mSv per year, which represents one-tenth of the occupational dose constraint
20 mSv per year, identical to the occupational limit for classified radiation workers
radprot.e: The public dose limit is 1 mSv/year effective dose from all licensed practices, as per ICRP 103 and adopted by South African regulations under the National Nuclear Regulator Act. This excludes natural background and medical exposure of the individual as a patient.

radprot.q: CALC: A radioactive source produces a dose rate of 200 μSv/h at 1 metre. Using the inverse square law, what is the dose rate at 5 metres?
radprot.a: 8 μSv/h — calculated by dividing the original dose rate by the square of the distance ratio
c: 8 μSv/h — calculated by dividing the original dose rate by the square of the distance ratio
40 μSv/h — calculated by dividing the original dose rate by the distance ratio linearly
2 μSv/h — calculated by dividing the original dose rate by the cube of the distance
1000 μSv/h — calculated by multiplying the original dose rate by the distance squared
radprot.e: Dose rate at 5 m = 200 × (1/5)² = 200 × 1/25 = 8 μSv/h. The inverse square law states that radiation intensity decreases with the square of the distance from a point source. This is a fundamental principle used in radiation protection for distance-based dose reduction.

radprot.q: For contamination control in a nuclear medicine department in South Africa, the most important measure is?
radprot.a: Designated contamination zones with regular wipe tests and appropriate waste segregation procedures
c: Designated contamination zones with regular wipe tests and appropriate waste segregation procedures
Continuous air monitoring with real-time alarms as the only necessary contamination control method
Total body counting of all staff at the beginning and end of every working shift daily
Restricting all radioactive materials to a single room with no movement permitted within the facility
radprot.e: Contamination control requires a systematic approach: designated clean/dirty zones, regular wipe tests (removable contamination), proper waste segregation (short-lived vs long-lived), personal protective equipment, and hand/foot monitoring. South African regulations require documented contamination monitoring programmes.

radprot.q: Skyshine in radiation facility design refers to?
radprot.a: Radiation that scatters from the atmosphere above an inadequately shielded roof back down to areas outside the facility
c: Radiation that scatters from the atmosphere above an inadequately shielded roof back down to areas outside the facility
Direct radiation leakage through windows and ventilation openings in the treatment room walls
Neutron activation of structural materials in the ceiling that emit secondary gamma radiation
Reflected radiation from nearby buildings that increases the ambient dose rate in the facility
radprot.e: Skyshine occurs when radiation escapes through a thin or absent roof, scatters off air molecules in the atmosphere, and returns to ground level outside the facility. This is particularly relevant for high-energy linacs (>10 MV) and must be considered in shielding calculations, especially when full overhead shielding is impractical.

radprot.q: For transport of radioactive materials in South Africa, Type A packages must?
radprot.a: Withstand normal conditions of transport without loss of containment or significant increase in radiation levels
c: Withstand normal conditions of transport without loss of containment or significant increase in radiation levels
Survive severe accident conditions including a 9-metre drop test and 800°C fire test for 30 minutes
Contain only exempt quantities of radioactive material that pose no significant radiation hazard
Be transported exclusively by dedicated nuclear transport vehicles with armed security escorts
radprot.e: Type A packages are designed for materials with activity below A₁ or A₂ values. They must withstand normal transport conditions (vibration, minor impacts, rain, stacking) per IAEA SSR-6 regulations adopted in South Africa. Type B packages are required for higher activities and must survive severe accident conditions.

radprot.q: In maze design for a linear accelerator room, the primary purpose of the maze is to?
radprot.a: Reduce scattered and leakage radiation reaching the entrance door through multiple reflections that attenuate the beam
c: Reduce scattered and leakage radiation reaching the entrance door through multiple reflections that attenuate the beam
Provide a direct line of sight from the control area to the patient for visual monitoring
Allow the treatment room door to be constructed from lightweight materials rather than heavy lead
Create an airlock system to prevent radioactive contamination from escaping the treatment vault
radprot.e: The maze (labyrinth) forces radiation to undergo multiple scatterings off walls before reaching the door, with significant attenuation at each scatter (~1/100 to 1/1000 per bounce). This reduces the shielding requirement for the door, which would otherwise need to be extremely heavy (several tonnes of lead/steel).

radprot.q: CALC: A radiation worker receives doses of 18, 15, 22, 12, and 19 mSv over five consecutive years. Does this comply with South African occupational dose limits?
radprot.a: Yes — the worker complies because the five-year average of 17.2 mSv is below 20 mSv and no year exceeds 50 mSv
c: Yes — the worker complies because the five-year average of 17.2 mSv is below 20 mSv and no year exceeds 50 mSv
No — the worker does not comply because one year exceeds the strict annual limit of 20 mSv
No — the worker does not comply because the total five-year dose of 86 mSv exceeds 80 mSv
Yes — the worker complies because each annual dose is below the 25 mSv single-year limit
radprot.e: Total = 18+15+22+12+19 = 86 mSv over 5 years. Average = 86/5 = 17.2 mSv/year (< 20 mSv). No single year exceeds 50 mSv (maximum was 22 mSv). The worker DOES comply. The limit is 100 mSv in 5 years (average 20 mSv/year), with no single year exceeding 50 mSv.

radprot.q: Neutron shielding in high-energy linac rooms (≥10 MV) typically requires?
radprot.a: Hydrogenous materials such as polyethylene or concrete with high water content to thermalise and capture neutrons
c: Hydrogenous materials such as polyethylene or concrete with high water content to thermalise and capture neutrons
High-Z materials such as lead or tungsten that effectively absorb neutrons through nuclear reactions
Evacuated voids in the walls that prevent neutron propagation through the shielding barrier
Magnetic deflection systems that divert neutrons away from occupied areas outside the room
radprot.e: Photoneutrons are produced when photon energies exceed ~8–10 MeV (threshold for giant dipole resonance). Fast neutrons are slowed (thermalised) by elastic collisions with hydrogen nuclei in hydrogenous materials (polyethylene, borated polyethylene, concrete). Boron-10 can be added to capture thermal neutrons. Standard concrete (~5% H₂O by weight) provides reasonable neutron shielding.

radprot.q: Radioactive waste management in South Africa requires short-lived waste (half-life < 100 days) to be?
radprot.a: Stored for decay in a designated area until activity falls below clearance levels, then disposed as conventional waste
c: Stored for decay in a designated area until activity falls below clearance levels, then disposed as conventional waste
Immediately transported to the national radioactive waste disposal facility at Vaalputs for burial
Incinerated in a licensed high-temperature facility to eliminate all radioactive contamination
Mixed with long-lived waste and treated as a single waste stream for permanent geological disposal
radprot.e: Short-lived radioactive waste (T½ < 100 days, common in nuclear medicine) is held in decay-in-storage. After sufficient decay (typically 10 half-lives), activity is confirmed below clearance levels by measurement, documentation is completed, and the waste is disposed of as non-radioactive. This is standard practice in SA hospitals.

radprot.q: Emergency procedures for a sealed source incident in a South African facility must include?
radprot.a: Immediate area evacuation, access control, dose rate monitoring, and notification of the National Nuclear Regulator
c: Immediate area evacuation, access control, dose rate monitoring, and notification of the National Nuclear Regulator
Attempting to manually retrieve and re-secure the source before notifying any regulatory authority
Contacting the source manufacturer for instructions before taking any protective actions on site
Sealing the entire building and waiting for international assistance from the IAEA emergency team
radprot.e: South African emergency procedures follow NNR requirements: evacuate and secure the area, establish a cordon based on dose rate measurements, account for all personnel, assess doses, and notify the NNR as required by the facility's emergency plan. The RPO must ensure emergency equipment (survey meters, PPE, signage) is readily available.

radprot.q: According to ICRP recommendations, what are the three fundamental principles of radiation protection?
radprot.a: Justification of practice, optimization of protection (ALARA), and individual dose limitation
c: Time minimization, distance maximization, and shielding optimization for all exposures
Justification of practice, optimization of protection (ALARA), and individual dose limitation
Containment of sources, monitoring of personnel, and decontamination of work areas
Risk assessment, hazard identification, and emergency response preparedness for all facilities
radprot.e: The three pillars of radiological protection are justification (benefit must outweigh detriment), optimization (ALARA), and dose limitation (individual doses must not exceed specified limits).

radprot.q: What is the recommended action level for an individual's monthly dosimeter reading that should trigger an investigation?
radprot.a: Any monthly reading exceeding the facility's established investigation level, typically 1-2 mSv per month
c: Only readings that exceed 50 mSv in a single month require any type of investigation action
Any monthly reading exceeding the facility's established investigation level, typically 1-2 mSv per month
Only cumulative annual readings above 20 mSv trigger an investigation into exposure history
No investigation is needed unless the annual regulatory dose limit has been fully exceeded
radprot.e: Facilities typically set investigation levels at 1-2 mSv/month. Exceeding this triggers a review of work practices and dose records, well before reaching the annual occupational limit.

radprot.q: What type of personal dosimeter is most commonly used for routine monitoring of external radiation exposure in hospitals?
radprot.a: Optically stimulated luminescence (OSL) dosimeter worn on the trunk at collar level
c: Electronic personal dosimeter providing only real-time audible alarm dose rate indication
Optically stimulated luminescence (OSL) dosimeter worn on the trunk at collar level
Pocket ionization chamber used exclusively for daily tracking of gamma ray exposure
Neutron bubble detector worn during all procedures involving diagnostic x-ray equipment
radprot.e: OSL dosimeters (e.g., Luxel) have largely replaced film badges and TLDs for routine hospital monitoring. They are worn at collar level (or waist under a lead apron) and processed monthly or quarterly.

radprot.q: In a radiation emergency involving a sealed source, what is the correct order of initial response actions?
radprot.a: Evacuate the area, restrict access, notify the radiation safety officer, and monitor for contamination
c: Attempt to retrieve and reshield the source before notifying any personnel about the situation
Evacuate the area, restrict access, notify the radiation safety officer, and monitor for contamination
Begin decontamination procedures immediately before assessing the radiation field levels present
Continue normal operations while waiting for the radiation safety officer to arrive and assess
radprot.e: The priority sequence is to protect people first (evacuate), prevent additional exposures (restrict access), activate the response chain (notify RSO), then characterize the situation (monitor).

radprot.q: What is the purpose of bioassay monitoring for radiation workers who handle unsealed radioactive sources?
radprot.a: To detect and quantify internal contamination from inhaled or ingested radioactive material
c: To measure the external radiation dose received from handling sealed radioactive sources only
To detect and quantify internal contamination from inhaled or ingested radioactive material
To verify that personal dosimeters are functioning correctly through comparison measurements
To determine the effectiveness of engineering controls in reducing airborne contamination levels
radprot.e: Bioassay (urine analysis, thyroid counting, whole body counting) detects internal contamination that personal dosimeters cannot measure, providing committed dose estimates from intakes of radioactive material.

radprot.q: What is the recommended survey instrument for detecting alpha contamination on surfaces in a nuclear medicine department?
radprot.a: A gas proportional counter or zinc sulfide scintillation detector held close to the surface
c: A sodium iodide scintillation detector calibrated for gamma energy spectroscopy measurements
A pressurized ionization chamber survey meter used at standard measurement distances noted
A gas proportional counter or zinc sulfide scintillation detector held close to the surface
A Geiger-Mueller pancake probe optimized for detecting high-energy beta particle emissions
radprot.e: Alpha particles have very short range, requiring detectors with thin entrance windows positioned close to the surface. Gas proportional counters and ZnS scintillators are designed for this purpose.

radprot.q: What is the concept of the 'controlled area' in radiation protection, and who may enter it?
radprot.a: An area where specific protection measures are required and access is restricted to authorized personnel
c: Any area where natural background radiation exceeds the national average measured value
An area where specific protection measures are required and access is restricted to authorized personnel
An area designated for storage of radioactive waste only with no personnel access permitted at all
Any area within 10 meters of an x-ray machine regardless of its operational status or shielding
radprot.e: Controlled areas have specific entry requirements, protection measures, and access restrictions. Only workers who have been trained, monitored, and authorized may enter during periods of potential exposure.

radprot.q: How does the inverse square law apply to radiation protection practice when managing distance from a point source?
radprot.a: Doubling the distance from a point source reduces the dose rate to one-quarter of its original value
c: Doubling the distance from a point source reduces the dose rate to one-half of its original value
Doubling the distance from a point source reduces the dose rate to one-quarter of its original value
Tripling the distance from a point source reduces the dose rate to one-third of its original value
Distance has no significant effect on dose rate for gamma-emitting sources below 1 megabecquerel
radprot.e: The inverse square law states that dose rate is proportional to 1/r². Doubling distance reduces dose rate by a factor of 4, making distance one of the most effective radiation protection tools.

radprot.q: What personal protective equipment is recommended when handling a patient who has received I-131 therapy?
radprot.a: Disposable gloves, gown, and shoe covers to prevent contamination spread from body fluids
c: Full-face respirator with organic vapor cartridges and a lead-lined protective apron
Disposable gloves, gown, and shoe covers to prevent contamination spread from body fluids
Only a lead apron is required since I-131 produces primarily gamma radiation exposure
No PPE is necessary as the patient dose is too low to create a contamination hazard
radprot.e: I-131 therapy patients excrete radioactivity in body fluids. Disposable gloves, gown, and shoe covers prevent contamination transfer, while lead aprons provide minimal benefit against the 364 keV gamma.

radprot.q: What are the regulatory requirements for posting radiation warning signs at the entrance to a radiation area?
radprot.a: The standard trefoil symbol with 'CAUTION: RADIATION AREA' where dose rates exceed 0.05 mSv/h
c: No signage is required if the radiation source is not in active clinical operation mode
The standard trefoil symbol with 'CAUTION: RADIATION AREA' where dose rates exceed 0.05 mSv/h
Only a verbal warning to personnel is sufficient without any physical sign posting needed
Signs are required only for areas exceeding 1 mSv/h dose rate at 30 cm from the source
radprot.e: Radiation areas (dose rates > 0.05 mSv/h) must be posted with the standard magenta/yellow trefoil radiation warning sign and appropriate cautionary text at all entrance points.

c: 5 mSv per year in modern radiation oncology
1 mSv per year effective dose

regs.q: Medical exposure of patients: does the 1 mSv/yr public dose limit apply?
regs.a: No - medical exposures are excluded from dose limits but must be justified and optimised
c: Yes - patients cannot exceed 1 mSv, based on established clinical physics practice
Yes - special patient limits exist, based on established clinical physics practice
No - medical exposures are excluded from dose limits but must be justified and optimised
Only for therapy, not diagnostics — further testing is not required by current international guidelines

regs.q: Dose constraint vs dose limit?
regs.a: Constraint is a planning tool set below the limit to ensure ALARA; limit is the legal maximum
c: Same concept — constraint and limit are interchangeable terms in radiation protection
Constraint is a planning tool set below the limit to ensure ALARA; limit is the legal maximum
Constraint is higher than limit — allows flexibility for justified high-dose procedures
Constraint replaces the limit in modern ICRP recommendations since Publication 103
regs.e: ICRP 103: dose constraint (e.g., 0.3 mSv/yr for shielding design) is individual optimisation. The limit (1 mSv public, 20 mSv/yr worker averaged) is the legal ceiling.

regs.q: ICRP 103 three principles of radiation protection?
regs.a: Justification, Optimisation (ALARA), Dose Limitation
c: Time, Distance, Shielding — the three practical methods of dose reduction
Justification, Optimisation (ALARA), Dose Limitation
Containment, Ventilation, Shielding — the three engineering controls for sealed sources
Detection, Prevention, Response — the three phases of radiation emergency management
regs.e: Justification: benefit > detriment. Optimisation: ALARA with economic/social factors. Dose limitation: individual limits for workers and public. These three underpin all SA radiation legislation.

regs.q: RPO responsibilities include all EXCEPT?

c: Monitoring occupational doses
Investigating radiation incidents
Prescribing patient treatment doses
Maintaining radiation safety records
regs.e: RPO: safety programme management, dose monitoring, area surveys, incident investigation, waste management, training. Treatment dose prescription is the radiation oncologist's responsibility.

regs.q: SAHPRA licence is required for which of these?

c: Only linacs — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Only sealed sources — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Only X-ray machines — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
All of these - any device producing or containing ionising radiation
regs.e: SAHPRA Group IV licence covers: diagnostic X-ray, CT, linacs, brachytherapy sources, NM sources, industrial radiography, research irradiators. Any ionising radiation source.

regs.q: Record retention for radiation worker dose records in SA?
regs.a: Until the worker reaches age 75, or for 30 years after employment ends (whichever is longer)
c: 5 years after employment ends, then records may be safely destroyed
Until the worker reaches age 75, or for 30 years after employment ends (whichever is longer)
10 years after the last recorded dose, regardless of worker age or employment status
Permanently — records must never be destroyed under any regulatory circumstance
regs.e: Long retention required because radiation-induced cancers can have 20-40 year latency. Records must be available for retrospective dose reconstruction and medicolegal purposes.

regs.q: HPCSA registration: what category covers medical physicists in SA?

regs.q: Transport of radioactive material in SA regulated by?
regs.a: SAHPRA and Department of Transport
c: Only SAHPRA
Only Department of Transport
SAHPRA and Department of Transport
No regulation exists

regs.q: SAHPRA reportable incident includes?
regs.a: Any unintended or accidental medical exposure significantly different from intended
c: Only patient death — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Only equipment failure causing injury — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Only dose to wrong patient — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Any unintended or accidental medical exposure significantly different from intended
regs.e: SAHPRA requires reporting of: wrong patient, wrong site, dose >25% above intended, equipment malfunction affecting dose, source loss, contamination. Near-misses should be recorded internally.

regs.q: Pregnant radiation worker: foetus dose limit once pregnancy declared?

c: 5 mSv — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy
Same as normal worker (20 mSv)
0.1 mSv — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
