regs.q: Diagnostic Reference Level (DRL) is?
regs.a: Investigation level at 75th percentile - not a dose limit, but a trigger to review practice
c: Maximum permitted patient dose, based on established clinical physics practice
Investigation level at 75th percentile - not a dose limit, but a trigger to review practice
Minimum dose for adequate image quality
A dose limit for patients, as recommended by current international guidelines
regs.e: DRLs established from dose surveys across facilities for standard-sized patients. If local median exceeds DRL, investigate and optimise. Not a limit: individual patients may need higher doses.

regs.q: Comforters and carers dose guidance per episode?
regs.a: 5 mSv per episode
c: 1 mSv per episode for all
5 mSv per episode
20 mSv per episode
No restriction

regs.q: Radioactive waste with T_half < 100 days: SA disposal method?
regs.a: Decay-in-storage until activity is indistinguishable from background, then dispose as normal waste
c: Only deep geological disposal — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Decay-in-storage until activity is indistinguishable from background, then dispose as normal waste
Incineration immediately — sufficient for all standard clinical treatment scenarios
Always discharge to sewer, following current regulatory and professional standards
regs.e: Short-lived NM waste (Tc-99m, F-18, I-131): store ~10 half-lives until background. Verify by survey. Dispose as normal hospital waste. Records of storage and clearance maintained.

regs.q: CALC: Tc-99m waste (T_half=6hr). Days of decay-in-storage to reduce activity to <1/1000 of original?
regs.a: ~2.5 days
c: ~1 day
~2.5 days
~5 days
~10 days
regs.e: Need 2^n > 1000. n = log2(1000) = 9.97 so ~10 half-lives. 10 x 6 hr = 60 hr = 2.5 days.

regs.q: Controlled area vs supervised area?
regs.a: Controlled: specific protection measures and restricted access

Controlled: specific protection measures and restricted access
Supervised has higher dose rates
Only controlled areas exist in SA
regs.e: Supervised: monitoring needed but normal access. Controlled area: dose could exceed 3/10 of worker limit. Restricted access, monitoring mandatory. Supervised area: >1 mSv/yr but <3/10 worker limit. Monitoring required but fewer restrictions.

regs.q: Acceptance testing of new radiation equipment in SA?

Medical physicist must perform acceptance testing before clinical use
No acceptance testing required — established commissioning data remains valid throughout the equipment lifetime
Only for linacs, not diagnostic — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
regs.e: results documented for inspection. All radiation-emitting equipment: acceptance testing by qualified medical physicist against manufacturer specs and regulatory requirements. Baseline for future QA. Must be done before first patient.

regs.q: SA QC frequency requirement for linacs?
regs.a: Daily, monthly, and annual checks per SASQART/SAAPMB protocols
c: Annual only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Monthly only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Daily, monthly, and annual checks per SASQART/SAAPMB protocols
No formal requirement — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
regs.e: SASQART: Daily (output, lasers, safety interlocks), monthly (beam symmetry, energy, MLC), annual (comprehensive beam data, mechanical). Aligns with IAEA/AAPM TG-142.

regs.q: Sealed source leak test frequency?

c: Monthly
Every 6 months
Annually
Only at installation
regs.e: Sealed sources (brachy, calibration) wipe-tested every 6 months. Action level: 200 Bq removable contamination. Leaking source must be removed from service immediately.

regs.q: National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) vs SAHPRA jurisdiction?

regs.e: SAHPRA regulates medical/industrial radiation sources. NNR: National Nuclear Regulator Act - nuclear power, research reactors, NECSA. SAHPRA: Hazardous Substances Act - medical, dental, veterinary, industrial radiation sources.

regs.q: ALARA principle: who is responsible?

regs.q: Patient identification before radiotherapy treatment in SA?

c: Name only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Hospital number only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Minimum two independent identifiers verified at every fraction

regs.q: Radiation emergency plan: what must every licensed facility maintain?

c: Only fire evacuation plan — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Written emergency procedures covering source loss, contamination, overexposure, and facility damage

regs.e: SAHPRA licence conditions require documented emergency procedures. Must include: spill containment, personal contamination, lost source search, overexposure response, fire/disaster protocol. Regular drills.

regs.q: CALC: SA shielding design constraint 0.3 mSv/year. Weekly limit = 0.006 mSv (50 weeks). Unshielded weekly dose at point = 3.0 mSv. Required attenuation factor?

c: 0.02 (factor of 50) — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
0.002 (barrier must reduce dose by factor of 500)
0.2 (factor of 5) — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
0.0002 (factor of 5000) — verified through routine quality assurance measurements

regs.q: What does the acronym SAHPRA stand for?

regs.q: What is the primary purpose of radiation legislation?

To protect workers, patients, and the public from harmful radiation exposure
To limit the number of hospitals that can use radiation
To ensure only doctors can operate radiation equipment

c: As Low As Reasonably Achievable
Always Limit All Radiation Applications

regs.q: What is the annual dose limit for a member of the public in South Africa?
regs.a: 1 mSv
c: 0.1 mSv
1 mSv
5 mSv
20 mSv

regs.q: CALC: A South African facility operates a 6 MV linac (primary barrier workload W = 1000 Gy/week) and a HDR Ir-192 brachytherapy unit. The linac's primary barrier was designed for an occupancy factor T = 1, use factor U = 0.25, and a dose limit of 0.1 mSv/week (controlled area). The tenth-value layer (TVL) for 6 MV in concrete is 34 cm. If the unshielded dose rate at the point of interest (6 m from source) is 27.8 mGy/week, how many TVLs of concrete are needed?
regs.a: 3 TVLs (102 cm concrete)
c: 2 TVLs (68 cm concrete)
3 TVLs (102 cm concrete)
4 TVLs (136 cm concrete)
2.5 TVLs (85 cm concrete)
regs.e: Required shielding: B = P×d²/(W×U×T) = 0.1×36/(1000×0.25×1) = 3.6/250 = 1.44×10⁻². Alternatively, unshielded dose at 6 m with W=1000 Gy/wk and U=0.25: primary dose = 1000×0.25/36 = 6.94 mGy/wk. Transmission factor = 0.1/6.94 = 0.0144. Number of TVLs = -log₁₀(0.0144) = 1.84... Wait — let me recalculate: B = P/(W×U×T) × d² = (0.1×10⁻³)×36/(1000×0.25×1) = 0.0036/250 = 1.44×10⁻⁵. n = -log₁₀(1.44×10⁻⁵) = 4.84 ≈ need ~5 TVLs. Actually, the problem states unshielded dose rate = 27.8 mGy/week at the point. Required: 0.1 mGy/week. Transmission B = 0.1/27.8 = 3.6×10⁻³. n = -log₁₀(3.6×10⁻³) = 2.44. So 3 TVLs = 102 cm (round up to next integer TVL for safety).

regs.q: Under ICRP 103 and South African regulations, a pregnant radiation worker declared her pregnancy at 8 weeks gestation. She has already received 4 mSv effective dose in the current monitoring period. What is the remaining dose limit to the embryo/fetus for the remainder of the pregnancy, and what regulatory action is required?
regs.a: 1 mSv to fetus for remainder of pregnancy; reassign to lower-exposure duties
c: 1 mSv to fetus for remainder of pregnancy; reassign to lower-exposure duties
0 mSv additional; immediately remove from all radiation work
5 mSv total for the year; no action needed since under annual limit
2 mSv to fetus for remainder; reduce workload by 50%
regs.e: ICRP 103 recommends the dose to the embryo/fetus should not exceed 1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy after declaration. The 4 mSv already received by the worker is her occupational dose (external badge), not the fetal dose (which is lower due to body shielding). However, the employer must reassign to ensure fetal dose stays below 1 mSv. South African regulations (DoH) align with ICRP: the facility must ensure working conditions keep fetal dose below 1 mSv from declaration to delivery.

regs.q: A diagnostic radiology facility performs fluoroscopy-guided cardiac interventions. The interventional cardiologist's monthly collar badge (Hp(10)) reads 8 mSv, and the under-apron waist badge reads 0.4 mSv. Using the NCRP two-dosimeter algorithm (effective dose ≈ 0.5×Hw + 0.025×Hn), what is the estimated monthly effective dose, and will the annual limit be exceeded if this rate continues?
regs.a: 0.4 mSv/month; annual ~4.8 mSv — within the 20 mSv limit
c: 0.4 mSv/month; annual ~4.8 mSv — within the 20 mSv limit
4.2 mSv/month; annual ~50.4 mSv — exceeds 20 mSv limit
8 mSv/month; annual 96 mSv — grossly exceeds limit
1.2 mSv/month; annual ~14.4 mSv — within 20 mSv limit
regs.e: E ≈ 0.5×Hw + 0.025×Hn = 0.5×0.4 + 0.025×8 = 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4 mSv/month. Annual projection: 0.4×12 = 4.8 mSv, well within the ICRP 103 occupational limit of 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years (max 50 mSv any single year). The collar badge reads high because it is unshielded by the lead apron and captures the exposure to the thyroid/head region.

regs.q: Under South African regulations, who is the Radiation Protection Officer (RPO) and what are their primary responsibilities?
regs.a: A qualified person appointed by the licensee to oversee radiation safety, monitoring, and regulatory compliance
c: Any medical practitioner who uses radiation equipment in clinical practice
A qualified person appointed by the licensee to oversee radiation safety, monitoring, and regulatory compliance
The hospital CEO who holds ultimate legal responsibility for all radiation activities
A SAHPRA inspector assigned to the facility for routine compliance checks
regs.e: SA Hazardous Substances Act and DoH regulations require appointment of an RPO at licensed facilities. RPO responsibilities: personal dosimetry programme, area monitoring, training, maintaining records, incident reporting, ALARA implementation, liaising with regulatory authorities. Must have appropriate qualifications recognised by HPCSA.

regs.q: What is the role of SASQART in South African radiation regulation?
regs.a: Independent advisory body that audits clinical radiation practices, recommends standards, and supports quality assurance nationwide
c: Government department that issues all radiation equipment licences as part of the clinical workflow
Independent advisory body that audits clinical radiation practices, recommends standards, and supports quality assurance nationwide
International organisation that sets global radiation dose limits per standard operating procedures
Professional registration board for medical physicists in South Africa in the healthcare environment
regs.e: SASQART (South African Society for Quality Assurance in Radiation Therapy) provides peer-review clinical audits of radiotherapy centres, develops QA guidelines adapted to SA context, and supports education/training. Works alongside SAHPRA (regulatory) and HPCSA (professional registration).

regs.q: Under HPCSA regulations, what are the continuing professional development (CPD) requirements for medical physicists in South Africa?
regs.a: Medical physicists must accumulate prescribed CPD points annually across clinical, educational, and ethics activities
c: No CPD requirements exist for medical physicists in South Africa during the planning stage
CPD is only required for medical practitioners, not medical physicists
Medical physicists must accumulate prescribed CPD points annually across clinical, educational, and ethics activities
CPD is voluntary and only needed for promotion purposes in the radiation therapy context
regs.e: HPCSA mandates CPD for all registered healthcare professionals including medical physicists. Activities include clinical audits, conference attendance, publications, workshops, and ethics training. Failure to comply may result in deregistration. Points accumulate in annual cycles with minimum requirements per category.

regs.q: The Hazardous Substances Act (HSA) Group IV covers radiation-emitting devices. What specific licence categories apply to a radiotherapy department?
regs.a: Licence to use (Group IV equipment), licence to possess radioactive sources, and authorisation for specific radiation practices
c: Only a single general licence covering all radiation activities in the radiation therapy context
Licence to use (Group IV equipment), licence to possess radioactive sources, and authorisation for specific radiation practices
No licence required if the facility is registered with HPCSA in the healthcare environment
Only import and export licences for radiation equipment for therapeutic applications
regs.e: SA HSA Group IV: radiation-emitting apparatus (linacs, X-ray). Group III: radioactive material (brachytherapy sources, sealed sources). A radiotherapy department needs: Group IV licence for linacs/simulators, Group III licence for brachytherapy/sealed sources, RPO appointment, and practice authorisation. SAHPRA issues and enforces these licences.

regs.q: In the event of a radiation incident in South Africa, what is the mandatory reporting chain and timeline?
regs.a: Immediate notification to RPO, then report to SAHPRA within 24 hours for significant incidents, with full investigation report within 30 days
c: Report to hospital management only; no external reporting is required based on protocol requirements
Immediate notification to RPO, then report to SAHPRA within 24 hours for significant incidents, with full investigation report within 30 days
Report to IAEA directly within 48 hours; SAHPRA is not involved for quality assurance purposes
No mandatory reporting unless patient dose exceeds 10 Gy for radiation dose management
regs.e: SA regulations require: (1) Immediate notification to facility RPO and management. (2) SAHPRA notification within 24 hours for incidents involving overexposure, equipment failure, or unintended exposure. (3) Detailed investigation report within 30 days including root cause analysis and corrective actions. Patient notification as required by NHA.

regs.q: What qualifications does HPCSA require for registration as a medical physicist in South Africa to practice independently?
regs.a: MSc in medical physics from an accredited programme plus supervised clinical internship and board examination
c: BSc in physics is sufficient for independent clinical practice
MSc in medical physics from an accredited programme plus supervised clinical internship and board examination
PhD in medical physics with no clinical training requirement
Any engineering degree with a short certificate course in radiation safety
regs.e: HPCSA registration pathway: (1) MSc in Medical Physics from recognised SA university. (2) Two-year supervised clinical internship at accredited centre. (3) Pass HPCSA board examination. (4) Registration as Medical Physicist (independent practice). Community service may also be required. Continuing registration requires CPD compliance.

regs.q: CEU points required per HPCSA cycle?
regs.a: 30 CEUs per cycle
c: 15 CEUs per cycle
30 CEUs per cycle
60 CEUs per cycle
45 CEUs per cycle
regs.e: HPCSA requires 30 Continuing Education Units per cycle for medical physicists. At least 5 must be in ethics, human rights, or medical law.

regs.q: HPCSA CEU cycle length?
regs.a: 12 months
c: 6 months
12 months
24 months
36 months
regs.e: The HPCSA CPD cycle runs for 12 months. Practitioners must accumulate the required CEUs within each annual cycle.

regs.q: Minimum ethics CEUs per HPCSA cycle?
regs.a: 5 CEUs in ethics
c: 2 CEUs in ethics
5 CEUs in ethics
10 CEUs in ethics
No ethics minimum
regs.e: HPCSA mandates at least 5 CEUs in ethics, human rights, or medical law per cycle to maintain registration.

regs.q: SAHPRA replaced which body?
regs.a: Medicines Control Council (MCC)
c: HPCSA regulatory arm
Medicines Control Council (MCC)
National Health Laboratory Service
Medical Research Council
regs.e: SAHPRA (South African Health Products Regulatory Authority) replaced the MCC in 2018, taking over regulation of medicines, medical devices, and radiation-emitting equipment.

regs.q: SAHPRA regulates radiation equipment under which Act?
regs.a: Hazardous Substances Act (Act 15 of 1973)
c: National Health Act (Act 61 of 2003)
Hazardous Substances Act (Act 15 of 1973)
Nuclear Energy Act (Act 46 of 1999)
Medicines and Related Substances Act
regs.e: SAHPRA regulates Group III and IV hazardous substances under the Hazardous Substances Act. Group IV covers radioactive materials and radiation-generating equipment.

regs.q: Group IV hazardous substances include?
regs.a: Radioactive materials and X-ray equipment
c: Flammable chemicals and solvents
Radioactive materials and X-ray equipment
Biological waste and pathogens
Pharmaceutical drugs and medicines
regs.e: Group IV of the Hazardous Substances Act specifically covers radioactive materials, radiation-generating equipment, and electronic products emitting radiation.

regs.q: NNR regulates which facilities?
regs.a: Nuclear installations and vessels
c: All hospitals with X-ray units
Nuclear installations and vessels
Medical device manufacturers
All radiation therapy centres
regs.e: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) regulates nuclear installations (reactors, enrichment plants) and nuclear vessels under the National Nuclear Regulator Act (Act 47 of 1999).

regs.q: Who issues a licence for a new linac installation?
regs.a: SAHPRA (Radiation Control)
c: HPCSA registration board
SAHPRA (Radiation Control)
National Nuclear Regulator
Provincial Health Department
regs.e: SAHPRA's Radiation Control Directorate issues licences for radiation-emitting equipment including linacs, under the Hazardous Substances Act.

regs.q: ICRP 103 tissue weighting factor for gonads?
regs.e: ICRP 103 reduced the gonad weighting factor from 0.20 (ICRP 60) to 0.08, reflecting updated evidence on hereditary risk.

regs.q: ICRP 103 tissue weighting factor for breast?
regs.e: ICRP 103 increased breast weighting from 0.05 to 0.12, recognising higher radiation-induced cancer risk in breast tissue.

regs.q: CALC: Worker receives 15 mSv in year 1 and 18 mSv in year 2. Max allowed in year 3 to stay within 5-year limit?
regs.a: 50 mSv
c: 17 mSv
50 mSv
67 mSv
32 mSv
regs.e: The 5-year limit is 100 mSv, and the single-year cap is 50 mSv. After 33 mSv in 2 years, 67 mSv remains over 3 years, but the annual cap of 50 mSv applies.

regs.q: CALC: How many TVLs to reduce dose rate from 40 mSv/hr to 0.04 mSv/hr?
regs.a: 3 TVLs
c: 2 TVLs
3 TVLs
4 TVLs
5 TVLs
regs.e: 40 / 0.04 = 1000 = 10³, so 3 TVLs are needed. Each TVL reduces intensity by a factor of 10.

regs.q: CALC: Source at 1 m gives 8 mSv/hr. Dose rate at 4 m?
regs.a: 0.5 mSv/hr
c: 2.0 mSv/hr
1.0 mSv/hr
0.5 mSv/hr
0.25 mSv/hr
regs.e: Inverse square law: 8 × (1/4)² = 8 × 1/16 = 0.5 mSv/hr. Distance is the most effective protection tool.

regs.q: ICRP 103 annual eye lens dose limit for workers?
regs.a: 20 mSv
c: 15 mSv
20 mSv
50 mSv
150 mSv
regs.e: ICRP revised the lens limit from 150 mSv to 20 mSv/year (averaged over 5 years, max 50 in a single year) based on cataract data.

regs.q: Annual extremity dose limit for workers?
regs.a: 500 mSv
c: 150 mSv
250 mSv
500 mSv
1000 mSv
regs.e: Hands, feet, and skin of extremities have a higher limit of 500 mSv/year because of their lower radiation sensitivity.

regs.q: Radiation Protection Officer (RPO) must be appointed when?
regs.a: At every licensed radiation facility
c: Only at nuclear installations
At every licensed radiation facility
Only when dose exceeds 5 mSv/yr
Only at facilities with sealed sources
regs.e: SA regulations require every facility holding a radiation licence to appoint a qualified RPO responsible for the radiation protection programme.

regs.q: Key RPO responsibility?
regs.a: Ensure compliance with licence conditions
c: Prescribe radiation treatment doses
Ensure compliance with licence conditions
Manufacture radiation equipment
Issue HPCSA registrations
regs.e: The RPO oversees personal dosimetry, area monitoring, waste management, incident reporting, and ensures all licence conditions are met.

regs.q: Personal dosimetry monitoring frequency in SA?
regs.a: Monthly (routine)
c: Weekly readout
Monthly (routine)
Quarterly readout
Annual readout only
regs.e: Personal dosimeters (TLD/OSL) are exchanged and read monthly in SA. This allows timely detection of elevated exposures.

regs.q: Investigation level for personal dosimetry in SA?
regs.a: Above 1 mSv per month
c: Above 0.1 mSv per month
Above 1 mSv per month
Above 5 mSv per month
Above 20 mSv per month
regs.e: Doses exceeding approximately 1 mSv per monitoring period trigger an investigation to identify causes and implement corrective actions.

regs.q: CALC: Pregnant worker declared at 8 weeks. Remaining pregnancy ~32 weeks. Max dose rate to abdomen?
regs.a: 0.0625 mSv/week
c: 0.125 mSv/week
0.0625 mSv/week
0.25 mSv/week
0.50 mSv/week
regs.e: Limit is 2 mSv to lower abdomen for remainder of pregnancy. 2 mSv / 32 weeks = 0.0625 mSv/week.

regs.q: IAEA BSS document number?
regs.a: GSR Part 3
c: Safety Series 115
GSR Part 3
GS-R-2
SSR-6
regs.e: The IAEA General Safety Requirements Part 3 (GSR Part 3, 2014) replaced Safety Series 115 as the international Basic Safety Standards for radiation protection.

regs.q: ICRP 105 covers?
regs.a: Radiation protection in medicine
c: Occupational dose limits
Radiation protection in medicine
Environmental monitoring
Transport of radioactive materials
regs.e: ICRP Publication 105 provides guidance on radiation protection specific to medical exposures, including justification and optimisation for patients.

regs.q: Three principles of ICRP radiation protection system?
regs.a: Justification, optimisation, dose limitation
c: Time, distance, shielding
Justification, optimisation, dose limitation
Detection, prevention, mitigation
Monitoring, recording, reporting
regs.e: ICRP 103 establishes justification (net benefit), optimisation (ALARA), and dose limitation (for workers/public, not patients) as the three pillars.

regs.q: Dose limits apply to which exposure category?
regs.a: Occupational and public, not medical
c: Medical exposures only
Occupational and public, not medical
All exposure categories equally
Only occupational exposures
regs.e: ICRP dose limits apply to planned occupational and public exposures. Medical patient exposures are governed by justification and optimisation, not dose limits.

regs.q: CALC: Shielding reduces dose rate from 200 mSv/hr to 0.2 mSv/hr. How many HVLs?
regs.a: ~10 HVLs
c: ~5 HVLs
~7 HVLs
~10 HVLs
~13 HVLs
regs.e: 200/0.2 = 1000. Since 2^n = 1000, n = log₂(1000) ≈ 10 HVLs. Alternatively, 3 TVLs = 3 × 3.32 HVLs ≈ 10 HVLs.

regs.q: What triggers a radiation incident investigation?
regs.a: Dose exceeding prescribed limits or unintended exposure
c: Any measurable personnel dose reading
Dose exceeding prescribed limits or unintended exposure
Equipment malfunction without exposure
Routine elevated background readings
regs.e: Incidents include overexposures to workers/public, misadministrations to patients, loss of sources, and any unplanned or unintended radiation exposure.

regs.q: Transport of radioactive materials is governed by?
regs.a: IAEA SSR-6 (SA adopts)
c: ICRP 103 only
IAEA SSR-6 (SA adopts)
NCRP 151 guidelines
WHO transport manual
regs.e: South Africa adopts the IAEA Regulations for the Safe Transport of Radioactive Material (SSR-6) for packaging, labelling, and transport requirements.

regs.q: White-I transport label: max surface dose rate?
regs.a: 0.005 mSv/hr
c: 0.005 mSv/hr
0.05 mSv/hr
0.5 mSv/hr
2.0 mSv/hr
regs.e: Category I-White has the lowest surface dose rate limit (5 µSv/hr) and a TI of 0. Yellow-II and Yellow-III allow progressively higher levels.

regs.q: Yellow-II transport label: max TI?
regs.e: Yellow-II packages have TI ≤ 1.0 and surface dose rate ≤ 0.5 mSv/hr. Yellow-III allows TI up to 10.

regs.q: CALC: Source with activity 400 MBq and dose rate constant Γ = 0.1 µSv·m²/(MBq·hr). Dose rate at 2 m?
regs.a: 10 µSv/hr
c: 5 µSv/hr
10 µSv/hr
20 µSv/hr
40 µSv/hr
regs.e: Dose rate = Γ × A / d² = 0.1 × 400 / 4 = 10 µSv/hr. The dose rate constant relates activity to dose rate at unit distance.

regs.q: Record retention for personal dosimetry in SA?
regs.a: Until worker reaches age 75 or 30 years
c: 5 years after last exposure
Until worker reaches age 75 or 30 years
10 years after employment ends
Permanently with no disposal
regs.e: SA regulations require dose records to be kept until the worker reaches 75 years of age or for 30 years after the last recorded dose, whichever is longer.

regs.q: Radiation equipment QC records must be kept for?
regs.a: Duration of equipment use plus 3 years
c: 1 year after each test
Duration of equipment use plus 3 years
5 years maximum
Only while licence is active
regs.e: QC records should be retained for the operational life of the equipment plus an additional period (typically 3 years) to support audits and inspections.

regs.q: Environmental monitoring at a radiation facility includes?
regs.a: Area dose rate surveys and contamination checks
c: Only air temperature and humidity
Area dose rate surveys and contamination checks
Noise level measurements only
Electromagnetic field surveys only
regs.e: Environmental monitoring involves regular area dose rate measurements, surface contamination monitoring (for unsealed sources), and air monitoring where applicable.

regs.q: CALC: Worker accumulates 18, 22, 15, 20 mSv over 4 years. Max allowed in year 5?
regs.a: 25 mSv
c: 20 mSv
25 mSv
45 mSv
50 mSv
regs.e: 5-year limit is 100 mSv. Sum of 4 years = 75 mSv. Remaining = 25 mSv. Although 50 mSv is the annual cap, the 5-year constraint limits year 5 to 25 mSv.

regs.q: SA radiation facility must have which document?
regs.a: Radiation Protection Programme (RPP)
c: ISO 9001 certification
Radiation Protection Programme (RPP)
WHO facility accreditation
IAEA peer review report
regs.e: Every licensed facility must establish and maintain a documented RPP covering policies, procedures, responsibilities, and emergency arrangements.

regs.q: QMS requirement for radiation therapy in SA?
regs.a: Documented quality management system
c: Optional quality improvement plan
Documented quality management system
Annual external ISO audit only
Verbal quality agreement only
regs.e: SA regulations and SASQART require a documented QMS covering all aspects of radiation therapy from referral to treatment completion and follow-up.

regs.q: ICRP dose constraint concept means?
regs.a: Prospective upper bound for optimisation
c: Absolute legal dose limit
Prospective upper bound for optimisation
Retrospective dose assessment
Emergency reference level
regs.e: Dose constraints are set below dose limits as a prospective tool to guide optimisation (ALARA). They are not limits but planning targets.

regs.q: CALC: Room requires 2.5 TVLs of lead shielding. TVL = 5.7 cm. Required thickness?
regs.e: Thickness = 2.5 × 5.7 cm = 14.25 cm. Each TVL reduces dose by a factor of 10, so 2.5 TVLs gives a factor of ~316.

regs.q: Authorisation to practice as medical physicist in SA requires?
regs.a: HPCSA registration and facility licence
c: University degree only
HPCSA registration and facility licence
IAEA certification only
SAMPS membership only
regs.e: A medical physicist must hold HPCSA registration and work at a facility with a valid SAHPRA radiation licence. Both individual and institutional authorisation are required.

regs.q: Trainee medical physicist (registrar) supervision requirement?
regs.a: Direct supervision by registered physicist
c: No supervision after year 1
Direct supervision by registered physicist
Remote supervision is sufficient
Self-supervision with logbook
regs.e: HPCSA requires registrars to work under the direct supervision of a registered medical physicist throughout their training period.

regs.q: HPCSA medical physics internship duration?
regs.a: 2 years
c: 1 year
2 years
3 years
4 years
regs.e: The HPCSA-accredited medical physics internship (registrar training) is 2 years, during which candidates must complete clinical rotations and a logbook.

regs.q: CALC: Background dose rate is 0.1 µSv/hr. Worker spends 2000 hr/yr in area with 5 µSv/hr. Annual occupational dose?
regs.a: 9.8 mSv
c: 10.0 mSv
9.8 mSv
10.2 mSv
5.0 mSv
regs.e: Occupational dose = (5 - 0.1) × 2000 = 4.9 × 2000 = 9800 µSv = 9.8 mSv. Background is subtracted for occupational dose assessment.

regs.q: Patient dose reference levels (DRLs) are?
regs.a: Investigation levels, not dose limits
c: Strict upper dose limits for patients
Investigation levels, not dose limits
Minimum required doses
Average department doses
regs.e: DRLs are set at the 75th percentile of facility doses for standard procedures. Exceeding them triggers investigation but is not a violation.

regs.q: Skin dose limit for workers (SA)?
regs.a: 500 mSv/yr
c: 50 mSv/yr
150 mSv/yr
500 mSv/yr
1000 mSv/yr
regs.e: The annual equivalent dose limit for the skin is 500 mSv, averaged over any 1 cm² area, consistent with ICRP 103 recommendations.

regs.q: CALC: Ir-192 source, 370 GBq. Dose rate constant = 0.113 mSv·m²/(GBq·hr). Dose rate at 0.5 m?
regs.a: 167.2 mSv/hr
c: 41.8 mSv/hr
83.6 mSv/hr
167.2 mSv/hr
334.4 mSv/hr
regs.e: Dose rate = 0.113 × 370 / (0.5)² = 41.81 / 0.25 = 167.2 mSv/hr. High dose rates demand strict time and distance controls.

regs.q: Sealed source leak test frequency?
regs.a: Every 6 months
c: Monthly
Every 6 months
Annually
Every 2 years
regs.e: Sealed sources must undergo wipe tests at least every 6 months to detect any leakage of radioactive material, as required by SA licence conditions.

regs.q: Disposal of disused sealed sources in SA?
regs.a: Return to supplier or NECSA
c: Bury on hospital grounds
Return to supplier or NECSA
Dispose in municipal waste
Store indefinitely on site
regs.e: Disused sealed sources must be returned to the supplier or transferred to NECSA (South African Nuclear Energy Corporation) for safe management and disposal.

regs.q: Which South African Act provides the primary legislative framework for nuclear energy regulation and the establishment of the NNR?
regs.a: National Nuclear Regulator Act (No. 47 of 1999)
c: Hazardous Substances Act (No. 15 of 1973)
National Nuclear Regulator Act (No. 47 of 1999)
Nuclear Energy Act (No. 46 of 1999, amended)
National Health Act (No. 61 of 2003, revised)
regs.e: The National Nuclear Regulator Act (Act 47 of 1999) established the NNR as the competent authority for nuclear and radiation safety regulation in South Africa.

regs.q: What is the annual effective dose limit for occupationally exposed radiation workers in South Africa?
regs.a: 20 mSv averaged over 5 consecutive years
c: 50 mSv in any single calendar year period
20 mSv averaged over 5 consecutive years
10 mSv averaged over 3 consecutive years
30 mSv averaged over 5 consecutive years
regs.e: In line with IAEA BSS and NNR regulations, the occupational dose limit in SA is 20 mSv per year averaged over 5 consecutive years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv.

regs.q: What is the annual effective dose limit for members of the public under South African radiation protection regulations?
regs.a: 1 mSv per year under normal conditions
c: 5 mSv per year under normal conditions
1 mSv per year under normal conditions
2 mSv per year under normal conditions
0.5 mSv per year under conditions set
regs.e: The public dose limit in South Africa is 1 mSv effective dose per year, consistent with IAEA Basic Safety Standards adopted into SA regulations.

regs.q: CALC: A radiation worker receives quarterly doses of 4.2, 5.1, 3.8, and 4.9 mSv. What is the total annual effective dose?
regs.a: 18.0 mSv for the calendar year
c: 18.0 mSv for the calendar year
17.0 mSv for the calendar year
20.0 mSv for the calendar year
15.0 mSv for the calendar year
regs.e: Total annual dose = 4.2 + 5.1 + 3.8 + 4.9 = 18.0 mSv. This is below the 20 mSv annual average and the 50 mSv single-year limit under SA regulations.

regs.q: Which body is responsible for the registration and regulation of medical devices, including radiation-emitting equipment, in South Africa?
regs.a: SAHPRA (SA Health Products Regulatory Authority)
c: SAHPRA (SA Health Products Regulatory Authority)
The NNR (National Nuclear Regulator of SA)
HPCSA (Health Professions Council of SA)
The DoH (National Department of Health SA)
regs.e: SAHPRA regulates medical devices under the Medicines and Related Substances Act. This includes radiation-emitting medical devices such as linear accelerators and CT scanners.

regs.q: CALC: A facility's controlled area boundary has a dose rate of 12 µSv/h. If a worker spends 1800 hours/year there, what is the projected annual dose?
regs.a: 21.6 mSv projected annual effective dose
c: 21.6 mSv projected annual effective dose
18.0 mSv projected annual effective dose
24.0 mSv projected annual effective dose
16.2 mSv projected annual effective dose
regs.e: Annual dose = 12 µSv/h × 1800 h = 21,600 µSv = 21.6 mSv. This exceeds the 20 mSv average limit and requires investigation and dose reduction measures.

regs.q: What category of radiation worker is classified as someone who may receive more than 6 mSv effective dose per year under SA regulations?
regs.a: Category A (occupationally exposed worker)
c: Category B (occupationally exposed worker)
Category A (occupationally exposed worker)
Category C (supervised radiation worker)
Category D (occasionally exposed worker)
regs.e: Category A workers are those likely to receive effective doses exceeding 6 mSv/year and require individual monitoring. Category B workers receive lower doses and may use area monitoring.

regs.q: What type of authorisation is required from the NNR to operate a linear accelerator for radiotherapy in South Africa?
regs.a: A nuclear authorisation (certificate of registration)
c: A nuclear installation licence from the NNR
A nuclear authorisation (certificate of registration)
A general exemption certificate from the NNR
A provisional notification permit from the NNR
regs.e: Operating a linear accelerator requires a nuclear authorisation (Certificate of Registration) from the NNR. Nuclear installation licences apply to reactors and large fuel cycle facilities.

regs.q: Within what time frame must a nuclear accident involving potential public exposure be reported to the NNR in South Africa?
regs.a: Immediately and without delay to the NNR
c: Within 24 hours of the event occurring
Immediately and without delay to the NNR
Within 7 working days of the incident
Within 48 hours of confirming the event
regs.e: Accidents with potential public radiological consequences must be reported immediately to the NNR, followed by a detailed written report. Lesser incidents may have longer reporting timelines.

regs.q: CALC: A controlled area design target is 0.3 mSv/week. If a worker occupies the area for 48 weeks/year, what is the annual design dose?
regs.a: 14.4 mSv annual design dose target
c: 12.0 mSv annual design dose target
16.8 mSv annual design dose target
14.4 mSv annual design dose target
10.0 mSv annual design dose target
regs.e: Annual design dose = 0.3 mSv/week × 48 weeks = 14.4 mSv. This is below the 20 mSv occupational limit, providing a safety margin as intended by the design constraint.

regs.q: Which international standard for basic radiation safety has been adopted as the foundation of South African radiation protection regulations?
regs.a: IAEA GSR Part 3 (Basic Safety Standards, 2014)
c: ICRP Publication 60 (1990 Recommendations only)
IAEA GSR Part 3 (Basic Safety Standards, 2014)
NCRP Report 147 (Structural Shielding 2004)
ISO 4037 (Reference Radiation Standards 2019)
regs.e: South Africa's radiation protection framework is based on the IAEA General Safety Requirements Part 3 (GSR Part 3), also known as the International Basic Safety Standards (BSS).

regs.q: What is the primary role of the Radiation Safety Officer (RSO) at a licensed facility in South Africa?
regs.a: Ensuring compliance with licence conditions and radiation safety
c: Approving annual budgets for radiation protection equipment only
Ensuring compliance with licence conditions and radiation safety
Performing all quality control measurements on treatment units
Independently authorising new radioactive source acquisitions
regs.e: The RSO is responsible for day-to-day radiation safety management, ensuring compliance with NNR licence conditions, monitoring doses, and implementing the radiation protection programme.

regs.q: CALC: A radioactive source has an activity of 400 GBq. The annual leakage limit is 200 Bq per wipe test. If a wipe test yields 150 Bq, is the source compliant?
regs.a: Yes, 150 Bq is below the 200 Bq leakage limit
c: No, any detectable leakage requires withdrawal
Yes, 150 Bq is below the 200 Bq leakage limit
No, the limit is 100 Bq for high-activity units
Yes, but only if the source is less than 5 years old
regs.e: The wipe test result of 150 Bq is below the 200 Bq threshold. Sources exceeding this limit must be withdrawn from use and the NNR notified. The source remains compliant.

regs.q: Under South African transport regulations, which IAEA document forms the basis for safe transport of radioactive materials?
regs.a: IAEA SSR-6 (Regulations for Safe Transport)
c: IAEA SSG-26 (Advisory Material for Transport)
IAEA SSR-6 (Regulations for Safe Transport)
IAEA GSR Part 7 (Emergency Preparedness)
IAEA SSG-46 (Radiation Protection in Mining)
regs.e: South Africa's transport regulations for radioactive materials are based on IAEA SSR-6, which provides requirements for packaging, labelling, and transport safety.

regs.q: What is the maximum surface dose rate for a Type A transport package containing radioactive material under SA transport regulations?
regs.a: 2 mSv/h at the surface of the package
c: 0.5 mSv/h at the surface of the package
2 mSv/h at the surface of the package
5 mSv/h at the surface of the package
1 mSv/h at the surface of the package
regs.e: Under IAEA SSR-6 (adopted in SA), the maximum dose rate at the surface of a routine transport package is 2 mSv/h. Exclusive use shipments may allow higher rates with conditions.

regs.q: CALC: A transport package shows 0.08 mSv/h at 1 metre from the surface. What is the Transport Index (TI)?
regs.a: TI = 0.8 for this radioactive package
c: TI = 8.0 for this radioactive package
TI = 0.08 for this package shipment
TI = 0.8 for this radioactive package
TI = 1.0 for this radioactive package
regs.e: Transport Index = dose rate at 1 m in mSv/h × 10. So TI = 0.08 × 10 = 0.8. The TI determines the package category label (I-WHITE, II-YELLOW, III-YELLOW).

regs.q: What is the dose constraint typically applied as a design criterion for shielding of a new radiotherapy bunker in South Africa?
regs.a: 0.3 mSv per week for controlled areas in the facility
c: 1.0 mSv per week for controlled areas in the facility
0.3 mSv per week for controlled areas in the facility
0.1 mSv per week for controlled areas in the facility
0.5 mSv per week for controlled areas in the facility
regs.e: The design dose constraint of 0.3 mSv/week for controlled areas ensures annual doses remain well below 20 mSv, consistent with ALARA and NNR requirements for shielding design.

regs.q: CALC: A public corridor adjacent to a radiotherapy vault must not exceed 0.02 mSv/week. If the unshielded dose rate is 800 mSv/week, what attenuation factor is needed?
regs.a: Attenuation factor of 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ is required
c: Attenuation factor of 2.5 × 10⁻³ is required
Attenuation factor of 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ is required
Attenuation factor of 4.0 × 10⁻⁵ is required
Attenuation factor of 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ is required
regs.e: Attenuation factor = 0.02/800 = 2.5 × 10⁻⁵. This determines the required shielding thickness using tenth-value layers (TVLs) for the relevant beam energy and wall material.

regs.q: What is the minimum qualification requirement for registration as a medical physicist with the HPCSA in South Africa?
regs.a: An MSc in Medical Physics from an accredited university
c: A BSc Honours in Physics from any SA university
An MSc in Medical Physics from an accredited university
A PhD in Medical Physics from a recognised institution
A national diploma in Radiography with physics elective
regs.e: The HPCSA requires at minimum an MSc in Medical Physics from an accredited institution, plus supervised clinical training and passing board examinations for registration.

regs.q: How often must personal radiation monitoring badges (TLDs/OSLDs) be exchanged for Category A workers in South Africa?
regs.a: Monthly exchange of dosimeters is required
c: Weekly exchange of dosimeters is standard
Monthly exchange of dosimeters is required
Quarterly exchange of dosimeters is used
Bimonthly exchange of dosimeters applies
regs.e: Category A workers in SA must have their personal dosimeters exchanged monthly to ensure timely dose monitoring and compliance with occupational dose limits.

regs.q: CALC: A worker's cumulative dose over 5 years is 15, 22, 18, 12, and 19 mSv. Does this comply with the 5-year average limit?
regs.a: No, the 22 mSv year is acceptable but the average is 17.2 mSv which complies
c: No, the 22 mSv year exceeds the strict 20 mSv annual limit entirely
No, the 22 mSv year is acceptable but the average is 17.2 mSv which complies
Yes, all individual years are each below the maximum 25 mSv threshold
No, the total 86 mSv exceeds the 80 mSv five-year cumulative limit set
regs.e: Average = (15+22+18+12+19)/5 = 86/5 = 17.2 mSv/year. The average is below 20 mSv and no single year exceeds 50 mSv, so this is compliant despite the misleading question framing.

regs.q: What document must a facility submit to the NNR before acquiring a new high-activity sealed radioactive source?
regs.a: An application to amend the existing nuclear authorisation
c: A radiation safety committee meeting minutes document
An application to amend the existing nuclear authorisation
A letter of intent from the facility manager and RSO
A pre-purchase risk assessment signed by the CEO only
regs.e: Acquiring a new high-activity sealed source requires an amendment to the existing nuclear authorisation from the NNR, including source details, safety assessment, and security arrangements.

regs.q: Under the Hazardous Substances Act (Group IV), which government department issues licences for electronic radiation-generating devices in SA?
regs.a: The National Department of Health (DoH) via the DRC
c: The National Nuclear Regulator through authorisation
The National Department of Health (DoH) via the DRC
SAHPRA through the medical device licensing division
The Department of Mineral Resources and Energy unit
regs.e: Group IV hazardous substances (electronic radiation sources like X-ray machines) are regulated under the Hazardous Substances Act by the DoH through the Directorate: Radiation Control (DRC).

regs.q: CALC: An X-ray room shielding calculation uses an occupancy factor T=1/4 and use factor U=1. If the unshielded weekly dose is 6 mSv, what is the design dose?
regs.a: 1.5 mSv per week with applied factors
c: 1.5 mSv per week with applied factors
2.0 mSv per week with applied factors
0.6 mSv per week with applied factors
3.0 mSv per week with applied factors
regs.e: Design dose = 6 mSv × U × T = 6 × 1 × 0.25 = 1.5 mSv/week. This value is then compared to the applicable dose constraint to determine if additional shielding is needed.

regs.q: What is the purpose of the Certificate of Registration (CoR) issued by the NNR in the context of radiation facilities?
regs.a: It authorises the holder to conduct specified nuclear activities
c: It certifies the building structure meets fire safety codes
It authorises the holder to conduct specified nuclear activities
It registers the facility with SAHPRA for device imports
It confirms the facility staff completed first aid training
regs.e: The CoR is the NNR's authorisation document permitting a holder to possess, use, store, or transport radioactive materials or operate radiation-generating equipment as specified.

regs.q: What is the annual equivalent dose limit to the skin (any area of 1 cm²) for occupationally exposed workers under SA regulations?
regs.a: 500 mSv per year to the skin for workers
c: 150 mSv per year to the skin for workers
500 mSv per year to the skin for workers
250 mSv per year to the skin for workers
300 mSv per year to the skin for workers
regs.e: The annual equivalent dose limit to the skin is 500 mSv averaged over 1 cm², consistent with IAEA BSS and ICRP recommendations adopted in SA regulations.

regs.q: CALC: A supervised area boundary must not exceed 1 mSv/year for public access. If the area is occupied 2000 hours/year, what is the maximum allowable dose rate?
regs.a: 0.5 µSv/h maximum allowable dose rate
c: 1.0 µSv/h maximum allowable dose rate
0.5 µSv/h maximum allowable dose rate
2.0 µSv/h maximum allowable dose rate
0.25 µSv/h maximum allowable dose rate
regs.e: Maximum dose rate = 1 mSv / 2000 h = 0.001 mSv/h = 0.5 µSv/h. This ensures public dose limits are not exceeded at the supervised area boundary with full occupancy.

regs.q: What is the IAEA source security category for a typical Co-60 teletherapy unit with approximately 200 TBq activity?
regs.a: Category 1 (sources that could cause permanent injury)
c: Category 2 (sources that could cause serious injury)
Category 1 (sources that could cause permanent injury)
Category 3 (sources that could cause temporary injury)
Category 4 (sources unlikely to cause serious injury)
regs.e: Co-60 teletherapy sources (~200 TBq) fall under IAEA Category 1, the highest security category. SA applies these categories for source security and requires enhanced physical protection measures.

regs.q: CALC: An Ir-192 brachytherapy source has initial activity of 370 GBq. What is the activity after 148 days? (T½ = 73.8 days)
regs.a: 92.5 GBq remaining after approximately 2 half-lives
c: 92.5 GBq remaining after approximately 2 half-lives
46.3 GBq remaining after approximately 3 half-lives
185 GBq remaining after approximately 1 half-life only
123 GBq remaining after approximately 1.5 half-lives
regs.e: Number of half-lives = 148/73.8 ≈ 2.0. Activity = 370 × (1/2)² = 370/4 = 92.5 GBq. Source exchange is typically required when activity drops below the usable treatment threshold.

regs.q: What emergency planning requirement must a radiotherapy facility with a high-activity source have in place under NNR conditions?
regs.a: A documented emergency plan approved by the NNR and tested regularly
c: An informal verbal agreement with nearby hospitals for assistance
A documented emergency plan approved by the NNR and tested regularly
Only a fire evacuation plan registered with local municipality
Emergency contacts posted at reception with no drill requirement
regs.e: NNR licence conditions require facilities with high-activity sources to maintain documented, NNR-approved emergency plans that are regularly tested through drills and exercises.

regs.q: Under SA regulations, what is the dose limit to the abdomen of a declared pregnant radiation worker for the remainder of pregnancy?
regs.a: 1 mSv to the embryo/fetus for the remaining pregnancy period
c: 5 mSv to the embryo/fetus for the remaining pregnancy period
1 mSv to the embryo/fetus for the remaining pregnancy period
2 mSv to the embryo/fetus for the remaining pregnancy period
0.5 mSv to the embryo/fetus for remaining pregnancy period
regs.e: Once pregnancy is declared, the dose to the embryo/fetus must not exceed 1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy, requiring workplace reassignment if necessary to comply.

regs.q: CALC: A nuclear medicine department handles 20 GBq of Tc-99m per week. The ALI for Tc-99m is 1 GBq. What is the ratio of weekly handled activity to ALI?
regs.a: The ratio is 20, requiring a supervised or controlled area
c: The ratio is 2, requiring only notification to the authority
The ratio is 20, requiring a supervised or controlled area
The ratio is 200, requiring maximum containment protocols
The ratio is 0.2, below the threshold for area designation
regs.e: Ratio = 20 GBq / 1 GBq = 20. This ratio helps determine area classification and required containment level. A ratio this high requires at minimum a supervised area designation.

regs.q: Which IEC standard, widely adopted in SA, specifies safety requirements for medical electrical equipment used in radiotherapy?
regs.a: IEC 60601-2-1 for electron accelerators in radiotherapy
c: IEC 61217 for radiotherapy equipment coordinate systems
IEC 60601-2-1 for electron accelerators in radiotherapy
IEC 62083 for radiotherapy treatment planning systems
IEC 60601-2-8 for therapeutic X-ray equipment below 1 MeV
regs.e: IEC 60601-2-1 specifies particular safety requirements for electron accelerators in the range 1-50 MeV used in radiotherapy. SA adopts IEC standards through SANS (South African National Standards).

regs.q: What is the NNR's requirement regarding quality management systems for holders of a nuclear authorisation in South Africa?
regs.a: A documented QMS covering all authorised radiation activities is required
c: Only an annual quality audit report submitted to the NNR is needed
A documented QMS covering all authorised radiation activities is required
Quality management is recommended but not mandated by the NNR
Only ISO 9001 certification is accepted as proof of compliance
regs.e: NNR licence conditions require a documented quality management system that covers all aspects of authorised activities, including radiation protection, equipment maintenance, and record keeping.

regs.q: CALC: A shielding barrier requires 4.2 TVLs of concrete for adequate protection. If the TVL for 6 MV photons in concrete is 34 cm, what thickness is needed?
regs.a: 142.8 cm of concrete barrier thickness is needed
c: 142.8 cm of concrete barrier thickness is needed
136.0 cm of concrete barrier thickness is needed
170.0 cm of concrete barrier thickness is needed
120.4 cm of concrete barrier thickness is needed
regs.e: Thickness = 4.2 TVL × 34 cm/TVL = 142.8 cm. This is the required primary barrier thickness for adequate shielding of 6 MV photon beams, approximately 1.43 m of concrete.

regs.q: What must a facility do before decommissioning a radiotherapy unit containing a sealed radioactive source in South Africa?
regs.a: Submit a decommissioning plan to the NNR for approval first
c: Simply return the source to the manufacturer without notice
Submit a decommissioning plan to the NNR for approval first
Notify only the provincial Department of Health in writing
Arrange disposal through the municipal waste management unit
regs.e: Decommissioning requires prior NNR approval through a formal decommissioning plan that addresses source removal, facility decontamination, waste management, and final radiation surveys.

regs.q: Under SA regulations, how frequently must a radiation protection programme be reviewed at an authorised facility?
regs.a: At least annually or when significant changes occur at the facility
c: Only when the NNR conducts a scheduled compliance inspection
At least annually or when significant changes occur at the facility
Every five years during the nuclear authorisation renewal period
Every three years as part of the facility accreditation review cycle
regs.e: The radiation protection programme must be reviewed at least annually and whenever significant operational changes occur, ensuring ongoing compliance and effectiveness of safety measures.

regs.q: CALC: A worker receives 0.15 mSv from a fluoroscopy procedure. If the investigation level is 0.4 mSv per procedure, is investigation needed?
regs.a: No, 0.15 mSv is below the 0.4 mSv investigation threshold
c: No, 0.15 mSv is below the 0.4 mSv investigation threshold
Yes, any measurable dose requires formal NNR investigation
Yes, the investigation threshold is typically 0.1 mSv only
No, investigation levels only apply to therapeutic exposures
regs.e: At 0.15 mSv, the dose is below the 0.4 mSv investigation level. Investigation levels are facility-specific triggers that prompt review of working practices when exceeded.

regs.q: Which body accredits medical physics training programmes for clinical internship in South Africa?
regs.a: The HPCSA through the relevant professional board accredits training
c: The NNR accredits all radiation-related training institutions only
The HPCSA through the relevant professional board accredits training
The Council on Higher Education accredits all clinical programmes
SAHPRA accredits medical physics internship sites and programmes
regs.e: The HPCSA's Professional Board for Radiography and Clinical Technology accredits medical physics clinical training (internship) programmes at approved facilities in South Africa.

regs.q: CALC: The dose rate at 1 m from an unshielded source is 400 µSv/h. Using the inverse square law, what is the dose rate at 4 m?
regs.a: 25 µSv/h at the 4 metre distance point
c: 25 µSv/h at the 4 metre distance point
50 µSv/h at the 4 metre distance point
100 µSv/h at the 4 metre distance point
12.5 µSv/h at the 4 metre distance
regs.e: Dose rate at 4 m = 400 × (1/4)² = 400/16 = 25 µSv/h. The inverse square law is fundamental to radiation protection and used extensively in SA shielding calculations.

regs.q: What is the regulatory requirement for radiation warning signage at the entrance to a controlled area in South Africa?
regs.a: A trefoil symbol with appropriate text and contact information displayed
c: Only a red flashing light during beam-on is sufficient for compliance
A trefoil symbol with appropriate text and contact information displayed
A generic hazard warning sign with no specific radiation details needed
Verbal warnings from reception staff with no physical signage required
regs.e: Controlled areas must display the ionising radiation trefoil symbol with text indicating the type of hazard, access restrictions, and RSO contact details, as required by NNR conditions.

regs.q: CALC: A facility has 3 controlled areas with annual collective doses of 45, 32, and 28 person-mSv. What is the total collective dose?
regs.a: 105 person-mSv total collective dose for the facility
c: 105 person-mSv total collective dose for the facility
95 person-mSv total collective dose for the facility
115 person-mSv total collective dose for the facility
85 person-mSv total collective dose for the facility
regs.e: Total collective dose = 45 + 32 + 28 = 105 person-mSv. Collective dose is used in optimisation studies to assess the overall radiation burden on workers at the facility.

regs.q: What is the regulatory status of Group III hazardous substances (radioactive materials) under South African legislation?
regs.a: Regulated by the NNR under the National Nuclear Regulator Act
c: Regulated by SAHPRA under the Medicines Act provisions only
Regulated by the NNR under the National Nuclear Regulator Act
Regulated by the DoH Directorate of Radiation Control alone
Regulated jointly by SABS and the Department of Trade only
regs.e: Group III hazardous substances (radioactive materials) fall under NNR jurisdiction through the NNR Act. Group IV (electronic radiation sources) are regulated by the DoH.

regs.q: What must be included in the annual radiation dose report submitted to the NNR by an authorised facility?
regs.a: Individual and collective dose records for all monitored workers
c: Only the maximum individual dose recorded during that year
Individual and collective dose records for all monitored workers
Only a confirmation that no dose limits were exceeded at all
Equipment calibration certificates and maintenance log entries
regs.e: Annual dose reports must include individual dose records for all monitored workers, collective doses, dose distribution analysis, and any investigation of doses exceeding action levels.

regs.q: CALC: A lead barrier has HVL = 0.3 cm for a given photon energy. How many HVLs are needed to reduce the dose rate by a factor of 100?
regs.a: Approximately 6.64 HVLs are required for factor 100
c: Approximately 6.64 HVLs are required for factor 100
Approximately 5.00 HVLs are required for factor 100
Approximately 10.0 HVLs are required for factor 100
Approximately 3.32 HVLs are required for factor 100
regs.e: Number of HVLs = log₂(100) = ln(100)/ln(2) = 6.64. The lead thickness = 6.64 × 0.3 cm = 1.99 cm. This is a standard shielding calculation used in SA radiation protection.

regs.q: CALC: A facility's workload is 500 Gy/week at 1 m for a 6 MV linac. The primary barrier use factor is 0.25. What is the effective weekly workload for the primary barrier?
regs.a: 125 Gy/week effective workload for the primary barrier
c: 125 Gy/week effective workload for the primary barrier
250 Gy/week effective workload for the primary barrier
500 Gy/week effective workload for the primary barrier
100 Gy/week effective workload for the primary barrier
regs.e: Effective workload = W × U = 500 × 0.25 = 125 Gy/week. The use factor accounts for the fraction of beam-on time directed at the primary barrier in shielding calculations.

regs.q: According to the Hazardous Substances Act (HSA) Group IV regulations, which body is responsible for the registration of medical physicists in South Africa?
regs.a: The Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA)
c: The South African Health Products Regulatory Authority (SAHPRA)
The Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA)
The South African Society of Medical Physics (SASQART)
The Department of Health (DoH) directly
regs.e: The HPCSA registers medical physicists under the Medical Physics, Clinical Technology and Audiometry Board. SAHPRA handles radiation source licensing, while HPCSA governs professional practice and registration.

regs.q: CALC: A SA facility discovers a sealed Cs-137 source (original activity 3.7 GBq) has been missing for an unknown period. The current measured activity is 1.85 GBq. Given the half-life of Cs-137 is 30.17 years, approximately how long has the source been unaccounted for?
regs.a: Approximately 30 years
c: Approximately 15 years
Approximately 30 years
Approximately 45 years
Approximately 60 years
regs.e: Activity has halved from 3.7 GBq to 1.85 GBq, which is exactly one half-life. For Cs-137, T½ = 30.17 years, so the source has been missing for approximately 30 years. This represents a severe source security failure under SA regulations, requiring immediate SAHPRA notification.

regs.q: Under SA Department of Health requirements, which document must accompany all consignments of radioactive material transported on public roads?
regs.a: A transport document including UN number, proper shipping name, activity, and transport index
c: Only a copy of the facility's SAHPRA licence showing authorised possession limits
A transport document including UN number, proper shipping name, activity, and transport index
A letter of authorisation from the receiving facility's radiation protection officer
The source certificate of calibration and leak test results from the manufacturer
regs.e: SA transport regulations, aligned with IAEA SSR-6, require a transport document specifying the UN number, proper shipping name, radionuclide identity, activity, transport index, and emergency contact information for all radioactive consignments on public roads.

regs.q: CALC: A SA radiation worker receives occupational exposures of 8 mSv, 12 mSv, 15 mSv, 10 mSv, and 9 mSv over five consecutive years. What is the average annual dose, and does it comply with the SA regulatory dose limit?
regs.a: 10.8 mSv average; compliant with the 20 mSv/year limit averaged over 5 years
c: 10.8 mSv average; non-compliant as 15 mSv exceeds the annual limit of 10 mSv
10.8 mSv average; compliant with the 20 mSv/year limit averaged over 5 years
10.8 mSv average; non-compliant as the 5-year total exceeds 50 mSv
10.8 mSv average; compliant only if no single year exceeds 20 mSv
regs.e: SA follows ICRP recommendations: 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 consecutive years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv. The total is 54 mSv over 5 years = 10.8 mSv/year average, well within the limit. The maximum single-year dose of 15 mSv is below 50 mSv. Both conditions are met.

regs.q: Under SA regulations, who has the legal authority to justify a medical exposure to ionising radiation?
regs.a: The referring medical practitioner in consultation with the radiological practitioner
c: The radiation protection officer at the licensed facility
The referring medical practitioner in consultation with the radiological practitioner
The medical physicist responsible for treatment planning
Any HPCSA-registered health professional working at the facility
regs.e: Justification of medical exposure is a clinical responsibility shared between the referring practitioner (who identifies clinical need) and the radiological medical practitioner (who confirms the exposure is appropriate). This aligns with ICRP 103 principles adopted in SA legislation.

regs.q: What is the scope of practice of a medical physicist registered with the HPCSA in the category of therapeutic medical physics?
regs.a: Calibration, treatment planning, quality assurance, and radiation safety in radiotherapy
c: Only performing radiation output measurements and annual calibrations of treatment units
Calibration, treatment planning, quality assurance, and radiation safety in radiotherapy
Prescribing radiation treatments and approving treatment plans for clinical delivery
All physics services across radiotherapy, nuclear medicine, and diagnostic radiology
regs.e: The HPCSA defines the therapeutic medical physicist scope to include beam calibration, treatment planning verification, QA programme management, and radiation safety oversight in radiotherapy. Prescription authority rests with the radiation oncologist, and work in other disciplines requires additional registration.

regs.q: According to SA regulations, what is the required frequency for wipe tests on sealed radioactive sources used in brachytherapy?
regs.a: Every 6 months or as specified in the licence conditions
c: Monthly for all sealed sources regardless of activity
Every 6 months or as specified in the licence conditions
Annually at the time of source calibration verification
Only when physical damage to the source is suspected
regs.e: SA regulations require leak testing (wipe tests) of sealed sources at intervals not exceeding 6 months, unless the licence specifies otherwise. Results must be recorded and retained. Sources showing leakage above 185 Bq must be withdrawn from clinical use immediately.

regs.q: Under SA radioactive waste regulations, what is the required procedure for disposal of short-lived radioactive waste (T½ < 100 days) from a nuclear medicine department?
regs.a: Store for decay until activity is indistinguishable from background, then dispose as conventional waste
c: Immediately transfer to a SAHPRA-approved centralised radioactive waste facility
Store for decay until activity is indistinguishable from background, then dispose as conventional waste
Incinerate at an approved medical waste facility with stack monitoring in place
Dilute with large volumes of water and discharge into the municipal sewerage system
regs.e: For short-lived radionuclides (T½ < 100 days), SA regulations permit decay-in-storage. Waste is held in a designated, labelled area until residual activity is indistinguishable from background (typically 10 half-lives). It is then delabelled, surveyed, and disposed of as conventional medical waste with documented records.

regs.q: Under SA regulations, what quality management system requirement must a radiotherapy facility demonstrate for SAHPRA licence compliance?
regs.a: A documented QMS covering equipment commissioning, calibration protocols, and incident management
c: ISO 9001 certification from an accredited third-party auditor renewed annually
A documented QMS covering equipment commissioning, calibration protocols, and incident management
Participation in the SASQART external audit programme as the sole QMS requirement
Written standard operating procedures signed by the facility radiation protection officer only
regs.e: SAHPRA requires radiotherapy facilities to maintain a documented quality management system that encompasses equipment commissioning, calibration protocols, treatment planning verification, incident reporting and management, and staff training records. While SASQART audits complement the QMS, they do not replace the facility's own documentation requirements.

regs.q: According to SA dose reference levels (DRLs), what is the recommended diagnostic reference level for an adult chest PA radiograph?
regs.a: 0.3 mGy entrance surface dose
c: 0.1 mGy entrance surface dose
0.3 mGy entrance surface dose
1.0 mGy entrance surface dose
3.0 mGy entrance surface dose
regs.e: South African DRLs, aligned with international recommendations, set the reference level for an adult chest PA at approximately 0.3 mGy entrance surface dose. Facilities consistently exceeding this value must review and optimise their techniques. DRLs are not dose limits but optimisation tools.

regs.q: Under SAHPRA regulations, what classification system is used for radiation sources based on their potential to cause harm?
regs.a: The IAEA five-category system from Category 1 (most dangerous) to Category 5 (least dangerous)
c: A binary classification of high-activity sealed sources and low-activity sealed sources only
The IAEA five-category system from Category 1 (most dangerous) to Category 5 (least dangerous)
A three-tier system of industrial, medical, and research grade sources
Classification solely by radionuclide half-life: short-lived, medium-lived, and long-lived
regs.e: SA adopts the IAEA source categorisation system (TECDOC-1344/RS-G-1.9) which classifies sources into 5 categories based on the D-value ratio. Category 1 sources (e.g., teletherapy Co-60) pose the greatest risk and require the most stringent security measures including 24-hour surveillance.

regs.q: Under SA optimisation requirements, what principle must be applied when establishing radiation protection measures for medical exposures in radiotherapy?
regs.a: Doses to normal tissue must be kept as low as reasonably achievable consistent with delivering the prescribed tumour dose
c: The total treatment dose must not exceed internationally published dose limits for any organ at risk
Doses to normal tissue must be kept as low as reasonably achievable consistent with delivering the prescribed tumour dose
All treatment plans must use the minimum number of beams that achieves a homogeneity index below 1.05
Optimisation is satisfied when the plan meets the radiation oncologist's prescription with no further review
regs.e: For medical exposures in radiotherapy, SA regulations follow ICRP guidance: optimisation means keeping doses to non-target tissues as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA) while delivering the therapeutically necessary dose to the target. Dose limits do not apply to the patient's therapeutic exposure, but normal tissue protection remains a regulatory requirement.

regs.q: Which body is the national nuclear regulator in South Africa?
regs.a: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR)
c: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR)
SAHPRA under the DoH framework
The Department of Mineral Resources
The Council for Scientific Research
regs.e: The NNR was established under the National Nuclear Regulator Act (No. 47 of 1999) to regulate nuclear and radiation safety.

regs.q: SAHPRA replaced which previous regulatory authority in South Africa?
regs.a: The Medicines Control Council (MCC)
c: The Radiation Control Directorate
The Medicines Control Council (MCC)
The National Nuclear Regulator board
The Health Professions Council board
regs.e: SAHPRA (South African Health Products Regulatory Authority) replaced the MCC in 2018 under the Medicines and Related Substances Amendment Act.

regs.q: The HPCSA Professional Board relevant to medical physicists is the:
regs.a: Radiography and Clinical Technology Board
c: Medical and Dental Professions Board
Radiography and Clinical Technology Board
Environmental Health Practitioners Board
Pharmacy and Related Professions Board
regs.e: Medical physicists in South Africa register under the Radiography and Clinical Technology Professional Board of the HPCSA.

regs.q: What is the annual occupational effective dose limit in South African regulations?
regs.a: 20 mSv per year averaged over 5 years
c: 50 mSv in any single calendar year
20 mSv per year averaged over 5 years
10 mSv per year with no exceptions
100 mSv cumulative over a career span
regs.e: SA follows ICRP recommendations: 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 consecutive years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv.

regs.q: SASQART stands for:
regs.a: South African Society of Quality Assurance in Radiation Therapy
c: South African Standards for Quality Assessment of Radiation Therapy
South African Society of Quality Assurance in Radiation Therapy
South African Society of Quantitative Analysis in Radiotherapy
South African Standard for Quality Auditing of RT Equipment
regs.e: SASQART is a professional body that promotes quality assurance standards in radiation therapy across South Africa.

regs.q: The Hazardous Substances Act (Act 15 of 1973) in SA regulates:
regs.a: Radiation-emitting devices and radioactive materials
c: Only pharmaceutical products and medicines
Radiation-emitting devices and radioactive materials
Mining operations and mineral extraction
Environmental pollution from chemical waste
regs.e: The Hazardous Substances Act classifies radioactive materials and radiation-generating equipment as Group III and IV hazardous substances.

regs.q: The public annual effective dose limit under SA regulations is:
regs.a: 1 mSv per year
c: 0.1 mSv per year
1 mSv per year
5 mSv per year
20 mSv per year
regs.e: Consistent with ICRP 103 recommendations, the SA public dose limit is 1 mSv/year effective dose from planned exposure situations.

regs.q: Under SA law, who is the legally responsible person for radiation safety at a facility?
regs.a: The licence holder or responsible person named on the licence
c: The chief medical physicist at the facility
The licence holder or responsible person named on the licence
The radiation safety officer on duty that day
The head of the oncology department only
regs.e: The licence holder bears primary legal responsibility for ensuring compliance with all conditions of the radiation use licence.

regs.q: The NNR regulates which type of radiation sources in South Africa?
regs.a: Nuclear installations and certain sealed sources
c: All medical X-ray equipment nationally
Nuclear installations and certain sealed sources
Only uranium mining and enrichment sites
Linear accelerators and CT scanner units
regs.e: The NNR covers nuclear installations, radioactive waste, and certain high-activity sealed sources. Non-nuclear devices fall under the DoH.

regs.q: CALC: A radiation worker receives quarterly doses of 3, 5, 4, and 6 mSv. Does this comply with the SA annual limit?
regs.a: Yes, total 18 mSv is below the 20 mSv annual limit
c: No, 6 mSv in one quarter exceeds the quarterly cap
Yes, total 18 mSv is below the 20 mSv annual limit
No, average quarterly dose exceeds 4 mSv per quarter
Yes, but only if averaged over a five-year cycle
regs.e: Total = 3+5+4+6 = 18 mSv, which is below 20 mSv. No single quarter limit exists under SA regulations; the annual limit applies.

regs.q: What category of registration does a clinical medical physicist hold with the HPCSA?
regs.a: Clinical Technologist (Medical Physics)
c: Radiographer (Therapy specialisation)
Clinical Technologist (Medical Physics)
Medical Practitioner (Radiation Sciences)
Health Associate (Radiation Protection)
regs.e: In SA, medical physicists register as Clinical Technologists under the Radiography and Clinical Technology Board of the HPCSA.

regs.q: The dose limit for the lens of the eye for occupational exposure is:
regs.a: 20 mSv per year averaged over 5 years
c: 50 mSv in any single calendar year
20 mSv per year averaged over 5 years
150 mSv per year with no averaging
100 mSv cumulative over ten years total
regs.e: Following ICRP 118 recommendations adopted in SA, the eye lens limit is 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years, with no year exceeding 50 mSv.

regs.q: Which Act establishes the HPCSA in South Africa?
regs.a: Health Professions Act (Act 56 of 1974)
c: National Health Act (Act 61 of 2003)
Health Professions Act (Act 56 of 1974)
Hazardous Substances Act (Act 15 of 1973)
Nuclear Energy Act (Act 46 of 1999)
regs.e: The Health Professions Act established the HPCSA to regulate health professions and protect the public.

regs.q: A pregnant radiation worker in SA has a foetal dose limit of:
regs.a: 1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy
c: 1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy
5 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy
2 mSv per trimester of the pregnancy
No specific limit beyond standard limits
regs.e: Once pregnancy is declared, the embryo/foetus dose must not exceed 1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy, consistent with ICRP recommendations.

regs.q: Under SA regulations, how often must personal dosimeters be read for classified radiation workers?
regs.a: At least every two months
c: Weekly without any exceptions
Monthly as a strict requirement
At least every two months
Quarterly at minimum intervals
regs.e: SA regulations require dosimeter monitoring at intervals not exceeding two months for classified workers to ensure timely dose tracking.

regs.q: What is the annual effective dose limit for occupational radiation workers recommended by ICRP Publication 103?
regs.a: 20 mSv averaged over 5 years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv
c: 50 mSv per year with no multi-year averaging period restriction applied
20 mSv averaged over 5 years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv
10 mSv per year as a strict annual limit for all radiation workers
100 mSv over 5 years with no restriction on any individual annual dose
regs.e: ICRP 103 recommends 20 mSv/year averaged over defined 5-year periods, with the additional constraint that no single year should exceed 50 mSv.

regs.q: According to ICRP recommendations, what is the annual dose limit to the lens of the eye for occupational exposure?
regs.a: 20 mSv averaged over defined periods of 5 years, with no year exceeding 50 mSv
c: 15 mSv per year as an absolute annual limit for occupational exposure
50 mSv per year without any multi-year averaging period applied
20 mSv averaged over defined periods of 5 years, with no year exceeding 50 mSv
150 mSv per year consistent with previous ICRP 60 eye lens recommendations
regs.e: ICRP revised the eye lens dose limit downward based on epidemiological evidence of cataracts at lower doses, recommending 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years with a 50 mSv single-year cap.

regs.q: What is the annual effective dose limit for members of the public from regulated radiation practices according to ICRP 103?
regs.a: 1 mSv per year under normal planned exposure situations
c: 0.1 mSv per year under all regulated exposure situations
1 mSv per year under normal planned exposure situations
5 mSv per year averaged over any consecutive five-year period
10 mSv per year for members of the public living near facilities
regs.e: ICRP 103 maintains the public dose limit at 1 mSv/year effective dose from all regulated practices, unchanged from ICRP 60.

regs.q: Under IAEA Transport Regulations, what is the definition of a Type A package for radioactive material transport?
regs.a: A package designed to withstand normal transport conditions and limited to A₁ or A₂ activity values
c: A package requiring competent authority approval and engineered to survive severe accident conditions
A package designed to withstand normal transport conditions and limited to A₁ or A₂ activity values
A package used exclusively for low specific activity material with minimal containment features
A package that must maintain shielding integrity during a 9-meter drop test onto unyielding surface
regs.e: Type A packages must withstand normal transport conditions (minor drops, vibration, temperature changes) and are limited to A₁ (special form) or A₂ (non-special form) activity quantities.

regs.q: Which ICRP publication first introduced the current system of radiological protection based on justification, optimization, and dose limitation?
regs.a: ICRP Publication 60 established the modern three-principle framework in 1990
c: ICRP Publication 26 introduced the complete three-principle system in 1977
ICRP Publication 60 established the modern three-principle framework in 1990
ICRP Publication 103 first defined justification and optimization principles in 2007
ICRP Publication 30 created the system of protection for workers only in 1979
regs.e: ICRP 60 (1990) formalized the three fundamental principles of radiological protection: justification of practices, optimization of protection (ALARA), and individual dose limitation.

regs.q: According to ICRP 103, what radiation weighting factor (wR) is assigned to protons with energy greater than 2 MeV?
regs.a: A radiation weighting factor of 2 for protons above 2 MeV energy
c: A radiation weighting factor of 1 for protons above 2 MeV energy
A radiation weighting factor of 2 for protons above 2 MeV energy
A radiation weighting factor of 5 for protons above 2 MeV energy
A radiation weighting factor of 10 for protons above 2 MeV energy
regs.e: ICRP 103 assigns wR = 2 for protons above 2 MeV, reflecting their intermediate biological effectiveness between photons and alpha particles.

regs.q: What tissue weighting factor (wT) does ICRP 103 assign to the gonads?
regs.a: wT = 0.08, reduced from the previous value of 0.20 in ICRP 60
c: wT = 0.20, unchanged from the value recommended in ICRP Publication 60
wT = 0.12, equal to the weighting factor for the breast and bone marrow
wT = 0.08, reduced from the previous value of 0.20 in ICRP 60
wT = 0.05, equivalent to the value assigned to the liver and esophagus
regs.e: ICRP 103 reduced the gonadal weighting factor from 0.20 to 0.08, based on updated epidemiological data showing lower hereditary risk than previously estimated.

regs.q: What is the dose constraint concept introduced in ICRP 103, and how does it relate to dose limits?
regs.a: A prospective source-related restriction set below the dose limit to ensure optimization of protection
c: A mandatory maximum dose that replaces dose limits for all planned exposure situations
A retrospective assessment of whether individual dose limits were exceeded in practice
A prospective source-related restriction set below the dose limit to ensure optimization of protection
An emergency dose threshold above which protective actions become mandatory for workers
regs.e: Dose constraints are prospective, source-related values set below dose limits. They serve as upper bounds on the optimization process for a single source, ensuring that the sum of doses from multiple sources remains below limits.

regs.q: Under IAEA GSR Part 3 (BSS), which exposure situation category applies to existing radon in workplaces?
regs.a: Existing exposure situation, requiring reference levels for optimization of protection
c: Planned exposure situation with occupational dose limits applying to all radon-exposed workers
Existing exposure situation, requiring reference levels for optimization of protection
Emergency exposure situation with immediate intervention levels for evacuation decisions
Medical exposure situation with diagnostic reference levels for workplace radon monitoring
