regs.e: Workplace radon is categorized as an existing exposure situation under the BSS, managed through reference levels rather than dose limits, with optimization to reduce exposures.

regs.q: What is the ICRP 103 recommended annual equivalent dose limit for the skin of occupational workers?
regs.a: 500 mSv averaged over any 1 cm² area of skin per year
c: 50 mSv averaged over any 1 cm² area of skin per year
150 mSv averaged over any 1 cm² area of skin per year
500 mSv averaged over any 1 cm² area of skin per year
1000 mSv averaged over any 1 cm² area of skin per year
regs.e: ICRP 103 maintains the occupational skin dose limit at 500 mSv/year, averaged over 1 cm² at the point of maximum dose, reflecting the relatively high radiation tolerance of skin.

regs.q: Which regulatory document provides the international requirements for radiation protection in medical uses of ionizing radiation?
regs.a: IAEA Safety Standards Series GSR Part 3, the International Basic Safety Standards
c: ICRP Publication 103, the Recommendations of the International Commission on Radiological Protection
IAEA Safety Standards Series GSR Part 3, the International Basic Safety Standards
UNSCEAR Report on Sources and Effects of Ionizing Radiation for medical exposures
WHO Guidelines on Radiation Safety for medical applications of ionizing radiation
regs.e: IAEA GSR Part 3 (BSS) provides binding international requirements for radiation protection across all applications, including comprehensive requirements for medical uses of ionizing radiation.

regs.q: According to ICRP 103, what radiation weighting factor is used for alpha particles and heavy ions?
regs.a: A radiation weighting factor of 20 for alpha particles and heavy ions
c: A radiation weighting factor of 5 for alpha particles and heavy ions
A radiation weighting factor of 10 for alpha particles and heavy ions
A radiation weighting factor of 20 for alpha particles and heavy ions
A radiation weighting factor of 50 for alpha particles and heavy ions
regs.e: ICRP 103 assigns wR = 20 to alpha particles and heavy ions, reflecting their high linear energy transfer and greater biological effectiveness per unit absorbed dose.

regs.q: What is the concept of 'diagnostic reference levels' (DRLs) as defined in ICRP and IAEA guidance?
regs.a: Investigation levels for typical patient doses that should trigger review when consistently exceeded
c: Maximum permissible dose limits for individual patients undergoing diagnostic imaging procedures
Investigation levels for typical patient doses that should trigger review when consistently exceeded
Mandatory dose constraints that facilities must not exceed for any single diagnostic examination
Target dose values that represent the minimum dose required for adequate diagnostic image quality
regs.e: DRLs are not dose limits but investigation levels set at the 75th percentile of observed dose distributions. Consistent exceedance indicates a need to review and optimize imaging protocols.

regs.q: Under international transport regulations, what is the maximum permissible radiation level at the surface of a Type A package?
regs.a: 2 mSv/h at the surface of the package under exclusive use conditions
c: 0.1 mSv/h at the surface of the package under all transport conditions
0.5 mSv/h at the surface of the package under normal transport conditions
2 mSv/h at the surface of the package under exclusive use conditions
10 mSv/h at the surface of the package under exclusive use conditions
regs.e: Under exclusive use conditions, the surface dose rate limit for packages is 2 mSv/h. Under normal (non-exclusive) conditions, the limit is 2 mSv/h at the surface but with a transport index limitation.

regs.q: What does ICRP 103 recommend regarding the dose limit for the embryo/fetus of a pregnant radiation worker?
regs.a: 1 mSv to the embryo/fetus for the remainder of pregnancy after declaration
c: No additional restriction beyond the standard occupational dose limit of 20 mSv per year
1 mSv to the embryo/fetus for the remainder of pregnancy after declaration
5 mSv to the embryo/fetus for the entire gestational period after declaration
0.5 mSv per month to the embryo/fetus from the date of pregnancy declaration
regs.e: ICRP 103 recommends limiting the dose to the embryo/fetus to 1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy after declaration, providing protection comparable to a member of the public.

regs.q: Which principle of radiological protection requires that the likelihood and magnitude of exposures be kept as low as reasonably achievable?
regs.a: The optimization principle, commonly known as ALARA in radiation protection practice
c: The justification principle applied to all practices involving ionizing radiation
The optimization principle, commonly known as ALARA in radiation protection practice
The dose limitation principle that sets maximum permitted individual dose levels
The precautionary principle applied when scientific uncertainty exists about effects
regs.e: Optimization (ALARA) requires that protection measures be optimized to keep doses as low as reasonably achievable, considering economic and societal factors.

regs.q: Under IAEA BSS, what action is required when a medical radiation incident results in a dose significantly different from that intended?
regs.a: Investigation, notification to the regulatory authority, and implementation of corrective actions
c: Immediate facility shutdown until the regulatory authority completes an independent investigation
Investigation, notification to the regulatory authority, and implementation of corrective actions
Only internal documentation of the event without external notification requirements needed
Automatic suspension of the responsible medical physicist license pending full review
regs.e: The BSS requires prompt investigation of significant medical radiation incidents, notification to the regulatory authority, informing the patient, and implementing corrective actions to prevent recurrence.

integrated.q: CT sim scanner HU for water reads +12 (tolerance ±5 HU). Physicist ignores it. How does this cascade through the entire RT chain?
integrated.a: Wrong electron density
c: Only affects image quality, not treatment
Wrong electron density
Only affects kV imaging, not MV treatment
Auto-corrected by TPS
integrated.e: wrong dose calc → wrong MU → wrong BED to patient. [DX+DOS+TPS+RB+QA] HU error → HU-to-ρₑ calibration curve shifts → TPS assigns wrong electron densities → photon attenuation miscalculated → dose distribution wrong → MU wrong → actual BED differs from planned. Entire chain corrupted.

integrated.q: CALC: Lung SBRT 54 Gy/3 fx planned with AcurosXB on 6FFF beam. PSQA in solid water shows -4.2% at isocentre. Physicist re-measures in heterogeneous thorax phantom: -1.8%. What happened and is the plan deliverable?
integrated.a: Heterogeneity effect: solid water can't replicate lung. -1.8% is within 5% E2E tolerance — pass
c: Plan fails — -4.2% is real, verified through standard quality assurance protocols
Heterogeneity effect: solid water can't replicate lung. -1.8% is within 5% E2E tolerance — pass
Both measurements fail — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Use the -4.2% as the true error, based on established clinical physics practice
integrated.e: [SRT+DOS+EQ+TPS+RB] AcurosXB accurately models lung heterogeneity. Solid water PSQA measures fluence delivery, not heterogeneity accuracy. The -4.2% reflects the expected difference between homogeneous and heterogeneous media. Thorax phantom -1.8% confirms delivery is correct.

integrated.q: CALC: PET/CT scan: 370 MBq F-18 (internal dose ~7 mSv) + CT at DLP=600 mGy·cm (k=0.015). Patient is 28-year-old woman. Total effective dose and cancer risk estimate (LNT, 5%/Sv)?
integrated.a: ~16 mSv total; excess risk ≈ 0.08% (1 in 1250)
c: ~7 mSv; negligible risk — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
~9 mSv; risk 0.045% — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
~16 mSv total; excess risk ≈ 0.08% (1 in 1250)
~25 mSv; risk 0.5% — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
integrated.e: [NM+DX+RP+RB+REG] CT: 600×0.015=9 mSv. PET: 7 mSv. Total=16 mSv. LNT risk: 0.016×0.05=0.0008=0.08%. Young female has higher radiosensitivity — actual risk may be 2-3× higher. Justification principle (REG) is critical.

integrated.q: CALC: SRS commissioning: Winston-Lutz = 0.7 mm, E2E dose = 3.8%, microDiamond OF for 5 mm cone = 0.54 (TRS-483 k applied). TPS uses these OFs. 18 Gy prescribed to 80% isodose. Max dose in plan?
c: 18 Gy for optimal treatment outcomes
20 Gy throughout the treatment course
30 Gy in clinical practice settings
integrated.e: [DOS+EQ+SRT+QA+TPS] WL 0.7mm ≤1mm ✓. E2E 3.8% ≤5% ✓. OFs measured per TRS-483 ✓. Max dose = prescribed/isodose = 18/0.80 = 22.5 Gy. This is the hotspot inside the target — standard for SRS.

integrated.q: CALC: Brain SRS: 21 Gy/1 fx. Brainstem Dmax=10 Gy. Cochlea Dmean=4 Gy. Using α/β=2 for both late OARs, which is the higher-risk OAR by EQD2?
integrated.a: Brainstem: EQD2=30 Gy vs Cochlea: EQD2=8 Gy
c: Cochlea is higher risk
Both equal — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Brainstem: EQD2=30 Gy vs Cochlea: EQD2=8 Gy
Neither is concerning — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
integrated.e: brainstem is limiting. [RB+SRT+DOS+TPS+EQ] Brainstem: EQD2=10×(10+2)/(2+2)=10×3=30 Gy₂. Cochlea: EQD2=4×(4+2)/(2+2)=4×1.5=6 Gy₂. Brainstem at 30 Gy₂ vs tolerance ~54 Gy₂ — safe but limiting. Cochlea well under tolerance (~45 Gy₂).

integrated.q: PET/CT for RT planning: CT acquired at 120 kVp with contrast. PET attenuation correction uses CT μ-map. Contrast agent causes false high HU. Three downstream errors?
integrated.a: Overestimated PET uptake + wrong electron density for dose calc + incorrect GTV delineation
c: Only affects PET image — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Only affects dose calculation — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Overestimated PET uptake + wrong electron density for dose calc + incorrect GTV delineation
No effect if contrast washes out — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
integrated.e: [DX+NM+TPS+DOS+EQ] (1) PET AC: high μ→over-correction→false hot spot. (2) HU-ρₑ: contrast elevates HU→wrong electron density→dose calc error. (3) GTV: oncologist may over-contour based on artefactual uptake. Triple error.

integrated.q: CALC: Lu-177 PSMA: 7.4 GBq, T_phys=6.7d. Tumour T_bio=4d, kidney T_bio=2d. (a) Effective T½ for each organ? (b) After 14 days, which retains more activity? (c) Is patient safe for public contact at 14 days?
integrated.a: Tumour T_eff=2.5d, kidney T_eff=1.5d
c: Both organs identical at 14 days
Tumour T_eff=2.5d, kidney T_eff=1.5d
Kidney retains more activity
Not safe — need 30 days
integrated.e: tumour retains ~3.2%, kidney ~0.2%; likely safe (low Γ for Lu-177). [NM+RB+DOS+RP+REG] Tumour: 1/T_eff=1/6.7+1/4=0.399→T_eff=2.51d. 14d=5.6 T½→(½)^5.6=2.1%. Kidney: 1/T_eff=1/6.7+1/2=0.649→T_eff=1.54d. 14d=9.1 T½→(½)^9.1=0.18%. Lu-177 Γ is low (~0.01 mSv·m²/(GBq·hr)), so dose rate at 1m negligible.

integrated.q: CALC: Prostate SBRT: 36.25 Gy/5 fx. Tumour α/β=1.5, rectum α/β=3, urethra α/β=5. Rectum gets 29 Gy/5, urethra gets 38 Gy/5. Which OAR has higher EQD2 relative to its own tolerance?
integrated.a: Urethra EQD2=68.4 Gy (tol ~70) is closer to limit than rectum EQD2=51 Gy (tol ~75)
c: Rectum is dose-limiting — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Both exceed tolerance — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Urethra EQD2=68.4 Gy (tol ~70) is closer to limit than rectum EQD2=51 Gy (tol ~75)
Cannot compare different OARs, based on established clinical physics practice

integrated.q: CALC: Fluoro-guided cardiac procedure: DAP=150 Gy·cm², fluoro time=45 min, entrance dose rate=40 mGy/min. Estimated peak skin dose? Deterministic threshold for erythema?
integrated.a: ~1800 mGy — approaches 2 Gy erythema threshold
c: ~150 mGy — no concern
~675 mGy — well below threshold
~1800 mGy — approaches 2 Gy erythema threshold
~5000 mGy — severe burn expected
integrated.e: document and follow up. [DX+RP+REG+DOS+QA] PSD ≈ rate×time = 40×45 = 1800 mGy. Erythema threshold ~2000 mGy. At 1800 mGy: close to threshold. SA regs & ICRP require documentation of high-dose fluoro procedures and patient follow-up for skin effects.

integrated.q: 10 MV FFF beam commissioned for SBRT. k_Q from TRS-398, OFs from TRS-483 with microDiamond, MLC from HD120. Neutron contamination: concern?
integrated.a: Yes — 10 MV is at photonuclear threshold
c: No — FFF means no neutrons
No — only above 15 MV
Yes — 10 MV is at photonuclear threshold
Only with flattening filter
integrated.e: must assess neutron dose for patient and shielding. [EQ+DOS+SRT+TPS+QA+RP] Photonuclear threshold ~8-10 MV. 10 MV FFF produces some neutrons (less than 10 MV FF due to no filter). Patient out-of-field dose includes neutron component. Vault shielding must account for neutrons. TRS-398 k_Q includes neutron effect on chamber.

integrated.q: Treatment machine output drifts +5% over 6 months (undetected between monthly checks). 50 patients treated in this period, average 60 Gy/30 fx. Estimated collective overdose and regulatory requirement?
integrated.a: Average patient received ~63 Gy
c: No action — within annual tolerance
Average patient received ~63 Gy
Only last patient affected
Only report if patient complains
integrated.e: ~50 patients affected; reportable incident to SAHPRA. [DOS+RB+RP+TPS+QA+REG] Linear drift: average ~+2.5% over period → most patients ~61.5 Gy, later patients ~63 Gy. BED₃ for late effects increases ~8-12%. Hazardous Substances Act requires SAHPRA notification. HPCSA requires investigation. Patients may need clinical follow-up.

integrated.q: Dose calibrator linearity test fails at high activities (reads 8% low above 4 GBq). Impacts on: (a) I-131 therapy dosing, (b) patient radiation protection, (c) staff exposure?
integrated.a: Patients receive 8% more activity
c: Only affects imaging quality
Patients receive 8% more activity
Only affects low-activity measurements
No clinical impact — 8% is acceptable
integrated.e: higher tumour dose AND higher radiation protection burden. [NM+EQ+QA+RP+DOS] Reads low → dispense more to reach 'target' → actual A is 8% higher → absorbed dose to thyroid 8% higher (MIRD: D∝Ã) → initial dose rate to staff/public 8% higher → discharge criteria delayed → room shielding margin reduced.

integrated.q: CALC: Liver SBRT: 50 Gy/5 fx (10 MV FFF). Mean liver dose = 18 Gy/5 fx. Liver α/β=2. 700 mL of 1500 mL liver in field. (a) BED to functional liver, (b) is this safe?
integrated.a: BED₂=50.4 Gy₂ to 47% of liver
c: Always safe regardless of volume
BED₂=50.4 Gy₂ to 47% of liver
Liver cannot tolerate any SBRT
Only safe with 3 fractions
integrated.e: safe if >700 mL liver spared to <15 Gy mean. [SRT+RB+DOS+EQ+RP] d=3.6. BED₂=18×(1+3.6/2)=18×2.8=50.4 Gy₂. Liver is parallel organ: mean dose + volume matter. AAPM TG-101: ≥700 mL must receive <15 Gy (in 5 fx). 800 mL spared → marginal but acceptable.

integrated.q: New PET/CT installed. Physicist must commission both PET and CT components. Name one QC test that validates each: (a) CT dosimetry, (b) PET quantitation, (c) PET/CT co-registration.
integrated.a: CTDIvol measurement, SUV accuracy with calibrated phantom, fusion alignment with known fiducials
c: Only need a water phantom — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
CTDIvol measurement, SUV accuracy with calibrated phantom, fusion alignment with known fiducials
Uniformity flood is sufficient for all, based on established clinical physics practice
Manufacturer acceptance test is enough, based on established clinical physics practice
integrated.e: [DX+NM+EQ+QA+DOS] (a) CTDIvol in 32/16 cm phantoms per AAPM. (b) Fill phantom with known F-18 concentration, verify scanner SUV reads within 10%. (c) CT/PET alignment with rod sources or dedicated phantom — spatial offset must be <2 mm.

integrated.q: CALC: Patient receives lung SBRT (54 Gy/3 fx, BED₁₀=151 Gy) then 4 weeks later a PET/CT (16 mSv). Then contralateral lung SBRT (48 Gy/4 fx). Combined tumour BED₁₀ of second course?
integrated.a: 105.6 Gy₁₀ (independent course
c: 256.6 Gy₁₀ — add both courses
105.6 Gy₁₀ (independent course
Cannot calculate
151 Gy₁₀ — same as first course
integrated.e: BEDs don't add for separate targets). [RB+DOS+SRT+NM+RP] Second course: d=12. BED₁₀=4×12×(1+12/10)=48×2.2=105.6 Gy₁₀. BEDs add only for SAME structure. Different targets = independent. BUT overlap regions (e.g., spinal cord) must sum EQD2 across both courses.

integrated.q: CALC: VMAT SRS plan: 3 arcs, 1800 total MU, 10 FFF (2400 MU/min). Delivery time estimate? If intra-fraction motion >1 mm detected by SGRT at 4 minutes, clinical decision?
integrated.a: ~45 s beam-on + gantry motion ≈ 3-4 min total
c: ~30 min — standard linac treatment
~45 s beam-on + gantry motion ≈ 3-4 min total
Ignore motion — margins cover it
Abort treatment completely
integrated.e: pause, re-verify with CBCT, consider replanning if persistent. [DOS+EQ+QA+TPS+SRT+RB] Beam-on: 1800/2400=0.75 min=45s across 3 arcs. Gantry travel ~1 min/arc. Total ~3-4 min. At 4 min: likely between arcs or post-delivery. >1mm motion in SRS (0-2mm margin) is significant. SGRT flags it → pause → CBCT → clinical judgement on reposition vs replan.

integrated.q: CALC: Y-90 SIRT: 2 GBq microspheres to liver. Pure beta emitter (no gamma). (a) External dose hazard? (b) Bremsstrahlung dose rate? (c) Main radiation protection concern?
integrated.a: Negligible external gamma
c: High gamma dose — isolate patient for days
Negligible external gamma
No radiation hazard at all
Same precautions as I-131
integrated.e: bremsstrahlung in tissue ~0.001 mSv/hr at 1m; contamination during preparation is main risk. [NM+RB+RP+DOS+REG] Y-90: pure β⁻ (Emax=2.28 MeV), no primary γ. Bremsstrahlung yield in tissue is very low. External dose rate negligible → patient can go home same day. Main risk: contamination during preparation/administration (spill of microspheres). Internal dose if ingested would be serious (β inside body).

integrated.q: RT planning CT acquired at 120 kVp, 3 mm slices. Ion chamber calibration done at 6 MV. TPS uses HU-ρₑ curve. If a different CT scanner is used for re-simulation, what must the physicist verify?
integrated.a: HU-ρₑ calibration curve specific to the new scanner, plus geometric accuracy and couch indexing
c: Nothing — all CT scanners are the same, based on established clinical physics practice
Only check slice thickness — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
HU-ρₑ calibration curve specific to the new scanner, plus geometric accuracy and couch indexing
Only verify kVp — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
integrated.e: [DOS+DX+QA+TPS+EQ] Each CT scanner has unique HU response (tube, filtration, detector, reconstruction). HU-ρₑ curve must be scanner-specific. Geometric accuracy (pixel size, couch sag, laser alignment) must match RT standards. Couch index must be flat-top and compatible with treatment couch.

integrated.q: New RT department commissioning checklist: which 5 activities span dosimetry, equipment, QA, TPS, radiation protection, and regulatory compliance?
integrated.a: TRS-398 calibration, beam data collection, TPS commissioning, shielding survey, SAHPRA license verification
c: Only need TRS-398 and shielding — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
TRS-398 calibration, beam data collection, TPS commissioning, shielding survey, SAHPRA license verification
Only manufacturer acceptance test needed — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Only need SAHPRA approval — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
integrated.e: [ALL] (1) DOS: TRS-398 absolute calibration. (2) EQ: Full beam data (PDDs, profiles, OFs). (3) TPS: Commission algorithm against measured data. (4) RP: Post-construction shielding survey per NCRP 151. (5) REG: SAHPRA license, RPO appointment, HPCSA registration of staff.

integrated.q: CALC: CT QA: measured CTDIvol = 22 mGy for abdomen protocol (DRL = 15 mGy). Scanner is 5 years old. What should the physicist do?
integrated.a: Exceeds DRL by 47%
c: Acceptable — DRL is only a guide
Exceeds DRL by 47%
Replace the scanner
Report to police
integrated.e: optimise protocol (increase pitch, reduce mAs, consider iterative recon), then remeasure. [DX+DOS+EQ+QA+RP] DRL exceeded → investigate. Not a hard limit but requires action: (1) check protocol parameters vs manufacturer recommendation, (2) verify calibration, (3) optimise: increase pitch (↓dose), reduce mAs (↓dose∝mAs), enable iterative recon (allow lower mAs). (4) Remeasure. (5) Notify referring clinicians of optimised protocols.

integrated.q: CALC: Lung SBRT: AcurosXB calculates PTV D95=48 Gy/4 fx. AAA recalculated: D95=52 Gy. Actual delivered dose is closer to which value, and why?
integrated.a: AcurosXB (48 Gy)
c: AAA (52 Gy) — simpler is better
Average of both (50 Gy)
AcurosXB (48 Gy)
Neither — need Monte Carlo
integrated.e: it solves transport equation accurately in lung; AAA overestimates in low density. [SRT+DOS+RB+EQ+QA+TPS] In lung (ρ~0.25 g/cm³), loss of lateral electronic equilibrium means less dose deposited than predicted by AAA (which uses convolution/superposition with limitations). AcurosXB solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation → accurate. 4 Gy difference = 8% → significant BED impact.

integrated.q: Nuclear medicine department incident: Tc-99m MAA vial (4 GBq) dropped and shattered on floor. Staff present. Immediate actions in order?
integrated.a: Evacuate area → contain spill (absorbent pads) → survey/decontaminate → wipe test → calculate doses → report to RPO and SAHPRA
c: Clean up immediately without PPE, following current regulatory and professional standards
Evacuate area → contain spill (absorbent pads) → survey/decontaminate → wipe test → calculate doses → report to RPO and SAHPRA
Only report if dose exceeds 1 mSv — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Ignore — Tc-99m decays in 6 hours, following current regulatory and professional standards
integrated.e: [NM+RP+DX+REG+QA] (1) Safety first: evacuate non-essential staff. (2) Don PPE, contain spread with absorbent. (3) Survey with GM counter. (4) Decontaminate. (5) Wipe test to confirm <release limits. (6) Dose assessment for exposed staff. (7) Report to RPO. (8) If significant, report to SAHPRA per Hazardous Substances Act.

integrated.q: CALC: H&N treatment: planned 70 Gy/35 fx. Machine breaks down for 5 days (week 5). Tumour Dprolif=0.6 Gy/day, Tk=28 days. Treatment resumes: BED lost and compensation strategy?
integrated.a: ~3 Gy BED lost; consider 6th fraction per week (accelerated) or weekend treatments to compensate
c: No loss — just extend treatment — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
~3 Gy BED lost; consider 6th fraction per week (accelerated) or weekend treatments to compensate
Start over from scratch — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Increase dose per fraction to 3 Gy, based on established clinical physics practice
integrated.e: [RB+DOS+SRT+TPS+EQ+QA] Break in week 5 (day 28+): repopulation already kicked in (Tk=28d). 5 days × 0.6 Gy/day = 3 Gy equivalent lost. Options: (1) add weekend fractions, (2) 6 fx/week, (3) do NOT increase fraction size (late effects). TPS replan not needed if same fx size. QA: verify output after machine repair.

integrated.q: Hospital builds combined RT/NucMed/Radiology wing. Which shielding standards apply to which rooms?
integrated.a: NCRP 151 for linac vaults, NCRP 147 for CT/X-ray rooms, AAPM TG-108 for PET/CT suites
c: Same standard for all rooms — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
NCRP 151 for linac vaults, NCRP 147 for CT/X-ray rooms, AAPM TG-108 for PET/CT suites
Only NCRP 151 needed — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Shielding not required for diagnostic
integrated.e: [RP+DOS+NM+DX+EQ+REG] Different radiation types and energies require different shielding approaches. MV therapy: NCRP 151 (primary+secondary+neutron barriers). Diagnostic: NCRP 147 (kV photons). PET/CT: TG-108 (511 keV annihilation photons from patients + CT scatter). Each has different workload, use factor, and material calculations.

integrated.q: CALC: PSQA for spine SBRT (27 Gy/3 fx) shows gamma pass rate 88% (3%/2mm). TG-218 action limit = 90%. Cord is the critical OAR. What is the safest clinical decision?
integrated.a: Fail — do not treat. Investigate cause (MLC, dose model, setup). Cord tolerance has no margin for delivery error
c: Treat anyway — 88% is close enough, following current regulatory and professional standards
Fail — do not treat. Investigate cause (MLC, dose model, setup). Cord tolerance has no margin for delivery error
Re-measure with looser criteria, following current regulatory and professional standards
Switch to 3D-CRT — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
integrated.e: [DOS+RB+QA+TPS+SRT+RP] 88% < 90% action limit per TG-218. Spine SBRT has sub-mm cord gradients. 2% of failing points could be in the cord region. BED to cord is already near tolerance (27/3=9 Gy/fx, cord Dmax ~18 Gy EQD2). No room for error. Must investigate: MLC calibration, TPS model, gantry sag, couch shift.

integrated.q: Patient with lung nodule: CT shows 12mm lesion. PET shows SUVmax=8. Biopsy confirms NSCLC. Planning CT with IV contrast acquired. Which physics considerations affect each step?
integrated.a: CT: kVp/noise/dose optimisation. PET: attenuation correction, SUV calibration. Planning CT: HU-ρₑ accuracy, contrast impact on dose calc
c: No physics involvement — purely clinical — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
CT: kVp/noise/dose optimisation. PET: attenuation correction, SUV calibration. Planning CT: HU-ρₑ accuracy, contrast impact on dose calc
Only dose calculation matters — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Only image quality matters — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
integrated.e: [NM+DX+RB+DOS+EQ] Diagnostic CT: optimise kVp/mAs for lesion detection (DX+QA). PET: scanner calibration, ACF, SUV accuracy for staging (NM+DOS). Planning CT: contrast affects HU→ρₑ (bolus timing matters), slice thickness for TPS accuracy, flat couch, laser alignment (DX+DOS+TPS+EQ).

integrated.q: CALC: Theranostic workflow: Ga-68 PSMA PET/CT (5 mSv) confirms metastatic prostate cancer. Patient receives Lu-177 PSMA (7.4 GBq × 4 cycles). Post-therapy SPECT/CT (3 mSv each × 4). Total diagnostic imaging dose?
integrated.a: 17 mSv total imaging dose (5 + 4×3)
c: 5 mSv — only first scan
17 mSv total imaging dose (5 + 4×3)
25 mSv
Cannot add PET and SPECT doses
integrated.e: [NM+DX+RB+RP+DOS] Diagnostic Ga-68 PET/CT: 5 mSv. 4× post-therapy SPECT/CT: 4×3=12 mSv. Total diagnostic imaging = 17 mSv. Note: therapeutic Lu-177 dose is MUCH higher (~Gy to organs) but is justified by treatment intent. Diagnostic doses add to stochastic risk.

integrated.q: Linac annual QA reveals: output = 0.985 cGy/MU, energy (TPR₂₀,₁₀) shifted from 0.671 to 0.685, MLC positions within 1mm. Which finding is most concerning and why?
integrated.a: Energy shift 0.671→0.685 (2.1%) exceeds ±1% tolerance
c: Output is most concerning — patients underdosed
All within tolerance
Energy shift 0.671→0.685 (2.1%) exceeds ±1% tolerance
MLC most concerning — affects IMRT
integrated.e: investigate target/bending magnet; output and PDD tables invalid. [DOS+EQ+QA+RP+REG] Output -1.5%: within ±2% annual tolerance. MLC ≤1mm: within tolerance. TPR₂₀,₁₀ shift of 0.014 (2.1%): FAILS ±1% tolerance. Energy change means: (1) all PDDs, TMRs, profiles are wrong, (2) k_Q changes, (3) patient dose distributions incorrect, (4) must stop treatment, recalibrate, potentially revalidate TPS data. Reportable if patients treated at wrong energy.

integrated.q: I-131 therapy room shielding design: patient administered 5.5 GBq, wall at 3m, adjacent office (T=1). Γ=0.055 mSv·m²/(GBq·hr). Patient in room for 5 days. Required lead shielding?
integrated.a: Calculate cumulative dose through wall, apply TVLs to meet 0.02 mSv/wk public limit
c: No shielding needed — walls sufficient — modern equipment self-monitoring eliminates the need for external verification
Standard 2mm Pb is always enough, based on established clinical physics practice
Calculate cumulative dose through wall, apply TVLs to meet 0.02 mSv/wk public limit
Same as linac vault shielding — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
integrated.e: [NM+RB+RP+REG+DOS] Initial rate at 3m: 0.055×5.5/9=0.0336 mSv/hr. Integrate over 5 days with decay (T_eff~5.5d): Ã=5.5×5.5×24/0.693=1047 GBq·hr. Dose at 3m = 0.055×1047/9 = 6.4 mSv over stay. Need B = 0.02/6.4×(5/7) = 0.0022 → ~2.7 HVLs of Pb at 364 keV.

integrated.q: Patient has 3 brain mets treated with single-isocenter multi-target SRS (SIMT). Why is this more demanding than single-met SRS from every physics perspective?
integrated.a: Rotational errors amplified at off-axis targets, each target needs independent dose verification, interplay between targets affects OAR doses
c: No difference — same technique — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
Only planning is harder — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Rotational errors amplified at off-axis targets, each target needs independent dose verification, interplay between targets affects OAR doses
Only QA is harder — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
integrated.e: [SRT+DOS+RB+QA+EQ+TPS] (1) EQ: rotational accuracy critical — 1° error = 1.7mm at 10cm off-axis. (2) QA: each target needs independent gamma analysis. (3) TPS: optimiser must balance coverage of 3 targets + all OARs. (4) DOS: MLC sequencing more complex, more MU, more interplay. (5) RB: different target sizes → different BEDs. (6) SRT: WL must verify all target positions.

integrated.q: CALC: Physicist supports 3 modalities. Weekly time: 4 hr linac vault (~2 μSv/hr), 6 hr CT room (~0.5 μSv/hr), 2 hr hot lab (~5 μSv/hr). Monthly occupational dose?
integrated.a: ~84 μSv/month
c: ~8 μSv/month
~42 μSv/month
~84 μSv/month
~500 μSv/month
integrated.e: [DOS+DX+NM+QA+EQ] Weekly: linac 4×2=8 + CT 6×0.5=3 + hot lab 2×5=10 = 21 μSv/wk. Monthly (4 weeks) = 84 μSv. Annual ≈ 1.1 mSv. Well within 20 mSv/yr. Multi-modality physicists accumulate dose from many sources.

integrated.q: CALC: Re-irradiation scenario: patient had 60 Gy/30 fx to pelvis (1 year ago). Now receives Lu-177 PSMA. Sacral nerve plexus received 48 Gy in first course. α/β=2. EQD2 from EBRT already received?
integrated.a: 43.2 Gy₂ — already near tolerance before Lu-177
c: 22.5 Gy₂ — well below tolerance, no concern for subsequent radiopharmaceutical therapy
43.2 Gy₂ — already near tolerance before Lu-177
65.0 Gy₂ — exceeds spinal cord tolerance, Lu-177 therapy must be cancelled entirely
85.5 Gy₂ — far above tolerance, indicates a treatment planning error requiring investigation
integrated.e: [RB+SRT+NM+DOS+RP] d=48/30=1.6. EQD2=48×(1.6+2)/(2+2)=48×3.6/4=48×0.9=43.2 Gy₂. Hmm: 48×3.6=172.8/4=43.2 Gy₂. Lu-177 will add additional dose to sacral plexus. Total must be tracked.

integrated.q: Patient with hip prosthesis: CT planning shows severe streak artefact. How does this affect: (1) contouring, (2) dose calculation, (3) biological effect?
integrated.a: Artefact obscures anatomy (contouring error), creates false HU (dose calc error), both compound to wrong BED delivery
c: Only affects image viewing — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Only affects contouring — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Artefact obscures anatomy (contouring error), creates false HU (dose calc error), both compound to wrong BED delivery
Metal artefact reduction fixes everything, following current regulatory and professional standards
integrated.e: [DX+DOS+QA+TPS+RB] (1) DX: streaks obscure tumour/OAR boundaries → contour uncertainty. (2) DOS+TPS: false HU values (both high and low) → electron density errors → dose calc wrong. (3) RB: compound errors mean actual BED delivered to target and OARs differs from planned. MAR algorithms help but don't fully solve. Consider MRI fusion for contouring.

integrated.q: CALC: PET technologist works with F-18 (Γ=0.143 mSv·m²/(GBq·hr)). Handles 20 patients/day, ~370 MBq each, average 2 min close contact at 0.5 m per patient. Monthly hand dose estimate?
integrated.a: ~14 mSv/month to hands
c: ~0.14 mSv/month
~1.4 mSv/month
~14 mSv/month to hands
~140 mSv/month
integrated.e: [NM+RP+RB+DX+REG] Per patient at 0.5m: 0.143×0.37/0.25=0.212 mSv/hr. For 2 min: 0.212×2/60=0.00707 mSv. Per day: 0.00707×20=0.141 mSv. Per month (22 working days): 0.141×22=3.1 mSv whole body. Hands closer (~0.2m during injection): ×(0.5/0.2)²=×6.25 → ~19.4 mSv/month to hands. Annual ~230 mSv — within 500 mSv extremity limit but needs monitoring.

integrated.q: New 6 MV FFF beam commissioned for cranial SRS. List the chain of physics tasks from beam data to first patient treatment.
integrated.a: Beam scanning → TRS-398 cal → TRS-483 small-field OFs → TPS modelling → E2E test → WL test → shielding survey → PSQA → clinical protocol
c: Just calibrate and treat — no additional physics considerations or verification steps are needed
Only need TPS commissioning — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Beam scanning → TRS-398 cal → TRS-483 small-field OFs → TPS modelling → E2E test → WL test → shielding survey → PSQA → clinical protocol
Manufacturer does everything, following current regulatory and professional standards
integrated.e: [DOS+EQ+SRT+QA+TPS+RP] (1) EQ: beam scanning (PDDs, profiles, OFs). (2) DOS: TRS-398 absolute calibration. (3) SRT+DOS: TRS-483 small-field OFs with microDiamond. (4) TPS: model beam data, validate. (5) QA: E2E test (spatial + dosimetric). (6) SRT: Winston-Lutz. (7) RP: shielding verification survey. (8) QA: patient-specific QA protocol. (9) REG: clinical protocol sign-off.

integrated.q: Paediatric patient (age 5): Tc-99m DMSA renal scan (100 MBq, ~1 mSv) followed by CT abdomen with contrast (DLP=300, k=0.015, SSDE factor=1.5 for small child). Total effective dose and special considerations?
integrated.a: ~7.75 mSv total; heightened concern due to age
c: ~1 mSv — only NM counts
~4.5 mSv — standard adult calculation
~7.75 mSv total; heightened concern due to age
~20 mSv — paediatric dose always higher
integrated.e: justify rigorously, use minimum diagnostic dose. [RB+NM+DX+RP+DOS+REG] DMSA: 1 mSv. CT: DLP×k = 300×0.015=4.5 mSv (phantom-based). SSDE: effectively ~4.5×1.5=6.75 mSv to child. Total ~7.75 mSv. Paediatric concern: 2-3× risk coefficient vs adult (BEIR VII). Justification principle paramount. Image Gently protocols. Consider US or MRI alternatives.

integrated.q: CALC: Lung SBRT 48 Gy/4 fx (6FFF). TPS: AcurosXB, 1.5 mm grid. PSQA: gamma 97% (3%/2mm). Ion chamber point dose agrees within 1.5%. Chest wall D0.5cc = 35 Gy. Rib fracture risk?
integrated.a: EQD2 for chest wall (α/β=3) = 35×(8.75+3)/(2+3) = 82.3 Gy₃
c: No risk — below tolerance — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
EQD2 for chest wall (α/β=3) = 35×(8.75+3)/(2+3) = 82.3 Gy₃
Cannot predict from dosimetry alone
Only if dose exceeds 50 Gy — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
integrated.e: significant rib fracture risk (~15-30%). [DOS+RB+SRT+TPS+QA+EQ] d=35/4=8.75. EQD2=35×11.75/5=35×2.35=82.3 Gy₃. Literature: chest wall EQD2 >80 Gy₃ → 15-30% rib fracture rate. QA confirms accurate delivery. Plan may be acceptable if alternative is worse, but patient should be counselled. Replanning to reduce D0.5cc if possible.

integrated.q: Hospital radiation safety committee annual review must cover all modalities. Which metrics must the RPO report for each area?
integrated.a: Staff doses, area survey results, incident reports, QC compliance, waste disposal records, patient dose audits
c: Only staff doses — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Only equipment QC results — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Staff doses, area survey results, incident reports, QC compliance, waste disposal records, patient dose audits
Only regulatory inspection results — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
integrated.e: [RP+DX+NM+REG+QA+DOS] Comprehensive RPO annual report: (1) Personal dosimetry summaries (all modalities). (2) Area monitoring results (vault, CT, NM). (3) Incident/near-miss log. (4) Equipment QC records (linac, CT, gamma camera, dose calibrator). (5) Radioactive waste disposal log. (6) Patient dose audits (DRLs for CT, prescribed vs delivered for RT). (7) Training records. (8) SAHPRA license status.

integrated.q: Oligometastatic patient: 3 liver mets on FDG PET/CT. Treated with SBRT 45 Gy/3 fx per met using 6FFF + AcurosXB. 3-month PET shows 2 met complete response (SUV<2), 1 met persistent (SUV=6). Combined physics interpretation?
integrated.a: BED₁₀=112.5 Gy (ablative). 2/3 response confirms dosimetric and radiobiological plan validity
c: BED₁₀=67.2 Gy — sublethal, likely to need retreatment within six months of completion
BED₁₀=100.0 Gy — curative threshold, standard for conventional lung cancer fractionation
BED₁₀=112.5 Gy (ablative). 2/3 response confirms dosimetric and radiobiological plan validity
BED₁₀=150.0 Gy — exceeds safe ablative range, risk of severe normal tissue complications
integrated.e: [SRT+RB+DOS+NM+DX+TPS] BED₁₀ = 3×15×(1+15/10) = 45×2.5=112.5 Gy₁₀. Highly ablative. 2/3 complete metabolic response validates the physics chain (calibration, TPS, delivery). 1/3 persistent: could be biological (hypoxic, radioresistant clone) rather than physics failure. PET quantification (NM) confirms differential response. OER, reoxygenation (RB) may explain resistance.

integrated.q: A medical physicist's typical day includes: morning linac QA (dosimetry, equipment), SBRT planning review (TPS, radiobiology), CT protocol optimisation (diagnostic, dose), afternoon Lu-177 therapy discharge assessment (nucmed, radprot), and monthly regulatory paperwork (regulations). What single error would cascade across ALL these domains?
integrated.a: Using an out-of-calibration electrometer
c: Forgetting to log CPD points
Misreading a patient chart
Using an out-of-calibration electrometer
Wearing wrong badge
integrated.e: corrupts linac output, SBRT dose, CT dose measurements, and dose calibrator readings for NM. [ALL DOMAINS] The electrometer is the common measurement link: (1) DOS: wrong linac calibration. (2) EQ+QA: all output checks wrong. (3) SRT: SBRT dose delivery wrong. (4) TPS: validated against wrong measurements. (5) DX: CT dose audit readings wrong. (6) NM: dose calibrator readings (if checked with same instrument) wrong. (7) RP: survey readings wrong. (8) REG: all QC records invalid. One instrument failure → system-wide corruption. Annual electrometer calibration is critical.

integrated.q: MR-linac adaptive workflow: daily MRI → AI autocontour → reoptimise plan → deliver. Which physics domains intersect and what is the unique dosimetric challenge?
integrated.a: EQ+TPS+DOS+RB+QA: electron return effect in magnetic field alters dose distribution at tissue-air interfaces
c: No dosimetric difference from conventional linac — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
EQ+TPS+DOS+RB+QA: electron return effect in magnetic field alters dose distribution at tissue-air interfaces
Only contouring changes — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Only imaging is different — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
integrated.e: [EQ+TPS+DOS+RB+QA] 1.5T magnetic field curves electron paths → electron return effect (ERE) at tissue-air boundaries → hot/cold spots. TPS must model B-field (Monte Carlo). AI autocontour enables fast online adaptive. QA: beam calibration in B-field requires special protocols. RB: adaptive replanning changes BED to OARs daily.

integrated.q: CALC: AI autocontouring saves 45 min per patient. Department treats 40 new patients/week. Annual time savings?
integrated.a: ~1560 hours/year
c: ~100 hours/year
~780 hours/year
~1560 hours/year
~5000 hours/year
integrated.e: [TPS+QA+DOS+EQ] 45 min × 40 pts/wk × 52 wks = 93,600 min = 1560 hours. At ~2080 working hours/year = 0.75 FTE. Massive efficiency gain. BUT: physicist must still verify AI contours — review time ~10-15 min. Net savings ~30 min/pt = ~1040 hr/yr.

integrated.q: Theranostic physics workflow: Ga-68 PSMA PET/CT → Lu-177 PSMA therapy → post-therapy SPECT/CT dosimetry → AI-based dose prediction for next cycle. Which quality checks span NM, dosimetry, and radiobiology?
integrated.a: PET scanner SUV calibration, dose calibrator accuracy, SPECT quantitation validation, and dose-response correlation using BED models
c: Only dose calibrator check — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
PET scanner SUV calibration, dose calibrator accuracy, SPECT quantitation validation, and dose-response correlation using BED models
Only SPECT uniformity — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
No QC needed between modalities — modern equipment self-monitoring eliminates the need for external verification
integrated.e: [NM+DOS+RB+QA+EQ] Full chain: (1) PET SUV accuracy (quantitation for staging). (2) Dose calibrator linearity at GBq levels (accurate administration). (3) SPECT/CT quantitative accuracy (dose estimation). (4) MIRD/voxel dosimetry. (5) BED models adapted for protracted low-dose-rate delivery. AI accelerates dose prediction for subsequent cycles.

integrated.q: AI-based PSQA (patient-specific quality assurance) replaces measurement how?
integrated.a: ML model trained on thousands of measured plans predicts gamma pass rate from plan complexity metrics — flags outliers for measurement
c: Eliminates all QA — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
ML model trained on thousands of measured plans predicts gamma pass rate from plan complexity metrics — flags outliers for measurement
Only recalculates dose — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Uses AI to move the detector, following current regulatory and professional standards
integrated.e: [DOS+QA+TPS+SRT+EQ] Systems like ArcherQA, PerFraction AI: trained on measured gamma results + plan features (MU, MLC complexity, modulation factor). Predicts pass rate. Low-risk plans skip measurement (virtual QA). High-risk flagged for physical measurement. Reduces QA workload ~40-60% while maintaining safety.

integrated.q: FLASH proton therapy: unique physics challenges spanning dosimetry, equipment, radiobiology, and radiation protection?
integrated.a: Ion chamber saturation at ultra-high dose rates, beam current limitations, uncertain RBE, and shielding for much higher instantaneous dose rates
c: Same as conventional proton therapy — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
Ion chamber saturation at ultra-high dose rates, beam current limitations, uncertain RBE, and shielding for much higher instantaneous dose rates
Only dosimetry changes — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Only shielding changes — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
integrated.e: [DOS+EQ+RB+RP] (1) DOS: ion chambers saturate at >100 Gy/s → need solid-state or calorimetric dosimetry. (2) EQ: cyclotron must deliver ~100× normal beam current. (3) RB: FLASH RBE may differ from conventional proton RBE of 1.1. (4) RP: instantaneous dose rates require reassessment of shielding (no change in total dose, but pulsed nature affects activation).

integrated.q: Photon-counting CT for radiotherapy planning: advantages spanning diagnostic, dosimetry, and TPS domains?
integrated.a: Multi-energy material decomposition
c: Only faster scanning
Multi-energy material decomposition
Only better image quality
No advantage for RT planning
integrated.e: more accurate electron density/SPR, reduced artefacts, and potential for contrast-enhanced tumour delineation. [DX+DOS+TPS+EQ] (1) DX: spectral data → material decomposition → direct electron density (no HU-ρₑ curve needed). (2) DOS: reduced stopping power ratio uncertainty for proton therapy (~0.5% vs 1.5%). (3) TPS: virtual monoenergetic images reduce metal artefacts. (4) Higher resolution for small structure delineation. Paradigm shift for RT planning CT.

integrated.q: Total-body PET combined with AI reconstruction and radiomics: how does this change the theranostic dosimetry paradigm?
integrated.a: Single ultra-low-dose scan captures whole-body pharmacokinetics
c: No change — same as current PET — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
Single ultra-low-dose scan captures whole-body pharmacokinetics
Only faster imaging — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Only for research — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
integrated.e: AI-driven voxel dosimetry → personalised activity prescription. [NM+DOS+RB+DX+TPS] Total-body PET: dynamic whole-body pharmacokinetics from single scan → time-activity curves for every organ simultaneously. AI segments organs + fits kinetic models → MIRD dose maps → ML predicts toxicity → optimise next therapy cycle activity. Moves from fixed-activity to truly personalised RPT.

integrated.q: Ethical consideration: AI autocontour and auto-planning require final human approval. Why is this medico-legally essential?
integrated.a: AI lacks clinical context (prior surgery, comorbidities, patient preferences)
c: AI is always correct — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Legal requirement only — AI is more accurate
AI lacks clinical context (prior surgery, comorbidities, patient preferences)
No ethical issue exists — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
integrated.e: accountability must rest with qualified professional. [ALL+REG] AI tools are decision support, not autonomous agents. Clinician must verify: (1) contours match clinical scenario. (2) Plan meets clinical intent. (3) Patient-specific factors accounted for. HPCSA/SAHPRA: registered professional remains responsible. AI errors without human oversight = negligence.

integrated.q: A modern medical physicist in 2026 must be competent across which emerging intersections that didn't exist 10 years ago?
integrated.a: AI/ML validation, MR-guided adaptive RT, theranostic dosimetry, FLASH radiobiology, and photon-counting CT physics
c: Only traditional dosimetry and shielding — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Only AI applications — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
AI/ML validation, MR-guided adaptive RT, theranostic dosimetry, FLASH radiobiology, and photon-counting CT physics
Only nuclear medicine and RT — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
integrated.e: [ALL DOMAINS] 2026 medical physicist: (1) Validate AI tools (autocontour, auto-plan, virtual QA). (2) Commission/QA MR-linac (B-field dosimetry, adaptive workflows). (3) Personalised RPT dosimetry (Lu-177, Ac-225). (4) Understand FLASH radiobiology and dosimetry challenges. (5) Implement spectral/photon-counting CT for RT planning. (6) Navigate regulatory landscape for AI medical devices. The field has fundamentally expanded.

integrated.q: Elekta Versa HD linac: Agility MLC has 160 leaves with 5mm leaf width at isocentre. Compared to Varian Millennium 120 (5mm central, 10mm outer), what is the dosimetric impact for head & neck VMAT?
integrated.a: More uniform 5mm resolution across full field improves conformity for small, irregular targets near OARs
c: No difference in plan quality — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
More uniform 5mm resolution across full field improves conformity for small, irregular targets near OARs
Only matters for fields >20 cm — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Millennium is always superior, following current regulatory and professional standards
integrated.e: [EQ+TPS+DOS+SRT] Agility: 5mm across all 40 cm field width. Millennium: 5mm only central 20 cm, 10mm outer leaves. For H&N VMAT with parotid/cord sparing, uniform 5mm resolution allows sharper dose gradients throughout the field. Interdigitation capability also differs between vendors.

integrated.q: Monaco TPS uses Monte Carlo dose calculation. What is the key commissioning difference vs Eclipse AAA/AcurosXB?
integrated.a: Monaco requires measured beam data to build a virtual source model
c: No commissioning difference — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
Monaco requires measured beam data to build a virtual source model
Monaco needs less beam data, based on established clinical physics practice
Eclipse is always more accurate
integrated.e: MC directly simulates particle transport through patient geometry. [TPS+DOS+EQ+QA] Monaco: virtual source model (VSM) tuned to measured data, then MC transports particles through CT. Eclipse AAA: analytical convolution/superposition. AcurosXB: deterministic Boltzmann transport. Each requires different commissioning approach. MC gold standard for heterogeneity accuracy.

integrated.q: CALC: Monaco plan shows 2% statistical uncertainty. What does this mean for a 200 cGy prescription?
integrated.a: Calculated dose at any voxel has ~4 cGy (1 SD) statistical noise from MC sampling
c: Plan is 2% wrong everywhere, based on established clinical physics practice
Calculated dose at any voxel has ~4 cGy (1 SD) statistical noise from MC sampling
Dose is exactly 200 cGy everywhere
Uncertainty is only in OARs, based on established clinical physics practice
integrated.e: [TPS+DOS+QA] MC statistical uncertainty: 2% of 200 cGy = 4 cGy (1 SD). Individual voxels fluctuate ±4 cGy randomly. Average over volumes is more accurate. Must balance computation time vs uncertainty. Clinical standard: 1-2% for final calculation. Higher uncertainty acceptable during optimisation.

integrated.q: Patient treated on Elekta linac planned on Monaco, but CT performed on Siemens scanner and contoured in MIM. What multi-system QA chain must the physicist verify?
integrated.a: CT HU accuracy, DICOM transfer fidelity at every step, coordinate system consistency, contour integrity, and dose calculation validation
c: Only check the final plan — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
CT HU accuracy, DICOM transfer fidelity at every step, coordinate system consistency, contour integrity, and dose calculation validation
Only verify MU calculation — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Trust vendor interoperability, following current regulatory and professional standards
integrated.e: [DX+TPS+DOS+QA+EQ+REG] Multi-vendor chain: (1) CT HU-to-density table validated for that scanner in Monaco. (2) DICOM RT Structure transfer MIM→Monaco checked for contour fidelity. (3) Patient orientation/coordinate systems consistent. (4) Plan transferred back for delivery. Each handoff is a potential failure point. End-to-end testing essential.

integrated.q: RayStation TPS offers both collapsed cone (CC) and Monte Carlo for photon dose calculation. When is MC essential over CC?
integrated.a: Small fields in lung/air, extreme heterogeneities, and very small targets where lateral electron disequilibrium dominates
c: Always use MC for everything, following current regulatory and professional standards
Small fields in lung/air, extreme heterogeneities, and very small targets where lateral electron disequilibrium dominates
CC is always sufficient — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
Only for electron beams — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
integrated.e: [TPS+DOS+SRT+EQ] CC convolution handles moderate heterogeneity well but fails at interfaces with large density changes when field sizes approach electron range. Lung SBRT with small PTV (<2 cm) near chest wall: MC shows 5-10% dose differences from CC. RayStation lets planner compare both algorithms on same geometry.

integrated.q: Elekta Unity MR-linac: 1.5T magnetic field affects dose deposition at tissue-air interfaces. What is this phenomenon?
integrated.a: Electron Return Effect (ERE) - Lorentz force curves electrons back into tissue at exit surfaces
c: No effect on dose — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
Electron Return Effect (ERE) - Lorentz force curves electrons back into tissue at exit surfaces
Only affects image quality — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Increases photon energy — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
integrated.e: [EQ+DOS+TPS+RB+QA] In 1.5T B-field, secondary electrons curve in circular paths (Lorentz force). At tissue→air boundaries (e.g., skin, trachea, rectum), electrons return to tissue → hot spots up to 140% at air-tissue interface. Monaco MC and Elekta's dose engine model this explicitly. Must be accounted for in planning.

integrated.q: ViewRay MRIdian uses 0.35T MR-linac. Advantage of lower field vs Elekta Unity 1.5T for dose calculation?
integrated.a: Smaller ERE at tissue-air interfaces due to weaker magnetic field
c: No difference — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
Smaller ERE at tissue-air interfaces due to weaker magnetic field
Better image quality at 0.35T
Higher dose rate at 0.35T, based on established clinical physics practice
integrated.e: but reduced MR image quality. [EQ+DOS+TPS+DX] 0.35T: ERE is smaller (electron radius of curvature larger) → less dose perturbation at interfaces. Trade-off: lower SNR MR images → contours may be less precise. Each system has distinct physics trade-offs. Clinical choice depends on treatment site.

integrated.q: CALC: Elekta linac output calibrated to 1.000 cGy/MU at d_max for 6MV, SSD=100cm, 10x10cm2. Monaco plan prescribes 200 cGy to isocentre at 10cm depth (TMR=0.660). SSD technique. Approximate MU?
integrated.e: [DOS+TPS+EQ] SSD technique: MU = D/(Output × TMR × field factors). Simplified: MU = 200/(1.0 × 0.660) = 303 MU. In reality, Sc, Sp, ISF, tray factors etc. apply. Monaco calculates MU from MC; physicist does independent hand calc to verify.

integrated.q: Independent MU check for VMAT plan: how does this work for a Monaco-planned Elekta delivery?
integrated.a: Secondary software (e.g., RadCalc, MU Check) or simplified 3D calculation recalculates dose independently from DICOM plan
c: Not needed for VMAT — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
Secondary software (e.g., RadCalc, MU Check) or simplified 3D calculation recalculates dose independently from DICOM plan
Just compare MU to similar past plans — no additional physics considerations or verification steps are needed
Only measurement-based QA needed — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
integrated.e: [DOS+QA+TPS+EQ] VMAT: cannot do simple hand MU calc. Independent calc software imports DICOM RT Plan + CT, recalculates dose using independent algorithm. Compare to Monaco prediction. Action level: typically 3-5% for point dose, or gamma analysis. Catches systematic TPS errors.

integrated.q: CyberKnife uses non-isocentric delivery with hundreds of pencil beams from a robotic arm. How does QA differ from isocentric linac QA?
integrated.a: Must verify robotic positioning accuracy at each node, beam-on time per beam, and end-to-end targeting with anthropomorphic phantom
c: Same QA as conventional linac — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
Must verify robotic positioning accuracy at each node, beam-on time per beam, and end-to-end targeting with anthropomorphic phantom
Only check output — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
No QA needed - robotic system self-checks — current protocols and vendor specifications provide adequate safety margins
integrated.e: [EQ+QA+SRT+DOS+TPS] CyberKnife: ~100-300 beams from different positions. QA: (1) Robot path accuracy (E2E test with ball cube). (2) Output per beam (AQA test). (3) Tracking accuracy (Synchrony). (4) Film/detector end-to-end phantom test. Different philosophy from gantry-based linac QA.

integrated.q: TomoTherapy (Accuray): helical delivery with binary MLC. Unique dosimetric commissioning requirement?
integrated.a: Characterise output as function of jaw width (1-5 cm) and pitch, plus longitudinal dose profile
c: Same as conventional linac — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
Characterise output as function of jaw width (1-5 cm) and pitch, plus longitudinal dose profile
Only transverse profiles needed — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
No commissioning required — current protocols and vendor specifications provide adequate safety margins
integrated.e: [EQ+DOS+QA+TPS] TomoTherapy: 6MV FFF-like beam, binary MLC (open/closed only), helical delivery. Unique: (1) Output varies with jaw width. (2) Pitch affects dose uniformity (ripple/thread effect). (3) MVCT imaging dose contributes to patient dose. (4) No standard 10×10 reference field → TRS-483 msr approach.

integrated.q: Gamma Knife Icon: 192 Co-60 sources with automated collimator changes. What physics spans NM, dosimetry, and equipment domains?
integrated.a: Source decay correction (NM), output factor per collimator (DOS), and cone-beam CT for frameless SRS (EQ)
c: Only source decay matters — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Source decay correction (NM), output factor per collimator (DOS), and cone-beam CT for frameless SRS (EQ)
Same physics as linac SRS — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
Only collimator output matters — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
integrated.e: [NM+DOS+EQ+SRT+QA] Gamma Knife: (1) 192 Co-60 sources decay at 1.1%/month → output recalibration monthly. (2) Four collimator sizes (4,8,16mm) with different output factors. (3) Icon adds CBCT → frameless → need verify CBCT-to-treatment coordinate accuracy. (4) Dose rate decreases over source life → longer treatment times.

integrated.q: Monaco Segment Shape Optimisation (SSO) vs Direct Machine Parameter Optimisation (DMPO): clinical difference for VMAT?
integrated.a: DMPO directly optimises deliverable MLC segments during optimisation, avoiding post-optimisation sequencing degradation
c: No difference — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
SSO is always better — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
DMPO directly optimises deliverable MLC segments during optimisation, avoiding post-optimisation sequencing degradation
DMPO only works for IMRT — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
integrated.e: [TPS+DOS+EQ+QA] SSO: optimises fluence first, then converts to deliverable segments (can lose quality in conversion). DMPO: optimises actual machine parameters (MLC positions, MU) directly → what you optimise is what you deliver. Most modern TPS use direct aperture/machine parameter optimisation. Monaco VMAT uses sequential DMPO.

integrated.q: Pinnacle TPS (Philips): collapsed cone convolution/superposition. Compared to Monte Carlo in lung SBRT, what is the known limitation?
integrated.a: Overestimates target coverage in low-density lung tissue due to inaccurate lateral scatter modelling
c: No limitation — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
Underestimates dose everywhere, verified through standard quality assurance protocols
Overestimates target coverage in low-density lung tissue due to inaccurate lateral scatter modelling
Only affects bone-tissue interfaces — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
integrated.e: [TPS+DOS+SRT+RB] Pinnacle CC: good for moderate heterogeneity but overestimates dose in lung (lateral electron transport not fully modelled). Can report 95% target coverage when MC shows only 85%. Critical for SBRT where PTV margins are small. Type B algorithm limitation well-documented.

integrated.q: Varian Halcyon vs Elekta Harmony: both are ring-gantry closed-bore linacs. What is the shared dosimetric advantage of ring-gantry design?
integrated.a: Faster gantry rotation enables rapid VMAT delivery
c: Higher beam energy — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Faster gantry rotation enables rapid VMAT delivery
Better image quality only
Only cosmetic difference — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
integrated.e: closed bore improves radiation safety and allows smaller vaults. [EQ+DOS+RP+QA] Ring gantry: (1) Faster rotation (15-60 s/arc vs 60s+ C-arm). (2) Closed bore → inherent shielding → smaller vault → lower construction cost. (3) Halcyon: dual-layer MLC, 6MV FFF only. Harmony: Agility MLC, single energy. Both designed for high-throughput departments. Physicist must commission within specific constraints (no physical wedges, limited SSD range).

integrated.q: Patient moves from Varian TrueBeam facility to Elekta Versa HD facility mid-treatment. Physics challenges spanning TPS, dosimetry, and equipment?
integrated.a: Beam model differences, MLC geometry differences, different VMAT delivery mechanics, and dose accumulation uncertainty across plans
c: Just transfer the plan file — no additional physics considerations or verification steps are needed
Beam model differences, MLC geometry differences, different VMAT delivery mechanics, and dose accumulation uncertainty across plans
Only recalculate MU — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
No physics issues if same nominal energy — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
integrated.e: [TPS+DOS+EQ+QA+RB+REG] Cannot simply transfer DICOM plan between vendors. Must: (1) Re-plan on new TPS with facility-specific beam model. (2) Account for MLC leaf width/design differences. (3) Composite dose = delivered fractions (old) + remaining fractions (new). (4) Deformable registration may be needed for dose accumulation. (5) Document everything for regulatory compliance.

integrated.q: Proton therapy vs photon VMAT for paediatric medulloblastoma CSI: which domains intersect in the physics comparison?
integrated.a: DOS (proton Bragg peak vs photon exit dose), RB (variable RBE), RP (reduced integral dose), TPS (range uncertainty), REG
c: Only dose distribution matters — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
DOS (proton Bragg peak vs photon exit dose), RB (variable RBE), RP (reduced integral dose), TPS (range uncertainty), REG
Only cost matters — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
No physics difference — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
integrated.e: [DOS+RB+RP+TPS+REG+EQ] Proton CSI: (1) Bragg peak → near-zero exit dose → lower integral dose → reduced secondary cancer risk (critical for children). (2) RBE uncertainty at end of range near critical structures. (3) Range uncertainty requires robust planning. (4) SA: limited proton access → referral policies. (5) Comprehensive physics comparison needed for MDT decision.

integrated.q: Elekta iViewGT EPID vs Varian aS1200 EPID: both used for portal dosimetry. What must the physicist validate?
integrated.a: Detector response linearity, dose rate dependence, ghosting/memory effects, and backscatter specific to each panel
c: Same validation for both — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
Detector response linearity, dose rate dependence, ghosting/memory effects, and backscatter specific to each panel
Only check spatial resolution — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
No validation needed - factory calibrated — established commissioning data remains valid throughout the equipment lifetime
integrated.e: [EQ+DOS+QA+TPS] EPID panels differ in: pixel pitch, scintillator material, backscatter from support arm, ghosting characteristics. Each must be independently validated for transit dosimetry or PSQA. Vendor-specific correction factors needed. Cannot assume one calibration applies to another.

integrated.q: CALC: Elekta Agility MLC interleaf leakage measured at 0.5%. For a 30-fraction VMAT treatment where MLC modulation factor is 3.5, estimate the interleaf leakage dose contribution to adjacent tissue?
integrated.a: ~1.75% of prescription dose
c: ~0.5% based on protocol requirements
~1.75% of prescription dose
~5% for the treatment procedure
~10% in the healthcare environment
integrated.e: [EQ+DOS+TPS+QA+RB] Interleaf leakage: 0.5% per leaf-pair. High modulation (MF=3.5) means leaves are closed for more MU than open. Leakage dose ~ 0.5% × MF = 1.75% of prescribed dose delivered as low-dose bath to surrounding tissue. Relevant for secondary cancer risk, especially in paediatric patients. Lower MLC leakage is always better.

integrated.q: Brachytherapy HDR Ir-192 source exchange: physics domains involved in pre-treatment QA?
integrated.a: NM (source calibration vs certificate), DOS (well chamber measurement), EQ (autoradiograph/dwell position accuracy), QA (transit time), REG
c: Only check source activity — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
NM (source calibration vs certificate), DOS (well chamber measurement), EQ (autoradiograph/dwell position accuracy), QA (transit time), REG
Only check dwell positions — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Same as linac QA — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
integrated.e: [NM+DOS+EQ+QA+REG+RP] Source exchange: (1) Well chamber measurement vs vendor certificate (±5% tolerance). (2) Autoradiograph or high-resolution detector to verify dwell positions (±1mm). (3) Transit dose correction factor. (4) Timer accuracy. (5) Emergency retraction test. (6) Old source disposal per SAHPRA regulations. (7) Update TPS source library.

integrated.q: Eclipse TPS vs Monaco TPS for lung SBRT plan comparison: which dose calculation algorithm is most accurate at the lung-tumour interface?
integrated.a: Monaco MC - explicitly simulates electron transport across density interfaces
c: Eclipse AAA — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Eclipse AcurosXB — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Monaco MC - explicitly simulates electron transport across density interfaces
All equivalent — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
integrated.e: [TPS+DOS+SRT+RB] Ranking at lung-tumour interface: MC (Monaco) > AcurosXB (Eclipse) > CC (Pinnacle/RayStation) > AAA (Eclipse). MC simulates individual particles. AcurosXB solves LBTE deterministically - very close to MC. AAA uses analytical kernel scaling - overestimates at interfaces. Clinical impact: 5-15% dose difference at tumour periphery.

integrated.q: Stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) for early lung cancer: which physics domains must the physicist master simultaneously?
integrated.a: SRT (stereotactic immobilisation/4DCT), DOS (small-field output factors), TPS (heterogeneity-corrected algorithm), QA (end-to-end), RB (BED>100Gy), RP (body dose)
c: Only SRT and dosimetry — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
SRT (stereotactic immobilisation/4DCT), DOS (small-field output factors), TPS (heterogeneity-corrected algorithm), QA (end-to-end), RB (BED>100Gy), RP (body dose)
Only treatment planning — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Same as conventional RT — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
integrated.e: [SRT+DOS+TPS+QA+RB+RP+EQ] SBRT is the ultimate multi-domain challenge: (1) 4DCT + ITV/iGTV concept. (2) Small fields → TRS-483. (3) Type C algorithm (MC/AcurosXB) mandatory in lung. (4) End-to-end phantom test for each protocol. (5) BED>100 Gy for tumour control. (6) OAR tolerances completely different from conventional RT. (7) IGRT (CBCT/ExacTrac) at every fraction.

integrated.q: SRS for brain metastases: single fraction 20 Gy to 1.5 cm target. Calculate BED to tumour (alpha/beta=10) and nearby brainstem constraint if brainstem max = 12 Gy (alpha/beta=2).
integrated.a: Tumour BED = 60 Gy
c: Tumour BED = 40 Gy; Brainstem BED = 24 Gy
Tumour BED = 60 Gy
Tumour BED = 20 Gy; Brainstem BED = 12 Gy
Both BEDs are equal
integrated.e: Brainstem BED = 84 Gy - brainstem is the dose-limiting structure. [SRT+RB+DOS+TPS+QA] Tumour: BED = 20(1+20/10) = 20×3 = 60 Gy. Brainstem: BED = 12(1+12/2) = 12×7 = 84 Gy. Despite lower physical dose, brainstem BED is higher because low alpha/beta amplifies fraction size effect. This is why single-fraction SRS must respect OAR constraints rigorously. Fractionated SRS (e.g., 27Gy/3fx) may be preferred for targets near brainstem.

integrated.q: VMAT QA using ArcCHECK (cylindrical diode array): what does it verify across equipment, dosimetry, and TPS domains?
integrated.a: Combined MLC motion, gantry speed, dose rate modulation, and agreement between TPS prediction and delivery
c: Only point dose — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Combined MLC motion, gantry speed, dose rate modulation, and agreement between TPS prediction and delivery
Only MLC accuracy — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Only dose rate — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
integrated.e: [QA+DOS+TPS+EQ] ArcCHECK: 1386 diodes on cylinder surface. Measures composite dose from full VMAT arc. Gamma analysis (3%/3mm, 2%/2mm) compares measured vs TPS-predicted dose on cylindrical surface. Catches: MLC errors, dose rate errors, gantry speed errors, TPS modelling errors. Does NOT verify absolute dose at isocentre (needs independent point measurement).

integrated.q: CALC: VMAT QA gamma analysis: 3%/2mm criteria, 95% pass rate threshold. Plan shows 93.5%. What should the physicist do?
integrated.a: Investigate - below action level. Check MLC logs, re-measure, consider tighter plan constraints if systematic
c: Approve - close enough to 95%, following current regulatory and professional standards
Investigate - below action level. Check MLC logs, re-measure, consider tighter plan constraints if systematic
Reject plan and replan immediately, following current regulatory and professional standards
Ignore - gamma analysis is not important, as recommended by current international guidelines
integrated.e: [QA+DOS+TPS+EQ+REG] 93.5% < 95% threshold = action level breached. Systematic approach: (1) Re-measure to confirm. (2) Check MLC log files for position errors. (3) Check for setup errors in QA device. (4) If real, investigate TPS modelling (small fields? extreme modulation?). (5) Consider replanning with lower modulation complexity. (6) Document investigation per SAHPRA QA protocols.

integrated.q: Elekta XVI CBCT for IGRT: physicist commissioning requirements spanning DX, EQ, QA, and RP domains?
integrated.a: Image quality (CNR, uniformity, spatial resolution), geometric accuracy vs treatment isocentre, imaging dose, and kV-MV isocentre coincidence
c: Only check image quality — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Image quality (CNR, uniformity, spatial resolution), geometric accuracy vs treatment isocentre, imaging dose, and kV-MV isocentre coincidence
Only verify isocentre — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
No commissioning needed — established commissioning data remains valid throughout the equipment lifetime
integrated.e: [DX+EQ+QA+RP+DOS] CBCT commissioning: (1) Catphan phantom: HU accuracy, uniformity, CNR, spatial resolution, slice thickness. (2) Geometric accuracy: CBCT isocentre vs MV treatment isocentre (must be <1mm for SRS). (3) Imaging dose: CTDIw measurement for clinical protocols. (4) Monthly QA trending. (5) Different from Varian OBI commissioning - vendor-specific parameters.

integrated.q: ExacTrac (BrainLab) infrared + kV X-ray image guidance: how does it integrate with linac delivery for frameless SRS?
integrated.a: IR tracks surface markers in real-time for intrafraction motion
c: Only infrared tracking — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Only kV imaging — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
IR tracks surface markers in real-time for intrafraction motion
Replaces CBCT entirely — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
integrated.e: kV images verify bony anatomy before and during beam-on. [EQ+SRT+QA+DX+DOS] ExacTrac: dual modality. (1) IR optical: continuous 6DOF position monitoring via reflective markers - detects motion during delivery (gating capability). (2) kV stereoscopic: 2D/2D match to DRRs for sub-mm skeletal verification. Combined: enables frameless SRS with <1mm accuracy. Physicist must validate both subsystems independently AND combined accuracy via end-to-end testing.

integrated.q: Deep inspiration breath hold (DIBH) for left breast radiotherapy: which physics domains intersect?
integrated.a: EQ (surface guidance/RPM), DOS (dose reproducibility across breath-hold cycles), QA (gating verification), TPS (margin reduction), and RB
c: Only patient compliance with breath-hold instruction — no physics domains directly involved
EQ (surface guidance/RPM), DOS (dose reproducibility across breath-hold cycles), QA (gating verification), TPS (margin reduction), and RB
Only equipment setup with respiratory monitoring belt — standard treatment otherwise
Only treatment planning with reduced margins — no impact on delivery or quality assurance
integrated.e: [EQ+DOS+TPS+RB+QA+RP] DIBH: (1) Heart moves away from chest wall → LAD artery dose drops 50-80%. (2) Must verify position reproducibility (AlignRT/SGRT or RPM). (3) Plan should be robust to small position variations. (4) RB: mean heart dose <4 Gy reduces cardiac mortality. (5) Gating window typically 3-5 mm. (6) Each breath hold 15-30 seconds. Multiple per field.

integrated.q: I-131 thyroid therapy in SA: physics domains spanning nuclear medicine, radiation protection, dosimetry, and regulation?
integrated.a: NM (uptake measurement, dosimetry), RP (patient isolation, discharge criteria, family dose), REG (SAHPRA notification), and DOS (thyroid dose calculation)
c: Only nuclear medicine physics is involved — standard therapy with no other domain interaction
NM (uptake measurement, dosimetry), RP (patient isolation, discharge criteria, family dose), REG (SAHPRA notification), and DOS (thyroid dose calculation)
Only radiation protection for staff — patient dosimetry is not required for I-131 therapy
Only regulatory compliance — I-131 therapy requires no physics involvement beyond licensing
integrated.e: [NM+RP+DOS+REG+RB] I-131 therapy: (1) NM: thyroid uptake scan (I-123 or I-131 tracer), whole-body dosimetry for metastases. (2) RP: patient room shielding, dose rate at 1m for discharge, family member dose estimates. (3) DOS: MIRD-based organ dosimetry. (4) REG: SAHPRA licence for therapeutic NM, waste management, contaminated linen protocol. (5) RB: thyroid remnant ablation vs metastatic disease requires different activity prescription rationale.

integrated.q: CALC: I-131 patient: measured dose rate 60 uSv/hr at 1 m at discharge. Family member spends average 4 hours/day at 2 m for 5 days. Estimated cumulative dose to family member? (Ignore decay for simplification)
integrated.a: ~150 uSv
c: ~30 uSv
~150 uSv
~600 uSv
integrated.e: [NM+RP+DOS+REG] At 2m: 60 × (1/2)^2 = 15 uSv/hr. 4 hr/day × 5 days = 20 hours. 15 × 20 = 300 uSv. With I-131 decay (T½=8d), effective exposure is less. Simplified (without decay): ~300 uSv. With decay over 5 days: ~70% of non-decay value ≈ 210 uSv. Well under 5 mSv carer constraint. More accurate calc would integrate decay.

integrated.q: FFF (Flattening Filter Free) beam: advantages and physics considerations spanning equipment, dosimetry, TPS, and radiobiology?
integrated.a: Higher dose rate (SRS/SBRT), softer beam profile, different scatter conditions, and potential radiobiological advantage at ultra-high dose rates
c: Only faster delivery — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Higher dose rate (SRS/SBRT), softer beam profile, different scatter conditions, and potential radiobiological advantage at ultra-high dose rates
Only lower head scatter — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
No physics difference from flattened beam — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
integrated.e: [EQ+DOS+TPS+RB+SRT+QA] FFF: (1) EQ: remove flattening filter → 2-4× higher dose rate (1200-2400 MU/min). (2) DOS: peaked profile (not flat), different PDD (softer), reduced head scatter. (3) TPS: beam model must accurately capture peaked profile. (4) RB: ultra-high dose rate may have radiobiological implications. (5) SRT: shorter treatment times for SRS/SBRT. (6) QA: profile constancy checks different from flat beams.

integrated.q: Total skin electron therapy (TSET): which physics domains make this uniquely challenging?
integrated.a: DOS (6-field technique, dose uniformity), EQ (extended SSD ~3-4m, beam spoiler), QA (in-vivo dosimetry at multiple body sites), RB (protecting nails, eyes, soles), RP
c: Same as standard electron treatment — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
DOS (6-field technique, dose uniformity), EQ (extended SSD ~3-4m, beam spoiler), QA (in-vivo dosimetry at multiple body sites), RB (protecting nails, eyes, soles), RP
Only dosimetry is different — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Only patient positioning — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
integrated.e: [DOS+EQ+QA+RB+RP+TPS] TSET (Stanford/modified technique): (1) 6 dual-field positions at extended SSD. (2) Beam spoiler for dose buildup. (3) Dose uniformity across entire skin (±10%). (4) In-vivo TLD/OSLD at 20+ body points. (5) Boost fields for underdosed areas (soles, perineum, scalp vertex). (6) Eye shields, nail protection. (7) Typically 36 Gy in 8-9 weeks. One of the most technically demanding RT techniques.

integrated.q: Monaco biological cost function optimisation vs Eclipse physical dose objectives: fundamental difference for VMAT planning?
integrated.a: Monaco uses TCP/NTCP-based biological objectives inherently
c: No real difference — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
Monaco uses TCP/NTCP-based biological objectives inherently
Eclipse uses biological only
Monaco cannot do physical objectives
integrated.e: Eclipse optimises dose-volume objectives that can include biological models as secondary evaluation. [TPS+RB+DOS+QA] Monaco: cost functions include biological (equivalent uniform dose, serial/parallel organ models). Plan quality driven by radiobiological parameters. Eclipse: primarily physical DVH objectives; biological evaluation (NTCP models) available but not the primary optimiser. RayStation: offers both. Planner must understand the optimisation philosophy of their specific TPS.
