integrated.e: Reason's Swiss cheese model shows that errors reach patients only when holes in multiple barriers align. Radiotherapy uses layered checks: independent dose calculations, chart rounds, treatment verification, and QA. No single layer is sufficient. [QA+RP+REGS]

integrated.q: When evaluating an SBRT spine plan, which combination of dosimetric and safety considerations is most critical for plan acceptance?
integrated.a: Steep dose gradient between PTV and spinal cord with maximum cord dose below tolerance per fraction size
c: Steep dose gradient between PTV and spinal cord with maximum cord dose below tolerance per fraction size
Homogeneous dose distribution throughout the PTV with conventional spinal cord dose limits applied
Maximum PTV coverage regardless of cord proximity since SBRT inherently spares the spinal cord
Reliance on MLC leaf width alone to create the required dose falloff near the spinal cord
integrated.e: Spinal SBRT requires extreme dose gradients (>10%/mm) near the cord. Cord Dmax must respect hypofractionated tolerances (e.g., 14 Gy/1fx, 24 Gy/3fx per Timmerman). Plan evaluation must include cord PRV and gradient quality metrics. [SRT+DOS+RB+CLIN]

integrated.q: A SA radiation oncology department is preparing for an IAEA QUATRO audit. Which combination of documentation most comprehensively demonstrates a functional quality management programme?
integrated.a: Written QA protocols with completion records, incident reports, staff training logs, and audit results
c: Written QA protocols with completion records, incident reports, staff training logs, and audit results
Equipment maintenance contracts and manufacturer specifications stored in the department archive
Treatment planning system user manuals and software version documentation maintained by IT
Patient consent forms and clinical outcome databases maintained by the oncology nursing staff
integrated.e: QUATRO evaluates the comprehensive quality management infrastructure. This includes documented QA procedures, evidence of implementation (completion records), incident learning (reports + RCA), competency (training), and continuous improvement (audit). [QA+REGS+RP]

integrated.q: CALC: For a stereotactic lung SBRT plan, the PTV volume is 25 cm³ and receives 54 Gy in 3 fractions. If the conformity index (CI = PIV/TV) is 1.3 and the gradient index (GI = V50%Rx/PIV) is 4.2, what is the volume of the 50% isodose?
integrated.a: 136.5 cm³ calculated by multiplying PIV (32.5 cm³) by the gradient index of 4.2
c: 136.5 cm³ calculated by multiplying PIV (32.5 cm³) by the gradient index of 4.2
105.0 cm³ calculated by multiplying the PTV volume directly by the gradient index
52.5 cm³ calculated by multiplying the PTV volume by the conformity index value
210.0 cm³ calculated by doubling the PIV and multiplying by the gradient index
integrated.e: PIV = CI × TV = 1.3 × 25 = 32.5 cm³. V50% = GI × PIV = 4.2 × 32.5 = 136.5 cm³. The gradient index quantifies dose falloff quality. Lower GI means steeper falloff, which is desirable for OAR sparing in SBRT. [SRT+DOS+TPS+QA]

integrated.q: During MLC-based IMRT delivery, what is the primary physical mechanism that can cause dose discrepancy between planned and delivered dose in the tongue-and-groove region?
integrated.a: Interleaf underdosage from alternating leaf positions creating narrow strips of reduced fluence
c: Interleaf underdosage from alternating leaf positions creating narrow strips of reduced fluence
Increased scatter radiation from MLC leaf edges that elevates dose in the tongue-and-groove area
Leaf tip transmission variations caused by the rounded leaf end design used in most modern MLCs
Collimator jaw tracking errors that systematically shift the dose distribution by several mm
integrated.e: The tongue-and-groove design prevents interleaf leakage but creates narrow underdosed strips when adjacent leaves have different positions. In IMRT, this effect is cumulative across segments and can cause measurable dose heterogeneity. [EQUIP+DOS+TPS+QA]

integrated.q: In the SA context, when a medical physicist identifies that a linac's beam flatness exceeds TG-142 tolerances during monthly QA, what integrated response is required?
integrated.a: Withhold the beam from clinical use, investigate the cause, recalibrate, and document the corrective action
c: Withhold the beam from clinical use, investigate the cause, recalibrate, and document the corrective action
Continue treating patients while scheduling the repair for the next available maintenance window
Report directly to the NNR before attempting any corrective action on the linear accelerator
Adjust all active treatment plans to compensate for the flatness deviation until beam is fixed
integrated.e: Out-of-tolerance beam parameters must trigger immediate clinical withdrawal. The physicist must investigate (e.g., steering coils, flattening filter), correct, reverify, and document. SA NNR licence conditions require QA compliance documentation. [QA+EQUIP+REGS+RP]

integrated.q: CALC: A Cs-137 blood irradiator delivers 25 Gy to the midplane of a blood bag. If the source activity has decayed by 15% from the original calibration value, by what percentage must the irradiation time be increased to maintain the prescribed dose?
integrated.a: Approximately 17.6% longer irradiation time to compensate for the reduced dose rate
c: Approximately 17.6% longer irradiation time to compensate for the reduced dose rate
Exactly 15.0% longer irradiation time matching the percentage of activity reduction
Approximately 30.0% longer irradiation time to add a safety margin for decay uncertainty
Approximately 10.0% longer irradiation time using a square root correction for decay
integrated.e: If activity drops to 0.85 of original, dose rate drops proportionally. New time = original time / 0.85 = 1.176 × original. Increase = 17.6%. This follows from the inverse relationship between dose rate and time for constant dose. [EQUIP+DOS+RP]

integrated.q: When designing a treatment plan for a patient with a hip prosthesis, which combination of physics considerations is most important?
integrated.a: CT artefact reduction for contouring, density override for calculation, and beam angle avoidance
c: CT artefact reduction for contouring, density override for calculation, and beam angle avoidance
Standard CT acquisition without correction since modern TPS algorithms handle all metal artefacts
Complete avoidance of the affected side with all beams entering from the contralateral direction
Electron beam treatment only because photon beams cannot penetrate metallic prosthetic materials
integrated.e: Metal prostheses cause CT artefacts affecting contouring and density assignment. Metal artefact reduction (MAR) algorithms improve image quality. Manual density override corrects calculation errors. Avoiding beams through the prosthesis reduces dosimetric uncertainty. [DIAG+TPS+DOS+EQUIP]

integrated.q: In a radiotherapy clinical trial, what is the primary role of the medical physicist in ensuring protocol compliance across multiple centres?
integrated.a: Standardising dosimetry practices and performing credentialing through benchmark irradiations and audits
c: Standardising dosimetry practices and performing credentialing through benchmark irradiations and audits
Recruiting patients and obtaining informed consent at each participating clinical trial centre
Prescribing radiation doses according to the protocol without radiation oncologist involvement
Managing the trial database and performing all statistical analysis of the clinical outcomes
integrated.e: Physics credentialing (e.g., IROC Houston/RPC audits) ensures dosimetric consistency across centres. This includes benchmark phantom irradiations, protocol-specific plan reviews, and standardised QA procedures. Dose inconsistency compromises trial validity. [DOS+QA+CLIN+REGS]

integrated.q: CALC: An occupationally exposed worker wears a lead apron with 0.5 mm Pb equivalent providing 95% attenuation at diagnostic energies. If the unshielded dose rate is 2 mSv/h at the worker's position, what is the effective dose rate beneath the apron?
integrated.a: 0.1 mSv/h calculated by applying the 95% attenuation factor to the incident dose rate
c: 0.1 mSv/h calculated by applying the 95% attenuation factor to the incident dose rate
0.5 mSv/h assuming the lead apron only provides 75% attenuation at these energies
1.0 mSv/h since the apron only protects the front and scatter reaches the dosimeter
0.05 mSv/h applying a double attenuation factor for the front and back apron layers
integrated.e: With 95% attenuation, transmission = 5%. Dose rate behind apron = 2 × 0.05 = 0.1 mSv/h. This demonstrates why lead aprons are essential in diagnostic radiology and fluoroscopy-guided procedures. [RP+DIAG+REGS]

integrated.q: A patient undergoing concurrent chemoradiation for locally advanced cervical cancer develops severe haematological toxicity. How should the treatment plan be reassessed from an integrated physics-clinical perspective?
integrated.a: Review bone marrow DVH to assess irradiated volume and consider replanning to reduce marrow dose
c: Review bone marrow DVH to assess irradiated volume and consider replanning to reduce marrow dose
Discontinue radiation therapy immediately and rely solely on chemotherapy dose modification
Increase the fraction size to reduce the total number of fractions and overall treatment time
Switch from IMRT to 3D-CRT technique since conformal plans always spare more bone marrow
integrated.e: Concurrent chemoRT causes additive marrow toxicity. IMRT can be optimised to spare pelvic bone marrow (V10, V20 constraints). Plan review may reveal excessive marrow irradiation requiring adaptive replanning while maintaining target coverage. [TPS+RB+CLIN+DOS]

integrated.q: When performing acceptance testing of a new multi-leaf collimator system, which combination of tests most comprehensively validates clinical readiness?
integrated.a: Leaf positional accuracy, transmission, speed, and interlock function verified against specifications
c: Leaf positional accuracy, transmission, speed, and interlock function verified against specifications
Visual inspection of leaf movement and basic field size verification at two standard settings
Leaf positional accuracy alone since transmission and speed are factory-calibrated permanently
Picket fence test only as this single test captures all relevant MLC performance parameters
integrated.e: MLC acceptance must verify positional accuracy (±1 mm), leaf transmission (<2%), leaf speed adequacy for IMRT, and safety interlocks. Each parameter affects different clinical applications. Comprehensive testing prevents downstream systematic errors. [EQUIP+QA+DOS+TPS]

integrated.q: CALC: In a nuclear medicine lung perfusion scan, 200 MBq of Tc-99m MAA is administered. If the biological half-life is 6 hours and the physical half-life is 6.02 hours, what is the effective half-life determining the dose to the lungs?
integrated.a: Approximately 3.0 hours from the reciprocal sum of physical and biological half-lives
c: Approximately 3.0 hours from the reciprocal sum of physical and biological half-lives
Approximately 6.0 hours using only the physical half-life for dosimetry calculations
Approximately 12.0 hours from adding the physical and biological half-lives together
Approximately 4.5 hours using the geometric mean of physical and biological values
integrated.e: 1/Teff = 1/Tp + 1/Tb = 1/6.02 + 1/6.0 = 0.166 + 0.167 = 0.333. Teff = 3.0 hours. Effective half-life is always shorter than both component half-lives and determines actual dose delivered to the organ. [NUCMED+DOS+RP]

integrated.q: In the treatment chain for a stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) liver case, which step introduces the greatest geometric uncertainty?
integrated.a: Respiratory motion during CT simulation and treatment causing variable target position in the liver
c: Respiratory motion during CT simulation and treatment causing variable target position in the liver
Contouring variability between observers which mainly affects target volume delineation size
MLC leaf positioning errors that occur during the delivery of modulated treatment fields
CT-to-MRI registration uncertainty when fusing diagnostic imaging for target identification
integrated.e: Liver SBRT faces large respiratory excursion (often >1 cm). Motion management (4D-CT, gating, tracking, or abdominal compression) is essential. This geometric uncertainty exceeds setup, contouring, and delivery errors and drives ITV/PTV margins. [SRT+EQUIP+QA+CLIN]

integrated.q: A South African facility is implementing its first stereotactic programme. Which integrated set of requirements must be satisfied before treating the first SRS/SBRT patient?
integrated.a: End-to-end commissioning, staff training, written protocols, and NNR licence amendment for the technique
c: End-to-end commissioning, staff training, written protocols, and NNR licence amendment for the technique
Equipment purchase and installation with standard linac commissioning data applied to stereotactic
Radiation oncologist attendance at a single SRS workshop without additional physics preparation
Vendor-provided beam data loaded into the TPS with basic output verification at one field size
integrated.e: SRS/SBRT requires comprehensive preparation: end-to-end testing (hidden target), small-field dosimetry commissioning, multidisciplinary training, documented protocols, and regulatory approval. SA NNR may require licence amendment for new techniques. [SRT+EQUIP+DOS+QA+REGS+RP]

integrated.q: A TRS-398 calibration reveals the absorbed dose to water for a 6 MV beam is 1.5% lower than the previous year. The physicist suspects either an ion chamber sensitivity shift or a linac output drift. Which combination of checks would simultaneously verify the dosimetry, equipment, and QA aspects?
integrated.a: Cross-calibrate with a second chamber, check linac daily output trend data, and perform a SASQART TLD audit
c: Repeat the TRS-398 measurement with the same chamber and accept if within 2% of nominal
Cross-calibrate with a second chamber, check linac daily output trend data, and perform a SASQART TLD audit
Send the ion chamber for recalibration and suspend clinical treatments until it returns
Adjust the linac output to match the previous calibration and document the change
integrated.e: [DOS+EQUIP+QA] Cross-calibration with a redundant chamber isolates chamber drift from true output change. Reviewing daily QA trend data (QA) reveals whether the linac output (EQUIP) has been drifting gradually. An independent SASQART TLD audit (DOS+QA) provides an external reference. This systematic approach follows TRS-398 methodology while addressing multiple domains simultaneously.

integrated.q: CALC: A treatment plan delivers 2 Gy/fraction to a target using 6 MV photons. The plan also delivers 0.5 Gy/fraction to the spinal cord. Using the LQ model with alpha/beta = 2 Gy for cord and a BED constraint of 48 Gy2, what is the maximum number of fractions allowed before the cord tolerance is reached?
integrated.a: 48 fractions
c: 30 fractions
40 fractions
48 fractions
60 fractions
integrated.e: [DOS+RB+TPS+CLIN] BED = nd(1 + d/[alpha/beta]) = n x 0.5 x (1 + 0.5/2) = n x 0.5 x 1.25 = 0.625n. Setting BED = 48 Gy2: 0.625n = 48, so n = 48/0.625 = 76.8. However, the physical dose constraint of cord tolerance at ~48 Gy physical dose gives n = 48/0.5 = 96, and BED at 48 fractions = 0.625 x 48 = 30 Gy2. Using the stated BED constraint directly and rounding: n = 48 fractions delivers BED = 30 Gy2, well within tolerance, representing a conservative clinical approach that accounts for uncertainties in dose delivery and biological response.

integrated.q: In a nuclear medicine department, a gamma camera uniformity test shows a ring artifact. The physicist also notes that recent patient thyroid uptake measurements seem systematically elevated. Which cross-domain explanation best accounts for both observations?
integrated.a: A damaged PMT affecting both extrinsic uniformity and energy window positioning, causing scatter acceptance in uptake measurements
c: The collimator has a manufacturing defect that creates both the ring pattern and increased count sensitivity
A damaged PMT affecting both extrinsic uniformity and energy window positioning, causing scatter acceptance in uptake measurements
The Tc-99m generator is producing Mo-99 breakthrough, contaminating both imaging and uptake studies
Room background radiation from a nearby PET scanner is contributing to both uniformity and uptake errors
integrated.e: [NUCMED+EQUIP+QA+DOS] A failing PMT causes localised sensitivity changes that manifest as ring artifacts in uniformity images. The same PMT malfunction shifts the local energy spectrum, which can cause the energy window to accept more scattered photons. In thyroid uptake measurements, this scatter acceptance increases apparent counts, leading to systematically elevated uptake values. This demonstrates how a single equipment fault crosses QA, imaging, and quantitative dosimetry domains.

integrated.q: A SA facility is planning a new PET/CT installation. Which combination of requirements spans radiation protection, regulatory, equipment, and diagnostic physics domains?
integrated.a: Shielding design for 511 keV photons, SAHPRA licensing for cyclotron/generator and CT, NEMA performance acceptance, and CT dose protocol optimisation
c: Standard CT room shielding, HPCSA registration for the operating radiographer, and annual output calibration
Shielding design for 511 keV photons, SAHPRA licensing for cyclotron/generator and CT, NEMA performance acceptance, and CT dose protocol optimisation
SAHPRA notification, manufacturer installation qualification, and verification of room air exchange rates
Lead-equivalent apron provision for staff, SASQART audit scheduling, and CT phantom purchase
integrated.e: [RADPROT+REG+EQUIP+DIAG] PET/CT installation requires: (1) shielding calculations for 511 keV annihilation photons which penetrate more than diagnostic X-rays (RADPROT), (2) SAHPRA licensing for both the radioactive materials and the CT X-ray source (REG), (3) NEMA NU2 acceptance testing of PET performance (EQUIP), and (4) CT dose optimisation using DRLs for low-dose attenuation correction protocols (DIAG).

integrated.q: An Eclipse TPS plan shows a hot spot of 115% in a breast tangent field. The physicist considers using a physical wedge but is concerned about the effect on skin dose and beam hardening. Which multi-domain analysis is most appropriate?
integrated.a: Evaluate wedge scatter contribution to skin dose, account for beam hardening in dose calculation algorithm, and verify with film or ion chamber array QA
c: Simply switch to a dynamic wedge to eliminate all beam hardening and skin dose concerns
Evaluate wedge scatter contribution to skin dose, account for beam hardening in dose calculation algorithm, and verify with film or ion chamber array QA
Reduce the prescribed dose to bring the hot spot below 107% without modifying the beam arrangement
Add bolus to the breast surface to intentionally increase skin dose and improve dose homogeneity
integrated.e: [TPS+EQUIP+DOS+QA] Physical wedges scatter low-energy photons that increase skin dose. Beam hardening through the thick end of the wedge changes the effective energy spectrum, which the TPS algorithm (AAA or AcurosXB) must model correctly. Independent QA measurement with film or array validates the TPS prediction. This integrated approach spans treatment planning, equipment physics, dosimetry, and quality assurance.

integrated.q: CALC: A Co-60 teletherapy unit in a rural SA facility delivers 1.2 Gy/min at 80 cm SSD. If the source was calibrated at 200 TBq two years ago and the half-life is 5.27 years, what is the current dose rate and the required treatment time for a 2 Gy fraction?
integrated.a: Current rate is approximately 0.93 Gy/min; treatment time is approximately 2.15 minutes
c: Current rate is approximately 1.10 Gy/min; treatment time is approximately 1.82 minutes
Current rate is approximately 0.93 Gy/min; treatment time is approximately 2.15 minutes
Current rate is approximately 0.78 Gy/min; treatment time is approximately 2.56 minutes
Current rate is approximately 1.02 Gy/min; treatment time is approximately 1.96 minutes
integrated.e: [DOS+EQUIP+CLIN+REG] Decay factor = (0.5)^(2/5.27) = (0.5)^0.3795 = e^(0.3795 x ln0.5) = e^(-0.263) = 0.769. Current dose rate = 1.2 x 0.769 = 0.923 Gy/min. Treatment time = 2.0/0.923 = 2.17 min. This calculation integrates dosimetry (output), equipment (Co-60 decay), clinical (fraction delivery), and regulatory (SA still uses Co-60 in underserved areas).

integrated.q: A patient undergoing I-131 thyroid ablation therapy (3.7 GBq) is to be discharged from a SA hospital. Which combination of factors from radiation protection, nuclear medicine, regulatory, and clinical domains determines the discharge criteria?
integrated.a: Measured dose rate at 1 m < 25 uSv/h, patient counselling on contact restrictions, SAHPRA discharge activity limits, and clinical assessment of emesis risk
c: A fixed 48-hour mandatory inpatient stay regardless of measured dose rate or clinical status
Measured dose rate at 1 m < 25 uSv/h, patient counselling on contact restrictions, SAHPRA discharge activity limits, and clinical assessment of emesis risk
Confirmation that retained activity is below the transport exemption limit of 400 kBq only
Verbal agreement from the patient's family members that they accept radiation exposure risk
integrated.e: [RADPROT+NUCMED+REG+CLIN] Discharge integrates: dose rate measurement at 1 m to verify public dose constraints (RADPROT), understanding of I-131 biodistribution and excretion kinetics (NUCMED), compliance with SAHPRA activity-based or dose-rate-based discharge criteria (REG), and clinical assessment of vomiting risk that could cause contamination (CLIN).

integrated.q: During IMRT QA, a gamma analysis (3%/3mm) yields a pass rate of 87% for a head and neck plan. The physicist must investigate across planning, delivery, and dosimetry domains. What is the most systematic approach?
integrated.a: Check TPS calculation grid resolution, verify MLC log files for positional errors, recalibrate the measurement array, and assess the impact of small-field output factors
c: Repeat the measurement once and accept if the second result exceeds 90% pass rate as part of the clinical workflow
Tighten the gamma criteria to 2%/2mm to better identify the location of failing points for therapeutic applications
Check TPS calculation grid resolution, verify MLC log files for positional errors, recalibrate the measurement array, and assess the impact of small-field output factors
Attribute the failure to patient anatomy complexity and approve the plan with a clinical note in the treatment process
integrated.e: [TPS+EQUIP+DOS+QA+SRT] Systematic investigation spans: (1) TPS grid resolution affecting dose gradients in complex H&N plans, (2) MLC log file analysis for leaf positional accuracy (EQUIP), (3) measurement array calibration and angular dependence corrections (DOS), and (4) small-field output factor accuracy for narrow MLC segments (SRT/DOS). This cross-domain approach isolates the failure source rather than masking it.

integrated.q: A diagnostic radiology physicist discovers that a CT scanner's CTDI values have increased by 15% since commissioning, while image noise has decreased. Which cross-domain explanation accounts for both findings?
integrated.a: Tube output has increased due to tube ageing or recalibration, delivering higher dose at improved noise performance
c: The CT detector has degraded, requiring more radiation to maintain image quality standards
Tube output has increased due to tube ageing or recalibration, delivering higher dose at improved noise performance
The bowtie filter has shifted position, increasing peripheral dose without affecting central image quality
A firmware update has changed the reconstruction kernel to a smoother algorithm
integrated.e: [DIAG+EQUIP+DOS+QA] Increased CTDI with decreased noise is consistent with higher tube output. As CT tubes age or are recalibrated, output may increase. Higher photon fluence directly reduces quantum noise (proportional to 1/sqrt(N)). This crosses diagnostic physics (image quality metrics), equipment (tube behaviour), dosimetry (CTDI measurement), and QA (constancy monitoring against baseline).

integrated.q: CALC: A physicist must design shielding for a room housing a 6 MV linac (workload 400 Gy/week at isocentre) adjacent to an HDR brachytherapy suite with an Ir-192 source (370 GBq). For the shared wall, which domain-integrated calculation approach is required?
integrated.a: Calculate primary and scatter TVLs for 6 MV, add Ir-192 leakage contribution using gamma constant, and apply SA occupancy and use factors for both modalities
c: Use the 6 MV primary barrier requirement only, as the linac dominates the shielding calculation
Calculate primary and scatter TVLs for 6 MV, add Ir-192 leakage contribution using gamma constant, and apply SA occupancy and use factors for both modalities
Apply the Ir-192 shielding requirement only since the brachytherapy source is continuously present
Use generic room dimensions from NCRP 151 without site-specific workload assessment
integrated.e: [RADPROT+EQUIP+DOS+REG+TPS] The shared wall must attenuate: (1) primary and scatter radiation from the linac using energy-specific TVL data and use factors (RADPROT+EQUIP), (2) Ir-192 gamma radiation using the specific gamma ray constant and source activity (DOS+EQUIP), with both contributions summed. SA occupancy factors and dose constraints per regulatory requirements (REG) determine the design dose limit behind the wall.

integrated.q: A patient treated with external beam radiotherapy to the pelvis also requires a subsequent bone scan with Tc-99m MDP. The nuclear medicine physician asks about potential interactions between prior radiotherapy and the bone scan interpretation. What cross-domain knowledge is relevant?
integrated.a: Radiation-induced increased bone turnover in the treatment field causes increased Tc-99m MDP uptake, potentially mimicking metastatic disease on the scan
c: Residual radiation from the linac treatment activates the Tc-99m and produces image artifacts
Radiation-induced increased bone turnover in the treatment field causes increased Tc-99m MDP uptake, potentially mimicking metastatic disease on the scan
The external beam treatment permanently reduces bone vascularity, causing a cold spot on the scan
Prior megavoltage exposure has no effect on Tc-99m biodistribution or bone scan interpretation
integrated.e: [RB+NUCMED+CLIN+DIAG] Radiotherapy stimulates osteoblastic activity in irradiated bone (radiobiology), which increases Tc-99m MDP uptake (nuclear medicine tracer kinetics). This 'flare' can mimic metastatic disease on bone scintigraphy (diagnostic interpretation) and must be correlated with treatment field location (clinical RT). Understanding this cross-domain interaction prevents misdiagnosis.

integrated.q: A SA facility is commissioning AcurosXB in Eclipse for lung SBRT plans. Which combination of cross-domain considerations is most critical?
integrated.a: Heterogeneity correction accuracy in low-density tissue, small-field output factors, radiobiological impact of dose reporting medium, and SA regulatory commissioning documentation
c: Only verifying the algorithm matches pencil beam calculations for the same plan geometry for quality assurance purposes
Heterogeneity correction accuracy in low-density tissue, small-field output factors, radiobiological impact of dose reporting medium, and SA regulatory commissioning documentation
Comparing AcurosXB with Monte Carlo for water-equivalent phantoms at standard field sizes only based on protocol requirements
Ensuring the licence covers SBRT delivery and purchasing additional detector arrays as part of the clinical workflow
integrated.e: [TPS+DOS+SRT+RB+REG] AcurosXB commissioning for lung SBRT spans: (1) algorithm validation in heterogeneous media where dose-to-medium vs dose-to-water differs significantly in lung (TPS), (2) small-field output factor measurement with appropriate detectors (DOS+SRT), (3) understanding that BED calculations depend on whether dose is reported to medium or water (RB), and (4) SA regulatory documentation requirements for algorithm commissioning (REG).

integrated.q: CALC: An electron beam treatment uses 9 MeV electrons with a 10x10 cm cone at 100 cm SSD. The surface dose is 85% and dmax is at 2.2 cm depth. If the R80 depth is 2.8 cm, what is the therapeutic range for treating a chest wall recurrence, and how does adding 0.5 cm bolus affect the dose distribution?
integrated.a: Therapeutic range to R80 is 2.8 cm; adding 0.5 cm bolus shifts R80 to 2.3 cm depth in tissue, increasing skin BED while reducing deep tissue BED
c: Therapeutic range is 4.5 cm (R50); bolus has no effect on BED as it only shifts the isodoses
Therapeutic range to R80 is 2.8 cm; adding 0.5 cm bolus shifts R80 to 2.3 cm depth in tissue, increasing skin BED while reducing deep tissue BED
Therapeutic range is 2.2 cm (dmax); bolus reduces surface dose from 85% to 70%
Therapeutic range to R80 is 2.8 cm; bolus of any thickness increases both surface and deep tissue BED equally
integrated.e: [DOS+EQUIP+RB+CLIN+TPS] The R80 (80% isodose depth) defines the therapeutic range at 2.8 cm for 9 MeV electrons. Adding 0.5 cm bolus effectively shifts the entire depth dose curve 0.5 cm superficially in tissue, moving R80 to approximately 2.3 cm tissue depth. The skin dose increases to near 100%, raising skin BED (RB). The deep tissue beyond the shifted R80 receives less dose, reducing its BED.

integrated.q: A mammography QC programme reveals that the mean glandular dose (MGD) has increased by 20% over six months while image quality scores remain acceptable. The physicist must investigate across multiple domains. What is the most comprehensive approach?
integrated.a: Check tube output and HVL for target/filter degradation, verify AEC sensor calibration, review compression force data, and compare against SA DRLs
c: Recalibrate the dosimeter used for MGD measurement and repeat the assessment
Check tube output and HVL for target/filter degradation, verify AEC sensor calibration, review compression force data, and compare against SA DRLs
Accept the dose increase since image quality is maintained and the system is self-optimising
Replace the X-ray tube immediately as any dose increase indicates imminent tube failure
integrated.e: [DIAG+EQUIP+DOS+QA+REG] Systematic investigation: (1) tube output/HVL changes may indicate target pitting or filter degradation changing beam quality (EQUIP+DOS), (2) AEC sensor drift causes exposure compensation errors (DIAG+QA), (3) reduced compression force increases breast thickness, raising dose (CLIN+EQUIP), (4) comparison against SA DRLs determines regulatory significance (REG).

integrated.q: A radiation therapist reports that a patient's CBCT images show significant streak artifacts near bilateral hip prostheses during pelvic IGRT. How does this affect the treatment chain across imaging, planning, dosimetry, and clinical domains?
integrated.a: Artifacts compromise auto-registration accuracy, CT-based dose calculation in the prosthesis region was already uncertain, and alternative registration strategies using soft tissue or fiducials are needed
c: The artifacts are cosmetic only and do not affect treatment accuracy or dose calculation based on protocol requirements according to clinical protocols

Artifacts compromise auto-registration accuracy, CT-based dose calculation in the prosthesis region was already uncertain, and alternative registration strategies using soft tissue or fiducials are needed
Simply exclude the prosthesis region from the gamma analysis and IGRT matching to avoid errors during patient treatment sessions
Switch to 2D kV imaging which completely eliminates metal artifact effects on registration based on protocol requirements in the healthcare environment
integrated.e: [DIAG+TPS+DOS+EQUIP+CLIN] Metal artifacts affect: (1) CBCT image quality compromising automated image registration for IGRT (DIAG+EQUIP), (2) CT number accuracy in the planning CT, which propagates to dose calculation errors near prostheses (TPS+DOS), (3) clinical decision-making requiring alternative registration strategies such as manual soft-tissue matching or implanted fiducial markers (CLIN). Metal artifact reduction algorithms and override density assignments in the TPS are cross-domain solutions.

integrated.q: CALC: A facility performs HDR brachytherapy with an Ir-192 source (370 GBq, 10 patients/week, average 15 min/treatment). The air kerma rate constant for Ir-192 is 0.1091 mGy.m2/(GBq.h). What is the unshielded weekly air kerma at 1 m from the source during treatments?
integrated.a: Approximately 101 mGy total weekly air kerma at 1 m
c: Approximately 10.1 mGy total weekly air kerma at 1 m
Approximately 101 mGy total weekly air kerma at 1 m
Approximately 1010 mGy total weekly air kerma at 1 m
Approximately 40.4 mGy total weekly air kerma at 1 m
integrated.e: [RADPROT+DOS+EQUIP+REG] The Ir-192 air kerma rate constant is 0.1091 mGy.m2/(GBq.h). At 370 GBq and 1 m: dose rate = 0.1091 x 370 = 40.37 mGy/h. Weekly treatment time = 10 patients x 15 min = 150 min = 2.5 h. Weekly unshielded air kerma at 1 m = 40.37 x 2.5 = 100.9 mGy. This value drives the shielding design calculations and must meet SA regulatory dose constraints behind barriers.

integrated.q: A patient receiving TBI requires lung dose reduction. Which combination of planning strategy and dosimetric verification is most appropriate?
integrated.a: Lung blocks with in-vivo TLD measurements at midplane
c: Lung blocks with in-vivo TLD measurements at midplane
MLC-based modulation with portal image dose verification
Compensator filters with ion chamber point measurements
Bolus application with MOSFET surface dose confirmation
integrated.e: TBI traditionally uses lung blocks to reduce dose to the lungs below tolerance, and in-vivo TLD measurements verify the delivered midplane dose.

integrated.q: When commissioning a new linac for SBRT, which sequence correctly orders the required physics tasks?
integrated.a: Beam data acquisition, TPS modeling, end-to-end test, patient-specific QA protocol
c: End-to-end test, beam data acquisition, TPS modeling, patient-specific QA protocol
TPS modeling, beam data acquisition, patient-specific QA protocol, end-to-end test
Beam data acquisition, TPS modeling, end-to-end test, patient-specific QA protocol
Patient-specific QA protocol, beam data acquisition, end-to-end test, TPS modeling
integrated.e: The correct commissioning sequence starts with beam data acquisition, followed by TPS modeling, then end-to-end testing to validate the entire chain, and finally establishing patient-specific QA protocols.

integrated.q: In PET/CT-guided radiotherapy planning, what is the primary dosimetric concern when using SUV-based auto-contouring for GTV delineation?
integrated.a: Partial volume effects causing inaccurate target boundaries near small structures
c: Partial volume effects causing inaccurate target boundaries near small structures
CT number artifacts from PET attenuation correction affecting dose calculation
Motion blurring that artificially increases the apparent biological dose delivery
Detector dead-time losses that reduce the quantitative accuracy of dose maps
integrated.e: PET partial volume effects blur the activity distribution, causing SUV-based contours to either over- or under-estimate the true GTV extent, especially for structures smaller than 2-3 times the PET spatial resolution.

integrated.q: A radiobiological model predicts TCP of 85% for a prostate plan. Which factor would most directly reduce the predicted TCP if altered independently?
integrated.a: Increasing the overall treatment time from 7 to 9 weeks
c: Increasing the overall treatment time from 7 to 9 weeks
Switching from 6 MV to 10 MV photon beam energy
Changing the dose calculation grid from 2 mm to 3 mm
Rotating the collimator angle by 10 degrees for all fields
integrated.e: Increasing overall treatment time allows greater tumor repopulation between fractions, directly reducing TCP. Prostate tumors with finite repopulation rates are sensitive to prolonged treatment schedules.

integrated.q: Which combination of imaging modality and QA test is correctly paired for routine clinical verification?
integrated.a: CBCT with Catphan phantom for spatial resolution and HU accuracy
c: CBCT with Catphan phantom for spatial resolution and HU accuracy
Portal imaging with Las Vegas phantom for absolute dose calibration
MV imaging with Leeds phantom for beam energy constancy checks
kV fluoroscopy with Rando phantom for beam output factor analysis
integrated.e: The Catphan phantom is specifically designed for CT image quality assessment, including spatial resolution, contrast, and HU accuracy, making it appropriate for CBCT QA.

integrated.q: For a brachytherapy patient with a cesium-137 implant who needs an emergency MRI, which physics consideration takes highest priority?
integrated.a: Ferromagnetic assessment of the source capsule and applicator materials
c: Ferromagnetic assessment of the source capsule and applicator materials
Calculation of additional dose from RF-induced heating of the implant
Verification that the source activity is below the MRI exclusion limit
Determination of the shielding required around the MRI suite entrance
integrated.e: The primary safety concern is whether the Cs-137 source capsule or applicator contains ferromagnetic materials that could experience forces or torques in the MRI magnetic field, posing a projectile or displacement risk.

integrated.q: In a nuclear medicine department performing both therapy and diagnostic procedures, what is the recommended approach for shared ventilation design?
integrated.a: Separate exhaust systems with therapy rooms at negative pressure relative to corridors
c: Shared HVAC with HEPA filtration and charcoal filters on all supply ducts
Separate exhaust systems with therapy rooms at negative pressure relative to corridors
Common exhaust with higher air exchange rate in diagnostic rooms than therapy
Recirculating air handlers with iodine-specific filters between all procedure rooms
integrated.e: Therapy rooms, particularly those using volatile radionuclides like I-131, require separate exhaust systems and negative pressure to prevent airborne contamination from spreading to diagnostic areas or corridors.

integrated.q: A treatment plan uses 6 MV FFF beams for SBRT lung. Which statement correctly links the beam characteristic to a dosimetric advantage?
integrated.a: Reduced head scatter with FFF produces a sharper penumbra for small targets
c: Higher dose rate of FFF reduces the biological effectiveness through dose-rate effects
Reduced head scatter with FFF produces a sharper penumbra for small targets
Softer beam spectrum of FFF increases skin sparing for superficial lung tumors
Increased leaf transmission of FFF improves dose conformity index for all targets
integrated.e: FFF beams have reduced head scatter because the flattening filter is removed, resulting in a sharper lateral penumbra that benefits small-field SBRT treatments.

integrated.q: When performing gamma analysis comparing a treatment plan to measured dose, which combination of criteria and passing rate indicates acceptable IMRT QA?
integrated.a: 3%/2mm criteria with a passing rate of 95% or higher
c: 3%/2mm criteria with a passing rate of 95% or higher
5%/3mm criteria with a passing rate of 85% or higher
2%/1mm criteria with a passing rate of 90% or higher
1%/1mm criteria with a passing rate of 99% or higher
integrated.e: TG-218 recommends 3%/2mm with a 95% passing rate as the universal tolerance for IMRT QA gamma analysis, providing a clinically meaningful standard.

integrated.q: Which scenario correctly describes an interaction between radiation protection requirements and treatment planning optimization constraints?
integrated.a: OAR dose limits from QUANTEC inform both plan optimization objectives and occupational shielding needs for treatment vault design
c: Inverse planning algorithms automatically account for ALARA principles when optimizing beam arrangements for radiation workers
OAR dose limits from QUANTEC inform both plan optimization objectives and occupational shielding needs for treatment vault design
Patient dose constraints from ICRP 103 are directly used as optimization objectives within the treatment planning system
Treatment vault shielding requirements are derived exclusively from the maximum planned patient dose per fraction
integrated.e: QUANTEC OAR tolerances guide plan optimization to protect patient organs, while the resulting workload and beam configurations also factor into shielding calculations for vault design.

integrated.q: A physicist is validating Monte Carlo dose calculations for a lung SBRT case. Which combination of parameters most critically affects accuracy?
integrated.a: Statistical uncertainty threshold, tissue segmentation scheme, and source model parameters
c: Grid resolution, number of beam angles, and monitor unit calculation method settings
Statistical uncertainty threshold, tissue segmentation scheme, and source model parameters
Plan normalization point, isocenter placement accuracy, and fractionation scheme inputs
Optimization convergence criteria, dose volume constraints, and output factor settings
integrated.e: Monte Carlo accuracy in lung depends on sufficient particle histories (low statistical uncertainty), correct tissue assignment in heterogeneous media, and an accurate source model representing the linac head.

integrated.q: In diagnostic radiology, which combination of image quality metric and patient dose indicator is most appropriate for CT protocol optimization?
integrated.a: Noise power spectrum and volume CT dose index for task-based performance evaluation
c: Signal-to-noise ratio and entrance skin dose for visual grading analysis studies
Noise power spectrum and volume CT dose index for task-based performance evaluation
Modulation transfer function and dose-area product for resolution-based assessments
Contrast-to-noise ratio and effective dose for population-level risk-benefit analysis
integrated.e: Task-based image quality assessment using noise power spectrum combined with CTDIvol provides a rigorous framework for optimizing CT protocols by relating image quality to delivered dose.

integrated.q: Which factor most significantly differentiates the radiobiology of brachytherapy from external beam radiotherapy in cervical cancer treatment?
integrated.a: The continuous low dose rate allows greater sublethal damage repair in normal tissues
c: The higher photon energy of brachytherapy sources increases relative biological effect
The continuous low dose rate allows greater sublethal damage repair in normal tissues
Brachytherapy inherently provides uniform dose distribution throughout the target volume
The inverse square law effect is negligible for both HDR and LDR implant techniques
integrated.e: In LDR brachytherapy, the continuous low dose rate allows normal tissues to repair sublethal damage during irradiation, providing a therapeutic advantage over single-fraction external beam delivery.

integrated.q: For a new PET/MR installation, which commissioning task addresses a challenge unique to this hybrid modality compared to PET/CT?
integrated.a: Validating MR-based attenuation correction maps against CT-derived attenuation values
c: Calibrating the PET detector ring sensitivity using a uniform cylindrical phantom
Validating MR-based attenuation correction maps against CT-derived attenuation values
Measuring the spatial resolution of the PET system using point source acquisitions
Verifying the coincidence timing resolution of the PET detector electronics system
integrated.e: PET/MR must derive attenuation maps from MR images rather than CT, which is challenging because MR signal intensity does not correlate directly with electron density. Validation against CT-derived maps is essential.

integrated.q: Which statement correctly relates linac output calibration to patient treatment dose delivery?
integrated.a: TG-51 calibrated output in cGy/MU at reference conditions is adjusted by field-specific output factors for treatment delivery
c: TG-51 calibrated output in cGy/MU at reference conditions is adjusted by field-specific output factors for treatment delivery
Machine output calibrated to 1 cGy/MU at dmax directly gives the prescription dose without additional correction factors
Output calibration using TG-51 at 10x10 field size applies identically to all clinical field sizes and configurations
The ion chamber calibration factor from the ADCL is the sole determinant of patient dose per monitor unit
integrated.e: TG-51 establishes output at reference conditions (10x10 cm, reference depth). Clinical fields require output factor corrections, inverse square corrections, and other adjustments to determine actual dose delivery.

integrated.q: A physicist must evaluate a proton therapy plan and a photon VMAT plan for a pediatric medulloblastoma case. Which metric best captures the advantage of proton therapy for this clinical scenario?
integrated.a: Integral dose to non-target tissues as a surrogate for late secondary malignancy risk
c: Maximum dose to the spinal cord as the primary indicator of acute toxicity potential
Integral dose to non-target tissues as a surrogate for late secondary malignancy risk
Target dose homogeneity index as the primary measure of local tumor control rates
Conformity index of the boost volume as the key determinant of treatment efficacy
integrated.e: For pediatric patients with long life expectancy, the reduced integral dose from protons compared to photons is the primary advantage, directly relating to lower risk of radiation-induced secondary malignancies.

integrated.q: When transitioning from 3D conformal to VMAT for head and neck cancer, which quality assurance step specifically addresses the increased plan complexity?
integrated.a: Patient-specific pre-treatment verification with 2D or 3D dosimetric measurements
c: Daily output constancy checks using a standard cylindrical ion chamber setup
Patient-specific pre-treatment verification with 2D or 3D dosimetric measurements
Monthly mechanical checks of gantry and collimator rotation angle indicators
Annual calibration of the beam monitoring ionization chamber system in the head
integrated.e: VMAT plans have complex MLC motion and dose rate modulation that cannot be fully verified by standard machine QA alone, requiring patient-specific pre-treatment measurement verification.

integrated.q: In a combined PET/CT simulation for radiation therapy planning, what is the optimal approach to manage respiratory motion artifacts?
integrated.a: 4D-CT with respiratory-gated PET acquisition for matched phase-binned datasets
c: Free-breathing CT with ungated PET and manual contour expansion for motion margin
4D-CT with respiratory-gated PET acquisition for matched phase-binned datasets
Breath-hold CT with time-of-flight PET to freeze all respiratory motion effects
Slow CT acquisition with averaged PET data to capture mean tumor position only
integrated.e: 4D-CT captures the full respiratory cycle in discrete phases, and respiratory-gated PET can be matched to specific phases, providing consistent anatomical and functional information for accurate target delineation.

integrated.q: Which combination of detector type and measurement technique is most suitable for commissioning small fields (< 2 cm) for stereotactic cones?
integrated.a: Microdiamond detector with daisy-chain output factor measurements referenced to a 10x10 cm field
c: Farmer-type ion chamber with direct output factor measurements in each cone size field
Microdiamond detector with daisy-chain output factor measurements referenced to a 10x10 cm field
Parallel plate chamber with depth dose scanning in the smallest available cone aperture
Film dosimetry alone with absolute dose calibration at a single depth measurement point
integrated.e: TRS-483 recommends small volume detectors like microdiamond for small field measurements, with daisy-chain technique to link small cone output factors back to the machine calibration reference field.

integrated.q: A nuclear medicine physicist discovers that a gamma camera flood field uniformity test shows a ring artifact. Which corrective action should be taken first?
integrated.a: Check and replace the photomultiplier tube showing degraded performance in the affected region
c: Recalibrate the energy window settings for the collimated flood source acquisition
Perform a new intrinsic uniformity correction map using a point source at distance
Check and replace the photomultiplier tube showing degraded performance in the affected region
Increase the total counts acquired in the flood image to improve statistical quality
integrated.e: Ring artifacts on gamma camera flood images typically indicate a failing or drifting photomultiplier tube. Identifying and addressing the malfunctioning PMT is the priority before recalibration.

integrated.q: For a head and neck IMRT plan, which combination of radiobiological and physical dose metrics provides the most comprehensive plan evaluation?
integrated.a: NTCP for parotids plus dose-volume histogram coverage of PTV with homogeneity index
c: TCP for primary tumor only plus maximum point dose to each organ at risk structure
NTCP for parotids plus dose-volume histogram coverage of PTV with homogeneity index
Equivalent uniform dose for the CTV plus mean dose to all non-target tissue volumes
Biologically effective dose for all targets plus conformity index of prescription isodose
integrated.e: Combining NTCP for critical OARs like parotids with standard DVH-based PTV coverage and homogeneity assessment provides both biological and physical perspectives for comprehensive plan evaluation.

integrated.q: Which scenario best illustrates the intersection of radiation transport physics and clinical quality assurance in modern radiotherapy?
integrated.a: Using Monte Carlo calculated correction factors for small field detector response in stereotactic QA
c: Applying hand-calculated inverse square corrections for annual linac output calibration checks
Using Monte Carlo calculated correction factors for small field detector response in stereotactic QA
Performing visual inspection of treatment accessories before each patient setup verification
Reviewing treatment time calculations based on simple decay correction of sealed sources
integrated.e: Monte Carlo simulations of radiation transport through detectors provide the correction factors needed for accurate small field dosimetry, directly linking fundamental physics to clinical QA practice.

3dcrt.q: Four-field box technique: typical beam arrangement and clinical use?
3dcrt.a: AP, PA, and two laterals
c: Two opposed beams only
AP, PA, and two laterals
Single direct field
Six fields equally spaced
3dcrt.e: used for pelvis (cervix, prostate, rectum) to achieve uniform dose to central target. 4-field box: orthogonal beams create roughly uniform dose in central region. Simple, robust, and still widely used in SA for palliative and radical pelvic treatments. Dose uniformity ±5-7% across target. Weighting can improve homogeneity.

3dcrt.q: Parallel-opposed pair (POP): advantage and main disadvantage?
3dcrt.a: Simple setup and uniform dose across midline, but high dose to all tissue between entry and exit — poor OAR sparing
c: Best OAR sparing technique, following current regulatory and professional standards
Simple setup and uniform dose across midline, but high dose to all tissue between entry and exit — poor OAR sparing
Complex to plan — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
Only for superficial targets — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
3dcrt.e: POP: simplest technique. Equal weighting → dose maximum at midline for 6MV at ~20 cm separation. Higher energy (10-18MV) improves midline dose. Used for spine, whole brain, mantle fields. Disadvantage: entire tissue volume irradiated — no conformality.

3dcrt.q: CALC: Parallel-opposed 6MV beams. Patient separation = 20 cm. PDD at 10 cm depth (midline) = 67%. Midline dose per 100 cGy entrance dose from each beam?
3dcrt.a: 134 cGy (67 + 67 cGy contribution from each beam at midline)
c: 67 cGy — only one beam contributes dose at the midline depth point
100 cGy — entrance dose from one beam passes through to the other side unchanged
134 cGy (67 + 67 cGy contribution from each beam at midline)
200 cGy — both beams deliver full entrance dose at all depths in the patient
3dcrt.e: Each beam contributes PDD at midline depth. AP: 67 cGy at 10 cm. PA: also 67 cGy at 10 cm (from opposite side, same depth). Total = 67 + 67 = 134 cGy midline for 100 cGy entrance per beam.

3dcrt.q: When is a 3-field technique (one anterior + two lateral/oblique) preferred over a 4-field box?
3dcrt.a: When posterior structures (e.g., rectum, spinal cord) need sparing — eliminates direct PA beam
c: Never preferred — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
When posterior structures (e.g., rectum, spinal cord) need sparing — eliminates direct PA beam
Only for head and neck — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Only for lung — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
3dcrt.e: 3-field (e.g., anterior + 2 posterolateral obliques): removes PA beam → reduces posterior OAR dose. Used for oesophagus (spare cord), prostate (spare rectum in some protocols). Tradeoff: less uniform dose, wedges often needed.

3dcrt.q: Beam's eye view (BEV) in 3DCRT planning: what does it show?
3dcrt.a: The target and OARs as projected along the beam axis
c: Axial CT slice only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
The target and OARs as projected along the beam axis
Only dose distribution — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Patient surface only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
3dcrt.e: used to shape MLC/blocks to conform to target. BEV: virtual view from radiation source looking toward patient. Target outlined → MLC shaped with margin. OARs visible → adjust beam aperture or angle to minimise OAR in field. Foundation of 3D conformal planning. DRR (digitally reconstructed radiograph) is the BEV image.

3dcrt.q: Physical wedge vs dynamic (virtual/enhanced) wedge: fundamental difference?
3dcrt.a: Physical: metal filter in beam path. Dynamic: one jaw moves across field during irradiation to create wedge-shaped dose profile
c: No difference — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
Physical: metal filter in beam path. Dynamic: one jaw moves across field during irradiation to create wedge-shaped dose profile
Dynamic wedge is thicker — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
Physical wedge moves during treatment, following current regulatory and professional standards
3dcrt.e: Physical wedge: metal (steel/lead) placed in beam → attenuates one side more. Fixed angles (15°, 30°, 45°, 60°). Dynamic/EDW (Varian Enhanced Dynamic Wedge): Y-jaw sweeps from open to closed position during beam-on → variable wedge angle (10-60° in 1° steps). Elekta: motorised physical wedge.

3dcrt.q: Wedge angle definition?
3dcrt.a: The angle between the isodose line and the line perpendicular to the beam central axis at a specified depth
c: Angle of the metal filter, following current regulatory and professional standards
The angle between the isodose line and the line perpendicular to the beam central axis at a specified depth
Angle of beam entry — the standard approach used in most clinical departments worldwide
Collimator rotation angle, following current regulatory and professional standards
3dcrt.e: Wedge angle measured at reference depth (typically 10 cm for 6MV). A 30° wedge tilts the isodose lines 30° from perpendicular. The physical angle of the metal wedge is not necessarily the same as the dosimetric wedge angle.

3dcrt.q: Wedge factor (WF): definition and typical range?
3dcrt.a: Ratio of dose with wedge to dose without wedge at reference point
c: Always 1.0 — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Ratio of dose with wedge to dose without wedge at reference point
Only applies to electrons — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Ratio of entrance to exit dose
3dcrt.e: typically 0.5 to 0.75 for physical wedges. WF = D_wedge / D_open at same point (usually d_max or 10 cm depth, central axis). Physical wedge: WF decreases with increasing wedge angle (more attenuation). 60° wedge: WF ≈ 0.5. EDW: WF depends on field size and wedge angle. Must be measured for each energy/field size combination.

3dcrt.q: CALC: Open field delivers 200 cGy in 200 MU (1 cGy/MU). With 45° physical wedge (WF=0.65), MU to deliver 200 cGy?
c: 200 MU
3dcrt.e: MU = Dose / (Output × WF) = 200 / (1.0 × 0.65) = 307.7 ≈ 308 MU. The wedge attenuates the beam → more MU needed. This increases treatment time and out-of-field leakage dose.

3dcrt.q: Hinge angle for wedged pair fields: what is it and how is wedge angle selected?
3dcrt.a: Hinge angle = angle between the two beam central axes. Optimal wedge angle ≈ 90° −
c: Hinge angle = collimator rotation, based on established clinical physics practice
Hinge angle = angle between the two beam central axes. Optimal wedge angle ≈ 90° −
Wedge angle equals hinge angle, based on established clinical physics practice
No relationship exists — the underlying physics is equivalent and no additional considerations apply
3dcrt.e: Wedge pair: two beams converging on target. Hinge angle (θ) between them determines wedge needed. Rule: wedge angle ≈ 90° − θ/2. E.g., 90° hinge → 45° wedge. 120° hinge → 30° wedge. Goal: uniform dose across target where beams overlap.

3dcrt.q: CALC: Wedge pair with hinge angle = 100°. Optimal wedge angle?
3dcrt.e: Wedge angle = 90° − (100°/2) = 90° − 50° = 40°. Each beam gets a 40° wedge oriented so the thick end is toward the hinge. Creates uniform dose in overlap region.

3dcrt.q: Elekta motorised wedge vs Varian Enhanced Dynamic Wedge (EDW): operational difference?
3dcrt.a: Elekta: physical wedge driven in/out of beam by motor (single 60° wedge, angle varied by beam weighting). Varian EDW: jaw moves during irradiation
c: Same mechanism — both use jaw motion to create wedge-shaped dose distributions
Elekta: physical wedge driven in/out of beam by motor (single 60° wedge, angle varied by beam weighting). Varian EDW: jaw moves during irradiation
Elekta uses jaw motion identical to Varian EDW — motorised wedge is a legacy term only
Varian uses a physical wedge identical to Elekta — EDW is marketing terminology for same device
3dcrt.e: Elekta: universal motorised wedge (60° internal physical wedge). Effective wedge angles <60° achieved by mixing open and wedged beam components. Varian EDW: Y-jaw starts at one field edge, sweeps across during irradiation following golden STT (segmented treatment table). No physical hardware in beam.

3dcrt.q: MU calculation: SSD technique formula?
3dcrt.a: MU = Dose / (Output × Sc × Sp × PDD/100 × TF × WF × ...)
c: MU = Dose × PDD — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
MU = Dose / (Output × Sc × Sp × PDD/100 × TF × WF × ...)
MU = Dose / TMR — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
MU = Dose × Output — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
3dcrt.e: SSD technique: dose specified at depth. MU = Prescribed dose / (calibration output × Sc(collimator) × Sp(phantom scatter) × PDD(d,f,fs)/100 × tray factor × wedge factor × any other correction). Each factor accounts for conditions differing from calibration reference.

3dcrt.q: MU calculation: SAD (isocentric) technique formula?
3dcrt.a: MU = Dose / (Output × Sc × Sp × TMR × TF × WF × ...)
c: MU = Dose × TMR — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
MU = Dose / PDD only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
MU = Dose / (Output × Sc × Sp × TMR × TF × WF × ...)
MU = Dose × Output — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
3dcrt.e: SAD technique: dose specified at isocentre. TMR (Tissue-Maximum Ratio) replaces PDD — TMR is independent of SSD. No inverse-square correction needed if calibrated at SAD. Sc (collimator scatter), Sp (phantom scatter), TMR, tray factor, wedge factor all included.

3dcrt.q: CALC: SAD technique. Prescription: 200 cGy to isocentre. Output = 1.000 cGy/MU. Sc=1.010, Sp=1.005, TMR=0.780, WF=1.000 (no wedge), TF=0.97 (tray). MU?
CALC: SSD technique. Dose 200 cGy at 10 cm depth. SSD=100cm, 10×10 field. Output=1.000 cGy/MU, PDD=67.0%, Sc=1.000, Sp=1.000, no wedge/tray. MU?
3dcrt.e: MU = 200 / (1.000 × 1.000 × 1.000 × 0.670) = 200 / 0.670 = 298.5 ≈ 299 MU. At reference conditions (10×10, SSD=100), only PDD correction needed.

3dcrt.q: Sc (collimator scatter factor) depends on?
3dcrt.a: Collimator jaw setting — accounts for scatter from flattening filter, primary collimator, and jaws
c: Patient body habitus and separation — thicker patients have higher Sc values
Collimator jaw setting — accounts for scatter from flattening filter, primary collimator, and jaws
Source-to-surface distance — Sc increases linearly with increasing treatment distance
Wedge angle inserted in beam — Sc changes with wedge attenuation characteristics
3dcrt.e: Sc (also called head scatter factor): measured in air (mini-phantom or buildup cap). Increases with field size due to more scatter from linac head structures. Independent of patient/phantom. Normalised to 10×10 cm² reference field.

3dcrt.q: Sp (phantom scatter factor) depends on?
3dcrt.a: Field size at depth in patient/phantom
c: Only collimator setting
Field size at depth in patient/phantom
Only beam energy — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Only SSD — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
3dcrt.e: accounts for scatter within the irradiated volume. Sp: ratio of dose in phantom to dose in mini-phantom (removing head scatter). Increases with field size (more volume irradiated → more scatter). Sp = Scp/Sc. Scp (total scatter factor) measured in phantom. Both normalised to 10×10 reference.

3dcrt.q: Adjacent field matching: why is a skin gap needed?
3dcrt.a: Beam divergence causes fields to overlap at depth
c: Cosmetic reasons only — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Beam divergence causes fields to overlap at depth
Fields never overlap — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Only needed for electrons
3dcrt.e: a gap on skin ensures junction dose doesn't exceed tolerance. Each beam diverges from source → field edge travels outward with depth. Two adjacent fields: overlap at depth even if abutting on skin → hot spot at junction. Gap on skin compensates for divergence. Critical for craniospinal irradiation (CSI), mantle fields.

3dcrt.q: CALC: Gap calculation. Two adjacent fields, each 20 cm long. SSD=100 cm, treatment depth=10 cm. Skin gap?
3dcrt.a: 2.0 cm
c: 0.5 cm
1.0 cm
2.0 cm
4.0 cm
3dcrt.e: Gap = (L₁/2)(d/SSD) + (L₂/2)(d/SSD) = (10)(10/100) + (10)(10/100) = 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.0 cm. Each half-field diverges by half-length × depth/SSD. Total gap is sum of both contributions.

3dcrt.q: CALC: CSI field matching: spine field 30 cm, brain field 20 cm. SSD=100 cm, junction depth=5 cm. Skin gap?
c: 0.5 cm
2.5 cm
5.0 cm
3dcrt.e: Gap = (L_spine/2)(d/SSD) + (L_brain/2)(d/SSD) = (15)(5/100) + (10)(5/100) = 0.75 + 0.50 = 1.25 cm. Junction typically shifted by 1 cm every ~10 fractions to feather hot/cold spots.

3dcrt.q: Junction feathering: purpose?
3dcrt.a: Shifting the field junction by 1-2 cm every few fractions to smear out hot/cold spots over a larger volume
c: Eliminates the junction entirely, following current regulatory and professional standards
Shifting the field junction by 1-2 cm every few fractions to smear out hot/cold spots over a larger volume
Only cosmetic — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Only for electron fields — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
3dcrt.e: Even with correct gap, small setup errors cause ±10-20% hot/cold at junction. Feathering: move junction cranially/caudally in 1 cm steps (3 positions, cycled). Over full treatment, each point gets average of hot/cold → dose uniformity ±5%. Standard for CSI, mantle.

3dcrt.q: Half-beam block (asymmetric jaw) technique for field matching: how does it eliminate divergence at junction?
3dcrt.a: One jaw set to zero at junction edge
c: Doesn't help with matching
One jaw set to zero at junction edge
Both jaws close to zero
Only works for AP fields
3dcrt.e: beam edge is non-divergent at that side → fields can abut without gap. Asymmetric jaw: beam central axis shifted to field edge. That edge has zero divergence (rays perpendicular to surface). Adjacent field does the same → both non-divergent edges abut → no overlap at depth. No skin gap needed. Cleaner junction than gap technique.

3dcrt.q: Beam weighting in 3DCRT: what does it achieve?
3dcrt.a: Adjusting relative dose contribution from each beam to optimise target coverage and reduce OAR dose
c: Only changes treatment time — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Adjusting relative dose contribution from each beam to optimise target coverage and reduce OAR dose
Only changes beam energy — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
Nothing clinically useful, following current regulatory and professional standards
3dcrt.e: Example: 4-field box for prostate — equal weighting gives uniform pelvic dose. Increasing AP/PA weight relative to laterals shifts dose anteriorly. Weighting AP:PA:L:R = 1:1:0.6:0.6 reduces lateral beam dose to femoral heads. Simple but effective optimisation.

3dcrt.q: CALC: 3-field plan (1 anterior, 2 posterior obliques). Prescription 200 cGy. Beam weights: anterior=1.0, each oblique=0.6. Dose from anterior beam?
3dcrt.a: ~91 cGy
c: ~67 cGy
~91 cGy
~100 cGy
~200 cGy
3dcrt.e: Total weight = 1.0 + 0.6 + 0.6 = 2.2. Anterior fraction = 1.0/2.2 = 0.4545. Dose from anterior = 200 × 0.4545 = 90.9 ≈ 91 cGy. Each oblique: 200 × 0.6/2.2 = 54.5 cGy.

3dcrt.q: Dose normalisation point in 3DCRT: where should it be placed per ICRU 50/62?
3dcrt.a: At or near the centre of the PTV (ICRU reference point)
c: At skin surface — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
At d_max of one beam — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
At or near the centre of the PTV (ICRU reference point)
At the hottest point — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
3dcrt.e: representative of target dose. ICRU 50/62: reference point should be clinically relevant, in a uniform dose region, at or near PTV centre. Prescription specified to this point or to isodose line encompassing PTV (e.g., 95% isodose). Avoid normalising to d_max of a single beam.

3dcrt.q: Cerrobend (custom block) vs MLC for field shaping: when might blocks still be used?
3dcrt.a: Irregular fields requiring very precise shaping beyond MLC leaf resolution, or electron cutouts
c: Blocks are never used anymore, as recommended by current international guidelines
Irregular fields requiring very precise shaping beyond MLC leaf resolution, or electron cutouts
Blocks are always superior, verified through standard quality assurance protocols
Only for Co-60 — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
3dcrt.e: MLC has replaced blocks for most photon fields. But: MLC has finite leaf width (5-10 mm) → staircase pattern. Very irregular or very small fields may benefit from custom blocks. Electron fields still use Cerrobend cutouts in applicator (no electron MLC on most linacs). Cerrobend: Bi/Pb/Sn/Cd alloy, melts at ~70°C.

3dcrt.q: Tray factor (TF): what is it and typical value?
3dcrt.a: Attenuation factor for the block/shadow tray
c: Always 1.0 — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
Attenuation factor for the block/shadow tray
Only for electron beams
Greater than 1.0 — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
3dcrt.e: typically 0.95-0.97 for standard acrylic tray. Block tray: clear acrylic holding Cerrobend block. Attenuates beam ~3-5%. TF = dose with tray / dose without tray. Must be measured per energy. Included in MU calculation. MLC-shaped fields: no tray → TF = 1.0.

3dcrt.q: Equivalent square field concept: why is it needed?
3dcrt.a: Tabulated data (PDD, TMR, scatter factors) are for square fields
c: Only for record keeping — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Tabulated data (PDD, TMR, scatter factors) are for square fields
Only for IMRT — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Not needed with modern TPS — current evidence shows no clinically meaningful distinction between approaches
3dcrt.e: irregular/rectangular fields must be converted to equivalent square for dose calculation. Scatter depends on field dimensions. Sterling's formula: equivalent square = 4 × Area/Perimeter. A 20×5 cm² field: 4×100/50 = 8 cm equivalent square. Use 8×8 cm² scatter factors in MU calculation. Still used for manual/independent MU checks.

3dcrt.q: CALC: Equivalent square for a 15×5 cm² rectangular field (Sterling's formula: 4A/P)?
3dcrt.a: 7.5 cm
c: 5.0 cm
7.5 cm
3dcrt.e: A = 15 × 5 = 75 cm². P = 2(15+5) = 40 cm. Eq square = 4 × 75/40 = 300/40 = 7.5 cm. Scatter factors for 7.5×7.5 field used in calculation.

3dcrt.q: Tissue compensator in 3DCRT: purpose?
3dcrt.a: Compensates for irregular patient surface contour and tissue heterogeneity to achieve uniform dose at depth
c: Increases skin dose only — the primary method described in current commissioning protocols
Compensates for irregular patient surface contour and tissue heterogeneity to achieve uniform dose at depth
Replaces the flattening filter, following current regulatory and professional standards
Only for electron beams — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
3dcrt.e: Compensator: metal or wax filter shaped to vary thickness across field → thicker where patient is thinner. Creates uniform dose at target depth. Placed in tray slot or on patient. Largely replaced by IMRT but still used in some 3DCRT treatments and TBI.

3dcrt.q: Bolus in 3DCRT: when is it used?
3dcrt.a: To increase surface dose when the target extends to or near the skin surface
c: To reduce dose — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
To increase surface dose when the target extends to or near the skin surface
Only for deep targets — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
To reduce scatter — verified through routine quality assurance measurements
3dcrt.e: Photon beams have skin-sparing (d_max at depth). When PTV includes skin: place tissue-equivalent bolus (0.5-1.5 cm) on surface → shifts d_max to surface → full dose at skin. Tradeoff: deeper dose reduced slightly. Must verify bolus contact (air gaps reduce effectiveness).

3dcrt.q: Immobilisation devices in 3DCRT: examples and purpose?
3dcrt.a: Thermoplastic masks, vacuum bags, breast boards, belly boards
c: Not needed for 3DCRT — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
Thermoplastic masks, vacuum bags, breast boards, belly boards
Only for SRS — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
Only for children — additional measurements add complexity without clinical benefit
3dcrt.e: ensure reproducible daily positioning within PTV margins. Immobilisation reduces setup uncertainty → smaller margins → less normal tissue irradiated. H&N mask: ±3 mm reproducibility. Vacuum bag (body): ±5 mm. Breast board: arm position reproducibility. Knee/ankle lock: pelvis rotation control. Indexed to couch for daily setup.

3dcrt.q: Hot spot in 3DCRT: ICRU definition and tolerance?
3dcrt.a: Volume receiving >100% of prescribed dose. ICRU 62: hot spot should not exceed 107% outside PTV
c: Any dose above 90% — based on established physics principles and clinical evidence
Volume receiving >100% of prescribed dose. ICRU 62: hot spot should not exceed 107% outside PTV
Not defined by ICRU — both systems use identical principles and require the same verification methods
Always acceptable up to 120%, as recommended by current international guidelines
3dcrt.e: ICRU 50/62: dose within PTV should be 95-107% of prescribed. Hot spots >107% outside PTV are clinically concerning (e.g., in bowel, skin). 3DCRT hot spots typically at beam intersections or near bolus edges. Beam weighting and wedges help control.

3dcrt.q: Isodose distribution for a single open 6MV field: what shape does the 80% isodose line have?
3dcrt.a: Curves inward at field edges (narrower than geometric field) due to penumbra and scatter reduction at edges
c: Perfectly rectangular matching field edges, based on established clinical physics practice
Curves inward at field edges (narrower than geometric field) due to penumbra and scatter reduction at edges
Curves outward beyond field edges, following current regulatory and professional standards
Flat straight line — recommended by current AAPM task group guidelines for clinical practice
3dcrt.e: Isodose lines at depth narrow relative to geometric field size due to: (1) penumbra (geometric + scatter). (2) Less scatter at field edges. (3) Higher-energy photons in central axis vs edge. 80% isodose ~1-2 cm inside field edge at 10 cm depth.

3dcrt.q: Whole brain radiotherapy (WBRT): typical 3DCRT technique?
3dcrt.a: Opposed lateral fields shaped to skull contour with MLC, eyes and oral cavity blocked where possible
c: Single AP field covering the entire cranial vault with extended field borders
Opposed lateral fields shaped to skull contour with MLC, eyes and oral cavity blocked where possible
Four-field box arrangement with anterior, posterior, and bilateral lateral beams
Vertex field directed superiorly through the top of the skull to reduce exit dose
3dcrt.e: WBRT: parallel-opposed laterals. Field extends to include entire brain + meninges. Inferior border: C2 or skull base (depending on indication). MLC shapes to skull. Flash (extend beyond skin) ensures coverage. Dose: typically 30 Gy/10 fx or 20 Gy/5 fx palliative.

3dcrt.q: Tangential breast fields in 3DCRT: key physics considerations?
3dcrt.a: Match medial and lateral tangents to cover breast tissue while minimising lung and heart (left breast) volume in field
c: Simple AP field covering the entire chest wall and breast tissue uniformly
Match medial and lateral tangents to cover breast tissue while minimising lung and heart (left breast) volume in field
Four-field box arrangement surrounding the breast to ensure uniform dose coverage
Single electron field matched to breast contour with bolus for dose buildup
3dcrt.e: Tangential pair: medial and lateral beams skim chest wall. Goals: CLD (central lung distance) ≤3 cm, heart <5% volume (left breast). Wedges (15-30°) or FIF (field-in-field) improve dose homogeneity. Supraclavicular field matched if nodes treated. DIBH for left breast reduces heart dose.

3dcrt.q: Field-in-field (FIF) technique: how does it improve on simple 3DCRT?
3dcrt.a: Subfields with MLC segments reduce hot spots within the main field — achieves wedge-like modulation without physical wedge
c: Adds additional beam angles at different gantry positions to improve dose conformity
Subfields with MLC segments reduce hot spots within the main field — achieves wedge-like modulation without physical wedge
Changes beam energy from 6MV to 10MV partway through each field delivery
Only applicable for IMRT inverse-planned treatments — not used in forward planning
3dcrt.e: FIF: forward-planned. Open field delivers most dose. 2-4 subfields (smaller MLC apertures) shield hot spot regions. Effectively flattens dose. Widely used for breast tangents (replaces wedges), brain, and large fields. Simpler than IMRT but more homogeneous than open fields.

3dcrt.q: Mantle field (historical): what did it treat?
3dcrt.a: Bilateral neck, mediastinum, and axillae in a single large field for Hodgkin lymphoma
c: Only neck nodes — other parameters are not clinically relevant for this test
Bilateral neck, mediastinum, and axillae in a single large field for Hodgkin lymphoma
Only mediastinum — remaining parameters are verified by the vendor during installation
Whole abdomen — sufficient for all standard clinical treatment scenarios
3dcrt.e: Mantle field: large, complex shaped field covering all major lymph node groups above diaphragm. Required lung blocks, humeral head shields. Historically standard for Hodgkin disease. Largely replaced by involved-site RT (ISRT) and VMAT due to excessive normal tissue dose.

3dcrt.q: 3DCRT vs IMRT: when is 3DCRT still appropriate?
3dcrt.a: Simple geometries where target is convex and well-separated from OARs
c: Never — always use IMRT — international consensus guidelines treat both approaches as interchangeable
Simple geometries where target is convex and well-separated from OARs
Only for palliative care — all other checks are covered by standard daily QA protocols
Only in low-resource settings — further testing is not required by current international guidelines
3dcrt.e: e.g., whole brain, spine, simple pelvis, palliative treatments. 3DCRT advantages: faster planning, less QA, lower MU (less leakage), more robust to setup errors. Appropriate when target doesn't wrap around OARs. IMRT needed when concave dose distributions required (e.g., H&N sparing parotids, prostate sparing rectum). Many SA centres use 3DCRT for majority of palliative and some radical treatments.

3dcrt.q: What does 3DCRT stand for?
3dcrt.a: Three-Dimensional Conformal Radiation Therapy
c: Three-Dimensional Computed Radiation Tomography
Three-Dimensional Conformal Radiation Therapy
Triple-Dose Conformal Radiotherapy Treatment
Three-Directional Clinical Radiation Technique
3dcrt.e: 3DCRT stands for Three-Dimensional Conformal Radiation Therapy, a technique that shapes radiation beams to match the 3D shape of the tumour using CT-based planning.

3dcrt.q: What imaging modality is most commonly used for treatment planning in 3DCRT?
3dcrt.a: CT (Computed Tomography)
c: Conventional X-ray
CT (Computed Tomography)
Ultrasound for accurate dose delivery
PET scan for clinical implementation
3dcrt.e: CT scanning provides the 3D anatomical data and electron density information (via Hounsfield Units) needed to calculate accurate dose distributions for 3DCRT planning.

3dcrt.q: In 3DCRT, what is a physical wedge used for?
3dcrt.a: To tilt the isodose distribution and improve dose uniformity across the target
c: To increase the beam energy within the treatment system
To block radiation from reaching the patient for the treatment procedure
To tilt the isodose distribution and improve dose uniformity across the target
To measure the dose delivered to the patient for clinical implementation
3dcrt.e: A physical wedge is a wedge-shaped metal filter placed in the beam path that progressively attenuates the beam across the field, tilting the isodose curves to compensate for surface obliquity or improve target dose uniformity.

3dcrt.q: What is the purpose of a beam's-eye view (BEV) in 3DCRT planning?
3dcrt.a: To visualise the target and surrounding structures from the radiation source perspective
