Combining multiple frames acquired from different beam angles to reduce speckle
Transmitting at multiple frequencies and averaging the returned signals
Applying coded excitation pulses to extend the maximum imaging depth
ultrasound.e: Spatial compounding steers the beam at multiple angles and averages the resulting frames. Since speckle patterns differ at each angle, averaging reduces speckle noise, improves border definition, and fills in shadowing artefacts.

ultrasound.q: CALC: What PRF is required to image to a maximum depth of 20 cm in soft tissue without range ambiguity?
ultrasound.e: PRF = c / (2 x depth) = 1540 / (2 x 0.20) = 1540 / 0.40 = 3850 Hz. The pulse must complete the round trip before the next pulse is transmitted to avoid range ambiguity artefacts.

ultrasound.q: What is the primary advantage of a curvilinear (convex) array transducer over a linear array?
ultrasound.a: Wider field of view at depth from a relatively compact footprint
c: Higher lateral resolution due to larger effective aperture
Wider field of view at depth from a relatively compact footprint
Better near-field resolution from closer element spacing
Higher frame rates from reduced number of scan lines
ultrasound.e: Curvilinear transducers have a convex surface that diverges scan lines, providing a wider field of view at depth compared to a linear array of similar contact length. They are commonly used for abdominal and obstetric imaging at 2-6 MHz.

ultrasound.q: What parameter in the AIUM QC phantom tests the minimum resolvable target separation along the beam axis?
ultrasound.a: Axial resolution target group
c: Axial resolution target group
Lateral resolution target group
Dead zone measurement wires
Grey-scale contrast targets
ultrasound.e: The AIUM phantom contains groups of nylon filament targets at progressively closer spacing along the beam axis. The smallest group in which individual targets can be distinguished determines the axial resolution of the system.

ultrasound.q: What is the speed of sound in bone compared to soft tissue?
ultrasound.a: Approximately 2-3 times faster than in soft tissue
c: Approximately the same as in soft tissue at 1540 m/s
Approximately half the speed found in soft tissue
Approximately 2-3 times faster than in soft tissue
Approximately 5-6 times faster than in soft tissue
ultrasound.e: Sound travels at approximately 3000-4000 m/s in bone compared to 1540 m/s in soft tissue. This speed mismatch causes significant refraction and reflection at bone-tissue interfaces, contributing to acoustic shadowing.

ultrasound.q: CALC: What is the wavelength of a 15 MHz linear array transducer in soft tissue?
ultrasound.e: Wavelength = c/f = 1540/15,000,000 = 0.0001027 m = 0.103 mm. High-frequency transducers (12-18 MHz) provide excellent resolution for superficial structures but have limited penetration depth.

ultrasound.q: What is the significance of the Curie temperature for piezoelectric transducer materials?
ultrasound.a: Above this temperature the material permanently loses its piezoelectric properties
c: Below this temperature the crystal produces maximum acoustic output
Above this temperature the material permanently loses its piezoelectric properties
At this temperature the resonant frequency shifts to the harmonic mode
Below this temperature the backing material absorbs all ringing energy
ultrasound.e: The Curie temperature is the point above which the aligned dipoles in a piezoelectric material become randomly oriented, permanently destroying the piezoelectric property. For PZT, this is approximately 300-350 degrees Celsius. Autoclaving must be avoided.

ultrasound.q: What causes acoustic shadowing posterior to a gallstone on ultrasound?
ultrasound.a: High reflection and absorption at the stone surface blocking distal transmission
c: Refraction of the beam around the curved stone surface edge
Diffraction spreading the beam laterally around the stone mass
High reflection and absorption at the stone surface blocking distal transmission
Destructive interference from multiple echoes within the stone
ultrasound.e: Gallstones have high acoustic impedance relative to bile, causing strong reflection. They also absorb and scatter acoustic energy, resulting in markedly reduced intensity beyond the stone, which appears as an anechoic shadow on the image.

ultrasound.q: In ultrasound QC, what does the sensitivity or penetration depth test measure?
ultrasound.a: Maximum depth at which echoes from the phantom material are visible
c: Minimum detectable target size at the transducer surface
Maximum depth at which echoes from the phantom material are visible
Number of distinguishable grey levels in the displayed image
Accuracy of distance measurements along the beam direction
ultrasound.e: The sensitivity test measures the maximum depth at which uniform phantom echoes are visible above noise level. It is affected by transducer frequency, output power, gain, and element integrity. A decrease over time may indicate transducer degradation.

ultrasound.q: CALC: If a 6 MHz pulse has a spatial pulse length of 0.77 mm, how many cycles does it contain?
ultrasound.a: 3 cycles
c: 2 cycles
3 cycles
4 cycles
5 cycles
ultrasound.e: Wavelength = 1540/6,000,000 = 0.2567 mm. Number of cycles = SPL/wavelength = 0.77/0.2567 = 3.0 cycles. Diagnostic imaging pulses typically contain 2-4 cycles to maintain good axial resolution.

ultrasound.q: The acoustic impedance (Z) of a medium is defined as:
ultrasound.a: The product of density and speed of sound (Z = ρc)
c: The ratio of frequency to wavelength of sound
The product of density and speed of sound (Z = ρc)
The attenuation coefficient divided by frequency
The amplitude of reflected sound waves only
ultrasound.e: Acoustic impedance Z = ρc (density × speed of sound). Impedance mismatches at tissue boundaries determine the fraction of reflected vs transmitted ultrasound energy.

ultrasound.q: What determines the axial resolution of an ultrasound transducer?
ultrasound.a: The spatial pulse length (shorter pulses give better axial resolution)
c: The diameter of the transducer crystal element
The spatial pulse length (shorter pulses give better axial resolution)
The frame rate of the ultrasound system
The depth setting selected by the operator
ultrasound.e: Axial resolution = SPL/2. Shorter pulses (higher frequency, more damping) improve axial resolution. Typical values: 0.5-1.0 mm for diagnostic frequencies.

ultrasound.q: Doppler ultrasound shift frequency is higher when:
ultrasound.a: Blood flow velocity is higher and directed toward the transducer
c: The transducer frequency is lower than 2 MHz
Blood flow velocity is higher and directed toward the transducer
The patient is positioned further from the transducer
The gain setting is increased on the machine
ultrasound.e: Doppler shift fd = 2f₀v cos(θ)/c. Higher velocity (v) and smaller angle (θ) between beam and flow direction give larger frequency shifts.

ultrasound.q: Acoustic shadowing in ultrasound occurs behind:
ultrasound.a: Highly attenuating or reflecting structures such as calcifications or bone
c: Fluid-filled structures like cysts or bladder
Highly attenuating or reflecting structures such as calcifications or bone
Soft tissue interfaces with matched impedance
Areas of high blood flow velocity throughout the treatment course
ultrasound.e: Structures that strongly reflect or absorb sound (gallstones, bone, calcifications) prevent transmission, creating a dark shadow distal to the structure.

ultrasound.q: The purpose of matching layers on ultrasound transducers is to:
ultrasound.a: Reduce impedance mismatch between the crystal and tissue for better energy transfer
c: Increase the operating frequency of the transducer
Reduce impedance mismatch between the crystal and tissue for better energy transfer
Filter out harmonic frequencies from the signal during the planning stage
Protect the crystal from physical damage during the planning stage
ultrasound.e: Matching layers have impedance between the crystal (high Z ~30 MRayl) and tissue (low Z ~1.5 MRayl), improving acoustic energy transmission into the patient.

ultrasound.q: CALC: If the speed of sound in soft tissue is 1540 m/s and the pulse-echo round trip time is 130 μs, what is the reflector depth?
ultrasound.a: 10 cm
c: 5 cm
10 cm
15 cm
20 cm
ultrasound.e: Depth = (c × t)/2 = (1540 × 130×10⁻⁶)/2 = (0.2002)/2 = 0.1001 m ≈ 10 cm. The factor of 2 accounts for the round-trip travel.

ultrasound.q: Tissue harmonic imaging improves image quality primarily by:
ultrasound.a: Using second harmonic frequency signals that have less aberration and clutter
c: Doubling the transmit power to increase penetration depth
Using second harmonic frequency signals that have less aberration and clutter
Reducing the frame rate to allow longer signal averaging
Switching to continuous wave mode instead of pulsed
ultrasound.e: Harmonics are generated in tissue (not at the surface), so they avoid body wall aberrations. They have narrower beam profiles and less side lobe artifacts.

ultrasound.q: The Nyquist limit in pulsed Doppler determines:
ultrasound.a: The maximum detectable frequency shift, equal to half the PRF
c: The minimum tissue depth that can be imaged
The maximum detectable frequency shift, equal to half the PRF
The spatial resolution of the B-mode image
The maximum number of focal zones available
ultrasound.e: Nyquist limit = PRF/2. Doppler shifts exceeding this cause aliasing (wrap-around artifact). Higher PRF increases the Nyquist limit but reduces maximum depth.

ultrasound.q: Speckle in ultrasound images is caused by:
ultrasound.a: Constructive and destructive interference of echoes from sub-resolution scatterers
c: Motion of the transducer during image acquisition
Constructive and destructive interference of echoes from sub-resolution scatterers
Electronic noise in the receiver amplifier circuits
Improper coupling between transducer and patient skin
ultrasound.e: Speckle is a coherent artifact from interference of echoes from scatterers smaller than the wavelength. It degrades contrast resolution but carries tissue signature information.

ultrasound.q: What is the purpose of time-gain compensation (TGC) in ultrasound?
ultrasound.a: To compensate for increasing attenuation with depth by amplifying deeper echoes more
c: To reduce the transmit power for superficial structures
To compensate for increasing attenuation with depth by amplifying deeper echoes more
To focus the beam at the selected depth
To remove Doppler shift artifacts from the image
ultrasound.e: Sound attenuates as it travels through tissue (~0.5 dB/cm/MHz in soft tissue). TGC applies depth-dependent gain so that similar reflectors appear equally bright regardless of depth.

ultrasound.q: The elevational (slice thickness) resolution of a linear array transducer is determined by:
ultrasound.a: The height of the transducer elements in the elevation plane
c: The number of elements in the linear array
The height of the transducer elements in the elevation plane
The pulse repetition frequency setting
The dynamic range of the receiver
ultrasound.e: Elevational resolution depends on element height and any acoustic lens focusing in that plane. It is typically the worst of the three resolution dimensions.

ultrasound.q: CALC: The attenuation coefficient in soft tissue is approximately 0.5 dB/cm/MHz. For a 5 MHz transducer imaging at 8 cm depth, what is the total round-trip attenuation?
ultrasound.a: 40 dB
c: 20 dB
40 dB
60 dB
80 dB
ultrasound.e: Attenuation = 0.5 dB/cm/MHz × 5 MHz × 16 cm (round trip = 2 × 8 cm) = 40 dB. This is why higher frequencies have limited penetration depth.

ultrasound.q: Aliasing in colour Doppler can be reduced by:
ultrasound.a: Increasing the PRF or using a lower frequency transducer
c: Decreasing the colour box size only
Increasing the PRF or using a lower frequency transducer
Adding more acoustic coupling gel
Reducing the B-mode gain setting
ultrasound.e: Increasing PRF raises the Nyquist limit. Lower transducer frequency reduces the Doppler shift for the same velocity, keeping it below the Nyquist limit.

ultrasound.q: What type of ultrasound transducer is best suited for cardiac imaging?
ultrasound.a: Phased array with small footprint for intercostal access
c: Large linear array for wide field of view
Phased array with small footprint for intercostal access
Convex array designed for abdominal scanning
High-frequency linear probe for superficial imaging
ultrasound.e: Phased arrays have a small footprint that fits between ribs while electronically steering the beam to create a wide sector image of the heart.

ultrasound.q: The mechanical index (MI) in ultrasound indicates the likelihood of:
ultrasound.a: Mechanical bioeffects such as cavitation in tissue
c: Thermal tissue damage from acoustic absorption
Mechanical bioeffects such as cavitation in tissue
Electromagnetic interference with other equipment
Transducer crystal fatigue during prolonged use
ultrasound.e: MI = peak negative pressure / √frequency. Higher MI increases cavitation risk. FDA limits MI to 1.9 for diagnostic ultrasound (except ophthalmic: 0.23).

ultrasound.q: The thermal index (TI) in ultrasound represents:
ultrasound.a: The ratio of acoustic power to that required to raise tissue temperature by 1°C
c: The absolute temperature of the transducer surface
The ratio of acoustic power to that required to raise tissue temperature by 1°C
The time required before thermal damage occurs
The cooling rate of tissue after ultrasound exposure
ultrasound.e: TI estimates potential for tissue heating. TI = W/W_deg where W_deg is the power needed for 1°C rise. ALARA principle: keep TI and MI as low as reasonably achievable.

ultrasound.q: Compound imaging in ultrasound improves image quality by:
ultrasound.a: Acquiring images from multiple steering angles and averaging them
c: Using two transducers simultaneously at different frequencies
Acquiring images from multiple steering angles and averaging them
Combining B-mode and M-mode displays on one screen
Increasing the transmit voltage to maximum output
ultrasound.e: Spatial compound imaging steers the beam at multiple angles, averaging the resulting images. This reduces speckle, improves contrast resolution, and fills in acoustic shadows.

ultrasound.q: What is the typical speed of sound assumed by ultrasound systems for distance calibration?
ultrasound.a: 1540 m/s (average soft tissue velocity)
c: 330 m/s (speed of sound in air)
1540 m/s (average soft tissue velocity)
3000 m/s (speed of sound in bone)
5000 m/s (speed of sound in metal)
ultrasound.e: All clinical ultrasound systems assume 1540 m/s for distance calculations. Deviations in fat (1450 m/s) or muscle (1580 m/s) cause minor distance errors.

ultrasound.q: Elastography in ultrasound measures:
ultrasound.a: Tissue stiffness by assessing strain or shear wave propagation speed
c: Blood flow velocity in deep vessels accurately
Tissue stiffness by assessing strain or shear wave propagation speed
The acoustic output power of the transducer
Bone mineral density through cortical surfaces
ultrasound.e: Elastography maps tissue stiffness — stiffer tissues (e.g., tumours) deform less under compression (strain) or propagate shear waves faster. Used for liver fibrosis, breast and thyroid lesions.

ultrasound.q: CALC: A Doppler ultrasound with transmit frequency 3.5 MHz measures a frequency shift of 2 kHz at an angle of 60°. What is the blood flow velocity? (c = 1540 m/s)
ultrasound.a: Approximately 44 cm/s
c: Approximately 22 cm/s
Approximately 44 cm/s
Approximately 88 cm/s
Approximately 176 cm/s

ultrasound.q: What is the acoustic impedance of soft tissue?
ultrasound.e: The acoustic impedance (Z) of soft tissue is approximately 1.63 x 10^6 kg/(m^2·s) or 1.63 MRayl. This value is the product of tissue density (~1050 kg/m^3) and the speed of sound in soft tissue (~1540 m/s).

ultrasound.q: CALC: If two media have acoustic impedances Z1 = 1.63 MRayl and Z2 = 7.80 MRayl, what is the intensity reflection coefficient at normal incidence?
ultrasound.e: The intensity reflection coefficient R = ((Z2 - Z1)/(Z2 + Z1))^2 = ((7.80 - 1.63)/(7.80 + 1.63))^2 = (6.17/9.43)^2 = 0.654^2 = 0.428 or approximately 43%. This large reflection occurs at soft tissue-bone interfaces.

ultrasound.q: Which transducer type is best suited for echocardiography through the intercostal spaces?
ultrasound.a: Phased array
c: Linear array
Curvilinear array
Phased array
Annular array
ultrasound.e: Phased array transducers have a small footprint that fits between ribs and electronically steer the beam to produce a sector-shaped image, making them ideal for cardiac imaging through narrow acoustic windows.

ultrasound.q: What causes Doppler aliasing in pulsed-wave ultrasound?
ultrasound.a: The Doppler shift exceeds half the pulse repetition frequency
c: The beam angle exceeds 60 degrees relative to flow
The Doppler shift exceeds half the pulse repetition frequency
The transducer frequency is set too low for the depth
The sample volume is placed too close to the vessel wall
ultrasound.e: Aliasing occurs when the Doppler frequency shift exceeds the Nyquist limit, which is half the PRF. This causes high-velocity flow to wrap around and appear as flow in the opposite direction on the spectral display.

ultrasound.q: Acoustic shadowing posterior to a structure indicates the structure is:
ultrasound.a: Highly attenuating or reflective
c: Fluid-filled and anechoic
Highly attenuating or reflective
Moving rapidly towards the transducer
Located at the focal zone depth
ultrasound.e: Acoustic shadowing occurs behind structures that strongly attenuate or reflect the ultrasound beam (e.g., calcifications, bone, gallstones), preventing sound from reaching deeper tissues and creating a dark region distally.

ultrasound.q: Posterior acoustic enhancement is most commonly seen behind which type of structure?
ultrasound.a: Fluid-filled cysts
c: Dense calcifications
Fluid-filled cysts
Gas-containing bowel
Fibrotic tissue masses
ultrasound.e: Fluid-filled cysts attenuate sound much less than surrounding soft tissue. The tissue beyond the cyst receives more sound energy than adjacent tissue at the same depth, resulting in increased brightness (posterior enhancement).

ultrasound.q: What is the Mechanical Index (MI) in ultrasound safety?
ultrasound.a: Peak rarefactional pressure divided by the square root of frequency
c: Average temporal intensity divided by spatial peak intensity
Peak rarefactional pressure divided by the square root of frequency
Thermal dose accumulated over the examination duration
Ratio of transmitted power to reflected power at tissue interfaces
ultrasound.e: The Mechanical Index (MI) = P_r / sqrt(f), where P_r is the peak rarefactional pressure (in MPa) and f is the frequency (in MHz). MI estimates the likelihood of cavitation-related bioeffects. An MI > 1.9 is generally not permitted for diagnostic use.

ultrasound.q: The Thermal Index (TI) represents:
ultrasound.a: The ratio of acoustic power to the power needed to raise tissue temperature by 1 degrees C
c: The maximum temperature rise measured during the scan
The ratio of acoustic power to the power needed to raise tissue temperature by 1 degrees C
The cumulative thermal dose in minutes at 43 degrees C equivalent
The rate of heat dissipation from the transducer surface
ultrasound.e: The Thermal Index is the ratio of the emitted acoustic power to the power required to raise tissue temperature by 1 degrees C. Variants include TIS (soft tissue), TIB (bone at focus), and TIC (cranial bone). The ALARA principle should guide TI management.

ultrasound.q: CALC: An ultrasound transducer operates at 5 MHz in soft tissue (c = 1540 m/s). What is the wavelength?
ultrasound.e: Wavelength = c/f = 1540 m/s / 5 x 10^6 Hz = 3.08 x 10^-4 m = 0.31 mm. Wavelength is a fundamental parameter that determines axial resolution, which is approximately half the spatial pulse length.

ultrasound.q: Axial resolution in ultrasound is primarily determined by:
ultrasound.a: Spatial pulse length
c: Beam width at the focus
Spatial pulse length
Number of scan lines per frame
Dynamic range of the receiver
ultrasound.e: Axial resolution = spatial pulse length / 2. Shorter pulses (higher frequency, wider bandwidth, fewer cycles per pulse) improve axial resolution. This is independent of beam geometry and is always superior to lateral resolution.

ultrasound.q: Which parameter primarily determines lateral resolution?
ultrasound.a: Beam width at the depth of interest
c: Pulse repetition frequency
Beam width at the depth of interest
Number of cycles in the pulse
Speed of sound in the medium
ultrasound.e: Lateral resolution equals the beam width at the depth of interest. It is best at the focal zone where the beam is narrowest. Multiple transmit focal zones can improve lateral resolution across a range of depths at the cost of frame rate.

ultrasound.q: Harmonic imaging in ultrasound improves image quality primarily by:
ultrasound.a: Receiving at double the transmitted frequency to reduce artifacts
c: Increasing the transmitted power to improve penetration depth
Receiving at double the transmitted frequency to reduce artifacts
Using two transducers simultaneously for triangulation
Applying matched filtering to compress the received pulse
ultrasound.e: In tissue harmonic imaging, the transducer transmits at a fundamental frequency f0 and receives at the second harmonic 2f0. Harmonics are generated by nonlinear propagation in tissue, reducing clutter, reverberation artifacts, and side-lobe artifacts.

ultrasound.q: What does elastography measure in ultrasound imaging?
ultrasound.a: Tissue stiffness based on shear wave propagation or strain response
c: Tissue perfusion based on microbubble contrast enhancement
Tissue stiffness based on shear wave propagation or strain response
Tissue temperature changes during therapeutic applications
Tissue composition through spectral analysis of backscatter
ultrasound.e: Elastography evaluates tissue stiffness (elastic modulus). Strain elastography measures tissue deformation under compression, while shear wave elastography measures shear wave speed, which is proportional to the square root of the shear modulus. It is valuable for liver fibrosis staging and breast lesion characterisation.

ultrasound.q: Ultrasound contrast agents consist primarily of:
ultrasound.a: Gas-filled microbubbles stabilised by a shell
c: Iodinated nanoparticles suspended in saline solution
Gas-filled microbubbles stabilised by a shell
Paramagnetic iron oxide particles in dextran coating
Barium sulfate microspheres coated with albumin protein
ultrasound.e: Ultrasound contrast agents (e.g., SonoVue/Lumason) are gas-filled microbubbles (1-10 micrometres diameter) with a lipid or protein shell. They oscillate in the ultrasound field, producing strong nonlinear echoes that enhance vascular imaging.

ultrasound.q: CALC: If the PRF is 8 kHz in pulsed-wave Doppler, what is the maximum Doppler shift that can be measured without aliasing?
ultrasound.a: 4 kHz
c: 2 kHz
4 kHz
8 kHz
16 kHz
ultrasound.e: The Nyquist limit states that the maximum detectable frequency shift is PRF/2 = 8000/2 = 4 kHz. Any Doppler shift above 4 kHz will alias and wrap around on the spectral display.

ultrasound.q: A linear array transducer is most appropriate for imaging:
ultrasound.a: Superficial structures such as thyroid and breast
c: Deep abdominal organs in obese patients
Superficial structures such as thyroid and breast
The adult heart through intercostal windows
Transvaginal pelvic structures at depth
ultrasound.e: Linear array transducers produce a rectangular field of view with uniform resolution across the image. They operate at higher frequencies (7-15 MHz) providing excellent near-field resolution, ideal for superficial structures like thyroid, breast, and musculoskeletal imaging.

ultrasound.q: CALC: A Doppler ultrasound measures a frequency shift of 2.5 kHz using a 5 MHz transducer at a 60 degree angle. What is the blood flow velocity? (c = 1540 m/s)
ultrasound.e: Using the Doppler equation: v = (fd x c) / (2 x f0 x cos theta) = (2500 x 1540) / (2 x 5x10^6 x cos 60 degrees) = 3,850,000 / (10,000,000 x 0.5) = 3,850,000 / 5,000,000 = 0.77 m/s.

ultrasound.q: Reverberation artifact in ultrasound appears as:
ultrasound.a: Equally spaced parallel lines deep to a strong reflector
c: A bright line at the edge of a curved structure
Equally spaced parallel lines deep to a strong reflector
A comet-tail of colour behind a moving structure
A dark band lateral to a rounded structure
ultrasound.e: Reverberation occurs when the ultrasound pulse bounces back and forth between two strong parallel reflectors (e.g., transducer face and a nearby strong interface). Each round trip is interpreted as a deeper structure, creating equally spaced echoes.

ultrasound.q: Increasing the bandwidth of an ultrasound transducer primarily improves:
ultrasound.a: Axial resolution by shortening the pulse length
c: Penetration depth by reducing attenuation losses
Axial resolution by shortening the pulse length
Temporal resolution by increasing the frame rate
Contrast resolution by widening the dynamic range
ultrasound.e: A wider bandwidth allows the transducer to produce shorter pulses with fewer cycles. Since axial resolution = SPL/2, shorter pulses directly improve axial resolution. Broadband transducers also support harmonic imaging across a wider frequency range.

ultrasound.q: Time-gain compensation (TGC) in ultrasound is used to:
ultrasound.a: Compensate for depth-dependent attenuation of the ultrasound beam
c: Correct for velocity errors caused by refraction artifacts
Compensate for depth-dependent attenuation of the ultrasound beam
Adjust the transmit power for different patient body habitus
Equalise the frame rate across different imaging depths
ultrasound.e: TGC applies increasing amplification to echoes returning from greater depths to compensate for the progressive attenuation of the ultrasound beam. This produces a uniform brightness display regardless of reflector depth.

ultrasound.q: CALC: The attenuation coefficient in soft tissue is approximately 0.5 dB/cm/MHz. What is the total attenuation for a 7 MHz pulse travelling to a reflector at 4 cm depth?
ultrasound.a: 28 dB
c: 14 dB
28 dB
42 dB
56 dB
ultrasound.e: Total attenuation = 0.5 dB/cm/MHz x 7 MHz x 8 cm (round trip = 2 x 4 cm) = 28 dB. The round-trip distance must be used since the pulse travels to the reflector and back. TGC must compensate for this loss.

ultrasound.q: What is the purpose of multiple transmit focus zones in ultrasound imaging?
ultrasound.a: To improve lateral resolution at multiple depths at the cost of frame rate
c: To increase the penetration depth for deep abdominal scanning
To improve lateral resolution at multiple depths at the cost of frame rate
To reduce the mechanical index for safer obstetric imaging
To enable simultaneous pulsed and continuous wave Doppler modes
ultrasound.e: Each focal zone requires a separate transmit pulse with different focusing delays. Multiple focal zones improve lateral resolution across a wider depth range, but reduce the frame rate proportionally since each line must be transmitted multiple times.

ultrasound.q: CALC: What is the imaging depth if the echo return time is 130 microseconds? (Speed of sound in tissue = 1540 m/s)
ultrasound.a: 10 cm
c: 5 cm
10 cm
15 cm
20 cm
ultrasound.e: Depth = (c x t) / 2 = (1540 m/s x 130 x 10^-6 s) / 2 = 0.2002 / 2 = 0.1001 m = 10 cm. The division by 2 accounts for the round-trip travel of the pulse.

ultrasound.q: Continuous-wave Doppler differs from pulsed-wave Doppler in that it:
ultrasound.a: Has no aliasing limit but cannot resolve depth of the signal
c: Has no aliasing limit but cannot resolve depth of the signal
Can measure low velocities but is limited to superficial depths
Requires a higher PRF but provides better spatial resolution
Uses a single crystal for both transmission and reception
ultrasound.e: CW Doppler uses separate transmit and receive elements operating continuously, so it has no PRF and therefore no Nyquist limit (no aliasing). However, it detects Doppler shifts along the entire beam path without depth discrimination (range ambiguity).

ultrasound.q: CALC: A curvilinear transducer operates at 3.5 MHz. What is the approximate axial resolution if the transducer produces 2-cycle pulses? (c = 1540 m/s)
ultrasound.e: Wavelength = 1540/3.5x10^6 = 0.44 mm. Spatial pulse length = n x wavelength = 2 x 0.44 = 0.88 mm. Axial resolution = SPL/2 = 0.88/2 = 0.44 mm. Fewer cycles per pulse yield better axial resolution.

ultrasound.q: The average speed of sound in soft tissue is approximately:
ultrasound.e: Ultrasound machines assume 1540 m/s for distance calculations.

ultrasound.q: Acoustic impedance (Z) is defined as:
ultrasound.a: The product of tissue density and sound velocity
c: The ratio of frequency to wavelength in tissue
The product of tissue density and sound velocity
The inverse of the attenuation coefficient
The speed of sound divided by tissue density
ultrasound.e: Z = density x velocity. Differences in Z at tissue interfaces determine the amount of reflection and transmission.

ultrasound.q: A piezoelectric element in a transducer converts:
ultrasound.a: Electrical energy to mechanical vibrations and vice versa
c: Light energy into ultrasound wave patterns
Electrical energy to mechanical vibrations and vice versa
Thermal energy into acoustic pressure waves
Magnetic fields into radiofrequency pulse signals
ultrasound.e: Piezoelectric materials deform when voltage is applied (transmit) and generate voltage when deformed by returning echoes (receive).

ultrasound.q: The backing layer in a transducer is designed to:
ultrasound.a: Dampen the pulse for improved axial resolution
c: Increase the intensity of transmitted pulses
Dampen the pulse for improved axial resolution
Focus the beam at a specific tissue depth
Match the transducer impedance to the skin
ultrasound.e: Heavy backing material absorbs ringing, shortening the pulse and improving axial resolution.

ultrasound.q: The matching layer on the transducer face serves to:
ultrasound.a: Reduce impedance mismatch between crystal and tissue
c: Increase the resonant frequency of the element
Reduce impedance mismatch between crystal and tissue
Protect the element from physical damage only
Focus the ultrasound beam at the focal depth
ultrasound.e: The matching layer has intermediate impedance, improving energy transmission. Its thickness is lambda/4.

ultrasound.q: Axial resolution in ultrasound is determined by:
ultrasound.a: The spatial pulse length of the transmitted pulse
c: The diameter of the transducer crystal used
The spatial pulse length of the transmitted pulse
The number of scan lines per image frame
The depth setting of the ultrasound machine
ultrasound.e: Axial resolution = SPL/2. Shorter pulses give better axial resolution.

ultrasound.q: CALC: A 5 MHz transducer in soft tissue (1540 m/s) has a wavelength of:
ultrasound.e: Wavelength = velocity/frequency = 1540/5,000,000 = 0.000308 m = 0.31 mm.

ultrasound.q: Lateral resolution is primarily determined by:
ultrasound.a: The beam width at the depth of interest
c: The pulse repetition frequency setting used
The beam width at the depth of interest
The number of cycles in each pulse train
The attenuation coefficient of the tissue
ultrasound.e: Lateral resolution equals beam width. It is best at the focal zone.

ultrasound.q: The Doppler shift frequency is proportional to:
ultrasound.a: Blood flow velocity and cosine of the Doppler angle
c: Blood flow velocity and cosine of the Doppler angle
Transducer frequency and sine of the Doppler angle
Acoustic impedance and depth of the target vessel
Pulse repetition frequency and tissue attenuation
ultrasound.e: fd = 2f0.v.cos(theta)/c.

ultrasound.q: At a Doppler angle of 90 degrees, the detected frequency shift is:
ultrasound.a: Zero regardless of flow velocity
c: Maximum for any given velocity
Zero regardless of flow velocity
Half of the transmitted frequency
Equal to the pulse repetition rate
ultrasound.e: cos(90) = 0, so the Doppler shift is zero.

ultrasound.q: Aliasing in pulsed-wave Doppler occurs when the flow velocity exceeds:
ultrasound.a: Half the pulse repetition frequency (Nyquist limit)
c: The speed of sound in the tissue medium
Half the pulse repetition frequency (Nyquist limit)
The maximum display range of the scanner
Twice the transmitted centre frequency value
ultrasound.e: The Nyquist limit = PRF/2.

ultrasound.q: Continuous-wave Doppler has which advantage over pulsed-wave Doppler?
ultrasound.a: No aliasing at any measured flow velocity
c: Better depth discrimination of flow signals
No aliasing at any measured flow velocity
Higher spatial resolution along the beam
Lower acoustic output power required total
ultrasound.e: CW Doppler has no PRF and no Nyquist limit. However, it lacks range resolution.

ultrasound.q: The colour flow Doppler display encodes which parameter as colour?
ultrasound.a: Mean velocity and direction of blood flow
c: Peak systolic velocity of blood flow only
Mean velocity and direction of blood flow
The acoustic impedance of surrounding tissue
The depth of the vessel from the transducer
ultrasound.e: Colour Doppler shows mean velocity and direction overlaid on B-mode.

ultrasound.q: Power Doppler displays information about:
ultrasound.a: The amplitude of the Doppler signal, not velocity
c: The direction and velocity of blood flow
The amplitude of the Doppler signal, not velocity
The spectral frequency content of the echo
The phase shift of the returning echo pulse
ultrasound.e: Power Doppler shows the integrated power of the Doppler signal.

ultrasound.q: A reverberation artefact appears as:
ultrasound.a: Equally spaced parallel lines at increasing depth
c: A bright curved line behind a cystic structure
Equally spaced parallel lines at increasing depth
A dark shadow behind a calcified structure
A single bright echo at the focal zone only
ultrasound.e: Reverberations occur when sound bounces between two strong reflectors.

ultrasound.q: Acoustic shadowing is seen posterior to:
ultrasound.a: Strongly attenuating or reflecting structures
c: Fluid-filled cysts and simple collections
Strongly attenuating or reflecting structures
Normal homogeneous soft tissue regions
Doppler-active blood vessel flow areas
ultrasound.e: Structures like gallstones and calcifications attenuate or reflect most of the beam.

ultrasound.q: Posterior acoustic enhancement occurs behind:
ultrasound.a: Low-attenuation fluid-filled structures
c: Dense calcified structures like gallstones
Low-attenuation fluid-filled structures
Gas-containing bowel loop segments
Highly vascular solid tissue masses
ultrasound.e: Fluid attenuates less than surrounding tissue.

ultrasound.q: A mirror image artefact in ultrasound occurs near:
ultrasound.a: Strong specular reflectors like the diaphragm
c: Weakly scattering parenchymal tissue regions
Strong specular reflectors like the diaphragm
Gas-tissue interfaces in bowel loops only
Fluid-filled structures in the pelvis area
ultrasound.e: The diaphragm acts as a mirror, displaying duplicates on the other side.

ultrasound.q: The mechanical index (MI) is related to the risk of:
ultrasound.a: Cavitation in tissue from the ultrasound beam
c: Thermal damage to tissue from absorption
Cavitation in tissue from the ultrasound beam
Electromagnetic interference with equipment
Long-term genetic damage from exposure
ultrasound.e: MI = peak rarefactional pressure / sqrt(frequency). Limit is 1.9 for diagnostic use.

ultrasound.q: The thermal index (TI) estimates:
ultrasound.a: The potential temperature rise in tissue in degrees C
c: The total acoustic energy output in milliwatts
The potential temperature rise in tissue in degrees C
The peak spatial intensity of the beam only
The duration of safe scanning exposure time
ultrasound.e: TI = acoustic power / power needed to raise tissue by 1C.

ultrasound.q: CALC: If a transducer has a 3 dB bandwidth of 2 MHz centred at 5 MHz, the Q factor is:
ultrasound.e: Q = centre frequency / bandwidth = 5/2 = 2.5.

ultrasound.q: Harmonic imaging uses echoes at which frequency relative to the transmitted frequency?
ultrasound.a: Twice the fundamental transmitted frequency
c: Half the fundamental transmitted frequency
Twice the fundamental transmitted frequency
The same fundamental frequency as transmitted
Three times the fundamental transmitted value
ultrasound.e: Tissue harmonic imaging filters for the second harmonic (2f0).

ultrasound.q: Increasing the ultrasound frequency results in:
ultrasound.a: Better resolution but reduced penetration depth
c: Better penetration but poorer resolution
Better resolution but reduced penetration depth
No change in either resolution or penetration
Improved penetration and improved resolution
ultrasound.e: Higher frequency = shorter wavelength = better resolution, but increased attenuation.

ultrasound.q: The attenuation coefficient in soft tissue is approximately:
ultrasound.a: 0.5 dB/cm/MHz
c: 0.1 dB/cm/MHz
0.5 dB/cm/MHz
1.0 dB/cm/MHz
5.0 dB/cm/MHz
ultrasound.e: The rule of thumb is 0.5 dB/cm/MHz.

ultrasound.q: Time gain compensation (TGC) is used to:
ultrasound.a: Compensate for attenuation of echoes with depth
c: Increase the transmitted pulse amplitude only
Compensate for attenuation of echoes with depth
Improve lateral resolution at all depth levels
Reduce patient exposure to acoustic energy
ultrasound.e: TGC applies increasing gain to echoes from deeper structures.

ultrasound.q: A phased array transducer steers the beam by:
ultrasound.a: Applying time delays to element excitation pulses
c: Mechanically rotating the transducer elements
Applying time delays to element excitation pulses
Switching between different crystal frequencies
Using a single large element with a curved face
ultrasound.e: Electronic beam steering uses precisely timed delays across the element array.

ultrasound.q: A linear array transducer produces which beam format?
ultrasound.a: Rectangular image with parallel scan lines
c: Sector-shaped image from a small footprint
Rectangular image with parallel scan lines
Trapezoidal image with diverging far field
Circular image from a rotating single element
ultrasound.e: Linear arrays fire groups of adjacent elements sequentially, producing parallel scan lines.

ultrasound.q: A curvilinear (convex) array combines features of:
ultrasound.a: Linear array resolution with a wider sector field of view
c: Phased array steering with mechanical rotation speed
Linear array resolution with a wider sector field of view
Annular array focusing with linear scan line density
Single element simplicity with electronic beam steering
ultrasound.e: Curvilinear arrays have a curved surface producing a wider field of view at depth.

ultrasound.q: Slice thickness artefact in ultrasound is caused by:
ultrasound.a: Finite beam width in the elevation plane
c: Excessive gain settings on the machine used
Finite beam width in the elevation plane
Mismatch in speed of sound assumption
Motion of the patient during image acquisition
ultrasound.e: The beam has finite thickness in the elevation direction.

ultrasound.q: Speckle in ultrasound images is caused by:
ultrasound.a: Constructive and destructive interference of scattered echoes
c: Electronic noise in the receiver amplifier circuits
Constructive and destructive interference of scattered echoes
Patient motion during the image acquisition scan
Improper coupling gel application on the skin
ultrasound.e: Speckle is a coherent noise pattern from interference of echoes from sub-resolution scatterers.

ultrasound.q: Spatial compound imaging reduces speckle by:
ultrasound.a: Averaging multiple frames from different steering angles
c: Increasing the transmitted pulse frequency used
Averaging multiple frames from different steering angles
Using a single focused beam from one direction
Decreasing the dynamic range of the display
ultrasound.e: Compound imaging steers the beam at multiple angles and averages the resulting frames.

ultrasound.q: The Fresnel zone (near field) length of a transducer is increased by:
ultrasound.a: Using a larger aperture or higher frequency
c: Using a smaller transducer element aperture
Using a larger aperture or higher frequency
Increasing the pulse repetition frequency rate
Decreasing the number of cycles per pulse
ultrasound.e: Near field length = D²/(4 lambda). Increasing aperture or frequency extends the near field.

ultrasound.q: CALC: The ALARA principle in diagnostic ultrasound recommends:
ultrasound.a: Using the lowest output power for diagnostic information
c: Always using maximum power for best image quality
Using the lowest output power for diagnostic information
Limiting scans to a maximum of five minutes total
Using only continuous wave mode during scanning
ultrasound.e: ALARA applies to ultrasound: use minimum MI and TI, limit exposure time.

ultrasound.q: A speed propagation artefact causes structures to appear:
ultrasound.a: Displaced in depth due to incorrect velocity assumption
c: Wider than their actual physical dimensions are
Displaced in depth due to incorrect velocity assumption
Brighter than surrounding normal tissue appears
Duplicated on both sides of a reflector surface
ultrasound.e: If actual tissue velocity differs from 1540 m/s, structures are displayed at incorrect depths.

ultrasound.q: In QA testing, which phantom is used to assess ultrasound image quality?
ultrasound.a: A tissue-mimicking phantom with targets at known positions
c: A water bath with metal pin reflectors only
A tissue-mimicking phantom with targets at known positions
A lead-acrylic phantom for spatial resolution
An air-filled test object with graded slots
ultrasound.e: Tissue-mimicking phantoms contain calibrated targets for assessing resolution and accuracy.

ultrasound.q: Elastography in ultrasound measures tissue:
ultrasound.a: Stiffness by assessing deformation under compression
c: Temperature during thermal therapy treatment
Stiffness by assessing deformation under compression
Perfusion using microbubble contrast agents
Oxygenation through spectral analysis methods
ultrasound.e: Elastography maps tissue stiffness. Harder tissue deforms less.

ultrasound.q: The maximum MI permitted for diagnostic ultrasound (non-ophthalmic) is:
ultrasound.e: The FDA limit for MI is 1.9 for all applications except ophthalmic (MI <= 0.23).

ultrasound.q: What type of transducer is best suited for cardiac imaging through intercostal spaces?
ultrasound.a: Phased array with small footprint
c: Linear array with large rectangular footprint
Phased array with small footprint
Curvilinear array with curved surface
Mechanical sector with rotating element
ultrasound.e: Phased arrays have a small footprint that fits between ribs while providing a wide sector field of view.

ultrasound.q: What is the piezoelectric effect's role in ultrasound transducer operation?
ultrasound.a: Converting electrical signals to mechanical vibrations and vice versa for imaging
c: Focusing the ultrasound beam electronically to a specific depth in the tissue
Generating Doppler frequency shifts to measure blood flow velocity and direction
Converting electrical signals to mechanical vibrations and vice versa for imaging
Filtering out harmonic frequencies to improve fundamental imaging signal quality
ultrasound.e: Piezoelectric crystals convert electrical voltage into mechanical pressure waves (transmit) and returning echoes back into electrical signals (receive), enabling both transmission and detection.

ultrasound.q: What physical principle does color Doppler ultrasound use to display blood flow information?
ultrasound.a: Detection of frequency shifts in reflected ultrasound caused by moving blood cells
c: Measurement of acoustic impedance changes between stationary and flowing blood volumes
Detection of frequency shifts in reflected ultrasound caused by moving blood cells
Analysis of ultrasound attenuation differences between oxygenated and deoxygenated blood
Comparison of pulse transit times between arterial and venous flow path segments
ultrasound.e: Moving blood cells cause a Doppler frequency shift proportional to their velocity. Color Doppler maps these shifts to colors (typically red/blue) indicating flow direction and speed.

mri.q: What does T1 relaxation describe?
mri.a: Recovery of longitudinal magnetization
c: Decay of transverse magnetization
Recovery of longitudinal magnetization
Loss of phase coherence among spins
Change in Larmor precession frequency
mri.e: T1 (spin-lattice) relaxation is the exponential recovery of longitudinal magnetization (Mz) as spins release energy to the surrounding lattice after RF excitation.

mri.q: What does T2 relaxation describe?
mri.a: Decay of transverse magnetization
c: Recovery of longitudinal magnetization
Decay of transverse magnetization
Increase in net magnetic moment
Shift in resonance frequency
mri.e: T2 (spin-spin) relaxation is the irreversible loss of transverse magnetization (Mxy) due to dephasing from spin-spin interactions.

mri.q: Which nucleus is most commonly used in clinical MRI?
mri.a: Hydrogen-1 (proton)
c: Carbon-13 isotope
Hydrogen-1 (proton)
Sodium-23 isotope
Phosphorus-31 isotope
mri.e: Hydrogen-1 is used because of its high natural abundance in the body (water and fat) and its large gyromagnetic ratio, giving the strongest MR signal.

mri.q: What is the Larmor frequency relationship?
mri.a: Frequency equals gyromagnetic ratio times field strength
c: Frequency equals field strength divided by gyromagnetic ratio
Frequency equals gyromagnetic ratio times field strength
Frequency equals gyromagnetic ratio squared times field
Frequency equals field strength minus gyromagnetic ratio
mri.e: The Larmor equation states omega = gamma x B0, where gamma is the gyromagnetic ratio and B0 is the static magnetic field strength.

mri.q: CALC: What is the Larmor frequency of hydrogen protons at 1.5 T? (gamma = 42.58 MHz/T)
mri.e: Using the Larmor equation: f = gamma x B0 = 42.58 MHz/T x 1.5 T = 63.87 MHz.

mri.q: CALC: What is the Larmor frequency of hydrogen protons at 3.0 T? (gamma = 42.58 MHz/T)
mri.e: Using the Larmor equation: f = gamma x B0 = 42.58 MHz/T x 3.0 T = 127.74 MHz.

mri.q: In a spin echo sequence, what is the purpose of the 180-degree refocusing pulse?
mri.a: To rephase spins and correct for static field inhomogeneities
c: To tip magnetization into the transverse plane initially
To rephase spins and correct for static field inhomogeneities
To completely destroy all remaining transverse magnetization
To selectively saturate fat signal in the imaging volume
mri.e: The 180-degree refocusing pulse reverses the phase accumulated by spins due to static B0 inhomogeneities, producing a true T2-weighted echo rather than T2*.

mri.q: What type of contrast weighting is obtained with short TR and short TE?
mri.a: T1-weighted contrast
c: T2-weighted contrast
T1-weighted contrast
Proton density weighted contrast
Diffusion-weighted contrast
mri.e: Short TR maximizes T1 differences between tissues, and short TE minimizes T2 effects, yielding T1-weighted images.

mri.q: What type of contrast weighting is obtained with long TR and long TE?
mri.a: T2-weighted contrast
c: T1-weighted contrast
T2-weighted contrast
Proton density weighted contrast
Susceptibility-weighted contrast
mri.e: Long TR minimizes T1 contrast between tissues, and long TE allows T2 differences to manifest, producing T2-weighted images.

mri.q: What differentiates gradient echo from spin echo sequences?
mri.a: Gradient echo uses gradient reversal instead of a 180-degree RF pulse
c: Gradient echo always uses a 90-degree excitation RF pulse
Gradient echo uses gradient reversal instead of a 180-degree RF pulse
Gradient echo requires longer TR values than spin echo
Gradient echo cannot produce T1-weighted image contrast
mri.e: Gradient echo sequences form an echo by reversing the readout gradient polarity, rather than using a 180-degree RF refocusing pulse. This makes them faster but sensitive to B0 inhomogeneities (T2* weighting).

mri.q: What is k-space in MRI?
mri.a: The spatial frequency domain where raw MR data is stored
c: The physical space inside the magnet bore of the scanner
The spatial frequency domain where raw MR data is stored
The region of the body selected for imaging acquisition
The calibration area used for coil sensitivity mapping
mri.e: K-space is the Fourier domain representation of the MR image. Each point contains spatial frequency information, and the image is reconstructed via inverse Fourier transform.

mri.q: Which part of k-space primarily determines image contrast?
mri.a: The central region of k-space
c: The peripheral edges of k-space
The central region of k-space
The upper-left quadrant of k-space
All regions of k-space contribute equally
mri.e: The central region of k-space contains low spatial frequency data which determines overall image contrast and signal intensity, while peripheral k-space encodes fine detail.

mri.q: What is the function of the slice-selection gradient in MRI?
mri.a: To excite only protons within a specific slice location
c: To encode spatial position along the frequency axis
To excite only protons within a specific slice location
To refocus dephasing spins after the RF excitation
To determine the field of view in the phase direction
mri.e: The slice-selection gradient creates a linear variation in B0 so that only protons in a narrow frequency band matching the RF pulse are excited, selecting a specific slice.

mri.q: How does increasing slice-selection gradient strength affect slice thickness?
mri.a: It decreases slice thickness for the same RF bandwidth
c: It increases the slice thickness proportionally
It decreases slice thickness for the same RF bandwidth
It has no effect on the resulting slice thickness
It doubles slice thickness while halving signal
mri.e: A steeper slice-selection gradient maps the same RF bandwidth to a narrower spatial range, resulting in thinner slices.

mri.q: What is the role of the phase-encoding gradient in MRI?
mri.a: To impart position-dependent phase shifts along one axis
c: To select the imaging slice along the z-axis direction
To impart position-dependent phase shifts along one axis
To generate the echo by reversing gradient polarity
To suppress fat signal using spectral differences
mri.e: The phase-encoding gradient is applied briefly before readout, giving spins at different positions along the phase-encode axis different phase shifts for spatial encoding.

mri.q: What is the role of the frequency-encoding (readout) gradient?
mri.a: To make spins at different positions precess at different frequencies
c: To select which slice of tissue is excited by the RF pulse
To make spins at different positions precess at different frequencies
To apply phase differences between successive excitations
To homogenize the static magnetic field across the volume
mri.e: The frequency-encoding gradient is applied during signal readout so spins at different positions along the readout direction precess at unique frequencies for spatial encoding.

mri.q: CALC: If TR = 500 ms and 256 phase-encoding steps, what is the minimum scan time for one average?
mri.a: 128 seconds
c: 64 seconds
128 seconds
256 seconds
512 seconds
mri.e: Scan time = TR x Npe x NEX = 500 ms x 256 x 1 = 128,000 ms = 128 seconds.

mri.q: CALC: If TR = 4000 ms and ETL = 16 with 256 phase-encoding steps, what is the scan time for 1 NEX?
mri.a: 64 seconds
c: 32 seconds
64 seconds
128 seconds
256 seconds
mri.e: For fast spin echo: scan time = TR x (Npe / ETL) x NEX = 4000 ms x (256/16) x 1 = 64,000 ms = 64 seconds.

mri.q: What is the primary safety concern of the static magnetic field (B0)?
mri.a: The projectile effect from ferromagnetic objects
c: Excessive RF energy deposition in patient tissue
The projectile effect from ferromagnetic objects
Peripheral nerve stimulation from rapid switching
Acoustic noise causing potential hearing damage
mri.e: The strong static magnetic field can attract ferromagnetic objects with great force, turning them into dangerous projectiles. This is the most critical safety hazard in MRI.

mri.q: In the ACR MRI safety zones, which zone contains the MRI magnet itself?
mri.a: Zone IV
c: Zone I
Zone II
Zone III
Zone IV
mri.e: The ACR defines Zone IV as the MRI scanner room itself, which has the highest magnetic field and strictest access control.

mri.q: What does SAR stand for in MRI safety?
mri.a: Specific Absorption Rate
c: Signal Attenuation Ratio
Specific Absorption Rate
Static Alignment Resistance
Spatial Acquisition Resolution
mri.e: SAR measures the rate of RF energy absorption by the body in watts per kilogram. It is a key safety parameter to prevent tissue heating during MRI.

mri.q: How does SAR change when moving from 1.5 T to 3.0 T at the same flip angle?
mri.a: SAR approximately quadruples
c: SAR remains approximately the same
SAR approximately doubles at 3.0 T
SAR approximately quadruples
SAR approximately increases eightfold
mri.e: SAR is proportional to B0 squared. Doubling the field strength from 1.5 T to 3.0 T increases SAR by a factor of (3.0/1.5)^2 = 4.

mri.q: CALC: If SAR at 1.5 T is 2.0 W/kg, what is the estimated SAR at 3.0 T with identical parameters?
mri.a: 8.0 W/kg
c: 4.0 W/kg
6.0 W/kg
8.0 W/kg
mri.e: SAR scales with B0^2. From 1.5 T to 3.0 T: SAR(3T) = 2.0 x (3.0/1.5)^2 = 2.0 x 4 = 8.0 W/kg.

mri.q: What causes peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) during MRI?
mri.a: Rapidly switching magnetic field gradients (dB/dt)
c: The strong static magnetic field pulling on nerves
Rapidly switching magnetic field gradients (dB/dt)
Radiofrequency energy deposited in peripheral tissue
Acoustic vibrations transmitted through the patient table
mri.e: PNS occurs when the rate of change of the magnetic field (dB/dt) from rapidly switching gradients induces electric currents that stimulate peripheral nerves.

mri.q: What is the chemical shift artifact in MRI?
mri.a: Spatial misregistration of fat and water due to frequency differences
c: Bright signal from motion during the acquisition sequence
Spatial misregistration of fat and water due to frequency differences
Signal void caused by metallic implants in the field
Wrap-around of anatomy from outside the field of view
mri.e: Fat and water protons resonate at slightly different frequencies (~3.5 ppm apart). The frequency-encoding gradient misregisters their positions, causing bright/dark bands at fat-water interfaces.

mri.q: CALC: Chemical shift between fat and water at 1.5 T in Hz? (shift = 3.5 ppm, f0 = 63.87 MHz)
mri.a: 224 Hz
c: 150 Hz
224 Hz
350 Hz
448 Hz
mri.e: Chemical shift in Hz = 3.5 ppm x 63.87 MHz = 3.5 x 63.87 = 223.5 Hz, approximately 224 Hz.

mri.q: CALC: Chemical shift between fat and water at 3.0 T in Hz? (shift = 3.5 ppm, f0 = 127.74 MHz)
mri.a: 447 Hz
c: 224 Hz
350 Hz
447 Hz
630 Hz
mri.e: Chemical shift in Hz = 3.5 ppm x 127.74 MHz = 447.1 Hz, approximately 447 Hz.

mri.q: What causes susceptibility artifacts in MRI?
mri.a: Local field distortions at interfaces of different magnetic susceptibilities
c: Misregistration from the chemical shift between fat and water
Local field distortions at interfaces of different magnetic susceptibilities
Incomplete sampling of the outer edges of k-space data
Motion of the patient between successive phase-encoding steps
mri.e: Susceptibility artifacts arise at boundaries between tissues with different magnetic susceptibilities (e.g., air-tissue, metal-tissue), causing local B0 distortions, signal loss, and geometric distortion.

mri.q: What causes aliasing (wrap-around) artifact in MRI?
mri.a: Anatomy outside the FOV being mapped into the image
c: Anatomy outside the FOV being mapped into the image
Patient motion causing ghosting along the phase direction
Gradient nonlinearity distorting the image at the edges
RF interference from external electronic equipment sources
mri.e: Aliasing occurs when the FOV is smaller than the anatomy being imaged. Signals from outside the FOV are undersampled and fold back, violating the Nyquist criterion.

mri.q: In which direction do ghosting artifacts from periodic motion typically appear?
mri.a: Along the phase-encode direction
c: Along the frequency-encode direction
Along the phase-encode direction
Along the slice-select direction
Equally in all three directions
mri.e: Ghosting from periodic motion (cardiac, respiratory) appears along the phase-encoding direction because the motion modulates signal between successive phase-encoding steps.

mri.q: How does increasing NEX (number of signal averages) affect SNR?
mri.a: SNR increases by the square root of NEX
c: SNR increases linearly with the number of averages
SNR increases by the square root of NEX
SNR doubles for each additional signal average
SNR is independent of the number of averages used
mri.e: Signal adds coherently while noise adds randomly, so SNR improves by sqrt(NEX). Doubling NEX from 1 to 2 increases SNR by sqrt(2) = 1.41.

mri.q: CALC: If SNR = 50 with NEX = 1, what is the SNR with NEX = 4?
mri.e: SNR scales with sqrt(NEX). SNR(new) = 50 x sqrt(4) = 50 x 2 = 100.

mri.q: How does doubling the matrix size (256 to 512) in one direction affect SNR, FOV constant?
mri.a: SNR decreases by approximately half
c: SNR increases by approximately half
SNR decreases by approximately half
SNR remains unchanged overall
SNR decreases by a factor of four
mri.e: Doubling the matrix halves the voxel dimension, reducing voxel volume by half. Since SNR is proportional to voxel volume, SNR is approximately halved.

mri.q: CALC: A voxel has dimensions 1 mm x 1 mm x 5 mm. What is the voxel volume?
mri.a: 5 mm cubed
c: 1 mm cubed
5 mm cubed
7 mm cubed
10 mm cubed
mri.e: Voxel volume = 1 x 1 x 5 = 5 mm cubed. Voxel volume directly affects SNR: larger voxels contain more spins and produce more signal.

mri.q: CALC: Pixel size for a 320 mm FOV with a 256 x 256 matrix?
mri.e: Pixel size = FOV / matrix = 320 mm / 256 = 1.25 mm in each in-plane direction.

mri.q: What is the primary mechanism of T1 shortening by gadolinium contrast agents?
mri.a: Dipole-dipole interaction between Gd electrons and water protons
c: Direct absorption of RF energy by gadolinium chelate molecules
Dipole-dipole interaction between Gd electrons and water protons
Displacement of water molecules from the imaging voxel volume
Chemical bonding of gadolinium ions to tissue protein structures
mri.e: Gadolinium has 7 unpaired electrons creating a strong magnetic moment. The dipole-dipole interaction with nearby water protons enhances T1 relaxation, brightening tissues on T1-weighted images.

mri.q: What adverse effect is associated with gadolinium in patients with severe renal impairment?
mri.a: Nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF)
c: Anaphylactic shock from iodine allergy
Nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF)
Acute hepatic failure and necrosis
Permanent sensorineural hearing loss
mri.e: NSF is a rare but serious condition involving fibrosis of skin, joints, and organs. It is linked to certain gadolinium agents in patients with GFR < 30 mL/min.

mri.q: Which fat suppression technique uses a frequency-selective RF pulse at the fat resonance?
mri.a: Spectral (chemical) fat saturation
c: Short tau inversion recovery (STIR)
Spectral (chemical) fat saturation
Dixon water-fat separation method
Spatial-spectral excitation pulses only
mri.e: Spectral fat saturation applies a frequency-selective RF pulse at the fat resonance frequency (~3.5 ppm from water) to selectively null fat signal before imaging excitation.

mri.q: What inversion time (TI) nulls fat signal in STIR at 1.5 T?
mri.a: Approximately 160 ms
c: Approximately 80 ms
Approximately 160 ms
Approximately 440 ms
Approximately 2200 ms
mri.e: In STIR, TI = T1 x ln(2). At 1.5 T, fat T1 is approximately 230 ms, so TI = 0.693 x 230 = 160 ms.

mri.q: CALC: Using TI = T1 x ln(2), what TI nulls CSF in FLAIR? (CSF T1 = 4000 ms)
mri.e: TI = T1 x ln(2) = 4000 x 0.693 = 2772 ms. This is the inversion time used in FLAIR to suppress bright CSF on T2-weighted images.

mri.q: What does diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) primarily measure?
mri.a: The random Brownian motion of water molecules in tissue
c: The blood flow velocity within capillary networks
The random Brownian motion of water molecules in tissue
The rate of contrast agent uptake in tissue
The spin-lattice relaxation rate of intracellular water
mri.e: DWI applies strong gradient pulses that sensitize the MR signal to microscopic water molecule movement. Restricted diffusion (e.g., acute stroke) appears bright on DWI.

mri.q: What does the b-value represent in diffusion-weighted imaging?
mri.a: The degree of diffusion weighting applied by the gradients
c: The baseline signal intensity before gradient application
The degree of diffusion weighting applied by the gradients
The maximum allowed gradient slew rate for the scan
The blood flow contribution to the diffusion signal
mri.e: The b-value (s/mm squared) reflects the strength and timing of diffusion-sensitizing gradients. Higher b-values provide greater diffusion weighting but lower SNR.

mri.q: CALC: S = S0 x exp(-b x ADC). If S0=1000, b=1000, ADC=0.8x10^-3 mm^2/s, what is S?
What is the BOLD effect used in functional MRI (fMRI)?
mri.a: Signal change from varying deoxyhemoglobin concentration in blood
c: Direct measurement of neuronal electrical activity signals
Signal change from varying deoxyhemoglobin concentration in blood
Contrast enhancement from injected paramagnetic agents
Diffusion changes during neural tissue activation events
mri.e: BOLD contrast relies on deoxyhemoglobin being paramagnetic. During neural activation, increased blood flow reduces local deoxyhemoglobin, increasing T2* signal.

mri.q: What MRI sequence is most commonly used for BOLD fMRI?
mri.a: Gradient echo echo-planar imaging (GE-EPI)
c: Spin echo with long echo train length
Gradient echo echo-planar imaging (GE-EPI)
Inversion recovery turbo spin echo
Three-dimensional spoiled gradient echo
mri.e: GE-EPI provides T2*-sensitivity to the BOLD effect and can acquire entire brain volumes rapidly (every 1-3 seconds) for adequate temporal resolution.

mri.q: What is the main advantage of echo-planar imaging (EPI)?
mri.a: Extremely fast acquisition of an entire image after one excitation
c: Superior spatial resolution compared to spin echo imaging
Extremely fast acquisition of an entire image after one excitation
Complete elimination of all susceptibility-related artifacts
Improved fat suppression without additional preparation pulses
mri.e: EPI can acquire all k-space lines after a single RF excitation by rapidly oscillating the readout gradient, enabling acquisition in 50-100 ms.

mri.q: What is parallel imaging in MRI (e.g., GRAPPA, SENSE)?
mri.a: Using multi-element coil data to undersample k-space and reduce scan time
c: Acquiring multiple slices simultaneously with a single RF pulse
Using multi-element coil data to undersample k-space and reduce scan time
Running two identical sequences in parallel on different channels
Doubling the gradient strength to halve the acquisition time
mri.e: Parallel imaging exploits spatial sensitivity differences between receiver coil elements to reconstruct images from undersampled k-space, reducing scan time by factor R with some SNR penalty.

mri.q: CALC: If parallel imaging acceleration R = 2, how does SNR change vs fully sampled?
mri.a: SNR decreases by a factor of sqrt(2), approximately 0.71x
c: SNR decreases by a factor of 2, exactly 0.50x
SNR decreases by a factor of sqrt(2), approximately 0.71x
SNR remains unchanged with parallel imaging
SNR decreases by a factor of 4, exactly 0.25x
mri.e: Parallel imaging reduces SNR by sqrt(R) from reduced sampling, plus additional g-factor penalty. At minimum, SNR(PI) = SNR(full) / sqrt(2) = 0.71x.

mri.q: The flip angle that maximizes signal in a spoiled gradient echo sequence is called?
mri.a: The Ernst angle
c: The Brewster angle
The Bloch angle
The Ernst angle
The Larmor angle
mri.e: The Ernst angle (cos alpha = exp(-TR/T1)) gives the optimal flip angle for maximum signal in a spoiled gradient echo at a given TR and tissue T1.

mri.q: CALC: Ernst angle for tissue T1 = 1000 ms at TR = 100 ms? (cos alpha = exp(-TR/T1))
mri.a: Approximately 26 degrees
c: Approximately 14 degrees
Approximately 26 degrees
Approximately 45 degrees
Approximately 58 degrees
mri.e: cos alpha = exp(-100/1000) = exp(-0.1) = 0.905. alpha = arccos(0.905) = 25.8 degrees, approximately 26 degrees.

mri.q: In MRI safety, what does 'MR Conditional' mean for an implant?
mri.a: The device is safe under specific defined MRI conditions
c: The device is completely unsafe for any MRI examination
The device is safe under specific defined MRI conditions
The device is safe for all MRI field strengths and sequences
The device has not yet been tested for MRI compatibility
mri.e: MR Conditional means the implant poses no known hazard in a specified MRI environment with specified conditions (e.g., field strength, SAR limits, specific coils).

mri.q: What is the main risk of scanning a patient with a cardiac pacemaker in MRI?
mri.a: RF-induced lead heating and potential cardiac stimulation
c: The pacemaker being forcibly pulled from the chest
RF-induced lead heating and potential cardiac stimulation
Complete erasure of all programmed pacemaker parameters
Immediate demagnetization of the pacemaker battery unit
mri.e: Pacemaker leads can act as antennas, concentrating RF energy at the electrode tip. This can cause tissue heating and unintended cardiac stimulation.

mri.q: What is the Dixon technique used for in MRI?
mri.a: Separating fat and water signals using their phase difference
c: Accelerating image acquisition by undersampling k-space
Separating fat and water signals using their phase difference
Correcting geometric distortion from gradient nonlinearity
Measuring tissue perfusion using arterial spin labeling
mri.e: Dixon acquires images when fat and water are in-phase and out-of-phase, then combines them to produce separate water-only and fat-only images.

mri.q: CALC: At 1.5 T, fat/water go in and out of phase with period ~4.4 ms. First out-of-phase TE?
mri.a: 2.2 ms
c: 1.1 ms
2.2 ms
4.4 ms
6.6 ms
mri.e: The first out-of-phase echo occurs at half the period: TE = 4.4 ms / 2 = 2.2 ms. The first in-phase echo is at TE = 4.4 ms.

mri.q: How does moving from 1.5 T to 3.0 T affect SNR?
mri.a: SNR approximately doubles
c: SNR remains approximately the same
SNR approximately doubles
SNR approximately quadruples
SNR increases by approximately 50 percent
mri.e: SNR is approximately linearly proportional to field strength. Moving from 1.5 T to 3.0 T roughly doubles the SNR.

mri.q: What is the purpose of shimming in MRI?
mri.a: To improve the homogeneity of the static magnetic field B0
c: To calibrate the RF transmitter power for patient size
To improve the homogeneity of the static magnetic field B0
To reduce acoustic noise produced by gradient switching
To align the patient correctly at magnet isocentre position
mri.e: Shimming uses passive (metal pieces) and active (shim coils) methods to correct B0 inhomogeneities. Good shimming is critical for fat suppression, EPI, and spectroscopy.

mri.q: CALC: If receiver bandwidth is 32 kHz and matrix is 256, bandwidth per pixel?
mri.a: 125 Hz/pixel
c: 62.5 Hz/pixel
125 Hz/pixel
250 Hz/pixel
500 Hz/pixel
mri.e: Bandwidth per pixel = total bandwidth / matrix = 32000 Hz / 256 = 125 Hz/pixel. Lower bandwidth increases SNR but worsens chemical shift artifact.

mri.q: CALC: Chemical shift artifact in pixels = freq shift / BW per pixel. At 3.0 T with 447 Hz shift and 125 Hz/pixel?
mri.a: 3.6 pixels
c: 1.8 pixels
2.4 pixels
3.6 pixels
7.2 pixels
mri.e: Chemical shift artifact = 447 Hz / 125 Hz/pixel = 3.6 pixels. Higher bandwidth per pixel reduces the artifact but lowers SNR.

mri.q: What unique challenge does the magnetic field pose for an MR-linac?
mri.a: The electron return effect alters dose distribution at tissue interfaces
c: MR-linac cannot produce diagnostic quality images during treatment
The electron return effect alters dose distribution at tissue interfaces
The linac beam is completely absorbed by the MRI cryostat
Patient positioning is impossible inside the combined bore
mri.e: In an MR-linac, the magnetic field causes secondary electrons to curve (Lorentz force), creating the electron return effect (ERE) at tissue-air interfaces, altering dose distributions.

mri.q: What does MRI stand for?
mri.a: Magnetic Resonance Imaging
c: Medical Radiation Imaging
Magnetic Resonance Imaging
Molecular Radiology Investigation
Mechanical Resonance Instrumentation
mri.e: MRI stands for Magnetic Resonance Imaging, a diagnostic imaging technique that uses strong magnetic fields and radiofrequency pulses to produce detailed images of internal body structures.

mri.q: Which atomic nucleus is most commonly used to generate the MRI signal?
mri.a: Hydrogen (proton)
c: Carbon-12
Hydrogen (proton)
Oxygen-16
Nitrogen-14
mri.e: Hydrogen nuclei (single protons) are used because hydrogen is extremely abundant in the body (in water and fat) and has a strong magnetic moment, producing a large detectable signal.

mri.q: Does MRI use ionising radiation to create images?
mri.a: No, it uses magnetic fields and radiofrequency waves
c: Yes, it uses X-rays in clinical practice settings
Yes, it uses gamma rays during the planning stage
No, it uses magnetic fields and radiofrequency waves
Yes, it uses beta particles for radiation dose management
mri.e: MRI does not use ionising radiation. It relies on the interaction of hydrogen nuclei with a strong static magnetic field and radiofrequency (RF) pulses to generate images.

mri.q: What unit is used to measure the strength of the main magnetic field in an MRI scanner?
mri.a: Tesla (T)
c: Gray (Gy)
Sievert (Sv)
Tesla (T)
Hertz (Hz)
mri.e: The Tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic flux density. Clinical MRI scanners typically operate at 1.5 T or 3 T. For reference, the Earth's magnetic field is approximately 0.00005 T.

mri.q: Which of the following is a major safety concern specific to MRI?
mri.a: Ferromagnetic objects becoming projectiles in the magnetic field
c: Radiation dose to the patient for the treatment procedure
Ferromagnetic objects becoming projectiles in the magnetic field
Allergic reaction to sound waves for the treatment procedure
Electrical burns from the imaging table
mri.e: The strong static magnetic field of an MRI scanner can attract ferromagnetic objects with great force, turning them into dangerous projectiles. This is why strict screening for metal implants and objects is mandatory before MRI.

mri.q: CALC: A 3T MRI brain scan uses a turbo spin echo (TSE) sequence with: TR = 4000 ms, TE = 100 ms, ETL (echo train length) = 16, matrix = 256 × 256, 1 NEX. Calculate the scan time. If the ETL is doubled to 32 to reduce scan time, what is the trade-off?
mri.a: Scan time = 64 s; doubling ETL to 32 halves time to 32 s but increases T2 blurring
c: Scan time = 128 s; doubling ETL to 32 halves time to 64 s but increases T2 blurring
Scan time = 64 s; doubling ETL to 32 halves time to 32 s but increases T2 blurring
Scan time = 64 s; doubling ETL to 32 halves time to 32 s but increases SNR
Scan time = 256 s; doubling ETL to 32 halves time to 128 s but decreases spatial resolution
mri.e: Scan time = TR × (phase encodes / ETL) × NEX = 4000 ms × (256/16) × 1 = 4000 × 16 = 64,000 ms = 64 s. With ETL = 32: time = 4000 × (256/32) × 1 = 4000 × 8 = 32,000 ms = 32 s. The trade-off: longer echo trains mean later echoes experience more T2 decay, causing blurring in the phase-encode direction (T2 filtering effect). The effective TE also shifts, potentially altering contrast. Signal from later echoes is lower, reducing edge definition. This is especially problematic for T1-weighted TSE where short echo trains are preferred.

mri.q: CALC: At 3T, the Larmor frequency is 127.74 MHz. A chemical shift artifact is observed between water and fat (3.5 ppm shift). The receive bandwidth is 250 Hz/pixel with a 256 × 256 matrix and 25 cm FOV. Calculate the chemical shift displacement in pixels and in millimetres.
mri.a: Chemical shift = 1.79 pixels = 1.75 mm
c: Chemical shift = 0.89 pixels = 0.87 mm
Chemical shift = 1.79 pixels = 1.75 mm
Chemical shift = 3.58 pixels = 3.50 mm
Chemical shift = 4.47 pixels = 4.37 mm
mri.e: Fat-water frequency difference: Δf = 3.5 ppm × 127.74 MHz = 3.5 × 127.74 = 447.1 Hz. Chemical shift in pixels = Δf / bandwidth per pixel = 447.1 / 250 = 1.788 ≈ 1.79 pixels. Pixel size = FOV / matrix = 250 mm / 256 = 0.977 mm. Displacement in mm = 1.79 × 0.977 = 1.749 ≈ 1.75 mm. At 3T, chemical shift artifacts are doubled compared to 1.5T for the same bandwidth. To reduce this artifact: increase bandwidth (reduces SNR) or use fat suppression techniques.

mri.q: CALC: An MRI department must verify SAR compliance for a body scan at 3T. The RF pulse deposits 4 W/kg whole-body SAR averaged over 6 minutes. The IEC limit for normal operating mode is 2 W/kg whole-body averaged over 6 minutes. By what factor must the flip angle be reduced to bring SAR within the normal mode limit, assuming SAR is proportional to the square of the flip angle?
mri.a: Reduce flip angle by factor of √2 (multiply by 0.707, e.g., 90° becomes ~64°)
c: Reduce flip angle by factor of 2 (e.g., 90° becomes 45°)
Reduce flip angle by factor of √2 (multiply by 0.707, e.g., 90° becomes ~64°)
Reduce flip angle by factor of 4 (e.g., 90° becomes 22.5°)
Reduce flip angle by factor of √3 (multiply by 0.577, e.g., 90° becomes ~52°)
mri.e: SAR ∝ (flip angle)² × (B₀)² × (duty cycle). Current SAR = 4 W/kg, limit = 2 W/kg. Need to reduce by factor of 2. Since SAR ∝ α², we need α_new²/α_old² = 1/2, so α_new/α_old = 1/√2 = 0.707. A 90° pulse becomes 90° × 0.707 = 63.6° ≈ 64°. This halves the SAR from 4 to 2 W/kg. Alternatively, the TR could be increased to reduce the duty cycle, or the number of slices reduced, but flip angle reduction is the most direct SAR-lowering strategy.

mri.q: In k-space for a standard 2D Cartesian acquisition, the centre of k-space determines image contrast and the periphery determines spatial resolution. A radial k-space acquisition fills k-space with spokes through the centre. Compared to Cartesian, what are the key advantages and the characteristic artifact of radial sampling?
mri.a: Advantages: motion robustness and oversampling of centre; characteristic artifact: streak artifacts from angular undersampling
c: Advantages: faster acquisition and higher SNR; characteristic artifact: Gibbs ringing
Advantages: motion robustness and oversampling of centre; characteristic artifact: streak artifacts from angular undersampling
Advantages: reduced chemical shift and no aliasing; characteristic artifact: zipper artifact
Advantages: reduced SAR and thinner slices; characteristic artifact: wrap-around artifact
mri.e: Radial (projection reconstruction) sampling passes every spoke through k-space centre, inherently oversampling low spatial frequencies (contrast information). This provides: (1) robustness to motion since each projection contains complete low-frequency data, (2) no phase-encode direction so no Nyquist ghosting. The characteristic artifact is streak artifacts (similar to CT backprojection) caused by angular undersampling — when insufficient radial spokes are acquired, the undersampling manifests as streaks radiating from high-contrast objects. For full Nyquist sampling, radial requires π/2 × N projections compared to N phase-encode steps in Cartesian.

mri.q: CALC: A diffusion-weighted EPI sequence at 3T uses b-values of 0 and 1000 s/mm². The signal in a brain lesion is 800 (b=0) and 400 (b=1000). In normal white matter, signals are 600 (b=0) and 150 (b=1000). Calculate the ADC for both regions and determine if the lesion shows restricted diffusion.
mri.a: Lesion ADC = 0.69 × 10⁻³ mm²/s; WM ADC = 1.39 × 10⁻³ mm²/s — lesion shows restricted diffusion
c: Lesion ADC = 0.69 × 10⁻³ mm²/s; WM ADC = 1.39 × 10⁻³ mm²/s — lesion shows restricted diffusion
Lesion ADC = 1.39 × 10⁻³ mm²/s; WM ADC = 0.69 × 10⁻³ mm²/s — lesion shows facilitated diffusion
Lesion ADC = 0.35 × 10⁻³ mm²/s; WM ADC = 0.69 × 10⁻³ mm²/s — both show restricted diffusion
Lesion ADC = 0.69 × 10⁻³ mm²/s; WM ADC = 1.39 × 10⁻³ mm²/s — T2 shine-through, not true restriction
mri.e: ADC = -ln(S₁/S₀)/(b₁-b₀). Lesion: ADC = -ln(400/800)/1000 = -ln(0.5)/1000 = 0.693/1000 = 0.693 × 10⁻³ mm²/s. White matter: ADC = -ln(150/600)/1000 = -ln(0.25)/1000 = 1.386/1000 = 1.386 × 10⁻³ mm²/s. The lesion has a lower ADC than normal white matter (0.69 vs 1.39 × 10⁻³), confirming true restricted diffusion (not T2 shine-through, which would show elevated ADC). Normal brain ADC ≈ 0.7-0.9 × 10⁻³ mm²/s. The lesion's ADC at the low end suggests acute infarct or hypercellular tumour. The ADC map eliminates T2 shine-through confound present on DWI images.

mri.q: In MRI, what is the relationship between T1 relaxation and magnetic field strength?
mri.a: T1 increases with field strength because the spectral density at the Larmor frequency decreases for tissue protons
c: T1 decreases with field strength, making T1-weighted imaging faster at 3T
T1 increases with field strength because the spectral density at the Larmor frequency decreases for tissue protons
T1 is independent of field strength and depends only on tissue composition
T1 changes unpredictably with field strength and cannot be characterised
mri.e: T1 relaxation requires energy transfer to the lattice at the Larmor frequency. At higher B0, the Larmor frequency increases, but the spectral density of molecular motions at that frequency decreases for most biological tissues. Result: T1 lengthens at higher fields (e.g., grey matter T1: ~900 ms at 1.5T → ~1200 ms at 3T). T2 is less field-dependent.

mri.q: What is the role of gradient coils in MRI spatial encoding?
mri.a: Gradients create a linear spatial variation in B0, enabling slice selection, frequency encoding, and phase encoding
c: Gradient coils generate the main static magnetic field for proton alignment
Gradients create a linear spatial variation in B0, enabling slice selection, frequency encoding, and phase encoding
Gradient coils only perform shimming to improve field homogeneity
Gradients are used exclusively for diffusion-weighted imaging sequences
mri.e: Three orthogonal gradient coils (x, y, z) superimpose linear field variations on B0. (1) Slice selection: gradient during RF excitation selects a plane. (2) Phase encoding: gradient between excitation and readout encodes spatial position as phase. (3) Frequency encoding: gradient during readout encodes position as frequency. Combined: 2D/3D spatial localisation.

mri.q: Why does chemical shift artefact occur in MRI and at which interface is it most visible?
mri.a: Fat and water protons precess at slightly different frequencies (~3.5 ppm); the artefact appears at fat-water interfaces along the frequency-encoding direction
c: Chemical shift occurs between bone and air interfaces due to susceptibility differences in clinical practice settings
Fat and water protons precess at slightly different frequencies (~3.5 ppm); the artefact appears at fat-water interfaces along the frequency-encoding direction
Chemical shift only occurs in gradient echo sequences and is absent in spin echo for radiation dose management
Chemical shift artefact is caused by gradient non-linearity at the edge of the field of view as part of the clinical workflow
mri.e: Fat protons resonate ~3.5 ppm (220 Hz at 1.5T, 440 Hz at 3T) lower than water protons. In frequency encoding, this shift is misregistered as a spatial displacement.
