clinical.a: Tumour shrinkage and weight loss causing changes in body contour and target volumes
c: Daily random setup errors exceeding the planning margin
Progressive dental artefact degradation on CBCT imaging
Tumour shrinkage and weight loss causing changes in body contour and target volumes
Gradual increase in parotid gland volume from radiation-induced oedema
clinical.e: Head and neck patients frequently experience significant weight loss (often >5 kg) and tumour shrinkage during a 7-week course of chemoradiation. These changes alter body contour, shift organs at risk, and can degrade plan quality, necessitating adaptive replanning typically around fraction 15-20.

clinical.q: What is the typical total dose prescribed for definitive radiotherapy of nasopharyngeal carcinoma?
clinical.a: 70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks
c: 60 Gy in 30 fractions over 6 weeks
66 Gy in 33 fractions over 6.5 weeks
70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks
74 Gy in 37 fractions over 7.5 weeks
clinical.e: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is treated definitively with 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary and involved nodes, with lower dose levels to elective nodal regions. Concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy is standard for stage II and above. IMRT/VMAT is the preferred technique to spare parotid glands.

clinical.q: CALC: A gap correction is needed for a patient who missed 5 treatment days during a 50 Gy/25 fraction course. Using 0.6 Gy/day proliferation correction, what additional dose compensates for the gap?
clinical.a: 3.0 Gy
c: 1.5 Gy
2.0 Gy
3.0 Gy
4.0 Gy
clinical.e: Gap compensation = number of missed days x daily tumour proliferation rate = 5 x 0.6 Gy/day = 3.0 Gy. This correction accounts for accelerated tumour repopulation during treatment interruptions. Treatment gaps are a significant concern in SA due to public holidays, machine breakdowns, and patient transport issues.

clinical.q: For Kaposi sarcoma in HIV-positive patients, what is the typical radiotherapy dose for localised cutaneous lesions?
clinical.a: 8 Gy in a single fraction or 20 Gy in 5 fractions
c: 30 Gy in 10 fractions over 2 weeks
8 Gy in a single fraction or 20 Gy in 5 fractions
50 Gy in 25 fractions over 5 weeks
40 Gy in 20 fractions over 4 weeks
clinical.e: Kaposi sarcoma is highly radiosensitive and responds well to low-dose radiotherapy. Single fractions of 8 Gy or short courses of 20 Gy in 5 fractions achieve high local control rates. This is particularly relevant in SA where endemic KS and HIV-associated KS are common presentations.

clinical.q: What is the primary clinical indication for using bolus material in electron beam therapy?
clinical.a: To shift the dose maximum closer to the skin surface for superficial targets
c: To increase beam penetration depth for deeper target coverage
To shift the dose maximum closer to the skin surface for superficial targets
To reduce bremsstrahlung contamination in the electron beam
To improve lateral dose uniformity at the field edges
clinical.e: Electron beams have a dose build-up region that results in a lower surface dose compared to the maximum dose. Tissue-equivalent bolus material placed on the skin shifts the depth dose curve upward, bringing the maximum dose closer to the surface for treating superficial targets like skin cancers and chest wall scars.

clinical.q: CALC: A breast tangent plan uses 40.05 Gy in 15 fractions. What is the dose per fraction?
clinical.e: Dose per fraction = 40.05/15 = 2.67 Gy. This is the UK START-B hypofractionated breast regimen, which has become standard practice. The slightly higher dose per fraction compared to 2.0 Gy conventional fractionation exploits the low alpha/beta ratio (~4 Gy) of breast cancer.

clinical.q: What is the recommended treatment for superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome caused by lung cancer when histology is unknown?
clinical.a: Start dexamethasone and obtain tissue diagnosis before initiating RT or chemotherapy
c: Immediate emergency radiotherapy with 20 Gy in 5 fractions
Endovascular SVC stenting as the sole definitive treatment
Start dexamethasone and obtain tissue diagnosis before initiating RT or chemotherapy
Immediate concurrent chemoradiation without tissue diagnosis
clinical.e: Current guidelines recommend obtaining a tissue diagnosis before treatment as SVC syndrome is rarely immediately life-threatening, and treatment depends on histology (SCLC responds to chemotherapy, NSCLC may need RT). Dexamethasone provides symptomatic relief. SVC stenting can provide rapid relief if needed.

clinical.q: In paediatric radiotherapy, what is the primary late effect concern when irradiating the spine in young children?
clinical.a: Growth retardation and spinal asymmetry due to damage to vertebral growth plates
c: Acute radiation myelopathy requiring treatment interruption
Growth retardation and spinal asymmetry due to damage to vertebral growth plates
Immediate onset scoliosis requiring surgical correction
Permanent motor neuron damage causing lower limb paralysis
clinical.e: Irradiation of the immature spine damages vertebral growth plates, leading to reduced sitting height, shortened trunk, and potential scoliosis if the dose distribution is asymmetric. This is why the entire vertebral body must be included in the field to ensure symmetric growth retardation in craniospinal irradiation.

clinical.q: What is the QUANTEC spinal cord dose constraint for conventional fractionation (2 Gy per fraction)?
clinical.a: Maximum dose of 50 Gy for a risk of myelopathy less than 0.2%
c: Maximum dose of 45 Gy for a risk of myelopathy less than 1%
Maximum dose of 50 Gy for a risk of myelopathy less than 0.2%
Maximum dose of 54 Gy for a risk of myelopathy less than 5%
Maximum dose of 60 Gy for a risk of myelopathy less than 0.1%
clinical.e: QUANTEC guidelines recommend that the maximum spinal cord dose should not exceed 50 Gy in conventional fractionation to maintain a myelopathy risk below 0.2%. At 60 Gy, the risk increases to approximately 6%. A 5 Gy planning margin (PRV) above the cord is common practice.

clinical.q: CALC: For a patient receiving whole brain radiotherapy of 30 Gy in 10 fractions, what is the EQD2 for late effects (alpha/beta = 2 Gy)?
clinical.a: 37.5 Gy EQD2
c: 30.0 Gy EQD2
33.8 Gy EQD2
37.5 Gy EQD2
42.0 Gy EQD2
clinical.e: EQD2 = D x (d + alpha/beta)/(2 + alpha/beta) = 30 x (3 + 2)/(2 + 2) = 30 x 5/4 = 37.5 Gy. The higher EQD2 compared to the physical dose reflects the increased biological effect of larger fraction sizes on late-responding tissues like the brain.

clinical.q: What is the standard treatment volume for whole-breast radiotherapy following breast-conserving surgery?
clinical.a: The entire ipsilateral breast tissue from the inframammary fold to the clavicle
c: Only the tumour bed with a 2 cm clinical margin around the lumpectomy cavity
The entire ipsilateral breast tissue from the inframammary fold to the clavicle
Both breasts treated simultaneously with bilateral opposed tangential fields
The chest wall and ipsilateral internal mammary nodes as a routine standard
clinical.e: Whole-breast irradiation covers the entire ipsilateral breast tissue using tangential fields. The field extends from the inframammary fold inferiorly to just below the clavicular head superiorly. Regional nodal irradiation is added based on axillary involvement, not routinely.

clinical.q: CALC: What is the equivalent square field size for a rectangular electron field of 8 cm x 14 cm?
c: 9.1 cm
clinical.e: The equivalent square for a rectangular field is calculated as 4 x Area/Perimeter = 4 x (8 x 14)/(2 x (8+14)) = 4 x 112/44 = 448/44 = 10.18 cm, approximately 10.2 cm. This is used to determine output factors and depth dose data for electron beams with rectangular cutouts.

clinical.q: In the SA public health context, what is the main reason for considering hypofractionation in breast and prostate cancer?
clinical.a: Shorter treatment times reduce waiting lists and improve access for underserved patients
c: Hypofractionation always produces superior tumour control probability
It eliminates the need for image-guided radiotherapy verification
Shorter treatment times reduce waiting lists and improve access for underserved patients
It reduces the requirement for physics quality assurance procedures
clinical.e: South Africa has a significant shortage of radiotherapy facilities, particularly in the public sector. Hypofractionated regimens reduce machine time per patient, decreasing waiting lists that can extend to months. Level 1 evidence supports hypofractionation in breast (START-B, FAST-Forward) and prostate (CHHiP, PACE-B) cancers.

clinical.q: What is the recommended total dose for definitive radiotherapy of squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx?
clinical.a: 70 Gy in 35 fractions with concurrent cisplatin
c: 60 Gy in 30 fractions with concurrent cetuximab
66 Gy in 33 fractions without concurrent systemic therapy
70 Gy in 35 fractions with concurrent cisplatin
74 Gy in 37 fractions with concurrent carboplatin
clinical.e: The standard of care for locally advanced oropharyngeal SCC is 70 Gy in 35 fractions with concurrent cisplatin (100 mg/m2 three-weekly or 40 mg/m2 weekly). HPV status influences prognosis but does not currently change standard treatment outside of clinical trials.

clinical.q: What is the main advantage of EPID (electronic portal imaging device) over film for treatment verification?
clinical.a: Immediate digital image availability for online review and correction before treatment
c: Higher spatial resolution enabling detection of submillimetre setup errors
Ability to provide volumetric 3D anatomical information of soft tissue
Immediate digital image availability for online review and correction before treatment
Elimination of all systematic setup errors through automatic correction
clinical.e: EPIDs provide immediate digital images that can be reviewed online (before treatment) or offline (after treatment). This enables real-time correction of setup errors before beam delivery. While film has comparable spatial resolution, its processing time prevents online use.

clinical.q: CALC: A patient is prescribed 50 Gy in 25 fractions to the prostate. If 3 fractions are missed due to a machine breakdown, what is the recommended compensated total dose using 0.6 Gy/day tumour repopulation?
clinical.a: 51.8 Gy in 25 fractions
c: 50.0 Gy in 25 fractions (no change)
50.9 Gy in 25 fractions
51.8 Gy in 25 fractions
53.6 Gy in 25 fractions
clinical.e: The gap adds 3 days of tumour repopulation. Compensation = 3 x 0.6 = 1.8 Gy. New total = 50 + 1.8 = 51.8 Gy, still delivered in 25 fractions (dose per fraction increases to 2.07 Gy). However, this must be balanced against normal tissue tolerances. Note: prostate has slow proliferation so gap corrections are less critical than for H&N tumours.

clinical.q: What is the standard fractionation for craniospinal irradiation (CSI) in paediatric medulloblastoma?
clinical.a: 23.4 Gy in 13 fractions to the craniospinal axis with a posterior fossa boost to 54 Gy
c: 36 Gy in 20 fractions to the craniospinal axis with no boost
23.4 Gy in 13 fractions to the craniospinal axis with a posterior fossa boost to 54 Gy
18 Gy in 10 fractions to the craniospinal axis with a whole-brain boost to 40 Gy
30 Gy in 15 fractions to the craniospinal axis with a posterior fossa boost to 60 Gy
clinical.e: Standard-risk medulloblastoma receives reduced-dose CSI of 23.4 Gy followed by a posterior fossa or tumour bed boost to 54-55.8 Gy. This reduces neurocognitive toxicity compared to older 36 Gy CSI protocols. High-risk disease still receives 36 Gy CSI.

clinical.q: Which clinical scenario most clearly requires interstitial brachytherapy rather than intracavitary brachytherapy for cervical cancer?
clinical.a: Bulky parametrial disease extending beyond the reach of intracavitary applicators
c: Early-stage IB1 cervical cancer with a small central tumour
Bulky parametrial disease extending beyond the reach of intracavitary applicators
Cervical cancer with concurrent weekly cisplatin chemotherapy
Post-hysterectomy vaginal cuff recurrence limited to mucosa
clinical.e: Interstitial brachytherapy using needles (e.g., Vienna, Utrecht, or Benidorm applicators with interstitial needles) allows dose delivery to parametrial tissue beyond the pear-shaped intracavitary isodose distribution. GEC-ESTRO recommends the combined intracavitary/interstitial approach for tumours >5 cm or with significant parametrial extension.

clinical.q: What is the role of fiducial markers in prostate radiotherapy?
clinical.a: To serve as radiographic surrogates for daily prostate position verification on kV imaging
c: To delineate the CTV boundary for treatment planning on CT simulation
To serve as radiographic surrogates for daily prostate position verification on kV imaging
To provide dose measurement points for in-vivo dosimetry during treatment
To function as tissue spacers between the prostate and rectum
clinical.e: Gold fiducial markers (typically 3) are implanted into the prostate before CT simulation. They are visible on kV imaging (CBCT or planar) and allow accurate daily localisation of the prostate, which moves due to variable rectal and bladder filling. They do not measure dose or act as spacers.

clinical.q: CALC: An HDR brachytherapy source has an air kerma strength of 40,000 U (1 U = 1 micro-Gy m2 h-1). What is the dose rate at 1 cm from the source in water, ignoring attenuation and scatter? (dose rate constant = 1.109 cGy h-1 U-1)
clinical.e: Dose rate = SK x dose rate constant / r^2 = 40,000 x 1.109 / 1^2 = 44,360 cGy/h. The dose rate constant (Lambda) converts air kerma strength to dose rate in water at 1 cm. For Ir-192, Lambda is approximately 1.109 cGy/(h·U) per TG-43.

clinical.q: What is the primary advantage of deep inspiration breath-hold (DIBH) technique for left-sided breast radiotherapy?
clinical.a: Reduces heart dose by increasing the distance between the heart and the chest wall tangent fields
c: Improves dose homogeneity within the breast target volume
Reduces heart dose by increasing the distance between the heart and the chest wall tangent fields
Eliminates the need for field-in-field or IMRT planning techniques
Reduces ipsilateral lung dose by compressing the lung parenchyma
clinical.e: During deep inspiration, the diaphragm descends and the chest wall moves anteriorly, displacing the heart away from the tangential breast fields. This can reduce the mean heart dose from approximately 3-5 Gy to 1-2 Gy, significantly reducing the long-term risk of ischaemic heart disease.

clinical.q: CALC: In LDR prostate brachytherapy using I-125 seeds, the prescribed dose is 145 Gy. If the half-life of I-125 is 59.4 days, what percentage of the total dose is delivered in the first 59.4 days?
clinical.e: In one half-life, the source activity drops to 50% of its initial value. The cumulative dose delivered follows 1 - e^(-lambda*t). At t = T_half, cumulative dose = 1 - 0.5 = 50% of total dose. So 50% of 145 Gy = 72.5 Gy is delivered in the first 59.4 days.

clinical.q: In clinical commissioning of a new linear accelerator, what is the correct sequence of measurements?
clinical.a: Mechanical checks, then beam data acquisition, then TPS modelling, then end-to-end testing
c: TPS modelling, then beam data acquisition, then mechanical checks, then patient treatment
Beam data acquisition, then mechanical checks, then end-to-end testing, then TPS modelling
Mechanical checks, then beam data acquisition, then TPS modelling, then end-to-end testing
End-to-end testing, then beam data acquisition, then mechanical checks, then TPS modelling
clinical.e: Clinical commissioning follows a logical sequence: (1) Verify mechanical and safety systems (interlocks, lasers, isocentre). (2) Acquire beam data (PDDs, profiles, output factors). (3) Model beams in the TPS and validate. (4) Perform end-to-end tests with anthropomorphic phantoms before clinical use.

clinical.q: What is the typical dose constraint for the contralateral parotid gland in head and neck IMRT to preserve salivary function?
clinical.a: Mean dose less than 26 Gy
c: Mean dose less than 20 Gy
Mean dose less than 26 Gy
Mean dose less than 32 Gy
Maximum dose less than 40 Gy
clinical.e: QUANTEC recommends a mean parotid dose below 25-26 Gy to maintain significant salivary function and reduce xerostomia risk. At least one parotid gland should be spared to this level. IMRT/VMAT techniques have made this achievable for most head and neck patients.

clinical.q: CALC: For an electron field requiring a skin collimation cutout, the treatment distance is 100 cm SSD and the cutout is at 95 cm. If the desired field size at the surface is 6 cm, what should the cutout dimension be?
clinical.a: 5.7 cm
c: 5.4 cm
5.7 cm
6.0 cm
6.3 cm
clinical.e: Using geometric projection: cutout size = desired size x (cutout distance/SSD) = 6 x (95/100) = 5.7 cm. The cutout is closer to the source than the skin surface, so it must be smaller than the desired field size at the surface. This divergence correction ensures the correct field size at the treatment surface.

clinical.q: What is the recommended waiting period after chemotherapy before starting radiotherapy for HIV-associated non-Hodgkin lymphoma?
clinical.a: Radiotherapy can follow chemotherapy without a mandatory waiting period if clinically indicated
c: A minimum of 6 weeks to allow haematological recovery
Radiotherapy can follow chemotherapy without a mandatory waiting period if clinically indicated
A minimum of 3 months to reduce treatment-related immunosuppression
Radiotherapy should only be given concurrently, never sequentially
clinical.e: In HIV-associated NHL, consolidation radiotherapy can follow chemotherapy without a fixed mandatory waiting period, provided blood counts are adequate. In the SA context, timely radiotherapy is important as patients may be lost to follow-up during long waiting intervals. ART should be continued throughout treatment.

clinical.q: What is the ICRU 50 recommendation for dose homogeneity within the PTV for 3D-CRT plans?
clinical.a: The dose should be between 95% and 107% of the prescribed dose
c: The dose should be between 90% and 110% of the prescribed dose
The dose should be between 95% and 107% of the prescribed dose
The dose should be between 93% and 105% of the prescribed dose
The dose should be between 97% and 103% of the prescribed dose
clinical.e: ICRU Report 50 recommends that the PTV dose should not exceed +7% or fall below -5% of the prescribed dose (i.e., 95-107%). This has been a fundamental planning evaluation criterion for conventional 3D conformal radiotherapy. ICRU 83 updated reporting recommendations for IMRT.

clinical.q: In lung SBRT, what is the minimum requirement for tumour motion management when respiratory excursion exceeds 5 mm?
clinical.a: Use of 4D-CT with ITV definition or real-time tumour tracking
c: Increase PTV margins to 15 mm uniformly without motion management
Use of 4D-CT with ITV definition or real-time tumour tracking
Treat only during free-breathing with standard 3D-CT planning
Use abdominal compression for all patients regardless of motion amplitude
clinical.e: When tumour motion exceeds 5 mm, active motion management is required. Options include 4D-CT to define the internal target volume (ITV), respiratory gating to treat in specific phases, or real-time tracking. Abdominal compression is one option to reduce motion but is not universally applicable.

clinical.q: CALC: A patient receives whole pelvis RT of 45 Gy in 25 fractions followed by an HDR brachytherapy boost of 4 x 7 Gy to point A. What is the cumulative D2cc bladder EQD2 if the bladder D2cc per fraction is 5.5 Gy (alpha/beta = 3 Gy)?
clinical.a: Approximately 92 Gy EQD2 total
c: Approximately 78 Gy EQD2 total
Approximately 85 Gy EQD2 total
Approximately 92 Gy EQD2 total
Approximately 99 Gy EQD2 total
clinical.e: EBRT EQD2 = 45 x (1.8+3)/(2+3) = 45 x 4.8/5 = 43.2 Gy. Brachy D2cc per fraction EQD2 = 5.5 x (5.5+3)/(2+3) = 5.5 x 8.5/5 = 9.35 Gy per fx, x 4 = 37.4 Gy. Some institutional approaches add EBRT contribution to bladder differently, but approximate total is ~80+12 = 92 Gy EQD2. GEC-ESTRO recommends D2cc bladder < 90 Gy EQD2.

clinical.q: What is the primary role of concurrent temozolomide with radiotherapy in high-grade glioma (Stupp protocol)?
clinical.a: Radiosensitisation during RT followed by adjuvant temozolomide for 6 cycles
c: Neoadjuvant tumour reduction before radiotherapy commences
Radiosensitisation during RT followed by adjuvant temozolomide for 6 cycles
Replacement of radiotherapy in elderly patients over 70 years
Post-radiation immunomodulation without concurrent administration
clinical.e: The Stupp protocol for glioblastoma multiforme combines 60 Gy in 30 fractions with daily temozolomide (75 mg/m2) during RT, followed by 6 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m2). MGMT methylation status predicts temozolomide benefit. This protocol improved median survival from 12 to 14.6 months.

clinical.q: What verification imaging frequency is recommended for radical head and neck radiotherapy with an online correction protocol?
clinical.a: Daily CBCT for the first 5 fractions, then at least weekly thereafter
c: Only at the first fraction and then at fraction 15 for replanning
Weekly EPID portal images without volumetric imaging
Daily CBCT for the first 5 fractions, then at least weekly thereafter
Every third fraction using orthogonal kV planar imaging only
clinical.e: Head and neck treatments require frequent verification due to tight PTV margins near critical structures (spinal cord, brainstem, parotids). Daily imaging initially captures systematic errors, and weekly imaging thereafter monitors progressive anatomical changes. Many centres use daily CBCT throughout for this site.

clinical.q: CALC: The nominal treatment distance for a TBI patient is 4 metres. If the standard calibration distance is 100 cm and the output at 100 cm is 1 cGy/MU, what is the approximate output at 4 metres using inverse square law?
clinical.a: 0.0625 cGy/MU
c: 0.0400 cGy/MU
0.0500 cGy/MU
0.0625 cGy/MU
0.0800 cGy/MU
clinical.e: Using inverse square: Output at 400 cm = 1 x (100/400)^2 = 1 x (0.25)^2 = 0.0625 cGy/MU. At the extended distances used in TBI, the dramatically reduced dose rate helps reduce pulmonary toxicity. Actual output must be measured and verified, as inverse square alone does not account for scatter changes.

clinical.q: What is the standard dose and fractionation for adjuvant radiotherapy following radical prostatectomy with positive surgical margins?
clinical.a: 64-66 Gy in 32-33 fractions to the prostate bed
c: 50 Gy in 25 fractions to the whole pelvis only
70 Gy in 35 fractions to the prostate bed
64-66 Gy in 32-33 fractions to the prostate bed
74 Gy in 37 fractions to the prostate bed and pelvic nodes
clinical.e: Adjuvant or salvage post-prostatectomy radiotherapy is typically prescribed to 64-66 Gy in 1.8-2.0 Gy fractions to the prostate bed. This is lower than definitive prostate RT (74-78 Gy) because the intact prostate gland has been removed. Pelvic nodal RT may be added based on risk stratification.

clinical.q: What is the standard fractionation schedule for radical treatment of cervical cancer in South Africa?
clinical.a: 45-50 Gy in 25 fractions over 5 weeks with concurrent cisplatin
c: 45-50 Gy in 25 fractions over 5 weeks with concurrent cisplatin
60 Gy in 30 fractions over 6 weeks with concurrent carboplatin
70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks with concurrent cisplatin
40 Gy in 20 fractions over 4 weeks with concurrent 5-fluorouracil
clinical.e: Standard of care for cervical cancer is external beam RT of 45-50 Gy in 25 fractions with weekly concurrent cisplatin (40 mg/m²), followed by brachytherapy boost. This is the protocol used in most SA oncology centres.

clinical.q: In whole breast irradiation after breast-conserving surgery, what is the conventional total dose and fractionation?
clinical.a: 50 Gy in 25 fractions of 2 Gy each over 5 weeks
c: 50 Gy in 25 fractions of 2 Gy each over 5 weeks
45 Gy in 20 fractions of 2.25 Gy each over 4 weeks
60 Gy in 30 fractions of 2 Gy each over 6 weeks
42 Gy in 15 fractions of 2.8 Gy each over 3 weeks
clinical.e: Conventional whole breast RT is 50 Gy in 25 fractions. Hypofractionated schedules (e.g. 40.05 Gy in 15 fractions) are increasingly used, but 50 Gy/25# remains the conventional reference standard.

clinical.q: CALC: A split-beam technique requires a field junction gap on the skin surface. If the superior field is 20 cm long with an SSD of 100 cm treating to 10 cm depth, what is the half-gap contribution from this field?
clinical.a: 1.0 cm
c: 0.5 cm
1.0 cm
1.5 cm
2.0 cm
clinical.e: Gap = (L₁/2) × (d/SSD) = (20/2) × (10/100) = 10 × 0.1 = 1.0 cm. This is the contribution from one field; total gap is sum of contributions from both fields.

clinical.q: According to QUANTEC, what is the maximum mean lung dose constraint to keep pneumonitis risk below 20%?
clinical.a: Mean lung dose should not exceed 20 Gy
c: Mean lung dose should not exceed 20 Gy
Mean lung dose should not exceed 15 Gy
Mean lung dose should not exceed 25 Gy
Mean lung dose should not exceed 10 Gy
clinical.e: QUANTEC guidelines recommend keeping the mean lung dose (MLD) ≤ 20 Gy to maintain symptomatic pneumonitis risk below approximately 20%. V20 < 30% is also commonly used.

clinical.q: What is the purpose of using a bolus material on the chest wall during post-mastectomy radiation therapy?
clinical.a: To increase the surface dose by bringing the dose buildup region to the skin
c: To increase the surface dose by bringing the dose buildup region to the skin
To decrease the exit dose and protect underlying lung from excess radiation
To act as a compensator that flattens the dose distribution at deeper tissues
To reduce electron scatter and improve the sharpness of the beam penumbra
clinical.e: Bolus (typically 0.5-1 cm tissue-equivalent material) is placed on the skin surface to shift the buildup region outward, effectively increasing the surface/skin dose. This is critical when the target (e.g. chest wall, surgical scar) is superficial.

clinical.q: In IMRT for head and neck cancer, what is the typical dose constraint for the parotid glands to preserve salivary function?
clinical.a: Mean dose to at least one parotid gland should be kept below 26 Gy
c: Mean dose to at least one parotid gland should be kept below 26 Gy
Maximum point dose to both parotid glands should be kept below 30 Gy
Mean dose to both parotid glands combined should be kept below 20 Gy
Volume receiving more than 15 Gy should be less than 50% for each gland
clinical.e: QUANTEC recommends mean parotid dose < 25-26 Gy to at least one gland to preserve useful salivary function. This is a key optimization objective in H&N IMRT planning.

clinical.q: CALC: For a craniospinal irradiation (CSI) field junction, if two adjacent fields each have a length of 16 cm at an SSD of 100 cm and the treatment depth is 5 cm, what is the required skin gap?
clinical.a: 0.8 cm
c: 0.4 cm
0.8 cm
1.2 cm
1.6 cm
clinical.e: Gap = (L₁/2)(d/SSD) + (L₂/2)(d/SSD) = (16/2)(5/100) + (16/2)(5/100) = 0.4 + 0.4 = 0.8 cm. Both fields contribute equally to the gap.

clinical.q: What is the standard radical dose and fractionation for definitive chemoradiation of locally advanced head and neck squamous cell carcinoma?
clinical.a: 70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks delivered concurrently with cisplatin
c: 70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks delivered concurrently with cisplatin
66 Gy in 33 fractions over 6.5 weeks delivered concurrently with cetuximab
60 Gy in 30 fractions over 6 weeks delivered concurrently with carboplatin
74 Gy in 37 fractions over 7.5 weeks delivered sequentially after cisplatin
clinical.e: The standard of care for radical treatment of locally advanced H&N SCC is 70 Gy in 35 fractions (2 Gy/fraction) with concurrent high-dose cisplatin (100 mg/m² on days 1, 22, 43).

clinical.q: In VMAT planning, what is the primary advantage over fixed-field IMRT for complex target volumes?
clinical.a: Continuous gantry rotation allows faster delivery and improved dose conformality
c: Continuous gantry rotation allows faster delivery and improved dose conformality
Fixed gantry angles provide more stable MLC positions and better reproducibility
Static beam delivery reduces interplay effects and improves target dose accuracy
Sequential arc segments enable sharper dose gradients at field edges than IMRT
clinical.e: VMAT delivers radiation during continuous gantry rotation with simultaneous MLC modulation and dose rate variation, enabling faster treatment times and often improved dose conformality compared to fixed-field step-and-shoot IMRT.

clinical.q: What is the recommended spinal cord maximum dose constraint according to QUANTEC for conventional fractionation?
clinical.a: Maximum dose of 50 Gy to the spinal cord using conventional fractionation
c: Maximum dose of 50 Gy to the spinal cord using conventional fractionation
Maximum dose of 60 Gy to the spinal cord using conventional fractionation
Maximum dose of 45 Gy to the spinal cord using conventional fractionation
Maximum dose of 54 Gy to the spinal cord using conventional fractionation
clinical.e: QUANTEC recommends a maximum spinal cord dose of 50 Gy (in 2 Gy fractions) to keep the risk of myelopathy below 0.2%. At 60 Gy the risk rises to approximately 6%.

clinical.q: CALC: A patient is prescribed 8 Gy single fraction for bone metastasis palliation. If the linac output is 1 cGy/MU at dmax for a 10×10 cm field at 100 cm SSD, and the treatment depth PDD is 65%, how many MU are needed?
clinical.e: MU = Dose / (Output × PDD) = 800 cGy / (1.0 cGy/MU × 0.65) = 800/0.65 = 1230.8 ≈ 1231 MU. This is a simple SSD-based MU calculation for a palliative single fraction.

clinical.q: Which immobilization device is most commonly used for head and neck radiation therapy to ensure reproducible patient positioning?
clinical.a: Thermoplastic mask with five-point fixation covering the head and shoulders
c: Thermoplastic mask with five-point fixation covering the head and shoulders
Vacuum cushion body mould extending from the vertex to below the shoulders
Alpha cradle foam cast with indexed baseplate covering the head and neck
Carbon fibre headrest with simple velcro forehead strap for stabilization
clinical.e: A 5-point thermoplastic mask (covering head, face, and shoulders) is the standard immobilization device for H&N RT. It provides rigid fixation with typical setup reproducibility of 3-5 mm.

clinical.q: In total body irradiation (TBI) for bone marrow transplant conditioning, what is the typical total dose when using a fractionated regimen?
clinical.a: 12 Gy delivered in 6 fractions of 2 Gy over 3 consecutive days
c: 12 Gy delivered in 6 fractions of 2 Gy over 3 consecutive days
10 Gy delivered in 5 fractions of 2 Gy over 5 consecutive days
14 Gy delivered in 7 fractions of 2 Gy over 4 consecutive days
8 Gy delivered in 4 fractions of 2 Gy over 2 consecutive days
clinical.e: A common fractionated TBI regimen is 12 Gy in 6 fractions (2 Gy BID over 3 days). Lung shielding is used to limit the lung dose to approximately 8-10 Gy to reduce pneumonitis risk.

clinical.q: What is the standard dose and fractionation for adjuvant whole brain radiation therapy (WBRT) for brain metastases?
clinical.a: 30 Gy in 10 fractions of 3 Gy delivered over 2 weeks
c: 30 Gy in 10 fractions of 3 Gy delivered over 2 weeks
20 Gy in 5 fractions of 4 Gy delivered over 1 week
40 Gy in 20 fractions of 2 Gy delivered over 4 weeks
25 Gy in 10 fractions of 2.5 Gy delivered over 2 weeks
clinical.e: 30 Gy in 10 fractions is the most widely used WBRT schedule for brain metastases. Alternative palliative schedules include 20 Gy in 5 fractions for patients with poor prognosis.

clinical.q: CALC: For a breast tangential field plan, the chest wall separation is 22 cm. Using 6 MV photons, what percentage depth dose would you expect at the midplane (11 cm depth) for a 15×15 cm field?
clinical.a: Approximately 62-65% based on standard 6 MV PDD tables
c: Approximately 62-65% based on standard 6 MV PDD tables
Approximately 75-78% based on standard 6 MV PDD tables
Approximately 50-53% based on standard 6 MV PDD tables
Approximately 85-88% based on standard 6 MV PDD tables
clinical.e: At 11 cm depth for a 6 MV beam with 15×15 cm field, the PDD is approximately 62-65%. This demonstrates why higher energy beams or field-in-field techniques may be needed for large breast separations.

clinical.q: In prostate EBRT using IMRT, what is the QUANTEC dose-volume constraint for the rectum to limit Grade 2+ rectal toxicity?
clinical.a: V70 should be less than 20% and V75 should be less than 15% of rectal volume
c: V70 should be less than 20% and V75 should be less than 15% of rectal volume
V60 should be less than 30% and V65 should be less than 20% of rectal volume
V50 should be less than 50% and V60 should be less than 35% of rectal volume
V75 should be less than 25% and V80 should be less than 20% of rectal volume
clinical.e: QUANTEC rectal constraints for prostate RT: V50 < 50%, V60 < 35%, V65 < 25%, V70 < 20%, V75 < 15%. These limits help keep Grade 2+ late rectal toxicity below 15%.

clinical.q: What electron beam energy is typically selected for treating a superficial chest wall tumour with a target thickness of 3 cm?
clinical.a: 9 MeV electrons, since the therapeutic range (R90) is approximately 2.7-3.0 cm
c: 9 MeV electrons, since the therapeutic range (R90) is approximately 2.7-3.0 cm
6 MeV electrons, since the therapeutic range (R90) is approximately 2.7-3.0 cm
12 MeV electrons, since the therapeutic range (R90) is approximately 2.7-3.0 cm
15 MeV electrons, since the therapeutic range (R90) is approximately 2.7-3.0 cm
clinical.e: The rule of thumb is E(MeV)/3 ≈ R90 in cm. For a 3 cm target, E ≈ 3 × 3 = 9 MeV. The 90% isodose depth for 9 MeV electrons is approximately 2.7-3.0 cm in water.

clinical.q: CALC: In a hypofractionated breast protocol of 40.05 Gy in 15 fractions, what is the dose per fraction?
clinical.a: 2.67 Gy per fraction
c: 2.50 Gy per fraction
2.67 Gy per fraction
2.85 Gy per fraction
3.00 Gy per fraction
clinical.e: Dose per fraction = Total dose / Number of fractions = 40.05 / 15 = 2.67 Gy. This is the UK START-B style hypofractionated breast schedule, increasingly adopted in SA.

clinical.q: What is the typical simulation position for a patient undergoing radical radiation therapy for lung cancer?
clinical.a: Supine with arms raised above the head in a wing board or arm shuttle device
c: Supine with arms raised above the head in a wing board or arm shuttle device
Prone with arms at the sides and a belly board to displace abdominal contents
Supine with arms at the sides using a vacuum cushion for thoracic stabilization
Lateral decubitus position with the affected lung side up and arms extended
clinical.e: Standard lung RT simulation position is supine with both arms above the head (using wing board, arm shuttle, or similar device) to move the arms out of the beam path for lateral and oblique fields.

clinical.q: In total skin electron therapy (TSET), what is the most commonly used electron beam energy at the patient surface?
clinical.a: 4-6 MeV to ensure adequate skin dose coverage with limited deep tissue penetration
c: 4-6 MeV to ensure adequate skin dose coverage with limited deep tissue penetration
9-12 MeV to ensure adequate skin dose coverage with moderate tissue penetration
15-18 MeV to ensure adequate coverage of both skin and subcutaneous tissues
2-3 MeV to limit all dose to the superficial epidermis with no dermal penetration
clinical.e: TSET typically uses 4-6 MeV electrons, which after beam degradation through extended SSD and scatter plates, deliver therapeutic dose to the skin surface while limiting dose to deeper structures.

clinical.q: CALC: What is the biologically effective dose (BED) for a palliative schedule of 20 Gy in 5 fractions assuming an alpha/beta ratio of 10 Gy for tumour?
clinical.a: 28 Gy₁₀ using the BED formula with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
c: 24 Gy₁₀ using the BED formula with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
28 Gy₁₀ using the BED formula with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
32 Gy₁₀ using the BED formula with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
36 Gy₁₀ using the BED formula with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
clinical.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 5 × 4 × (1 + 4/10) = 20 × 1.4 = 28 Gy₁₀. This is a common palliative schedule used for symptomatic metastatic disease.

clinical.q: What is the QUANTEC brainstem maximum dose constraint for conventional fractionation to limit the risk of permanent cranial neuropathy?
clinical.a: Maximum point dose to the brainstem should not exceed 54 Gy
c: Maximum point dose to the brainstem should not exceed 54 Gy
Maximum point dose to the brainstem should not exceed 60 Gy
Maximum point dose to the brainstem should not exceed 48 Gy
Maximum point dose to the brainstem should not exceed 45 Gy
clinical.e: QUANTEC recommends a maximum brainstem dose of 54 Gy for conventional fractionation. Small volumes (1-10 cc) may tolerate up to 59 Gy with low risk of permanent neuropathy.

clinical.q: In the treatment of paediatric medulloblastoma, what is the reduced craniospinal irradiation (CSI) dose used for standard-risk patients?
clinical.a: 23.4 Gy CSI followed by a posterior fossa boost to a total of 54-55.8 Gy
c: 23.4 Gy CSI followed by a posterior fossa boost to a total of 54-55.8 Gy
36.0 Gy CSI followed by a posterior fossa boost to a total of 54-55.8 Gy
18.0 Gy CSI followed by a posterior fossa boost to a total of 50-50.4 Gy
30.0 Gy CSI followed by a posterior fossa boost to a total of 60-61.2 Gy
clinical.e: Standard-risk paediatric medulloblastoma receives reduced CSI dose of 23.4 Gy (from historic 36 Gy) followed by a posterior fossa boost to 54-55.8 Gy, combined with adjuvant chemotherapy.

clinical.q: CALC: For a four-field box technique treating a prostate at 100 cm SAD, if the anterior field TMR at the treatment depth is 0.72 and the linac output is 1.0 cGy/MU, how many MU are needed to deliver 50 cGy to the isocentre from the anterior field?
clinical.a: 69 MU to deliver the prescribed dose contribution from the anterior field
c: 56 MU to deliver the prescribed dose contribution from the anterior field
69 MU to deliver the prescribed dose contribution from the anterior field
82 MU to deliver the prescribed dose contribution from the anterior field
75 MU to deliver the prescribed dose contribution from the anterior field
clinical.e: MU = Dose / (Output × TMR) = 50 / (1.0 × 0.72) = 50/0.72 = 69.4 ≈ 69 MU. In a 4-field box, each field delivers a portion of the total isocentre dose.

clinical.q: What is the role of concurrent cisplatin chemotherapy in the radical treatment of cervical cancer with radiation?
clinical.a: It acts as a radiosensitizer that enhances tumour cell kill and improves local control
c: It acts as a radiosensitizer that enhances tumour cell kill and improves local control
It serves as a cytoreductive agent that shrinks the tumour before radiation commences
It functions as a radioprotector that selectively shields normal tissues from damage
It works as an immunomodulator that triggers systemic anti-tumour immune responses
clinical.e: Concurrent cisplatin (40 mg/m² weekly) acts primarily as a radiosensitizer, enhancing radiation-induced DNA damage in tumour cells. Multiple randomised trials showed improved overall survival with concurrent chemoRT for cervical cancer.

clinical.q: In 3DCRT for prostate cancer, what beam arrangement is most commonly used to achieve adequate target coverage while sparing the rectum?
clinical.a: Four-field box technique using anterior, posterior, and two lateral opposed beams
c: Four-field box technique using anterior, posterior, and two lateral opposed beams
Three-field technique using one anterior and two posterior oblique wedged beams
Six-field coplanar technique using equally spaced beams at 60-degree intervals
Two-field technique using right and left lateral opposed beams with wedge filters
clinical.e: The four-field box (AP, PA, right lateral, left lateral) is the classic 3DCRT technique for prostate. It provides good target coverage with reasonable OAR sparing, though IMRT/VMAT have largely superseded it.

clinical.q: CALC: A patient requires retreatment of the spinal cord region. The original treatment delivered 40 Gy in 20 fractions. Using an alpha/beta of 2 Gy for spinal cord, what was the original BED?
clinical.a: 80 Gy₂ calculated using the linear-quadratic BED formula
c: 60 Gy₂ calculated using the linear-quadratic BED formula
80 Gy₂ calculated using the linear-quadratic BED formula
70 Gy₂ calculated using the linear-quadratic BED formula
90 Gy₂ calculated using the linear-quadratic BED formula
clinical.e: BED = nd(1 + d/(α/β)) = 20 × 2 × (1 + 2/2) = 40 × 2.0 = 80 Gy₂. This is critical for evaluating cumulative spinal cord tolerance in retreatment scenarios.

clinical.q: What is the standard palliative dose fractionation schedule for painful uncomplicated bone metastases recommended by international guidelines?
clinical.a: 8 Gy in a single fraction, which has equivalent pain relief outcomes to longer courses
c: 8 Gy in a single fraction, which has equivalent pain relief outcomes to longer courses
30 Gy in 10 fractions, which provides superior long-term pain control over other regimens
20 Gy in 5 fractions, which is the minimum dose needed for effective tumour regression
3 Gy in 10 fractions, which has the lowest risk of pathological fracture complications
clinical.e: Multiple RCTs have shown that 8 Gy single fraction provides equivalent pain relief to multi-fraction regimens (20 Gy/5# or 30 Gy/10#) for uncomplicated bone metastases, with higher retreatment rates but greater convenience.

clinical.q: In HDR brachytherapy for cervical cancer, what is the typical dose per fraction when using intracavitary applications as a boost after external beam RT?
clinical.a: 7 Gy per fraction to Point A, typically delivered in 3-5 fractions
c: 7 Gy per fraction to Point A, typically delivered in 3-5 fractions
4 Gy per fraction to Point A, typically delivered in 8-10 fractions
10 Gy per fraction to Point A, typically delivered in 2-3 fractions
5 Gy per fraction to Point A, typically delivered in 6-8 fractions
clinical.e: Common HDR cervix brachytherapy schedules include 7 Gy × 4 fractions or similar regimens to Point A (or HR-CTV D90 in image-guided brachytherapy). The ABS recommends total EQD2 of 80-90 Gy to the target.

clinical.q: What is the recommended QUANTEC dose constraint for the optic nerves and chiasm to prevent radiation-induced optic neuropathy?
clinical.a: Maximum dose should not exceed 55 Gy using conventional fractionation schedules
c: Maximum dose should not exceed 55 Gy using conventional fractionation schedules
Maximum dose should not exceed 45 Gy using conventional fractionation schedules
Maximum dose should not exceed 60 Gy using conventional fractionation schedules
Maximum dose should not exceed 50 Gy using conventional fractionation schedules
clinical.e: QUANTEC recommends a maximum optic nerve/chiasm dose of 55 Gy (conventional fractionation) to keep the risk of optic neuropathy below 3-7%. This is a critical constraint for brain and H&N treatments.

clinical.q: CALC: In a mantle field technique, the field junction gap is calculated for an upper and lower field. If the upper field half-length is 12 cm and treatment depth is 8 cm at 100 cm SSD, what is the gap contribution from the upper field?
clinical.a: 0.96 cm gap contribution from the upper field alone
c: 0.48 cm gap contribution from the upper field alone
0.96 cm gap contribution from the upper field alone
1.20 cm gap contribution from the upper field alone
0.72 cm gap contribution from the upper field alone
clinical.e: Gap contribution = (L/2) × (d/SSD) = (24/2) × (8/100)... Wait: half-length is given as 12 cm, so gap = 12 × (8/100) = 12 × 0.08 = 0.96 cm.

clinical.q: What is the significance of the HPCSA registration for medical physicists practising radiation therapy in South Africa?
clinical.a: It is a legal requirement for practising clinical medical physics independently in SA
c: It is a legal requirement for practising clinical medical physics independently in SA
It is an optional professional credential that improves employment prospects in SA
It is only required for medical physicists working in the public healthcare sector
It is a research qualification needed to supervise physics residency training in SA
clinical.e: HPCSA (Health Professions Council of South Africa) registration is mandatory for all medical physicists practising independently in South Africa. It is a legal requirement under the Health Professions Act.

clinical.q: In a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) IMRT plan for head and neck cancer, what are the typical dose levels for the three target volumes?
clinical.a: 70 Gy to gross disease, 63 Gy to high-risk subclinical, and 56 Gy to low-risk nodal regions
c: 70 Gy to gross disease, 63 Gy to high-risk subclinical, and 56 Gy to low-risk nodal regions
66 Gy to gross disease, 60 Gy to high-risk subclinical, and 54 Gy to low-risk nodal regions
74 Gy to gross disease, 66 Gy to high-risk subclinical, and 60 Gy to low-risk nodal regions
60 Gy to gross disease, 54 Gy to high-risk subclinical, and 48 Gy to low-risk nodal regions
clinical.e: A common SIB schedule delivers 70 Gy (2.12 Gy/#), 63 Gy (1.8 Gy/#), and 56 Gy (1.6 Gy/#) in 33 fractions to the three dose levels. This treats all volumes simultaneously rather than using sequential cone-downs.

clinical.q: CALC: What is the equivalent dose in 2 Gy fractions (EQD2) for a brachytherapy fraction of 7 Gy, assuming an alpha/beta ratio of 10 Gy for tumour?
clinical.a: 9.92 Gy EQD2 per fraction for tumour with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
c: 7.50 Gy EQD2 per fraction for tumour with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
9.92 Gy EQD2 per fraction for tumour with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
8.40 Gy EQD2 per fraction for tumour with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
11.2 Gy EQD2 per fraction for tumour with alpha/beta of 10 Gy
clinical.e: EQD2 = D × (d + α/β)/(2 + α/β) = 7 × (7 + 10)/(2 + 10) = 7 × 17/12 = 7 × 1.417 = 9.92 Gy. This converts a single 7 Gy fraction to its equivalent in 2 Gy fractions.

clinical.q: What is the primary clinical advantage of deep inspiration breath-hold (DIBH) technique in left-sided breast radiation therapy?
clinical.a: It displaces the heart away from the treatment field, reducing cardiac dose significantly
c: It displaces the heart away from the treatment field, reducing cardiac dose significantly
It compresses the breast tissue to create a more uniform dose distribution throughout
It increases lung density to reduce the volume of lung receiving therapeutic radiation
It stabilizes the chest wall motion to improve daily setup reproducibility at treatment
clinical.e: DIBH inflates the lungs, which pushes the heart posteriorly and inferiorly, increasing the distance between the heart and the tangential breast fields. This typically reduces mean heart dose by 40-60%.

clinical.q: In the FAST-Forward trial for breast cancer, what was the ultra-hypofractionated dose schedule shown to be non-inferior to the standard?
clinical.a: 26 Gy in 5 fractions delivered over 1 week as the recommended schedule
c: 26 Gy in 5 fractions delivered over 1 week as the recommended schedule
28 Gy in 5 fractions delivered over 1 week as the recommended schedule
30 Gy in 5 fractions delivered over 1 week as the recommended schedule
24 Gy in 5 fractions delivered over 1 week as the recommended schedule
clinical.e: The FAST-Forward trial demonstrated that 26 Gy in 5 fractions over 1 week was non-inferior to 40 Gy in 15 fractions for local tumour control and had comparable normal tissue effects. This schedule is being adopted in SA.

clinical.q: CALC: For electron beam therapy, using the rule of thumb E/2 for the practical range (Rp), what is the approximate practical range of a 16 MeV electron beam in tissue?
clinical.a: 8 cm practical range in water-equivalent tissue
c: 4 cm practical range in water-equivalent tissue
6 cm practical range in water-equivalent tissue
8 cm practical range in water-equivalent tissue
10 cm practical range in water-equivalent tissue
clinical.e: The practical range Rp ≈ E(MeV)/2 in cm. For 16 MeV: Rp ≈ 16/2 = 8 cm. The therapeutic range R90 ≈ E/3 ≈ 5.3 cm, and R80 ≈ E/2.5 ≈ 6.4 cm.

clinical.q: What is the recommended dose constraint for the femoral heads in pelvic IMRT according to QUANTEC guidelines?
clinical.a: V50 should be less than 5% to minimize the risk of avascular necrosis
c: V50 should be less than 5% to minimize the risk of avascular necrosis
V40 should be less than 20% to minimize the risk of avascular necrosis
V45 should be less than 10% to minimize the risk of avascular necrosis
V60 should be less than 5% to minimize the risk of avascular necrosis
clinical.e: QUANTEC recommends V50 < 5% for the femoral heads to keep the risk of femoral head necrosis acceptably low. Some protocols use a maximum dose constraint of 52 Gy.

clinical.q: In concurrent chemoradiation for locally advanced non-small cell lung cancer, what is the standard radiation dose and fractionation?
clinical.a: 60 Gy in 30 fractions of 2 Gy delivered over 6 weeks with concurrent chemotherapy
c: 60 Gy in 30 fractions of 2 Gy delivered over 6 weeks with concurrent chemotherapy
66 Gy in 33 fractions of 2 Gy delivered over 6.5 weeks with concurrent chemotherapy
50 Gy in 25 fractions of 2 Gy delivered over 5 weeks with concurrent chemotherapy
74 Gy in 37 fractions of 2 Gy delivered over 7.5 weeks with concurrent chemotherapy
clinical.e: 60 Gy in 30 fractions is the standard dose for concurrent chemoRT in locally advanced NSCLC, based on the RTOG 0617 trial which showed that dose escalation to 74 Gy was inferior to 60 Gy.

clinical.q: CALC: A treatment field requires a half-beam block (asymmetric jaw) technique. If the original symmetric field size is 20×20 cm at isocentre, what is the effective field size when one jaw is closed to the central axis?
clinical.a: 10×20 cm effective field size with one jaw closed to the central axis
c: 5×20 cm effective field size with one jaw closed to the central axis
10×20 cm effective field size with one jaw closed to the central axis
10×10 cm effective field size with one jaw closed to the central axis
15×20 cm effective field size with one jaw closed to the central axis
clinical.e: Closing one jaw to the central axis halves the field in that dimension: 20 cm becomes 10 cm. The perpendicular dimension remains 20 cm, giving a 10×20 cm field. This technique eliminates beam divergence at the junction.

clinical.q: In IMRT planning, the modulation of the beam intensity is achieved through:
clinical.a: Dynamic or step-and-shoot MLC leaf movement during beam delivery
c: Varying the source-to-surface distance for each beam angle
Dynamic or step-and-shoot MLC leaf movement during beam delivery
Manual insertion of compensating filters in the beam path
Adjusting the linac energy for each segment of the field
clinical.e: IMRT uses the MLC to modulate beam intensity across the field. In step-and-shoot (segmental) IMRT, the beam pauses while leaves move. In dynamic (sliding window) IMRT, leaves move continuously during irradiation, creating fluence maps optimised by inverse planning.

clinical.q: VMAT differs from fixed-field IMRT in that it:
clinical.a: Delivers treatment with continuous gantry rotation, varying dose rate and MLC positions
c: Uses fewer monitor units due to simpler beam arrangements
Delivers treatment with continuous gantry rotation, varying dose rate and MLC positions
Requires separate forward and inverse planning optimisation steps
Cannot achieve comparable dose conformity to step-and-shoot IMRT
clinical.e: VMAT (Volumetric Modulated Arc Therapy) delivers modulated dose while the gantry continuously rotates. Dose rate, gantry speed, and MLC positions all vary simultaneously. This typically achieves comparable or superior plan quality with significantly shorter treatment times compared to fixed-field IMRT.

clinical.q: The Conformity Index (CI) in treatment plan evaluation is defined as:
clinical.a: The ratio of the prescription isodose volume to the PTV volume
c: The maximum dose divided by the prescription dose
The ratio of the prescription isodose volume to the PTV volume
The standard deviation of dose within the target volume
The percentage of the PTV receiving 95% of the prescribed dose
clinical.e: The RTOG Conformity Index = V_RI / V_PTV, where V_RI is the volume of the reference (prescription) isodose and V_PTV is the planning target volume. A CI of 1.0 indicates perfect conformity. Values >1 indicate the prescription isodose extends beyond the PTV.

clinical.q: The Homogeneity Index (HI) as defined by ICRU 83 is calculated as:
c: (Dmax - Dmin) / Dprescription
(D95% - D5%) / Dmean
clinical.e: ICRU 83 defines HI = (D2% - D98%) / D50%, where D2% represents near-maximum dose, D98% represents near-minimum dose, and D50% represents the median dose. A smaller HI indicates a more homogeneous dose distribution. Ideal HI is 0.

clinical.q: In IGRT, daily CBCT imaging before treatment primarily serves to:
clinical.a: Verify patient positioning and account for anatomical changes relative to the planning CT
c: Calculate the actual dose delivered to the patient on that day
Verify patient positioning and account for anatomical changes relative to the planning CT
Replace the need for initial simulation and treatment planning
Monitor for radiation-induced side effects during the treatment course
clinical.e: Daily CBCT allows visualisation of soft tissue anatomy for patient setup verification and detection of anatomical changes (tumour shrinkage, weight loss, organ filling variations). Registration with the planning CT enables online corrections to reduce setup margins.

clinical.q: Adaptive radiotherapy refers to the process of:
clinical.a: Modifying the treatment plan during the course of therapy based on observed changes
c: Using a single robust plan that accounts for all possible anatomical variations
Modifying the treatment plan during the course of therapy based on observed changes
Applying uniform setup margins to account for random positioning errors
Treating different target volumes on alternating treatment days sequentially
clinical.e: Adaptive RT involves replanning or adjusting the treatment plan during the course of therapy in response to observed changes such as tumour shrinkage, weight loss, or organ deformation. This can be offline (scheduled replans) or online (daily adaptation on MR-Linac systems).

clinical.q: CALC: A breast cancer patient is prescribed 42.56 Gy in 16 fractions. What is the dose per fraction?
clinical.e: Dose per fraction = 42.56 / 16 = 2.66 Gy. This hypofractionated schedule uses fewer, larger fractions and has become standard for whole breast RT based on evidence from trials such as START and FAST-Forward.

clinical.q: Which statement about concurrent chemoradiotherapy is correct?
clinical.a: Concurrent chemotherapy acts as a radiosensitiser to enhance local tumour control
c: Chemotherapy must be given at least 48 hours before each radiation fraction
Concurrent chemotherapy acts as a radiosensitiser to enhance local tumour control
The radiation dose must be reduced by 20% when chemotherapy is given concurrently
Concurrent treatment eliminates the need for surgical intervention in all cases
clinical.e: Concurrent chemoRT is standard for many locally advanced cancers (cervix, head and neck, lung, oesophagus). The chemotherapy (e.g., cisplatin, 5-FU) sensitises tumour cells to radiation, improving local control. Toxicity is increased compared to RT alone, requiring careful patient selection.

clinical.q: In palliative radiotherapy, a common fractionation schedule for bone metastases is:
clinical.a: 8 Gy in a single fraction
c: 2 Gy in 1 fraction daily for 5 days
8 Gy in a single fraction
50 Gy in 25 fractions over 5 weeks
70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks
clinical.e: Single fraction 8 Gy is widely used for uncomplicated bone metastases, providing equivalent pain relief to multi-fraction regimens (e.g., 20 Gy/5 or 30 Gy/10) with greater convenience. This is particularly relevant in SA where patient travel distances can be significant.

clinical.q: In brachytherapy, the advantage of HDR over LDR treatment is:
clinical.a: Outpatient treatment with short fraction times and optimisable dwell positions
c: Lower integral dose to the patient over the complete treatment course
Outpatient treatment with short fraction times and optimisable dwell positions
No requirement for radiation protection measures during treatment delivery
Superior dose conformity due to the use of multiple radioactive sources
clinical.e: HDR brachytherapy (typically using Ir-192) allows outpatient treatment with treatment times of minutes. Individual dwell positions and times can be optimised to tailor the dose distribution. This is particularly important for cervical cancer treatment in SA, where HDR is the standard approach.

clinical.q: Electron beam therapy is most appropriate for treating:
clinical.a: Superficial targets such as skin cancers and chest wall with defined depth control
c: Deep-seated pelvic tumours requiring high dose at depth
Superficial targets such as skin cancers and chest wall with defined depth control
Bilateral head and neck nodal volumes with sharp dose gradients
Large volume lung tumours with significant tissue heterogeneity
clinical.e: Electrons have a finite range in tissue determined by their energy (~0.5 cm per MeV for the therapeutic range). The rapid dose fall-off beyond Rp spares deeper structures, making electrons ideal for treating superficial targets like skin cancers, post-mastectomy chest wall, and boosting superficial nodes.

clinical.q: The primary dosimetric advantage of proton therapy over photon therapy is:
clinical.a: The Bragg peak allowing dose deposition with minimal exit dose
c: Higher relative biological effectiveness throughout the treatment volume
The Bragg peak allowing dose deposition with minimal exit dose
Shorter treatment times due to higher instantaneous dose rates
Better imaging capability for real-time tumour tracking during treatment
clinical.e: Protons deposit the majority of their energy at the Bragg peak with virtually no exit dose beyond it. This reduces integral dose to normal tissues compared to photons, particularly beneficial for paediatric cases and tumours near critical structures.

clinical.q: Total body irradiation (TBI) is primarily used as conditioning before:
clinical.a: Haematopoietic stem cell transplantation for haematological malignancies
c: Surgical resection of widespread metastatic solid tumour disease
Haematopoietic stem cell transplantation for haematological malignancies
Initiation of immunotherapy for checkpoint inhibitor treatment
Whole-brain radiotherapy for multiple cerebral metastases treatment
clinical.e: TBI delivers a uniform dose to the entire body as part of the conditioning regimen before bone marrow or stem cell transplant. It serves to eradicate residual leukaemia/lymphoma cells and immunosuppress the recipient. Typical doses are 12 Gy in 6 fractions or 2 Gy single fraction.

clinical.q: Total Skin Electron Therapy (TSET) is used primarily for the treatment of:
clinical.a: Mycosis fungoides and other cutaneous T-cell lymphomas
c: Widespread melanoma metastases involving the skin
Mycosis fungoides and other cutaneous T-cell lymphomas
Generalised psoriasis refractory to topical treatment
Kaposi sarcoma lesions in immunocompromised patients
clinical.e: TSET typically delivers 30-36 Gy to the skin surface using 6-9 MeV electrons from multiple beam angles (Stanford technique with 6 dual fields). It is the standard treatment for mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma) involving large skin areas.

clinical.q: The treatment planning process begins with:
clinical.a: Patient immobilisation and CT simulation in the treatment position
c: Inverse optimisation of beam fluence maps using dose objectives
Patient immobilisation and CT simulation in the treatment position
Dose calculation on the planning CT using the chosen algorithm
Contouring of target volumes and organs at risk on imaging data
clinical.e: The planning process starts with patient immobilisation (using devices such as thermoplastic masks, vacuum cushions, or breast boards) followed by CT simulation in the treatment position. This provides the anatomical dataset for contouring and dose calculation.

clinical.q: CALC: A VMAT plan for prostate cancer has a PTV volume of 180 cm^3. The volume enclosed by the prescription isodose (78 Gy) is 210 cm^3. What is the RTOG Conformity Index?
clinical.e: RTOG CI = V_RI / V_PTV = 210 / 180 = 1.17. This indicates the prescription isodose volume is 17% larger than the PTV. RTOG considers CI between 1.0 and 2.0 as acceptable, with values closer to 1.0 being ideal.

clinical.q: The gradient index in stereotactic planning is used to evaluate:
clinical.a: The rate of dose fall-off beyond the target, indicating normal tissue sparing
c: The uniformity of dose distribution within the planning target volume
The rate of dose fall-off beyond the target, indicating normal tissue sparing
The conformity of the prescription isodose to the target shape
The maximum point dose within organs at risk near the target
clinical.e: The Gradient Index = V_50% / V_100% (volume of half the prescription isodose divided by the prescription isodose volume). A lower GI indicates steeper dose fall-off and better sparing of surrounding normal tissue, which is critical in SRS/SBRT planning.

clinical.q: In hypofractionated breast radiotherapy, the FAST-Forward trial demonstrated equivalence with which schedule?
clinical.a: 26 Gy in 5 fractions over 1 week
c: 40 Gy in 15 fractions over 3 weeks
26 Gy in 5 fractions over 1 week
42.56 Gy in 16 fractions over 3 weeks
50 Gy in 25 fractions over 5 weeks
clinical.e: The FAST-Forward trial showed that 26 Gy in 5 daily fractions was non-inferior to the standard 40 Gy in 15 fractions for local tumour control after breast-conserving surgery, with comparable normal tissue effects. This is particularly relevant for resource-constrained settings in SA.

clinical.q: CALC: A cervical cancer brachytherapy plan prescribes 7 Gy per fraction for 4 fractions (HDR). Combined with 45 Gy EBRT in 25 fractions, what is the total EQD2 to point A? (alpha/beta = 10 Gy for tumour)
clinical.a: Approximately 85 Gy EQD2
c: Approximately 73 Gy EQD2
Approximately 78 Gy EQD2
Approximately 85 Gy EQD2
Approximately 92 Gy EQD2
clinical.e: EBRT EQD2 = 45 x (2+10)/(2+10) = 45 Gy. BT EQD2 per fraction = 7 x (7+10)/(2+10) = 7 x 17/12 = 9.92 Gy. Total BT EQD2 = 4 x 9.92 = 39.67 Gy. Total = 45 + 39.67 = approximately 85 Gy EQD2. This meets the recommended 85 Gy or greater EQD2 for cervical cancer point A dose.

clinical.q: Which IGRT technique provides the best soft tissue contrast for daily prostate localisation?
clinical.a: Cone-beam CT or MR-guided imaging
c: Orthogonal kV portal imaging with bony anatomy matching
Cone-beam CT or MR-guided imaging
Optical surface monitoring with infrared camera tracking
Ultrasound-based transabdominal prostate localisation systems
clinical.e: CBCT and MRI provide volumetric soft tissue visualisation allowing direct prostate localisation. MR-guided systems (e.g., Elekta Unity MR-Linac) provide superior soft tissue contrast without additional radiation dose. Fiducial markers with kV imaging are an alternative widely used approach.

clinical.q: The standard fractionation for radical breast radiotherapy is:
clinical.a: 40-42.5 Gy in 15-16 fractions over 3 weeks
c: 50 Gy in 25 fractions over 5 weeks only
40-42.5 Gy in 15-16 fractions over 3 weeks
60 Gy in 30 fractions over 6 weeks total
30 Gy in 10 fractions over 2 weeks total
clinical.e: Hypofractionated breast RT (START/FAST trials) has become standard.

clinical.q: The QUANTEC spinal cord dose constraint for conventional fractionation is:
clinical.a: Maximum dose less than 50 Gy
c: Maximum dose less than 30 Gy
Maximum dose less than 45 Gy
Maximum dose less than 50 Gy
Maximum dose less than 60 Gy
clinical.e: QUANTEC recommends Dmax < 50 Gy for <0.2% risk of myelopathy.

clinical.q: The radical dose for head and neck squamous cell carcinoma is typically:
clinical.a: 70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks
c: 50 Gy in 25 fractions over 5 weeks
60 Gy in 30 fractions over 6 weeks
70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks
80 Gy in 40 fractions over 8 weeks
clinical.e: Standard radical dose for H&N SCC is 70 Gy in 35 fractions.

clinical.q: The QUANTEC mean lung dose constraint to limit pneumonitis risk to <20% is:
clinical.a: Mean lung dose less than 20 Gy
c: Mean lung dose less than 10 Gy
Mean lung dose less than 20 Gy
Mean lung dose less than 30 Gy
Mean lung dose less than 40 Gy
clinical.e: QUANTEC: V20 < 30-35% and mean lung dose < 20 Gy.

clinical.q: For prostate cancer, the typical radical dose using conventional fractionation is:
clinical.a: 76-80 Gy in 38-40 fractions
c: 60 Gy in 30 fractions total
76-80 Gy in 38-40 fractions
50 Gy in 25 fractions total
90 Gy in 45 fractions total
clinical.e: Dose escalation studies have shown improved control with doses of 76-80 Gy.

clinical.q: The brainstem maximum dose constraint per QUANTEC is:
clinical.a: Maximum point dose less than 54 Gy
c: Maximum point dose less than 40 Gy
Maximum point dose less than 54 Gy
Maximum point dose less than 60 Gy
Maximum point dose less than 70 Gy
clinical.e: QUANTEC: Dmax < 54 Gy for < 5% risk of neuropathy/necrosis.

clinical.q: CALC: A palliative spine treatment delivers 30 Gy in 10 fractions (alpha/beta = 2 Gy). What is the BED to the cord?
clinical.a: 75 Gy2
c: 45 Gy2
60 Gy2
75 Gy2
90 Gy2

clinical.q: The parotid gland mean dose constraint to preserve salivary function is:
clinical.a: Mean dose less than 26 Gy to at least one parotid
c: Mean dose less than 10 Gy to both parotids
Mean dose less than 26 Gy to at least one parotid
Mean dose less than 40 Gy to both parotids
Mean dose less than 50 Gy to at least one parotid
clinical.e: QUANTEC: mean parotid dose < 25-26 Gy reduces risk of xerostomia.

clinical.q: The optic nerve and chiasm maximum dose tolerance is:
clinical.a: 54 Gy in conventional fractionation
c: 40 Gy in conventional fractionation
54 Gy in conventional fractionation
60 Gy in conventional fractionation
70 Gy in conventional fractionation
clinical.e: QUANTEC: Dmax < 54-55 Gy for < 3% risk of optic neuropathy.

clinical.q: Whole brain radiotherapy (WBRT) for brain metastases is typically prescribed as:
clinical.a: 30 Gy in 10 fractions over 2 weeks
c: 20 Gy in 5 fractions over 1 week
30 Gy in 10 fractions over 2 weeks
40 Gy in 20 fractions over 4 weeks
50 Gy in 25 fractions over 5 weeks
clinical.e: 30 Gy/10# is the most common WBRT schedule.

clinical.q: The heart V25Gy constraint for breast radiotherapy is:
clinical.a: Less than 10% of the heart volume
c: Less than 5% of the heart volume
Less than 10% of the heart volume
Less than 20% of the heart volume
Less than 30% of the heart volume
clinical.e: QUANTEC: V25 < 10% and mean heart dose < 26 Gy.

clinical.q: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy for cervical cancer uses which standard agent?
clinical.a: Weekly cisplatin at 40 mg/m2
c: 5-fluorouracil daily throughout treatment
Weekly cisplatin at 40 mg/m2
Carboplatin every three weeks only
Temozolomide daily during the treatment
clinical.e: Weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 is the standard concurrent agent.

clinical.q: The rectum V50Gy constraint for prostate radiotherapy per QUANTEC is:
clinical.a: Less than 50% of the rectal volume
c: Less than 25% of the rectal volume
Less than 35% of the rectal volume
Less than 50% of the rectal volume
Less than 65% of the rectal volume

clinical.q: A simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique delivers:
clinical.a: Different dose levels to different volumes in one plan
c: A uniform dose to the entire treatment volume
Different dose levels to different volumes in one plan
A boost field after completing the initial phase
Sequential treatments on alternating treatment days
clinical.e: SIB delivers high dose to GTV and lower dose to elective volumes simultaneously.

clinical.q: The typical prescription for palliative bone metastases in a single fraction is:
clinical.a: 8 Gy in 1 fraction
c: 4 Gy in 1 fraction
8 Gy in 1 fraction
12 Gy in 1 fraction
20 Gy in 1 fraction
clinical.e: 8 Gy single fraction provides equivalent pain relief to multi-fraction schedules.

clinical.q: CALC: A lung SBRT plan delivers 54 Gy in 3 fractions. What is the dose per fraction?
clinical.a: 18 Gy
c: 12 Gy
15 Gy
18 Gy
24 Gy
clinical.e: Dose per fraction = 54/3 = 18 Gy.

clinical.q: The GTV (Gross Tumour Volume) is defined as:
clinical.a: The visible or palpable extent of the tumour
c: The entire organ containing the tumour mass
The visible or palpable extent of the tumour
The region including microscopic tumour spread
The volume receiving the prescription dose level
clinical.e: GTV is the demonstrable tumour seen on imaging or clinical exam.

clinical.q: The CTV-to-PTV margin accounts for:
clinical.a: Setup uncertainty and internal organ motion
c: Microscopic disease spread beyond visible tumour
Setup uncertainty and internal organ motion
Penumbra width of the treatment beam edges
Dose calculation algorithm inaccuracy factors
clinical.e: The PTV margin encompasses setup errors and internal motion.

clinical.q: The kidney dose constraint per QUANTEC for bilateral kidney irradiation is:
clinical.a: Mean dose less than 18 Gy to both kidneys
c: Mean dose less than 10 Gy to both kidneys
Mean dose less than 18 Gy to both kidneys
Mean dose less than 25 Gy to both kidneys
Mean dose less than 35 Gy to both kidneys
clinical.e: QUANTEC: mean kidney dose < 18 Gy bilaterally.

clinical.q: In cervical cancer radiotherapy, Point A is defined at:
clinical.a: 2 cm lateral and 2 cm superior to the external os
c: 3 cm lateral and 3 cm superior to the external os
2 cm lateral and 2 cm superior to the external os
1 cm lateral and 1 cm superior to the external os
2 cm posterior and 2 cm inferior to the uterus
clinical.e: Point A represents the approximate location of the uterine artery crossing the ureter.

clinical.q: The liver mean dose constraint per QUANTEC is:
clinical.a: Mean dose less than 28 Gy for whole liver
c: Mean dose less than 15 Gy for whole liver
Mean dose less than 28 Gy for whole liver
Mean dose less than 40 Gy for whole liver
Mean dose less than 50 Gy for whole liver
clinical.e: QUANTEC: mean liver dose < 28 Gy (normal liver).

clinical.q: Accelerated radiotherapy aims to reduce the overall treatment time to counter:
clinical.a: Tumour repopulation during the treatment course
c: Tumour hypoxia increasing during treatment
Tumour repopulation during the treatment course
Normal tissue repair between treatment fractions
Setup errors accumulating over many fractions
clinical.e: Accelerated RT delivers the total dose in fewer calendar days.

clinical.q: The bladder V65Gy constraint for pelvic radiotherapy per QUANTEC is:
clinical.a: Less than 50% of the bladder volume
c: Less than 25% of the bladder volume
Less than 50% of the bladder volume
Less than 70% of the bladder volume
Less than 90% of the bladder volume
clinical.e: QUANTEC: V65 < 50% and V80 < 15%.

clinical.q: Deep inspiration breath hold (DIBH) in left breast RT primarily reduces dose to:
clinical.a: The heart and left anterior descending artery
c: The ipsilateral lung parenchyma volume
The heart and left anterior descending artery
The contralateral right breast tissue
The brachial plexus nerve structures
clinical.e: DIBH displaces the heart away from the tangential fields.

clinical.q: The standard fractionation for glioblastoma (GBM) radiotherapy is:
clinical.a: 60 Gy in 30 fractions with concurrent temozolomide
c: 40 Gy in 15 fractions with no chemotherapy
60 Gy in 30 fractions with concurrent temozolomide
50 Gy in 25 fractions with concurrent cisplatin
70 Gy in 35 fractions with concurrent bevacizumab
clinical.e: Stupp protocol: 60 Gy/30# + concurrent temozolomide.

clinical.q: The femoral head dose constraint in pelvic radiotherapy is:
clinical.a: V50Gy less than 5% or Dmax less than 52 Gy
c: V30Gy less than 50% of the femoral head
V50Gy less than 5% or Dmax less than 52 Gy
Mean dose less than 20 Gy to both heads
No constraint needed for the femoral heads
clinical.e: Limiting femoral head dose reduces risk of avascular necrosis.

clinical.q: Elective nodal irradiation in head and neck cancer typically receives:
clinical.a: 54-56 Gy in 2 Gy or 1.6-1.8 Gy per fraction
c: 40-44 Gy in 2.0 Gy fractions per treatment
54-56 Gy in 2 Gy or 1.6-1.8 Gy per fraction
66-70 Gy in 2.0 Gy fractions per treatment
30-36 Gy in 3.0 Gy fractions per treatment
clinical.e: Elective volumes receive lower doses than gross disease.

clinical.q: The cochlea dose constraint to limit sensorineural hearing loss is:
clinical.a: Mean dose less than 45 Gy
c: Mean dose less than 25 Gy
Mean dose less than 35 Gy
Mean dose less than 45 Gy
Mean dose less than 55 Gy
clinical.e: QUANTEC: mean cochlear dose < 45 Gy.

clinical.q: The oesophagus mean dose constraint per QUANTEC is:
clinical.a: Mean dose less than 34 Gy
c: Mean dose less than 20 Gy
Mean dose less than 34 Gy
Mean dose less than 50 Gy
Mean dose less than 60 Gy
clinical.e: QUANTEC: mean oesophageal dose < 34 Gy.

statistics.q: What does a p-value of 0.03 indicate?
statistics.a: 3% probability of result under null hypothesis
c: 3% chance the alternative hypothesis is true
3% probability of result under null hypothesis
97% confidence in the experimental result
3% total measurement uncertainty level
statistics.e: The p-value is the probability of observing the data (or more extreme) assuming the null hypothesis is true.

statistics.q: Which measure of central tendency is most affected by outliers?
statistics.a: Arithmetic mean
c: Arithmetic mean
Median value
Mode of the set
Geometric mean
statistics.e: The arithmetic mean uses every value in its calculation, so extreme outliers pull it toward them.

statistics.q: In a normal distribution, what percentage falls within +/-2 standard deviations?
statistics.e: The empirical rule: ~68.3% within +/-1 sigma, ~95.4% within +/-2 sigma, ~99.7% within +/-3 sigma.

statistics.q: A Type I error in hypothesis testing is also known as:
statistics.a: A false positive result
c: A false positive result
A false negative result
A correct rejection of H0
A measurement bias error
statistics.e: A Type I error occurs when the null hypothesis is true but is incorrectly rejected. Its probability is denoted alpha.

statistics.q: A Type II error in hypothesis testing is also known as:
statistics.a: A false negative result
c: A false positive result
A false negative result
A systematic bias error
A random sampling error
statistics.e: A Type II error occurs when the null hypothesis is false but fails to be rejected. Its probability is denoted beta.

statistics.q: What statistical distribution best models radioactive decay counting?
statistics.a: Poisson distribution
c: Normal distribution
Poisson distribution
Binomial distribution
Uniform distribution
statistics.e: Radioactive decay is a random process with a known average rate and rare individual events, well modeled by the Poisson distribution.

statistics.q: For Poisson-distributed radiation counts, the standard deviation equals:
statistics.a: The square root of the mean counts
c: The mean counts value itself
The square root of the mean counts
Half of the mean count value
The mean divided by sample size
statistics.e: For a Poisson distribution, variance equals the mean, so standard deviation sigma = sqrt(mu).

statistics.q: CALC: A detector records 10,000 counts. What is the standard deviation?
statistics.a: 100 counts
c: 10 counts
50 counts
100 counts
1000 counts
statistics.e: For Poisson statistics, sigma = sqrt(N) = sqrt(10000) = 100 counts. Percent uncertainty is 1%.

statistics.q: CALC: If 2500 counts are recorded, what is the percentage uncertainty (1 sigma)?
statistics.e: sigma = sqrt(2500) = 50. Percentage uncertainty = (50/2500) x 100% = 2.0%.

statistics.q: What does a 95% confidence interval mean?
statistics.a: 95% of such intervals contain the true value
c: The true value is 95% likely in this interval
95% of such intervals contain the true value
There is a 5% chance the result is wrong
The measurement has 95% accuracy overall
statistics.e: A 95% CI means if the experiment were repeated many times, 95% of constructed intervals would contain the true parameter.

statistics.q: Which test compares means of two independent groups with normal data?
statistics.a: Independent samples t-test
c: Chi-square test of means
Independent samples t-test
Wilcoxon rank-sum test
One-way ANOVA analysis
statistics.e: The independent samples t-test compares means of two independent normally distributed groups.

statistics.q: When should a paired t-test be used instead of an independent t-test?
statistics.a: When measurements are taken on the same subjects
c: When sample sizes are unequal between groups
When measurements are taken on the same subjects
When the data distribution is clearly skewed
When comparing more than two group means
statistics.e: A paired t-test is used when measurements are correlated, such as before/after on the same subjects.

statistics.q: What is the purpose of ANOVA?
statistics.a: To compare means across three or more groups
c: To test correlation between two variables
To compare means across three or more groups
To compare variances of two populations
To assess normality of a data distribution
statistics.e: ANOVA tests whether the means of three or more groups differ significantly, avoiding multiple pairwise t-tests.

statistics.q: What does the chi-square test evaluate?
statistics.a: Whether observed frequencies differ from expected
c: Whether two continuous means are different
Whether observed frequencies differ from expected
Whether a dataset follows a t-distribution
Whether sample variance equals population variance
statistics.e: The chi-square test compares observed categorical frequencies to expected frequencies to determine statistical significance.

statistics.q: In linear regression, what does the R-squared value represent?
statistics.a: Proportion of variance explained by the model
c: Proportion of variance explained by the model
The slope of the best-fit regression line
The correlation coefficient of raw data
The probability the regression is significant
statistics.e: R-squared indicates the fraction of total variance in the dependent variable that is explained by the regression model.

statistics.q: What does a Bland-Altman plot display?
statistics.a: Differences vs. averages of two measurement methods
c: Correlation between two measurement methods
Differences vs. averages of two measurement methods
Cumulative distribution of measurement errors
Standard deviations across repeated measurements
statistics.e: A Bland-Altman plot shows the difference between two methods against their average, revealing systematic bias and limits of agreement.

statistics.q: On a Bland-Altman plot, the limits of agreement are typically:
statistics.a: Mean difference +/- 1.96 standard deviations
c: Mean difference +/- 1.00 standard deviations
Mean difference +/- 1.65 standard deviations
Mean difference +/- 1.96 standard deviations
Mean difference +/- 2.58 standard deviations
statistics.e: The 95% limits of agreement are mean difference +/- 1.96 SD, encompassing 95% of the differences between methods.

statistics.q: What does the area under the ROC curve (AUC) represent?
statistics.a: Overall diagnostic accuracy of the test
c: The test sensitivity at optimal threshold
