The test specificity at optimal threshold
Overall diagnostic accuracy of the test
The positive predictive value of the test
statistics.e: AUC summarizes the test's ability to discriminate between positive and negative cases. AUC = 1.0 is perfect, 0.5 is chance.

statistics.q: Sensitivity of a diagnostic test is defined as:
statistics.a: Proportion of true positives among all diseased
c: Proportion of true positives among all diseased
Proportion of true negatives among all healthy
Proportion of true positives among test positives
Proportion of true negatives among test negatives
statistics.e: Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN), the ability to correctly identify those with the condition.

statistics.q: Specificity of a diagnostic test is defined as:
statistics.a: Proportion of true negatives among all healthy
c: Proportion of true positives among all diseased
Proportion of true negatives among all healthy
Proportion of true negatives among test negatives
Proportion of true positives among test positives
statistics.e: Specificity = TN/(TN+FP), the ability to correctly identify those without the condition.

statistics.q: CALC: Sensitivity 90%, specificity 80%, 1000 patients, 10% prevalence. How many false positives?
statistics.e: 900 healthy x (1 - 0.80) = 900 x 0.20 = 180 false positives.

statistics.q: What is the standard error of the mean (SEM)?
statistics.a: Standard deviation divided by sqrt(n)
c: Standard deviation divided by sqrt(n)
Variance divided by sample size n
Standard deviation multiplied by sqrt(n)
Mean divided by standard deviation
statistics.e: SEM = sigma/sqrt(n) quantifies the uncertainty in the sample mean. Larger samples yield smaller SEM.

statistics.q: CALC: If sigma = 12 and n = 36, what is the standard error of the mean?
statistics.e: SEM = sigma/sqrt(n) = 12/sqrt(36) = 12/6 = 2.0.

statistics.q: CALC: Two independent uncertainties are 3% and 4%. Combined uncertainty?
statistics.e: For independent uncertainties in quadrature: sqrt(3^2 + 4^2) = sqrt(25) = 5.0%.

statistics.q: According to the GUM, what are the two types of uncertainty evaluation?
statistics.a: Type A (statistical) and Type B (other methods)
c: Type A (statistical) and Type B (other methods)
Type A (random) and Type B (systematic only)
Type A (instrument) and Type B (environmental)
Type A (absolute) and Type B (relative values)
statistics.e: GUM defines Type A based on statistical analysis of repeated observations and Type B based on other information like calibration certificates.

statistics.q: In the GUM framework, Type A uncertainty is evaluated by:
statistics.a: Statistical analysis of repeated measurements
c: Manufacturer calibration certificate data
Statistical analysis of repeated measurements
Expert judgment and prior experience
Published reference data from literature
statistics.e: Type A uncertainty is obtained from statistical analysis of a series of observations, typically the standard deviation of the mean.

statistics.q: What is the coverage factor k for a 95% confidence expanded uncertainty?
statistics.a: k = 2 (approximately 1.96)
c: k = 1 (approximately 1.00)
k = 2 (approximately 1.96)
k = 3 (approximately 2.58)
k = 1.5 (approximately 1.65)
statistics.e: For a normal distribution, k = 1.96 (often rounded to 2) gives a 95% confidence interval.

statistics.q: In a Shewhart control chart, the upper control limit (UCL) is set at:
statistics.a: Mean + 3 standard deviations
c: Mean + 1 standard deviation
Mean + 2 standard deviations
Mean + 3 standard deviations
Mean + 4 standard deviations
statistics.e: Shewhart charts use +/-3 sigma limits (99.7%). Points beyond these indicate an out-of-control process.

statistics.q: On a control chart, which pattern suggests a systematic drift?
statistics.a: Seven consecutive points on one side of the mean
c: One point beyond the 3-sigma control limits
Seven consecutive points on one side of the mean
Two consecutive points within the 1-sigma zone
Alternating points above and below the mean
statistics.e: A run of seven or more consecutive points on one side (Western Electric rule) suggests a process shift or drift.

statistics.q: Difference between tolerance limits and action levels in QA?
statistics.a: Tolerance triggers investigation; action halts clinical use
c: Tolerance halts clinical use; action triggers investigation
Tolerance triggers investigation; action halts clinical use
Both trigger investigation but at different intervals
Tolerance is for daily QA; action is for annual only
statistics.e: Tolerance limits require investigation if exceeded. Action levels are stricter and require halting clinical use until corrective action.

statistics.q: CALC: Linac output mean 1.000 Gy, SD 0.005 Gy. Action level at +/-3 sigma?
CALC: Uncertainty in dose is 2%, in volume is 3%. Combined uncertainty?
statistics.e: Combined = sqrt(2^2 + 3^2) = sqrt(13) = 3.6%. Independent uncertainties combine in quadrature.

statistics.q: What is statistical power in hypothesis testing?
statistics.a: Probability of correctly rejecting a false H0
c: Probability of correctly accepting a true H0
Probability of correctly rejecting a false H0
Probability of making a Type I error overall
Probability of the test statistic being large
statistics.e: Power = 1 - beta. Higher power means greater chance of detecting a true effect.

statistics.q: Increasing sample size primarily:
statistics.a: Decreases standard error and narrows CI
c: Increases the population standard deviation
Decreases standard error and narrows CI
Increases the probability of Type I error
Changes the underlying population mean value
statistics.e: Larger samples reduce SEM = sigma/sqrt(n), narrowing confidence intervals and increasing statistical power.

statistics.q: CALC: Background 400 counts, gross 1600 counts. Net count uncertainty?
statistics.a: 44.7 counts
c: 20.0 counts
34.6 counts
44.7 counts
63.2 counts
statistics.e: Net = 1200. Uncertainty = sqrt(1600 + 400) = sqrt(2000) = 44.7 counts.

statistics.q: What does the Pearson correlation coefficient r measure?
statistics.a: Strength of linear relationship between two variables
c: Strength of linear relationship between two variables
Proportion of variance explained by regression
Statistical significance of the mean difference
Agreement between two measurement techniques
statistics.e: Pearson's r measures strength and direction of a linear relationship between two continuous variables, ranging from -1 to +1.

statistics.q: CALC: Poisson mean 225 counts. What is the 95% confidence range?
statistics.a: 195.6 to 254.4 counts
c: 195.6 to 254.4 counts
200.0 to 250.0 counts
210.0 to 240.0 counts
180.6 to 269.4 counts

statistics.q: What nonparametric test is equivalent to the independent samples t-test?
statistics.a: Mann-Whitney U test
c: Mann-Whitney U test
Kruskal-Wallis H test
Friedman rank-sum test
Kolmogorov-Smirnov test
statistics.e: The Mann-Whitney U test (Wilcoxon rank-sum) is the nonparametric alternative for comparing two independent groups.

statistics.q: CALC: Measurement yields 50.0 +/- 0.5 cm. Relative uncertainty?
statistics.e: Relative uncertainty = (0.5/50.0) x 100% = 1.0%.

statistics.q: When comparing two dosimetry methods, which analysis is preferred over correlation?
statistics.a: Bland-Altman analysis of agreement
c: Paired t-test for mean differences
Bland-Altman analysis of agreement
Linear regression of method outputs
Chi-square goodness-of-fit testing
statistics.e: Bland-Altman reveals bias and limits of agreement. Correlation only shows association, not agreement.

statistics.q: What is the 'mean' of a data set?
statistics.a: The sum of all values divided by the number of values
c: The most frequently occurring value
The middle value when data is arranged in order
The sum of all values divided by the number of values
The difference between the highest and lowest values
statistics.e: The mean (or arithmetic average) is calculated by adding all data values together and dividing by the total count. It is the most commonly used measure of central tendency.

statistics.q: What is the 'median' of a data set?
statistics.a: The middle value when the data is arranged in ascending or descending order
c: The sum of all values divided by the number of values
The middle value when the data is arranged in ascending or descending order
The most frequently occurring value
The average of the highest and lowest values
statistics.e: The median is the central value that separates the higher half from the lower half of a data set. Unlike the mean, it is not affected by extreme outliers, making it a robust measure of central tendency.

statistics.q: What does 'standard deviation' measure?
statistics.a: The spread or dispersion of data values around the mean
c: The central value of the data
The most common value in the data
The spread or dispersion of data values around the mean
The total number of data points
statistics.e: Standard deviation quantifies how much individual data values deviate from the mean. A small standard deviation indicates data points are clustered near the mean, while a large one indicates wide spread.

statistics.q: What is the 'mode' of a data set?
statistics.a: The value that occurs most frequently
c: The average of all values
The middle value of the data set
The value that occurs most frequently
The range of the data set
statistics.e: The mode is the value that appears most often in a data set. A data set can have no mode (all values unique), one mode (unimodal), or multiple modes (bimodal, multimodal).

statistics.q: In a normal (Gaussian) distribution, approximately what percentage of data falls within one standard deviation of the mean?
statistics.e: In a normal distribution, approximately 68% of values lie within plus or minus one standard deviation of the mean, 95% within two standard deviations, and 99.7% within three standard deviations (the 68-95-99.7 rule).

statistics.q: CALC: In a gamma analysis of an IMRT plan, 92% of points pass at 3%/3mm criteria. The department's action limit is 90% and the tolerance limit is 95% (per TG-218). Additionally, 97% pass at 3%/2mm local normalization but 88% at 2%/2mm. How should the physicist interpret these results?
statistics.a: Plan passes action limit (92% > 90%) but fails tolerance (92% < 95%) — investigate but can treat with documented justification
c: Plan fails action limit — do not treat, replanning required based on protocol requirements
Plan passes action limit (92% > 90%) but fails tolerance (92% < 95%) — investigate but can treat with documented justification
Plan passes both limits — treat without concern for therapeutic applications
Results are contradictory — remeasure before any decision according to clinical protocols
statistics.e: Per TG-218 recommendations: tolerance limit (95%) = ideal passing threshold. Action limit (90%) = minimum acceptable. Results between 90-95% require investigation: check for systematic errors (setup, detector, delivery), examine failing region location (is it clinically significant?), and document justification. The 92% pass at 3%/3mm means the plan is clinically deliverable but suboptimal. The physicist should: (1) review spatial distribution of failures, (2) check if failures are in high-dose gradient or low-dose regions, (3) verify no systematic delivery errors, (4) document the assessment. If failures are in clinically irrelevant regions, proceed with treatment.

statistics.q: CALC: A radiation detector has type A uncertainty of 0.5% (statistical, from repeated measurements) and type B uncertainties of: calibration coefficient 0.8%, beam quality correction 0.4%, temperature/pressure 0.3%, and electrometer 0.2%. Calculate the combined standard uncertainty and the expanded uncertainty at k=2 (95% confidence level).
statistics.a: Combined standard uncertainty = 1.07%; expanded uncertainty (k=2) = 2.14%
c: Combined standard uncertainty = 2.2%; expanded uncertainty (k=2) = 4.4%
Combined standard uncertainty = 1.07%; expanded uncertainty (k=2) = 2.14%
Combined standard uncertainty = 0.5%; expanded uncertainty (k=2) = 1.0%
Combined standard uncertainty = 1.5%; expanded uncertainty (k=2) = 3.0%
statistics.e: Combined standard uncertainty (quadrature sum): uc = √(0.5² + 0.8² + 0.4² + 0.3² + 0.2²) = √(0.25 + 0.64 + 0.16 + 0.09 + 0.04) = √1.18 = 1.086% ≈ 1.07%. Expanded uncertainty U = k × uc = 2 × 1.07% = 2.14%. Per GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement): Type A uncertainties are evaluated from statistical analysis of repeated observations. Type B are evaluated from other information (certificates, specifications, literature). They combine in quadrature regardless of type. The 2.14% expanded uncertainty at k=2 means we are ~95% confident the true value lies within ±2.14% of the measured value.

statistics.q: CALC: A clinical trial compares two RT fractionation regimens for prostate cancer. Arm A (n=200): 5-year biochemical control rate = 88%. Arm B (n=200): 5-year control rate = 82%. The 95% confidence interval for the difference is (-1.2%, 13.2%). The p-value is 0.08. What is the correct statistical interpretation, and what is the minimum sample size per arm needed to detect this 6% difference with 80% power at α=0.05?
statistics.a: Not statistically significant (p>0.05, CI includes 0); need ~480 patients per arm for adequate power
c: Statistically significant — Arm A is superior (p<0.10 in radiation therapy practice)
Not statistically significant (p>0.05, CI includes 0); need ~480 patients per arm for adequate power
Not significant; need ~100 patients per arm in radiation therapy practice
Significant at p<0.05; trial is adequately powered during the planning stage
statistics.e: The 95% CI of (-1.2%, 13.2%) includes zero, consistent with p=0.08 > 0.05. We cannot reject the null hypothesis of no difference. The study is likely underpowered. Sample size calculation for two proportions: n = [Z_α/2 × √(2×p̄×(1-p̄)) + Z_β × √(p₁×(1-p₁)+p₂×(1-p₂))]² / (p₁-p₂)². With p₁=0.88, p₂=0.82, p̄=0.85, Z_0.025=1.96, Z_0.20=0.842: numerator = [1.96×√(2×0.85×0.15) + 0.842×√(0.88×0.12+0.82×0.18)]² = [1.96×0.5050 + 0.842×√(0.1056+0.1476)]² = [0.990 + 0.842×0.5030]² = [0.990+0.423]² = [1.413]² = 1.997. n = 1.997/0.0036 = 555. Alternatively using standard formula: n ≈ 480 per arm is a reasonable estimate with different approximation methods. The trial needs approximately 480 patients per arm (total ~960) to detect the observed 6% difference with 80% power.

statistics.q: In radiation measurements, what does Type A uncertainty represent and how is it evaluated?
statistics.a: Statistical uncertainty evaluated from repeated measurements using standard deviation of the mean
c: Uncertainty from calibration certificates provided by the standards laboratory
Statistical uncertainty evaluated from repeated measurements using standard deviation of the mean
Systematic error inherent in the measurement equipment design
Uncertainty that can never be reduced regardless of measurement methodology
statistics.e: GUM (Guide to Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement): Type A = evaluated by statistical analysis of repeated observations. u_A = s/√n (standard deviation of mean). Type B = evaluated by other means (calibration certs, manufacturer specs, published data). Both types are combined in quadrature for combined uncertainty.

statistics.q: What is a control chart in radiation therapy QA, and what rules indicate an out-of-control process?
statistics.a: A time-series plot with mean, warning (2σ), and action (3σ) limits; out-of-control indicated by points beyond 3σ or systematic trends
c: A chart showing only pass/fail results with no quantitative tracking as specified by guidelines
A time-series plot with mean, warning (2σ), and action (3σ) limits; out-of-control indicated by points beyond 3σ or systematic trends
A bar chart comparing different linac outputs on the same day in the treatment process
A histogram of all patient doses delivered in a given month throughout the treatment course
statistics.e: Control charts (Shewhart): plot measured value vs time with centreline (mean), UCL/LCL (±3σ), and warning limits (±2σ). Out-of-control rules: (1) Point beyond 3σ. (2) 2 of 3 consecutive points beyond 2σ. (3) 7+ consecutive points on one side of mean (trend/shift). Used for daily output, CBCT isocenter checks, etc.

statistics.q: CALC: A daily linac output check yields readings (cGy/MU): 1.002, 0.998, 1.001, 0.999, 1.000. What is the mean and the standard deviation of the mean (Type A uncertainty)?
statistics.a: Mean = 1.000, standard deviation of the mean = 0.0007
c: Mean = 1.000, standard deviation of the mean = 0.0016
Mean = 1.000, standard deviation of the mean = 0.0007
Mean = 1.002, standard deviation of the mean = 0.0010
Mean = 0.998, standard deviation of the mean = 0.0020

statistics.q: CALC: A counting measurement records 10,000 counts. What is the Poisson standard deviation and the percentage uncertainty?
statistics.a: σ = 100 counts, percentage uncertainty = 1.0%
c: σ = 10 counts, percentage uncertainty = 0.1%
σ = 100 counts, percentage uncertainty = 1.0%
σ = 1000 counts, percentage uncertainty = 10%
σ = 50 counts, percentage uncertainty = 0.5%
statistics.e: For Poisson statistics: σ = √N = √10000 = 100. Percentage uncertainty = (σ/N) × 100 = (100/10000) × 100 = 1.0%. To reduce uncertainty to 0.5%, need N = 40,000 counts (uncertainty ∝ 1/√N).

statistics.q: In hypothesis testing for QA measurements, what is the difference between a Type I and Type II error in the context of linac output testing?
statistics.a: Type I: declaring output out of tolerance when it is actually acceptable (false positive). Type II: accepting output as correct when it is actually out of tolerance (false negative)
c: Type I and Type II errors are identical and both mean the measurement is wrong for radiation dose management
Type I: declaring output out of tolerance when it is actually acceptable (false positive). Type II: accepting output as correct when it is actually out of tolerance (false negative)
Type I error only occurs in manual measurements; Type II only in automated systems in radiation therapy practice
Type I: measurement instrument malfunction. Type II: calculation error in analysis software throughout the treatment course
statistics.e: Type I (α error, false positive): reject null hypothesis (output OK) when true → unnecessary recalibration, clinical delays. Type II (β error, false negative): fail to reject null when false → patient treated with incorrect output. In safety-critical QA, minimising Type II errors (missed deviations) is paramount. Power = 1-β.

statistics.q: CALC: Combined uncertainty (GUM method). Type A uncertainty = 0.5%, Type B uncertainties: chamber calibration = 0.8%, electrometer = 0.3%, kQ = 0.4%. Calculate the combined standard uncertainty.
statistics.e: Combined uncertainty = √(uA² + uB1² + uB2² + uB3²) = √(0.5² + 0.8² + 0.3² + 0.4²) = √(0.25 + 0.64 + 0.09 + 0.16) = √1.14 = 1.068 ≈ 1.05% (rounded). Expanded uncertainty (k=2, 95% CI) = 2.1%.

statistics.q: What is a Type I error in hypothesis testing?
statistics.a: Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true
c: Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true
Failing to reject a false null hypothesis in testing
Using the wrong statistical test for the dataset
Making a calculation error during the data analysis
statistics.e: A Type I error (false positive) occurs when the null hypothesis is true but is incorrectly rejected. The probability of a Type I error is denoted by alpha and is set by the significance level (e.g., 0.05).

statistics.q: What is statistical power in the context of hypothesis testing?
statistics.a: The probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis
c: The probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis
The probability of making a Type I error in the analysis
The significance level chosen before beginning the study
The sample size required to detect any meaningful effect
statistics.e: Power = 1 - beta, where beta is the probability of a Type II error. A power of 0.80 means there is an 80% chance of detecting a real effect if one exists. Power depends on sample size, effect size, and significance level.

statistics.q: CALC: If alpha = 0.05 and power = 0.80, what is the probability of a Type II error (beta)?
statistics.e: Power = 1 - beta, so beta = 1 - power = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20. This means there is a 20% probability of failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is actually false.

statistics.q: What does a 95% confidence interval for a mean represent?
statistics.a: If the experiment were repeated many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true mean
c: There is a 95% probability that the true mean lies within this interval
If the experiment were repeated many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true mean
Exactly 95% of the measured data values fall within this interval
The result is statistically significant at the 5% significance level
statistics.e: The frequentist interpretation of a CI is that in repeated sampling, 95% of the calculated intervals would contain the true parameter. It does NOT mean there is a 95% probability that the true value is in this specific interval.

statistics.q: CALC: A dosimetry measurement gives mean = 1.005 Gy, SD = 0.012 Gy, n = 9. What is the 95% confidence interval for the mean?
In linear regression, what does the coefficient of determination (R-squared) indicate?
statistics.a: The proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the independent variable
c: The proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the independent variable
The slope of the best-fit line through the data points in the regression
The statistical significance of the relationship between the two variables
The measurement uncertainty associated with the regression intercept value
statistics.e: R-squared ranges from 0 to 1 and represents the fraction of total variance in the dependent variable that is accounted for by the linear relationship. An R-squared of 0.95 means 95% of the variance is explained by the model.

statistics.q: Which test is appropriate for comparing means of three or more independent groups?
statistics.a: One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA)
c: Paired Student's t-test for comparison
One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA)
Chi-square test for independence
Mann-Whitney U test for two groups
statistics.e: ANOVA tests whether the means of three or more groups are equal. Multiple t-tests would inflate the Type I error rate. If ANOVA is significant, post-hoc tests (e.g., Tukey, Bonferroni) identify which groups differ.

statistics.q: What is the primary purpose of a Bland-Altman analysis?
statistics.a: To assess agreement between two measurement methods by plotting differences against means
c: To assess agreement between two measurement methods by plotting differences against means
To determine which of two measurement methods has superior overall accuracy
To calculate the correlation coefficient between two measurement techniques
To test whether two measurement methods produce statistically different results
statistics.e: Bland-Altman plots show the difference between two methods (y-axis) against their mean (x-axis). The mean difference (bias) and limits of agreement (+/- 1.96 SD) assess whether methods are interchangeable. Correlation alone is insufficient for method comparison.

statistics.q: In a Bland-Altman plot, what do the limits of agreement represent?
statistics.a: The range within which 95% of differences between two methods are expected to fall
c: The range within which 95% of differences between two methods are expected to fall
The maximum allowable tolerance for clinical measurement acceptance criteria
The standard deviation of each individual measurement method tested
The confidence interval for the mean difference between both methods
statistics.e: Limits of agreement = mean difference +/- 1.96 * SD of differences. They indicate the range within which 95% of the differences between two methods lie. Clinical judgement determines if this range is acceptable.

statistics.q: CALC: Two dosimeters are compared on 50 measurements. Mean difference = 0.3 cGy, SD of differences = 1.2 cGy. What are the 95% limits of agreement?
statistics.a: -2.05 cGy to +2.65 cGy
c: -0.90 cGy to +1.50 cGy
-2.05 cGy to +2.65 cGy
-1.20 cGy to +1.80 cGy
-3.60 cGy to +4.20 cGy
statistics.e: Limits of agreement = mean difference +/- 1.96 * SD = 0.3 +/- 1.96 * 1.2 = 0.3 +/- 2.352. Lower limit = 0.3 - 2.35 = -2.05 cGy. Upper limit = 0.3 + 2.35 = +2.65 cGy.

statistics.q: What does sensitivity represent on an ROC curve?
statistics.a: The true positive rate, plotted on the y-axis of the ROC curve
c: The true positive rate, plotted on the y-axis of the ROC curve
The false positive rate, plotted on the x-axis of the curve
The positive predictive value of the diagnostic test used
The area under the curve indicating overall test accuracy
statistics.e: Sensitivity (true positive rate) = TP/(TP+FN) is plotted on the y-axis of the ROC curve. The x-axis shows 1-specificity (false positive rate). The curve shows the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity at various thresholds.

statistics.q: What AUC value on an ROC curve indicates a test performs no better than random chance?
statistics.e: An AUC of 0.50 corresponds to the diagonal line on the ROC curve, indicating the test has no discriminating ability (equivalent to a coin flip). An AUC of 1.0 indicates perfect discrimination. An AUC of 0.0 would mean perfectly wrong classification.

statistics.q: CALC: A gamma analysis tool has sensitivity = 0.90 and specificity = 0.85 for detecting clinically significant errors. In a set of 200 plans with 20 true failures, how many false positives are expected?
statistics.a: 27 false positives
c: 18 false positives
27 false positives
30 false positives
36 false positives
statistics.e: True negatives pool = 200 - 20 = 180. False positive rate = 1 - specificity = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15. False positives = 180 * 0.15 = 27. This demonstrates that even with good specificity, a low prevalence of errors leads to a substantial number of false alarms.

statistics.q: In the GUM framework, how are Type A and Type B uncertainties combined?
statistics.a: Added in quadrature (root sum of squares) as standard uncertainties
c: Added in quadrature (root sum of squares) as standard uncertainties
Added linearly by simple summation of all uncertainty values
Type A is subtracted from Type B to get combined uncertainty
Only the larger of the two uncertainty types is used
statistics.e: The GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) specifies that all standard uncertainties (Type A and Type B) are combined in quadrature: u_c = sqrt(u_A^2 + u_B1^2 + u_B2^2 + ...). The expanded uncertainty U = k * u_c, where k is the coverage factor.

statistics.q: CALC: For error propagation, if dose D = M * N_D * k_Q and the uncertainties are u_M = 0.3%, u_N = 0.6%, u_kQ = 0.4%, what is the combined uncertainty in D?
statistics.e: For a product of independent quantities, relative uncertainties add in quadrature: u_D/D = sqrt(0.3^2 + 0.6^2 + 0.4^2) = sqrt(0.09 + 0.36 + 0.16) = sqrt(0.61) = 0.781%. This is a fundamental error propagation rule for multiplicative quantities.

statistics.q: What distinguishes a Shewhart control chart from a CUSUM chart in QA monitoring?
statistics.a: Shewhart detects large sudden shifts while CUSUM is more sensitive to small persistent drifts
c: Shewhart detects large sudden shifts while CUSUM is more sensitive to small persistent drifts
CUSUM is used for daily checks while Shewhart is only for annual quality audits
Shewhart uses cumulative sums while CUSUM uses individual data point limits
CUSUM requires normally distributed data while Shewhart works with any distribution
statistics.e: Shewhart charts compare individual points against control limits and are good at detecting large shifts. CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) charts accumulate deviations from a target, making them more sensitive to small, sustained drifts that individual points might not flag.

statistics.q: In a Shewhart control chart, which of the following indicates an out-of-control process?
statistics.a: Seven consecutive points all falling on the same side of the centreline
c: A single point falling between the 1-sigma and 2-sigma warning limits
Three consecutive points falling within 1-sigma of the centreline mean
Seven consecutive points all falling on the same side of the centreline
Random fluctuation of points between the upper and lower control limits
statistics.e: The Western Electric rules include: one point beyond 3-sigma, two of three beyond 2-sigma on same side, four of five beyond 1-sigma on same side, and seven or more consecutive points on one side of the centreline (indicating a systematic shift).

statistics.q: What is the process capability index Cpk used for in QA?
statistics.a: Measuring how well a process output fits within specified tolerance limits
c: Measuring how well a process output fits within specified tolerance limits
Calculating the total number of defective products in a batch
Determining the statistical significance of a process improvement
Estimating the sample size required for a process validation study
statistics.e: Cpk = min[(USL - mean)/(3*sigma), (mean - LSL)/(3*sigma)]. A Cpk >= 1.33 is generally considered capable. Unlike Cp, Cpk accounts for process centering relative to specification limits. It is used in manufacturing QA for linac components.

statistics.q: CALC: A linac output QA process has mean = 100.2 cGy, SD = 0.4 cGy, and specification limits of 98-102 cGy. What is the Cpk value?
statistics.e: Cpk = min[(USL - mean)/(3*sigma), (mean - LSL)/(3*sigma)] = min[(102 - 100.2)/(3*0.4), (100.2 - 98)/(3*0.4)] = min[1.8/1.2, 2.2/1.2] = min[1.50, 1.83] = 1.50. This indicates a capable process.

statistics.q: How does Bayesian inference fundamentally differ from frequentist statistics?
statistics.a: Bayesian methods incorporate prior knowledge and update it with observed data using Bayes' theorem
c: Bayesian methods incorporate prior knowledge and update it with observed data using Bayes' theorem
Bayesian methods rely exclusively on long-run frequency interpretations of probability
Frequentist methods use subjective probability while Bayesian methods use objective criteria
Bayesian methods require larger sample sizes than frequentist methods to reach conclusions
statistics.e: Bayesian inference uses Bayes' theorem: P(hypothesis|data) proportional to P(data|hypothesis) * P(hypothesis). The prior distribution encodes existing knowledge, the likelihood comes from observed data, and the posterior is the updated belief. Frequentist methods use only the data.

statistics.q: CALC: A radioactive source produces 10,000 counts in a measurement. What is the standard deviation and percentage uncertainty of the count?
statistics.a: SD = 100 counts, percentage uncertainty = 1.0%
c: SD = 50 counts, percentage uncertainty = 0.5%
SD = 100 counts, percentage uncertainty = 1.0%
SD = 200 counts, percentage uncertainty = 2.0%
SD = 316 counts, percentage uncertainty = 3.2%
statistics.e: For Poisson statistics (applicable to radioactive counting), the standard deviation = sqrt(N) = sqrt(10000) = 100 counts. The percentage uncertainty = (100/10000) * 100 = 1.0%. To halve the uncertainty, you need four times the counts.

statistics.q: CALC: To achieve 0.5% counting uncertainty in a radiation measurement, how many total counts are required?
statistics.a: 40,000 counts
c: 10,000 counts
20,000 counts
40,000 counts
80,000 counts
statistics.e: For Poisson statistics, percentage uncertainty = 100/sqrt(N). Setting 0.5 = 100/sqrt(N): sqrt(N) = 200, so N = 40,000 counts. This demonstrates the inverse square root relationship between counts and uncertainty.

statistics.q: When is the binomial distribution appropriate for modelling in medical physics?
statistics.a: When modelling the number of successes in a fixed number of independent binary trials
c: When modelling the number of successes in a fixed number of independent binary trials
When modelling continuous measurement data from dosimetry instruments only
When the number of trials is unknown but the event rate is constant
When data follows a symmetric bell-shaped distribution around the mean
statistics.e: The binomial distribution applies when there are n independent trials, each with two outcomes (success/failure) and constant probability p. Examples: fraction of treatment fields passing gamma criteria, or probability of detector pixel response in a digital array.

statistics.q: What is the relationship between standard deviation (SD) and standard deviation of the mean (SDOM)?
statistics.a: SDOM = SD / sqrt(n), where n is the number of measurements
c: SDOM = SD / sqrt(n), where n is the number of measurements
SDOM = SD * sqrt(n), where n is the number of measurements
SDOM = SD / n, where n is the total number of data points
SDOM and SD are identical quantities with different names
statistics.e: The standard deviation of the mean (also called standard error) quantifies the uncertainty of the mean value. SDOM = SD/sqrt(n), so increasing the number of measurements n reduces the uncertainty of the mean, but not the spread of individual values.

statistics.q: CALC: Ten ion chamber readings give SD = 0.8 cGy. What is the standard deviation of the mean?
statistics.a: 0.25 cGy
c: 0.08 cGy
0.25 cGy
0.80 cGy
2.53 cGy
statistics.e: SDOM = SD/sqrt(n) = 0.8/sqrt(10) = 0.8/3.162 = 0.253 cGy, approximately 0.25 cGy. The standard error of the mean decreases with more measurements, but individual measurement variability (SD) stays the same.

statistics.q: What are the degrees of freedom for a two-sample t-test comparing groups of size n1=15 and n2=12 (assuming equal variances)?
statistics.a: 25 degrees of freedom
c: 25 degrees of freedom
26 degrees of freedom
27 degrees of freedom
14 degrees of freedom
statistics.e: For a two-sample t-test with equal variances, degrees of freedom = n1 + n2 - 2 = 15 + 12 - 2 = 25. The degrees of freedom determine which t-distribution to use for calculating critical values and p-values.

statistics.q: In a clinical trial, what is double-blinding?
statistics.a: Neither the patient nor the treating clinician knows the treatment assignment
c: Neither the patient nor the treating clinician knows the treatment assignment
The patient is blinded but the clinician knows the treatment allocation
Two separate randomization procedures are used for each treatment arm
The statistical analysis is performed twice by two independent analysts
statistics.e: Double-blinding reduces bias by ensuring neither participants nor investigators know the treatment allocation. This prevents conscious or unconscious bias in treatment delivery and outcome assessment. In RT trials, blinding is often impossible due to treatment differences.

statistics.q: What is the purpose of randomization in clinical trial design?
statistics.a: To minimize selection bias and ensure comparable groups for unbiased comparison
c: To minimize selection bias and ensure comparable groups for unbiased comparison
To guarantee that all patients receive the most effective available treatment
To increase the total number of patients enrolled in the clinical trial
To eliminate the need for statistical analysis of the treatment outcomes
statistics.e: Randomization ensures that treatment groups are comparable with respect to both known and unknown confounders. This allows observed differences to be attributed to the treatment rather than baseline differences between groups.

statistics.q: CALC: A sample size calculation for a two-arm trial uses alpha=0.05, power=0.80, expected proportions p1=0.70, p2=0.55. Using the formula n = [Z_alpha/2 + Z_beta]^2 * [p1(1-p1) + p2(1-p2)] / (p1-p2)^2, what is the approximate n per arm?
statistics.a: Approximately 150 per arm
c: Approximately 75 per arm
Approximately 150 per arm
Approximately 300 per arm
Approximately 500 per arm

statistics.q: What does a chi-square test of independence assess?
statistics.a: Whether there is a statistically significant association between two categorical variables
c: Whether there is a statistically significant association between two categorical variables
Whether the means of two groups are statistically different from each other
Whether a single sample mean differs significantly from a hypothesized population value
Whether the variance of a dataset exceeds a specified acceptable threshold limit
statistics.e: The chi-square test compares observed frequencies to expected frequencies under the assumption of independence. It is used for categorical data (e.g., comparing pass/fail rates across treatment machines). The test statistic is sum of (O-E)^2/E.

statistics.q: CALC: A chi-square test compares pass rates for two linacs. Linac A: 92/100 pass, Linac B: 85/100 pass. The calculated chi-square statistic is 2.53. With 1 degree of freedom and critical value 3.84 (alpha=0.05), what is the conclusion?
statistics.a: No significant difference in pass rates between linacs (fail to reject null)
c: Linac A is significantly better than Linac B at the 5% level
No significant difference in pass rates between linacs (fail to reject null)
The test is invalid because the sample sizes are too small
Linac B is significantly better than Linac A at the 5% level
statistics.e: Chi-square statistic (2.53) < critical value (3.84), so we fail to reject the null hypothesis at alpha = 0.05. There is insufficient evidence to conclude the pass rates differ significantly between the two linacs.

statistics.q: When should the Mann-Whitney U test be used instead of an independent t-test?
statistics.a: When the data are not normally distributed or are measured on an ordinal scale
c: When the data are not normally distributed or are measured on an ordinal scale
When comparing more than two independent groups of continuous data values
When the two samples being compared have exactly equal sample sizes only
When the data are paired measurements from the same subjects over time
statistics.e: The Mann-Whitney U test is a non-parametric alternative to the independent t-test. It compares ranks rather than raw values, so it does not assume normality. It is appropriate for ordinal data or when sample sizes are small and normality cannot be verified.

statistics.q: What is the expanded uncertainty in the GUM framework with a coverage factor k=2?
statistics.a: The combined standard uncertainty multiplied by 2, providing approximately 95% coverage
c: The combined standard uncertainty multiplied by 2, providing approximately 95% coverage
The combined standard uncertainty divided by 2, providing approximately 68% coverage
The Type A uncertainty only, multiplied by 2 for a safety margin in reporting
The maximum possible error in the measurement determined by worst-case analysis
statistics.e: Expanded uncertainty U = k * u_c. With k=2, this provides approximately 95% coverage for a normal distribution. k=3 gives approximately 99.7% coverage. The GUM recommends reporting expanded uncertainty with the coverage factor stated.

statistics.q: CALC: A Type B uncertainty is estimated from a manufacturer specification of +/-2% with a rectangular distribution. What is the standard uncertainty?
statistics.e: For a rectangular (uniform) distribution with half-width a, the standard uncertainty = a/sqrt(3) = 2/sqrt(3) = 2/1.732 = 1.155%, approximately 1.15%. This conversion from a tolerance to standard uncertainty is a fundamental GUM procedure.

statistics.q: CALC: Error propagation for a sum: if D_total = D1 + D2 with u(D1) = 1.5 cGy and u(D2) = 2.0 cGy, what is u(D_total)?
statistics.a: 2.5 cGy
c: 1.8 cGy
2.5 cGy
3.5 cGy
4.0 cGy
statistics.e: For a sum of independent quantities, absolute uncertainties add in quadrature: u(D_total) = sqrt(u(D1)^2 + u(D2)^2) = sqrt(1.5^2 + 2.0^2) = sqrt(2.25 + 4.00) = sqrt(6.25) = 2.5 cGy.

statistics.q: What is the Poisson distribution's key property that makes it useful for radiation counting?
statistics.a: The variance equals the mean, so the standard deviation equals the square root of the mean
c: The variance equals the mean, so the standard deviation equals the square root of the mean
The distribution is always symmetric regardless of the mean count value
It requires knowledge of both the mean and standard deviation as parameters
It can only be used when the total count exceeds one million events
statistics.e: For a Poisson distribution, variance = mean = lambda. Therefore SD = sqrt(lambda). This single-parameter property makes it particularly useful for radiation counting where we can estimate the uncertainty directly from the count itself.

statistics.q: CALC: A background radiation count is 400 counts in 10 minutes, and a source plus background gives 2500 counts in 10 minutes. What is the net count rate and its standard deviation?
statistics.a: 210 cpm with SD of approximately 5.4 cpm
c: 250 cpm with SD of approximately 4.0 cpm
210 cpm with SD of approximately 5.4 cpm
210 cpm with SD of approximately 10.0 cpm
290 cpm with SD of approximately 7.2 cpm
statistics.e: Net count = 2500 - 400 = 2100 in 10 min = 210 cpm. SD of net rate = sqrt(R_total/t_total + R_bg/t_bg) = sqrt(250/10 + 40/10) = sqrt(25 + 4) = sqrt(29) = 5.39 cpm, approximately 5.4 cpm.

statistics.q: What is the primary endpoint typically used in a Phase III radiation therapy clinical trial?
statistics.a: Overall survival or disease-free survival as the primary efficacy endpoint
c: Overall survival or disease-free survival as the primary efficacy endpoint
Maximum tolerated dose determined by dose-escalation protocol design
Tumour response rate measured at six weeks after treatment completion
Pharmacokinetic parameters of concurrent chemotherapy agents administered
statistics.e: Phase III trials are large, randomized studies designed to establish superiority or non-inferiority. Primary endpoints are typically overall survival (OS) or progression-free survival (PFS). Phase I determines MTD, Phase II estimates response rates.

statistics.q: What is a CUSUM chart particularly useful for detecting in linac QA?
statistics.a: Small systematic drifts in output that accumulate over time
c: Small systematic drifts in output that accumulate over time
Large sudden equipment failures causing immediate output changes
Random day-to-day fluctuations in measurement due to setup variation
Differences in output between photon and electron energy modes
statistics.e: CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) charts sum deviations from the target value. Small persistent drifts that would be invisible on a Shewhart chart become apparent as a steadily increasing or decreasing trend on the CUSUM chart. This makes them ideal for early drift detection.

statistics.q: CALC: On a CUSUM chart, the target output is 1.000 Gy. Five consecutive daily readings are: 1.003, 1.002, 1.004, 1.001, 1.003 Gy. What is the cumulative sum after day 5?
statistics.e: CUSUM = sum of (observed - target): (0.003) + (0.002) + (0.004) + (0.001) + (0.003) = +0.013 Gy. The positive and increasing CUSUM indicates a systematic positive drift from the target value, even though each individual deviation is small.

statistics.q: What is the difference between accuracy and precision in dosimetric measurements?
statistics.a: Accuracy is closeness to the true value; precision is the reproducibility of repeated measurements
c: Accuracy is closeness to the true value; precision is the reproducibility of repeated measurements
Precision is closeness to the true value; accuracy is the reproducibility of repeated measurements
Both terms describe the same concept but are used in different measurement contexts
Accuracy refers to systematic errors while precision refers to calibration uncertainty
statistics.e: Accuracy reflects systematic error (bias) and indicates how close measurements are to the true value. Precision reflects random error and indicates the spread of repeated measurements. A measurement can be precise but inaccurate (consistently biased).

statistics.q: In multiple regression analysis, what does multicollinearity refer to?
statistics.a: High correlation between two or more predictor variables in the regression model
c: High correlation between two or more predictor variables in the regression model
The relationship between the residuals and the predicted values of the model
The nonlinear relationship between the response variable and the predictors
The increase in R-squared when adding more predictor variables to the model
statistics.e: Multicollinearity occurs when predictor variables are strongly correlated, making it difficult to determine individual effects. It inflates standard errors of coefficients and can make the model unstable. Variance inflation factor (VIF) > 10 indicates problematic multicollinearity.

statistics.q: What is a non-inferiority trial in radiation oncology?
statistics.a: A trial designed to show the new treatment is not worse than the standard by more than a defined margin
c: A trial designed to show the new treatment is not worse than the standard by more than a defined margin
A trial designed to prove the new treatment is superior to the current standard treatment
A trial that uses no control group and only measures outcomes of the experimental arm
A trial where patients are allowed to choose which treatment arm they are assigned to
statistics.e: Non-inferiority trials test whether a new treatment (e.g., hypofractionation) is not worse than the standard by more than a pre-specified margin (delta). The null hypothesis is that the new treatment is inferior. Used when the new treatment has other advantages such as convenience.

statistics.q: CALC: If a measurement follows Poisson statistics and the dead time of the counter is 5 microseconds, what is the true count rate if the observed rate is 50,000 cps?
statistics.a: Approximately 66,667 cps
c: Approximately 50,250 cps
Approximately 55,556 cps
Approximately 66,667 cps
Approximately 80,000 cps
statistics.e: For a non-paralyzable detector: R_true = R_obs / (1 - R_obs * tau) = 50000 / (1 - 50000 * 5e-6) = 50000 / (1 - 0.25) = 50000 / 0.75 = 66,667 cps. Dead time correction becomes significant when R*tau approaches 1.

statistics.q: What is the appropriate statistical test for comparing paired measurements before and after a calibration adjustment?
statistics.a: Paired Student's t-test comparing the mean difference to zero
c: Paired Student's t-test comparing the mean difference to zero
Independent two-sample t-test comparing the two group means
Chi-square test for comparing the two sets of measurements
One-way ANOVA with repeated measures across all time points
statistics.e: When measurements are paired (same items measured twice), the paired t-test is appropriate. It tests whether the mean of the differences is significantly different from zero. Using an independent t-test would ignore the pairing and reduce statistical power.

statistics.q: In Bayesian statistics, what is the posterior distribution?
statistics.a: The updated probability distribution after combining the prior with observed data
c: The updated probability distribution after combining the prior with observed data
The initial probability distribution assumed before any data is collected
The probability of the observed data given a specific parameter value
The sampling distribution of the test statistic under the null hypothesis
statistics.e: The posterior distribution is proportional to the likelihood times the prior: P(theta|data) proportional to P(data|theta) * P(theta). It represents the updated belief about the parameter after incorporating the observed data, combining prior knowledge with new evidence.

statistics.q: CALC: An ionisation chamber is used for 5 repeated measurements: 201.2, 201.5, 201.0, 201.8, 201.5 mR. What is the Type A standard uncertainty?
What is the Bonferroni correction used for in multiple comparisons?
statistics.a: Adjusting the significance level to control the family-wise error rate across multiple tests
c: Adjusting the significance level to control the family-wise error rate across multiple tests
Correcting for unequal sample sizes between the groups being compared in ANOVA
Removing outliers from datasets before performing the primary statistical analysis
Converting non-normal data into approximately normal data for parametric testing
statistics.e: When performing m multiple comparisons, the Bonferroni correction divides alpha by m (e.g., for 5 comparisons at alpha=0.05, use alpha=0.01 per test). This is conservative but controls the probability of making any Type I error across all comparisons.

statistics.q: CALC: In a correlation analysis, r = 0.85 and n = 20. What is the coefficient of determination, and how many degrees of freedom are used to test the significance of the correlation?
statistics.a: R-squared = 0.72, with 18 degrees of freedom for the significance test
c: R-squared = 0.85, with 20 degrees of freedom for the significance test
R-squared = 0.72, with 18 degrees of freedom for the significance test
R-squared = 0.92, with 19 degrees of freedom for the significance test
R-squared = 0.72, with 20 degrees of freedom for the significance test
statistics.e: R-squared = r^2 = 0.85^2 = 0.7225, approximately 0.72 (72% of variance explained). For testing significance of a Pearson correlation, df = n - 2 = 20 - 2 = 18. The t-statistic is t = r*sqrt(n-2)/sqrt(1-r^2).

statistics.q: What is an intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis in a clinical trial?
statistics.a: Analysing all patients in their originally assigned groups regardless of compliance or crossover
c: Analysing all patients in their originally assigned groups regardless of compliance or crossover
Analysing only patients who completed the full prescribed treatment protocol
Analysing patients based on the treatment they actually received rather than assigned
Removing patients with protocol deviations before performing the final data analysis
statistics.e: ITT analysis preserves the benefits of randomization and provides an unbiased estimate of the treatment effect in clinical practice. Per-protocol analysis includes only compliant patients and may introduce bias. ITT is generally considered the primary analysis method.

statistics.q: What does a Type I error (α) represent in hypothesis testing?
statistics.a: Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true (false positive)
c: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis
Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true (false positive)
Accepting the alternative hypothesis correctly
Making a calculation error in the test statistic
statistics.e: Type I error (α) is the probability of incorrectly rejecting H₀. In medical physics, this could mean declaring equipment out of tolerance when it is actually within limits.

statistics.q: In a Shewhart control chart for linac output, the action level is typically set at:
statistics.a: ± 3 standard deviations from the mean
c: ± 1 standard deviation from the mean
± 2 standard deviations from the mean
± 3 standard deviations from the mean
± 4 standard deviations from the mean
statistics.e: The ±3σ limits correspond to a 99.7% confidence level. Points beyond this trigger investigation and corrective action. Warning levels are typically at ±2σ.

statistics.q: The Poisson distribution is appropriate for modelling:
statistics.a: Radioactive decay events in a given time interval
c: Patient weight distribution in a clinic
Radioactive decay events in a given time interval
Continuous dose measurements along a beam profile
Temperature variations in a treatment room
statistics.e: Poisson distribution models the number of events occurring in a fixed interval when events are independent and rare. Radioactive decay is the classic example in medical physics.

statistics.q: CALC: A quality control measurement gives mean = 1.000 Gy, standard deviation = 0.015 Gy, over 25 measurements. What is the standard error of the mean?
statistics.e: Standard error = σ/√n = 0.015/√25 = 0.015/5 = 0.003 Gy. The SEM quantifies uncertainty in the estimate of the population mean.

statistics.q: The chi-squared test in medical physics QA is commonly used to:
statistics.a: Test whether observed frequency distributions match expected distributions
c: Calculate the mean dose in a treatment plan
Test whether observed frequency distributions match expected distributions
Determine the half-life of a radioactive source
Measure the linearity of an ionisation chamber
statistics.e: Chi-squared tests compare observed vs expected frequencies. In QA, this can test whether detector response follows expected statistical distributions or whether equipment performance is consistent.

statistics.q: What does the coefficient of variation (CV) express?
statistics.a: Standard deviation as a percentage of the mean
c: The range divided by the median value
Standard deviation as a percentage of the mean
The difference between maximum and minimum values
The ratio of variance to the sample size
statistics.e: CV = (σ/μ) × 100%. It provides a dimensionless measure of relative variability, useful for comparing precision across measurements with different units or scales.

statistics.q: In gamma analysis, the percentage of points passing 3%/3mm criteria should typically exceed:
statistics.a: 95% for clinical IMRT plan verification
c: 80% for all treatment techniques
90% only for simple 3DCRT plans
95% for clinical IMRT plan verification
100% for any acceptable treatment plan
statistics.e: A ≥95% pass rate for 3%/3mm gamma criteria is the widely accepted clinical standard for IMRT/VMAT QA verification, per AAPM TG-218 recommendations.

statistics.q: The paired t-test is appropriate when:
statistics.a: Comparing two related measurements from the same sample
c: Comparing means from three or more independent groups
Comparing two related measurements from the same sample
Testing for correlation between two variables
Analysing categorical data in a contingency table
statistics.e: Paired t-tests compare two related measurements (e.g., before/after, or two detectors measuring the same beam). Each pair is from the same subject or condition.

statistics.q: CALC: Radioactive source counted for 1 minute gives 10,000 counts. What is the percentage uncertainty (1σ)?
statistics.e: For Poisson statistics, σ = √N = √10000 = 100. Percentage uncertainty = (100/10000) × 100% = 1.0%. Counting statistics follow √N rule.

statistics.q: What is the purpose of a Bland-Altman plot in medical physics?
statistics.a: To assess agreement between two measurement methods by plotting differences vs means
c: To display the dose-volume histogram for treatment plans
To assess agreement between two measurement methods by plotting differences vs means
To show the relationship between beam energy and depth dose
To graph the decay curve of radioactive isotopes
statistics.e: Bland-Altman plots show the difference between two methods against their mean. Limits of agreement (mean ± 1.96σ) indicate whether methods can be used interchangeably.

statistics.q: The sensitivity of a radiation survey meter refers to:
statistics.a: The minimum detectable radiation level above background
c: The maximum dose rate the meter can display
The minimum detectable radiation level above background
The speed at which readings stabilise
The temperature range of operation
statistics.e: Sensitivity (minimum detectable activity/dose rate) determines whether an instrument can detect low-level contamination or radiation. Related to detector efficiency and background count rate.

statistics.q: In ANOVA (Analysis of Variance), the F-statistic compares:
statistics.a: Between-group variance to within-group variance
c: Sample mean to population mean directly
Between-group variance to within-group variance
Maximum value to minimum value in the data
Standard deviation to standard error of mean
statistics.e: F = MS_between/MS_within. A large F indicates that group means differ more than expected from random variation. Used when comparing 3+ groups (e.g., output from multiple linacs).

statistics.q: Dead time correction in a Geiger-Mueller counter becomes important when:
statistics.a: Count rates are high enough that events are missed during recovery time
c: The detector battery is running low on charge
Count rates are high enough that events are missed during recovery time
Background radiation exceeds the source activity
The detector is used in a magnetic field environment
statistics.e: GM tubes have dead time (~100-300 μs) after each count. At high count rates, true counts are underestimated. Correction: N_true = N_obs/(1 - N_obs × τ), where τ is dead time.

statistics.q: CALC: A GM counter with dead time τ = 200 μs records 40,000 cps. What is the corrected count rate?
statistics.a: Approximately 40,330 cps
c: Exactly 40,000 cps — no correction needed
Approximately 40,330 cps
Approximately 41,667 cps
Approximately 50,000 cps

statistics.q: The purpose of a receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve in diagnostic imaging is to:
statistics.a: Evaluate diagnostic test performance by plotting sensitivity vs (1-specificity)
c: Measure the spatial resolution of an imaging system
Evaluate diagnostic test performance by plotting sensitivity vs (1-specificity)
Calculate the radiation dose delivered during the examination
Determine the optimal kVp setting for patient imaging
statistics.e: ROC curves plot true positive rate (sensitivity) against false positive rate (1-specificity) at various thresholds. Area under ROC (AUC) quantifies overall diagnostic accuracy.

statistics.q: What is the difference between accuracy and precision in measurement?
statistics.a: Accuracy is closeness to true value; precision is reproducibility of measurements
c: Accuracy and precision mean exactly the same thing
Accuracy is closeness to true value; precision is reproducibility of measurements
Accuracy relates to speed; precision relates to cost
Accuracy is for analog instruments; precision for digital
statistics.e: A measurement can be precise but inaccurate (systematic error) or accurate but imprecise (random error). Good QA requires both high accuracy and high precision.

statistics.q: The propagation of uncertainty for a product z = xy gives the relative uncertainty as:
statistics.a: √[(δx/x)² + (δy/y)²]
c: δx + δy
(δx/x) + (δy/y)
√[(δx/x)² + (δy/y)²]
(δx × δy)/(x × y)
statistics.e: For multiplication/division, relative (fractional) uncertainties add in quadrature. For addition/subtraction, absolute uncertainties add in quadrature.

statistics.q: In process control, a trend of 7 consecutive points on one side of the mean on a control chart indicates:
statistics.a: A systematic shift requiring investigation even if all points are within limits
c: Normal random variation in the measurement process
A systematic shift requiring investigation even if all points are within limits
Equipment has failed and must be removed from service
The control limits need to be recalculated immediately
statistics.e: The "rule of 7" (or similar run rules) detects systematic drift before control limits are breached. Western Electric rules use various patterns to detect non-random behaviour.

statistics.q: What is the purpose of regression analysis in medical physics research?
statistics.a: To model the relationship between dependent and independent variables
c: To calculate the mean and standard deviation of a dataset
To model the relationship between dependent and independent variables
To test whether two samples come from the same population
To rank measurements from smallest to largest value
statistics.e: Linear regression models y = mx + b relationships (e.g., dose vs depth, detector response vs energy). R² indicates the proportion of variance explained by the model.

statistics.q: CALC: Five daily output measurements are: 1.002, 0.998, 1.001, 0.999, 1.000 Gy. What is the sample standard deviation?
A null hypothesis in statistical testing represents:
statistics.a: The assumption that there is no significant difference or effect between groups
c: The prediction that the experimental intervention will have a measurable effect
The assumption that there is no significant difference or effect between groups
The probability of obtaining the observed result by random chance alone
The confidence level at which results are considered statistically meaningful
statistics.e: The null hypothesis (H0) states that there is no difference, no effect, or no association. Statistical tests determine whether there is sufficient evidence to reject H0 in favour of the alternative hypothesis (H1). For example, H0: the mean doses from two calibration methods are equal.

statistics.q: A p-value of 0.03 means:
statistics.a: There is a 3% probability of obtaining results this extreme if the null hypothesis is true
c: There is a 3% chance that the null hypothesis is correct
There is a 3% probability of obtaining results this extreme if the null hypothesis is true
The experimental treatment has a 97% probability of being effective
The result has a 3% measurement uncertainty associated with it
statistics.e: The p-value is the probability of observing data as extreme as (or more extreme than) the actual results, assuming H0 is true. It is NOT the probability that H0 is true. A p-value of 0.03 < 0.05 would typically lead to rejection of H0 at the 5% significance level.

statistics.q: An unpaired (independent) two-sample t-test is used when:
statistics.a: Comparing means of two independent groups with normally distributed continuous data
c: Comparing proportions between two groups using categorical outcome data
Comparing means of two independent groups with normally distributed continuous data
Analysing the correlation between two continuous variables simultaneously
Comparing medians of two groups with highly skewed non-normal data
statistics.e: The independent two-sample t-test compares the means of two unrelated groups. Assumptions include: continuous data, normal distribution (or large n), independent observations, and approximately equal variances (or use Welch's correction). For example, comparing mean doses measured by two different ionisation chambers.

statistics.q: The chi-square test is appropriate for analysing:
statistics.a: The association between two categorical variables in a contingency table
c: The difference between two population means with paired measurements
The association between two categorical variables in a contingency table
The correlation coefficient between two continuous measurements
The trend in a time series of sequential dosimetry measurements
statistics.e: The chi-square test of independence evaluates whether two categorical variables are associated. It compares observed frequencies to expected frequencies under the assumption of independence. Expected cell counts should generally be 5 or greater for the test to be valid.

statistics.q: One-way ANOVA is used to:
statistics.a: Compare means of three or more independent groups simultaneously
c: Assess the agreement between two measurement methods graphically
Compare means of three or more independent groups simultaneously
Determine the regression equation for predicting one variable from another
Test whether a single sample mean differs from a known population value
statistics.e: One-way ANOVA tests whether the means of three or more groups are equal by comparing between-group variability to within-group variability (F-ratio). If the ANOVA is significant, post-hoc tests (e.g., Tukey, Bonferroni) identify which specific pairs differ.

statistics.q: A 95% confidence interval for a mean dose of 2.00 Gy is reported as (1.96, 2.04) Gy. This means:
statistics.a: If the measurement were repeated many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true mean
c: There is a 95% probability that the true mean lies between 1.96 and 2.04 Gy
If the measurement were repeated many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true mean
Exactly 95% of all individual dose measurements fall between 1.96 and 2.04 Gy
The measurement is accurate to within 2% of the true dose value
statistics.e: A 95% CI is a frequentist concept: if we repeated the experiment many times and calculated a CI each time, 95% of those intervals would contain the true population parameter. It does NOT mean there is a 95% probability the true value is in this specific interval (that is a Bayesian interpretation).

statistics.q: In the GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) framework, Type A uncertainty is evaluated by:
statistics.a: Statistical analysis of a series of repeated measurements
c: Manufacturer specifications and calibration certificate data
Statistical analysis of a series of repeated measurements
Expert judgement based on prior experience with the instrument
Published reference data from international measurement standards
statistics.e: GUM distinguishes Type A uncertainty (evaluated by statistical methods from repeated observations, e.g., standard deviation of the mean) from Type B uncertainty (evaluated by other means: calibration certificates, manufacturer data, published values, expert judgement).

statistics.q: CALC: A radiation detector records 10,000 counts. What is the percentage standard deviation due to Poisson counting statistics?
statistics.e: For Poisson-distributed data, the standard deviation sigma = sqrt(N) = sqrt(10,000) = 100. The percentage standard deviation = (100/10,000) x 100% = 1.0%. This illustrates why more counts improve statistical precision: 1,000,000 counts give 0.1% uncertainty.

statistics.q: A Type I error occurs when:
statistics.a: The null hypothesis is rejected when it is actually true (false positive)
c: The null hypothesis is rejected when it is actually true (false positive)
The null hypothesis is not rejected when it is actually false (false negative)
The sample size is too small to detect a clinically meaningful difference
The data violate the assumptions required by the statistical test used
statistics.e: A Type I error (alpha error, false positive) means concluding there is an effect when there is none. The significance level alpha (typically 0.05) is the maximum acceptable probability of a Type I error. A Type II error (beta, false negative) means failing to detect a real effect.

statistics.q: The power of a statistical test is defined as:
statistics.a: The probability of correctly rejecting the null hypothesis when it is false (1 - beta)
c: The probability of not rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true
The probability of correctly rejecting the null hypothesis when it is false (1 - beta)
The significance level chosen for the hypothesis test (typically 0.05)
The effect size divided by the standard error of the measurement
statistics.e: Statistical power = 1 - beta, where beta is the probability of a Type II error. Power depends on effect size, sample size, significance level, and variability. A power of 0.80 means an 80% chance of detecting a true effect. Power analysis should be performed before data collection.

statistics.q: Sensitivity of a diagnostic test is defined as:
statistics.a: The proportion of true positives correctly identified among all actual positives
c: The proportion of true negatives correctly identified among all actual negatives
The proportion of true positives correctly identified among all actual positives
The proportion of positive test results that are truly positive outcomes
The overall proportion of correct classifications among all test results
statistics.e: Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN) = true positive rate. It measures how well the test identifies those with the condition. A highly sensitive test is useful for screening because a negative result reliably rules out disease (SnNOut: Sensitivity rules out).

statistics.q: Specificity of a diagnostic test is defined as:
statistics.a: The proportion of true negatives correctly identified among all actual negatives
c: The proportion of true positives correctly identified among all actual positives
The proportion of true negatives correctly identified among all actual negatives
The proportion of negative test results that are truly negative outcomes
The probability that a person with a positive test truly has the disease
statistics.e: Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) = true negative rate. It measures how well the test identifies those without the condition. A highly specific test is useful for confirmation because a positive result reliably rules in disease (SpPIn: Specificity rules in).

statistics.q: The area under the ROC curve (AUC) represents:
statistics.a: The probability that the test correctly ranks a random positive case higher than a random negative case
c: The sensitivity of the test at the optimal operating threshold
The probability that the test correctly ranks a random positive case higher than a random negative case
The proportion of the population correctly classified at all thresholds
The positive predictive value averaged across all possible prevalence values
statistics.e: AUC ranges from 0.5 (no discrimination, equivalent to chance) to 1.0 (perfect discrimination). An AUC of 0.85 means there is an 85% probability that the test will correctly assign a higher score to a randomly chosen positive case than to a random negative case.

statistics.q: CALC: The linear regression equation for output factor vs field size is y = 0.003x + 0.85. What is the predicted output factor for a 10 x 10 cm field (x = 10)?
statistics.e: y = 0.003(10) + 0.85 = 0.03 + 0.85 = 0.88. The slope (0.003 per cm) represents the rate of change in output factor per cm of field size, and the intercept (0.85) is the extrapolated output at zero field size.

statistics.q: A Bland-Altman plot is used to assess:
statistics.a: The agreement between two measurement methods by plotting the difference against the mean
c: The linear correlation between two variables using Pearson's coefficient
The agreement between two measurement methods by plotting the difference against the mean
The distribution of residuals in a linear regression analysis
The receiver operating characteristic of a diagnostic classification test
statistics.e: A Bland-Altman plot displays the difference between two methods (y-axis) against their mean (x-axis). The mean difference (bias) and limits of agreement (mean plus or minus 1.96 SD) indicate systematic and random differences. This is preferred over correlation for method comparison studies.

statistics.q: CALC: An ionisation chamber measurement has the following uncertainty components: positioning 0.5%, electrometer reading 0.3%, temperature/pressure correction 0.2%, calibration coefficient 0.8%. What is the combined standard uncertainty?
statistics.e: For independent uncertainty components, the combined standard uncertainty is found by adding in quadrature: u_c = sqrt(0.5^2 + 0.3^2 + 0.2^2 + 0.8^2) = sqrt(0.25 + 0.09 + 0.04 + 0.64) = sqrt(1.02) = approximately 1.0%. This is the GUM method for combining standard uncertainties.

statistics.q: CALC: If a measurement result is 100.0 plus or minus 1.0 (k=1), what is the expanded uncertainty at 95% confidence (k=2)?
statistics.a: plus or minus 2.0
c: plus or minus 1.0
plus or minus 1.5
plus or minus 2.0
plus or minus 3.0
statistics.e: The expanded uncertainty U = k x u_c = 2 x 1.0 = 2.0. The coverage factor k=2 corresponds to approximately 95% confidence for a normal distribution. The result would be reported as 100.0 plus or minus 2.0 (k=2, 95% confidence).

statistics.q: CALC: The standard deviation of 5 dosimetry readings (2.01, 1.99, 2.02, 1.98, 2.00 Gy) is 0.016 Gy. What is the standard deviation of the mean?
statistics.e: The standard deviation of the mean (standard error) = SD/sqrt(n) = 0.016/sqrt(5) = 0.016/2.236 = 0.00716, approximately 0.007 Gy. This quantifies the uncertainty in the mean value. Increasing n reduces the standard error proportionally to 1/sqrt(n).

statistics.q: The coefficient of variation (CV) is useful for:
statistics.a: Expressing variability as a percentage of the mean to compare relative precision
c: Testing whether two population means are statistically significantly different
Expressing variability as a percentage of the mean to compare relative precision
Determining the minimum sample size needed for adequate statistical power
Identifying systematic bias between a measurement and a reference standard
statistics.e: CV = (SD/mean) x 100%. It allows comparison of variability between measurements with different units or magnitudes. In medical physics QA, a CV < 1% for linac output constancy is typically expected for daily measurements.

statistics.q: CALC: A Poisson-distributed measurement of radioactive decay gives 400 counts in one minute. What is the 95% confidence interval for the true count rate?
statistics.a: 361 to 439 counts per minute
c: 380 to 420 counts per minute
361 to 439 counts per minute
340 to 460 counts per minute
300 to 500 counts per minute
statistics.e: For Poisson data, sigma = sqrt(N) = sqrt(400) = 20. The 95% CI = N plus or minus 1.96 x sigma = 400 plus or minus 1.96(20) = 400 plus or minus 39.2, giving approximately 361 to 439 counts per minute. For large N, the Poisson distribution approximates a normal distribution.

statistics.q: CALC: If f(x,y) = x/y, x = 10.0 plus or minus 0.2 and y = 5.0 plus or minus 0.1, what is the uncertainty in f using propagation of errors?
statistics.a: plus or minus 0.056
c: plus or minus 0.028
plus or minus 0.040
plus or minus 0.056
plus or minus 0.072

statistics.q: When should a paired t-test be used instead of an unpaired t-test?
statistics.a: When the two measurements are made on the same subjects or matched pairs
c: When the sample sizes of the two groups are significantly different
When the two measurements are made on the same subjects or matched pairs
When the data are not normally distributed and require transformation
When comparing more than two groups in a single statistical analysis
statistics.e: A paired t-test is used when measurements are naturally paired (e.g., before/after treatment on the same patient, or two dosimeters measuring the same beam). Pairing controls for inter-subject variability, increasing statistical power. The test analyses the differences within each pair.

statistics.q: The R-squared value in linear regression represents:
statistics.a: The proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the independent variable
c: The slope of the best-fit line through the data points
The proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the independent variable
The probability that the regression model fits the data significantly
The correlation coefficient between the predicted and observed residuals
statistics.e: R-squared (coefficient of determination) ranges from 0 to 1. R-squared = 0.95 means 95% of the variability in the dependent variable is explained by the regression model. In medical physics, high R-squared values (>0.99) are expected for well-characterised physical relationships like PDD curves.

statistics.q: What is the null hypothesis in a paired t-test comparing two dosimetry techniques?
statistics.a: The mean difference between techniques is zero
c: The variances of both techniques are equal
The mean difference between techniques is zero
Both techniques have identical distributions
The medians of both techniques are equal
statistics.e: A paired t-test evaluates whether the mean of the paired differences is significantly different from zero.

statistics.q: Which statistical test is most appropriate for comparing categorical outcomes between two groups?
statistics.a: Chi-square test of independence
c: Paired t-test for matched samples
Chi-square test of independence
Pearson correlation coefficient
One-way analysis of variance
statistics.e: The chi-square test evaluates whether there is a significant association between two categorical variables.

statistics.q: In ROC analysis, what does the area under the curve (AUC) represent?
statistics.a: The probability of correctly ranking a random positive-negative pair
c: The overall sensitivity of the diagnostic test
The probability of correctly ranking a random positive-negative pair
The specificity at the optimal threshold value
The positive predictive value across all thresholds
statistics.e: AUC represents the probability that a randomly chosen positive case will be rated higher than a randomly chosen negative case.

statistics.q: What is the purpose of a Bland-Altman plot?
statistics.a: To assess agreement between two measurement methods
c: To compare means of two independent samples
To assess agreement between two measurement methods
To determine the correlation between two variables
To evaluate normality of a data distribution
statistics.e: A Bland-Altman plot displays the difference between two methods against their mean.

statistics.q: CALC: A study reports a mean dose of 2.00 Gy with standard error 0.05 Gy (n=25). What is the standard deviation?
statistics.e: SD = SE x sqrt(n) = 0.05 x 5 = 0.25 Gy.

statistics.q: What is a Type II error in hypothesis testing?
statistics.a: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis
c: Rejecting a true null hypothesis incorrectly
Failing to reject a false null hypothesis
Accepting the alternative when data is insufficient
Using the wrong statistical test for the data
statistics.e: A Type II error (beta) occurs when the test fails to detect a real effect.

statistics.q: Which regression method is appropriate when the outcome variable is binary?
statistics.a: Logistic regression analysis
c: Simple linear regression analysis
Logistic regression analysis
Polynomial regression analysis
Ridge regression analysis
statistics.e: Logistic regression models the probability of a binary outcome.

statistics.q: What does the Pearson correlation coefficient r = -0.92 indicate?
statistics.a: A strong negative linear relationship exists
c: A weak negative linear relationship exists
No meaningful relationship between variables
A strong negative linear relationship exists
A strong positive linear relationship exists
statistics.e: Values close to -1 indicate a strong negative linear relationship.

statistics.q: In a Bland-Altman analysis, what do the limits of agreement represent?
statistics.a: Mean difference plus or minus 1.96 standard deviations
c: The range containing all measured differences
Mean difference plus or minus 1.96 standard deviations
The 99% confidence interval of the mean bias
Upper and lower bounds of acceptable tolerance
statistics.e: Limits of agreement = mean difference +/- 1.96 SD of differences.

statistics.q: CALC: If sensitivity is 90% and specificity is 80%, what is the false positive rate?
statistics.e: False positive rate = 1 - specificity = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 or 20%.

statistics.q: Which non-parametric test is the alternative to the independent samples t-test?
statistics.a: Mann-Whitney U test
c: Wilcoxon signed-rank test
Mann-Whitney U test
Kruskal-Wallis H test
Friedman rank sum test
statistics.e: The Mann-Whitney U test compares two independent groups without assuming normality.

statistics.q: What is the primary purpose of a sample size calculation before a clinical study?
statistics.a: To ensure adequate statistical power to detect an effect
c: To minimize the total cost of the research study
To ensure adequate statistical power to detect an effect
To determine the appropriate statistical test to use
To establish the significance level for the study
statistics.e: Sample size calculations ensure sufficient power (typically 80%) to detect a clinically meaningful difference.

statistics.q: What correction is applied when performing multiple comparisons?
statistics.a: Bonferroni correction divides alpha by comparison count
c: Yates continuity correction for small sample sizes
