anatomy.a: A solid structure from the rectosigmoid junction to the anal verge
c: Only the anterior rectal wall within 1 cm of the prostate
A solid structure from the rectosigmoid junction to the anal verge
A hollow structure including only the rectal wall and not contents
Only the portion of rectum within the high-dose treatment volume
anatomy.e: The rectum is typically contoured as a solid structure (including wall and contents) from the rectosigmoid junction (approximately the level of S2/S3) to the anorectal junction or anal verge. QUANTEC rectal constraints include V50 < 50%, V60 < 35%, V65 < 25%, V70 < 20%, V75 < 15%.

anatomy.q: The larynx dose constraint to preserve voice quality in head and neck RT is:
anatomy.a: Mean larynx dose < 44 Gy and Dmax < 66 Gy
c: Mean larynx dose < 30 Gy and Dmax < 50 Gy
Mean larynx dose < 44 Gy and Dmax < 66 Gy
Mean larynx dose < 55 Gy and Dmax < 70 Gy
Mean larynx dose < 60 Gy and Dmax < 74 Gy
anatomy.e: To preserve voice quality and laryngeal function, mean laryngeal dose should be kept < 44 Gy where possible. The larynx should be contoured from the tip of the epiglottis to the inferior border of the cricoid cartilage, including the arytenoids and vocal cords.

anatomy.q: Level V cervical lymph nodes are located in:
anatomy.a: The posterior triangle of the neck, posterior to the sternocleidomastoid muscle
c: The anterior compartment of the neck between the hyoid and sternum
Along the internal jugular vein from the hyoid to the clavicle
The posterior triangle of the neck, posterior to the sternocleidomastoid muscle
The supraclavicular fossa medial to the scalene muscles
anatomy.e: Level V (posterior triangle) nodes are bounded by the posterior border of the SCM anteriorly, the anterior border of trapezius posteriorly, and the clavicle inferiorly. Level VA extends from skull base to the inferior border of the cricoid, and level VB from the cricoid to the clavicle.

anatomy.q: The QUANTEC dose constraint for the contralateral (uninvolved) lung in unilateral lung cancer treatment is:
c: V5 < 30% to the contralateral lung
V10 < 15% to the contralateral lung
V20 < 10% to the contralateral lung
V30 < 20% to the contralateral lung
anatomy.e: Limiting dose to the contralateral lung is critical to preserve pulmonary function, especially if the patient may require future surgery. V5 and V20 to the contralateral lung are commonly constrained. MLD to the contralateral lung should ideally be kept below 7-8 Gy.

anatomy.q: Which cranial nerve passes through the foramen ovale?
c: Maxillary division of trigeminal nerve (V2)
Mandibular division of trigeminal nerve (V3)
Facial nerve motor branch division (VII)
Glossopharyngeal nerve trunk division (IX)
anatomy.e: V3 exits through foramen ovale.

anatomy.q: Which lymph node level contains the jugulodigastric nodes?
c: Level I (submental and submandibular)
Level II (upper jugular nodes)
Level III (middle jugular nodes)
Level IV (lower jugular nodes)
anatomy.e: Level II nodes are in the upper third of the jugular chain.

anatomy.q: What is the typical dose constraint for the brainstem in conventional fractionation?
anatomy.a: Maximum dose of 54 Gy to the whole structure
c: Maximum dose of 45 Gy to the whole structure
Maximum dose of 54 Gy to the whole structure
Maximum dose of 60 Gy to the whole structure
Maximum dose of 70 Gy to the whole structure
anatomy.e: Brainstem tolerance is typically Dmax < 54 Gy.

anatomy.q: Which structure separates the anterior and posterior triangles of the neck?
c: Sternocleidomastoid muscle (SCM)
Platysma muscle of the neck
Anterior scalene muscle group
Omohyoid muscle of the neck
anatomy.e: The SCM divides the neck into anterior and posterior triangles.

anatomy.q: What vertebral level corresponds to the carina on CT imaging?
c: Approximately the T2-T3 vertebral level
Approximately the T5-T6 vertebral level
Approximately the T8-T9 vertebral level
Approximately the T10-T11 vertebral level
anatomy.e: The carina is typically at T5-T6.

anatomy.q: Which organ at risk is most critical when treating left-sided breast cancer?
anatomy.a: The heart and left anterior descending artery
c: The contralateral right breast tissue
The heart and left anterior descending artery
The ipsilateral brachial plexus nerves
The thyroid gland in the lower neck
anatomy.e: The heart, particularly the LAD, is most critical in left breast RT.

anatomy.q: Where are Level VII lymph nodes located?
anatomy.a: In the superior mediastinum below the suprasternal notch
c: In the retropharyngeal space behind the pharynx
Along the spinal accessory nerve in posterior neck
In the superior mediastinum below the suprasternal notch
In the parotid gland region of the upper neck
anatomy.e: Level VII nodes are upper mediastinal nodes.

anatomy.q: What is the typical contouring boundary between Level Ib and Level IIa nodes?
anatomy.a: The posterior edge of the submandibular gland
c: The anterior belly of the digastric muscle
The posterior edge of the submandibular gland
The hyoid bone at the midline of the neck
The inferior border of the lateral pterygoid
anatomy.e: Level Ib transitions to Level IIa at the posterior submandibular gland.

anatomy.q: Which pelvic structure has a tolerance dose of approximately 65-70 Gy?
c: The rectum and rectal wall organ
The urinary bladder wall organ
The bilateral femoral head bones
The small bowel loop structures
anatomy.e: Bladder tolerates relatively high doses with TD 5/5 around 65-70 Gy.

anatomy.q: What anatomical structure defines the inferior border of the parotid gland?
anatomy.a: The angle of the mandible bone
c: The mastoid tip of the temporal bone
The angle of the mandible bone
The hyoid bone at neck midline
The C2 vertebral body level
anatomy.e: The parotid extends to approximately the angle of the mandible inferiorly.

anatomy.q: Which lung dose-volume parameter is most predictive of radiation pneumonitis?
anatomy.a: Mean lung dose (MLD) to both lungs
c: Maximum point dose to either lung
Mean lung dose (MLD) to both lungs
Minimum dose to 95% of lung volume
Dose to 1 cc of the lung volume
anatomy.e: MLD and V20 are the most validated predictors.

anatomy.q: What is the brachial plexus dose constraint in conventional fractionation?
anatomy.a: Maximum dose should not exceed 66 Gy
c: Maximum dose should not exceed 50 Gy
Maximum dose should not exceed 54 Gy
Maximum dose should not exceed 60 Gy
Maximum dose should not exceed 66 Gy
anatomy.e: Brachial plexus tolerance is approximately 66 Gy.

anatomy.q: Which structure passes through the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm?
c: Aorta and thoracic duct structures
Esophagus and vagus nerve trunks
Inferior vena cava and phrenic nerve
Azygos vein and sympathetic chain
anatomy.e: The esophageal hiatus at T10 transmits the esophagus and vagal trunks.

anatomy.q: What is the superior border of Level III cervical lymph nodes?
anatomy.a: The inferior border of the hyoid bone
c: The superior border of the thyroid cartilage
The inferior border of the hyoid bone
The lower border of the cricoid cartilage
The base of skull at the jugular foramen
anatomy.e: Level III extends from the hyoid bone to the cricoid cartilage.

anatomy.q: Which pelvic organ has the lowest radiation tolerance?
anatomy.a: Small bowel with TD 5/5 of 45-50 Gy
c: Bladder with TD 5/5 of approximately 65 Gy
Rectum with TD 5/5 of approximately 60 Gy
Small bowel with TD 5/5 of 45-50 Gy
Femoral heads with TD 5/5 of about 52 Gy
anatomy.e: Small bowel has the lowest tolerance among common pelvic structures.

anatomy.q: What landmark defines the separation between supraglottic and glottic larynx?
c: The epiglottic tip at the tongue base
The laryngeal ventricle floor level
The inferior cricoid cartilage border
The thyroid cartilage notch superiorly
anatomy.e: The supraglottic extends to the floor of the laryngeal ventricle.

anatomy.q: What is the typical PRV margin added to the spinal cord for planning?
anatomy.a: 5 mm uniform expansion around the cord
c: 2 mm uniform expansion around the cord
5 mm uniform expansion around the cord
10 mm uniform expansion around the cord
No margin is used for the spinal cord
anatomy.e: A 5 mm PRV margin is typically applied.

anatomy.q: Which muscle group forms the pelvic floor and is relevant in prostate contouring?
c: The obturator internus muscle group
The levator ani muscle complex
The piriformis muscle structures
The iliacus muscle group complex
anatomy.e: The levator ani forms the pelvic diaphragm.

anatomy.q: What is the dose constraint for femoral heads in pelvic radiotherapy?
anatomy.a: V50 Gy should be less than 5-10% volume
c: V30 Gy should be less than 5-10% volume
V40 Gy should be less than 15-20% volume
V50 Gy should be less than 5-10% volume
V60 Gy should be less than 5-10% volume
anatomy.e: V50 < 5-10% to prevent avascular necrosis.

anatomy.q: Which structure is the key landmark for identifying the carotid bifurcation on CT?
anatomy.a: The superior border of the thyroid cartilage
c: The inferior border of the mandible bone
The superior border of the thyroid cartilage
The angle of the mandible bone region
The inferior border of the cricoid cartilage
anatomy.e: The carotid typically bifurcates near the superior thyroid cartilage.

anatomy.q: What is the retropharyngeal lymph node space bounded by posteriorly?
anatomy.a: The prevertebral fascia and longus colli muscles
c: The posterior pharyngeal wall mucosa layer
The prevertebral fascia and longus colli muscles
The sternocleidomastoid muscle laterally
The carotid sheath and its contents
anatomy.e: Retropharyngeal nodes lie between pharyngeal constrictors and prevertebral fascia.

anatomy.q: What is the approximate depth of the prostate apex from the skin surface?
anatomy.a: Approximately 7-10 cm from the anterior skin
c: Approximately 3-5 cm from the anterior skin
Approximately 7-10 cm from the anterior skin
Approximately 12-15 cm from anterior skin
Approximately 15-20 cm from anterior skin
anatomy.e: The prostate is typically 7-10 cm deep from anterior skin.

anatomy.q: Which thoracic structure is most at risk during esophageal cancer treatment?
anatomy.a: The spinal cord posterior to the esophagus
c: The spinal cord posterior to the esophagus
The trachea anterior to the esophagus
The sternum in the anterior chest wall
The scapulae in the posterior chest wall
anatomy.e: The spinal cord is the primary dose-limiting OAR.

anatomy.q: What is the CTV-PTV margin typically used for prostate IGRT with daily imaging?
c: 1-2 mm uniform margin all around
3-5 mm with reduced posterior margin
7-10 mm uniform margin all around
10-15 mm uniform margin all around
anatomy.e: With daily IGRT, margins of 3-5 mm are typical, posterior often reduced to 3 mm.

anatomy.q: Which cranial nerve is at greatest risk during parotid gland irradiation?
c: The trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V)
The facial nerve (cranial nerve VII)
The vagus nerve (cranial nerve X)
The hypoglossal nerve (cranial XII)
anatomy.e: CN VII traverses through the parotid gland.

anatomy.q: What is the penile bulb and why is it contoured in prostate RT?
anatomy.a: Erectile tissue; sparing helps preserve sexual function
c: A lymph node region requiring elective irradiation
Erectile tissue; sparing helps preserve sexual function
Part of the urethral sphincter for continence
A bony landmark used for image registration only
anatomy.e: Dose to the penile bulb correlates with erectile dysfunction.

anatomy.q: Which contouring atlas is most commonly referenced for head and neck nodal levels?
anatomy.a: The consensus guidelines by Gregoire et al.
c: The RTOG whole brain contouring atlas
The consensus guidelines by Gregoire et al.
The ICRU Report 83 volume definitions
The AAPM TG-263 naming conventions only
anatomy.e: Gregoire et al. provide detailed CT-based delineation of cervical lymph node levels.

anatomy.q: What structures are included in the cardiac contouring for breast RT?
anatomy.a: Pericardium, all chambers, and the LAD coronary artery
c: Only the left ventricle and interventricular septum
Pericardium, all chambers, and the LAD coronary artery
The great vessels and mediastinal fat only
Only the myocardium without the pericardial sac
anatomy.e: Heart contouring should include pericardium, all chambers, and LAD.

anatomy.q: What is the obturator lymph node group and where is it located?
anatomy.a: Nodes along the obturator vessels in the pelvis
c: Nodes along the obturator vessels in the pelvis
Nodes along the external iliac artery chain
Nodes within the inguinal canal structures
Nodes posterior to the sacral promontory
anatomy.e: Obturator nodes lie in the obturator fossa.

anatomy.q: What is the cochlea dose constraint in head and neck RT?
anatomy.a: Mean dose should be kept below 45 Gy
c: Mean dose should be kept below 25 Gy
Mean dose should be kept below 35 Gy
Mean dose should be kept below 45 Gy
Mean dose should be kept below 55 Gy
anatomy.e: Cochlear mean dose < 45 Gy to minimize hearing loss.

anatomy.q: Which vertebral level marks the inferior extent of the thoracic esophagus?
anatomy.a: The gastroesophageal junction at approximately T11
c: The level of the carina at approximately T5-T6
The gastroesophageal junction at approximately T11
The diaphragmatic hiatus at approximately T8-T9
The aortic arch level at approximately T3-T4
anatomy.e: The thoracic esophagus extends to the GEJ at approximately T11.

anatomy.q: What anatomical space contains the internal mammary lymph nodes?
anatomy.a: The parasternal space along the internal mammary vessels
c: The axillary fossa lateral to the chest wall
The parasternal space along the internal mammary vessels
The supraclavicular fossa above the clavicle
The posterior mediastinum near the thoracic spine
anatomy.e: Internal mammary nodes lie in the parasternal region within 3 cm of the sternum.

anatomy.q: What is the optic chiasm dose constraint in conventional fractionation?
anatomy.a: Maximum dose should not exceed 54 Gy
c: Maximum dose should not exceed 40 Gy
Maximum dose should not exceed 46 Gy
Maximum dose should not exceed 54 Gy
Maximum dose should not exceed 60 Gy
anatomy.e: Optic chiasm tolerance is Dmax < 54 Gy.

anatomy.q: Which structure defines the lateral boundary of Level V cervical lymph nodes?
anatomy.a: The anterior border of the trapezius muscle
c: The posterior border of the SCM muscle
The anterior border of the trapezius muscle
The lateral border of the scalene muscles
The transverse processes of cervical vertebrae
anatomy.e: Level V is bounded posteriorly by the anterior border of the trapezius.

anatomy.q: What is the clinical significance of the inguinal ligament in pelvic contouring?
anatomy.a: It marks the boundary between pelvis and lower limb
c: It marks the boundary between pelvis and lower limb
It serves as the superior border for the bladder
It defines the lateral extent of the rectal CTV
It indicates the location of the pelvic diaphragm
anatomy.e: The inguinal ligament separates iliac from inguinal nodal regions.

anatomy.q: What is the recommended dose constraint for the larynx as an organ at risk?
anatomy.a: Mean dose below 40-45 Gy to preserve voice
c: Mean dose below 20-25 Gy to preserve voice
Mean dose below 30-35 Gy to preserve voice
Mean dose below 40-45 Gy to preserve voice
Mean dose below 55-60 Gy to preserve voice
anatomy.e: Mean larynx dose below 40-45 Gy helps preserve voice quality.

anatomy.q: Which structure is used as the posterior boundary when contouring the prostate?
anatomy.a: The anterior rectal wall on CT or MR imaging
c: The posterior bladder wall on imaging studies
The anterior rectal wall on CT or MR imaging
The pelvic floor levator ani muscles only
The posterior pubic symphysis bone margin
anatomy.e: The anterior rectal wall defines the posterior prostate boundary.

anatomy.q: What are Waldeyer's ring structures relevant to head and neck RT?
anatomy.a: The palatine, lingual, pharyngeal, and tubal tonsils
c: The major and minor salivary gland tissues
The palatine, lingual, pharyngeal, and tubal tonsils
The cervical lymph node chain levels I through V
The pharyngeal constrictor muscle ring only
anatomy.e: Waldeyer's ring is a ring of lymphoid tissue.

anatomy.q: What is the seminal vesicle dose when included in prostate CTV?
anatomy.a: Typically receives 45-50 Gy for elective coverage
c: Typically receives 25-30 Gy for elective coverage
Typically receives 45-50 Gy for elective coverage
Typically receives 60-66 Gy for elective coverage
Typically receives 74-78 Gy for elective coverage
anatomy.e: Proximal 1-2 cm typically receive 45-50 Gy electively.

imrt.q: What is the fundamental difference between forward planning and inverse planning?
imrt.a: In inverse planning, the planner specifies desired dose objectives and the computer optimizes beam parameters
c: In forward planning, the computer automatically selects beam angles
Inverse planning does not use MLC during the planning stage based on protocol requirements
In inverse planning, the planner specifies desired dose objectives and the computer optimizes beam parameters
Forward planning always produces better plans than inverse planning
imrt.e: Inverse planning is the cornerstone of IMRT. The planner defines dose-volume objectives for targets and OARs, and an optimization algorithm iteratively adjusts fluence maps to best meet those objectives.

imrt.q: What does a fluence map represent in IMRT planning?
imrt.a: The spatial distribution of beam intensity across each treatment field
c: The dose distribution within the patient in radiation therapy practice
The MLC leaf positions during treatment for quality assurance purposes
The monitor units for each segment in clinical practice settings
The spatial distribution of beam intensity across each treatment field
imrt.e: A fluence map describes the desired intensity pattern for each beam. After optimization, the fluence map is converted into deliverable MLC leaf sequences (leaf sequencing).

imrt.q: In step-and-shoot IMRT, how is the modulated beam delivered?
imrt.a: The beam is turned off while MLC leaves move to new positions, then turned on to deliver each segment
c: The MLC leaves move continuously while the beam is on within the treatment system
The gantry rotates continuously during delivery for radiation dose management
Jaws are used instead of MLC to modulate the beam for the treatment procedure
The beam is turned off while MLC leaves move to new positions, then turned on to deliver each segment
imrt.e: Step-and-shoot (segmental) IMRT delivers discrete segments sequentially. The beam is off during MLC transitions and on during segment irradiation, resulting in a series of uniform-intensity subfields.

imrt.q: In sliding window (dynamic) IMRT, what characterizes the delivery?
imrt.a: Opposing MLC leaf pairs move at different speeds while the beam remains on continuously
c: The gantry rotates while leaves are static throughout the treatment course
Only one leaf bank moves during delivery throughout the treatment course
Opposing MLC leaf pairs move at different speeds while the beam remains on continuously
The beam is pulsed on and off rapidly in clinical practice settings
imrt.e: In sliding window IMRT, each pair of opposing leaves sweeps across the field at varying speeds. The gap between opposing leaves and their relative velocities modulate the fluence profile.

imrt.q: What three parameters vary simultaneously during VMAT delivery?
imrt.a: Gantry rotation speed, dose rate, and MLC leaf positions
c: Couch position, collimator angle, and dose rate
Jaw positions, energy, and gantry speed
Beam energy, couch angle, and MLC positions
Gantry rotation speed, dose rate, and MLC leaf positions
imrt.e: VMAT simultaneously modulates gantry speed, dose rate, and MLC positions during continuous gantry rotation, enabling highly conformal treatments with fewer MUs and shorter delivery times compared to fixed-field IMRT.

imrt.q: What is Varian's commercial implementation of VMAT called?
imrt.a: RapidArc
c: SmartArc
VMAT Pro
RapidArc
HybridArc
imrt.e: RapidArc is Varian's proprietary VMAT solution. Elekta's equivalent is called VMAT (sometimes referred to with SmartArc optimization in Pinnacle TPS).

imrt.q: What is a key advantage of dual-arc VMAT over single-arc VMAT?
imrt.a: Greater modulation capability for complex target geometries and improved dose conformity
c: Reduced treatment time compared to single-arc in radiation therapy practice
Lower total monitor units in clinical practice settings
Greater modulation capability for complex target geometries and improved dose conformity
Simpler quality assurance requirements per standard operating procedures
imrt.e: Dual-arc VMAT provides additional degrees of freedom for modulation, which is beneficial for complex cases such as head and neck treatments with multiple dose levels. The trade-off is longer treatment time.

imrt.q: Which optimization algorithm uses a stochastic approach to avoid local minima?
imrt.a: Simulated annealing
c: Gradient descent
Conjugate gradient
Newton's method
Simulated annealing
imrt.e: Simulated annealing is a stochastic optimization method that can accept worse solutions with a probability that decreases over time (cooling schedule), allowing it to escape local minima that trap deterministic gradient-based methods.

imrt.q: What is the primary disadvantage of gradient-based optimization algorithms in IMRT?
imrt.a: They can become trapped in local minima
c: They require excessively long computation times
They can become trapped in local minima
They cannot handle dose-volume constraints
They only work with step-and-shoot delivery
imrt.e: Gradient-based methods follow the steepest descent direction and converge efficiently, but the IMRT optimization problem is non-convex, so these methods may converge to a local minimum rather than the global optimum.

imrt.q: In multi-criteria optimization (MCO), what does the Pareto surface represent?
imrt.a: The set of plans where no objective can be improved without worsening another
c: The maximum achievable dose to the target within the treatment system
The set of plans where no objective can be improved without worsening another
Plans that meet all hard constraints according to clinical protocols
The optimal beam angle configuration according to clinical protocols
imrt.e: The Pareto surface (or Pareto front) represents the set of non-dominated solutions. For any plan on this surface, improving one objective necessarily degrades at least one other objective. MCO allows the planner to navigate this surface interactively.

imrt.q: What is the role of priority or weight values in IMRT objective functions?
imrt.a: They determine the relative importance of each optimization objective
c: They set absolute dose limits that cannot be exceeded
They control the number of iterations in optimization
They determine the relative importance of each optimization objective
They define the number of beams to use
imrt.e: Weights (priorities) in the objective function control the trade-off between competing objectives. Higher weights make the optimizer work harder to satisfy that particular objective, though the final result depends on the interplay of all weights.

imrt.q: What is the typical MLC leaf width at isocentre for standard IMRT treatments?
imrt.a: 5 mm
c: 2.5 mm
10 mm
5 mm
20 mm
imrt.e: Most modern MLCs (e.g., Varian Millennium 120-leaf MLC) have 5 mm leaf width at isocentre in the central region (inner 20 cm) and 10 mm in the outer region. High-definition MLCs use 2.5 mm centrally.

imrt.q: What is the tongue-and-groove effect in MLC-based IMRT?
imrt.a: An underdose pattern at the junction between adjacent MLC leaves due to their interlocking design
c: Overdosing at the leaf ends due to rounding per standard operating procedures
Increased penumbra caused by leaf transmission for clinical implementation
Dose variation due to leaf speed limitations per standard operating procedures
An underdose pattern at the junction between adjacent MLC leaves due to their interlocking design
imrt.e: MLC leaves interlock via a tongue-and-groove design to minimize interleaf leakage. When adjacent leaves define different field edges in separate segments, the overlap region receives less dose, creating characteristic underdose lines.

imrt.q: What is the typical interleaf leakage for modern MLCs?
imrt.a: Approximately 1-2%
c: Less than 0.1%
Approximately 5-10%
Approximately 1-2%
Approximately 15-20%
imrt.e: Modern MLC designs with tongue-and-groove interlocking achieve interleaf leakage of approximately 1-2%. This leakage contributes to dose outside the intended field and is particularly important for highly modulated IMRT plans.

imrt.q: Why do rounded MLC leaf ends affect IMRT dosimetry?
imrt.a: They shift the effective radiation field edge from the geometric leaf tip position, requiring a dosimetric leaf gap correction
c: They increase interleaf leakage per standard operating procedures for therapeutic applications
They eliminate the tongue-and-groove effect for accurate dose delivery according to clinical protocols
They shift the effective radiation field edge from the geometric leaf tip position, requiring a dosimetric leaf gap correction
They cause higher leaf transmission for accurate dose delivery in the radiation therapy context
imrt.e: Rounded leaf ends are designed to maintain a constant penumbra width regardless of leaf position, but they cause the 50% dose line to shift from the geometric leaf tip. A dosimetric leaf gap (DLG) correction accounts for this shift in dose calculations.

imrt.q: TG-119 is primarily associated with which activity?
imrt.a: IMRT commissioning benchmarking
c: Annual linac QA
Electron beam calibration
IMRT commissioning benchmarking
Brachytherapy source verification
imrt.e: AAPM TG-119 provides benchmark plans and expected dosimetric results for IMRT commissioning. It includes test cases (prostate, head-and-neck, C-shape, multi-target) to verify that a clinic's IMRT planning and delivery system produces acceptable results.

imrt.q: Which of the following is NOT a TG-119 benchmark test case?
imrt.a: Lung SBRT
c: Prostate
Head and neck
C-shape
Lung SBRT
imrt.e: TG-119 includes four test cases: prostate, head-and-neck, C-shape (to test concave dose distributions), and multi-target. Lung SBRT is not one of the TG-119 benchmark cases.

imrt.q: What does the gamma analysis metric evaluate in IMRT QA?
imrt.a: Combined dose difference and distance-to-agreement between measured and calculated dose distributions
c: Only the absolute dose difference at each point within the treatment system
Combined dose difference and distance-to-agreement between measured and calculated dose distributions
Only the spatial shift between measured and planned distributions
The uniformity of the delivered dose within the treatment system
imrt.e: Gamma analysis combines dose difference (DD) and distance-to-agreement (DTA) criteria into a single metric. A point passes if gamma <= 1, meaning it satisfies the combined criteria. Common criteria are 3%/3mm or 2%/2mm.

imrt.q: What is the most commonly used gamma analysis criteria for routine IMRT QA?
imrt.e: 3% dose difference / 3 mm distance-to-agreement is the most widely used clinical criteria for routine IMRT patient-specific QA, with a typical passing rate threshold of 95%.

imrt.q: What is the difference between global and local gamma analysis?
imrt.a: Global normalizes dose difference to the maximum dose, while local normalizes to the dose at each evaluation point
c: Global uses a larger DTA criterion than local for radiation dose management
Global evaluates only the target region while local evaluates the entire field
Global normalizes dose difference to the maximum dose, while local normalizes to the dose at each evaluation point
There is no clinically significant difference for therapeutic applications
imrt.e: In global gamma, the dose difference criterion is a percentage of the global maximum (or prescription) dose. In local gamma, it is a percentage of the local dose at each point, making local gamma much more sensitive in low-dose regions.

imrt.q: What is the typical minimum acceptable gamma passing rate for clinical IMRT QA using 3%/3mm criteria?
imrt.e: A gamma passing rate of >= 95% with 3%/3mm global criteria is the widely accepted clinical threshold for IMRT patient-specific QA, as recommended by TG-218.

imrt.q: Which device uses a cylindrical diode array for 3D IMRT/VMAT QA?
imrt.a: ArcCHECK
c: MapCHECK
MatriXX
ArcCHECK
Farmer chamber
imrt.e: ArcCHECK (Sun Nuclear) is a cylindrical phantom with 1386 diode detectors arranged in a helical pattern, specifically designed for rotational therapy (VMAT) verification with 3D dose reconstruction capability.

imrt.q: What type of detector array does MapCHECK use?
imrt.a: 2D diode array
c: Ionization chamber array
Film
Diamond detectors
2D diode array
imrt.e: MapCHECK (Sun Nuclear) uses a planar array of 1527 silicon diode detectors for 2D dose verification. It is commonly used for patient-specific IMRT QA with composite or per-field measurements.

imrt.q: What is the Delta4 system used for in radiation therapy?
imrt.a: 3D pre-treatment IMRT/VMAT verification using two orthogonal diode arrays
c: Patient positioning verification for the treatment procedure
MLC calibration per standard operating procedures
3D pre-treatment IMRT/VMAT verification using two orthogonal diode arrays
Beam energy verification as part of the clinical workflow
imrt.e: The Delta4 phantom (ScandiDos) contains two orthogonal planar diode arrays that measure dose in a cross configuration, allowing 3D dose reconstruction for IMRT/VMAT plan verification.

imrt.q: How is EPID-based dosimetry used for IMRT QA?
imrt.a: The electronic portal imaging device measures exit fluence which is back-projected to reconstruct dose in the patient or phantom
c: It directly measures dose at the isocentre for quality assurance purposes for therapeutic applications
The electronic portal imaging device measures exit fluence which is back-projected to reconstruct dose in the patient or phantom
It replaces the need for ion chamber measurements in radiation therapy practice
It only measures the beam profile shape during the planning stage for therapeutic applications
imrt.e: EPID dosimetry uses the amorphous silicon flat panel detector to measure transmitted fluence during or before treatment. Sophisticated algorithms convert the EPID signal to dose, enabling either pre-treatment or in-vivo verification.

imrt.q: What is the conformity index (CI) as defined by RTOG?
imrt.a: The ratio of the prescription isodose volume to the target volume
c: The ratio of maximum dose to prescription dose
The ratio of target volume receiving 95% dose to total target volume
The ratio of mean dose to prescription dose
The ratio of the prescription isodose volume to the target volume
imrt.e: RTOG defines CI as the ratio of the volume of the prescription isodose (V_RI) to the planning target volume (V_PTV). An ideal CI = 1.0. Values > 1 indicate the prescription isodose extends beyond the PTV.

imrt.q: What does the homogeneity index (HI) measure in treatment planning?
imrt.a: The uniformity of dose within the target volume
c: The conformity of the prescription isodose to the target shape
The steepness of dose fall-off outside the target
The uniformity of dose within the target volume
The ratio of integral dose to target dose
imrt.e: HI quantifies dose uniformity within the PTV. A common definition is HI = (D2% - D98%) / D50%. Lower values indicate more homogeneous dose distributions. ICRU 83 recommends reporting D2%, D98%, and D50%.

imrt.q: What does the gradient index (GI) quantify?
imrt.a: The steepness of dose fall-off beyond the target volume
c: The uniformity of dose inside the target
The steepness of dose fall-off beyond the target volume
The conformity of isodose lines to the target
The ratio of maximum to minimum target dose
imrt.e: The gradient index is defined as the ratio of the volume receiving 50% of the prescription dose to the volume receiving the prescription dose (V50%/V100%). A lower GI indicates steeper dose fall-off, which is desirable for sparing adjacent normal tissue.

imrt.q: What is simultaneous integrated boost (SIB)?
imrt.a: Delivering different dose levels to different target volumes within the same treatment fraction
c: A sequential boost delivered immediately after the initial plan
Using two different beam energies in the same field
Delivering different dose levels to different target volumes within the same treatment fraction
Boosting dose rate during the last portion of each fraction
imrt.e: SIB allows different dose prescriptions to be delivered to different target volumes (e.g., gross disease vs. elective nodal regions) simultaneously in each fraction. This is a major advantage of IMRT/VMAT over 3D-CRT.

imrt.q: What is the interplay effect in IMRT?
imrt.a: The dosimetric error caused by the interaction between MLC leaf motion and organ/tumor motion due to respiration
c: The interference pattern between opposing beam pairs for radiation dose management
The dosimetric error caused by the interaction between MLC leaf motion and organ/tumor motion due to respiration
The interaction between gantry rotation and couch movement according to clinical protocols
The effect of tongue-and-groove on adjacent leaf pairs for accurate dose delivery
imrt.e: The interplay effect occurs when the dynamic motion of MLC leaves during IMRT delivery interacts with respiratory tumor motion, creating hot and cold spots. It is particularly significant for single-fraction deliveries but tends to average out over multiple fractions.

imrt.q: Which technique helps mitigate the interplay effect in IMRT for lung tumors?
imrt.a: Respiratory gating or breath-hold techniques
c: Increasing the number of beams
Using higher energy photons
Respiratory gating or breath-hold techniques
Reducing the prescription dose
imrt.e: Respiratory gating limits beam delivery to specific phases of the breathing cycle, reducing tumor motion during irradiation. Other mitigation strategies include breath-hold, abdominal compression, and using more fractions to average out the interplay effect.

imrt.q: What is robust optimization in radiation therapy planning?
imrt.a: An optimization approach that accounts for uncertainties (setup, range, motion) to produce plans less sensitive to errors
c: Using the maximum number of beams for every plan for radiation dose management
Optimizing with the highest possible grid resolution in the treatment process
An optimization approach that accounts for uncertainties (setup, range, motion) to produce plans less sensitive to errors
Planning with the largest possible margins during the planning stage in radiation therapy practice
imrt.e: Robust optimization explicitly includes setup and range uncertainties in the optimization process, creating plans that maintain acceptable dose distributions across a range of error scenarios rather than just the nominal scenario.

imrt.q: What is RapidPlan in the Eclipse treatment planning system?
imrt.a: A knowledge-based planning tool that uses a library of prior plans to predict achievable DVH objectives
c: An automatic beam angle selection tool as part of the clinical workflow
A knowledge-based planning tool that uses a library of prior plans to predict achievable DVH objectives
A dose calculation algorithm per standard operating procedures
A patient-specific QA module in clinical practice settings in clinical practice settings
imrt.e: RapidPlan uses machine learning on a database of high-quality prior plans to generate patient-specific DVH estimates based on anatomical features. These predictions guide optimization objectives, improving plan quality and consistency.

imrt.q: What is the modulation complexity score (MCS)?
imrt.a: A metric that quantifies the complexity of an IMRT plan based on MLC leaf positions and segment areas
c: The ratio of total MU to prescription dose within the treatment system
A metric that quantifies the complexity of an IMRT plan based on MLC leaf positions and segment areas
A measure of the number of beams used in the radiation therapy context
The standard deviation of leaf speeds during delivery for accurate dose delivery
imrt.e: MCS ranges from 0 (most complex) to 1 (least complex/open field). It considers leaf position variability and area variability between adjacent segments. Lower MCS values correlate with higher plan complexity and potentially lower QA passing rates.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT plan has a gamma passing rate of 92% at 3%/3mm. The action limit per TG-218 is 95%. What should be done?
imrt.a: Investigate the cause of failure; the plan should not be treated without resolution
c: Treat the patient since 92% is close enough to 95%
Investigate the cause of failure; the plan should not be treated without resolution
Increase the gamma criteria to 5%/5mm to improve the passing rate
Reduce the prescription dose by 3%
imrt.e: TG-218 recommends an action limit of 95% for 3%/3mm global gamma. A result below this requires investigation (e.g., checking for setup errors, recalculation, replanning) before the plan can be clinically used.

imrt.q: CALC: If the prescription dose is 200 cGy and gamma analysis uses 3% global dose difference, what is the dose difference threshold?
imrt.a: 6 cGy
c: 3 cGy
6 cGy
9 cGy
60 cGy
imrt.e: With global normalization, the dose difference criterion is 3% of the maximum (or prescription) dose: 200 cGy x 0.03 = 6 cGy. Any point within 6 cGy or 3 mm DTA passes.

imrt.q: For head and neck IMRT, how many beam angles are typically used?
imrt.a: 7-9 beams
c: 3-4 beams
5-6 beams
7-9 beams
12-15 beams
imrt.e: Head and neck IMRT typically uses 7-9 coplanar beams to achieve adequate modulation for the complex concave target volumes wrapping around the spinal cord and parotid glands.

imrt.q: Which organ at risk is the primary dose-limiting structure in head and neck IMRT?
imrt.a: Spinal cord
c: Brain
Spinal cord
Mandible
Thyroid
imrt.e: The spinal cord is typically the primary dose-limiting OAR in head and neck IMRT, with a maximum dose constraint commonly set at 45-50 Gy. The brainstem and parotid glands are also critical OARs in these plans.

imrt.q: What is a key dosimetric goal for parotid gland sparing in head and neck IMRT?
imrt.a: Mean dose to at least one parotid gland < 26 Gy
c: Maximum dose to both parotids < 10 Gy
Mean dose to at least one parotid gland < 26 Gy
V50 to both parotids < 50%
No dose to parotid glands
imrt.e: QUANTEC guidelines recommend keeping the mean dose to at least one parotid gland below 20-26 Gy to preserve salivary function and reduce xerostomia. This is a key advantage of IMRT over conventional techniques for H&N.

imrt.q: For prostate IMRT, what is the typical number of beams used?
imrt.a: 5-7 beams
c: 2-3 beams
5-7 beams
9-11 beams
15 or more beams
imrt.e: Prostate IMRT typically uses 5-7 coplanar beams arranged equidistantly or with optimized angles to achieve conformal coverage while sparing the rectum and bladder.

imrt.q: What is the primary advantage of VMAT over fixed-field IMRT for prostate treatment?
imrt.a: Shorter treatment delivery time
c: Shorter treatment delivery time
Better target coverage
Lower integral dose
Reduced sensitivity to setup errors
imrt.e: VMAT can deliver a prostate treatment in 2-3 minutes compared to 10-15 minutes for 7-field IMRT, significantly reducing treatment time. Plan quality is generally comparable or slightly better with VMAT.

imrt.q: Which IMRT technique is most commonly used for whole breast irradiation?
imrt.a: Tangential field-in-field (forward-planned IMRT)
c: 9-field inverse-planned IMRT
Tangential field-in-field (forward-planned IMRT)
Single-arc VMAT as part of the clinical workflow
Electron beam IMRT in the radiation therapy context
imrt.e: Whole breast IMRT commonly uses forward-planned field-in-field technique with tangential beams. Subfields are created manually to reduce hot spots, improving dose homogeneity compared to standard wedged tangents.

imrt.q: What is VMAT-TBI?
imrt.a: Total body irradiation delivered using volumetric modulated arc therapy
c: A VMAT technique for brain tumors in the treatment process
Total body irradiation delivered using volumetric modulated arc therapy
A specialized VMAT for thyroid treatments in the radiation therapy context
A total breast irradiation VMAT technique for quality assurance purposes
imrt.e: VMAT-TBI uses multiple isocentre VMAT arcs to deliver total body irradiation, offering improved dose homogeneity and organ sparing compared to traditional extended-distance AP/PA TBI techniques.

imrt.q: What is a key challenge in VMAT-TBI compared to conventional TBI?
imrt.a: Field junction management between multiple isocentres
c: Insufficient dose rate for therapeutic applications
MLC leaf width too narrow during patient treatment sessions
Inability to achieve 12 Gy total dose
Field junction management between multiple isocentres
imrt.e: VMAT-TBI requires multiple isocentres to cover the entire body length, creating field junctions that must be carefully managed to avoid overdosing or underdosing at junction regions.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT plan delivers 180 cGy per fraction to the PTV. If a SIB plan delivers 200 cGy to the high-risk PTV and 180 cGy to the standard-risk PTV in the same fraction, what is the dose per fraction ratio between high and standard risk?
imrt.e: The ratio is 200/180 = 1.111. In SIB, different target volumes receive different fractional doses simultaneously, requiring careful consideration of the radiobiological implications of the altered fractionation.

imrt.q: What is the leaf sequencing step in IMRT planning?
imrt.a: Converting the optimized fluence map into deliverable MLC leaf positions and monitor units
c: Ordering the treatment beams by gantry angle per standard operating procedures
Selecting which MLC leaves to use for radiation dose management
Converting the optimized fluence map into deliverable MLC leaf positions and monitor units
Checking for collisions between gantry and couch in clinical practice settings
imrt.e: Leaf sequencing (or leaf segmentation for step-and-shoot) converts the ideal optimized fluence map into a series of deliverable MLC apertures with associated MUs. This step can introduce differences between the optimized and deliverable plans.

imrt.q: What is direct machine parameter optimization (DMPO)?
imrt.a: An optimization approach that directly optimizes MLC segments and MU rather than fluence maps
c: Optimizing machine output directly from beam data for the treatment procedure
Calibrating machine parameters based on optimization results
An optimization approach that directly optimizes MLC segments and MU rather than fluence maps
A QA technique for verifying machine settings within the treatment system
imrt.e: DMPO (also called direct aperture optimization) bypasses the fluence optimization and leaf sequencing steps by directly optimizing deliverable MLC apertures and weights. This eliminates degradation from leaf sequencing conversion.

imrt.q: What effect does increasing the number of IMRT segments have on plan quality?
imrt.a: Generally improves modulation and plan quality but increases delivery time and MU
c: Always worsens plan quality due to increased leakage
Has no effect on plan quality during patient treatment sessions
Generally improves modulation and plan quality but increases delivery time and MU
Reduces the conformity index within the treatment system
imrt.e: More segments allow finer fluence modulation, improving conformity and OAR sparing. However, more segments increase total MU (more leakage dose), delivery time, and potentially reduce QA passing rates due to small-field effects.

imrt.q: Why is MU verification important for IMRT plans?
imrt.a: To provide an independent check that the planned MU will deliver the correct dose
c: To provide an independent check that the planned MU will deliver the correct dose
It is required to calibrate the MLC for accurate dose delivery
To verify the beam energy throughout the treatment course
To check patient positioning for radiation dose management
imrt.e: Independent MU verification (second-check calculation) serves as a safety check against TPS errors. It uses a simplified calculation method to verify that the planned MU will deliver a dose within acceptable tolerance of the intended prescription.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT beam has a planned MU of 250. Independent MU verification calculates 245 MU. What is the percentage difference?
imrt.e: Percentage difference = (250 - 245) / 250 x 100% = 2.0%. This is within the typical 5% tolerance for IMRT independent MU verification, so the check passes.

imrt.q: What dose calculation algorithm limitation is most significant for IMRT in lung?
imrt.a: Inaccurate modeling of lateral electronic disequilibrium in low-density tissue
c: Inability to calculate dose in bone for accurate dose delivery
Inaccurate modeling of lateral electronic disequilibrium in low-density tissue
Overestimation of surface dose as specified by guidelines
Failure to account for beam divergence for accurate dose delivery
imrt.e: In lung tissue, lateral electronic disequilibrium occurs because secondary electrons travel farther in low-density media. Pencil beam algorithms handle this poorly; Monte Carlo or Acuros XB provide more accurate results for IMRT in lung.

imrt.q: Which dose calculation algorithm is most accurate for IMRT dose calculation in heterogeneous media?
imrt.a: Monte Carlo
c: Monte Carlo
Collapsed cone convolution
Analytical anisotropic algorithm (AAA)
Pencil beam convolution
imrt.e: Monte Carlo simulation tracks individual particle histories and explicitly models all physics interactions, providing the most accurate dose calculation in heterogeneous media, including air cavities and lung tissue.

imrt.q: What is the impact of small segments in IMRT on dose delivery accuracy?
imrt.a: Small segments are more sensitive to MLC positional errors, leaf transmission, and output factor uncertainties
c: Small segments deliver dose more accurately than large segments for accurate dose delivery
Small segments are more sensitive to MLC positional errors, leaf transmission, and output factor uncertainties
Small segments have no impact on accuracy throughout the treatment course
Small segments only affect step-and-shoot but not sliding window delivery
imrt.e: Small IMRT segments (<4 cm² equivalent square) are subject to increased dosimetric uncertainty from MLC position errors, output factor variations, and the breakdown of electronic equilibrium, potentially degrading delivery accuracy.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT segment has a field size equivalent to 2 cm x 2 cm. The output factor for this field size is 0.92 relative to the reference 10 x 10 cm² field. If the calibration output is 1 cGy/MU at 10 x 10 cm², what is the output per MU for this segment?
imrt.a: 0.92 cGy/MU
c: 0.80 cGy/MU
0.92 cGy/MU
1.00 cGy/MU
1.08 cGy/MU
imrt.e: Output = calibration output x output factor = 1.0 cGy/MU x 0.92 = 0.92 cGy/MU. Small field output factors are critical for accurate IMRT dose delivery, as many segments may be small.

imrt.q: What is a dose-volume histogram (DVH) constraint in IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: A specification that limits the volume of a structure receiving more or less than a certain dose
c: A specification that limits the volume of a structure receiving more or less than a certain dose
A limit on the maximum dose to any single point in clinical practice settings
A constraint on the total MU of the plan based on protocol requirements
A limit on the number of beams used based on protocol requirements
imrt.e: DVH constraints (e.g., V20 < 30% for lung, meaning no more than 30% of lung volume receives 20 Gy or more) guide the optimizer to produce clinically acceptable dose distributions. They can be hard constraints or soft objectives.

imrt.q: What is the difference between a hard constraint and a soft objective in IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: A hard constraint must be met and cannot be violated, while a soft objective is a goal that the optimizer tries to achieve but can compromise on
c: Hard constraints apply to targets and soft objectives apply to OARs as part of the clinical workflow
A hard constraint must be met and cannot be violated, while a soft objective is a goal that the optimizer tries to achieve but can compromise on
Hard constraints are always dose-based and soft objectives are volume-based for accurate dose delivery
There is no practical difference in modern optimization based on protocol requirements
imrt.e: Hard constraints define inviolable limits (e.g., spinal cord maximum < 50 Gy), while soft objectives have associated penalties that increase as the objective is violated. The optimizer minimizes the total penalty cost function.

imrt.q: What is the typical maximum dose rate used in VMAT delivery?
imrt.a: 600 MU/min
c: 100 MU/min
300 MU/min
600 MU/min
2400 MU/min
imrt.e: Standard VMAT delivery typically uses a maximum dose rate of 600 MU/min, though flattening-filter-free (FFF) beams can achieve much higher rates. The dose rate varies continuously during VMAT to achieve the desired modulation.

imrt.q: What is the maximum gantry rotation speed during VMAT delivery on most linacs?
imrt.a: Approximately 1 revolution per minute (6 degrees/second)
c: 0.5 revolutions per minute during the planning stage
Approximately 1 revolution per minute (6 degrees/second)
3 revolutions per minute as part of the clinical workflow
10 revolutions per minute during the planning stage
imrt.e: Most linacs have a maximum gantry speed of about 1 RPM (360 degrees/min or 6 degrees/second). The optimizer must account for this mechanical limitation when planning VMAT treatments.

imrt.q: In VMAT, what happens when the optimizer requires high modulation at a particular gantry angle?
imrt.a: The gantry slows down and/or dose rate increases at that angle
c: The beam is turned off at that angle in the healthcare environment
Additional arcs are automatically added
The gantry slows down and/or dose rate increases at that angle
The MLC aperture is widened during the planning stage
imrt.e: When more modulation is needed at a specific gantry angle, the optimizer can slow the gantry speed (increasing dwell time) and/or increase the dose rate to deliver more MU from that direction.

imrt.q: What is the control point spacing typically used in VMAT optimization?
imrt.a: 2-4 degrees
c: 0.5 degrees
2-4 degrees
10-15 degrees
30 degrees
imrt.e: VMAT plans typically use control points every 2-4 degrees of gantry rotation. Between control points, MLC positions, dose rate, and gantry speed are interpolated. Finer spacing provides more modulation capability but increases computation time.

imrt.q: CALC: A VMAT arc uses control points every 2 degrees over a full 360-degree rotation. How many control points are there?
imrt.e: Number of control points = 360/2 + 1 = 181 (including both start and end points at 0 and 360 degrees). Each control point defines MLC positions, cumulative MU, and gantry angle.

imrt.q: Which quality metric considers both conformity and dose to surrounding normal tissue?
imrt.a: Paddick conformity index
c: RTOG homogeneity index
Mean target dose
Paddick conformity index
imrt.e: The Paddick conformity index (also called the conformation number) equals (TV_PIV)² / (TV x PIV), where TV_PIV is target volume within the prescription isodose, TV is target volume, and PIV is prescription isodose volume. It penalizes both under-coverage and excess irradiated volume.

imrt.q: CALC: The prescription isodose volume is 150 cm³ and the PTV is 120 cm³. The PTV volume covered by the prescription isodose is 115 cm³. What is the Paddick conformity index?
imrt.e: Paddick CI = (TV_PIV)² / (TV x PIV) = (115)² / (120 x 150) = 13225 / 18000 = 0.735 ≈ 0.73. This accounts for both target coverage and conformity.

imrt.q: What is the RTOG protocol criterion for an acceptable conformity index?
imrt.a: CI between 1.0 and 2.0
c: CI between 0.5 and 1.0
CI between 1.0 and 2.0
imrt.e: RTOG defines CI = prescription isodose volume / target volume. A CI of 1.0 is ideal. Values between 1.0 and 2.0 indicate minor protocol deviation. Values outside this range indicate major deviation.

imrt.q: What does ICRU Report 83 recommend for dose reporting in IMRT?
imrt.a: Report D50% (median dose) as the IMRT prescription dose along with D2% and D98%
c: Report only the maximum dose point in radiation therapy practice
Report the dose at the isocentre in the radiation therapy context
Report D50% (median dose) as the IMRT prescription dose along with D2% and D98%
Report the mean dose to the PTV only according to clinical protocols
imrt.e: ICRU 83 specifically addresses dose reporting for IMRT/VMAT. It recommends D50% as a surrogate for the prescription dose, D2% as near-maximum dose, and D98% as near-minimum dose, replacing the older point-dose concepts.

imrt.q: What is a major concern with the increased low-dose bath in IMRT compared to 3D-CRT?
imrt.a: Potential increased risk of secondary malignancies
c: Increased skin dose throughout the treatment course
Potential increased risk of secondary malignancies
Higher acute toxicity in radiation therapy practice
Reduced target coverage throughout the treatment course
imrt.e: IMRT spreads low-dose radiation over larger normal tissue volumes compared to 3D-CRT. This increased integral dose raises theoretical concerns about secondary malignancy risk, particularly important for younger patients with long life expectancy.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT plan uses 7 beams with total MU of 1400 for a 200 cGy fraction. What is the average MU per beam?
imrt.a: 200 MU
c: 100 MU
200 MU
350 MU
700 MU
imrt.e: Average MU per beam = 1400 / 7 = 200 MU. In practice, individual beams vary significantly in MU depending on the modulation required from each direction.

imrt.q: CALC: A VMAT plan delivers 200 cGy per fraction with a total of 450 MU. A 7-field IMRT plan for the same patient uses 850 MU. What is the MU reduction with VMAT?
imrt.e: MU reduction = (850 - 450) / 850 x 100% = 400/850 x 100% = 47.1% ≈ 47%. Reduced MU means less leakage radiation and shorter treatment time.

imrt.q: What is the purpose of a Normal Tissue Objective (NTO) in IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: To control dose to unspecified normal tissue outside the target and named OARs
c: To increase dose to the target volume in the healthcare environment
To control dose to unspecified normal tissue outside the target and named OARs
To limit the maximum MU per beam for accurate dose delivery
To ensure uniform dose rate during delivery in the healthcare environment
imrt.e: NTO provides a global dose fall-off constraint for tissue not explicitly contoured as OARs. It helps reduce dose bath and integral dose by penalizing dose to all normal tissue beyond a specified fall-off distance from the target.

imrt.q: In what clinical scenario is IMRT clearly superior to 3D-CRT?
imrt.a: Head and neck cancers with concave target volumes wrapping around the spinal cord
c: Head and neck cancers with concave target volumes wrapping around the spinal cord
Simple parallel-opposed pair treatments during the planning stage
Single-field brain metastasis treatments per standard operating procedures
Emergency palliative spine treatments throughout the treatment course
imrt.e: IMRT excels when the target has a concave shape adjacent to critical OARs, as in head and neck where the PTV may wrap around the spinal cord. 3D-CRT cannot produce concave isodose distributions.

imrt.q: What is the collimator angle rotation strategy often used in VMAT?
imrt.a: Setting the collimator at a non-zero angle to reduce tongue-and-groove effect
c: Always keeping the collimator at 0 degrees
Rotating the collimator continuously during delivery
Setting the collimator to 90 degrees for all arcs
Setting the collimator at a non-zero angle to reduce tongue-and-groove effect
imrt.e: A small collimator rotation (e.g., 10-30 degrees) spreads the tongue-and-groove underdose lines across different positions, smearing out the effect. For dual-arc, complementary collimator angles further reduce this artifact.

imrt.q: What is aperture-based optimization in VMAT?
imrt.a: Optimization that directly adjusts MLC aperture shapes and weights at each control point
c: Optimization based only on jaw positions throughout the treatment course
Fluence-based optimization followed by leaf sequencing
Optimization using only circular collimators based on protocol requirements
Optimization that directly adjusts MLC aperture shapes and weights at each control point
imrt.e: Aperture-based optimization directly manipulates deliverable MLC apertures at each control point during VMAT optimization, avoiding the fluence-to-segments conversion step and ensuring the optimized plan is inherently deliverable.

imrt.q: What TG report provides guidelines for IMRT patient-specific QA tolerance limits and methodologies?
c: TG-51
imrt.e: AAPM TG-218 provides comprehensive recommendations for tolerance limits and methodologies for IMRT measurement-based verification QA, including universal tolerance and action limits for gamma analysis.

imrt.q: According to TG-218, what is the recommended tolerance limit for gamma passing rate at 3%/3mm?
imrt.a: 95% with an action limit and investigation triggered below this
c: 90% as specified by guidelines for quality assurance purposes
95% with an action limit and investigation triggered below this
97% within the treatment system as specified by guidelines
100% in radiation therapy practice for quality assurance purposes
imrt.e: TG-218 establishes 95% as the universal tolerance limit for 3%/3mm global gamma analysis. Plans failing below this require investigation and potential replanning before treatment.

imrt.q: What is the primary purpose of per-field IMRT QA versus composite QA?
imrt.a: Per-field QA allows identification of which specific beam has an error
c: Per-field QA is faster to perform
Per-field QA allows identification of which specific beam has an error
Composite QA is never clinically acceptable
Per-field QA provides higher spatial resolution
imrt.e: Per-field (per-beam) QA measures each beam individually, enabling isolation of beam-specific errors. Composite QA measures the cumulative dose from all beams, which can mask compensating errors between fields.

imrt.q: What is the recommended method for absolute point dose verification in IMRT QA?
imrt.a: Ion chamber measurement in a high-dose, low-gradient region
c: Film measurement at the beam edge in the radiation therapy context
Ion chamber measurement in a high-dose, low-gradient region
TLD measurement at the surface for the treatment procedure
Diode measurement at dmax during patient treatment sessions
imrt.e: An ion chamber placed in a high-dose, low-gradient region provides the most reliable absolute dose verification. The low gradient minimizes the impact of positional uncertainty on the measurement.

imrt.q: CALC: Ion chamber measurement for IMRT QA reads 195 cGy. The TPS predicts 200 cGy at the same point. What is the percentage difference?
imrt.e: Percentage difference = (195 - 200) / 200 x 100% = -2.5%. The negative sign indicates the measurement is lower than predicted. This is within the typical ±3% tolerance.

imrt.q: What is the typical tolerance for ion chamber point dose measurement in IMRT QA?
imrt.e: A tolerance of ±3% is widely accepted for ion chamber point dose measurements in IMRT QA when the chamber is placed in a high-dose, low-gradient region.

imrt.q: What causes the dose calculation accuracy to degrade for very small IMRT segments?
imrt.a: Loss of charged particle equilibrium and source occlusion effects
c: Increased photon scatter for therapeutic applications
Loss of charged particle equilibrium and source occlusion effects
Higher beam energy in small fields as specified by guidelines
Detector over-response in small fields during the planning stage
imrt.e: Very small segments (< 2x2 cm) may lack charged particle equilibrium, and the finite source size causes partial source occlusion. These effects are difficult for conventional dose algorithms to model accurately.

imrt.q: What is the advantage of using Acuros XB over AAA for IMRT dose calculations?
imrt.a: More accurate dose calculation in heterogeneous media, particularly near air-tissue interfaces
c: Faster calculation speed in the treatment process for therapeutic applications
Better handling of large field sizes in the healthcare environment
More accurate dose calculation in heterogeneous media, particularly near air-tissue interfaces
Simpler commissioning requirements throughout the treatment course
imrt.e: Acuros XB solves the linear Boltzmann transport equation, providing near-Monte Carlo accuracy for heterogeneous media. It is particularly superior to AAA near air cavities (e.g., sinuses in H&N) and in lung tissue.

imrt.q: What is the penumbra region and why is it important in IMRT?
imrt.a: The region of rapid dose fall-off at field edges; accurate modeling is critical for dose calculations in highly modulated plans
c: The central high-dose region of the beam according to clinical protocols during the planning stage
The region of rapid dose fall-off at field edges; accurate modeling is critical for dose calculations in highly modulated plans
The build-up region near the surface for radiation dose management within the treatment system
The region of maximum backscatter based on protocol requirements for quality assurance purposes
imrt.e: The penumbra region is where dose changes rapidly near field edges. In IMRT, with many small segments, a large fraction of the treatment area consists of penumbral regions, making accurate penumbra modeling essential for dose accuracy.

imrt.q: CALC: A homogeneity index is calculated as (D2% - D98%) / D50%. If D2% = 107%, D98% = 95%, and D50% = 100%, what is the HI?
imrt.e: HI = (D2% - D98%) / D50% = (107 - 95) / 100 = 12 / 100 = 0.12. A lower HI indicates better homogeneity. Values < 0.10 are generally considered good.

imrt.q: What does the term 'degeneracy' refer to in IMRT planning?
imrt.a: The existence of multiple different beam configurations that produce similar dose distributions
c: A plan that fails to meet objectives for the treatment procedure
The existence of multiple different beam configurations that produce similar dose distributions
The degradation of plan quality during delivery for clinical implementation
A beam with zero modulation during the planning stage in radiation therapy practice
imrt.e: IMRT plan degeneracy means many different fluence map solutions can produce nearly identical clinical dose distributions. This is why IMRT plans can vary between planners and planning systems while achieving similar plan quality.

imrt.q: What is the typical MLC leaf speed limit on modern linacs?
imrt.a: 2.5 cm/s
c: 0.5 cm/s
1.0 cm/s
2.5 cm/s
10 cm/s
imrt.e: Most modern linacs have a maximum MLC leaf speed of approximately 2.5 cm/s. This mechanical limit constrains the achievable modulation in sliding window IMRT and VMAT, particularly for highly modulated plans.

imrt.q: What is the effect of MLC leaf transmission on IMRT dose distributions?
imrt.a: It contributes additional dose beneath closed leaves, increasing dose to regions intended to be shielded
c: It has no measurable effect on clinical dose distributions for clinical implementation
It reduces dose to the target volume during patient treatment sessions
It contributes additional dose beneath closed leaves, increasing dose to regions intended to be shielded
It only affects the surface dose as part of the clinical workflow
imrt.e: MLC leaf transmission (typically 1.5-2%) allows radiation through closed leaves. In IMRT with many segments and high MU, this cumulative transmitted dose can be clinically significant, particularly for OARs that are always beneath closed leaves.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT plan has a total of 1200 MU and 1.8% MLC transmission. Approximately how many MU-equivalent of transmission dose is delivered?
c: 2.2 MU
216 MU
imrt.e: Transmission MU equivalent = 1200 x 0.018 = 21.6 MU. This represents the additional dose beneath closed MLC leaves, which contributes to integral dose and dose to OARs.

imrt.q: Why is collimator jaw tracking important in IMRT delivery?
imrt.a: The jaws follow the MLC aperture to reduce transmission and leakage through MLC leaves
c: It increases the maximum field size for therapeutic applications
The jaws follow the MLC aperture to reduce transmission and leakage through MLC leaves
It improves the penumbra sharpness for radiation dose management
It allows faster gantry rotation during patient treatment sessions
imrt.e: Jaw tracking (dynamic jaw tracking) moves the jaws to closely follow the active MLC aperture, reducing the area of closed MLC leaves exposed to radiation. This minimizes transmission and leakage dose, reducing integral dose.

imrt.q: What is the Varian Millennium 120-leaf MLC configuration?
imrt.a: 40 pairs of 5 mm leaves centrally and 20 pairs of 10 mm leaves on each outer side
c: 120 pairs of 5 mm leaves in the healthcare environment
60 pairs of 5 mm leaves across the entire field based on protocol requirements
40 pairs of 5 mm leaves centrally and 20 pairs of 10 mm leaves on each outer side
All 120 leaves at 2.5 mm width in the treatment process
imrt.e: The Millennium 120 MLC has 60 leaf pairs per bank: 40 inner pairs with 5 mm width (covering central 20 cm) and 20 outer pairs with 10 mm width (covering 10 cm on each side), projecting at isocentre.

imrt.q: What leaf width does the Varian HD120 MLC use in the central region?
imrt.a: 2.5 mm
c: 2.5 mm
3.0 mm
5.0 mm
imrt.e: The HD120 MLC has 32 pairs of 2.5 mm leaves centrally (covering the inner 8 cm) and 28 pairs of 5 mm leaves on each side, offering higher spatial resolution for SRS/SRT applications.

imrt.q: What is auto-planning in radiation therapy?
imrt.a: An automated system that iteratively adjusts optimization parameters to achieve clinical goals without manual intervention
c: Automatic patient positioning using CBCT for radiation dose management during patient treatment sessions
An automated system that iteratively adjusts optimization parameters to achieve clinical goals without manual intervention
Automated beam angle selection only according to clinical protocols for the treatment procedure
Automatic contouring of target volumes for quality assurance purposes according to clinical protocols
imrt.e: Auto-planning (e.g., Pinnacle Auto-Planning, Eclipse auto-planning) uses iterative algorithms to automatically adjust optimization objectives based on achievability, mimicking experienced planner behavior and reducing inter-planner variability.

imrt.q: CALC: A 3%/3mm gamma analysis yields 97% passing. A 2%/2mm analysis of the same plan yields 88%. Why is the difference so large?
imrt.a: Tighter criteria (2%/2mm) are more sensitive to small dose and spatial discrepancies
c: The 2%/2mm analysis has a calculation error
Tighter criteria (2%/2mm) are more sensitive to small dose and spatial discrepancies
The 3%/3mm result is always inaccurate
2%/2mm and 3%/3mm results should always be within 2% of each other
imrt.e: Reducing the gamma criteria from 3%/3mm to 2%/2mm dramatically increases sensitivity to dose differences and spatial offsets. The tighter criteria capture discrepancies that the wider criteria tolerate, so large drops in passing rate are expected.

imrt.q: What is the primary advantage of EPID-based IMRT QA over array-based QA?
imrt.a: Higher spatial resolution for detecting errors
c: Lower cost of equipment according to clinical protocols
Faster setup time for radiation dose management
Higher spatial resolution for detecting errors
No need for phantom for the treatment procedure
imrt.e: EPIDs have pixel sizes of approximately 0.4 mm, providing much higher spatial resolution than diode or ion chamber arrays (typically 5-10 mm spacing). This allows detection of small-scale delivery errors that arrays may miss.

imrt.q: What is log file-based IMRT QA?
imrt.a: Verification of delivery accuracy by analyzing MLC position, gantry angle, and MU data recorded by the linac during treatment
c: A method of documenting patient treatments for quality assurance purposes during patient treatment sessions
Verification of delivery accuracy by analyzing MLC position, gantry angle, and MU data recorded by the linac during treatment
Recording patient complaints during treatment in the healthcare environment
A method for tracking linac downtime within the treatment system as specified by guidelines
imrt.e: Machine log files record actual delivery parameters (MLC positions, gantry angle, dose rate, MU) at high temporal resolution. These can be used to reconstruct the delivered dose for comparison with the planned dose, providing an alternative to measurement-based QA.

imrt.q: What is a limitation of planar dose measurement for VMAT QA?
imrt.a: It samples dose in only one plane, potentially missing clinically relevant 3D dose errors
c: It cannot measure absolute dose within the treatment system
It samples dose in only one plane, potentially missing clinically relevant 3D dose errors
It is incompatible with rotational delivery for quality assurance purposes
It requires more time than 3D measurements based on protocol requirements
imrt.e: Planar measurements collapse the 3D dose distribution onto a 2D plane, which can mask 3D errors. A plan may pass 2D gamma analysis while having clinically significant dose errors in the 3D patient geometry.

imrt.q: What is the concept of dose painting in IMRT?
imrt.a: Prescribing heterogeneous dose distributions within the target based on functional imaging data
c: Manually drawing isodose lines on planning images in clinical practice settings
Using contrast agents to visualize the target during treatment
Prescribing heterogeneous dose distributions within the target based on functional imaging data
Painting fiducial markers on the patient's skin for quality assurance purposes
imrt.e: Dose painting uses functional imaging (e.g., PET-based FDG or FMISO uptake) to identify subregions of varying radioresistance within a tumor. IMRT can then deliver higher doses to resistant subvolumes, potentially improving local control.

imrt.q: What is the typical beam energy used for IMRT/VMAT treatments?
imrt.a: 6 MV
c: 6 MV
4 MV
15 MV
18 MV
imrt.e: 6 MV is the most commonly used energy for IMRT/VMAT because it offers good tissue penetration with sharper penumbra and lower neutron contamination compared to higher energies. Higher energies may be considered for deep-seated large targets.

imrt.q: Why are higher beam energies (>10 MV) generally avoided for IMRT?
imrt.a: Wider penumbra, higher neutron contamination, and larger out-of-field dose
c: They cannot produce modulated beams for accurate dose delivery
Higher energies have lower output during the planning stage
Wider penumbra, higher neutron contamination, and larger out-of-field dose
They are incompatible with MLC delivery per standard operating procedures
imrt.e: Higher energies produce wider penumbra (blurring dose gradients), generate photoneutrons above 8-10 MV (additional patient dose), and have larger out-of-field dose. Since IMRT achieves conformity through modulation rather than penetration, 6 MV is usually preferred.

imrt.q: What is the Elekta Agility MLC leaf width at isocentre?
imrt.a: 5 mm for all 160 leaves
c: 2.5 mm for all leaves
5 mm for all 160 leaves
10 mm for central leaves and 5 mm for outer leaves
Variable width from 3 mm to 10 mm
imrt.e: The Elekta Agility MLC has 160 leaves (80 pairs) all with a uniform 5 mm width at isocentre, covering a 40 cm x 40 cm field. Its design features fast leaf speed (up to 3.5 cm/s) and low transmission.

imrt.q: What is the main advantage of the Agility MLC over older Elekta MLCs?
imrt.a: Faster leaf speed and dynamic leaf guide enabling rapid VMAT delivery
c: Smaller leaf width for quality assurance purposes
Fewer total leaves for accurate dose delivery
Faster leaf speed and dynamic leaf guide enabling rapid VMAT delivery
Higher leaf transmission in the treatment process
imrt.e: The Agility MLC features faster leaf speeds (up to 3.5 cm/s) and a dynamic leaf guide that moves to minimize the gap between leaves and guide, reducing transmission and enabling highly modulated VMAT delivery.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT plan has a conformity index (RTOG definition) of 1.5 for a PTV of 200 cm³. What is the volume of the prescription isodose?
imrt.a: 300 cm³
c: 133 cm³
200 cm³
300 cm³
400 cm³
imrt.e: CI = V_RI / V_PTV, so V_RI = CI x V_PTV = 1.5 x 200 = 300 cm³. This means the prescription isodose encompasses 300 cm³, which is 100 cm³ more than the 200 cm³ PTV.

imrt.q: What is the purpose of a ring structure (planning help structure) in IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: To control dose fall-off gradient outside the PTV by placing dose objectives on a shell surrounding the target
c: To define the target volume more accurately as specified by guidelines
To control dose fall-off gradient outside the PTV by placing dose objectives on a shell surrounding the target
To protect the couch from excessive dose for the treatment procedure
To calculate the bolus thickness needed for quality assurance purposes
imrt.e: Ring structures (dose fall-off shells) are created at specific distances from the PTV. Dose objectives placed on these rings control how rapidly dose falls off outside the target, improving conformity and sparing of nearby OARs.

imrt.q: What is a common fractionation scheme for head and neck SIB-IMRT?
imrt.a: 70 Gy in 35 fractions to gross disease, 56 Gy to high-risk, 54 Gy to low-risk nodes
c: 60 Gy in 30 fractions to all volumes as part of the clinical workflow
70 Gy in 35 fractions to gross disease, 56 Gy to high-risk, 54 Gy to low-risk nodes
50 Gy in 25 fractions to all volumes for therapeutic applications
80 Gy in 40 fractions to gross disease only for radiation dose management
imrt.e: A typical H&N SIB scheme delivers 70 Gy/35 fx (2 Gy/fx) to gross tumor, 63 Gy/35 fx (1.8 Gy/fx) or 56 Gy/35 fx (1.6 Gy/fx) to high-risk nodes, and 54.25 Gy/35 fx (1.55 Gy/fx) to elective nodal volumes.

imrt.q: CALC: In a SIB plan, the high-risk PTV receives 70 Gy in 35 fractions and the low-risk PTV receives 54.25 Gy in 35 fractions. What is the dose per fraction to the low-risk PTV?
imrt.e: Dose per fraction = 54.25 Gy / 35 fractions = 1.55 Gy/fraction. This lower dose per fraction to elective volumes provides a radiobiological advantage for late-responding normal tissues.

imrt.q: What is the primary disadvantage of step-and-shoot IMRT compared to sliding window IMRT?
imrt.a: Longer delivery time due to beam-off periods during MLC transitions
c: Lower plan quality per standard operating procedures
More MLC wear for radiation dose management
Longer delivery time due to beam-off periods during MLC transitions
Higher total MU in the treatment process
imrt.e: Step-and-shoot IMRT requires the beam to be turned off while MLC leaves reposition between segments. This dead time increases overall delivery time compared to sliding window IMRT where the beam remains on continuously.

imrt.q: What advantage does step-and-shoot IMRT have over sliding window IMRT?
imrt.a: Easier QA verification since each segment is a static field
c: Better dose conformity according to clinical protocols
Lower MU during the planning stage for the treatment procedure
Faster delivery time for radiation dose management
Easier QA verification since each segment is a static field
imrt.e: Step-and-shoot segments are essentially static fields, making individual segment verification straightforward. Each segment can be independently checked for MLC position accuracy, dose output, and field shape.

imrt.q: What is the typical number of segments per beam in step-and-shoot IMRT?
imrt.a: 5-15 segments per beam
c: 1-2 segments per beam
5-15 segments per beam
30-50 segments per beam
100+ segments per beam
imrt.e: Step-and-shoot IMRT typically uses 5-15 segments per beam, depending on complexity. More segments allow better approximation of the ideal fluence map but increase delivery time and MU.

imrt.q: What is the convergence error in IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: The difference between the optimized fluence-based dose and the final calculated dose after leaf sequencing and dose computation
c: The error in gantry angle positioning for accurate dose delivery for clinical implementation
The statistical error in Monte Carlo calculations throughout the treatment course
The difference between planned and measured dose for radiation dose management
The difference between the optimized fluence-based dose and the final calculated dose after leaf sequencing and dose computation
imrt.e: Convergence error arises because the optimization uses simplified dose models, and the final dose is calculated with the full algorithm after leaf sequencing. This discrepancy can lead to the final plan not meeting the optimization objectives.

imrt.q: How can convergence error in IMRT planning be reduced?
imrt.a: Using intermediate dose calculation during optimization and direct machine parameter optimization
c: Increasing the number of beams for the treatment procedure
Reducing the number of optimization iterations per standard operating procedures
Using intermediate dose calculation during optimization and direct machine parameter optimization
Using a higher energy beam for the treatment procedure for accurate dose delivery
imrt.e: Intermediate dose calculations during optimization update the dose using the full algorithm periodically, reducing the gap between optimization and final dose. DMPO eliminates the leaf sequencing step entirely, removing that source of convergence error.

imrt.q: What is volumetric repainting in the context of motion management for IMRT?
imrt.a: Delivering each portion of the dose distribution multiple times to average out motion effects
c: Painting different dose levels to different target volumes
Using multiple imaging modalities for contouring
Delivering each portion of the dose distribution multiple times to average out motion effects
Repeating the entire treatment course if motion is detected
imrt.e: Volumetric repainting delivers each segment or arc portion multiple times with reduced MU per pass. The statistical averaging of multiple deliveries reduces the interplay effect, producing a more uniform dose distribution despite respiratory motion.

imrt.q: CALC: A local gamma analysis at a point where the measured dose is 20 cGy uses 3%/3mm criteria. What is the dose difference threshold at this point?
imrt.a: 0.6 cGy
c: 0.2 cGy
0.6 cGy
3.0 cGy
6.0 cGy
imrt.e: For local gamma, the dose difference threshold is 3% of the local dose: 20 cGy x 0.03 = 0.6 cGy. This is much tighter than global gamma (where the threshold would be 3% of the max dose), making local gamma very sensitive in low-dose regions.

imrt.q: What is the dose threshold commonly applied in gamma analysis to exclude low-dose points?
imrt.a: 10% of the maximum dose
c: 1% of the maximum dose
5% of the maximum dose
10% of the maximum dose
50% of the maximum dose
imrt.e: A low-dose threshold of 10% of the maximum dose is commonly used to exclude low-dose points from gamma analysis. These points are often in regions of steep gradient or outside the treatment field where small spatial errors produce large percentage differences.

imrt.q: What is the purpose of plan complexity metrics in IMRT?
imrt.a: To predict deliverability and QA outcomes before measurement
c: To determine the optimal beam energy
To calculate the prescription dose
To select the appropriate detector for QA
To predict deliverability and QA outcomes before measurement
