imrt.e: Plan complexity metrics (MCS, plan modulation index, aperture irregularity) quantify how complex a plan is. Higher complexity correlates with lower QA passing rates and greater sensitivity to delivery errors, helping identify plans that may be problematic.

imrt.q: What is the plan delivery efficiency metric?
imrt.a: The ratio of MU to prescription dose, indicating how efficiently the plan delivers the intended dose
c: The ratio of treatment time to setup time during patient treatment sessions
The ratio of MU to prescription dose, indicating how efficiently the plan delivers the intended dose
The percentage of beam-on time versus total appointment time
The ratio of planned dose to measured dose for clinical implementation
imrt.e: Plan delivery efficiency (MU/cGy) indicates how many MU are needed per cGy of prescription dose. Lower values indicate more efficient delivery. Highly modulated plans have higher MU/cGy ratios due to increased modulation.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT plan uses 800 MU to deliver 200 cGy. What is the MU-to-dose ratio?
imrt.a: 4.0 MU/cGy
c: 0.25 MU/cGy
2.0 MU/cGy
4.0 MU/cGy
8.0 MU/cGy
imrt.e: MU/dose = 800 MU / 200 cGy = 4.0 MU/cGy. A typical 3D-CRT plan might have ~2 MU/cGy, so 4.0 indicates moderate IMRT modulation. Higher values indicate more modulation and more MLC leakage.

imrt.q: What is the fluence smoothing step in IMRT planning?
imrt.a: A post-optimization filter that reduces high-frequency fluence variations to improve deliverability
c: Averaging the dose over the target volume for accurate dose delivery
Smoothing the patient surface contour for clinical implementation
A post-optimization filter that reduces high-frequency fluence variations to improve deliverability
Filtering noise from CT images before planning within the treatment system
imrt.e: Fluence smoothing applies a low-pass filter to the optimized fluence map to remove fine modulation details that are difficult to deliver accurately. This improves deliverability and QA results at the potential cost of slightly reduced plan quality.

imrt.q: What is the effect of increasing optimization iterations in IMRT?
imrt.a: The objective function decreases but with diminishing returns, and the plan may become more complex
c: Plan quality always improves linearly in radiation therapy practice
The plan becomes simpler with more iterations according to clinical protocols
There is no effect beyond 50 iterations for clinical implementation
The objective function decreases but with diminishing returns, and the plan may become more complex
imrt.e: More iterations allow the optimizer to further reduce the objective function, but the improvement per iteration decreases. Excessive iterations can create unnecessarily complex fluence patterns that are difficult to deliver accurately.

imrt.q: What is the dose grid resolution typically used for IMRT final dose calculations?
imrt.a: 2.5 mm
c: 1.0 mm
2.5 mm
5.0 mm
imrt.e: A dose grid resolution of 2.5 mm is commonly used for final IMRT dose calculations, providing a good balance between accuracy and computation time. For SRS/SBRT, finer grids (1-2 mm) are recommended.

imrt.q: CALC: If the dose calculation grid is 2.5 mm and the PTV is 3 cm in diameter, approximately how many voxels span the PTV diameter?
imrt.e: Number of voxels = 30 mm / 2.5 mm = 12 voxels across the PTV diameter. This provides adequate spatial resolution for dose calculation in the target volume.

imrt.q: What is the minimum MLC segment area typically enforced during IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: 2-4 cm²
c: 0.5 cm²
2-4 cm²
10 cm²
25 cm²
imrt.e: A minimum segment area of 2-4 cm² is typically enforced to avoid very small segments where output factor uncertainty, charged particle disequilibrium, and MLC position errors significantly affect dose accuracy.

imrt.q: What is the minimum MU per segment typically enforced in step-and-shoot IMRT?
imrt.a: 2-5 MU
c: 0.5 MU
2-5 MU
50 MU
imrt.e: A minimum of 2-5 MU per segment is typically set to ensure reproducible delivery. Very low MU segments have poor dose delivery reproducibility due to linac start-up transients and dose rate variations.

imrt.q: What is the concept of equivalent uniform dose (EUD) in IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: A single dose value that, if delivered uniformly to a structure, would produce the same biological effect as the actual heterogeneous dose distribution
c: The average dose to a structure for radiation dose management per standard operating procedures
The maximum dose in a structure in the healthcare environment for the treatment procedure in radiation therapy practice
The dose at the centre of a structure as part of the clinical workflow as part of the clinical workflow
A single dose value that, if delivered uniformly to a structure, would produce the same biological effect as the actual heterogeneous dose distribution
imrt.e: EUD uses the generalized equivalent uniform dose formalism with parameter 'a' to weight the dose distribution. For serial organs (large 'a'), EUD approaches Dmax; for parallel organs (small 'a'), it approaches mean dose. EUD-based optimization can produce superior plans.

imrt.q: In EUD-based optimization, what 'a' parameter value is used for a serial organ like the spinal cord?
imrt.a: A large positive value (e.g., a = 20)
c: a = -20 per standard operating procedures
a = 0 in the treatment process
a = 1 as part of the clinical workflow
A large positive value (e.g., a = 20)
imrt.e: For serial organs, where maximum dose is the primary concern, a large positive 'a' value makes the EUD approximate the maximum dose. For a = 1, EUD equals mean dose. For targets, negative 'a' values emphasize cold spots.

imrt.q: What is the purpose of a dose-volume histogram (DVH) in IMRT plan evaluation?
imrt.a: To summarize the 3D dose distribution as a plot of volume versus dose for each structure
c: To summarize the 3D dose distribution as a plot of volume versus dose for each structure
To display the spatial dose distribution in 2D as specified by guidelines
To show the beam's eye view of the target per standard operating procedures
To calculate the number of MU needed in radiation therapy practice
imrt.e: DVHs compress the 3D dose distribution into a concise graphical format, showing what fraction of each structure receives at least a given dose (cumulative DVH) or a specific dose range (differential DVH). They are essential for IMRT plan evaluation.

imrt.q: What information is lost in a DVH compared to the full 3D dose distribution?
imrt.a: Spatial information about where dose is deposited within the structure
c: The total dose to the structure for quality assurance purposes
The maximum dose point as part of the clinical workflow
Spatial information about where dose is deposited within the structure
The volume of the structure according to clinical protocols
imrt.e: DVHs show the dose-volume relationship but not the spatial distribution. A hot spot near an OAR and one far from it would appear identical on the DVH. This is why isodose review remains essential alongside DVH analysis.

imrt.q: What is the typical treatment time for a single-arc VMAT delivery?
imrt.a: 1-3 minutes
c: 1-3 minutes
30 seconds
5-10 minutes
15-20 minutes
imrt.e: Single-arc VMAT typically completes delivery in 1-3 minutes, depending on the dose per fraction and modulation complexity. This is significantly faster than multi-field IMRT delivery.

imrt.q: What is the SmartArc optimization algorithm used in?
imrt.a: Philips Pinnacle treatment planning system
c: Varian Eclipse in the radiation therapy context
Philips Pinnacle treatment planning system
Elekta Monaco for accurate dose delivery
RayStation in clinical practice settings
imrt.e: SmartArc is the VMAT optimization algorithm in the Philips Pinnacle TPS. It progressively increases angular resolution during optimization to efficiently generate VMAT plans.

imrt.q: Which TPS uses Monte Carlo-based optimization for IMRT/VMAT?
imrt.a: Elekta Monaco
c: Varian Eclipse
Philips Pinnacle
Elekta Monaco
BrainLab iPlan
imrt.e: Monaco uses a Monte Carlo-based dose engine (XVMC) for both optimization and final dose calculation, providing highly accurate dose computation in heterogeneous media for IMRT/VMAT planning.

imrt.q: What is the biological cost function approach in IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: Optimization using biological models (TCP, NTCP) instead of or in addition to physical dose objectives
c: Calculating the financial cost of treatment in radiation therapy practice
Estimating the time needed for treatment within the treatment system
Optimization using biological models (TCP, NTCP) instead of or in addition to physical dose objectives
Determining the number of fractions needed for therapeutic applications
imrt.e: Biological optimization uses tumor control probability (TCP) and normal tissue complication probability (NTCP) models as objective functions, potentially producing more clinically relevant plans than purely physical dose-volume objectives.

imrt.q: CALC: A prostate VMAT plan achieves D95 = 76 Gy for an 80 Gy prescription. What percentage of the prescription does D95 represent?
imrt.e: D95 as percentage of prescription = 76/80 x 100% = 95%. This means 95% of the PTV receives at least 95% of the prescription dose, which is a common plan acceptability criterion.

imrt.q: What is the interplay between MLC resolution and IMRT plan quality?
imrt.a: Finer MLC leaf width allows better conformity for small targets and sharper dose gradients
c: Larger leaves always produce better IMRT plans
MLC leaf width has no impact on IMRT plans
Finer MLC leaf width allows better conformity for small targets and sharper dose gradients
Finer leaves increase the tongue-and-groove effect proportionally
imrt.e: Finer MLC leaves (e.g., 2.5 mm vs 5 mm) provide better spatial resolution for shaping the fluence map, resulting in improved conformity and steeper dose gradients, particularly beneficial for small or irregularly shaped targets.

imrt.q: What is the role of the flattening filter in IMRT beam delivery?
imrt.a: In standard IMRT, it produces a uniform fluence profile that is then modulated by the MLC
c: It is not used in IMRT for clinical implementation during patient treatment sessions
In standard IMRT, it produces a uniform fluence profile that is then modulated by the MLC
It shapes the beam to match the target contour in radiation therapy practice
It filters out low-energy photons only in radiation therapy practice
imrt.e: The flattening filter creates a uniform open-field profile. In IMRT, this uniform profile is then modulated by MLC leaf patterns. FFF beams can also be used for IMRT/VMAT, where the optimizer accounts for the non-uniform input profile.

imrt.q: What is the advantage of using FFF beams for VMAT?
imrt.a: Higher dose rate capability reducing treatment time, especially for hypofractionated treatments
c: Better beam flatness in the treatment process within the treatment system
Higher dose rate capability reducing treatment time, especially for hypofractionated treatments
Narrower penumbra at all field sizes in the radiation therapy context
Lower MLC transmission as specified by guidelines for quality assurance purposes
imrt.e: FFF beams can deliver dose rates 4-5 times higher than flattened beams (up to 2400 MU/min for 6 MV FFF). This is particularly advantageous for SBRT and hypofractionated VMAT treatments where large doses per fraction are needed.

imrt.q: What is partial arc VMAT?
imrt.a: A VMAT delivery that uses less than a full 360-degree rotation
c: A VMAT delivery that uses less than a full 360-degree rotation
Delivering VMAT in multiple sessions
Using only half of the MLC leaves
Treating only part of the target volume per arc
imrt.e: Partial arcs limit gantry rotation to specific angular ranges, which is useful when radiation from certain directions is undesirable (e.g., avoiding contralateral breast, or beam entry through prosthetics).

imrt.q: What is non-coplanar VMAT?
imrt.a: VMAT arcs delivered with the couch rotated to different angles, creating arcs in different planes
c: VMAT arcs with varying collimator angles only during the planning stage
VMAT with beams entering from only one hemisphere for quality assurance purposes
VMAT arcs delivered with the couch rotated to different angles, creating arcs in different planes
VMAT using multiple isocentres for clinical implementation
imrt.e: Non-coplanar VMAT uses couch rotations to create arcs in multiple planes, providing additional geometric degrees of freedom. This is particularly useful for brain targets where non-coplanar approaches can improve OAR sparing.

imrt.q: CALC: A VMAT plan has an arc length of 340 degrees with control points every 2 degrees. If the gantry completes this arc in 60 seconds at constant speed, what is the gantry rotation speed in degrees per second?
imrt.a: 5.67 degrees/second
c: 2.83 degrees/second
4.00 degrees/second
5.67 degrees/second
6.00 degrees/second
imrt.e: Gantry speed = 340 degrees / 60 seconds = 5.67 degrees/second. This is within the typical maximum speed of 6 degrees/second for most linacs.

imrt.q: What is the primary challenge in IMRT for lung cancer?
imrt.a: Respiratory motion causing interplay effects and target position uncertainty
c: Insufficient beam penetration based on protocol requirements
Excessive MLC leakage in the radiation therapy context
Respiratory motion causing interplay effects and target position uncertainty
Too many critical structures to spare for radiation dose management
imrt.e: Respiratory motion presents the primary challenge for lung IMRT: target position changes during delivery cause interplay effects with MLC motion, dose blurring, and potential geographic miss. Motion management strategies are essential.

imrt.q: What motion management approach uses a 4D-CT to create an internal target volume (ITV) for lung IMRT?
imrt.a: Encompassing all respiratory phases to account for tumor motion extent
c: Using only the exhale phase for planning
Encompassing all respiratory phases to account for tumor motion extent
Treating only during the inhale phase
Ignoring motion if amplitude is less than 1 cm
imrt.e: The ITV approach uses 4D-CT to determine the extent of tumor motion throughout the respiratory cycle. The ITV encompasses the GTV positions from all phases, ensuring the tumor is always within the treated volume without requiring gating.

imrt.q: What is the typical IMRT fractionation for definitive prostate radiation therapy?
imrt.a: 78-80 Gy in 39-40 fractions
c: 40 Gy in 20 fractions
60 Gy in 30 fractions
78-80 Gy in 39-40 fractions
90 Gy in 45 fractions
imrt.e: Conventional fractionation for prostate IMRT is typically 78-80 Gy at 2 Gy per fraction. Dose escalation beyond 70 Gy has shown improved biochemical control. Hypofractionated regimens (60 Gy/20 fx or 36.25 Gy/5 fx) are also used.

imrt.q: For prostate IMRT, what is the typical rectal dose constraint?
c: Mean dose < 30 Gy
Maximum dose < 60 Gy
V50 < 50% only
imrt.e: QUANTEC recommends rectal dose constraints such as V70 < 25%, V75 < 15% to keep the risk of Grade >= 2 rectal toxicity below 15%. These constraints are critical objectives in prostate IMRT optimization.

imrt.q: What is the advantage of IMRT over 3D-CRT for nasopharyngeal carcinoma?
imrt.a: Superior parotid gland sparing while maintaining target coverage for the horseshoe-shaped PTV
c: Faster treatment delivery as specified by guidelines in the treatment process
Simpler treatment planning according to clinical protocols
Superior parotid gland sparing while maintaining target coverage for the horseshoe-shaped PTV
No need for image guidance for quality assurance purposes
imrt.e: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma targets often wrap around the brainstem and are adjacent to parotid glands. IMRT's ability to create concave dose distributions provides dramatically superior parotid sparing and reduced xerostomia rates compared to 3D-CRT.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT plan delivers 50 Gy to 95% of the PTV. The D5% is 54 Gy. What is the maximum point dose if it is 3% higher than D5%?
imrt.e: Maximum dose = D5% x 1.03 = 54 x 1.03 = 55.62 Gy ≈ 55.6 Gy. This represents 111.2% of the 50 Gy prescription, which would be within typical RTOG guidelines.

imrt.q: What is the concept of 'class solution' in IMRT?
imrt.a: A standardized set of beam parameters and optimization templates for a specific treatment site
c: A solution used only for classified patients as part of the clinical workflow
A standardized set of beam parameters and optimization templates for a specific treatment site
A plan that meets all RTOG criteria throughout the treatment course
The optimal solution found by the optimizer for quality assurance purposes
imrt.e: Class solutions (site-specific templates) standardize beam numbers, angles, optimization objectives, and constraints for common treatment sites. They improve planning efficiency, consistency, and serve as a starting point for patient-specific optimization.

imrt.q: What is the leaf sequencing efficiency?
imrt.a: The ratio of the delivered fluence profile to the optimized fluence profile
c: The speed of MLC leaf movement for accurate dose delivery
The percentage of leaves used in each segment
The ratio of the delivered fluence profile to the optimized fluence profile
The time taken for leaf sequencing calculation
imrt.e: Leaf sequencing efficiency measures how well the deliverable MLC apertures approximate the ideal optimized fluence. Higher efficiency means less degradation from the leaf sequencing step. It depends on the sequencing algorithm and MLC characteristics.

imrt.q: What is the effect of couch attenuation on VMAT dose distributions?
imrt.a: The treatment couch attenuates beams passing through it, creating dose reduction for posterior and oblique beams
c: Couch attenuation has no effect on VMAT according to clinical protocols
The treatment couch attenuates beams passing through it, creating dose reduction for posterior and oblique beams
The couch increases dose due to backscatter in the healthcare environment
Couch effects only matter for electron beams per standard operating procedures
imrt.e: The treatment couch (typically carbon fiber) attenuates photon beams by 2-5% depending on beam angle and couch construction. For VMAT with 360-degree arcs, couch modeling in the TPS is essential to avoid dose calculation errors.

imrt.q: CALC: If the treatment couch attenuates a posterior beam by 3% and the planned dose from that direction is 40 cGy, what dose reduction occurs?
imrt.a: 1.2 cGy
c: 0.4 cGy
1.2 cGy
3.0 cGy
4.0 cGy
imrt.e: Dose reduction = 40 cGy x 0.03 = 1.2 cGy. While small for individual beams, cumulative couch attenuation across multiple posterior gantry angles in VMAT can be clinically significant if not accounted for.

imrt.q: What is the purpose of the Progressive Resolution Optimizer (PRO) in Eclipse?
imrt.a: A multi-resolution optimization algorithm that begins with coarse resolution and progressively refines for VMAT planning
c: An image registration tool for clinical implementation as specified by guidelines
A multi-resolution optimization algorithm that begins with coarse resolution and progressively refines for VMAT planning
A contouring algorithm per standard operating procedures in the healthcare environment
A beam angle selection tool based on protocol requirements according to clinical protocols
imrt.e: PRO starts optimization with large angular spacing and coarse fluence, progressively increasing angular resolution and fluence detail. This approach efficiently explores the solution space before refining, reducing computation time while maintaining plan quality.

imrt.q: What is the Photon Optimizer (PO) in Eclipse used for?
imrt.a: IMRT and VMAT optimization using dose-volume and EUD-based objectives
c: Photon beam calibration in the radiation therapy context
IMRT and VMAT optimization using dose-volume and EUD-based objectives
CT image reconstruction throughout the treatment course
Photon scatter calculation within the treatment system
imrt.e: The Photon Optimizer in Eclipse handles inverse optimization for both IMRT and VMAT plans, supporting upper/lower dose-volume objectives, mean dose objectives, EUD-based objectives, and normal tissue objectives.

imrt.q: What is the split-field technique in head and neck IMRT?
imrt.a: Matching IMRT fields superiorly with a conventional anterior lower neck field inferiorly
c: Splitting the target into left and right halves
Matching IMRT fields superiorly with a conventional anterior lower neck field inferiorly
Using two different energies in the same field
Dividing treatment into morning and afternoon sessions
imrt.e: The split-field technique uses IMRT for the primary and upper neck targets while matching a conventional anterior field for the lower neck/supraclavicular region. This simplifies planning but creates a field junction requiring careful management.

imrt.q: What is the advantage of extended-field IMRT over split-field techniques for H&N?
imrt.a: Eliminates field junctions and provides more uniform dose throughout the entire treatment volume
c: Faster treatment delivery for quality assurance purposes for accurate dose delivery
Fewer monitor units per standard operating procedures in the treatment process
Eliminates field junctions and provides more uniform dose throughout the entire treatment volume
Lower dose to the spinal cord based on protocol requirements
imrt.e: Extended-field IMRT treats the entire target volume (primary, upper neck, and lower neck) with a single IMRT plan, eliminating the junction line that can cause hot or cold spots in split-field techniques.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT plan has D2% = 52.5 Gy, D98% = 47.5 Gy, and D50% = 50 Gy. Calculate the homogeneity index per ICRU 83.
imrt.e: HI = (D2% - D98%) / D50% = (52.5 - 47.5) / 50 = 5/50 = 0.10. This indicates good homogeneity. ICRU 83 recommends this formulation as it uses near-max and near-min rather than absolute extremes.

imrt.q: What is the impact of incorrect heterogeneity corrections on IMRT dose calculations in the lung?
imrt.a: Dose to lung tumors can be overestimated by pencil beam algorithms compared to Monte Carlo
c: Heterogeneity corrections have minimal impact in lung for radiation dose management
All algorithms produce identical results in lung within the treatment system
Dose is always underestimated in lung tissue for clinical implementation
Dose to lung tumors can be overestimated by pencil beam algorithms compared to Monte Carlo
imrt.e: Pencil beam algorithms overestimate dose in and near lung tissue because they do not accurately account for lateral electron transport in low-density media. The overestimation can be 5-15% in some scenarios, making algorithm choice critical for lung IMRT.

imrt.q: What is the role of MLC model parameters in TPS commissioning for IMRT?
imrt.a: MLC parameters like DLG and transmission factor directly affect dose calculation accuracy for all IMRT plans
c: They only affect 3D-CRT plans per standard operating procedures during the planning stage
They are used only for machine calibration as specified by guidelines
They have no impact on clinical dose calculations in the healthcare environment
MLC parameters like DLG and transmission factor directly affect dose calculation accuracy for all IMRT plans
imrt.e: MLC model parameters (DLG, transmission, tongue-and-groove width) are critical inputs for the TPS dose calculation engine. Incorrect values lead to systematic dose errors in all IMRT/VMAT plans, making careful measurement and commissioning essential.

imrt.q: What commissioning test verifies the accuracy of IMRT dose calculation and delivery end-to-end?
imrt.a: End-to-end test using an anthropomorphic phantom with imaging, planning, and dose measurement
c: Output factor measurement only as specified by guidelines
MLC position accuracy test according to clinical protocols
End-to-end test using an anthropomorphic phantom with imaging, planning, and dose measurement
Beam profile scanning in water during patient treatment sessions
imrt.e: End-to-end testing involves CT scanning an anthropomorphic phantom, creating an IMRT plan, delivering it, and measuring the dose with detectors inside the phantom. This tests the entire chain from imaging through planning to delivery.

imrt.q: What is the purpose of the IROC (formerly RPC) credentialing phantom program?
imrt.a: To verify that institutions can accurately plan and deliver IMRT before enrolling patients in clinical trials
c: To calibrate linac output based on protocol requirements during patient treatment sessions
To verify that institutions can accurately plan and deliver IMRT before enrolling patients in clinical trials
To test MLC leaf position accuracy per standard operating procedures
To validate CT scanner performance as specified by guidelines in the radiation therapy context
imrt.e: IROC Houston sends anthropomorphic phantoms to institutions for end-to-end IMRT testing. The institution must CT scan, plan, deliver, and return the phantom. IROC analyzes the results to credential institutions for NCI-funded clinical trials.

imrt.q: What is the IROC head and neck phantom passing criterion for dose?
imrt.a: ±7% dose difference or 4 mm DTA
c: ±3%/3mm during patient treatment sessions
±7% dose difference or 4 mm DTA
±5%/3mm for radiation dose management
±10%/5mm for the treatment procedure
imrt.e: The IROC H&N phantom uses ±7% dose difference or 4 mm distance-to-agreement as passing criteria. Despite these seemingly generous criteria, approximately 20-30% of institutions have historically failed on their first attempt.

imrt.q: What is the primary concern with using very high modulation in IMRT plans?
imrt.a: Increased sensitivity to delivery errors, higher MLC leakage dose, and potentially lower QA passing rates
c: The plan will not converge within the treatment system per standard operating procedures
The beam energy will change in the radiation therapy context as part of the clinical workflow
Increased sensitivity to delivery errors, higher MLC leakage dose, and potentially lower QA passing rates
Patient setup becomes more difficult in the healthcare environment
imrt.e: Highly modulated plans have many small segments, steep fluence gradients, and high MU. These features increase sensitivity to MLC positional errors, contribute more leakage dose, and make plans harder to deliver accurately.

imrt.q: CALC: A plan complexity metric called the modulation factor (MF) is defined as total MU divided by MU for an equivalent open field. If the IMRT plan uses 950 MU and the equivalent open field would need 250 MU, what is the MF?
imrt.e: MF = 950 / 250 = 3.8. Higher modulation factors indicate more complex plans. Values above 3-4 suggest high modulation that warrants careful QA and review of plan deliverability.

imrt.q: What is adaptive IMRT?
imrt.a: Modifying the IMRT plan during the course of treatment based on anatomical or dosimetric changes
c: Adapting beam energy based on tumor size throughout the treatment course
Using adaptive optimization algorithms only during patient treatment sessions
Modifying the IMRT plan during the course of treatment based on anatomical or dosimetric changes
Planning IMRT with adaptive MLC leaves in the radiation therapy context
imrt.e: Adaptive IMRT involves replanning during treatment to account for tumor shrinkage, weight loss, or anatomical changes (common in H&N). It may use periodic reimaging and replanning to maintain optimal dose distributions throughout the course.

imrt.q: What is the effect of weight loss during head and neck IMRT?
imrt.a: Changes in external contour and tissue density alter dose distribution, potentially increasing dose to OARs like the parotid glands
c: Weight loss has no impact on dose distribution for radiation dose management in the treatment process
It always improves plan quality during patient treatment sessions during patient treatment sessions
Changes in external contour and tissue density alter dose distribution, potentially increasing dose to OARs like the parotid glands
It only affects skin dose within the treatment system per standard operating procedures
imrt.e: Significant weight loss (common in H&N patients) changes the body contour, shifts internal anatomy, and alters beam path lengths. Parotid glands may shift medially into high-dose regions, increasing xerostomia risk. Adaptive replanning may be needed.

imrt.q: What is the concept of 4-pi radiotherapy?
imrt.a: Non-coplanar VMAT using many arcs at different couch angles to irradiate from nearly all directions
c: Treating four patients simultaneously throughout the treatment course
Radiation delivery from exactly four beam angles in the healthcare environment
A planning technique using four different energies as specified by guidelines
Non-coplanar VMAT using many arcs at different couch angles to irradiate from nearly all directions
imrt.e: 4-pi radiotherapy maximizes the geometric advantage by using many non-coplanar arcs, approaching irradiation from the full solid angle (4-pi steradians). This provides maximum dose conformity and OAR sparing, particularly for brain and centrally located tumors.

imrt.q: CALC: A gamma analysis evaluates 10,000 points. If 9,650 points pass the 3%/3mm criteria, what is the gamma passing rate?
imrt.e: Passing rate = 9650 / 10000 x 100% = 96.5%. This exceeds the TG-218 tolerance limit of 95%, so the plan passes gamma analysis.

imrt.q: What is the clinical significance of the mean dose to normal tissue in IMRT?
imrt.a: It correlates with the risk of late effects for parallel organs and contributes to secondary malignancy risk assessment
c: Mean normal tissue dose has no clinical relevance in clinical practice settings
It only affects acute toxicity as part of the clinical workflow during the planning stage
It correlates with the risk of late effects for parallel organs and contributes to secondary malignancy risk assessment
It determines the maximum tolerable prescription dose during patient treatment sessions
imrt.e: For parallel organs (lung, liver, kidney), mean dose correlates with complication probability. The integral dose to normal tissue also relates to secondary malignancy risk, which is particularly important for pediatric and young adult patients.

imrt.q: CALC: An IMRT plan has a gradient index of 4.5. If the prescription isodose volume (V100%) is 80 cm³, what is the V50% volume?
imrt.a: 360 cm³
c: 180 cm³
240 cm³
360 cm³
450 cm³
imrt.e: GI = V50% / V100%, so V50% = GI x V100% = 4.5 x 80 = 360 cm³. A lower GI indicates steeper dose fall-off. A GI of 4.5 suggests moderate dose fall-off.

imrt.q: What is the primary reason for performing patient-specific IMRT QA?
imrt.a: To verify that the complex treatment plan can be accurately delivered by the specific linac for each individual patient
c: To verify that the complex treatment plan can be accurately delivered by the specific linac for each individual patient
To calibrate the linac before each treatment based on protocol requirements
To check patient positioning accuracy in the healthcare environment as specified by guidelines
To verify the CT simulation images during the planning stage in radiation therapy practice
imrt.e: Patient-specific QA verifies the entire chain: TPS calculation accuracy, MLC delivery accuracy, and linac output consistency for each unique IMRT plan. It catches patient-specific errors that machine QA alone cannot detect.

imrt.q: CALC: A dual-arc VMAT plan delivers 200 cGy with Arc 1 contributing 55% and Arc 2 contributing 45% of the dose. How many cGy does each arc deliver?
imrt.a: Arc 1: 110 cGy, Arc 2: 90 cGy
c: Arc 1: 100 cGy, Arc 2: 100 cGy
Arc 1: 110 cGy, Arc 2: 90 cGy
Arc 1: 120 cGy, Arc 2: 80 cGy
Arc 1: 130 cGy, Arc 2: 70 cGy
imrt.e: Arc 1: 200 x 0.55 = 110 cGy. Arc 2: 200 x 0.45 = 90 cGy. In dual-arc VMAT, the dose contribution is not always equal between arcs; the optimizer distributes MU based on modulation needs from different directions.

imrt.q: What is the fundamental principle of inverse planning in IMRT?
imrt.a: Defining dose objectives and letting the optimizer find fluence
c: Manually adjusting beam weights to achieve desired dose
Defining dose objectives and letting the optimizer find fluence
Using fixed beam arrangements from a standard library
Forward calculating dose from predetermined MLC patterns
imrt.e: The optimizer iteratively adjusts fluence maps to minimize an objective function.

imrt.q: What is the gamma analysis criterion most commonly used for IMRT QA?
imrt.a: 3%/3mm with a pass rate threshold of 95%
c: 1%/1mm with a pass rate threshold of 99%
2%/2mm with a pass rate threshold of 97%
3%/3mm with a pass rate threshold of 95%
5%/5mm with a pass rate threshold of 90%
imrt.e: 3%/3mm with 95% pass rate is the most widely adopted standard per TG-218.

imrt.q: What does the DVH NOT explicitly show?
imrt.a: The spatial location of dose within the structure
c: The maximum dose received by the structure volume
The spatial location of dose within the structure
The volume receiving at least a specified dose level
The mean dose across the entire structure volume
imrt.e: DVH compresses 3D information, losing spatial data.

imrt.q: What MLC leaf width is typically used for head and neck IMRT?
imrt.a: 5 mm leaf width at isocenter projected distance
c: 2.5 mm leaf width at isocenter projected distance
5 mm leaf width at isocenter projected distance
10 mm leaf width at isocenter projected distance
20 mm leaf width at isocenter projected distance
imrt.e: 5 mm leaves provide good resolution for complex H&N targets.

imrt.q: What conformity index value indicates ideal conformity?
imrt.a: Ratio of prescription isodose volume to PTV; ideal is 1.0
c: Ratio of maximum to minimum PTV dose; ideal is 1.0
Ratio of prescription isodose volume to PTV; ideal is 1.0
Ratio of mean dose to prescribed dose; ideal is 0.5
Ratio of treated volume to irradiated volume; ideal 2.0
imrt.e: CI = 1.0 means perfect conformity.

imrt.q: What is the primary advantage of VMAT over fixed-field IMRT?
imrt.a: Shorter treatment delivery time with continuous rotation
c: Higher maximum dose achievable within the target
Shorter treatment delivery time with continuous rotation
Elimination of the need for MLC modulation completely
Better target coverage in all clinical treatment sites
imrt.e: VMAT typically reduces treatment time by 50-70%.

imrt.q: What is the tongue-and-groove effect in MLC-based IMRT?
imrt.a: Underdosage between adjacent leaves due to leaf design
c: Overdosage at leaf tips from rounded leaf transmission
Underdosage between adjacent leaves due to leaf design
Increased scatter from leaf bank material composition
Dosimetric effect of leaf travel speed limitations
imrt.e: The T&G design prevents leakage but creates underdosed regions.

imrt.q: CALC: 285 points pass out of 300 total. What is the gamma pass rate?
What is the HI formula according to ICRU 83?
imrt.a: HI = (D2% - D98%) / D50% for the target volume
c: HI = Dmax / Dmin for the entire target volume
HI = (D2% - D98%) / D50% for the target volume
HI = Dmean / Dprescribed for the target volume
HI = (D95% - D5%) / Dmean for the target volume
imrt.e: A smaller HI indicates better dose homogeneity.

imrt.q: What is the difference between constraints and objectives in inverse planning?
imrt.a: Constraints are hard limits; objectives are goals with penalties
c: Constraints are soft goals; objectives are absolute limits
Constraints are hard limits; objectives are goals with penalties
Both serve identical functions in the optimization
Constraints apply to targets; objectives apply to OARs only
imrt.e: Constraints cannot be violated; objectives incur penalties.

imrt.q: What MLC parameter describes radiation through closed leaf pairs?
imrt.a: Interleaf and intraleaf transmission factor
c: Dosimetric leaf gap correction factor value
Interleaf and intraleaf transmission factor
Dynamic leaf offset table correction value
Rounded leaf tip penumbra width factor
imrt.e: MLC transmission is typically 1-2%.

imrt.q: What is the gradient index used to evaluate in SBRT planning?
imrt.a: The rate of dose falloff outside the target volume
c: The uniformity of dose within the target volume
The rate of dose falloff outside the target volume
The conformity of prescription isodose to PTV
The maximum dose to surrounding normal tissues
imrt.e: GI = V50%Rx / V100%Rx. Lower values indicate steeper falloff.

imrt.q: What does the modulation factor indicate about an IMRT plan?
imrt.a: The ratio of maximum to average segment intensity
c: The total number of beam angles used in the plan
The ratio of maximum to average segment intensity
The percentage of MLC leaves in motion per segment
The ratio of plan MU to a comparable 3D-CRT plan
imrt.e: Higher modulation factors indicate more complex fluence patterns.

imrt.q: CALC: D2% = 52.5 Gy, D98% = 47.5 Gy, D50% = 50 Gy. What is HI?
What is the dosimetric leaf gap (DLG)?
imrt.a: Effective radiation field extension beyond the leaf tip
c: Physical gap between opposing leaf tip surfaces
Effective radiation field extension beyond the leaf tip
Minimum allowed separation between adjacent leaves
Distance between backup jaw and leaf tip position
imrt.e: DLG accounts for rounded leaf tip transmission, typically 1-2 mm.

imrt.q: What is the typical MLC leaf speed limitation for dynamic IMRT?
imrt.a: Maximum leaf speed of approximately 2.5 cm/s
c: Maximum leaf speed of approximately 0.5 cm/s
Maximum leaf speed of approximately 2.5 cm/s
Maximum leaf speed of approximately 10.0 cm/s
Maximum leaf speed of approximately 25.0 cm/s
imrt.e: Typical maximum is about 2.5 cm/s (Varian).

imrt.q: What is the purpose of a normal tissue objective (NTO)?
imrt.a: To control dose falloff in unspecified normal tissue regions
c: To set maximum dose limits for all named OAR structures
To control dose falloff in unspecified normal tissue regions
To enforce target coverage requirements during planning
To limit the total monitor units delivered per fraction
imrt.e: NTO helps reduce integral dose and prevents hot spots in unnamed regions.

imrt.q: What quality metric does R50% evaluate?
imrt.a: The ratio of 50% isodose volume to PTV volume
c: The dose received by 50% of the target volume
The ratio of 50% isodose volume to PTV volume
The volume receiving exactly 50% of prescription
The conformity at the 50% dose level only
imrt.e: R50% measures dose spillage. Lower values indicate tighter confinement.

imrt.q: What is the sliding window technique in dynamic MLC delivery?
imrt.a: Opposing leaves sweep across the field with varying gap
c: MLC segments are delivered sequentially with beam off
Opposing leaves sweep across the field with varying gap
Leaves alternate between open and closed positions
The entire leaf bank shifts between discrete positions
imrt.e: Leaves move continuously during irradiation with varying gap widths.

imrt.q: What does AAPM TG-218 address?
imrt.a: Tolerance limits and methodologies for IMRT QA measurements
c: Commissioning requirements for new linear accelerators
Tolerance limits and methodologies for IMRT QA measurements
Clinical implementation guidelines for proton therapy
Stereotactic radiosurgery quality assurance procedures
imrt.e: TG-218 recommends 3%/2mm gamma with 95% tolerance and 90% action limit.

imrt.q: CALC: IMRT plan delivers 800 MU for 2 Gy. 3D-CRT delivers 250 MU. What is the MU ratio?
What is the impact of small field segments on IMRT accuracy?
imrt.a: Increased dosimetric uncertainty from detector volume averaging
c: Improved dose conformity with smaller field segments
Increased dosimetric uncertainty from detector volume averaging
Reduced treatment time due to fewer monitor units
Better MLC position accuracy for small leaf openings
imrt.e: Small segments suffer from lateral disequilibrium and volume averaging.

imrt.q: What is the role of the cost function in IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: It quantifies deviation of current plan from desired goals
c: It calculates the total treatment cost for the patient
It quantifies deviation of current plan from desired goals
It determines the number of beams needed for treatment
It measures the physical dose rate during delivery
imrt.e: The optimizer minimizes the cost function to balance competing goals.

imrt.q: What is the interplay effect in VMAT delivery?
imrt.a: Dose error from interaction between MLC and organ motion
c: Interference between overlapping beam angles in VMAT
Dose error from interaction between MLC and organ motion
Variation in dose rate during continuous gantry rotation
Scatter contribution from adjacent treatment fields
imrt.e: Fractionation mitigates the interplay effect by averaging over many fractions.

imrt.q: What does plan complexity analysis typically evaluate?
imrt.a: MLC aperture irregularity and modulation extent metrics
c: The clinical appropriateness of the prescribed dose
MLC aperture irregularity and modulation extent metrics
The accuracy of the dose calculation algorithm used
Patient anatomy suitability for IMRT technique
imrt.e: Higher complexity correlates with larger planned vs delivered dose discrepancies.

imrt.q: What is the purpose of fluence smoothing in IMRT optimization?
imrt.a: To reduce plan complexity and improve deliverability
c: To increase dose homogeneity within the target volume
To reduce plan complexity and improve deliverability
To eliminate all hot spots from the treatment plan
To match the fluence to a predefined clinical template
imrt.e: Smoothing reduces sharp intensity gradients, improving deliverability.

imrt.q: CALC: 3%/3mm gamma criteria. What is the dose tolerance for a point at 200 cGy?
imrt.a: 6 cGy
c: 3 cGy
6 cGy
9 cGy
60 cGy
imrt.e: 3% of 200 cGy = 6 cGy.

imrt.q: What is multi-criteria optimization (MCO)?
imrt.a: Exploring trade-offs between competing objectives interactively
c: Optimizing a single objective function with constraints
Exploring trade-offs between competing objectives interactively
Running multiple optimizations with random starting points
Selecting beam angles based on predefined quality criteria
imrt.e: MCO generates Pareto-optimal plans for interactive trade-off navigation.

imrt.q: What is the primary concern with high MU IMRT plans?
imrt.a: Increased peripheral dose from head scatter and leakage
c: Reduced dose accuracy within the primary treatment field
Increased peripheral dose from head scatter and leakage
Slower delivery causing more intrafraction patient motion
Higher electrical power consumption by the accelerator
imrt.e: High MU increases whole-body dose from MLC leakage.

imrt.q: What is aperture-based optimization in VMAT planning?
imrt.a: Directly optimizing MLC shapes and weights at each angle
c: Optimizing fluence maps then converting to MLC segments
Directly optimizing MLC shapes and weights at each angle
Using predefined aperture templates from a clinical library
Optimizing only the dose rate variation during rotation
imrt.e: Direct aperture optimization produces directly deliverable plans.

imrt.q: What is the primary goal of inverse planning optimization in IMRT treatment planning?
imrt.a: To determine optimal beam fluence patterns that satisfy dose-volume objectives
c: To manually adjust beam weights until the planner achieves an acceptable plan
To determine optimal beam fluence patterns that satisfy dose-volume objectives
To select the best gantry angles from a predefined set of standard beam arrangements
To calculate dose distributions using Monte Carlo simulation for each beam angle
imrt.e: Inverse planning uses mathematical optimization to find fluence maps that best meet the specified dose-volume constraints and objectives for targets and organs at risk.

imrt.q: What is the interplay effect in VMAT delivery for moving targets such as lung tumors?
imrt.a: Dose errors from the interaction between MLC leaf motion and target respiratory motion
c: Interference between adjacent VMAT arcs causing dose calculation convergence errors
Dose errors from the interaction between MLC leaf motion and target respiratory motion
Gantry speed variations that cause monitor unit delivery rate fluctuations
Collimator rotation artifacts that affect the MLC leaf positioning accuracy
imrt.e: During VMAT, the MLC leaves move continuously while the target also moves with respiration. Their relative motion can create hot and cold spots within the target volume.

imrt.q: What is the primary purpose of MLC leaf sequencing in step-and-shoot IMRT delivery?
imrt.a: To decompose the optimized fluence map into a series of deliverable MLC segments
c: To verify that each MLC leaf position matches the planned position during delivery
To decompose the optimized fluence map into a series of deliverable MLC segments
To determine the optimal number of gantry angles needed for adequate plan quality
To calculate the dose rate modulation pattern for each segment of the treatment
imrt.e: Leaf sequencing converts the continuous optimized fluence map into discrete MLC apertures (segments) with associated monitor units that can be physically delivered by the linac.

imrt.q: What metric is commonly used to evaluate IMRT plan quality for target dose homogeneity?
imrt.a: The homogeneity index calculated from near-maximum and near-minimum target doses
c: The gamma passing rate at 3%/3mm criteria for patient-specific QA measurements
The maximum dose to any single voxel anywhere within the treatment plan volume
The homogeneity index calculated from near-maximum and near-minimum target doses
The monitor unit efficiency defined as prescription dose divided by total monitor units
imrt.e: The homogeneity index (e.g., D2%-D98%/D50%) quantifies dose uniformity within the target. Lower values indicate more homogeneous dose distributions within the PTV.

imrt.q: What factor primarily limits the deliverability of highly modulated IMRT plans?
imrt.a: Mechanical constraints of MLC leaf speed, travel range, and minimum gap settings
c: The total number of available gantry angles on the linear accelerator system
Mechanical constraints of MLC leaf speed, travel range, and minimum gap settings
The processing speed of the treatment planning system optimization algorithm
The size of the patient anatomy relative to the maximum available field dimensions
imrt.e: MLC leaves have finite maximum speed, travel limits, and minimum interleaf gaps. Highly modulated plans may require leaf motions that exceed these mechanical capabilities.

igrt.q: What does CBCT stand for in radiotherapy imaging?
igrt.a: Cone-beam computed tomography
c: Circular-beam computed tomography
Cone-beam computed tomography
Collimated-beam CT
Convergent-beam CT
igrt.e: CBCT uses a cone-shaped X-ray beam and a flat-panel detector to acquire volumetric images in a single gantry rotation, enabling 3D verification of patient position at the treatment unit.

igrt.q: Which detector type is most commonly used for kV CBCT on modern linacs?
igrt.a: Amorphous silicon flat-panel detector
c: CCD camera
Amorphous silicon flat-panel detector
Gas ionisation chamber array
Photomultiplier tube array
igrt.e: Amorphous silicon (a-Si) flat-panel detectors are the standard for kV on-board imaging. They provide good spatial resolution and are robust enough for the clinical environment.

igrt.q: What is the typical imaging dose from a single pelvic kV CBCT scan?
c: 1-5 mGy
igrt.e: A typical pelvic kV CBCT delivers approximately 10-30 mGy per scan. Over a 30-fraction course, cumulative imaging dose can reach 0.3-0.9 Gy, which should be considered in the overall dose assessment.

igrt.q: What is the primary advantage of kV imaging over MV imaging for IGRT?
igrt.a: Superior soft-tissue contrast due to photoelectric effect dominance
c: Higher penetration through dense bone during patient treatment sessions
Superior soft-tissue contrast due to photoelectric effect dominance
Lower cost of equipment in radiation therapy practice
Faster image acquisition within the treatment system
igrt.e: At kV energies, the photoelectric effect dominates, providing Z-cubed-dependent contrast between tissues. At MV energies, Compton scatter dominates and contrast depends mainly on electron density, resulting in much poorer soft-tissue differentiation.

igrt.q: What is an EPID in the context of radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Electronic portal imaging device
c: Electronic portal imaging device
External patient immobilisation device
Electron-positron interaction detector
Enhanced positioning and imaging device
igrt.e: An EPID is a flat-panel detector mounted opposite the treatment head that captures exit MV radiation to produce portal images for treatment verification.

igrt.q: In 2D/2D matching for IGRT, orthogonal kV images are compared to:
igrt.a: Digitally reconstructed radiographs (DRRs) from the planning CT
c: Previous treatment portal images for the treatment procedure
Digitally reconstructed radiographs (DRRs) from the planning CT
Simulator films in the treatment process
Diagnostic X-rays taken at consultation
igrt.e: DRRs are synthetic radiographs computed from the planning CT dataset. They serve as the reference images for 2D/2D matching, allowing comparison of bony anatomy or fiducials between the planned and actual patient positions.

igrt.q: What is the main limitation of 2D/2D kV matching compared to CBCT matching?
igrt.a: Cannot visualise soft-tissue targets directly
c: Higher radiation dose
Cannot visualise soft-tissue targets directly
Requires longer acquisition time
Cannot detect rotational errors
igrt.e: 2D planar imaging relies on bony anatomy or implanted markers. Unlike CBCT, it cannot directly visualise soft-tissue structures, limiting its utility for sites where soft-tissue matching is needed.

igrt.q: In CBCT-to-CT registration, what type of image registration is most commonly used for daily setup verification?
igrt.a: Rigid registration
c: Deformable registration
Rigid registration
Feature-based registration
Landmark-only registration
igrt.e: Rigid registration (3 translations + 3 rotations = 6 degrees of freedom) is standard for daily CBCT-to-planning-CT matching. It is fast, robust, and sufficient for correcting setup errors when anatomy has not significantly deformed.

igrt.q: What are the six degrees of freedom (6DOF) corrected by a robotic couch?
igrt.a: 3 translations and 3 rotations (pitch, roll, yaw)
c: 3 translations and 3 rotations (pitch, roll, yaw)
3 translations and 3 scale factors
6 translational axes
3 translations and 3 shear corrections
igrt.e: A 6DOF couch can correct translational shifts (left-right, superior-inferior, anterior-posterior) plus rotational errors (pitch around the lateral axis, roll around the longitudinal axis, yaw around the vertical axis).

igrt.q: Which imaging artefact is most problematic in CBCT and degrades soft-tissue contrast?
igrt.a: Scatter artefacts causing cupping and shading
c: Scatter artefacts causing cupping and shading
Chemical shift artefact for accurate dose delivery
Susceptibility artefact in radiation therapy practice
Zipper artefact in clinical practice settings
igrt.e: CBCT suffers from excessive scatter radiation due to the large cone-beam geometry. This causes cupping artefacts (lower HU values in the centre), shading, and streaks, which degrade HU accuracy and soft-tissue contrast compared to fan-beam CT.

igrt.q: What is 4DCT used for in radiation therapy?
igrt.a: Capturing tumour motion throughout the respiratory cycle for motion-encompassing target volumes
c: Measuring blood flow to the tumour in the healthcare environment
Capturing tumour motion throughout the respiratory cycle for motion-encompassing target volumes
Creating 4-dimensional dose distributions during the planning stage
Tracking cardiac motion for cardiac ablation for therapeutic applications
igrt.e: 4DCT acquires CT data correlated with respiratory phase, producing multiple 3D image sets representing different phases of breathing. This allows definition of the internal target volume (ITV) encompassing the full range of tumour motion.

igrt.q: In 4DCT, what is the difference between phase sorting and amplitude sorting?
igrt.a: Phase sorting bins data by respiratory phase percentage; amplitude sorting bins by breathing signal position
c: Phase sorting uses MV images while amplitude sorting uses kV images
Phase sorting bins data by respiratory phase percentage; amplitude sorting bins by breathing signal position
They are identical methods with different names
Phase sorting requires breath-hold while amplitude sorting uses free breathing
igrt.e: Phase sorting assigns images to bins based on their position in the respiratory cycle (0%, 10%, 20%...). Amplitude sorting bins based on the actual displacement of the surrogate signal. Amplitude sorting can be more robust for irregular breathing patterns.

igrt.q: What is the Active Breathing Coordinator (ABC) system?
igrt.a: A device that temporarily suspends breathing at a reproducible lung volume using a valve
c: A device that temporarily suspends breathing at a reproducible lung volume using a valve
A system that tracks tumour motion with electromagnetic transponders
A ventilator used during anaesthesia for SBRT
A coaching system that displays breathing waveforms to patients
igrt.e: The ABC system (Elekta) uses a spirometer and balloon valve to hold the patient's breath at a predetermined lung volume, creating a reproducible anatomy for treatment. It is commonly used for left-sided breast treatments to spare the heart.

igrt.q: What is the primary clinical advantage of deep inspiration breath-hold (DIBH) for left-sided breast radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Increased distance between the heart and treatment field, reducing cardiac dose
c: Better tumour coverage per standard operating procedures
Reduced lung dose throughout the treatment course
Increased distance between the heart and treatment field, reducing cardiac dose
Shorter treatment time within the treatment system
igrt.e: DIBH inflates the lungs, pushing the heart inferiorly and posteriorly away from the left breast/chest wall tangent fields. This can reduce mean heart dose by 50% or more, significantly lowering the risk of late cardiac toxicity.

igrt.q: The Varian RPM system uses which surrogate for respiratory motion?
igrt.a: An infrared-reflective marker block placed on the patient's abdomen
c: Implanted electromagnetic transponders in the radiation therapy context
An infrared-reflective marker block placed on the patient's abdomen
A belt strain gauge around the chest in clinical practice settings
Nasal airflow thermistors in the radiation therapy context
igrt.e: The RPM system tracks an external marker block with reflective dots placed on the patient's abdomen using an infrared camera. The vertical displacement of the block serves as a surrogate for internal respiratory motion.

igrt.q: What is the Calypso system?
igrt.a: An electromagnetic transponder-based real-time tumour tracking system
c: A surface-guided radiation therapy system
An electromagnetic transponder-based real-time tumour tracking system
An optical tracking system for SRS
A respiratory gating system using spirometry
igrt.e: Calypso (Varian) uses implanted electromagnetic beacon transponders tracked in real-time by an external antenna array. It provides continuous 3D position monitoring without additional imaging dose, commonly used for prostate treatments.

igrt.q: Which surface-guided radiation therapy (SGRT) system uses structured light to create a real-time 3D surface map of the patient?
igrt.a: AlignRT (Vision RT)
c: ExacTrac
Calypso
AlignRT (Vision RT)
CyberKnife Synchrony
igrt.e: AlignRT uses multiple camera pods projecting a speckle pattern onto the patient's surface and capturing the reflected pattern to reconstruct a real-time 3D surface. It enables non-ionising, markerless patient positioning and intrafraction monitoring.

igrt.q: What is the primary advantage of SGRT over conventional IGRT for intrafraction monitoring?
igrt.a: Continuous real-time monitoring without additional radiation dose
c: Better bony anatomy visualisation in radiation therapy practice
Continuous real-time monitoring without additional radiation dose
Higher spatial resolution than CBCT for the treatment procedure
Direct tumour visualisation in the treatment process
igrt.e: SGRT provides continuous, non-ionising surface monitoring throughout treatment delivery. Unlike CBCT which provides a snapshot, SGRT can detect intrafraction motion in real-time and trigger beam hold if the patient moves beyond tolerance.

igrt.q: Gold fiducial markers implanted in the prostate are typically what size?
igrt.a: 1-3 mm diameter, 3-5 mm length
c: 0.1 mm diameter, 0.5 mm length
1-3 mm diameter, 3-5 mm length
5-10 mm diameter, 10-15 mm length
10 mm diameter, 20 mm length
igrt.e: Prostate fiducial markers are typically small gold seeds approximately 1-3 mm in diameter and 3-5 mm long. They are visible on kV imaging and CBCT and serve as surrogates for prostate position.

igrt.q: The ExacTrac system by BrainLab uses which imaging modality for patient positioning?
igrt.a: Stereoscopic kV X-ray imaging combined with infrared surface tracking
c: MV portal imaging only according to clinical protocols
Stereoscopic kV X-ray imaging combined with infrared surface tracking
CBCT only per standard operating procedures
Ultrasound imaging in radiation therapy practice
igrt.e: ExacTrac combines floor-mounted or ceiling-mounted kV X-ray tubes with infrared camera tracking. The kV images verify internal anatomy (bony or fiducial) while IR tracking monitors the patient surface in real-time.

igrt.q: In CyberKnife, the Synchrony system performs which function?
igrt.a: Real-time respiratory motion tracking and dynamic beam adjustment
c: Static patient positioning verification
Real-time respiratory motion tracking and dynamic beam adjustment
MLC-based intensity modulation as part of the clinical workflow
Automatic contouring of tumour volumes based on protocol requirements
igrt.e: Synchrony correlates internal fiducial positions (from periodic X-ray imaging) with external LED markers (tracked continuously) to build a motion model. The robotic arm dynamically adjusts beam pointing to follow tumour motion in real-time.

igrt.q: What is mutual information in the context of image registration?
igrt.a: A similarity metric based on the statistical relationship between image intensity distributions
c: The physical overlap of two image volumes for therapeutic applications
A similarity metric based on the statistical relationship between image intensity distributions
A measure of spatial resolution for accurate dose delivery
The degree of image distortion for radiation dose management
igrt.e: Mutual information measures the statistical dependence between intensity distributions of two images. It is particularly useful for multi-modality registration (e.g., CT-MRI) because it does not require the images to have the same intensity scale.

igrt.q: What is the fundamental difference between rigid and deformable image registration?
igrt.a: Rigid preserves distances and angles; deformable allows local non-linear transformations
c: Rigid uses manual alignment; deformable is automatic
Rigid preserves distances and angles; deformable allows local non-linear transformations
Rigid is for 2D images; deformable is for 3D images
Rigid uses MV images; deformable uses kV images
igrt.e: Rigid registration applies global translations and rotations (6 DOF), preserving the shape of structures. Deformable image registration (DIR) applies local, voxel-level transformations that can account for organ deformation, weight loss, and tumour changes.

igrt.q: Which of the following is a key quality assurance check for deformable image registration accuracy?
igrt.a: Evaluation of the deformation vector field for physical plausibility and landmark consistency
c: Visual inspection of image brightness only in radiation therapy practice
Evaluation of the deformation vector field for physical plausibility and landmark consistency
Checking that HU values are unchanged according to clinical protocols
Verifying that the registration runs in under 1 minute for therapeutic applications
igrt.e: DIR QA involves checking DVF smoothness (no folding/tearing), landmark-based accuracy assessment, contour propagation comparison, and inverse consistency. TG-132 provides guidance on DIR validation in radiotherapy.

igrt.q: AAPM TG-132 provides guidance on which aspect of IGRT?
igrt.a: Use of image registration and fusion in radiotherapy
c: CBCT quality assurance for the treatment procedure
MLC commissioning for therapeutic applications
Use of image registration and fusion in radiotherapy
Brachytherapy source calibration
igrt.e: TG-132 (2017) addresses the clinical use of image registration and fusion algorithms, including rigid and deformable registration, accuracy assessment, and quality assurance recommendations.

igrt.q: What distinguishes online adaptive radiotherapy from offline adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Online adaptation modifies the plan while the patient is on the treatment couch; offline adapts between fractions
c: Online uses MV imaging; offline uses kV imaging based on protocol requirements
Online adaptation modifies the plan while the patient is on the treatment couch; offline adapts between fractions
Online is fully automatic; offline requires physician input for radiation dose management
Online is only for proton therapy; offline is for photon therapy in the healthcare environment
igrt.e: Online ART performs plan modification in real-time with the patient on the couch (minutes), allowing same-session adaptation. Offline ART reviews accumulated data between fractions and generates new plans for subsequent treatments.

igrt.q: In a 'plan of the day' adaptive workflow, what happens at each treatment fraction?
igrt.a: The best-fitting plan is selected from a pre-generated library based on daily anatomy
c: A completely new plan is optimised from scratch as specified by guidelines
The best-fitting plan is selected from a pre-generated library based on daily anatomy
The original plan is used without modification for therapeutic applications
The physician manually adjusts beam angles in the healthcare environment
igrt.e: Plan-of-the-day approaches create a library of plans covering likely anatomical variations (e.g., different bladder filling states). At each fraction, daily imaging determines which plan best matches the current anatomy.

igrt.q: The Varian Ethos system performs online adaptive radiotherapy using which approach?
igrt.a: AI-driven auto-contouring on daily CBCT with rapid plan re-optimisation
c: Plan library selection only based on protocol requirements
AI-driven auto-contouring on daily CBCT with rapid plan re-optimisation
MR-guided adaptation per standard operating procedures
Manual recontouring by the physician for radiation dose management
igrt.e: Ethos uses artificial intelligence to auto-segment structures on the daily CBCT, then rapidly re-optimises the treatment plan. The entire online adaptive workflow targets completion within approximately 15 minutes including physician review.

igrt.q: What is HyperSight on the Varian Halcyon/Ethos platform?
igrt.a: An enhanced CBCT imaging mode with iterative reconstruction for improved image quality
c: A beam monitoring system for therapeutic applications
An enhanced CBCT imaging mode with iterative reconstruction for improved image quality
A surface guidance camera system as part of the clinical workflow
A motion management respiratory gating device in clinical practice settings
igrt.e: HyperSight is Varian's advanced CBCT reconstruction technology using iterative algorithms to significantly improve soft-tissue contrast and reduce artefacts compared to standard FDK reconstruction, enabling better anatomical visualisation for adaptive workflows.

igrt.q: The Elekta Unity MR-Linac combines which two technologies?
igrt.a: 1.5 T MRI scanner and a 7 MV linear accelerator
c: 0.35 T MRI and a 6 MV linac
1.5 T MRI scanner and a 7 MV linear accelerator
3 T MRI and a 10 MV linac
1.5 T MRI and a cobalt-60 source
igrt.e: The Elekta Unity integrates a 1.5 T diagnostic-quality MRI with a 7 MV standing-wave linac on a ring gantry. This combination enables real-time MR imaging during treatment delivery with excellent soft-tissue contrast.

igrt.q: What is the electron return effect (ERE) in MR-guided radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Secondary electrons curve back to tissue interfaces due to the magnetic field, causing local dose enhancement
c: Electrons are accelerated by the magnetic field increasing beam energy
Secondary electrons curve back to tissue interfaces due to the magnetic field, causing local dose enhancement
Electrons are stripped from atoms causing increased ionisation throughout the volume
The treatment beam is deflected by the magnetic field
igrt.e: The Lorentz force from the magnetic field causes secondary electrons leaving a tissue surface (e.g., into air cavities) to curve back, depositing additional dose at the exit interface. This electron return effect must be accounted for in MR-linac treatment planning.

igrt.q: The ViewRay MRIdian system originally used which radiation source before transitioning to a linac?
igrt.a: Cobalt-60 sources
c: Cobalt-60 sources
Iridium-192 sources
Caesium-137 sources
A 6 MV linear accelerator from inception
igrt.e: The original MRIdian system used three Co-60 sources with a 0.35 T MRI. ViewRay later developed the MRIdian Linac, replacing the cobalt sources with a 6 MV FFF linac while maintaining the 0.35 T MRI.

igrt.q: What magnetic field strength does the ViewRay MRIdian MR-Linac use?
igrt.e: The ViewRay MRIdian uses a 0.35 T split-bore superconducting MRI. While this lower field strength provides less SNR than the 1.5 T Unity, it results in a smaller electron return effect and reduced susceptibility artefacts near air-tissue interfaces.

igrt.q: CALC: Using the van Herk margin recipe M = 2.5 Sigma + 0.7 sigma, calculate the CTV-to-PTV margin if systematic error (Sigma) = 3 mm and random error (sigma) = 4 mm.
c: 7.5 mm
8.8 mm
igrt.e: M = 2.5 x 3 + 0.7 x 4 = 7.5 + 2.8 = 10.3 mm. The van Herk formula ensures that for 90% of patients, the CTV receives at least 95% of the prescribed dose.

igrt.q: In the van Herk margin recipe, which error type has the larger coefficient and thus greater impact on margins?
igrt.a: Systematic error (coefficient 2.5)
c: Random error (coefficient 2.5)
Systematic error (coefficient 2.5)
Both have equal coefficients
Neither; the formula uses only one error type
igrt.e: Systematic errors shift the dose distribution consistently in one direction for every fraction, having a much larger impact on target coverage than random errors which tend to blur the dose. Hence the coefficient for systematic error (2.5) is much larger than for random error (0.7).

igrt.q: According to ICRU 62, what is the internal margin (IM)?
igrt.a: A margin accounting for variations in target size, shape, and position relative to anatomical reference points
c: A margin for setup errors only for therapeutic applications for quality assurance purposes
A margin accounting for variations in target size, shape, and position relative to anatomical reference points
The expansion from GTV to CTV in radiation therapy practice for quality assurance purposes
A margin for beam penumbra based on protocol requirements in clinical practice settings
igrt.e: ICRU 62 introduced the concept of the internal margin (IM) to account for internal organ motion and deformation. Combined with the setup margin (SM), these form the planning target volume margin: PTV = CTV + IM + SM (or combined as ITV + SM).

igrt.q: ICRU Report 83 applies to which treatment technique?
igrt.a: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT)
c: 3D conformal radiation therapy
Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT)
Brachytherapy in radiation therapy practice
Proton therapy for quality assurance purposes
igrt.e: ICRU 83 (2010) provides prescribing, recording, and reporting guidelines specifically for IMRT. It introduces the concept of reporting dose to the near-minimum (D98%), near-maximum (D2%), and median dose (D50%) rather than point doses.

igrt.q: What is the difference between systematic and random setup errors?
igrt.a: Systematic errors are consistent offsets reproduced at every fraction; random errors vary unpredictably from fraction to fraction
c: Systematic errors are larger than random errors by definition for therapeutic applications
Systematic errors are consistent offsets reproduced at every fraction; random errors vary unpredictably from fraction to fraction
Systematic errors only occur in the lateral direction during the planning stage
Random errors can only be detected with CBCT during patient treatment sessions
igrt.e: Systematic errors arise from consistent biases (e.g., simulation positioning error) and shift the dose distribution. Random errors fluctuate between fractions and blur the dose distribution. Daily IGRT reduces both but is most effective at catching systematic errors early.

igrt.q: CALC: A department measures setup errors for 20 prostate patients (5 fractions each). The population systematic error is Sigma = 2.5 mm and population random error is sigma = 3.0 mm. Using van Herk (M = 2.5*Sigma + 0.7*sigma), what is the PTV margin?
igrt.e: M = 2.5 x 2.5 + 0.7 x 3.0 = 6.25 + 2.10 = 8.35 mm. In practice this would typically be rounded to 8 or 9 mm.

igrt.q: What is intrafraction motion?
igrt.a: Patient or organ movement that occurs during a single treatment fraction delivery
c: Movement between treatment fractions in radiation therapy practice
Patient or organ movement that occurs during a single treatment fraction delivery
Motion of the gantry during treatment for quality assurance purposes
MLC leaf movement during IMRT delivery as specified by guidelines
igrt.e: Intrafraction motion includes breathing, peristalsis, cardiac motion, and involuntary patient movement during beam-on time. It is distinct from interfraction motion (changes between daily treatments) and requires real-time monitoring strategies.

igrt.q: Which AAPM Task Group report provides recommendations for quality assurance of IGRT technologies?
c: TG-51
igrt.e: TG-179 (2012) addresses QA for image-guided radiation therapy, covering geometric accuracy, image quality, dose from imaging, and integration with treatment delivery systems.

igrt.q: AAPM TG-180 addresses which specific topic?
igrt.a: Image guidance doses delivered during radiotherapy
c: MLC quality assurance
Image guidance doses delivered during radiotherapy
Stereotactic radiosurgery commissioning
Electron beam calibration
igrt.e: TG-180 provides guidance on quantifying, managing, and reporting radiation dose delivered through image guidance procedures including CBCT, planar kV, and MV imaging during the course of radiotherapy.

igrt.q: The Winston-Lutz test, when applied to IGRT, verifies what?
igrt.a: Coincidence of the imaging isocentre with the radiation isocentre
c: CBCT image quality as specified by guidelines
MLC leaf position accuracy for radiation dose management
Coincidence of the imaging isocentre with the radiation isocentre
Couch weight-bearing capacity during the planning stage
igrt.e: For IGRT, the Winston-Lutz test (or its variant) ensures that the coordinate system used for image guidance correctly corresponds to the radiation isocentre. Any mismatch would translate to systematic targeting errors.

igrt.q: What is CNR in the context of CBCT image quality?
igrt.a: Contrast-to-noise ratio: the difference in signal between two regions divided by the noise
c: Central normalisation ratio for the treatment procedure
Contrast-to-noise ratio: the difference in signal between two regions divided by the noise
Cumulative noise reduction for the treatment procedure
Cone-beam normalisation reference in radiation therapy practice
igrt.e: CNR = (mean HU region A - mean HU region B) / noise (standard deviation of background). Higher CNR indicates better ability to distinguish between adjacent tissues, which is critical for soft-tissue matching in IGRT.

igrt.q: Why are HU values in CBCT generally less accurate than in diagnostic fan-beam CT?
igrt.a: Increased scatter in the cone-beam geometry degrades HU accuracy
c: CBCT uses lower tube voltage in the radiation therapy context
CBCT detectors have lower quantum efficiency
Increased scatter in the cone-beam geometry degrades HU accuracy
CBCT acquires fewer projections for the treatment procedure
igrt.e: The wide cone-beam produces significantly more scatter than a collimated fan beam. This scatter contaminates the projection data, causing cupping artefacts, shading, and HU inaccuracies that make CBCT unsuitable for direct dose calculation without correction.

igrt.q: CALC: A head CBCT protocol delivers 1.5 mGy per scan. If a patient receives daily CBCT for 30 fractions, what is the total imaging dose?
igrt.a: 45 mGy
c: 15 mGy
30 mGy
45 mGy
60 mGy
igrt.e: Total imaging dose = 1.5 mGy x 30 fractions = 45 mGy = 0.045 Gy. While small relative to treatment dose, this should be documented and considered in the overall dose assessment per TG-180 recommendations.

igrt.q: What is transit dosimetry?
igrt.a: Measurement of the radiation beam after passing through the patient using the EPID
c: Measuring dose at the skin entrance only within the treatment system
Measurement of the radiation beam after passing through the patient using the EPID
Calculating dose during patient transport during the planning stage
Measuring scatter radiation in the treatment room
igrt.e: Transit dosimetry uses the EPID to measure the beam exiting the patient. By comparing measured and predicted exit fluence, errors in patient positioning, anatomy changes, or delivery can be detected, serving as an independent treatment verification method.

igrt.q: Log file analysis for treatment verification involves examining which data?
igrt.a: Machine delivery parameters recorded during treatment (MLC positions, gantry angle, MU, dose rate)
c: Patient breathing traces only per standard operating procedures
Machine delivery parameters recorded during treatment (MLC positions, gantry angle, MU, dose rate)
CBCT image quality metrics per standard operating procedures
Room radiation survey measurements as specified by guidelines
igrt.e: Treatment log files (e.g., Varian DynaLog or TrajectoryLog files) record actual MLC leaf positions, gantry angles, dose rates, and MU delivered at high temporal resolution. Comparing planned vs actual parameters can reveal delivery errors.

igrt.q: What is the typical spatial resolution of kV CBCT used in radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Approximately 1 mm (5-8 lp/cm)
c: 0.1 mm (50 lp/cm) for clinical implementation
Approximately 1 mm (5-8 lp/cm)
5 mm (1 lp/cm) within the treatment system
10 mm (0.5 lp/cm) in modern radiation oncology
igrt.e: kV CBCT typically achieves spatial resolution of approximately 5-8 line pairs per cm, corresponding to about 0.6-1.0 mm. This is lower than diagnostic CT but sufficient for IGRT matching and anatomy verification.

igrt.q: In respiratory gating, what defines the 'gating window'?
igrt.a: The portion of the respiratory cycle during which the beam is turned on
c: The physical window in the treatment room for radiation dose management
The time between imaging and treatment for therapeutic applications
The portion of the respiratory cycle during which the beam is turned on
The MLC leaf travel range per standard operating procedures
igrt.e: The gating window defines the acceptable range of the respiratory signal during which radiation is delivered. A smaller window reduces motion artefacts but increases treatment time. Typical gating windows correspond to 20-40% duty cycle.

igrt.q: CALC: If a respiratory-gated treatment has a 30% duty cycle and the ungated treatment time is 5 minutes, what is the approximate gated treatment time?
igrt.a: 16.7 minutes
c: 6.5 minutes
10.0 minutes
16.7 minutes
25.0 minutes
igrt.e: Gated time = ungated time / duty cycle = 5 / 0.30 = 16.7 minutes. The beam is only on for 30% of the respiratory cycle, so treatment takes approximately 3.3 times longer.

igrt.q: Which of the following is NOT a commercially available surface-guided radiation therapy system?
igrt.a: SyncTraX
c: AlignRT
Catalyst (C-RAD)
IDENTIFY (Varian)
SyncTraX
igrt.e: AlignRT (Vision RT), Catalyst (C-RAD), and IDENTIFY (Varian) are all commercial SGRT systems. SyncTraX is not a recognised commercial SGRT product.

igrt.q: For SGRT-based DIBH breast treatments, what is a typical positional tolerance for beam gating?
igrt.a: 3 mm
c: 0.1 mm
1 mm
3 mm
10 mm
igrt.e: A 3 mm tolerance is commonly used for SGRT-based DIBH monitoring. If the surface displacement exceeds this threshold, the beam is automatically held until the patient returns to the correct breath-hold position.

igrt.q: What is the main clinical application of SGRT in frameless cranial SRS?
igrt.a: Real-time intrafraction monitoring to detect patient head movement
c: Replacing the need for treatment planning
Real-time intrafraction monitoring to detect patient head movement
Measuring brain tumour oxygenation for accurate dose delivery
Automated dose calculation within the treatment system
igrt.e: In frameless SRS, SGRT provides continuous surface monitoring of the patient's head position, detecting submillimetre movements that might compromise the accuracy of stereotactic treatments. It complements initial imaging-based setup.

igrt.q: What is the Feldkamp-Davis-Kress (FDK) algorithm?
igrt.a: A filtered back-projection algorithm for reconstructing CBCT images from cone-beam projections
c: A deformable registration algorithm per standard operating procedures
An MLC optimisation algorithm in the radiation therapy context
A filtered back-projection algorithm for reconstructing CBCT images from cone-beam projections
A dose calculation algorithm for proton therapy throughout the treatment course
igrt.e: The FDK algorithm is the standard analytical reconstruction method for CBCT. It extends the fan-beam filtered back-projection to the 3D cone-beam geometry. While fast, it is sensitive to scatter and truncation artefacts.

igrt.q: Iterative CBCT reconstruction algorithms offer what primary advantage over FDK?
igrt.a: Improved image quality and reduced artefacts, especially at lower doses
c: Faster reconstruction time in the treatment process
Improved image quality and reduced artefacts, especially at lower doses
Higher spatial resolution throughout the treatment course
No need for a flat-panel detector in the radiation therapy context
igrt.e: Iterative reconstruction algorithms (e.g., used in HyperSight, iCBCT) model the imaging physics more accurately, suppressing scatter and noise artefacts. They can achieve diagnostic-like image quality at reduced dose, but require more computational time.

igrt.q: In MV portal imaging, the dominant interaction is:
igrt.a: Compton scattering
c: Photoelectric effect
Compton scattering
Pair production
Coherent scattering
igrt.e: At megavoltage energies used in treatment beams (6-18 MV), Compton scattering is the dominant interaction in tissue. Since Compton interactions depend primarily on electron density rather than atomic number, MV images have inherently poor soft-tissue contrast.

igrt.q: What is the main advantage of MV portal imaging over kV imaging?
igrt.a: Uses the treatment beam, ensuring exact geometric alignment with the treatment field
c: Better soft-tissue contrast in clinical practice settings
Lower patient dose in the treatment process for clinical implementation
Uses the treatment beam, ensuring exact geometric alignment with the treatment field
Higher spatial resolution during patient treatment sessions
