igrt.e: MV portal images are formed by the actual treatment beam, so they inherently show the treatment field geometry. There is no cross-calibration needed between separate imaging and treatment coordinate systems.

igrt.q: How many projections are typically acquired in a full-rotation kV CBCT scan?
igrt.a: 300-700 projections over 360 degrees
c: 10-20 projections in the radiation therapy context
50-100 projections per standard operating procedures
300-700 projections over 360 degrees
2000-5000 projections
igrt.e: A typical kV CBCT scan acquires 300-700 projection images over a 360-degree (or sometimes 200-degree) gantry rotation, with the number depending on the protocol, frame rate, and gantry speed.

igrt.q: What is a half-fan (offset detector) CBCT acquisition?
igrt.a: The detector is shifted laterally to cover a larger field of view using a 360-degree rotation
c: Using half the normal mAs to reduce dose for radiation dose management
The detector is shifted laterally to cover a larger field of view using a 360-degree rotation
Acquiring only 180 degrees of projections for radiation dose management
Using the detector at half the normal frame rate in the healthcare environment
igrt.e: In half-fan mode, the flat-panel detector is shifted laterally so that each projection covers a different half of the patient. A full 360-degree rotation is required to reconstruct the complete volume, doubling the FOV compared to full-fan mode.

igrt.q: What is the Elekta XVI system?
igrt.a: Elekta's kV cone-beam CT imaging system for IGRT
c: Elekta's treatment planning system
Elekta's kV cone-beam CT imaging system for IGRT
Elekta's MLC control system
Elekta's record and verify system
igrt.e: XVI (X-ray Volume Imaging) is Elekta's on-board kV CBCT system. It consists of a kV X-ray tube and flat-panel detector mounted on retractable arms on the linac gantry.

igrt.q: What is the Varian OBI (On-Board Imager) system?
igrt.a: Varian's kV imaging system for radiographic and CBCT image guidance
c: Varian's MV imaging system in radiation therapy practice
A patient monitoring camera system in clinical practice settings
Varian's kV imaging system for radiographic and CBCT image guidance
Varian's treatment planning module in radiation therapy practice
igrt.e: The OBI system includes a kV X-ray source and amorphous silicon flat-panel detector mounted on robotic arms perpendicular to the MV beam axis. It provides 2D radiographs, fluoroscopy, and CBCT for patient positioning.

igrt.q: What is the typical field of view (FOV) for a standard full-fan CBCT of the head?
igrt.a: Approximately 25 cm
c: 10 cm for therapeutic applications
Approximately 25 cm
46 cm as specified by guidelines
60 cm in modern radiation oncology
igrt.e: Standard full-fan CBCT head protocols typically have a FOV of approximately 25 cm, which is sufficient to cover the cranial anatomy. Body protocols using half-fan mode can achieve FOVs up to 45-50 cm.

igrt.q: CALC: The imaging dose from a pelvis CBCT is 25 mGy per scan. A patient receives daily CBCT for 25 fractions plus 5 additional verification CBCTs. What is the total imaging dose in cGy?
igrt.a: 75 cGy
c: 30 cGy
50 cGy
75 cGy
100 cGy
igrt.e: Total scans = 25 + 5 = 30. Total dose = 30 x 25 mGy = 750 mGy = 75 cGy. This represents a non-trivial dose that should be documented, especially for paediatric or young adult patients.

igrt.q: For a prostate patient with implanted fiducial markers, which imaging approach provides the fastest daily setup?
igrt.a: Orthogonal kV images with automatic marker detection
c: Full CBCT with soft-tissue matching
Orthogonal kV images with automatic marker detection
MV portal images with manual matching
Ultrasound-based localisation
igrt.e: Orthogonal kV images with automatic fiducial detection can be acquired and matched in under 30 seconds, significantly faster than CBCT. The gold markers provide high-contrast landmarks that are easily detected by automated algorithms.

igrt.q: What is the primary disadvantage of using fiducial markers for IGRT?
igrt.a: Invasive implantation procedure with associated risks
c: High cost of the markers
Invasive implantation procedure with associated risks
Markers are not visible on kV images
Markers cause excessive imaging artefacts
igrt.e: Fiducial marker placement requires an invasive procedure (e.g., transrectal or transperineal for prostate) with associated risks including bleeding, infection, and marker migration. Not all patients are suitable candidates.

igrt.q: Fiducial marker migration is most concerning for which treatment site?
igrt.a: Lung (risk of pneumothorax and migration within airways)
c: Prostate for quality assurance purposes
Brain per standard operating procedures
Lung (risk of pneumothorax and migration within airways)
Pelvis in clinical practice settings
igrt.e: Lung fiducial placement carries risks of pneumothorax, and markers can migrate through airways. Migration can lead to inaccurate target localisation if not detected. Some centres use bronchoscopic placement to reduce risks.

igrt.q: What is the No Action Level (NAL) offline correction protocol?
igrt.a: Average the first N setup errors and apply a single systematic correction for subsequent fractions
c: Apply every detected error immediately as a couch shift in the treatment process
Average the first N setup errors and apply a single systematic correction for subsequent fractions
Only correct errors larger than the action level in the radiation therapy context
Do not apply any corrections at all as specified by guidelines
igrt.e: The NAL protocol (de Boer and Heijmen) averages setup errors from the first few fractions (typically 3-5) to estimate and correct the systematic error. No action level threshold is applied; the mean correction is always used.

igrt.q: What is the Shrinking Action Level (SAL) offline correction protocol?
igrt.a: Apply a correction if the running average exceeds a threshold that decreases with more measurements
c: Apply corrections based on a fixed threshold throughout treatment
Apply a correction if the running average exceeds a threshold that decreases with more measurements
Reduce margins after each correction in the radiation therapy context
Only correct the largest single error observed as part of the clinical workflow
igrt.e: The SAL protocol (Bel et al.) uses a decreasing action level (alpha/sqrt(N)) as more measurements are accumulated, converging more quickly on the systematic error. It is more efficient than fixed action level protocols at eliminating systematic errors.

igrt.q: CALC: Using the NAL protocol, a prostate patient's first 3 fractions show lateral shifts of +2 mm, +4 mm, and +3 mm. What correction should be applied?
igrt.a: 3.0 mm
c: 2.0 mm
3.0 mm
4.0 mm
9.0 mm
igrt.e: NAL correction = mean of first N measurements = (2 + 4 + 3) / 3 = 9 / 3 = 3.0 mm. This correction is applied as a systematic shift to all subsequent fractions to remove the estimated systematic error.

igrt.q: What is the electron streaming effect in MR-guided radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Electrons streaming along the magnetic field direction causing dose enhancement along the B0 axis
c: Enhanced electron backscatter at high-Z interfaces for therapeutic applications
Electrons streaming along the magnetic field direction causing dose enhancement along the B0 axis
Increased penumbra in all directions for the treatment procedure
Dose reduction in the target volume in the healthcare environment
igrt.e: In addition to the electron return effect at tissue-air interfaces, electrons preferentially stream along the B0 field direction, causing asymmetric dose distributions and potential dose enhancement in tissues along the magnetic field axis.

igrt.q: Why must treatment planning algorithms for MR-Linacs account for the magnetic field?
igrt.a: The Lorentz force on charged particles alters dose deposition patterns, especially near tissue-air interfaces
c: The magnetic field changes photon beam energy for radiation dose management
The Lorentz force on charged particles alters dose deposition patterns, especially near tissue-air interfaces
MRI coils absorb significant radiation in the treatment process in the radiation therapy context
The magnetic field causes beam divergence throughout the treatment course
igrt.e: The external magnetic field deflects secondary electrons via the Lorentz force, causing dose distribution changes. Monte Carlo or dedicated algorithms that model electron transport in a magnetic field are required for accurate dose calculation on MR-Linacs.

igrt.q: In the Elekta Unity MR-Linac adaptive workflow, what is the typical online session time target?
igrt.a: Approximately 15-30 minutes for the complete adapt-and-treat session
c: Under 5 minutes throughout the treatment course
Approximately 15-30 minutes for the complete adapt-and-treat session
1-2 hours in the treatment process during the planning stage
Same as conventional treatment (5-10 minutes)
igrt.e: The Unity online adaptive workflow targets approximately 15-30 minutes including MR acquisition, recontouring/review, plan re-optimisation, plan QA, and treatment delivery. Reducing this time is an active area of development.

igrt.q: What are the two adaptive workflow options on the Elekta Unity?
igrt.a: Adapt-to-Position (ATP) and Adapt-to-Shape (ATS)
c: Plan-of-the-Day and Re-optimise
Adapt-to-Position (ATP) and Adapt-to-Shape (ATS)
Online and Offline adaptation
Manual and Automatic adaptation
igrt.e: ATP re-positions the original plan isocentre based on daily anatomy (fast). ATS involves recontouring structures on the daily MRI and full plan re-optimisation (slower but accounts for anatomical changes). ATS is the more commonly used workflow.

igrt.q: What is the primary challenge of using MRI for treatment planning dose calculation?
igrt.a: MRI does not provide electron density information needed for dose calculation
c: MRI images are too low resolution per standard operating procedures
MRI does not provide electron density information needed for dose calculation
MRI is too expensive for routine use for the treatment procedure
MRI cannot image bony anatomy for therapeutic applications
igrt.e: MRI provides excellent soft-tissue contrast but no direct electron density data. Synthetic CT generation (from MRI using atlas-based or deep learning methods) or bulk density assignment is needed for dose calculation in MR-only or MR-Linac workflows.

igrt.q: What is a synthetic CT (sCT) in the context of MR-guided RT?
igrt.a: A pseudo-CT image generated from MRI data for dose calculation purposes
c: A CT scan acquired with contrast enhancement
A pseudo-CT image generated from MRI data for dose calculation purposes
A low-dose CT used for screening
A CT scan with artificially enhanced resolution
igrt.e: Synthetic CT images are generated from MRI using atlas-based registration, bulk density override, or increasingly deep learning methods. They provide the electron density information needed for accurate photon dose calculation without requiring an actual CT scan.

igrt.q: CALC: A patient's systematic setup error is estimated from 4 fractions with measurements of 1.0, 2.0, 1.5, and 2.5 mm. What is the estimated systematic error?
igrt.e: Systematic error estimate = mean = (1.0 + 2.0 + 1.5 + 2.5) / 4 = 7.0 / 4 = 1.75 mm. This represents the best estimate of the consistent positioning bias for this patient.

igrt.q: In the context of IGRT, what does the acronym DRR stand for?
igrt.a: Digitally reconstructed radiograph
c: Digital radiation record
Digitally reconstructed radiograph
Dose reduction ratio
Dynamic reference registration
igrt.e: A DRR is a synthetic 2D projection image computed from a 3D CT dataset by ray-tracing through the volume. It simulates what a conventional radiograph would look like and serves as the reference for 2D image matching.

igrt.q: Which image registration metric is most suitable for registering images from the same modality (e.g., CBCT to CT)?
igrt.a: Cross-correlation
c: Cross-correlation
Mutual information
Sum of squared differences (only for identical scanners)
Feature-based matching
igrt.e: Cross-correlation is well-suited for mono-modality registration as it assumes a linear relationship between image intensities. Mutual information works for both mono- and multi-modality but is computationally more expensive.

igrt.q: What type of DIR algorithm uses a biomechanical model of tissue properties?
igrt.a: Finite element method (FEM)-based DIR
c: B-spline free-form deformation
Demons algorithm
Finite element method (FEM)-based DIR
Optical flow-based DIR
igrt.e: FEM-based DIR incorporates tissue mechanical properties (elasticity, compressibility) into the deformation model. This produces physically realistic deformations and can handle sliding motion at tissue interfaces better than purely intensity-based methods.

igrt.q: What is inverse consistency in deformable image registration?
igrt.a: The forward and inverse deformation fields should be inverses of each other
c: The deformation should be symmetric about the midpoint
The forward and inverse deformation fields should be inverses of each other
The registration result should not depend on image orientation
The DVF magnitude should be uniform
igrt.e: Inverse consistency means that registering image A to B and then B to A should yield deformations that are exact inverses. Violations of inverse consistency indicate registration inaccuracies. TG-132 recommends this as a DIR quality metric.

igrt.q: What is the Jacobian determinant used for in DIR quality assessment?
igrt.a: Detecting non-physical deformations such as tissue folding or tearing
c: Measuring image contrast as part of the clinical workflow
Calculating registration speed for accurate dose delivery
Detecting non-physical deformations such as tissue folding or tearing
Determining optimal image resolution for quality assurance purposes
igrt.e: The Jacobian determinant of the DVF indicates local volume change. Values less than or equal to zero indicate non-physical folding/tearing of the deformation field. Values near 1 indicate volume preservation. This is a key DIR validation metric.

igrt.q: What is dose accumulation in adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Mapping and summing dose distributions from multiple fractions onto a common anatomy using DIR
c: Adding up monitor units across fractions in the radiation therapy context
Mapping and summing dose distributions from multiple fractions onto a common anatomy using DIR
Calculating the dose rate at each gantry angle as specified by guidelines
Measuring cumulative imaging dose within the treatment system
igrt.e: Dose accumulation uses DIR to map daily dose distributions (which may be on different anatomies) back to a reference anatomy, enabling assessment of the true cumulative dose to each structure over the entire treatment course.

igrt.q: CALC: A CBCT scan uses 120 kV, 20 mA, and 20 ms per frame with 600 frames. What is the total mAs for the scan?
igrt.a: 240 mAs
c: 120 mAs
240 mAs
360 mAs
600 mAs
igrt.e: Total mAs = mA x ms/frame x number of frames / 1000 = 20 x 20 x 600 / 1000 = 240 mAs. The mAs determines the imaging dose and image quality (noise). Lower mAs reduces dose but increases noise.

igrt.q: In respiratory-gated radiotherapy, what is the 'baseline drift' problem?
igrt.a: Gradual shift in the resting position of the breathing signal over the treatment session
c: Detector calibration error for therapeutic applications
Gradual shift in the resting position of the breathing signal over the treatment session
Beam energy variation during gating as specified by guidelines
MLC speed limitation during beam-on as specified by guidelines
igrt.e: Baseline drift occurs when the patient's average breathing position changes during treatment (e.g., due to relaxation or muscle fatigue). This can cause the gating window to no longer correspond to the intended anatomical position, potentially leading to geographic miss.

igrt.q: Which respiratory motion management technique eliminates organ motion rather than compensating for it?
igrt.a: Breath-hold (DIBH/ABC)
c: Respiratory gating
Real-time tumour tracking
Breath-hold (DIBH/ABC)
ITV-based planning
igrt.e: Breath-hold techniques effectively freeze the anatomy in a fixed position, eliminating respiratory motion during beam delivery. Other techniques accommodate or track the motion but do not eliminate it.

igrt.q: What is the ITV (Internal Target Volume) concept?
igrt.a: A volume encompassing the CTV throughout its range of internal motion
c: A volume equivalent to the GTV plus a microscopic disease margin
A volume encompassing the CTV throughout its range of internal motion
The volume irradiated to the prescribed dose
A margin for setup errors only
igrt.e: The ITV (introduced in ICRU 62) encompasses the CTV through all phases of internal physiological motion (breathing, peristalsis, cardiac, etc.). It is commonly defined as the union of CTVs from all phases of a 4DCT. PTV = ITV + setup margin.

igrt.q: What is the MIP (Maximum Intensity Projection) used for in 4DCT?
igrt.a: Creating a composite image showing the maximum HU at each voxel across all phases for ITV delineation
c: Generating a minimum dose projection in the radiation therapy context
Creating a composite image showing the maximum HU at each voxel across all phases for ITV delineation
Calculating average tumour motion for quality assurance purposes
Producing a cine-mode display of breathing per standard operating procedures
igrt.e: MIP images take the highest voxel value across all respiratory phases. For lung tumours (high contrast against aerated lung), MIP effectively shows the entire motion envelope, facilitating ITV contouring. It is less useful in abdomen or mediastinum.

igrt.q: The average intensity projection (AIP/AVG) from a 4DCT represents:
igrt.a: The time-weighted average anatomy over all respiratory phases
c: The maximum tumour extent for quality assurance purposes
The minimum tumour extent during the planning stage
The time-weighted average anatomy over all respiratory phases
A single respiratory phase at end-exhale
igrt.e: The AIP averages voxel values across all phases, producing an image representing the average anatomy. It is useful for generating the reference image for dose calculation in ITV-based planning and shows the blurred/averaged position of moving structures.

igrt.q: What is the typical magnitude of diaphragm motion during normal tidal breathing?
c: 1-2 mm
igrt.e: During quiet tidal breathing, the diaphragm typically moves 10-20 mm in the superior-inferior direction. Tumours near the diaphragm (lower lung, liver, kidney) can exhibit similar magnitudes of respiratory motion.

igrt.q: CALC: A lung tumour moves 15 mm in SI and 5 mm in AP during breathing. What is the 3D motion vector magnitude?
igrt.e: Assuming negligible lateral motion, 3D vector = sqrt(15^2 + 5^2) = sqrt(225 + 25) = sqrt(250) = 15.8 mm. This total excursion determines the ITV size and the potential benefit of motion management.

igrt.q: In a resource-limited South African radiotherapy centre, which IGRT approach is most feasible for initial implementation?
igrt.a: 2D kV orthogonal imaging with bony anatomy matching
c: Daily MR-guided adaptive therapy
2D kV orthogonal imaging with bony anatomy matching
Real-time electromagnetic transponder tracking
Daily 4D CBCT with deformable registration
igrt.e: 2D kV imaging is the most basic and affordable IGRT approach, available on most modern linacs. It requires minimal additional hardware and training, making it suitable for initial IGRT implementation in resource-constrained settings like many SA centres.

igrt.q: What IGRT technologies are most commonly available in South African radiotherapy departments?
igrt.a: EPID portal imaging and kV CBCT on newer linacs
c: MR-Linac systems at all major centres
EPID portal imaging and kV CBCT on newer linacs
Universal availability of real-time tracking systems
AI-based adaptive radiotherapy at all centres
igrt.e: Most SA departments have EPID capability, and newer linac installations include kV CBCT (OBI or XVI). Advanced technologies like MR-Linac, SGRT, and real-time tracking are limited to a few well-resourced centres. IGRT adoption varies significantly between public and private sectors.

igrt.q: What is the primary barrier to implementing daily CBCT-guided radiotherapy in many South African public hospitals?
igrt.a: Extended treatment time reducing already limited patient throughput
c: Lack of electricity in clinical practice settings
Extended treatment time reducing already limited patient throughput
CBCT being prohibited in South Africa in the treatment process
Patient refusal of imaging for radiation dose management
igrt.e: SA public hospitals face high patient loads with limited linac capacity. Adding 5-10 minutes per patient for CBCT acquisition and matching significantly reduces daily throughput. This must be balanced against the clinical benefit of improved targeting accuracy.

igrt.q: What is in-vivo dosimetry in the context of treatment verification?
igrt.a: Measuring dose delivered to the patient during treatment using detectors placed on or near the patient
c: Pre-treatment phantom measurements for accurate dose delivery
Measuring dose delivered to the patient during treatment using detectors placed on or near the patient
Dose calculation by the TPS for clinical implementation in radiation therapy practice
Post-treatment blood sample analysis during the planning stage
igrt.e: In-vivo dosimetry uses detectors (diodes, TLDs, MOSFETs, or OSLDs) on the patient's skin or in body cavities to measure the actual delivered dose. It serves as an independent check of the treatment delivery chain and is recommended by IAEA for error detection.

igrt.q: Which detector is most commonly used for in-vivo entrance dose measurement?
igrt.a: Semiconductor diode
c: Farmer ionisation chamber
Semiconductor diode
Film dosimeter
Calorimeter
igrt.e: Semiconductor diodes provide real-time dose readout, are small enough for skin placement, and have adequate sensitivity for entrance dose measurements. Correction factors for field size, SSD, temperature, and angular dependence must be applied.

igrt.q: What does the acronym SGRT stand for?
igrt.a: Surface-guided radiation therapy
c: Stereotactic gated radiation therapy
Surface-guided radiation therapy
Sequential gantry rotation therapy
Segmented grid radiation therapy
igrt.e: SGRT uses optical surface imaging to guide patient setup and monitor position during treatment. It provides a non-ionising, real-time complement to conventional X-ray based IGRT.

igrt.q: What is the role of artificial intelligence in adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Auto-segmentation of structures on daily images and rapid plan quality prediction
c: Replacing the radiation therapist at the console
Auto-segmentation of structures on daily images and rapid plan quality prediction
Directly controlling the radiation beam energy
Eliminating the need for quality assurance
igrt.e: AI enables fast auto-contouring on daily CBCT/MR images (critical for online ART timelines), dose prediction, plan quality assessment, and outcome prediction. It accelerates the adaptive workflow but requires physician oversight and validation.

igrt.q: Deep learning-based auto-segmentation for online adaptive RT must meet which critical requirement?
igrt.a: Speed (contouring completed within minutes) while maintaining clinical accuracy
c: 100% accuracy with zero errors for accurate dose delivery
Speed (contouring completed within minutes) while maintaining clinical accuracy
Ability to work without any training data in the treatment process
Direct dose calculation capability in radiation therapy practice
igrt.e: Online ART requires contours within 1-3 minutes to maintain feasible session times. Deep learning models can achieve this speed while approaching expert-level accuracy, though physician review remains essential for safety.

igrt.q: CALC: A DIBH treatment shows the heart moves 12 mm inferiorly from free-breathing position. If the original mean heart dose was 4.0 Gy, and the dose gradient is 0.2 Gy/mm, estimate the DIBH mean heart dose.
igrt.a: 1.6 Gy
c: 1.6 Gy
2.8 Gy
3.2 Gy
4.0 Gy
igrt.e: Dose reduction = 12 mm x 0.2 Gy/mm = 2.4 Gy. DIBH mean heart dose = 4.0 - 2.4 = 1.6 Gy. This 60% reduction illustrates the significant cardiac sparing achievable with DIBH for left-sided breast treatments.

igrt.q: What is the typical gantry rotation time for a CBCT acquisition on a modern linac?
igrt.a: 30-60 seconds
c: 1-5 seconds
30-60 seconds
3-5 minutes
10-15 minutes
igrt.e: A full 360-degree CBCT acquisition typically takes 30-60 seconds of gantry rotation. Total time including reconstruction and matching may be 2-5 minutes. This is faster than MR-based imaging but slower than 2D kV radiographs.

igrt.q: What is the Varian TrueBeam kV imaging system called?
igrt.a: On-Board Imager (OBI)
c: XVI within the treatment system
On-Board Imager (OBI)
ExacTrac as part of the clinical workflow
iViewGT in the radiation therapy context
igrt.e: The TrueBeam uses the On-Board Imager (OBI) for kV radiography and CBCT. XVI is Elekta's system, ExacTrac is BrainLab's, and iViewGT is Elekta's older EPID-based MV portal imaging system.

igrt.q: In CBCT, what causes ring artefacts?
igrt.a: Defective or miscalibrated detector elements
c: Patient breathing motion within the treatment system
Defective or miscalibrated detector elements
Beam hardening through bone
Incorrect gantry speed in the treatment process
igrt.e: Ring artefacts appear as concentric circles in reconstructed CBCT images. They are caused by individual detector pixels that consistently read too high or too low, producing a systematic error at a fixed radial distance in the reconstruction.

igrt.q: What is the bowtie filter in CBCT and what is its purpose?
igrt.a: A shaped attenuator that reduces dose to the patient periphery and equalises detector signal
c: A filter that removes low-energy photons from the kV beam
A shaped attenuator that reduces dose to the patient periphery and equalises detector signal
A filter that shapes the MV treatment beam in the healthcare environment
An anti-scatter grid placed on the detector for the treatment procedure
igrt.e: The bowtie filter is thinner at the centre and thicker at the edges, compensating for the shorter path length through the patient periphery. This reduces peripheral dose, improves dynamic range utilisation, and reduces scatter.

igrt.q: What is the primary purpose of the kV source in a linac-based IGRT system?
igrt.a: Acquiring high-contrast images for patient positioning with low dose
c: Treating superficial tumours in radiation therapy practice
Acquiring high-contrast images for patient positioning with low dose
Calibrating the MV treatment beam for the treatment procedure
Providing backscatter measurements in clinical practice settings
igrt.e: The kV source is dedicated to imaging, providing superior soft-tissue and bony contrast at much lower dose than MV imaging. It operates independently of the treatment beam and is used for 2D radiographs, fluoroscopy, and CBCT.

igrt.q: What is the geometric relationship between the kV imaging system and the MV treatment beam on a typical linac?
igrt.a: The kV system is mounted at 90 degrees to the MV beam on the gantry
c: They share the same source according to clinical protocols
The kV system is mounted at 90 degrees to the MV beam on the gantry
The kV source is on the floor throughout the treatment course
They are on separate gantries within the treatment system
igrt.e: On Varian (OBI) and Elekta (XVI) systems, the kV tube and detector are mounted on retractable arms at 90 degrees to the MV beam axis. This orthogonal arrangement allows simultaneous treatment and imaging access.

igrt.q: CALC: If systematic error is reduced from 4 mm to 1 mm using daily CBCT IGRT, and random error remains 3 mm, how much does the van Herk margin decrease?
igrt.a: 7.5 mm reduction (from 12.1 mm to 4.6 mm)
c: 3.0 mm reduction for therapeutic applications
5.0 mm reduction for quality assurance purposes
7.5 mm reduction (from 12.1 mm to 4.6 mm)
10.0 mm reduction for clinical implementation
igrt.e: Without IGRT: M = 2.5(4) + 0.7(3) = 10.0 + 2.1 = 12.1 mm. With daily IGRT: M = 2.5(1) + 0.7(3) = 2.5 + 2.1 = 4.6 mm. Reduction = 12.1 - 4.6 = 7.5 mm. Daily imaging dramatically reduces systematic error, enabling significant margin reduction.

igrt.q: Which clinical scenario benefits MOST from online adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Cervical cancer with daily variations in bladder and rectal filling
c: Brain metastasis with rigid skull fixation
Cervical cancer with daily variations in bladder and rectal filling
Spine SBRT with bony matching as part of the clinical workflow
Extremity sarcoma with stable anatomy in the healthcare environment
igrt.e: Cervical cancer targets are surrounded by organs (bladder, rectum, bowel) with highly variable daily filling. Online ART can adapt the plan to the current anatomy, reducing margins and improving therapeutic ratio compared to a single static plan.

igrt.q: What is the role of quality assurance for SGRT systems?
igrt.a: Verifying geometric accuracy of surface detection, isocentric calibration, and camera alignment
c: Only checking camera focus in the healthcare environment according to clinical protocols
Verifying geometric accuracy of surface detection, isocentric calibration, and camera alignment
Measuring radiation dose from the projector as part of the clinical workflow
Testing network connectivity only during patient treatment sessions
igrt.e: SGRT QA includes daily isocentric verification, periodic geometric accuracy tests using calibration phantoms, camera alignment checks, surface reconstruction accuracy assessment, and integration testing with the linac beam control system.

igrt.q: What phantom is commonly used for CBCT image quality QA?
igrt.a: CATPHAN phantom
c: Farmer chamber
CATPHAN phantom
Alderson RANDO phantom
Cheese phantom
igrt.e: The CATPHAN phantom (Phantom Laboratory) contains modules for evaluating spatial resolution, contrast, uniformity, HU accuracy, and geometric accuracy. It is widely used for both diagnostic CT and CBCT quality assurance.

igrt.q: What CBCT image quality parameters should be assessed in monthly QA according to TG-179?
igrt.a: HU constancy, spatial resolution, contrast-to-noise ratio, uniformity, and geometric accuracy
c: Only spatial resolution according to clinical protocols
HU constancy, spatial resolution, contrast-to-noise ratio, uniformity, and geometric accuracy
Only geometric accuracy throughout the treatment course
Only noise levels in the treatment process during patient treatment sessions
igrt.e: TG-179 recommends monthly assessment of multiple image quality metrics. HU constancy (not absolute accuracy since CBCT HU is unreliable), spatial resolution, CNR, uniformity, and geometric accuracy are all critical for ensuring reliable image guidance.

igrt.q: What is the recommended tolerance for CBCT geometric accuracy (isocentre coincidence)?
igrt.a: Less than or equal to 2 mm
c: Less than or equal to 0.1 mm
Less than or equal to 1 mm
Less than or equal to 2 mm
Less than or equal to 5 mm
igrt.e: TG-179 recommends that the CBCT imaging isocentre should coincide with the radiation isocentre to within 2 mm. For SRS/SBRT applications, tighter tolerances (less than or equal to 1 mm) are typically required.

igrt.q: CALC: A patient is treated with 35 fractions of pelvis RT. Each fraction uses one CBCT (22 mGy) and two orthogonal kV images (0.5 mGy each). What is the total imaging dose?
igrt.a: 805 mGy
c: 770 mGy
805 mGy
840 mGy
1050 mGy
igrt.e: Per fraction: 22 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 23 mGy. Total = 23 x 35 = 805 mGy = 0.805 Gy. This cumulative imaging dose should be documented and considered in the risk-benefit assessment.

igrt.q: What is the primary concern with cumulative imaging dose from daily CBCT in paediatric patients?
igrt.a: Higher radiation sensitivity and longer life expectancy increase stochastic risk from imaging dose
c: Children cannot tolerate CBCT scans throughout the treatment course
Higher radiation sensitivity and longer life expectancy increase stochastic risk from imaging dose
CBCT image quality is worse in smaller patients in the radiation therapy context
CBCT causes immediate deterministic effects in children in the radiation therapy context
igrt.e: Paediatric patients have higher radiosensitivity and many more years of life for stochastic effects to manifest. The additional 0.5-1.0 Gy of imaging dose over a treatment course is proportionally more significant. Low-dose protocols and alternative guidance strategies should be considered.

igrt.q: What is the primary purpose of a region of interest (ROI) or clip box in CBCT-to-CT matching?
igrt.a: Restricting the registration to anatomically relevant structures and excluding interfering anatomy
c: Reducing the file size of the images within the treatment system
Restricting the registration to anatomically relevant structures and excluding interfering anatomy
Increasing the imaging dose to the region during the planning stage
Improving the CBCT spatial resolution throughout the treatment course
igrt.e: A clip box focuses the registration algorithm on the region most relevant to the target (e.g., prostate and surrounding structures). This excludes irrelevant anatomy (e.g., femoral heads, bowel gas) that might confound the registration.

igrt.q: CyberKnife uses which unique feature for beam delivery?
igrt.a: A compact linac mounted on a robotic arm with 6 degrees of freedom
c: A conventional C-arm gantry in radiation therapy practice
A compact linac mounted on a robotic arm with 6 degrees of freedom
A ring-based gantry like TomoTherapy according to clinical protocols
A gamma knife-like multi-source design during patient treatment sessions
igrt.e: CyberKnife mounts a compact 6 MV linac on a KUKA robotic arm that can direct beams from hundreds of non-coplanar angles. Combined with real-time tracking, the robot dynamically adjusts aim to follow target motion.

igrt.q: CyberKnife spine tracking (Xsight Spine) uses what for image guidance?
igrt.a: Stereoscopic kV X-rays matched to DRRs using bony anatomy features
c: MV portal imaging in the healthcare environment
Implanted fiducial markers only according to clinical protocols
Stereoscopic kV X-rays matched to DRRs using bony anatomy features
Surface optical tracking throughout the treatment course
igrt.e: Xsight Spine tracking uses two orthogonal kV X-ray sources to acquire images that are automatically registered to DRRs from the planning CT. The vertebral bony anatomy serves as an intrinsic landmark for frameless spine radiosurgery.

igrt.q: What is the difference between interfraction and intrafraction imaging?
igrt.a: Interfraction imaging occurs before each fraction for setup; intrafraction imaging occurs during beam delivery
c: Interfraction imaging occurs before each fraction for setup; intrafraction imaging occurs during beam delivery
They are the same process for accurate dose delivery for clinical implementation
Interfraction uses kV; intrafraction uses MV only for radiation dose management
Interfraction is weekly; intrafraction is daily per standard operating procedures
igrt.e: Interfraction imaging (pre-treatment CBCT, kV radiographs) corrects for day-to-day setup variation. Intrafraction imaging (kV monitoring, surface tracking, cine MR) detects and responds to patient/organ movement during the actual treatment delivery.

igrt.q: What is the purpose of a couch model (virtual couch) in the TPS for IGRT treatments?
igrt.a: Accounting for beam attenuation through the treatment couch in dose calculation
c: Ensuring the patient fits on the couch for clinical implementation
Accounting for beam attenuation through the treatment couch in dose calculation
Calculating couch weight limits during the planning stage
Modelling couch sag for positioning based on protocol requirements
igrt.e: The treatment couch attenuates posterior beams by 2-5% or more. Including an accurate couch model in the TPS ensures dose calculations account for this attenuation, preventing under-dosing of the target from posterior beam angles.

igrt.q: What is the relationship between IGRT frequency and PTV margin size?
igrt.a: More frequent IGRT enables smaller margins by reducing residual systematic errors
c: More frequent IGRT requires larger margins as specified by guidelines
More frequent IGRT enables smaller margins by reducing residual systematic errors
IGRT frequency has no effect on margins in the treatment process
IGRT always requires 0 mm margins per standard operating procedures
igrt.e: Daily online IGRT reduces systematic setup errors to the residual registration accuracy (typically 1-2 mm). This allows significant margin reduction compared to weekly or no image guidance. The van Herk formula quantifies this relationship.

igrt.q: CALC: A 4DCT shows a liver tumour at position 10 mm (exhale) and 30 mm (inhale) in the SI direction. If the gating window is set to the exhale phase covering 25-35% of the cycle, what is the approximate residual tumour motion within the gate?
igrt.a: Approximately 2-5 mm (10-25% of total excursion)
c: Zero motion in clinical practice settings
Approximately 2-5 mm (10-25% of total excursion)
10 mm according to clinical protocols
20 mm per standard operating procedures
igrt.e: With a gating window near exhale (most stable phase) covering about 30% of the cycle, the residual motion is typically 10-25% of the full excursion. For a 20 mm total excursion, this is roughly 2-5 mm. The exact value depends on the patient's breathing pattern.

igrt.q: What is MR-guided real-time adaptive gating on the MRIdian system?
igrt.a: Continuous cine MR imaging during treatment that automatically gates the beam based on target position
c: Pre-treatment MR imaging only for clinical implementation for therapeutic applications
Continuous cine MR imaging during treatment that automatically gates the beam based on target position
Respiratory belt-based gating for the treatment procedure per standard operating procedures
Gating based on external fiducial markers as part of the clinical workflow
igrt.e: MRIdian real-time adaptive gating acquires 2D cine MR images at several frames per second during beam delivery. The system tracks the target boundary and automatically holds the beam when the target moves outside a predefined boundary.

igrt.q: What is plan robustness in the context of adaptive RT?
igrt.a: The plan's ability to maintain acceptable dose distribution despite anatomical changes
c: The mechanical strength of the treatment couch based on protocol requirements
The plan's ability to maintain acceptable dose distribution despite anatomical changes
The speed of plan optimisation for quality assurance purposes
The plan's file storage reliability in the healthcare environment
igrt.e: A robust plan maintains acceptable target coverage and OAR sparing across a range of expected anatomical variations and setup uncertainties. Robustness evaluation helps determine when plan adaptation is necessary versus when the original plan remains adequate.

igrt.q: What triggers re-planning in offline adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Significant anatomical changes detected on imaging (weight loss, tumour shrinkage, organ filling changes)
c: Every treatment fraction automatically for radiation dose management
Significant anatomical changes detected on imaging (weight loss, tumour shrinkage, organ filling changes)
Equipment malfunction only in clinical practice settings for therapeutic applications
Patient request only per standard operating procedures throughout the treatment course
igrt.e: Offline ART monitors accumulated dose and anatomical changes over the treatment course. Re-planning is triggered when changes (e.g., tumour regression, weight loss, systematic organ displacement) cause unacceptable deviation from the planned dose distribution.

igrt.q: What is the maximum couch rotation correction typically available on a standard 6DOF couch?
igrt.a: Approximately 3 degrees in each rotational axis
c: 0.1 degrees for quality assurance purposes
Approximately 3 degrees in each rotational axis
15 degrees as specified by guidelines
45 degrees in radiation therapy practice
igrt.e: 6DOF couches (e.g., HexaPOD, PerfectPitch) typically allow rotational corrections up to about 3 degrees in pitch, roll, and yaw. Larger rotational errors require manual patient repositioning.

igrt.q: What is the role of the radiation therapist (RTT) in online adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Operating the system, verifying auto-contours, and escalating to the physician for approval
c: No role; the system is fully autonomous during the planning stage
Operating the system, verifying auto-contours, and escalating to the physician for approval
Only positioning the patient for quality assurance purposes
Only pressing the beam-on button for therapeutic applications
igrt.e: RTTs play a critical role in online ART: initial patient setup, monitoring auto-segmentation quality, adjusting contours when needed, coordinating with the physician for plan approval, and overseeing treatment delivery. The workflow requires enhanced RTT training.

igrt.q: CALC: An MR-Linac adaptive session takes 25 minutes on average. If a linac treats 30 patients/day in conventional 15-minute slots, how many adaptive patients can be treated in the same time?
igrt.a: 18 patients
c: 12 patients
18 patients
25 patients
30 patients
igrt.e: Total available time = 30 x 15 = 450 minutes. Adaptive patients = 450 / 25 = 18 patients. This 40% reduction in throughput is a major consideration for workflow planning and resource allocation.

igrt.q: What is the electron return effect most clinically relevant for?
igrt.a: Treatments near air cavities (lung, rectum, sinuses) on MR-Linac
c: All treatment sites equally in radiation therapy practice
Treatments near air cavities (lung, rectum, sinuses) on MR-Linac
Only brain treatments for therapeutic applications
Only prostate treatments throughout the treatment course
igrt.e: The ERE is most pronounced at tissue-air interfaces where electrons exiting tissue into air are curved back by the magnetic field. This causes dose hot spots at the exit surface, particularly relevant for lung, head/neck, and rectal treatments on MR-Linac.

igrt.q: What is the typical B0 field orientation relative to the beam in the Elekta Unity MR-Linac?
igrt.a: Perpendicular to the radiation beam (beam travels through the bore)
c: Parallel to the radiation beam in the healthcare environment
Perpendicular to the radiation beam (beam travels through the bore)
At 45 degrees to the beam in the radiation therapy context
The field orientation varies with gantry angle
igrt.e: In the Unity, the 1.5 T field is along the bore axis (S-I patient direction), and the beam enters perpendicular to this from the rotating gantry. The perpendicular arrangement causes the Lorentz force to curve electrons in the plane perpendicular to B0.

igrt.q: What shielding challenge does the MR-Linac present compared to a conventional linac?
igrt.a: The magnetic field requires special linac beam transport design and the treatment room needs MR-compatible RF shielding
c: No shielding is needed as specified by guidelines in the treatment process
The magnetic field requires special linac beam transport design and the treatment room needs MR-compatible RF shielding
Only lead shielding is needed in clinical practice settings as specified by guidelines
Standard bunker design is sufficient with no modifications as part of the clinical workflow
igrt.e: MR-Linacs require special considerations: the accelerating waveguide must be shielded from the magnetic field (or designed to operate within it), the treatment room needs RF shielding for MRI, and the magnetic fringe field affects room design and equipment placement.

igrt.q: Which image registration approach is required when the patient anatomy has changed significantly between planning and treatment?
igrt.a: Deformable image registration (DIR)
c: Rigid registration only
Deformable image registration (DIR)
No registration is needed
Manual point-based alignment
igrt.e: When anatomy deforms (weight loss, tumour shrinkage, organ filling changes), rigid registration cannot capture the local changes. DIR models voxel-level deformations and is essential for accurate dose accumulation and contour propagation in adaptive workflows.

igrt.q: What is contour propagation in adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Transferring contours from one image set to another using image registration
c: Drawing contours from scratch on every image in clinical practice settings
Transferring contours from one image set to another using image registration
Deleting contours that are no longer needed within the treatment system
Converting 2D contours to 3D volumes for clinical implementation
igrt.e: Contour propagation uses DIR to map contours from the planning CT to daily images. This saves time compared to re-contouring from scratch and is essential for online adaptive workflows where speed is critical.

igrt.q: CALC: A B-spline DIR uses a control point grid spacing of 10 mm in a 30 x 30 x 20 cm volume. How many control points are there?
igrt.e: Number of control points = (300/10) x (300/10) x (200/10) = 30 x 30 x 20 = 18,000. Each control point has 3 displacement components (x, y, z), giving 54,000 degrees of freedom for the deformation field.

igrt.q: What is the tolerance for daily IGRT couch shift corrections before requiring physicist review?
igrt.a: Typically 10-20 mm (larger shifts may indicate setup problems)
c: Any shift requires physicist review for quality assurance purposes
Typically 10-20 mm (larger shifts may indicate setup problems)
No tolerance limits are needed as specified by guidelines
50 mm in any direction for quality assurance purposes
igrt.e: Most institutions set action levels (e.g., 10-20 mm) beyond which a shift triggers review by a physicist or physician. Large shifts may indicate incorrect patient position, wrong isocentre, or anatomical changes requiring re-planning.

igrt.q: What is the main advantage of the Halcyon/Ethos bore-based design for IGRT?
igrt.a: Faster CBCT acquisition due to higher gantry rotation speed
c: Larger treatment field size in the healthcare environment
Higher beam energy options for quality assurance purposes
Faster CBCT acquisition due to higher gantry rotation speed
Ability to perform MR imaging for radiation dose management
igrt.e: The Halcyon/Ethos ring gantry design enables gantry rotation speeds up to 4 RPM (vs approximately 1 RPM for C-arm linacs), allowing CBCT acquisition in approximately 15 seconds. This significantly reduces imaging time and potential for motion artefacts.

igrt.q: What is an action level protocol for IGRT?
igrt.a: A set of rules defining when and how to correct setup errors based on their magnitude
c: A protocol for emergency situations only in the treatment process
A set of rules defining when and how to correct setup errors based on their magnitude
A calibration procedure for the imaging system based on protocol requirements
A patient communication protocol within the treatment system
igrt.e: Action level protocols specify thresholds for applying corrections: online (correct every fraction), offline (correct systematic errors only), or hybrid approaches. They balance correction accuracy against workflow efficiency and are site-specific.

igrt.q: What is the 'adapted plan' in online adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: A new or modified treatment plan generated during the treatment session based on daily anatomy
c: The original treatment plan during the planning stage in radiation therapy practice
A new or modified treatment plan generated during the treatment session based on daily anatomy
A backup plan for equipment failure per standard operating procedures
The physician's initial prescription in clinical practice settings
igrt.e: The adapted plan is created or selected in real-time based on the daily imaging, accounting for current anatomical configuration. It undergoes rapid QA checks and physician approval before delivery in the same session.

igrt.q: CALC: For a gated treatment with 25% duty cycle and a 4-second breathing cycle, what is the beam-on time per cycle?
igrt.a: 1.0 second per cycle
c: 0.25 seconds
1.0 second per cycle
2.0 seconds
3.0 seconds
igrt.e: Beam-on time = duty cycle x cycle time = 0.25 x 4 = 1.0 second per breathing cycle. The remaining 3 seconds are beam-off periods when the target is outside the gating window.

igrt.q: What is the purpose of a 4D CBCT?
igrt.a: Visualising respiratory motion at the time of treatment for verifying motion management strategy
c: Providing higher resolution images than standard CBCT for radiation dose management
Reducing imaging dose in radiation therapy practice in clinical practice settings
Visualising respiratory motion at the time of treatment for verifying motion management strategy
Replacing the need for 4DCT simulation in clinical practice settings
igrt.e: 4D CBCT sorts acquired projection data by respiratory phase, creating a time-resolved volumetric image. It verifies that the tumour motion at treatment matches the motion observed during simulation, validating the ITV or gating parameters.

igrt.q: What does 'geometric accuracy' refer to in IGRT QA?
igrt.a: The accuracy with which the imaging system localises the treatment isocentre
c: The shape of the radiation field for therapeutic applications
The accuracy with which the imaging system localises the treatment isocentre
The MLC leaf positioning accuracy in the treatment process
The accuracy of dose calculation during the planning stage
igrt.e: Geometric accuracy is the most critical IGRT QA parameter. It ensures that shifts determined from image registration correctly translate to couch movements that align the target with the radiation isocentre. End-to-end testing with known offsets is the gold standard.

igrt.q: What is an end-to-end test for IGRT?
igrt.a: A comprehensive test simulating the entire clinical workflow from imaging to treatment delivery using a phantom
c: Testing only the imaging system during patient treatment sessions in radiation therapy practice
A comprehensive test simulating the entire clinical workflow from imaging to treatment delivery using a phantom
Testing only the treatment beam in the radiation therapy context in radiation therapy practice
A test of the record and verify system during patient treatment sessions
igrt.e: An end-to-end test places a phantom with known geometry through CT simulation, planning, setup on the linac with IGRT, and treatment delivery. It verifies the entire chain including coordinate transfers, registration, couch corrections, and beam delivery accuracy.

igrt.q: What is the recommended frequency for IGRT geometric accuracy QA (isocentre coincidence)?
igrt.a: Daily
c: Annually
Monthly
Weekly
Daily
igrt.e: Geometric accuracy of the imaging isocentre relative to the radiation isocentre should be verified daily (typically as part of morning QA) because this is the fundamental basis for all IGRT corrections. Monthly comprehensive checks with phantoms supplement daily tests.

igrt.q: In MR-guided RT, what is the function of the beam gating boundary (BGD)?
igrt.a: A contour drawn on the MR image defining the acceptable target position for beam delivery
c: The maximum beam energy boundary throughout the treatment course
A contour drawn on the MR image defining the acceptable target position for beam delivery
The MLC field boundary for clinical implementation in clinical practice settings
The dose gradient limit for radiation dose management
igrt.e: On MR-Linac systems like MRIdian, the physician draws a gating boundary around the target on the cine MR image. The beam is only delivered when the target structure remains within this boundary, providing anatomy-based (not surrogate-based) gating.

igrt.q: What is the key advantage of anatomy-based gating on MR-Linac compared to surrogate-based gating?
igrt.a: Direct visualisation of the actual target rather than relying on an external surrogate correlation
c: Faster treatment times based on protocol requirements for clinical implementation
Direct visualisation of the actual target rather than relying on an external surrogate correlation
Lower imaging dose per standard operating procedures for radiation dose management
Simpler workflow as specified by guidelines for clinical implementation
igrt.e: External surrogates (abdominal markers, spirometry) may not perfectly correlate with internal tumour motion. MR-based gating directly visualises the target, eliminating correlation errors. This is particularly advantageous for abdominal targets where surrogate correlation can be poor.

igrt.q: CALC: A prostate SBRT treatment uses 5 fractions. Daily kV/kV matching takes 2 minutes and CBCT takes 5 minutes. If switching from kV/kV to CBCT matching, what is the additional total imaging time over the full treatment?
igrt.a: 15 minutes
c: 5 minutes
10 minutes
15 minutes
25 minutes
igrt.e: Additional time per fraction = 5 - 2 = 3 minutes. Total additional time = 3 x 5 = 15 minutes over the full course. This trade-off between time and soft-tissue visualisation must be considered in workflow planning.

igrt.q: What is the purpose of an imaging dose report for a radiotherapy patient?
igrt.a: Documenting cumulative radiation exposure from all imaging procedures during the treatment course
c: Reporting the treatment dose only for therapeutic applications
Documenting cumulative radiation exposure from all imaging procedures during the treatment course
Recording MRI scan times for quality assurance purposes during patient treatment sessions
Reporting room radiation survey results for quality assurance purposes
igrt.e: Per TG-180 recommendations, cumulative imaging dose from CT simulation, CBCT, kV radiographs, and portal imaging should be documented. This is especially important for paediatric patients and those receiving multiple courses of radiation.

igrt.q: What is the difference between rigid body registration and deformable registration in terms of degrees of freedom?
igrt.a: Rigid: 6 DOF (3 translations, 3 rotations); Deformable: potentially millions of DOF (per-voxel displacement)
c: Rigid: 3 DOF; Deformable: 6 DOF in radiation therapy practice according to clinical protocols
Rigid: 6 DOF (3 translations, 3 rotations); Deformable: potentially millions of DOF (per-voxel displacement)
Both have 6 DOF within the treatment system as specified by guidelines
Rigid: 12 DOF; Deformable: 6 DOF according to clinical protocols
igrt.e: Rigid registration has exactly 6 degrees of freedom. Deformable registration assigns a displacement vector (3 components) to each voxel, resulting in 3 x N degrees of freedom where N is the number of voxels, potentially millions of parameters.

igrt.q: What is the typical dose from a single MV portal image?
igrt.e: MV portal images deliver significantly more dose than kV images because they use the therapeutic beam. Typical doses range from 1-10 cGy per image depending on MU used and field size. This higher dose limits the frequency of MV imaging.

igrt.q: What is the Demons algorithm in deformable image registration?
igrt.a: An intensity-based DIR algorithm that uses optical flow concepts to compute voxel displacements
c: A rigid registration method for radiation dose management
An intensity-based DIR algorithm that uses optical flow concepts to compute voxel displacements
A feature-based registration technique as part of the clinical workflow
A mutual information optimisation method within the treatment system
igrt.e: The Demons algorithm (Thirion) models the registration as a diffusion process where intensity differences between images drive voxel displacements. Variants include symmetric, diffeomorphic, and log-domain implementations. It is widely used in radiotherapy applications.

igrt.q: What is the clinical significance of the 'dose painting' concept in adaptive RT?
igrt.a: Delivering heterogeneous dose within the target based on functional imaging to boost high-risk subvolumes
c: Painting the dose distribution on the patient's skin within the treatment system
Delivering heterogeneous dose within the target based on functional imaging to boost high-risk subvolumes
Using coloured dose washes in the TPS in clinical practice settings
Applying higher dose to all structures uniformly for quality assurance purposes
igrt.e: Dose painting uses functional imaging (PET, fMRI, DCE-MRI) to identify biologically distinct subregions within tumours. Adaptive RT can modify the dose distribution over the course of treatment based on tumour response, potentially improving local control.

igrt.q: CALC: An IGRT system has a registration accuracy of 1.5 mm. If this is the only source of systematic error and random error is 2 mm, what is the minimum achievable PTV margin using van Herk?
igrt.e: M = 2.5(1.5) + 0.7(2.0) = 3.75 + 1.40 = 5.15 mm. This represents the theoretical minimum margin achievable with this IGRT system, limited by registration accuracy and random intrafraction motion.

igrt.q: What is the purpose of the kV source model in CBCT dose estimation?
igrt.a: Characterising the X-ray spectrum and output for accurate patient dose calculation from imaging
c: Calibrating the treatment beam throughout the treatment course
Characterising the X-ray spectrum and output for accurate patient dose calculation from imaging
Optimising image quality only based on protocol requirements
Determining the focal spot size in the healthcare environment
igrt.e: Accurate CBCT dose estimation requires knowledge of the kV source characteristics including tube voltage, filtration, beam quality, and output. These parameters feed into dose calculation models (Monte Carlo or empirical) for quantifying imaging dose to the patient.

igrt.q: What is the Varian IDENTIFY system?
igrt.a: Varian's surface-guided radiation therapy system using structured light projection
c: A patient identification barcode system during patient treatment sessions
Varian's surface-guided radiation therapy system using structured light projection
A beam identification and verification tool according to clinical protocols
A radionuclide identification device within the treatment system
igrt.e: IDENTIFY is Varian's SGRT solution that uses structured light cameras to create a real-time 3D surface map of the patient. It provides setup guidance, intrafraction monitoring, and can interface with the linac for automated beam gating.

igrt.q: What is C-RAD Catalyst used for in radiation therapy?
igrt.a: Optical surface scanning for patient positioning, motion monitoring, and respiratory gating
c: Chemical analysis of radiation dosimeters during patient treatment sessions
Optical surface scanning for patient positioning, motion monitoring, and respiratory gating
Catalysing chemical reactions in gel dosimeters in radiation therapy practice
Accelerating dose calculation for clinical implementation
igrt.e: C-RAD Catalyst is an SGRT system using projected light patterns and camera detection to create real-time 3D surface maps. It supports patient positioning, breathing monitoring for gated treatments, and intrafraction motion detection.

igrt.q: What is the 'couch walk' test in IGRT QA?
igrt.a: Verifying that the couch moves accurately by the exact amount requested by the IGRT system
c: Testing the maximum weight capacity of the couch as part of the clinical workflow
Verifying that the couch moves accurately by the exact amount requested by the IGRT system
Walking around the couch during treatment during patient treatment sessions
Testing couch speed at different angles in the treatment process
igrt.e: The couch walk test applies known shifts via the IGRT system and verifies the actual couch displacement matches using an independent measurement (dial indicator, graph paper, or known phantom offset). This tests the entire chain from registration to couch motion.

igrt.q: CALC: A kV CBCT delivers 3 mGy per scan to the thyroid in a head-and-neck patient treated with 33 fractions. If the excess relative risk of thyroid cancer is 7.7 per Gy, what is the approximate excess relative risk from imaging?
igrt.e: Total thyroid imaging dose = 3 mGy x 33 = 99 mGy = 0.099 Gy. Excess relative risk = 7.7/Gy x 0.099 Gy = 0.76 or 76%. Note: this linear model is a simplification and the therapeutic dose to the region would dominate the risk calculation in practice.

igrt.q: What is the 'plan library' approach in adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Pre-generating multiple plans for anticipated anatomical variations and selecting the best daily match
c: A library of published treatment protocols for radiation dose management
Pre-generating multiple plans for anticipated anatomical variations and selecting the best daily match
A collection of template plans from other patients for the treatment procedure
Storing all historical plans in a database for quality assurance purposes
igrt.e: The plan library (plan-of-the-day) approach creates several plans covering expected variations (e.g., small/medium/large bladder). Daily imaging selects the best-matching plan. It is faster than online re-optimisation and does not require physician presence.

igrt.q: What is the primary advantage of the Elekta Unity 1.5 T MRI over the ViewRay 0.35 T for imaging?
igrt.a: Higher signal-to-noise ratio providing superior image quality
c: Lower cost for accurate dose delivery
Higher signal-to-noise ratio providing superior image quality
Smaller electron return effect for accurate dose delivery
Faster imaging time for radiation dose management
igrt.e: SNR scales approximately linearly with field strength. The 1.5 T Unity provides approximately 4x the SNR of the 0.35 T MRIdian, enabling higher resolution images and better tissue contrast. However, the higher field increases the electron return effect.

igrt.q: What is the Dice similarity coefficient used for in adaptive RT?
igrt.a: Quantifying the overlap between two contours to assess auto-segmentation or contour propagation accuracy
c: Measuring dose calculation accuracy during patient treatment sessions
Quantifying the overlap between two contours to assess auto-segmentation or contour propagation accuracy
Calculating registration speed for the treatment procedure in clinical practice settings
Measuring image noise according to clinical protocols in the treatment process
igrt.e: DSC = 2|A intersection B| / (|A| + |B|), ranging from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap). It is widely used to compare auto-generated contours against manual expert contours for DIR validation, auto-segmentation evaluation, and inter-observer variability assessment.

igrt.q: What is the Hausdorff distance metric used for in contour comparison?
igrt.a: Measuring the maximum distance between two contour surfaces
c: The average distance between contour centres
Measuring the maximum distance between two contour surfaces
The volume difference between contours
The dose difference at contour boundaries
igrt.e: The Hausdorff distance finds the maximum of the minimum distances between points on two contour surfaces. It captures worst-case local deviations, complementing volume-overlap metrics like DSC that may miss localised errors.

igrt.q: What is the primary safety concern with online adaptive RT?
igrt.a: Limited time for comprehensive plan quality checks increases risk of undetected errors
c: Excessive imaging dose throughout the treatment course
Limited time for comprehensive plan quality checks increases risk of undetected errors
Patient claustrophobia during patient treatment sessions
Equipment overheating in clinical practice settings
igrt.e: Online ART compresses the plan approval process from days to minutes. This leaves less time for physics checks, physician contour review, and plan evaluation, increasing the risk that errors in auto-segmentation, dose calculation, or plan quality go undetected.

igrt.q: CALC: An EPID-based transit dosimetry system measures 5% deviation between predicted and measured exit dose. If the prescribed fraction dose is 2 Gy, what is the absolute dose deviation?
igrt.a: 0.1 Gy
c: 0.1 Gy
0.5 Gy
igrt.e: Absolute deviation = 5% x 2 Gy = 0.10 Gy per fraction. Over 25 fractions, this could accumulate to 2.5 Gy if systematic, which is clinically significant. Investigation is warranted to determine if the deviation is real or artefactual.

igrt.q: What is the significance of the carina as an anatomical landmark in thoracic IGRT?
igrt.a: It is a stable bony-adjacent landmark used for matching when the tumour is not directly visible
c: It moves significantly with breathing as part of the clinical workflow
It is a stable bony-adjacent landmark used for matching when the tumour is not directly visible
It is only visible on MRI for accurate dose delivery for quality assurance purposes
It serves as the treatment isocentre in clinical practice settings
igrt.e: The carina (tracheal bifurcation) is relatively stable with respiration compared to lower lobe structures. It provides a useful surrogate landmark for setup verification in thoracic treatments when using 2D kV imaging and bony anatomy matching.

igrt.q: What is the emerging role of AI in IGRT quality assurance?
igrt.a: Automated detection of registration errors, anatomical changes, and treatment delivery anomalies
c: Replacing all QA procedures entirely according to clinical protocols
Automated detection of registration errors, anatomical changes, and treatment delivery anomalies
Only improving image reconstruction speed during the planning stage
Eliminating the need for physicists in the radiation therapy context
igrt.e: AI/deep learning is being developed to automatically flag poor registrations, detect clinically significant anatomical changes requiring re-planning, identify delivery errors from log files, and predict when adaptive re-planning will be beneficial, augmenting (not replacing) physicist oversight.

igrt.q: CALC: A Jacobian determinant analysis of a DIR shows a minimum value of -0.2 in a 5 cm^3 region. What does this indicate and is the DIR acceptable?
igrt.a: Non-physical tissue folding has occurred; the DIR is NOT acceptable in that region
c: Normal volume compression; acceptable in clinical practice settings
Slight volume expansion; acceptable during the planning stage
Non-physical tissue folding has occurred; the DIR is NOT acceptable in that region
Perfect volume preservation; ideal result throughout the treatment course
igrt.e: A negative Jacobian determinant indicates the deformation field has folded (topology violation where tissue passes through itself). This is physically impossible and indicates a DIR failure. The Jacobian should be positive everywhere; values near 1 indicate volume preservation, between 0 and 1 indicate compression, greater than 1 indicate expansion.

igrt.q: What is the typical imaging dose from a single CBCT acquisition?
igrt.a: Approximately 1-3 cGy per CBCT scan acquired
c: Approximately 0.01-0.05 cGy per CBCT scan
Approximately 1-3 cGy per CBCT scan acquired
Approximately 10-20 cGy per CBCT scan acquired
Approximately 50-100 cGy per CBCT scan acquired
igrt.e: A typical CBCT delivers approximately 1-3 cGy to isocenter.

igrt.q: What type of image registration maximizes mutual information?
igrt.a: Intensity-based automatic registration algorithm
c: Manual landmark-based registration by the user
Intensity-based automatic registration algorithm
Contour-based surface matching registration
Fiducial marker point-based alignment method
igrt.e: Mutual information works well for multi-modality registration.

igrt.q: What is the main advantage of MR-linac over conventional linac with CBCT?
igrt.a: Superior soft tissue contrast without additional radiation dose
c: Higher treatment beam energy for deep-seated tumors
Superior soft tissue contrast without additional radiation dose
Faster treatment delivery time for all clinical sites
Elimination of the need for any patient immobilization
igrt.e: MR-linac provides excellent soft tissue visualization without ionizing radiation.

igrt.q: What does the van Herk margin recipe (2.5 sigma + 0.7 sigma) account for?
igrt.a: Systematic and random setup errors for 90% population coverage
c: Only random daily positioning variations of patients
Systematic and random setup errors for 90% population coverage
Internal organ motion and deformation effects only
Dosimetric uncertainty from treatment planning systems
igrt.e: M = 2.5*Sigma + 0.7*sigma ensures 90% of patients receive at least 95% of prescribed dose.

igrt.q: What is the purpose of a 4D-CT scan in radiation therapy?
igrt.a: To capture respiratory motion and define an ITV
c: To improve CT image resolution for contouring
To capture respiratory motion and define an ITV
To reduce CT imaging dose to the patient overall
To create a synthetic MRI from CT data acquired
igrt.e: 4D-CT acquires images across the respiratory cycle.

igrt.q: What is SGRT primarily used for?
igrt.a: Real-time patient surface monitoring for position verification
c: Replacing all volumetric imaging during treatment
Real-time patient surface monitoring for position verification
Measuring skin dose during external beam delivery
Creating 3D surface models for bolus fabrication
igrt.e: SGRT uses optical surface imaging to monitor patient position.

igrt.q: What is deformable image registration (DIR) used for in adaptive RT?
igrt.a: Mapping contours and dose between different anatomies
c: Correcting for gantry sag in treatment machines
Mapping contours and dose between different anatomies
Improving kV image quality for setup verification
Calculating MU for adapted treatment plan delivery
igrt.e: DIR enables contour propagation and dose accumulation across fractions.

igrt.q: What is the Dice similarity coefficient used to evaluate?
igrt.a: Spatial overlap between two segmented volumes
c: Image quality of CBCT versus planning CT scans
Spatial overlap between two segmented volumes
Accuracy of dose calculation in a treatment plan
Magnitude of patient setup error in millimeters
igrt.e: DSC ranges from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).

igrt.q: CALC: Systematic error Sigma = 3 mm, random error sigma = 2 mm. What is the CTV-PTV margin using van Herk?
igrt.a: 8.9 mm
c: 5.0 mm
7.5 mm
8.9 mm

igrt.q: What respiratory management technique uses external surrogates to gate the beam?
igrt.a: Respiratory gating with an external marker system
c: Active breathing coordinator with spirometry valve
Respiratory gating with an external marker system
Free-breathing ITV approach with 4D-CT planning
Voluntary deep inspiration breath hold technique
igrt.e: External marker gating tracks a surrogate on the patient's abdomen.

igrt.q: What is the typical spatial resolution of a linac-mounted CBCT?
igrt.a: Approximately 1 mm at isocenter region
c: Approximately 0.1 mm at isocenter region
Approximately 1 mm at isocenter region
Approximately 5 mm at isocenter region
Approximately 10 mm at isocenter region
igrt.e: Linac CBCT achieves approximately 1 mm resolution.

igrt.q: What is an action level in IGRT setup corrections?
igrt.a: The threshold error requiring a positional correction
c: The maximum permissible imaging dose per fraction
The threshold error requiring a positional correction
The tolerance for image quality degradation over time
The minimum detectable shift by the imaging system
igrt.e: Typical action levels are 2-3 mm for standard sites.

igrt.q: What is the main limitation of 2D-2D image guidance compared to CBCT?
igrt.a: Limited soft tissue visualization in planar images
c: Higher radiation dose to the patient per acquisition
Limited soft tissue visualization in planar images
Inability to detect rotational setup error components
Longer image acquisition time compared to CBCT scan
igrt.e: Planar imaging provides excellent bony anatomy but poor soft tissue contrast.

igrt.q: What correction protocol applies the average of recent setup errors?
igrt.a: Extended no action level (eNAL) protocol
c: Online correction protocol with daily matching
Extended no action level (eNAL) protocol
Shrinking action level (SAL) offline protocol
Immediate full correction at every fraction
igrt.e: eNAL calculates running mean of setup errors as a systematic correction.

igrt.q: What CBCT artifact is caused by high-density materials like dental fillings?
igrt.a: Streak artifacts radiating from the dense object
c: Ring artifacts appearing as concentric circles
Streak artifacts radiating from the dense object
Motion blur artifacts from patient breathing
Cupping artifact from beam hardening effects
igrt.e: High-density materials cause photon starvation and beam hardening.

igrt.q: What is the role of fiducial markers in prostate IGRT?
igrt.a: To serve as surrogate for prostate position on imaging
c: To immobilize the prostate during beam delivery
To serve as surrogate for prostate position on imaging
To provide dose enhancement within the target volume
To create contrast for diagnostic MRI evaluation
igrt.e: Gold markers provide reliable prostate localization.

igrt.q: What is plan adaptation in online adaptive radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Modifying the treatment plan based on daily anatomy
c: Changing the prescription dose after five fractions
Modifying the treatment plan based on daily anatomy
Adjusting couch position to match the reference image
Switching beam energy based on patient body habitus
igrt.e: Online adaptive RT reoptimizes based on same-day imaging.

igrt.q: CALC: CBCT shows shifts of 4 mm right, 2 mm anterior, 3 mm superior. What is the 3D vector?
igrt.a: 5.4 mm
c: 3.0 mm
5.4 mm
9.0 mm

igrt.q: What is the primary challenge of using CBCT for dose calculation?
igrt.a: Inaccurate HU values due to scatter contamination
c: Insufficient spatial resolution for structure delineation
Inaccurate HU values due to scatter contamination
Excessive imaging dose that limits scan frequency
Incompatibility with treatment planning system format
igrt.e: CBCT suffers from scatter contamination and HU inaccuracies.

igrt.q: What does intrafraction motion refer to?
igrt.a: Patient or organ movement during a single treatment fraction
c: Setup error measured before each treatment fraction
Patient or organ movement during a single treatment fraction
Systematic change in anatomy across all fractions
The planned couch shift applied between beam deliveries
igrt.e: Intrafraction motion occurs during beam delivery.

igrt.q: What is the advantage of electromagnetic transponders (e.g., Calypso)?
igrt.a: Continuous real-time target tracking without radiation
c: Superior image quality compared to CBCT systems
Continuous real-time target tracking without radiation
Elimination of all patient immobilization devices
Ability to track all anatomical structures simultaneously
igrt.e: Electromagnetic transponders provide continuous localization at 10-25 Hz.

igrt.q: What is the 6DOF couch correction capable of?
igrt.a: Three translational and three rotational corrections
c: Six translational shifts in orthogonal directions
Three translational and three rotational corrections
Six rotational corrections around different axes
Three translational corrections with couch rotation
igrt.e: 6DOF applies lateral, longitudinal, vertical plus pitch, roll, and yaw.

igrt.q: What is the ITV concept and when is it used?
igrt.a: Internal target volume encompassing motion; used for mobile tumors
c: Irradiated tissue volume receiving the prescribed dose
Internal target volume encompassing motion; used for mobile tumors
Isocenter tracking volume for adaptive plan selection
Integrated treatment volume for combined modality therapy
igrt.e: ITV = CTV + internal margin for physiological motion.

igrt.q: What quality assurance test verifies imaging-treatment isocenter coincidence?
igrt.a: Winston-Lutz or BB phantom alignment test
c: Picket fence test for MLC leaf position accuracy
Winston-Lutz or BB phantom alignment test
Starshot test for radiation isocenter accuracy
Light-radiation field coincidence test on linac
igrt.e: BB phantom imaging verifies kV/MV isocenter coincidence.

igrt.q: What is DIBH used for?
igrt.a: Displacing the heart from the treatment field in breast RT
c: Improving CT image quality by eliminating motion blur
Displacing the heart from the treatment field in breast RT
Increasing tumor oxygenation during beam delivery
Reducing respiratory motion for all abdominal treatments
igrt.e: DIBH displaces the heart inferiorly and posteriorly.

igrt.q: What is the primary magnetic field strength of current MR-linac systems?
igrt.a: 0.35 T or 1.5 T depending on the vendor system
c: 0.1 T standardized across all vendor platforms
0.35 T or 1.5 T depending on the vendor system
3.0 T matching standard diagnostic MRI scanners
7.0 T for ultra-high resolution tumor imaging
igrt.e: ViewRay operates at 0.35 T, Elekta Unity at 1.5 T.

igrt.q: What is the electron return effect (ERE) in MR-linac treatments?
igrt.a: Electrons curve back to tissue interfaces due to magnetic field
c: Electrons are accelerated forward by the magnetic field
Electrons curve back to tissue interfaces due to magnetic field
Secondary electrons are completely absorbed by the field
The magnetic field reduces electron contamination at surface
igrt.e: Electrons follow curved paths due to Lorentz force, depositing extra dose at exit surfaces.

igrt.q: What does the Jacobian determinant of a deformation field indicate?
igrt.a: Local volume change: values below 1 indicate compression
c: The accuracy of the registration algorithm used
Local volume change: values below 1 indicate compression
The smoothness of the deformation vector field
The number of iterations needed for convergence
igrt.e: Jacobian < 1 indicates compression, > 1 expansion, negative values indicate folding (non-physical).

igrt.q: What is synthetic CT used for in MR-guided radiotherapy?
igrt.a: Converting MRI data to pseudo-CT for dose calculation
c: Creating diagnostic quality images from CBCT data
Converting MRI data to pseudo-CT for dose calculation
Generating 4D imaging from a single 3D acquisition
Replacing planning CT for all treatment planning needs
igrt.e: Synthetic CT assigns electron density values to MRI, enabling dose calculation without a separate CT scan.

igrt.q: What is the primary purpose of cone-beam CT (CBCT) in radiation therapy treatment delivery?
igrt.a: To verify patient anatomy and position immediately before or during treatment
c: To generate diagnostic-quality images for initial treatment planning purposes
To verify patient anatomy and position immediately before or during treatment
To calculate the delivered dose distribution for each individual treatment fraction
To replace conventional CT simulation for all radiation therapy patient setups
igrt.e: CBCT acquires volumetric images at the treatment unit, enabling comparison with planning CT to verify patient positioning and detect anatomical changes before irradiation.

igrt.q: What image registration method aligns bony anatomy between CBCT and planning CT images?
igrt.a: Rigid registration using translations and rotations in six degrees of freedom
c: Deformable registration that warps the image voxels to match soft tissue changes
Rigid registration using translations and rotations in six degrees of freedom
Mutual information-based automatic contouring of the target volume boundary
Fiducial marker triangulation using implanted radiopaque seed coordinates
igrt.e: Rigid registration matches bony structures by finding optimal translational and rotational shifts (6 DOF), assuming the skeletal anatomy does not deform between scans.
