igrt.q: What is the primary advantage of surface-guided radiation therapy (SGRT) over other IGRT methods?
igrt.a: Non-ionizing real-time patient surface monitoring without additional imaging dose
c: Higher spatial resolution for detecting internal organ motion during treatment
Non-ionizing real-time patient surface monitoring without additional imaging dose
Better visualization of deep-seated tumors compared to volumetric imaging methods
More accurate fiducial marker detection than conventional kilovoltage radiography
igrt.e: SGRT uses optical cameras to continuously track the patient surface in real time without radiation exposure, enabling intrafraction motion management especially for breast and SRS treatments.

igrt.q: In adaptive radiotherapy, what triggers the decision to replan a patient's treatment?
igrt.a: Significant anatomical changes detected on imaging that compromise plan quality
c: Completion of a predefined number of treatment fractions in the prescription
Significant anatomical changes detected on imaging that compromise plan quality
Changes in the treatment machine calibration output exceeding tolerance limits
Updates to the treatment planning system dose calculation algorithm version
igrt.e: Adaptive RT modifies the plan when imaging reveals changes such as tumor shrinkage, weight loss, or cavity filling that cause the original dose distribution to become suboptimal.

igrt.q: What is the typical imaging dose from a single pelvic CBCT acquisition on a linac-mounted system?
igrt.a: Approximately 10-30 mGy per volumetric CBCT acquisition of the pelvis
c: Approximately 0.1-0.5 mGy per volumetric CBCT acquisition of the pelvis
Approximately 1-3 mGy per volumetric CBCT acquisition of the pelvis
Approximately 10-30 mGy per volumetric CBCT acquisition of the pelvis
Approximately 100-200 mGy per volumetric CBCT acquisition of the pelvis
igrt.e: Pelvic CBCT delivers roughly 10-30 mGy per scan, which is a small fraction of the therapeutic dose but accumulates over a full treatment course and should be considered.

igrt.q: What is the purpose of a kilovoltage (kV) imaging system mounted on a linear accelerator?
igrt.a: To acquire 2D radiographs or 3D CBCT images for patient position verification
c: To deliver low-energy therapeutic beams for superficial skin cancer treatments
To acquire 2D radiographs or 3D CBCT images for patient position verification
To measure transmitted dose behind the patient for in-vivo dosimetry calculations
To provide real-time dose rate monitoring during megavoltage beam delivery
igrt.e: The onboard kV system (e.g., Varian OBI, Elekta XVI) acquires planar or volumetric images with superior soft-tissue contrast compared to MV portal imaging for position verification.

brachy.q: What is the half-life of Ir-192, the most commonly used HDR brachytherapy source?
brachy.a: 73.8 days
c: 59.4 days
73.8 days
87.4 days
120.4 days
brachy.e: Ir-192 has a half-life of 73.8 days. This necessitates source exchanges approximately every 3-4 months to maintain reasonable treatment times.

brachy.q: In TG-43 formalism, what does the dose rate constant (Lambda) represent?
brachy.a: Dose rate to water at 1 cm along the transverse axis per unit air kerma strength
c: Maximum dose rate at any point around the source
Dose rate to water at 1 cm along the transverse axis per unit air kerma strength
Ratio of dose in tissue to dose in air at the source surface
Dose rate at the prescription point normalized to activity
brachy.e: The dose rate constant Lambda is the dose rate to water at the reference point (r=1 cm, theta=90 deg) per unit air kerma strength. It includes effects of source geometry, encapsulation, self-filtration, and scattering in water.

brachy.q: Which radionuclide is most commonly used for permanent prostate seed implant brachytherapy?
brachy.e: I-125 is the most common isotope for permanent prostate seed implants. It emits low-energy photons (27-35 keV), has a half-life of 59.4 days, and delivers a prescription dose of approximately 145 Gy.

brachy.q: What is the ICRU definition of high dose rate (HDR) brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Dose rate greater than 12 Gy/h
c: Dose rate greater than 2 Gy/h
Dose rate greater than 6 Gy/h
Dose rate greater than 12 Gy/h
Dose rate greater than 20 Gy/h
brachy.e: Per ICRU: LDR is 0.4-2 Gy/h, MDR is 2-12 Gy/h, and HDR is >12 Gy/h. Modern HDR afterloaders deliver dose rates of several hundred Gy/h at 1 cm.

brachy.q: In the Manchester system for intracavitary brachytherapy, where is Point A defined?
brachy.a: 2 cm superior to the external os and 2 cm lateral to the uterine canal
c: 3 cm superior to the external os and 3 cm lateral to the canal
2 cm superior to the external os and 2 cm lateral to the uterine canal
2 cm inferior to the external os and 2 cm lateral to the canal
1 cm superior to the internal os and 2 cm lateral to the canal
brachy.e: Point A is 2 cm superior to the external cervical os along the uterine canal and 2 cm lateral. It approximates where the uterine artery crosses the ureter in the paracervical triangle.

brachy.q: CALC: An Ir-192 source has initial air kerma strength of 40,000 U. What is the approximate value after 74 days?
brachy.e: SK = 40000 x (1/2)^(74/73.8) = 40000 x 0.4993 = ~19,800 U. After approximately one half-life the air kerma strength drops to half.

brachy.q: What is the geometry function G(r,theta) for a point source in TG-43 formalism?
brachy.a: 1/r squared
c: 1/r squared
beta/(L*r*sin(theta))
exp(-mu*r)/r squared
brachy.e: For a point source, G(r,theta)=1/r^2 (inverse square law). For a line source, G(r,theta)=beta/(L*r*sin(theta)), where beta is the angle subtended by the source and L is active length.

brachy.q: What is a typical HDR cervical brachytherapy prescription combined with 45 Gy EBRT?
brachy.a: 7 Gy x 4 fractions to Point A or HR-CTV D90
c: 5 Gy x 3 fractions for accurate dose delivery
7 Gy x 4 fractions to Point A or HR-CTV D90
9 Gy x 2 fractions for the treatment procedure
4 Gy x 6 fractions for the treatment procedure
brachy.e: A common regimen is 7 Gy x 4 fractions combined with 45-50 Gy EBRT, giving a total EQD2 of ~85-90 Gy to the target.

brachy.q: What instrument is the clinical standard for brachytherapy source calibration?
brachy.a: Well-type ionization chamber (re-entrant chamber)
c: Farmer-type ionization chamber
Well-type ionization chamber (re-entrant chamber)
Parallel plate ionization chamber
Thimble chamber with build-up cap
brachy.e: A well-type (re-entrant) ionization chamber provides nearly uniform response along its length, ideal for measuring source strength. ADCLs calibrate these against NIST primary standards.

brachy.q: What is the unit of air kerma strength (SK)?
brachy.a: U (1 U = 1 uGy*m^2/h = 1 cGy*cm^2/h)
c: R/h at 1 meter throughout the treatment course
U (1 U = 1 uGy*m^2/h = 1 cGy*cm^2/h)
mgRaEq during patient treatment sessions
Bq/cm^3 during the planning stage
brachy.e: Air kerma strength is specified in units of U. TG-43 mandates SK, replacing older quantities like mgRaEq and apparent activity.

brachy.q: In GEC-ESTRO recommendations, what does HR-CTV stand for?
brachy.a: High-Risk Clinical Target Volume
c: High-Resolution Computed Target Volume
High-Risk Clinical Target Volume
High-Rate Cervical Treatment Volume
Hyper-Radiosensitive Clinical Treatment Volume
brachy.e: HR-CTV represents the macroscopic tumor extent at brachytherapy time, including the whole cervix and residual tumor. It is the primary target for dose optimization in IGABT.

brachy.q: What is the average photon energy emitted by Ir-192?
brachy.a: Approximately 380 keV
c: Approximately 28 keV
Approximately 140 keV
Approximately 380 keV
Approximately 662 keV
brachy.e: Ir-192 emits a complex gamma spectrum with average energy ~380 keV (range ~136 keV to 1.06 MeV). This intermediate energy allows reasonable tissue penetration with practical shielding.

brachy.q: CALC: An LDR I-125 prostate implant prescribes 145 Gy total. What is the approximate initial dose rate?
brachy.a: 7 cGy/h
c: 3 cGy/h
7 cGy/h
15 cGy/h
25 cGy/h
brachy.e: Initial dose rate = D_total x 0.693/T_half = 14500 cGy x 0.693/(59.4 x 24 h) = ~7 cGy/h, within the LDR range.

brachy.q: What does the radial dose function g(r) account for in TG-43?
brachy.a: Dose fall-off along the transverse axis due to scatter and attenuation, excluding inverse square law
c: Inverse square law dose fall-off in radiation therapy practice
Dose fall-off along the transverse axis due to scatter and attenuation, excluding inverse square law
Angular variation in dose rate according to clinical protocols
Source encapsulation effects only for radiation dose management
brachy.e: g(r) accounts for dose fall-off along the transverse axis due to absorption and scatter in water, with the geometry function (inverse square) factored out. Normalized to unity at r=1 cm.

brachy.q: What is the typical active length of an HDR Ir-192 source?
brachy.e: Modern HDR Ir-192 sources have active length ~3.5-5 mm and outer diameter ~0.9-1.1 mm, allowing travel through narrow catheters and applicators.

brachy.q: What is the GEC-ESTRO D2cc constraint for bladder in cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Less than 90 Gy EQD2 (alpha/beta = 3)
c: Less than 70 Gy EQD2 per standard operating procedures
Less than 80 Gy EQD2 in the healthcare environment
Less than 90 Gy EQD2 (alpha/beta = 3)
Less than 100 Gy EQD2 in the healthcare environment
brachy.e: GEC-ESTRO recommends bladder D2cc < 90 Gy EQD2. Rectum and sigmoid constraints are D2cc < 75 Gy EQD2.

brachy.q: In HDR brachytherapy, what is a dwell position?
brachy.a: A location along the applicator where the source stops to deliver radiation for a programmed time
c: The resting location of the source inside the afterloader in the healthcare environment
A location along the applicator where the source stops to deliver radiation for a programmed time
The patient position during treatment in radiation therapy practice
The treatment planning reference point based on protocol requirements
brachy.e: A dwell position is where the source pauses to deliver dose. Varying dwell times and positions allows dose distribution optimization.

brachy.q: What is the half-life of Pd-103 used in permanent prostate implants?
brachy.a: 17 days
c: 10 days
17 days
27 days
59.4 days
brachy.e: Pd-103 has a half-life of 17 days, shorter than I-125 (59.4 days), giving a higher initial dose rate. Pd-103 monotherapy prescription is typically 125 Gy.

brachy.q: What is the typical prescription distance for intraluminal bronchial HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: 1 cm from the source axis
c: 0.5 cm from the source axis
1 cm from the source axis
2 cm from the source axis
At the mucosal surface
brachy.e: Dose is typically prescribed at 1 cm from the source axis. Common fractionation includes 7.5 Gy x 3 or 10 Gy x 2 for palliative endobronchial treatment.

brachy.q: CALC: What is the EQD2 for 6 Gy x 5 fractions HDR (alpha/beta = 10 Gy)?
What does the anisotropy function F(r,theta) describe in TG-43?
brachy.a: Angular variation of dose rate around the source due to self-filtration and oblique capsule filtration
c: Inverse square law correction at different angles in the radiation therapy context
Angular variation of dose rate around the source due to self-filtration and oblique capsule filtration
Scatter dose at various angles for therapeutic applications for therapeutic applications
Radial dose normalization only during patient treatment sessions
brachy.e: F(r,theta) describes angular dose rate variation due to self-filtration, oblique encapsulation filtration, and radioactivity distribution. F(r,90 deg) = 1 by definition.

brachy.q: What applicator does the Fletcher-Suit-Delclos system use?
brachy.a: Tandem and ovoids
c: Ring and tandem
Tandem and ovoids
Cylinder applicator
Interstitial needle template
brachy.e: The Fletcher-Suit-Delclos system uses a tandem (intrauterine tube) and ovoids (colpostats in vaginal fornices).

brachy.q: What is the typical step size between dwell positions in HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.e: Typical step size is 2.5-5 mm, with 5 mm as the common default. Modern afterloaders can step as small as 1 mm.

brachy.q: What is the D90 prescription dose for I-125 prostate implant monotherapy?
brachy.a: 145 Gy
c: 100 Gy
125 Gy
145 Gy
160 Gy
brachy.e: I-125 monotherapy prescribes 145 Gy to D90. Pd-103 monotherapy prescribes 125 Gy to D90.

brachy.q: What is the primary advantage of a ring applicator over ovoids in cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Fixed geometry relative to the tandem improving reproducibility
c: Higher dose rate to Point A throughout the treatment course
Fixed geometry relative to the tandem improving reproducibility
Better vaginal mucosal coverage per standard operating procedures
Reduced bladder dose in all cases during patient treatment sessions
brachy.e: The ring provides a fixed geometric relationship with the tandem, improving inter-fraction reproducibility. Favored in MRI-guided brachytherapy (e.g., Vienna applicator).

brachy.q: CALC: An Ir-192 source was 41,000 U on Jan 1. What is the air kerma strength 45 days later?
brachy.a: Approximately 27,100 U
c: Approximately 20,500 U
Approximately 23,800 U
Approximately 27,100 U
Approximately 31,400 U

brachy.q: What key updates did TG-43U1 provide over TG-43?
brachy.a: Updated consensus datasets, eliminated apparent activity, clarified interpolation methods
c: Entirely new dose calculation formalism throughout the treatment course
Updated consensus datasets, eliminated apparent activity, clarified interpolation methods
Extended TG-43 to HDR sources only in the healthcare environment
Replaced line source geometry with Monte Carlo for accurate dose delivery
brachy.e: TG-43U1 (2004) provided consensus dosimetry datasets for multiple source models, eliminated apparent activity in favor of SK, and clarified interpolation and extrapolation methods.

brachy.q: What is transit dose in HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Additional dose deposited while the source travels between dwell positions and to/from the safe
c: Dose at the first dwell position in the healthcare environment
Additional dose deposited while the source travels between dwell positions and to/from the safe
Difference between planned and measured dose according to clinical protocols
Dose during source loading within the treatment system within the treatment system
brachy.e: Transit dose is unplanned dose deposited as the source moves. It is small but can be significant near catheter entry points and is not accounted for in most TPS.

brachy.q: What is the Xoft Axxent electronic brachytherapy source?
brachy.a: A miniature X-ray tube producing 50 kV X-rays
c: A miniature Ir-192 sealed source
A miniature X-ray tube producing 50 kV X-rays
A miniature Co-60 source
A beta-emitting P-32 source
brachy.e: The Xoft Axxent uses a miniature X-ray tube (up to 50 kVp). No radioactive source is needed; when powered off, no radiation. Used for breast, GYN, and skin treatments.

brachy.q: In COMS eye plaque brachytherapy, where is the dose prescribed?
brachy.a: The tumor apex (inner sclera plus tumor height, minimum 5 mm from inner sclera)
c: The center of the eye for quality assurance purposes
The outer scleral surface during the planning stage
The tumor apex (inner sclera plus tumor height, minimum 5 mm from inner sclera)
1 cm from the plaque surface as part of the clinical workflow
brachy.e: COMS prescribes 85 Gy to the tumor apex over 3-7 days using I-125 seeds in a gold-alloy plaque.

brachy.q: How often are HDR Ir-192 sources typically exchanged?
brachy.a: Every 3-4 months
c: Every 1-2 weeks
Every 3-4 months
Every 6-8 months
Annually
brachy.e: Sources are exchanged every 3-4 months when activity drops to ~25-35% of initial strength making treatment times impractical.

brachy.q: What is the GEC-ESTRO D2cc constraint for rectum in cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Less than 75 Gy EQD2 (alpha/beta = 3)
c: Less than 60 Gy EQD2 in radiation therapy practice
Less than 70 Gy EQD2 as part of the clinical workflow
Less than 75 Gy EQD2 (alpha/beta = 3)
Less than 90 Gy EQD2 for radiation dose management
brachy.e: Rectum D2cc < 75 Gy EQD2. Higher doses correlate with increased late rectal toxicity. Validated by the EMBRACE study.

brachy.q: What safety interlock must HDR treatment rooms have?
brachy.a: Door interlock that retracts the source if the door opens during treatment
c: Lead-lined ceiling during the planning stage
Door interlock that retracts the source if the door opens during treatment
Audible alarm at console only as part of the clinical workflow
Backup battery to continue treatment during power failure
brachy.e: Door interlocks retract the source to the safe if the door opens. Additional required features: radiation monitors, emergency retract buttons inside and outside the room, and backup battery for retraction.

brachy.q: CALC: What is the approximate TVL of lead for Ir-192?
brachy.a: 16 mm
c: 5 mm
16 mm
30 mm
50 mm
brachy.e: TVL of lead for Ir-192 is ~16 mm; HVL is ~5 mm. The intermediate energy (~380 keV) allows practical room shielding.

brachy.q: Why is source position verification performed before HDR treatment?
brachy.a: To confirm actual dwell positions match planned positions relative to the applicator
c: To measure source activity in the treatment process
To confirm actual dwell positions match planned positions relative to the applicator
To calibrate the TPS per standard operating procedures
To check room radiation levels for therapeutic applications
brachy.e: Errors in source positioning (indexer length, transfer tube, applicator reconstruction) cause significant dosimetric errors. Methods include autoradiography and dedicated QA devices.

brachy.q: What imaging modality is the gold standard for GEC-ESTRO image-guided adaptive brachytherapy?
brachy.a: MRI
c: CT
MRI
Ultrasound
PET/CT
brachy.e: MRI provides superior soft tissue contrast for delineating cervix, tumor (HR-CTV), and OARs. T2-weighted sequences are recommended by GEC-ESTRO.

brachy.q: How many catheters are typically used in prostate HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: 14-18 catheters
c: 4-6 for accurate dose delivery
8-10 per standard operating procedures
14-18 catheters
25-30 in the radiation therapy context
brachy.e: Typically 14-18 interstitial catheters placed transperineally under TRUS guidance. Number varies with prostate size and clinical scenario.

brachy.q: What does D90 represent in brachytherapy planning?
brachy.a: The minimum dose received by 90% of the target volume
c: Dose at 90 degrees from the source
The minimum dose received by 90% of the target volume
Dose rate at 90 cm in the healthcare environment
Dose in 90 minutes in clinical practice settings
brachy.e: D90 is a DVH parameter: the minimum dose covering 90% of the target. Primary metric for prostate seed implants and cervical IGABT (HR-CTV D90).

brachy.q: What is the half-life of Cs-137?
brachy.a: 30.17 years
c: 5.27 years
30.17 years
73.8 days
1600 years
brachy.e: Cs-137 half-life is 30.17 years, emitting a monoenergetic 662 keV gamma ray. Its long half-life was practical for LDR brachytherapy before HDR Ir-192 afterloading became standard.

brachy.q: CALC: If dose rate constant for I-125 is 0.965 cGy/(h*U) and SK = 2.5 U, what is the dose rate at the reference point?
brachy.a: 2.41 cGy/h
c: 0.965 cGy/h
2.41 cGy/h
4.82 cGy/h
9.65 cGy/h
brachy.e: At reference point (r=1 cm, theta=90 deg), g(1)=1, F(1,90)=1, so dose rate = Lambda x SK = 0.965 x 2.5 = 2.41 cGy/h.

brachy.q: What is the primary advantage of Co-60 over Ir-192 as an HDR source?
brachy.a: Much longer half-life (5.27 years) eliminating frequent source exchanges
c: Lower photon energy for thinner shielding
Much longer half-life (5.27 years) eliminating frequent source exchanges
Smaller physical source size
Better dose distribution from monoenergetic emission
brachy.e: Co-60 half-life is 5.27 years vs 73.8 days for Ir-192. However, higher energy (1.17 and 1.33 MeV) requires more shielding, and larger source size affects near-source dose distributions.

brachy.q: What is V100 in prostate seed implant evaluation?
brachy.a: Percentage of prostate volume receiving at least 100% of prescription dose
c: Volume receiving 100 Gy during patient treatment sessions
Percentage of prostate volume receiving at least 100% of prescription dose
Volume of the 100th seed for accurate dose delivery
The 100% isodose volume in radiation therapy practice
brachy.e: V100 should be > 90% for adequate coverage. V150 and V200 assess dose homogeneity.

brachy.q: What emergency equipment must be in an HDR brachytherapy room?
brachy.a: Long-handled forceps, shielded container, and radiation survey meter
c: Only a survey meter during patient treatment sessions
Long-handled forceps, shielded container, and radiation survey meter
Only emergency retract button and battery
Lead apron and thyroid shield during patient treatment sessions
brachy.e: Needed in case the source fails to retract: forceps to remove the applicator, a lead pig for storage, and a survey meter to confirm retraction. Emergency procedures must be posted.

brachy.q: Which MRI sequence is most useful for HR-CTV delineation in cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: T2-weighted
c: T1-weighted
T2-weighted
FLAIR
Diffusion-weighted
brachy.e: T2-weighted MRI shows residual tumor as intermediate-to-high signal within dark cervical stroma. GEC-ESTRO recommends T2-weighted for contouring.

brachy.q: CALC: An HDR plan has 20 dwell positions each with 8 s dwell time. What is the total dwell time?
brachy.a: 160 seconds
c: 80 seconds
120 seconds
160 seconds
200 seconds
brachy.e: Total = 20 x 8 = 160 s. Actual treatment time is longer due to source transit.

brachy.q: What is the Sievert integral used for in brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Calculating dose around a linear source by integrating point source contributions along active length with oblique filtration
c: Calculating biological dose equivalent for therapeutic applications during the planning stage
Determining shielding requirements in radiation therapy practice for quality assurance purposes
Calculating dose around a linear source by integrating point source contributions along active length with oblique filtration
Computing total integral dose throughout the treatment course within the treatment system
brachy.e: The Sievert integral integrates contributions from infinitesimal point elements along the source, accounting for oblique capsule filtration. Modern TG-43 uses tabulated parameters instead.

brachy.q: How often should well chamber constancy be verified for HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Before each source exchange with periodic check source verification
c: Annually only per standard operating procedures
Monthly with any source according to clinical protocols
Before each source exchange with periodic check source verification
Only at commissioning for radiation dose management
brachy.e: Constancy is verified before calibrating each new source. TG-56 recommends periodic checks using a long-lived source. Readings should be within 1% of baseline.

brachy.q: What distinguishes graphical optimization from inverse planning in HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Graphical manually adjusts isodose lines; inverse planning uses dose-volume objectives to optimize dwell times automatically
c: Graphical is faster but less accurate in radiation therapy practice for the treatment procedure
Graphical manually adjusts isodose lines; inverse planning uses dose-volume objectives to optimize dwell times automatically
Graphical uses CT while inverse uses MRI only for quality assurance purposes
They are the same technique for the treatment procedure during the planning stage
brachy.e: Graphical optimization interactively shapes dose by dragging isodose lines. Inverse planning (IPSA, HIPO) uses objectives to automatically calculate optimal dwell times.

brachy.q: What is the energy of the Cs-137 gamma ray?
brachy.a: 662 keV
c: 356 keV
511 keV
662 keV
1.25 MeV
brachy.e: Cs-137 emits a monoenergetic 662 keV gamma from Ba-137m daughter after beta decay.

brachy.q: In South Africa, which body licenses brachytherapy facilities?
brachy.a: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR)
c: Department of Health for accurate dose delivery
SAHPRA as part of the clinical workflow
The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR)
South African Bureau of Standards
brachy.e: The NNR issues nuclear authorizations and enforces compliance under the National Nuclear Regulator Act (Act 47 of 1999).

brachy.q: What is the typical prescribed dose for COMS I-125 eye plaque brachytherapy?
brachy.a: 85 Gy to the tumor apex
c: 45 Gy based on protocol requirements
65 Gy for quality assurance purposes
85 Gy to the tumor apex
100 Gy as part of the clinical workflow
brachy.e: COMS prescribes 85 Gy to the tumor apex over 3-7 days using I-125 seeds in a gold-alloy plaque.

brachy.q: CALC: EQD2 for 7 Gy x 4 fractions, alpha/beta = 3 Gy?
brachy.e: EQD2 = 28 x (7+3)/(2+3) = 28 x 10/5 = 56.0 Gy. High dose per fraction increases biological dose for late-responding tissues.

brachy.q: What is the purpose of applicator commissioning in brachytherapy?
brachy.a: To determine source path geometry, offsets, and verify TPS reconstruction parameters
c: To sterilize applicators during patient treatment sessions
To determine source path geometry, offsets, and verify TPS reconstruction parameters
To measure applicator attenuation in the healthcare environment
To ensure applicators fit patient anatomy throughout the treatment course
brachy.e: Commissioning determines source channel geometry, first dwell position offset, inter-channel spacing, and TPS reconstruction parameters. Critical for accurate dose calculation.

brachy.q: What is pulsed dose rate (PDR) brachytherapy?
brachy.a: An HDR source delivers small fractions (pulses) hourly to simulate continuous LDR radiobiology
c: Continuous LDR delivery with Cs-137 during patient treatment sessions
An HDR source delivers small fractions (pulses) hourly to simulate continuous LDR radiobiology
Alternating high and low dose rates in one treatment for therapeutic applications
Electronic brachytherapy with pulsed X-rays according to clinical protocols
brachy.e: PDR uses a stepping source to deliver short pulses (~10-30 min) every hour, simulating LDR radiobiology while using remote afterloading.

brachy.q: What is the recommended tolerance for HDR source position accuracy?
brachy.a: Within 1-2 mm
c: Within 0.1 mm
Within 1-2 mm
Within 5 mm
Within 10 mm
brachy.e: 1-2 mm accuracy is required. Steep dose gradients near sources mean small errors cause significant dosimetric deviations.

brachy.q: What is the decay mode of Ir-192?
brachy.a: Beta-minus (95%) and electron capture (5%)
c: Pure alpha decay for therapeutic applications
Pure beta-minus only during patient treatment sessions
Beta-minus (95%) and electron capture (5%)
Pure electron capture only
brachy.e: Ir-192 decays by beta-minus (95%) to Pt-192 and electron capture (5%) to Os-192. Resulting gamma rays produce the clinically useful spectrum.

brachy.q: What does afterloading mean in brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Applicators are placed first, then the radioactive source is loaded into them remotely
c: Source is placed directly into tumor during surgery
Applicators are placed first, then the radioactive source is loaded into them remotely
Source is loaded before the patient enters in the radiation therapy context
Radiation is delivered after patient leaves for quality assurance purposes
brachy.e: Afterloading places non-radioactive applicators first, verifies position with imaging, then loads the source remotely. This minimizes staff radiation exposure.

brachy.q: What is the purpose of Point B in the Manchester system?
brachy.a: To estimate dose to the obturator lymph nodes at the pelvic wall
c: To estimate the maximum rectal dose
To estimate dose to the obturator lymph nodes at the pelvic wall
To define the bladder reference point
To determine the tandem insertion depth
brachy.e: Point B is 5 cm from midline (or 3 cm lateral to Point A) at the same level. It represents the obturator lymph node region at the pelvic sidewall.

brachy.q: What is the typical outer diameter of an HDR Ir-192 source capsule?
brachy.a: Approximately 0.9-1.1 mm
c: Approximately 0.3-0.5 mm
Approximately 0.9-1.1 mm
Approximately 2.0-3.0 mm
Approximately 5.0 mm
brachy.e: The source capsule is ~0.9-1.1 mm in outer diameter, allowing passage through 6 French (2 mm) catheters.

brachy.q: CALC: What is the decay factor for Ir-192 after 30 days?
brachy.e: Decay factor = (0.5)^(30/73.8) = (0.5)^0.4065 = 0.756. About 75.6% of the initial activity remains after 30 days.

brachy.q: In South African regulations, what is the annual dose limit for a radiation worker?
brachy.a: 20 mSv averaged over 5 years, not exceeding 50 mSv in any single year
c: 10 mSv per year based on protocol requirements
20 mSv averaged over 5 years, not exceeding 50 mSv in any single year
50 mSv per year averaged over 3 years for radiation dose management
100 mSv per year with ALARA in clinical practice settings
brachy.e: South African regulations align with ICRP: 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 consecutive years, no single year exceeding 50 mSv.

brachy.q: What is the primary radiobiological advantage of HDR over LDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Shorter overall treatment time reducing tumor repopulation
c: Greater sublethal damage repair in normal tissues
Shorter overall treatment time reducing tumor repopulation
More uniform dose distribution
Higher oxygen enhancement ratio
brachy.e: HDR's shorter treatment time (minutes vs days) reduces tumor cell repopulation during treatment, important for rapidly proliferating tumors like cervical cancer.

brachy.q: What is the Ru-106 eye plaque used for?
brachy.a: Treatment of small to medium uveal melanomas and retinoblastoma using beta radiation
c: Treatment of brain metastases near the optic nerve
Treatment of small to medium uveal melanomas and retinoblastoma using beta radiation
Imaging of ocular blood flow for therapeutic applications
External beam treatment of orbital lymphoma during the planning stage
brachy.e: Ru-106 plaques emit beta particles (max 3.54 MeV from Rh-106 daughter) with rapid dose fall-off, ideal for shallow tumors < 5 mm thick.

brachy.q: What does IPSA stand for in HDR brachytherapy planning?
brachy.a: Inverse Planning Simulated Annealing
c: Integrated Planning System Algorithm
Inverse Planning Simulated Annealing
Isodose Point Source Approximation
Internal Prescription Surface Algorithm
brachy.e: IPSA uses simulated annealing optimization to find optimal dwell times based on dose-volume objectives and constraints in HDR brachytherapy TPS.

brachy.q: What is the recommended tolerance when calibrating a new HDR source against manufacturer certificate?
brachy.a: Within 3%
c: Within 1%
Within 3%
Within 5%
Within 10%
brachy.e: AAPM recommends agreement within 3% of the manufacturer's stated air kerma strength. Discrepancy exceeding 5% requires investigation before clinical use.

brachy.q: CALC: What is the air kerma rate at 1 m from a 370 GBq Ir-192 source (air kerma rate constant = 0.0361 uGy*m^2/(MBq*h))?
brachy.a: 13.4 mGy/h
c: 1.34 mGy/h
13.4 mGy/h
134 mGy/h
1340 mGy/h
brachy.e: Air kerma rate = 0.0361 x 370,000 / 1^2 = 13,357 uGy/h = 13.4 mGy/h at 1 m.

brachy.q: What is the GEC-ESTRO D2cc constraint for sigmoid in cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Less than 75 Gy EQD2 (alpha/beta = 3)
c: Less than 65 Gy EQD2 in the healthcare environment
Less than 75 Gy EQD2 (alpha/beta = 3)
Less than 85 Gy EQD2 for accurate dose delivery
Less than 90 Gy EQD2 per standard operating procedures
brachy.e: Sigmoid D2cc should be < 75 Gy EQD2, the same constraint as rectum.

brachy.q: What is the main disadvantage of CT-based planning vs MRI for cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Inferior soft tissue contrast making tumor delineation from normal cervix difficult
c: Higher radiation dose from imaging as specified by guidelines
Inferior soft tissue contrast making tumor delineation from normal cervix difficult
Inability to visualize the applicator in the healthcare environment
Longer imaging time for quality assurance purposes
brachy.e: CT provides poor pelvic soft tissue contrast, making tumor delineation difficult. CT contours tend to overestimate the target. However, CT is widely available and shows applicators well.

brachy.q: In interstitial breast brachytherapy (APBI), what is the typical HDR prescription?
brachy.a: 34 Gy in 10 fractions (3.4 Gy twice daily over 5 days)
c: 20 Gy in 5 fractions in the treatment process
34 Gy in 10 fractions (3.4 Gy twice daily over 5 days)
50 Gy in 25 fractions during the planning stage
45 Gy in 15 fractions in radiation therapy practice
brachy.e: HDR interstitial APBI delivers 34 Gy in 10 fractions (3.4 Gy BID) over 5 days per NSABP/GEC-ESTRO guidelines.

brachy.q: What type of radiation does I-125 emit?
brachy.a: Low-energy X-rays and gamma rays (27-35 keV)
c: High-energy gamma rays (1.17 and 1.33 MeV)
Low-energy X-rays and gamma rays (27-35 keV)
Beta particles only
Alpha particles and gamma rays
brachy.e: I-125 emits characteristic X-rays and gamma rays at 27-35 keV via electron capture decay. Low energy provides rapid dose fall-off and minimal shielding requirements.

brachy.q: What is TRAK in brachytherapy dosimetry?
brachy.a: Total Reference Air Kerma: the sum of air kerma contributions from all dwell positions, used as an integral dose indicator
c: Treatment Room Air Kerma for shielding calculations throughout the treatment course
Total Reference Air Kerma: the sum of air kerma contributions from all dwell positions, used as an integral dose indicator
Transverse Radial Air Kerma at 1 cm from source throughout the treatment course
Target Reference Absorbed Kerma for dose prescription in the treatment process
brachy.e: TRAK = sum(SK_i x t_i) for all dwell positions. It is an integral measure of the total radiation output of a treatment plan, useful for plan comparison and quality indicators. It cannot be derived from point dose alone.

brachy.q: What is the function of the indexer (check cable) in an HDR afterloader?
brachy.a: To verify catheter patency and measure channel length before sending the radioactive source
c: To shield the source during transit for quality assurance purposes
To verify catheter patency and measure channel length before sending the radioactive source
To measure source activity throughout the treatment course
To control dwell time for accurate dose delivery as part of the clinical workflow
brachy.e: The check cable is sent through each channel before treatment to verify the pathway is clear and measure length. If obstructed, the afterloader will not send the radioactive source.

brachy.q: What is the Vienna applicator?
brachy.a: A tandem-ring applicator with interstitial needle channels for combined IC/IS cervical brachytherapy
c: A tandem-ovoid for vaginal cuff treatment in clinical practice settings
A tandem-ring applicator with interstitial needle channels for combined IC/IS cervical brachytherapy
A cylinder applicator for endometrial cancer based on protocol requirements
An eye plaque for retinoblastoma for therapeutic applications
brachy.e: The Vienna applicator combines ring-and-tandem with interstitial needle channels in the ring, allowing combined intracavitary/interstitial treatment for tumors with parametrial extension.

brachy.q: What is the typical seed activity for I-125 prostate permanent implants?
brachy.a: 0.3-0.5 mCi per seed
c: 0.3-0.5 mCi per seed
1-5 mCi for radiation dose management
10-20 mCi in the radiation therapy context
brachy.e: Individual seed activities are 0.3-0.5 mCi (11-18.5 MBq). A complete implant uses 80-120 seeds, totaling ~25-50 mCi.

brachy.q: What is the ICRU reference point for the bladder in cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: The posterior surface of the Foley balloon on the lateral radiograph
c: The center of the bladder for quality assurance purposes
The posterior surface of the Foley balloon on the lateral radiograph
The bladder neck as part of the clinical workflow
2 cm superior to the symphysis pubis throughout the treatment course
brachy.e: The ICRU 38 bladder reference point is on the posterior surface of the Foley balloon (filled with 7 cc contrast), representing the closest bladder wall point to the sources.

brachy.q: CALC: A prostate implant has 100 seeds at 0.4 mCi each. What is the total implanted activity?
brachy.a: 40 mCi
c: 4 mCi
20 mCi
40 mCi
400 mCi
brachy.e: Total = 100 x 0.4 = 40 mCi (1480 MBq).

brachy.q: What is the dose rate effect in brachytherapy radiobiology?
brachy.a: At lower dose rates, increased sublethal damage repair occurs during irradiation, reducing biological effectiveness
c: Higher dose rates always produce more cell killing regardless of cell type
At lower dose rates, increased sublethal damage repair occurs during irradiation, reducing biological effectiveness
Dose rate has no effect on biological outcome per standard operating procedures
Lower dose rates cause more DNA double-strand breaks in clinical practice settings
brachy.e: At low dose rates, cells repair sublethal damage during irradiation, reducing biological effect per Gy. This is the basis for LDR's advantage: greater sparing of late-responding tissues relative to tumor.

brachy.q: What QA checks should be performed daily on an HDR afterloader?
brachy.a: Source position accuracy, timer accuracy, and radiation monitor function
c: Only visual inspection of the unit during the planning stage
Source position accuracy, timer accuracy, and radiation monitor function
Full source calibration with well chamber in clinical practice settings
Only door interlock verification for clinical implementation
brachy.e: Daily QA per TG-56/TG-59 includes source position check, timer accuracy, radiation monitors, door/console interlocks, audiovisual communication, and backup battery.

brachy.q: What is the EMBRACE study?
brachy.a: An international multicenter study on MRI-guided adaptive brachytherapy for cervical cancer
c: A randomized trial of surgery vs radiation for cervical cancer
An international multicenter study on MRI-guided adaptive brachytherapy for cervical cancer
A phase I dose-escalation trial for prostate brachytherapy
A trial comparing HDR vs LDR for endometrial cancer
brachy.e: EMBRACE demonstrated excellent local control (>90%) and acceptable toxicity with MRI-guided adaptive brachytherapy following GEC-ESTRO recommendations for cervical cancer.

brachy.q: What HR-CTV D90 goal is recommended for cervical cancer brachytherapy (combined EBRT + brachy)?
brachy.a: Greater than 85-90 Gy EQD2 (alpha/beta = 10)
c: Greater than 60 Gy EQD2 per standard operating procedures
Greater than 75 Gy EQD2 as specified by guidelines
Greater than 85-90 Gy EQD2 (alpha/beta = 10)
Greater than 100 Gy EQD2 as specified by guidelines
brachy.e: EMBRACE shows local control >95% when HR-CTV D90 exceeds 85-90 Gy EQD2. Below this, local control drops significantly for large tumors.

brachy.q: CALC: What is the dose rate at 2 cm from an Ir-192 point source if it is 400 cGy/h at 1 cm?
brachy.a: 100 cGy/h
c: 50 cGy/h
100 cGy/h
200 cGy/h
400 cGy/h
brachy.e: Inverse square law: D(2) = 400 x (1/2)^2 = 100 cGy/h.

brachy.q: What is the typical number of channels in an Elekta Flexitron HDR afterloader?
brachy.a: Up to 40 channels
c: Up to 10 for clinical implementation
Up to 20 during the planning stage
Up to 40 channels
Up to 60 during patient treatment sessions
brachy.e: The Flexitron supports up to 40 channels for HDR and PDR brachytherapy using Ir-192 or Co-60 with 1 mm minimum step size.

brachy.q: What is the anisotropy constant in TG-43?
brachy.a: A single value averaged over all angles to simplify 1D dose calculations when 2D F(r,theta) data is unavailable
c: Maximum anisotropy at 1 cm according to clinical protocols as part of the clinical workflow
Annual source calibration drift correction as specified by guidelines
A single value averaged over all angles to simplify 1D dose calculations when 2D F(r,theta) data is unavailable
The anisotropy at the source tip only per standard operating procedures
brachy.e: The anisotropy constant (phi_an) is the solid-angle-weighted average of dose rate over 4-pi relative to the transverse plane dose rate. It simplifies 1D calculations for seed implants.

brachy.q: What is a transfer tube in HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: A tube connecting the afterloader to the applicator, providing a pathway for source transit
c: A source calibration device in clinical practice settings
A tube connecting the afterloader to the applicator, providing a pathway for source transit
A room shielding component during patient treatment sessions
An applicator sterilization channel in the radiation therapy context
brachy.e: Transfer tubes connect afterloader output channels to patient applicators/catheters. Their length must be accurately known for treatment planning as errors affect source positioning.

brachy.q: What vaginal cylinder diameter is most commonly used for cuff brachytherapy?
brachy.e: Diameters of 2.5-3.5 cm are most common. The largest comfortable cylinder is chosen to maximize mucosal contact and push OARs away from the source.

brachy.q: CALC: An HDR source of 10 Ci initial activity: what remains after 148 days (two Ir-192 half-lives)?
brachy.a: 2.5 Ci
c: 2.5 Ci
5.0 Ci
7.5 Ci
brachy.e: After 2 half-lives: A = 10 x (1/2)^2 = 2.5 Ci.

brachy.q: What is the dose homogeneity index (DHI) in interstitial brachytherapy?
brachy.a: DHI = 1 - V150/V100, where higher values indicate more homogeneous dose
c: DHI = D90/D100 for radiation dose management
DHI = 1 - V150/V100, where higher values indicate more homogeneous dose
DHI = Dmax/Dmin for accurate dose delivery
DHI = V200/V100 for therapeutic applications
brachy.e: DHI = 1 - V150/V100. A value of 1.0 means no volume exceeds 150% (perfectly homogeneous). Typical values are 0.60-0.85. DHI + DNR = 1.

brachy.q: What is the primary concern with interstitial brachytherapy for head and neck cancers?
brachy.a: Risk of soft tissue necrosis and osteoradionecrosis due to high local doses
c: Inability to access the tumor during the planning stage
Risk of soft tissue necrosis and osteoradionecrosis due to high local doses
Excessive staff radiation exposure as specified by guidelines
Inability to achieve therapeutic dose rates for quality assurance purposes
brachy.e: High local doses risk soft tissue necrosis and mandibular osteoradionecrosis. Proper implant geometry, optimization, and mucosal spacing from mandible are essential.

brachy.q: What is the Paris system for interstitial brachytherapy?
brachy.a: A dosimetry system using parallel equidistant sources with the reference dose at 85% of the basal dose rate
c: A system using crossed needles in triangular arrangement as specified by guidelines
A dosimetry system using parallel equidistant sources with the reference dose at 85% of the basal dose rate
A loading pattern using variable-strength seeds according to clinical protocols
A dose optimization algorithm for HDR in the radiation therapy context
brachy.e: The Paris system uses parallel, equidistant, uniformly-loaded sources. The basal dose rate is the minimum between adjacent sources. The prescribed dose is 85% of basal dose rate, defining the reference isodose.

brachy.q: What survey is recommended after HDR source retraction?
brachy.a: Survey the patient with a calibrated detector to confirm the source has fully retracted
c: No survey needed if afterloader shows safe position based on protocol requirements
Survey the patient with a calibrated detector to confirm the source has fully retracted
Only survey room perimeter walls in the treatment process
Only check console display for radiation dose management
brachy.e: Surveying the patient/applicator region confirms the source is not stuck. This is an essential safety step before staff enter the room.

brachy.q: What is the typical treatment duration for an LDR Cs-137 intracavitary insertion?
brachy.a: 48-72 hours
c: 15-30 minutes
2-4 hours
48-72 hours
7-10 days
brachy.e: LDR treatments with Cs-137 required 48-72 hours of continuous irradiation at low dose rates (0.4-2 Gy/h) with the patient in a shielded room.

brachy.q: What is the ICRU reference point for the rectum in cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: On the anterior rectal wall, 5 mm behind the posterior vaginal wall on lateral radiograph
c: The center of the rectal lumen for therapeutic applications
On the anterior rectal wall, 5 mm behind the posterior vaginal wall on lateral radiograph
3 cm posterior to the tandem per standard operating procedures
At the anal verge for accurate dose delivery within the treatment system
brachy.e: The ICRU 38 rectal point is 5 mm behind the posterior vaginal wall on lateral radiograph, estimating maximum rectal dose. Now largely supplemented by volumetric D2cc reporting.

brachy.q: What is the advantage of HIPO over IPSA optimization?
brachy.a: HIPO limits dwell time gradients between adjacent positions, reducing hot spots along catheters
c: HIPO is faster in computation based on protocol requirements
HIPO limits dwell time gradients between adjacent positions, reducing hot spots along catheters
HIPO does not require contoured volumes for clinical implementation
HIPO uses a different source model in the healthcare environment
brachy.e: HIPO adds dwell time gradient constraints, preventing abrupt dwell time changes between adjacent positions that create undesirable hot spots along catheter tracks.

brachy.q: What is the main safety concern during HDR source exchange?
brachy.a: Proper handling, transport, and disposal of the decayed source and safe loading of the new one
c: Contamination of the afterloader room in clinical practice settings
Proper handling, transport, and disposal of the decayed source and safe loading of the new one
Damage to the afterloader motor for therapeutic applications
Reprogramming the TPS for accurate dose delivery within the treatment system
brachy.e: Source exchange involves handling high-activity materials. Key concerns include proper transport containers, radiation surveys, wipe tests, documentation, and verification of new source position and activity.

brachy.q: What regulation governs transport of brachytherapy sources in South Africa?
brachy.a: NNR regulations aligned with IAEA Transport Regulations (SSR-6)
c: Only the Road Traffic Act for quality assurance purposes
NNR regulations aligned with IAEA Transport Regulations (SSR-6)
SAHPRA pharmaceutical transport guidelines
No specific regulations exist for radiation dose management
brachy.e: Transport is regulated by NNR in alignment with IAEA SSR-6. Type B containers are required for HDR sources. Proper documentation and labeling are mandatory.

brachy.q: CALC: What is the EQD2 for a single 15 Gy fraction (alpha/beta = 3 Gy)?
What is the Quimby system in interstitial brachytherapy?
brachy.a: A system using uniformly-loaded sources giving non-uniform dose with higher central dose
c: A system using differentially-loaded sources for uniform dose
A system using uniformly-loaded sources giving non-uniform dose with higher central dose
A single central source with peripheral shielding for the treatment procedure
A computer optimization algorithm during patient treatment sessions
brachy.e: The Quimby system uses equal-activity sources distributed uniformly, producing non-uniform dose (higher centrally). The prescribed dose is the minimum peripheral dose.

brachy.q: What is the role of rectal retractors or packing in cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: To increase distance between sources and rectum, reducing rectal dose via inverse square law
c: To improve CT image quality for therapeutic applications
To increase distance between sources and rectum, reducing rectal dose via inverse square law
To fix applicator position only in clinical practice settings
To compress the bladder according to clinical protocols
brachy.e: Gauze packing pushes the rectum posteriorly. Even small distance increases significantly reduce rectal dose due to inverse square law, helping meet D2cc constraints.

brachy.q: What is the typical HDR Ir-192 source activity at installation?
c: 0.5-1 Ci according to clinical protocols
50-100 Ci for quality assurance purposes
500-1000 Ci based on protocol requirements
brachy.e: Initial activity of 5-10 Ci provides practical treatment times of a few minutes per fraction. Treatment times increase as the source decays.

brachy.q: What is the primary advantage of electronic brachytherapy over isotope-based?
brachy.a: No radioactive source handling, storage, or disposal requirements
c: Higher dose rates than Ir-192 in the treatment process
No radioactive source handling, storage, or disposal requirements
Better depth dose in the healthcare environment
MRI compatibility in radiation therapy practice
brachy.e: Electronic brachytherapy eliminates radioactive source concerns: no decay corrections, no NNR licensing for radioactive material, no emergency retraction, and minimal shielding due to low-energy X-rays.

brachy.q: CALC: How many HVLs of lead reduce Ir-192 exposure by a factor of 100?
brachy.a: Approximately 6.6 HVLs
c: Approximately 3.3
Approximately 6.6 HVLs
Approximately 10
Approximately 20
brachy.e: Number of HVLs = log2(100) = ln(100)/ln(2) = 6.64. With HVL ~5 mm lead, this requires ~33 mm.

brachy.q: What is the concept of dwell time modulation in HDR optimization?
brachy.a: Varying source dwell time at each position to shape the dose distribution
c: Changing the source step size as part of the clinical workflow
Varying source dwell time at each position to shape the dose distribution
Modulating source activity electronically during patient treatment sessions
Alternating between different sources for radiation dose management
brachy.e: Dwell time modulation is the primary optimization method: increasing dwell times at some positions and reducing at others shapes the dose to conform to target while sparing OARs.

brachy.q: What is the biological rationale for PDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: PDR simulates LDR radiobiology by allowing sublethal damage repair between hourly pulses
c: PDR delivers higher total dose than LDR in the treatment process
PDR simulates LDR radiobiology by allowing sublethal damage repair between hourly pulses
PDR provides better tumor control for radiation dose management
PDR eliminates normal tissue complications per standard operating procedures
brachy.e: PDR delivers ~0.5-1 Gy pulses hourly. Inter-pulse repair simulates continuous LDR, preserving the therapeutic ratio while allowing staff room access between pulses.

brachy.q: In the GEC-ESTRO framework, what is the IR-CTV?
brachy.a: Intermediate-Risk Clinical Target Volume: HR-CTV plus areas of potential microscopic spread
c: Internal Reference Clinical Treatment Volume during the planning stage
Intermediate-Risk Clinical Target Volume: HR-CTV plus areas of potential microscopic spread
Initial Response CTV after EBRT in radiation therapy practice
Irradiation Reference CTV according to clinical protocols
brachy.e: IR-CTV includes HR-CTV plus grey zones with potential microscopic disease at diagnosis. The IR-CTV D90 goal is typically >60 Gy EQD2.

brachy.q: What applicator is standard for vaginal cuff brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Single-channel vaginal cylinder
c: Tandem and ovoids
Single-channel vaginal cylinder
Interstitial template
Ring applicator
brachy.e: A single-channel vaginal cylinder is standard post-hysterectomy. Dose is prescribed at 5 mm depth, commonly 7 Gy x 3 fractions.

brachy.q: CALC: Dose at 3 cm from a point source if dose at 1 cm is 900 cGy?
brachy.a: 100 cGy
c: 50 cGy
100 cGy
300 cGy
450 cGy
brachy.e: Inverse square: D(3) = 900 x (1/3)^2 = 900/9 = 100 cGy.

brachy.q: What is the purpose of a wipe test on brachytherapy sources?
brachy.a: To detect surface contamination indicating compromised source encapsulation
c: To clean the source before insertion for therapeutic applications
To detect surface contamination indicating compromised source encapsulation
To measure source activity according to clinical protocols
To verify the source serial number for the treatment procedure
brachy.e: Wipe tests detect removable contamination indicating capsule damage. NNR requires testing every 6 months. Contamination >185 Bq (5 nCi) indicates a leaking source.

brachy.q: What is the typical fractionation for HDR vaginal cuff brachytherapy monotherapy?
brachy.a: 7 Gy x 3 fractions at 5 mm depth
c: 5 Gy x 2 at surface
7 Gy x 3 fractions at 5 mm depth
10 Gy x 5 at 1 cm
3 Gy x 10 at surface
brachy.e: 7 Gy x 3 at 5 mm from surface is a common adjuvant monotherapy regimen for intermediate-risk endometrial cancer post-hysterectomy.

brachy.q: What material are COMS eye plaques made from?
brachy.a: Gold alloy (Modulay)
c: Stainless steel
Gold alloy (Modulay)
Tungsten in the healthcare environment
Lead as specified by guidelines
brachy.e: Modulay gold alloy provides excellent shielding of low-energy I-125 photons, protecting posterior tissues while directing dose toward the tumor.

brachy.q: What is the effect of tissue heterogeneity on I-125 dose distributions?
brachy.a: Significant: photoelectric interactions dominate at low energy making dose sensitive to tissue composition
c: No effect since TG-43 accounts for tissue type in clinical practice settings
Significant: photoelectric interactions dominate at low energy making dose sensitive to tissue composition
Minor effect only beyond 5 cm during patient treatment sessions
Only affects lung tissue based on protocol requirements for quality assurance purposes
brachy.e: At 27-35 keV, photoelectric absorption dominates with strong Z-dependence. TG-43 calculates in water, so calcifications, bone, and plaque backing significantly affect actual vs predicted dose.

brachy.q: CALC: Combined EQD2 (alpha/beta=10) for 45 Gy EBRT (1.8 Gy/fx) plus 7 Gy x 4 HDR brachy?
brachy.a: Approximately 84 Gy
c: Approximately 65 Gy
Approximately 73 Gy
Approximately 84 Gy
Approximately 95 Gy
brachy.e: EBRT EQD2 = 45 x 11.8/12 = 44.25 Gy. Brachy EQD2 = 28 x 17/12 = 39.67 Gy. Total = 83.9 Gy, approximately 84 Gy.

brachy.q: What is the role of TRUS in prostate brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Real-time image guidance for seed or needle placement during the implant procedure
c: Post-implant dosimetry only per standard operating procedures
Real-time image guidance for seed or needle placement during the implant procedure
Source calibration verification in clinical practice settings
Treatment planning dose calculation in clinical practice settings
brachy.e: TRUS provides real-time prostate, needle, and seed visualization during both LDR and HDR procedures, enabling accurate template-guided needle placement.

brachy.q: What is dose-volume adaptation in image-guided brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Re-imaging and re-planning each fraction based on current anatomy and tumor response
c: Using the same plan for all fractions in radiation therapy practice
Re-imaging and re-planning each fraction based on current anatomy and tumor response
Applying population-based constraints without imaging
Scaling all dwell times uniformly for therapeutic applications
brachy.e: Adaptive planning re-optimizes each fraction based on current imaging, allowing dose escalation to residual tumor while respecting OAR constraints specific to each fraction's geometry.

brachy.q: What is the maximum beta energy of Ru-106 (via Rh-106)?
brachy.a: 3.54 MeV
c: 0.67 MeV
1.49 MeV
3.54 MeV
5.26 MeV
brachy.e: Rh-106 daughter emits beta particles with maximum energy 3.54 MeV. Rapid dose fall-off makes Ru-106 plaques suitable for shallow ocular tumors.

brachy.q: When should post-implant CT be performed for prostate I-125 dosimetric evaluation?
brachy.a: Day 30 post-implant to allow edema resolution
c: Immediately after procedure
Day 30 post-implant to allow edema resolution
Day 90 post-implant for radiation dose management
6 months post-implant for the treatment procedure
brachy.e: Day 30 allows prostate edema to resolve. Immediate post-implant edema artificially reduces D90 and V100.

brachy.q: CALC: Seeds needed for a 40 cm^3 prostate at 2.5 seeds/cm^3?
brachy.a: 100 seeds
c: 50 for accurate dose delivery
75 in the radiation therapy context
100 seeds
150 for accurate dose delivery
brachy.e: 100 = 2.5 x 40. This is a rough nomogram estimate; TPS determines final placement.

brachy.q: What is the maximum tumor thickness suitable for Ru-106 eye plaque?
brachy.a: Approximately 5 mm
c: Approximately 2 mm
Approximately 5 mm
Approximately 10 mm
Approximately 15 mm
brachy.e: Beta radiation has limited penetration; Ru-106 suits tumors up to ~5 mm. For thicker tumors, I-125 photon plaques (COMS) are preferred.

brachy.q: What is reference air kerma rate (RAKR)?
brachy.a: Air kerma rate at 1 m from the source in free space along the transverse bisector
c: Air kerma rate at the source surface
Air kerma rate at 1 m from the source in free space along the transverse bisector
Average air kerma rate over all angles at 1 cm
Air kerma rate inside the patient at the prescription point
brachy.e: RAKR is the ICRU/IAEA convention: kerma rate to air at 1 m, along the perpendicular bisector, corrected for scatter. Related to SK by: SK = RAKR x d_ref^2.

brachy.q: What is the typical dose rate at 1 cm from a 10 Ci Ir-192 source?
brachy.a: Approximately 400-500 Gy/h
c: 10-50 Gy/h throughout the treatment course
100-200 Gy/h in the radiation therapy context
Approximately 400-500 Gy/h
1000-2000 Gy/h during patient treatment sessions
brachy.e: A 10 Ci source produces ~400-500 Gy/h at 1 cm, allowing treatment in a few minutes. Well within HDR range (>12 Gy/h).

brachy.q: Why is tandem length measured in cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: To determine uterine cavity length for proper source loading and to avoid perforation
c: To calculate source activity needed in the radiation therapy context
To determine uterine cavity length for proper source loading and to avoid perforation
To measure cervical diameter within the treatment system
To estimate tumor volume in the healthcare environment
brachy.e: Tandem length guides active dwell position selection and prevents uterine perforation. Typical lengths are 4-8 cm.

brachy.q: CALC: If dose rate is 500 Gy/h at 1 cm, how long to deliver 5 Gy?
brachy.a: 36 seconds
c: 18 seconds
36 seconds
60 seconds
360 seconds
brachy.e: Time = 5/500 h = 0.01 h = 36 seconds.

brachy.q: What is the recommended inter-needle spacing in the Paris system?
brachy.a: 8-15 mm depending on target size
c: 2-4 mm based on protocol requirements
8-15 mm depending on target size
20-30 mm for the treatment procedure
40-50 mm during the planning stage
brachy.e: Paris system spacing: 8-15 mm for single-plane, 10-20 mm for volume implants. Sources must be parallel and equidistant with active length extending beyond the target.

brachy.q: How does dose rate affect the therapeutic ratio in brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Lower dose rates preferentially spare late-responding tissues (low alpha/beta), improving the therapeutic ratio
c: Lower dose rates kill tumors more effectively for quality assurance purposes
Lower dose rates preferentially spare late-responding tissues (low alpha/beta), improving the therapeutic ratio
Dose rate has no effect per standard operating procedures as specified by guidelines
Higher dose rates always improve therapeutic ratio based on protocol requirements
brachy.e: More sublethal repair occurs at lower dose rates. Late-responding tissues (low alpha/beta) benefit more from repair than tumors (high alpha/beta), improving the therapeutic ratio.

brachy.q: What is the required leak testing frequency for sealed brachytherapy sources in South Africa?
brachy.a: Every 6 months
c: Monthly
Every 6 months
Annually
Every 5 years
brachy.e: NNR requires leak testing every 6 months. Contamination >185 Bq (5 nCi) indicates a leaking source that must be withdrawn.

brachy.q: What is the role of a multichannel vaginal cylinder?
brachy.a: To allow asymmetric dose distributions for eccentric vaginal lesions while sparing adjacent OARs
c: To increase treatment dose for quality assurance purposes
To allow asymmetric dose distributions for eccentric vaginal lesions while sparing adjacent OARs
To treat the entire vaginal canal simultaneously in the treatment process
To replace imaging during treatment during patient treatment sessions
brachy.e: Multichannel cylinders (e.g., Miami applicator) have peripheral channels for differential loading, creating asymmetric dose for eccentric tumors.

brachy.q: CALC: BED for 7 Gy x 4 fractions with alpha/beta = 10 Gy?
What is the Utrecht applicator?
brachy.a: A tandem-ovoid applicator with interstitial needle channels through the ovoids for combined IC/IS treatment
c: A ring applicator without needles during patient treatment sessions
A tandem-ovoid applicator with interstitial needle channels through the ovoids for combined IC/IS treatment
A single-channel rectal cylinder throughout the treatment course
An electronic brachytherapy balloon throughout the treatment course
brachy.e: The Utrecht applicator enables combined intracavitary/interstitial treatment via needle channels through the ovoids, similar to the Vienna applicator's ring-based approach.

brachy.q: What is the half-life of Cs-131?
brachy.a: 9.7 days
c: 5.3 days
9.7 days
17 days
30 days
brachy.e: Cs-131 half-life is 9.7 days, delivering 90% of dose within 33 days. Attractive for rapidly proliferating tumors.

brachy.q: What is the typical prescription for intraluminal esophageal HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: 10-12 Gy in 2 fractions at 1 cm from source axis as a boost
c: 5 Gy single fraction at surface in clinical practice settings
10-12 Gy in 2 fractions at 1 cm from source axis as a boost
25 Gy in 5 fractions at 2 cm for the treatment procedure
40 Gy in 10 fractions at 0.5 cm for therapeutic applications
brachy.e: Esophageal boost: 10-12 Gy in 2 fractions at 1 cm from source axis, combined with EBRT. Also used for palliation of dysphagia.

brachy.q: What is the main limitation of TG-43 formalism?
brachy.a: Assumes homogeneous water medium, ignoring tissue heterogeneities, applicator shielding, and inter-source effects
c: Cannot calculate dose for HDR sources for radiation dose management
Assumes homogeneous water medium, ignoring tissue heterogeneities, applicator shielding, and inter-source effects
Only works for point sources in clinical practice settings for clinical implementation
Requires Monte Carlo for every patient as specified by guidelines for radiation dose management
brachy.e: TG-43 calculates in infinite homogeneous water. It misses patient heterogeneities, applicator shielding, and inter-source attenuation, causing errors especially for low-energy sources like I-125.

brachy.q: CALC: What fraction of total I-125 implant dose is delivered in the first half-life (59.4 days)?
brachy.e: For permanent implant: fraction in time t = 1 - e^(-lambda*t). After one half-life: 1 - e^(-ln2) = 1 - 0.5 = 50%.

brachy.q: What is the function of the safe (vault) in an HDR afterloader?
brachy.a: A heavily shielded container that stores the source when not treating, reducing radiation levels
c: A treatment planning workstation for radiation dose management
A heavily shielded container that stores the source when not treating, reducing radiation levels
A backup power supply in the radiation therapy context for therapeutic applications
An applicator sterilization compartment as specified by guidelines
brachy.e: The safe is a tungsten or depleted uranium shielded compartment. When not treating, the source retracts to the safe, reducing external radiation to near background.

brachy.q: What is TG-186 about?
brachy.a: Model-based dose calculation algorithms for brachytherapy beyond TG-43 water assumptions
c: Updated LDR seed calibration protocols within the treatment system
Model-based dose calculation algorithms for brachytherapy beyond TG-43 water assumptions
HDR afterloader QA protocols in radiation therapy practice
Applicator design specifications throughout the treatment course
brachy.e: TG-186 recommends transitioning from TG-43 to model-based dose calculation algorithms (MBDCAs) that account for tissue heterogeneities, applicator shielding, and patient anatomy (e.g., collapsed-cone, Monte Carlo).

brachy.q: What is the typical prescription distance for biliary HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: 1 cm from the source axis
c: 0.5 cm from source axis
1 cm from the source axis
2 cm from source axis
3 cm from source axis
brachy.e: Biliary brachytherapy prescribes at 1 cm from source axis, similar to esophageal and bronchial protocols. Typical doses: 10-15 Gy in 2-3 fractions as boost for cholangiocarcinoma.

brachy.q: How should a new HDR source be verified upon receipt?
brachy.a: Measurement with a calibrated well chamber compared to manufacturer's certificate
c: Visual inspection of the capsule in the radiation therapy context
Measurement with a calibrated well chamber compared to manufacturer's certificate
Autoradiograph comparison with previous source in radiation therapy practice
Monte Carlo simulation per standard operating procedures
brachy.e: The new source air kerma strength is measured using an ADCL-calibrated well chamber and compared to the certificate. Agreement within 3% is expected.

brachy.q: CALC: What fraction of initial Ir-192 activity remains after 110 days?
brachy.a: Approximately 35%
c: Approximately 15%
Approximately 25%
Approximately 35%
Approximately 50%
brachy.e: (0.5)^(110/73.8) = (0.5)^1.49 = 0.356 or ~35%. Near the end of typical clinical use.

brachy.q: What is the appropriate emergency response if an HDR source fails to retract?
brachy.a: Enter with survey meter, disconnect applicator from afterloader, remove applicator from patient into shielded container, evacuate patient
c: Wait indefinitely for automatic retraction for accurate dose delivery as specified by guidelines
Enter with survey meter, disconnect applicator from afterloader, remove applicator from patient into shielded container, evacuate patient
Call manufacturer and wait in clinical practice settings in the radiation therapy context
Turn off main power only in the radiation therapy context for the treatment procedure
brachy.e: Emergency procedure: enter with survey meter, quickly disconnect and remove applicator with source using forceps, place in lead pig, evacuate patient, secure room. Minimize time in room.

brachy.q: What is the difference between stranded and loose I-125 seeds?
brachy.a: Stranded seeds are linked in bioabsorbable suture at fixed spacing, reducing seed migration
c: Stranded seeds have higher activity in clinical practice settings
Stranded seeds are linked in bioabsorbable suture at fixed spacing, reducing seed migration
Loose seeds give more uniform dose for accurate dose delivery
Stranded seeds are only for Pd-103 for the treatment procedure
brachy.e: Stranded seeds are linked in bioabsorbable material (e.g., Vicryl) at ~1 cm spacing, reducing migration to lungs or pelvis and maintaining geometry better than loose seeds.

brachy.q: What is the typical HDR interstitial boost fractionation for oral cavity cancers?
brachy.a: 3-4 Gy per fraction, twice daily, total 15-25 Gy
c: Single 15 Gy fraction based on protocol requirements
3-4 Gy per fraction, twice daily, total 15-25 Gy
10 Gy x 3 fractions throughout the treatment course
2 Gy x 15 fractions as specified by guidelines
brachy.e: HDR interstitial boost for oral cavity: 3-4 Gy BID (minimum 6 hours apart) to 15-25 Gy total, in addition to 45-50 Gy EBRT.

brachy.q: CALC: Source strength needed to deliver 10 Gy at 1 cm in 5 min (Lambda = 1.12 cGy/(h*U))?

Approximately 5,357 U
Approximately 10,714 U

brachy.q: What does AAPM TG-56 address?

c: Small field dosimetry according to clinical protocols
Code of practice for brachytherapy physics: calibration, QA, and safety procedures
Electronic brachytherapy only for therapeutic applications
Eye plaque dosimetry according to clinical protocols
brachy.e: TG-56 provides comprehensive guidance on brachytherapy physics: source calibration, treatment planning, quality assurance, and radiation safety for both LDR and HDR.

brachy.q: What imaging is recommended for post-implant prostate seed evaluation?
brachy.a: CT scan for seed localization and dosimetric analysis
c: Plain X-ray only for seed count
CT scan for seed localization and dosimetric analysis
MRI only based on protocol requirements
No imaging needed for therapeutic applications
brachy.e: CT is standard for post-implant evaluation. Seeds are easily identified and dose distributions calculated. CT-MRI fusion can improve prostate delineation. D90, V100, V150, V200 are evaluated.

brachy.q: What is equivalent uniform dose (EUD) in brachytherapy?
brachy.a: A single dose value that would produce the same biological effect as the actual heterogeneous distribution
c: Dose at geometric center of implant in the treatment process for the treatment procedure
A single dose value that would produce the same biological effect as the actual heterogeneous distribution
Average dose across treatment volume per standard operating procedures
Maximum dose within target for the treatment procedure during the planning stage
brachy.e: EUD summarizes non-uniform dose as a single equivalent dose. Useful for plan comparison in brachytherapy where dose heterogeneity is inherently high.

brachy.q: What is the Henschke applicator used for?

c: Endobronchial brachytherapy in clinical practice settings
Intracavitary cervical brachytherapy with a compact tandem-and-colpostat design
Rectal brachytherapy for quality assurance purposes
Eye plaque treatment as specified by guidelines
brachy.e: The Henschke is a compact tandem-and-ovoid system for cervical brachytherapy, one of the early afterloading-compatible designs.

brachy.q: CALC: What is the BED for a permanent I-125 implant (145 Gy, alpha/beta=3.1, using the simplified approximation BED = D x RE factor ~1.5)?
brachy.a: Approximately 220 Gy
c: Approximately 145 Gy
Approximately 180 Gy
Approximately 220 Gy
Approximately 310 Gy
brachy.e: Using Dale's incomplete repair model for permanent implants with typical prostate parameters (repair half-time 1.5 h, alpha/beta 3.1 Gy), the RE factor is approximately 1.5, giving BED ~ 145 x 1.5 = ~220 Gy.

brachy.q: How many orthogonal radiographs are needed for Manchester-type cervical brachytherapy planning?
brachy.a: Two (AP and lateral)
c: One AP only
Two (AP and lateral)
Three (AP, lateral, oblique)
Four at 45-degree intervals
brachy.e: Two orthogonal radiographs reconstruct applicator geometry and calculate dose to Points A, B, and ICRU reference points. Now being superseded by volumetric CT/MRI planning.

brachy.q: Does I-125 emit beta particles?
brachy.a: No, I-125 decays by electron capture and emits only X-rays and gamma rays
c: Yes, low-energy beta at 0.15 MeV in the healthcare environment
Yes, high-energy beta at 0.67 MeV in the radiation therapy context
No, I-125 decays by electron capture and emits only X-rays and gamma rays
Yes, beta-plus with positron emission during the planning stage
brachy.e: I-125 decays by electron capture to Te-125, emitting characteristic X-rays and gamma rays (27-35 keV) but no beta particles. This contributes to the sharp dose fall-off.

brachy.q: What is the RPO's role in South African brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Oversee radiation safety, regulatory compliance, source inventory, and emergency procedures
c: Perform treatment planning for all patients as specified by guidelines
Oversee radiation safety, regulatory compliance, source inventory, and emergency procedures
Insert applicators during procedures for radiation dose management
Manufacture and repair sources in the radiation therapy context
brachy.e: The RPO manages personnel monitoring, area surveys, source inventory/security, waste management, emergency procedures, and NNR regulatory compliance.

brachy.q: What is the significance of the incomplete repair model in brachytherapy?
brachy.a: It accounts for sublethal damage repair during protracted irradiation, affecting biological effectiveness at different dose rates
c: It models lethal DNA damage repair only as specified by guidelines in clinical practice settings
It accounts for sublethal damage repair during protracted irradiation, affecting biological effectiveness at different dose rates
It predicts complete tumor elimination after a threshold dose for radiation dose management
It only applies to acute-responding tissues for accurate dose delivery in the healthcare environment
brachy.e: The incomplete repair model (Dale, Thames) describes sublethal repair during continuous or fractionated irradiation. Critical for comparing LDR, PDR, and HDR regimens.

brachy.q: CALC: Concrete thickness (TVL = 60 mm for Ir-192) to reduce exposure by factor 1000?
brachy.a: 180 mm
c: 60 mm
120 mm
180 mm
240 mm
brachy.e: 1000 = 10^3 requires 3 TVLs. 3 x 60 = 180 mm.

brachy.q: What is the typical prescription depth for vaginal cuff HDR brachytherapy?

5 mm from the cylinder surface
10 mm from the surface
20 mm from the surface
brachy.e: Standard prescription is 5 mm depth from the applicator surface, covering vaginal mucosa and submucosa where recurrence risk is highest.

brachy.q: What is the dose rate constant for typical Ir-192 HDR sources?
brachy.a: Approximately 1.108-1.12 cGy/(h*U)
c: Approximately 0.50 during the planning stage
Approximately 0.965 in radiation therapy practice
Approximately 1.108-1.12 cGy/(h*U)
Approximately 2.50 during patient treatment sessions
brachy.e: Typical Ir-192 HDR source dose rate constants (e.g., microSelectron v2, GammaMed Plus) are ~1.108-1.12 cGy/(h*U), source-model specific per TG-43U1 consensus data.

brachy.q: Why are CT markers placed in applicator channels before planning CT?
brachy.a: To identify source channel path and first dwell position for accurate applicator reconstruction
c: To calibrate the CT scanner in radiation therapy practice
To identify source channel path and first dwell position for accurate applicator reconstruction
To shield patient from CT radiation during the planning stage
To measure applicator attenuation per standard operating procedures
brachy.e: Radio-opaque markers define the source path on CT images, enabling accurate source position reconstruction in the TPS, critical for correct dose calculation.

brachy.q: What is dose point optimization in brachytherapy planning?
brachy.a: Adjusting dwell times to achieve desired doses at specified anatomical points
c: Random dwell time assignment according to clinical protocols
Adjusting dwell times to achieve desired doses at specified anatomical points
Optimization based on source activity only according to clinical protocols
Using fixed dwell time everywhere for therapeutic applications
brachy.e: Dose point optimization targets specific points (Point A, bladder, rectal points). It is simpler than volume-based optimization using DVH objectives but less comprehensive.

brachy.q: CALC: Total EQD2 (alpha/beta=10) for 50 Gy EBRT (2 Gy/fx) plus 5.5 Gy x 5 HDR brachy?
brachy.a: Approximately 85.5 Gy
c: Approximately 72 Gy
Approximately 80 Gy
Approximately 85.5 Gy
Approximately 95 Gy
brachy.e: EBRT EQD2 = 50 x 12/12 = 50 Gy. Brachy EQD2 = 27.5 x 15.5/12 = 35.5 Gy. Total = 85.5 Gy.

brachy.q: Why add interstitial needles to intracavitary applicators for cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: To improve coverage of parametrial disease that intracavitary sources alone cannot reach
c: To reduce treatment time as part of the clinical workflow
To improve coverage of parametrial disease that intracavitary sources alone cannot reach
To simplify planning during patient treatment sessions
To lower total body dose in the treatment process for accurate dose delivery
brachy.e: Combined IC/IS brachytherapy places needles into the parametrium, improving HR-CTV D90 for tumors with significant parametrial extension. Vienna and Utrecht applicators facilitate this.

brachy.q: What is the relationship between apparent activity and air kerma strength?
brachy.a: SK = Aapp x air kerma rate constant; apparent activity is source-model dependent and no longer recommended
c: They are identical as specified by guidelines for accurate dose delivery
SK = Aapp x air kerma rate constant; apparent activity is source-model dependent and no longer recommended
Apparent activity is always larger during patient treatment sessions
SK can only be measured, not calculated for clinical implementation
brachy.e: Apparent activity = SK / (air kerma rate constant). It is source-model dependent and can be misleading; TG-43U1 eliminated it in favor of directly specifying SK.

brachy.q: What radiation survey is required after each HDR treatment?
brachy.a: Survey of patient, treatment room, and afterloader area to confirm source retraction
c: Console check only for clinical implementation per standard operating procedures
Survey of patient, treatment room, and afterloader area to confirm source retraction
Survey waiting room only for the treatment procedure
No survey if afterloader shows source in safe in radiation therapy practice
brachy.e: A survey of the patient/applicator area, room, and afterloader is mandatory after every HDR treatment to confirm the source is safely stored.

brachy.q: What is the GTV-to-HR-CTV relationship in GEC-ESTRO cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: GTV is residual macroscopic tumor; HR-CTV includes GTV plus the whole cervix and grey zones
c: GTV and HR-CTV are identical based on protocol requirements
GTV is residual macroscopic tumor; HR-CTV includes GTV plus the whole cervix and grey zones
HR-CTV is always smaller than GTV per standard operating procedures
GTV is on CT only, HR-CTV on MRI only throughout the treatment course
brachy.e: At brachytherapy, GTV is visible residual tumor on MRI. HR-CTV encompasses GTV plus entire cervix including grey zones. HR-CTV adapts across fractions as tumor responds.

brachy.q: What is the role of nomograms in prostate seed planning?
brachy.a: To estimate seed number and activity based on prostate volume for pre-planning
c: To replace computer planning entirely as part of the clinical workflow
To estimate seed number and activity based on prostate volume for pre-planning
To calculate exact post-implant dosimetry as part of the clinical workflow
To determine radioactive half-life as part of the clinical workflow
brachy.e: Nomograms provide initial estimates based on prostate volume, allowing pre-ordering. Final plans are TPS-optimized.

brachy.q: What is the recommended minimum time between HDR brachytherapy fractions?
brachy.a: At least 6 hours
c: At least 1 hour
At least 6 hours
At least 24 hours
At least 1 week
brachy.e: Minimum 6-hour interval allows sublethal damage repair in normal tissues between fractions.

brachy.q: What is the dose non-uniformity ratio (DNR) in brachytherapy?
brachy.a: DNR = V150/V100, measuring the extent of high-dose regions
c: DNR = Dmax/Dmin during the planning stage
DNR = V150/V100, measuring the extent of high-dose regions
DNR = V200/V150 in the healthcare environment
DNR = D90/D10 in the radiation therapy context
brachy.e: DNR = V150/V100 indicates the fraction of treated volume receiving 50%+ above prescription dose. Lower values mean more homogeneous implants. DNR + DHI = 1.

brachy.q: What does NNR require for personnel monitoring of brachytherapy workers in South Africa?
brachy.a: Mandatory individual dosimeters with dose records maintained for at least 30 years
c: Area monitoring only in the healthcare environment
Mandatory individual dosimeters with dose records maintained for at least 30 years
Monitoring only if exceeding 5 mSv/year in radiation therapy practice
Self-reported dose estimates in clinical practice settings
