brachy.e: NNR requires individual monitoring (TLD or OSL badges) for all classified radiation workers. Records must be kept 30 years. Extremity monitoring may be needed for source handling.

brachy.q: CALC: Dose at 6 cm from source axis if dose at 3 cm is 7 Gy (inverse square)?
What does AAPM TG-59 address?
brachy.a: HDR brachytherapy treatment delivery: safety procedures, quality management, and regulatory compliance
c: LDR prostate seed dosimetry throughout the treatment course for the treatment procedure
HDR brachytherapy treatment delivery: safety procedures, quality management, and regulatory compliance
Well chamber calibration only in radiation therapy practice for therapeutic applications
Monte Carlo dose calculation for quality assurance purposes for therapeutic applications
brachy.e: TG-59 covers safe HDR delivery: staffing, quality management, emergency procedures, treatment protocols, and regulatory compliance.

brachy.q: What dosimetric concern arises with a sharply anteverted or retroverted tandem?
brachy.a: Increased OAR dose: bladder for anteverted, rectum for retroverted, due to reduced source-to-OAR distance
c: Reduced target dose requiring higher activity for quality assurance purposes
Increased OAR dose: bladder for anteverted, rectum for retroverted, due to reduced source-to-OAR distance
Uniform dose increase to all tissues in the treatment process for therapeutic applications
No dosimetric impact during patient treatment sessions for radiation dose management
brachy.e: Uterine angulation brings tandem sources closer to either bladder (anteverted) or rectum (retroverted), increasing OAR dose via inverse square law. Optimization must account for this geometry.

brachy.q: CALC: Dose rate at 1 cm from a point source (SK=20,000 U, Lambda=1.11 cGy/(h*U), g(1)=1, F(1,90)=1)?
c: 11,100 cGy/h according to clinical protocols
44,400 cGy/h for therapeutic applications
2,220 cGy/h throughout the treatment course
brachy.e: Dose rate = Lambda x SK x G(1,90) x g(1) x F(1,90) = 1.11 x 20000 x 1 x 1 x 1 = 22,200 cGy/h = 222 Gy/h.

brachy.q: What is the concept of overall treatment time (OTT) importance in cervical cancer brachytherapy?
brachy.a: OTT of EBRT plus brachytherapy should be kept under 7-8 weeks to minimize tumor repopulation
c: OTT has no impact on outcomes as part of the clinical workflow
OTT of EBRT plus brachytherapy should be kept under 7-8 weeks to minimize tumor repopulation
OTT should exceed 10 weeks for adequate normal tissue recovery
OTT only matters for prostate brachytherapy during the planning stage
brachy.e: Prolonged OTT allows accelerated tumor repopulation in cervical cancer. Each day of treatment extension beyond 7-8 weeks reduces local control by approximately 0.5-1%. Timely completion of brachytherapy is critical.

brachy.q: What is the NNR requirement for radiation warning signs at a brachytherapy treatment room in South Africa?
brachy.a: Clearly visible trefoil radiation warning signs at all entrances with illuminated warning lights during treatment
c: No signage is required if the room has a door interlock for therapeutic applications
Clearly visible trefoil radiation warning signs at all entrances with illuminated warning lights during treatment
Only a written notice inside the room for clinical implementation throughout the treatment course
Signs are only required for Co-60 teletherapy rooms during patient treatment sessions
brachy.e: NNR requires prominent radiation warning signs (international trefoil symbol) at all entrances to brachytherapy rooms. Illuminated 'Radiation On' warning lights must activate during HDR treatment delivery. These are mandatory regulatory requirements.

brachy.q: CALC: For a prostate I-125 implant, if D90 = 160 Gy and V100 = 95%, what is the V150 if DHI = 0.65?
brachy.e: DHI = 1 - V150/V100. 0.65 = 1 - V150/95. V150/95 = 0.35. V150 = 0.35 x 95 = 33.25%. This indicates 33.25% of the prostate receives 150% or more of the prescription dose.

brachy.q: What is the typical dose rate classification boundary between LDR and HDR?
brachy.a: LDR is below 2 Gy/hr; HDR is above 12 Gy/hr
c: LDR is below 0.5 Gy/hr; HDR is above 2 Gy/hr
LDR is below 2 Gy/hr; HDR is above 12 Gy/hr
LDR is below 5 Gy/hr; HDR is above 20 Gy/hr
LDR is below 10 Gy/hr; HDR is above 50 Gy/hr
brachy.e: ICRU defines LDR as 0.4-2 Gy/hr, MDR as 2-12 Gy/hr, HDR as >12 Gy/hr.

brachy.q: In the TG-43 formalism, what does the geometry function G(r,theta) account for?
brachy.a: The spatial distribution of radioactivity within the source
c: Attenuation and scatter in the surrounding medium
The spatial distribution of radioactivity within the source
The energy spectrum of photons emitted by source
The dose rate at the reference point of 1 cm
brachy.e: The geometry function accounts for the spatial distribution of activity within the source.

brachy.q: What is the reference point for cervical cancer brachytherapy in the Manchester system?
brachy.a: Point A, 2 cm superior and 2 cm lateral to os
c: Point A, 2 cm superior and 2 cm lateral to os
Point B, 5 cm lateral to the midline of pelvis
Point P, at the pelvic wall obturator region
The ICRU rectal reference point at rectum wall
brachy.e: Point A is 2 cm superior and 2 cm lateral to the external os.

brachy.q: CALC: An Ir-192 source has air kerma strength 40000 U and dose rate constant 1.108 cGy/(h·U). What is the dose rate at the reference point?
brachy.a: 44320 cGy/h
c: 36101 cGy/h
40000 cGy/h
44320 cGy/h
48640 cGy/h
brachy.e: Dose rate = 1.108 x 40000 = 44320 cGy/h.

brachy.q: What is the purpose of the well-type ionization chamber in brachytherapy?
brachy.a: To measure air kerma strength of brachytherapy sources
c: To calibrate external beam output in water phantom
To measure air kerma strength of brachytherapy sources
To verify dose distribution around the applicator
To check the positional accuracy of the afterloader
brachy.e: A well chamber provides nearly uniform response for measuring source strength.

brachy.q: What is the recommended frequency for HDR source calibration?
brachy.a: At each source exchange and periodically thereafter
c: Only at initial installation of the equipment
At each source exchange and periodically thereafter
Once every twelve months during annual QA only
Only when output deviates by more than five percent
brachy.e: HDR sources must be calibrated at each source exchange.

brachy.q: In GYN brachytherapy, what does the tandem and ring applicator treat?
brachy.a: The uterine canal and cervical os region combined
c: Only the vaginal cuff after hysterectomy surgery
The uterine canal and cervical os region combined
The parametrial tissues and pelvic sidewalls only
The external genitalia and perineal skin surface
brachy.e: The tandem treats the uterine canal, the ring covers the cervix and upper vaginal fornices.

brachy.q: What is the half-life of I-125 used in permanent prostate seed implants?
brachy.a: 59.4 days
c: 17.0 days
59.4 days
73.8 days
120.4 days
brachy.e: I-125 has a half-life of 59.4 days.

brachy.q: What is the COMS eye plaque used for?
brachy.a: Treatment of uveal melanoma with I-125 seed plaques
c: Treatment of retinoblastoma with external beam RT
Treatment of uveal melanoma with I-125 seed plaques
Diagnostic imaging of orbital tumor vascularity
Shielding of the lens during orbital irradiation
brachy.e: COMS plaques use I-125 seeds in a gold-backed carrier.

brachy.q: What emergency equipment is required in an HDR brachytherapy suite?
brachy.a: Emergency source container, forceps, and survey meter
c: Backup afterloader unit and spare source cable
Emergency source container, forceps, and survey meter
Lead aprons and thyroid shields for all staff
Portable X-ray unit for source localization only
brachy.e: Emergency equipment must include shielded container, forceps, and survey meter.

brachy.q: CALC: If an Ir-192 source had 10 Ci on day 0, what is the activity after 74 days?
brachy.a: 5.0 Ci
c: 2.5 Ci
5.0 Ci
7.5 Ci
9.0 Ci
brachy.e: Ir-192 half-life is 73.8 days, so after one half-life: 10/2 = 5.0 Ci.

brachy.q: What does the anisotropy function F(r,theta) describe in TG-43?
brachy.a: Angular variation of dose rate around the source axis
c: Radial dose falloff with increasing distance only
Angular variation of dose rate around the source axis
Effect of tissue heterogeneity on dose calculation
Source strength variation with time due to decay
brachy.e: The anisotropy function accounts for angular dependence of dose rate.

brachy.q: What is the D90 parameter in prostate brachytherapy?
brachy.a: The minimum dose covering 90% of the prostate volume
c: The dose at 90 mm from the source center point
The minimum dose covering 90% of the prostate volume
The dose delivered in the first 90 minutes
Ninety percent of the prescribed dose to prostate
brachy.e: D90 should be at least 140-145 Gy for I-125 implants.

brachy.q: What is the primary advantage of Ir-192 over Co-60 for HDR?
brachy.a: Smaller source size enables narrower applicator channels
c: Significantly longer half-life reduces source changes
Smaller source size enables narrower applicator channels
Higher photon energy improves tissue penetration
Lower cost per source exchange for the facility
brachy.e: Ir-192 has higher specific activity, allowing very small sources.

brachy.q: What safety interlock prevents HDR treatment if the door is open?
brachy.a: Door interlock that retracts the source automatically
c: Audio alarm that alerts staff to close the door
Door interlock that retracts the source automatically
Visual warning light outside the treatment room
Manual override switch requiring physicist approval
brachy.e: The door interlock automatically retracts the source.

brachy.q: What is the recommended rectal dose constraint for cervical HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: D2cc of rectum should be below 75 Gy EQD2 total
c: D2cc of rectum should be below 65 Gy EQD2 total
D2cc of rectum should be below 75 Gy EQD2 total
D2cc of rectum should be below 85 Gy EQD2 total
D2cc of rectum should be below 90 Gy EQD2 total
brachy.e: GEC-ESTRO recommends D2cc rectum < 75 Gy EQD2.

brachy.q: What imaging modality is recommended for GYN brachytherapy by GEC-ESTRO?
brachy.a: MRI for soft tissue and tumor visualization
c: Conventional orthogonal radiograph films only
MRI for soft tissue and tumor visualization
CT scan with contrast for all clinical cases
Ultrasound for real-time applicator placement
brachy.e: GEC-ESTRO recommends MRI-based planning for cervical cancer brachytherapy.

brachy.q: What is the Pd-103 half-life and typical application?
brachy.a: 17 days; used for permanent prostate implants
c: 17 days; used for permanent prostate implants
60 days; used for temporary cervical insertions
74 days; used for HDR afterloader treatments
120 days; used for eye plaque therapy sessions
brachy.e: Pd-103 has a half-life of 17 days with mean photon energy of 21 keV.

brachy.q: What does the radial dose function g(r) account for in TG-43?
brachy.a: Dose falloff along the transverse axis due to medium
c: Angular variation of dose around the source axis
Dose falloff along the transverse axis due to medium
Geometric divergence from an ideal point source
Activity distribution along the source active length
brachy.e: g(r) describes dose falloff due to absorption and scatter, excluding geometric falloff.

brachy.q: What is the purpose of the source position check in HDR QA?
brachy.a: To verify dwell positions match planned locations
c: To measure the absolute dose rate of the source
To verify dwell positions match planned locations
To confirm source activity matches the certificate
To test the emergency retraction mechanism speed
brachy.e: Positional accuracy should be within 1-2 mm.

brachy.q: What is the Vienna applicator used for in GYN brachytherapy?
brachy.a: A tandem and ring with interstitial needle capability
c: An intravaginal cylinder for post-hysterectomy cases
A tandem and ring with interstitial needle capability
A single channel tandem for endometrial treatment
An ovoid pair without tandem for vaginal cuff
brachy.e: The Vienna applicator combines tandem/ring with interstitial needles.

brachy.q: What survey meter reading at 1 meter confirms safe HDR source retraction?
brachy.a: Background radiation level with no elevated reading
c: Less than 2 mR/hr above normal background levels
Background radiation level with no elevated reading
Less than 10 mR/hr at the treatment room door
Any reading below the annual dose limit rate
brachy.e: After retraction, readings should be at background level.

brachy.q: What is the HR-CTV in MRI-guided cervical brachytherapy?
brachy.a: High-risk CTV: residual GTV plus cervix at brachytherapy
c: The original gross tumor volume at time of diagnosis
High-risk CTV: residual GTV plus cervix at brachytherapy
The entire parametrial region requiring dose coverage
The intermediate risk volume including uterine corpus
brachy.e: HR-CTV D90 goal is at least 87 Gy EQD2.

brachy.q: What is the primary shielding material in COMS eye plaques?
brachy.a: Gold alloy backing for photon attenuation
c: Lead sheet for general radiation shielding
Gold alloy backing for photon attenuation
Tungsten insert for high-Z dose reduction
Stainless steel shell for structural support
brachy.e: Gold alloy attenuates I-125 photons, protecting posterior structures.

brachy.q: What is the typical dwell position step size used in HDR planning?
brachy.a: 2.5 to 5.0 mm between consecutive dwell positions
c: 0.5 to 1.0 mm between consecutive dwell positions
2.5 to 5.0 mm between consecutive dwell positions
10.0 to 15.0 mm between consecutive positions
20.0 to 25.0 mm between consecutive positions
brachy.e: Most HDR afterloaders use step sizes of 2.5-5 mm.

brachy.q: What is the significance of air kerma strength (SK) in brachytherapy?
brachy.a: It specifies source output independent of source design
c: It measures total activity inside the source capsule
It specifies source output independent of source design
It determines the half-life of the radioactive source
It indicates the maximum treatable tumor size only
brachy.e: Air kerma strength characterizes source output independent of construction.

brachy.q: What is the transit dose in HDR brachytherapy?
brachy.a: Dose delivered while the source moves between dwell positions
c: Dose delivered while the source moves between dwell positions
The planned dose at each programmed dwell position
Dose to transit organs like bowel during treatment
The dose rate during the source loading procedure
brachy.e: Transit dose is typically small but can be significant for many channels.

brachy.q: CALC: An Ir-192 source needs replacement when activity falls below 3 Ci. If initial activity is 10 Ci, approximately how many days until replacement?
brachy.a: Approximately 129 days after initial calibration
c: Approximately 74 days after initial calibration
Approximately 100 days after initial calibration
Approximately 129 days after initial calibration
Approximately 185 days after initial calibration

brachy.q: What is pulsed dose rate (PDR) brachytherapy?
brachy.a: HDR source delivering small pulses to simulate LDR radiobiology
c: Continuous low dose rate delivery over several days
HDR source delivering small pulses to simulate LDR radiobiology
Fractionated HDR with daily treatment sessions only
External beam with pulsed delivery at the target
brachy.e: PDR uses an HDR source to deliver small hourly pulses, approximating LDR radiobiology.

brachy.q: What is the typical prescription dose for HDR cervical brachytherapy per fraction?
brachy.a: 7 Gy per fraction to Point A or HR-CTV D90
c: 3 Gy per fraction to Point A or HR-CTV D90
7 Gy per fraction to Point A or HR-CTV D90
12 Gy per fraction to Point A or HR-CTV D90
20 Gy per fraction to Point A or HR-CTV D90
brachy.e: Common HDR schedules include 7 Gy x 4 fractions or similar, combined with 45-50 Gy external beam.

electron.q: What is the approximate energy rule for estimating R80 in centimeters for clinical electron beams?
electron.a: R80 in cm is approximately E/3 in MeV
c: R80 in cm is approximately E/2 in MeV
R80 in cm is approximately E/3 in MeV
R80 in cm is approximately E/4 in MeV
R80 in cm is approximately E/5 in MeV
electron.e: The rule of thumb E/3 provides a quick estimate of the depth of the 80% isodose in centimeters for electron beams.

electron.q: What is the practical range (Rp) of a 12 MeV electron beam in water, using the E/2 approximation?
electron.a: Approximately 6.0 cm in water depth
c: Approximately 3.0 cm in water depth
Approximately 4.0 cm in water depth
Approximately 6.0 cm in water depth
Approximately 8.0 cm in water depth
electron.e: Rp is approximately E(MeV)/2 in cm, so for 12 MeV, Rp is about 6 cm. This represents the maximum depth electrons penetrate.

electron.q: What is the approximate surface dose for a 6 MeV electron beam relative to dmax?
electron.a: Approximately 70-75% of the maximum dose
c: Approximately 50-55% of the maximum dose
Approximately 70-75% of the maximum dose
Approximately 85-90% of the maximum dose
Approximately 95-99% of the maximum dose
electron.e: Lower energy electron beams (6 MeV) have lower surface doses around 70-75%. Surface dose increases with increasing electron energy.

electron.q: How does surface dose change as electron beam energy increases from 6 to 20 MeV?
electron.a: Surface dose increases with higher energy
c: Surface dose decreases with higher energy
Surface dose increases with higher energy
Surface dose remains constant at all levels
Surface dose fluctuates unpredictably here
electron.e: Higher energy electrons are more forward-directed, producing more dose at the surface. Surface dose can reach 95% or more at 20 MeV.

electron.q: What is the depth of maximum dose (dmax) for a 9 MeV electron beam in water?
electron.a: Approximately 2.0 to 2.3 cm deep
c: Approximately 0.5 to 1.0 cm deep
Approximately 2.0 to 2.3 cm deep
Approximately 3.5 to 4.0 cm deep
Approximately 5.0 to 5.5 cm deep
electron.e: For electron beams, dmax is roughly E/4 in cm. For 9 MeV, dmax is approximately 2.0-2.3 cm.

electron.q: What is the approximate relationship between R50 and the mean energy at the surface (E0)?
electron.a: E0 (MeV) = 2.33 times R50 in cm
c: E0 (MeV) = 1.50 times R50 in cm
E0 (MeV) = 2.33 times R50 in cm
E0 (MeV) = 3.00 times R50 in cm
E0 (MeV) = 4.20 times R50 in cm
electron.e: Per TG-25 and TG-70, the mean electron energy at the phantom surface E0 = 2.33 x R50 (in cm in water).

electron.q: Which parameter is used to determine the most probable energy (Ep,0) of an electron beam?
electron.a: The practical range Rp measured in water
c: The practical range Rp measured in water
The depth of maximum dose value dmax
The half-value depth R50 in the beam
The bremsstrahlung tail percentage value
electron.e: Ep,0 = 0.22 + 1.98*Rp + 0.0025*Rp^2, where Rp is the practical range in water. This gives the most probable energy at the surface.

electron.q: What causes the bremsstrahlung tail beyond Rp in electron beam depth-dose curves?
electron.a: Photon contamination from electron interactions
c: Neutron production from nuclear interactions
Photon contamination from electron interactions
Pair production events inside the phantom
Characteristic X-rays from atomic excitation
electron.e: As electrons interact with matter, they produce bremsstrahlung (braking radiation) photons, creating a low-dose tail beyond the practical range.

electron.q: What is the typical bremsstrahlung contamination dose beyond Rp for clinical electron beams?
electron.a: Approximately 1-5% of the maximum dose
c: Approximately 1-5% of the maximum dose
Less than 0.1% of the maximum dose
Approximately 10-15% of maximum dose
Approximately 20-25% of maximum dose
electron.e: Bremsstrahlung contamination is typically 1-5% of dmax dose, increasing with energy. It is clinically relevant for sensitive structures beyond the target.

electron.q: Why are higher energy electrons (>15 MeV) less commonly used in clinical practice?
electron.a: They have broader penumbra and less sharp falloff
c: They produce too much surface dose for patients
They have broader penumbra and less sharp falloff
They are not available on clinical linear accelerators
They cannot penetrate through skin surface layer
electron.e: Higher energy electrons scatter more, producing broader penumbra and less sharp dose falloff, reducing their dosimetric advantage over photon beams.

electron.q: What is the primary purpose of the dual scattering foil system in electron beam delivery?
electron.a: To produce a flat and uniform electron field
c: To reduce the bremsstrahlung contamination level
To produce a flat and uniform electron field
To increase beam energy at patient surface
To collimate the beam to the desired field size
electron.e: The dual scattering foil system spreads the narrow pencil beam into a broad, flat field. The first foil scatters, the second flattens the profile.

electron.q: How does the second scattering foil differ from the first in a dual-foil system?
electron.a: It has a thicker center to flatten the beam profile
c: It uses a lower-Z material to reduce energy loss
It has a thicker center to flatten the beam profile
It is identical to the first foil in construction
It is placed directly at the electron applicator
electron.e: The second foil is typically shaped (thicker at center, thinner at edges) or made of dual materials to differentially scatter and flatten the beam.

electron.q: What percentage of incident electron energy is typically lost in the scattering foil system?
electron.a: Approximately 2-5% of the incident beam energy
c: Less than 0.5% of the incident beam energy
Approximately 2-5% of the incident beam energy
Approximately 10-15% of the beam energy used
Approximately 20-30% of the beam energy used
electron.e: The scattering foils cause a small energy loss and energy spread, typically a few percent, while producing the broad uniform clinical field.

electron.q: What is the main function of electron applicators (cones) in electron beam therapy?
electron.a: To define the field size and reduce penumbra
c: To increase the energy of the electron beam used
To define the field size and reduce penumbra
To filter out photon contamination in the beam
To produce a flat dose profile at the surface
electron.e: Electron applicators collimate the beam close to the patient surface, defining the treatment field and sharpening the penumbra.

electron.q: Why must electron applicators extend close to the patient skin surface?
electron.a: To minimize in-air scatter and penumbra spread
c: To maximize bremsstrahlung production in beam
To minimize in-air scatter and penumbra spread
To increase the surface dose to the patient
To reduce the total treatment time for patient
electron.e: Electrons scatter significantly in air. Placing the applicator close to the surface limits the air gap and maintains a sharp penumbra.

electron.q: What are the standard electron applicator sizes available on most clinical linacs?
electron.e: Standard applicator sizes are typically 6x6, 10x10, 15x15, 20x20, and 25x25 cm, though this varies by manufacturer.

electron.q: What alloy is commonly used to make custom electron field-shaping cutouts?
electron.a: Cerrobend, a low melting point alloy material
c: Cerrobend, a low melting point alloy material
Pure lead sheets of varying thickness levels
Tungsten alloy blocks for field shaping use
Stainless steel inserts for beam collimation
electron.e: Cerrobend (Lipowitz alloy) melts at 70C, making it easy to cast custom shapes. It contains bismuth, lead, tin, and cadmium.

electron.q: What is the minimum Cerrobend thickness needed to block a 12 MeV electron beam?
electron.a: Approximately 12-15 mm of Cerrobend alloy
c: Approximately 5-7 mm of Cerrobend alloy
Approximately 12-15 mm of Cerrobend alloy
Approximately 25-30 mm of Cerrobend alloy
Approximately 40-50 mm of Cerrobend alloy
electron.e: A rule of thumb is 1 mm of Cerrobend per MeV, plus a safety margin. For 12 MeV, 12-15 mm provides adequate shielding.

electron.q: What is an electron insert factor (or cutout factor) in electron dosimetry?
electron.a: The ratio of output with cutout to open cone output
c: The ratio of output with cutout to open cone output
The ratio of surface dose to maximum dose at depth
The ratio of electron dose to photon dose in field
The ratio of penumbra width to total field size used
electron.e: The insert factor corrects for output changes when a custom cutout is placed in the applicator, compared to the open applicator output.

electron.q: How do small cutouts affect the output of electron beams compared to the open cone?
electron.a: Small cutouts decrease the output of the beam
c: Small cutouts increase the output of the beam
Small cutouts decrease the output of the beam
Small cutouts have no effect on beam output
Small cutouts only affect the beam penumbra
electron.e: Small cutouts reduce lateral scatter equilibrium, decreasing the central axis dose. This requires an output correction (insert factor < 1).

electron.q: What geometric parameter is used to characterize irregular electron cutout shapes for output factors?
electron.a: The equivalent square derived from area and perimeter
c: The maximum diameter of the cutout opening used
The equivalent square derived from area and perimeter
The minimum dimension of the cutout field shape
The geometric center position of the cutout field
electron.e: The equivalent square = 4 x Area/Perimeter (or using the Hogstrom method). This allows irregular shapes to be related to measured square field data.

electron.q: What is the purpose of bolus in electron beam therapy?
electron.a: To increase surface dose and shift the depth dose
c: To reduce surface dose and spare the skin layer
To increase surface dose and shift the depth dose
To collimate the electron beam at the surface
To shield underlying critical structures from dose
electron.e: Bolus is placed on the skin to increase surface dose and shift the therapeutic range to shallower depths, useful for superficial targets.

electron.q: What material is most commonly used for electron beam bolus in clinical practice?
electron.a: Tissue-equivalent wax or superflab material
c: Lead sheets of varying thickness dimensions
Tissue-equivalent wax or superflab material
Aluminum or copper sheets for beam therapy
Styrofoam blocks of appropriate size for use
electron.e: Tissue-equivalent bolus (wax, Superflab, wet gauze) has near-unit density and conforms to the patient surface for uniform dose buildup.

electron.q: How is custom 3D-printed bolus beneficial compared to flat sheet bolus for electrons?
electron.a: It conforms precisely to surface irregularities
c: It provides higher density than standard bolus
It conforms precisely to surface irregularities
It eliminates all air gaps automatically here
It increases the beam energy at the surface
electron.e: 3D-printed bolus matches the patient surface contour precisely, eliminating air gaps that cause dose inhomogeneities with flat bolus sheets.

electron.q: What thickness of bolus is needed to bring dmax to the skin surface for a 9 MeV beam?
electron.a: Approximately 2.0 cm of tissue-equivalent bolus
c: Approximately 0.5 cm of tissue-equivalent bolus
Approximately 2.0 cm of tissue-equivalent bolus
Approximately 4.0 cm of tissue-equivalent bolus
Approximately 6.0 cm of tissue-equivalent bolus
electron.e: Since dmax for 9 MeV is about 2 cm (E/4), approximately 2 cm of bolus shifts the maximum dose to the skin surface.

electron.q: What is the purpose of skin collimation in electron beam therapy treatments?
electron.a: To sharpen field edges for small or irregular areas
c: To increase the dose rate for faster treatments
To sharpen field edges for small or irregular areas
To reduce surface dose in the treatment region
To flatten the beam profile at the skin surface
electron.e: Skin collimation (lead sheets on the skin) provides a sharper penumbra than the applicator alone, useful for eyelids, ears, and small fields.

electron.q: What thickness of lead is typically needed for skin collimation of a 9 MeV electron beam?
electron.a: Approximately 3-4 mm of lead on the skin surface
c: Approximately 0.5-1 mm of lead on skin surface
Approximately 3-4 mm of lead on the skin surface
Approximately 8-10 mm of lead on skin surface
Approximately 15-20 mm of lead on skin surface
electron.e: Lead skin collimation requires roughly 0.5 mm per MeV. For 9 MeV, approximately 3-4 mm of lead provides adequate shielding.

electron.q: What is the primary concern with using internal shielding in electron beam therapy?
electron.a: Backscatter from the shield increases local dose
c: The shield blocks the beam entirely from target
Backscatter from the shield increases local dose
The shield creates significant beam hardening here
Internal shields are too heavy for patient comfort
electron.e: High-Z internal shields cause electron backscatter, increasing dose to tissue on the entrance side by 30-70% depending on energy and material.

electron.q: How is the backscatter dose from internal shielding typically mitigated?
electron.a: Placing a low-Z absorber upstream of the shield
c: Using thinner shielding material for treatments
Placing a low-Z absorber upstream of the shield
Increasing the electron beam energy being used
Removing the shield during treatment delivery now
electron.e: A low-Z material (e.g., wax or aluminum) placed on the beam-entrance side of the lead absorbs backscattered electrons, reducing the dose enhancement.

electron.q: What is the approximate backscatter factor for lead internal shielding at 9 MeV?
electron.a: Approximately 1.5 to 1.7 (50-70% increase)
c: Approximately 1.1 to 1.2 (10-20% increase)
Approximately 1.5 to 1.7 (50-70% increase)
Approximately 2.0 to 2.5 (100-150% increase)
Approximately 3.0 to 3.5 (200-250% increase)
electron.e: Electron backscatter from lead can enhance the dose by 50-70% at 9 MeV. The backscatter factor decreases with increasing energy.

electron.q: In the hand calculation method for electron dose, what is the standard reference SSD?
electron.a: 100 cm SSD for standard electron treatments
c: 80 cm SSD for standard electron treatments
100 cm SSD for standard electron treatments
110 cm SSD for standard electron treatments
120 cm SSD for standard electron treatments
electron.e: Electron beams are typically calibrated and treated at 100 cm SSD, with the applicator end near the patient surface.

electron.q: What is the effective SSD concept in electron beam dosimetry?
electron.a: A virtual source position for inverse-square calculations
c: The distance from the applicator end to patient skin
A virtual source position for inverse-square calculations
The distance from the scattering foils to the skin
The distance from the jaws to patient skin surface
electron.e: The effective SSD locates a virtual point source that, when used with inverse-square law, correctly predicts output changes with SSD for electrons.

electron.q: How does the effective SSD typically compare to the nominal SSD for electron beams?
electron.a: Effective SSD is less than the nominal 100 cm SSD
c: Effective SSD equals the nominal 100 cm SSD value
Effective SSD is less than the nominal 100 cm SSD
Effective SSD is greater than nominal 100 cm SSD
Effective SSD varies randomly around 100 cm SSD
electron.e: The virtual source for electrons is located between the scattering foils and applicator, so effective SSD is typically 80-95 cm depending on energy and cone.

electron.q: What is the standard formula for electron beam monitor unit calculation at standard SSD?
electron.a: MU = Dose / (Output x PDD x cutout factor)
c: MU = Dose / (Output x PDD x cutout factor)
MU = Dose x Output x PDD x cutout factor used
MU = Dose / (Output x TMR x field size factor)
MU = Dose x PDD / (Output x cutout factor here)
electron.e: The basic electron MU formula divides the prescribed dose by the product of calibration output, PDD at depth, and the cutout (insert) factor.

electron.q: In electron beam dosimetry, how does the PDD curve change with increasing field size?
electron.a: PDD increases and then plateaus above equilibrium size
c: PDD continuously increases with larger field sizes
PDD decreases with increasing field size in beams
PDD increases and then plateaus above equilibrium size
PDD remains constant regardless of the field size
electron.e: As field size increases, lateral scatter equilibrium is established. Beyond this equilibrium field size, the PDD curve no longer changes.

electron.q: What is the lateral scatter equilibrium field size for a 12 MeV electron beam?
electron.a: Approximately 5-6 cm equivalent square field
c: Approximately 2-3 cm equivalent square field
Approximately 5-6 cm equivalent square field
Approximately 10-12 cm equivalent square field
Approximately 15-20 cm equivalent square field
electron.e: The equilibrium field size is approximately the practical range in cm. For 12 MeV (Rp~6 cm), the equilibrium size is about 5-6 cm.

electron.q: What surface irregularity effect is most problematic in electron beam treatments?
electron.a: Hot and cold spots from surface contour variations
c: Increased bremsstrahlung at surface irregularities
Hot and cold spots from surface contour variations
Complete loss of dose at irregular surface areas
Beam deflection away from the treatment field used
electron.e: Surface irregularities cause electrons to scatter differently, creating hot spots (at concavities) and cold spots (at convexities) in the dose distribution.

electron.q: What happens to the electron dose distribution at the edge of an air cavity in tissue?
electron.a: Hot spots form on the distal side of the cavity
c: The dose distribution is completely unaffected now
Cold spots form on the distal side of the cavity
Hot spots form on the distal side of the cavity
The cavity causes uniform dose reduction overall
electron.e: The reduced density of air allows electrons to penetrate further, and lateral scattering at interfaces creates dose hot spots distal to cavities.

electron.q: What measurements are required during commissioning of a clinical electron beam?
electron.a: PDD curves, profiles, and output factors for each cone
c: Only central axis PDD curves for each beam energy
PDD curves, profiles, and output factors for each cone
Only output factors at dmax for each applicator used
Only beam profiles at dmax depth for each energy here
electron.e: Comprehensive commissioning requires depth dose curves, cross-plane and in-plane profiles at multiple depths, and output factors for all energy/cone combinations.

electron.q: What detector is recommended for electron beam PDD measurements during commissioning?
electron.a: An electron diode or parallel plate ion chamber
c: A thimble (cylindrical) ionization chamber only
An electron diode or parallel plate ion chamber
A film dosimeter placed perpendicular to beam
A thermoluminescent dosimeter at each depth used
electron.e: Electron diodes or parallel plate chambers are preferred for electron PDD measurements due to their thin sensitive volume and minimal perturbation.

electron.q: At what depth should electron beam output factors be measured during commissioning?
electron.a: At the depth of maximum dose (dmax) for each energy
c: At the surface of the water phantom being used
At the depth of maximum dose (dmax) for each energy
At a fixed depth of 5 cm for all electron energies
At the practical range (Rp) for each beam energy
electron.e: Output factors are measured at dmax (or a reference depth) for each energy and applicator combination to establish dose-per-MU tables.

electron.q: What scanning system is typically used for electron beam profile and PDD measurements?
electron.a: A computer-controlled 3D water scanning phantom
c: A manual depth dose measurement jig in solid phantom
A computer-controlled 3D water scanning phantom
A stack of radiochromic film sheets in a phantom
An array of thermoluminescent dosimeters in water
electron.e: Automated 3D water scanning systems (e.g., IBA Blue Phantom, PTW MP3) are standard for efficient acquisition of electron depth dose and profile data.

electron.q: What does TG-25 provide guidance on for clinical electron beam dosimetry?
electron.a: Clinical electron beam dosimetry and dose calculations
c: Calibration of electron beams using ion chambers only
Clinical electron beam dosimetry and dose calculations
Quality assurance procedures for linear accelerators
Brachytherapy source calibration and commissioning
electron.e: AAPM TG-25 (1991) provides comprehensive recommendations for clinical electron beam dosimetry including PDD data, output factors, and dose calculations.

electron.q: What is the key update provided by TG-70 compared to the original TG-25 report?
electron.a: Updated dosimetry data and calculation recommendations
c: A completely new calibration protocol for electrons
Updated dosimetry data and calculation recommendations
New electron beam delivery hardware requirements now
Guidelines for proton beam therapy commissioning use
electron.e: TG-70 (2009) updates TG-25 with improved clinical dosimetry recommendations, updated dose calculation methods, and revised data tables for modern beams.

electron.q: What ion chamber type does TG-51 recommend for electron beam calibration below 6 MeV?
electron.a: A parallel plate ionization chamber is required
c: A Farmer-type cylindrical ionization chamber only
A parallel plate ionization chamber is required
Any chamber type may be used for calibration
A micro ionization chamber is required for this
electron.e: TG-51 requires parallel plate chambers for electron beams with R50 < 4.3 cm (approximately < 10 MeV) due to gradient and perturbation effects.

electron.q: What is the reference depth for electron beam calibration according to TG-51?
electron.a: dref = 0.6R50 - 0.1 cm in water phantom
c: dref = dmax for each electron beam energy
dref = 0.6R50 - 0.1 cm in water phantom
dref = R50 for each electron beam energy used
dref = 5 cm for all electron beam energies
electron.e: TG-51 specifies the reference depth as dref = 0.6*R50 - 0.1 cm, which places the measurement point in a region of low dose gradient.

electron.q: What quality conversion factor is used in TG-51 electron beam calibration?
electron.a: kQ factor that converts Co-60 calibration to beam quality
c: kQ factor that converts Co-60 calibration to beam quality
The stopping power ratio for water to air medium
The perturbation correction factor for the chamber
The polarity correction factor for the ion chamber
electron.e: TG-51 uses kQ (or kR50 for electrons) to convert the Co-60 calibration factor to the user's beam quality, simplifying the calibration process.

electron.q: Why is the gradient correction important when using cylindrical chambers in electron beams?
electron.a: The dose gradient across the chamber volume is steep
c: The chamber has poor energy response in electrons
The dose gradient across the chamber volume is steep
The chamber wall material attenuates electrons used
The chamber stem causes significant scatter of beam
electron.e: The steep dose gradient in electron beams means the dose varies significantly across the cylindrical chamber volume, requiring a shift correction.

electron.q: What is the effective point of measurement shift for a cylindrical chamber in electrons?
electron.a: 0.5 times the inner radius upstream of center
c: At the geometric center of the chamber cavity
0.5 times the inner radius upstream of center
1.0 times the inner radius upstream of center
0.6 times the inner radius upstream of center
electron.e: For cylindrical chambers in electron beams, the effective point of measurement is shifted 0.5r (half the cavity radius) toward the source.

electron.q: What is the polarity effect in electron beam ion chamber measurements?
electron.a: Different readings with positive vs negative voltage
c: Different readings with positive vs negative voltage
Change in readings due to temperature variations now
Variation in reading due to recombination of ions
Difference between readings in photon and electrons
electron.e: The polarity effect causes different ionization readings depending on the collection voltage polarity. The correction is Ppol = (M+ + M-)/(2M).

electron.q: What is the typical polarity correction factor range for electron beam measurements?
electron.a: Between 0.98 and 1.02 for most clinical beams
c: Between 0.90 and 0.95 for most clinical beams
Between 0.98 and 1.02 for most clinical beams
Between 1.05 and 1.10 for most clinical beams
Exactly 1.000 for all clinical electron beams
electron.e: Polarity corrections are typically small (within 2%) for well-designed chambers. Larger corrections may indicate chamber or measurement issues.

electron.q: What is the primary mode of energy loss for therapeutic electrons in tissue?
electron.a: Coulomb interactions with atomic orbital electrons
c: Bremsstrahlung radiation production in the tissue
Coulomb interactions with atomic orbital electrons
Nuclear interactions producing secondary neutrons
Cherenkov radiation emission in the tissue medium
electron.e: Therapeutic electrons lose energy primarily through collisional (ionization and excitation) interactions with orbital electrons of the medium atoms.

electron.q: How does the ratio of radiative to collisional stopping power change with electron energy?
electron.a: Radiative fraction increases with electron energy
c: Radiative fraction decreases with electron energy
Radiative fraction increases with electron energy
The ratio remains constant at all energy levels
Radiative fraction peaks at medium energies only
electron.e: Bremsstrahlung (radiative) losses increase approximately linearly with energy and Z, becoming significant above 10 MeV in high-Z materials.

electron.q: What is multiple Coulomb scattering and its relevance to electron beam therapy?
electron.a: Cumulative small-angle deflections that broaden the beam
c: Single large-angle scattering events deflecting beam
Cumulative small-angle deflections that broaden the beam
Energy loss from multiple ionization interactions
Production of secondary electrons along beam path
electron.e: Multiple Coulomb scattering causes many small-angle deflections that cumulatively broaden the beam and affect penumbra and depth dose.

electron.q: How does scattering power relate to the atomic number (Z) of the medium?
electron.a: Scattering power increases with higher Z materials
c: Scattering power decreases with higher Z materials
Scattering power increases with higher Z materials
Scattering power is independent of atomic number
Scattering power peaks for medium Z materials only
electron.e: Higher Z materials have more nuclear charge, causing greater Coulomb scattering. This is why lead scatters electrons much more than water.

electron.q: What is the most common clinical application of electron beam therapy?
electron.a: Post-mastectomy chest wall irradiation treatment
c: Deep-seated pelvic tumor treatment plans used
Post-mastectomy chest wall irradiation treatment
Whole brain radiotherapy for metastatic disease
Spinal cord irradiation for primary tumors here
electron.e: Electron beams are widely used for chest wall irradiation after mastectomy, where the target is superficial and lung sparing is critical.

electron.q: Why are electron beams preferred over photon beams for chest wall treatments?
electron.a: Rapid dose falloff spares the underlying lung tissue
c: Electrons provide better skin sparing than photons
Rapid dose falloff spares the underlying lung tissue
Electrons have a higher RBE than photon beams here
Electrons produce no exit dose through the patient
electron.e: The sharp dose falloff of electrons beyond dmax limits dose to the underlying lung, which is a critical concern in chest wall treatments.

electron.q: What electron energy is typically used for a standard chest wall boost treatment?
electron.a: 6 to 9 MeV depending on chest wall thickness
c: 4 to 5 MeV depending on chest wall thickness
6 to 9 MeV depending on chest wall thickness
12 to 16 MeV depending on chest wall thickness
18 to 20 MeV depending on chest wall thickness
electron.e: Chest wall thickness is typically 2-3 cm, requiring 6-9 MeV electrons to provide adequate coverage with R90 at the deep chest wall margin.

electron.q: What is the role of electron beams in head and neck cancer treatment?
electron.a: Boost dose to superficial lymph nodes or tumor bed
c: Primary treatment of deep-seated pharyngeal tumors
Boost dose to superficial lymph nodes or tumor bed
Whole neck irradiation as the sole beam modality
Treatment of the spinal cord posterior to pharynx
electron.e: Electrons are commonly used for boost treatments to superficial cervical nodes or post-operative tumor beds in head and neck cancer.

electron.q: How are electron beams used in the treatment of skin cancers?
electron.a: Direct treatment of superficial lesions with bolus
c: As a boost after full course of photon therapy
Direct treatment of superficial lesions with bolus
Only for deep invasive melanoma treatment plans
Combined with brachytherapy for skin treatment
electron.e: Low-energy electrons (4-9 MeV) with bolus are used to treat superficial skin cancers, providing adequate dose to the lesion while sparing deeper tissues.

electron.q: What is the purpose of internal eye shields during electron treatment of eyelid lesions?
electron.a: To protect the lens and posterior eye structures
c: To increase the dose to the eyelid lesion target
To protect the lens and posterior eye structures
To flatten the dose profile across the eyelid area
To reduce scatter to the contralateral eye region
electron.e: Internal eye shields (typically tungsten or lead with a wax coating) are placed under the eyelid to block electrons from reaching the lens and retina.

electron.q: What is Total Skin Electron Irradiation (TSEI) primarily used to treat?
electron.a: Mycosis fungoides and cutaneous T-cell lymphoma
c: Widespread melanoma metastases on the skin layer
Mycosis fungoides and cutaneous T-cell lymphoma
Basal cell carcinoma with multiple skin lesions
Kaposi sarcoma lesions distributed across the body
electron.e: TSEI is the standard radiation treatment for mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma), delivering uniform dose to the entire skin surface.

electron.q: What is the typical electron beam energy used for TSEI treatments?
electron.a: 4 to 6 MeV at the patient treatment position
c: 2 to 3 MeV at the patient treatment position
4 to 6 MeV at the patient treatment position
9 to 12 MeV at the patient treatment position
15 to 20 MeV at the patient treatment position
electron.e: TSEI uses low energy electrons (4-6 MeV) degraded by the extended distance to deliver dose primarily to the skin (upper few mm).

electron.q: What is the typical treatment distance (SSD) used for TSEI treatments?
electron.a: 300 to 400 cm from the source to patient position
c: 100 to 120 cm from the source to patient position
150 to 200 cm from the source to patient position
300 to 400 cm from the source to patient position
500 to 600 cm from the source to patient position
electron.e: Extended distances (3-4 m) are used in TSEI to create a large enough field to encompass the entire body height.

electron.q: How many beam angles are typically used in the Stanford TSEI technique?
electron.a: Six dual-field angles (anterior, posterior, obliques)
c: Two opposing fields (anterior and posterior only)
Four beam angles covering the cardinal directions
Six dual-field angles (anterior, posterior, obliques)
Eight beam angles evenly spaced around the body
electron.e: The Stanford technique uses 6 treatment positions (AP, PA, and 4 obliques) with dual angled beams at each position for uniform skin coverage.

electron.q: What is the typical total dose prescribed for TSEI treatment of mycosis fungoides?
electron.a: 30 to 36 Gy delivered to the entire skin surface
c: 10 to 15 Gy delivered to the entire skin surface
20 to 24 Gy delivered to the entire skin surface
30 to 36 Gy delivered to the entire skin surface
45 to 50 Gy delivered to the entire skin surface
electron.e: Standard TSEI prescriptions are 30-36 Gy delivered over 8-10 weeks, with supplemental boosts to areas that are underdosed.

electron.q: What body areas typically require supplemental boost fields during TSEI?
electron.a: Skin folds, perineum, scalp vertex, and soles area
c: Only the anterior chest wall and abdominal region
Skin folds, perineum, scalp vertex, and soles area
Only the extremities (arms and legs) are boosted
Only the posterior trunk requires supplemental dose
electron.e: Self-shielded areas (perineum, inframammary folds, scalp vertex, soles) receive inadequate dose and require supplemental electron boosts.

electron.q: What is electron arc therapy and when is it clinically used?
electron.a: Continuous gantry rotation delivering electrons to curved surfaces
c: Static multiple electron fields angled at the target volume
Continuous gantry rotation delivering electrons to curved surfaces
Electron beams modulated by dynamic MLC leaf motion control
Electrons delivered through a rotating applicator on the gantry
electron.e: Electron arc therapy rotates the gantry while delivering electrons, treating curved surfaces like the chest wall with uniform dose to a specified depth.

electron.q: What is the primary advantage of electron arc therapy over fixed electron fields?
electron.a: More uniform dose to curved surfaces like the chest wall
c: Higher dose rate delivery for shorter treatment times
More uniform dose to curved surfaces like the chest wall
Elimination of bremsstrahlung contamination in the beam
Better skin sparing compared to fixed electron beam fields
electron.e: Electron arc therapy provides more uniform dose along curved anatomy because the beam continuously adjusts to the surface curvature during treatment.

electron.q: What is mixed beam therapy using electrons and photons?
electron.a: Combining electron and photon beams to optimize dose distribution
c: Using electron contamination in photon beams for treatment
Combining electron and photon beams to optimize dose distribution
Alternating between electron and photon beams each fraction
Filtering photon beams to create a mixed radiation spectrum
electron.e: Mixed beam therapy combines the sharp falloff of electrons with the penetration of photons to achieve optimal dose distributions for specific geometries.

electron.q: In what clinical scenario is a mixed electron-photon beam technique most beneficial?
electron.a: When the target extends from surface to moderate depth
c: When treating a very deep-seated abdominal tumor target
When the target extends from surface to moderate depth
When maximum skin sparing is the primary clinical goal
When treating bilateral targets simultaneously in field
electron.e: Mixed beams are useful when the target extends from near the surface to moderate depth, combining electron surface coverage with photon depth penetration.

electron.q: How does an air gap between the applicator and patient surface affect electron beams?
electron.a: It increases the penumbra and decreases the output
c: It only increases the output with no other effect
It increases the penumbra and decreases the output
It decreases the penumbra and increases beam output
It has no measurable effect on the beam qualities
electron.e: Air gaps allow electrons to scatter in air, broadening the penumbra and reducing central axis output due to loss of lateral scatter equilibrium.

electron.q: What is the maximum recommended air gap between the applicator and patient surface?
electron.a: Generally no more than 5 cm for acceptable dosimetry
c: Generally no more than 2 cm for acceptable dosimetry
Generally no more than 5 cm for acceptable dosimetry
Generally no more than 10 cm for acceptable dosimetry
Generally no more than 15 cm for acceptable dosimetry
electron.e: Air gaps up to about 5 cm are generally acceptable, though output and penumbra corrections should be applied. Larger gaps degrade the beam significantly.

electron.q: How does extended SSD affect the depth of the 80% isodose (R80) for electron beams?
electron.a: R80 decreases slightly with increasing SSD values
c: R80 increases significantly with increasing SSD used
R80 decreases slightly with increasing SSD values
R80 remains completely unchanged with SSD changes
R80 first increases then decreases with SSD change
electron.e: Extended SSD slightly reduces R80 due to increased beam divergence and reduced scatter, though the effect is small for moderate SSD changes.

electron.q: What is the inverse square law correction for electron beam output at extended SSD?
electron.a: Use effective SSD rather than nominal SSD in the formula
c: Use nominal SSD directly in the inverse square formula
Use effective SSD rather than nominal SSD in the formula
No inverse square correction is needed for electrons
Use the applicator end distance as the source position
electron.e: Electron virtual source is not at the target, so the effective SSD (virtual source position) must be used for accurate inverse square corrections.

electron.q: What is the penumbra definition used for electron beam field size specification?
electron.a: The distance between the 80% and 20% isodose lines
c: The distance between the 90% and 10% isodose lines
The distance between the 80% and 20% isodose lines
The distance between the 50% and 50% isodose lines
The distance between the 95% and 5% isodose lines
electron.e: Electron beam penumbra is typically defined as the distance between the 80% and 20% dose levels on the beam profile at a given depth.

electron.q: How does electron beam penumbra change with increasing depth in the patient?
electron.a: Penumbra increases significantly with depth in tissue
c: Penumbra remains constant at all depths in tissue
Penumbra decreases with increasing depth in tissue
Penumbra increases significantly with depth in tissue
Penumbra only changes near the practical range depth
electron.e: Multiple Coulomb scattering causes progressive beam broadening with depth, significantly widening the penumbra at greater depths.

electron.q: How does field flatness change with depth for electron beams compared to photons?
electron.a: Electrons show more rapid flatness degradation with depth
c: Electron flatness remains more stable than photon beams
Electrons show more rapid flatness degradation with depth
Flatness behavior is identical for electrons and photons
Electron flatness improves progressively with the depth
electron.e: Electron beam profiles degrade rapidly with depth due to scattering, showing significant constriction and loss of flatness at depths near R50.

electron.q: What is beam profile constriction in electron beam therapy?
electron.a: Narrowing of the useful field width with increasing depth
c: Widening of the beam profile at the surface of patient
Narrowing of the useful field width with increasing depth
Flattening of the dose profile at the maximum dose depth
The appearance of horns at the beam edges at depth dmax
electron.e: Electron beam profiles constrict (narrow) with depth because scatter equilibrium is lost at the field edges, reducing the effective field size at depth.

electron.q: What margin should be added to the target when defining the electron field size?
electron.a: Approximately 1 cm margin on each side of the target
c: No additional margin is needed beyond the target area
Approximately 1 cm margin on each side of the target
Approximately 3 cm margin on each side of the target
Approximately 5 cm margin on each side of the target
electron.e: A margin of about 1 cm is typically added to account for penumbra and field constriction, ensuring the 90% isodose covers the target at depth.

electron.q: What is the recommended detector for small electron field dosimetry?
electron.a: A small-volume parallel plate chamber or diode detector
c: A large Farmer chamber for maximum signal to noise ratio
A small-volume parallel plate chamber or diode detector
A large-area parallel plate chamber for averaging dose
Film dosimetry only is acceptable for these measurements
electron.e: Small fields require detectors with small collecting volumes to avoid averaging dose gradients. Small parallel plate chambers or diodes are preferred.

electron.q: Below what equivalent square field size do electron output factors become unreliable?
electron.a: Below approximately 4 cm equivalent square field size
c: Below approximately 2 cm equivalent square field size
Below approximately 4 cm equivalent square field size
Below approximately 8 cm equivalent square field size
Below approximately 10 cm equivalent square size
electron.e: Below about 4 cm equivalent square, lateral scatter equilibrium is not established and output factors change rapidly with small size variations.

electron.q: How does field size affect the depth of dmax for electron beams?
electron.a: dmax shifts to shallower depths for smaller field sizes
c: dmax shifts to deeper depths for smaller field sizes
dmax shifts to shallower depths for smaller field sizes
dmax remains constant regardless of the field size used
dmax shifts to the surface for all small field sizes
electron.e: Smaller fields lack lateral scatter equilibrium, shifting dmax to shallower depths and changing the overall shape of the PDD curve.

electron.q: What is the effect of bone inhomogeneity on electron beam dose distributions?
electron.a: Bone reduces penetration and causes dose perturbations
c: Bone has no effect on electron dose distributions used
Bone reduces penetration and causes dose perturbations
Bone increases the penetration depth of the electrons
Bone only affects the surface dose not the depth dose
electron.e: Bone (higher density and Z) increases scattering and reduces the range of electrons, causing dose perturbations at and beyond bone interfaces.

electron.q: How does lung tissue affect the depth dose curve of an electron beam?
electron.a: The electron range extends deeper due to lower density
c: The electron range decreases due to higher scattering
The electron range extends deeper due to lower density
Lung tissue has no effect on the electron dose curve
The dose at dmax increases significantly in lung area
electron.e: Lung tissue (density ~0.25-0.3 g/cm3) allows electrons to penetrate much further, extending the depth dose curve and potentially overdosing tissues beyond.

electron.q: What is the pencil beam algorithm and its role in electron dose calculation?
electron.a: It models dose by summing contributions from narrow beamlets
c: It calculates dose by ray tracing along the central axis
It models dose by summing contributions from narrow beamlets
It uses measured data lookup tables for dose at all points
It solves the Boltzmann transport equation analytically here
electron.e: The pencil beam algorithm decomposes the broad beam into narrow pencil beams, summing their dose contributions using scattering kernels for each beamlet.

electron.q: What is the main limitation of pencil beam algorithms for electron dose calculation?
electron.a: Poor accuracy near tissue inhomogeneities and interfaces
c: Excessive computation time for clinical treatment plans
Poor accuracy near tissue inhomogeneities and interfaces
Inability to handle oblique beam incidence on the skin
Cannot calculate dose for energies above 12 MeV beam
electron.e: Pencil beam algorithms poorly model lateral scatter perturbations at tissue interfaces, leading to dose errors near bone, lung, and air cavities.

electron.q: What advantage does Monte Carlo dose calculation offer for electron beam planning?
electron.a: Most accurate modeling of electron transport in tissue
c: Fastest calculation speed for clinical planning use
Most accurate modeling of electron transport in tissue
Simplest algorithm to commission and implement clinically
Requires no patient CT data for dose calculation here
electron.e: Monte Carlo simulates individual particle histories, accurately modeling scatter, energy loss, and heterogeneity effects for superior electron dose accuracy.

electron.q: What is the typical statistical uncertainty goal for clinical Monte Carlo electron calculations?
electron.a: Approximately 1-2% statistical uncertainty in the target
c: Approximately 0.1% statistical uncertainty in target
Approximately 1-2% statistical uncertainty in the target
Approximately 5-10% statistical uncertainty in target
Statistical uncertainty is not relevant for Monte Carlo
electron.e: Clinical Monte Carlo calculations typically aim for 1-2% statistical uncertainty in the target region, balancing accuracy with reasonable computation time.

electron.q: What Monte Carlo code is commonly used for electron beam dose calculations?
electron.a: EGSnrc or its derivatives such as BEAMnrc/DOSXYZnrc
c: FLUKA only for all clinical electron calculations used
EGSnrc or its derivatives such as BEAMnrc/DOSXYZnrc
MCNP exclusively for electron beam dose calculations
Geant4 only for clinical treatment planning purposes
electron.e: EGSnrc (and BEAMnrc/DOSXYZnrc) is widely used for linac modeling and electron dose calculations. Commercial TPS may use simplified MC engines.

electron.q: What variance reduction technique is commonly used to speed up Monte Carlo electron simulations?
electron.a: Particle splitting and Russian roulette techniques used
c: Increasing the step size of all electron transport used
Particle splitting and Russian roulette techniques used
Reducing the energy cutoff to zero for all particles
Ignoring all bremsstrahlung interactions in the model
electron.e: Particle splitting increases statistics in important regions while Russian roulette eliminates unimportant particles, significantly reducing computation time.

electron.q: What is the clinical significance of the R50 parameter in electron beam dosimetry?
electron.a: It defines the beam quality and determines mean energy
c: It represents the maximum treatment depth for target
It defines the beam quality and determines mean energy
It specifies the field size for lateral equilibrium
It determines the surface dose value of the beam used
electron.e: R50 (depth of 50% dose) is the primary beam quality specifier for electrons. E0 = 2.33 x R50 relates it to mean energy at the phantom surface.

electron.q: How is the photon contamination in electron beams typically measured?
electron.a: From the bremsstrahlung tail beyond practical range Rp
c: By measuring dose at the surface of the phantom used
From the bremsstrahlung tail beyond practical range Rp
By subtracting the electron PDD from a photon PDD curve
Using a magnetic spectrometer to separate the components
electron.e: Photon contamination is estimated from the dose in the bremsstrahlung tail region beyond Rp, where no primary electrons remain.

electron.q: What is the typical beam flatness specification for electron beams at reference depth?
electron.a: Within plus or minus 3% over 80% of field width used
c: Within plus or minus 1% over 90% of field width used
Within plus or minus 3% over 80% of field width used
Within plus or minus 5% over 70% of field width used
Within plus or minus 10% over 50% of field width area
electron.e: Electron beam flatness is typically specified as +/-3% variation over the central 80% of the field width at the reference depth.

electron.q: What is the beam symmetry specification for clinical electron beams?
electron.a: Within 2-3% difference between symmetric field halves
c: Within 0.5% difference between symmetric field halves
Within 2-3% difference between symmetric field halves
Within 5-7% difference between symmetric field halves
Within 10% difference between symmetric halves of beam
electron.e: Symmetry is specified as the maximum difference between corresponding points on opposite sides of the central axis, typically within 2-3%.

electron.q: What is the energy spectrum of clinical electron beams at the patient surface?
electron.a: A broad spectrum peaked near the most probable energy
c: A monoenergetic beam at the exact nominal energy value
A broad spectrum peaked near the most probable energy
A flat uniform spectrum spanning from zero to maximum
A narrow Gaussian peak at exactly the mean energy used
electron.e: Clinical electrons have a broad energy spectrum due to interactions in the scattering foils and air. The peak is near Ep,0, with a tail to lower energies.

electron.q: What is the energy spread (FWHM) of a typical clinical electron beam at the surface?
electron.a: Approximately 10-15% of the mean energy at surface
c: Approximately 1-2% of the mean energy at the surface
Approximately 10-15% of the mean energy at surface
Approximately 25-30% of the mean energy at surface
Approximately 40-50% of the mean energy at surface
electron.e: After traversing scattering foils and air, the energy spectrum broadens to approximately 10-15% FWHM relative to the mean energy.

electron.q: How does the electron energy spectrum change with depth in the patient?
electron.a: Mean energy decreases linearly with depth in the phantom
c: Mean energy remains constant throughout all of depth
Mean energy decreases linearly with depth in the phantom
Mean energy increases with depth due to beam hardening
Mean energy fluctuates unpredictably with beam depth
electron.e: The mean energy decreases approximately linearly with depth: E(z) = E0(1 - z/Rp), where z is depth and Rp is the practical range.

electron.q: What is the relationship between electron beam energy and the dose rate at dmax?
electron.a: Dose rate typically decreases with increasing energy
c: Dose rate increases with increasing electron energy
Dose rate typically decreases with increasing energy
Dose rate remains constant for all electron energies
Dose rate peaks at intermediate energies around 12 MeV
electron.e: Higher energy electrons require thicker scattering foils, increasing energy loss and bremsstrahlung, which reduces the dose rate at dmax.

electron.q: What is the typical dose rate for clinical electron beams at 100 cm SSD?
electron.a: Approximately 200 to 1000 cGy per minute at dmax
c: Approximately 10 to 50 cGy per minute at dmax depth
Approximately 200 to 1000 cGy per minute at dmax
Approximately 2000 to 5000 cGy per minute at dmax
Approximately 10000 to 20000 cGy per min at dmax
electron.e: Clinical electron beam dose rates are typically 200-1000 cGy/min at dmax, varying with energy and machine type.

electron.q: Which TG report provides the protocol for absolute dose calibration of electron beams?
electron.a: TG-51 for clinical reference dosimetry of electron beams
c: TG-25 for clinical reference dosimetry of electron beams
TG-51 for clinical reference dosimetry of electron beams
TG-142 for clinical reference dosimetry of electron beams
TG-100 for clinical reference dosimetry of electron beams
electron.e: AAPM TG-51 provides the protocol for absorbed dose calibration of photon and electron beams using ion chambers calibrated in Co-60.

electron.q: What is the PDD gradient correction (Pgr) in electron beam dosimetry?
electron.a: Correction for dose variation across the chamber volume
c: Correction for temperature and pressure effects in air
Correction for dose variation across the chamber volume
Correction for recombination losses in the ion chamber
Correction for stem and cable irradiation in electron beam
electron.e: Pgr corrects for the dose gradient across the chamber sensitive volume, which is significant in electron beams due to steep PDD gradients.

electron.q: What is the replacement correction factor (Prepl) for parallel plate chambers in electrons?
electron.a: Unity (1.0) for well-guarded parallel plate chambers
c: Typically 0.95 for parallel plate chambers in electrons
Unity (1.0) for well-guarded parallel plate chambers
Typically 1.05 for parallel plate chambers in electrons
Variable depending on the electron beam energy used
electron.e: For well-guarded parallel plate chambers, Prepl = 1.0 because the thin entrance window and guard ring minimize fluence perturbation.

electron.q: What is the cross-calibration technique for parallel plate chambers in electron beams?
electron.a: Calibrating against a Farmer chamber in a high-energy electron beam
c: Calibrating against a Farmer chamber in a high-energy electron beam
Calibrating the parallel plate chamber directly in a Co-60 beam only
Using manufacturer specifications without any field measurements at all
Comparing readings to a standard electrometer calibration factor value
electron.e: Parallel plate chambers can be cross-calibrated against a calibrated Farmer chamber in a high-energy electron beam where both chamber types are accurate.

electron.q: What is the ion recombination correction factor (Pion) and how is it measured?
electron.a: Correction for charge lost to recombination using two voltages
c: Correction for charge lost to recombination using two voltages
Correction for charge gained from external radiation sources
Correction for leakage current in the ionization chamber used
Correction for humidity effects on ion chamber response values
electron.e: Pion corrects for incomplete ion collection. It is measured using the two-voltage technique, comparing readings at full and reduced voltage.

electron.q: What is the typical tolerance for electron beam output constancy in monthly QA?
electron.a: Within plus or minus 3% of the baseline calibration
c: Within plus or minus 1% of the baseline calibration
Within plus or minus 3% of the baseline calibration
Within plus or minus 5% of the baseline calibration
Within plus or minus 10% of baseline calibration
electron.e: TG-142 recommends monthly electron output constancy checks within +/- 3% for each electron energy and cone combination.

electron.q: How often should electron beam PDD and profile constancy be checked per TG-142?
electron.a: Annually as part of the comprehensive QA program
c: Daily before each patient treatment begins for day
Monthly as part of the routine QA program checks
Annually as part of the comprehensive QA program
Only after major linac repairs or component changes
electron.e: TG-142 recommends annual verification of electron PDD and profile constancy as part of the comprehensive annual QA program.

electron.q: What is the recommended tolerance for electron beam energy constancy (R50)?
electron.a: Within plus or minus 2 mm of baseline R50 value
c: Within plus or minus 1 mm of baseline R50 value
Within plus or minus 2 mm of baseline R50 value
Within plus or minus 5 mm of baseline R50 value
Within plus or minus 10 mm of baseline R50 value
electron.e: TG-142 specifies that R50 should be within 2 mm of the commissioning baseline to ensure energy constancy.

electron.q: What water phantom size is recommended for electron beam commissioning measurements?
electron.a: At least 30 x 30 x 30 cm water phantom dimensions
c: At least 10 x 10 x 10 cm water phantom dimensions
At least 20 x 20 x 20 cm water phantom dimensions
At least 30 x 30 x 30 cm water phantom dimensions
At least 50 x 50 x 50 cm water phantom dimensions
electron.e: The phantom should be large enough to provide full scatter conditions, typically at least 30 x 30 x 30 cm for standard electron beam measurements.

electron.q: What is the standard cone (applicator) used as the reference for electron beam calibration?
electron.a: The 10 x 10 cm applicator at standard 100 cm SSD
c: The 6 x 6 cm applicator at standard 100 cm SSD used
The 10 x 10 cm applicator at standard 100 cm SSD
The 15 x 15 cm applicator at standard 100 cm SSD
The 25 x 25 cm applicator at standard 100 cm SSD
electron.e: The 10 x 10 cm applicator (open cone, no insert) at 100 cm SSD is the standard reference geometry for electron beam calibration.

electron.q: What is the electron cone factor (applicator factor) in electron beam dosimetry?
electron.a: Ratio of output with a given cone to the reference cone
c: Ratio of output with a given cone to the reference cone
Ratio of electron dose to photon dose in the same cone
Ratio of surface dose to dmax dose for a given cone used
Ratio of penumbra width to field size for a cone value
electron.e: The cone (applicator) factor is the ratio of dose per MU at dmax for a given cone to that of the reference (10x10) cone at the same energy.

electron.q: How do electron applicator walls contribute to the dose at the field edges?
electron.a: They scatter electrons into the field adding to edge dose
c: They absorb all scattered electrons at field edges used
They scatter electrons into the field adding to edge dose
They have no effect on the dose near the field edges
They produce bremsstrahlung that contaminates the beam
electron.e: Applicator walls scatter electrons back into the field, contributing to the dose near the edges and affecting the output factor for different applicators.

electron.q: What is the minimum field size below which electron beam PDD changes significantly?
electron.a: When the field size approaches the practical range value
c: When the field size is less than 2 cm for all energies
When the field size approaches the practical range value
When the field size drops below the 10x10 cm reference
When the field size exceeds the 25x25 cm applicator
electron.e: When the field size is comparable to the practical range, lateral scatter equilibrium is lost and the PDD changes shape, shifting dmax shallower.

electron.q: What is the Hogstrom equivalent square method for irregular electron cutouts?
electron.a: Equivalent square equals four times area divided by perimeter
c: Equivalent square equals the square root of the cutout area
Equivalent square equals four times area divided by perimeter
Equivalent square equals the longest diagonal of the cutout
Equivalent square equals two times the minimum field dimension
electron.e: The Hogstrom method uses equivalent square = 4A/P (where A is area and P is perimeter) to relate irregular cutouts to square field output data.

electron.q: When is the Hogstrom equivalent square method least accurate for electron cutouts?
electron.a: For highly elongated or very irregular field shapes used
c: For circular or near-square cutout shapes being used
For highly elongated or very irregular field shapes used
For large fields above 15 cm equivalent square value
For cutouts used with low electron energies under 6 MeV
electron.e: The equivalent square method assumes a reasonably compact shape. Highly elongated or irregular shapes may not achieve the predicted lateral equilibrium.

electron.q: What is the density of Cerrobend alloy used for electron field shaping?
electron.a: Approximately 9.4 g/cm3 in standard alloy composition
c: Approximately 5.2 g/cm3 in standard alloy composition
Approximately 9.4 g/cm3 in standard alloy composition
Approximately 11.3 g/cm3 in standard alloy composition
Approximately 14.0 g/cm3 in standard alloy composition
electron.e: Cerrobend has a density of about 9.4 g/cm3, which is lower than pure lead (11.3 g/cm3), so slightly thicker inserts are needed.

electron.q: What are the constituent metals in standard Cerrobend (Lipowitz) alloy?
electron.a: Bismuth, lead, tin, and cadmium in specific ratios
c: Tungsten, copper, nickel, and iron in specific ratios
Bismuth, lead, tin, and cadmium in specific ratios
Lead, antimony, silver, and zinc in specific ratios
Gold, platinum, iridium, and tin in specific ratios
electron.e: Cerrobend contains 50% bismuth, 26.7% lead, 13.3% tin, and 10% cadmium by weight, creating a low melting point eutectic alloy.

electron.q: How is divergence accounted for when cutting Cerrobend inserts for electron cones?
electron.a: The cutout is shaped to match beam divergence at the insert plane
c: Divergence is ignored since electrons scatter significantly in air
The cutout is shaped to match beam divergence at the insert plane
A flat cutout is used and the SSD is adjusted to compensate here
The cutout is made smaller than the target to correct for spread
electron.e: The cutout should be shaped accounting for beam divergence at the insert plane level to ensure proper field size at the patient surface.

electron.q: What happens to the electron beam dose distribution in the presence of a step surface irregularity?
electron.a: Hot and cold spots occur near the step edge in dose
c: The dose distribution remains completely uniform here
Hot and cold spots occur near the step edge in dose
Only a cold spot forms distal to the step edge area
Only a hot spot forms proximal to the step edge here
electron.e: A step creates dose inhomogeneities: electrons scatter from the higher surface into the lower region creating hot spots, and vice versa.

electron.q: What is the effect of a sharp surface convexity on electron beam dose distribution?
electron.a: Lateral scatter out of the region creates a cold spot
c: A hot spot forms due to increased scatter convergence
Lateral scatter out of the region creates a cold spot
The dose distribution is not affected by convexity here
Surface dose is eliminated at the convex surface area
electron.e: At convex surfaces, electrons scatter outward (away from the central region), reducing the dose and creating a cold spot under the convexity.

electron.q: What is the effect of a sharp surface concavity on electron beam dose distribution?
electron.a: Scatter convergence into the region creates a hot spot
c: A cold spot forms due to divergent electron scattering
Scatter convergence into the region creates a hot spot
The dose distribution is not affected by concavity here
Surface dose is reduced significantly at the concavity
electron.e: At concave surfaces, electrons scatter inward (toward the central region), increasing the dose and creating a hot spot under the concavity.

electron.q: How does a high-Z material like a dental filling affect electron beam dose locally?
electron.a: Backscatter increases dose upstream and reduces downstream dose
c: The filling has no significant effect on dose distribution here
Backscatter increases dose upstream and reduces downstream dose
The filling only increases dose downstream due to beam hardening
The filling completely absorbs all electrons without any scatter
electron.e: High-Z materials cause significant electron backscatter (increasing upstream dose by 30-70%) and attenuate the beam downstream.

electron.q: What is the typical field size range where electron output factors are relatively constant?
electron.a: Above the lateral equilibrium field size for that energy
c: Only at the exact 10 x 10 cm reference field size used
Above the lateral equilibrium field size for that energy
For all field sizes from 2 cm to 25 cm square in size
Output factors are never constant for any field size range
electron.e: Once the field exceeds the lateral scatter equilibrium size (approximately Rp), output factors plateau and become relatively constant.

electron.q: How is the gap between adjacent electron fields managed in clinical practice?
electron.a: Fields are abutted at the surface with feathered junctions
c: Fields are overlapped by 2 cm to ensure full coverage used
Fields are abutted at the surface with feathered junctions
A 3 cm gap is left between fields to avoid hot spots here
Adjacent electron fields are never used in clinical practice
electron.e: Electron field junctions are managed by abutting at the surface and potentially feathering (shifting) the junction to avoid persistent hot/cold spots.

electron.q: What is the dose inhomogeneity concern at electron-electron field junctions?
electron.a: Hot spots from overlapping penumbra regions at the junction
c: Cold spots from beam divergence away from the junction
Hot spots from overlapping penumbra regions at the junction
Uniform dose across the junction with no inhomogeneity
Bremsstrahlung contamination concentrated at the junction
