electron.e: The broad electron penumbra creates significant overlap at field junctions, producing hot spots that can be 20-40% above the prescribed dose.

electron.q: What is the dose inhomogeneity concern at electron-photon field junctions?
electron.a: Complex dose gradients from different beam characteristics
c: No concerns exist at electron-photon field junctions used
Only cold spots occur at electron-photon junctions here
Complex dose gradients from different beam characteristics
The photon beam dominates and eliminates electron effects
electron.e: Electron-photon junctions create complex dose distributions because the two modalities have very different depth dose and penumbra characteristics.

electron.q: What is the typical prescription isodose line for electron beam treatments?
electron.a: The 90% isodose line (or 80% for some institutions)
c: The 100% isodose line at the depth of maximum dose
The 90% isodose line (or 80% for some institutions)
The 50% isodose line at the half-value depth point
The 70% isodose line for all electron prescriptions
electron.e: Electron treatments are commonly prescribed to the 90% isodose (or 80% in some protocols) to ensure adequate target coverage at depth.

electron.q: What electron energy would be most appropriate for treating a parotid bed boost at 2 cm depth?
electron.a: 6 MeV electrons to adequately cover the target area
c: 4 MeV electrons to adequately cover the target area
6 MeV electrons to adequately cover the target area
12 MeV electrons to adequately cover target at depth
16 MeV electrons to adequately cover target at depth
electron.e: Using E/3 rule: 6/3 = 2 cm for R80. This adequately covers a 2 cm deep target while sparing the underlying spinal cord.

electron.q: What is the clinical rationale for using electrons to boost the tumor bed after lumpectomy?
electron.a: To deliver additional dose to the superficial surgical cavity
c: To replace the entire course of whole breast photon therapy
To deliver additional dose to the superficial surgical cavity
To treat the axillary and supraclavicular lymph node regions
To deliver dose to the contralateral breast simultaneously
electron.e: Electron boosts after whole breast photon therapy deliver additional dose to the superficial lumpectomy cavity to improve local control rates.

electron.q: What is the typical electron boost dose prescribed after whole breast irradiation?
electron.a: 10 to 16 Gy in 5 to 8 fractions with electron beam
c: 2 to 4 Gy in 1 to 2 fractions with electron beam used
10 to 16 Gy in 5 to 8 fractions with electron beam
25 to 30 Gy in 12 to 15 fractions with electron beam
40 to 50 Gy in 20 to 25 fractions with electron beam
electron.e: A typical electron boost delivers 10-16 Gy in 2 Gy fractions to the tumor bed after 45-50 Gy of whole breast photon irradiation.

electron.q: How are electron beams used in the treatment of superficial inguinal lymph nodes?
electron.a: Direct electron fields to treat nodes at shallow depth
c: Opposed photon fields with electron beam as a boost only
Direct electron fields to treat nodes at shallow depth
Electron arc therapy rotating around the inguinal area
TSEI technique focused on the inguinal region of patient
electron.e: Superficial inguinal nodes can be treated with direct electron fields, selecting energy to cover the node depth while sparing deeper femoral vessels.

electron.q: What is the nose bridge electron technique used in clinical practice?
electron.a: Treating medial canthal or nasal skin cancers with electrons
c: A photon beam arrangement for treating nasal cavity tumors
Treating medial canthal or nasal skin cancers with electrons
A specialized IMRT technique for paranasal sinus cancers
An electron arc technique for the entire nose surface area
electron.e: Low-energy electrons with small cutouts and internal eye shields treat skin cancers near the nose and medial canthus while protecting the eyes.

electron.q: What is the virtual source position for electron beams and how is it determined?
electron.a: Found by back-projecting beam edge profiles to convergence point
c: It is always at the x-ray target position inside the linac head
Found by back-projecting beam edge profiles to convergence point
It is at the exit window of the accelerator waveguide assembly
It is at the last scattering foil position inside the linac head
electron.e: The virtual source is determined by projecting the 50% beam edges back from profiles at multiple SSDs until they converge at a point upstream.

electron.q: How does the virtual source distance vary with electron beam energy?
electron.a: Virtual source moves closer to isocenter at higher energies
c: Virtual source moves farther from isocenter at higher energies
Virtual source moves closer to isocenter at higher energies
Virtual source position is constant for all electron energies
Virtual source position is randomly variable with beam energy
electron.e: Higher energy electrons scatter less, making the beam more forward-directed and moving the virtual source closer to the applicator/isocenter.

electron.q: What is the electron beam spoiler and when is it used clinically?
electron.a: A thin scattering material placed near skin to increase surface dose
c: A thick lead sheet used to completely block the electron beam path
A thin scattering material placed near skin to increase surface dose
A device placed in the linac head to reduce the beam energy level
A magnetic field device used to steer the electron beam direction
electron.e: A spoiler (thin acrylic or metal sheet) near the patient increases surface dose by scattering electrons, useful when skin coverage is needed.

electron.q: What effect does a 1 cm air gap between bolus and skin have on electron dose?
electron.a: It reduces surface dose and degrades dose distribution quality
c: It has absolutely no effect on the dose distribution at all
It reduces surface dose and degrades dose distribution quality
It increases the surface dose above the expected bolus value
It only affects the dose beyond the practical range of beam
electron.e: Air gaps between bolus and skin allow electrons to scatter, reducing the surface dose buildup effect and creating dose inhomogeneities.

electron.q: What is the radiative stopping power and when does it become significant for electrons?
electron.a: Energy loss by bremsstrahlung, significant above 10 MeV in tissue
c: Energy loss by ionization, significant at all clinical energies
Energy loss by bremsstrahlung, significant above 10 MeV in tissue
Energy loss by nuclear reactions, significant above 20 MeV only
Energy loss by Cherenkov radiation, significant above 5 MeV here
electron.e: Radiative stopping power (bremsstrahlung) increases with energy and Z. In tissue (low Z), it becomes notable above 10 MeV and dominant at very high energies.

electron.q: What is the critical energy where radiative and collisional stopping powers are equal?
electron.a: Approximately 800 MeV divided by (Z + 1.2) of material
c: Approximately 100 MeV divided by (Z + 1.2) of material
Approximately 800 MeV divided by (Z + 1.2) of material
Approximately 1600 MeV divided by (Z + 1.2) of material
Approximately 2400 MeV divided by (Z + 1.2) of material
electron.e: The critical energy Ec ~ 800/(Z+1.2) MeV. For water (Zeff~7.4), Ec ~ 93 MeV, well above clinical energies, confirming collisional losses dominate.

electron.q: What is the restricted stopping power and why is it used in dosimetry calculations?
electron.a: Stopping power excluding high-energy delta rays that escape
c: Total stopping power including all forms of energy loss here
Stopping power excluding high-energy delta rays that escape
Stopping power for electrons below a threshold energy level
Stopping power measured only in the Bragg peak region used
electron.e: Restricted stopping power excludes energy transferred to delta rays above a cutoff energy, representing energy deposited locally in the cavity volume.

electron.q: What is the Spencer-Attix cavity theory and its relevance to electron dosimetry?
electron.a: Relates dose in medium to dose in cavity using stopping power ratios
c: Calculates dose from first principles without any measured input data
Relates dose in medium to dose in cavity using stopping power ratios
Describes only photon interactions in an ionization chamber cavity
Applies exclusively to large cavities in megavoltage photon beams
electron.e: Spencer-Attix theory uses restricted stopping power ratios to relate dose measured in the air cavity of an ion chamber to dose in the surrounding medium.

electron.q: What is the water-to-air stopping power ratio used for in electron beam dosimetry?
electron.a: Converting ionization chamber readings to dose in water medium
c: Determining the beam energy at the phantom surface position
Converting ionization chamber readings to dose in water medium
Calculating the bremsstrahlung contamination in the electron beam
Measuring the electron beam penumbra width at treatment depth
electron.e: The water-to-air stopping power ratio is a key factor in converting air ionization measurements to absorbed dose in water via cavity theory.

electron.q: How does the water-to-air stopping power ratio change with electron beam energy?
electron.a: It decreases slightly with increasing electron energy values
c: It increases significantly with increasing electron energy
It decreases slightly with increasing electron energy values
It remains constant for all clinical electron beam energies
It oscillates unpredictably with changing electron energies
electron.e: The water-to-air stopping power ratio decreases slightly from about 1.13 at 4 MeV to about 1.03 at 20 MeV for clinical electron beams.

electron.q: What is the Gaussian pencil beam model for electron dose calculation?
electron.a: Models each pencil beam as a Gaussian lateral dose spread
c: Models the full beam as a single Gaussian depth dose curve
Models each pencil beam as a Gaussian lateral dose spread
Models the beam energy spectrum as a Gaussian distribution
Models the angular distribution as a single Gaussian peak
electron.e: The Gaussian pencil beam model describes the lateral dose spread of each narrow pencil beam as a Gaussian function whose width increases with depth.

electron.q: What parameter in the pencil beam model describes the lateral spread with depth?
electron.a: Sigma (the lateral spread parameter) increasing with depth
c: The beam divergence angle from the virtual source point
Sigma (the lateral spread parameter) increasing with depth
The field size factor at the reference treatment depth used
The mean free path of electrons in the tissue material
electron.e: Sigma (standard deviation of the Gaussian spread) increases with depth due to multiple Coulomb scattering, characterizing the lateral dose distribution.

electron.q: What is the Fermi-Eyges theory in the context of electron beam transport?
electron.a: A small-angle multiple scattering theory for electron transport
c: A theory describing electron energy loss via bremsstrahlung
A small-angle multiple scattering theory for electron transport
A theory for calculating photon beam attenuation in tissue
A nuclear interaction model for high-energy electron beams
electron.e: Fermi-Eyges theory describes the angular and spatial spread of electrons due to multiple small-angle Coulomb scattering, fundamental to pencil beam algorithms.

electron.q: What is the main advantage of electron Monte Carlo over pencil beam in lung treatments?
electron.a: Accurately models lateral transport in low-density tissue regions
c: Faster calculation speed for complex treatment planning cases
Accurately models lateral transport in low-density tissue regions
Better resolution of surface dose in the buildup dose region
More stable numerical convergence for the iterative solutions
electron.e: Monte Carlo accurately models the increased lateral scattering in low-density lung tissue, which pencil beam algorithms significantly underestimate.

electron.q: What is the condensed history technique used in Monte Carlo electron transport?
electron.a: Groups many small interactions into single larger steps
c: Simulates every individual electron interaction explicitly
Groups many small interactions into single larger steps
Ignores all interactions below a certain energy threshold
Uses analytical solutions instead of random sampling here
electron.e: Condensed history groups many small-angle scattering and energy loss events into single steps, making electron MC computationally feasible.

electron.q: What is the electron transport cutoff energy (ECUT) in Monte Carlo simulations?
electron.a: Energy below which electrons are absorbed and deposited locally
c: Maximum energy of electrons tracked in the Monte Carlo code
Energy below which electrons are absorbed and deposited locally
The threshold energy for bremsstrahlung photon production
The minimum energy needed to penetrate the phantom surface
electron.e: ECUT is the energy threshold below which electron transport is stopped and remaining energy is deposited locally, saving computation time.

electron.q: What typical ECUT value is used for clinical electron Monte Carlo calculations?
electron.a: 0.521 MeV (total energy including rest mass of electron)
c: 0.100 MeV (total energy including rest mass of electron)
0.521 MeV (total energy including rest mass of electron)
1.000 MeV (total energy including rest mass of electron)
5.000 MeV (total energy including rest mass of electron)
electron.e: ECUT = 0.521 MeV (0.010 MeV kinetic + 0.511 MeV rest mass) is commonly used, providing accurate results with reasonable computation time.

electron.q: What is the TG-51 addendum and what update does it provide for electron dosimetry?
electron.a: Updated kQ values and measurement guidance for modern beams
c: A completely new protocol replacing the original TG-51 report
Updated kQ values and measurement guidance for modern beams
New requirements for daily output checks on electron beams
Guidance only for flattening-filter-free photon beam modes
electron.e: The TG-51 addendum provides updated kQ data, measurement recommendations, and guidance for implementing TG-51 with modern equipment.

electron.q: What is the recommended chamber orientation for parallel plate chambers in electron beams?
electron.a: Entrance window perpendicular to the beam central axis
c: Entrance window parallel to the beam central axis line
Entrance window perpendicular to the beam central axis
Chamber oriented at 45 degrees to the beam central axis
Any orientation is acceptable for parallel plate chambers
electron.e: Parallel plate chambers should be oriented with the entrance window facing the beam (perpendicular to the central axis) for proper electron measurements.

electron.q: What is the guard ring width requirement for parallel plate chambers per TG-51?
electron.a: Guard ring width should be at least equal to cavity height
c: Guard ring width should be at least twice cavity height
Guard ring width should be at least equal to cavity height
No guard ring is required for parallel plate chambers
Guard ring width must be exactly half of cavity height
electron.e: The guard ring width should be at least equal to the plate separation (cavity height) to ensure proper collection field and minimize edge effects.

electron.q: What commonly used parallel plate chamber is recommended for electron beam dosimetry?
electron.a: PTW Roos, Exradin A11, or NACP chamber models used
c: PTW Farmer 30013 cylindrical chamber model exclusively
PTW Roos, Exradin A11, or NACP chamber models used
IBA CC13 compact cylindrical chamber model exclusively
Standard Nuclear Associates model 30-716 survey meter
electron.e: The PTW Roos, Exradin A11/A10, and NACP chambers are well-characterized parallel plate chambers commonly used for electron beam dosimetry.

electron.q: What is the waterproof sleeve requirement for parallel plate chambers in water phantoms?
electron.a: A thin waterproof sleeve (less than 1 mm PMMA) is required
c: No waterproof sleeve is needed for these chamber types
A thin waterproof sleeve (less than 1 mm PMMA) is required
A thick 5 mm acrylic sleeve must be used for protection
Only rubber sleeves are acceptable for waterproofing use
electron.e: A thin waterproof sleeve (typically <1 mm PMMA) protects the chamber in water without significantly perturbing the electron fluence.

electron.q: How does the stopping power ratio change with depth in an electron beam?
electron.a: It increases with depth as mean electron energy decreases
c: It decreases with depth as mean electron energy decreases
It increases with depth as mean electron energy decreases
It remains constant at all depths in the electron beam
It first decreases then increases with depth in the beam
electron.e: As electrons lose energy with depth, the water-to-air stopping power ratio increases because low-energy electrons have higher stopping power ratios.

electron.q: What is the dose-per-MU concept for electron beams in clinical treatment delivery?
electron.a: The calibrated output in cGy per monitor unit at reference depth
c: The total dose delivered in a single treatment fraction given
The calibrated output in cGy per monitor unit at reference depth
The maximum dose rate achievable by the linear accelerator
The ratio of measured to calculated dose for a treatment plan

electron.q: What is the typical photon jaw setting when using electron applicators on a Varian linac?

c: Jaws are fully open at 40 x 40 cm for all electron cones
Jaws are set to match or slightly exceed the cone size used
Jaws are fully closed since the cone defines the field size

electron.q: What is the role of the electron beam monitor chamber in the linac treatment head?
electron.a: To monitor dose rate, total dose, symmetry, and flatness
c: To scatter the electron beam into a broad flat field
To monitor dose rate, total dose, symmetry, and flatness
To filter out photon contamination from the electron beam
To shape the electron energy spectrum before the patient
