electron.q: How does the bending magnet energy selection system work for electron beams?
electron.a: The magnet bends only electrons of the correct momentum value
c: The magnet accelerates electrons to the desired energy level
The magnet bends only electrons of the correct momentum value
The magnet removes all photon contamination from the beam
The magnet focuses the beam to a point at the isocenter
electron.e: The bending magnet acts as a momentum analyzer, selecting electrons within a narrow energy range and directing them toward the treatment head.

electron.q: What is the energy slit in a linac and how does it affect electron beams?
electron.a: A narrow opening after the bend that selects a specific energy range
c: A filter placed in the beam path to attenuate the beam intensity
A narrow opening after the bend that selects a specific energy range
A collimator that defines the maximum field size for all electron beams
A window that separates the vacuum system from the treatment head area
electron.e: The energy slit after the bending magnet blocks electrons outside the desired energy range, defining the energy spread of the clinical beam.

electron.q: What is the dose homogeneity index used to evaluate electron treatment plans?
electron.a: The ratio of maximum to minimum dose within the target volume
c: The ratio of surface dose to the depth of maximum dose value
The ratio of maximum to minimum dose within the target volume
The ratio of the electron dose to the photon contamination
The ratio of the prescribed dose to the mean target dose value
electron.e: Dose homogeneity index evaluates the uniformity of dose within the target, considering both hot spots and cold spots in the electron plan.

electron.q: What is the recommended scanning speed for electron beam depth dose measurements?
electron.a: Slow enough to avoid smoothing of the steep dose gradient
c: As fast as possible to minimize measurement time needed
Slow enough to avoid smoothing of the steep dose gradient
Exactly 1 cm per second for all depth dose measurements
Speed does not matter for electron depth dose scanning
electron.e: The scanning speed must be slow enough (typically 2-5 mm/s) that the detector response keeps up with the steep gradients in electron PDD curves.

electron.q: What is the ionization-to-dose conversion for electron beam depth dose measurements?
electron.a: Multiply ionization by depth-dependent stopping power ratios
c: Ionization and dose curves are identical for electron beams
Multiply ionization by depth-dependent stopping power ratios
Divide ionization by a constant factor at all depth values
No conversion is needed when using an electron diode here
electron.e: The depth-ionization curve must be converted to depth-dose using water-to-air stopping power ratios that vary with depth (energy) in the beam.

electron.q: Why are electron diodes preferred over ion chambers for relative electron dosimetry?
electron.a: Their response is approximately proportional to dose directly
c: They have better energy independence than ion chambers used
Their response is approximately proportional to dose directly
They have much larger sensitive volumes for better signal
They do not require any cable connections for measurements
electron.e: Silicon diodes have stopping power ratios (Si/water) that vary less with energy than air/water ratios, so their signal is nearly proportional to dose.

electron.q: What is the over-response correction needed for unshielded electron diodes?
electron.a: Correction for increased sensitivity to low-energy scattered electrons
c: Correction for decreased sensitivity at high dose rate values
Correction for increased sensitivity to low-energy scattered electrons
Correction for directional dependence of the diode response used
Correction for temperature sensitivity in the measurement setup
electron.e: Unshielded diodes over-respond to low-energy scattered electrons at depth, requiring corrections especially near the practical range.

electron.q: What is the recommended frequency for electron beam output calibration per TG-51?
electron.a: Full calibration annually with monthly constancy checks done
c: Full calibration daily with no additional checks required
Full calibration annually with monthly constancy checks done
Full calibration every five years with annual checks done
Full calibration only after major repairs are completed
electron.e: TG-51 recommends full annual calibration. Monthly constancy checks per TG-142 ensure output stability between annual calibrations.

electron.q: What is the effect of the electron beam trimmer on Elekta linear accelerators?
electron.a: Trimmers shape the field edge and improve the beam penumbra
c: Trimmers increase the beam energy for deeper penetration
Trimmers shape the field edge and improve the beam penumbra
Trimmers remove all photon contamination from electron beam
Trimmers replace the need for external electron applicators
electron.e: Elekta linacs use adjustable trimmers (secondary collimators near the patient) to define the electron field and sharpen the penumbra.

electron.q: What is the difference between Varian and Elekta electron beam delivery systems?
electron.a: Varian uses applicator cones while Elekta uses adjustable trimmers
c: Both systems use identical electron delivery mechanisms for beams
Varian uses applicator cones while Elekta uses adjustable trimmers
Varian uses trimmers while Elekta uses applicator cones for beams
Only Varian linacs can deliver clinical electron beams for therapy
electron.e: Varian uses fixed applicator cones with custom cutouts, while Elekta uses photon jaws plus adjustable trimmers for electron field definition.

electron.q: What is the typical energy range of clinical electron beams available on modern linacs?
electron.a: 4 to 22 MeV with several selectable energies available
c: 1 to 3 MeV with only two energies selectable on linac
4 to 22 MeV with several selectable energies available
25 to 50 MeV with continuous energy selection on linac
50 to 100 MeV for deep-seated tumor treatment on linac
electron.e: Modern linacs typically offer 4-22 MeV electron beams in increments (e.g., 6, 9, 12, 16, 20 MeV), covering most clinical needs.

electron.q: What is the X-ray contamination percentage typically acceptable in clinical electron beams?
electron.a: Less than 5% for energies up to 20 MeV electron beams
c: Less than 1% for all clinical electron beam energies
Less than 5% for energies up to 20 MeV electron beams
Less than 10% for all clinical electron beam energies
Less than 15% for energies above 15 MeV electron beams
electron.e: X-ray contamination should be less than 5% (typically 1-4%) for clinical beams up to 20 MeV. Higher contamination indicates head shielding issues.

electron.q: What is the role of the scattering foil carousel in a multimode linac?
electron.a: Positions the correct scattering foil for each electron energy
c: Filters the photon beam to produce different beam energies
Positions the correct scattering foil for each electron energy
Rotates the flattening filter into position for photon mode
Moves the ion chamber into and out of the beam path used
electron.e: The carousel rotates different scattering foils into the beam path for each electron energy, as each energy requires different foil thickness for optimal flatness.

electron.q: What is the effect of titanium exit window on the electron beam characteristics?
electron.a: Causes small energy loss and angular spread of the beam
c: Has no measurable effect on the electron beam at all
Causes small energy loss and angular spread of the beam
Completely blocks all low-energy electrons from the beam
Produces significant bremsstrahlung contamination above 10%
electron.e: The thin titanium exit window separates the vacuum from air, causing a small energy loss and minor angular spread of the electron beam.

electron.q: What is the TSEI skin dose uniformity requirement across the entire body surface?
electron.a: Plus or minus 10% dose uniformity across the body surface
c: Plus or minus 2% dose uniformity across the body surface
Plus or minus 5% dose uniformity across the body surface
Plus or minus 10% dose uniformity across the body surface
Plus or minus 20% dose uniformity across body surface
electron.e: TSEI aims for +/-10% uniformity across the body, though self-shielded areas (folds, soles) may fall outside this and require supplemental fields.

electron.q: What special dosimetry considerations apply to TSEI beam calibration?
electron.a: Extended distance calibration with degraded beam energy spectrum
c: Standard calibration at 100 cm SSD with normal beam setup
Extended distance calibration with degraded beam energy spectrum
Calibration using film dosimetry exclusively for measurements
No special considerations beyond normal electron calibration
electron.e: TSEI calibration must account for the extended treatment distance (3-4m), degraded energy spectrum, and the dual-field angular technique.

electron.q: What in-vivo dosimeters are commonly used for TSEI dose verification on patients?
electron.a: TLDs, OSLDs, or radiochromic film placed on the patient skin
c: Ion chambers taped to the patient surface during treatment
TLDs, OSLDs, or radiochromic film placed on the patient skin
Electronic portal imaging device used during treatment only
MOSFET detectors implanted subcutaneously before treatment
electron.e: TLDs, OSLDs, and radiochromic film are thin, lightweight dosimeters placed on the patient skin at multiple locations to verify TSEI dose uniformity.

electron.q: What is the difference between Class I and Class II parallel plate chambers?
electron.a: Class I needs no perturbation correction; Class II requires one
c: Class I is for photons only; Class II is for electrons only
Class I needs no perturbation correction; Class II requires one
Class I is waterproof; Class II requires a waterproof sleeve
Class I is smaller volume; Class II is larger volume overall
electron.e: Class I parallel plate chambers (e.g., NACP) are designed so perturbation corrections are unity. Class II chambers require perturbation corrections.

electron.q: What is the electron beam penumbra at dmax compared to the penumbra at R50 depth?
electron.a: Penumbra at R50 is significantly wider than at dmax depth
c: Penumbra is identical at dmax and R50 depths in the beam
Penumbra at dmax is wider than the penumbra at R50 depth
Penumbra at R50 is significantly wider than at dmax depth
Penumbra at R50 is narrower than at dmax depth in the beam
electron.e: Electron penumbra increases dramatically with depth due to scattering. At R50, the penumbra can be 2-3 times wider than at dmax.

electron.q: How does the 90% isodose contour shape change with depth for electron beams?
electron.a: It constricts inward significantly at greater depth values
c: It expands outward significantly with increasing depth
It constricts inward significantly at greater depth values
It maintains the same shape at all depths in the beam
It first expands then constricts with increasing depth
electron.e: The 90% isodose constricts with depth as scattered electrons at the field edges are lost, reducing the effective treatment field size at depth.

electron.q: What clinical structure is most at risk from the bremsstrahlung tail in chest wall electrons?
electron.a: The lung parenchyma underlying the chest wall target area
c: The skin surface overlying the chest wall treatment area
The lung parenchyma underlying the chest wall target area
The contralateral breast tissue on the opposite side
The spine at the posterior midline of the patient body
electron.e: The bremsstrahlung tail delivers a low but non-negligible dose to the lung beyond the target, contributing to lung toxicity risk.

electron.q: What is the recommended bolus thickness verification method before electron treatment?
electron.a: CT simulation with bolus in place to verify dose coverage
c: Visual inspection of the bolus thickness is sufficient
CT simulation with bolus in place to verify dose coverage
Measuring bolus thickness with a ruler only is adequate
No verification is needed if using commercial bolus only
electron.e: CT simulation with bolus in place allows verification of bolus fit, air gaps, and dose distribution to ensure adequate target coverage.

electron.q: What is the effect of using the wrong electron cone during patient treatment?
electron.a: Incorrect field size, output, and dose distribution at target
c: Only a minor change in penumbra with no other effect
Incorrect field size, output, and dose distribution at target
The linac interlock system prevents any beam delivery here
Only the surface dose is affected with no depth dose change
electron.e: Using the wrong cone changes the field size, output factor, and potentially the PDD, leading to incorrect dose delivery. Interlocks should prevent this.

electron.q: What is the energy degradation of TSEI electrons at 3-4 meter treatment distance?
electron.a: Energy is reduced to approximately 3-4 MeV at the patient skin
c: Energy is unchanged from the nominal linac output energy used
Energy is reduced to approximately 3-4 MeV at the patient skin
Energy increases due to forward-scattering in the air column
Energy is reduced to less than 1 MeV at the patient position
electron.e: At 3-4 m distance, the nominal 6 MeV beam is degraded to approximately 3-4 MeV due to air scattering, suitable for treating the superficial skin.

electron.q: What is the role of the gantry angle in the dual-field TSEI treatment technique?
electron.a: Angled beams overlap to create a uniform vertical dose profile
c: The gantry is always at zero degrees for TSEI treatment used
Angled beams overlap to create a uniform vertical dose profile
Angled beams reduce the bremsstrahlung contamination in beam
The gantry angle determines the treatment energy for patient
electron.e: Two fields angled above and below horizontal (typically +/-15-20 degrees) overlap at the patient plane to create a vertically uniform dose distribution.

electron.q: What is the electron beam abutment problem when matching to a photon field?
electron.a: The wide electron penumbra creates large hot or cold spots at junction
c: There is no dosimetric issue when abutting electron and photon fields
The wide electron penumbra creates large hot or cold spots at junction
Only cold spots occur at the junction of electron and photon fields
The photon field must always overlap the electron field by 3 cm here
electron.e: The broad electron penumbra (wider than photon) creates hot/cold spots at the junction. Gap or overlap optimization and junction feathering are needed.

electron.q: What is the recommended method for measuring electron beam output for small cutouts?
electron.a: Measure each cutout individually rather than interpolating values
c: Interpolate from large field measurements using area-perimeter
Measure each cutout individually rather than interpolating values
Use the open cone output factor for all cutout sizes directly
Calculate from Monte Carlo without any measurements necessary
electron.e: For small cutouts where output varies rapidly with size, direct measurement is recommended rather than relying on interpolation from larger fields.

electron.q: What is the clinical significance of the 80-20% penumbra width in electron beams?
electron.a: It determines the margin needed to ensure target dose coverage
c: It is only used for quality assurance and has no clinical use
It determines the margin needed to ensure target dose coverage
It defines the maximum treatable depth for the electron beam
It determines the monitor units needed for treatment delivery
electron.e: The penumbra width directly impacts the field margin needed to ensure the 90% isodose covers the target, especially at the depth of interest.

electron.q: What is the Clarkson integration method used for in electron beam dosimetry?
electron.a: Calculating output for irregular fields using sector integration
c: Measuring depth dose curves in heterogeneous phantom media
Calculating output for irregular fields using sector integration
Determining the beam energy from the measured PDD curve data
Computing the scatter correction factor for extended SSD beams
electron.e: Clarkson integration divides irregular fields into sectors, calculating scatter contributions from each to determine the total output at the central axis.

electron.q: What is modulated electron radiation therapy (MERT) and its potential clinical advantage?
electron.a: Uses energy and intensity modulation for conformal electron dose delivery
c: A technique using only the highest available electron energy for therapy
Uses energy and intensity modulation for conformal electron dose delivery
A method of delivering electrons through the MLC without applicator use
A technique combining electron beams with proton beams simultaneously now
electron.e: MERT modulates both electron energy and intensity across the field to create highly conformal dose distributions, potentially improving on conventional electrons.

electron.q: What is the approximate relationship between electron beam energy (in MeV) and the depth of the 80% isodose line in water (in cm)?
electron.a: The R80 depth in cm is approximately equal to the beam energy in MeV divided by 2.8
c: The R80 depth in cm is approximately equal to the beam energy in MeV divided by 2.0
The R80 depth in cm is approximately equal to the beam energy in MeV divided by 2.8
The R80 depth in cm is approximately equal to the beam energy in MeV divided by 4.0
The R80 depth in cm is approximately equal to the beam energy in MeV divided by 5.0
electron.e: The therapeutic range R80 (depth of the 80% dose) is approximately E(MeV)/2.8 cm in water, providing a practical rule for selecting electron energy based on target depth.

electron.q: What effect does an oblique surface incidence have on electron beam dose distributions?
electron.a: It shifts the dose distribution toward the surface and increases dose inhomogeneity across the field
c: It has no measurable effect on the depth dose curve or lateral dose distribution shape
It shifts the dose distribution toward the surface and increases dose inhomogeneity across the field
It increases the penetration depth proportionally to the cosine of the incidence angle
It reduces the surface dose and improves the depth dose uniformity within the target
electron.e: Oblique incidence causes the effective path length to increase, shifting the dose distribution shallower relative to the surface, and creates dose non-uniformity due to varying path lengths across the field.

electron.q: What is the primary purpose of using a bolus material over the skin surface in electron beam therapy?
electron.a: To reduce the effective depth of the therapeutic range and increase surface dose to the target
c: To reduce the effective depth of the therapeutic range and increase surface dose to the target
To filter out low-energy electrons and harden the beam for deeper tissue penetration
To reduce skin dose by providing additional buildup material above the treatment surface
To improve lateral dose uniformity by scattering electrons at the phantom surface level
electron.e: Bolus shifts the dose distribution closer to the skin surface, effectively raising the surface dose and making the therapeutic range shallower, which is useful when the target extends to or near the skin surface.

electron.q: How does field size affect the central axis depth dose of electron beams for small cutout fields?
electron.a: Below the equilibrium field size, the depth of maximum dose decreases and output factor drops
c: Field size has no effect on electron depth dose as long as the cutout is larger than 3 cm
Below the equilibrium field size, the depth of maximum dose decreases and output factor drops
Smaller fields increase the penetration depth because lateral scatter loss stiffens the beam
Reducing the field size increases the output factor due to improved lateral scatter conditions
electron.e: When the field radius is less than the practical range of electrons, lateral scatter equilibrium is lost, reducing the depth of dmax and the output factor compared to the reference field size.

electron.q: What is the typical surface dose (as a percentage of maximum dose) for clinical electron beams in the 6-20 MeV range?
electron.a: Approximately 75-95%, increasing with higher beam energy values
c: Approximately 30-50%, relatively independent of the electron beam energy
Approximately 55-70%, decreasing with higher beam energy values
Approximately 75-95%, increasing with higher beam energy values
Essentially 100% at all clinical electron beam energy levels used
electron.e: Electron beams have relatively high surface doses (75-95% of dmax) that increase with energy due to increased forward scattering of higher energy electrons.

electron.q: What is the recommended method for determining output factors for irregularly shaped electron cutouts?
electron.a: Sector integration method using equivalent square field calculations or direct measurement for each cutout
c: Using the output factor for the nearest standard square field size without any corrections
Applying a universal correction factor of 0.95 to the applicator output for all cutouts
Sector integration method using equivalent square field calculations or direct measurement for each cutout
Calculating the output factor from the ratio of cutout area to the applicator field area
electron.e: The sector integration method calculates an equivalent field size by averaging radii in sectors from the central axis, accounting for the irregular shape's effect on lateral scatter contributions to the central axis.

electron.q: In electron beam therapy, what is the practical range (Rp) and how is it clinically significant?
electron.a: The depth where the extrapolated descending dose curve meets the bremsstrahlung tail, defining the maximum tissue depth receiving electron dose
c: The depth of maximum dose used as the normalization point for all electron depth dose curves
The depth where the dose falls to 50% of the maximum dose along the central beam axis line
The depth where the extrapolated descending dose curve meets the bremsstrahlung tail, defining the maximum tissue depth receiving electron dose
The depth beyond which no measurable radiation dose exists in the patient treatment volume
electron.e: Rp is determined by extrapolating the steep portion of the depth dose curve to intersect the bremsstrahlung background. It defines the maximum depth of electron penetration and is critical for protecting structures beyond the target.

electron.q: What material is most commonly used for electron beam skin collimation (internal shielding), and what thickness is typically required?
electron.a: Lead or Cerrobend of thickness approximately equal to half the practical range in that material
c: Aluminum sheets of 1-2 mm thickness for all clinical electron beam energy ranges
Lead or Cerrobend of thickness approximately equal to half the practical range in that material
Steel plates of 5 mm standard thickness regardless of the electron beam energy used
Tungsten alloy inserts of 0.5 mm thickness placed directly on the patient skin surface
electron.e: Lead or Cerrobend is used for skin collimation, with thickness determined to stop the maximum energy electrons. The rule of thumb is thickness approximately equal to Rp(lead)/2 plus a safety margin.

electron.q: How does the dose distribution change when an electron beam passes through a small air cavity within tissue?
electron.a: Hot spots appear at the distal cavity wall and cold spots occur at the proximal cavity wall
c: The dose distribution is completely uniform as electrons scatter to fill the cavity region
Hot spots appear at the distal cavity wall and cold spots occur at the proximal cavity wall
The dose decreases uniformly throughout the cavity region with no wall interface effects
Cold spots appear at both the proximal and distal cavity walls due to scatter losses
electron.e: At the distal wall of an air cavity, electrons scatter back into the interface, creating a hot spot. At the proximal wall, the loss of backscatter from air creates a slight cold spot relative to homogeneous conditions.

electron.q: What is the approximate rule of thumb for selecting electron beam energy to treat a target at a given depth?
electron.a: Choose energy in MeV approximately 3.3 times the target depth in centimeters of tissue
c: Choose energy in MeV approximately 2.0 times the target depth in centimeters of tissue
Choose energy in MeV approximately 3.3 times the target depth in centimeters of tissue
Choose energy in MeV approximately 5.0 times the target depth in centimeters of tissue
Choose energy in MeV equal to the target depth in centimeters with no multiplier needed
electron.e: Since R80 ≈ E/2.8 and the therapeutic target should be within the 80-90% isodose, selecting E ≈ 3.3 × depth(cm) ensures adequate coverage of the target volume with the therapeutic dose level.

electron.q: What is the x-ray contamination dose at depth beyond the electron range, and what factors affect its magnitude?
electron.a: Typically 1-5% of dmax dose, increasing with beam energy and decreasing with field size reduction
c: Negligible at less than 0.1% and independent of all beam and field size parameters
Typically 1-5% of dmax dose, increasing with beam energy and decreasing with field size reduction
Approximately 10-15% of dmax dose and constant regardless of energy or field size used
Greater than 20% of dmax dose for energies above 12 MeV requiring additional shielding
electron.e: Bremsstrahlung contamination (x-ray tail) is typically 1-5% of dmax, increases with higher electron energy due to more bremsstrahlung production, and decreases with smaller fields due to reduced scatter volume.

electron.q: How does the penumbra width of electron beams compare to photon beams, and what physical process explains this difference?
electron.a: Electron penumbra is wider due to multiple Coulomb scattering causing lateral spread that increases with depth
c: Electron penumbra is narrower because electrons have a finite range limiting lateral spread of dose
Electron penumbra is identical to photon penumbra when measured at equivalent treatment depth ranges
Electron penumbra is wider due to multiple Coulomb scattering causing lateral spread that increases with depth
Electron penumbra width depends only on the collimation system and is independent of beam energy
electron.e: Multiple Coulomb scattering causes electrons to deviate laterally from their initial direction. This scattering increases with depth, producing wider penumbra than photon beams and bulging of the isodose lines.

proton.q: What is the characteristic feature of the proton depth-dose curve called?
proton.a: The Bragg peak at end of range
c: The Bragg peak at end of range
The build-up region at surface
The penumbra at field edges
The bremsstrahlung tail region
proton.e: Protons deposit maximum dose near the end of their range, forming the Bragg peak, which is the defining characteristic of proton depth-dose curves.

proton.q: What happens to dose beyond the Bragg peak in a pristine proton beam?
proton.a: Dose drops to essentially zero
c: Dose drops to essentially zero
Dose continues at reduced level
Dose increases exponentially
Dose remains at plateau level
proton.e: Beyond the Bragg peak, protons have stopped completely, so there is no dose deposited (unlike photons which have an exit dose).

proton.q: What is a Spread-Out Bragg Peak (SOBP) used for in proton therapy?
proton.a: To create uniform dose across the target depth
c: To create uniform dose across the target depth
To minimize the entrance dose to the skin
To sharpen the lateral beam penumbra edge
To reduce neutron contamination downstream
proton.e: An SOBP is created by superimposing multiple pristine Bragg peaks of different energies/weights to produce a uniform dose region covering the entire target volume in depth.

proton.q: Which accelerator type produces a continuous beam with fixed extraction energy?
proton.a: A cyclotron accelerator system
c: A cyclotron accelerator system
A synchrotron accelerator system
A linear induction accelerator
A betatron accelerator system
proton.e: Cyclotrons produce a continuous beam at a fixed maximum energy. Energy degradation is performed externally using a degrader to achieve lower energies.

proton.q: How does a synchrotron differ from a cyclotron for proton therapy?
proton.a: It accelerates protons to variable energies directly
c: It accelerates protons to variable energies directly
It produces a continuous beam at fixed energy
It uses a fixed magnetic field for acceleration
It requires no radiofrequency cavity system
proton.e: A synchrotron can vary the extraction energy pulse by pulse, eliminating the need for an external energy degrader. However, it produces a pulsed beam rather than continuous.

proton.q: What is the typical clinical proton energy range used for deep-seated tumors?
proton.a: 200 to 250 MeV for deep targets
c: 200 to 250 MeV for deep targets
50 to 80 MeV for deep-seated sites
500 to 600 MeV for deep targets
10 to 30 MeV for deep-seated sites
proton.e: Proton energies of 200-250 MeV correspond to ranges of approximately 26-38 cm in water, sufficient to reach most deep-seated tumors.

proton.q: What is the clinically used RBE value for protons in treatment planning?
proton.a: A constant generic value of 1.1
c: A constant generic value of 1.1
A constant generic value of 1.5
A variable value averaging 2.0
A constant generic value of 0.9
proton.e: A generic RBE of 1.1 is used clinically for protons, meaning protons are considered 10% more biologically effective than photons per unit dose.

proton.q: In pencil beam scanning (PBS), how is the lateral dose distribution formed?
proton.a: By magnetically steering individual pencil beams
c: By magnetically steering individual pencil beams
By using a scattering foil to spread the beam
By rotating a range modulator wheel device
By inserting a patient-specific brass aperture
proton.e: PBS uses scanning magnets to steer a narrow proton pencil beam across the target volume, placing individual spots at planned positions to build up the dose distribution.

proton.q: What device is used in passive scattering to spread the proton beam laterally?
proton.a: Single or double scattering foil system
c: Single or double scattering foil system
A pair of orthogonal scanning magnets
A multi-leaf collimator at the nozzle
A flattening filter downstream of target
proton.e: Passive scattering systems use one or two scattering foils, typically made of high-Z material like lead, to spread the narrow beam into a broad, uniform lateral field.

proton.q: What is the typical range uncertainty margin applied in proton therapy planning?
proton.a: 3.5% of range plus 1-3 mm additional
c: 3.5% of range plus 1-3 mm additional
0.5% of range plus 0.1 mm additional
10% of range plus 10 mm additional margin
No margin is needed for proton therapy
proton.e: A commonly used formula for range uncertainty is 3.5% of the range + 1-3 mm, accounting for CT calibration uncertainties, biological uncertainties, and setup errors.

proton.q: What is the primary source of range uncertainty in proton therapy?
proton.a: CT number to stopping power conversion
c: CT number to stopping power conversion
Patient setup positioning uncertainty
Beam energy measurement uncertainty value
Scanning magnet calibration tolerance
proton.e: The largest contributor to range uncertainty is the conversion of CT Hounsfield units to relative proton stopping power ratios, which introduces approximately 1-3% uncertainty.

proton.q: How can dual-energy CT (DECT) benefit proton therapy treatment planning?
proton.a: By improving stopping power ratio estimation
c: By improving stopping power ratio estimation
By eliminating all metal artifact effects
By directly measuring the proton beam range
By replacing Monte Carlo dose calculations
proton.e: DECT provides additional information about tissue composition, enabling more accurate estimation of proton stopping power ratios compared to single-energy CT.

proton.q: What is the primary advantage of proton therapy over photon therapy?
proton.a: Reduced integral dose to normal tissues
c: Reduced integral dose to normal tissues
Higher tumor dose conformality at target
Better penumbra sharpness in all cases
Faster treatment delivery time overall
proton.e: The finite range and Bragg peak of protons result in significantly less integral dose to normal tissues compared to photon therapy, especially beneficial for pediatric patients.

proton.q: Which pediatric tumor is one of the most common indications for proton therapy?
proton.a: Medulloblastoma requiring craniospinal axis
c: Medulloblastoma requiring craniospinal axis
Wilms tumor of the kidney in children
Neuroblastoma stage IV-S with regression
Acute lymphoblastic leukemia CNS disease
proton.e: Medulloblastoma requiring craniospinal irradiation is a prime indication for proton therapy because protons dramatically reduce dose to the heart, lungs, bowel, and other anterior structures.

proton.q: What is the main concern with using protons for pediatric patients regarding secondary cancer?
proton.a: Neutron contamination from beam interactions
c: Neutron contamination from beam interactions
Higher RBE causing more DNA double breaks
Increased scattered photon dose to organs
Greater electron contamination at the skin
proton.e: Nuclear interactions of protons produce secondary neutrons, which have high RBE and can contribute to secondary cancer risk, particularly in passive scattering.

proton.q: Which beam delivery technique produces less neutron contamination?
proton.a: Pencil beam scanning delivery technique
c: Pencil beam scanning delivery technique
Double scattering passive delivery mode
Single scattering passive delivery mode
Uniform scanning passive delivery mode
proton.e: PBS produces significantly less neutron contamination because it requires fewer beam-modifying devices in the beam path.

proton.q: What is the approximate lateral penumbra of a proton pencil beam at depth?
proton.a: Approximately 5-15 mm depending on energy
c: Approximately 5-15 mm depending on energy
Approximately 0.5-1 mm regardless of depth
Approximately 30-50 mm at therapeutic depth
Approximately identical to photon penumbra
proton.e: Proton lateral penumbra broadens with depth due to multiple Coulomb scattering. At depth for high energies, it can be 10-15 mm.

proton.q: What causes the lateral penumbra of protons to broaden with depth?
proton.a: Multiple Coulomb scattering interactions
c: Multiple Coulomb scattering interactions
Nuclear fragmentation reaction products
Energy straggling along the beam path
Inverse square law divergence effects
proton.e: Multiple Coulomb scattering causes protons to undergo many small-angle deflections as they traverse tissue, resulting in progressive lateral spread.

proton.q: What is energy straggling in proton therapy?
proton.a: Statistical variation in energy loss of protons
c: Statistical variation in energy loss of protons
Systematic shift in beam energy at nozzle
Lateral deflection due to magnetic scanning
Change in beam energy during the treatment
proton.e: Energy straggling refers to the statistical fluctuation in energy loss among protons traveling through matter, which causes broadening of the Bragg peak at greater depths.

proton.q: How does the Bragg peak width change with increasing proton range?
proton.a: The Bragg peak becomes broader in width
c: The Bragg peak becomes broader in width
The Bragg peak becomes narrower in width
The Bragg peak width remains unchanged
The Bragg peak splits into two peaks
proton.e: As range increases, energy straggling accumulates, causing the Bragg peak to broaden. Higher energy beams therefore have wider Bragg peaks.

proton.q: What is the purpose of a range shifter in proton beam delivery?
proton.a: To reduce beam energy for treating shallow targets
c: To reduce beam energy for treating shallow targets
To increase beam energy for deep-seated tumors
To laterally spread the beam across the field
To modulate beam intensity during treatments
proton.e: A range shifter is a slab of material placed in the beam path to degrade the proton energy, allowing treatment of shallow targets when the minimum accelerator energy is too high.

proton.q: What is a range modulator wheel used for in passive scattering?
proton.a: To create a spread-out Bragg peak in depth
c: To create a spread-out Bragg peak in depth
To scan the beam laterally across the field
To verify the beam range before treatment
To filter out neutron contamination dose
proton.e: A range modulator wheel is a rotating device with steps of varying thickness that produces beams of different energies combining to form an SOBP.

proton.q: What is the function of a patient-specific aperture in passive scattering?
proton.a: To conform the lateral beam shape to the target
c: To conform the lateral beam shape to the target
To modulate the beam energy across the field
To verify the patient position before delivery
To compensate for tissue density variations
proton.e: Brass apertures are custom-milled for each patient/field to shape the lateral extent of the proton beam to match the target projection.

proton.q: What is the purpose of a range compensator in passive scattering?
proton.a: To conform distal dose edge to target shape
c: To conform distal dose edge to target shape
To shape lateral beam profile uniformly
To increase the overall proton beam range
To reduce neutron dose to the patient body
proton.e: A range compensator is a patient-specific device shaped to compensate for tissue heterogeneities and irregular target shapes to conform the distal dose edge.

proton.q: What is robustness evaluation in proton treatment planning?
proton.a: Assessment of plan quality under uncertainties
c: Assessment of plan quality under uncertainties
Measurement of beam current during delivery
Verification of patient immobilization quality
Evaluation of accelerator mechanical stability
proton.e: Robustness evaluation assesses how well the dose distribution maintains clinical acceptability under various uncertainty scenarios including setup errors and range uncertainties.

proton.q: What does robust optimization in proton planning specifically account for?
proton.a: Setup and range uncertainty scenarios combined
c: Setup and range uncertainty scenarios combined
Only the CT image reconstruction artifacts
Only the patient respiratory motion pattern
Only the accelerator beam energy variations
proton.e: Robust optimization includes multiple uncertainty scenarios directly in the optimization to create plans that are resilient to these errors.

proton.q: How many scenarios are typically used in minimax robust optimization for protons?
proton.a: Approximately 9 to 12 uncertainty scenarios
c: Approximately 9 to 12 uncertainty scenarios
Exactly 2 uncertainty scenario evaluations
More than 100 random scenario samples
Only 1 nominal scenario is evaluated
proton.e: Typical minimax robust optimization uses 9-12 scenarios: 6 setup shifts, 2 range shifts, and the nominal scenario, optimizing to the worst-case.

proton.q: What is the interplay effect in proton pencil beam scanning?
proton.a: Interaction between beam scanning and organ motion
c: Interaction between beam scanning and organ motion
Interference between adjacent proton pencil beams
Crosstalk between scanning magnets in nozzle
Overlap between different treatment field angles
proton.e: The interplay effect occurs when the temporal pattern of spot delivery interacts with periodic organ motion, causing dose inhomogeneities.

proton.q: Which motion management technique is commonly used to mitigate interplay effects?
proton.a: Volumetric repainting or rescanning method
c: Volumetric repainting or rescanning method
Deep inspiration breath hold exclusively
Fiducial marker tracking with gating
Increasing the beam spot size at nozzle
proton.e: Repainting delivers the dose in multiple passes over the target volume, averaging out the interplay effect over multiple breathing cycles.

proton.q: What is the main challenge of treating lung tumors with proton therapy?
proton.a: Density variations affecting range significantly
c: Density variations affecting range significantly
Tumor size limitations with proton beams
Inability to image during proton treatment
Proton beams cannot penetrate lung tissue
proton.e: Lung tissue has very low density compared to soft tissue, causing large range variations as the tumor moves with breathing.

proton.q: What is the stopping power ratio and why is it important for protons?
proton.a: Ratio of energy loss in tissue versus water
c: Ratio of energy loss in tissue versus water
Ratio of beam current before and after body
Ratio of prescribed dose to delivered dose
Ratio of proton to photon dose at depth
proton.e: The relative stopping power is the ratio of the linear stopping power in a material to that in water. Accurate RSP values are critical for predicting proton range.

proton.q: What is the Bethe-Bloch formula primarily used to calculate for protons?
proton.a: The electronic stopping power in material
c: The electronic stopping power in material
The nuclear scattering cross-section value
The lateral penumbra width at each depth
The neutron production yield per proton
proton.e: The Bethe-Bloch formula describes the mean rate of energy loss of charged particles like protons as they traverse matter.

proton.q: How does proton stopping power change as the proton slows down?
proton.a: Stopping power increases as velocity decreases
c: Stopping power increases as velocity decreases
Stopping power decreases as velocity decreases
Stopping power remains constant with velocity
Stopping power oscillates with velocity change
proton.e: According to the Bethe-Bloch formula, stopping power is inversely proportional to the square of velocity, so as protons slow down, they lose energy more rapidly, creating the Bragg peak.

proton.q: What is the approximate entrance-to-peak dose ratio for a pristine Bragg peak?
proton.a: Approximately 1:3 to 1:4 entrance to peak
c: Approximately 1:3 to 1:4 entrance to peak
Approximately 1:1 entrance to peak dose ratio
Approximately 1:10 entrance to peak dose value
Approximately 2:1 entrance to peak dose ratio
proton.e: For a pristine Bragg peak, the peak dose is typically 3-4 times the entrance dose, giving the characteristic sharp dose spike at end of range.

proton.q: What nuclear interaction contributes to dose proximal to the Bragg peak?
proton.a: Non-elastic nuclear interactions producing fragments
c: Non-elastic nuclear interactions producing fragments
Pair production at high proton beam energies
Compton scattering of secondary gamma photons
Photoelectric absorption within tissue medium
proton.e: Non-elastic nuclear interactions remove protons from the primary beam and produce secondary particles, reducing the peak-to-entrance ratio.

proton.q: What is the dose unit used for proton therapy planning that accounts for RBE?
proton.a: Cobalt Gray Equivalent or Gy(RBE) units
c: Cobalt Gray Equivalent or Gy(RBE) units
Standard Gray with no correction applied
Sievert for stochastic effect assessment
Roentgen equivalent physical dose units
proton.e: Proton doses are reported in Gy(RBE) = physical dose x RBE (1.1), also historically called Cobalt Gray Equivalent.

proton.q: Where along the SOBP is the LET highest for proton beams?
proton.a: At the distal falloff edge of the SOBP
c: At the distal falloff edge of the SOBP
At the entrance region of the proton beam
At the center of the SOBP target volume
Uniformly distributed throughout the SOBP
proton.e: LET increases sharply at the distal edge of the SOBP because protons are slowing down rapidly near the end of their range.

proton.q: Why is the constant RBE of 1.1 considered a limitation in proton planning?
proton.a: RBE actually increases with LET at distal edge
c: RBE actually increases with LET at distal edge
RBE of 1.1 overestimates biological effect
Clinical evidence shows RBE equals exactly 1.0
RBE varies only with patient age not depth
proton.e: The constant RBE of 1.1 underestimates the biological effect at the distal edge where LET is elevated, potentially leading to higher-than-expected biological damage.

proton.q: What is LET-based optimization in proton treatment planning?
proton.a: Steering high-LET regions away from critical OARs
c: Steering high-LET regions away from critical OARs
Maximizing LET throughout the entire target volume
Using LET to calculate physical dose distributions
Adjusting beam current based on LET measurements
proton.e: LET-based optimization aims to minimize high-LET dose to critical structures by manipulating beam angles and spot weights.

proton.q: What is the typical spot size (sigma in air) for PBS at the nozzle exit?
proton.a: Approximately 3-7 mm sigma depending on energy
c: Approximately 3-7 mm sigma depending on energy
Approximately 0.1-0.3 mm sigma at all energies
Approximately 20-30 mm sigma at all energies
Approximately 50-100 mm sigma at all energies
proton.e: PBS spot sizes typically range from 3 mm sigma at highest energies to 7 mm or more at lowest energies in air at isocenter.

proton.q: What is the difference between discrete spot scanning and continuous scanning PBS?
proton.a: Spot scanning stops beam between spots; continuous does not
c: Spot scanning stops beam between spots; continuous does not
Continuous scanning uses passive scattering elements
Spot scanning can only treat small field sizes
Continuous scanning requires patient-specific hardware
proton.e: In discrete spot scanning, the beam is turned off between spots. In continuous scanning, the beam remains on while scanning magnets move.

proton.q: What is the main advantage of continuous scanning over discrete spot scanning?
proton.a: Faster delivery time with reduced beam-off periods
c: Faster delivery time with reduced beam-off periods
Better dose conformality to irregular target shapes
Elimination of all interplay effect concerns
Higher maximum deliverable dose rate achieved
proton.e: Continuous scanning eliminates the dead time needed to switch the beam off and on between spots, resulting in faster delivery.

proton.q: What is prompt gamma imaging used for in proton therapy?
proton.a: Real-time in vivo range verification method
c: Real-time in vivo range verification method
Pre-treatment patient alignment verification
Beam current monitoring during the delivery
Post-treatment residual activity assessment
proton.e: Prompt gamma rays emitted during nuclear interactions correlate with proton range, enabling real-time range verification during treatment.

proton.q: How does PET-based range verification work in proton therapy?
proton.a: Detecting positron emitters created by beam interactions
c: Detecting positron emitters created by beam interactions
Injecting FDG tracer before proton treatment delivery
Using PET scanner to measure proton beam directly
Detecting Cerenkov radiation from proton beam path
proton.e: Proton nuclear interactions produce positron-emitting isotopes in tissue. Post-treatment PET imaging detects these to verify the irradiated volume.

proton.q: What is the main limitation of offline PET range verification?
proton.a: Biological washout of positron emitters over time
c: Biological washout of positron emitters over time
PET scanners cannot detect proton-produced isotopes
The spatial resolution is worse than ten centimeters
Positron emitters are not produced by proton beams
proton.e: Biological washout and physical decay reduce the PET signal between treatment delivery and imaging, degrading range verification accuracy.

proton.q: Which positron-emitting isotope produced in proton therapy has the longest half-life?
proton.a: Carbon-11 with a half-life of about 20 minutes
c: Carbon-11 with a half-life of about 20 minutes
Oxygen-15 with a half-life of about 2 minutes
Nitrogen-13 with a half-life of about 10 minutes
Fluorine-18 with half-life of about 110 minutes
proton.e: Among commonly produced isotopes, 11C (t1/2 = 20.4 min) has the longest half-life. 18F is not typically produced in significant quantities.

proton.q: What is TG-224 primarily concerned with in proton therapy?
proton.a: Quality assurance for proton therapy facilities
c: Quality assurance for proton therapy facilities
Commissioning of photon linear accelerators
Brachytherapy source calibration procedures
Electron beam dosimetry calibration protocol
proton.e: AAPM TG-224 provides comprehensive recommendations for quality assurance of proton therapy systems.

proton.q: What dosimeter is recommended as the primary standard for proton beam calibration?
proton.a: A calibrated plane-parallel ionization chamber
c: A calibrated plane-parallel ionization chamber
Thermoluminescent dosimeters in solid water
Radiochromic film with optical density reader
Silicon diode detector with buildup material
proton.e: TRS-398 and TG-51 addendum recommend calibrated ionization chambers as the primary standard for proton beam output calibration.

proton.q: What is the recommended daily output constancy check tolerance for proton therapy?
proton.a: Within plus or minus 3 percent of baseline
c: Within plus or minus 3 percent of baseline
Within plus or minus 10 percent of baseline
Within plus or minus 0.1 percent of baseline
Within plus or minus 20 percent of baseline
proton.e: Daily output constancy checks for proton beams typically have a tolerance of plus or minus 3%, consistent with TG-224 recommendations.

proton.q: Why is proton therapy particularly beneficial for pediatric craniospinal irradiation?
proton.a: It significantly reduces dose to developing organs
c: It significantly reduces dose to developing organs
It provides higher dose to the spinal cord target
It eliminates the need for general anesthesia
It shortens overall treatment time substantially
proton.e: Proton CSI dramatically reduces dose to the heart, lungs, bowel, kidneys, and other anterior structures compared to photon CSI.

proton.q: What is the advantage of proton therapy for uveal melanoma treatment?
proton.a: High dose conformality preserving the eye globe
c: High dose conformality preserving the eye globe
Complete elimination of treatment-related cataracts
No need for patient immobilization during therapy
Treatment can be completed in a single fraction
proton.e: Proton therapy for uveal melanoma achieves excellent local control while preserving the eye structure and often useful vision.

proton.q: What is the typical proton energy used for ocular tumor treatments?
proton.a: 60 to 70 MeV for shallow ocular targets
c: 60 to 70 MeV for shallow ocular targets
200 to 250 MeV for ocular tumor targets
10 to 15 MeV for shallow ocular targets
400 to 500 MeV for ocular tumor targets
proton.e: Ocular treatments use low-energy proton beams (60-70 MeV, range approximately 3 cm) because the eye is superficial.

proton.q: What is carbon ion therapy's main advantage over proton therapy?
proton.a: Higher RBE especially for radioresistant tumors
c: Higher RBE especially for radioresistant tumors
Lower cost of facility and beam production
Wider availability at treatment centers globally
Simpler treatment planning dose calculations
proton.e: Carbon ions have higher LET and RBE (2-3 or higher in the Bragg peak) compared to protons, making them more effective against radioresistant tumors.

proton.q: What is nuclear fragmentation and why is it relevant to carbon ion therapy?
proton.a: Projectile breakup creating lighter ion fragments
c: Projectile breakup creating lighter ion fragments
Target nucleus splitting into two equal halves
Electron capture by the carbon ion projectile
Fusion of carbon ions with tissue nuclei atoms
proton.e: Nuclear fragmentation occurs when carbon ions break apart during nuclear collisions, producing lighter fragments that travel beyond the Bragg peak.

proton.q: What is the fragmentation tail in carbon ion therapy?
proton.a: Dose beyond Bragg peak from lighter fragments
c: Dose beyond Bragg peak from lighter fragments
Entrance dose reduction before the Bragg peak
Lateral dose spread from scattering interactions
Neutron dose in the treatment room shielding
proton.e: Unlike protons, carbon ions produce a fragmentation tail: lighter fragments from nuclear breakup have longer ranges than the primary beam.

proton.q: What is the typical RBE of carbon ions in the Bragg peak region?
proton.a: Approximately 2.5 to 3.5 in the peak region
c: Approximately 2.5 to 3.5 in the peak region
Approximately 1.0 to 1.1 in the peak region
Approximately 10 to 15 in the peak region
Approximately 0.5 to 0.8 in the peak region
proton.e: Carbon ion RBE varies with depth and tissue type but is typically 2.5-3.5 in the Bragg peak region, significantly higher than for protons.

proton.q: Which dose calculation algorithm is considered most accurate for proton therapy?
proton.a: Monte Carlo simulation-based dose calculation
c: Monte Carlo simulation-based dose calculation
Pencil beam analytical algorithm calculation
Collapsed cone convolution superposition method
Equivalent path length ray-tracing algorithm
proton.e: Monte Carlo methods track individual particle histories including all physics interactions, providing the most accurate dose calculation.

proton.q: What is the main limitation of analytical pencil beam algorithms for protons?
proton.a: Inaccuracy in highly heterogeneous tissue regions
c: Inaccuracy in highly heterogeneous tissue regions
Inability to calculate dose in water phantoms
Excessive computation time exceeding Monte Carlo
Inability to handle any tissue heterogeneity
proton.e: Analytical pencil beam algorithms use simplified models for lateral scatter that become inaccurate at tissue interfaces with large density differences.

proton.q: What is the advantage of Monte Carlo over pencil beam algorithms for proton planning?
proton.a: Better accuracy near tissue heterogeneity interfaces
c: Better accuracy near tissue heterogeneity interfaces
Faster calculation speed for clinical workflows
Simpler commissioning and beam model input data
No need for CT imaging of the patient body
proton.e: Monte Carlo accurately models particle transport including multiple scattering and nuclear interactions at heterogeneity interfaces.

proton.q: What is the water equivalent thickness (WET) concept in proton therapy?
proton.a: Thickness of water with same proton energy loss
c: Thickness of water with same proton energy loss
Physical thickness of tissue along the beam path
Maximum range of protons in any given material
Minimum energy required to traverse a material
proton.e: WET converts the physical thickness of any material to the equivalent thickness of water that would produce the same proton energy loss.

proton.q: What imaging modality can directly measure proton stopping power?
proton.a: Proton CT or proton radiography imaging
c: Proton CT or proton radiography imaging
Standard single-energy X-ray CT scanning
Magnetic resonance imaging with contrast agent
Ultrasound imaging with Doppler flow mode
proton.e: Proton CT directly measures the residual energy of protons traversing the patient, providing stopping power maps without HU-to-SPR conversion uncertainty.

proton.q: What is the 4D-CT scan used for in proton therapy planning?
proton.a: To assess tumor motion due to respiration cycle
c: To assess tumor motion due to respiration cycle
To improve the CT image spatial resolution
To measure proton stopping power directly
To create an enhanced contrast image set
proton.e: 4D-CT captures multiple CT phases throughout the respiratory cycle, allowing assessment of tumor and organ motion critical for proton therapy.

proton.q: What is the purpose of beam gating in proton therapy for mobile tumors?
proton.a: Delivering beam only during specific motion phases
c: Delivering beam only during specific motion phases
Increasing beam energy during deep breathing
Reducing the treatment room radiation shielding
Eliminating the need for any immobilization device
proton.e: Respiratory gating delivers the proton beam only during a specific phase of the breathing cycle, reducing the effect of respiratory motion.

proton.q: What is beam tracking in particle therapy?
proton.a: Dynamically adjusting beam position to follow target
c: Dynamically adjusting beam position to follow target
Monitoring beam current during delivery session
Recording beam spot positions after treatment
Verifying beam alignment with room lasers daily
proton.e: Beam tracking adjusts spot positions and energies in real-time to follow target motion. This is technically challenging and not yet widely implemented.

proton.q: What commissioning data is needed for a PBS proton beam model?
proton.a: Depth doses, spot profiles, and absolute output
c: Depth doses, spot profiles, and absolute output
Only the accelerator RF frequency parameters
Only photon beam percentage depth dose curves
Only the gantry rotation speed measurements
proton.e: PBS beam model commissioning requires integrated depth dose curves, lateral spot profiles at multiple energies, and absolute dose calibration.

proton.q: What detector is commonly used to measure pristine Bragg peaks during commissioning?
proton.a: A large-area parallel plate ionization chamber
c: A large-area parallel plate ionization chamber
A small-volume thimble ionization chamber only
A plastic scintillator fiber-optic detector
A Geiger-Mueller tube survey meter device
proton.e: Large-area plane-parallel ionization chambers are used to capture the full integral dose of pencil beam spots for measuring depth dose curves.

proton.q: What is the multilayer ionization chamber (MLIC) used for in proton QA?
proton.a: Rapid measurement of depth dose and range values
c: Rapid measurement of depth dose and range values
Measurement of lateral beam profile uniformity
Absolute dose calibration of proton beam output
Verification of scanning magnet field strength
proton.e: An MLIC consists of stacked parallel plate chambers with absorber plates, enabling rapid measurement of depth dose curves for range verification.

proton.q: What is patient-specific QA in proton PBS treatment?
proton.a: Verification of delivered dose for each patient plan
c: Verification of delivered dose for each patient plan
Daily calibration of the proton accelerator unit
Annual comprehensive machine performance check
Monthly review of treatment planning software
proton.e: Patient-specific QA involves delivering the actual treatment plan to a phantom and comparing measured dose to planned dose.

proton.q: What device is commonly used for 2D patient-specific QA in proton PBS?
proton.a: An ionization chamber array detector device
c: An ionization chamber array detector device
A single large-volume cylindrical ion chamber
A calorimeter for absolute dose measurement
A Faraday cup for beam current measurement
proton.e: 2D ionization chamber arrays are commonly used for patient-specific QA, measuring planar dose distributions for gamma analysis comparison.

proton.q: What is the log file analysis approach to proton PBS quality assurance?
proton.a: Using machine delivery logs to reconstruct dose
c: Using machine delivery logs to reconstruct dose
Reading patient treatment summary documents
Analyzing the accelerator maintenance log book
Reviewing the radiation safety officer reports
proton.e: Log file QA uses recorded spot positions, energies, and monitor units from the delivery system to reconstruct the delivered dose distribution.

proton.q: What is the nozzle in a proton therapy system?
proton.a: The final beam delivery assembly at treatment head
c: The final beam delivery assembly at treatment head
The particle source at the accelerator entrance
The beam transport magnet power supply unit
The patient positioning robotic couch system
proton.e: The nozzle is the treatment head containing the final beam shaping and monitoring elements including scanning magnets, dose monitors, and range shifter.

proton.q: What is the typical weight of a proton therapy gantry?
proton.a: Approximately 100 to 200 metric tons total
c: Approximately 100 to 200 metric tons total
Approximately 1 to 5 metric tons total weight
Approximately 1000 to 2000 metric tons total
Approximately 10 to 20 kilograms total weight
proton.e: Proton gantries are massive structures (100-200+ tons) because they must contain large bending magnets to steer the high-rigidity proton beam.

proton.q: What is the purpose of the beam transport system in a proton facility?
proton.a: To guide the beam from accelerator to treatment room
c: To guide the beam from accelerator to treatment room
To generate the proton beam from hydrogen gas
To calculate the treatment dose distribution plan
To monitor patient position during beam delivery
proton.e: The beam transport system uses dipole and quadrupole magnets in an evacuated beamline to steer and focus the proton beam.

proton.q: What is the energy selection system (ESS) in a cyclotron-based proton facility?
proton.a: Degrader and magnets to select desired beam energy
c: Degrader and magnets to select desired beam energy
Software that selects optimal treatment plan energy
Electronic system that controls RF cavity frequency
Sensor that measures the extracted beam energy
proton.e: The ESS includes an energy degrader, momentum analysis slits, and bending magnets to reduce the fixed cyclotron energy to the desired treatment energy.

proton.q: What is the main disadvantage of using an energy degrader with a cyclotron?
proton.a: Significant beam current loss at lower energies
c: Significant beam current loss at lower energies
Inability to achieve any energy below 200 MeV
Increased treatment time for all beam energies
Degradation of lateral beam profile uniformity
proton.e: Energy degradation produces a broad energy spectrum; momentum selection slits then select a narrow band, rejecting most of the beam at low energies.

proton.q: What is the superconducting synchrocyclotron and what advantage does it offer?
proton.a: Compact accelerator enabling single-room proton systems
c: Compact accelerator enabling single-room proton systems
Large accelerator requiring multi-room facility only
Accelerator that can only produce carbon ion beams
Conventional accelerator with room-temperature magnets
proton.e: Superconducting synchrocyclotrons use very high magnetic fields to shrink the accelerator size, enabling compact single-room proton therapy systems.

proton.q: What is the typical magnetic rigidity of a 250 MeV proton beam?
proton.a: Approximately 2.4 Tesla-meters beam rigidity
c: Approximately 2.4 Tesla-meters beam rigidity
Approximately 0.01 Tesla-meters beam rigidity
Approximately 50 Tesla-meters beam rigidity
Approximately 500 Tesla-meters beam rigidity
proton.e: Magnetic rigidity for a 250 MeV proton is about 2.4 Tm, which determines the size of bending magnets needed in the gantry and beam transport system.

proton.q: What is the proton beam current typically used for PBS treatments?
proton.a: Approximately 1 to 5 nanoamperes at nozzle
c: Approximately 1 to 5 nanoamperes at nozzle
Approximately 1 to 5 milliamperes at nozzle
Approximately 1 to 5 microamperes at nozzle
Approximately 1 to 5 amperes at the nozzle
proton.e: PBS uses relatively low beam currents (1-5 nA) because the narrow pencil beam delivers dose to small spots sequentially.

proton.q: What is the monitor unit (MU) calibration reference condition for proton beams?
proton.a: Dose to water at reference depth for reference field
c: Dose to water at reference depth for reference field
Dose to air at the nozzle exit window surface
Dose to plastic phantom at surface point only
Dose to lead at maximum range depth position
proton.e: Proton MU calibration is performed by measuring dose to water at a reference depth under defined reference conditions.

proton.q: What is the TRS-398 protocol used for in proton therapy?
proton.a: Absorbed dose to water determination from ion chambers
c: Absorbed dose to water determination from ion chambers
Treatment planning system commissioning validation
Patient-specific quality assurance measurements only
Radiation protection survey methodology guidelines
proton.e: IAEA TRS-398 provides a code of practice for determining absorbed dose to water using ionization chambers, applicable to proton beams.

proton.q: What beam quality specifier is used for proton beams in TRS-398?
proton.a: Residual range Rres at measurement point depth
c: Residual range Rres at measurement point depth
Tissue-phantom ratio TPR20/10 value only
Percentage depth dose at 10 cm PDD(10)
Half-value layer in aluminum millimeters
proton.e: TRS-398 uses the residual range Rres as the beam quality specifier for selecting the kQ correction factor for proton beams.

proton.q: What correction factor accounts for proton beam quality in ionization chamber dosimetry?
proton.a: The beam quality correction factor kQ value
c: The beam quality correction factor kQ value
The temperature-pressure correction factor PTP
The ion recombination correction factor Pion
The polarity correction factor Ppol for charge
proton.e: The kQ factor corrects the chamber calibration factor to the actual proton beam quality, accounting for stopping power ratios and perturbation factors.

proton.q: What is the w-value (mean energy per ion pair) for protons in air?
proton.a: Approximately 34.2 eV per ion pair in air
c: Approximately 34.2 eV per ion pair in air
Approximately 3.42 eV per ion pair in air
Approximately 342 eV per ion pair in air
Approximately 13.6 eV per ion pair in air
proton.e: The mean energy required to produce one ion pair in air for protons is approximately 34.2 eV, important for ionization chamber dosimetry.

proton.q: Why are plane-parallel chambers preferred for low-energy proton beam dosimetry?
proton.a: Reduced perturbation effects at shallow depths
c: Reduced perturbation effects at shallow depths
Higher sensitivity to radiation per unit dose
Lower cost compared to cylindrical chamber types
Easier waterproof construction for water tanks
proton.e: Plane-parallel chambers have minimal perturbation of particle fluence and a well-defined effective point of measurement at the front window.

proton.q: What is the typical dose rate for conventional proton therapy delivery?
proton.a: Approximately 1 to 5 Gy per minute delivered
c: Approximately 1 to 5 Gy per minute delivered
Approximately 0.001 Gy per minute delivered
Approximately 100 to 500 Gy per minute rate
Approximately 1000 Gy per second delivery rate
proton.e: Conventional proton therapy delivers dose at approximately 1-5 Gy/min to the target volume.

proton.q: What is FLASH proton therapy?
proton.a: Ultra-high dose rate proton beam delivery method
c: Ultra-high dose rate proton beam delivery method
A technique using very low dose rate delivery
A fractionation scheme with many small fractions
A method of pulsed beam delivery at normal rate
proton.e: FLASH therapy delivers radiation at ultra-high dose rates (>40 Gy/s), which has shown a protective effect on normal tissues in preclinical studies.

proton.q: What dose rate threshold is generally considered necessary for the FLASH effect?
proton.a: Greater than 40 Gy per second instantaneous
c: Greater than 40 Gy per second instantaneous
Greater than 40 Gy per minute instantaneous
Greater than 1 Gy per hour instantaneous rate
Greater than 0.1 Gy per second rate overall
proton.e: The FLASH effect has been observed at dose rates exceeding approximately 40 Gy/s, though exact thresholds are still being investigated.

proton.q: What is the proposed biological mechanism of the FLASH effect?
proton.a: Oxygen depletion protecting normal tissue cells
c: Oxygen depletion protecting normal tissue cells
Enhanced DNA repair in all irradiated tissues
Increased free radical production in tumor cells
Selective heating of tumor tissue vasculature
proton.e: The leading hypothesis is that ultra-high dose rates transiently deplete oxygen in normal tissues, reducing radiation damage.

proton.q: What is the challenge of dosimetry for FLASH proton therapy?
proton.a: Standard detectors show saturation at high dose rates
c: Standard detectors show saturation at high dose rates
FLASH beams cannot ionize any detector material
No dosimeters exist that can detect proton beams
FLASH dose rates are too low for any detector
proton.e: At FLASH dose rates, ionization chambers suffer from severe ion recombination, making standard dosimetry protocols inaccurate.

proton.q: What is proton arc therapy?
proton.a: Continuous beam delivery during gantry rotation arc
c: Continuous beam delivery during gantry rotation arc
Treatment using only a single fixed beam angle
Proton therapy using a non-rotating fixed beamline
Electron arc therapy converted to proton treatment
proton.e: Proton arc therapy delivers PBS spots from many gantry angles during continuous rotation, analogous to VMAT for photons.

proton.q: What is a potential advantage of proton arc therapy over fixed-field proton plans?
proton.a: Improved dose conformality and reduced skin dose
c: Improved dose conformality and reduced skin dose
Simpler treatment planning optimization process
Shorter commissioning time for beam delivery setup
Elimination of all range uncertainty concerns
proton.e: Proton arc therapy distributes entrance dose over many angles, reducing skin dose and potentially improving conformality.

proton.q: What is the spot spacing parameter in PBS proton treatment planning?
proton.a: Distance between adjacent spot positions on grid
c: Distance between adjacent spot positions on grid
Time interval between consecutive spot deliveries
Energy difference between successive beam layers
Angular separation between treatment beam angles
proton.e: Spot spacing defines the distance between adjacent spot centers in the lateral plane, typically set to a fraction of the spot sigma.

proton.q: What is the energy layer switching time in PBS and why does it matter?
proton.a: Time to change beam energy between depth layers
c: Time to change beam energy between depth layers
Time to move patient between treatment sessions
Time to rotate gantry between different angles
Time for beam current to stabilize at startup
proton.e: Energy layer switching time (typically 0.5-5 seconds) significantly impacts total treatment delivery time for PBS.

proton.q: Which system feature allows synchrotrons to have faster energy layer switching?
proton.a: Direct energy variation without external degrader
c: Direct energy variation without external degrader
Use of superconducting RF cavities for acceleration
Continuous beam extraction at all times during use
Smaller magnetic rigidity than cyclotron beams

proton.q: What is the typical spot position accuracy tolerance for PBS delivery?
proton.a: Within plus or minus 1 mm at isocenter plane
c: Within plus or minus 1 mm at isocenter plane
Within plus or minus 1 cm at isocenter plane
Within plus or minus 10 mm at isocenter point
Within plus or minus 5 cm at isocenter plane
proton.e: PBS spot position accuracy is typically required to be within plus or minus 1 mm at isocenter.

proton.q: What is the function of the dose monitor ionization chambers in the nozzle?

c: To measure and control delivered beam dose in real time
To shape the lateral beam profile for treatment
To degrade the proton beam energy as required
To verify patient position before beam delivery
proton.e: Dose monitor chambers continuously measure beam fluence/dose and trigger beam termination when the prescribed dose is reached.

proton.q: What is the typical number of energy layers in a PBS treatment field?
proton.a: Approximately 20 to 60 energy layers per field
c: Approximately 20 to 60 energy layers per field
Typically only 1 to 2 energy layers per field
Approximately 500 to 1000 layers per field
Exactly 100 energy layers for every field size
proton.e: A typical PBS field uses 20-60 energy layers to cover the target depth extent, each corresponding to a different proton energy.

proton.q: What is single-field uniform dose (SFUD) in proton PBS planning?
proton.a: Each field independently delivers uniform target dose
c: Each field independently delivers uniform target dose
Only one beam angle is used for entire treatment
A single energy layer covers the whole target depth
Uniform dose is delivered only to skin surface
proton.e: In SFUD, each field is individually optimized to deliver a uniform dose to the target, providing more robustness than MFO.

proton.q: What is multi-field optimization (MFO) in proton PBS planning?
proton.a: All fields optimized simultaneously for total dose
c: All fields optimized simultaneously for total dose
Multiple patients treated with same beam settings
Multiple fractions planned in one optimization run
Multiple machines used for a single treatment plan
proton.e: In MFO (also called IMPT), spot weights from all fields are optimized simultaneously, offering better OAR sparing.

proton.q: Why is SFUD generally considered more robust than MFO for proton plans?

SFUD uses more treatment beam angles than MFO
SFUD has smaller spot sizes than MFO delivery
MFO cannot account for range uncertainties at all

Passive scattering with modulated range shifter
Proton therapy using uniform field intensities only
A combination of photon IMRT with proton boost

proton.q: What is the clinical concern about the end-of-range effect in proton therapy?

c: Elevated RBE at distal edge near critical structures
Insufficient dose at the distal target boundary
Excessive neutron production at end of beam range
Mechanical damage to patient tissue from protons

proton.q: What is the approximate LET of protons at the entrance region of the beam?

c: Approximately 0.5 to 1.0 keV per micrometer
Approximately 20 to 50 keV per micrometer
Approximately 100 to 200 keV per micrometer
Approximately 500 to 800 keV per micrometer

Approximately 0.1 to 0.3 keV per micrometer
Approximately 100 to 300 keV per micrometer
Approximately 1000 to 2000 keV per micrometer

c: Slightly lower OER than photons at Bragg peak
Significantly higher OER than photons everywhere
Identical OER to photons at all beam positions
OER equals zero for all proton beam energies

c: Lower alpha/beta tissues show higher proton RBE
Higher alpha/beta tissues show higher proton RBE
Alpha/beta ratio has no effect on proton RBE

proton.q: What is the main advantage of proton therapy for head and neck cancer treatment?

Higher tumor control probability than photon therapy
Elimination of need for concurrent chemotherapy agents
Shorter treatment course than photon-based therapy

c: Dental fillings can cause range uncertainty artifacts
All teeth must be extracted before proton therapy
Dental guards are never used in proton treatments
Proton beams cannot penetrate dental structures

c: Reduced dose to bladder and rectum compared to IMRT
Proven superior tumor control over photon IMRT
Complete elimination of erectile dysfunction risk
Significantly shorter overall treatment time needed

proton.q: What challenge does rectal gas pose for proton prostate treatment?

c: Variable gas volume causes daily range variations
Gas increases the biological effect of proton dose
Gas prevents proton beams from reaching prostate
Gas has no effect on proton therapy at all

c: To avoid range uncertainty through variable rectal filling
To minimize dose to bilateral femoral head bones
To increase the lateral penumbra at target depth
To reduce the overall treatment delivery time needed

c: Replanning treatment based on anatomical changes
Using the same plan for all treatment fractions
Adapting the beam energy during a single fraction
Changing treatment modality from protons to photons
proton.e: Adaptive proton therapy involves monitoring anatomical changes and replanning when changes compromise the plan quality.

proton.a: Proton dose is more sensitive to anatomy changes
c: Proton dose is more sensitive to anatomy changes
Photon therapy never requires any plan adaptation
Proton therapy always requires daily replanning
Adaptive therapy is equally important for both

proton.q: What is the typical field-specific range margin formula used in proton planning?

c: 3.5% of water equivalent range plus 1 mm setup
0% of range with no additional margin needed
15% of range plus 15 mm additional margin
50% of range plus 50 mm additional margin
proton.e: A common range margin recipe is 3.5% x WER + 1 mm, accounting for CT calibration uncertainty and other systematic uncertainties.

c: Expanding compensator to account for setup uncertainty
Smoothing dose distribution with additional filters
Spreading beam laterally using scattering foil system
Averaging dose over multiple treatment fractions

proton.q: What is the purpose of a snout in the proton therapy nozzle?

c: To hold aperture and compensator close to patient
To generate the proton beam within the nozzle
To monitor the beam dose during treatment session
To scan the beam laterally across treatment field

c: To reduce lateral penumbra broadening from scatter
To increase the proton beam energy at patient
To reduce neutron contamination reaching the patient

proton.a: There is no HVL equivalent because protons have finite range
c: There is no HVL equivalent because protons have finite range
The HVL of protons is identical to that of photons
Proton HVL is measured in centimeters of lead only
HVL equals exactly half the proton practical range

proton.q: What is the relationship between proton energy and range in water?

c: Range approximately proportional to energy to power 1.8
Range is linearly proportional to proton beam energy
Range is inversely proportional to beam energy value
Range is independent of proton beam energy level

c: Approximately 15 to 16 cm in water medium
Approximately 1 to 2 cm in water medium depth
Approximately 50 to 60 cm in water medium
Approximately 150 cm in water medium exactly

proton.q: What is the approximate range of a 230 MeV proton beam in water?
proton.a: Approximately 32 to 33 cm in water medium
c: Approximately 32 to 33 cm in water medium
Approximately 5 to 6 cm in water medium depth
Approximately 100 cm in water medium exactly
Approximately 230 cm in water medium depth

proton.q: What is the clinical significance of the distal dose falloff in proton therapy?

c: Enables sparing of distal critical structures
Causes excessive dose to structures beyond target
Has no clinical impact on treatment outcomes
Prevents treatment of deep-seated tumor targets

c: Radioactive isotope production in patient and hardware

Energizing of beam transport magnets during use

proton.q: What material are patient-specific apertures typically made from?

c: Brass alloy machined by computer-controlled milling
Lead cast in a patient-specific custom mold shape
Tungsten alloy fabricated by 3D printing method

c: To broaden narrow carbon Bragg peaks in depth
To remove beam current ripple from synchrotron
To filter out fragmentation tail dose contribution

c: The local effect model (LEM) for biological dosing
A fixed RBE value of 1.1 for all treatments
The same model used for proton therapy planning

Measuring beam current at the nozzle exit window
Determining patient setup accuracy before treatment

Lower cost per treatment fraction delivered total
Better availability at treatment centers worldwide

Common epithelial cancers like breast and prostate
All pediatric tumors regardless of histology type

Monitoring beam position during treatment delivery
Calculating dose at depth in patient body tissue

Using identical beam parameters for every treatment

Excessive neutron dose at the field boundaries
Inability to verify dose at the field junction

By using only single-field treatment techniques
