c: To provide landscaping and aesthetic improvements to facility
To provide drainage away from the vault foundation walls
To provide additional shielding using the mass of packed earth
To create a buffer zone for emergency evacuation routes
shielding.e: Earth berms supplement wall shielding with additional mass at lower cost than concrete. Packed earth has a density of ~1.5-1.8 g/cm3 and provides useful attenuation.

shielding.q: What is the density of packed earth used as supplemental shielding?
shielding.a: Approximately 1.5-1.8 g/cm3
c: Approximately 0.5-0.8 g/cm3
Approximately 1.5-1.8 g/cm3
Approximately 2.3-2.5 g/cm3
Approximately 3.5-4.0 g/cm3
shielding.e: Packed earth has a density of approximately 1.5-1.8 g/cm3, less than concrete (2.35 g/cm3) but useful as supplemental shielding when space is available.

shielding.q: What factor must be considered when a linac vault is located below ground level?
shielding.a: Ground water and soil provide additional shielding for lower walls
c: Below-ground vaults cannot use concrete for the barrier walls
Ground water and soil provide additional shielding for lower walls
Underground facilities are exempt from all shielding requirements
Radon gas makes underground vaults unsuitable for treatment
shielding.e: Below-grade construction benefits from soil shielding for walls below ground level, potentially reducing required concrete thickness for those barriers.

shielding.q: What is the concept of the design dose rate (P/T) used in shielding calculations?
shielding.a: The adjusted dose limit accounting for occupancy at the protected point
c: The maximum dose rate the machine can physically deliver
The adjusted dose limit accounting for occupancy at the protected point
The dose rate measured during the initial shielding survey
The theoretical dose rate with zero shielding in place
shielding.e: The design dose rate P/T combines the dose limit (P) and occupancy factor (T) to give the effective dose rate limit at the point of interest during beam-on time.

shielding.q: Why is IMRT delivery a concern for shielding design?
shielding.a: IMRT increases head leakage workload due to higher monitor units
c: IMRT uses higher beam energies than conventional treatments
IMRT increases head leakage workload due to higher monitor units
IMRT produces more scatter from larger field sizes used
IMRT treatments are longer, requiring thicker primary barriers
shielding.e: IMRT typically requires 2-5 times more MU than conventional treatments, significantly increasing leakage radiation. This must be accounted for in the leakage workload.

shielding.q: What IMRT factor does NCRP 151 recommend for adjusting leakage workload?
shielding.a: A multiplicative factor C (typically 2-5) applied to leakage workload
c: A reduction factor of 0.5 applied to the primary barrier only
A multiplicative factor C (typically 2-5) applied to leakage workload
An additive factor of 100 Gy/week to the total workload value
No adjustment factor; standard workload values are sufficient
shielding.e: The IMRT factor C accounts for the increased MU per treatment Gy. NCRP 151 recommends multiplying the leakage workload by this factor (typically 2-5 depending on technique).

shielding.q: What is the typical lead equivalent of gypsum (drywall) wallboard?
shielding.a: Negligible shielding value for radiation protection purposes
c: Approximately 0.5 mm Pb equivalent per sheet of drywall
Approximately 1.0 mm Pb equivalent per sheet of drywall
Approximately 2.0 mm Pb equivalent per sheet of drywall
Negligible shielding value for radiation protection purposes
shielding.e: Standard gypsum wallboard has negligible radiation shielding value. Lead-lined drywall (with lead sheet laminated to gypsum) is used when shielding is needed in partition walls.

shielding.q: What is lead-lined drywall and where is it commonly used?
shielding.a: Gypsum board with bonded lead sheet, used in diagnostic imaging rooms
c: Drywall painted with lead-based paint for X-ray room walls
Gypsum board with bonded lead sheet, used in diagnostic imaging rooms
Standard drywall used only in uncontrolled corridor areas
High-density drywall made with barium sulfate filler material
shielding.e: Lead-lined drywall has a lead sheet (typically 0.8-3.2 mm) bonded to one side of the gypsum board, commonly used for diagnostic X-ray, CT, and nuclear medicine room walls.

shielding.q: What is the required overlap for lead-lined drywall at joints and edges?
shielding.a: Lead sheets must overlap to prevent radiation gaps at all joints
c: No overlap is needed if sheets are placed edge-to-edge tightly
Lead sheets must overlap to prevent radiation gaps at all joints
A gap of up to 5 mm is acceptable between adjacent lead sheets
Only horizontal joints require overlap but not vertical joints
shielding.e: Lead sheets must overlap at all joints (typically 1-2 cm) to prevent radiation streaming through gaps. Nails/screws must also be covered with lead patches.

shielding.q: What is the shielding design goal difference between NCRP 147 and older NCRP 49?
shielding.a: NCRP 147 uses air kerma-based design goals; NCRP 49 used exposure-based limits
c: NCRP 147 requires thicker barriers than the older NCRP 49 report
NCRP 147 uses air kerma-based design goals; NCRP 49 used exposure-based limits
NCRP 147 only applies to therapy; NCRP 49 was for diagnostic only
There is no significant difference between NCRP 147 and NCRP 49
shielding.e: NCRP 147 modernized the approach by using air kerma, updated workload distributions, and more realistic occupancy and use factors compared to the older NCRP 49.

shielding.q: What is the effect of oblique incidence on barrier transmission?
shielding.a: Oblique incidence increases the effective barrier thickness traversed
c: Oblique incidence has no effect on the barrier performance
Oblique incidence decreases the effective thickness traversed
Oblique incidence increases the effective barrier thickness traversed
Oblique incidence only matters for neutrons and not photons
shielding.e: When radiation strikes a barrier at an oblique angle, the path length through the material is longer than the physical thickness (by factor 1/cos theta), increasing effective attenuation.

shielding.q: What is the slant thickness correction for radiation hitting a barrier at angle theta?
shielding.a: Effective thickness = actual thickness / cos(theta)
c: Effective thickness = actual thickness x cos(theta)
Effective thickness = actual thickness / cos(theta)
Effective thickness = actual thickness x sin(theta)
Effective thickness = actual thickness regardless of angle
shielding.e: The slant factor 1/cos(theta) increases the path length through the barrier for obliquely incident radiation, providing more attenuation than the perpendicular thickness alone.

shielding.q: What is the scatter radiation intensity from CT typically expressed as?
shielding.a: Scatter fraction times CTDI at a reference distance and angle
c: A fixed percentage of the tube output at all angles uniformly
Scatter fraction times CTDI at a reference distance and angle
Total patient dose multiplied by a constant buildup factor
DLP divided by room volume as a uniform dose distribution
shielding.e: CT scatter is characterized by the scatter fraction (energy and angle dependent) applied to the CTDI or DLP-based workload at specific distances and angles from the patient.

shielding.q: What unique shielding consideration applies to fluoroscopy rooms with C-arm equipment?
shielding.a: The beam direction is variable, requiring uniform shielding in all barriers
c: No shielding is needed because fluoroscopy uses very low dose rates
Only the floor requires shielding for C-arm fluoroscopy equipment
The beam direction is variable, requiring uniform shielding in all barriers
Only ceiling shielding is required for C-arm fluoroscopy units
shielding.e: C-arm fluoroscopy units can direct the beam in multiple orientations, requiring that all walls be designed for the possibility of primary beam incidence.

shielding.q: What is the HVL of lead for typical diagnostic X-rays (80-100 kVp)?
shielding.a: Approximately 0.25-0.30 mm of lead
c: Approximately 0.05-0.10 mm of lead
Approximately 0.25-0.30 mm of lead
Approximately 1.0-1.50 mm of lead
Approximately 3.0-4.00 mm of lead
shielding.e: At diagnostic energies, the HVL of lead is approximately 0.25-0.30 mm. This high attenuation efficiency makes thin lead sheets effective for diagnostic room shielding.

shielding.q: What special shielding consideration applies to mammography rooms?
shielding.a: Lower energy X-rays require less shielding; often standard walls suffice
c: Mammography requires the thickest shielding of all imaging rooms
Lower energy X-rays require less shielding; often standard walls suffice
Only the ceiling needs shielding in a mammography imaging room
Mammography rooms need the same shielding as CT scanner rooms
shielding.e: Mammography uses 25-35 kVp, producing very low-energy X-rays that are easily attenuated. Standard construction walls often provide adequate shielding without additional lead.

shielding.q: What is the typical workload assumption for a busy CT scanner per NCRP 147?
shielding.a: Based on the number of patients and average DLP per examination
c: A fixed value of 1000 mA-min per week for all CT scanners
Based on the number of patients and average DLP per examination
The maximum tube output multiplied by 40 hours per week
The same workload as a general radiographic room by default
shielding.e: NCRP 147 uses DLP-based workload distributions that account for the actual mix of CT examinations performed, providing more accurate shielding calculations.

shielding.q: What is the shielding concern for PET uptake rooms?
shielding.a: Patients emit 511 keV photons during the 45-60 minute uptake period
c: PET tracers emit alpha particles that require thick shielding
Patients emit 511 keV photons during the 45-60 minute uptake period
Uptake rooms need no shielding since patients are not yet scanned
The cyclotron radiation is the main concern in uptake areas
shielding.e: After F-18 FDG injection, patients emit 511 keV annihilation photons during the uptake period (typically 45-60 minutes), requiring adequate wall shielding for adjacent areas.

shielding.q: What is the typical wall shielding requirement for a PET uptake room?
shielding.a: 2-3 mm lead equivalent or equivalent concrete thickness
c: No shielding beyond standard wall construction needed
2-3 mm lead equivalent or equivalent concrete thickness
10-15 mm lead equivalent for adequate attenuation
25-30 mm lead equivalent for full protection level
shielding.e: PET uptake rooms typically require 2-3 mm lead equivalent to attenuate 511 keV photons from patients during the uptake period to acceptable levels in adjacent spaces.

shielding.q: What is the concept of dose rate constant (specific gamma ray constant) used in nuclear medicine shielding?
shielding.a: The dose rate per unit activity at a standard reference distance
c: The rate at which radioactive material loses activity over time
The dose rate per unit activity at a standard reference distance
The maximum dose a patient receives from administered activity
The constant relating beam energy to penetration depth
shielding.e: The dose rate constant (Gamma, in units like mSv*m2/(GBq*hr)) gives the dose rate at 1 m from a point source of unit activity, used to calculate unshielded dose rates.

shielding.q: What is the dose rate constant for Tc-99m at 1 meter?
shielding.a: Approximately 0.020 mSv*m2/(GBq*hr)
c: Approximately 0.020 mSv*m2/(GBq*hr)
Approximately 0.077 mSv*m2/(GBq*hr)
Approximately 0.143 mSv*m2/(GBq*hr)
Approximately 0.350 mSv*m2/(GBq*hr)
shielding.e: Tc-99m has a relatively low dose rate constant of ~0.020 mSv*m2/(GBq*hr) due to its single 140 keV gamma ray, making it easier to shield than higher-energy emitters.

shielding.q: What nuclear medicine procedure typically requires the most room shielding?
shielding.a: I-131 therapy with high administered activities
c: Tc-99m bone scan with standard imaging doses
I-131 therapy with high administered activities
Tl-201 stress test with standard cardiac dose
Tc-99m MAA lung perfusion scan with low doses
shielding.e: I-131 therapy uses high activities (3.7-7.4 GBq or more), emits penetrating 364 keV gammas, and has a long 8-day half-life, requiring substantial room shielding.

shielding.q: What is the TVL of lead for I-131 (364 keV)?
shielding.a: Approximately 7-8 mm of lead
c: Approximately 2-3 mm of lead
Approximately 7-8 mm of lead
Approximately 15-18 mm of lead
Approximately 30-35 mm of lead
shielding.e: The TVL of lead for I-131 (364 keV) is approximately 7-8 mm. Multiple TVLs are needed for therapy rooms due to the high administered activities.

shielding.q: How does NCRP 147 account for the chest Bucky (wall-mounted receptor) in workload calculations?
shielding.a: It assigns a specific fraction of total workload to upright examinations
c: It ignores the chest Bucky in all shielding calculations
It assigns a specific fraction of total workload to upright examinations
It assumes 100% of workload is directed at the chest Bucky
It uses the Bucky only for scatter but not primary calculations
shielding.e: NCRP 147 distributes the total room workload between the table and upright Bucky based on typical clinical practice, with specific use factors for each beam direction.

shielding.q: What is the pre-cast concrete block limitation for linac vault construction?
shielding.a: Joints between blocks can create streaming paths if not properly filled
c: Pre-cast blocks cannot achieve adequate density for shielding
Pre-cast blocks are too expensive for any vault construction
Joints between blocks can create streaming paths if not properly filled
Pre-cast blocks absorb moisture and lose shielding effectiveness
shielding.e: Pre-cast block joints must be properly filled with grout to prevent radiation streaming. Monolithic (poured-in-place) concrete avoids this concern but has other construction constraints.

shielding.q: What is the recommended vault interior dimension for a typical linac room?
shielding.a: Approximately 6-8 m long by 5-7 m wide by 3-4 m high
c: Approximately 3-4 m long by 3-4 m wide by 2.5 m high
Approximately 6-8 m long by 5-7 m wide by 3-4 m high
Approximately 12-15 m long by 10-12 m wide by 5-6 m high
Approximately 20-25 m long by 15-20 m wide by 8-10 m high
shielding.e: Typical linac vault interior dimensions are 6-8 m long by 5-7 m wide with a ceiling height of 3-4 m, providing adequate clearance for gantry rotation and patient access.

shielding.q: What is the typical primary barrier thickness for a 6 MV linac vault in ordinary concrete?
shielding.a: Approximately 1.5-2.0 m (150-200 cm)
c: Approximately 0.3-0.5 m (30-50 cm)
Approximately 1.5-2.0 m (150-200 cm)
Approximately 3.0-4.0 m (300-400 cm)
Approximately 5.0-6.0 m (500-600 cm)
shielding.e: A 6 MV primary barrier typically requires 1.5-2.0 m of ordinary concrete, depending on workload, distance, and occupancy of the area beyond the barrier.

shielding.q: What is the typical secondary barrier thickness for a 6 MV linac vault?
shielding.a: Approximately 1.0-1.3 m (100-130 cm) of concrete
c: Approximately 0.3-0.5 m (30-50 cm) of concrete
Approximately 1.0-1.3 m (100-130 cm) of concrete
Approximately 2.0-2.5 m (200-250 cm) of concrete
Approximately 3.0-4.0 m (300-400 cm) of concrete
shielding.e: Secondary barriers for 6 MV are typically 1.0-1.3 m, thinner than primary barriers because they only attenuate scatter and leakage rather than the direct beam.

shielding.q: What is the exposure rate from an unshielded F-18 patient (370 MBq) at 1 meter?
shielding.a: Approximately 53 microSv/hr at time of injection
c: Approximately 5 microSv/hr at time of injection
Approximately 53 microSv/hr at time of injection
Approximately 530 microSv/hr at time of injection
Approximately 5300 microSv/hr at time of injection
shielding.e: Using the dose rate constant of ~0.143 mSv*m2/(GBq*hr): 0.143 x 0.370 = 0.053 mSv/hr = 53 microSv/hr at 1 m from a 370 MBq F-18 patient.

shielding.q: What is the time-averaged dose rate concept and why does it matter?
shielding.a: Dose averaged over occupancy time; used for weekly dose limit compliance
c: The instantaneous peak dose rate during beam-on operation
Dose averaged over occupancy time; used for weekly dose limit compliance
The dose rate averaged over the lifetime of the shielding
The background radiation rate subtracted from measured values
shielding.e: Time-averaged dose rate accounts for the duty cycle (fraction of time beam is on) and is used to demonstrate compliance with weekly design goals like P = 0.1 mSv/week.

shielding.q: How does NCRP 151 define the 'two-source' rule for combining barrier requirements?
shielding.a: If barriers differ by <1 TVL, use the larger and add 1 HVL
c: Always add the two barrier thicknesses directly together
If barriers differ by <1 TVL, use the larger and add 1 HVL
Always use the smaller of the two calculated thicknesses
If barriers differ by >2 TVL, add both thicknesses together
shielding.e: When two radiation sources require barrier thicknesses within 1 TVL of each other, use the thicker value and add one HVL. If they differ by more than 1 TVL, the thicker alone suffices.

shielding.q: What is the weekly design goal for an embryo/fetus per NCRP?
shielding.a: 0.5 mSv per month (approximately 0.125 mSv/week)
c: 5.0 mSv per month for the entire pregnancy period
0.5 mSv per month (approximately 0.125 mSv/week)
Same as general public at 0.02 mSv per week limit
Same as occupational worker at 0.10 mSv per week
shielding.e: NCRP recommends a monthly embryo/fetus dose limit of 0.5 mSv, not exceeding 5 mSv for the entire pregnancy. This may affect shielding for areas occupied by pregnant workers.

shielding.q: What is the density of borated polyethylene (BPE) used in neutron shielding?
shielding.a: Approximately 0.95-1.0 g/cm3
c: Approximately 0.5-0.6 g/cm3
Approximately 0.95-1.0 g/cm3
Approximately 2.3-2.5 g/cm3
Approximately 5.0-5.5 g/cm3
shielding.e: BPE has a density of approximately 0.95-1.0 g/cm3, similar to water. Despite its low density, it is highly effective for neutron shielding due to its high hydrogen content.

shielding.q: What percentage of boron is typically present in BPE for neutron shielding?
shielding.a: Approximately 5% boron by weight
c: Approximately 1% boron by weight
Approximately 5% boron by weight
Approximately 15% boron by weight
Approximately 30% boron by weight
shielding.e: Standard BPE contains approximately 5% natural boron by weight (of which ~20% is B-10). This provides adequate thermal neutron capture without compromising hydrogen content.

shielding.q: What is the purpose of a warning light at the entrance to a radiation therapy vault?
shielding.a: To indicate when the radiation beam is energized and treatment is active
c: To illuminate the hallway for patient safety while walking
To indicate when the radiation beam is energized and treatment is active
To show the status of the HVAC system inside the vault
To signal that the door interlock system is being tested
shielding.e: Warning lights (typically red = beam on) alert personnel that the radiation beam is active, preventing inadvertent entry into the vault during treatment.

shielding.q: What additional shielding component is needed at the base of linac vault walls?
shielding.a: The wall-floor junction must maintain full barrier thickness without gaps
c: Additional lead lining is required on all floor surfaces
Extra ventilation ducts must be installed at the wall base
The wall-floor junction must maintain full barrier thickness without gaps
Steel reinforcement plates are always needed at the wall base
shielding.e: The wall-floor junction is a common weak point in shielding. Proper construction ensures continuity of the barrier without voids or reduced thickness at this critical interface.

shielding.q: What is the shielding consideration for the linac cable trench or conduit chase?
shielding.a: Conduit paths must not create direct streaming paths through barriers
c: Cable trenches are too small to affect shielding integrity
Conduit paths must not create direct streaming paths through barriers
All cables must be shielded with lead sleeves individually
Cable trenches only matter for primary barrier penetrations
shielding.e: Cable trenches and conduit chases can create radiation streaming paths if they penetrate barriers in a straight line. Bends and backfill are used to maintain shielding integrity.

shielding.q: What is the shielding evaluation frequency recommended for existing therapy installations?
shielding.a: After any equipment change, room modification, or use pattern change
c: Once every ten years regardless of any changes to equipment
After any equipment change, room modification, or use pattern change
Only at the time of initial installation and never again
Monthly surveys are required for all therapy room installations
shielding.e: Shielding re-evaluation is needed when equipment is changed (e.g., new linac with higher energy), room modifications occur, or usage patterns change significantly.

shielding.q: What type of dosimeter is commonly used for area monitoring behind shielding barriers?
shielding.a: OSL or TLD badge placed at representative locations
c: Real-time electronic personal dosimeters worn by staff
OSL or TLD badge placed at representative locations
Film badges placed directly on the barrier inner surface
Pocket ionization chambers checked hourly by personnel
shielding.e: OSL (optically stimulated luminescence) or TLD (thermoluminescent dosimeter) badges are placed at representative occupied locations behind barriers for ongoing dose monitoring.

shielding.q: What reading should be expected from area monitors behind adequate shielding?
shielding.a: Well below the design goal (typically <0.02 mSv/week for uncontrolled areas)
c: Exactly equal to the shielding design goal of 0.1 mSv per week
Well below the design goal (typically <0.02 mSv/week for uncontrolled areas)
Zero dose reading indicating perfect shielding barrier integrity
Up to five times the design goal is acceptable for barriers
shielding.e: Area monitor readings should be well below the applicable design goal. Readings approaching or exceeding the design goal warrant investigation and possible remediation.

shielding.q: What is the photoelectric effect's role in radiation shielding?
shielding.a: It completely absorbs the incident photon, providing maximum attenuation
c: It scatters photons forward with minimal energy loss amount
It completely absorbs the incident photon, providing maximum attenuation
It converts photons to neutrons in high-Z shield materials
It only occurs at energies above 1.022 MeV in any material
shielding.e: The photoelectric effect completely removes the incident photon (absorbed by an atom with electron ejection), making it the most effective attenuation mechanism per interaction.

shielding.q: Why is lead less effective per unit mass for shielding photons in the 2-5 MeV range?
shielding.a: Compton scattering dominates and depends mainly on electron density
c: Lead becomes transparent at energies between 2 and 5 MeV
Pair production is impossible in lead above 2 MeV energy
Compton scattering dominates and depends mainly on electron density
The K-edge of lead falls exactly in the 2-5 MeV energy range
shielding.e: In the 2-5 MeV range, Compton scattering dominates and depends on electron density (electrons per gram), which is similar for most materials, reducing lead's advantage over lighter materials.

shielding.q: At what photon energy does pair production become possible?
shielding.a: Above 1.022 MeV (twice the electron rest mass energy)
c: Above 0.511 MeV (one electron rest mass energy)
Above 1.022 MeV (twice the electron rest mass energy)
Above 2.044 MeV (four electron rest mass energy)
Above 10.0 MeV (nuclear binding energy threshold)
shielding.e: Pair production requires photon energy of at least 1.022 MeV (2 x 0.511 MeV) to create an electron-positron pair. The cross-section increases with Z^2 of the absorber.

shielding.q: What is the advantage of using interlocking concrete blocks for vault construction?
shielding.a: They allow vault modification or decommissioning without demolition
c: They provide better shielding than poured concrete per unit volume
They are the only concrete option that meets radiation codes
They allow vault modification or decommissioning without demolition
They eliminate the need for rebar structural reinforcement
shielding.e: Interlocking concrete blocks can be disassembled, allowing vault expansion, equipment replacement, or facility decommissioning without destructive demolition of poured walls.

shielding.q: What is the radiation quality factor (Q) for photons used in shielding dose equivalent calculations?
shielding.a: Q = 1 for all photon energies
c: Q = 1 for all photon energies
Q = 5 for diagnostic energy photons
Q = 10 for megavoltage photons
Q = 20 for cobalt-60 gamma rays
shielding.e: The quality factor for photons (X-rays and gamma rays) is 1 at all energies. Neutrons have higher quality factors (5-20) depending on energy.

shielding.q: What quality factor does NCRP assign to neutrons from medical linacs for shielding purposes?
shielding.a: An average quality factor of approximately 10
c: A quality factor of exactly 1 for all neutrons
A quality factor of exactly 5 for all neutrons
An average quality factor of approximately 10
A quality factor of exactly 20 for all neutrons
shielding.e: NCRP uses an average Q of approximately 10 for the mixed neutron spectrum from medical linacs, though Q varies from 5 to 20 depending on neutron energy.

shielding.q: What is the recommended location for a radiation survey meter during a linac acceptance survey?
shielding.a: At the nearest occupied point beyond each barrier
c: Directly on the inner surface of each shielding barrier
At the nearest occupied point beyond each barrier
Only at the vault entrance door and nowhere else
At a standard distance of exactly 10 meters from the vault
shielding.e: Survey measurements are taken at the nearest occupied points beyond each barrier (typically 30 cm from the outer surface) to verify compliance with design goals.

shielding.q: What is the maximum source-to-barrier distance typically assumed in a Co-60 room?
shielding.a: Approximately 3-5 m depending on room dimensions
c: Always exactly 1 m from the source by standard
Approximately 3-5 m depending on room dimensions
Always exactly 10 m from the source by regulation
Approximately 15-20 m for large therapy rooms
shielding.e: In a typical Co-60 room, the source-to-barrier distance is approximately 3-5 m, depending on room size. This distance is used in inverse square law calculations.

shielding.q: How does NCRP 151 treat scatter from the treatment couch and floor?
shielding.a: It includes a patient scatter fraction and room scatter components
c: Couch and floor scatter are considered completely negligible
It includes a patient scatter fraction and room scatter components
Only the patient scatter matters; room scatter is always ignored
Floor scatter doubles the total barrier requirement thickness
shielding.e: NCRP 151 accounts for scatter from the patient as the primary scatter source and includes contributions from room surfaces (walls, floor) as secondary scatter components.

shielding.q: What is the advantage of placing a linac vault below grade (underground)?
shielding.a: Earth provides supplemental shielding and reduces above-grade barrier needs
c: Underground vaults are exempt from all regulatory dose limits
It eliminates the need for any concrete in the vault walls
Earth provides supplemental shielding and reduces above-grade barrier needs
Below-grade locations have naturally lower background radiation
shielding.e: Below-grade construction allows the surrounding earth to supplement wall shielding, potentially reducing concrete thickness requirements and construction costs.

shielding.q: What compliance testing documentation must be maintained for a shielded facility?
shielding.a: Survey results, calculations, drawings, and regulatory correspondence
c: Only the final approval letter from the regulatory authority
Survey results, calculations, drawings, and regulatory correspondence
Only the architect's original construction blueprint drawings
No documentation is required once the facility is operational
shielding.e: Complete records including shielding design calculations, construction drawings, survey measurements, and regulatory correspondence must be maintained for the life of the facility.

shielding.q: What is the role of AAPM Task Group reports in shielding design practice?
shielding.a: They provide practical implementation guidance supplementing NCRP reports
c: They replace all NCRP reports as the sole regulatory standard
They provide practical implementation guidance supplementing NCRP reports
They apply only to academic research facilities and not clinics
They address only diagnostic shielding and never therapy vaults
shielding.e: AAPM TG reports (e.g., TG-108 for PET shielding) provide practical guidance, worked examples, and clarifications that supplement the NCRP framework documents.

shielding.q: What is the principal goal of a shielding compliance evaluation?
shielding.a: To verify that dose rates in occupied areas meet design goals and regulatory limits
c: To determine the exact composition of barrier construction materials
To verify that dose rates in occupied areas meet design goals and regulatory limits
To measure the energy spectrum of radiation passing through barriers
To calculate the total collective dose to all facility staff members
shielding.e: Shielding compliance evaluation confirms that measured dose rates at all occupied locations around the facility are within the applicable design goals and regulatory limits.

shielding.q: According to NCRP 151, what is the recommended shielding design goal for a controlled area adjacent to a radiotherapy treatment vault?
shielding.a: 0.1 mSv per week
c: 0.02 mSv per week
0.1 mSv per week
0.5 mSv per week
1.0 mSv per week
shielding.e: NCRP 151 recommends a shielding design goal of 0.1 mSv/week (5 mSv/year) for controlled areas where occupational workers are present.

shielding.q: What is the primary purpose of a maze entrance in a linear accelerator treatment vault?
shielding.a: To attenuate scattered and leakage radiation reaching the door through multiple wall reflections
c: To provide a direct line-of-sight path for visual monitoring of the patient during treatment
To attenuate scattered and leakage radiation reaching the door through multiple wall reflections
To increase the room volume for better ventilation and ozone dispersal after beam operation
To create space for mounting additional shielding on the inner walls of the treatment room
shielding.e: The maze forces radiation to undergo multiple scatterings off walls before reaching the door, greatly reducing the radiation level and the required door shielding thickness.

shielding.q: For a 6 MV linac, what is the approximate tenth-value layer (TVL) of concrete for primary beam shielding?
shielding.a: Approximately 34 cm of standard density concrete
c: Approximately 17 cm of standard density concrete
Approximately 24 cm of standard density concrete
Approximately 34 cm of standard density concrete
Approximately 51 cm of standard density concrete
shielding.e: For 6 MV primary beams, the first TVL in standard concrete (density 2.35 g/cm3) is approximately 34 cm. The equilibrium TVL is slightly less due to beam hardening.

shielding.q: In shielding calculations, what does the use factor (U) represent for a primary barrier?
shielding.a: The fraction of the workload during which the beam is directed toward that barrier
c: The fraction of the workload during which the beam is directed toward that barrier
The ratio of scattered radiation to primary radiation at one meter from the target
The occupancy factor for the area immediately adjacent to the shielding barrier wall
The percentage of time the treatment machine operates at its maximum dose rate output
shielding.e: The use factor quantifies how often the primary beam points toward a specific barrier, typically 0.25 for walls and 1.0 for the floor in conventional treatment room layouts.

shielding.q: What is the occupancy factor (T) recommended by NCRP 151 for an uncontrolled area such as a public corridor adjacent to a treatment vault?
shielding.a: T = 1/5 for corridors and lobbies without permanent workstations
c: T = 1 for all publicly accessible areas regardless of usage patterns
T = 1/5 for corridors and lobbies without permanent workstations
T = 1/20 for all uncontrolled areas outside the treatment facility
T = 1/40 for hallways immediately adjacent to treatment vault walls
shielding.e: NCRP 151 assigns T = 1/5 for corridors and similar areas with transient occupancy, reflecting that people do not remain in these spaces for extended periods.

shielding.q: When calculating secondary barrier thickness, which two radiation components must be considered?
shielding.a: Patient-scattered radiation and linac head leakage radiation contributions
c: Primary beam and patient-scattered radiation reaching the secondary barrier area
Patient-scattered radiation and linac head leakage radiation contributions
Neutron radiation and activation products from the linac treatment head assembly
Skyshine radiation and groundshine radiation reflected from surrounding surfaces
shielding.e: Secondary barriers must attenuate both patient scatter (dependent on field size and angle) and head leakage (typically 0.1% of primary beam), with the two contributions evaluated independently and combined.

shielding.q: For a high-energy linac (≥10 MV), what additional shielding consideration becomes significant compared to a 6 MV unit?
shielding.a: Photoneutron production requiring supplementary neutron shielding at the door and maze
c: Increased electron contamination requiring additional lead lining of the treatment vault
Photoneutron production requiring supplementary neutron shielding at the door and maze
Higher ozone production levels requiring thicker concrete walls for gas containment
Greater activation of room air requiring sealed ventilation with delayed exhaust release
shielding.e: Above approximately 8 MV, photoneutron production from high-Z materials in the linac head becomes significant, requiring polyethylene or borated polyethylene shielding at maze entrances and doors.

shielding.q: What is the standard workload assumption used in NCRP 151 for a busy conventional linear accelerator?
shielding.a: 1000 Gy per week at one meter from the x-ray target
c: 100 Gy per week at one meter from the x-ray target
500 Gy per week at one meter from the x-ray target
1000 Gy per week at one meter from the x-ray target
2000 Gy per week at one meter from the x-ray target
shielding.e: NCRP 151 suggests a workload of 1000 Gy/week at 1 m for a busy conventional linac, based on typical patient throughput and dose per treatment field.

shielding.q: In vault shielding design, what is the scatter fraction at 90 degrees for a 6 MV beam with a 20x20 cm field at 1 meter?
shielding.a: Approximately 6 × 10⁻⁴ per 400 cm² field area
c: Approximately 2 × 10⁻² per 400 cm² field area
Approximately 6 × 10⁻³ per 400 cm² field area
Approximately 6 × 10⁻⁴ per 400 cm² field area
Approximately 2 × 10⁻⁵ per 400 cm² field area
shielding.e: The scatter fraction at 90 degrees for 6 MV beams is approximately 6 × 10⁻⁴ per 400 cm² of field area, as tabulated in NCRP 151 for shielding calculations.

shielding.q: What is the IDR (instantaneous dose rate) limit typically used for determining whether an interlock is required on a treatment vault door?
shielding.a: 20 μSv in any one hour at 30 cm from the accessible surface
c: 2 μSv in any one hour at 30 cm from the accessible surface
20 μSv in any one hour at 30 cm from the accessible surface
100 μSv in any one hour at 30 cm from the accessible surface
500 μSv in any one hour at 30 cm from the accessible surface
shielding.e: Regulatory guidance typically requires door interlocks or additional shielding if the instantaneous dose rate exceeds 20 μSv/h at 30 cm from the door surface during beam operation.

shielding.q: When designing a primary barrier, how is the required number of TVLs calculated from the transmission factor B?
shielding.a: n = −log₁₀(B), where B is the ratio of design goal to unshielded dose rate
c: n = −log₁₀(B), where B is the ratio of design goal to unshielded dose rate
n = −ln(B), using the natural logarithm of the barrier transmission factor
n = 1/B, giving the number of TVLs as the reciprocal of transmission factor
n = B × TVL, multiplying the transmission by the tenth-value layer thickness
shielding.e: The number of TVLs is calculated as n = −log₁₀(B), where B = P·d²/(W·U·T). Each TVL reduces the radiation intensity by a factor of 10.

shielding.q: For skyshine calculations in an outdoor area above a treatment vault with a thin roof, what is the primary concern?
shielding.a: Radiation scattering upward through the ceiling and back down to ground level beyond the facility
c: Direct primary beam penetration through the vault ceiling to aircraft flying overhead
Radiation scattering upward through the ceiling and back down to ground level beyond the facility
Neutron activation of rainwater collecting on the roof surface of the treatment vault
Electromagnetic interference from the linac affecting nearby communication equipment
shielding.e: Skyshine occurs when radiation exits through the ceiling, scatters in the atmosphere, and returns to ground level at distances beyond the facility, potentially affecting public areas.

shielding.q: What is the typical density of barite concrete used for radiation shielding, and why is it preferred over standard concrete in some installations?
shielding.a: Approximately 3.5 g/cm³, allowing thinner walls while providing equivalent attenuation
c: Approximately 2.35 g/cm³, offering better structural strength than standard concrete
Approximately 3.5 g/cm³, allowing thinner walls while providing equivalent attenuation
Approximately 4.5 g/cm³, providing superior neutron shielding compared to standard mix
Approximately 5.0 g/cm³, required for all vaults housing energies above 10 megavolts
shielding.e: Barite concrete has a density of approximately 3.5 g/cm³ compared to 2.35 g/cm³ for standard concrete, allowing thinner barrier walls while achieving the same photon attenuation.

shielding.q: In NCRP 151, what is the recommended approach when primary and secondary barrier calculations yield similar thickness requirements at a wall?
shielding.a: Design the entire wall as a primary barrier to simplify construction and ensure adequate protection
c: Use the secondary barrier thickness since it is always the more conservative estimate
Design the entire wall as a primary barrier to simplify construction and ensure adequate protection
Average the two calculated thicknesses and add one additional half-value layer margin
Use the thinner secondary barrier thickness to reduce construction costs at that location
shielding.e: When primary and secondary barrier requirements are comparable, it is practical and conservative to design the entire wall as a primary barrier, avoiding complex construction with different thicknesses.

shielding.q: What is the half-value layer (HVL) of lead for Co-60 gamma radiation shielding?
shielding.a: Approximately 12 mm of lead for Co-60 gamma energies
c: Approximately 4 mm of lead for Co-60 gamma energies
Approximately 8 mm of lead for Co-60 gamma energies
Approximately 12 mm of lead for Co-60 gamma energies
Approximately 20 mm of lead for Co-60 gamma energies
shielding.e: The HVL of lead for Co-60 (average energy 1.25 MeV) is approximately 12 mm. Lead is less effective per unit thickness at these energies compared to lower-energy sources.

shielding.q: For a maze in a high-energy linac vault, which method is used to estimate the neutron dose equivalent at the maze entrance door?
shielding.a: The maze transmission method using successive wall reflections and a tenth-value distance
c: Direct Monte Carlo simulation is always required for every maze neutron calculation
The maze transmission method using successive wall reflections and a tenth-value distance
Simple inverse square law calculation from the linac head to the door location
Assuming neutron dose is a fixed percentage of the photon leakage at all locations
shielding.e: NCRP 151 provides a method for calculating neutron fluence at the maze door using successive reflections along the maze path, each characterized by a reduction per unit length related to the maze cross-section.

shielding.q: When performing a radiation survey of a newly constructed treatment vault, at what locations should measurements be taken?
shielding.a: At accessible surfaces of all barriers, maze door, ceiling, and any penetrations or conduits
c: Only at the maze entrance door since this is the sole potential exposure pathway
At accessible surfaces of all barriers, maze door, ceiling, and any penetrations or conduits
Exclusively at locations specified by the architectural drawings and shielding report only
Only at points where personnel dosimeters will be worn by the radiation therapy staff
shielding.e: A comprehensive radiation survey must cover all accessible barrier surfaces, the door, ceiling (for skyshine potential), and any penetrations for cables or ducts that could provide streaming paths.

shielding.q: What is the effect of laminated (layered) shielding using lead and concrete on photon attenuation efficiency?
shielding.a: Lead provides efficient high-Z attenuation while concrete thermalizes and absorbs secondary radiation
c: Laminated shielding is always less effective than an equivalent thickness of pure concrete
Lead provides efficient high-Z attenuation while concrete thermalizes and absorbs secondary radiation
The order of materials has no effect on total attenuation for broad-beam geometry
Concrete must always be placed before lead to prevent pair production in the lead
shielding.e: In laminated shielding, lead efficiently attenuates primary photons through photoelectric and pair production, while concrete moderates and absorbs any secondary neutrons and provides additional photon attenuation.

shielding.q: According to NCRP 151, what value of head leakage should be assumed for shielding design of a medical linear accelerator?
shielding.a: 0.1% of the useful beam dose rate at one meter from the source
c: 0.01% of the useful beam dose rate at one meter from the source
0.05% of the useful beam dose rate at one meter from the source
0.1% of the useful beam dose rate at one meter from the source
1.0% of the useful beam dose rate at one meter from the source
shielding.e: Linac manufacturers are required to limit leakage to 0.1% of the useful beam at 1 m from the target, and this value is used as the design assumption in NCRP 151 shielding calculations.

ct.q: What is the Hounsfield unit (HU) value of water in a properly calibrated CT scanner?
ct.a: 0 HU
c: 0 HU
100 HU
ct.e: By definition, the Hounsfield scale assigns water a value of 0 HU. This is one of the two fixed reference points, with air at -1000 HU.

ct.q: What is the Hounsfield unit (HU) value of air in a properly calibrated CT scanner?
c: 0 HU
ct.e: Air is defined as -1000 HU on the Hounsfield scale. This is one of two reference points used for CT number calibration.

ct.q: Which detector type is most commonly used in modern multi-detector CT scanners?
ct.a: Solid-state ceramic scintillators
c: Solid-state ceramic scintillators
High-pressure xenon gas detectors
Cadmium zinc telluride crystals
Silicon photomultiplier arrays
ct.e: Modern MDCT scanners predominantly use solid-state ceramic scintillator detectors coupled with photodiodes for efficient x-ray detection.

ct.q: What does CTDI stand for in CT dosimetry?
ct.a: Computed Tomography Dose Index
c: Computed Tomography Dose Index
Computed Tomographic Data Input
CT Detector Integration value
CT Dose Indication parameter
ct.e: CTDI stands for Computed Tomography Dose Index. It is the fundamental dose descriptor in CT that quantifies radiation output from a single axial rotation.

ct.q: In helical CT, what is the definition of pitch?
ct.a: Table travel per rotation divided by total collimation width
c: Table travel per rotation divided by total collimation width
Total collimation width divided by table travel per rotation
Gantry rotation speed divided by the total scan length value
Detector row width multiplied by the number of detector rows
ct.e: Pitch is defined as the ratio of table travel per gantry rotation to the total nominal beam collimation width. A pitch of 1 means no gaps or overlaps.

ct.q: Which CT artifact is caused by differential attenuation of low-energy photons through dense structures?
ct.a: Beam hardening artifact
c: Beam hardening artifact
Partial volume artifact
Ring artifact pattern
Motion blur artifact
ct.e: Beam hardening occurs because lower-energy photons are preferentially absorbed by dense materials, shifting the mean beam energy and causing cupping or streak artifacts.

ct.q: What is the typical range of Hounsfield units for cortical bone?
ct.e: Dense cortical bone typically measures between +500 and +1500 HU depending on its density and mineralization.

ct.q: What is the standard phantom diameter used for measuring CTDI in body CT dosimetry?
ct.a: 32 cm diameter acrylic phantom
c: 32 cm diameter acrylic phantom
16 cm diameter acrylic phantom
24 cm diameter acrylic phantom
10 cm diameter acrylic phantom
ct.e: The standard body CTDI phantom is 32 cm in diameter. The 16 cm phantom is used for head CT dosimetry measurements.

ct.q: Which reconstruction algorithm uses back-projection with a high-pass filter to reduce blurring?
ct.a: Filtered back projection (FBP)
c: Filtered back projection (FBP)
Algebraic reconstruction method
Maximum likelihood estimation
Statistical iterative approach
ct.e: Filtered back projection applies a ramp filter to projection data before back-projecting, removing the blurring inherent in simple back projection.

ct.q: What type of CT artifact appears as concentric rings or bands in the reconstructed image?
ct.a: Ring artifact from a faulty detector element
c: Ring artifact from a faulty detector element
Beam hardening from dense bone structures
Partial volume from tissue boundaries edge
Photon starvation through thick body parts
ct.e: Ring artifacts occur when a single detector element is miscalibrated or malfunctioning, producing a consistent error that maps to a ring in the reconstructed image.

ct.q: What is the purpose of automatic tube current modulation (ATCM) in CT scanning?
ct.a: To adjust mA based on patient attenuation and maintain image quality
c: To adjust mA based on patient attenuation and maintain image quality
To increase spatial resolution by varying the focal spot size used
To reduce beam hardening artifacts in dense anatomy scanned
To synchronize gantry rotation with the patient breathing cycle
ct.e: ATCM adjusts tube current in real-time based on patient size and anatomy attenuation to maintain consistent image quality while minimizing radiation dose.

ct.q: What does DLP stand for in CT dosimetry?
ct.a: Dose Length Product
c: Dose Length Product
Detector Line Profile
Digital Length Parameter
Dose Limit Protocol
ct.e: DLP is the Dose Length Product, calculated as CTDIvol multiplied by scan length. It represents the total integrated dose for a complete CT examination.

ct.q: In CT simulation for radiation therapy, what is the primary purpose of the flat tabletop?
ct.a: To replicate treatment positioning geometry accurately
c: To replicate treatment positioning geometry accurately
To reduce scatter radiation reaching the CT detectors
To improve spatial resolution of the CT image data
To decrease patient dose during the simulation scan
ct.e: A flat tabletop in CT simulation replicates the treatment couch geometry on the linear accelerator, ensuring accurate patient positioning and treatment planning.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the bowtie filter in a CT scanner?
ct.a: To equalize photon fluence across the fan beam field
c: To equalize photon fluence across the fan beam field
To remove low-energy photons from the primary x-ray beam
To collimate the beam to the desired slice thickness used
To reduce scattered radiation from reaching detector array
ct.e: The bowtie filter is thicker at the periphery and thinner at the center, compensating for the typically thinner patient edges to equalize detector signal across the fan beam.

ct.q: Which of the following best describes the partial volume artifact in CT imaging?
ct.a: Averaging of different tissue densities within one voxel
c: Averaging of different tissue densities within one voxel
Streaks caused by high-density metallic implant material
Concentric ring patterns from faulty detector elements
Blurring caused by involuntary patient motion during scan
ct.e: Partial volume artifact occurs when multiple tissue types occupy a single voxel, and their attenuation values are averaged, creating inaccurate CT numbers at boundaries.

ct.q: What is the effect of increasing pitch on radiation dose in helical CT scanning?
ct.a: Radiation dose decreases as pitch increases
c: Radiation dose decreases as pitch increases
Radiation dose increases as pitch increases
Radiation dose is independent of pitch value
Radiation dose first rises then decreases
ct.e: Increasing pitch means the table moves faster per rotation, reducing overlap of radiation exposure. Dose is inversely proportional to pitch when mA is held constant.

ct.q: What is CTDIvol and how is it calculated from CTDIw?
ct.a: CTDIvol equals CTDIw divided by the pitch factor
c: CTDIvol equals CTDIw divided by the pitch factor
CTDIvol equals CTDIw multiplied by the pitch factor
CTDIvol equals CTDIw multiplied by the scan length
CTDIvol equals CTDIw divided by the tube current
ct.e: CTDIvol accounts for gaps or overlaps in helical scanning by dividing CTDIw by the pitch. This gives the average dose within the scanned volume.

ct.q: In dual-energy CT, what physical basis allows material decomposition?
ct.a: Different energy dependence of photoelectric and Compton interactions
c: Different energy dependence of photoelectric and Compton interactions
Variation in Rayleigh scattering cross-sections with material type
Differences in pair production thresholds among various tissues
Changes in Bremsstrahlung yield with varying atomic number values
ct.e: Dual-energy CT exploits the fact that photoelectric absorption depends strongly on atomic number and energy, while Compton scattering is relatively energy-independent.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the 4D-CT scan in radiation therapy planning?
ct.a: To capture tumor motion throughout the respiratory cycle phases
c: To capture tumor motion throughout the respiratory cycle phases
To improve spatial resolution beyond standard CT capabilities
To reduce metal artifacts from implanted fiducial markers
To enable dual-energy tissue characterization of tumors
ct.e: 4D-CT acquires images correlated with respiratory phase, allowing visualization of tumor and organ motion for defining treatment margins in thoracic and abdominal treatments.

ct.q: What is the formula for calculating the Hounsfield unit of a material?
ct.a: HU = 1000 x (mu_tissue - mu_water) / mu_water
c: HU = 1000 x (mu_tissue - mu_water) / mu_water
HU = 1000 x (mu_tissue + mu_water) / mu_water
HU = 1000 x (mu_water - mu_tissue) / mu_tissue
HU = 1000 x (mu_tissue - mu_water) / mu_bone
ct.e: The Hounsfield unit is defined relative to water attenuation. This formula yields 0 for water and -1000 for air, establishing the standard CT number scale.

ct.q: Which factor has the greatest effect on CT spatial resolution in the scan plane?
ct.a: Focal spot size and detector aperture width
c: Focal spot size and detector aperture width
Tube current and exposure time per rotation
Patient centering position on the scan table
Scan field of view and display matrix size
ct.e: In-plane spatial resolution is primarily determined by the focal spot size and detector aperture, which define the minimum resolvable object size.

ct.q: What is SSDE in CT dosimetry?
ct.a: Size-Specific Dose Estimate based on patient dimensions
c: Size-Specific Dose Estimate based on patient dimensions
Standard Scanner Dose Evaluation for all protocols
Scatter-to-Signal Detection Efficiency of detectors
System Sensitivity Dose Equivalent for calibration
ct.e: SSDE adjusts CTDIvol using patient size-dependent conversion factors, providing a more accurate dose estimate for individual patients.

ct.q: What is the typical gantry rotation time of a modern high-speed CT scanner?
ct.a: 0.25 to 0.35 seconds per full rotation
c: 0.25 to 0.35 seconds per full rotation
1.0 to 2.0 seconds per full rotation
5.0 to 8.0 seconds per full rotation
0.01 to 0.05 seconds per full rotation
ct.e: Modern CT scanners achieve rotation times as fast as 0.25 seconds, enabling cardiac CT and reducing motion artifacts in clinical imaging.

ct.q: Which component of the CT system converts x-ray photons to visible light in solid-state detectors?
ct.a: Scintillator crystal layer in the detector
c: Scintillator crystal layer in the detector
Photomultiplier tube behind the collimator
Anti-scatter grid placed before detectors
High-voltage anode inside the x-ray tube
ct.e: In solid-state detectors, the scintillator absorbs x-rays and converts them to visible light detected by photodiodes.

ct.q: What is the primary advantage of iterative reconstruction over filtered back projection?
ct.a: Reduced image noise at equivalent or lower radiation dose
c: Reduced image noise at equivalent or lower radiation dose
Faster reconstruction speed with less computing required
Improved spatial resolution in all three imaging planes
Elimination of all metallic artifact streak patterns
ct.e: Iterative reconstruction uses statistical modeling and repeated refinement to reduce noise, enabling diagnostic quality images at lower doses compared to FBP.

ct.q: In cone-beam CT, what is the primary source of image quality degradation compared to diagnostic CT?
ct.a: Increased scatter radiation reaching the flat panel detector
c: Increased scatter radiation reaching the flat panel detector
Higher radiation dose delivered to the patient per image
Use of a rotating anode tube with a larger focal spot
Limited number of detector rows available for acquisition
ct.e: CBCT uses a wide cone beam that generates significantly more scatter than the narrow fan beam of diagnostic CT, degrading contrast resolution.

ct.q: What is the typical Hounsfield unit range for fat tissue in CT imaging?
ct.e: Fat tissue typically measures between -50 and -100 HU due to its lower density and atomic number compared to water and soft tissue.

ct.q: How does increasing kVp affect CT image contrast between soft tissues?
ct.a: Contrast between soft tissues decreases with higher kVp
c: Contrast between soft tissues decreases with higher kVp
Contrast between soft tissues increases with higher kVp
Contrast between soft tissues remains unchanged by kVp
Contrast first increases then decreases at very high kVp
ct.e: Higher kVp reduces the proportion of photoelectric interactions relative to Compton, reducing attenuation differences between soft tissues.

ct.q: What phantom is used for the ACR CT accreditation quality assurance program?
ct.a: ACR CT accreditation phantom with multiple test modules
c: ACR CT accreditation phantom with multiple test modules
Catphan 600 phantom with contrast resolution inserts
AAPM TG-233 dose verification cylindrical phantom unit
Gammex tissue characterization multi-rod phantom model
ct.e: The ACR CT accreditation phantom contains modules for testing CT number accuracy, low contrast resolution, spatial resolution, and uniformity.

ct.q: What is the relationship between slice thickness and partial volume artifacts?
ct.a: Thicker slices increase partial volume artifact severity
c: Thicker slices increase partial volume artifact severity
Thinner slices increase partial volume artifact severity
Slice thickness has no effect on partial volume artifacts
Only helical scans produce any partial volume artifacts
ct.e: Thicker slices include more tissue types within a single voxel, increasing the averaging effect that causes partial volume artifacts.

ct.q: In a CT x-ray tube, what is the purpose of the rotating anode?
ct.a: To distribute heat over a larger area on the target
c: To distribute heat over a larger area on the target
To generate a more uniform x-ray beam spectrum output
To reduce the effective focal spot size for resolution
To increase the x-ray tube output efficiency percentage
ct.e: The rotating anode spreads the electron beam impact over a larger area, allowing higher power loading without melting the target material.

ct.q: What is the CTDIw formula in terms of CTDI100 measurements?
ct.a: CTDIw = (1/3)CTDI_center + (2/3)CTDI_periphery
c: CTDIw = (1/3)CTDI_center + (2/3)CTDI_periphery
CTDIw = (2/3)CTDI_center + (1/3)CTDI_periphery
CTDIw = (1/2)CTDI_center + (1/2)CTDI_periphery
CTDIw = (1/4)CTDI_center + (3/4)CTDI_periphery
ct.e: CTDIw is a weighted average using one-third of the center measurement and two-thirds of the peripheral measurement.

ct.q: Which tube current modulation approach adjusts mA in the x-y plane based on patient shape?
ct.a: Angular (rotational) tube current modulation method
c: Angular (rotational) tube current modulation method
Longitudinal (z-axis) tube current modulation method
Temporal tube current modulation gating approach
Spectral tube current modulation switching mode
ct.e: Angular modulation adjusts mA during each rotation based on the patient's cross-sectional shape.

ct.q: What is photon starvation artifact in CT imaging?
ct.a: Severe noise and streaks from insufficient photons through dense regions
c: Severe noise and streaks from insufficient photons through dense regions
Overexposure artifacts from excessive tube current in thin areas
Ring artifacts caused by photon pile-up in rapid detector rows
Motion blur resulting from insufficient temporal sampling rate
ct.e: Photon starvation occurs when very few photons reach detectors through highly attenuating paths, causing severe noise and streak artifacts.

ct.q: What is the primary advantage of photon-counting CT detectors over conventional energy-integrating detectors?
ct.a: Ability to measure individual photon energies for spectral imaging
c: Ability to measure individual photon energies for spectral imaging
Significantly faster gantry rotation speed during CT acquisition
Much larger detector coverage along the z-axis direction
Lower manufacturing cost compared to scintillator detectors
ct.e: Photon-counting detectors count individual x-ray photons and measure their energy, enabling intrinsic spectral imaging and improved dose efficiency.

ct.q: What type of interpolation is used to generate planar data from helical CT raw data?
ct.a: Z-axis interpolation of helical projection data sets
c: Z-axis interpolation of helical projection data sets
Fourier domain interpolation of frequency components
Angular interpolation across different fan beam angles
Radial interpolation between adjacent detector channels
ct.e: Helical CT requires z-interpolation to estimate planar projection data at each slice position from the continuous helical data.

ct.q: In CT-to-density calibration for treatment planning, what does the calibration curve relate?
ct.a: CT Hounsfield units to relative electron density values
c: CT Hounsfield units to relative electron density values
Detector signal intensity to absolute photon flux rate
Reconstruction kernel type to spatial resolution values
Scanner rotation speed to temporal resolution measures
ct.e: The CT-to-density calibration curve maps HU values to relative electron density, needed for accurate dose calculations in treatment planning.

ct.q: What is the typical spatial resolution of a modern diagnostic CT scanner?
ct.a: 0.5 to 0.6 mm at standard reconstruction settings
c: 0.5 to 0.6 mm at standard reconstruction settings
0.01 to 0.05 mm at standard reconstruction settings
2.0 to 3.0 mm at standard reconstruction settings
5.0 to 10.0 mm at standard reconstruction settings
ct.e: Modern diagnostic CT scanners typically achieve in-plane spatial resolution of 0.5-0.6 mm using standard reconstruction kernels.

ct.q: How does scatter radiation affect CT number accuracy in cone-beam CT?
ct.a: Scatter causes CT numbers to be less accurate with cupping artifacts
c: Scatter causes CT numbers to be less accurate with cupping artifacts
Scatter improves CT number accuracy by adding more signal data
Scatter has minimal effect on CT number accuracy measurements
Scatter only affects spatial resolution not CT number accuracy
ct.e: Scatter adds a low-frequency signal to projections, reducing contrast and causing cupping artifacts that make CT numbers inaccurate.

ct.q: What is the purpose of an anti-scatter grid in CT detectors?
ct.a: To reduce scattered photons from reaching detector elements
c: To reduce scattered photons from reaching detector elements
To collimate the primary beam to the correct slice width
To filter low-energy photons from the x-ray beam output
To distribute heat load evenly across the detector array
ct.e: Anti-scatter grids placed between detector elements absorb scattered photons traveling at oblique angles, improving contrast resolution.

ct.q: What is the primary purpose of using a lower kVp for pediatric CT scanning?
ct.a: To reduce radiation dose while maintaining adequate contrast
c: To reduce radiation dose while maintaining adequate contrast
To increase spatial resolution in smaller patient anatomy
To eliminate all motion artifacts in pediatric patients
To improve CT number accuracy for bone density scans
ct.e: Lower kVp reduces dose and can improve iodine contrast due to increased photoelectric effect, while maintaining image quality in smaller patients.

ct.q: In CT perfusion imaging, what parameter is derived from the maximum slope of the time-density curve?
ct.a: Blood flow rate in the tissue of interest region
c: Blood flow rate in the tissue of interest region
Blood-brain barrier permeability surface product
Mean transit time through the capillary bed area
Total blood volume within the tissue region scanned
ct.e: The maximum slope method relates the maximum slope of tissue enhancement to blood flow, using the arterial input function as reference.

ct.q: What is the main disadvantage of using a sharp (high-resolution) reconstruction kernel?
ct.a: Increased image noise compared to smooth kernels used
c: Increased image noise compared to smooth kernels used
Decreased spatial resolution in the reconstructed images
Longer reconstruction time with no image quality benefit
Introduction of ring artifacts in the reconstructed data
ct.e: Sharp kernels enhance high-frequency information but also amplify high-frequency noise, resulting in noisier images compared to smooth kernels.

ct.q: How is the scan field of view (SFOV) different from the display field of view (DFOV)?
ct.a: SFOV defines the maximum reconstruction area; DFOV selects the displayed portion
c: SFOV defines the maximum reconstruction area; DFOV selects the displayed portion
SFOV controls detector collimation width; DFOV controls the slice thickness
SFOV determines the tube current used; DFOV controls the image noise level
SFOV and DFOV are identical parameters with different naming conventions
ct.e: SFOV determines the maximum reconstructable area, while DFOV selects what portion of the image is displayed and its pixel size.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the pre-patient collimator in a CT scanner?
ct.a: To define the x-ray beam width along the z-axis direction
c: To define the x-ray beam width along the z-axis direction
To shape the beam intensity profile across the fan angle
To remove scattered photons generated within the patient
To modulate tube current based on patient attenuation
ct.e: The pre-patient collimator restricts the x-ray beam width in the z-direction, determining the total beam collimation.

ct.q: In dual-energy CT using rapid kVp switching, what is the typical switching speed?
ct.a: Sub-millisecond switching between high and low kVp
c: Sub-millisecond switching between high and low kVp
Switching every 5-10 seconds during a full rotation
One full rotation at each energy level sequentially
Switching every 100-200 milliseconds during scanning
ct.e: Rapid kVp switching systems alternate between high and low kVp in sub-millisecond intervals during a single rotation.

ct.q: What is the maximum CT number typically displayed on a standard 12-bit CT scale?
ct.e: A 12-bit CT scale has 4096 levels. With -1024 as the minimum, the maximum is +3071 HU.

ct.q: What is the primary purpose of CT number calibration using a water phantom?
ct.a: To verify that water measures 0 HU and noise is within tolerance
c: To verify that water measures 0 HU and noise is within tolerance
To calibrate the detector gain for maximum spatial resolution
To determine the optimal kVp for each clinical CT protocol
To measure the focal spot size of the x-ray tube target
ct.e: Water phantom scans verify CT number accuracy (water = 0 HU), assess noise levels, and check uniformity as part of daily CT QA.

ct.q: What is the typical anode material used in CT x-ray tubes?
ct.a: Tungsten or tungsten-rhenium alloy target
c: Tungsten or tungsten-rhenium alloy target
Copper or copper-beryllium alloy target
Molybdenum or rhodium pure metal target
Aluminum or aluminum-zinc alloy target
ct.e: CT tubes use tungsten or tungsten-rhenium alloy anodes because tungsten has a high atomic number for efficient x-ray production and a high melting point for heat tolerance.

ct.q: What is the effect of reducing slice thickness on CT image noise?
ct.a: Noise increases as slice thickness decreases
c: Noise increases as slice thickness decreases
Noise decreases as slice thickness decreases
Noise is unaffected by slice thickness changes
Noise first decreases then increases sharply
ct.e: Thinner slices contain fewer photons per voxel, resulting in higher quantum noise. Noise is inversely proportional to the square root of slice thickness.

ct.q: What is the Nyquist frequency and its significance in CT spatial resolution?
ct.a: The maximum spatial frequency that can be sampled without aliasing
c: The maximum spatial frequency that can be sampled without aliasing
The minimum contrast difference detectable by the CT scanner
The optimal tube current for maintaining acceptable noise level
The frequency of gantry rotation during helical acquisition
ct.e: The Nyquist frequency equals 1/(2 x sampling interval) and represents the highest spatial frequency that can be accurately represented without aliasing artifacts.

ct.q: Which CT quality assurance test checks for uniformity of CT numbers across the image?
ct.a: Water phantom uniformity test measuring center vs periphery
c: Water phantom uniformity test measuring center vs periphery
Line pair phantom test for spatial resolution verification
Low contrast module test with small density differences
Slice thickness measurement using ramp or bead phantom
ct.e: The uniformity test measures CT numbers at center and peripheral ROIs of a water phantom, checking that variation stays within acceptable limits (typically 5 HU).

ct.q: What is the function of the data acquisition system (DAS) in a CT scanner?
ct.a: To convert analog detector signals to digital data for processing
c: To convert analog detector signals to digital data for processing
To generate the x-ray beam at the specified tube parameters
To physically rotate the gantry at the programmed speed rate
To position the patient table at the correct scan location
ct.e: The DAS amplifies, multiplexes, and digitizes the analog electrical signals from the detectors, preparing the raw data for reconstruction.

ct.q: In 4D-CT, what external signal is most commonly used to sort images into respiratory phases?
ct.a: An external respiratory surrogate such as abdominal belt or marker
c: An external respiratory surrogate such as abdominal belt or marker
ECG signal from electrodes placed on the patient's chest area
Blood oxygen saturation measured by a pulse oximeter device
Acoustic microphone signal detecting the patient breathing sounds
ct.e: 4D-CT typically uses an external respiratory monitoring device (e.g., RPM system, bellows belt) to correlate image acquisition with respiratory phase for sorting.

ct.q: What is the primary purpose of the extended CT number range (e.g., extended HU) in some scanners?
ct.a: To accurately represent very dense materials like metal implants
c: To accurately represent very dense materials like metal implants
To improve low contrast resolution in soft tissue imaging
To reduce image noise in standard diagnostic CT protocols
To enable faster reconstruction of large volume CT datasets
ct.e: Extended HU scales (beyond the standard 12-bit range) can represent materials denser than bone, such as metal implants, without truncation artifacts.

ct.q: What is the primary limitation of xenon gas detectors compared to solid-state detectors?
ct.a: Lower detection efficiency and geometric efficiency overall
c: Lower detection efficiency and geometric efficiency overall
Higher manufacturing cost for the detector array assembly
Greater susceptibility to temperature-related drift errors
Incompatibility with multi-slice CT scanner architectures
ct.e: Xenon gas detectors have lower quantum detection efficiency (~60-70%) compared to solid-state (~98-99%) and require pressure vessels that limit geometric design flexibility.

ct.q: How does patient centering in the gantry affect CT image quality and dose?
ct.a: Off-centering increases noise and may affect dose modulation accuracy
c: Off-centering increases noise and may affect dose modulation accuracy
Off-centering improves spatial resolution due to magnification effect
Patient centering has no measurable effect on image quality data
Off-centering only affects the display but not actual image data
ct.e: Off-centering causes the bowtie filter to mismatch the patient profile, increasing noise and potentially causing ATCM to deliver incorrect mA, increasing dose.

ct.q: What is the typical energy pair used in dual-energy CT scanning?
ct.a: 80 kVp and 140 kVp tube potential settings
c: 80 kVp and 140 kVp tube potential settings
40 kVp and 60 kVp tube potential settings
120 kVp and 200 kVp tube potential settings
50 kVp and 80 kVp tube potential settings
ct.e: Dual-energy CT most commonly uses 80 kVp and 140 kVp (or sometimes 100/140 kVp) to maximize spectral separation for material decomposition.

ct.q: What is the window width and level typically used for viewing lung tissue on CT?
ct.a: Window width 1500 HU, window level -600 HU
c: Window width 1500 HU, window level -600 HU
Window width 400 HU, window level +40 HU
Window width 2500 HU, window level +500 HU
Window width 80 HU, window level +40 HU
ct.e: Lung windows use a wide window width (~1500 HU) centered at approximately -600 HU to display the full range of aerated lung parenchyma.

ct.q: What does the term 'effective mAs' refer to in helical CT scanning?
ct.a: The tube current-time product divided by the pitch value
c: The tube current-time product divided by the pitch value
The tube current multiplied by the gantry rotation time
The total scan time multiplied by the average tube current
The tube current divided by the number of detector rows
ct.e: Effective mAs = mAs/pitch, accounting for the dose effect of pitch. It normalizes the mAs to represent the actual radiation contribution per slice.

ct.q: Which CT artifact results from patient movement during data acquisition?
ct.a: Motion artifact causing blurring or double contours in images
c: Motion artifact causing blurring or double contours in images
Ring artifact producing concentric circular patterns in images
Beam hardening causing dark bands between dense structures
Cone beam artifact producing shading near the scan edges
ct.e: Patient motion during CT acquisition causes inconsistent projection data, resulting in blurring, streaking, or double contours in the reconstructed image.

ct.q: What is the role of the slip ring in modern CT scanners?
ct.a: To allow continuous gantry rotation for helical scanning mode
c: To allow continuous gantry rotation for helical scanning mode
To transfer cooling fluid to the x-ray tube during scanning
To maintain electrical isolation between the patient and gantry
To control the speed of table movement during acquisition
ct.e: Slip rings provide continuous electrical contact between the rotating gantry and stationary components, enabling continuous rotation needed for helical CT acquisition.

ct.q: What is the difference between prospective and retrospective ECG gating in cardiac CT?
ct.a: Prospective triggers acquisition at set cardiac phases; retrospective acquires continuously
c: Prospective triggers acquisition at set cardiac phases; retrospective acquires continuously
Prospective uses higher dose than retrospective gating in all cardiac phases
Retrospective triggers acquisition at set phases; prospective acquires continuously
There is no practical difference between prospective and retrospective gating
ct.e: Prospective gating triggers the x-ray beam only during selected cardiac phases, reducing dose. Retrospective gating acquires data continuously and selects phases afterward.

ct.q: How does the reconstruction matrix size affect pixel size in a CT image?
ct.a: Pixel size equals the DFOV divided by the matrix dimension
c: Pixel size equals the DFOV divided by the matrix dimension
Pixel size is determined solely by the detector element size
Pixel size equals the matrix dimension divided by DFOV
Pixel size is fixed regardless of matrix or field of view
ct.e: Pixel size = DFOV/matrix size. A 512 x 512 matrix with a 25 cm DFOV gives 0.49 mm pixels. Larger matrices yield smaller pixels.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the Gammex RMI phantom in radiation therapy CT simulation?
ct.a: To establish the CT number to electron density calibration curve
c: To establish the CT number to electron density calibration curve
To measure the spatial resolution of the CT scanner system
To verify the accuracy of the CT laser alignment system
To assess the low contrast detectability of the CT scanner
ct.e: The Gammex phantom contains inserts of known electron density, allowing creation of the HU-to-electron-density calibration curve used in treatment planning.

ct.q: What is the typical noise level (standard deviation in HU) for a properly calibrated CT scanner in water?
ct.a: 3 to 7 HU standard deviation at standard protocols
c: 3 to 7 HU standard deviation at standard protocols
0.1 to 0.5 HU standard deviation at standard protocols
20 to 30 HU standard deviation at standard protocols
50 to 100 HU standard deviation at standard protocols
ct.e: A well-calibrated CT scanner typically shows noise of 3-7 HU (standard deviation) in a water phantom at standard clinical scan parameters.

ct.q: What causes the cupping artifact in CT images of uniform cylindrical phantoms?
ct.a: Beam hardening causing lower HU values at the center of the object
c: Beam hardening causing lower HU values at the center of the object
Detector saturation causing higher HU values at the periphery
Scattered radiation causing higher HU values at the center area
Partial volume effects at the edges of the cylindrical phantom
ct.e: Beam hardening preferentially removes low-energy photons in longer path lengths through the center, making the beam appear less attenuating and reducing central CT numbers.

ct.q: What reconstruction approach uses deep learning neural networks to reduce noise in CT images?
ct.a: AI-based or deep learning reconstruction algorithms
c: AI-based or deep learning reconstruction algorithms
Standard filtered back projection with ramp filtering
Simple algebraic reconstruction technique iterations
Maximum intensity projection rendering technique
ct.e: Deep learning reconstruction uses trained neural networks to reduce noise while preserving spatial resolution and image texture, representing the latest advance in CT reconstruction.

ct.q: What is the approximate Hounsfield unit value of soft tissue (muscle) in CT?
ct.a: +40 to +60 HU for typical muscle tissue
c: +40 to +60 HU for typical muscle tissue
+200 to +400 HU for typical muscle tissue
-50 to -20 HU for typical muscle tissue
+500 to +700 HU for typical muscle tissue
ct.e: Muscle tissue typically has CT numbers in the range of +40 to +60 HU, reflecting its slightly higher density than water.

ct.q: In CT dose optimization, what is the ALARA principle?
ct.a: As Low As Reasonably Achievable radiation dose for diagnostic quality
c: As Low As Reasonably Achievable radiation dose for diagnostic quality
Always Limit Automatic Reconstruction Algorithm dose calculations
Adjusted Linear Attenuation Ratio Assessment for dose tracking
Automatic Longitudinal Axis Reduction Algorithm for dose control
ct.e: ALARA means using the lowest radiation dose that still provides diagnostic quality images, balancing image quality with patient radiation protection.

ct.q: What is the effect of pitch greater than 1 on image quality in helical CT?
ct.a: Potential for increased slice sensitivity profile broadening occurs
c: Potential for increased slice sensitivity profile broadening occurs
Spatial resolution improves significantly in all directions
Image noise decreases substantially compared to pitch of one
CT number accuracy improves with higher pitch values used
ct.e: Pitch >1 can broaden the slice sensitivity profile (effective slice thickness), potentially reducing z-axis resolution and creating interpolation artifacts.

ct.q: What dose metric is displayed on the CT console dose report for each scan series?
ct.a: CTDIvol and DLP values for each acquisition series
c: CTDIvol and DLP values for each acquisition series
Entrance skin dose and organ dose for all tissues
Effective dose in millisieverts for the entire exam
Absorbed dose to each individual organ being scanned
ct.e: CT scanners display CTDIvol (mGy) and DLP (mGy-cm) on the dose report. These are standardized metrics but not direct patient dose measurements.

ct.q: What is the primary advantage of a dual-source CT scanner for dual-energy imaging?
ct.a: Simultaneous acquisition at two energies with independent tube control
c: Simultaneous acquisition at two energies with independent tube control
Lower radiation dose compared to all other dual-energy approaches
Higher spatial resolution than single-source CT scanning systems
Elimination of all beam hardening artifacts in the final images
ct.e: Dual-source CT uses two tube-detector pairs at different kVp settings, acquiring simultaneous dual-energy data with independent control of mA for each energy.

ct.q: What is the ion chamber used for CTDI measurements called?
ct.a: 100 mm long pencil ionization chamber
c: 100 mm long pencil ionization chamber
Farmer-type thimble ionization chamber
Parallel plate ionization chamber device
Free-air ionization chamber apparatus
ct.e: CTDI measurements use a 100 mm long pencil ionization chamber placed in a CTDI phantom to integrate the dose profile along the z-axis.

ct.q: What is the typical effective dose from a standard adult abdomen-pelvis CT scan?
ct.a: Approximately 8 to 15 mSv effective dose
c: Approximately 8 to 15 mSv effective dose
Approximately 0.01 to 0.05 mSv effective dose
Approximately 50 to 100 mSv effective dose
