Approximately 0.5 to 1.0 mSv effective dose
ct.e: A standard adult abdomen-pelvis CT delivers approximately 8-15 mSv effective dose, depending on the protocol, patient size, and scanner technology.

ct.q: What is the penumbra effect in CT and how does it relate to dose?
ct.a: Dose beyond the nominal beam width from geometric x-ray source size
c: Dose beyond the nominal beam width from geometric x-ray source size
Increased contrast at tissue boundaries due to edge enhancement
Reduced noise at the center of the scan field of view area
Improved spatial resolution from overlapping detector elements
ct.e: The penumbra is the region of reduced intensity beyond the collimated beam edge, caused by the finite focal spot size. It contributes to wasted dose.

ct.q: How does tube voltage (kVp) affect radiation dose in CT scanning?
ct.a: Dose increases approximately as the square of the kVp change
c: Dose increases approximately as the square of the kVp change
Dose increases linearly with the kVp value selected
Dose decreases as kVp increases due to higher penetration
Dose is independent of the kVp setting used for scanning
ct.e: CT dose is approximately proportional to kVp squared. Reducing from 120 to 80 kVp decreases dose by roughly (80/120)^2 = 44% of the original.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the over-beaming correction in multi-detector CT?
ct.a: To account for dose from x-rays beyond the active detector width edges
c: To account for dose from x-rays beyond the active detector width edges
To correct for variations in tube output during gantry rotation
To compensate for patient motion during the CT data acquisition
To adjust CT numbers for beam hardening through dense anatomy
ct.e: Over-beaming refers to the x-ray beam extending beyond the active detector rows due to penumbra, wasting dose. Wider collimation reduces the relative fraction wasted.

ct.q: In CBCT used for image-guided radiation therapy, what is the typical imaging dose per scan?
ct.a: Approximately 1 to 3 cGy per CBCT acquisition
c: Approximately 1 to 3 cGy per CBCT acquisition
Approximately 50 to 100 cGy per CBCT acquisition
Approximately 0.001 to 0.01 cGy per CBCT acquisition
Approximately 10 to 30 cGy per CBCT acquisition
ct.e: A typical CBCT scan on a linear accelerator delivers approximately 1-3 cGy, which should be considered in the overall treatment dose accounting.

ct.q: What is the Feldkamp-Davis-Kress (FDK) algorithm used for?
ct.a: Cone-beam CT reconstruction from circular orbit data sets
c: Cone-beam CT reconstruction from circular orbit data sets
Fan-beam CT iterative reconstruction with noise penalty
Dual-energy material decomposition from spectral data
4D-CT respiratory phase sorting and image binning
ct.e: The FDK algorithm is an approximate 3D reconstruction method for cone-beam geometry using a single circular orbit, widely used in CBCT systems.

ct.q: What is the significance of the modulation transfer function (MTF) in CT imaging?
ct.a: It quantifies spatial resolution as a function of spatial frequency
c: It quantifies spatial resolution as a function of spatial frequency
It measures the radiation dose output per unit tube current
It determines the optimal window and level display settings
It calculates the contrast-to-noise ratio of tissue objects
ct.e: The MTF describes how well the CT system transfers contrast at different spatial frequencies, providing a comprehensive measure of spatial resolution performance.

ct.q: What is the typical number of views (projections) acquired per rotation in modern CT?
ct.a: Approximately 1000 to 2000 views per full rotation
c: Approximately 1000 to 2000 views per full rotation
Approximately 10 to 50 views per full rotation scan
Approximately 50 to 100 views per full rotation scan
Approximately 10000 to 50000 views per rotation scan
ct.e: Modern CT scanners acquire approximately 1000-2000 angular projections per rotation to provide sufficient sampling for high-quality image reconstruction.

ct.q: How does the reconstruction field of view affect pixel size in CT images?
ct.a: Smaller DFOV results in smaller pixel size and better displayed detail
c: Smaller DFOV results in smaller pixel size and better displayed detail
Smaller DFOV results in larger pixel size with reduced noise level
DFOV has no direct relationship to the pixel size in CT images
Larger DFOV always provides superior spatial resolution results
ct.e: Pixel size = DFOV/matrix. Reducing DFOV with the same matrix size decreases pixel size, potentially displaying finer detail if the system resolution supports it.

ct.q: What is the primary purpose of using organ-based tube current modulation in CT?
ct.a: To reduce dose to radiosensitive organs like breast and thyroid tissue
c: To reduce dose to radiosensitive organs like breast and thyroid tissue
To increase spatial resolution over specific anatomical organ regions
To improve CT number accuracy for organs with high blood flow
To eliminate all motion artifacts in moving organs like the heart
ct.e: Organ-based TCM reduces tube current when the tube faces radiosensitive organs (e.g., anterior for breasts) while compensating in other angular positions.

ct.q: What is the approximate CT number for iodinated contrast material at typical diagnostic concentrations?
ct.a: +100 to +300 HU depending on concentration and kVp used
c: +100 to +300 HU depending on concentration and kVp used
-100 to -50 HU depending on concentration and kVp used
+1000 to +2000 HU depending on concentration used
+30 to +50 HU depending on concentration and kVp used
ct.e: Iodinated contrast at diagnostic concentrations typically enhances tissues to +100 to +300 HU, with higher enhancement at lower kVp due to the iodine k-edge.

ct.q: What is the main advantage of a wider detector coverage (more rows) in CT?
ct.a: Faster volume coverage and reduced scan time for patients
c: Faster volume coverage and reduced scan time for patients
Higher in-plane spatial resolution per individual image
Lower radiation dose per individual CT slice acquired
Better low contrast resolution in each reconstructed image
ct.e: Wider detector coverage (e.g., 320 rows covering 16 cm) allows faster volume scanning, whole-organ coverage in a single rotation, and reduced motion artifacts.

ct.q: How does the CT number of iodine change when scanning at 80 kVp compared to 120 kVp?
ct.a: Iodine CT number increases at 80 kVp due to the k-edge effect
c: Iodine CT number increases at 80 kVp due to the k-edge effect
Iodine CT number decreases at 80 kVp due to less penetration
Iodine CT number remains unchanged regardless of kVp value
Iodine CT number becomes negative at lower kVp settings used
ct.e: At 80 kVp, more photons have energies near iodine's k-edge (33.2 keV), increasing photoelectric absorption and thus increasing the CT number of iodine.

ct.q: What is streak artifact in CT and what is its most common cause?
ct.a: Linear dark or bright lines caused by metal or high-density objects
c: Linear dark or bright lines caused by metal or high-density objects
Circular patterns caused by miscalibrated detector elements
Blurred edges caused by involuntary respiratory patient motion
Wavy distortion caused by electromagnetic interference signals
ct.e: Streak artifacts appear as bright or dark lines radiating from high-density objects (metals, dense bone) due to beam hardening, photon starvation, or nonlinear partial volume effects.

ct.q: What is the purpose of noise index or quality reference mAs in automatic exposure control?
ct.a: To set the target image quality level for the dose modulation system
c: To set the target image quality level for the dose modulation system
To define the maximum tube current allowed during CT scanning
To specify the reconstruction kernel used for image processing
To determine the pitch factor for helical acquisition protocols
ct.e: Noise index (GE) or quality reference mAs (Siemens) defines the desired image quality level, and the AEC system adjusts mA to achieve that target across varying patient anatomy.

ct.q: What is the effect of increasing reconstruction slice thickness on noise?
ct.a: Noise decreases because more photons contribute to each thicker slice
c: Noise decreases because more photons contribute to each thicker slice
Noise increases because thicker slices have more partial volume
Noise remains constant regardless of reconstruction slice thickness
Noise increases proportionally with the reconstruction slice width
ct.e: Reconstructing thicker slices averages more data, increasing the effective number of photons per voxel and reducing quantum noise.

ct.q: In CT simulation, what is the purpose of external laser alignment systems?
ct.a: To define the isocenter and set reference marks on the patient skin
c: To define the isocenter and set reference marks on the patient skin
To verify CT number calibration accuracy during simulation scans
To monitor patient breathing for 4D-CT acquisition protocols
To measure the radiation dose delivered during simulation scan
ct.e: External lasers in CT simulation define the coordinate system and allow placement of reference marks on the patient for reproducible positioning during treatment.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the z-axis dose efficiency metric in multi-detector CT?
ct.a: To measure what fraction of the beam contributes to useful image data
c: To measure what fraction of the beam contributes to useful image data
To calculate the total patient dose from a complete CT examination
To determine the optimal pitch for helical scanning acquisition
To assess the spatial resolution along the patient long axis
ct.e: Z-axis dose efficiency measures the ratio of the dose profile contributing to imaging versus total dose delivered, accounting for penumbra waste in MDCT.

ct.q: What is the advantage of using tin filtration with the high-energy tube in dual-source dual-energy CT?
ct.a: It increases spectral separation between the two x-ray energy spectra
c: It increases spectral separation between the two x-ray energy spectra
It reduces the overall cost of the dual-source CT scanner system
It eliminates the need for a second x-ray tube in the gantry
It increases patient dose to improve image quality at both energies
ct.e: Tin filtration on the high-kVp tube hardens the spectrum, increasing the mean energy separation between the two beams and improving material decomposition accuracy.

ct.q: What is the relationship between mAs and radiation dose in CT?
ct.a: Radiation dose is directly proportional to the mAs value used
c: Radiation dose is directly proportional to the mAs value used
Radiation dose is inversely proportional to the mAs value
Radiation dose changes with the square of the mAs value
Radiation dose is independent of the mAs value selected
ct.e: CT dose (CTDI) is directly proportional to mAs. Doubling the mAs doubles the dose, making mAs reduction a straightforward dose optimization strategy.

ct.q: What is the primary purpose of using iterative metal artifact reduction (MAR) algorithms?
ct.a: To reduce streak artifacts caused by metallic implants in the body
c: To reduce streak artifacts caused by metallic implants in the body
To improve spatial resolution around metallic surgical instruments
To increase the CT number accuracy of metal implant materials
To eliminate all imaging artifacts in CT scans of all types
ct.e: MAR algorithms modify sinogram data in regions affected by metal, replacing corrupted projections with interpolated or iteratively refined data to reduce streaks.

ct.q: How many respiratory phases are typically binned in a standard 4D-CT acquisition?
ct.a: 10 phases representing the full respiratory breathing cycle
c: 10 phases representing the full respiratory breathing cycle
2 phases representing only inspiration and expiration
100 phases for high temporal resolution of breathing
50 phases for comprehensive respiratory motion analysis
ct.e: Standard 4D-CT typically sorts images into 10 respiratory phases (0% to 90%), each representing 10% of the breathing cycle, providing sufficient temporal sampling.

ct.q: What is the cone beam artifact in multi-detector CT and when is it most significant?
ct.a: Shading artifacts from cone angle that worsen with wider detector coverage
c: Shading artifacts from cone angle that worsen with wider detector coverage
Ring artifacts that appear only in the center of the image field
Motion artifacts specific to cardiac imaging with narrow detector
Beam hardening that occurs only in cone-beam CT systems used
ct.e: Cone beam artifacts arise because the 2D FBP algorithm assumes parallel rays, while wider detector arrays have increasingly divergent cone angles, causing shading near the scan edges.

ct.q: What is the purpose of adaptive statistical iterative reconstruction (ASIR) in CT?
ct.a: To blend iterative and FBP reconstruction for noise reduction
c: To blend iterative and FBP reconstruction for noise reduction
To perform pure iterative reconstruction without FBP input
To enhance spatial resolution beyond the detector limit
To correct for patient motion during CT data acquisition
ct.e: ASIR blends a percentage of iterative reconstruction with FBP, reducing noise while maintaining familiar image texture. Users select the blending percentage.

ct.q: What is the typical scan duration for a modern whole-body CT examination?
ct.a: Approximately 5 to 15 seconds for the full acquisition
c: Approximately 5 to 15 seconds for the full acquisition
Approximately 2 to 5 minutes for the full acquisition
Approximately 30 to 60 minutes for full acquisition
Approximately 0.1 to 0.5 seconds for full acquisition
ct.e: Modern MDCT scanners with fast rotation and wide detector coverage can complete a whole-body scan in 5-15 seconds, reducing motion artifacts.

ct.q: What is the significance of the iodine k-edge at 33.2 keV in dual-energy CT?
ct.a: It causes sharp increase in iodine attenuation exploited for material separation
c: It causes sharp increase in iodine attenuation exploited for material separation
It eliminates all beam hardening artifacts when iodine contrast is present
It allows iodine to be completely invisible at energies below 33 keV value
It reduces the radiation dose required when iodine contrast agent is used
ct.e: The iodine k-edge at 33.2 keV causes a dramatic increase in photoelectric absorption, which dual-energy CT exploits by using one energy near and one far from this edge.

ct.q: What is the purpose of performing a CT scout (topogram/scanogram) before the diagnostic scan?
ct.a: To plan scan range and enable automatic exposure control calculations
c: To plan scan range and enable automatic exposure control calculations
To provide diagnostic images equivalent to conventional radiographs
To calibrate the CT number scale before the actual scan begins
To measure the patient's respiratory rate for 4D-CT protocols
ct.e: The scout image allows the operator to define scan range and the AEC system to estimate patient attenuation for tube current modulation planning.

ct.q: In CT-to-density calibration, what happens in the region between soft tissue and bone on the calibration curve?
ct.a: A distinct change in slope occurs due to different attenuation mechanisms
c: A distinct change in slope occurs due to different attenuation mechanisms
The calibration curve remains perfectly linear across this region
CT numbers become unreliable and cannot be used for planning
The electron density values plateau and remain nearly constant
ct.e: The HU-to-density curve shows a slope change around 100 HU because Compton scattering dominates in soft tissue while photoelectric effect becomes more significant in bone.

ct.q: What is the primary disadvantage of model-based iterative reconstruction (MBIR)?
ct.a: Very long reconstruction time compared to FBP and hybrid methods
c: Very long reconstruction time compared to FBP and hybrid methods
Increased image noise compared to filtered back projection method
Inability to reconstruct images with thin slice thickness settings
Higher radiation dose requirement than standard FBP protocols
ct.e: MBIR incorporates detailed system models (optics, statistics, object) for superior image quality but requires significantly longer computation time than FBP or hybrid methods.

ct.q: What physical property does a photon-counting detector directly measure?
ct.a: The energy of each individual x-ray photon that hits the detector
c: The energy of each individual x-ray photon that hits the detector
The total integrated signal from all photons during exposure
The spatial position of scattered photons within the patient
The time delay between successive photon detection events
ct.e: Photon-counting detectors measure the energy of each individual photon using energy-discriminating thresholds, unlike energy-integrating detectors that sum all photon contributions.

ct.q: What is the standard matrix size used for CT image reconstruction?
ct.a: 512 x 512 pixels for standard clinical reconstruction
c: 512 x 512 pixels for standard clinical reconstruction
256 x 256 pixels for standard clinical reconstruction
128 x 128 pixels for standard clinical reconstruction
1024 x 1024 pixels for standard clinical reconstruction
ct.e: The standard CT reconstruction matrix is 512 x 512 pixels, though some scanners offer 768 x 768 or 1024 x 1024 for high-resolution applications.

ct.q: How does scatter affect contrast resolution in CT imaging?
ct.a: Scatter degrades contrast resolution by adding non-position-specific signal
c: Scatter degrades contrast resolution by adding non-position-specific signal
Scatter improves contrast resolution by increasing total detected signal
Scatter has no measurable effect on the contrast resolution of CT
Scatter only affects spatial resolution without changing any contrast
ct.e: Scatter adds a relatively uniform background signal to detector readings, reducing the contrast-to-noise ratio and degrading low contrast detectability.

ct.q: What is the typical CT number uniformity tolerance for a quality assurance water phantom scan?
ct.a: Within plus or minus 5 HU of center ROI across the field
c: Within plus or minus 5 HU of center ROI across the field
Within plus or minus 50 HU of center ROI across the field
Within plus or minus 0.1 HU of center ROI across field
Within plus or minus 100 HU of center ROI across field
ct.e: CT number uniformity is typically required to be within +/- 5 HU between center and peripheral ROIs in a water phantom for quality assurance.

ct.q: What is the difference between axial (sequential) and helical (spiral) CT acquisition?
ct.a: Axial acquires slice-by-slice; helical acquires with continuous table motion
c: Axial acquires slice-by-slice; helical acquires with continuous table motion
Axial uses higher dose than helical for equivalent image quality result
Helical provides thinner slices than axial mode at all settings used
There is no practical difference in image quality between the modes
ct.e: In axial mode, the table is stationary during each rotation. In helical mode, the table moves continuously during acquisition, enabling faster scanning.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the noise power spectrum (NPS) in CT image quality assessment?
ct.a: To characterize the spatial frequency distribution and magnitude of noise
c: To characterize the spatial frequency distribution and magnitude of noise
To measure the maximum spatial resolution of the CT scanner system
To determine the optimal window and level for image display use
To calculate the radiation dose delivered during a CT examination
ct.e: The NPS shows how noise is distributed across spatial frequencies, providing more complete noise characterization than standard deviation alone, especially for iterative reconstruction.

ct.q: In dual-energy CT, what is virtual non-contrast (VNC) imaging?
ct.a: Generating synthetic unenhanced images by removing iodine contribution
c: Generating synthetic unenhanced images by removing iodine contribution
Acquiring a separate non-contrast scan before the dual-energy scan
Reducing window width to minimize contrast enhancement visibility
Using a special filter to remove all contrast from raw projection data
ct.e: VNC uses dual-energy material decomposition to subtract the iodine contribution, generating synthetic unenhanced images and potentially eliminating the need for a true non-contrast scan.

ct.q: What is the typical CTDI phantom material and why is it chosen?
ct.a: Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA/acrylic) for tissue-equivalent attenuation
c: Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA/acrylic) for tissue-equivalent attenuation
Solid aluminum cylinder for high attenuation standardization values
Water-filled container for exact tissue density matching results
Polyethylene foam for low density soft tissue equivalent values
ct.e: CTDI phantoms are made of PMMA (acrylic) because its x-ray attenuation properties approximate soft tissue, and it provides a durable, reproducible phantom material.

ct.q: What is the effect of increasing the number of detector rows on over-beaming?
ct.a: More detector rows reduce the relative dose wasted from over-beaming
c: More detector rows reduce the relative dose wasted from over-beaming
More detector rows increase the relative dose wasted by over-beaming
The number of detector rows has no effect on over-beaming losses
Over-beaming only occurs in single-slice CT scanner configurations
ct.e: The penumbra size is fixed by geometry, so with more detector rows the useful beam is wider and the penumbra fraction of total beam width decreases.

ct.q: What is the purpose of using size-based CT protocols for pediatric imaging?
ct.a: To adjust technique factors based on patient weight or cross-section size
c: To adjust technique factors based on patient weight or cross-section size
To increase spatial resolution for smaller anatomical structures seen
To improve temporal resolution for faster-moving pediatric organs
To standardize all pediatric scans to a single universal protocol
ct.e: Size-based protocols adjust kVp and mAs according to patient weight or diameter, ensuring appropriate image quality while minimizing dose for each patient size.

ct.q: What is the relationship between temporal resolution and gantry rotation time in CT?
ct.a: Temporal resolution is approximately half the gantry rotation time period
c: Temporal resolution is approximately half the gantry rotation time period
Temporal resolution equals exactly the full gantry rotation time
Temporal resolution is twice the gantry rotation time period used
Temporal resolution is unrelated to the gantry rotation time used
ct.e: Using half-scan reconstruction (180 degrees + fan angle), temporal resolution is approximately half the rotation time, e.g., 0.15 s for a 0.3 s rotation.

ct.q: What is the diagnostic reference level (DRL) used for in CT dose management?
ct.a: A benchmark dose level for identifying unusually high dose CT protocols
c: A benchmark dose level for identifying unusually high dose CT protocols
The maximum legally permitted dose for any CT examination performed
The minimum dose required to produce diagnostic quality CT images
An absolute dose limit that must never be exceeded per patient
ct.e: DRLs are benchmark dose levels (typically 75th percentile of survey data) used to identify facilities or protocols with unusually high doses for investigation.

ct.q: What type of material causes the most severe metal artifacts in CT images?
ct.a: High atomic number metals like titanium, steel, or dental amalgam
c: High atomic number metals like titanium, steel, or dental amalgam
Low atomic number plastics like polyethylene or polycarbonate
Soft tissue materials with high water content such as muscle
Air-filled cavities within the patient body being scanned
ct.e: High-Z metals cause severe artifacts due to extreme beam hardening, photon starvation, and scatter, with dense metals like steel producing worse artifacts than titanium.

ct.q: In CT simulation for radiation therapy, why is a large bore scanner preferred?
ct.a: To accommodate patients in treatment positions with immobilization devices
c: To accommodate patients in treatment positions with immobilization devices
To improve spatial resolution compared to standard diagnostic scanners
To reduce radiation dose by increasing the source-to-isocenter distance
To enable dual-energy scanning for tissue characterization purposes
ct.e: Large bore CT scanners (typically 80-85 cm vs 70 cm diagnostic) accommodate patients in treatment positions with arms up and immobilization devices in place.

ct.q: What is the primary difference between third-generation and fourth-generation CT scanner geometry?
ct.a: Third-gen rotates tube and detectors; fourth-gen has a stationary detector ring
c: Third-gen rotates tube and detectors; fourth-gen has a stationary detector ring
Third-gen uses fan beam geometry; fourth-gen uses parallel beam geometry
Third-gen has solid-state detectors; fourth-gen uses only gas detectors
Third-gen is single-slice only; fourth-gen supports multi-slice scanning
ct.e: In third-generation CT, both tube and detector array rotate together. Fourth-generation has a stationary ring of detectors with only the tube rotating.

ct.q: What is the staircase (stair-step) artifact in helical CT?
ct.a: Jagged edges on reformatted images from wide collimation and high pitch
c: Jagged edges on reformatted images from wide collimation and high pitch
Concentric ring patterns from periodic detector calibration errors
Bright streaks radiating from metallic implants in the scan field
Uniform darkening of the image center from beam hardening effects
ct.e: Staircase artifacts appear in sagittal/coronal reformats when slice thickness or pitch is too large, creating jagged edges on structures oriented along the z-axis.

ct.q: What is the effect of increasing the scan field of view (SFOV) on spatial resolution?
ct.a: Larger SFOV generally reduces displayed spatial resolution per pixel
c: Larger SFOV generally reduces displayed spatial resolution per pixel
Larger SFOV improves spatial resolution across the entire image
SFOV has no relationship to spatial resolution in CT imaging
Larger SFOV improves only the z-axis spatial resolution value
ct.e: Larger SFOV with the same matrix size means larger pixels, reducing the ability to display fine detail even if the inherent resolution is unchanged.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the AAPM Task Group 204 report on SSDE?
ct.a: To provide size-specific dose estimates adjusting CTDIvol for patient size
c: To provide size-specific dose estimates adjusting CTDIvol for patient size
To standardize CT reconstruction algorithms across all scanner vendors
To establish quality assurance testing procedures for CT simulators
To define requirements for 4D-CT acquisition in radiation therapy
ct.e: AAPM TG-204 provides conversion factors based on patient effective diameter to convert CTDIvol to SSDE for more patient-specific dose estimation.

ct.q: How does dual-energy CT differentiate between calcium and iodine?
ct.a: By exploiting different energy-dependent attenuation curves of each material
c: By exploiting different energy-dependent attenuation curves of each material
By measuring the physical density of each material with CT numbers
By using the spatial distribution pattern to separate the materials
By applying motion tracking algorithms to identify moving contrast
ct.e: Calcium and iodine have different atomic numbers and k-edges, causing their attenuation to change differently with energy, enabling dual-energy material decomposition.

ct.q: What is the typical frequency of CT number accuracy checks in a clinical QA program?
ct.a: Daily or at minimum weekly verification using water phantom scans
c: Daily or at minimum weekly verification using water phantom scans
Only once per year during the annual physics survey review
Every five years as part of major equipment recertification
Monthly verification is considered the maximum needed frequency
ct.e: ACR and most QA guidelines recommend daily CT number accuracy checks using a water phantom, with more comprehensive tests performed monthly and annually.

ct.q: What is the heel effect and how does it affect CT image quality?
ct.a: Variation in x-ray intensity along the tube axis due to anode self-filtration
c: Variation in x-ray intensity along the tube axis due to anode self-filtration
Reduction in spatial resolution at the edges of the CT scan field
Increased scatter radiation near the heel of the patient's foot
Motion artifact caused by rapid gantry deceleration at scan end
ct.e: The heel effect causes non-uniform beam intensity along the anode-cathode axis due to varying path lengths through the anode. In CT, it is partially compensated by bowtie filters and calibration.

ct.q: What is the purpose of a solid water or tissue-equivalent phantom for CT QA?
ct.a: To verify CT number accuracy for materials of known density values
c: To verify CT number accuracy for materials of known density values
To measure the maximum spatial resolution of the CT scanner system
To calibrate the automatic exposure control system parameters
To assess the temporal resolution of the CT scanner acquisition
ct.e: Tissue-equivalent phantoms with known density inserts verify that the scanner accurately measures CT numbers across a clinically relevant range of densities.

ct.q: What is the primary concern regarding CT dose to children compared to adults?
ct.a: Children have higher radiation sensitivity and longer life for effects to manifest
c: Children have higher radiation sensitivity and longer life for effects to manifest
Children require higher dose than adults for adequate image quality results
Children have larger body habitus requiring more radiation penetration
CT dose to children is identical to adult dose with no increased risk
ct.e: Children are more radiosensitive than adults and have a longer remaining lifespan for potential radiation-induced effects to develop, making dose optimization critical.

ct.q: What is the purpose of iterative reconstruction in reducing metal artifacts?
ct.a: To model the physics of photon interactions with metal for better correction
c: To model the physics of photon interactions with metal for better correction
To completely remove all metallic objects from the reconstructed CT image
To increase the CT number of metal to more accurate values overall
To speed up reconstruction time for scans containing metal implants
ct.e: Iterative reconstruction can incorporate physical models of beam hardening and photon starvation near metals, iteratively correcting for these effects to reduce artifacts.

ct.q: What is the relationship between the number of projections and CT image quality?
ct.a: Insufficient projections cause view aliasing streak artifacts in images
c: Insufficient projections cause view aliasing streak artifacts in images
More projections always reduce spatial resolution in the final image
The number of projections has no effect on reconstructed image quality
Fewer projections improve contrast resolution by reducing noise level
ct.e: Too few angular projections cause view aliasing artifacts (fine streaks radiating from sharp edges). Sufficient angular sampling is needed for artifact-free reconstruction.

ct.q: What is the effective dose conversion factor used to estimate effective dose from DLP?
ct.a: Region-specific k-factors multiplied by DLP in mGy-cm units
c: Region-specific k-factors multiplied by DLP in mGy-cm units
A universal factor of 1.0 applied to DLP for all body regions
Patient weight divided by the total DLP value of the scan
CTDIvol divided by the scan length for the examination done
ct.e: Effective dose is estimated as E = k x DLP, where k is a region-specific conversion factor (e.g., 0.015 mSv/(mGy-cm) for abdomen in adults).

ct.q: What is the primary advantage of wide-area detector CT for cardiac imaging?
ct.a: Whole heart coverage in a single rotation without helical artifacts
c: Whole heart coverage in a single rotation without helical artifacts
Higher spatial resolution than standard multi-detector CT systems
Lower radiation dose than any other cardiac CT imaging technique
Faster gantry rotation speed compared to standard CT scanners
ct.e: Wide-area detectors (e.g., 16 cm coverage) can image the entire heart in one rotation, eliminating helical artifacts and reducing motion-related misregistration.

ct.q: How does the focal spot size affect the spatial resolution of a CT scanner?
ct.a: Smaller focal spot provides better spatial resolution but limits power output
c: Smaller focal spot provides better spatial resolution but limits power output
Larger focal spot always provides superior spatial resolution results
Focal spot size has no measurable impact on spatial resolution
Focal spot size only affects temporal resolution not spatial values
ct.e: A smaller focal spot reduces geometric unsharpness and improves spatial resolution, but concentrates heat on a smaller area, limiting maximum tube power.

ct.q: What is the function of adaptive collimation in helical CT scanning?
ct.a: To block unnecessary radiation at the start and end of the helical scan
c: To block unnecessary radiation at the start and end of the helical scan
To automatically adjust slice thickness during the scan acquisition
To modulate the fan beam angle based on the patient body shape
To reduce scattered radiation by adjusting the detector collimation
ct.e: Adaptive collimation uses dynamic pre-patient collimation to block the x-ray beam at scan start and end positions where data doesn't contribute to reconstruction, reducing dose.

ct.q: What information does the CT dose structured report (RDSR) contain?
ct.a: Detailed dose parameters for each irradiation event in the examination
c: Detailed dose parameters for each irradiation event in the examination
Only the total effective dose in millisieverts for the patient
Spatial resolution measurements for quality assurance records
Image quality metrics including noise and contrast resolution data
ct.e: The RDSR (DICOM Radiation Dose Structured Report) contains CTDIvol, DLP, scan length, kVp, mA, and other parameters for each irradiation event.

ct.q: What is the purpose of using tube current modulation along the z-axis?
ct.a: To reduce dose in less attenuating body regions while maintaining quality
c: To reduce dose in less attenuating body regions while maintaining quality
To increase spatial resolution in the craniocaudal scanning direction
To compensate for detector sensitivity variations along the z-axis
To eliminate helical interpolation artifacts in reformatted images
ct.e: Z-axis (longitudinal) tube current modulation adjusts mA along the scan length based on the varying attenuation of different anatomical regions.

ct.q: In 4D-CT, what is the difference between phase-based and amplitude-based sorting?
ct.a: Phase sorts by breathing cycle percentage; amplitude by chest wall position
c: Phase sorts by breathing cycle percentage; amplitude by chest wall position
Phase sorts by kVp variation; amplitude by mA variation during scan
Phase sorts by table position; amplitude by gantry rotation angle used
There is no practical difference between phase and amplitude sorting
ct.e: Phase-based sorting assigns images to time points in the breathing cycle. Amplitude-based sorting uses the actual displacement of the respiratory signal, which may better handle irregular breathing.

ct.q: What is the typical contrast resolution of a diagnostic CT scanner?
ct.a: Approximately 3-5 mm at 0.3-0.5% contrast difference
c: Approximately 3-5 mm at 0.3-0.5% contrast difference
Approximately 0.1 mm at 0.01% contrast difference level
Approximately 10 mm at 5-10% contrast difference level
Approximately 50 mm at 10-20% contrast difference level
ct.e: Diagnostic CT can detect objects of approximately 3-5 mm in size at 0.3-0.5% contrast difference under standard conditions, far superior to plain radiography.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the CT number linearity test in quality assurance?
ct.a: To verify measured HU values match expected values for known materials
c: To verify measured HU values match expected values for known materials
To assess the maximum spatial resolution of the CT scanner system
To measure the radiation dose output from the x-ray tube source
To check the accuracy of automatic exposure control modulation
ct.e: CT number linearity tests use phantom inserts of known density to verify that measured CT numbers are accurate and linear across the clinical range.

ct.q: What is the main challenge of using CBCT for adaptive radiation therapy planning?
ct.a: Poor CT number accuracy limits dose calculation reliability for planning
c: Poor CT number accuracy limits dose calculation reliability for planning
Excessive radiation dose from CBCT prevents frequent reimaging use
Limited spatial resolution makes tumor delineation impossible to do
CBCT images cannot be transferred to treatment planning systems
ct.e: CBCT has poor CT number accuracy due to scatter, making direct dose calculation unreliable. Corrections or deformable registration to planning CT are needed.

ct.q: What is the characteristic appearance of a windmill artifact in CT?
ct.a: Alternating bright and dark streaks rotating around high-contrast edges
c: Alternating bright and dark streaks rotating around high-contrast edges
Concentric rings centered on a single malfunctioning detector row
Uniform darkening of the image from excessive scatter radiation
Jagged stair-step pattern along the z-axis in coronal reformats
ct.e: Windmill artifacts appear as alternating bright/dark streaks radiating from high-contrast boundaries, caused by undersampling in multi-slice helical CT with cone beam geometry.

ct.q: What is the purpose of detector afterglow correction in CT?
ct.a: To compensate for residual light emission from scintillators after exposure ends
c: To compensate for residual light emission from scintillators after exposure ends
To increase the detection efficiency of the scintillator crystal material
To reduce the electronic noise generated by the photodiode circuitry
To calibrate the detector gain settings before each patient scan
ct.e: Scintillator afterglow (phosphorescence) can contaminate subsequent measurements. Correction algorithms compensate for this temporal lag in detector response.

ct.q: What is the maximum scan length limitation imposed by the 100 mm CTDI chamber?
ct.a: CTDI100 underestimates dose for scan lengths exceeding the chamber length
c: CTDI100 underestimates dose for scan lengths exceeding the chamber length
No scan length limitation exists because CTDI is length-independent
CTDI100 overestimates dose for all scan lengths regardless of size
The chamber length limits the maximum physical scan range possible
ct.e: The 100 mm chamber doesn't capture the full dose profile tails for long scan lengths, causing CTDI100 to underestimate the equilibrium dose, particularly for wide beams.

ct.q: What is the primary purpose of CT perfusion in stroke evaluation?
ct.a: To identify salvageable ischemic brain tissue (penumbra) for treatment
c: To identify salvageable ischemic brain tissue (penumbra) for treatment
To measure the total blood volume in the entire brain structure
To detect skull fractures not visible on standard CT head scans
To evaluate the CT number accuracy in the posterior fossa region
ct.e: CT perfusion maps blood flow, volume, and transit time to differentiate infarct core from salvageable penumbra, guiding thrombolysis or thrombectomy decisions.

ct.q: What detector material is used in current commercial photon-counting CT systems?
ct.a: Cadmium zinc telluride (CZT) or cadmium telluride (CdTe) crystals
c: Cadmium zinc telluride (CZT) or cadmium telluride (CdTe) crystals
Gadolinium oxysulfide ceramic scintillator detector material
High-pressure xenon gas in sealed chamber detector units
Bismuth germanate scintillation crystal detector elements
ct.e: Clinical photon-counting CT detectors use CZT or CdTe semiconductor materials that directly convert x-ray photons to electrical signals with energy discrimination capability.

ct.q: What is the purpose of automatic kVp selection in CT dose optimization?
ct.a: To choose optimal tube voltage based on patient size and exam indication
c: To choose optimal tube voltage based on patient size and exam indication
To maintain a constant mA regardless of patient size differences
To eliminate the need for contrast agent in CT angiography studies
To improve spatial resolution by selecting the smallest focal spot
ct.e: Automatic kVp selection algorithms choose the optimal tube voltage based on patient size and clinical task, potentially reducing dose especially for smaller patients and contrast-enhanced studies.

ct.q: What is the contrast-to-noise ratio (CNR) and why is it important?
ct.a: The ratio of contrast difference to noise that determines lesion detectability
c: The ratio of contrast difference to noise that determines lesion detectability
The maximum number of Hounsfield units achievable by the CT scanner
The ratio of spatial resolution to temporal resolution of the system
The percentage of scattered radiation reaching the detector elements
ct.e: CNR = (HU_lesion - HU_background)/noise. It determines whether a lesion can be detected above the noise floor and is a key metric for diagnostic image quality.

ct.q: What is the typical bore diameter of a diagnostic CT scanner?
ct.a: 70 cm diameter bore for standard diagnostic scanning
c: 70 cm diameter bore for standard diagnostic scanning
50 cm diameter bore for standard diagnostic scanning
100 cm diameter bore for standard diagnostic scanning
40 cm diameter bore for standard diagnostic scanning
ct.e: Standard diagnostic CT scanners have a 70 cm bore diameter. CT simulators for radiation therapy typically have 80-85 cm bores to accommodate treatment positioning.

ct.q: How does increasing the pitch above 1.0 affect the slice sensitivity profile in helical CT?
ct.a: The effective slice thickness broadens beyond the nominal collimation width
c: The effective slice thickness broadens beyond the nominal collimation width
The effective slice thickness narrows below the nominal collimation
The slice sensitivity profile remains unchanged with pitch changes
The effective slice thickness oscillates depending on table position
ct.e: At pitch >1, the z-interpolation must reach further between rotations, broadening the slice sensitivity profile and effectively increasing the slice thickness.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the Catphan phantom in CT quality assurance testing?
ct.a: Comprehensive assessment of spatial resolution, contrast, uniformity, and geometry
c: Comprehensive assessment of spatial resolution, contrast, uniformity, and geometry
Measurement of radiation dose output from the CT scanner x-ray tube
Calibration of the CT number to electron density conversion curve
Verification of patient positioning laser alignment accuracy only
ct.e: The Catphan phantom is a comprehensive QA phantom with modules for high-contrast resolution, low-contrast resolution, uniformity, geometry, and slice thickness assessment.

ct.q: What is the primary advantage of dual-layer (sandwich) detector design for dual-energy CT?
ct.a: Simultaneous dual-energy acquisition from every projection without registration issues
c: Simultaneous dual-energy acquisition from every projection without registration issues
Higher spatial resolution compared to single-layer detector configurations
Lower manufacturing cost than conventional single-layer detector systems
Ability to acquire data at more than two different energy levels
ct.e: Dual-layer detectors have a top layer absorbing low-energy photons and a bottom layer absorbing high-energy photons, providing perfectly registered dual-energy data from every acquisition.

ct.q: What is the Hounsfield unit value typically expected for cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)?
ct.a: Approximately 0 to 15 HU for normal CSF fluid
c: Approximately 0 to 15 HU for normal CSF fluid
Approximately -500 to -300 HU for CSF fluid
Approximately +200 to +400 HU for CSF fluid
Approximately +100 to +150 HU for CSF fluid
ct.e: CSF has a density close to water, so its CT number is typically 0 to 15 HU. Elevated CSF density may indicate hemorrhage or high protein content.

ct.q: What is the purpose of dose notification and alert values in CT dose management?
ct.a: To warn operators when dose exceeds preset thresholds for specific exams
c: To warn operators when dose exceeds preset thresholds for specific exams
To automatically stop the CT scan when dose reaches maximum limits
To calculate the effective dose for each individual patient scanned
To adjust the tube current based on the accumulated scan dose
ct.e: Dose notification alerts operators when CTDIvol or DLP exceeds facility-set thresholds, prompting review. Dose alerts are set higher and require acknowledgment before proceeding.

ct.q: What is the impact of gantry tilt on CT image reconstruction?
ct.a: Tilted acquisitions complicate multiplanar reformats and 3D reconstructions
c: Tilted acquisitions complicate multiplanar reformats and 3D reconstructions
Gantry tilt improves spatial resolution in the tilted imaging plane
Gantry tilt has no effect on image reconstruction or image quality
Gantry tilt eliminates partial volume artifacts in all scan planes
ct.e: Gantry tilt (used to reduce eye lens dose in head CT) creates non-orthogonal data that complicates sagittal/coronal reformats and 3D volume rendering.

ct.q: What is the primary cause of electronic noise in CT detector systems?
ct.a: Thermal fluctuations in the detector electronics and readout circuitry
c: Thermal fluctuations in the detector electronics and readout circuitry
Scattered photons from the patient reaching the detector elements
Mechanical vibration of the gantry during high-speed rotation mode
Electromagnetic interference from the x-ray tube power supply
ct.e: Electronic noise arises from thermal fluctuations in detector electronics and amplifiers. It is independent of x-ray flux and becomes relatively more significant at low dose levels.

ct.q: In 4D-CT for radiation therapy, what is the internal target volume (ITV)?
ct.a: The union of tumor volumes across all respiratory phases of breathing
c: The union of tumor volumes across all respiratory phases of breathing
The volume of the tumor at maximum inspiration phase only seen
The planning target volume minus the setup margin used clinically
The gross tumor volume plus a standard 5 mm expansion margin
ct.e: The ITV encompasses the tumor position across all respiratory phases, representing the full range of tumor motion to ensure coverage during treatment delivery.

ct.q: What is the typical tube current range for adult body CT examinations?
ct.a: 100 to 500 mA depending on patient size and protocol used
c: 100 to 500 mA depending on patient size and protocol used
1 to 10 mA depending on patient size and protocol used
1000 to 5000 mA depending on patient size and protocol
10 to 30 mA depending on patient size and protocol used
ct.e: Adult body CT typically uses tube currents of 100-500 mA, with higher values for larger patients and lower values for dose-optimized protocols using AEC.

ct.q: What is the Radon transform and its relationship to CT reconstruction?
ct.a: The mathematical basis representing line integrals that CT measures and inverts
c: The mathematical basis representing line integrals that CT measures and inverts
The algorithm used to calibrate CT detectors before patient scanning
The filter applied to projection data in iterative reconstruction only
The correction method for beam hardening artifacts in dense bone
ct.e: CT projection data represents the Radon transform of the object's attenuation distribution. CT reconstruction mathematically inverts this transform to recover the image.

ct.q: What is the typical scan protocol for CT-to-density calibration in radiation therapy?
ct.a: Scan the density phantom at the clinical treatment planning protocol settings
c: Scan the density phantom at the clinical treatment planning protocol settings
Scan at maximum dose with the smallest available field of view size
Use a pediatric low-dose protocol to minimize phantom irradiation
Scan at multiple kVp settings and average all the resulting curves
ct.e: The CT-to-density calibration should be performed using the same kVp, reconstruction kernel, and protocol settings used for treatment planning CT scans.

ct.q: How does the detector element size affect spatial resolution in CT?
ct.a: Smaller detector elements enable higher spatial resolution imaging
c: Smaller detector elements enable higher spatial resolution imaging
Larger detector elements provide better spatial resolution imaging
Detector element size has no impact on spatial resolution at all
Detector size only affects the noise but not spatial resolution
ct.e: Detector element size is a fundamental limit on spatial resolution. Smaller elements can resolve finer details but may have lower individual detection efficiency.

ct.q: What is the purpose of using a reference mAs or noise index value in automatic exposure control?
ct.a: To define the desired image quality target that guides mA modulation
c: To define the desired image quality target that guides mA modulation
To set the maximum kVp allowed during the CT scan acquisition
To specify the reconstruction algorithm used for image processing
To determine the pitch factor applied during helical acquisition
ct.e: The reference mAs (Siemens) or noise index (GE) sets the target image quality, and the AEC system modulates mA to achieve this target across varying anatomy.

ct.q: What is the primary reason for performing daily CT quality assurance tests?
ct.a: To detect scanner malfunctions before they affect clinical patient images
c: To detect scanner malfunctions before they affect clinical patient images
To satisfy billing requirements for insurance reimbursement claims
To calibrate the scanner for each specific patient to be scanned
To generate documentation solely for regulatory inspection purposes
ct.e: Daily QA detects drift in CT number accuracy, noise, and artifacts early, ensuring consistent image quality and preventing misdiagnosis from scanner malfunction.

ct.q: What is the difference between sequential and spiral (helical) CT scanning modes?
ct.a: Sequential scans one slice at a time; spiral moves table continuously during scan
c: Sequential scans one slice at a time; spiral moves table continuously during scan
Sequential is faster than spiral for whole body CT examinations done
Spiral provides better slice sensitivity profiles than sequential mode
There is no difference in dose between sequential and spiral modes
ct.e: Sequential (axial) mode acquires one or more slices with the table stationary, then moves. Spiral mode moves the table continuously for faster coverage.

ct.q: What is the typical energy threshold settings in a photon-counting CT detector?
ct.a: Multiple thresholds (e.g., 25, 50, 75, 90 keV) for energy binning of photons
c: Multiple thresholds (e.g., 25, 50, 75, 90 keV) for energy binning of photons
A single threshold at 0 keV to count all photons equally detected
Two thresholds matching conventional dual-energy kVp scan values
No energy thresholds since all photons are treated identically
ct.e: Photon-counting detectors use multiple energy thresholds to sort photons into energy bins, enabling multi-energy spectral imaging from a single acquisition.

ct.q: What is the relationship between CT dose and patient effective diameter?
ct.a: Smaller patients receive higher organ doses at the same scanner output setting
c: Smaller patients receive higher organ doses at the same scanner output setting
Larger patients always receive higher organ doses than smaller patients
Patient size has no effect on the organ dose received during scanning
Organ dose is the same regardless of patient size at given settings
ct.e: At the same CTDIvol, smaller patients receive higher organ doses because less tissue attenuation means more dose reaches central organs. This is why SSDE was developed.

ct.q: What is the concept of virtual monoenergetic imaging in dual-energy CT?
ct.a: Synthesizing images at a specific keV from dual-energy data decomposition
c: Synthesizing images at a specific keV from dual-energy data decomposition
Acquiring CT images using a perfectly monochromatic x-ray beam source
Filtering polychromatic CT images to remove scatter radiation signal
Applying a single reconstruction kernel to dual-energy acquisition data
ct.e: Virtual monoenergetic images simulate what the CT image would look like if acquired with a monochromatic beam at a chosen keV, reducing beam hardening and optimizing contrast.

ct.q: What is the primary benefit of using bismuth shielding in CT scanning?
ct.a: To reduce surface dose to radiosensitive organs like breast and thyroid
c: To reduce surface dose to radiosensitive organs like breast and thyroid
To improve spatial resolution in the shielded anatomical region
To increase CT number accuracy in the region beneath the shield
To eliminate all artifacts in the region covered by the shielding
ct.e: Bismuth shielding placed over radiosensitive organs reduces surface dose by 30-40%, though it can increase noise and artifacts if not properly positioned.

ct.q: What physical parameter does the ramp filter enhance in filtered back projection?
ct.a: High spatial frequencies to compensate for 1/r blurring in back projection
c: High spatial frequencies to compensate for 1/r blurring in back projection
Low spatial frequencies to improve soft tissue contrast resolution
Mid-range frequencies for optimal noise reduction in the image
All frequencies equally to maintain uniform image characteristics
ct.e: The ramp filter linearly amplifies spatial frequencies, compensating for the 1/r blurring inherent in back projection. In practice, it is combined with a low-pass window to control noise.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the AAPM Task Group 233 report?
ct.a: To standardize performance evaluation of CT systems including task-based metrics
c: To standardize performance evaluation of CT systems including task-based metrics
To define CT dose limits for all clinical examination categories done
To establish guidelines for 4D-CT in radiation therapy applications
To specify requirements for dual-energy CT material decomposition
ct.e: TG-233 provides a framework for comprehensive CT performance evaluation using task-based image quality metrics like detectability index, replacing traditional metrics.

ct.q: What is the significance of the Compton scatter interaction in CT imaging?
ct.a: It is the dominant interaction at diagnostic energies and causes image degradation
c: It is the dominant interaction at diagnostic energies and causes image degradation
It occurs only at very high energies above 1 MeV threshold value
It improves image contrast by increasing differences between tissues
It has no effect on CT image quality at diagnostic energy levels
ct.e: Compton scatter is the dominant interaction in the diagnostic energy range. Scattered photons degrade contrast and add noise when they reach detectors.

ct.q: How does the CT-to-density curve differ for different kVp settings?
ct.a: Different kVp values produce slightly different calibration curves for dense materials
c: Different kVp values produce slightly different calibration curves for dense materials
The calibration curve is identical and kVp-independent for all materials
Lower kVp produces higher electron density values for all materials
Higher kVp eliminates the need for any density calibration curves
ct.e: The HU-to-density relationship changes slightly with kVp because the relative contributions of photoelectric and Compton interactions change, primarily affecting high-Z materials.

ct.q: What is the primary purpose of the daily warm-up procedure for CT x-ray tubes?
ct.a: To gradually heat the anode and prevent thermal shock to the tube components
c: To gradually heat the anode and prevent thermal shock to the tube components
To calibrate the CT number scale for accurate diagnostic imaging today
To verify the spatial resolution meets manufacturer specifications daily
To test the automatic exposure control system before patient scans
ct.e: Tube warm-up gradually heats the anode to operating temperature, preventing thermal stress that could damage the anode disc or bearings.

ct.q: What is the primary limitation of using effective dose to assess individual patient CT risk?
ct.a: Effective dose is a population-averaged metric not applicable to individuals
c: Effective dose is a population-averaged metric not applicable to individuals
Effective dose overestimates the risk for all patient categories used
Effective dose can only be measured directly and never estimated
Effective dose does not account for any radiation type differences
ct.e: Effective dose uses tissue weighting factors averaged over age and sex, making it unsuitable for individual risk assessment. It is useful only for comparing protocols and procedures.

ct.q: What is the purpose of a CT dose registry such as the ACR DIR?
ct.a: To benchmark facility dose levels against national data for optimization use
c: To benchmark facility dose levels against national data for optimization use
To provide real-time dose alerts during individual patient CT scans
To replace the need for on-site CT physicist quality assurance work
To calculate individual patient cancer risk from CT radiation dose
ct.e: Dose registries collect dose data from participating facilities, enabling comparison against national benchmarks and identification of optimization opportunities.

ct.q: What is the concept of dose length product (DLP) and how is it related to patient risk?
ct.a: DLP integrates dose over scan length and correlates with total energy deposited
c: DLP integrates dose over scan length and correlates with total energy deposited
DLP measures the peak dose at any single point within the patient body
DLP represents the dose rate during the CT scan acquisition period
DLP is identical to effective dose and directly indicates cancer risk
ct.e: DLP (mGy-cm) = CTDIvol x scan length, representing total radiation output. It can be converted to approximate effective dose using region-specific k-factors.

ct.q: What is the purpose of tube current modulation in the angular direction during CT scanning?
ct.a: To reduce mA in less attenuating projections while maintaining image quality
c: To reduce mA in less attenuating projections while maintaining image quality
To increase spatial resolution in the anterior-posterior scanning direction
To compensate for the heel effect variation across the fan beam angle
To eliminate cone beam artifacts during wide detector CT acquisitions
ct.e: Angular TCM reduces mA for projections through less attenuating paths (e.g., AP through thin chest) and increases for more attenuating paths (lateral through thick thorax).

ct.q: What is the typical CT number tolerance for water in daily quality assurance?
ct.a: Water should measure 0 HU plus or minus 4 to 5 HU tolerance
c: Water should measure 0 HU plus or minus 4 to 5 HU tolerance
Water should measure 0 HU plus or minus 50 HU tolerance
Water should measure 0 HU plus or minus 0.1 HU tolerance
Water should measure 0 HU plus or minus 20 HU tolerance
ct.e: ACR guidelines specify water CT number accuracy within +/- 5 HU of 0 for daily QA, ensuring consistent and accurate CT number calibration.

ct.q: In dual-energy CT, what is the basis for uric acid versus calcium differentiation in gout imaging?
ct.a: Uric acid and calcium have different atomic numbers and energy-dependent attenuation
c: Uric acid and calcium have different atomic numbers and energy-dependent attenuation
Uric acid is always lower density than calcium at any x-ray energy level
Motion of uric acid crystals allows temporal subtraction from calcium
Uric acid absorbs no x-rays while calcium absorbs all x-ray photons
ct.e: Uric acid (primarily C, H, N, O - low Z) and calcium (Z=20) show different dual-energy attenuation ratios, enabling material-specific color-coded identification.

ct.q: What is the impact of patient respiratory motion on 4D-CT image quality?
ct.a: Irregular breathing causes sorting artifacts with blurring or duplication
c: Irregular breathing causes sorting artifacts with blurring or duplication
Respiratory motion has no effect on 4D-CT image quality results
Only very fast breathing rates affect 4D-CT image quality obtained
Respiratory motion improves 4D-CT images by providing more data
ct.e: Irregular breathing causes images to be missorted into incorrect respiratory phases, creating artifacts such as duplicated or missing anatomy in reconstructed 4D-CT volumes.

ct.q: What is the typical scan speed (table feed) range for helical CT acquisition?
ct.a: 20 to 200 mm per second depending on pitch and collimation used
c: 20 to 200 mm per second depending on pitch and collimation used
1 to 5 mm per second depending on pitch and collimation used
500 to 1000 mm per second at all available pitch settings
0.1 to 1 mm per second at standard clinical scan settings
ct.e: Table speed = pitch x collimation / rotation time. With modern scanners, table feeds of 20-200+ mm/s are achievable depending on collimation and pitch.

ct.q: What is the role of the x-ray tube housing in a CT scanner?
ct.a: To contain radiation leakage and provide oil cooling for the tube assembly
c: To contain radiation leakage and provide oil cooling for the tube assembly
To collimate the x-ray beam to the desired fan beam angle width
To modulate the tube voltage during dual-energy CT acquisitions
To rotate the anode at the required speed for CT scanning mode
ct.e: The tube housing provides radiation shielding to limit leakage radiation, contains insulating oil for cooling, and provides electrical insulation for the high-voltage components.

ct.q: What is the relationship between voxel size and CT image noise?
ct.a: Smaller voxels contain fewer photons and thus have higher noise levels
c: Smaller voxels contain fewer photons and thus have higher noise levels
Smaller voxels always produce lower noise due to better sampling
Voxel size has no relationship to the noise in CT images at all
Larger voxels always have higher noise than smaller voxel sizes
ct.e: Smaller voxels contain fewer photons, increasing quantum noise. Noise is inversely proportional to the square root of voxel volume (product of pixel area and slice thickness).

ct.q: What is the purpose of multi-planar reformation (MPR) in CT imaging?
ct.a: To generate coronal, sagittal, or oblique images from axial volumetric data
c: To generate coronal, sagittal, or oblique images from axial volumetric data
To reduce radiation dose by reconstructing fewer images per scan
To improve the spatial resolution beyond the acquisition parameters
To correct for motion artifacts in the original axial CT images
ct.e: MPR creates images in any arbitrary plane from volumetric CT data. Quality depends on the isotropic nature of the voxels in the original acquisition.

ct.q: What is the significance of achieving isotropic voxels in CT acquisition?
ct.a: Equal resolution in all three dimensions for high-quality multiplanar reformats
c: Equal resolution in all three dimensions for high-quality multiplanar reformats
Lower radiation dose compared to anisotropic voxel acquisitions used
Faster image reconstruction compared to anisotropic voxel data sets
Elimination of all partial volume artifacts in the reconstructed data
ct.e: Isotropic voxels (equal dimensions in x, y, z) ensure that reformatted images in any plane have the same spatial resolution as the original axial images.

ct.q: What is the maximum heat capacity typically specified for modern CT x-ray tubes?
ct.a: 5 to 8 million heat units (MHU) for high-performance tubes
c: 5 to 8 million heat units (MHU) for high-performance tubes
100 to 500 heat units (HU) for standard CT tube designs
50 to 100 million heat units (MHU) for all modern tubes
1000 to 5000 heat units (HU) for high-performance tubes
ct.e: Modern CT tubes have anode heat storage capacities of 5-8 MHU with high cooling rates, enabling sustained high-power scanning for protocols like CT perfusion.

ct.q: What is the primary advantage of using AI-based deep learning reconstruction in CT?
ct.a: Superior noise reduction while preserving spatial resolution and image texture
c: Superior noise reduction while preserving spatial resolution and image texture
Faster reconstruction than filtered back projection at all settings
Complete elimination of all types of CT artifacts in final images
No computational hardware requirements beyond standard workstations
ct.e: Deep learning reconstruction algorithms can reduce noise more effectively than iterative reconstruction while better preserving spatial resolution and natural image appearance.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the ACR CT phantom module that contains low-contrast cylinders?
ct.a: To test the scanner's ability to detect subtle density differences in objects
c: To test the scanner's ability to detect subtle density differences in objects
To measure the maximum spatial resolution in line pairs per centimeter
To verify the CT number accuracy of the scanner for known materials
To assess the geometric accuracy of distance measurements in images
ct.e: The low-contrast module contains cylinders with small HU differences from background, testing the scanner's low-contrast detectability at the clinical dose level.

ct.q: What is the overranging dose penalty in helical CT scanning?
ct.a: Extra rotations at scan start and end needed for interpolation but not imaging
c: Extra rotations at scan start and end needed for interpolation but not imaging
Increased dose from using pitch values less than 1.0 for all scans
Higher mA settings required at the beginning of each scan protocol
Additional dose from the scout scan acquired before helical scanning
ct.e: Helical CT requires additional rotations beyond the prescribed scan range for z-interpolation, delivering dose to tissue not included in the final images.

ct.q: How does the choice of reconstruction kernel affect CT-to-density calibration?
ct.a: Different kernels can shift CT numbers slightly, affecting calibration accuracy
c: Different kernels can shift CT numbers slightly, affecting calibration accuracy
Reconstruction kernels have no effect on CT numbers or calibration
Sharp kernels always produce more accurate CT numbers than smooth ones
Only iterative kernels affect CT numbers; FBP kernels do not change them
ct.e: Different reconstruction kernels can cause small shifts in CT numbers, so the same kernel used for clinical planning scans should be used for density calibration.

ct.q: What is the concept of noise-equivalent dose in CT imaging?
ct.a: The dose that would produce equivalent noise in a reference imaging condition
c: The dose that would produce equivalent noise in a reference imaging condition
The maximum dose allowed by regulatory agencies for any CT scan done
The dose contribution from electronic noise alone in the detector
The dose measured at the exact center of the CTDI body phantom
ct.e: Noise-equivalent dose normalizes image quality comparisons across different scanners and protocols by determining what dose would produce equivalent noise.

ct.q: What is the primary challenge of photon-counting CT at high x-ray flux rates?
ct.a: Pulse pileup causing miscounting and spectral distortion at high count rates
c: Pulse pileup causing miscounting and spectral distortion at high count rates
Insufficient spatial resolution compared to energy-integrating detectors
Excessive electronic noise that overwhelms the photon counting signal
Mechanical failure of the detector crystals under high flux exposure
ct.e: At high x-ray flux, photon pulses overlap (pileup), causing undercounting of photons and energy misassignment. This is a fundamental challenge for photon-counting technology.

ct.q: What is the purpose of the maximum intensity projection (MIP) in CT post-processing?
ct.a: To display the highest HU value along each projection ray through the volume
c: To display the highest HU value along each projection ray through the volume
To reduce noise by averaging all voxels along the projection direction
To create cross-sectional images from the raw projection data acquired
To subtract soft tissue to better visualize low-density lung structures
ct.e: MIP displays the maximum voxel value along each ray, useful for visualizing contrast-enhanced vessels and high-density structures like calcifications and nodules.

ct.q: What is the effect of beam filtration on CT x-ray spectrum and patient dose?
ct.a: Added filtration hardens the beam, reducing skin dose while maintaining penetration
c: Added filtration hardens the beam, reducing skin dose while maintaining penetration
Added filtration softens the beam and increases patient dose significantly
Beam filtration has no effect on spectrum or patient dose in CT imaging
Added filtration removes high-energy photons and increases skin dose
ct.e: Filtration removes low-energy photons that contribute to skin dose but not to image formation, hardening the spectrum and improving dose efficiency.

ct.q: What is the role of quality reference images in CT protocol optimization?
ct.a: To provide benchmark image quality that optimized protocols must match or exceed
c: To provide benchmark image quality that optimized protocols must match or exceed
To serve as the sole method for measuring radiation dose in CT scanning
To replace the need for physicist involvement in protocol development
To automatically adjust scan parameters without any human oversight
ct.e: Quality reference images define acceptable diagnostic image quality, ensuring that dose optimization efforts do not compromise clinical diagnostic capability.

ct.q: What is the primary difference between CBCT on a linac versus a diagnostic CT scanner?
ct.a: Linac CBCT has lower contrast resolution, more scatter, and poorer CT number accuracy
c: Linac CBCT has lower contrast resolution, more scatter, and poorer CT number accuracy
Linac CBCT has superior spatial resolution and contrast compared to diagnostic CT
Linac CBCT delivers significantly higher dose than any diagnostic CT protocol
There is no clinically meaningful difference between linac CBCT and diagnostic CT
ct.e: Linac-mounted CBCT uses a flat panel detector and wide cone beam, producing more scatter and lower contrast resolution than diagnostic CT with its focused fan beam.

ct.q: What is the significance of the k-factor conversion in estimating effective dose from DLP?
ct.a: It provides a region-specific approximation to convert DLP to effective dose
c: It provides a region-specific approximation to convert DLP to effective dose
It exactly calculates the absorbed dose to every organ in the body
It converts CTDIvol to entrance skin dose for regulatory compliance
It measures the scatter-to-primary ratio for image quality assessment
ct.e: The k-factor (mSv/mGy-cm) is a region-specific conversion factor derived from Monte Carlo simulations that provides a rough estimate of effective dose from DLP.

ct.q: What does the CT dose index (CTDI) represent in computed tomography dosimetry?
ct.a: The average absorbed dose from a series of contiguous slices in a standard phantom
c: The total radiation dose delivered to the patient during a complete CT examination
The peak skin dose measured at the surface of the patient during a CT scan
The average absorbed dose from a series of contiguous slices in a standard phantom
The effective dose calculated using tissue weighting factors for each scanned organ
ct.e: CTDI represents the average dose in the scan plane from a series of contiguous slices, accounting for scattered radiation from adjacent slices, measured in standard PMMA phantoms.

ct.q: How is the dose-length product (DLP) calculated in CT scanning?
ct.a: By multiplying CTDIvol by the total scan length in centimeters
c: By multiplying tube current (mA) by total rotation time in seconds
By multiplying CTDIvol by the total scan length in centimeters
By dividing the total mAs by the pitch factor and slice thickness
By integrating the dose profile across the full width at half maximum
ct.e: DLP = CTDIvol × scan length, providing a measure of total radiation output for a complete CT acquisition that accounts for both dose intensity and scan extent.

ct.q: What type of CT artifact is caused by dense metallic implants such as hip prostheses?
ct.a: Streak artifacts from photon starvation and beam hardening near the metal
c: Ring artifacts from miscalibrated individual detector elements in the array
Streak artifacts from photon starvation and beam hardening near the metal
Motion artifacts from involuntary patient movement during scan acquisition
Partial volume artifacts from tissues of different density within one voxel
ct.e: Metal objects severely attenuate the beam in certain projections, causing inconsistent data that manifests as bright and dark streaks radiating from the metal in reconstructed images.

ct.q: What is the primary advantage of iterative reconstruction over filtered back projection in CT?
ct.a: Reduced image noise at equivalent dose levels enabling potential dose reduction
c: Faster reconstruction speed allowing real-time image display during scanning
Reduced image noise at equivalent dose levels enabling potential dose reduction
Elimination of all beam hardening artifacts without additional corrections needed
Higher spatial resolution through improved deconvolution of the system point spread function
ct.e: Iterative reconstruction uses statistical modeling and regularization to suppress noise more effectively than FBP, enabling diagnostic quality images at lower radiation doses.

ct.q: What CT scan parameter directly controls the radiation dose per rotation for a given kVp?
ct.a: Tube current-time product (mAs) which determines photon fluence per rotation
c: Gantry rotation speed measured in rotations per second or rotation period
Tube current-time product (mAs) which determines photon fluence per rotation
Reconstruction field of view (DFOV) selected for the region of interest
Display window width and level settings used for image visualization
ct.e: The mAs value directly determines the number of X-ray photons produced per rotation. Doubling mAs doubles the dose proportionally for a given kVp and filtration.

ct.q: How does helical pitch factor affect radiation dose in spiral CT scanning?
ct.a: Increasing pitch reduces dose proportionally by decreasing beam overlap between rotations
c: Increasing pitch increases dose because more rotations are needed for coverage
Pitch has no effect on dose as automatic tube current modulation compensates
Increasing pitch reduces dose proportionally by decreasing beam overlap between rotations
Increasing pitch increases dose due to the need for wider detector collimation
ct.e: Pitch is defined as table travel per rotation divided by beam width. Higher pitch means less overlap between rotations, reducing the cumulative dose for a given scan length.

informatics.q: What does DICOM stand for in medical imaging?
informatics.a: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine
c: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine
Digital Information and Control in Medicine
Data Interchange and Communication for Medicine
Digital Integration and Capture of Monitors
informatics.e: DICOM is the international standard for transmitting, storing, and sharing medical imaging information. It defines formats and communication protocols for interoperability.

informatics.q: Which DICOM object stores radiation therapy structure sets including organ contours?
informatics.a: RT Structure Set (RTSTRUCT)
c: RT Plan (RTPLAN) storage object
RT Structure Set (RTSTRUCT)
RT Dose (RTDOSE) storage file
RT Image (RTIMAGE) data object
informatics.e: The RT Structure Set SOP class stores contoured regions of interest (ROIs) including target volumes and organs at risk referenced to a CT image series.

informatics.q: What is the purpose of a DICOM Service-Object Pair (SOP) class?
informatics.a: Defines a service and information object combination
c: Defines a service and information object combination
Specifies hardware requirements for imaging devices
Establishes encryption protocols for data transfer
Configures network bandwidth for image streaming
informatics.e: A SOP class combines an Information Object Definition (IOD) with a DICOM service such as Storage, Query/Retrieve, or Verification to define a specific function.

informatics.q: Which DICOM transfer syntax uses JPEG 2000 lossless compression?
c: 1.2.840.10008.1.2.1 Explicit VR Little Endian
1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.50 JPEG Baseline Lossy
informatics.e: Transfer syntax UID 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.90 specifies JPEG 2000 Image Compression in lossless mode, preserving full image fidelity for diagnostic use.

informatics.q: What is the default DICOM transfer syntax if none is explicitly negotiated?
informatics.a: Implicit VR Little Endian (1.2.840.10008.1.2)
c: Implicit VR Little Endian (1.2.840.10008.1.2)
Explicit VR Little Endian (1.2.840.10008.1.2.1)
Explicit VR Big Endian (1.2.840.10008.1.2.2)
JPEG Lossless first-order prediction syntax
informatics.e: Implicit VR Little Endian is the default transfer syntax in DICOM. Every conformant implementation must support it as a fallback.

informatics.q: Which DICOM-RT object contains the dose distribution calculated by the TPS?
informatics.a: RT Dose (RTDOSE) object
c: RT Plan (RTPLAN) object
RT Dose (RTDOSE) object
RT Structure Set (RTSTRUCT)
RT Beams Treatment Record
informatics.e: The RTDOSE object stores the 3D dose matrix, DVH data, and dose reference points computed by the treatment planning system.

informatics.q: What information is primarily stored in a DICOM RT Plan (RTPLAN) object?
informatics.a: Beam geometry, MLC positions, and monitor units
c: Beam geometry, MLC positions, and monitor units
Patient CT images and reconstruction parameters
3D dose distributions and dose volume histograms
Contoured structures and regions of interest data
informatics.e: The RTPLAN object encodes the treatment plan parameters including beam angles, jaw/MLC settings, monitor units, and prescription information.

informatics.q: What is the DICOM verification service (C-ECHO) used for?
informatics.a: Testing network connectivity between DICOM nodes
c: Testing network connectivity between DICOM nodes
Verifying integrity of transferred image pixel data
Authenticating user credentials on PACS systems
Validating DICOM file format compliance checks
informatics.e: C-ECHO is a simple ping-like service that confirms two DICOM Application Entities can establish an association and communicate over the network.

informatics.q: What port number is traditionally used for DICOM network communication?
informatics.a: Port 104 (or 11112 as alternate)
c: Port 104 (or 11112 as alternate)
Port 443 (standard HTTPS protocol)
Port 8080 (HTTP alternate default)
Port 3306 (database service access)
informatics.e: DICOM associations traditionally use port 104 (requires root/admin privileges) or port 11112 as a non-privileged alternative.

informatics.q: Which DICOM service is used to retrieve images from a PACS archive?
informatics.a: C-MOVE or C-GET query/retrieve service
c: C-STORE push-based storage service only
C-MOVE or C-GET query/retrieve service
C-ECHO connectivity verification service
C-FIND search and discovery service only
informatics.e: C-MOVE and C-GET are DICOM retrieve services. C-MOVE instructs the SCP to push images to a specified destination, while C-GET delivers directly to the requester.
