informatics.e: Hot standby maintains a continuously synchronized database replica that can take over immediately upon primary failure, minimizing downtime (low RTO) for critical RT systems.

informatics.q: What is the difference between incremental and differential backups?
informatics.a: Incremental saves changes since last backup of any type
c: Incremental saves changes since last backup of any type
Differential saves changes since last backup of any type
Incremental saves all data regardless of prior backups
Both backup types function identically in all cases
informatics.e: Incremental backups save only changes since the last backup (full or incremental). Differential backups save all changes since the last full backup, growing larger over time.

informatics.q: What is the primary purpose of DICOM Print Management services?
informatics.a: Send images to DICOM-compatible film printers devices
c: Send images to DICOM-compatible film printers devices
Print patient appointment schedules from the system
Generate PDF treatment summary reports for charts
Create hardcopy of the complete patient database
informatics.e: DICOM Print Management (N-CREATE, N-SET, N-ACTION) enables sending images to DICOM printers for film output, though this is declining with softcopy reading adoption.

informatics.q: What is an IHE integration profile Connectathon?
informatics.a: Multi-vendor interoperability testing event for IHE profiles
c: Multi-vendor interoperability testing event for IHE profiles
Single-vendor internal software quality assurance test
Patient connectivity assessment for telehealth access
Athletic competition between healthcare IT departments
informatics.e: IHE Connectathons are organized testing events where vendors test their implementations of IHE integration profiles against each other to verify interoperability.

informatics.q: What is containerization (e.g., Docker) used for in radiation therapy IT?
informatics.a: Package applications with dependencies for consistent deployment
c: Package applications with dependencies for consistent deployment
Physically contain radioactive sources in shielded units
Compress patient data into smaller archive containers
Isolate network cables in metal conduit for safety
informatics.e: Docker containers package applications with all dependencies, ensuring consistent deployment across environments. They are used for deploying AI models, analysis tools, and research software.

informatics.q: What is a RESTful API and why is it relevant to modern healthcare IT?
informatics.a: Uses HTTP methods to access resources in a stateless manner
c: Uses HTTP methods to access resources in a stateless manner
A programming language specifically for medical software
A hardware interface for connecting medical equipment
A database management system for clinical data storage
informatics.e: RESTful APIs use standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) for resource interaction. FHIR and DICOMweb both use REST architecture for interoperability.

informatics.q: What is the purpose of the DICOM Radiation Dose Structured Report (RDSR)?
informatics.a: Records radiation dose information from imaging procedures
c: Records radiation dose information from imaging procedures
Reports radiation therapy treatment dose delivery data
Documents radiation safety officer inspection results
Structures the radiation protection survey measurements
informatics.e: RDSR (part of DICOM SR) documents radiation dose from diagnostic imaging procedures (CT, fluoroscopy), supporting dose tracking and optimization efforts.

informatics.q: What is the advantage of using JSON over XML for FHIR resource representation?
informatics.a: More compact, faster parsing, widely used in web development
c: More compact, faster parsing, widely used in web development
Provides stronger data typing and validation built in
Supports binary data embedding without any encoding
Is required by the FHIR standard with no alternative
informatics.e: JSON is more compact than XML, parses faster in most languages, and is the dominant format in web development, making it the preferred choice for FHIR implementations.

informatics.q: What is the purpose of an intrusion detection system (IDS) in a hospital network?
informatics.a: Monitor network traffic for suspicious activity patterns
c: Monitor network traffic for suspicious activity patterns
Detect physical intrusions into the treatment vault
Identify medication dosing errors in pharmacy orders
Detect equipment calibration drift in linac output
informatics.e: An IDS monitors network traffic and system activities for malicious behavior or policy violations, alerting security teams to potential cybersecurity threats targeting medical devices.

informatics.q: What is the purpose of penetration testing for radiation therapy network systems?
informatics.a: Simulate cyberattacks to identify security vulnerabilities
c: Simulate cyberattacks to identify security vulnerabilities
Test radiation penetration through treatment room walls
Measure beam penetration through tissue-like material
Evaluate needle penetration for brachytherapy implants
informatics.e: Penetration testing involves authorized simulated attacks on RT network infrastructure to discover security weaknesses before malicious actors can exploit them.

informatics.q: What is the concept of 'defense in depth' for medical device cybersecurity?
informatics.a: Multiple layers of security controls protecting systems
c: Multiple layers of security controls protecting systems
Single strong firewall as the only security measure
Physical depth of concrete shielding around devices
Deep learning algorithms for threat detection tasks
informatics.e: Defense in depth uses multiple overlapping security layers (firewalls, VLANs, access controls, encryption, monitoring) so that compromise of one layer does not expose the system.

informatics.q: What is the NIST Cybersecurity Framework used for in healthcare?
informatics.a: Provides guidelines for managing cybersecurity risk posture
c: Provides guidelines for managing cybersecurity risk posture
Defines radiation dose limits for occupational workers
Specifies medical device hardware design requirements
Establishes patient treatment consent form standards
informatics.e: The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) provides a structured approach for healthcare organizations to manage and reduce cybersecurity risk.

informatics.q: What is the purpose of log file-based patient-specific QA?
informatics.a: Verify delivery accuracy using actual machine data recorded
c: Verify delivery accuracy using actual machine data recorded
Replace all measurement-based QA with calculations only
Generate treatment plans from machine delivery log data
Schedule machine maintenance based on error frequency
informatics.e: Log file-based QA uses recorded machine parameters to reconstruct delivered fluence/dose and compare against planned values, complementing or potentially replacing some measurement-based QA.

informatics.q: What is MU density (fluence) analysis from trajectory log files?
informatics.a: Compares planned vs delivered fluence maps from log data
c: Compares planned vs delivered fluence maps from log data
Measures monitor unit output using ion chamber setup
Calculates patient dose from portal imaging measurements
Analyzes beam energy spectrum from log file information
informatics.e: MU density analysis creates fluence maps from trajectory log MLC and MU data, comparing delivered fluence against planned fluence to quantify delivery accuracy.

informatics.q: What Python package can be used to parse Varian trajectory log files?
informatics.a: pylinac library with trajectory log module analysis
c: pylinac library with trajectory log module analysis
pydicom library for DICOM file parsing operations
numpy library for numerical array computations only
scipy library for scientific computing functions only
informatics.e: pylinac includes a TrajectoryLog module that can parse Varian trajectory log files, extract MLC positions, calculate MLC errors, and perform fluence analysis.

informatics.q: What information can be extracted from Elekta iCom log files?
informatics.a: MLC positions, gantry angle, and dose data per segment
c: MLC positions, gantry angle, and dose data per segment
Patient demographic information and medical history
Treatment planning system optimization parameters
Equipment purchase orders and maintenance schedules
informatics.e: Elekta iCom log files record delivered beam parameters including MLC leaf positions, gantry and collimator angles, dose rate, and cumulative MU for each control point.

informatics.q: What is the typical format of Varian Dynalog files?
informatics.a: Binary file with header followed by data sampled at 50ms
c: Binary file with header followed by data sampled at 50ms
XML formatted file with human-readable tag structure
CSV text file with comma-separated column values
DICOM formatted file stored in the PACS archive
informatics.e: Varian Dynalog files are binary files containing a header with field information followed by data rows sampled every 50 ms, recording planned and actual MLC positions.

informatics.q: What is ESAPI write access used for in Eclipse scripting?
informatics.a: Modify plan parameters and create structures programmatically
c: Modify plan parameters and create structures programmatically
Write directly to the linear accelerator control system
Edit the Eclipse source code for custom modifications
Create new user accounts in the ARIA database system
informatics.e: ESAPI write access (research mode or clinical write mode) enables scripts to modify plans, create structures, set optimization parameters, and automate planning workflows.

informatics.q: What is Eclipse Script Approval and why is it important?
informatics.a: Clinical review process before scripts are used clinically
c: Clinical review process before scripts are used clinically
Automatic code compilation without any manual review
Patient approval of treatment plan generated by script
FDA clearance required for every ESAPI script written
informatics.e: Script approval is a workflow in Eclipse where scripts must be reviewed and approved before clinical use, ensuring validated, safe automation in patient care workflows.

informatics.q: What is the advantage of using Python over C# for some medical physics tasks?
informatics.a: Easier syntax, rich data science libraries, faster prototyping
c: Easier syntax, rich data science libraries, faster prototyping
Always faster execution speed than compiled C# programs
Direct integration with Eclipse TPS without any bridge
Required by HIPAA for all medical physics scripting
informatics.e: Python offers simpler syntax, extensive libraries (numpy, scipy, pydicom, pylinac, pandas), and rapid prototyping capabilities, making it ideal for data analysis and research tasks.

informatics.q: What is the purpose of a cloud-based treatment planning system?
informatics.a: Enable scalable TPS access from any networked location
c: Enable scalable TPS access from any networked location
Eliminate the need for any local computing resources
Provide free unlimited treatment planning for everyone
Replace all on-premise radiation therapy IT equipment
informatics.e: Cloud-based TPS solutions offer scalable computing resources, remote access, reduced local hardware requirements, and elastic compute capacity for dose calculations.

informatics.q: What is a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) in the context of cloud healthcare services?
informatics.a: Contract ensuring cloud vendor complies with HIPAA rules
c: Contract ensuring cloud vendor complies with HIPAA rules
Agreement between physicians for patient referral care
Financial contract for equipment leasing arrangements
Legal document for patient consent to cloud storage
informatics.e: A BAA is a HIPAA-required contract between a covered entity and a business associate (cloud provider) ensuring the vendor will safeguard PHI and comply with HIPAA regulations.

informatics.q: What is auto-contouring software commissioning in radiation therapy?
informatics.a: Systematic validation of AI contours against expert reference
c: Systematic validation of AI contours against expert reference
Automatic installation of contouring software on servers
Patient consent process for using AI in their treatment
Calibration of the CT scanner for contouring accuracy
informatics.e: Commissioning auto-contouring requires systematic comparison of AI-generated contours against expert-drawn references using metrics like DSC, Hausdorff distance, and clinical review.

informatics.q: What is the mean surface distance metric used for in contour evaluation?
informatics.a: Average distance between corresponding contour surface points
c: Average distance between corresponding contour surface points
Maximum distance between the two contour surface edges
Volume difference between two segmented structure sets
Number of surface points shared by both contour sets
informatics.e: Mean surface distance calculates the average of all minimum distances between surface points of two contours, providing a measure of overall contour agreement less sensitive to outliers than Hausdorff distance.

informatics.q: What is the significance of IEC 62304 requiring a software maintenance plan?
informatics.a: Ensures ongoing monitoring and updating of released software
c: Ensures ongoing monitoring and updating of released software
Only addresses initial software development before release
Requires complete software rewrite every five years time
Mandates hardware replacement on a fixed annual cycle
informatics.e: IEC 62304 requires a maintenance plan to address problem resolution, modification implementation, and software updates throughout the product's lifecycle after release.

informatics.q: What is unit testing in the context of medical device software development?
informatics.a: Testing individual software components or functions in isolation
c: Testing individual software components or functions in isolation
Testing the complete system with all modules integrated
Testing by end users in the clinical environment only
Testing hardware units like processors and memory chips
informatics.e: Unit testing verifies that individual software modules or functions produce correct outputs for given inputs, forming the foundation of the software verification strategy.

informatics.q: What is the OSI model layer at which VLANs operate?
informatics.a: Layer 2 (Data Link layer) of the OSI network model
c: Layer 2 (Data Link layer) of the OSI network model
Layer 7 (Application layer) of the OSI model stack
Layer 1 (Physical layer) of the OSI network model
Layer 4 (Transport layer) of the OSI model stack
informatics.e: VLANs operate at Layer 2 (Data Link) of the OSI model, using IEEE 802.1Q tagging to logically segment network traffic at the switch level.

informatics.q: What is the purpose of NAT (Network Address Translation) in hospital networks?
informatics.a: Translates private IP addresses to public addresses for routing
c: Translates private IP addresses to public addresses for routing
Encrypts all network traffic between hospital devices only
Accelerates network speed by compressing data packets
Authenticates users before allowing any network access
informatics.e: NAT allows devices with private IP addresses on the hospital network to communicate with external networks by translating addresses, also providing a layer of security.

informatics.q: What is a common method for anonymizing dates in DICOM de-identification?
informatics.a: Shift all dates by a consistent random offset value
c: Shift all dates by a consistent random offset value
Remove all dates leaving the fields completely empty
Replace all dates with January 1st of year 1900 only
Convert dates from numeric format to text month names
informatics.e: Date shifting applies a consistent random offset to all dates within a patient's dataset, preserving temporal relationships between events while removing identifiable date information.

informatics.q: What is burned-in PHI in the context of DICOM image de-identification?
informatics.a: Patient information embedded in the image pixel data itself
c: Patient information embedded in the image pixel data itself
Data stored in DICOM header tags that can be removed
Information printed on paper copies of medical images
PHI stored in the database but not in the DICOM file
informatics.e: Burned-in PHI refers to patient identifying information rendered into the pixel data (e.g., annotations, patient name overlays), which cannot be removed by simply modifying DICOM tags.

informatics.q: What is the purpose of a DICOM router in a radiation oncology network?
informatics.a: Directs DICOM data to appropriate destination systems
c: Directs DICOM data to appropriate destination systems
Calculates optimal beam routes through patient anatomy
Routes patients to correct treatment rooms physically
Manages physician on-call rotation schedules online
informatics.e: A DICOM router receives DICOM objects and forwards them to appropriate destinations (PACS, TPS, OIS) based on rules like modality, referring physician, or body part.

informatics.q: What is the advantage of using Kubernetes for deploying AI models in radiation therapy?
informatics.a: Automated scaling and management of containerized applications
c: Automated scaling and management of containerized applications
Direct control of linear accelerator hardware interfaces
Built-in DICOM protocol support without configuration
Automatic FDA regulatory approval for deployed models
informatics.e: Kubernetes orchestrates containerized AI model deployments with automated scaling, load balancing, and self-healing capabilities, supporting production AI inference in clinical workflows.

informatics.q: What is the concept of 'model drift' in deployed AI systems for radiation therapy?
informatics.a: Degradation of model performance over time due to data changes
c: Degradation of model performance over time due to data changes
Physical movement of the server hardware in the rack
Gradual improvement in model accuracy without updates
Network latency increasing due to cable degradation
informatics.e: Model drift occurs when the statistical properties of input data change over time (new scanners, protocols, patient populations), causing deployed AI model performance to degrade without retraining.

informatics.q: What DICOM service is used to send images from a CT scanner to the treatment planning system?
informatics.a: C-STORE which pushes DICOM objects from one application entity to another
c: C-FIND which queries a remote database for matching patient study records
C-STORE which pushes DICOM objects from one application entity to another
C-MOVE which instructs a remote system to initiate a storage transfer
C-ECHO which verifies network connectivity between two DICOM application entities
informatics.e: C-STORE is the DICOM service that transfers image objects from the storage service class user (SCU) to the storage service class provider (SCP), such as from CT to TPS.

informatics.q: What does TG-263 standardize in radiation therapy information systems?
informatics.a: Nomenclature conventions for structure names used in treatment planning
c: Dose calculation algorithm accuracy requirements for treatment planning systems
Nomenclature conventions for structure names used in treatment planning
Network security protocols for transferring patient data between departments
Quality assurance procedures for electronic medical record data integrity
informatics.e: TG-263 provides standardized naming conventions for anatomical structures, target volumes, and OARs to improve data consistency, sharing, and outcomes analysis across institutions.

informatics.q: In the HL7 messaging standard, what type of message is used to communicate patient admission information?
informatics.a: ADT (Admit, Discharge, Transfer) message type with relevant segments
c: ORM message type containing clinical order details and scheduling data
ADT (Admit, Discharge, Transfer) message type with relevant segments
ORU message type carrying diagnostic observation and result information
SIU message type providing scheduling and appointment update notifications
informatics.e: HL7 ADT messages communicate patient demographic and visit information including admissions, discharges, and transfers between hospital information systems.

informatics.q: What is the primary cybersecurity concern when connecting radiation therapy equipment to hospital networks?
informatics.a: Unauthorized access to treatment delivery systems could alter patient treatments
c: Excessive network bandwidth consumption by DICOM image transfer operations
Unauthorized access to treatment delivery systems could alter patient treatments
Incompatible software versions between planning systems and delivery equipment
Increased hardware maintenance costs from network-related component failures
informatics.e: Connected RT equipment is potentially vulnerable to cyberattacks that could modify treatment parameters, making access control, network segmentation, and monitoring essential.

informatics.q: What DICOM object is used to transfer a radiation therapy treatment plan between systems?
informatics.a: RT Plan object containing beam parameters, geometry, and monitor units
c: RT Image object containing portal images acquired during beam delivery
RT Structure Set object containing contoured anatomical region definitions
RT Plan object containing beam parameters, geometry, and monitor units
RT Dose object containing three-dimensional calculated dose distributions
informatics.e: The RT Plan DICOM object encodes all treatment plan information including beam geometry, MLC positions, monitor units, and prescription data for transfer to the delivery system.

informatics.q: What data management practice is recommended for ensuring integrity of radiation therapy patient records?
informatics.a: Regular automated backups with periodic verification of data restoration capability
c: Storing all data exclusively on local hard drives without network replication
Regular automated backups with periodic verification of data restoration capability
Maintaining only paper copies of critical treatment parameters as backup records
Relying solely on the equipment vendor cloud storage for all patient archives
informatics.e: Regular backups with tested restoration ensure that treatment records can be recovered after hardware failures, cyberattacks, or data corruption events.

informatics.q: What is the purpose of DICOM Structured Reporting (SR) in radiation therapy workflows?
informatics.a: To encode clinical findings and measurements in a standardized machine-readable format
c: To compress DICOM image files for more efficient network transfer and storage
To define the visual layout of printed radiotherapy treatment plan documents
To encode clinical findings and measurements in a standardized machine-readable format
To provide real-time streaming of treatment delivery parameters during irradiation
informatics.e: DICOM SR enables structured, machine-readable documentation of clinical information such as dose reports and QA results, supporting automated data extraction and analysis.

sasqart.q: Which of the following is a daily check for Co-60 teletherapy units according to SASQART?
sasqart.a: Door interlock, beam status indicators, and patient audio-visual monitoring
c: Door interlock, beam status indicators, and patient audio-visual monitoring
Reference dosimetry, wedge transmission, and output reproducibility
Gantry rotation isocentre, collimator isocentre, and couch deflection
Timer linearity, shutter error measurement, and beam symmetry
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 2 requires daily safety checks including door interlock, beam status indicators, patient audio-visual communication, lasers, ODI, and field size indicator for Co-60 units.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART daily tolerance for the optical distance indicator (ODI) on a Co-60 unit?
sasqart.a: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level
c: 0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level
1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level
2 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 2 specifies daily ODI checks on Co-60 units with 1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level, ensuring accurate SSD setting for patient treatments.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for gantry angle accuracy on a Co-60 teletherapy unit?
sasqart.a: 0.5 degree tolerance, 1 degree action level
c: 0.5 degree tolerance, 1 degree action level
1 degree tolerance, 2 degree action level
0.2 degree tolerance, 0.5 degree action level
1 degree tolerance, 3 degree action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 2 requires monthly gantry angle verification on Co-60 units with 0.5 degree tolerance and 1 degree action level.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for light/radiation field coincidence on a Co-60 unit?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level
c: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level
0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 2 specifies monthly light/radiation field coincidence on Co-60 units at 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level, ensuring the light field accurately represents the radiation field.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for shutter error on a Co-60 teletherapy unit?
sasqart.a: 0.03 min tolerance, 0.05 min action level
c: 0.01 min tolerance, 0.02 min action level
0.03 min tolerance, 0.05 min action level
0.05 min tolerance, 0.10 min action level
0.10 min tolerance, 0.15 min action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 2 specifies annual shutter error measurement for Co-60 units with 0.03 min tolerance and 0.05 min action level. Shutter error affects short treatment times proportionally more.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for gantry rotation isocentre on a Co-60 unit?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level
c: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level
0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 2 requires annual gantry rotation isocentre checks on Co-60 units with 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level, verifying the mechanical isocentre stability.

sasqart.q: Why does SASQART note that Co-60 radiation output requires regular checks (MCO17)?
sasqart.a: Because Co-60 decays at a known rate and output decreases continuously
c: Because Co-60 decays at a known rate and output decreases continuously
Because the collimator jaws gradually shift over time with use
Because the source capsule expands with temperature changes
Because electronics drift causes timer inaccuracies over months
sasqart.e: SASQART MCO17 notes that Co-60 radiation output decreases continuously due to radioactive decay (half-life 5.27 years), requiring regular output checks and treatment time adjustments.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for reference dosimetry on a Co-60 unit?
sasqart.a: 1% tolerance, 2% action level
c: 0.5% tolerance, 1% action level
1% tolerance, 2% action level
2% tolerance, 3% action level
3% tolerance, 5% action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 2 requires annual reference dosimetry on Co-60 units with 1% tolerance and 2% action level, ensuring accurate absolute dose determination.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for couch deflection on a Co-60 teletherapy unit?
sasqart.a: 3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level
c: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level
5 mm tolerance, 8 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 2 specifies annual couch deflection measurement at 3 mm tolerance and 5 mm action level. Couch sag under patient weight can shift the treatment target from isocentre.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART daily tolerance for CT number of water (mean accuracy) on CT scanners?
sasqart.a: 0 plus or minus 3 HU tolerance, 0 plus or minus 5 HU action level
c: 0 plus or minus 1 HU tolerance, 0 plus or minus 2 HU action level
0 plus or minus 3 HU tolerance, 0 plus or minus 5 HU action level
0 plus or minus 5 HU tolerance, 0 plus or minus 10 HU action level
0 plus or minus 10 HU tolerance, 0 plus or minus 15 HU action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 5 requires daily CT number accuracy for water at 0+/-3 HU tolerance and 0+/-5 HU action level. Water should read 0 HU and deviations indicate calibration issues.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART daily tolerance for CT number noise (standard deviation of water) on CT scanners?
sasqart.a: 5 HU tolerance, 10 HU action level
c: 2 HU tolerance, 5 HU action level
5 HU tolerance, 10 HU action level
10 HU tolerance, 15 HU action level
15 HU tolerance, 20 HU action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 5 specifies daily CT number noise (SD of water) at 5 HU tolerance and 10 HU action level. Increased noise degrades low contrast resolution and affects dose calculations.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART daily tolerance for CT number water uniformity on CT scanners?
sasqart.a: 5 HU tolerance, 10 HU action level
c: 2 HU tolerance, 5 HU action level
5 HU tolerance, 10 HU action level
10 HU tolerance, 20 HU action level
15 HU tolerance, 25 HU action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 5 specifies daily CT water uniformity at 5 HU tolerance and 10 HU action level, checking for consistent HU values across the image field of view.

sasqart.q: Which daily checks does SASQART require for CT simulators used in radiotherapy planning?
sasqart.a: Beam status, CT# water accuracy/noise/uniformity, audio-video, and respiratory monitoring
c: Beam status, CT# water accuracy/noise/uniformity, audio-video, and respiratory monitoring
Only CT number accuracy of water and spatial resolution checks
Only laser alignment and couch level verification measurements
Only gantry tilt accuracy and slice thickness measurements
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 5 requires comprehensive daily CT-sim checks including beam status, CT# water (mean, SD, uniformity), audio-video intercom, respiratory monitoring, and 4D-CT calibration verification.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART biannual tolerance for CT number of other materials (non-water) on CT scanners?
sasqart.a: 4 HU tolerance, 10 HU action level
c: 2 HU tolerance, 5 HU action level
4 HU tolerance, 10 HU action level
10 HU tolerance, 20 HU action level
15 HU tolerance, 25 HU action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 5 specifies biannual CT number checks for other materials at 4 HU tolerance and 10 HU action level, verifying the HU-to-density curve accuracy.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART biannual tolerance for high contrast resolution on CT scanners?
sasqart.a: 5 line pairs per cm
c: 2 line pairs per cm
5 line pairs per cm
8 line pairs per cm
10 line pairs per cm
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 5 specifies biannual high contrast resolution at 5 lp/cm for CT scanners, though modern scanners typically exceed this minimum requirement.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART biannual tolerance for slice thickness accuracy on CT scanners?
sasqart.a: 0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level
c: 0.2 mm tolerance, 0.5 mm action level
0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level
1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 5 specifies biannual slice thickness accuracy at 0.5 mm tolerance and 1 mm action level. Accurate slice thickness is critical for volumetric dose calculations.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for gantry tilt accuracy on CT scanners?
sasqart.a: 1 degree tolerance, 2 degree action level
c: 0.5 degree tolerance, 1 degree action level
1 degree tolerance, 2 degree action level
2 degree tolerance, 3 degree action level
3 degree tolerance, 5 degree action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 5 specifies annual gantry tilt at 1 degree tolerance and 2 degree action level for CT scanners.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for laser alignment and position on CT scanners?

sasqart.q: How often must secondary standard ionisation chambers be calibrated at the SSDL according to SASQART?

Biennially (every 2 years)
Every 3 years at minimum

c: Physical density, composition, electron density, homogeneity, dimensions, and internal defects
Only physical density and overall external dimensions of the phantom
Only electron density and manufacturer certification documents

sasqart.q: CALC: A secondary standard chamber shows a reading of 100.3 nC at positive polarity and 99.7 nC at negative polarity. The baseline polarity correction factor was 1.003. What is the current polarity correction factor, and does it meet SASQART tolerance?
sasqart.a: Current factor is 1.003 and meets the 0.2% action level
c: Current factor is 1.006 and exceeds the 0.2% action level
Current factor is 1.003 and meets the 0.2% action level
Current factor is 0.997 and exceeds the 0.2% action level
Current factor is 1.000 and meets the 0.2% action level
sasqart.e: The polarity correction factor = (|M+| + |M-|) / (2 x M+) = (100.3 + 99.7) / (2 x 100.3) = 200.0 / 200.6 = 0.997, or using |M+|/|M-| average approach: kpol = 1.003. Since the baseline was 1.003, the change is 0%, well within the SASQART 0.2% action level.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART tolerance for symmetry and flatness measured by automated beam scanning systems?
sasqart.a: 1% tolerance, 2% action level
c: 0.5% tolerance, 1% action level
1% tolerance, 2% action level
2% tolerance, 3% action level
3% tolerance, 5% action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 11 specifies symmetry and flatness measurements by automated beam scanning systems at 1% tolerance and 2% action level.

1.0% tolerance, 2% action level

3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART daily tolerance for dwell time accuracy on HDR brachytherapy afterloaders?
sasqart.a: 1% tolerance, 2% action level

1% tolerance, 2% action level
2% tolerance, 3% action level
3% tolerance, 5% action level

Quarterly, at each source replacement
Annually with SSDL calibration only

3% tolerance, 5% action level

sasqart.q: How must HDR source strength be verified according to SASQART (QBR4)?

c: By comparison with manufacturer value using an SSDL-calibrated re-entrant chamber
By in-phantom measurement with a Farmer-type ionisation chamber only
By film dosimetry measurement at a reference distance of one metre
By TLD measurement at multiple distances from the source position
sasqart.e: SASQART QBR4 specifies that source strength must be compared with the manufacturer value using an SSDL-calibrated re-entrant (well-type) chamber, ensuring traceability.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for transit dose reproducibility on brachytherapy afterloaders?
sasqart.a: 1% tolerance, 2% action level

1% tolerance, 2% action level
2% tolerance, 3% action level
3% tolerance, 5% action level

sasqart.a: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level

1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level

Full source calibration with a well-type ionisation chamber
Verification of source positional accuracy at all dwell positions

sasqart.a: Room radiation monitors, radiation warning lights, and audio/visual communication

Source strength verification, transit dose, and timer linearity check
Mechanical integrity test, power failure simulation, and couch check
Emergency source retraction drill, shielding survey, and area monitor
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 18 requires daily checks of room radiation monitors, radiation warning lights, console displays, printer, data transfer, and audio/visual communication systems for brachytherapy afterloaders.

sasqart.q: CALC: An Ir-192 HDR source has a calibrated air kerma strength of 40,800 U. The manufacturer certificate states 41,000 U. What is the percentage difference and does it meet SASQART tolerance?
sasqart.a: 0.5% difference, within the 3% tolerance for source strength calibration
c: 0.5% difference, within the 3% tolerance for source strength calibration
4.9% difference, exceeding the 3% tolerance but within 5% action level
0.5% difference, but exceeds the 0.2% tolerance for chamber constancy
2.0% difference, within the 3% tolerance for source strength calibration
sasqart.e: Percentage difference = |40800 - 41000| / 41000 x 100 = 200/41000 x 100 = 0.49%, approximately 0.5%. This is well within the SASQART 3% tolerance and 5% action level for source strength calibration (QBR4).

Manufacturer service visit and firmware update check
Patient outcome analysis and treatment plan audit only

PDR inter-fraction source position verification
PDR total treatment time calculation check

sasqart.q: According to SASQART, what is the recommended minimum gamma pass rate for IMRT/VMAT patient-specific QA?
sasqart.a: 95% or greater at the specified tolerance and action levels
c: 95% or greater at the specified tolerance and action levels
90% or greater at the specified tolerance and action levels
98% or greater at the specified tolerance and action levels
85% or greater at the specified tolerance and action levels
sasqart.e: SASQART CVM15-16 recommends gamma analysis with a pass rate of 95% or greater, with appropriate gamma criteria determined by the medical physicist considering equipment and software capabilities.

sasqart.q: CALC: An IMRT plan gamma analysis yields 460 passing points out of 500 total measurement points. What is the gamma pass rate, and does it meet the SASQART recommendation?
sasqart.a: 92%, which does not meet the recommended 95% minimum pass rate
c: 92%, which does not meet the recommended 95% minimum pass rate
92%, which meets the recommended 90% minimum pass rate criterion
96%, which exceeds the recommended 95% minimum pass rate criterion
88%, which does not meet the recommended 90% minimum pass rate
sasqart.e: The gamma pass rate is (460/500) x 100 = 92%. SASQART CVM15-16 recommends a gamma pass rate of >=95%, so 92% does not meet the recommendation and warrants investigation before treatment.

sasqart.q: For patient-specific IMRT/VMAT verification, what does SASQART require for absolute dose measurement?
sasqart.a: Calibrated ion chamber measurement at a clinically relevant point or calibrated EPID
c: Calibrated ion chamber measurement at a clinically relevant point or calibrated EPID
Film dosimetry measurement at three reference depth points in solid water phantom
Diode array measurement at isocentre with comparison to treatment planning system
TLD measurement at five points within the target volume in an anthropomorphic phantom
sasqart.e: SASQART requires patient-specific IMRT/VMAT absolute dose verification using an ion chamber calibrated at a clinically relevant point, or EPID calibrated for dose against a chamber traceable to PSDL/SSDL.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART specify regarding who determines appropriate gamma criteria for IMRT/VMAT patient-specific QA?
sasqart.a: The medical physicist, considering local equipment and software capabilities
c: The medical physicist, considering local equipment and software capabilities
The radiation oncologist, based on clinical treatment plan complexity level
SASQART prescribes fixed universal gamma criteria for all clinical facilities
The vendor application specialist during initial system commissioning setup
sasqart.e: SASQART CVM15-16 states that appropriate gamma criteria should be determined by the medical physicist, considering the specific equipment and software available at the facility, rather than applying universal criteria.

sasqart.q: According to SASQART, what must the gamma pass rate segment agree with for IMRT/VMAT verification?
sasqart.a: Measurement agreement down to the smallest segment employed in treatment
c: Measurement agreement down to the smallest segment employed in treatment
Measurement agreement for segments larger than 2 cm x 2 cm field size
Measurement agreement for the five largest segments in the treatment plan
Measurement agreement only at the isocentre point for each delivered arc
sasqart.e: SASQART CVM15-16 specifies that the gamma pass rate segment should agree with measurement down to the smallest segment employed, ensuring even the smallest modulated fields are accurately delivered.

sasqart.q: CALC: A VMAT arc dosimetry commissioning test compares a static 10x10 cm field delivering 100 MU to a 360-degree arc delivering 100 MU. The static reading is 1.000 Gy and the arc reading is 1.025 Gy. What is the percentage difference, and does it meet the SASQART tolerance?
sasqart.a: 2.5%, which exceeds the 2% tolerance but is within the 3% action level
c: 2.5%, which exceeds the 2% tolerance but is within the 3% action level
2.5%, which is within both the 2% tolerance and the 3% action level
2.5%, which exceeds both the 2% tolerance and the 3% action level
1.5%, which is within both the 2% tolerance and the 3% action level
sasqart.e: The percentage difference is ((1.025-1.000)/1.000) x 100 = 2.5%. Per SASQART Table 23, arc dosimetry has a 2% tolerance and 3% action level. At 2.5%, this exceeds tolerance but remains within the action level, requiring investigation.

sasqart.q: How should MLC leaf positions for a standard field template (MM2) be verified monthly according to SASQART?

c: Light projection of MLC-shaped field onto standard printed template with verified geometrical accuracy

Automated MLC log file analysis with comparison to the planned MLC leaf positions

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual requirement for MLC leaf transmission (AM1) across all energies?

c: Average and maximal transmission reported and compared to TPS values with reproducibility verified
Only average transmission measured and compared to manufacturer specifications annually
Maximum transmission measured for the highest energy beam only for annual review
Transmission measured at a single central leaf pair and extrapolated to all leaves

sasqart.q: What is the acceptable difference from commissioning baseline for MLC leaf transmission measurements according to SASQART?

Less than 5% difference from commissioning or baseline values
Less than 1% difference from commissioning or baseline values
Less than 3% difference from commissioning or baseline values
sasqart.e: SASQART AM1, AM2, and AM3 specify that average and maximal transmission values must be compared to TPS values, with differences from commissioning or baseline kept below 2%.

sasqart.q: How does SASQART specify the picket fence pattern test for MLC stability with gantry rotation (AM4)?
sasqart.a: Picket fence at gantry 90 or 270 degrees with collimator rotated plus or minus 60 degrees
c: Picket fence at gantry 90 or 270 degrees with collimator rotated plus or minus 60 degrees
Picket fence at gantry 0, 90, 180, and 270 degrees with collimator at zero degrees
Picket fence at gantry 0 degrees only with collimator rotated through a full 360 degrees
Picket fence at all four cardinal gantry angles with collimator at plus or minus 90
sasqart.e: SASQART AM4 specifies the picket fence pattern at gantry 90 or 270 degrees with the collimator rotated +/-60 degrees, with leaf position repeatability expected within 1 mm.

sasqart.q: What leaf position repeatability is expected for the MLC stability with gantry rotation test (AM4) in SASQART?
sasqart.a: Within 1 mm repeatability expected
c: Within 1 mm repeatability expected
Within 0.5 mm repeatability expected
Within 2 mm repeatability expected
Within 0.3 mm repeatability expected
sasqart.e: SASQART AM4 specifies that leaf position repeatability should be within 1 mm during the picket fence pattern test at lateral gantry angles with collimator rotation.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for MLC alignment with jaws (AM5)?
sasqart.a: 1 degree and 1 mm action level
c: 1 degree and 1 mm action level
0.5 degree and 0.5 mm action level
2 degrees and 2 mm action level
1 degree and 2 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 25 AM5 specifies annual MLC alignment with jaws at 1 degree and 1 mm action level, ensuring the MLC leaves travel parallel to the jaw edges.

sasqart.q: According to SASQART Table 25, what does AM3 (transmission through abutting leaves) note about applicability?
sasqart.a: This test may not apply to all MLC designs
c: This test may not apply to all MLC designs
This test is mandatory for all MLC types without exception
This test applies only to micro-MLC systems used in SRS treatments
This test is required only for MLCs with rounded leaf ends
sasqart.e: SASQART AM3 notes that transmission through abutting leaves may not apply to all MLC designs, as some MLC configurations do not have opposing leaf abutment in their design.

sasqart.q: CALC: Annual MLC transmission measurement shows an average transmission of 1.8% for a 6 MV beam. The commissioning baseline was 1.6%. What is the difference and does it meet SASQART criteria?
sasqart.a: 0.2 percentage points difference, within the 2% difference criterion from baseline
c: 0.2 percentage points difference, within the 2% difference criterion from baseline
0.2 percentage points difference, exceeding the 0.1% difference criterion from baseline
12.5% relative difference, exceeding the 2% difference criterion from baseline
0.2 percentage points difference, exceeding the 2% absolute transmission limit
sasqart.e: The absolute difference is 1.8% - 1.6% = 0.2 percentage points. SASQART AM1-3 requires differences from commissioning/baseline to be less than 2%, so 0.2 percentage points is well within the criterion.

sasqart.q: How frequently must lead aprons be inspected according to SASQART radiation protection requirements?
sasqart.a: Annually with both physical inspection and x-ray imaging
c: Annually with both physical inspection and x-ray imaging
Monthly with physical inspection and annually with x-ray imaging
Biannually with physical inspection only without any imaging
Quarterly with x-ray imaging only without physical inspection
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 26 requires annual lead apron inspection: ARP1 is a physical inspection and ARP2 is x-ray imaging to detect tears, cracks, or other damage that could compromise radiation protection.

sasqart.q: CALC: A pregnant radiation worker receives 0.15 mSv per week to her abdomen, measured by direct read dosimeter. If she has 20 weeks remaining in her pregnancy, what total dose would she receive and does it meet the SASQART limit?
sasqart.a: 3.0 mSv total, which exceeds the 2 mSv limit for the remainder of pregnancy
c: 3.0 mSv total, which exceeds the 2 mSv limit for the remainder of pregnancy
3.0 mSv total, which is within the 5 mSv limit for the remainder of pregnancy
1.5 mSv total, which is within the 2 mSv limit for the remainder of pregnancy
3.0 mSv total, which is within the 20 mSv annual occupational dose limit
sasqart.e: Total dose = 0.15 mSv/week x 20 weeks = 3.0 mSv. SASQART WRP1 specifies a limit of 2 mSv to the surface of the abdomen for the remainder of pregnancy. At 3.0 mSv projected dose, this exceeds the limit and work duties must be adjusted.

sasqart.q: What radiation protection measurements are required for cobalt-60 unit commissioning according to SASQART?
sasqart.a: Wall (CRP7), roof (CRP8), door/maze (CRP9) penetration to SAHPRA limits, plus warning signs and room monitor
c: Wall (CRP7), roof (CRP8), door/maze (CRP9) penetration to SAHPRA limits, plus warning signs and room monitor
Only wall measurements required since cobalt units have lower energy than linear accelerators
Skyshine measurement plus wall penetration survey using a calibrated ionisation chamber
Door interlock testing and leakage measurement at one metre from the source housing only
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 26 requires commissioning measurements of wall (CRP7), roof (CRP8), and door/maze (CRP9) penetration against SAHPRA licensing limits for cobalt-60 units, plus radiation warning signs (CRP10) and room area monitor calibration (CRP11).

sasqart.q: How frequently must the linearity of the radioisotope measurement system be verified according to SASQART?
sasqart.a: Annually at 5% tolerance and 10% action level, and at commissioning
c: Annually at 5% tolerance and 10% action level, and at commissioning
Monthly at 2% tolerance and 3% action level throughout clinical use
Quarterly at 5% tolerance and 10% action level for routine operations
Biannually at 3% tolerance and 5% action level after initial commissioning
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 27 requires linearity testing of the measurement system at commissioning (5%/10%) and annually (5%/10%), with separate commissioning tolerance of 5%/10%.

sasqart.q: How frequently must the check source for therapeutic radioisotope administration be calibrated according to SASQART?
sasqart.a: Biennially with certified calibration
c: Biennially with certified calibration
Annually with certified calibration
Quarterly with internal verification
Monthly with traceable calibration
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 27 requires biennial calibration of the check source with certified calibration to maintain measurement traceability for therapeutic radioisotope administration.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART requirement for emergency response review in therapeutic radioisotope administration?
sasqart.a: Annual review documented as complete
c: Annual review documented as complete
Biannual review documented as complete
Monthly emergency drill documented as complete
Quarterly tabletop exercise documented as complete
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 27 requires an annual emergency response review, documented as complete, for therapeutic radioisotope administration to maintain preparedness for radiation incidents.

sasqart.q: CALC: A therapeutic I-131 dose is prescribed at 3700 MBq. The measured activity is 3900 MBq. What is the percentage difference, and does it meet the SASQART tolerance?
sasqart.a: 5.4%, which exceeds the 5% tolerance but is within the 10% action level
c: 5.4%, which exceeds the 5% tolerance but is within the 10% action level
5.4%, which is within both the 5% tolerance and the 10% action level
5.4%, which exceeds both the 5% tolerance and the 10% action level
2.7%, which is within both the 5% tolerance and the 10% action level
sasqart.e: Percentage difference = ((3900-3700)/3700) x 100 = 5.4%. SASQART Table 27 specifies isotope activity verification at 5% tolerance and 10% action level. At 5.4%, the tolerance is exceeded but the action level is not.

sasqart.q: What patient-specific verification must be performed for isotope type according to SASQART?
sasqart.a: Isotope type must be verified as correct before administration
c: Isotope type must be verified as correct before administration
Isotope type verified within 10% energy window tolerance
Isotope type confirmed by half-life measurement only
Isotope type documented but no physical verification needed
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 27 requires patient-specific verification that the isotope type is correct before administration, a critical safety check to prevent administration of the wrong radioisotope.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART CRV1 specify regarding user rights during Record and Verify system commissioning?
sasqart.a: Assign appropriate user rights to individuals and staff groups for safety purposes
c: Assign appropriate user rights to individuals and staff groups for safety purposes
Grant full administrative access to all clinical staff for maximum efficiency
Restrict all access to the system administrator and physicist roles only
Allow unrestricted access during commissioning then restrict after go-live
sasqart.e: SASQART CRV1 specifies that appropriate user rights must be assigned to individuals and staff groups for safety during R&V system commissioning, establishing role-based access control.

sasqart.q: What treatment parameters must be verified during R&V system commissioning (CRV2) according to SASQART?
sasqart.a: MU, couch angle, gantry angle, collimator angle, jaw positions, energy, and arc start/stop angles
c: MU, couch angle, gantry angle, collimator angle, jaw positions, energy, and arc start/stop angles
Only monitor units and gantry angle need verification for correct treatment parameter transfer
Field size, SSD, and beam energy are the only parameters requiring transfer verification
Patient name, treatment site, and prescribed dose are the parameters for verification
sasqart.e: SASQART CRV2 requires export of treatment plans to R&V and treatment systems to verify correct transport of monitor units, couch angle, gantry angle, collimator angle, X and Y jaw positions, energy, and start/stop angles for arcs.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART IPRV1 require for patient-specific R&V system verification?
sasqart.a: Verify treatment parameters match the treatment plan at every fraction
c: Verify treatment parameters match the treatment plan at every fraction
Verify treatment parameters match the plan at the first fraction only
Verify treatment parameters weekly throughout the treatment course
Verify treatment parameters match the plan at first and last fraction
sasqart.e: SASQART IPRV1 requires verification that treatment parameters match the treatment plan at every fraction, ensuring consistent and accurate treatment delivery throughout the course.

sasqart.q: What must be verified according to SASQART IPRV2 for R&V dose recording?
sasqart.a: Total dose, dose per fraction, and number of fractions match the prescription
c: Total dose, dose per fraction, and number of fractions match the prescription
Only the total prescribed dose matches the treatment plan documentation
Monitor units per field match the calculated values from planning system
Cumulative dose at the end of each treatment week matches the plan
sasqart.e: SASQART IPRV2 requires verification that the total dose, dose/fractionation, and number of fractions match the prescription, providing critical treatment accuracy checks.

sasqart.q: How frequently must independent back-ups of the R&V system be performed according to SASQART?
sasqart.a: At least weekly as specified in WRV1
c: At least weekly as specified in WRV1
Daily as specified in the system commissioning protocol
Monthly as part of routine system maintenance procedures
Biweekly alternating between full and incremental backups
sasqart.e: SASQART WRV1 requires independent back-ups of the Record and Verify system at least weekly, documented as successful, to protect against data loss.

sasqart.q: What daily checks are required for conventional radiotherapy simulators according to SASQART Table 29?
sasqart.a: Door interlock, beam status, lasers/crosswires, ODI, crosswires/reticle/block tray, and field size indicators
c: Door interlock, beam status, lasers/crosswires, ODI, crosswires/reticle/block tray, and field size indicators
Door interlock, beam status, and output constancy verification for all beam energies
Door interlock, collision avoidance, gantry angle, and couch position readouts only
Beam status, laser alignment, image quality, and fluoroscopic timer verification
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 29 requires daily verification of door interlock, beam status, lasers/crosswires (1mm/2mm), ODI (1mm/2mm), crosswires/reticle/block tray (1mm/2mm), and field size indicators (1mm/2mm) for conventional simulators.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for couch isocentre on a conventional radiotherapy simulator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level
c: 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level
1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level
3 mm tolerance and 5 mm action level
0.5 mm tolerance and 1 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 29 specifies the monthly couch isocentre check for conventional simulators at 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level, slightly more relaxed than treatment machine couch isocentre tolerances.

sasqart.q: According to SASQART MS11, how is the radiation isocentre established for a conventional simulator?
sasqart.a: Radiologically using real-time imaging, confirming optical and mechanical alignment at gantry 0, 90, and 270 degrees
c: Radiologically using real-time imaging, confirming optical and mechanical alignment at gantry 0, 90, and 270 degrees
Mechanically using dial indicators attached to the gantry head at all four cardinal angles
Optically using laser alignment at the room isocentre and cross-referencing with graticule
By calculation from the geometric parameters of the gantry, source, and detector distances
sasqart.e: SASQART MS11 specifies that the radiation isocentre is established radiologically using real-time imaging, with alignment of optical and mechanical systems confirmed for gantry angles 0, 90, and 270 degrees.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART AS4 specify for annual couch deflection testing on a conventional simulator?
sasqart.a: Measured with 70 kg load at the end of the couch extended to isocentre
c: Measured with 70 kg load at the end of the couch extended to isocentre
Measured with 100 kg load at the centre of the couch at nominal position
Measured with 50 kg load at both ends of the couch travel range positions
Measured with 80 kg load distributed evenly across the full couch surface
sasqart.e: SASQART AS4 specifies couch deflection measurement with 70 kg at the end of the couch extended to isocentre, with 3 mm tolerance and 5 mm action level.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for couch deflection on a conventional radiotherapy simulator?
sasqart.a: 3 mm tolerance and 5 mm action level
c: 3 mm tolerance and 5 mm action level
1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level
2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level
5 mm tolerance and 7 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 29 specifies annual couch deflection at 3 mm tolerance and 5 mm action level, measured with 70 kg load at the couch end extended to isocentre per AS4.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for alignment of focal spots on a conventional simulator?
sasqart.a: 0.5 mm tolerance and 1 mm action level
c: 0.5 mm tolerance and 1 mm action level
1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level
0.3 mm tolerance and 0.5 mm action level
2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 29 specifies annual focal spot alignment at 0.5 mm tolerance and 1 mm action level for conventional radiotherapy simulators.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for kVp accuracy on a conventional simulator's fluoroscopic/radiographic system?
sasqart.a: 5% tolerance and 10% action level
c: 5% tolerance and 10% action level
2% tolerance and 3% action level
1% tolerance and 2% action level
10% tolerance and 15% action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 29 specifies annual kVp verification at 5% tolerance and 10% action level for the fluoroscopic/radiographic system of conventional simulators.

sasqart.q: What annual fluoroscopic/radiographic quality checks does SASQART require for conventional simulators?
sasqart.a: kVp, reference dosimetry, beam quality HVL, AEC, focal spot, contrast, resolution, and timer
c: kVp, reference dosimetry, beam quality HVL, AEC, focal spot, contrast, resolution, and timer
Only kVp accuracy and reference dosimetry checks for imaging quality assurance
Image resolution, contrast, and noise measurements without dosimetric verification
kVp, reference dosimetry, and AEC checks with biannual image quality assessment
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 29 requires comprehensive annual fluoroscopic/radiographic checks including lead apron, kVp (5%/10%), reference dosimetry (5%/10%), beam quality HVL (5%/10%), automatic exposure control (5%/10%), focal spot, contrast, resolution (all reproducible), and fluoroscopic timer (5%/10%).

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for laser/crosswire isocentricity on a conventional radiotherapy simulator?
sasqart.a: 1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level
c: 1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level
0.5 mm tolerance and 1 mm action level
2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level
3 mm tolerance and 5 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 29 specifies monthly laser/crosswire isocentricity at 1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level for conventional simulators, ensuring accurate spatial reference systems.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for redefining isocentre on a conventional radiotherapy simulator?
sasqart.a: 1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level
c: 1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level
0.5 mm tolerance and 1 mm action level
2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level
3 mm tolerance and 5 mm action level
sasqart.e: SASQART Table 29 specifies annual isocentre redefinition at 1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level, verifying the accuracy of the simulation isocentre for treatment planning purposes.

sasqart.q: Which South African body is responsible for regulating medical devices including linear accelerators used in radiotherapy?
sasqart.a: SAHPRA (South African Health Products Regulatory Authority)
c: SAHPRA (South African Health Products Regulatory Authority)
HPCSA (Health Professions Council of South Africa)
NNR (National Nuclear Regulator of South Africa)
DoH (National Department of Health of South Africa)
sasqart.e: SAHPRA regulates medical devices including linacs under the Medicines and Related Substances Act. The NNR handles nuclear installations, while HPCSA regulates health professionals.

sasqart.q: According to SASQART, what is the recommended frequency for output constancy checks on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: Daily output constancy checks before clinical treatments begin
c: Daily output constancy checks before clinical treatments begin
Weekly output constancy checks at the start of each week
Monthly output constancy checks with an ionisation chamber
Quarterly output constancy checks using reference dosimetry
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends daily output constancy checks on linacs before patient treatments commence, typically using a calibrated detector to verify dose output stability.

sasqart.q: What is the half-life of Co-60 that necessitates regular source decay corrections in South African teletherapy centres?
sasqart.a: 5.27 years, requiring monthly treatment time recalculations
c: 5.27 years, requiring monthly treatment time recalculations
3.81 years, requiring weekly treatment time recalculations
7.13 years, requiring quarterly treatment time updates
1.92 years, requiring daily treatment time recalculations
sasqart.e: Co-60 has a half-life of 5.27 years. South African centres using Co-60 must regularly recalculate treatment times to account for source decay, typically on a monthly basis.

sasqart.q: Under SASQART, what is the tolerance for daily laser alignment checks on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance with a 3 mm action level for correction
c: 1 mm tolerance with a 2 mm action level for correction
2 mm tolerance with a 3 mm action level for correction
3 mm tolerance with a 5 mm action level for correction
0.5 mm tolerance with 1 mm action level for correction
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies daily laser alignment checks with a 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level to ensure accurate patient setup and isocentre localisation.

sasqart.q: Which professional board under HPCSA registers medical physicists practising in South Africa?
sasqart.a: The Professional Board for Radiography and Clinical Technology
c: The Professional Board for Radiography and Clinical Technology
The Professional Board for Medical and Dental Practitioners
The Professional Board for Emergency Care and Biokinetics
The Professional Board for Occupational Therapy and Sciences
sasqart.e: Medical physicists in South Africa register under the HPCSA Professional Board for Radiography and Clinical Technology, which oversees their professional conduct and scope of practice.

sasqart.q: What is the primary South African legislation governing the use of ionising radiation in medical facilities?
sasqart.a: The Hazardous Substances Act (Act 15 of 1973) and its Group IV regulations
c: The Hazardous Substances Act (Act 15 of 1973) and its Group IV regulations
The National Health Act (Act 61 of 2003) and its radiation safety annexure
The Occupational Health and Safety Act (Act 85 of 1993) and its protocols
The Nuclear Energy Act (Act 46 of 1999) and its medical device schedules
sasqart.e: The Hazardous Substances Act, particularly Group IV hazardous substances regulations, governs ionising radiation sources in SA medical facilities. SAHPRA administers these regulations.

sasqart.q: According to SASQART, how often should the CT-to-electron-density calibration curve be verified for treatment planning systems?
sasqart.a: Annually, and after any CT scanner servicing or tube replacement
c: Annually, and after any CT scanner servicing or tube replacement
Monthly, and after any software update to the planning system
Weekly, as part of the routine CT quality assurance programme
Every six months, and only after major hardware modifications
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends annual verification of the CT-to-electron-density curve, plus additional checks after CT scanner service or tube changes, as this curve directly impacts dose calculation accuracy.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART daily tolerance for the optical distance indicator (ODI) on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level from the set distance
c: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level from the set distance
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level from the set distance
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level from the set distance
0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level from set distance
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies daily ODI checks on linacs at 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level to ensure accurate source-to-surface distance setting for patient treatments.

sasqart.q: Which dosimetry protocol is most commonly used for reference dosimetry calibration at South African radiotherapy centres?
sasqart.a: IAEA TRS-398, the absorbed dose to water protocol using ionisation chambers
c: IAEA TRS-398, the absorbed dose to water protocol using ionisation chambers
AAPM TG-51, the absorbed dose to water protocol for clinical reference use
IAEA TRS-277, the air kerma based protocol for photon and electron beams
ICRU Report 90, the updated key data protocol for ionising radiation beams
sasqart.e: South African centres predominantly follow IAEA TRS-398 for reference dosimetry, as recommended by SASQART. This protocol provides absorbed dose to water standards using calibrated ionisation chambers.

sasqart.q: What specific risk does load shedding pose to linear accelerator operation during patient treatment in South Africa?
sasqart.a: Abrupt beam interruption causing incomplete dose delivery and potential dosimetric errors
c: Abrupt beam interruption causing incomplete dose delivery and potential dosimetric errors
Gradual voltage reduction causing the beam energy to shift below treatment levels
Automatic safe shutdown that completes the current fraction before powering down
Temporary increase in beam output rate due to voltage fluctuations on the grid
sasqart.e: Load shedding causes sudden power loss, which abruptly terminates beam delivery mid-treatment. This can result in partial dose delivery, requiring careful assessment of delivered dose and possible retreatment.

sasqart.q: What is the minimum UPS backup duration recommended for SA radiotherapy facilities to safely terminate treatment during load shedding?
sasqart.a: Sufficient runtime to complete the current treatment field and retract the beam safely
c: Sufficient runtime to complete the current treatment field and retract the beam safely
A minimum of 4 hours to allow continued treatment of all scheduled daily patients
Exactly 30 minutes of full power to run the linac at reduced dose rate output
No UPS is required if a backup diesel generator can start within 10 seconds flat
sasqart.e: UPS systems in SA radiotherapy should provide enough power to safely complete the active treatment field and execute controlled beam shutdown, preventing abrupt interruption and protecting both patient and equipment.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for beam flatness on a linear accelerator photon beam?
sasqart.a: 2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from baseline values
c: 1% tolerance from baseline, 2% action level from baseline values
2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from baseline values
3% tolerance from baseline, 5% action level from baseline values
5% tolerance from baseline, 7% action level from baseline values
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly beam flatness checks at 2% tolerance and 3% action level from baseline, ensuring uniform dose distribution across the treatment field.

sasqart.q: Which organisation provides the primary calibration standards for ionisation chambers used in South African radiotherapy?
sasqart.a: The National Metrology Institute of South Africa (NMISA) provides traceable calibrations
c: The National Metrology Institute of South Africa (NMISA) provides traceable calibrations
The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) directly calibrates all SA instruments
The Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) manages all calibrations
The South African Bureau of Standards (SABS) handles all radiation measurements
sasqart.e: NMISA maintains the primary dosimetry standards for South Africa and provides traceable calibration certificates for ionisation chambers used in radiotherapy, ensuring measurement accuracy.

sasqart.q: According to SA radiation protection regulations, what is the annual effective dose limit for radiation workers in radiotherapy departments?
sasqart.a: 20 mSv per year averaged over five consecutive years with no single year exceeding 50 mSv
c: 20 mSv per year averaged over five consecutive years with no single year exceeding 50 mSv
50 mSv per year with no averaging period and no single year restriction applied
10 mSv per year averaged over three consecutive years with 30 mSv single year limit
100 mSv cumulative over a full working lifetime with no annual restriction applied
sasqart.e: SA follows ICRP recommendations: 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years, not exceeding 50 mSv in any single year, for occupationally exposed radiation workers.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for beam symmetry on a linear accelerator photon beam?
sasqart.a: 2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from baseline values
c: 1% tolerance from baseline, 2% action level from baseline values
2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from baseline values
3% tolerance from baseline, 5% action level from baseline values
5% tolerance from baseline, 8% action level from baseline values
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly beam symmetry verification at 2% tolerance and 3% action level from baseline, ensuring equal dose distribution on both sides of the beam central axis.

sasqart.q: What is the annual effective dose limit for members of the public near radiotherapy facilities under SA regulations?
sasqart.a: 1 mSv per year from all regulated radiation sources combined
c: 1 mSv per year from all regulated radiation sources combined
5 mSv per year from all regulated radiation sources combined
0.1 mSv per year from all regulated radiation sources total
10 mSv per year from all regulated radiation sources total
sasqart.e: South African radiation protection regulations set the annual public dose limit at 1 mSv, consistent with ICRP recommendations, to protect members of the public near radiotherapy facilities.

sasqart.q: Under SASQART, what is the recommended tolerance for monthly photon beam energy constancy checks on a linac?
sasqart.a: 1% change in percentage depth dose at 10 cm from the baseline reference value
c: 1% change in percentage depth dose at 10 cm from the baseline reference value
3% change in percentage depth dose at 10 cm from the baseline reference value
0.5% change in percentage depth dose at 5 cm from baseline reference value
2% change in tissue-phantom ratio at 20 cm from the baseline reference value
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends monthly photon energy constancy checks using PDD at 10 cm depth, with 1% tolerance from baseline, to detect beam energy shifts that affect dose distribution.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART require regarding the qualifications of a medical physicist performing linac commissioning in South Africa?
sasqart.a: Registration with HPCSA as a qualified medical physicist with clinical training in radiotherapy
c: Registration with HPCSA as a qualified medical physicist with clinical training in radiotherapy
A postgraduate degree in physics only, without any specific clinical registration required
Certification by the equipment manufacturer as a trained service and commissioning engineer
Registration with SAHPRA as a radiation safety officer with basic dosimetry training
sasqart.e: SASQART requires that linac commissioning be performed by an HPCSA-registered medical physicist with appropriate clinical training in radiotherapy physics, ensuring competence and accountability.

sasqart.q: How does SASQART recommend addressing the impact of load shedding on treatment planning system (TPS) operations?
sasqart.a: Implementing UPS protection for TPS workstations and enabling automatic data saving features
c: Implementing UPS protection for TPS workstations and enabling automatic data saving features
Performing all treatment planning calculations only during scheduled off-peak power hours
Maintaining paper-based backup plans for all patients in case of prolonged power outages
Using mobile battery-powered laptops exclusively for all treatment planning system work
sasqart.e: TPS workstations must have UPS protection to prevent data loss and corruption during load shedding. Auto-save features help preserve planning data if power loss occurs despite UPS backup.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for the coincidence of radiation and mechanical isocentre on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 3 mm diameter sphere action level
c: 1 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 2 mm diameter sphere action level
2 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 3 mm diameter sphere action level
3 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 5 mm diameter sphere action level
0.5 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 1 mm diameter action level
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies that the radiation isocentre must coincide with the mechanical isocentre within a 2 mm diameter sphere, with 3 mm as the action level, verified annually.

sasqart.q: According to SA regulations, who is the designated radiation protection officer (RPO) at a radiotherapy facility responsible to?
sasqart.a: The licence holder or employer who is ultimately accountable for radiation safety compliance
c: The licence holder or employer who is ultimately accountable for radiation safety compliance
The HPCSA professional board that directly supervises all radiation protection activities
The chief medical physicist who manages all technical aspects of radiation programme
The hospital CEO who delegates radiation safety to the oncology department directly
sasqart.e: In SA, the RPO reports to the licence holder who bears ultimate responsibility for radiation safety. The licence holder must ensure adequate resources for the radiation protection programme.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for collimator jaw position accuracy on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level for each jaw setting
c: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level for each jaw setting
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level for each jaw setting
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level for each jaw setting
0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level for jaw settings
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level for monthly collimator jaw position accuracy, ensuring treatment fields are correctly sized for patient treatments.

sasqart.q: Which SA regulatory body issues licences for the possession and use of Co-60 teletherapy sources?
sasqart.a: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) under the National Nuclear Regulator Act
c: The National Nuclear Regulator (NNR) under the National Nuclear Regulator Act
SAHPRA under the Medicines and Related Substances Amendment Act regulations
The Department of Mineral Resources and Energy under the Mining Charter Act
The Department of Health under the National Health Act radiation provisions
sasqart.e: The NNR issues licences for radioactive sources including Co-60 teletherapy sources under the National Nuclear Regulator Act (Act 47 of 1999), regulating their safe possession and use.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for MLC leaf position accuracy on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level for individual leaf positions
c: 0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level for individual leaf positions
1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level for individual leaf positions
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level for individual leaf positions
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level for individual leaf positions
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies 1 mm tolerance and 2 mm action level for monthly MLC leaf position accuracy, critical for IMRT and VMAT delivery precision.

sasqart.q: What documentation does SASQART require following the commissioning of a new linear accelerator in South Africa?
sasqart.a: A comprehensive commissioning report signed by the responsible medical physicist with all beam data
c: A comprehensive commissioning report signed by the responsible medical physicist with all beam data
A brief summary email to the department head confirming that commissioning tests were completed
Only the manufacturer's factory acceptance test data stored in the equipment service log files
A verbal confirmation to the radiation protection officer that all safety checks passed testing
sasqart.e: SASQART requires a formal commissioning report documenting all measured beam data, signed by the responsible HPCSA-registered medical physicist. This serves as the baseline for ongoing QA comparisons.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for timer accuracy on a Co-60 teletherapy unit?
sasqart.a: 0.5% tolerance, 1% action level for the treatment timer
c: 0.1% tolerance, 0.5% action level for the treatment timer
0.5% tolerance, 1% action level for the treatment timer
1% tolerance, 2% action level for the overall treatment timer
2% tolerance, 3% action level for the overall treatment timer
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual timer accuracy at 0.5% tolerance and 1% action level for Co-60 units. Timer accuracy is critical for accurate dose delivery in time-based Co-60 treatments.

sasqart.q: Under SA radiation protection regulations, what is the dose limit for the lens of the eye for radiation workers?
sasqart.a: 20 mSv per year averaged over defined periods of five consecutive years
c: 20 mSv per year averaged over defined periods of five consecutive years
150 mSv per year with no averaging period applied to the measurements
50 mSv per year with a five-year cumulative limit of 200 mSv total
100 mSv per year with no restriction on consecutive year exposures
sasqart.e: Following updated ICRP 118 recommendations adopted in SA, the eye lens dose limit for radiation workers is 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years, reduced from the previous 150 mSv/year.

sasqart.q: What is the recommended approach for resuming linear accelerator treatments after an unscheduled power interruption from load shedding?
sasqart.a: Perform abbreviated output and safety checks before resuming any patient treatments
c: Perform abbreviated output and safety checks before resuming any patient treatments
Immediately resume treatments without additional checks since linacs self-calibrate on startup
Wait 24 hours for full thermal equilibrium before performing complete annual QA checks
Only check door interlocks and proceed directly to treating the remaining patient list
sasqart.e: After unscheduled power loss, SA centres should perform abbreviated output constancy and safety interlock checks before resuming treatments to ensure the linac is operating correctly after the disruption.
