sasqart.q: According to SASQART, what is the tolerance for daily output constancy of a linear accelerator photon beam?
sasqart.a: 3% tolerance from baseline, 5% action level from baseline output value
c: 1% tolerance from baseline, 2% action level from baseline output value
2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from baseline output value
3% tolerance from baseline, 5% action level from baseline output value
5% tolerance from baseline, 7% action level from baseline output value
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies daily output constancy at 3% tolerance and 5% action level from baseline for linac photon beams, consistent with TG-142 recommendations adapted for SA practice.

sasqart.q: What specific radiation protection requirement applies to the entrance door of a linac bunker in South Africa?
sasqart.a: An interlocking system that prevents beam operation when the treatment room door is open
c: An interlocking system that prevents beam operation when the treatment room door is open
A manual warning sign only, with no electronic interlock system required by regulation
A time-delay mechanism that allows 30 seconds of beam operation with the door ajar
A radiation alarm that sounds inside the bunker but does not terminate beam output
sasqart.e: SA regulations require a door interlock that terminates or prevents beam activation when the bunker door is open. This is a critical safety system verified daily under SASQART requirements.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for wedge transmission factor constancy on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from baseline value
c: 1% tolerance from baseline, 2% action level from baseline value
2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from baseline value
3% tolerance from baseline, 5% action level from baseline value
5% tolerance from baseline, 7% action level from baseline value
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly wedge factor constancy at 2% tolerance and 3% action level from baseline, ensuring consistent dose modification by physical or enhanced dynamic wedges.

sasqart.q: What is the SA regulatory requirement regarding personal radiation monitoring for radiotherapy staff?
sasqart.a: All designated radiation workers must wear approved personal dosimeters and have records maintained
c: All designated radiation workers must wear approved personal dosimeters and have records maintained
Only the radiation protection officer needs to wear a dosimeter during operational hours
Personal dosimetry is optional for staff working behind shielded barriers at all times
Dosimeters are only required during commissioning or servicing of treatment equipment
sasqart.e: SA regulations mandate personal dosimetry for all designated radiation workers. Dose records must be maintained, reported regularly, and kept for at least 30 years or until the worker reaches age 75.

sasqart.q: How does SASQART adapt TG-142 recommendations for facilities that rely heavily on Co-60 teletherapy units?
sasqart.a: By including specific QA tables for Co-60 units covering source decay, shutter error, and timer checks
c: By including specific QA tables for Co-60 units covering source decay, shutter error, and timer checks
By simply applying all TG-142 linac tolerances directly to Co-60 units with no modifications
By exempting Co-60 units entirely from formal QA requirements due to their simpler design
By requiring Co-60 units to meet stricter tolerances than linacs in all dosimetric parameters
sasqart.e: SASQART includes dedicated QA tables for Co-60 teletherapy units (Table 2) with parameters specific to these machines, such as source decay, shutter error, and timer linearity, reflecting their continued use in SA.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for collimator rotation isocentre on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 3 mm diameter sphere action level
c: 1 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 2 mm diameter sphere action level
2 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 3 mm diameter sphere action level
3 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 5 mm diameter sphere action level
0.5 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 1 mm diameter action level
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual collimator rotation isocentre within a 2 mm diameter sphere with 3 mm action level, verifying the collimator rotates concentrically about the defined isocentre.

sasqart.q: In the SA context, what role does the Directorate: Radiation Control play in radiotherapy regulation?
sasqart.a: It issues Group IV licences for electronic radiation-generating equipment like linear accelerators
c: It issues Group IV licences for electronic radiation-generating equipment like linear accelerators
It only provides advisory guidelines without any licensing or enforcement authority at all
It manufactures radiation monitoring devices distributed to all radiotherapy centres in SA
It trains and certifies medical physicists independently from the HPCSA registration board
sasqart.e: The Directorate: Radiation Control (under SAHPRA/DoH) issues Group IV licences for radiation-generating devices such as linacs and CT simulators under the Hazardous Substances Act.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART tolerance for annual reference dosimetry output calibration on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 1% tolerance from the declared calibration value with 2% action level applied
c: 0.5% tolerance from the declared calibration value with 1% action level
1% tolerance from the declared calibration value with 2% action level applied
2% tolerance from the declared calibration value with 3% action level applied
3% tolerance from the declared calibration value with 5% action level applied
sasqart.e: SASQART requires annual reference dosimetry within 1% tolerance and 2% action level, ensuring absolute dose output accuracy for patient treatments across all photon and electron energies.

sasqart.q: What emergency procedure should SA radiotherapy facilities have for Co-60 source stuck in the exposed position?
sasqart.a: An emergency source retraction procedure and evacuation plan approved by the radiation protection committee
c: An emergency source retraction procedure and evacuation plan approved by the radiation protection committee
A simple instruction to switch off the main power supply and wait for the service engineer
No specific procedure is needed since modern Co-60 units have fully redundant safety systems
A protocol to manually push the source back using tools kept inside the treatment room
sasqart.e: SA regulations require documented emergency procedures for Co-60 source malfunction, including manual source retraction methods, patient evacuation protocols, and notification chains to the NNR.

sasqart.q: According to SASQART, what is the required frequency for end-to-end testing of the radiotherapy treatment chain?
sasqart.a: Annually, and after major equipment upgrades or software changes to any system in the chain
c: Annually, and after major equipment upgrades or software changes to any system in the chain
Only once during initial commissioning with no requirement for periodic repeat verification
Monthly, using a full anthropomorphic phantom with all clinical treatment beam configurations
Daily, as part of the routine morning quality assurance programme before patient treatments
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends annual end-to-end tests covering CT simulation through treatment delivery, plus additional tests after major system changes, to verify the entire treatment chain integrity.

sasqart.q: What is the SA regulatory requirement for radiation warning signs at the entrance to a linac treatment room?
sasqart.a: A trefoil radiation symbol, beam status indicator, and warning text must be clearly displayed
c: A trefoil radiation symbol, beam status indicator, and warning text must be clearly displayed
Only a generic caution sign is required with no specific radiation symbol mandated by law
A flashing red light alone is sufficient without any trefoil symbol or text being needed
Warning signs are only required inside the treatment room for the benefit of staff only
sasqart.e: SA regulations mandate the trefoil radiation warning symbol, active beam status indicators (typically red/green lights), and appropriate warning text at all radiotherapy treatment room entrances.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for couch rotation isocentre accuracy on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 3 mm diameter sphere action level
c: 1 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 2 mm diameter sphere action level
2 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 3 mm diameter sphere action level
3 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 5 mm diameter sphere action level
0.5 mm diameter sphere tolerance, 1 mm diameter action level
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual couch rotation isocentre within a 2 mm diameter sphere with 3 mm action level, ensuring the couch rotates concentrically around the treatment isocentre.

sasqart.q: How should SA radiotherapy departments manage treatment scheduling during announced load shedding periods?
sasqart.a: Schedule critical treatments around load shedding windows and ensure generator backup is available
c: Schedule critical treatments around load shedding windows and ensure generator backup is available
Cancel all patient treatments on any day where load shedding is announced by the utility
Continue treating patients normally and rely on linac internal battery backup for power
Transfer all patients to private facilities that are exempt from national load shedding
sasqart.e: SA departments should proactively schedule treatments around known load shedding periods and maintain generator backup to minimize treatment interruptions, especially for patients on accelerated fractionation schemes.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART requirement for independent dose calculation verification (second check) before patient treatment?
sasqart.a: An independent dose calculation must verify the TPS output to within 3% for standard fields
c: An independent dose calculation must verify the TPS output to within 3% for standard fields
No second check is required if the TPS has been commissioned within the previous year
A second check is optional and left to the discretion of the treating radiation oncologist
Independent verification is only required for palliative treatments with single fractions
sasqart.e: SASQART requires independent dose verification (manual calculation or second TPS) agreeing within 3% for standard fields. This serves as a critical safety check against TPS errors.

sasqart.q: Under which category does SAHPRA classify linear accelerators for regulatory purposes in South Africa?
sasqart.a: Class D medical devices requiring the highest level of regulatory scrutiny and conformity assessment
c: Class D medical devices requiring the highest level of regulatory scrutiny and conformity assessment
Class A medical devices requiring only basic registration with no additional audit required
Class B medical devices requiring moderate regulatory oversight and periodic safety reviews
Class C medical devices requiring standard conformity assessment and annual inspection only
sasqart.e: SAHPRA classifies linacs as Class D (highest risk) medical devices under the medical device regulations, requiring rigorous conformity assessment, registration, and post-market surveillance.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for electron beam output constancy on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from baseline output value
c: 1% tolerance from baseline, 2% action level from baseline output value
2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from baseline output value
3% tolerance from baseline, 5% action level from baseline output value
5% tolerance from baseline, 7% action level from baseline output value
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly electron beam output constancy at 2% tolerance and 3% action level, slightly tighter than daily checks to ensure ongoing accurate electron dose delivery.

sasqart.q: What is the regulatory requirement for notification to authorities after a radiation incident at a SA radiotherapy centre?
sasqart.a: Immediate notification to the relevant regulatory authority within 24 hours of the incident occurring
c: Immediate notification to the relevant regulatory authority within 24 hours of the incident occurring
Written notification within 30 days only if the patient received more than double the prescribed dose
No notification is required if the incident was detected and corrected before treatment ended
Verbal notification to the hospital management only with no regulatory reporting obligation
sasqart.e: SA regulations require prompt notification (within 24 hours) to the relevant regulatory authority (SAHPRA or NNR) of radiation incidents, followed by a detailed written investigation report.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART requirement for patient-specific quality assurance for IMRT treatments?
sasqart.a: Pre-treatment verification of each IMRT plan using measurement-based or independent calculation methods
c: Pre-treatment verification of each IMRT plan using measurement-based or independent calculation methods
No patient-specific QA is needed if commissioning was thorough and validated appropriately
Only random sampling of 10% of IMRT plans requires pre-treatment verification measurements
Patient-specific QA is only needed for the first five IMRT patients treated on a new linac
sasqart.e: SASQART requires patient-specific IMRT QA for each plan, using methods such as ion chamber point dose, film or array measurements, or validated independent calculation algorithms.

sasqart.q: What gamma analysis criteria does SASQART recommend for IMRT patient-specific quality assurance?
sasqart.a: 3%/3mm criteria with a pass rate of 90% or higher for clinical acceptance of the plan
c: 3%/3mm criteria with a pass rate of 90% or higher for clinical acceptance of the plan
5%/5mm criteria with a pass rate of 80% or higher for clinical acceptance of plans
1%/1mm criteria with a pass rate of 99% or higher for clinical acceptance of plans
2%/2mm criteria with a pass rate of 95% or higher for acceptance of complex plans
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends the widely used 3%/3mm gamma criteria with a minimum 90% pass rate for IMRT QA, consistent with international standards for routine clinical plan verification.

sasqart.q: What specific challenge does the geographic distribution of SA radiotherapy centres pose for SASQART implementation?
sasqart.a: Rural and underserved centres may have limited access to qualified medical physicists and calibration services
c: Rural and underserved centres may have limited access to qualified medical physicists and calibration services
All SA radiotherapy centres are located in major urban areas so no geographic challenges exist
Only private sector facilities face geographic challenges since public hospitals are well distributed
The main challenge is that SASQART standards only apply to facilities in the Gauteng province
sasqart.e: SA's geographic distribution means some radiotherapy centres, especially in rural areas, face challenges accessing qualified medical physicists and timely calibration services from NMISA.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART requirement for backup of electronic treatment records at SA radiotherapy facilities?
sasqart.a: Regular automated backups with offsite storage and documented disaster recovery procedures in place
c: Regular automated backups with offsite storage and documented disaster recovery procedures in place
Manual weekly backups stored on USB drives kept in the treatment room filing cabinet only
No backup requirements exist since all records are maintained on paper as the primary copy
Cloud-only storage with no local backup required at the treating facility or department
sasqart.e: SASQART requires robust data backup procedures including regular automated backups with offsite storage, particularly important in SA where load shedding increases the risk of data corruption.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for light field to radiation field coincidence on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level on each side of the field
c: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level on each side of the field
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level on each side of the field
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level on each side of the field
0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level on each field side
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly light-radiation field coincidence at 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level per side, ensuring the light field accurately represents the radiation treatment field.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART recommend regarding the frequency of radiation survey measurements around linac bunkers?
sasqart.a: After initial installation, after any shielding modifications, and at regular annual intervals
c: After initial installation, after any shielding modifications, and at regular annual intervals
Only during initial commissioning with no requirement for subsequent periodic surveys
Monthly surveys using calibrated survey meters at all designated measurement points
Every five years as part of the facility re-licensing application documentation
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends radiation surveys after installation, after shielding changes, and annually to confirm that dose rates outside the bunker remain within regulatory limits for staff and public.

sasqart.q: What is the SA requirement for shielding design of new radiotherapy bunkers?
sasqart.a: Shielding must reduce dose rates to below regulatory limits at all occupied areas outside the bunker
c: Shielding must reduce dose rates to below regulatory limits at all occupied areas outside the bunker
A standard wall thickness of 100 cm concrete is used regardless of beam energy or workload
Shielding calculations are optional if the bunker is located at least 50 metres from public areas
Only primary barriers require shielding while secondary and scatter barriers are not regulated
sasqart.e: SA regulations require that bunker shielding be designed to reduce dose rates at all occupied locations to below regulatory limits, considering workload, use factor, occupancy, and beam energy.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART recommended tolerance for daily door interlock function testing on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: Functional (pass/fail test): the interlock must terminate beam when the door is opened
c: Functional (pass/fail test): the interlock must terminate beam when the door is opened
The interlock must terminate beam within 5 seconds of the door being fully opened
A visual indicator check only is sufficient without actual beam termination verification
Door interlock testing is only required on a monthly basis, not as a daily check
sasqart.e: SASQART requires daily door interlock verification as a functional pass/fail test. The beam must terminate immediately when the treatment room door is opened, with no grace period allowed.

sasqart.q: According to SASQART, what is the recommended frequency for calibration of dosimetry equipment used for routine linac QA?
sasqart.a: Ionisation chambers should be recalibrated every two years at an accredited calibration laboratory
c: Ionisation chambers should be recalibrated every two years at an accredited calibration laboratory
Annual recalibration is required for all dosimetry equipment without exception in every case
Recalibration is only needed if the dosimeter reading deviates by more than 5% from expected
No recalibration is required if the chamber has a valid factory calibration certificate
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends biennial (every 2 years) recalibration of ionisation chambers at NMISA or an accredited secondary standards laboratory with NMISA-traceable calibration factors.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for couch vertical position accuracy on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level from the indicated position value
c: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level from the indicated position value
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level from the indicated position value
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level from the indicated position value
0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level from indicated position
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual couch vertical position accuracy at 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level, ensuring the couch height readout accurately reflects the actual position.

sasqart.q: What is the role of the radiation protection committee (RPC) at a SA radiotherapy facility?
sasqart.a: Overseeing the radiation protection programme, reviewing incidents, and ensuring regulatory compliance
c: Overseeing the radiation protection programme, reviewing incidents, and ensuring regulatory compliance
Performing daily machine QA checks and verifying output constancy before patient treatments
Treating patients and prescribing radiation doses as part of the clinical oncology team duties
Maintaining and servicing the linear accelerators according to manufacturer specifications only
sasqart.e: The RPC oversees the facility's radiation protection programme, reviews incidents, ensures staff dose monitoring, and maintains regulatory compliance. It typically includes the RPO, medical physicists, and management.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART requirement for beam data verification when upgrading treatment planning system software?
sasqart.a: Re-verification of beam model output against original commissioning data for all clinical beam configurations
c: Re-verification of beam model output against original commissioning data for all clinical beam configurations
No verification is needed since software upgrades do not affect stored beam model data at all
Complete re-commissioning of all beam data from scratch including new measurements on each beam
Only verification of the highest and lowest photon energy beams with no electron checks needed
sasqart.e: SASQART requires that TPS software upgrades be followed by verification of beam model calculations against original commissioning data to detect any changes in dose calculation accuracy.

sasqart.q: Under SA law, what training is required for operators of radiation-emitting equipment in radiotherapy?
sasqart.a: Formal training in equipment operation and radiation safety, with competency assessment and documentation
c: Formal training in equipment operation and radiation safety, with competency assessment and documentation
On-the-job training by a colleague is sufficient with no formal documentation or records required
Only reading the equipment manufacturer's user manual is needed before operating the equipment
No specific training is required if the operator holds any healthcare professional qualification
sasqart.e: SA regulations require documented formal training in equipment operation and radiation safety for all operators, including competency assessment. This applies to radiation therapists, physicists, and other staff.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for beam quality (TPR20,10 or PDD) constancy for photon beams?
sasqart.a: 1% tolerance from baseline, 2% action level from the commissioning baseline value
c: 0.5% tolerance from baseline, 1% action level from the baseline commissioning value
1% tolerance from baseline, 2% action level from the commissioning baseline value
2% tolerance from baseline, 3% action level from the commissioning baseline value
3% tolerance from baseline, 5% action level from the commissioning baseline value
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual beam quality constancy at 1% tolerance and 2% action level from baseline, using TPR20,10 or PDD(10) to detect energy shifts in photon beams.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART specify about the frequency of MLC transmission factor checks?
sasqart.a: Annual verification of MLC transmission factor against commissioning baseline measurements
c: Annual verification of MLC transmission factor against commissioning baseline measurements
Daily verification before each IMRT treatment session as part of morning quality checks
No specific frequency is stated since MLC transmission does not change over time at all
Monthly verification is required only for centres performing VMAT treatment techniques
sasqart.e: SASQART requires annual MLC transmission factor verification against baseline values. Changes in MLC transmission can affect IMRT/VMAT dose distributions and must be monitored.

sasqart.q: What is the SA regulatory requirement for medical surveillance of radiation workers in radiotherapy departments?
sasqart.a: Pre-employment and periodic medical examinations assessing fitness to work with ionising radiation
c: Pre-employment and periodic medical examinations assessing fitness to work with ionising radiation
Only a pre-employment medical examination is required with no follow-up assessments needed
Medical surveillance is optional and depends on the employer's occupational health policies
Annual blood tests only, specifically complete blood counts to check for radiation effects
sasqart.e: SA regulations require pre-employment and periodic medical examinations for radiation workers to assess fitness, detect any health effects, and maintain an occupational health record.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART daily tolerance for beam status indicator (radiation on) function check?
sasqart.a: Functional (pass/fail): indicators must activate when radiation beam is on
c: Functional (pass/fail): indicators must activate when radiation beam is on
Indicators must activate within 3 seconds of beam initiation being started
Only the audible indicator needs to function; visual indicators are optional
Beam status indicators only need monthly verification, not daily testing
sasqart.e: SASQART requires daily verification that beam status indicators (visual and audible) function correctly when the beam is activated, providing immediate warning to anyone approaching the treatment room.

sasqart.q: According to SA regulations, what is the dose limit for the extremities (hands and feet) of radiation workers?
sasqart.a: 500 mSv equivalent dose per year to the hands, feet, or skin
c: 500 mSv equivalent dose per year to the hands, feet, or skin
150 mSv equivalent dose per year to the hands, feet, or skin
250 mSv equivalent dose per year to the hands, feet, or skin
50 mSv equivalent dose per year to all extremities of workers
sasqart.e: SA follows ICRP recommendations setting the extremity dose limit at 500 mSv/year for hands, feet, and skin of radiation workers, relevant for brachytherapy source handling procedures.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART requirement for independent monitor unit calculation verification for 3D conformal treatments?
sasqart.a: An independent MU check within 3% of the planned value before the first treatment fraction
c: An independent MU check within 3% of the planned value before the first treatment fraction
No independent MU verification is required for standard 3D conformal treatment plans
MU verification is only needed for treatments using beam energies above 10 MV photons
Independent verification within 10% is acceptable for palliative conformal treatments
sasqart.e: SASQART requires an independent MU calculation agreeing within 3% of the TPS-calculated value for 3D conformal plans, serving as a critical check against planning errors.

sasqart.q: What specific consideration does SASQART address regarding generator backup for radiotherapy departments during load shedding?
sasqart.a: Generators must provide stable voltage and frequency suitable for sensitive radiotherapy equipment operation
c: Generators must provide stable voltage and frequency suitable for sensitive radiotherapy equipment operation
Any standard construction-grade generator is adequate for powering linear accelerators directly
Generator power is only needed for lighting and air conditioning, not for treatment equipment
Generators are not recommended since they produce power fluctuations harmful to all linacs
sasqart.e: SA radiotherapy departments need generators with stable voltage and frequency output, as linacs are sensitive to power quality. Voltage regulators and power conditioning may be needed between generator and linac.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for electron beam energy constancy verification?
sasqart.a: 2% change in the R50 or practical range value from the commissioning baseline measurement
c: 1% change in the R50 or practical range value from the commissioning baseline measurement
2% change in the R50 or practical range value from the commissioning baseline measurement
3% change in the R50 or practical range value from the commissioning baseline measurement
5% change in the R50 or practical range value from the commissioning baseline measurement
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual electron energy verification using R50 or practical range with 2% tolerance from baseline, detecting energy shifts that alter dose distribution depth characteristics.

sasqart.q: What does the HPCSA ethical framework require regarding informed consent for radiation therapy in South Africa?
sasqart.a: Patients must be informed of treatment risks, benefits, alternatives, and expected side effects before consent
c: Patients must be informed of treatment risks, benefits, alternatives, and expected side effects before consent
Verbal consent from the patient is sufficient without any written documentation being required
Consent is only required for clinical trial patients and not for standard treatment protocols
The referring physician's consent is adequate without separate consent from the patient needed
sasqart.e: HPCSA ethical rules require comprehensive informed consent including disclosure of risks, benefits, and alternatives. This must be documented and is a legal and ethical requirement for radiotherapy in SA.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for gantry angle indicator accuracy on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 1 degree tolerance, 2 degree action level from the true gantry angle
c: 0.5 degree tolerance, 1 degree action level from the true angle
1 degree tolerance, 2 degree action level from the true gantry angle
2 degree tolerance, 3 degree action level from the true gantry angle
0.2 degree tolerance, 0.5 degree action level from true angle
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly gantry angle verification at 1 degree tolerance and 2 degree action level, ensuring accurate beam direction for all treatment setups.

sasqart.q: What is the SA regulatory framework for the safe transport of Co-60 sources to and from radiotherapy facilities?
sasqart.a: Transport must comply with IAEA transport regulations as adopted in SA national legislation and NNR requirements
c: Transport must comply with IAEA transport regulations as adopted in SA national legislation and NNR requirements
No specific transport regulations exist; the source supplier handles all logistics independently
Sources can be transported in any standard lead-lined container without regulatory oversight
Transport regulations only apply when sources are moved internationally across SA borders
sasqart.e: SA adopts IAEA transport regulations (SSR-6) for radioactive material transport. Co-60 source transport requires Type B packaging, route planning, emergency procedures, and NNR notification.

sasqart.q: According to SASQART, what beam data must be measured during linac commissioning for each photon energy?
sasqart.a: PDD curves, beam profiles, output factors, wedge factors, and absolute dose calibration measurements
c: PDD curves, beam profiles, output factors, wedge factors, and absolute dose calibration measurements
Only central axis PDD curves and absolute output at the reference field size are required
Manufacturer-supplied beam data is acceptable without independent measurement verification
Only beam profiles at dmax are needed along with reference dose calibration measurements
sasqart.e: SASQART requires comprehensive commissioning measurements including PDD, profiles at multiple depths, output factors for all field sizes, wedge factors, and absolute calibration for each photon energy.

sasqart.q: What is the recommended procedure when a SA radiotherapy centre discovers a systematic dosimetric error affecting multiple patients?
sasqart.a: Immediately halt affected treatments, notify the regulatory authority, investigate, and assess patient dose impact
c: Immediately halt affected treatments, notify the regulatory authority, investigate, and assess patient dose impact
Continue treatments while investigating and only notify the authority after completing the review
Correct the error for future patients without reviewing previously treated patient dose records
Report the error in the next annual regulatory compliance report and adjust procedures going forward
sasqart.e: SA regulations require immediate cessation of affected treatments, prompt regulatory notification, thorough investigation, patient dose assessment, and clinical follow-up for all potentially affected patients.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for electron applicator output factor constancy?
sasqart.a: 2% tolerance, 3% action level from baseline output factor values
c: 1% tolerance, 2% action level from baseline output factor values
2% tolerance, 3% action level from baseline output factor values
3% tolerance, 5% action level from baseline output factor values
5% tolerance, 7% action level from baseline output factor values
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly electron applicator output factor constancy at 2% tolerance and 3% action level from baseline, verifying consistent output for each cone/applicator combination.

sasqart.q: What is the SA requirement for waste management of disused Co-60 sources from teletherapy units?
sasqart.a: Sources must be returned to the manufacturer or transferred to a licensed waste management facility
c: Sources must be returned to the manufacturer or transferred to a licensed waste management facility
Sources can be stored indefinitely on-site in the treatment room without any time restriction
Disused sources must be disposed of in the hospital's general radioactive waste storage area
The source decays to safe levels after 10 years and can then be discarded as normal waste
sasqart.e: SA NNR regulations require disused Co-60 sources to be returned to the supplier or transferred to an authorised radioactive waste facility. Long-term on-site storage requires specific NNR approval.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART requirement for cross-calibration when a new ionisation chamber replaces an existing one?
sasqart.a: Cross-calibration against the reference chamber with documented comparison before clinical use begins
c: Cross-calibration against the reference chamber with documented comparison before clinical use begins
No cross-calibration needed if the new chamber has a valid NMISA calibration certificate
Cross-calibration is only required if the new chamber is from a different manufacturer model
The new chamber can be used immediately if its serial number is registered with the facility
sasqart.e: SASQART requires that replacement chambers be cross-calibrated against the reference chamber in a clinical beam before use, even with a valid calibration certificate, to verify consistency.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for collimator angle indicator accuracy on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 1 degree tolerance, 2 degree action level from the true collimator angle
c: 0.5 degree tolerance, 1 degree action level from the true angle value
1 degree tolerance, 2 degree action level from the true collimator angle
2 degree tolerance, 3 degree action level from the true collimator angle
0.2 degree tolerance, 0.5 degree action level from true angle value
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly collimator angle accuracy at 1 degree tolerance and 2 degree action level, ensuring correct field orientation for conformal and IMRT treatments.

sasqart.q: What specific challenge does South Africa's dual public-private healthcare system pose for SASQART implementation?
sasqart.a: Significant resource disparities between public and private facilities affect uniform standard implementation
c: Significant resource disparities between public and private facilities affect uniform standard implementation
Private facilities are exempt from SASQART standards since they follow international guidelines only
Public facilities have more resources for QA compliance than private radiotherapy departments do
The dual system creates no challenges since SASQART applies equally with full compliance everywhere
sasqart.e: SA's public-private divide means resource-constrained public centres may struggle to meet the same QA standards as well-equipped private facilities, requiring SASQART to balance ideal practice with achievable minimums.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for output factor variation with gantry angle on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 1% tolerance, 2% action level from the reference gantry position output value
c: 0.5% tolerance, 1% action level from the reference gantry position output
1% tolerance, 2% action level from the reference gantry position output value
2% tolerance, 3% action level from the reference gantry position output value
3% tolerance, 5% action level from the reference gantry position output value
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual verification that output does not vary by more than 1% with gantry angle, important for arc therapy and non-zero gantry angle treatments.

sasqart.q: What continuing professional development (CPD) requirement does HPCSA mandate for registered medical physicists?
sasqart.a: Accumulation of specified CPD points through approved activities over each defined compliance period
c: Accumulation of specified CPD points through approved activities over each defined compliance period
No CPD requirements exist once initial HPCSA registration has been successfully completed
Attendance at one international conference per year is the only CPD requirement imposed
Only medical physicists in academic positions are required to fulfil CPD point obligations
sasqart.e: HPCSA requires all registered medical physicists to accumulate CPD points through approved activities (courses, conferences, publications) during each compliance period to maintain registration.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART recommend for managing the warm-up period of a linear accelerator after power restoration following load shedding?
sasqart.a: Follow the manufacturer's recommended warm-up procedure and verify output stability before treating patients
c: Follow the manufacturer's recommended warm-up procedure and verify output stability before treating patients
No warm-up period is needed since modern linacs are immediately stable after power restoration
A mandatory 4-hour warm-up period is required regardless of the linac manufacturer or model
Only a visual inspection of the linac exterior is needed before resuming clinical treatments
sasqart.e: After power restoration, linacs require manufacturer-specified warm-up and beam stabilisation before clinical use. Output constancy must be verified to ensure the beam is stable and accurate for patient treatment.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART requirement regarding the availability of emergency stop mechanisms on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: Emergency off buttons must be accessible both inside the treatment room and at the control console
c: Emergency off buttons must be accessible both inside the treatment room and at the control console
Only one emergency stop button at the control console is required by SA safety regulations
Emergency stops are optional if the door interlock system is functioning correctly at all times
Emergency stops are only required inside the treatment room and not at the operator console
sasqart.e: SASQART requires emergency stop buttons at multiple locations including inside the treatment room and at the control console, ensuring rapid beam termination from any position in an emergency.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for CT number uniformity across the scan field of a CT simulator?
sasqart.a: 5 HU tolerance, 10 HU action level between centre and peripheral measurement regions
c: 2 HU tolerance, 5 HU action level between centre and peripheral measurement regions
5 HU tolerance, 10 HU action level between centre and peripheral measurement regions
10 HU tolerance, 15 HU action level between centre and peripheral regions of interest
15 HU tolerance, 20 HU action level between centre and peripheral regions of interest
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly CT number uniformity at 5 HU tolerance and 10 HU action level, comparing central and peripheral ROIs to ensure consistent CT numbers across the field of view.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART specify about the minimum staffing requirements for a medical physics department in a radiotherapy centre?
sasqart.a: At least one qualified and registered medical physicist must be available per treatment unit on site
c: At least one qualified and registered medical physicist must be available per treatment unit on site
One medical physicist can cover an unlimited number of treatment units across multiple sites
Medical physics services can be entirely outsourced with no on-site physicist required at all
Two medical physicists per treatment unit are required for all SA radiotherapy facilities
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends at least one HPCSA-registered medical physicist per treatment unit to ensure adequate coverage for daily QA, treatment planning, and clinical physics support.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for tray transmission factor constancy on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: 1% tolerance, 2% action level from the baseline tray factor measurement
c: 0.5% tolerance, 1% action level from baseline tray factor measurement
1% tolerance, 2% action level from the baseline tray factor measurement
2% tolerance, 3% action level from the baseline tray factor measurement
3% tolerance, 5% action level from the baseline tray factor measurement
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual tray factor constancy at 1% tolerance and 2% action level from baseline, verifying that block tray attenuation remains consistent for dose calculations.

sasqart.q: What is the SA requirement for pregnancy declaration policies for female radiation workers in radiotherapy departments?
sasqart.a: Facilities must have a written policy enabling voluntary pregnancy declaration with dose management measures
c: Facilities must have a written policy enabling voluntary pregnancy declaration with dose management measures
Pregnant workers must immediately resign from their position for the duration of the pregnancy
No specific pregnancy policy is required under current South African radiation safety regulations
Pregnancy testing is mandatory on a monthly basis for all female radiation workers in the unit
sasqart.e: SA regulations require facilities to have pregnancy declaration policies. Once declared, the embryo/foetus dose must not exceed 1 mSv for the remainder of pregnancy through work reassignment if needed.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART daily tolerance for audio-visual patient monitoring system function?
sasqart.a: Functional (pass/fail): audio and video monitoring must both be operational during treatments
c: Functional (pass/fail): audio and video monitoring must both be operational during treatments
Only the video monitor needs to function; audio communication is optional for daily use
Audio-visual monitoring is only required for paediatric patients and not for adult patients
Monthly verification of audio-visual systems is sufficient under SASQART requirements
sasqart.e: SASQART requires daily functional verification of both audio and video patient monitoring systems. Both must be operational before commencing patient treatments to ensure patient safety.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART recommend for the frequency of portal imaging or EPID QA checks?
sasqart.a: Monthly image quality checks including spatial resolution, contrast, and uniformity of the imaging panel
c: Monthly image quality checks including spatial resolution, contrast, and uniformity of the imaging panel
Daily full image quality assessment before each patient verification imaging session begins
Annual checks only since EPID panels are highly stable and rarely degrade over time
No specific EPID QA is mentioned in SASQART since it only covers therapeutic beam checks
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends monthly EPID/portal imaging QA covering spatial resolution, contrast, and uniformity to ensure reliable image quality for patient setup verification.

sasqart.q: What is the SA regulatory requirement for record keeping of radiation dose measurements at radiotherapy facilities?
sasqart.a: All dosimetry records must be retained for a minimum period as specified by regulatory requirements
c: All dosimetry records must be retained for a minimum period as specified by regulatory requirements
Records only need to be kept for one year after the patient completes their treatment course
Electronic records are not accepted; only paper-based records satisfy regulatory requirements
Record keeping is at the discretion of the facility with no minimum retention period set
sasqart.e: SA regulations require retention of all dosimetry and radiation safety records for specified minimum periods (typically the patient's lifetime plus several years) to enable retrospective dose assessment.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for CT laser alignment accuracy on a CT simulator?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level from the defined reference position
c: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level from the defined reference position
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level from the defined reference position
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level from the defined reference position
0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level from defined reference position
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly CT simulator laser alignment at 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level, ensuring accurate patient marking for treatment setup reproducibility.

sasqart.q: What specific provision does SASQART make for facilities that cannot meet the recommended QA frequency due to staffing constraints?
sasqart.a: A risk-based approach allowing documented justification for reduced frequency with minimum safety standards met
c: A risk-based approach allowing documented justification for reduced frequency with minimum safety standards met
Complete exemption from all QA requirements until sufficient staff are hired and trained properly
No provision exists; all facilities must meet exact requirements regardless of staffing levels
Transfer of all QA responsibilities to the equipment manufacturer's service engineering team
sasqart.e: SASQART acknowledges SA resource constraints and allows a risk-based approach where facilities document justifications for adjusted QA frequency while maintaining minimum mandatory safety checks.

sasqart.q: What is the SA requirement for controlled area designation around a linear accelerator treatment room?
sasqart.a: The treatment room and immediate surroundings must be designated as controlled areas with restricted access
c: The treatment room and immediate surroundings must be designated as controlled areas with restricted access
Only the area directly in front of the primary beam path needs to be designated as controlled
Controlled area designation is optional if adequate shielding brings dose rates below public limits
The entire hospital building containing the linac must be designated as a controlled radiation area
sasqart.e: SA regulations require the linac treatment room and areas where dose rates may exceed specified levels to be designated as controlled areas with access restricted to authorised personnel only.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for the virtual wedge or enhanced dynamic wedge factor constancy?
sasqart.a: 2% tolerance, 3% action level from the baseline wedge factor measurement value
c: 1% tolerance, 2% action level from the baseline wedge factor measurement value
2% tolerance, 3% action level from the baseline wedge factor measurement value
3% tolerance, 5% action level from the baseline wedge factor measurement value
5% tolerance, 7% action level from the baseline wedge factor measurement value
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual enhanced dynamic wedge factor constancy at 2% tolerance and 3% action level from baseline, verifying consistent dose modification across wedge angles.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART recommend regarding the use of in-vivo dosimetry for treatment verification in SA centres?
sasqart.a: In-vivo dosimetry is recommended as an additional safety check especially for new techniques or complex treatments
c: In-vivo dosimetry is recommended as an additional safety check especially for new techniques or complex treatments
In-vivo dosimetry is mandatory for every single treatment fraction delivered at all SA centres
In-vivo dosimetry is not mentioned in SASQART and has no role in SA radiotherapy practice
In-vivo dosimetry is only applicable to Co-60 units and not recommended for linac treatments
sasqart.e: SASQART recommends in-vivo dosimetry as an additional verification layer, particularly for new treatment techniques, complex cases, or when commissioning new equipment in SA centres.

sasqart.q: What is the SA regulatory approach to ensuring radiotherapy equipment safety after installation by the manufacturer?
sasqart.a: Independent acceptance testing by the facility's medical physicist before any clinical use commences
c: Independent acceptance testing by the facility's medical physicist before any clinical use commences
The manufacturer's installation report alone is sufficient to authorise clinical use immediately
A third-party international inspection agency must certify the equipment before patient use
Only the radiation protection officer needs to approve the installation for clinical operation
sasqart.e: SA requires that the facility's HPCSA-registered medical physicist independently perform acceptance testing after manufacturer installation, verifying that the equipment meets specifications before clinical use.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for kV imaging (OBI/XVI) image quality on a linear accelerator?
sasqart.a: Spatial resolution and contrast must meet baseline specifications established during commissioning tests
c: Spatial resolution and contrast must meet baseline specifications established during commissioning tests
No specific tolerances exist for kV imaging since it is not directly used for dose delivery
Only annual checks of kV imaging quality are required under current SASQART guidelines
kV imaging quality is managed entirely by the manufacturer's remote monitoring service team
sasqart.e: SASQART requires monthly verification that kV imaging spatial resolution and contrast meet commissioning baselines, ensuring reliable image-guided radiotherapy for accurate patient positioning.

sasqart.q: What is the SA requirement for the qualifications of a radiation protection officer at a radiotherapy facility?
sasqart.a: Appropriate training in radiation protection with knowledge of relevant legislation and regulatory requirements
c: Appropriate training in radiation protection with knowledge of relevant legislation and regulatory requirements
Any staff member can serve as RPO without specific radiation protection training or experience
Only a medical physicist can serve as RPO and no other professional category is acceptable
The RPO must hold a doctoral degree in nuclear physics or radiation biophysics specifically
sasqart.e: SA regulations require the RPO to have appropriate radiation protection training and knowledge of relevant legislation. While medical physicists often serve as RPO, the role can be filled by other suitably trained professionals.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for the half-value layer (HVL) measurement of a linac photon beam?
sasqart.a: HVL must be consistent with the stated beam energy within manufacturer-specified tolerances
c: HVL must be consistent with the stated beam energy within manufacturer-specified tolerances
HVL is not measured for megavoltage beams and only applies to diagnostic X-ray equipment
HVL must be measured daily and must match the commissioning value within 0.1 mm aluminium
HVL measurement is replaced entirely by percentage depth dose constancy checks for linacs
sasqart.e: While HVL is more commonly associated with kV beams, SASQART recognises beam quality verification through TPR20,10 or PDD methods for MV beams, which serve an equivalent energy verification purpose.

sasqart.q: What specific impact can voltage fluctuations from an unstable power grid have on linear accelerator beam characteristics?
sasqart.a: Voltage instability can cause beam energy and dose rate fluctuations affecting treatment delivery accuracy
c: Voltage instability can cause beam energy and dose rate fluctuations affecting treatment delivery accuracy
Voltage fluctuations have no effect on modern linacs due to internal power regulation systems
Only the gantry motor speed is affected while the beam remains completely stable and unaffected
Voltage variations only impact the cooling system with no direct effect on the treatment beam
sasqart.e: Voltage fluctuations affect the linac RF system, potentially altering beam energy and dose rate. SA facilities should use voltage stabilisers to ensure consistent beam quality despite grid instability.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART requirement for QA of brachytherapy source strength verification in SA facilities?
sasqart.a: Independent source strength verification using a calibrated well-type chamber before clinical use of each source
c: Independent source strength verification using a calibrated well-type chamber before clinical use of each source
The manufacturer's source certificate alone is sufficient without any independent verification required
Source strength only needs checking annually after initial acceptance of the brachytherapy source
Visual inspection of the source encapsulation is the only verification needed before clinical use
sasqart.e: SASQART requires independent verification of brachytherapy source strength using a calibrated well-type ionisation chamber with traceable calibration, before any clinical use of new sources.

sasqart.q: What is the SA regulatory requirement for the disposal of disused electronic radiation-generating equipment such as old linacs?
sasqart.a: Decommissioning must follow regulatory procedures including component surveys and licence amendment notification
c: Decommissioning must follow regulatory procedures including component surveys and licence amendment notification
Old linacs can be disposed of as general electronic waste without regulatory notification needed
Only the waveguide assembly requires special disposal while other linac components are unrestricted
Disused linacs must be stored on-site indefinitely as they may become activated over time
sasqart.e: Decommissioning linacs in SA requires regulatory notification, radiation surveys of potentially activated components, licence amendments, and proper disposal following applicable waste regulations.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART monthly tolerance for the coincidence of CBCT isocentre with the treatment isocentre?
sasqart.a: 2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level between imaging and treatment isocentres
c: 1 mm tolerance, 2 mm action level between imaging and treatment isocentres
2 mm tolerance, 3 mm action level between imaging and treatment isocentres
3 mm tolerance, 5 mm action level between imaging and treatment isocentres
0.5 mm tolerance, 1 mm action level between the two isocentres
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies monthly CBCT-to-treatment isocentre coincidence at 2 mm tolerance and 3 mm action level, critical for accurate image-guided radiotherapy patient setup corrections.

sasqart.q: What does SASQART specify regarding the qualifications needed to perform daily linac QA checks in SA facilities?
sasqart.a: Trained radiation therapists may perform daily checks under the oversight of a qualified medical physicist
c: Trained radiation therapists may perform daily checks under the oversight of a qualified medical physicist
Only HPCSA-registered medical physicists are permitted to perform any QA checks on linacs
Equipment manufacturer service engineers must perform all daily quality assurance checks
Any hospital staff member can perform daily QA without specific training or supervision
sasqart.e: SASQART allows trained radiation therapists to perform routine daily QA checks (output, lasers, interlocks) under medical physicist oversight, recognising practical staffing realities in SA.

sasqart.q: What is the SASQART annual tolerance for photon beam profile constancy at multiple depths?
sasqart.a: 2% tolerance, 3% action level from baseline profile measurements at specified depths
c: 1% tolerance, 2% action level from baseline profile measurements at specified depths
2% tolerance, 3% action level from baseline profile measurements at specified depths
3% tolerance, 5% action level from baseline profile measurements at specified depths
5% tolerance, 7% action level from baseline profile measurements at specified depths
sasqart.e: SASQART specifies annual beam profile constancy at 2% tolerance and 3% action level from baseline at multiple depths, verifying consistent beam flatness and symmetry across the treatment field.

sasqart.q: What approach does SASQART recommend for managing QA when a single medical physicist serves multiple SA treatment facilities?
sasqart.a: A documented QA schedule with prioritised safety checks and trained delegates performing routine daily tasks
c: A documented QA schedule with prioritised safety checks and trained delegates performing routine daily tasks
The physicist must be physically present at every facility simultaneously during all operating hours
QA can be indefinitely postponed at unstaffed facilities until the physicist is available on site
Remote QA via telephone consultation is acceptable as a permanent replacement for on-site checks
sasqart.e: SASQART acknowledges the SA reality of limited physics staffing and recommends structured QA schedules with trained delegates (radiation therapists) performing daily checks under physicist oversight across multiple sites.

aiml.q: Which type of machine learning requires labelled training data?
aiml.a: Supervised learning
c: Supervised learning
Unsupervised learning
Self-supervised learning
Reinforcement learning
aiml.e: Supervised learning uses input-output pairs (labelled data) to learn a mapping function, unlike unsupervised learning which finds patterns in unlabelled data.

aiml.q: In a convolutional neural network (CNN), what operation is primarily responsible for extracting spatial features from images?
aiml.a: Convolution with learnable filters
c: Max pooling
Convolution with learnable filters
Fully connected matrix multiplication
Batch normalization
aiml.e: Convolutional layers apply learnable filters (kernels) across the input to detect spatial features like edges, textures, and patterns at various hierarchical levels.

aiml.q: What is the primary purpose of the validation set during model training?
aiml.a: To tune hyperparameters and monitor for overfitting during training
c: To train the model weights via backpropagation
To tune hyperparameters and monitor for overfitting during training
To provide the final unbiased performance estimate
To augment the training data with additional examples
aiml.e: The validation set is used during training to evaluate model performance on unseen data, guiding hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. The test set provides the final unbiased evaluation.

aiml.q: Which loss function is most commonly used for multi-class classification tasks in medical image analysis?
aiml.a: Categorical cross-entropy
c: Mean squared error
Categorical cross-entropy
Hinge loss
Huber loss
aiml.e: Categorical cross-entropy measures the divergence between predicted probability distributions and true class labels, making it the standard choice for multi-class classification.

aiml.q: A deep learning model achieves 99% accuracy on training data but only 65% on test data. What is this an example of?
aiml.a: Overfitting
c: Underfitting
Overfitting
Optimal generalization
Label leakage
aiml.e: A large gap between training and test performance indicates overfitting, where the model memorizes training data patterns (including noise) rather than learning generalizable features.

aiml.q: What is one complete pass through the entire training dataset called?
aiml.a: An epoch
c: A batch
An epoch
An iteration
A cycle
aiml.e: An epoch refers to one complete forward and backward pass of all training examples through the network. Multiple epochs are typically needed for convergence.

aiml.q: Which regularization technique randomly sets a fraction of neuron activations to zero during training?
aiml.a: Dropout
c: L2 regularization
Dropout
Batch normalization
Data augmentation
aiml.e: Dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training, forcing the network to learn redundant representations and reducing co-adaptation, which helps prevent overfitting.

aiml.q: What does the ReLU activation function output for a negative input value?
aiml.a: Zero
c: The input value unchanged
Zero
Negative one
The absolute value of the input
aiml.e: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) outputs max(0, x), returning zero for any negative input and the input itself for positive values, helping mitigate the vanishing gradient problem.

aiml.q: In k-fold cross-validation with k=5, what percentage of data is used for validation in each fold?
aiml.e: In 5-fold cross-validation, the dataset is split into 5 equal parts. Each fold uses 1 part (20%) for validation and the remaining 4 parts (80%) for training.

aiml.q: If a model has high bias and low variance, which of the following best describes its behavior?
aiml.a: It consistently underfits the data across different training sets
c: It memorizes the training data
It consistently underfits the data across different training sets
It performs very differently on different training sets
It overfits on small datasets only
aiml.e: High bias and low variance indicate underfitting: the model is too simple to capture the underlying data patterns, producing consistent but inaccurate predictions across different training sets.

aiml.q: Which hyperparameter most directly controls how large each weight update step is during gradient descent?
aiml.a: Learning rate
c: Batch size
Learning rate
Number of epochs
Number of hidden layers
aiml.e: The learning rate scales the gradient to determine the step size for each weight update. Too large causes divergence; too small causes slow convergence.

aiml.q: What type of neural network architecture is best suited for sequential data such as time-series radiation dose measurements?
aiml.a: Recurrent neural network (RNN)
c: Convolutional neural network (CNN)
Recurrent neural network (RNN)
Generative adversarial network (GAN)
Autoencoder
aiml.e: RNNs maintain hidden states that capture temporal dependencies, making them naturally suited for sequential data where the order and timing of observations matter.

aiml.q: L2 regularization adds a penalty term to the loss function proportional to what?
aiml.a: The sum of squared weights
c: The sum of absolute weights
The sum of squared weights
The number of non-zero weights
The gradient magnitude
aiml.e: L2 regularization (weight decay) adds lambda * sum(w^2) to the loss, encouraging smaller weight values and smoother decision boundaries to reduce overfitting.

aiml.q: When training a deep learning model, what is the effect of using a very large batch size compared to a small batch size?
aiml.a: More stable gradients but potentially poorer generalization
c: More noisy gradients and faster convergence
More stable gradients but potentially poorer generalization
Faster training time per epoch regardless of hardware
Guaranteed better final accuracy
aiml.e: Large batches produce more accurate gradient estimates (less noise) but can converge to sharper minima that generalize poorly. Small batches introduce beneficial noise that can help escape local minima.

aiml.q: In an encoder-decoder architecture for segmentation, what is the primary role of the decoder?
aiml.a: To upsample feature maps and recover spatial resolution for pixel-wise prediction
c: To compress the input into a low-dimensional latent representation
To upsample feature maps and recover spatial resolution for pixel-wise prediction
To classify the entire image into a single category
To detect and remove noise from the input image
aiml.e: The decoder progressively upsamples the compressed feature representation back to the original input resolution, producing dense per-pixel predictions needed for segmentation.

aiml.q: What mechanism allows a neural network to learn which spatial regions or channels of a feature map are most relevant?
aiml.a: Attention mechanism
c: Max pooling
Batch normalization
Attention mechanism
Skip connection
aiml.e: Attention mechanisms compute importance weights for different spatial locations or feature channels, allowing the network to focus on the most relevant information for the task.

aiml.q: Vision Transformers (ViT) process images by dividing them into what?
aiml.a: Fixed-size patches that are linearly embedded as token sequences
c: Individual pixels processed one at a time
Fixed-size patches that are linearly embedded as token sequences
Multi-scale pyramids at different resolutions
Random crops with overlapping regions
aiml.e: ViT splits an image into fixed-size non-overlapping patches, flattens and linearly projects each patch into an embedding, then processes the sequence of patch embeddings with a standard Transformer encoder.

aiml.q: For volumetric medical image analysis (e.g., CT scans), what type of convolution operates in three spatial dimensions?
aiml.a: 3D convolution
c: 1D convolution
2D convolution
3D convolution
Depthwise separable convolution
aiml.e: 3D convolutions use volumetric kernels (e.g., 3x3x3) to capture spatial features across all three dimensions simultaneously, preserving inter-slice context in volumetric data like CT and MRI.

aiml.q: What is the purpose of dilated (atrous) convolutions in segmentation networks?
aiml.a: To increase the receptive field without increasing the number of parameters
c: To reduce the spatial resolution of feature maps
To increase the receptive field without increasing the number of parameters
To perform channel-wise feature recalibration
To enable variable-length input processing
aiml.e: Dilated convolutions insert gaps between kernel elements, expanding the receptive field exponentially with each layer while maintaining the same number of parameters and spatial resolution.

aiml.q: What distinguishes instance segmentation from semantic segmentation?
aiml.a: Instance segmentation differentiates individual objects of the same class
c: Instance segmentation only works on binary masks
Instance segmentation differentiates individual objects of the same class
Semantic segmentation provides higher spatial accuracy
Instance segmentation does not require labelled training data
aiml.e: Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel but does not distinguish between different instances of the same class, whereas instance segmentation identifies each separate object.

aiml.q: In knowledge distillation, what does the student network learn from?
aiml.a: The soft probability outputs (logits) of a larger teacher network
c: The raw training data only
The soft probability outputs (logits) of a larger teacher network
The architecture diagram of the teacher network
A hand-crafted set of rules derived from the teacher
aiml.e: Knowledge distillation transfers knowledge from a large teacher model to a smaller student model by training the student to match the teacher's soft output distributions, which contain richer information than hard labels.

aiml.q: What advantage does a patch-based approach offer when training deep learning models on large medical images?
aiml.a: It reduces GPU memory requirements while allowing training on high-resolution images
c: It guarantees faster convergence than full-image training
It eliminates the need for data augmentation
It reduces GPU memory requirements while allowing training on high-resolution images
It always produces better segmentation accuracy than full-image approaches
aiml.e: Medical images (e.g., whole-slide pathology, high-res CT) often exceed GPU memory. Patch-based training extracts smaller sub-regions, enabling training on high-resolution data within hardware constraints.

aiml.q: In the VGG network architecture, what design principle characterizes the convolutional layers?
aiml.a: Uniform use of small 3x3 convolutional filters throughout
c: Large 11x11 filters in early layers
Uniform use of small 3x3 convolutional filters throughout
Inception modules with multiple parallel filter sizes
Depthwise separable convolutions to reduce parameters
aiml.e: VGG demonstrated that stacking multiple small 3x3 convolutional filters achieves the same effective receptive field as larger filters while using fewer parameters and adding more non-linearity.

aiml.q: Which object detection architecture directly predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities in a single forward pass without region proposals?
aiml.a: YOLO (You Only Look Once)
c: Faster R-CNN
YOLO (You Only Look Once)
R-CNN
Selective Search
aiml.e: YOLO is a single-stage detector that treats detection as a regression problem, dividing the image into a grid and predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities simultaneously in one pass.

aiml.q: The Dice similarity coefficient (DSC) for auto-segmentation evaluation ranges from 0 to 1. What does a DSC of 1.0 indicate?
aiml.a: Perfect overlap between the predicted and reference contours
c: No overlap between contours
Perfect overlap between the predicted and reference contours
50% overlap between contours
The contours have identical volumes but may not overlap
aiml.e: DSC = 2|A intersect B| / (|A| + |B|). A value of 1.0 means the predicted segmentation and reference standard are identical in every voxel.

aiml.q: Which metric is most sensitive to outlier segmentation errors such as a small island of contour far from the true structure?
aiml.a: Hausdorff distance
c: Dice similarity coefficient
Mean surface distance
Hausdorff distance
Volumetric overlap ratio
aiml.e: The Hausdorff distance measures the maximum distance between the closest points on two surfaces, making it highly sensitive to outlier contour errors that other volumetric metrics may miss.

aiml.q: What is the mean surface distance (MSD) metric used for in auto-segmentation evaluation?
aiml.a: Measuring the average distance between the surfaces of two contours
c: Measuring volumetric overlap between two regions
Measuring the average distance between the surfaces of two contours
Counting the number of misclassified voxels
Evaluating the smoothness of a single contour
aiml.e: MSD calculates the average of all closest-point distances between the surfaces of the predicted and reference contours, providing a clinically intuitive measure of overall contour agreement.

aiml.q: When commissioning a commercial auto-segmentation system, what is the recommended benchmark for acceptable performance?
aiml.a: Inter-observer variability among expert clinicians
c: A Dice score above 0.95 for all structures
Inter-observer variability among expert clinicians
Zero manual edits required on all test cases
Agreement with a single expert's contours within 1 mm
aiml.e: AAPM and ESTRO guidelines recommend that auto-segmentation performance should be compared against inter-observer variability, as this represents the inherent uncertainty in manual contouring.

aiml.q: Which common imaging artifact can cause auto-segmentation models to fail, particularly near dental work or surgical hardware?
aiml.a: Metal artifacts (streaking and beam hardening)
c: Motion blur from breathing
Metal artifacts (streaking and beam hardening)
Quantum noise from low-dose protocols
Truncation artifacts from small field-of-view
aiml.e: Metal implants cause severe CT artifacts (streaking, beam hardening, photon starvation) that distort anatomy and can cause auto-segmentation models to produce grossly inaccurate contours.

aiml.q: What is a commonly cited clinical acceptability threshold for Dice similarity coefficient of parotid gland auto-segmentation?
aiml.e: For parotid glands, DSC values above 0.80 are generally considered clinically acceptable for auto-segmentation, though this varies by structure and clinical context.

aiml.q: What is the primary clinical benefit of implementing auto-segmentation in a radiation therapy workflow?
aiml.a: Significant reduction in contouring time while maintaining consistency
c: Elimination of the need for physician review of contours
Significant reduction in contouring time while maintaining consistency
Complete removal of inter-observer variability
Guaranteed improvement in treatment outcomes
aiml.e: Auto-segmentation can reduce contouring time from hours to minutes for a typical case, improving workflow efficiency while providing a consistent starting point for physician review and editing.

aiml.q: When reviewing auto-segmented contours, which workflow step is considered essential before clinical use?
aiml.a: Physician review and manual editing of all auto-generated contours
c: Automatic approval if the Dice score exceeds 0.90
Physician review and manual editing of all auto-generated contours
Running the auto-segmentation twice to check reproducibility
Comparison against a single atlas-based result
aiml.e: Current guidelines require that all auto-generated contours undergo physician review and editing as needed before clinical use, as even high-performing models can produce clinically unacceptable errors.

aiml.q: Approximately how many training cases are typically recommended as a minimum for training a deep learning auto-segmentation model from scratch for a single anatomical site?
aiml.a: 100-300 cases
c: 5-10 cases
20-50 cases
100-300 cases
10,000+ cases
aiml.e: While data requirements vary, published literature generally suggests 100-300 well-curated, consistently contoured cases as a practical minimum for training robust single-site auto-segmentation models.

aiml.q: Which morphological post-processing operation is commonly applied to auto-segmented contours to remove small isolated false-positive regions?
aiml.a: Morphological opening (erosion followed by dilation)
c: Morphological closing (dilation followed by erosion)
Morphological opening (erosion followed by dilation)
Gaussian blurring of the probability map
Histogram equalization of the input image
aiml.e: Morphological opening (erosion then dilation) removes small isolated regions and thin protrusions while approximately preserving the shape and size of larger structures.

aiml.q: What is a key failure mode of auto-segmentation when applied to patients with unusual anatomy, such as post-surgical changes?
aiml.a: The model may hallucinate structures that have been surgically removed
c: The model always produces an error message and refuses to segment
The model may hallucinate structures that have been surgically removed
The model automatically switches to atlas-based segmentation
The model produces contours that are more accurate than for normal anatomy
aiml.e: Deep learning models trained predominantly on normal anatomy may generate contours for organs that have been surgically removed (e.g., contouring a kidney that was resected), highlighting the need for physician review.

aiml.q: DLCExpert is an auto-contouring solution based on which segmentation approach?
aiml.a: Deep learning contouring using trained neural networks
c: Atlas-based segmentation with deformable registration
Deep learning contouring using trained neural networks
Statistical shape modeling with active contours
Rule-based segmentation using Hounsfield unit thresholds
aiml.e: DLCExpert (Deep Learning Contouring Expert) uses deep learning neural networks trained on expert-delineated contours to provide automated segmentation for radiation therapy planning.

aiml.q: What statistical technique does RapidPlan use to model the relationship between geometric features and achievable DVH values?
aiml.a: Principal component analysis (PCA)
c: K-means clustering
Principal component analysis (PCA)
Support vector regression
Random forest ensemble
aiml.e: RapidPlan uses PCA to reduce the dimensionality of DVH data across a library of prior plans, extracting the principal modes of variation that relate geometric features to achievable dose distributions.

aiml.q: What is typically considered the minimum number of high-quality training plans needed for a robust RapidPlan model?
aiml.a: 20 to 40 plans
c: 5 to 10 plans
20 to 40 plans
100 to 200 plans
500 or more plans
aiml.e: Varian recommends approximately 20-40 high-quality, clinically approved plans to train a RapidPlan model. Fewer plans risk poor generalizability, while more plans show diminishing returns.

aiml.q: Which geometric feature is most critical in RapidPlan's DVH estimation for an organ at risk?
aiml.a: OAR-to-PTV overlap volume and distance
c: Patient body mass index
OAR-to-PTV overlap volume and distance
Beam energy spectrum
Couch angle configuration
aiml.e: The spatial relationship between the OAR and PTV, particularly their overlap volume and closest distance, is the dominant geometric feature driving achievable dose in knowledge-based planning.

aiml.q: In Pinnacle AutoPlanning, what is the primary optimization strategy used to iteratively improve plan quality?
aiml.a: Progressive optimization with automated objective adjustment
c: Single-pass fluence optimization only
Progressive optimization with automated objective adjustment
Manual planner intervention at each iteration
Fixed template application without adaptation
aiml.e: Pinnacle AutoPlanning uses a progressive optimization engine that automatically tightens OAR objectives and adjusts priorities iteratively, mimicking an experienced planner's workflow without manual intervention.

aiml.q: What is the key concept behind multi-criteria optimization (MCO) as implemented in RayStation?
aiml.a: Generating a set of Pareto-optimal plans that allow interactive navigation of tradeoffs
c: Selecting a single globally optimal plan using genetic algorithms
Generating a set of Pareto-optimal plans that allow interactive navigation of tradeoffs
Optimizing only for target coverage while ignoring OAR constraints
Using random sampling to find the best plan from thousands of candidates
aiml.e: MCO in RayStation pre-computes a Pareto surface of non-dominated plans, allowing planners to interactively slide between tradeoffs (e.g., target coverage vs. OAR sparing) in real time.

aiml.q: In dose mimicking for AI treatment planning, what does the model learn to replicate?
aiml.a: The 3D dose distribution from a reference clinically approved plan
c: The beam configuration and MLC leaf positions only
The 3D dose distribution from a reference clinically approved plan
The physician's contour preferences
The linac calibration parameters
aiml.e: Dose mimicking trains an optimization to reproduce a reference dose distribution voxel-by-voxel, effectively transferring the quality of expert-generated plans to new patients.

aiml.q: What is a Pareto-optimal plan in the context of radiotherapy treatment planning?
aiml.a: A plan where no objective can be improved without worsening at least one other objective
