c: A plan that achieves the absolute minimum dose to all organs at risk
A plan where no objective can be improved without worsening at least one other objective
A plan that uses the fewest number of beams possible
A plan that maximizes monitor units for treatment efficiency
aiml.e: A Pareto-optimal plan lies on the Pareto frontier, meaning any improvement in one planning objective necessarily comes at the cost of degrading another, representing the best achievable tradeoff.

aiml.q: How does reinforcement learning differ from supervised learning when applied to treatment planning?
aiml.a: It learns a policy through trial-and-error interaction with a planning environment using reward signals
c: It requires paired input-output training data from expert plans
It learns a policy through trial-and-error interaction with a planning environment using reward signals
It uses only unsupervised clustering of patient anatomies
It directly copies beam parameters from the most similar prior patient
aiml.e: Reinforcement learning agents interact with a treatment planning system as an environment, receiving rewards for achieving clinical goals and learning an optimization policy without requiring explicit expert demonstrations.

aiml.q: What is the primary advantage of using a 3D U-Net architecture for deep learning dose prediction?
aiml.a: Skip connections preserve spatial resolution while the encoder captures global context
c: It requires only 2D axial slices and ignores volumetric context
Skip connections preserve spatial resolution while the encoder captures global context
It eliminates the need for contoured structures as input
It directly outputs deliverable MLC sequences
aiml.e: The U-Net encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections allows the network to learn both coarse global dose patterns and fine local dose gradients, making it well-suited for voxel-level 3D dose prediction.

aiml.q: What is the primary concern regarding generalizability of AI planning models across different linac vendors?
aiml.a: Differences in MLC design, beam modeling, and dose calculation algorithms affect plan characteristics
c: All linac vendors produce identical dose distributions
Differences in MLC design, beam modeling, and dose calculation algorithms affect plan characteristics
Vendor-specific software licenses prevent cross-platform model use
Patient anatomy changes depending on the treatment machine
aiml.e: MLC leaf width, transmission factors, beam modeling parameters, and dose calculation engines differ between vendors, so AI models trained on one vendor's plans may not produce optimal or deliverable plans on another vendor's equipment.

aiml.q: What is GE TrueFidelity in the context of CT image reconstruction?
aiml.a: A deep learning-based image reconstruction algorithm trained on high-quality filtered back projection images
c: A deep learning-based image reconstruction algorithm trained on high-quality filtered back projection images
A hardware upgrade that replaces the CT detector array
An iterative reconstruction method based on algebraic reconstruction technique
A post-processing noise filter applied after standard reconstruction
aiml.e: GE TrueFidelity (DLIR) is a deep learning reconstruction algorithm trained on high-dose FBP reference images to produce low-noise images while maintaining the image texture and spatial resolution characteristics of FBP.

aiml.q: What is a key advantage of DLR over iterative reconstruction regarding image texture?
aiml.a: DLR better preserves natural image texture similar to FBP while still reducing noise
c: DLR produces a characteristically waxy or plastic texture
DLR better preserves natural image texture similar to FBP while still reducing noise
DLR always produces grainy images regardless of settings
DLR and iterative reconstruction produce identical textures
aiml.e: A common criticism of iterative reconstruction is the unnatural, blotchy texture at aggressive strength settings. DLR algorithms are specifically trained to maintain FBP-like texture while achieving comparable or superior noise reduction.

aiml.q: What percentage of CT radiation dose reduction has been demonstrated as achievable with deep learning reconstruction while maintaining diagnostic image quality?
aiml.a: Up to 40-60%
c: Less than 5%
Up to 40-60%
Over 90%
aiml.e: Studies have demonstrated that DLR can enable 40-60% dose reduction compared to standard FBP reconstruction while maintaining acceptable image quality for clinical diagnosis, depending on the clinical task and body region.

aiml.q: What type of training data is typically used for supervised deep learning CT reconstruction models?
aiml.a: Paired low-dose and corresponding high-dose (or full-dose) CT image datasets
c: Unpaired random CT images from different patients
Paired low-dose and corresponding high-dose (or full-dose) CT image datasets
Only synthetic phantom images without patient data
MRI images converted to CT Hounsfield units
aiml.e: Supervised DLR models are trained on paired datasets where low-dose images serve as input and corresponding high-dose or full-dose images serve as the target ground truth, teaching the network to map noisy images to clean ones.

aiml.q: How does deep learning reconstruction affect quantitative HU accuracy in CT imaging?
aiml.a: DLR is designed to preserve HU accuracy while reducing noise, maintaining quantitative fidelity
c: DLR systematically shifts HU values upward by 50-100 HU
DLR is designed to preserve HU accuracy while reducing noise, maintaining quantitative fidelity
DLR eliminates the ability to measure HU values entirely
DLR converts all images to relative electron density
aiml.e: A critical requirement for clinical DLR algorithms is preservation of quantitative HU accuracy, as HU values are essential for tissue characterization, contrast enhancement assessment, and radiation therapy dose calculation.

aiml.q: What regulatory pathway do most AI-based CT reconstruction algorithms follow for market clearance in the United States?
aiml.a: FDA 510(k) premarket notification
c: FDA Premarket Approval (PMA)
FDA 510(k) premarket notification
No regulatory clearance is required
Institutional Review Board approval only
aiml.e: Most DLR algorithms receive FDA clearance through the 510(k) pathway by demonstrating substantial equivalence to predicate reconstruction devices, as they are classified as Class II medical devices.

aiml.q: How does deep learning accelerate MRI acquisition?
aiml.a: By reconstructing diagnostic-quality images from undersampled k-space data
c: By increasing the main magnetic field strength
By reconstructing diagnostic-quality images from undersampled k-space data
By replacing the RF coils with neural network hardware
By eliminating the need for gradient coils
aiml.e: DL-based MRI reconstruction learns to fill in missing k-space data from accelerated (undersampled) acquisitions, enabling shorter scan times while maintaining image quality that would normally require full k-space sampling.

aiml.q: What type of artifacts can deep learning reconstruction help reduce in CT imaging?
aiml.a: Metal artifacts, beam hardening, and noise-related streaks
c: Only ring artifacts from detector miscalibration
Metal artifacts, beam hardening, and noise-related streaks
Motion artifacts exclusively
DLR cannot reduce any type of artifact
aiml.e: DLR networks trained on artifact-affected and artifact-free image pairs can learn to suppress metal artifacts, beam hardening streaks, and photon starvation noise, though performance varies by artifact type and severity.

aiml.q: What is radiogenomics in the context of radiomics?
aiml.a: The study linking imaging phenotype features to underlying genomic or molecular characteristics of tumors
c: Using radiation to modify the genome for therapeutic purposes
The study linking imaging phenotype features to underlying genomic or molecular characteristics of tumors
Genomic sequencing performed during radiation treatment
A radiation dose calculation method based on genetic algorithms
aiml.e: Radiogenomics investigates correlations between radiomic imaging features and tumor genomic profiles (e.g., gene expression, mutations), potentially enabling non-invasive assessment of molecular tumor characteristics through imaging.

aiml.q: What is the primary clinical motivation for generating synthetic CT (sCT) images from MRI in radiotherapy planning?
aiml.a: To enable MRI-only treatment planning by providing electron density information for dose calculation
c: To enable MRI-only treatment planning by providing electron density information for dose calculation
To replace MRI with CT for all contouring tasks
To improve MRI image resolution for target delineation
To reduce the cost of MRI scanners in radiation oncology departments
aiml.e: MRI provides superior soft-tissue contrast for contouring but lacks electron density information needed for dose calculation. Synthetic CT bridges this gap, eliminating the need for a separate CT scan and its associated registration uncertainties.

aiml.q: CycleGAN is preferred over pix2pix for MRI-to-CT synthesis when:
aiml.a: Paired MRI-CT training datasets are unavailable
c: The model needs to run in real time during treatment
Paired MRI-CT training datasets are unavailable
The output must be in DICOM-RT format
Only a single MRI sequence is available as input
aiml.e: CycleGAN uses cycle-consistency loss to learn mappings between two unpaired image domains, making it suitable when corresponding MRI-CT pairs are difficult to obtain. Pix2pix requires spatially aligned paired training data.

aiml.q: In pix2pix-based synthetic CT generation, what type of training data is required?
aiml.a: Spatially registered paired MRI and CT images from the same patients
c: Spatially registered paired MRI and CT images from the same patients
Unpaired MRI and CT images from different patient cohorts
Only MRI images with manual HU annotations by physicians
CT images alone, with MRI simulated via data augmentation
aiml.e: Pix2pix is a conditional GAN that learns a pixel-to-pixel mapping and therefore requires spatially aligned paired images for supervised training, unlike CycleGAN which works with unpaired data.

aiml.q: What is a major limitation of bulk density override methods compared to deep learning-based synthetic CT generation?
aiml.a: Bulk density methods cannot accurately capture heterogeneous tissue interfaces and complex anatomy
c: Bulk density methods are more computationally expensive than deep learning
Bulk density methods require paired training data while deep learning does not
Bulk density methods cannot accurately capture heterogeneous tissue interfaces and complex anatomy
Bulk density methods always overestimate bone density in the pelvis
aiml.e: Bulk density override assigns uniform HU values to segmented tissue classes, missing the continuous density variations at interfaces. DL-based sCT generates voxel-wise HU values that capture anatomical heterogeneity more accurately.

aiml.q: What is the most appropriate QA metric for evaluating the image quality of a synthetic CT compared to a reference planning CT?
aiml.a: Mean absolute error (MAE) in Hounsfield Units
c: Signal-to-noise ratio of the MRI input
Mean absolute error (MAE) in Hounsfield Units
The number of training epochs used in the model
Compression ratio of the DICOM file
aiml.e: MAE in HU quantifies the average voxel-wise deviation between synthetic and real CT images. It is commonly reported alongside dose difference metrics to comprehensively evaluate sCT accuracy for clinical use.

aiml.q: During clinical commissioning of synthetic CT for MRI-only planning, what is the standard validation approach?
aiml.a: Comparing dose distributions calculated on sCT against those on the co-acquired planning CT using gamma analysis and DVH metrics
c: Visual inspection of the sCT images by a single radiation oncologist
Comparing dose distributions calculated on sCT against those on the co-acquired planning CT using gamma analysis and DVH metrics
Testing the sCT on a single phantom and extrapolating to all patient anatomies
Verifying that the sCT file can be imported into the treatment planning system
aiml.e: Clinical commissioning requires dosimetric validation on a cohort of patients, comparing sCT-based and CT-based dose calculations using gamma analysis (e.g., 1%/1mm) and DVH parameter differences for targets and OARs.

aiml.q: How can machine learning improve gamma analysis in patient-specific QA?
aiml.a: By predicting gamma pass rates from plan complexity metrics without requiring physical measurement
c: By increasing the spatial resolution of the gamma calculation grid
By predicting gamma pass rates from plan complexity metrics without requiring physical measurement
By replacing the gamma index with a simpler percentage difference metric
By automatically adjusting the linac output to achieve 100% pass rates
aiml.e: ML models trained on historical QA data can predict gamma pass/fail outcomes from plan features such as modulation complexity score and leaf travel, enabling risk-stratified QA where only high-risk plans require full measurement.

aiml.q: What is the modulation complexity score (MCS) used for in AI-based plan quality assessment?
aiml.a: It quantifies the degree of MLC modulation in IMRT/VMAT plans and serves as a predictor of deliverability and QA outcomes
c: It measures the patient's anatomical complexity for treatment planning
It quantifies the degree of MLC modulation in IMRT/VMAT plans and serves as a predictor of deliverability and QA outcomes
It calculates the total monitor units required for a treatment fraction
It rates the difficulty of organ-at-risk contouring on a 1-10 scale
aiml.e: MCS incorporates leaf position variability and aperture area changes across control points. Higher modulation complexity correlates with lower gamma pass rates, making MCS a valuable input feature for ML-based QA prediction models.

aiml.q: What role does natural language processing (NLP) play in radiation therapy incident learning systems?
aiml.a: NLP parses free-text incident reports to automatically classify event types, identify root causes, and detect safety trends
c: NLP translates treatment plans into patient-friendly language
NLP parses free-text incident reports to automatically classify event types, identify root causes, and detect safety trends
NLP generates radiation safety regulations from published guidelines
NLP converts spoken physician directives into machine-readable treatment parameters
aiml.e: Incident learning systems accumulate large volumes of free-text reports. NLP can automatically categorize these reports, extract contributing factors, and identify recurring patterns that might be missed in manual review, improving patient safety.

aiml.q: How can anomaly detection algorithms be applied to treatment delivery monitoring?
aiml.a: By learning normal delivery patterns and flagging statistically unusual deviations in real-time machine parameters
c: By requiring manual entry of expected parameters before each treatment
By learning normal delivery patterns and flagging statistically unusual deviations in real-time machine parameters
By replacing all interlock systems with software-based controls
By comparing each delivery only to the immediately preceding fraction
aiml.e: Unsupervised anomaly detection models learn the distribution of normal delivery parameters and can identify outliers in MLC motion, dose rate, or gantry speed that may indicate equipment malfunction or setup errors.

aiml.q: What is the role of AI in commissioning data analysis for beam modeling in treatment planning systems?
aiml.a: To assist in detecting measurement outliers, smoothing beam data, and optimizing beam model parameters to match measured data
c: To eliminate the need for any water tank measurements
To assist in detecting measurement outliers, smoothing beam data, and optimizing beam model parameters to match measured data
To generate commissioning data entirely from Monte Carlo simulations without measurements
To automatically certify the TPS for clinical use without human validation
aiml.e: AI tools can identify erroneous data points in measured beam profiles and depth doses, assist in data smoothing, and help optimize beam model parameters, reducing commissioning time while maintaining accuracy.

aiml.q: What is a key advantage of AI-based automated linac QA trending?
aiml.a: It can detect subtle systematic drifts in machine performance that may not be apparent from individual daily measurements
c: It eliminates the need for baseline measurements during commissioning
It can detect subtle systematic drifts in machine performance that may not be apparent from individual daily measurements
It automatically recalibrates the linac output without physicist involvement
It replaces the annual QA program mandated by regulatory bodies
aiml.e: AI trending tools analyze longitudinal QA data to identify gradual drifts in output, symmetry, or MLC positioning that fall within individual tolerance limits but indicate a developing issue requiring corrective action.

aiml.q: What is the purpose of SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values in the context of explainable AI for medical physics?
aiml.a: To quantify the contribution of each input feature to a specific model prediction, providing local interpretability
c: To measure the overall accuracy of the AI model on the test dataset
To quantify the contribution of each input feature to a specific model prediction, providing local interpretability
To compress the AI model for deployment on edge devices
To generate synthetic training data for underrepresented patient groups
aiml.e: SHAP values, based on game-theoretic Shapley values, assign each input feature a contribution score for a given prediction. This allows clinicians to understand why the model made a specific recommendation, supporting clinical trust and regulatory requirements.

aiml.q: What is automation complacency in the context of AI-assisted radiotherapy?
aiml.a: The tendency for clinicians to over-rely on AI outputs and reduce their own vigilance in checking for errors
c: The AI system becoming less accurate over time due to software aging
A regulatory requirement to automate all routine QA tasks
The tendency for clinicians to over-rely on AI outputs and reduce their own vigilance in checking for errors
The process of gradually replacing manual tasks with automated workflows
aiml.e: Automation complacency is a well-documented human factors issue where users develop excessive trust in automated systems, leading to reduced critical evaluation of AI outputs. This can allow AI errors to propagate unchecked in clinical workflows.

aiml.q: What is the primary advantage of using a 3D U-Net for voxel-level dose prediction in radiotherapy?
aiml.a: It preserves spatial context through skip connections while learning hierarchical features from CT and contour data
c: It preserves spatial context through skip connections while learning hierarchical features from CT and contour data
It requires no training data and uses physics-based constraints exclusively
It generates dose predictions only at the isocentre point for speed
It eliminates the need for beam angle selection by predicting optimal gantry positions
aiml.e: The 3D U-Net encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections preserves fine spatial detail while the deep layers capture global dose distribution patterns, making it ideal for voxel-wise dose prediction.

aiml.q: In AI-based dose-volume histogram (DVH) prediction, what is a common loss function used to train the model?
aiml.a: Mean squared error between predicted and ground-truth DVH curves
c: Cross-entropy loss on voxel classification labels
Mean squared error between predicted and ground-truth DVH curves
Dice loss computed on binary dose masks
Hinge loss between dose bins and organ contours
aiml.e: DVH prediction models typically minimize the MSE between the predicted and planned DVH values at sampled dose points, directly optimizing for dose-volume accuracy.

aiml.q: What type of input features are most commonly used for ML-based normal tissue complication probability (NTCP) modeling?
aiml.a: DVH parameters, clinical factors, and patient demographics
c: Raw CT pixel intensities without any preprocessing
DVH parameters, clinical factors, and patient demographics
Only the prescribed dose and number of fractions
Beam energy spectra and head scatter factors
aiml.e: Modern ML-based NTCP models incorporate DVH-derived features (e.g., Dmean, V20), clinical covariates (e.g., chemotherapy status), and demographics to improve toxicity prediction beyond traditional Lyman-Kutcher-Burman models.

aiml.q: How does multi-task learning benefit simultaneous prediction of tumor control probability (TCP) and NTCP?
aiml.a: Shared feature representations between tasks act as implicit regularization, improving generalization for both predictions
c: It trains two completely separate models and averages their outputs for robustness
It eliminates the need for labeled outcome data by using self-supervision
Shared feature representations between tasks act as implicit regularization, improving generalization for both predictions
It converts the regression problem into a simpler binary classification task
aiml.e: Multi-task learning jointly optimizes TCP and NTCP objectives, forcing the network to learn shared representations that capture common dose-response features, which regularizes the model and reduces overfitting.

aiml.q: What is the primary purpose of Monte Carlo denoising using deep learning in radiation therapy?
aiml.a: To produce low-noise dose distributions from fewer particle histories, reducing computation time
c: To replace the Monte Carlo algorithm entirely with a neural network
To produce low-noise dose distributions from fewer particle histories, reducing computation time
To eliminate the need for CT images in dose calculation
To convert pencil beam calculations into Monte Carlo accuracy
aiml.e: DL-based MC denoising trains on pairs of noisy (few histories) and clean (many histories) dose distributions, enabling clinically acceptable dose accuracy with 10-100x fewer histories.

aiml.q: In uncertainty quantification for AI dose prediction, what does Monte Carlo dropout provide?
aiml.a: An approximation of Bayesian inference by sampling multiple stochastic forward passes at test time
c: A method to select the optimal dropout rate during training only
An approximation of Bayesian inference by sampling multiple stochastic forward passes at test time
A technique to reduce the model size for faster inference on clinical hardware
A regularization that eliminates all prediction uncertainty
aiml.e: Monte Carlo dropout keeps dropout active during inference and runs multiple forward passes to generate a distribution of predictions, approximating Bayesian posterior uncertainty.

aiml.q: What is the key advantage of using AI for real-time dose calculation during adaptive radiotherapy?
aiml.a: Inference speed of seconds compared to minutes for conventional algorithms, enabling online plan adaptation
c: It completely eliminates the need for quality assurance of adapted plans
Inference speed of seconds compared to minutes for conventional algorithms, enabling online plan adaptation
It always produces more accurate doses than Monte Carlo simulation
It removes the requirement for daily imaging before treatment
aiml.e: AI dose engines can compute full 3D dose distributions in seconds, making online adaptive workflows feasible where conventional dose calculations would take too long for the patient to remain on the treatment couch.

aiml.q: In patient-specific QA dose prediction, what does an AI model typically predict?
aiml.a: The expected gamma pass rate or dose difference map for a given treatment plan
c: The optimal measurement device and setup for QA verification
The expected gamma pass rate or dose difference map for a given treatment plan
The number of measurement points needed for adequate sampling
Whether the physicist should accept or reject the plan without measurement
aiml.e: AI-based patient-specific QA models predict gamma pass rates or dose error maps from plan complexity metrics and MLC parameters, flagging plans likely to fail before measurement.

aiml.q: What is a key challenge in using ML for survival prediction from radiotherapy treatment plans?
aiml.a: Censored outcome data requires specialized loss functions such as the Cox partial likelihood
c: Treatment plans have too few parameters to serve as meaningful input features
Censored outcome data requires specialized loss functions such as the Cox partial likelihood
Neural networks cannot process time-to-event data in any format
Survival data is always normally distributed and requires no special handling
aiml.e: Clinical survival data is right-censored (patients lost to follow-up), requiring survival-specific losses like Cox partial likelihood or ranking losses rather than standard regression objectives.

aiml.q: How does biological dose optimization with AI differ from conventional physical dose optimization?
aiml.a: It incorporates radiobiological models and learned tissue-specific response parameters to optimize for predicted clinical outcomes
c: It incorporates radiobiological models and learned tissue-specific response parameters to optimize for predicted clinical outcomes
It replaces dose-volume constraints with simple maximum dose limits on all structures
Biological optimization only considers tumor dose and ignores all organs at risk
It uses fixed alpha/beta ratios for all tissues without any patient-specific adaptation
aiml.e: AI biological optimization learns to incorporate tissue-specific radiosensitivity parameters and outcome data to optimize treatment plans for predicted biological effect rather than just physical dose distributions.

aiml.q: In toxicity prediction models for radiotherapy, which approach has shown the most improvement over traditional NTCP models?
aiml.a: Gradient-boosted decision trees incorporating dosimetric, clinical, and genomic features
c: Simple linear regression using prescribed dose only
K-nearest neighbors using only patient age and tumor stage
Gradient-boosted decision trees incorporating dosimetric, clinical, and genomic features
Unsupervised clustering of patient images without dose information
aiml.e: Ensemble methods like gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost, LightGBM) consistently outperform traditional NTCP models by capturing non-linear interactions among dosimetric, clinical, and multi-omic features.

aiml.q: What metric is most appropriate for evaluating AI-based treatment response assessment during a radiotherapy course?
aiml.a: Area under the ROC curve (AUC) for classifying responders versus non-responders
c: Structural similarity index (SSIM) between pre- and post-treatment images
Area under the ROC curve (AUC) for classifying responders versus non-responders
Peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) of the follow-up CT scan
Root mean square error of predicted versus actual tumor volume
aiml.e: Treatment response assessment is typically framed as a binary classification (responder vs. non-responder), making AUC the standard discrimination metric, often supplemented by calibration analysis.

aiml.q: What is the main advantage of using deep learning for adaptive dose modification during fractionated radiotherapy?
aiml.a: It can integrate daily imaging changes to predict optimal dose adjustments faster than replanning from scratch
c: It can integrate daily imaging changes to predict optimal dose adjustments faster than replanning from scratch
It guarantees that the total dose matches the original plan exactly at every voxel
It eliminates the need for physician review of any plan changes
It only requires the original CT scan and never uses daily imaging data
aiml.e: DL-based adaptive dose modification leverages daily imaging (CBCT, MRI) to rapidly predict optimal dose adjustments, enabling fraction-by-fraction plan adaptation without full re-optimization.

aiml.q: In outcome prediction from treatment plans, which feature extraction approach captures the full spatial dose distribution?
aiml.a: 3D convolutional neural networks applied directly to the dose grid
c: Extracting only the maximum dose to each organ at risk
Using the mean dose to the PTV as the sole predictor variable
3D convolutional neural networks applied directly to the dose grid
Manual selection of two DVH points per structure
aiml.e: 3D CNNs process the entire voxel-level dose distribution, capturing spatial dose patterns and dose-anatomy relationships that DVH-based features cannot represent.

aiml.q: What is the primary purpose of dose prediction AI models in knowledge-based planning (KBP)?
aiml.a: To generate achievable dose distributions from patient anatomy that guide the optimizer toward high-quality plans
c: To replace the treatment planning system optimizer entirely
To generate achievable dose distributions from patient anatomy that guide the optimizer toward high-quality plans
To eliminate the need for contouring organs at risk before planning
To determine the optimal number of treatment fractions for each patient
aiml.e: KBP dose prediction models learn from prior high-quality plans to predict achievable dose distributions for new patients, which then serve as optimization objectives to reduce plan quality variability.

aiml.q: What is federated learning in the context of multi-institutional medical physics AI?
aiml.a: A distributed training approach where models are trained locally at each institution and only model updates are shared, keeping patient data on-site
c: A centralized database where all institutions upload their patient data for joint model training
A distributed training approach where models are trained locally at each institution and only model updates are shared, keeping patient data on-site
A method of transferring a pre-trained model from one institution to another without any updates
A technique for generating synthetic patient data that can be freely shared across institutions
aiml.e: Federated learning enables collaborative model training across institutions by exchanging only model weight updates (gradients) rather than raw patient data, preserving privacy while leveraging diverse datasets.

aiml.q: What is differential privacy in the context of AI training on medical data?
aiml.a: A mathematical framework that adds calibrated noise to model updates to bound the information any single patient contributes
c: Encrypting all patient data at rest and in transit during model training
A mathematical framework that adds calibrated noise to model updates to bound the information any single patient contributes
Removing all patient identifiers from the training dataset before model training begins
Training separate models for each patient to prevent any data mixing
aiml.e: Differential privacy provides a formal guarantee (quantified by epsilon) that the model's output is statistically indistinguishable whether or not any single patient's data was included in training.

aiml.q: In federated learning for radiotherapy AI, what is the purpose of secure aggregation?
aiml.a: To combine model updates from multiple institutions such that the central server cannot inspect individual institution updates
c: To encrypt the final global model so it cannot be used without a license key
To ensure each institution trains for exactly the same number of epochs
To combine model updates from multiple institutions such that the central server cannot inspect individual institution updates
To validate that each institution's data meets minimum quality standards
aiml.e: Secure aggregation uses cryptographic protocols so the aggregation server only sees the combined result of all institutions' updates, preventing reverse-engineering of any single institution's data.

aiml.q: What do the FAIR data principles stand for in medical physics data infrastructure?
aiml.a: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable
c: Fast, Accurate, Integrated, Reproducible
Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable
Federated, Anonymized, Indexed, Regulated
Flexible, Automated, Intelligent, Reliable
aiml.e: FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) guide data management to maximize the utility and longevity of research data, increasingly adopted in medical physics for AI training datasets.

aiml.q: What is the primary challenge of data harmonization across institutions for training radiotherapy AI models?
aiml.a: Variability in imaging protocols, contouring conventions, planning techniques, and data formats across sites
c: All institutions use identical equipment and protocols, making harmonization unnecessary
Variability in imaging protocols, contouring conventions, planning techniques, and data formats across sites
Insufficient computational resources at any single institution to process the data
Legal prohibition on comparing treatment data between any two institutions
aiml.e: Inter-institutional variability in scanner vendors, acquisition parameters, contouring guidelines, dose calculation algorithms, and data storage formats creates distribution shifts that degrade model generalizability.

aiml.q: What standard is used for structured reporting of radiation therapy data to enable AI training pipelines?
aiml.a: DICOM RT objects and DICOM Structured Reporting (SR)
c: DICOM RT objects and DICOM Structured Reporting (SR)
HL7 FHIR exclusively without any DICOM components
Proprietary vendor-specific binary formats from each TPS
PDF reports scanned and stored in the hospital PACS
aiml.e: DICOM Structured Reporting provides standardized templates for encoding clinical and dosimetric data in machine-readable format, facilitating automated extraction for AI model training alongside standard DICOM RT objects.

aiml.q: What is the primary motivation for using annotation tools with active learning in radiotherapy AI workflows?
aiml.a: To prioritize labeling the most informative samples, reducing annotation effort while maximizing model performance
c: To automatically generate perfect ground-truth labels without human input
To prioritize labeling the most informative samples, reducing annotation effort while maximizing model performance
To replace physician expertise with crowd-sourced annotations from non-experts
To ensure every case in the dataset receives equal annotation time
aiml.e: Active learning selects the samples where the model is most uncertain for expert annotation, maximizing information gain per labeled case and reducing the burden of manual contouring or outcome labeling.

aiml.q: In multi-institutional model validation for radiotherapy AI, what is external validation?
aiml.a: Testing a trained model on data from institutions not involved in training to assess generalizability
c: Testing a trained model on data from institutions not involved in training to assess generalizability
Validating the model using a held-out subset of the training institution's data
Having the model developer verify the results match expectations before publication
Running the model on synthetic data generated by a GAN from the training set
aiml.e: External validation on independent institutional data is the gold standard for assessing whether an AI model generalizes beyond the training population and site-specific biases.

aiml.q: What is the main challenge of data curation for training radiotherapy AI models?
aiml.a: Ensuring consistency and quality of contours, dose calculations, and outcome labels across heterogeneous clinical records
c: The total amount of storage space required for DICOM RT files on institutional servers
Ensuring consistency and quality of contours, dose calculations, and outcome labels across heterogeneous clinical records
Converting all data from DICOM format to JPEG images for neural network compatibility
Obtaining ethics approval, which is the only significant barrier to data curation
aiml.e: Data curation requires addressing inconsistent naming conventions, variable contour quality, different dose calculation algorithms, missing outcome data, and labeling errors across retrospective clinical datasets.

aiml.q: What privacy-preserving technique allows multiple institutions to jointly compute a function over their combined data without revealing individual datasets?
aiml.a: Secure multi-party computation (SMPC)
c: Transfer learning with frozen weights
Data augmentation with random transforms
Secure multi-party computation (SMPC)
Batch normalization across distributed nodes
aiml.e: SMPC uses cryptographic protocols enabling multiple parties to jointly compute agreed-upon functions (e.g., model training) while each party's input data remains private from all others.

aiml.q: What unique challenge does FLASH radiotherapy present for AI-based dose rate monitoring?
aiml.a: Ultra-high dose rates (>40 Gy/s) require microsecond-scale detector readout and real-time AI inference for quality assurance
c: FLASH uses the same dose rates as conventional therapy so no special monitoring is needed
Ultra-high dose rates (>40 Gy/s) require microsecond-scale detector readout and real-time AI inference for quality assurance
FLASH therapy delivers dose over hours, requiring AI to manage prolonged monitoring fatigue
AI is only used for post-treatment analysis since real-time monitoring is impossible at any dose rate
aiml.e: FLASH RT delivers entire fractions in milliseconds at dose rates exceeding 40 Gy/s, requiring AI systems capable of ultra-fast beam monitoring and real-time QA to ensure the FLASH effect is achieved.

aiml.q: What is the primary role of AI in MR-linac adaptive workflows?
aiml.a: Fast auto-segmentation and dose recalculation on daily MRI to enable online adaptive replanning within clinical time constraints
c: Eliminating the need for any imaging during treatment delivery entirely
Fast auto-segmentation and dose recalculation on daily MRI to enable online adaptive replanning within clinical time constraints
Converting MR images to CT images for treatment delivery without any dosimetric input
Scheduling patients based on MRI scanner availability without regard to clinical priority
aiml.e: MR-linac workflows require rapid (<15 min) online plan adaptation; AI enables this by performing fast auto-contouring on daily MRI and accelerating dose recalculation for the adapted plan.

aiml.q: In proton therapy, what is the primary application of AI for range verification?
aiml.a: Predicting and verifying the proton beam range from prompt gamma or PET imaging to detect range errors in real time
c: Predicting and verifying the proton beam range from prompt gamma or PET imaging to detect range errors in real time
Calculating the water equivalent path length from the treatment planning system only
Adjusting the beam energy at the cyclotron based on patient weight alone
Eliminating range uncertainty margins from all proton treatment plans
aiml.e: AI analyzes prompt gamma emissions or post-treatment PET images to verify that the proton beam stopped at the intended depth, enabling detection of range errors caused by anatomical changes or setup uncertainties.

aiml.q: How can AI improve brachytherapy treatment planning?
aiml.a: By predicting optimal dwell positions and times from patient anatomy, reducing planning time while maintaining plan quality
c: By eliminating the need for imaging during applicator placement entirely
By predicting optimal dwell positions and times from patient anatomy, reducing planning time while maintaining plan quality
By replacing the physicist with a fully autonomous planning robot
By using only a single standard plan template for all patients regardless of anatomy
aiml.e: AI-based brachytherapy planning learns from historically optimized plans to predict dwell positions and dwell times for new patients, significantly reducing interactive planning time while achieving comparable dose distributions.

aiml.q: What is a digital twin in the context of radiation therapy?
aiml.a: A computational model that mirrors a specific patient's anatomy, physiology, and treatment response for simulation and prediction
c: A backup copy of the treatment planning system stored on a second server
A computational model that mirrors a specific patient's anatomy, physiology, and treatment response for simulation and prediction
A duplicate patient record created in the electronic health record system for auditing
A phantom that exactly matches a patient's external body contour for dosimetric verification
aiml.e: Digital twins integrate patient-specific imaging, dosimetric, biological, and clinical data into a dynamic computational model that can simulate treatment scenarios and predict individual outcomes.

aiml.q: What is the main application of natural language processing (NLP) for clinical notes in radiation oncology?
aiml.a: Automated extraction of treatment toxicities, outcomes, and clinical variables from unstructured free-text notes
c: Translating clinical notes from English into other languages for international collaboration
Automated extraction of treatment toxicities, outcomes, and clinical variables from unstructured free-text notes
Generating random patient names for anonymization of clinical trial records
Formatting clinical notes into visually appealing documents for patient distribution
aiml.e: NLP enables automated mining of unstructured clinical notes to extract structured outcome data (toxicities, recurrence, response) at scale, supporting large retrospective studies and AI model training.

aiml.q: What is the primary concern with using large language models (LLMs) for medical physics clinical decision support?
aiml.a: Hallucination of plausible-sounding but factually incorrect clinical information that could impact patient safety
c: LLMs are too slow to generate any useful output within clinical timeframes
Hallucination of plausible-sounding but factually incorrect clinical information that could impact patient safety
LLMs cannot process any form of numerical or quantitative data
They require more GPU memory than exists in any commercial computing system
aiml.e: LLMs can generate confident but incorrect medical physics information (hallucinations), posing patient safety risks if outputs are used without expert verification, necessitating robust validation frameworks.

aiml.q: What is a foundation model in the context of medical imaging for radiation therapy?
aiml.a: A large model pre-trained on broad imaging data that can be fine-tuned for multiple downstream tasks with limited labeled data
c: A model trained exclusively on a single institution's data for one specific segmentation task
The first version of any AI model before it undergoes hyperparameter tuning
A large model pre-trained on broad imaging data that can be fine-tuned for multiple downstream tasks with limited labeled data
A physics-based analytical dose calculation algorithm used as a baseline comparison
aiml.e: Foundation models learn generalizable visual representations from large-scale pre-training (e.g., on millions of medical images), enabling efficient adaptation to specific RT tasks like segmentation or registration with minimal labeled examples.

aiml.q: What is self-supervised learning and why is it valuable for medical physics AI?
aiml.a: Training on unlabeled data using pretext tasks (e.g., predicting masked image regions), leveraging abundant unlabeled medical images
c: Training where the model labels its own data using a separate supervised classifier
Training on unlabeled data using pretext tasks (e.g., predicting masked image regions), leveraging abundant unlabeled medical images
A method where the model trains itself without any data input whatsoever
Supervised training where the loss function is automatically selected by the framework
aiml.e: Self-supervised learning designs pretext tasks from unlabeled data (masked image modeling, contrastive objectives) to learn useful representations, critical in medical physics where labeled data is scarce but unlabeled scans are abundant.

aiml.q: In contrastive learning for medical imaging, what does the model learn to do?
aiml.a: Map augmented views of the same image close together and views of different images far apart in a learned embedding space
c: Classify each image into predefined diagnostic categories using labeled data
Generate new synthetic images by learning the data distribution
Map augmented views of the same image close together and views of different images far apart in a learned embedding space
Segment organs by contrasting foreground and background pixel intensities
aiml.e: Contrastive learning (e.g., SimCLR, MoCo) trains encoders to produce similar embeddings for augmented versions of the same image (positive pairs) and dissimilar embeddings for different images (negative pairs).

aiml.q: Why is few-shot learning particularly relevant for rare disease sites in radiation therapy?
aiml.a: It enables model training with very few labeled examples, addressing the scarcity of data for uncommon tumor types
c: It enables model training with very few labeled examples, addressing the scarcity of data for uncommon tumor types
It requires even more training data than standard approaches but produces better results
It eliminates the need for any disease-specific data by using generic algorithms
It is only applicable to common disease sites where data is already plentiful
aiml.e: Few-shot learning techniques (meta-learning, prototypical networks) can learn from just a handful of examples, addressing the fundamental challenge that rare tumor sites have insufficient cases for conventional deep learning.

aiml.q: What is continual (lifelong) learning in the context of clinical AI deployment for radiotherapy?
aiml.a: The ability of a model to learn from new data over time without forgetting previously learned knowledge
c: Training a model once during commissioning and never updating it again
Retraining the model from scratch whenever new data becomes available
The ability of a model to learn from new data over time without forgetting previously learned knowledge
Running the same model continuously on a dedicated GPU without power interruptions
aiml.e: Continual learning addresses catastrophic forgetting by enabling models to incrementally incorporate new institutional data, protocol changes, or equipment updates while retaining performance on previously learned tasks.

aiml.q: What is the main potential application of AI for linac commissioning automation?
aiml.a: Automated analysis of beam scanning data and generation of beam models with consistency checks across measured datasets
c: Completely replacing the medical physicist in the commissioning process
Automated analysis of beam scanning data and generation of beam models with consistency checks across measured datasets
Reducing the required beam data measurements to a single depth dose curve
Eliminating the need for independent verification of the commissioned beam model
aiml.e: AI can automate beam data analysis (PDDs, profiles, output factors), detect measurement anomalies, generate TPS beam models, and perform cross-consistency checks, reducing commissioning time while maintaining rigor.

aiml.q: How can neural radiance fields (NeRF) be applied to 4D imaging in radiation therapy?
aiml.a: By learning a continuous spatiotemporal representation of patient anatomy from sparse imaging data to synthesize novel viewpoints and time points
c: By replacing the CT scanner with a neural network that generates images without any physical measurement
By learning a continuous spatiotemporal representation of patient anatomy from sparse imaging data to synthesize novel viewpoints and time points
By compressing 4D CT datasets into smaller file sizes for archival storage
By classifying each CT voxel as tumor or normal tissue across all breathing phases
aiml.e: NeRF learns implicit 3D/4D representations from limited projection data, enabling high-quality synthesis of intermediate breathing phases and novel viewpoints, potentially reducing imaging dose in 4DCT.

aiml.q: What is the primary use of diffusion models for medical image generation in radiotherapy?
aiml.a: Generating realistic synthetic CT or MRI images for data augmentation and training when real patient data is limited
c: Modeling the physical diffusion of radiation dose through tissue
Generating realistic synthetic CT or MRI images for data augmentation and training when real patient data is limited
Simulating the diffusion of contrast agent through the vasculature during imaging
Computing the heat diffusion equation for hyperthermia treatment planning
aiml.e: Diffusion models (e.g., DDPM) learn to generate high-fidelity synthetic medical images by iteratively denoising from Gaussian noise, providing privacy-preserving data augmentation for training other AI models.

aiml.q: What is the purpose of AI-powered automated treatment plan narratives?
aiml.a: Automatically generating human-readable plan summaries from structured DICOM RT data for clinical documentation
c: Creating fictional patient case studies for medical student education purposes
Automatically generating human-readable plan summaries from structured DICOM RT data for clinical documentation
Translating treatment plan parameters into machine code for the linear accelerator
Writing research papers about treatment planning techniques without human input
aiml.e: AI-generated plan narratives use NLP to convert structured RT plan data (beam arrangements, dose distributions, DVH statistics) into standardized clinical summaries, reducing documentation burden on physicians.

aiml.q: What is AI-powered peer review in radiation therapy treatment planning?
aiml.a: Automated identification of plan quality outliers and potential errors by comparing against learned standards from historical plans
c: A system where two AI models review each other's predictions without any human involvement
Automated identification of plan quality outliers and potential errors by comparing against learned standards from historical plans
An online forum where physicists vote on the best treatment plan for each case
Manual review of AI-generated contours by a single physician before clinical use
aiml.e: AI peer review systems learn from databases of reviewed plans to flag potential errors, suboptimal plan quality, or deviations from institutional standards, supporting the human peer review process.

aiml.q: What advantage does multi-modal AI (combining imaging, clinical, and genomic data) offer for radiation therapy outcome prediction?
aiml.a: It captures complementary information across data types, improving predictive accuracy beyond any single modality alone
c: It only uses whichever single data modality has the highest individual accuracy
It captures complementary information across data types, improving predictive accuracy beyond any single modality alone
It reduces the total amount of data needed for training by eliminating redundant modalities
It eliminates the need for feature selection since all data is equally informative
aiml.e: Multi-modal fusion integrates imaging features (tumor radiomics), clinical variables (stage, comorbidities), and genomic markers (gene expression signatures) to capture different aspects of treatment response that no single modality fully represents.

aiml.q: What is the potential role of quantum computing in AI for radiation therapy?
aiml.a: Accelerating optimization problems such as treatment plan optimization and molecular simulation for radiobiology
c: Replacing all classical computers in radiation therapy departments within the next year
Accelerating optimization problems such as treatment plan optimization and molecular simulation for radiobiology
Performing standard image segmentation tasks faster than GPUs for all clinical cases
Eliminating the need for treatment planning by directly computing the optimal beam delivery
aiml.e: Quantum computing may accelerate combinatorial optimization (beam angle, fractionation selection), molecular dynamics for radiobiological modeling, and certain ML training tasks, though practical clinical applications remain largely theoretical.

aiml.q: In AI-based patient scheduling optimization for radiation therapy, what is the primary objective?
aiml.a: Minimizing patient wait times and maximizing machine utilization while respecting clinical priority and treatment constraints
c: Scheduling all patients at the same time to maximize throughput regardless of treatment complexity
Minimizing patient wait times and maximizing machine utilization while respecting clinical priority and treatment constraints
Assigning patients randomly to treatment slots to ensure fairness across all individuals
Scheduling only based on physician availability without considering machine or room constraints
aiml.e: AI scheduling algorithms use reinforcement learning or constraint optimization to balance competing objectives: minimizing delays for urgent cases, reducing patient wait times, and maximizing linac utilization.

aiml.q: How can AI chatbots be used for patient education in radiation therapy?
aiml.a: Providing personalized, on-demand answers to common treatment questions while escalating complex concerns to clinical staff
c: Replacing all in-person consultations between patients and radiation oncologists
Providing personalized, on-demand answers to common treatment questions while escalating complex concerns to clinical staff
Making treatment decisions on behalf of patients who are unable to attend clinic visits
Generating treatment consent forms without any physician review or approval
aiml.e: AI chatbots can address frequently asked questions about RT side effects, treatment logistics, and preparation instructions 24/7, improving patient experience while reducing repetitive workload on clinical staff.

aiml.q: What is the primary challenge of using AI for electron therapy planning?
aiml.a: Complex dose distributions with sharp falloff and lateral scatter make accurate prediction more difficult than for photon beams
c: Electron therapy is no longer used clinically so AI development has stopped entirely
Complex dose distributions with sharp falloff and lateral scatter make accurate prediction more difficult than for photon beams
Electron beams have perfectly uniform dose distributions that require no optimization
AI cannot process the small field sizes typically used in electron therapy
aiml.e: Electron beams exhibit complex dose distributions due to large-angle scattering, surface irregularities, and tissue heterogeneities, presenting a more challenging prediction problem for AI than megavoltage photon beams.

aiml.q: What is the primary advantage of AI for treatment outcome reporting in radiation therapy?
aiml.a: Automated, standardized extraction and reporting of outcomes data across patients, enabling consistent registry population and quality metrics
c: Eliminating the need to follow up with patients after treatment completion
Automated, standardized extraction and reporting of outcomes data across patients, enabling consistent registry population and quality metrics
Generating outcomes data without ever treating the patient using purely predictive modeling
Reporting only positive treatment outcomes while filtering out adverse events automatically
aiml.e: AI automates outcome data extraction from clinical records using NLP and structured data mining, enabling consistent population of treatment registries and quality reporting across large patient cohorts.

aiml.q: What is the primary architecture used in most auto-segmentation models for organ-at-risk delineation?

c: U-Net with encoder-decoder structure
Fully connected feedforward network
Recurrent neural network (LSTM)
Support vector machine classifier
aiml.e: U-Net's encoder-decoder structure with skip connections is the dominant architecture for medical image segmentation, enabling precise pixel-level organ delineation.

aiml.q: During auto-segmentation commissioning, what metric quantifies the spatial overlap between predicted and ground-truth contours?

c: Mean absolute error (MAE)
Dice similarity coefficient (DSC)
Root mean square deviation
Pearson correlation coefficient
aiml.e: The Dice similarity coefficient measures the overlap between two contour sets, ranging from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect agreement).

Achievable DVH parameter ranges

aiml.q: What is the key geometric feature that RapidPlan uses to estimate organ sparing potential?

c: Beam's eye view projections
CT number histogram of target
Overlap volume histogram (OVH)
Patient surface mesh distance

aiml.q: What distinguishes deep learning image reconstruction (DLIR) from iterative reconstruction in CT?

c: DLIR uses trained neural networks
DLIR applies analytic back-projection

c: Higher patient radiation dose needed
Faster reconstruction with less noise
Eliminates need for raw projection data
Removes all image artifacts entirely

aiml.q: In radiomics, what does the term 'feature extraction' refer to?

c: Manually drawing regions of interest
Quantifying image texture and shape
Removing noise from medical images
Segmenting tumors from normal tissue
aiml.e: Radiomics feature extraction involves computing quantitative descriptors of image intensity, texture, shape, and wavelet properties from defined regions of interest.

c: Dosiomics only uses PET image data
Dosiomics extracts features from dose maps
Dosiomics ignores spatial information

aiml.q: In the context of AI-based QA, what does a virtual EPID model predict?

aiml.q: What FDA regulatory classification applies to most AI-based medical devices in radiation therapy?

aiml.q: Under the EU AI Act, how would an AI-based auto-contouring tool for radiation therapy most likely be classified?

aiml.q: What is federated learning in the context of medical physics AI?

c: Combining multiple model predictions
Centralized training on cloud servers
Training models without sharing patient data
Transferring pretrained model weights
aiml.e: Federated learning trains AI models across multiple institutions by sharing only model updates (gradients), keeping patient data local and private.

aiml.q: What is a key advantage of federated learning over centralized training for multi-institutional studies?

Patient data remains at each institution

Site-specific selection and spectrum bias
Algorithmic convergence instability

aiml.a: Classifying images as real or synthetic
